text: Brief Hospital Course: When ___ was admitted to the floor, vitals were t:98.9 bp:150/97 p:113 rr:22 SaO295% RA. He complained of ongoing cough and wheezing and moderate dyspnea on exertion though breathing was comfortable at rest. He was started on antibiotics- azithromycin and ceftriaxone. . At around 11PM, patient was triggerred for increased SOB requiring more oxygen supplement, O2 Sat 84% on RA improved to low 90% on 4L which then improved to mid 90% on NRB. He received lasix 40 mg IV, nebs, and Solumedrol 40 IV x1. At around midnight, patient got up to the bathroom. He was noted to have borderline temp at 100.3, BP 148/94, HR 120, RR 20. As he was returning from the bathroom, he felt more SOB with O2Sat down to 70% on RA which improved to 96% on NRB. Then 50 % on NRB. Code blue was called. Patient was intubated given his respiratory distress on ___ and underwent femoral vein catheterization. Subsequently, he underwent CTA to rule out for PE. PE was ruled out, but patient was found to have pulmonary edema and multifocal pneumonia. He was started on Vancomycin 1000mg IV q12 and Cefepime 2g IV q8. Of note, a TTE was done which revealed EF 40%, approximately his baseline. Patient was extubated on ___ and was saturating well on 3L in the mid ___. He continued to wheeze and was started on Advair and Ipratropium and Albuterol nebulizers. His infectious work up was unrevealing, and came back negative for EBV, aspergillus, influenza, parainfluenza, RSV, adenovirus, legionella, H. flu and C.diff. He was started on Azithromycin 500mg ___ on ___. He was transferred to the floor on ___ where he was oxygenating well on room air to the high ___. However, he was still wheezing a lot, so he was started on Solumedrol 30mg IV ___. On steroids, the wheezing improved significantly. On ___, Vancomycin and Cefepime were discontinued and Levofloxacin 750mg ___ qd was started. The Solumedrol was discontinued and Prednisone 30mg ___ qd was started. ___ remained afebrile throughout his admission on the floor. He was no longer wheezing and breathing comfortably without any oxygen requirements. In regards to his bright red blood per rectum, he did not have another such episode. The lower GI bleed was thought to be secondary to hemorrrhoids.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with bleeding from your rectum. At that time, your blood counts were within normal limits and you were in stable condition. When you were on the floor, you had respiratory distress and stopped breathing on your own. At that time, you were transferred to the intensive care unit and had a tube in your throat to help you breathe for 3 days. During that time, you had a CAT scan of your chest which showed pulmonary edema (fluid in your lungs) and a widespread pneumonia in your lungs. You were started on antibiotics intravenously (Vancomycin and Cefepime) for the pneumonia and Lasix (a fluid pill) to remove the water from your lungs. An echocardiogram was taken of your heart which showed that your heart function was stable. Your responded very well to the treatment and you the tube was removed from your throat on ___. You were wheezing a lot and were started on Albuterol, Ipratropium, and and Advair nebulizer treatments to help with your breathing. Your were also started on Azithromycin, another antibiotic. On ___, you were transfered from the intensive care unit to to the floor. You were still wheezing a lot, so we started you on intravenous steroids which reduced the wheezing. Since on the floor, you have remained afebrile.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female history of CAD (medically managed), HTN, TIA, recurrent diffuse large B-cell lymphoma, CKD (baseline ___, diet-controlled DM2, who presents with several days of episodic dyspnea and productive cough. #Dyspnea, productive cough: Ms ___ presented with 4 days of fatigue, paroxysms of productive cough, dyspnea, and wheezing. She had chest and abdominal soreness related to coughing; no fevers, or hemoptysis. She was well appearing, without tachycardia, fever or hypoxemia, and her exam was only positive for expiratory wheezing (patient not on long acting beta 2 agonist or steroids at home). Labs were notable for: lack of leukocytosis, and elevated D-dimer; flu swab negative. CXR without evidence of pneumonia or pulmonary edema and LENIs were negative. Her constellation of symptoms was most consistent with a viral respiratory infection, likely bronchitis given lack of nasal/sinus symptoms. Antibiotics were not given. She was given symptomatic treatment with benzonatate, dextromethorphan-guaifenesin, Tylenol, and ipratropium/albuterol nebs PRN. Because her asthma symptoms suggested she had moderate persistent asthma, she was given a prescription for Advair HFA and she will follow up with her primary care physician. #Elevated D-dimer: Patient's D-dimer elevated at 3124 on presentation. Given her complaint of dyspnea and chest pain, this was initially concerning for possible PE. However, symptoms seem infectious in nature and ___, EKG and CXR are negative. She has no risk factors like recent surgery or bedrest, no prior DVT/PE, no hemoptysis, and although she has a history of recurrent lymphoma she is not currently under treatment for an active malignancy. She remained afebrile, heart rate was normal and she was not hypoxemic on room air at rest or on ambulation. Further, there are alternative causes to explain her elevated D-dimer like infection (although she lacks SIRS criteria), chronic renal failure and underlying cardiovascular disease. Because of this, she was not started on empiric anticoagulation and V/Q scan (bc she would not have CTA in setting of CKD) was deferred. #Hypertension BP elevated on admission but improved during the day. She was continued on her home regimen: -metoprolol succinate 75mg PO daily -HCTZ 12.5mg PO daily -losartan 50mg PO daily #CAD She was continued on home aspirin, metoprolol, and statin. #History of gastritis: We reduced her home dose of ranitidine to 150mg PO daily from BID given CKD. #Chronic pain The patient has chronic pain due to arthritis. Her pain is mostly in her lower back and in her knees. She is on the narcotics contract for Percocet, which she takes up to three times daily. However, she only necessitated Tylenol PRN to control her pain during hospitalization. #CKD: Patient with hx of CKD, likely due to HTN and DM. BUN/Cr monitored, and nephrotoxic agents were avoided/medications were renally dosed TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== [ ] She was started on Advair given her moderate persistent asthma symptoms. She can discuss this with her primary care provider on follow up appointment on ___. [ ] Ranitidine was decreased from BID to daily due to CKD. [ ] Blood cultures collected in ED: 1 aerobic bottle grew GPCs in clusters. Most likely contaminant as patient has no leukocytosis, remained afebrile, and had no other concerning symptoms. Primary team will follow up final results. Patient was informed of the result and consulted that should she become febrile, have rigors, chills or aches, or any other concerning symptom, she should return to the ED or call primary care provider.
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of shortness of breath, productive cough and an abnormal lab. After imaging, and monitoring you overnight, it is believed that your shortness of breath, and your cough are due to a viral respiratory illness that inflamed your lungs giving you "bronchitis." This most likely worsened your underlying asthma also and is why you have been needing your inhalers more frequently. A chest X Ray was done, which did not show any evidence of pneumonia. You also had ultrasounds done of your legs, which did not show any clots. Because you had been using your asthma inhaler frequently, we are discharging you with a daily inhaler to use to prevent asthma symptoms. You can discuss continuing this medication with Dr. ___ at your next appointment, which is on ___ at 1:40pm (see below for details). There was also one blood culture bottle out of four that was positive for bacteria. We think that this finding is likely a contaminant from your skin. You do not have a fever, or elevated white blood cell count, and do not think you have this bacteria in your blood.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with multiple medical problems with subacute dyspnea, likely ___ URI -> viral bronchitis. . # Dyspnea -Most likely etiology is viral URI, progressing to viral bronchitis. Poor baseline pulmo substrate given underlying restrictive physiology ___ osteoporosis -> kyphosis) and sarcoid. Outpt pulmonary evaluation attributed chronic exertional dyspnea to body habitus, obesity, dCHF, and restrictive pattern related to history of sarcoid. ___ also identifies significant deconditioning and suggests candidacy for pulmonary rehabilitation. Pt presented afebrile and non-hypoxic. She was started on nebulizer therapy albuterol and ipratropium, continued home advair. Her symptoms also improved with guaifenisen. Peak flow stable in low 300s. Regarding VTE risk, patient has supratherapeutic INR, and has been therapeutic per her report. Patient does have IVC filter not documented elsewhere, per patient placed many years ago; this raises possibility of chronic venous thromboembolism as etiology of pulmonary artery hypertension, which also could be contributing to dyspnea however pt was noted to be nontachycardic, sat'ing 95-98% on RA. CXR shows left basilar atelectasis, no clear infiltrate, and is consistent with prior films- do not suspect underlying pneumonia given lack of fever, leukocytosis. No chest pain, and normal nuclear stress recently made myocardial ischemia unlikely. On HD #2 she was started on PO prednisone taper (60mg x 3, 40mg x4, 20mg x 4 and 10mg x3) for facilitated recovery of URI given her poor pulmonary substrate. She was observed 48hours on steroids and felt much improved at time of discharge. Observed ambulating - O2 sat 98% on RA after walking. Discharged home with nebulizer to continue therapy w albuterol and ipratropium. Plan to complete course of steroids at home. Covered by ISS in house. ___ noted to range from 120-270 on 60mg prednisone. Started on metformin 500mg BID for home coverage of hyperglycemia. Given clinical stability and improvement w nebs administration, no clinical indication for antibiotics. . # A. fib: s/p AV node ablation and PPM placement. INR supratherapeutic on admission. Dose was held on admission and restarted when INR 2.0 - pt restarted on home dose of 4mg daily with 5mg M and F. INR was monitored daily. No dose adjustment was made as pt was not started on abx. Device interrogated ___, showed complete AV block and underlying a. fib. . # Hypothyroidism- cont levothyroxine home dose. . # HTN- continued on home atenolol. . # Osteoporosis c/b compression fractures, thoracic kyphosis- continued home vit D, calcium . # Pancreatic insufficiency- continued home pancrealipase . # Type II DM- not on home meds; SSI here while on steroids. dc'd on temporary metformin regimen. . # Back pain- continued home pain regimen hydrocodone/APAP Q6 PRN . # FEN: No IVF, replete electrolytes, cardiac/diabetic diet # Prophylaxis: Subcutaneous heparin, no indication for ppi, kept on home bowel regimen # Access: peripherals # Code: full
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for bronchitis and shortness of breath. We believe that you are having difficulty breathing due to a viral infection that began last week prior to your admission. It is likely that your underlying lung disease contributes to your difficulty breathing even when you contract a mild cold or upper respiratory illness. Your symptoms were alleviated by nebulizer treatments. Please continue at home. Your chest xray and lung exam do not indicate that you have a pneumonia or require any antibiotics. Given your underlying lung disease, you were started on a course of steroids to help decrease inflammation and speed recovery. This is a taper and will need to be continued after discharge. You need to treat your blood sugars which will be temporarily elevated with metformin in the interim.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Productive cough and SOB: ABG pH 7.35 pCO2 44 pO266 in ED. No h/o fevers or chills. Saw temporary improvement with inhalers and a short course of PO steroids two weeks ago. CXR and CTA showing no PNA, PE, or dissection. Likely COPD exacerbation, although could not exclude bronchitis, GERD. Pt improved slowly on high dose steroids (received Prednisone 60 mg in the ED, Solumedrol 125 mg IV on hospital day #1, then continued on Prednisone 60 mg qdaily for the rest of hospital stay), albuterol/atrovent nebs, and azithromycin. Cough was persistent and especially severe in the morning. PFTs were done which showed mild-moderate obstructive pattern. He was sent home with Rx for steroid taper over 10 days, 2 more days of azithromycin to complete a course of 5 days, combivent inhaler, and spiriva. On the day of discharge, he was able to maintain O2 sat 93-95% at rest and 92-94% on ambulation on room air. He will follow up in resident clinic in early ___. . # Smoking cessation: Pt showed strong interest in quitting smoking. He was maintained on Nicotine patch during his hospital stay and was given an Rx for use as outpatient. He was given a phone number and web address to help quit smoking, and he will follow up as outpatient on his progress. . # H/o EtOH abuse: last drink was the day prior to admission. Smell of alcohol in breath. Pt stated that he drinks rarely now, only ~6 times in the last year. Pt was maintained on CIWA with Valium 5 mg Q4 hrs PRN for CIWA>10. He only required two doses of Valium during his hospital stay with maximum CIWA score of 10. . # Eosinophilia on admission labs: unknown etiology. Absolute eosinophilia 660. No h/o atopy other than ?hay fever as a child. It was unknown whether pt was exposed to mold/fungus at ___ where he lives. There was no evidence of PNA on CTA. The eosinophilia had resolved when CBC with diff was repeated next day. . # Diffuse abdominal pain: may be gastritis, GERD symptoms, or related to recent EtOH drinking. Resolved with prilosec 20 mg PO daily.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath. It was likely a flare of chronic obstructive pulmonary disease. We treated you with steroids, nebulizers and antibiotic. You should continue to take Prednisone for 10 more days as directed. You should also take 2 more days of Azithromycin (antibiotic) to complete a course of 5 days. We are giving you prescriptions for Spiriva (taken once a day), and Combivent inhalers (taken as needed, up to 4 times a day). We encourage you to stop smoking. Smoking cessation will prevent your lung disease from getting worse. There are several resources to help you quit smoking. On the Web: ___/ or on the phone: ___ or ___. We are also giving you a prescription for nicotine patches that can help with cravings.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ yo male with h/o asthma CLL s/p recent chemo and recurrent pneumonias, including a recent admission during which he was treated with Zosyn x 3 days, then 7d course of Moxifloxacin, now readmitted for persistent shortness of breath. . # SOB/cough: Pt was initially started on Zosyn as it was unclear if pt failed on outpt regimen. However, pt appeared clinically well and continued to improve, so Zosyn was discontinued the next day. Pt continued to be afebrile. Sputum culture only showed commensal respiratory flora. Pt was discharged to finish a 7d course of antibiotics. Pt is being followed by outpt ID regarding his recurrent pneumonias. . # CLL: Pt is followed by Dr. ___ as outpt, last chemo was Fludarabine/Cytoxan/Rituxan on ___. Pt was continued on home Acyclovir, Bactrim SS, Allopurinol. Per primary Onc team recommendation, pt was given IVIG during this admission. However, only half the dose was given as pt developed allergica reaction with shortness of breath and stridor. Infusion was stopped and pt was given Salumederol. Pt recovered well. Pt was instructed with Dr. ___ after discharge. . # Diarrhea: Pt had diarrhea ___ loose stools per day) that was resolving upon admission. C diff was sent and was negative. . # HTN: Was well-controlled. Pt was continued home Atenolol. . # BPH: Pt was continued on home Tamsulosin. . # Asthma: Pt was wheezy on exam, but satting well on RA. Pt was continued on home Fluticasone-Salmeterol and Albuterol PRN. . # Pt was on a regular diet and on SC Heparin for DVT ppx. Pt was full code.
summary: You were admitted because you had unresolved cough and shortness of breath after being treated for a pneumonia. You were initially started on an IV antibiotic, however, it was soon discontinud because you looked well, your symptoms were resolving and the chest X ray showed improved pneumonia. You can continue your course of Moxifloxacin at home. Also, you were also given IVIG during this admission, however, only half the dose was administered because you started developing shortness of breath. It is important that you follow-up with Dr. ___ soon to determine if you need further dosing of this IVIG.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F w/ PMH of IDDM with chronic abdominal pain who presents with fever and cough s/p central line placement at ___. # Fever: CXR clear, U/A negative (urine culture only showed vaginal flora contaminant), blood cultures are negative/pending, and no other obvious source for her fevers. She may have a viral bronchitis given her respiratory symptoms and her lung exam, which has predisposed her to an asthma exacerbation. The line also appeared clear, decreasing the suspicion for a line infection. #Asthma Exacerbation: Patient has a history of asthma and was in the midst of an exacerbation on arrival to our floor. She was placed on standing nebulizers, in addition to oral prednisone. She was convinced to take her oral prednisone 40 mg Daily during her admission, and her wheezing improved significantly. Her plan was for a total 7 day course of prednisone for treatment of an asthma exacerbation. #Diabetes: Patient has a history of DKA in ___ setting of actue illness. She never developed and AG while in house, but after starting the prednisone, she developed blood sugars into the 400s. As such, she was made to remain in the hospital until her U500 scale could be adjusted as listed in the discharge medications. On this new regimen, her blood sugars resolved into the 100s. # Guardianship: Guardian is ___ ___ patient had asked to leave against medical advice, but could not do so given the concern for the medical team for futher precipitation of DKA in the setting of prednisone therapy for asthma exacerbation. Eventually her blood sugar improved with the change in her U500 regimen recommended by ___. PENDING LABS ___ Blood culture x 2 PND
summary: You were admitted because you had fevers at home after the placement of a central tunneled line in your neck. We obtained blood cultures from you which did not show any evidence of infection, and your chest x-ray also did not show any evidence of infection. We spoke to your providers at ___ they had also performed a work-up of your fevers, and did not find any evidence of infection. Because we were unable to find any evidence of infection, you are safe to leave the hospital. You were having an asthma exacerbation, and you improved immensely on oral steroids; the steroids did cause your blood sugars to rise, and so the ___ team made recommendations for your U500 dosing if you continue to take your steroids, as we strongly recommend.

Her plan was for a total 7 day course of prednisone for treatment of an asthma exacerbation. #Diabetes: Patient has a history of DKA in ___ setting of actue illness. She never developed and AG while in house, but after starting the prednisone, she developed blood sugars into the 400s. As such, she was made to remain in the hospital until her U500 scale could be adjusted as listed in the discharge medications. On this new regimen, her blood sugars resolved into the 100s. # Guardianship: Guardian is ___ ___ patient had asked to leave against medical advice, but could not do so given the concern for the medical team for futher precipitation of DKA in the setting of prednisone therapy for asthma exacerbation. Eventually her blood sugar improved with the change in her U500 regimen recommended by ___. PENDING LABS ___ Blood culture x 2 PND

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ y.o. man with history of asthma and tobacco abuse presenting with exacerbation of his chronic cough and increasing dyspnea consistent with an asthma vs COPD exacerbation. 1) Asthma exacerbation: The patient presented with exacerbation of his chronic cough as well as dyspnea likely due to asthma exacerbation. Given his benign chest radiograph, wheezing, and eosinophilia asthma exacerbation was considered the most likely diagnosis. He briefly desaturated with a paroxysm of coughing in the ED but then recovered and remained without further hypoxia at rest. He was treated with prednisone burst, standing bronchodilators, montleukast, and corticosteroid inhaler with good effect on his dyspnea and cough. Attempts were made to better manage possible element of COPD and his allergic rhinitis (see below) and he was discharged on standing ipratroprium/albuterol inhaler for five days, to complete a five day course of prednisone, and with instructions to continue fluticasone inhaler, montleukast, and his home Levalbuterol PRN until follow up with pulmonary. He will follow up with pulmonary as an outpatient to work on better asthma control and also for assessment of PFTs to help detect any element of coexistent COPD and manage that as well. 2) ?COPD: The patient has a long smoking history and though his history of childhood asthma and eosinophilia suggest this presentation more likely due to an asthma flare he may have coexistent COPD. Therefore, when he complained of increased sputum production on hospital day 3 he was started on a five day course of azithromycin as if for a chronic bronchitis flare. He also was discharged on anticholinergic as well as beta agonist inhaler therapy. Otherwise treatment of COPD and Asthma flares are essentially identical. He will follow up with pulmonary for better evaluation of the reversiblity of his reactive airway disease. 3) Allergic Rhinitis: The patient has chronic postnasal drip that likely is contributing to cough and he reports exposure to allergens seemed to have been part of his worsening. He was on azelastine nasal spray, which was continued. He was also started on fluticasone nasal spray and cetirizine with some effect though it was much to early to see full nasal steroid benefit at time of discharge. He will follow up with his ENT physician and allergist. 4) Tobacco Abuse: The patient was cautioned multiple times on the dangers of cigarette smoking as a trigger for his asthma as well as potentially contributing to more permanent damage to his lungs. He expressed an intent to quit. 5) Cough syncope: During a severe paroxysm of coughing the patient was noted to have an episode of syncope in the presence of the medical attending. Regained consciousness in <5 seconds spontaneously and was completely stable, not confused, and without any deficits. He reports this has happened approximately two times before. Patient was strongly cautioned on being aware of this and making sure to sit down or avoid dangerous situations until cough is better controlled. He knows he should sit down or pull over his car should he have severe coughing fits. 6) His hypertension was stable on his home amlodipine and metoprolol 7) GERD: He was continued on home ranitidine and omeprazole. He was discharged to follow up with his PCP and pulmonary clinic. Transitional Issues: -Leukocytosis: The patient had a leukocytosis with neutrophil predominance without bands at the time of discharge. This was thought most likely due to steroids. He will have this rechecked at PCP follow up.
summary: You were admitted to the ___ because you have had much worse coughing recently, with some shortness of breath. Your cough has been occurring for months. We believe that this is due to an exacerbation of asthma, most likely complicated by allergic factors and possibly underlying lung disease. Congratulations on stopping smoking. If you were to continue to smoke, it is likely that you would have more frequent lung issues, and any underlying lung disease would only get worse. While in the hospital, you received oral steroids (Prednisone), which should help to bring down the inflammation in your lungs. You should take the prednisone (40mg by mouth) through ___. While in the hospital, we took you on a walk test down the ___, during which you breathed normally. We have also started you on Singulair, Flonase and a fluticasone inhaler, all of which should improve your breathing and potentially improve your cough. We also believe that you should start on Zyrtec as well. For your home albuterol inhaler, you should use a spacer, which improves the delivery to your lungs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: HOSPITAL COURSE: ___ F with unclear underlying lung disease presenting with acute worsening of chronic respiratory distress and cough; noted to be hyopxic on arrival to ED. Initially admitted to the MICU where she received antibiotics and steroids and hypoxia improved to requirement of 3L by NC; she was called out to the general medical floor ___. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Respiratory distress/hypoxia: Initial differential diagnosis broad. Flu ruled out with DFA. Pneumonia considered, but patient was afebrile on admission and CXR without focal consolidation. Nonetheless, Azithromycin and Levaquin given (patient completed 7-day course of Levaquin). Reflux esophagitis and aspiration investigated with video swallow and barium swallow. CT chest concerning for ground-glass opacities. Pulmonology was consulted, but no bronchoscopy was pursued. However, she had a vasculitis work-up that was negative. Eosinophilic pneumonitis was considered, especially in the context of elevated eosinophils on differential and elevated IgE (___). She improved with steroids, Levaquin, albuterol nebs, and multiple medications to suppress her cough (Guaifenesin + Codeine syrup, Guaifenesin 600mg PO BID, Benzonatate 200mg TID). She was weaned to room air by HD6, and was satting >96% at time of discharge. Her lung exam was notable for poor air movement throughout admission, but air movement continued to improve with incentive spirometer, cough suppression, and steroids. It was recommended that the patient be discharged to rehab for re-conditioning prior to returning home, but she declined. She also declined ___ services. She was discharged on prednisone 50mg daily with Bactrim SS 1 tab daily for PCP ___. # Cough: Patient with dry/chronic cough, but cough was her biggest complaint, and also contributed substantially to her shortness of breath. Cough was controlled as indicated above. After cough was better controlled, patient's respiratory status dramatically improved. # Chest pain: Likely secondary to chronic cough. Chest pain was controlled with Tylenol, Ibuprofen, and Oxycodone. As her cough improved, so did her chest pain. # Loss to follow up: Patient has been set up with multiple follow up appointments in the past that she never attended. However, she requested follow-up with Dr. ___ this admission, and it was reiterated how important follow-up is in terms of clarifying her diagnosis and preventing frequent hospital admissions. She was also seen by social work. INACTIVE ISSUES: # Code: full
summary: You returned to the hospital because of increased difficulty breathing, and cough with chest pain. You were initially admitted to the ICU, but your breathing was better after starting antibiotics and steroids, so you were transferred to the floor. On the floor, your cough was better controlled with anti-cough medications, and your oxygen requirement decreased over a few days. At the time of discharge, your cough was much improved, you had good levels of oxygen in your blood, and your chest pain was slightly better. Although the team and physical therapists suggested you go to rehabilitation before returning home, you declined, stating you planned to get stronger at your daughter's house prior to returning to ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment: ___ with HCV, autoimmune hepatitis (stable on IFN, ribavirin, prednisone) and mild obstructive lung disease admitted ___ with 1 week of worsening cough, daily fevers. Patient was also noted to have a worsening incarcerated L inguinal hernia. # Fever/Cough: Initially thought to be due to acute bacterial respiratory infection on chronic lung disease, although differential was broad given patient's immunocompromise. Patient was started on azithromycin, ipratropium, and albuterol nebs. The patient was febrile overnight and was changed to levofloxacin on HD2. She continued to spike fevers with no improvement in cough or lung exam despite antibiotics; however, her vitals remained stable without evidence of SIRs physiology. Her WBC remained within normal limits as well but was difficult to interpret since she is on interferon and prednisone. Chest CT was obtained ___ which showed a peripheral tree and ___ pattern most consistent with acute bronchitis ddx including viral, bacterial, MAC, PCP, or fungal causes. Numerous infectious disease studies were sent. Her sputum gram stain and culture were negative for bacteria, her sputum PCP DFA was negative, induced sputum AFB stains negative x 2 for ___, urine legionella negative and nasal swab for rsv, flu and paraflu were negative. Patient's U/A was also negative. Her abdominal CT initially showed gallbladder enlargement and pericholecystic fluid but U/S showed no evidence of acute cholecystitis or abscess. Medications, including her interferon/ribavirin was considered as another possible cause for her fatigue, less likely to cause fevers, and also considered as a less likely but possible cause of her lung disease (bronchiolitis obliterans is a rare adverse effect). ID was consulted and recommended bronchoscopy to rule out PCP or MAC, despite negative sputum tests, given patient's immunocompromised state and also recommended continuing levofloxacin empiric treatment for 5 days. On HD6, patient reported subjective improvement in her illness, her fevers were low grade with no spikes throughout the day, and decreased cough with improved lung exam. She went for bronchoscopy ___ and tolerated the procedure well. BAL fluid showed no evidence of infection (gram stain, PCP DFA and AFB negative) and cultures were no growth to date on ___. . # Inguinal hernia: Patient's inguinal hernia worsened on HD2 likely secondary to increased intraabdominal pressure from coughing. She was transferred to surgery ___ for L inguinal hernia repair with mesh under general anethesia. She tolerated the procedure well and was transferred back to medicine ___ with her incision remained clean dry and intact and pain controlled without any medication. She will follow up in surgery clinic in two weeks. . # Chronic liver disease (HCV, autoimmune hepatitis, PBC): Appears stable (no jaundice, varices, or uremic sx, LFTs wnl, abdominal CT and U/S negative) and last HCV viral load undetectable ___. Following hepatology recs from ___ patient was continued on her interferon, ribavirin, prednisone and her ursodiol for primary biliary cirrhosis. . # CODE: FULL # CONTACT: Daughter ___ # DISPO: Home with PCP, ___, and Hepatology f/u.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for fever and cough. You had several blood tests and imaging studies. Your CT scan of the chest showed a bronchitis and bronchiolitis. You were treated with antibiotics, and your fever improved. Infectious disease was consulted and recommended a test for viruses which was so far negative. You underwent bronchoscopy which showed no source of the infection at time of discharge. You will need to have a repeat CT scan in ___ weeks to compare your lung findings. Please discuss this with your primary care doctor. During your hospitalization, you also developed an inguinal hernia due to coughing. You underwent hernia repair with the surgery team.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT & PLAN: ___ h/o HTN, DM2 who presents with a complaint of fall and shortness of breath, found to have hyponatremia. # Hyponatremia: Ms. ___ was admitted with Na 126. This was attributed to a combination factors: prerenal given FeNa 0.2%, presence of ketones in urine (suggesting poor PO intake), rising BUN and Cr. This was further aggravated by chlorthalidone which may have confounded the urine Na results on presentation - and creating a pseudo-SIADH picture. CT and CXR generally unremarkable. She was given IVF and recheck of the Na increased to 131. Additional workup including TSH - which was normal. Adrenal insufficiency was felt unlikely given absence of hypotension, hyperkalemia. There were also no new medications. She was instructed to hold off on chlorthalidone, but to continue to take the other anti-hypertensive medications. She should follow up with her PCP for consideration of other anti-hypertensive alternatives. # Dyspnea: unclear etiology. CXR mostly unremarkable except for small bilateral pleural effusions. She was mildly hypoxic on presentation but improved over the course of the hospitalization. There was no desaturation with ambulation. She was treated with nebs with good effect. The dyspnea was attributed to reactive airway disease in setting of recent construction/poor ventilation in her living situation. She was given albuterol inhalers on discharge. # Hypertension: will continue home atenolol and amlodipine. Chlorthalidone was held for the time being. # Diabetes: metformin was held and she was treated with insulin sliding scale in the hospital. # Hypothyroidism: continue home levothyroxine # DVT ppx: Heparin SC # Diet: Regular diabetic # IV access: PIV # Precautions: None # Code status: Full, presumed, HCP was not present # Dispo: Home (without service needs)
summary: As you know, you were admitted with cough, shortness of breath - in the setting of construction being performed in your residence. Workup here (including chest x-ray, blood tests) showed no evidence of pneumonia. You were given inhalers while you in the hospital - and did not require any oxygen on discharge. You may consider taking albuterol inhalers, as needed, in times when you get short of breath (possibly from the exposure to dust and particles in the air). We would recommend staying at your daughter's house in the interim while the construction is completed at your home. You were largely admitted to the hospital for low sodium levels. This low sodium was attributed to significant dehydration - further compounded by the chlorthalidone medication. The sodium improved moderately after intravenous fluids were administered. In the meantime, we encourage usual fluid/salt intake and to hold off on taking the chlorthalidone until seeing your primary care doctor. We believe that the chlorthalidone (part of the atenolol/chlorthalidone combination pill) is responsible for the low salt. Please withhold from taking this combination pill and use atenolol alone (prescription was given).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted to the floor in a stable condition. She was treated for an acute asthma exacerbation secondary to upper respiratory tract infection. The patient had been on two courses of antibiotics and Prednisone, prescribed to her at the ___. On admission, her antibiotics were stopped, and chest x-ray showed no sings of PNA. A V/Q scan was obtained, showing no signs of PE. In addition, cardiac enzymes were negative for MI. The patient's EKG and telemetry showed no events. She was maintained on albuterol and ipratropium nebs, as well as 60mg Prednisone daily. Her O2 saturation remained >95% on RA throughout the duration of her admission. The patient's vital signs also were stable throughout her entire hospital course. The patient's blood glucose levels were erratic due to her prednisone treatment and dietary non-compliance with diabetic diet. It was noticed on Hospital Day 2 that the patient's husband was bringing her outside fast food and snacks which were not consistent with a low sugar diet. Thus, the patient's insulin scale was augmented, increasing her NPH to 45U in the am and 25U in pm, along with regular ISS. The remainder of her home medications were consistent. A pulmonary consultation was obtained, recommending a change in her home asthma medication and a continuation of Prednisone, in addition to Fluticasone nasal spray. ___ was arranged with Pulmonary and Sleep Medicine for ___. An Echocardiogram was also performed showing LVEF >55% and trace mitral regurgitation. Ms. ___ was discharged in a stable condition with PCP ___ with Dr. ___.
summary: You were admitted with worsening of your asthma after having an upper respiratory tract infection. You presented to the hospital with shortness of breath and left-sided chest pain. While in the hospital, we determined that you did not have pneumonia or blood clots in your lungs. Also, you were worked up for heart attack, and that was found to be negative. While in the hospital, you were treated for your asthma symptoms, with steroids and nebulilzers. Your blood sugars were monitored and found to be very high, so your insulin was adjusted accordingly. However, while on steroids, it is important to maintain good glycemic control. You need to also work on better dietary compliance, eating more diabetic-friendly foods. Physical therapy also worked with you to increase your ambulation and you were given a walker.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with a history of asthma who presents with tachypnea, likely an asthma exacerbation and anxiety. #. Asthma exacerbation: It was unclear what may have triggered an exacerbation in this patient, perhaps a viral URI that is now resolving and/or spring pollens. Her smoking likely exacerbated her prolonged symptoms. The morning following admission, the patient's symptoms had improved. She was given another 60 mg of prednisone and a prescription for 2 more days to complete a 5 day course. She received albuterol and ipratropium nebs Q4H while hospitalized as well. She was counseled to stop smoking and to use fluticasone for at least month as maintenance therapy to decrease the inflammation in her lungs. She was advised to establish care and follow-up with a physician to monitor her symptoms and adjust her medications accordingly. #. Anxiety: Although the patient initially denied anxiety, this was an issue for her overnight in the hospital. She received ativan 1 mg PO to help with sleep and anxiety. She later received guaifenesin with codeine x 1 for cough and anxiety. Social work was consulted to assist the patient. Outpatient primary care follow-up was arranged for the patient. #. Chest pain: The patient's chest pain was pleuritic in nature and reproduced with palpation. There was no evidence of pulmonary infection or PE on CXR and CTA. The patient was advised that her chest pain was most likely musculoskeletal and caused by frequent coughing spells over the past month and exacerbated by her hyperventilation. She was given tylenol 1 g Q6H prn and advised to use tylenol as needed at home for her symptoms.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for evaluation of shortness of breath. This was, in part, a result of an asthma exacerbation that may have been triggered by a viral infection or ___ pollens. Please stop smoking as smoke will continue to irritate your airways and make it more difficult for your lung tissue to heal. Your chest pain is likely musculoskeletal and related to your coughing episodes. Rapid breathing will make it more uncomfortable. If you find yourself breathing rapidly, use your albuterol inhaler and consciously try to breath more slowly. Your insurance company was contacted regarding your albuterol prescription so that you can refill it. You should take prednisone for a few more days (prescription provided). You should also start taking fluticasone, for at least the next month, to decrease the inflammation in your lungs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o F with hx of asthma and chronic pansinusitis who presents with three day history of shortness of breath. Treated for pneumonia, but all cultures negative. Bronchoscopy showed eosinophilia predominance. Most ___ diagnosis is ANCA (-) ___. . # ___ - based on esoinophils seen in bronchoscopy and negative cultures, is the most likely diagnosis. Did have history of severe sinusitis and questionable asthma. Biopsy from bronchoscopy was still pending at the time of discharge. With upper lobe opacities and recent steroids could be an rare infectious process like fungal or aspergillosis or silicosis, but based on all the negative smears and cultures, makes it less likely. Abx were stopped after three days, was monitored for fevers, and remained afebrile and stable. Was initially treated with IV steroids which were transitioned to orals while on the floor. She was sent out on a long 6+ week taper, which will be followed by her pulmonologist. She was started on bactrim for PCP ___. She will also need careful monitoring of her blood sugars while on steroids. Her PCP was called and we discussed outpatient plan before she was discharged. Evenutally will need PFTs and pulmonary follow up as an outpatient. . # Shortness of breath - see above, likely secondary to ___. Was satting fine on room air at the time of discharge. Continued albuterol and advair as an outpatient. ___ worked with the patient and was able to see that her ambulatory sats were within normal limits and she was moving around at her baseline. . # Sinusitis - on recent CT, patient with total opacification of sinuses. Is s/p steroids and cipro course for these with plan for outpatient ENT surgery this week. Has had long standing sinusitis and hx of psuedomonas. Sinusitis is at baseline and does not appear to be infected at this time. She will f/u with her ENT doctor as an outpatient to discuss the need for surgery for the impacted sinuses. . # Asthma - again, ___ related to ___, often precedes the diagnosis by ___ years (from Uptodate). Will continue with advair and albuterol nebs as needed. . # Elevated Blood sugars - likely was from the steroids, was on insulin sliding scale while an inpatient, but decided not to treat as an outpatient. She will follow up closely and have ___ to help check her sugars the first few days after she is discharged home. Hope with the taper that they will begin to normalize. . # Insomnia - likely a side effect of the steroids, trazadone was used for treatment. . # Discharge - was sent home in good condition, feeling well with close followup.
summary: You were admitted for 3 days of very severe shortness of breath. You were admitted to the Intensive Care Unit and monitored very closely. Initially it was thought you might have a pneumonia, so you started on antibiotics. The pulmonologists did a bronchoscopy and found that you had a large amount of cells called eosinophils. This is sometimes associated with a disease called ___ disease. ___ disease is sometimes associated with asthma and sinusitis. It is an autoimmune disease. The best treatment for this type of disease is steroids, so you will need to go home on a pill called prednisone. This pill has some side effects for which you will need to take even more medicines. Please start taking Calcium and Vitamin D. Also take Bactrim to prevent any other lung infections.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ yo M with PMH bronchiectasis, pacemaker ___ pericarditis, and asthma with recent history of progressive, episodic explosive diarrhea accompanied by abdominal pain, cold sweats, nausea, and shortness of breath. # Shortness of Breath - pt had 2 EKG's and 3 sets of CE which were all negative. He was kept on telemetry and had sinus bradycardia throughout admission that occasionally showed pacer spikes. In the ED, it was reported that he had a ventricular rate in the ___, so his pacer was interrogated and found to be working properly. He should follow up per routine with his cardiologist. # Diarrhea - Pt's symptoms had resolved by the time he reached the floor and they did not return during pt's brief hospital course. Differential included lactose intolerance, celiac, IBD, IBS, infection, pancreatic insufficiency, carcinoid, ___, and VIPoma. LFT's, CBC, lipase, and electrolytes were normal. Pt did not have BM during hospital stay, so stool tests were never performed. It was recommended that he could follow up with his PCP or GI to obtain stool tests. It was also suggested to check serum for fasting gastrin level and TSH, and urine for 5-HIAA. He was instructed to follow up with GI within 1 month. # Abdominal pain - work up as above for diarrhea. A CT abdomen/pelvis was done in ER that was normal except for a small area of perinephric stranding --> per radiology could be consistent with a prior kidney stone. A U/A was obtained that had small RBCs, so it was suggested that kidney stones could be causing these episodic abdominal pains. It was recommended to follow up with his PCP to obtain ___ non-con CT, bloodwork, and U/A to evaluate for kidney stones and hematuria and possibly obtain a referral to urology. Pain had resolved by the time of discharge. # Hematuria - found on U/A, which was collected after CT results showed perinephric stranding. Work up as outpatient with PCP ___ urology referral. # Asthma, chronic stable - continued on home meds # bronchiectasis - not active during this admission # Transition Issues: -Appt with PCP on ___ --> pt will call for sooner Appt -Appt with GI on ___ -Repeat non-con CT abd/pelvis, possible Urology referral for hematuria -repeat U/A, CHEM-7, get urine 5-HIAA -check fasting serum gastrin and TSH -Check stool studies for ova, parasites, C. diff, giardia, electrolytes, fat, occult blood, and WBCs. -follow up with cardiologist per routine
summary: You came to ___ because you were having abdominal pain, diarrhea, nausea, sweating, and shortness of breath. You had had episodes similar to this in the past. Because of your shortness of breath, we checked an EKG and blood tests which did not show any sign of a heart attack. A cardiologist also came to check your pacer and found it to be working normally. To evaluate your abominal pain, you got a CT with contrast, which only showed a small area of inflammation around your kidney which could be consistent with a prior kidney stone. Your urine sample also had a small amount of blood in it, so we considered that you might have had a kidney stone that caused your abdominal pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: A/P: ___. woman with history of HTN, DM and Asthma who presented with SOB, cough, and dizziness that could be related to asthma exacerbation. # Shortness of breath: Pt has history of asthma and her SOB in the setting of cleaning is likely an asthma exacerbation. While there was no documentation of wheezing on exam, her symptoms did improve with duonebs. On the floor patient had inspiratory crackles, which could occur in asthma. Pt did not appear fluid overloaded on exam and so we did not feel she required diuresis. The patient peak flow was 250. She was treated with nebulizers and continued on her home advair. The patient's respiratory symptoms improved over the course of her hospital stay and on the day of discharge she was back to her baseline. She was discharged on her home medications and told to follow up with her PCP. . # Pre-syncope: No dizziness this morning, but still with mild orthostasis. She was encouraged to take in fluids PO, but she continued to be orthostatic. When she was walked by physical therapy, her heart rate increased to the 130's. She was encouraged again to take PO at received a 1L NS bolus. On the day of discharge she was up and walking and her heart rate did not go above 100. Pt was not orthostatic at the time of discharge. She was encouraged to drink more fluid at home to avoid dehydration. . # Cold lower extremities: Pt has some discoloration of lower extremities and cold distal digits. She had thready, but palpable pulses of her lower extremities and doppler pulses were audible b/l. The patient's feet discoloration improved as her hospital stay continued. At the time of discharge, her feet were warm to touch, but slightly discolored still. . Acute Kidney injury: Pt creatinine is 1.2 with a baseline of 0.9. FeUrea 61% so not a complete pre-renal picture. She was given a 1L NS bolus and encouraged to drink fluids PO. Her creatinine at the time of discharge was still 1.2, but she was not having any urinary symptoms. She will follow up with her PCP and her creatinine should be trended at this time. . # Anemia/neutropenia: Pt has history, but her counts are stable at this time. Her PCP should ___ to monitor her CBC,
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath and dizziness. After an evaluation in the Emergency room and again on the floor it was felt that you were having a mild asthma exacerbation and was dehydrated. You were given nebulizer treatment for her asthma and encouraged to drink a lot of fluids. On the second day of your admission, you were walked by physical therapy and your heart rate became very fast. It was felt this was because you still needed to drink more fluids. We also had a catscan done to make sure there were no blood clots in your lungs since you had a fast heart rate and some difficulty breathing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old male with hypertension, hypothyroidism who presented with acute onset shortness of breath and wheezing after two doses of naprosyn for right hip pain. The wheezing and shortness of breath resolved with two albuterol nebulizer treatments in the ED, per the patient. #1. SOB/Wheezing: Possible etiologies for this included infection, pulmonary embolism, airway disease such as asthma, pulmonary edema, or allergic reaction to medication. Given the patient's presentation and history, the diagnosis that considered most likely was hyperreactive airway disease secondary to naprosyn use/allergy. -Chest xray on ___ was without evidence of pneumonia. V/Q scan suggestive of airway disease and low likelihood of pulmonary embolus. There was no pleuritic component to the patient's chest pain; no history to suggest DVT. -Troponins negative x 3, no EKG changes, no chest pain throughout admission. -Patient remained afebrile, had no leukocytosis, or anything in history or exam to suggest infectious process. -Given patient's history of allergies with wheezing and the fact that he was naprosyn naive, his symptoms were deemed likely airway reactivity secondary to this medication, especially since it resolved with albuterol and with discontinuation of the medication. -Patient may benefit from pulmonary function testing as outpatient and possible consideration for antihistamine treatment and albuterol inhaler prn for wheezing-will recommend this to patient's PCP and have informed patient of same. The patient was discharged with a prescription for albuterol inhaler for use as needed for wheezing. #2. Hypertension, Hypothyroidism: we continued the patient's home medications. Code: Full (discussed with patient) The patient was deemed medically fit for discharge to home on ___. He was scheduled for close follow-up with his primary care provider, Dr. ___, for ___.
summary: you were admitted to the hospital because of shortness of breath and wheezing. We think that you experienced these symptoms because of naprosyn use during the two days prior to admission. Given your history of hypersensitivity/allergies, it is likely that the airways in your lungs reacted to this medication, causing your symptoms. It is our recommendation that you do not use naprosyn and that you avoid the use of other non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs) such as ibuprofen (Advil, Motrin), and Aleve unless recommended by your primary care doctor, and even then with caution and attentiveness to your symptoms. We have added naprosyn as a drug allergy in your ___ medical record.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of CVID on IVIG weekly, severe persistent asthma, GERD, chronic bronchiectasis, with 4 recent admissions for respiratory distress and cough at her local ___ transferred to ___ for further evaluation. #Chronic Bronchiectasis #Severe Persistent Asthma with Acute Exacerbation #Airway stenosis She has had several recent presentations for respiratory distress that initially improved with antibiotics, then worsened after conclusion of antibiotics. Pulmonology was consulted, who advised a bronchoscopy, PFTs as inpatient, and discontinuation of antibiotics. The PFTs on ___ were about 2 hours after her last use of a bronchodilator, and showed a mixed picture of lung disease (FEV1 25% predicted, FVC 62% predicted, FEV1/FVC 46% predicted). They will follow up with this patient as an outpatient. IP performed a bronchoscopy, which showed multifocal airway stenosis. They used balloon dilation to widen these airways and advised continuation of her steroids for 5 more days (last day ___, followed by a taper. They will follow up with the patient in about a month. ENT performed a nasopharyngeoscope which showed no evidence of significant vocal cord pathology, no significant subglottic stenosis, and no paradoxical vocal cord motion. The following lab tests were all negative: sputum gram stain, urine Legionella Ag, MRSA swab, ANCA, step pneumo Ag, AFB smear, urine histoplasma, glucan, and galactomannan. Respiratory culture with no growth other than commensal flora. It was determined that current pneumonia is unlikely given the patient has been afebrile, and there was no clinical worsening despite being off antibiotics for several days. Therefore, her presentation is likely consistent with chronic lung disease from her known severe asthma as well as fibrosis secondary to GERD, and airway stenosis. She will need to be evaluated for other possible contributing factors as an outpatient (sleep study to evaluate for OSA, pH probe, impedance, manometry and LPR for GERD, and a nasal biopsy for ciliary dyskinesia). In the hospital she was treated with duonebs, albuterol, mucinex, chest ___ (though patient declined) and prednisone 60mg. Prednisone taper as an outpatient, per below under transitional issues. #GERD Given clinical findings and visualization on bronchoscopy, is likely contributing to lung disease. Patient currently denies symptoms. - increased her home omeprazole dose to 40mg BID #CVID Her history of inadequate response to a vaccine is diagnostic of CVID. While on IVIG, given therapeutic replacement of all antibody lines, cannot meaningfully interpret her normal Ig quantitative panel. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [] As an outpatient, consider possibly trial off SQ IG injections and rechecking levels to confirm diagnosis [] Will need to follow-up ?ciliary dyskinesia with sinus biopsy as an outpatient. [] Sleep study to evaluate for OSA as outpatient [] Further GERD/LPR evaluation as outpatient with pH probe, impedance, and manometry [] Will f/u with PCP locally in ___ [] Will f/u at ___ for General Pulm, Allergy, and IP [] Patient will complete course of 60mg pred on ___, then will have steroid taper as described: 50mg x3d, 40mg x3d, 30mg x3d, 20mg x3d, 10mg x3d [] Patient is currently off antibiotics. If she begins having fevers again or has worsening cough or dyspnea, should be re-evaluated for antibiotic course.
summary: You were brought to the hospital for evaluation of your shortness of breath and recurrent pneumonias You were evaluated by our pulmonology, interventional pulmonology, and head and neck teams. Your lungs, blood, and urine was evaluated for infectious markers, which were negative. You had pulmonary function tests, which showed "mixed" lung disease. This means that there is some scarring of your lungs, plus your airways can become blocked to decrease the flow of air (like asthma). The interventional pulmonology team performed a bronchoscopy and saw narrowing of your airways. They made these wider using a balloon. Your vocal cords were visualized and were normal. You were taken off antibiotics to increase the yield for bacteria from the bronchoscopy. Because you looked clinically well and did not start having fevers, you were kept off the antibiotics.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with history of lung cancer and left sided CVA with right-sided hemiparesis and aphagia discharged last week and sent to ___ now presenting with fever, tachycardia and shortness of breath. Patient was initially admitted to the ICU with respiratory distress. He has a history of COPD discharged on ___ for apparent flare. Had not been receiving Atrovent at ___. # Respiratory Distress. Initially admitted to the ICU. While in the ICU, he was continued on Atrovent and Albuterol nebs, as well as Spiriva. Treated with Vancomycin and Zosyn for HAP and started on burst course of steroids for COPD flare. Respiratory status stabilized the morning of admission and transferred to hospital floor. Cont'd on albuterol, atrovent and switched to Advair. Vancomycin stopped, continued on Zosyn via PICC line placed by ___. Pred burst planned for taper at rehab. O2 requirement weaned to 2L at discharge. . # Mental Status. On arrival to hospital floor pt was alert and awake and later became somnolent in the afternoon. Vital signs continued to remain normal and cardiac workup, CT head, and lab tets were negative for cause of AMS changes. Pt slept well the first night and was not somnolent aferwards. Pt noted to be awake and alert remainder of the hospitalization. . # Fever/Elevated WBC. Elevated WBC noted to be decreasing during the hospitalization, with no fevers noted after starting antibiotics. CXR/CT scan suggestive of RLL infiltrate, UA and UCx positive for UTI. Pt initially tx'd with Vanc/Zosyn with d/c on Zosyn. Afebrile with equivocal WBC count in context of steroids. . # Urinary Tract Infection. UA/UCx positive for staph negative strep UTI. Sensitive to penicillins, covered with Zosyn for concomittant PNA. Foley changed in ICU. . # S/p left MCA stroke/Anticoagulation. Initial INR subtherapeutic. Likely related to preious admin of Levoflox and Coumadin increasing warfarin blood levels in last hospitalization. In absence of Levoflox started on 5mg Coumadin with therapuetic range achieved. . # Diabetes Mellitus. Sugars continued to remain in 200s/300s as prednisone cont'd with increased tube feeds. Lantus/ ISS increased to match sugars with reasonable control achieved on d/c. Will need to address coverage as prednisone is tapered in rehab. . # Hyperlipidemia. Pt was continued on statin. . # CAD s/p MI and angioplasty in ___. Pt was continued on ASA and Lopressor with target BPs acheived. EKGs showed no interval changes with no rises in CEs noted. . # FEN. PEG tube on admission. Tube feeds brought to goal with lytes repleted PRN.
summary: We evaluated and treated your shortness of breath and think that your symptoms were most consistent with pneumonia combined with a COPD exacerbation. You are being discharged on treatment for both of these problems and will continue your physical and occupational therapy at ___. You did have a day where you became tired and sleepy. The workup we performed involved checking your electrolytes, blood levesl, heart function, and checking your brain for any changes in blood supply. the work-up was negative and your sleepiness imptoved over the next few days where your family noted you were back to your typical self.

In absence of Levoflox started on 5mg Coumadin with therapuetic range achieved. . # Diabetes Mellitus. Sugars continued to remain in 200s/300s as prednisone cont'd with increased tube feeds. Lantus/ ISS increased to match sugars with reasonable control achieved on d/c. Will need to address coverage as prednisone is tapered in rehab. . # Hyperlipidemia. Pt was continued on statin. . # CAD s/p MI and angioplasty in ___. Pt was continued on ASA and Lopressor with target BPs acheived. EKGs showed no interval changes with no rises in CEs noted. . # FEN. PEG tube on admission. Tube feeds brought to goal with lytes repleted PRN.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt admitted to ___ floor for shortness of breath. She was ruled out for an acute myocardial infarction with negative ECG changes and three sets of negative cardiac enzymes. She was ultrafiltrated and dialyzed, but continued to have SOB. She was recieving nebulizer treatments with minimal symptomatic relief. Because of her difficult inspiratory sounds there was a concern for paroxysmal vocal cord adduction. ENT was consulted and a laryngoscope showed normal vocal cords. Cardiology was consulted and recomended a stress MIBI to look for posible reversible defects. No significant reversable defects were found and cardiology said there was nothing to intervene upon. Patient was dialyzed to a dry weight of 60kg. Shortness of breath was at her baseline. Patient was discarged with an appointment made with her PCP and information to make an appointment with Dr. ___. Dr. ___ nearly daily with the patient's daughter and reported that he answered her questions to her apparent satisfaction. The patient and her daughter reported to Dr. ___ she wished to return home on discharge and not seek other options (e.g., SNF) where more support might be available. . Problems: # SOB: Patient reports increasing sob over the last 2 days. Per last note, EDW is 64 kg and pt is 68.5 kg currently. CXR consistent with pulmonary edema that is slightly worse than last cxr in early ___. In the ED, patient was very wheezy and was given nebs, prednisone and azithromycin for presumed asthma flare which could also be playing a role. No evidence of pna, ptx on cxr or active ischemia on ekg or cardiac enzymes. On presentation to the floor, pt was on her baseline oxygen and appeared comfortable. She continued to be short of breath and was dialzed to her dry weight. . # CORONARIES: Pt with known extensive CAD. Currently no evidence to suggest ACS. Pt Ruled out for MI./ Her cardiac meds were continued and a statin was started. . # RHYTHM: Currently in SR with Bi-V pacing. INR was therapeutic. Patient discharged on 3mg coumadin daily. Will follow levels at dialysis . # ESRD: Dialysis was continued. Renal meds were continued. . # Hypothyroid: TSH 5 and free t4 1.3 in early ___. Cont levothyroxine. . # DM2: Continue home Lantus and Humalog SS regimen. Last A1c checked in ___ was 7.7%. Continue gabapentin for peripheral neuropathy. . # Anemia: HCT slightly above baseline. Recent Fe studies non-revealing for a cause, though AoCD is suspected given her comorbidities. Pt recieves Epo with dialysis. . # S/p AKA: Pt with pain at site of AKA. Daughter reports running out of lidoderm patches recently and pt reports those help the most. Pt given persciption for lidoderm patches. . # Medication changes: Patient given refills for almost all meds as she reports she is out of most of them. Given short supply of Ambien 5mg qHS. Per Dr. ___ patient and her daughter told him she had an ample supply of insulin at home and did not require a refill of this and that they knew how to dose it appropriately.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for shortness of breath and chest pain. Your INR was also found to be elevated. You were found to have a lot of extra fluid. You were dialized multiple times to remove the fluid from your lungs. We now think that your dry weight is about 60kg after dialysis. Please be careful of the amount of salt that you eat in a day. It should be less than 2mg total. Also try to drink less than 2 liters of water in a day. Your INR came down to a normal level. We changed your coumadin to 4mg on ___, and ___, and 3mg on ___ and ___. Please continue to have your INR checked regularly. Your home medications were continued. No other changes were made.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ lady with recent discharge for PNA who presented w/ 3 days of SOB and 1 day of chest pain that was initially concerning for unresolved pneumonia but her exam and studies were reassuring that this represented post-infectious pneumonia as well as musculoskeletal shoulder pain so she was discharged home. # Right chest pain: musculoskeletal. She presented with right-sided chest pain that was focal and reproducible with light palpation on exam. The pain is likely due to myofascial pain syndrome given her hx and relief with NSAIDs. Given recent negative work-up for rheumatological and infectious causes, we did not perform repeat CK, ___ and ___ testing. Prior work-up showed elevated CRP and ESR. Shoulder joint pain was considered due to equivocal shoulder impingement testing and poor active range of motion. ACS is unlikely given negative Troponin x 2 and normal ECG. Also unlikley to be pericarditis due to focal pain and negative pericardial rub on exam. She was treated with Naproxen and Morphine and experienced some relief. # Shortness of breath: Reactive airways. Given her recent history of PNA 10 days PTA, her symptoms were initially concerning for recurrent or undertreated pneumonia. However, her CXR is not worsened from prior and she has been afebrile. Due to her hx of Asthma, wheezing on exam, and relief of dyspnea with Albuterol, she likely has a Post-pneumonia reactive airway disease. We also considered chronic eosinophilic pneumonia given the peripheral eosinophilia but were unimpressed by the CXR findings. SOB is not likely to be PE given no leg swelling and nl O2 sat on RA. She was initially given Ceftriaxone 1g IV and Azithromycin 500mg IV in the ED but that was subsequently discontinued due to low likelihood of pneumonia. She was encouraged to use her home albuterol which she reported improved the dyspnea. # Myofascial pain syndrome: chronic. Patient has a hx of myofascial pain syndrome and currently reports diffuse muscular pain which was reproducible on exam with light palpation. A rheum work-up for inflammatory arthritis was negative during her last admission in ___ except for elevated CRP and ESR. We controlled her pain with NSAIDs and Morphine and increased the frequency of Gabapentin to 300mg TID. On discharge, we added Acetaminophen, Ibuprofen, and increased frequency of Gabapentin to 300mg TID. # Hypertension: pt has a hx of HTN and was stable with SBP in 110s during admission. She was continued on her home medication of Amlodipine 1 mg qd and HCTZ 25MG QD # Allergic Rhinitis: stable. She reported increased post-nasal drip. She was instructed to follow the Loratadine 10mg qd prescription. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: #. Pending at the time of discharge: None # Code: Full (discussed with patient) # Communication: Patient # Emergency Contact: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted with cough, shortness of breath, and chest wall pain and were admitted because there was concern that your previous pneumonia might not have been adequately treated. Your history, exam, labs, and imaging were reassuring. Your cough and shortness of breath are likely related to your asthma. Please continue to take your Fluticasone inhaler twice a day as prescribed (even if you do not have symptoms). Then you can use the Albuterol as needed. It is VERY IMPORTANT that you stop smoking completely, as this can cause your breathing to become much worse and also increases your risk of lung cancer. As for your chest wall pain, we made sure you were not having a heart attack. On your previous admission, you were ruled out for inflammatory causes of pain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. Dyspnea / COPD: Patient appears to have a baseline dyspneic state with his COPD (night time and ambulatory supplemental O2), although he reports recent worsening. Pneumonia unlikely to be responsible for recent worsening of dyspnea. We felt that entire presentation explained by poor COPD control due to medication non-compliance (due to his inability to read) rather than a true COPD exacerbation. He was able to maintain oxygen saturation in range of 93 - 96% on RA while ambulating on ___. We attempted to look for other causes of dyspnea given his multiple hospitalizations. Per patient history, patient not likely to have OSA as he reports no known snoring, no night time awakenings with PND, no daytime sleepiness, no morning headaches, and he feels well-rested when he awakes. ECHO ___ was negative for valvular or other structural abnormality to suggest cardiac etiology of dyspnea. While hospitalized, he was continued on home inhalers including advair, spiriva, ipratropium PRN, albuterol PRN while hospitalized. Plan was to help him with medication management by having pills placed in pill boxes by a ___ after discharge. #. RLL infiltrate: Radiographic RLL inflitrate on admission portable CXR was later noted on PA and LAT on ___ to have no evidence of acute infiltrate or change from prior PA and LAT CXR. Remained afebrile. No leukocytosis. Urine legionella antigen negative. Sputum culture at admission was insufficient specimen for analysis. Though no evidence of ___ continue 5 days levofloxacin 750 mg as the patient improved from admission. #. Chest pain: Patient reported sharp left sided chest pain for 5 days duration at time of admission. Chest pain resolved and was not present following presentation to medical floor. Three sets of negative cardiac enzymes with no concerning telemetry events. Chest pain not related to acute MI. #. Diarrhea: Patient reports history of diarrhea 4 days leading up to admission, but no diarrhea during admission. #. Long-term steroid use: Patient on 10 mg predisone daily for COPD control. We continued calcium and vitamin D as well as PPI. Reinitiated dapsone 100 mg daily for PCP ___. #. Anxiety: Patient did not require clonazepam while hospitalized.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain and shortness of breath. At time of your admission we performed a chest xray which showed a right lung pneumonia, so we started you on levofloxacin, an antibiotic. Your last dose of levofloxacin will be on ___ to complete a ___lthough the pneumonia may partially explain your shortness of breath, it is unlikely to explain your chest pain. We performed three sets of lab tests that ruled out a heart attack as cause of your chest pain. While you were in the hospital we also performed imaging of your heart to make sure it was not the cause of your shortness of breath. We found that your heart looks healthy and normal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo F who presents with worsening dyspnea on exertion and renal failure. . # DOE: Most likely COPD exacurbation, in the setting of improving with nebs and steroids. Alternative etiologies such as PNA and ACS are less likely given story. Volume overload in setting of CKD is less likely given CXR. Received one dose of levo in ED. No signs of PNA/bronchitis. The pt improved and was not continued on a steroid taper due to her improvement and difficulty with blood glucose control. She was discharged on tiotropium, albuterol, fluticasone, and 5 day course of levoquin. . # Acute on chronic renal failure: Baseline Cr 4 last several months ago, now 7.4. Likely secondary to hypertension/diabetes. No new medications. Currently on NaHCO3. Had recent AV graft placed by Dr. ___ on ___. Discussed with transplant and renal to determine whether pt needs dialysis now or can wait until next week for initiation as it does not appear to be urgent need at this time and the graft needed further healing per surgery. Was determined that she was non-urgent so she will initiate dialysis in ___ days. Hepatitis panel was drawn and is pending for pre-dialysis. No PPD placed yet. She was continued on 40mg of PO lasix and scheduled to follow up with renal. . # Renal mass: Needs follow-up biopsy, seen by urology and set for outpt MRI in upcoming days for further eval. . # DM: was on metformin in past but was d/c'd during last admission due to renal failure. was not sent home on anything and was to f/u with PCP which pt could not do. has high blood sugars this AM likely secondary to IV solumedrol. unclear what pt's baseline sugars are, but most recent A1c in ___ is 6.9. ___ was consulted and recommended that she check and record her sugars BID, they did not recommend any hyperglycemic agents or insulin at this time. . # HTN: home meds. . # HL: home simvastatin. . # Thyroid nodule: TSH wnl during last admission. Needs follow-up biopsy, never done. . # Social: pt is illiterate. SW consult for resources, med compliance poor likely due to health literacy issues. . Comm: With pt. summary: You were admitted due to shortness of breath from a COPD exacerbation likely caused by a upper respiratory infection. You were treated with IV steroids, antibiotics, nebulizers and your breathing improved. In conjunction with the renal and transplant team it was determined that you did not need to initiate dialysis at this point and that this would be deferred for ___ days per Dr. ___ you ___ follow up with. You were also found to have elevated blood sugars that was probably due to the steroids you were given. Please continue checking your blood sugars twice daily for this. Keep a diary to bring to your primary care doctor. For your COPD, you will be discharged on fluticasone and tiotropium inhalers to take daily for control of your symptoms. You will also continue to take albuterol as needed. You will also take the antibiotic levofloxacin for 2 more days. There were no other changes to your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o female with a history of HTN, HLD, and pre-diabetes who presented with one day of coughing fits associated with substernal chest burning, SOB, and post tussive emesis in the context of having started a new job three weeks ago in a ___ facility. # Cough Patient's dry cough with shortness of breath and chest discomfort in the setting of recently starting a new job with environmental dust exposure was concerning for possible asthma exacerbated by environmental allergens . Patient given albuterol inhaler which was also noted to alleviate cough. Pneumonia also thought as potential etiology of cough given that patient presented with leukocytosis of 13.2 and mild retrocardiac opacification on CXR. The patient received one dose of levaquin upon admission. However patient denied any recent viral prodrome, myalgia, fever, or chills. However given some likelihood of pneumonia patient discharged on 5 day course of azithromycin. In addition patient discharged with PRN albuterol inhaler. Should have more formal outpatient work up and diagnosis of asthma with PFT's as outpatient. #Chest pain Patient's associated chest pain in context of cough was also further evaluated and determined to be non-cardiac in origin. Trop trended and were negative. EKG obtained without obvious evidence of new ischemia. Only notable for sinus tachycardia with nonspecific T wave changes. Upon chart review ___ EKG from Atrius was notable for anteroseptal MI (age indeterminent). PE also considered and D- dimer was within normal limits. # Elevated CK CK found to be elevated to 701. Patient denied any myalgias and renal function was uncompromised. Elevated CK was concerning in setting of atorvastatin 80 mg started in ___ and as such it was stopped. In addition given that azithromycin has theoretic interaction with atorvastatin it was also thought prudent to hold this until azithromycin dose complete. It is important to note that the CK level was elevated before starting azithromycin. CK level should be repeated as outpatient and restarting atorvastatin considered at that time. # HTN Patient remained with systolic blood pressure in the 150's-160's. Irbesartan continued. # HLD Atorvastatin stopped as above. # Vitamin D Deficiency Vit D continued. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ================ -Please recheck CBC to ensure normalization of WBC, CK to ensure improvement -restart atorvostatin if CK normalizes once Z-pack is complete -consider outpatient PFT testing to confirm asthma diagnosis Medication changes include stopping atorvostatin, starting PRN albuterol inhaler and Z-pack
summary: You came in with coughing fits that improved with an inhaler. We think this is most likely do to some asthma or irritation of the airway, possibly from dust exposure at your job. For this we will send you with a medication called an albuterol inhaler that it is important to take whenever you feel short of breath or feel like you are coughing. The possibility that these symptoms are from pneumonia or lung infection are unlikely, however we will give you a medication called azithromycin for you take for a total 5 day course. While taking this medication it is important to stop taking atorvostatin because of a possible interaction between these medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY ================= Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with hypertension, who is an active ___ PPD cigarette smoker, who presented to ED on ___ with acute dyspnea and wheezing in the setting of 1 week of of fevers, chills, cough, and runny nose. In the ED, she briefly required BiPAP and was admitted for treatment of COPD/asthma exacerbation though notably patient has no prior diagnosis of COPD/asthma. She was treated with oral prednisone, azithromycin, and inhalers, with improvement in her symptoms. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [] Will require PFTs to properly diagnose COPD/asthma [] Continue reinforcing smoking cessation and offer medications if necessary. Discharged on 14 mg/day patch, consider tapering to 7 mg/day in ___ weeks then off with nicotine gum if well tolerated. [] Patient reported poor adherence to amlodipine. summary: You were in the hospital because you were having trouble breathing and were admitted for management You were treated for a chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) exacerbation with steroids, antibiotics, and inhaled medications that help to open your airways and decrease inflammation. We discussed COPD with you and talked about how one of the most important and effective ways to treat it is to stop smoking. Please continue to take 40 mg prednisone and 250 mg of azithromycin once a day for the next two days. Please use your Advair inhaler twice a day, every day. Please take your 5 mg of amlodipine once a day, EVERYDAY. Please do not continue to smoke cigarettes. There are many smoking cessation tools available to you and you are encouraged to discuss them further with your primary care doctor.
