text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ yo F with PMH of asthma, p/w acute SOB, chest tightness, and wheeze, c/w asthma flare, likely triggered by URI vs chemical irritants. . # Asthma, acute - Pt was admitted to the medical floor and continued on systemic steroids, standing MDI doses of albuterol and ipratropium. She was also given a peak flow meter and instructed in its use. We also reviewed proper technique in using MDI, and she was very adept and competent, so nebulizers were not used. She remained on room air and had no hypoxia. There was no evidence of infection on her CXR, so she was not treated for pneumonia. Of note, she did develop symptoms of sinusitis, with nasal discharge / drainage, facial pain / tenderness, so she was placed on a course of antibiotics (see below). Her symptoms improved with the above treatment. In reviewing her history, the most likely trigger for her asthma flare were most likely a combination of cold weather, URI/sinusitis, and chemical irritants (cleaning products at home, hair products at cosmetology school). We advised her to remain at home for the next few days after discharge and not return to school until her symptoms had improved significantly. . Additionally, in reviewing her history with her, per her report, she has consistent required more than 2 courses of oral steroids annually for asthma flare. As such, she classifies into "mild persistent" category of asthma, and based on guidelines, she is being initiated on inhaled corticosteroid (ICS). Given that she is being discharged on systemic steroids, ICS can be started after she finishes the prednisone burst. We also recommend that she continue standing albuterol until she is seen in f/u with her PCP. . # Sinusitis, acute: the pt was noted to have significant sinus drainage and facial tenderness. Although this is most likely viral sinusitis, in the setting of acute asthma flare, we will empirically treat for bacterial sinsusitis with a course of Augmentin x 5 days total and also use inhaled Flonase spray. . Transitional Issues: 1. f/u with PCP to assess for return to baseline respiratory status, resume using albuterol MDI PRN 2. complete course of antibiotics for acute bacterial sinusitis .
summary: You presented to the hospital with worsening shortness of breath and wheezing, despite using your inhaler at home. This is likely due to an exacerbation of your asthma. You also have a sinusitis, and although likely viral in origin, given your asthma flare, we will treat for bacterial sinusitis. Your asthma flare is likely due to exposure to both the URI and chemical irritants at home (cleaning supplies) and cosmetology school. Given that you have frequent exacerbations requiring PO Prednisone, your asthma classifies into the mild persistent category and you would benefit from an inhaled corticosteroid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with a history of excessive ___ bleeding with unrevealing hematological workup, connective tissues disorder, diabetes, and paroxysmal a-fib p/w x2 days slurred speech/ataxia with negative Neurologic work-up with hospital course complicated by dyspnea on exertion with no evidence of new lung disease. text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH of asthma, hiatal hernia with GERD, HTN, paroxysmal SVT, paroxysmal AFib not on coumadin, AS, and CKD, admitted for SOB. The most likely explanation for her presenting symptoms was viral bronchitis. Patient remained afebrile and hemodynamically stable with improving respiratory status on standing albuterol nebulizers. She was discharged in stable condition with plan to closely follow-up with Dr. ___ a week. . # Dyspnea: The most likely etiology was viral bronchitis based on history and exam. There were no new infiltrates seen on CXR. Patient received a dose of levofloxacin in ED, but given low suspicion for bacterial pneumonia, antibiotics were not continued on the floor. There was a question about pulmonary edema contributing to her dyspnea, given her recent self-decrease of diuretic dosing (went from taking lasix 20mg daily alternating with 40mg daily to just 20mg daily). However, CXR not suggestive of edema. With albuterol nebulizer treatments plus home Advair, her respiratory status improved quickly. On the morning of her discharge, her O2 sat was in the mid ___ on RA. Will follow-up with PCP and ___. . # Acute on chronic renal failure: Cr upon admission was 1.7, elevated from baseline 1.4. ___ was likely secondary to prerenal azotemia. Patient received gentle IV fluid hydration with 250cc NS. Irbesartan was held. Cr trended down to approximate baseline of 1.5 prior to discharge. . # Hyponatremia: Likely hypovolemic hyponatremia, which resolved following 250cc NS as above. . # Pyuria: UA showed pyuria, though as patient was asymptomatic, did not treat with antibiotics. Urine culture was still pending at time of discharge. . # Paroxysmal Afib: She remained stable on her home amiadarone regimen. . # HTN: She remained hemodynamically stable. She was continued on metoprolol. Irbesartan was held given ___ on CKD as above, though can be restarted in outpatient setting given Cr has trended down to baseline. . # Hypothyroidism: Most recent TSH is 3.1 from ___. She remained stable on home levothyroxine 50mcg daily. . # Chronic arthritis pain: Pain was controlled with her home regimen of oxycodone 2.5mg qhs and tylenol prn. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Code: DNR/DNI - discussed at length with patient - Will follow-up with Dr. ___ in one week - Needs to complete medication reconciliation with Dr. ___ was asked to bring her medications to her next appointment - Urine and blood cultures still pending at time of discharge and will need to be followed-up
summary: You were admitted to the ___ on ___ for shortness of breath. We think this was caused by viral bronchitis. Your chest x-ray did not show a pneumonia. You remained free of fever and stable throughout your hospital stay. Your breathing improved with albuterol nebulizer treatments and Advair. We did not give your diuretics during your stay given your dehydration. Your lab tests indicated some worsening of your kidney function. We think this was due to dehydration. Your kidney function improved after we gave some IV fluids. It is important that you drink plenty of fluids daily to avoid becoming dehydrated in the future. Your urine sample showed findings of a possible urinary tract infection. You did not have any symptoms, so we did not give you antibioitcs. The urine culture is still pending at the time of your discharge. We will contact you by phone if the test returns positive.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ with a PMH of ___ syndrome and asthma who presented with cough, fever and chills with mild leukocytosis and CXR findings c/w pneumonia. Bronchospasm consistent with asthma was also evident on physical exam. The patient was treated with high dose steroids and antibiotics for asthma exacerbation and pneumonia and discharged with extensive follow-up. . This was a brief hospitalization lasting under 48 hours. The hospital course in full is summarized below: . PROBLEM #1: Shortness of Breath: The patient had prolonged expiratory wheezes on exam consistent with his known asthma. He additionally had CXR findings consistent with RLL PNA. Given accompanying fevers, chills, night sweats, leukocytosis, CXR and exam findings, we opted to treat for community aquired pneumonia and asthma. The patient recieved Azithromycin and Ceftriaxone while in-house. He was discharged on Azithromycin 250mg QD for 4 days and Cefpodoxime 200mg BID for 14 days. For his asthma, he was given nebulizers and his home inhalers; these were likewise continued at time of discharge. He recieved two days of pulse-dose Prednisone while in house and was given a prescription for one additional day for a total of three days high dose prednisone. After speaking with his outpatient doctors at the ___, it was determined that the patient should then return to his tapered, low-dose Prednisone regimen (5mg QOD). The patient requested an appointment with ___; an appointment was made with Dr. ___ for 10:30AM the following day. The patient was discharged without any shortness of breath but still with some wheezing. He was ambulating well with 02 sats approaching 100% on ambulation. . PROBLEM #2: ___ Syndrome: Mr. ___ is followed by the ___ Rheumatology and Pulmonology Groups (but expressed a strong desire to switch to ___ pulmonology as described above). Per discussion with his rheumatologist at the ___, the patient appeared at his baseline churg ___ burden. As described, he was discharged on his home low-dose taper of 5mg Prednisone QOD, to begin after his final dose of high dose Prednisone for asthma. Follow-up with his ___ rheumatologist (Dr. ___, ___ was arranged for ___ at 10AM. Follow-up with a new ___ pulmonologist waw arranged as described above. . PROBLEM #3: Hypertension: Patient was not hypertensive on admission. His home medications were continued. . PROBLEM #4: FEN: Patient recieved 3L NS. Electrolytes were monitored per usual. . PROBLEM #5: COMMUNICATION: with patient, unaccompanied, with whom the clinical diagnosis and prognosis was discussed and questions were answered to the best of my ability. . CODE: Remained full.
summary: You were admitted with pneumonia and asthma. You were given nebulizers, antibiotics, intravenous fluids, and high dose steroids. You recovered quickly. You will need to complete a course of antibiotics to ensure resolution of your infection. Likewise, you will need a very short course of high dose steroids before you resume your usual lower dose. Prescriptions are provided for these and your other medications in the attached pages. We have not made any other changes in your medications. You stated that you would like to have outpatient follow-up at our facility. We have set-up an appointment for you at our pulmonology clinic.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old female with past medical history alcoholic and HCV cirrhosis complicated by pancytopenia, encephalopathy, portal hypertension with ascites, and varices, OSA not on CPAP, atrial fibrillation not on anticoagulation, asthma and tracheobronchomalacia admitted ___ with asthma exacerbation, bright red blood per rectum thought to be hemorrhoidal bleeding, course complicated by alcohol withdrawal, alcoholic hepatitis, positive blood culture, leaving against medical advice. # Discharge against medical advice As below, patient left against medical advice, despite counseling otherwise. She was able to Patient verbalized her understanding of the risks including worsening infection, liver failure and death. Discussed with patient's HCP/sister and patient's PCP, neither of whom were able to convince patient to stay in the hospital. Discharged home with prescriptions for prednisone, azithromycin, thiamine, folate. # GI Bleed suspected to be secondary to hemorrhoids Patient presented with several days of bright red blood per rectum. History became more clear as hospitalization progressed--patient reported initial onset of watery diarrhea, followed by bright red blood per rectum. Hgb similar to prior baseline. Given history of varices and cirrhosis (as below) patient was seen by hepatology consult service who agreed history consistent with hemorrhoidal bleed. During hospital stay, patient noted to have small amount of blood most noticeably on wiping. Hgb remained stable, was 8.8 at discharge. # Moderate Persistent Asthma with Acute Exacerbation Presented with several days of rhinorrhea, sore throat and cough. CXR without signs of consolidation. Flu negative. Trigger thought to be recent viral URI. Initially with wheezing, rapidly improving with bronchodilators, steroids and azitrhomycin. Continued home Flonase, loratadine, symbicort, tiotropium. Peak flow prior to discharge was 310. # Diarrhea Patient reported watery stool ___ times per day x several days. Cdiff PCR positive, toxin negative, not felt to represent infection. In setting of above symptoms, thought to be viral in etiology. Treated supportively and symptoms improved. # Alcoholic Hepatitis # Alcoholic Cirrhosis # Alcohol abuse Patient with history of cirrhosis complicated by pancytopenia, encephalopathy, portal hypertension with ascites, and varices, who presented in the setting of a recent alcohol binge with elevated LFTs. Hepatology saw patient, recommended against additional treatment given DF 28. On hospital day 3, DF increased to 32.5. Patient left against medical advice before any additional management could be pursued. Continued rifAXIMin. Provided prescription for thiamine and folate. # Alcohol Withdrawal Patient presented with alcohol level 200s. On hospital day 2 developed increasing tremor and anxiety, with CIWA scores > 10. She was started on PO Ativan. She left against medical advice before withdrawal symptoms resolved. # GPC Sepsis At 36 hours, admission blood culture grew GPCs. Started empiric IV vancomycin. Patient left against medical advice before additional information was available. As above, she was able to verbalize understanding of her diagnosis and its potential life threatening complications. # Chronic Iron deficiency anemia Continued iron # Lower back pain Continued Gabapentin # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation Continued ASA #OSA Not adherent to CPAP, so was monitored with nocturnal O2 sat monitoring > 30 minutes spent on discharge
summary: You were admitted with an exacerbation of your asthma and blood in your stool. You were seen by GI specialists who thought the bleeding was likely from hemorrhoids. We also found that you had worsening liver failure--we think this is due to alcohol. You also had signs of alcohol withdrawal. You also had a blood test that was concerning that you might have an infection in your blood stream. Before we could further treat you, you opted to leave against medical advice. You were able to verbalize your understanding of your life threatening condition, and that you might die without further treatment You are now leaving the hospital against medical advice.

Discharged home with prescriptions for prednisone, azithromycin, thiamine, folate. # GI Bleed suspected to be secondary to hemorrhoids Patient presented with several days of bright red blood per rectum. History became more clear as hospitalization progressed--patient reported initial onset of watery diarrhea, followed by bright red blood per rectum. Hgb similar to prior baseline. Given history of varices and cirrhosis (as below) patient was seen by hepatology consult service who agreed history consistent with hemorrhoidal bleed. During hospital stay, patient noted to have small amount of blood most noticeably on wiping. Hgb remained stable, was 8.8 at discharge. # Moderate Persistent Asthma with Acute Exacerbation Presented with several days of rhinorrhea, sore throat and cough. CXR without signs of consolidation. Flu negative. Trigger thought to be recent viral URI. Initially with wheezing, rapidly improving with bronchodilators, steroids and azitrhomycin. Continued home Flonase, loratadine, symbicort, tiotropium. Peak flow prior to discharge was 310. # Diarrhea Patient reported watery stool ___ times per day x several days. Cdiff PCR positive, toxin negative, not felt to represent infection. In setting of above symptoms, thought to be viral in etiology. Treated supportively and symptoms improved. # Alcoholic Hepatitis # Alcoholic Cirrhosis # Alcohol abuse Patient with history of cirrhosis complicated by pancytopenia, encephalopathy, portal hypertension with ascites, and varices, who presented in the setting of a recent alcohol binge with elevated LFTs. Hepatology saw patient, recommended against additional treatment given DF 28. On hospital day 3, DF increased to 32.5. Patient left against medical advice before any additional management could be pursued. Continued rifAXIMin. Provided prescription for thiamine and folate. # Alcohol Withdrawal Patient presented with alcohol level 200s. On hospital day 2 developed increasing tremor and anxiety, with CIWA scores > 10. She was started on PO Ativan. She left against medical advice before withdrawal symptoms resolved. # GPC Sepsis At 36 hours, admission blood culture grew GPCs. Started empiric IV vancomycin. Patient left against medical advice before additional information was available. As above, she was able to verbalize understanding of her diagnosis and its potential life threatening complications. # Chronic Iron deficiency anemia Continued iron # Lower back pain Continued Gabapentin # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation Continued ASA #OSA Not adherent to CPAP, so was monitored with nocturnal O2 sat monitoring > 30 minutes spent on discharge

text: Brief Hospital Course: #Tracheobronchomalacia: Per OSH report, patient has severe tracheobronchomalacia s/p Dumon Y stent placement on ___. He underwent a pulmonary function test which revealed obstructive disease. Flex bronchoscopy showed large amount of mucus and mucus plugging. Rigid bronchoscopy was subsequently performed and his stent was removed. Patient endorses subjective increase in shortness of breath after stent removal but did not appear in distress, tachypneic, and his O2 saturation was good at 95%. Dynamic tracheal CT revealed mild tracheobronchomalacia. His pulmonary function test revealed obstructive disease consistent with his asthma; his repeat pulmonary function test without the airway stent revealed further decreased function. He is to follow up with Interventional Pulmonology Clinic for further management. . #Asthma: Patient complains of increasing SOB after stent removal as above but had no episode of acute asthma exacerbation while in the hospital. He was on 4LNC which maintained his O2 Saturation around 95% throughout the admission. He was continued on his home regimen of asthma medication and nebulizer including: prednisone, salmeterol, fluticasone, albuterol nebulizer and ipratropium nebulizer. . #Chest pain: Patient complains of pleuritic chest pain ___ with tenderness upon palpation throughout the hospitalization. He was treated with oxycodone SR 20mg BID, acetaminophen. . #Chronic back pain: Patient did not complain of back pain while in the hospital. He was treated with oxycodone SR 20mg BID, acetaminophen as above and lidocaine patch in addition. . #Pulmonary nodules: On CT, pt has multiple bilateral pulmonary nodules and should have CT follow up with in a year to assess stability. . #Diet: Regular . #Code: Full . #Dispo: Home
summary: ___ went to the outside hospital due to increased shortness of breath and chest pain after a recent stent placement to treat tracheobronchomalacia. There they found that your shortness of breath was likely due to increased secretions in your lungs but no evidence of infection was found. Your chest pain was attributed to the recent procedure. Upon your improvement, ___ were transferred to the ___ for further management of your tracheobronchomalacia. Here you were seen by both the interventional pulmonology and thoracic surgery team. Your airway stent was removed; subsequent pulmonary function tests and CT scan revealed mild tracheobronchomalacia. Your shortness of breath became slightly worse after the stent removal. ___ were continued on home asthma medication and oxygen supplementation. Your chest pain and back pain was managed with your home regimen of pain medication, and lidocaine patch/acetaminophen. Your blood pressure was kept in good control with your home BP medication. Lastly, you were found to have multiple small lung nodules and should get a chest CT scan to follow up within a year.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with PMH significant for COPD and Asthma presents with oxygen desaturation x 3 days. # Hypoxia: On admission, the concern was for pulmonary embolism given an elevated D-dimer in the 900s. A CTPA was done which was initially read as having a pulmonary embolism, and the patient was started on a heparin gtt. Eight hours later the CT was overread, indicating that there were no PEs, and instead that there was concern for a mass in the right side of lung that could be interpreted as a malignancy. The HCP was informed of this finding, and between physicians and HCP it was decided not to intervene on this finding, given the patient's age, comorbidities, and poor risk/benefit ratio. The patient did not have a WBC count, was not febrile, and this led us away from a concern for a pneumonia or an infectious process. The patient was very wheezy on exam, and responded well to standing nebs, as well as systemic steroids, satting ___ on room air upon discharge. The patient was discharged on a pulse of Prednisone 60 mg for the next 5 days to ease inflammation for presumed reactive airway disease. # Urinary Tract Infection: Patient has history of UTIs. In-house, the patient was treated initially with Levofloxacin, and then Cipro IV for a total of 3 days. However, on day of discharge the urine sensitivities returned, and the patient was found to have an E. Coli resistant to Cipro. Given her age, the patient was presumed to be a complicated UTI, and was sent out on Macrobid BID, given the culture's sensitivity, for the next ___ days. # Acute Renal Failure: Patient's Acute Renal Failurewas most likely pre-renal given her improvement with IVF resuscitation, and despite the kidney injury of a contrast dye load (although this may not be apparent for the next few days). She improved with fluid resuscitation. # Hypertension: Patient is currently normotensive, but there was concern in ED that patient had been hypotensive. She responded well to fluid resuscitation, and was continued on her home BP medications. # Coronary Artery disease: The patient likely has a history of CAD which is not overtly mentioned, as she takes Plavix as well. Troponin slightly elevated a 0.02 on admission, which trended down to 0.01 in the setting of ___. Patient unable to endorse chest pain, but EKG does not reveal ST elevations or depressions. We continued her Simvastatin, Plavix in house. # Dementia: Per granddaughter patient at baseline is AAOx2.The grand-daughter will come by at 8 ___ to help speak to the patient and explain our thought process, in addition to helping provide her with her medications. We continued her Aricept, Seroquel, Trazodone, and would want her continued on Namenda and Loratadine (if she requires it) upon her discharge. # Dysphagia: Has been an issue for the patient in the past. Speech and swallow evaluated in house, and recommended that she have a PO diet of nectar-thick liquids and pureed solids, 1:1 supervision with all PO intake, Meds crushed with puree, TID oral care, and swallowing ___ at facility or here as an outpatient. # CODE STATUS - DNR/DNI # PENDING LABS - ___ Blood Culture, Routine-PENDING # PCP ___: - Consider Palliative Care if family desire for comfort - Maintain vigilance for reoccurance of UTI
summary: You were admitted because you were having trouble breathing and felt short of breath. While you were in the hospital, we started on you on medications to help open up your airways, as well as steroids to help reduce inflammation in your lungs. Your breathing improved in the hospital, and we felt safe letting you go back to ___. We also did a CT scan of your lungs, which showed a finding that made us concerned that a cancer might be present. After speaking with the people who help take care of you and make decisions for you, it was decided that the risk of a procedure to discover the nature of the mass in your lungs outweighed the benefit of possible reinstatement. Our speech and swallow specialists evaluated you, and thought that you were choking on some of your food and fluid, and changed the thickness of the food you ingest. We also found that you had a urinary tract infection, for which we are giving you antibiotics to take for the next ___ days. START Prednisone 50 mg Daily for the next five days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old female with h/o PE on coumadin, COPD/asthma, HTN/DM2, found down on floor, noted to have fever, leukocytosis w/bandemia and elevated lactate. #. Sepsis/community acquired PNA/fever- On admission, patient met SIRS criteria for temperature, white count and heart rate. She was found to have a pneumonia on both cxr and CT scan. She was initially treated with broad spectrum antibiotics, but she improved dramatically, so she was switched to oral levaquin, which she tolerated well. # Hilar lymphadenopathy: Found to have enlarged perihilar lymph nodes on CT scan of the chest. These are likely reactive to the pneumonia, but radiologists advise to also r/o any possible primary malignancy. She has known renal mass which is being followed by ___ urologist Dr ___ also has irregular thyroid seen on CT scan. I was in contact with PCP regarding this, and advised f/u renal MRI to see if renal mass is enlarging or consideration of renal biopsy to definitively r/o that the ___ does not represent metastatic disease from renal cell carcinoma. I also advised outpatient thyroid ultrasound. . #. ?Syncope/found down: Patient was found down at home w/ evidence of defecation. I suspect that she became hypotensive on account of her pneumonia and had some kind of vasovagal event. There were no arrhythmias seen on telemetry, and Brain MRI did not show any lesion or CVA. She ambulated well with ___ in the hospital. . #. Acute renal failure: Resolved in the hospital. Instructed to resume cozaar on returning home. . # History of PE: INR therapeutic at time of discharge. #Ear pain-Patient complained of severe intermittent pain near left ear. No pain over mastoid or parotid. Has long standing hearing loss and tinnitus. Exam of ear revealed cerumenosis and perforated Left TM (old). She was seen by ENT who felt that she had TMJ syndrome and advised NSAIDS. Her pain was very well treated with NSAIDS. Given that she will be using them at home on a prn basis, she was also started on omeprazole #. DM2: She was advised to resume home meds on discharge. However, the quinolone antibiotics may precipitate hypoglycemia, so she was advised to take half the dose of glyburide for three days, during which she would be taking quinolone abx. . #. COPD/asthma: Continued on home meds. . #. HTN: Well controlled after she recovered from her SIRS presentation. .
summary: You were admitted after being found on the ground in your home. You were found to have a pneumonia, and you improved with antibiotics. You also had some evidence of muscle breakdown (because you were down on the ground for a while), but this cleared with IV fluids. The CAT scan of your chest shows not only the pneumonia, but also a known kidney mass and an irregularity of your thyroid gland. In addition, there are enlarged lymph nodes in your chest. These lymph nodes likely increased in size because of the pneumonia. However, Dr ___ make sure that they decrease in size by repeating a CAT scan of your chest in ___ months. In addition, you need an MRI of your kidneys. If the mass in your kidney is growing, you should have a biopsy of your kidney. You also need an ultrasound of your thyroid gland. You had ear pain, and had an MRI of your head. It did not reveal a cause of your ear pain. You were seen by an ear specialist, who feels that your pain is from your temporo-mandibular joint, otherwise known as TMJ. Please take ibuprofen with food when you have discomfort there. Please discuss this with Dr ___ this discomfort persists.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with h/o genital herpes who is called out from MICU after course with severe legionella PNA, never requiring intubation. Pt is currently sating well on 4L NC with metabolic alkalosis thought due to N/V. 1)Pneumonia/ respiratory distress- On admission the patient had a clear CXR. Had an asthma attack in the ED, and then another asthma attack on ___. Both responded immediately to albuterol. Since the second attack she has had persistent coughing productive of clear sputum. Found on CXR on ___ to have a large pneumonia and she was started on azithromycin and ceftriaxone. She continued to require increasing O2 to maintain her saturation. On ___ she went for CTA which was negative for PE. It is unclear if this PNA is related to her underlying presenting symptoms or if this is a superimposed process. On ___ her antibiotics were broadened to vanc, increased dose of ceftriaxone, azithro, and doxycycline. She continued to receive albuterol and atrovent nebs as needed. Pulmonary was consulted and a sputum culture was sent. Due to increasing respiratory distress and increasing oxygen requirements, patient was transferred to MICU. Patient was given oxygen via high flow mask and transiently required non invasive positive pressure ventilation. Patient did not require intubation however. Infectious workup revealed legionella pneumonia. Patient was treated initially with double coverage using Levofloxacin and Azithromycin, with subsequent switch to monotherapy using IV Azithromycin. Patient improved significantly and was able to be transferred out of the MICU with oxygen supplementation via nasal cannula. On the medicine floor, the patient's respiratory status remained improved on oxygen. She was continued on Azithromycin. Her oxygen requirement decreased during the remainder of her course. She remained afebrile while on the medicine floor. On discharge, she was off oxygen. She was afebrile and her rash had resolved. 2)Nausea, vomiting, metabolic alkalosis: Per the patient's report, nausea and vomiting did not begin until transfer to the MICU. Her nausea and vomiting continued on and off during her MICU stay, but was adequately controlled on Zofran. Arterial blood gas prior to transfer to the medicine floor revealed a metabolic alkalosis thought secondary to vomiting. After transfer to the floor, her nausea and vomiting remained well controlled on Zofran. On discharge, she denied nausea or vomiting. 3)Anemia - Patient has new anemia with Hct in the low 30's. Hemolysis labs (except for an elevated LDH) are not elevated. Her anemia may be due to the cause of her underlying process. 4)Transaminitis - Patient with a new transaminitis. Likely secondary to legionella. On discharge, transaminitis had resolved. 5)Hypotension - Patient developed significant autodiuresis after MICU stay. She had episodic hypotension which was occasionally symptomatic in the setting of 2L daily diuresis. Patient was instructed to increase po intake and salt intake and was ambulatory without symptoms at the time of discharge.
summary: You have been admitted with fever, rash, shortness of breath, cough, and joint pain and were found to be positive for legionella in your urine. You had a prolonged course, with hypoxia leading to a transfer to the medical ICU, but never required intubation. Your respiratory status improved and you were transferred to the medicine floor where you progressively improved. You were started on azithromycin for legionella, and you will need to continue the antibiotic course until ___. You also had episodes of low blood pressure. Please continue to drink fluids and increase your salt intake over the next few days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with history of Type 1 refractory Celiac's disease on ___ and significant medication/food allergies who presented with diarrhea and dehydration. # Diarrhea/dehydration: The etiology of her diarrhea may be due to either continued celiac/fructose/lactose intake, IBS, osmotic diarrhea from artificial sugars. Stool studies were negative. Diarrhea occured after ___ minutes after food intake so unlikely bacterial overgrowth. There is no clear pattern to her diarrhea although notably she reports no diarrhea when fasting so this was felt to be unlikely persistent inflammatory enteropathy while on ___. Initially she was kept on liquids only with improvement in stool output. Then, her diet was slowly advanced. Her diet was modified with the help of her outpatient gastroenterologist and nutritionist to create a gluten free, lactose free, low fodmap, low osmolar diet. Please see OMR notes detailing diet. She was not allowed to eat cafeteria food as it is not gluten free. Her home prns were changed to standing including bentyl and donnatol. Cholestyramine was added for bulking and fibercon was recommended daily-bid as an outpatient for bulking. She was continued on home lactase and florastor supplements. She was given IVF for hydration and instructed to use pedialyte for outpatient hydration if needed. Her stools slowly became formed and less frequent, and she was discharged after not having a bowel movement for 24 hours. Upon discharge, her donnatol and bentyl were changed back to prn. She was continued on mesalamine and ___ throughout. # Hyponatremia: Likely intermittently hypovolemic given amount of stool output. IVF was given to replete losses. Also has a history of SIADH and her salt tabs were continued. Continued her psych medications that may predispose to hyponatremia given her stability on this regimen. # Coagulopathy: Initially vitamin K deficiency given poor PO intake and but now s/p vitamin K x3 days. No current bleeding. PTT now elevated on hep sq, so hep sq was discontinued and the patient ambulated tid. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Bipolar disorder: Continued bupropion, clonazepam, chlorpromazine, and divalproex with lactose tabs. # Asthma: Continued zafirlukast, fluticasone-salmeterol. # B12 deficiency: Continued cyanocobalamin oral, but held IM doses. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # Code status: confirmed full.
summary: You were admitted for diarrhea and dehydration. It is unclear why the diarrhea started but we have slowly helped it to stop. We would like to try a new diet which is a low osmolality, low FODMAP, no lactose, gluten free diet, as a trial until your next visit with Dr. ___. In addition, we would like to use bulking agents to decrease the amount of diarrhea you normally have. We recommend Fibercon daily at home, and you can increase this to twice daily if your diarrhea returns. We incresed your bentyl and donnatol to standing doses and had you take these whether you needed them or not. At home, we recommend using them only as needed now that your stools have stopped. You can add these back in as standing if the diarrhea starts up again. We also started cholestyramine and you should continue this at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with asthma admitted with severe asthma exacerbation. # Severe asthma exacerbation / hypercarbic respiratory failure: Patient admitted to the floor initially and placed on IV solumedrol with scheduled nebs. She decompensated overnight on the night of admission and was sent to the MICU where she was started on BiPAP. ___ and her outpatient pulmonologist were consulted; per their recommendations she received 5 day course of azithromycin. She was continued on BiPAP overnight with wean to face-mask on ___. She continued to have diffuse wheezes throughout on ___ intially requiring continuous nebs. She was then weaned to q2h albuterol nebs and then she was successfully weaned to NC and albuterol nebs were spaced throughout the day on ___. On transfer to the floor, the patient remained diffusely wheezy but with decreased work of breathing and O2 sats ranging 96-100% on 4L NC. CXR did not show any clear evidence of pneumonia; she was found to have a patchy opacity in the LLL but this was re-evaluated several times and thought to be actually overlying breast tissue. She had a CTA which showed no PE, but did have some bronchial thickening and linear atelectasis at the bases. Her steroids were changed to oral prednisone 60mg daily on ___ with plan for steroid taper by 10mg q3days, to be held at 10mg daily. # Hypertension: During this admission, she was hypertensive with SBP 170s in the setting of getting albuterol and steroids. She received IV labetalol while in the MICU with good response. Her hypertension improved with decreasing albuterol and steroid requirements. Longer-term antihypertensive therapy was discussed with the patient but introduction of additional medications was deferred at this time per patient's wishes. On discharge, her blood pressures were ranging 130s-150s.
summary: You were admitted due to an asthma exacerbation. You were given nebs, intravenous steroids, and antibiotics. Your exacerbation became so severe that you had to go to the intensive care unit, where you were placed on non-invasive BiPap ventilation for a short period of time. You were eventually weaned off of this and subsequently weaned off of all supplemental oxygen. Your steroids were switched to oral and you finished your antibiotic course. You had a CT scan of your chest which showed no clots and did show some thickening of your bronchioles (airways, consistent with your known lung disease), as well as several nodules that will need to be followed. You were significantly improved and were discharged. You will go home with a steroid taper, then will stay on a lower dose of steroids indefinitely until you see Dr. ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Impression: This is a ___ woman admitted with shortness of breath and hypoxia with ambulation to 90% in the ED. The differential includes pneumonia (no present on CXR or exam), bronchospastic disease (no wheezing, but symptomatic improvement with albuterol neb), myasthenia ___ flare (less likely given discussion with her outpatient neurologist), and much less likely PE (given the negative ddimer). Further workup included PFTs, which are pending at discharge. A bronchodilator bedside test (ambulatory saturation before and after a nebulizer) revealed both symptomatic as well as oxygenation improvement. The patient will be discharged with an albuterol MDI, which has helped her in the past. 1. bronchospasm/hypoxia: Resolved with albuterol trial. No acute infection suspected, more likely related to subacute infection. Patient to followup with PCP to discuss ___ results, and see if return visit to pulmonologist is warranted (last seen in ___ by ___. 2. Myasthenia ___: Discussed with her outpatient neurologist that she should continue CellCept, prednisone, and pyridostigmine with atropine without change. She will follow up with Dr ___ as previously scheduled. 3. Hypertension/leg swelling: Patient was recently started on lasix, decreased to 20mg. She should continue this without change. Given her history of erythema nodosum, it may be warranted to exclude subacte rheumatologic causes of leg swelling as an outpatient, pending further course. 4. GERD: Continue acid reduction treatment, and restart esomeprazole on discharge. Case discussed verbally with her internist, Dr ___, and her outpatient neurologist, Dr ___ on the day of discharge.
summary: As you know, you came to us for shortness of breath, and were found to have a low oxygen level when you walked (90%). This improved after you had a breathing treatment (albuterol) and did not fall below 95%. It is likely that your recent sinus infection was due to a virus that has made your breathing a little more short. This should improve with time, and you should see Dr ___ it does not improve. Your myasthenia ___ may have made it harder for you to recover. You had pulmonary function tests taken, but the results are still not back. Dr ___ is aware of this, and will follow up on this test for you. We are giving you an albuterol inhaler to use if you get very short of breath again, although we hope you will not need it often. Please continue to use the zyrtec for your sinus congestion, and stay hydrated as well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o male with PMHx of asthma s/p intubation in ___ who p/w SOB c/w asthma flare and allergic reaction. . # Shortness of breath: Symptoms were secondary to asthma exacerbation as well as an allergic reaction/angioedema. No clear exposure history although reportedly did have swelling of face and lips and does have some swelling under left eye. Patient has history of similar reaction to ibuprofen, but he does not recall taking anything similar. Patient was treated with epipen at OSH and responded well. On neck x-ray, there was some concern for soft tissue swelling, but CT neck with contrast ruled this out. Given some wheezing on exam and improvement with nebs, also component of asthma exacerbation. Patient was started on prednisone and treated with albuterol and ipratropium nebs. He will complete as prednisone taper on discharge. Patient reported some symptoms slightly suspicious for pleuritic chest pain. D dimer was elevated at 788, but patient was not tachycardic. This pain improved with prednisone and nebs, and his oxygen saturation levels improved; thus, suspicion for pulmonary embolism was very low and CTA was deferred. On discharge, patient will complete the prednisone taper and continue asthma medications. . # GERD: Continued home prilosec. . TRANSITIONS OF CARE: -full code -should f/u with scheduled ENT appointment at ___ for evaluation of known polyps -will complete prednisone taper (see discharge meds) -Recommend outpatient Pulmonary follow-up
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with difficulty breathing secondary to asthma exacerbation as well as an allergic reaction. You were treated with epinephrine for the allergic reaction. There was some concern initially for swelling in your throat, but a CAT scan showed that you did NOT have this, which was reassuring. It is unclear what your allergy was to, but since you had similar symptoms in the past from taking ibuprofen, you should avoid ibuprofen containing medices such as motrin and advil. For the asthma, we treated you with prednisone and nebulizers. You responded well and did not require any oxygen. On discharge, you should complete a prednisone taper (slowly decrease the dose) as per instructions below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o F with PMhx of morbid obesity, T2DM, anemia, OSA, asthma complicated by recurrent hospitalizations who p/w ___ days of worsening SOB, wheezing and cough not relieved by prolonged CPAP use. #Acute Asthma Exacerbation in the context of recent viral illness: CXR did now show any opacities to suggest PNA or edema. Lung exam and symptoms were much improved within ___ days of treatment with Pred, Alb/Atrovent nebs and Azithromycin 500mg. Ambulatory Sats > 90% on pt felt back to baseline by the time of discharge with much improved peak flows. Pt was continued on CPAP for OSA and has a home machine. #Contaminated blood culture: Pt had 1 of 2 blood cxs from admission grow GPCs which were later identified as coag negative staph. All subsequent surveillance Cx were negative for growth and this was felt most c/w contamination as pt remained afebrile without any leukocytosis. #DMII: BG were poorly controlled after prednisone burst for asthma. Pt was covered with insulin while in house but hyperglycemia delayed discharge due to high BG on the afternoon of planned discharge. Prednisone was decreased to 20mg for a planned ___s pt reported history of rebound respiratory symptoms with rapid/short courses. Pt was monitored for another 24hrs and was discharged on Metformin 1000mg BID and instructed to increase Glimepiride to 4mg daily throughout the last three days of prednisone to prevent hyperglycemia at home. Pt was encouraged to drink lots of water and avoid high sugar foods for a few days after discharge. #HTN: Continued HCTZ 25mg daily #Chronic Pain: Continued home Gabapentin and Tizanidine # Report of Bed Bugs: pt was continued on contact precautions throughout admission and was seen by SW. Currently being addressed in her living situation. Transition issues: - Blood sugars and resp symptoms at PCP ___
summary: You were admitted with an asthma exacerbation likely triggered by a virus that improved with steroids, nebs and supportive care. You were monitored an additional day on antibiotics because of concern for possible bloodstream infection but it turns out that it is most likely a contaminant. We will continue to monitor the cultures and will let you know if anything changes. Your blood sugars have been running high in the hospital while on steroids and we have been using insulin to cover the highs. You should continue taking Metformin and Glimepiride 4mg (two tabs) while we are decreasing the prednisone. We anticipate that your blood glucose while continue to run a little high over the next few days but should return to normal after you finish the prednisone. Please drink lots of water to maintain hydration and avoid high sugar foods in the meantime.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old woman visiting from ___, with a medical history significant only for asthma without known hospitalizations/intubations, peak flows of ~ 50% of expected, presenting with dyspnea, scattered wheezes on exam, CXR without evidence of PNA, baseline lytes not suggestive of acidemia, breathing comfortably on floor; altogether her presentation is consistent with acute asthma exacerbation. # Asthma exacerbation. Given the lack of other exposures, this flare was likely exacerbated by her recent upper respiratory infection. She was started on prednisone 40mg, given albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers, given magnesium sulfate, and was started on a five day course of azithromycin. She made a quick recovery and was ambulating without significant shortness of breath within hours of admission to the medicine service. She should maintain her prednisone for five days, complete the course of azithromycin, and take her albuterol nebulizers as needed. She also has at least moderate asthma that may be under-treated. She should follow up with her primary doctor in ___, who can consider additional therapy. It was recommended that she wait until ___ before flying back to ___.
summary: You were admitted to ___ on ___ with a flare of your asthma probably due to a recent upper respiratory illness. You described a shortness of breath that would not go away despite using the albuterol inhaler you regularly take for your asthma. We treated you with additional albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers, magnesium sulfate, a steroid called prednisone, and an antibiotic called azithromycin. A measurement of your breathing called a peak flow was found to be reduced at 200. A repeat measurement was 300, indicating improvement. Within hours of your admission you felt well enough to leave and were able to walk without significant shortness of breath.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and Plan: ___ year-old male with a history of asthma, hypothyroidism and recent rib fractures who presents from home with worsening cough and chest pain. ##Cough: There was no evidence of pneumonia on admission CXR. The most likely etiology was thought to be recent bronchitis overlying his chronic asthma. Any infection should have been effectively treated with his outpatient courses of antibiotics, but he continued to have residual severe cough worsened by pain. For his cough, the patient was treated with oral prednisone taper, albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers, and fluticasone-salmeterol disks. Smoking cessation was reinforced. ##R flank pain The patient's right flank pain was thought most likely due to the fractures of his ___ and 8th ribs, shown on admission CXR. The rib fractures were thought most likely due to severe cough. His pain was controlled initially with IV dilaudid and later with po dilaudid and po MSContin. CT chest on ___ showed no evidence of pathologic fracture. ##Hypothyroidism The patient was stable on his home levothyroxine. ##Nutrition/Prophylaxis: The patient was given heparin 5000U TID for DVT prophylaxis and kept on a regular po diet.
summary: You were admitted for persistent cough as well as pain in your right rib cage. For your cough, we gave you oral steroids to reduce possible inflammation from your asthma. We also gave you inhaled albuterol nebulizers and your home Advair. Since your CXR showed no evidence of pneumonia and you had finished two courses of antibiotics as an outpatient already, you were not given antibiotics. For your right rib pain, chest x-ray confirmed fractures of your ___ and 8th ribs on your right side, which was known from a previous chest x-ray from your outpatient visit on ___. We gave you dilaudid for pain control. A chest CT showed fractures but no other reason for the fractures. Your cough may take a long time to go away as you were recently a smoker. Please stop smoking as it will help your cough go away and be beneficial to your health.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/oF with subacute worsening of shortness of breath likely multifactorial related to volume overload and reactive airway disease and restrictive physiology. # Dyspnea: Likely a component of volume overload especially given recent diuretic reduction coupled with bronchospasm from underlying reactive airway disease. CTA on admission negative for PE. Patient initially diuresed with IV lasix and then transitioned to outpatient PO regimen of 80mg daily (on last hospital admission she had been discharged on furosemide twice weekly). Cardiology consulted given concern for managing volume overload in setting of severe AS and tenuous fluid balance. Cardiology suggested continuing oral furosemide regimen and no further diuresis as patient's residual dyspnea likely related more to reactive airway disease. Patient seen by pulmonary consult team for reactive airway disease management and they recommended continued use of atrovent, holding albuterol, reducing prednisone to outpatient regimen of 10mg daily and getting outpt sleep study given strong suspicion for OSA. Pulmonary also recommended titrating O2 to keep sats >93%. Patient sating in high 90's on 2L at time of discharge. Would suggest continuing to titrte down O2 to keep sats greater than 93%. Outpatient sleep study ordered--patient should be contacted by sleep lab for appt. Would eventually transition patient from nebulized ipratropium to inhaler form and discharge her on this medication. # UTI: Patient found to have ESBL E.Coli. Started on IV Meropenem. She should complete a 14 day course to end ___. # AFib: Patient remained rate controlled on outpatient regimen of metoprolol 50mg BID. Warfarin reduced to 1mg daily. This is a warfarin dose reduction (she was taking ___ daily) thus INR should be monitored closely and adjusted as necessary to maintain INR ___. # Acute Renal Failure: Likely pre-renal in setting of giving IV lasix. Renal function improved to Cr of 1 after changing patient back to her outpatient regimen of furosemide 80mg daily. # S/P Left Hip Fixation for Femur Fx: Surgery on ___. Staples removed during this hospitalization and post-op femur xray checked and looked good. Patient should follow up with ortho in 4 weeks. She can continue taking tylenol for pain control. # RA/SLE: Prednisone reduced to 10mg daily. Patient should continue prophylactic dose of Bactrim until ___ as this reduced dose does not require PCP ___. # Glaucoma: Continued on home eye drops # Peripheral neuropathy: Continued gabapentin # Anxiety: Continued outpatient Lorazepam. # GERD: Continued omeprazole. CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath which we believe was related to fluid overload and your underlying lung disease. You were restarted on your regular dosing of lasix. You were seen by the cardiology team who believe your aortic stenosis is stable and do not believe that this is the major contributor to your breathing problems. You were also seen by the pulmonary doctors who suggested that you start a new inhaler called ipratropium, stop using albuterol and have an outpatient sleep study. An order has been placed for this study and you should be contacted by the sleep lab. You also were found to have a urinary tract infection and were started on IV antibiotics. You were seen by the orthopedic surgery team who removed your staples.

text: Brief Hospital Course: At admission her vitals were been stable and she her sats were 92% RA-> 98% 2L. Peak flows at admission were 280 and 300 and the patient had bilateral wheezes on exam. CXR demonstrated infiltrate vs atelectasis and she received a dose of levofloxacin as well as combivent. She also received 2L of NS with a total of 40mEq of K+. Because her lactate was elevated, a CT abdomen and pelvis was obtained that did not show any acute process and her levsin was continued. Her antibiotics were discontinued as she remained afebrile and without a white count. She continued to receive duo nebs and was started on a 5 day course of corticosteroids (last day ___ for her asthma exacerbation. Teaching was provided on the correct usage of her inhalers and a home visit was scheduled with ___ ___ to ensure proper home usage of medications. Of note, a focal nodular opacity in the right lower lobe posteriorly was found on CT of the abdomen. This appeared unchanged from a CT previous CT dated ___, and on discussion with the radiology department the apperance is more consistent with scarring rather than neoplasm. Would recommend follow-up in 6 months for evidence of progression, however if nodule remains stable further follow-up is not indicated. The patient's diabetes was also managed. Her laboratory work was suggestive of a lactic acidosis therefore her metformin was discontinued. Her lactate levels decreased approximately one day after this medication change. She was sent home on glyburide and asked to discontinue metformin use because of the risk of metabolic acidosis. This lactic acidosis was thougth to be due to the metformin as her lactate continued to increase from 2.5 to 3.2 despite adequate fluid resuscitation and correction of mild hypoxia- despite these measures her lactate continued to rise. ___ was also found to have a microcytic anemic on admission with a Hct of 34.7. Iron studies were normal, therefore this was attributed to anemia of chrnoic disease.
summary: You were hospitalized for an acute asthma exacerbation thought to be caused by a viral infection. In the hospital, you were treated for your asthma and diabetes. We stopped your Metformin because we thought it may be contributing to a metabolic disturbance in your blood- please do not take this medication anymore! We also continued to treat your abdominal pain, hypertension, cholesterol and reflux disease while you were in the hospital. We are changing some of your medications on discharge. Please stop taking your metformin. Instead of metformin, you will be taking glyburide 5 mg daily for your diabetes. You were also started on prednisone for your asthma. Please continue to take this medication for 3 days after discharge. We also discussed the proper usage of your asthma inhalers. You should use 2 puffs of your Fluticasone inhaler two times daily even if you are not having asthma symptoms. Your combivent inhaler should be used for treatment of your asthma when you are having symptoms. Please use ___ puffs when you are symptomatic.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with asthma, recurrent C. diff, diabetes, presenting to the emergency department complaining of cough, exertional dyspnea, sore throat, malaise, weakness, diarrhea. . # Asthma exacerbation: The patient's shortness of breath was felt to be related to asthma exacerbation. CXR did not show pneumonia. The patient was treated with a 5-day steroid taper, with rapid improvement in her symptoms. Home asthma medications were continued (albuterol, theophylline, zafirlukast, Advair, loratidine). The patient's breathing was back to baseline at the time of discharge. . # Strep viridans bactermia: Patient had Strep vividans in ___ blood cultures. TTE was negative for vegetation. The patient was seen by the ID service, who recommended ___ to evaluate for endocarditis and also CT abdomen/pelvis to evaluate for abscess. The patient refused these studies and elected to be treated ___ IV antibiotics alone. She will complete a 2-week course of IV vancomycin on ___. The patient remained afebrile throughout her course. If the patient develops a fever, she needs to return to the hospital. . # C. diff colitis: The patient has a known history of C. diff. C. diff PCR was positive on ___. She was started on oral vancomycin. . # Hyperglycemia: The patient was managed with an insulin sliding scale while on steroids. Her hyperglycemia resolved once the steroid taper was complete. She was discharged off of all diabetes medications. She used to tape glipizide but this was stopped . # Relative neutropenia: The patient developed a relative neutropenia, with a normal ANC of ___ but 30% neutrophils on the day of discharge. summary: You came to the hospital with cough, sore throat, difficulty breathing, and diarrhea. You were treated with steroids for an asthma exacerbation. You found to have a bloodstream infection with a type of bacteria called Strep viridans. The source of the infectious was unclear. You were seen by the infectious disease service, who recommended a transesophgeal echocardiogram to evaluate for a heart valve infection. The infectious disease service also recommended a CT scan of your abdomen andn pelvis to evaluate for an abscess. You declined both of these studies. If you have any fever you need to return to the hospital right away, as this could be a sign that your bloodstream infection is not responding to the antibiotics. You are being treated with an antibiotic called vancomycin. This is an intravenous medication. You had a special IV called PICC placed prior to discharge. The PICC can be removed once your course of vancomycin is complete. While on vancomycin, you will need some laboratory monitoring, including a complete blood count, with differential, vancomycin trough, and liver and kidney function tests. This should be done on ___. You were diagnosed with C. diff. You are being treated with oral vancomycin. Please continue this while you are on IV vancomycin and for 2 weeks afterward.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT/PLAN: ___ yo man w/ hx of schistosomiasis and ascarisas infection treated in ___ presenting with new cough and wheezing. . COUGH: patient presented with several days of dry cough and a one day history of wheezing and chest tighness. In the ED the patient's D-Dimer was elevated and a CTA chest was preformed, which did not show evidence of PE but did show ground glass opacities suggestive of, but not diagnostic of pneumonia. These could represent either atypical bacterial or viral infection given his sick contact at work but this finding would not explain the degree of brochospasm he is currently having espically as he has no hx of reactive airway disease. Incidental note of reccent schistosomiasis and ascaris treatment in ___ was made and a possible allergic response to dead pulmonary schistosoma was entertained. Patient was treated symptomatically with standing nebulizers and a Zpack. He was discharged with PRN combivent inhaler (provided education prior to discharge) and to complete a 5 day course of azithromycin. Recommendation for PCP follow up in a week if symptoms do not improve over the next ___ days. . HTN: stable, continue lisinopril 20 mg daily and asa 81 mg daily. . HEP B: per atrius records, not on therapy, will defer to outpatient setting. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -pt is a full code -pt was advised to follow up with PCP if symptoms do not resolve -continued ID follow up deffered to outpatient provider.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for evaluation of your chest pressure and shortness of breath. A CAT Scan of your lungs was performed and fortunately did not show any evidence of a blood clot in your lungs. You were diagnosed with a pneumonia and treated with antibiotics. Some of your wheezing was felt to possibly be related to an allergic reaction to the schistosomiasis you were previously treated for and you were given inhaled medications to treat these symptoms. You should expect the wheezing and shortness of breath to improve over the next week. You can also use over the counter allergy and cough medications to treat your symptoms.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ transferred with elevated troponin I and chest/jaw discomfort during an obstacle course race. Negative Trop x2. summary: You were recently transferred here with an abnormal troponin test and chest/jaw discomfort. You were evaluated with EKG and further blood tests which were reassuring. Given your heart murmur and some small changes on your EKG, we are recommending that you get a formal transthoracic echocardiogram (TTE), which is a picture of your heart. We are also recommending that you get a stress test. We are recommending that you take two new medications, atorvastatin, for your high cholesterol, and a baby aspirin, to help reduce your risk of heart disease. Please schedule an appointment with your ___ primary care doctor as soon as possible to discuss these tests and to follow up.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ hx severe COPD and HTN who presents with 2d of cough and shortness of breath consistent with a COPD exacerbation. #COPD exacerbation: Patient initially required frequent nebulizer treatments in the ED and was admitted to the ICU. By the time she was in the ICU, she was not in distress. She was started on treatment of COPD with prednisone 60mg and azithromycin 500mg x1 then 250mg daily along with frequent nebulized treatments. CXR showed no focal infiltrate. She was transfered to the floor the following day. She had complaints of sore throat on admission with cough/secretions - possibly consistent with viral URI. Other potential exacerbating factors are possibly allergies (during this time of the season), postnasal gtt, and GERD symptoms. She has a baseline severe COPD/Chronic asthma with PFT FEV1 0.66 (41%) and reversal airway component (>12% increase in FEV1, FVC with bronchodilators), so I felt that along with the usual COPD treatments, the reactive airway component (and any exacerbating factors) should also be treated aggressively. She was treated with duonebs ATC and then PRN, advair, azithro, singulair, and prednisone. Afrin was given PRN for postnasal gtt, prilosec increased to 40 mg BID for likely GERD component. She had an allergic reaction to cetirizine and thus an allergy medication was not initiated during this stay. Zyrtec may be considered as an outpt. She was also given codeine for cough suppression as this might be precipitating bronchospasm. He was then transitioned to her home spiriva and weaned of oxygen. She was able to ambulate (with walker) and keep O2 sats above 90%. She was cleared by ___ to go home (with home ___. She was recommended to follow with her PCP in the following ___ weeks. # HTN: mildly hypertensive during this hospitalization (SBP 160). She was continued on clonidine, hydralazine. HCTZ was increased to 25 mg daily. # FEN: diet, replete electrolytes # Prophylaxis: - DVT: pneumoboots, heparin BID - GI: omeprazole # Access: PIVs # Communication: son ___ ___, son ___ ___, or ___ ___ # Code: DNR/DNI
summary: ___. As you know, you were admitted for shortness of breath consistent with worsening asthma. You were given multiple treatments in the intensive care unit and this was continued on the regular medicine floors. A chest x-ray here showed no evidence of pneumonia. We believe that the asthma exacerbation was likely due to a number of factors: possible a cold (viral infection), allergy, and acid reflux. You are given medications to assist with improving your breathing. Please be sure that your house does not have any mold (during this summer months) that can possibly worsen your breathing. Also avoiding places with a lot of smoking would also help.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Primary Reason for Hospitalization: Patient is a ___ female with past medical history significant for diabetes, hypertension, and hyperlipidemia who presented with 3 weeks of worsening shortness of breath and wheezing when supine and dyspnea on exertion particularly severe last night. She was found on CT to have findings consistent with small airway disease and was treated with inhaled and systemic steroids and inhaled bronchodilators. She was discharged with improved exercise tolerance and improved oxygen saturation on ambulation. . ACUTE CARE: . 1. Small airway disease: Patient presented with DOE, wheezing and paroxysmal nocturnal dyspnea. CT showed no pulmonary embolism, no pleural effusion, and no pulmonary edema, but was consistant with small airway disease. She initially had ambulatory oxygen saturation to the mid 80's on room air with ambulation and occasional dips at rest as well. This process of small airway inflammation is possibly from asthma vs. hot tub lung vs. other environmental irritant vs. viral bronchitis. With a rapid taper of oral corticosteroids and treatment with inhaled corticosteroids and bronchodilators, patient's symptoms improved and she had oxygen sats to low 90's on room air with ambulation. She was discharged with pulmonary followup with a suspicion of underlying OSA vs pulmonary hypertension as well. . CHRONIC CARE: . 1. Hypertension: Patient was continued on her home BP meds with moderately ___ pressures. . 2. Hypercholesteremia: Patient was continued on home Lipitor. . 3. Type II Diabetes: Oral hypoglycemics were held on admission but ___ over hospital course. Glycemic control was managed with insulin sliding scale. Patient's blood sugars were initially difficult to control while on oral corticosteroid therapy but were improving on discharge. She was instructed to monitor sugars at home and call her doctor if they were over 400 at home. . TRANSITIONAL CARE: 1. CODE: FULL 2. CONTACT:Patient and her husband 3. PENDING STUDIES: 4. ___: - PCP - recommend thyroid u/s to assess for nodules seen on CT scan - may need f/u of pulmonary nodule with repeat CT scan in 12 months
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath. Your oxygen levels were found to be low. Your cardiac enzymes and EKG were reassuring that this was unrelated to your heart. A CAT scan of your chest showed no clot or pneumonia. We checked an echocardiogram, which showed no major change in your heart function from prior. We gave you diuretics to take extra fluid off of your lungs. We had the pulmonary doctors ___, and based on our assessment with them of your physical exam and CT findings, we feel that you have bronchitis, either from an environmental irritant, allergen, or related to a viral infection that will clear. We think you may have a component of sleep apnea and recommend that you have a sleep study done as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE: ___ yo male with HIV (CD4 678) presented with dyspnea at both rest/exertion and hypoxia to low ___ on room air for several days. ACUTE ISSUES ============ #Hypoxia, dyspnea on exertion, and wheezing: Etiology unclear, but attributed to an unspecified reactive airway disease, perhaps triggered by a viral illness. Patient reported progressive dyspnea on exertion for several days, wheezing, and hypoxia.He had a leukocytosis with mild cough, but no fever and normal CXR. There was no concern for CA- or PJP pneumonia due to normal CXR and CD4 count >500. He was treated with duonebs, 5 days of steroids (1x IV methylpred, 4x po prednisone 60 mg), and 5 days of azithromycin. He received levofloxacin and bactrim in the ED, but these were not continued. On last day of prednisone, he had no further oxygen requirement. Pulm was consulted, as patient has had previous episodes of unexplained hypoxia and has a history of childhood asthma. Chest CT showed no interstitial disease, but other relatively non-specific findings. PFTs showed no gross abnormalities, however, interpretation was limited as patient received bronchodilator 1 hour prior to test. On day of discharge he had no oxygen requirement, however, he was discharged with supplemental oxygen and albuterol amps with nebulizer in case he has further episodes of hypoxia. He will follow-up with pulmin ___ weeks and get a TTE with bubble study and repeat PFTs as an outpatient. #Hyponatremia: Sodium 130 on admission. Most likely hypovolemic in the setting of dyspnea and increased insensible losses vs SIADH release due to acute lung process. No changes in mental status. Resolved with PO intake. CHRONIC ISSUES ============= #Type II diabetes: While on prednisone, patient had hyperglycemia in the 200-300s. Per the patient, his BG also rises to 280 on a daily basis. His long-acting glargine was increased while inpatient, but he was discharged back on his home regimen, as he had finished his prednisone. He was instructed to call his PCP if BG>300 at home. #Anemia, macrocytic: Patient is near baseline (___). #HIV: Last CD4 count 678 in ___. On atripla at home. He was given efavirenz and Emtricitabine-Tenofovir while inpatient. Discharged back on atripla. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== #Patient will follow-up with pulmonary as an outpatient. Final PFT results pending on discharge. He will schedule TTE with bubble study and repeat PFTs before his appointment. #Blood glucose was elevated while on prednisone (last dose ___ and should return to normal. Patient discharged on home regimen with no changes.
summary: You were admitted due to low levels of oxygen in your blood. Chest xray did not show any explanation. You were empirically started on breathing treatments, steroids and antibiotics for a possible exacerbation of reactive airway disease. We consulted the pulmonary specialists, as this is not the first time that you have presented with low oxygen levels. They recommended several studies to evaluate your lungs. Chest CT did not show signs of interstitial lung disease, and the other findings were relatively non-specific. You also had pulmonary function tests, which were relatively normal, but the interpretation was limited because you had a breathing treatment prior to the test. The pulmonary doctors would ___ to see you in their clinic to further evaluate you in ___ weeks. They recommened an ultrasound of your heart, which was not done during your stay, and a repeat lung function test before seeing them. You can schedule these appointments by calling the numbers below. You will be sent home with supplemental oxygen and breathing treatments to use with nebulizer machine for shortness of breath/wheezing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Dr. ___ is a ___ hx TBM/idiopathic intermittent airway anaphylaxis, DMII ___ years, on insulin), HTN (diet-controlled), asthma, and ?sarcoidosis who presents as a transfer from ___ for IP evaluation for TBM and cough. #Paroxysmal cough: Presented with 3 month history of frequent episodes of coughing paroxysms improved with sedation (propofol/lorazepam) and has been diagnosed with TBM in ___. Cough was felt to be triggered by entity separate from tracheobronchomalacia. Underwent extensive work-up for GERD including barium swallow, laryngoscopy, and impedance monitoring which only showed signs concerning for potential laryngopharyngeal reflux. Was started on high dose PPI and H2 blocker for presumptive reflux. Also started on advair for asthma, fexofenadine for possible allergic component, and lyrica for possible neurogenic mediated cough. Had several episodes lasting ~40 minutes initially in hospital, only received IV Ativan for 1 of those episodes, and had no further episodes for 5 days prior to discharge. Prior to discharge, it was brought to the medical team's attention that prior to symptom resolution there was concern for exaggerated coughing on the patient's part in order to receive more propofol and concern for addiction given the prior treatments with propofol. #Tracheobronchomalacia: Diagnosed with TBM in ___ and referred here for evaluation and management. CT trachea here showing TBM L>R. PFTs with normal FEV1, FVC. IP and thoracic surgery consulted regarding possible stenting. Repeat dynamic bronch demonstrated significant tracheobronchomalacia. Stent trial was considered but trachea was too large for stent placement. Option of surgical tracheobronchoplasty was considered but given cough improved with medical management, Dr. ___ preferred to opt for conservative management and IP and thoracic surgery agreed there was unclear benefit from surgery. #Laryngopharyngeal reflux: Bronchoscopy with evidence concerning for laryngopharyngeal reflux. Continued on PPI BID. Impedance monitoring on PPI w/o evidence of acid or non-acid reflux. #Asthma: ___ year history of mild asthma. Has used albuterol intermittently. Clear lung exam. PFTs on ___ with normal FEV1, FVC. Started on Advair this admission TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [] To continue Lyrica 75mg BID for ___ months, then taper over 1 month [] Continued on PPI. Please follow-up and determine whether continued therapy indicated # Communication/HCP: Dr. ___ (phone number: ___ Wife (Mrs. ___ (intl phone #: ___ # Code: Full, confirmed
summary: You were having severe episodes of coughing. There was concern that tracheobronchomalacia could be causing your symptoms You underwent extensive work-up including CT trachea, bronchoscopy, laryngoscopy, modified barium swallow, impedance monitoring. This revealed that you do have significant tracheobronchomalacia. You were started on several medications to control your cough including: Advair, Lyrica, pantoprazole, ranitidine, fexofenadine. Your coughing episodes improved and you preferred to pursue conservative management instead of undergoing surgery Please continue taking Lyrica 75mg twice a day for ___ months and afterwards taper off over the course of 1 month. Please continue taking pantoprazole twice a day.

No wheeze to suggest bronchospasm, however, patient reports cough is her main symptom during asthma exaccerbations. She reported that she often gets exaccerbations after viral illnesses in the ___. She also reported that she generally increases her prednisone to 20mg during exaccerbations. She was satting well on RA throughout admission, CXR on admission negative for PNA. We maximized her nebulizer treatmetn and started 5 day course of prednisone at 40mg PO daily. We continued tamiflu (last dose on ___. # Asthma Exaccerbation: Patient has long standing history of asthma with exaccerbations typically presenting similar to current hospital presentation. Neurovascularly intact on exam. ___ have been side effect from methylpred received in ED. Pins and needles improved on ___ in the AM. Did not return during hospitalization. summary: You came into the hospital after experiencing worsening cough and shortness of breath at home. You were recently diagnosed with the flu and were started on a medication to help fight the infection. Your symptoms were caused by an asthma attack that was triggered by the flu virus. You were given extra nebulizer and inhaler treatments during your hospital stay. One of your medications, Prednisone, was increased to help decrease your cough and shortness of breath. You were discharged on an increased dose of Prednisone for a few days. Once the 40mg of predisone is complete please restart your home 5mg of prednisone daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ PMHx asthma (no previous hospitalizations prior to current illness), presents for shortness of breath and cough for the past month, found to have pneumonia here. ACTIVE ISSUES: ============== # Bibasilar Pneumonia/Asthma: Given lobular appearance on imaging, likely represents bacterial pneumonia in the setting of multiple prednisone courses for presumed asthma exacerbation from viral URI in ___. Additionally had a positive blood culture that grew Streptococcus pneumoniae resistant to azithromycin and tetracycline, which may explain why she did not improve after getting this antibiotic as an outpatient. She was started on IV antibiotics, which were narrowed to ceftrixone for two days, on which she experienced dramatic improvement. Her pneumonia was considered a CAP versus HCAP She was discharged on a course of Levaquin 750mg x5days. Because she had been on >21 days prednisone as an outpatient, she was also given a prednisone taper. Her asthma medications were continued here. She was additionally given a cough regimen of guaifenesin for symptomatic relief. # (+)Blood Culture for Strep pneumoniae: Single bottle on day of admission, other cultures have been no growth to date. Given highly likely pulmonary source, no further workup was initiated as an inpatient. Per ___ guidelines, transient bacteremia in setting of CAP does not preclude transition to oral antibiotics, which was done on the day of discharge. # Sepsis/Clinical Hypovolemia: Met ___ SIRS criteria on presentation with likely source (pulmonary). VS improved from day of admission, back to baseline HR of 50-60s. Patient endorses eating well, taking good fluids on the day of discharge. # Contaminated UA: 64 WBCs with Large Leuks, but 20 Epithelial cells were also seen. Patient denied urinary frequeny or dysuria. She did have an orange color to her urine initially, which improved with better hydration. # Back Pain: L-sided, course coincides with her worsening cough. Not thought to represent pulmonary embolism or parapneumonic process given physical exam and imaging. Controlled with Ibuprofen, Tylenol, Flexeril and Lidoderm. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # h/o Thrush: L rear buccal surface sloughing noted on admission exam, but no thrush lesions. Home nystatin solution was continued. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Discharged on Levofloxacin 750mg daily x5d (Last dose ___. - Discharged on steroid taper given >21d on this medication. - If febrile after 5 days, may have parapneumonic process requiring further evauation. - Consideration of CXR in ___ weeks to ensure resolution of infiltrate.
summary: You were admitted for pneumonia. We started ___ on a course of antibiotics that ___ will finish as an outpatient. This will cover your infection in the lung and the transient blood infection ___ had. We also recommend continuing your pain medications for the next several days to control your left-sided back pain. Reducing this pain will allow ___ to take deep breaths, which will help keep the airways clear (and prevent worsening of the pneumonia). We will also give ___ a prednisone taper on discharge, as ___ have been on this medication for a while, and sudden discontinuation can cause withdrawal symptoms. Please see below for your medications and appointments. Again, thank ___ for allowing us to participate in your care.
