text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY ==================== Pt is a ___ yo F w/ ___ notable for asthma requiring intubation as a child who presented with 1 week of fever, chills, generalized myalgias, cough and SOB, found to have asthma exacerbation and lobar PNA, treated with 5 day course of steroids and levofloxacin. Hospital course complicated by repeated episodes of SVT (believed to be orthodromic AVRT). TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== [] Will need closer follow up and mgmt plan for asthma, discharged on fluticasone BID, can likely consider just doing PRN when having URIs in the future [] Patient had several episodes of SVTs, can reconsider electrophysiology study and ablation if she continues to have recurrent episodes of palpitations or syncope once recovered from pneumonia [] Recommend discontinuing Adderall given SVTs [] treponemal Ab for Syphilis still pending at discharge [] incidental finding of 2mm LUL nodule on CT chest ___, unlikely to require interval imaging given low risk. ACUTE ISSUES: ============== # R middle lobe pneumonia Pt presented with one week of fevers, cough, myalgias, initially thought to be c/w viral URI. CXR on admission was not concerning for PNA. However, pt's sx of cough worsened and she became febrile with a productive cough and ongoing wheezing, and repeat CXR (___) showed e/o right middle lobe pneumonia. Flu swap, resp panel/cx neg, negative urine legionella/step pneumo, blood cxs. She was treated with a 5 day course of levofloxacicn. Lack of improvement by D3 (___) so got CTA which was unremarkable and troponins neg. Symptomatic cough relief by the time of discharge. # Asthma Exacerbations Pt has hx of severe asthma exacerbations requiring intubation as a child, in the absence of URIs actually has intermittent asthma. On admission treated for asthma exacerbation iso lobar PNA. Pt was given prednisone 50 mg (5 day total treatment). Levalbuterol and ipratropium nebs used instead of albuterol due to SVTs as below. Discharged with ipratropium as well as fluticasone which she can continue to take until PCP follow up, at which point it may be appropriate to just take fluticasone during periods of URIs. She notes a history of heart racing episodes with albuterol, of note. # SVT On presentation, pt had asymptomatic SVT episodes to 170s thought to be due to albuterol. She then triggered on the floor with HRs 200s, initial EKG showed a narrow complex tachycardia with P waves consistent with either atrial tachycardia given different morphology P waves from baseline EKG vs retrograde P waves but with prolonged RP interval which would be consistent with orthodromic AVRT. Per EP suspect this was likely a reentrant rhythm with long RP. Rhythm recurred during hospitalization, sometimes responsive to vagal maneuvers. Unremarkable TTE. Plan to f/u with EP if this continues after treatment of her PNA. # Constipation Pt with constipation, abdominal discomfort, and bloating, resolved with bowel regimen. # Bacterial vaginosis Pt complained of sx of vaginal discharge while inpt, given 1 day fluconazole. She was screened for GC/CT/HIV which were negative but treponemal Ab for Syphilis was still pending at discharge. # Uptrending BUN, resolved Pt's BUN uptrending during admission, likely ___ poor PO intake and dehydration, resolved. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # ADD Pt is on Adderall at home which she will frequently take when she is working. Consider stopping as outpatient iso newly found SVT given stimulant effect # Anxiety Pt rarely takes Xanax 1 mg for anxiety. Given Ramelteon for insomnia while inpatient.
summary: You had fevers, chills, feeling fatigued, and shortness of breath concerning for an asthma exacerbation and were found to have pneumonia in your right lung You were given steroids for the asthma exacerbation and the initial thought was this was a virus. You ultimately developed a pneumonia which we saw on a chest X-ray, so you were given antibiotics (levofloxacin) for 5 days. You continued to have a cough so we gave you medications to help with your symptoms and pain. You had episodes of your heart going very fast so were seen by the Cardiologists. This was most likely due to frequent use of the inhalers in addition to your infection and being on steroids. Thir heart rhythm is not dangerous but if it continues to bother you, your primary care doctor ___ schedule you to see the cardiologists as outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ h/o breast cancer s/p partial mastectomy and chemo/XRT ___, PE ___ on Xarelto, asthma, radiation fibrosis, s/p pleurodesis who presented to the ED with R flank pain, associated with dyspnea. # Dyspnea # Acute asthma exacerbation: Patient presented with shortness of breath which she states started on the day of admission. She described a sharp pain localized to the lower right back worse with inspiration. She has an extensive history of asthma poorly controlled with numerous hospitalizations for asthma exacerbation. On arrival to the ED, she was noted to have accessory muscle use and to be hypoxic to 90% on room air. CT chest showed no evidence of infection, PE or effusion. Right flank pain was thought to contribute to worsening SOB due to poor inspiratory effort. She was given duonebs throughout the admission and O2 for comfort. She weaned herself off of oxygen on day prior to discharge. Pulmonology was consulted and they recommended pain control, incentive spirometry, continuing with nebulizers and starting an 11 day course of steroids with taper due to presumed asthma exacerbation. The patient's breathing returned to baseline and was able to ambulate without dyspnea. The patient was discharged with close follow up. # Right flank Pain # Hepatic cyst rupture On admission, the patient complained of severe right flank pain of 1 day duration and CT showed one large simple hepatic cyst and multiple smaller simple cysts. CT showed no evidence of PE, and UA showed no evidence of infection or blood to suggest renal infection or calculus. Free perihepatic fluid was noted concerning for hepatic cysts rupture. This was believed to explain right flank pain. The morning following admission the patient's flank pain resolved with lidocaine patch. It was believed that the flank pain was originally due to cyst rupture and therefore consistent with sharp initial pain rapidly resolving. The patient's pain was controlled with Tylenol, Oxycodone PRN and a lidocaine patch. The patient was discharged with follow up for liver cysts as no intervention was indicated for findings of simple liver cysts. # Leukocytosis The patient initially presented with an elevated white blood count to 15.6. There were no focal signs of infection or inflammation. This was likely due to possible ruptured cyst vs. pain vs. asthma exacerbation. Blood cultures remained negative, CRP showed some nonspecific inflammation. Leukocytosis down trended and WBC was 8.2 at discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # History of pulmonary embolism: CTA was negative for PE on ___. The patient is followed by Dr ___, and remaines on low dose rivaroxaban. This was continued throughout admission. # Lower extremity edema, chronic likely due to venous stasis: Remained at baseline during hospitalization. Home Lasix was held post-contrast to monitor renal function. It was restarted prior to discharge following several repeat Creatinine within normal range. # GERD: Patient was continued on home omeprazole 20 mg bid # Itching Patient was continued on home ranitidine 150 bid # Restless leg syndrome and neuropathy: Pt had stable tenderness to gentle touch and neuropathic pain in lower extremities bilaterally. She was continued on home venlafaxine XL 75mg daily, pramipexole 1mg qhs, clonazepam 0.5 mg bid. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Will continue steroid taper for a total of 11 days (___): 50mg on ___, 40mg on ___, 30 mg on ___, ___, 20 mg on ___, 10mg on ___. - Will need to follow up with outpatient pulmonologist at ___ for resolution of symptoms following steroid course - Continue home symbicort and albuterol nebulizers as needed - Use lidocaine patch and Tylenol as needed to control pain to ensure it does not affect your breathing.
summary: You came to ___ because you were having severe back pain and shortness of breath. You were found to have liver cysts and an asthma exacerbation. Please see more details listed below about what happened while you were in the hospital and your instructions for what to do after leaving the hospital. We did imaging of your abdomen and found you to have several liver cysts, with one that possibly ruptured. We determined that this is likely what was causing your back pain. You were also having trouble breathing. The lung doctors saw ___ and we agreed that you were having an asthma exacerbation. We gave you steroids and continue nebulizers.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: Ms. ___ is a ___ F here with shortness of breath and hypoxia that was felt to be from BiPAP non-compliance at home. Patient had BiPAP and agressive diuresis while inpatient with improvement in her symptoms. She was discharged to rehab for further recovery prior to discharge home. . # OSA/Hypercarbia: Patient presented with hypnogogic hallucinations and day time somulnece after an extended period of non-compliance with her BiPAP machine. the patient was seen by our pulmonologists and found to have a pCO2 of 108 when not using BiPAP that improved to the ___ while on BiPAP. In addition to BiPAP use while sleeping the patinet was aggressively diursed (negative ___ L a day) in order to decrease and chest wall restriction and component of pulmonary edema that may have been adding to her work of breathing. Patient was discharged to rehab to have continued bipap administration and physical therapy and had a follow up in sleep clinic scheduled for after discharge from rehab. Settings on BiPAP were IPAP 19, EPAP 15. . # HYPOXIA: patient was noted to be hypoxic on room air with O2 saturations as low as 85% the patient required several liters of oxygen by nasal canula to maintain saturations in the high ___ while at rest. These symptoms were felt to be the result of obesity hypoventilation syndrome rather than another process. The patient was discharged to rehab with the plan that lower than normal oxygen saturations ie titrating nasal O2 to 85-92% rather than high ___ in order to avoid pulmonary toxicity from oxygen. Active diuresis should be continued with goal negative ___ L daily until creatinine rises. . # NOROVIRUS: patient developed high output stool that was c diff toxin negative and resolved after 48 hours while inpatinet felt to be related to viral enteritis. . # UTI: Patient had a Urine Cx from admission growing cipro/bactrim/ceftriaxone resistant E coli that was treated with nitrofurontin for a 3 day course. . # Lymphemdema: Patient has extreme lower extremity lymphedema and certianly part of her compromised respiratory status oculd be from pulmonary edema though exam is very limited. Anti-fungal cream per home regimen. . # MOUTH SORES: Evidence of tongue trauma on exam along the lateral tongue surface abutting bite surface. ___ be result of angioedema from lisinopril as patient gives temporally related history of worsening tounge edmea after starting this medication versus generalized edema. Patinet was started on losartan with discontinuation of ace inhibitor and treated with viscous lidocaine for symptom control. . # HYPERTENSION: Patient was switched to losartan 25 mg with adequate blood pressure control. . # ASTHMA: bronchospastic process was felt to contribute to hypoxia and patient was given nebulizers. . # OA: Stable on home diclofenac and oxycodone prn . # MORBID OBESITY: encouraged weight loss and discuss bariatric surgery . # MICROCYTIC ANEMIA: Iron deficient according to ___ iron panel continued supplemental iron . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Patient is a full code -Patient should be on BiPAP whenever sleeping -Please continue aggressive diuresis until creatinine rises.
summary: Both of these symptoms were felt to be related to retaining carbon dioxide in your lungs. This difficulty breathing is related to your weight as well as fluid in your lungs. You were put on your BiPAP machine and your breathing improved. It is extremely important that you continue to use your machine WHENEVER you are sleeping. Not doing so can be very dangerous and may shorten your life dramatically. Part of your problems with breathing come from being overweight and you should try dieting to help improve your breathing. We also gave you medications to help you remove fluids from your lungs. During your hospitalization you developed an a viral diarrheal illness that improved by the time of your discharge.

Patient was discharged to rehab to have continued bipap administration and physical therapy and had a follow up in sleep clinic scheduled for after discharge from rehab. Settings on BiPAP were IPAP 19, EPAP 15. . # HYPOXIA: patient was noted to be hypoxic on room air with O2 saturations as low as 85% the patient required several liters of oxygen by nasal canula to maintain saturations in the high ___ while at rest. These symptoms were felt to be the result of obesity hypoventilation syndrome rather than another process. The patient was discharged to rehab with the plan that lower than normal oxygen saturations ie titrating nasal O2 to 85-92% rather than high ___ in order to avoid pulmonary toxicity from oxygen. Active diuresis should be continued with goal negative ___ L daily until creatinine rises. . # NOROVIRUS: patient developed high output stool that was c diff toxin negative and resolved after 48 hours while inpatinet felt to be related to viral enteritis. . # UTI: Patient had a Urine Cx from admission growing cipro/bactrim/ceftriaxone resistant E coli that was treated with nitrofurontin for a 3 day course. . # Lymphemdema: Patient has extreme lower extremity lymphedema and certianly part of her compromised respiratory status oculd be from pulmonary edema though exam is very limited. Anti-fungal cream per home regimen. . # MOUTH SORES: Evidence of tongue trauma on exam along the lateral tongue surface abutting bite surface. ___ be result of angioedema from lisinopril as patient gives temporally related history of worsening tounge edmea after starting this medication versus generalized edema. Patinet was started on losartan with discontinuation of ace inhibitor and treated with viscous lidocaine for symptom control. . # HYPERTENSION: Patient was switched to losartan 25 mg with adequate blood pressure control. . # ASTHMA: bronchospastic process was felt to contribute to hypoxia and patient was given nebulizers. . # OA: Stable on home diclofenac and oxycodone prn . # MORBID OBESITY: encouraged weight loss and discuss bariatric surgery . # MICROCYTIC ANEMIA: Iron deficient according to ___ iron panel continued supplemental iron . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Patient is a full code -Patient should be on BiPAP whenever sleeping -Please continue aggressive diuresis until creatinine rises.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o ___ speaking only female with a remote history of sigmoid colon cancer s/p resection in ___ who presents with 1 month of productive cough and SOB most likely due to viral infection. She was also found to have a chronic LUL granulomatous nodule on imaging, active TB ruled out with induced sputum x 3. # Viral bronchitis/SOB: Pt described worsening cough and SOB over the last month. Given associated sore throat, nasal congestion and evidence of bronchitis on CT from OSH clinical picture was most consistent with viral respiratory infection. During this hospitalization SOB was associated with coughing fits and sputum production, likely with mucous plugging. There was also likely a component of inflammation and reactive airway disease secondary to viral infection given intermittent wheeze and decreased air movement. These symptoms improved with staggered albuterol/ipratropium nebs and mucolytics. She initially was on supplemental oxygen however this was weaned off and at the time of discharge she was satting >90% on room air at rest as well as with ambulation. She was transitioned from nebs to albuterol inhalers, with instruction on use prior to discharge. She was also discharged with guaifenesin-dextromethorphan and benzonatate for cough suppression. Will need follow up with her PCP. # LUL calcified granulomatous lesion: Given chronic LUL granulomatous lesion on imaging (not previously treated for active or latent TB per patient report), duration of symptoms and recent travel to ___ she was initially placed in negative pressure room on precautions while she was ruled out for TB with induced sputums x 3. Preliminary results from all 3 samples returned negative prior to discharge. PPD was also performed which returned negative (read on ___, 0mm), ruling out latent TB. # Abdominal/anterior chest pain: Pt complained of abdominal and chest discomfort with coughing thought to be muscular in nature. There was also thought to be a component of reflux given description of symptoms. Exam remained benign. She was treated with acetaminophen as needed for pain as well as GI cocktail with good effect. # Acute urticaria: She developed a new urticarial rash on ___. This was most likely a contact allergy. Offending allergen remained unclear, possibly oxygen tubing from nasal canula or hospital gown. Bedding was changed. She was treated with PRN benedryl and sarna with improvement. # Eosinophilia: Absolute eosinophil count of 1538 (moderate category) on admission. Unclear significance given normalization on repeat labs ___ although has been noted to have eosinophilia in the past. This may be related to environmental allergies. If this persists in the outpatient setting consider further workup. # Microscopic Hematuria: RBCs noted on UA. This appears to be chronic based on records. Etiology remains unclear. Consider further workup as an outpatient. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Started albuterol inhaler, cough suppressants for supportive care - Assess for resolution of rash, prescribed benedryl and sarna PRN - Consider further workup for chronic eosinophilia - Consider further workup for chronic microscopic hematuria - Consider further workup of normocytic anemia - Incidental LUL pulmonary nodule vs granuloma measuring 8mm, non-calcified in superior lingular segment noted on OSH CT. Needs 6 month follow up CT - CODE STATUS: Full, confirmed - CONTACT: ___, ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of worsening cough and difficulty breathing. A chest XRAY was performed that showed a lesion in the left upper lobe of your lung that had been present previously. Given your symptoms and recent travel to ___ we placed you in respiratory isolation and got sputum samples to rule out active tuberculosis that returned negative. We placed a PPD (skin test for tuberculosis) that was also negative. Since this result was negative you do not need any further treatment with antibiotics. While you were here you were treated with nebulizers to help with your breathing along with medication to bring up mucus and help with cough. This cough will likely take several weeks to resolve completely. You can use the albuterol inhaler at home if you feel wheezy or short of breath with coughing. You had some abdominal pain from muscle strain due to coughing. You can continue taking tylenol at home for this after discharge (up to total of 3grams per day maximum). You also developed a rash that looked like hives from an allergy to something that came in contact with your skin. You can take benedryl (diphendyramine) and apply sarna lotion as needed for itch.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ female w/tracheobronchomalacia s/p tracheotomy, asthma, GERD, chronic bronchitis, p/w SOB, dizziness, and thick green secretions from her trach. #Shortness of breath - It was thought that her symptoms were caused by inflammation and increased secretions obstructing the trach tube in the setting of a treat and resolving respiratory infection. CXR was negative for acute disease. Mild gaseous distention of the splenic flexure, without evidence for obstruction. CBC showed leukocytosis to 13.7, which trended down during her course. Her oxygen saturation was 98-100% on room air throughout her hospitalization. Her lung exam was notable for expiratory wheeze at the beginning but was CTAB for several days before discharge. For this problem, she was put on an aggressive expectorant regimen including mucomyst nebs and guaifenesin; interventional pulmonology performed bronchoscopy and debrided trach secretions. During the course of her hospitalization, Ms. ___ subsequently became tachycardic >140 in the setting of receiving both standing and prn albuterol. ECG was normal showing sinus tachycardia. Chest CTA was performed and ruled out pulmonary emboli, it subsequently also showed ground glass changes that were interpreted by the primary team to be consistent with resolving pneumonia. Sputum cultures were negative for pathogens. On discharge, her SOB was improved, leukocytosis normalized, and she was able to maintain an SpO2 of 98% after ambulation. #Dizziness - Patient complained of dizziness throughout her admission, She was found to be orthostatic. She was fluid resuscitated with IV fluids, and encouraged to take more POs. Her dizziness had resolved on discharge. #Tracheobronchomalacia - fiberoptic laryngoscopy in ___ showed laryngeal stenosis and scarring and suggested that she may not have any closure of her vocal cords. Interventional pulmonology was asked to assess airway using bronchoscopy. They found "bronchopulmonary dysplasia with chronic tracheostomy tube and laryngeal stenosis status post bronchoscopy with patent tracheostomy tube and patent distal airways with minimal secretions." They recommended followup with ENT for their recommendations for the laryngeal stenosis. An appointment with ENT was made on discharge. #Asthma - asthma exacerbation may have contributed to her airway problems at the beginning of her illness but it was felt to be well controlled since patient was treated with short-course 40mg prednisone and continued on her outpatient asthma regimen. When patient became tachycardic, albuterol was replaced by xopenex and frequency of treatments were decreased. On discharge, her SpO2 was 98% on RA and she did not have expiratory wheezes on lung exam. #GERD - Not an active issue during this admission, but given her upper airway malformation, her omeprazole dose was increased to 20mg BID to decrease her chances of aspiration pneumonia.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of difficulty breathing through your treacheostomy site and with increased mucous secretions. Your chest x-ray remained unchanged and we did not find evidence of active infection. The interventional lung doctors performed ___ through the tracheostomy and cleared some of your secretions, we believe this was the cause of your symptoms. Furthermore, a chest CT scan was performed to rule out blood clots in your lung that may be causing shortness of breath and fast heart beats. This test was negative for clots in the lung. You should continue the evalauation of your throat by the ear nose and throat doctors.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with RA, HTN, NPH s/p shunt, CRI, with recent discharge from ___ for bronchitis (___) p/w left flank pain that began suddenly during a bout of coughing. ## Pleuritis. DDX of left flank pain beginning suddenly during a bout of coughing, pain on inspiration/coughing only - rib fracture v. pleuritis. In ED, PE was considered as patient recently immobile (recent hospital admission), could not get CT scan due to CRI and V/Q scan indeterminate. However, patient's wells score = 1.5, O2 sat >94%, no tachycardia, pain reproducible on palpation - very low likelihood of PE so no empiric treatment started. Patient underwent rib series x-rays, which showed no fractures. Most likely etiology of pain is pleuritic inflammation from bronchitis/excessive coughing. Mrs. ___ was given tylenol 1G q8h for pain (NSAIDS contraindicated as she has CRI) with good relief, pain much improved at discharge. ## Bronchitis with reactive airway disease. Patient completed 5 days of azithromycin and prednisone just 2 days prior to current admission. She was noted to have significant rhonchi on exam consistent with bronchitis and frequent dry coughing likely ___ reactive airway disease. Her symptoms improved greatly with nebulized albuterol, inhaled steroids, and re-starting PO steroids. She was discharged on 20mg prednisone for 3 more days with follow-up with her PMD on ___. ## Chronic Renal Insufficiency. Patient's Cr slightly elevated from baseline on admission (Cr 2.4 from baseline ~2). Patient was given gentle IV hydration, home regimen of HCTZ was held and Cr returned to 1.9 within one day. She was re-started on HCTZ on discharge. ----Inactive Issues--- # Rheumatoid arthritis: Followed by Dr. ___, on monthly gold injections for ___ years. # Hypertension. BPs well controlled during admission. Continue HCTZ 12.5mg daily on discharge. # Normal pressure hydrocephalus status post VP shunt. No acute issues. # Urge incontinence: On oxybutynin 5mg daily. # Seasonal allergies: On loratadine 10mg daily. # Osteoporosis: Diagnosed in ___ by bone density, on alendronate # GERD. Continue home regimen of omeprazole 20mg qhs # Restless legs syndrome: No acute issues. # Anemia: Continue iron supplement on discharge. ----Transitional Issues---- # Patient referred for ___ evaluation on discharge for assessment of needs as well as checking on patient's recovery as she was a re-admission # F/U PMD on ___, assess progress
summary: You were admitted to ___ with sudden-onset left flank pain in the setting of being treated for bronchitis. ___ had a chest x-ray and rib x-ray while you were admitted, which did not show any pneumonia or rib fractures. ___ also had a V/Q scan (an imaging test to evaluate for conditions such as blood clots in the lungs), which was indeterminate. However, ___ were deemed to be at very low risk for blood clot. Your flank pain is most likely due to inflammation of the tissues near your lungs and ribs from your bronchitisYou will need to continue taking steroids to treat your bronchitis for 3 more days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of severe asthma with multiple admissions for asthma exacerbation who presented with another asthma exacerbation, thought to be triggered by an upper respiratory tract infection. She was treated with a short steroid burst and received supportive care for her upper respiratory tract infection. Her symptoms slowly improved and she was discharged on prednisone 40 mg daily until follow up with her primary care physician and pulmonologist. She did experience RUQ abdominal pain and has known cholelithiasis. A RUQ u/s showed cholelithiasis, but no evidence of choleycystitis. She also had a drop in Hct to 31, with guaiac positive stool, while remaining hemodynamically stable. GI was consulted and felt that endoscopy was not necessary but agreed with starting PPI given high dose steroids. #Asthma exacerbation: Patient has longstanding hx of severe asthma, w/ recent prednisone taper from 60mg in ___, when she became ill with a URI and has now had worsening dyspnea/cough. CXR was reassuring against PNA. Ambulatory sats remained in the range of 91-93%. The team encouraged ambulation and she was able to work with ___. She was continued on 40mg prednisone daily until patient and Dr. ___ was made aware of her admission. She was also continued on Levalbuterol nebs q2h prn (given tremor on albuterol, cetirizine, montelukast, fluticasone-salmeterol, and dapsone for PCP ppx given longstanding steroids. We monitored daily Peak Flows (currently at 225; at home 250-300), and encouraged continuous ambulation until her ambulatory sats reached the mid-90s. #Anemia-patient was admitted with a Hct of 34/36 and after 5 days of admission had a very sharp drop to a Hct of 31. It was stable x2 (___) and the patient did not endorse dizziness or lightheadedness outside of her usual range. She did endorse black, but not loose stools and stated that stools have always been dark due to Iron intake. She was ordered for hemolysis labs which were unremarkable. GI saw her and did not believe that endoscopy was necessary at this time, as she was not acutely bleeding and it appears to have resolved. In ___, she did have an upper endoscopy which showed some gastritis in antrum, but no ulcers or duodenal lesions. She was Guiac positive on ___. GI recommended transitioing her to Pantoprozole 40mg BID and D/C ranitidine. #Cough/URI: Patient presented with cough and asthma exacerbation with URI symptoms. This rapidly improved with guaifenesin and acapella valve. CXR on admission did not show evidence of pneumonia. Iptratropium nebs were to improve mucous clearance and she was placed on a 5 day course of azithromycin, which was completed on ___. #pAfib: She was admitted with a supra therapeutic INR. Coumadin was held and became therapeutic after 4 days. She was then continued on her 2.5mg daily regimen with daily INRs. Transitional Issues: [ ] Follow up with PCP and pulmonologist (Dr. ___ [ ] Steroid taper as an outpatient [ ] continue PPI BID given concern for GIB and high dose steroids [ ] re-check H/H as an out-patient within 5 days (with INR) [ ] consider further evaluation of cholelithiasis/RUQ abdominal pain [ ] Pt's Lasix dose switched to QOD from daily given transient episodes of dizziness during admission # Code Status: Full # Contact Info: ___ (boyfriend) ___
summary: You presented to the hospital with an asthma exacerbation. This was likely triggered by an upper respiratory tract infection. We treated you with antibiotics and an increased prednisone dose. During your admission, your blood counts dropped and your stool showed evidence of blood in it. We started you on a medication to protect your stomach from irritation while you are on steroids. We also talked to the GI doctors who ___ that you did not need an endoscopy but agreed that you should be on a medication to protect your stomach (pantoprazole). You experienced abdominal pain and have known gallstones. An abdominal ultrasound showed that you have gallstones, but they are not obstructing your bile duct or causing inflammation. Your symptoms slowly improved and you are now feeling well enough to go home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o woman with DM, HTN admitted with Influenza B infection and acute asthma exacerbation. CXR showed no acute abnormalities. She was treated with a course of ostelamavir and a prednisone taper. Course complicated by steroid-induced hyperglycemia. ___ was consulted and recommended insulin NPH once daily and insulin aspart sliding scale while on prednisone. She received diabetic and insulin administration education. On day prior to discharge, she had a trending pulse ox on RA with nadir O2sat of 92. Pt was found to have a new diagnosis of atrial fibrillation incidentally noted on telemetry. Pt was asymptomatic. Brief episode of nonsustained RVR with activity. Otherwise, HR remained well controlled on home carvedilol dose. After a few hours, she spontaneously converted to NSR. TTE showed no significant valvular abnormalities. CHADS2vasc score was 3 for DM, HTN, and gender giving her a stroke risk of > 3%/yr. She had no risk factors for bleeding complications. After a risk vs benefit discussion, pt agreed to start therapeutic anticoagulation with a newer agent. Based on her insurance company's formulary, she was started on Rivaroxaban.
summary: You were evaluated for cough and shortness of breath. You tested positive for the flu and were started on medication for the flu as well as steroids and breathing treatments for your asthma. You improved slowly with this treatment. You will need to taper your prednisone and administer insulin as follows: prednisone 40 mg daily ___: administer 20 units of insulin NPH daily and Humalog sliding scale as needed prednisone 20 mg daily ___: administer 10 units of insulin NPH daily and Humalog sliding scale as needed prednisone 10 mg daily ___: no insulin NPH, administer sliding scale as needed only You were diagnosed with atrial fibrillation. After discussing the risks verses benefits of starting blood thinners, you agreed to go initiate therapy. You were started on a medication called dabigatran for anticipated life-long treatment. Please stop taking hydrochlorothiazide until you are seen by your primary care physician due to low blood pressures while you were in the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is ___ year old woman with multiple medical problems presenting with chest pain and SOB. # Dyspnea/Chest pain: The patient had a history of asthma and reported being on a prednisone taper for the last 2 weeks for a asthma flare. Her symptoms were reportedly similar to her known asthmatic symptoms but with additional left chest pain. ___ EKG without strong evidence of new T wave inversions compared to previous. Cardiac enzymes were negative x 3. O2 saturation was stable. The patients symptoms was felt to be consistent with her asthma and she was continued on advair, singulair, allegra and flonase. Duonebs were initiated and the patient was restarted on prednisone. She was discharged on a steroid taper. She was instructed to follow-up with her primary care physician. # DM: Pt was hyperglycemic on admission (BG: 450) and the patient reports that this was because of the prednisone given in ___. She continued to have elevated blood sugars throughout her admission, again felt to be related to her steroid use. Her home regimen of insulin was increased with moderate control. summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath and chest pain. Our studies show that your symptoms were not related to your heart and that they are most likely due to your asthma. You have been treated with steroids and inhalers which you should continue to use until directed otherwise by your Primary care doctor, ___. ___. Your blood sugar was elevated during your hospitalization and this is probably due to the use of prednisone. We have recommended several changes to your insulin use. First, you should continue to use your Lantus(Glargine) but at a new dose of 22U every morning. In addition, you should take your blood sugar with meals and before bed and use the insulin sliding scale to determine an additional ammount of Lispro(Humolog) insulin you should take for correction.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/Hx of chronic reactive airway disease ___ GERD, environmental allergies, and significant environmental chemical and allergen exposures p/w two weeks of dyspnea and cough preceded by fever and malaise. Clinically stable s/p duonebs and PO steroids. # Reactive Airway Disease Patient has known RAD from GERD, documented on pulmonary consult notes from ___. He does not have a documented diagnosis of COPD. It is possible that he has developed COPD from this chronic aspiration along with a chronic pneumonitis from the pesticides but we have no evidence of this directly. It is also quite possible that in addition to his RAD from GERD that he has a viral URI causing additional airway irritation which is the most likely. History suggestive of pneumonia (likely viral) again makes RAD the most likely diagnosis. Pt. started on prednisone burst for ___s freuqent nebulizers. His symptoms improved significantly and he was discharged home. His peak flow improved to >400 (unknown baseline) and he was saturating well at rest and ambulating. # GERD Significantly symptomatic from GERD. Transitioned to pantoprazole 40mg daily and started on calcium carbonate PRN. # Benign Hypertension Continued on atenolol. # BPH without obstruction Continue on Tamsulosin, Oxybutinin. summary: You were admitted to the medicine service for evaluation and treatment of your shortness of breath. This was thought to be a result of inflammation in the lungs triggered by a viral pneumonia. It also seemed that your acid reflux made your symptoms worse. Further, it is possible that your lungs have been affected by exposure to pesticides over many years, making them more vulnerable to such inflammation. You were able to breathe more easily after receiving inhaled medicines that open up your airways and pills that treat inflammation. Upon discharge, you should continue to take prednisone pills and use the albuterol and ipratropium inhalers, as described in detail below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yr old female hx DMII,hyperlipidemia, asthma, mixed connective disuse disease admitted ___ with shortness of breath and cough. CXR negative for pneumonia. Acute Asthma Exacerbation =Pt was afebrile and hemodynamically stable without hypoxia on initial presentation. CXR neg and flu swab negative. Trigger of asthma exacerbation is thought to be a viral respiratory infection. Pt was treated with po prednisone and duonebs with improvement in her respiratory status. Pt is to complete a 7 day course of prednisone 60mg daily on discharge and then resume chronic home prednisone 7.5mg daily. Otitis External/tympanic membrane perforation -Pt was seen by primary care for this issue a few days prior to presentation and found to have otitis externa. Pt was started on corticosporin gtts and had been using them until the evening of ___ when she noted diminished hearing. Exam notable for possible left TM perforation so corticosporin gtts stopped and ofloxacin gtts started on ___. Plan is to treat with po augmentin x 7days and ofloxacin gtts x 7days. An ENT consult was requested but pt did NOT want to wait to be seen by them so pt has been advised to f/u with primary care for ENT/audiology evaluation. Diabetes Mellitus Type II -Pt was placed on an insulin sliding scale/diabetic diet while in house. -Home diabetes medications were held and pt advised to resume them on discharge. - Mixed connective Tissue Disorder -Continue home prednisone 7.5mg daily after completing 7 day course of 60mg daily. note recent change in her dose to 7.5mg daily. - abatacept, hydroxychloroquine continued
summary: Your chest x-ray did not show any evidence of pneumonia and your flu test was negative. You were treated with prednisone and nebulizer treatments with improvement in your condition. We are providing a few more days of a higher dose of prednisone. Please complete the course of the higher dose of prednisone as directed and then resume your usual daily dose (7.5mg daily). You have an infection in your ears and we will treat this with ofloxacin ear drops as well as augmentin. There was concern for a perforation (tear) in your ear drum so we recommend follow up as an outpatient with an audiologist as well as an ENT specialist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Hypoxia and Dyspnea: Pt was initially admitted to floor on ceftriaxone and azithromycin for presumed PNA as well as steroids for COPD exacerbation. He became more tachypneic (to the ___ during the night of his first hospital day with poor air movement and worsening C02 retention as well as serial ABGs showing persistent acidemia, hypoxia, and hypercarbia, and was transfered to ICU for BIPAP. Patient poorly tolerated Bipap on and off throughout his first day in the ICU. Overnight while on BiPAP, patient became acutely agitated/confused with worsening C02/acidosis on ABG, increased tachypnea and worsening air movement. He was intubated for hypercarbic respiratory failure. Pt's f/u CXR showed blossoming of RLL PNA. Pt was treated for CAP with 5 day course of levaquin. He was able to be successfully extubated on hospital day 4, transiently on Bipap and then stable on 3L NC. He was treated initially with 60mg of steroids and then tapered slowly for a planned 14 day taper. He was continued on albuterol, his advair dose was increased and he had a successful trial of tiotropium. He was able to be transferred back to the floor where he was continued on his antibiotics, steroids, and standing/PRN inhalers and was hemodynamically stable as well as stable from a respiratory standpoint. He still demonstrated persistent desaturation to the 70% range during ambulation, however even at this time patient said he was "fine" and denied any SOB, lightheadedness, or other symptoms of hypoxia. He was kept inpatient until his ambulatory O2 saturation remained in the 90% range and then discharged home. . # EKG changes: Patient had TWI on admission EKG. He was ruled out for MI with 2 sets of negative cardiac enzymes. Several serial EKGs showed persistent TWI but no new changes. Patient was started on ASA and discharged on this. Echo showed R ventricle hypokinesis and moderate RV dilation but this was believed to be ___ patient's known severe pulmonary HTN as a consequence of his severe COPD. . # Fall: Patient had a fall on his R hip while in the ICU when he jumped up from bed and tried to ambulate without assistance. He reported no pain and exam showed no focal tenderness or ecchymoses. Physical exam was monitored for any complications of the fall, but the patient had no further pain, evidence of bleeding and was able to ambulate on his own (monitored closely by ___ without difficulty. . # Schizophrenia: Patient appeared to be stable on zyprexa. His zyprexa was continued throughout his hospital course and EKG was monitored for QTc prolongation. Patient did not have any complications of schizophrenia during his hospitizations but he was extremely anxious to leave the hospital as he does not like to be hospitalized and this makes him feel trapped. Per his home health aide, it was difficult to convince him to come into the hospital in the first place even when his home O2 saturation was in the ___. . # FEN: Monitored lytes, repleted lytes as needed, cardiac/heart healthy diet . . PPX: SC heparin, PPI (given steroids), bowel regimen . # Code Status: Full . # Contacts: - PCP: ___, MD ___ ___, Phone ___ - Home health aide: ___ ___ OR call the company (___) at ___ and get ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because your oxygen level at home was low and you were having some trouble breathing. We think that this was caused by an exacerbation of your known COPD as well as a pneumonia infection on top of the COPD. We treated you with several different inhaled medications for your COPD, as well as oxygen, as well as steroids and also an antibiotic for pneumonia. At one point during your hospital stay, you started breathing very fast and your blood showed that your oxygen was dangerously low. Because of this, you were taken to the ICU and intubated to help with your breathing because we were afraid you may tire out to the point where you could no longer breathe on your own. You were intubated for 1 day, then the tube was removed and you did well, and were able to transfer back to the floor. On the regular medical floor, your breathing was good however your oxygen was still low when you walked, so we kept you in the hospital until we felt you were stable from a breathing standpoint.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY ================== ___ with PMH notable for atrial fibrillation s/p PVI ___, aflutter s/p ablation in the past, liposarcoma s/p resection, asthma, now with asthma exacerbation and community acquired pneumonia. ACUTE ISSUES ============== # FEVERS and # ACUTE HYPOXEMIC RESPIRATORY FAILURE due to # COMMUNITY ACQUIRED PNEUMONIA and # ACUTE-ON-CHRONIC ASTHMA EXACERBATION: Presented with fevers and chills, leukocytosis, cough. Flu a/b, urine legionella and strep pneumo were negative. Fungitell and beta-d-glucan were pending on day of discharge (these were sent as the patient was recently on high dose steroids and could not remember whether he was also on bactrim prophylaxis). Allergy to penicillin, but tolerated ceftriaxone and cefpodoxime. Initially on supplemental oxygen, but was able to wean off to room air day two days before discharge after receiving 50 mg prednisone on day of admission, and 40 mg prednisone the subsequent days. He also received azithromycin and ceftriaxone, which was transitioned to cefpodoxime before day of discharge. He was maintained on his home inhalers (which were approved by pharmacy and he preferred to not keep in a lock box), and received nebulized levalbuterol as needed. He was discharged with antibiotics to complete a five day course for CAP - azithromycin day 1 was ___, last dose through ___ and cefpodoxime (ceftriaxone day 11: ___, cefpodoxime continue through ___. # LEUKOCYTOSIS In the setting of steroid use and active infection. Improved throughout admission. CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES: ======================= # NORMOCYTIC ANEMIA At baseline, stable throughout admission. # HISTORY OF ATRIAL FIBRILLATION/ATRIAL FLUTTER: No anticoagulation per outpatient discussions and Pt preference. He has recently seen Electrophysiology in ___ (Pt of Dr. ___ and again voiced his desire to avoid anticoagulation at this time. He was maintained on his home baby ASA and metoprolol. He was in sinus rhythm on telemetry. # HISTORY OF HYPERTENSION: He was continued on his home spironolactone and hydrochlorothiazide (split given no combo pill in-house)as well as home lisinopril. He was normotensive throughout his admission # HISTORY OF HYPERLIPIDEMIA: Home low-dose pravastatin. # HSV SUPPRESSION: Home acyclovir 200mg q24h. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== [ ] steroid taper: patient was discharged with one more day of prednisone 40 mg ___, and then 3 days of prednisone 30 mg daily until follow up with pulmonology ___. Please continue to taper steroids as clinically appropriate. [ ] azithromycin and cefpodoxime to finish ___ [ ] Patient with stable normocytic anemia Hgb 11.1 on day of discharge. Please work up as outpatient as clinically appropriate. #CODE: Full (confirmed) #CONTACT: ___ (Wife) ___ Mr. ___ is clinically stable for discharge today. The total time spent today on discharge planning, counseling and coordination of care was greater than 30 minutes.
summary: You were having trouble breathing You were diagnosed with community-acquired pneumonia, and required extra oxygen to help you breath. You were given antibiotics through your IV and then by mouth. You improved and were breathing much better, and didn't need any extra oxygen. Because you have an allergy to penicillin, we cautiously gave you a small dose of penicillin's cousin, an antibiotic called ceftriaxone (a cephalosporin). You tolerated this well without any allergic reaction, so then you received the full dose and did well. You can safely take cephalosporin antibiotics in the future. You were given steroids in the hospital for your asthma flair.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a history of bronchopulmonary dysplasia and tracheomalacia s/p multiple reconstructive surgeries, chronic tracheostomy, asthma, and GERD, who presented with several weeks of ongoing production of thick green sputum, increased secretions, general weakness, dizziness, and new sputum cultures positive for MRSA, consistent with acute on chronic tracheitis. #Tracheitis The patient's presenting symptoms of green sputum production, SOB, chills, subjective fatigue and poor PO intake, no leukocytosis and clear CXR resembled previous admissions for acute exacerbations of tracheobronchitis in the setting of chronic tracheostomy and poor baseline secretion control. New on this admission was a sputum culture positive for sparse growth of MRSA. In the ED, she received ceftazadine, aztreonam, and vancomycin, and on the floor was descalated to Vancomycin. Her symptoms quickly improved and was clinically ___ throughout her admission. Nebulizer treatments were continued. She was discharged home to complete 10 day course of IV vancomycin with Pulm, ID, ENT, and PCP follow up. - vancomycin q12h to be completed on ___ - vanc trough to be drawn ___ and faxed to PCP - follow up with Primary care provider, pulmonologist, and ENT. # Asthma: Preadmission nebulizer treatments were continued. # GERD: She was continued on home regimen of Loratadine 10mg QD, omperazole 40mg QD, ranitidine 150mg QD. # OCP: She received her oral contraceptive of levonorgestrel 1.5mg and ___ 1mg/20mcg as per her home medications. Albuterol 0.083% Neb Soln 1 NEB IH Q6H:PRN sob, wheezing 2. budesonide 0.5 mg/2 mL inhalation BID 3. ___ Phosphate 5 mL PO Q6H:PRN cough 4. Ipratropium Bromide Neb 1 NEB IH Q8H:PRN SOB 5. Microgestin ___ (21) (norethindrone ___ estradiol) ___ ___ oral Qday 6. omeprazole 40 mg oral Qday 7. Ranitidine 150 mg PO DAILY 8. Sodium Chloride 3% Inhalation Soln 15 mL NEB DAILY 9. Acetaminophen 650 mg PO Q6H:PRN pain/fever 10. Loratadine 10 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with increased sputum production, shortness of breath, and chest pain. Your sputum cultures that were obtained in the emergency room grew a bacteria called MRSA. You were found to have an acute worsening of your tracheitis. You were given IV antibiotics to treat your infection. We discharged you on IV Vancomycin which you will administer to yourself through your port to complete a 14 day course, stopping on ___. It is important that you finish your course of antibiotics as instructed, and that you continue your home nebulizer treatments. In addition, make sure to perform daily tracheostomy cleaning to prevent future infections.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia, Chronic Stable Asthma. #Cough #Dyspnea on exertion without hypoxia or tachypnea #Rib pain - diffuse, worse with coughing Patient reports biopsy confirmed dx of COP/BOOP at ___ ___ ___ years ago. Recently treated with steroids and several courses of abx. On arrival and throughout her hospital stay, patient noted to be hemodynamically stable, without hypoxia or tachypnea on room air. Ambulatory sats were 98-100%. Lung sounds were clear. Pulmonary consulted after transfer from OSH for further evaluation of her potentially acute on chronic lung disease. Recommended lab tests, of which RF was wnl, ___ negative, ANCA negative, and several other studies that were pending at the time of discharge. They also recommended additional imaging. CXR showed no obvious pathology. CT chest was also normal (see pertinent results above for full final read). Pulmonology initially recommended IV solumedrol, which the patient tolerated well (with no rash or signs of clinically significant allergic reaction). This was transitioned to oral prednisone, 20 mg, after the CT chest showed no evidence of active lung disease. For her cough, she was treated symptomatically with tessalon perles, dextromethorphan, and guaifenison. She was also started on pantoprazole once daily for chronic cough. For her pleuritic chest pain, she was initially treated with IV morphine, this was discontinued after the normal CT chest, and she was treated with tylenol and PRN ibuprofen. Pulmonology recommended that she follow up with her primary Pulmonologist at ___ within 1 week. The Pulmonology consult team will follow-up on her pending lab results and contact the patient with these results. We strongly advised her to quit smoking and provided her with nicotine patch, which she declined. #Pruritis and Rash - Possible allergic reaction to steroids. We discussed the patient's case with the Allergy consult service, who informed us that skin testing for possible oral steroid allergies was not available for inpatients except in emergencies. Given her clinical stability, we recommended that Ms ___ follow up with ___ Allergy clinic as an outpatient for skin testing, if she continues to have pruritis and mild skin rash with receiving oral steroids for her lung disease. An appointment has been made for her on ___ at 14:45. It was not clear that her pruritis and intermittent, very mild skin rash were in fact due to steroids, as several of her other medications on transfer(notably morphine) could contribute or be causative for pruritis and rash. Pruritis can continue to be treated symptomatically with continued Benadryl PRN. Of note, patient should not take short-acting antihistamines (Benadryl) for a full 3 days prior to allergen skin testing, and should not take long-acting antihistamines (e.g. Claritin, Allegra) for a full 7 days prior to allergen skin testing.
summary: You were transferred to ___ for further evaluation of your cough, shortness of breath, and pleuritic chest pain. You underwent a CXR, a CT scan of the chest, and were evaluated by the Pulmonary specialists. The CT scan of the chest showed no significant abnormalities. A number of lab studies were sent, and these will be followed up on by the Pulmonary doctors. ___ cough was treated symptomatically and you were started on prednisone (an oral steroid) for possible steroid-responsive lung disease. The pulmonary doctors recommend that ___ follow up with your primary Pulmonologist, Dr. ___, at ___ ___ within 1 week. You should not smoke cigarettes or other tobacco products. You should not smoke any other substances, as any smoke may contribute to worsening of your lung symptoms. For the rib/muscular pain you can take Tylenol and ibuprofen as needed, as well as cyclobenzaprine at bedtime for the next ___ days.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: #Cryptogenic Organizing Pneumonia, Chronic Stable Asthma. #Cough #Dyspnea on exertion without hypoxia or tachypnea #Rib pain - diffuse, worse with coughing Patient reports biopsy confirmed dx of COP/BOOP at ___ ___ ___ years ago. Recently treated with steroids and several courses of abx. On arrival and throughout her hospital stay, patient noted to be hemodynamically stable, without hypoxia or tachypnea on room air. Ambulatory sats were 98-100%. Lung sounds were clear. Pulmonary consulted after transfer from OSH for further evaluation of her potentially acute on chronic lung disease. Recommended lab tests, of which RF was wnl, ___ negative, ANCA negative, and several other studies that were pending at the time of discharge. They also recommended additional imaging. CXR showed no obvious pathology. CT chest was also normal (see pertinent results above for full final read). Pulmonology initially recommended IV solumedrol, which the patient tolerated well (with no rash or signs of clinically significant allergic reaction). This was transitioned to oral prednisone, 20 mg, after the CT chest showed no evidence of active lung disease. For her cough, she was treated symptomatically with tessalon perles, dextromethorphan, and guaifenison. She was also started on pantoprazole once daily for chronic cough. For her pleuritic chest pain, she was initially treated with IV morphine, this was discontinued after the normal CT chest, and she was treated with tylenol and PRN ibuprofen. Pulmonology recommended that she follow up with her primary Pulmonologist at ___ within 1 week. The Pulmonology consult team will follow-up on her pending lab results and contact the patient with these results. We strongly advised her to quit smoking and provided her with nicotine patch, which she declined. #Pruritis and Rash - Possible allergic reaction to steroids. We discussed the patient's case with the Allergy consult service, who informed us that skin testing for possible oral steroid allergies was not available for inpatients except in emergencies. Given her clinical stability, we recommended that Ms ___ follow up with ___ Allergy clinic as an outpatient for skin testing, if she continues to have pruritis and mild skin rash with receiving oral steroids for her lung disease. An appointment has been made for her on ___ at 14:45. It was not clear that her pruritis and intermittent, very mild skin rash were in fact due to steroids, as several of her other medications on transfer(notably morphine) could contribute or be causative for pruritis and rash. Pruritis can continue to be treated symptomatically with continued Benadryl PRN. Of note, patient should not take short-acting antihistamines (Benadryl) for a full 3 days prior to allergen skin testing, and should not take long-acting antihistamines (e.g. Claritin, Allegra) for a full 7 days prior to allergen skin testing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with asthma, bronchiectasis, ectodermal dysplasia, humoral immunity deficiency NOS on IVIG trial presenting with worsening dyspnea and productive cough. # Bronchiectasis exacerbation: Her presentation was more consistent with subacute to worsening chronic infectious process ___ setting of bronchiestasis and humoral deficiency NOS. CT Chest significant for interval worsening of bronchiectatic process, sputum cultures growing MSSA. Initially started on empiric broad coverage with Vancomycin, Cefepime and Doxycycline then based on IDs recommendations narrowed to ceftriaxone and doxycycline. Of note had allergic reaction to vancomycin, see details below. Was continued on ceftriaxone and doxycycline until discharge with clinical improvement, she was discharged on cefpodoxime and doxycycline to complete a 10-day course of antibiotics. Negative broad work-up including PJP smear, Cryptococcal serum Ag, Histoplasma Urine Ag, Aspergillus GM and Ab. Beta-D-glucan was positive which was thought to be related to her receiving IVIG. Had 3x induced sputum for AFB smears which were negative and culture which were pending at the time of discharge. #Iron deficiency anemia: MCV 73, ferritin 22, TRF 333, serum iron 25, TIBC 433, %Sat 5.4%. Started Ferrous sulfate 325mg PO BID w/ondansetron 4mg PO q8hr:PRN for nausea with iron. Nutrition saw the patient as an inpatient as well. #Humoral immunodeficiency: previously followed as an outpatient by Dr. ___ at ___. Received IVIG on ___, will have outpatient f/u. # Asthma exacerbation: Given significant wheezing and dyspnea she received prednisone 50mg ___ the hospital with a taper (decrease by 10mg each week) as well as albuterol/ipratropium nebs q4-6h and prn. Tiotropium, montelukast and ceterizine were continued. As symbicort was NF, she was bridged with Advair while ___ house. She was discharged on albuterol nebulizers and with a new nebulizer machine. # GERD: Continued on home omeprazole TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================== - patient has switched insurance and is no longer eligible for ___ outpatient primary care - Her new PCP is ___ at ___ -> she needs a referral to see a new pulmonologist within ___ weeks of discharge from ___ - she received her dose of IVIG ___ house on ___ - she received pneumococcal vaccine on ___ - she received home nebulizer machine and was discharged w/nebs - pending infectious studies should be followed up by PCP and pulmonologist FULL CODE Fiance, ___ ___ HCP is sister, ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Tiotropium Bromide 1 CAP IH DAILY 2. LORazepam 0.5 mg PO DAILY:PRN anxiety 3. Orsythia (levonorgestrel-ethinyl estrad) 0.1-20 mg-mcg oral DAILY 4. triamcinolone acetonide 55 mcg nasal DAILY 5. Montelukast 10 mg PO DAILY 6. budesonide-formoterol 160-4.5 mcg/actuation inhalation BID 7. Omeprazole 20 mg PO BID 8. Cetirizine 10 mg PO DAILY 9. albuterol sulfate 90 mcg/actuation inhalation Q4H:PRN wheezing/dyspnea 10. Sodium Chloride Nasal ___ SPRY NU BID:PRN nasal congestion 11. levonorgestrel-ethinyl estrad 0.1-20 mg-mcg oral DAILY
summary: You came to ___ because of increasing shortness of breath, wheezing and cough producing yellow sputum with increasing need for inhalers as well as a fever that "broke" prior to admission. When you came to the hospital, we took a chest X-Ray that was concerning for a pneumonia ___ several spots ___ both lungs. While you were ___ the hospital, we treated you for both a bronchiectasis and an asthma flare and started you on antibiotics and 60mg of prednisone. Cultures of your sputum identified a bacteria called Staph aureus and you were continued on the appropriate antibiotics for a total of 8 days (___). We also contacted our colleagues ___ pulmonology and infectious disease and they suggested additional tests for less common infections that were all negative.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with DM1, HTN, asthma, nonrelated living renal transplant in ___ on cellcept and sirolimus presenting with influenza-like illness. 1. Influenza- Found to have Influenza A on nasopharyngeal swab, likely H1N1 although final results still pending at the time of discharge. As the patient presented less than 48 hours after the onset of symptoms and was at high risk for complications, started on os___ 75mg BID x 5 days. Also continued supportive care with tylenol prn fever. Given that the patient was immunocompromised with a chronic respiratory illness, a high index of suspicion was maintained for complications such as an asthma exacerbation or bacterial superinfection (see below). By the time of discharge, the patient had been afebrile for > 24 hours and his symptoms had markedly improved. 2. Community acquired pneumonia: On ___, the patient was still intermittently febrile to 101, complained of new left sided pleuritic pain and worsening cough. CXR revealed a new left lower lung infiltrate, consistent with a diagnosis of pneumonia. Patient started on a seven day course of levofloxacin. 3. Asthma: Presented with symptoms of mild dyspnea and wheezing on exam, consistent with diagnosis of asthma exacerbation. Throughout hospital stay, the patient was maintained on his home medications of advair with albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers prn wheezing. Peak flow was monitored daily and always remained above 400, so patient was not felt to benefit from systemic corticosteriods. At the time of discharge, the patient was breathing comfortably on room air and only requiring nebs twice every ___ hours. 4. DM type 1: stable, continued on outpatient regimen of lantus and preparandial insulin with sliding scale inulin to correct for any hyperglycemia. Blood glucose remained at/ near goal throughout hospitalization with preparandial glucose ranging from 80 - 150 mg/dL. 5. s/p renal transplant in ___: Patient continued on regimen of cellcept and sirolimus (level measured within therapeutic range). Also continued on bactrim for primary prophylaxis of PCP in setting of immunosuppression. Renal function was trended daily and remained at baseline of 1.1. creatinine increased to 1.3 from baseline of 1.1. 6. HTN: Throughout hospital stay, patient's blood pressure remained slightly elevated with SBP in 130- 160s. Continued on home medications of amlodipine +/ - ramipril (enrolled in sirolimus/ ramapril study). Further optimization of blood pressure was deferred to the outpatient setting, once the patient was not acutely ill.
summary: You came to the hospital for fevers, body aches, headache, and cough. You were given fluids and Tylenol/Oxycodone for your pain. Blood cultures and labs were sent, which came back normal. You were swabbed for a flu test, which came back positive for influenza A. You were treated with Tamiflu while you were in the hospital and you will need to continue for a total duration of 5 days. While you were in the hospital you also developed evidence of a pneumonia as evidenced by a repeat chest X-ray and increased cough with sputum. We started you on levofloxacin, an antibiotic that you will need to take for a total of 7 days. Make sure to drink plenty of fluids, get plenty of rest, eat healthy foods, wash your hands frequently and stay home to avoid spreading the flu to other people (for at least 48 hours after the resolution of your fever).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo W with PMH of Type ___ DM, MDS, B-thalassemia trait who is called out of the MICU after resolution of septic shock ___ pyelonephritis from obstructive nephrolithiasis, now s/p stent and nephrostomy tube placement. 1. septic shock secondary to obstructive nephrolithiasis: Pt was admitted with septic shock from urosepsis from obstructive pyelonephritis. Lactate up to 5 range, fevers to 104, leukocytosis to 14 with 8 bands. Now s/p 4 liters NS in ED. Antibiotics given in ED with IV Ceftriaxone and gentamicin. Patient had associated N/V, flank pain. She is now status post ___ nephrostomy placement which was complicated by worse shortness of breath prior to procedure so patient was intubated in ___ suite. Sepsis is now complicated by emerging DIC picture. Urology & ___ following closely. Patient s/p temporary stent in ___, then L percutaneous nephrostomy tube placed. Admitted to MICU where received aggresive fluid resuscitation, maintained initially on gent/zosyn, then just zosyn for antibiotic coverage. Urine grew out pan sensitive Ecoli and Blood from A-line grew out GPCs. Pt had RIJ removed due to concern for line infection and LIJ was placed under sterile conditions. After replacement of the CVL, pt was able to be weaned from pressors and started having increased UOP from nephrostomy and foley. Pt was gently diuresed, she was successfully extubated on ___. Pt was transitioned from zosyn to ampicillin and than to oral amoxicillin with plan for 14 day course (___). Patient will follow up with urology as an outpatient for definitive treatment. 2. Afib with RVR: likely related to acute illness & volume overload and pt was loaded with Amiodarone over wkd and went back into sinus. During SBT on ___, pt developed AF and after discussion with primary cardiologist, started on oral load of Amiodarone with plan for titration in 2wks, overall goal for rhythm control and avoid risks of anti-coag. Rate control with goal HR in 60- 80s achieved with metoprolol 25mg TID. TTE on ___ showed preserved LVEF, LA normal. Through the remainder of hospital stay, continued gentle diuresis with lasix 40mg IV prn to meet net fluid balance of - 1L/day. Remained in NSR on discharge. 3. Respiratory distress: Resolving. Pt developped respiratory distress while in the ___ suite when she became tachypneic and had decreased oxygen saturations which were likely due to fluid overload as patient has known ___ and had received 4L IVFs, 6 Units FFP. Pt self extubated on ___ and was able to be weaned off oxygen quickly. Despite meeting diuresis goals of -1 to -1.5 L/day, the patient remained subjectively dyspneic with signs of volume overload on exam. CXR on ___ showed new interstitial edema and small pleural effusions, so she was diuresed further on morning of discharge with improvement in symptoms. 4. ARF: Presented with acute renal failure secondary to hydronephrosis and severe septic shock. With reversal of pathophysiology, creatinine improved form peak 2.5 to 1.1 on discharge. 5. Transaminitis: Likely ___ shock. LFTs continue trending down but she will need further monitoring as an outpt. 6. Anemia / MDS: Pt with longstanding history of transfusion requiring MDS with anemia. She was transfused for goal hct>23. Diff remains abnormal at baseline. 7. Asthma: Pt with history of asthma who was intubated for tachypnea and volume overload. There is still some evidence of cardiac vs asthmatic wheeze. Continued on Albuterol nebs while diuresing. 8. Type ___ DM: Outpatient oral medications held on admission and patient maintained on basal insulin with sliding scale through duration of hospital stay.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with left sided back pain and were found to have a kidney stone blocking the outflow of urine and causing a severe infection. You were monitored in the intensive care unit, initially requiring medications to help maintain your blood pressure and a breathing tube. The urologists put a stent in one of your ureters and placed a tube to help drain urine from the right kidney. While you were sick, you developed a heart arrhythmia called atrial fibrillation and you were started on amiodarone to help the rhythm return to normal. You were treated with antibiotics, which you will need to take for a total of two weeks. You will eventually need to have the kidney stone surgical removed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ ___ woman with pmhx of asthma and sarcoidosis, presented to the ___ ED complaining of shortness of breath and wheezing. In the ED, her initial VS were 97.2, 89, 173/89, 22, 98%RA. Labs were notable for normal WBC, Cr 1.2 (baseline 0.9). A CXR was neg for an acute process. The patient was given duonebs and dexamethasone for suspected asthma exacerbation. Peak flow was 220 after 3 neb treatments, so the patient was admitted to medicine for further evaluation. On transfer to the floor, the patient appeared mildly uncomfortable, though she spoke in full sentences with good air movement. She received two rounds of duonebs. During her hospitalization, Ms. ___ received two doses of Prednisone 60mg IV, before being transitioned to PO Prednisone on ___. She used ipratroprium/albuterol nebulizer treatments as needed. She was maintained on her home asthma medications. With this regimen, her peak flow increased to 400. Ms. ___ also reported improvement of symptoms, with decreased chest tightness and shortness of breath on exertion. She continued to have normal oxygen saturations on room air. Ms. ___ was discharged home on ___, with planned follow up with her Primary Care and Pulmonary doctors.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital on ___ for an acute exacerbation of your asthma. You were treated with nebulizer treatments (albuterol/ipratropium) and IV (intravenous) Prednisone, and then Prednisone pills. With this treatment, your Peak Flow increased from 220 to 400. You will be discharged on oral prednisone 60 mg daily. This medication dose will be decreased over time. Your primary care doctor, ___ manage this. Please resume your normal outpatient treatment regimen for asthma. We have not made any changes to your medications, except to increase the dose of prednisone. We encourage you to participate in your normal activities as much as you feel able to.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ F with hx of asthma (no formal PFTs, on albuterol inhaler only) who presented to ___ with cough and wheezing, consistent with asthma exacerbation. # Asthma # Viral infection Pt presented to the ED after several visits with her PCP for cough/wheezing/URI sx at the end of ___. She was treated as an outpatient with azithromycin as as well as two separate steroid courses. Asthma likely exacerbated by viral URI and post-nasal drip, given her persistent severe cough and wheezing after full antibiotic course as an outpt. Her chest x-ray was normal. CTA was also performed due to persistent sx, but was w/o PE or other abnormalities. Flu test negative, Resp viral panel negative, as well as Pertussis PCR. Her symptoms were difficult to control. Her breathing improved with standing duonebs, IV methylprednisolone, and aggressive cough suppressant medications: standing Tylenol, guaifenesin with dextromethorphan q3H, standing codeine q4h, Tessalon perles. She was also given Flonase and Flovent inhaler twice a day. She was switched to PO prednisone 40mg on ___. She will begin a prednisone taper shortly after discharge: Prednisone 40 mg for 2 days (___), prednisone 30 mg for 3 days (___), 20 mg for 3 days (___), 10 mg for 3 days (___), and 5 mg for 3 days (___). As an outpatient, she also will attend pulmonology follow-up appointment for formal PFTs. *Transitional issues*: -NEW MEDICATIONS: Prednisone taper: Prednisone 40 mg for 2 days (___), prednisone 30 mg for 3 days (___), 20 mg for 3 days (___), 10 mg for 3 days (___), and 5 mg for 3 days (___) -Tylenol ___ mg TID, guafenesin with dextromorphan, codeine, tessalon perles -Patient should attend follow up appointment with PCP and pulmonology ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list may be inaccurate and requires futher investigation. 1. Albuterol Inhaler 1 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN Shortness of breath 2. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID
summary: You came to the hospital because you had a severe cough and wheezing. While you were in the hospital, you were given nebulizers, IV steroids, and aggressive cough medications. Your cough improved on this regimen. Since you had already finished a 5-day antibiotic course (Azithromycin) before you came to the hospital, you were not given any more antibiotics. You had both xray and CT imaging that showed no infection or other problem in your lungs. When you leave the hospital, you will likely have a persistent cough for a week or so due to the inflammation in your airways, but it should improve with time, as it has in the hospital. You will continue on the cough medications, flovent inhaler twice per day, and prednisone. You will gradually decrease the prednisone dose (see below).

Albuterol Inhaler 1 PUFF IH Q6H:PRN Shortness of breath 2. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a pmh of poorly controlled asthma and frequent flares who presents s/p PEA arrest secondary to status asthmaticus +/- bilateral PTX. . # Respiratory distress: She presented from an OSH with bilateral chest tubes after needle decompression. She was intubated, and received solumedrol IV now on oral Prednisone taper. She is now s/p extubation and satting well on RA. Chest tubes d/ced by IP yesterday, some concern for small residual PTX on the right but recent CXR demonstrate no concerning PTX at this time. Dressings c/d/i. - History of asthma requiring 2 previous trips to the ED for treatment. Most recent exacerbation was "a few months ago." Lately, patient did say that there has been a construction site near her house and she has noticed her asthma worsening, using her inhalers during past week. She notes dust that covers everything in her house. - Patient also had bilateral subsegmental PE in RUL, RLL, LUL, L lingula demonstrated by CTA. Patient has reported allergic rxn to shellfish, gets anaphylaxis, so initiated pred/benedryl/cimetidine protocol prior to obtaining CTA. CTA went smoothly. Both of these processes could likely have caused her presentation and it is unclear if her PE could have set off an acute asthma exerbation or if her PE is subclinical. Currently, she is at RA, satting well and feeling well. Patient was guaic negative and started on anticoagulation (coumadin and lovenox bridge). - Patient was discharged on home medications (symbicort inhaler BID, albuterol q4-6h prn, as well as prednisone taper, to end on ___. For bilateral sub-segmental PE, patient was sent home on Lovenox to bridge, coumadin 5 po daily for 3 months. -Patient was set up with PCP and pulm appointments to f/u as outpatient. . # PEA arrest: 8 minutes of resuscitation, likely secondary to hypoxia from status asthmaticus but PE could have contributed to this picture as well. There is a possibility that the clot was bigger at one point and perhaps after 8 min of CPR, clot was dislodged and traveled more distally. Patient spon regained consciousness and then initiated cooling at ___. This was d/c upon arrival to ___ ED. -see above for workup and tx for asthma/PE. . # Anxiety: Patient becomes teary when talking about her asthma attack. Family also concerned that patient's anxiety at home could trigger asthma attacks. - Recommended F/U with PCP for longterm anxiety treatment - Informed patient and family about how to set up Lifeline at their request . # Leukocytosis: Likely secondary to acute inflammatory process and steroids. Now normalized. . Ongoing tobacco abuse: patient was counseled extensively about smoking cessation. Patient was very adamant about quitting after leaving hospital and understands that smoking increases her risk of future asthma attacks as well as a hypercoag state that could lead to another PE. . # Code: Full . . Pending tests: none. . Transitional Issues: Patient will need to have INR checked at PCP's office for initiation of Coumadin. We have called her PCP to set this up for her. We have also set her up to see a pulmonologist as outpatient to evaluate PE/asthma.
summary: You presented to the hospital with symptoms of shortness of breath and was urgently rescusitated and intubated. You were admitted for an acute asthma excerbation, which likely precipitated this event. We also found that you had sub-segmental pulmonary emboli in your lungs, on both sides. This means that you had small clots that traveled in the arteries that supply your lungs. Combination of smoking and using oral contraceptives increase this risk, especially in the setting of your family history. It is therefore critically important to your health to quit smoking. It is unclear if this pulmonary emboli event contributed to your asthma exacerbation or whether this triggered you to have shortness of breath and loss of pulse independently. We would like for you to follow up with a pulmonologist as an outpatient visit to assess your risk for future clots and exacerbations. In the mean time, we are treating you with coumadin, an oral anticoagulant, which prevents future clots for building up in your legs and your lungs. This medication will need to be taken for at least 3 months, to be stopped by your pulmonologist or PCP. This medication requires frequent follow up and blood tests to make sure that you have the correct blood level.
