text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ HIV+ on ART, recently quit smoking, presenting with 3 episodes of paroxsymal coughing leading to syncope who was admitted for observation and telemetry. Problems: 1. Syncope 2. Cough and shortness of breath 3. HIV 4. Smoking # The patient presented to ___ ED with approximately one week of constant coughing. This coughing was productive for sputum. He was initially treated as an outpatient for CAP with azithromycin and he completed a full ___espite the antibiotics the patient still continued to have coughing fits. Of note, the patient has a signficant smoking history and he recently quit. He arrived to the hospital after 3 episodes of cough induced syncope. All of which were witnessed. During this time, he never had any symptoms to suggest seizure. The patient was observed in the ED and started on oral prednisone. He was later transferred to the floor for overnight monitoring and telemetry. While on the floor nebulizers were very helpful in helping the patient to clear his secretions. By HD2, his lungs were clear to ausculation bilaterally and his nebulizers were helping him to clear the sputum from his lungs. Of note, all of his EKG's during this hospitalization were normal. He had no events on telemetry. However, he never had any syncopal episodes while he was admitted. The patient was discharged on a 5 day course of 40mg of oral prednisone and an ipratroprium/albuterol inhaler. He was further counciled on the harms of smoking and the importance of not restarting smoking cigarettes. Given his rapid response to the inhalers and steroids combined with his history of smoking, we feel like this episode might have been an exacerbation of possible underlying COPD. He was instructed on the proper use of ipratroprium inhalers with a spacer. -Follow up with PCP -___ 2 puffs QID -Albuterol prn -Consider outpatient pulmonary follow up for PFTs. -5 day course of 40mg oral prednisone. The patient was continued on his atripla. No changes were made to the patient's exsisting outpatient medication regimen. Transitional issues: -Ensure smoking cessation -Consider outpatient pulmonary workup with PFT's -Continue Atripla
summary: You were hospitalized for coughing induced syncope (fainting). We did not find any evidence for other more concerning causes of fainting such as heart disease or seizures. You have been monitored for the past 48 hours without any syncopal episodes. You cough is likely secondary to acute bronchitis and it has improved significantly with inhalations and prednison. You are now safe for discharge from the hospital. Given your tendency to faint from coughing we strongly urge you to avoid any activity like driving or operating heavy machinary which may put you or others at risk if you should faint. You should avoid such activity untill your coughs has completely resolved.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of severe asthma and recurrent URI over the past few months presenting with dyspnea and wheezing in the setting of pharyngitis and hoarseness. # Asthma exacerbation: Patient with decreased peak flow, symptomatic dyspnea and requiring frequent nebulizer treatments. Required stacked nebs and IV magnesium in the ED. She was started on frequent albuterol nebulizers, prednisone burst and given augmentin to treat URI (see below, likely trigger). On the day of discharge she was able to ambulate without dyspnea and was requiring nebulizers no more frequently than every 4 hours. She was discharged with instructions to taper her prednisone and given an albuterol rescue inhaler. She was also set up to see new Asthma Specialist as outpatient. # Pharyngitis: patient with recurrent pharyngitis and URI over the past several months. Reportedly her ENT exam was normal earlier this month, and she was diagnosed with glosspharyngeal neuralgia. She met ___ CENTOR criteria, and has been treated with multiple antibiotics, so she is unlikely to have GAS. Also with negative monospot as outpatient. Recent throat culture with beta strep, not GAS. She has been on steroids off and on, and uses ICS, so she could be considered somewhat immunocompromised in that area, but there was no obvious infection. She does cite issues with tonsilloliths over the past months, which can be evaluated further as outpatient with ENT. She was continued on augmentin with instructions to finish augmentin course started by outpatient ENT, and to stop valacylcovir as there was no obvious viral component. She was given prednisone burst as above, and connected to outpatient ENT where she can obtain second opinion on utility of tonsillectomy. # Leukocytosis: Presented with leukocytosis to 11.3. Likely secondary to steroid use vs. acute infection. Resolved during hospitalization. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== - continue prednisone with taper (40mg ___, 30mg ___, 20mg ___, 10mg ___ - continue augmentin for total of 7 days - follow-up with outpatient ENT as scheduled
summary: You were admitted for difficulty breathing likely due to an asthma exacerbation. You were given frequent nebulizers, prednisone and continued on antibiotics. We think this was likely triggered by an upper respiratory tract infection. For this, we kept you on augmentin, which was started by our patient ENT. We stopped the valacyclovir because we did not think there was a viral infection. We are providing you with a rescue inhaler to use if you do not have your nebulizer with you. Also, we recommend that you complete the course of augmentin prescribed to you by your ENT physician. We have recommended that you taper the prednisone over the course of a few days: On ___, take 40mg. On ___, take 30mg. On ___, take 20mg. On ___, take 10mg. We have set you up with a new Asthma Specialist at ___ and ENT physician as well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with history of asthma/COPD and Rheumatoid arthritis on Enbrel admitted with bronchitis and acute asthma exacerbation. #Asthma exacerbation/Bronchitis: Patient had evidence of both bronchitis, with cough and sputum production x3 weeks, along with asthma exacerbation with bronchospastic cough and poor air movement that improved with bronchodilator therapy. Given that the patient is immunosuppressed due to Enbrel and had failed therapy with azithromycin, levofloxacin was started for treatment of bacterial bronchitis to complete a ___dditionally, prednisone 40mg po daily x5 days was started along with albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers. The patient felt much improved prior to discharge and was discharged to continue duonebs until she felt back to baseline. Home advair, singulair, and fexofenadine were continued. Robitussin DM was also used to control cough with good effect. #Hypertension: Home lisinopril was continued #Rheumatoid arthritis: Patient was instructe not to take Enbrel until she has discussed her situation with her Rheumatologist given her likely active infection. #GERD: Home nexium was continued. #CODE: Full #Disposition
summary: You were admitted with an exacerbation of your asthma most likely due to bronchitis. You improved with increased frequency of nebulizers and prednisone. You should continue your nebulizers until you feel back to your baseline and you should continue your prednisone for another 3 days. Since you are on Enbrel and your symptoms had not resolved for over 3 weeks, you have been started on an antibiotic called levofloxacin which you should take for a full 7 day course to end ___. You should take your prednisone until ___. You are being discharged with an additional bronchodilator medication which you should take along with albuterol in your nebulizer machine called Atrovent (ipratropium). Take these two medications by nebulizer every 6 hours until your symptoms are close to baseline then you may reduce the frequency. Once you ___ longer need to take the two nebulizer medications you should restart your Spiriva.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with history of difficult to control asthma with historical intubation for asthma who presents with a few days of worsening DOE and wheezing in the setting of cold exposure but no evidence of URI or respiratory infection. Active Diagnoses ================ # Moderate Persistent Asthma with exacerbation: Based on her history of asthma and her symptoms of dyspnea, wheezing, cough, and chest tightness this was initially felt to be consistent with an asthma flare. She was on medium dose fluticasone, albuterol, and montelukast prior to admission. She states that she had palpitations on Advair previously, presumably from the salmeterol. She had no significant improvement on standing albuterol/ipratropium nebs and steroids in the ER for 24 hours so she was admitted. She again failed to improve after 48 hours of inpatient continuation of steroids/nebs. CXR was not concerning for PNA and she had no leukocytosis. Pulmonology was consulted who felt the time course for her symptoms was not classic for an asthma exacerbation. They recommended CT trache to evaluate for tracheobronchomalacia. However, before this was completed the patient's symptoms rapidly improved so that further testing was deferred to the outpatient setting. Of note, her peak flows were 430-450 at the peak of her symptoms and she was not hypoxemic, though she was tachypneic to the high ___. She was treated with a steroid burst for 6 days. She was discharged on Advair (fluticasone-salmeterol) instead of fluticasone inhaler so that she would have a ___. If she does not tolerate the salmeterol because of palpitations/tachycardia, she may benefit from higher dose fluticasone (440mcg BID). #Lactatemia: Patient had a lactate on ABG of 5.8 on ___. This was thought to be due to a combination of metformin (continued on admission) and repeated albuterol nebulizers, which may cause elevated lactate in the absence of tissue hypoperfusion. Chronic Diagnoses ================= #Diabetes mellitus type 2 Documented history of diabetes mellitus treated with metformin and insulin at home. While an inpatient, her metformin was initially continued. She had poorly controlled glucose while on steroids and was told to take 40 units of Lantus for the 2 days post-discharge while on steroids and then to resume her home regimen. #Hypertension Documented history of hypertension treated with losartan, hydrochlorothiazide, clonidine, and nifedipine. Her home regime was increased slightly while an inpatient, but she was discharged on her home medications. Transitional Issues =================== -consider outpatient evaluation of her symptoms to exclude tracheobronchomalacia or subglottal stenosis -consider increasing steroid dose, especially if the patient does not tolerate salmeterol -consider outpatient PFTs
summary: you were admitted after your shortness of breath and wheezing did not improve with nebulizers and steroids in the ER. While you were an inpatient you were treated with steroids and nebulizer treatments for your wheezing. We initially thought that your symptoms were from an asthma flare because you did not have signs of an infection. Typically asthma gets better more rapidly, but you were having symptoms after 3 days of treatment. We had the lung specialists see you and they recommended more advanced imaging of your chest. However, your symptoms resolved suddenly on the day of discharge so we thought that you could have this done as an outpatient. While you were here your blood pressure and blood sugars were a little higher because of the steroids. This is temporary.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ gentleman man with T1DM, cyclic vomiting syndrome, and asthma, admitted to the FICU with DKA and asthma exacerbation. # DKA: precipitant is likely N/V as well as poor compliance with home insulin. Abdomen is tender, but most likely in the setting of vomiting/DKA and also has chronic similar abdominal pain. He was started on an insulin gtt and fluids then transitioned to a subQ regimen. Per ___ Consult team: NPH 10U at breakfast while on prednisone, Glargine 22U at bedtime, Sliding scale with meals. summary: You were seen at ___ for DKA and an asthma exacerbation. You were given a medication for your asthma (prednisone) that can cause your blood sugar to be high. Please take the 10U NPH insulin for the remain three (3) days of your steroid course in addition to your Humalog sliding scale and your home-dose lantus. Please note that we have reduced your sliding scale while eating to: 4units for blood sugar 71-100 +1unit for every 50 over 100 blood sugar (eg 5U for 120, 6U for 170, etc) At bedtime: +2units for every 50 over 200 (eg 2U for 230, 4units for 270, etc) Once your meals return to normal, please restart your home sliding scale. We strongly recommend that you make an appointment with your endocrinologist within the next two weeks.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH significant for asthma presents with worsening cough, wheezing and shortness of breath. # Asthma exacerbation: The patient's presentation was consistent with her prior asthma exacerbations. This episode was likely triggered by a viral URI/bronchitis. It was unclear why her current exacerbation persisted beyond her usual course of 2 days. CXR was negative for pneumonia. CT chest showed mild air trapping and subsegmental atelectasis. Because of her prolonged course the patient was treated empirically for atypical pneumonia for 5 days. She was placed on standing albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers, as well as advair. She will complete a 10 day course of prednisone 60mg. The patient still complained of dyspnea on the day of discharge, though much improved from her admission symptoms. She never required supplemental oxygen. Peak flows on day of discharge ranged from 400-500cc, which is well above her baseline. # Leukocytosis: The patient's WBC trended up during hospitalization. She only complained of cough. She was never febrile and did not have any new infectious symptoms. CXR only showed a band of atelecatasis, and CT chest was negative for pneumonia. We felt her leukocytosis was most likely due to steroids. # Chest pain: The patient complained of intermittent chest pain, localized to her sternum. The patient described chest tightness. At times, her pain was reproducible with palpation. EKG showed new T wave inversion in V2 and T-wave flattening in V3. Troponins were negative x 2. Repeat EKG showed resolution of T-wave abnormalities. It was felt her chest pain was multifactorial: musculoskeletal after recent URI and from asthma. Your low sodium was likely due to the stress of either the procedure or your asthma exacerbation, which commonly happens; it corrected itself and was normal at the time of your discharge. We stopped your hydrochlorathiazide, which you should not take once leaving the hospital. Please record your heart rhythm three times daily as well as any symptoms as instructed using the ___ ___ Monitor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o M with hx of hypogammaglobulinemia, asthma, GERD, LBP who presented to ___ with complaints of total-body numbness, which largely resolved except for residual L leg numbness, Left low back pain, headaches, and fever. . # Fever/HA: Inital concern for bacterial menningitis, however CSF not consistent. viral/aseptic meningitis a possibility, so acyclovir was continued, but then d/ced when HSV PCR was negative. no other evidence of infection with nml CXR and UA and no epidural abscess on MRI. Due to concern that IgG can cause headache and asceptic meningitis pt, IgG was held and pt was told not to take IgG until told to do so by his allergist, Dr. ___. CSF IgG level was checked, and was still pending at the time of discharge. Headache was managed with tylenol and fiorecet with little effect. In addition to headache pt also complaind of L Low back pain, which appeared to be an exacerbation of his chronic LBP. This pain was managed with dilaudid PCA, which was transitioned to Oxycodone. Pt was given a prescription for 4 day supply. He was scheduled for an appointment with his allergist Dr. ___ 4 days after discharge. Dr. ___ was emailed about the ___ hospital stay, and was told that he would need to follow up the CSF IgG levels. The pt was very concerned that because he was unable to get an appointment with his PCP ___ 2 weeks he would not have enough oxycodone. This was communicated to Dr. ___. Dr. ___ agreed to assess his pain, and treat it as he saw fit. In the end, it was felt that the most likely etiology of the pt's complaints was a viral illness, but could not rule out IgG causing asceptic meningitis unitl CSF IgG levels returned. . # Numbness/Weakness: Pt had numbness of the Left Leg with no clear dermatomal distribution. MRI of the spine failed to show any lesions. Strength of the Left Lower extremity is also slightly diminished. Gait was limited by Left Low back pain. B12, RPR, TSH were checked and were normal. Neuro was consulted, and believed his symptoms to be functional and related to chronic LBP, and unlikely to be due to any neurologic lesion. . #. Low Back Pain: Pt complained of considerable back pain throughout his stay. Pain required dilaudid PCA which was transitioned to Oxycodone. . # Enlarged Uvula: Pt was noted to have an enlarged uvula to ~3cm, which caused moderate odynophagia but no resp compromise. On exam no stridor was appreciated. ENT was consulted and felt the enlarged uvula to be ___ a viral syndrome which was likely underlying all of the pts. symptoms. 3 doses of decadron were recommended. After the decadron, uvula returned to normal size. Upon discharge uvula remained normal size. . # Hypogammaglobulinemia: As discussed above, there was concern that IgG was causing headache and possibley an asceptic meningitis. IgG was held, and pt was given Naproxen to treat headache, as NSAIDS are known to treat IgG related headaches fairly well. . # Asthma: Continued inhalers and singulair
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with fever, sore throat, nausea, Left leg numbness, headache, and Left lower back pain. There was initial concern for meningitis so a lumbar puncture (AKA a spinal tap) was performed. No bacteria were found with the spinal fluid and viral tests were negative. Therefore, bacterail meningitis was ruled out and antibiotics were stopped. To evaluate your back pain and leg numbness MRI was performed, but was unable to find a cause of pain. Hip Xray was negative for fracture, and ultrasound of the leg failed to find any clotts. During your admission your sore throat and headache continued, and you were noted to have an enlarged uvula. The ENT service evaluated you using Fiberoptic laryngoscopy and felt that the swelling was likely due to swelling after a viral syndrome. You recieved 3 doses of a steroid, and the swelling reduced and on discharge the uvula was normal size. Sometimes IgG infusion can cause headaches. Therefore, we held your IgG dose and did a test on the CSF taken during your spinal tap. The result of this test takes a few days to come back. Before you administer yourself the IgG again we want to make sure this test result is normal. Therefore, you have an appointment with Dr. ___ on ___ and he will tell you whether or not to take the IgG. It is likely that you had a viral illness that caused all of your symptoms. The viral illness likely caused exacerbation of your low back pain and headache. As the viral illness improves, I expect your back pain and headache to improve as well. In the meantime I will give you a perscription for Naproxen and Oxycodone. I will give you enough to last until your appintment with Dr. ___ if you are still having pain at that time he can give you another perscription.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F w/pm hx significant for seasonal allergies only presents with new subacute onset of dyspnea likely related to recent viral URI with reactive airway disease resulting in a post viral bronchitis. #Reactive Airway Disease #Post viral bronchitis Patient has history of reaction to cold and has chronic smoke exposure. Patient's dyspnea and hypoxia improved with nebulizer treatments, thus viral infection with reactive airway disease is most likely. She was also treated with 5 day course of azithromycin for possible underlying atypical PNA. Legionella antigen was negative and strep pneumo was negative. D dimer 541 (boardline nl for age adjusted PE). Clinically suspicion for PE low with a wells of 0. No interval development of PNA/opacity on CXR. A CT scan was negative for nodules, fibrosis, PE, or PNA. She did have some evidence of small airway disease. She was treated with 3 of 5 days of azithromycin. She used scheduled albuterol nebulizers. She should consider outpatient PFTs. Initially she required 2 L O2, by discharge she was saturating >90% on Ra. #Cold Sore: Treated with course of acyclovir. Transitional issues: ============== [] ___ with PCP ___ 1 week [] Consider outpatient PFT with methycholine challenge (cold asthma Hx) [] Consider pulmonary follow up for her small airway disease [] Patient was discharged with albuterol nebulizer which is a new medication for her. It can be used every 4 hours as needed for difficulty breathing FEV1 on discharge 280 Code Status: Full HCP: ___ (boyfriend) ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were having difficulty breathing. We took an x-ray of your lung to look for pneumonia and we did not find any evidence to suggest a bacteria pneumonia. We think you have what is called reactive airway disease this means your lungs become inflamed easily even after a cold. This may have caused a bacterial process, so we will treat you with a total 5 day course of antibiotics with azythromycin. Your CT of your chest did not show a clot or PNA, it did show some evidence of inflammation in your airways that will be followed up as an outpatient. You were treated with nebulizer treatments to make your lungs less tight. We encouraged the use of an incentive spirometer to keep your lungs open. Continue taking the azythromycin for 2 more days. We will send you home with an albuterol inhaler, you can take 2 puffs of that every 4 hours as needed to allow you to breath easier. We recommend you see your primary care doctor within the week to ensure you are improving.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of alzheimer's dementia, prior MIs, CVA ___, HTN, HL presenting with SOB. . #SOB: Pt was sent to ED due to concerns from nurses at ___ for labored breathing and wheezing. Pt did not have known hx of asthma, COPD or smoking. Also did not have hx of DVT or PE. Per son, episodes of SOB/wheezing have been occurring occasionally for ~1 month. At ED, she had received 1 dose of levofloxacin and cefepime for possible health-care associated PNA but as she did not have cough, fever, or leukocytosis, these were not continued. CXR was of poor quality but did not show clear consolidation or edema. On exam, pt had audible wheezing and RR in ___, worse when approached by medical staff. She reported feeling "nervous" and was given 0.25mg po haldol x 2 for possible anxiety-induced tachypnea. She was initially kept on 4L oxygen by NC with O2 consistently in high ___. She was weaned off and remained in the high ___ on room air. Wheezing improved with ipratropium/albuterol nebs, short course of prednisone, and z-pack. She was discharged to nursing home with instructions to complete a course of prednisone 40mg and azithromycin 250mg (5 days total). Wheezing was likely due to self-limited reactive airway disease triggered by a viral infection. Of note, blood cultures were still pending on discharge. . #CAD: Pt with hx of multiple MIs. EKG on admission showed old inferior infarct and 1st degree AV block. She complained about chest pain on ___. She reported pain relief with 1 SL nitroglycerin tab. EKG was unchanged from admission. Troponin was less than 0.01. She was continued on her statin during hospital admission and monitored on telemetry without any significant events. Aspirin was discontinued b/c she was already on aggrenox. . #Hx of CVA: Pt with hx of CVA ___. She was continued on aggrenox at home dose. On day of originally expected discharge, pt was noted to have difficulty getting up into wheelchair. She seemed to have weakness more pronounced on the left side in upper and lower extremities. She was also less active in general, having to be fed rather than eating herself. CT head w/o contrast was obtained which showed right frontal encephalomalacia and multiple hypodensities likely related to chronic ischemia. Her physical therapist was contacted who reported that pt has always had more weakness on the left side and that she was likely deconditioned from her hospital stay. She was evaluated by physical therapy at the hospital who recommended discharge to rehab given impaired strength and balance. . #HTN: No acute issues. She was continued on triamterene/hctz at home dose . #Alzheimers: No acute issues. On discussion with her family, her mental status was at baseline. She was alert to self and knew names of family members. Was not oriented to place or time, which is her baseline. She was continued on namenda and aricept at home doses . # Chronic renal insufficiency: Cr was elevated to 1.6 on admission. Records were obtained from OSHs where she had received care in the past; most recent Cr recorded was 1.4. By admission, Cr was 1.5.
summary: You were admitted with labored breathing and wheezing. Your chest xray did not reveal a pneumonia. Your symptoms improved with nebulizers, steroids, and antibiotics. You were discharged with two more days of the steroids and antibiotics. Also during your hospital stay you complained of chest pain which self-resolved. Your EKG and blood tests did not indicate that you were having a heart attack and you did not complain of any further pain. Additionally, you were noted to have left sided weakness during your hospital course. We called your physical therapist who confirmed that this was not new. A CT scan of your head showed old changes but nothing acute. You were seen by our physical therapist who agreed that it would be best for you to go to a rehab facility where you would have more medical attention.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT: The patient is an ___ y.o. F with ashtma, bronchiectasis with pseudomonas colonization, who presents with shortness of breath and cough. SOB/cough: Likely due to exacerbation of chronic bronchitis/bronchiectasis . Was initially given IV Cefepime, PO prednisone 60mg and nebs. The Cefepime was discontinued as there was no evidence of pneumonia and she was afebrile. Chest CT showed bilateral bronchial wall thickening, bronchiectasis and multiple areas of mucoid impaction with bilateral scattered peribronchial patchy opacities with interval worsening compared to prior. Distribution of bronchiectasis is predominantly in the right middle lobe and lingula and in the lower lobes bilaterally. My impression is that she is proibably developing mucous plugging due to inability to clear her secretions. The reason for this is multifactorial, and I discussed these and possible interventions with her family: In the setting of bronchiectasis she will produce sputum consistently. The goal is to expectorate it and keep it moist. -Keeping her well hydrated (PO) will help. -Using a humidifier in the ___ months may help. -There are are 2 dogs in the house. Avoiding the hair/dander as much as possible may help. -Sitting upright, out of bed, will help her pulmonary mechanics. Sleeping with the head of her bed elevated may also help. -Using an inline humidifier with her supplemental oxygen by nasal cannula may help. She was transitioned to PO prednisone from IV methylprednisolone on ___. I will discharge her on 60 mg of prednisone, and would recommend slower taper than previously to avoid a flare of bronchospasm. I would ask her PCP and outpatient pulmonologist to decrease the prednisone as they deem appropriate in the outpatient setting. She has an appointment with her PCP ___ ___, and it may be reasonable at that time to reduce the dose to 40 mg/day if she is doing well. She is also given inhaled steroids via MDI, nebulzed bronchodilators, and inhaled (nebulized) Tobramycin - the latter for a total of 10 days. The reason for the Tobra is that on the CT she did have a suggestion of increased bronchial inflammation, and has a hsitory of pseudomonas pulmonary infection/colonization. ___ consult evaluated her and felt she would be safe for home discharge. Her family verified that she has a fully functioning nebulizer at home, and did not need a new one. I did give her a script for a hoispital bed to keep the HOB elevated at night.
summary: You were admitted with worsening cough and breathing thought related to your chronic bronchitis. You were treated with antibiotics, prednisone and nebulizer. We discussed, with your family present, rhat you will need to be up and out of bed (even if only in a chair) for as much of the day possible. You should sleep with your head elevated if this helps your breathing. Your family was instructed on how to perform chest ___ and can do this several times per day to help you bring up mucous with your cough. We discussed the need for you to drink plenty of fluids so that the mucous in your lungs does not dry out. I also strongly recommend a humidifier in the room you sleep in and avoiding dog hair (dander) as much as possible.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with asthma, bronchitis, tracheobronchomalacia, OSA, hypertension, recurrent UTI c/b pyelo s/p R nephrectomy and partial L nephrectomy, multiple VTE episodes on Coumadin, and recent admission for GI bleed and terminal ileitis thought to be atypical Crohn's flare now on budesonide, who presented with hypoxia and productive cough x 6 days, concerning for bronchitis/URI, as well as L flank pain c/f renal colic. Her course was complicated by abdominal pain due to constipation. # Bronchitis/upper airway infection: The patient presented with productive cough and dyspnea. On exam, she was diffusely rhonchorous. There was no consolidation on CXR. She remained afebrile with stable vital signs. Sputum culture with heavy growth of Moraxella. She was started on Levofloxacin 500 mg PO QDaily x 7 days (___) and prednisone 40 mg x 5 days (___). She was put on a steroid taper of 20mg x2 days and 10mg x2 days, then d/c. She received duonebs and humidified oxygen for symptomatic relief. She was continued on her home ___. At time of discharge, the patient was breathing comfortably on room air with stable vital signs. # Abdominal pain: Pt has chronic RLQ pain, which she presented with on admission. This pain was unlikely due to ileitis given no concerning findings on CT, normal labs. She developed diffuse abdominal pain and distension likely due to constipation as pt had some relief with bowel movements. KUB showed no evidence of toxic megacolon, perforation, or obstruction. Her constipation was treated with an aggressive bowel regimen including docusate, miralax, bisacodyl, and senna with simethicone. She was continued on her home budesonide. Before discharge she had several bowel movements with improvement in her pain. She was discharged on a bowel regimen. # H/o VTE on Coumadin: The patient's INR was monitored during admission. Her INR rose to 3.5, likely due to starting levofloxacin. Her Coumadin was held for one day and restarted at 2.5mg for one day. She was then restarted at 2mg per ___ clinic recommendations. Her INR at discharge was 2.1. She will follow-up in ___ clinic on ___ ___. # L flank pain: The patient presented with +CVA tenderness, non-obstructing stone seen on CT. Symptoms consistent with renal colic. Pyelonephritis was unlikely given no CT findings of hydronephrosis, UA neg for pyuria, leukocytosis improved. She was treated with IV fluids. She received her fosfomycin once every 10 days ___ Day 10). Her flank pain improved. # RLE swelling and pain: Patient developed new onset lower extremity swelling and calf pain without significant trauma or inciting event. RLE LENIs were negative. Her swelling and tenderness had resolved by discharge. # Elevated lactate: Likely due to pulmonary infection. She received 3 L IVF. Repeat lactate improved. #Microcytic anemia: Chronic, s/p recent GI bleed. H/H was monitored throughout admission and remained stable. her H/H was 7.___.8 at discharge. #?Crohn's disease: Had terminal ileitis a few weeks ago. Patient continued on budesonide. # GERD: Continued on omeprazole. # Hypothyroidism: Continued on levothyroxine. # Hypertension: Continued lisinopril and diltiazem # Steroid-induced hyperglycemia: Continued home Humalog sliding scale. Transitional Issues: - Started on Levofloxacin 500mg QDay x7 days. Last day ___ - Initiated prednisone taper: 20mg on ___ and ___, 10mg ___ and ___, then discontinue - Warfarin was held on ___ for INR>3, restarted at 2 .5g on ___. She will continue with 2 mg (dose reduced with ongoing antibiotic interaction) on ___ - Patient instructed to have INR checked on ___ and have results faxed to ___ - Patient discharged with bowel regimen: Bisacodyl and Miralax - Patient will followup with Interventional Pulmonary for tracheobronchomalacia
summary: You came into the hospital because of difficulty breathing and cough, as well as left-sided flank pain. You were found to have a bacterial infection of your upper respiratory system. You were treated with an antibiotic, which you will continue at home. CT of your abdomen showed a small kidney stone, which was likely causing your flank pain. This stone is small enough to pass on its own. You were given IV fluids and pain medicine. You developed abdominal pain due to constipation. You were given medicine which caused you to have bowel movements. Your symptoms improved and we now feel that you are well enough to go home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/PMHx severely poorly controlled asthma, PFT in ___ FVC 2.53L, FEV1/FVC 55% vs. ___ PFT FVC 4.39L, FEV1/FVC 48%, continued tobacco use, frequent exacerbator in the setting of medication non-adherence, poorly controlled T2DM (A1C ___, HTN, and hx of multiple perirectal abscesses and fistulas was admitted for Status Asthmaticus. # Status Asthmaticus: Patient has severely poorly controlled asthma, PFT in ___ FVC 2.53L, FEV1 predicted 40%, FEV1/FVC 55% vs. ___ PFT FVC 4.39L, FEV1/FVC 48%, persistent tobacco use, frequent exacerbator in the setting of medication noncompliance. Now s/p IV methylpred 125mg and Mg 2g IV x2, albuterol, ipratropium, continuous nebs and heliox with improvement and stabilization of respiratory status. Per outpatient pulmonologist's ___ in At___) note it appears that patient's asthma has been challenging to control due to non-compliance and inconsistent follow-up. Patient is a Continuous smoker and perhaps may have component of COPD. He was treated with standing duoneb Q4H and albuterol Q2H PRN with stabilization of his respiratory status. Patient was started on prednisone 60mg (___) for a slow taper (down by 10 mg every 3 days). He was successfully weaned off of the 70% face mask to 4L NC upon FICU Call out. Was off oxygen, with peak flows 280 on Am of discharge. We would recommend for patient to follow-up with outpatient pulmonology for PFT study and also perhaps consideration for xolair therapy. # Leukocytosis: Upon admission, patient's WBC was 18, most likely from recent steroid use. CXR showed no acute cardiopulmonary process. WBC was stable at 19 on the day of discharge. # Elevated Lactic: Patient's lactate was 2.9 on admission and peaked at 3.3 but subsequently downtrended. This is likely in the setting of albuterol use and prolonged increased work of breathing. He was not taking metformin at home, so this was not thought to contribute to his elevated lactate on admission. # Hypertension: Elevated in the setting of severe respiratory distress up to 200s/100s, but after resolution of his respiratory distress, he remained normotensive in 130s/70s. He is not on any home antihypertensive medications. # DMII w/ hyperglycemia: patient has not been taking his home metformin, last A1c was 8.5%. Not on home insulin. We continued him on ISS while inpatient, but he exhibited poor blood glucose control, likely exacerbated by prednisone therapy. Strongly encouraged him to resume home medication of metformin, particularly while on prednisone taper. Recommended he take 1000 mg Metfromin XR daily while on prednisone, and discuss with Dr. ___ PCP, re: potentially starting an additional agent for control of his diabetes, as he reports already starting to have peripheral neuropathy. #HLD: continue home atorva. #Smoking cessation: patient restarted on bupropion during this hospitalization as he says it helped him previously. Encouraged continued smoking abstinence. #Med noncompliance: counseled patient about importance of medication compliance. #Follow-up: PCP ___ appt. scheduled w/ Dr. ___ on ___. #Time spent: 40 minutes spent on discharge related activities on day of discharge.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with worsening shortness of breath due to an asthma exacerbation. You were treated briefly in the intensive care unit, with steroids and nebulizer treatments, and improved. Your peak flow on the day of discharge was 280. You are being discharged on a prednisone taper to try to prevent a recurrent asthma exacerbation. While you are on prednisone, your blood sugars will be more elevated than usual. It is critically important that you take you diabetes medication (metformin) to prevent long-term consequences of diabetes. We discussed this extensively and you agreed to take the metformin as prescribed. We also discussed the importance of continuing to not smoke, as any smoke inhalation can potentially worsen your asthma or cause an acute asthma flare.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female recently hospitalized for angioedema attributed to angiotensin receptor blocker returns with hypoxemic respiratory failure secondary to recurrent angioedema. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== [] Patient is acutely allergic to ACE-I and ARBs with severe angioedema. This should continue to be documented and communicated to all future healthcare providers. [] Currently on single agent control of hypertension with amlodipine. Defer to PCP if ___ second agent is required for better control. [] Recommend follow up of C1 esterase levels. [] Creatinine 1.4 on discharge and patient was encouraged to continue good PO hydration. Recommend follow up labs in 1 week to follow resolution of kidney injury. [] Given patient's asthma and now allergic reactions, recommend allergist workup to determine if she has any other potential triggers or severe allergens. She denied any food/seasonal allergies during this admission but did come in already with extensive anti-allergy medications from home, including diphenhydramine and fenofoxadine. [] Patient has been discharged with an additional dose of prednisone 40mg and Benadryl 50mg, along with prescription of Epipen to be used if throat closing/anaphylaxis occurs. ACUTE/ACTIVE ISSUES ===================== #Angioedema secondary to ___ allergy ___ was first hospitalized on ___ for angioedema. She received epinephrine and dexamethasone and was monitored in the surgical ICU. Her combination losartan/hydrocholorthiazide was discontinued at the time. She was well until ___ when she developed recurrent acute airway compromise and dysphagia. While there was minimal aryepiglottic fold edema on emergent fiberoptic laryngoscopy, she had posterior tongue edema, so was intubated. She had a cuff leak the following day affording extubation. She received dexamethasone and antihistamines. Combination HCTZ-losartan tablets were found to have inadvertently remained in her pill box and are thought to be the causative agent. She was not hypotensive and did not have any pertinent skin findings. She was successfully weaned to room air and remained stable. Her amlodipine was initially held and then restarted with no acute airway compromise. Her severe allergy to ACE-Inhibitors and ARBs has been documented. Patient and her son have been educated on avoiding these classes of medications. C1 Esterase levels pending, but C1 esterase deficiency thought to be less likely given the patient's age. We recommend thorough work up with an allergist/immunologist to determine potential other allergens. #Asthma Patient has a six-year history of asthma. She presented with persistent wheezing and shortness of breath, suggestive of asthma exacerbation in the setting of recent angioedema and intubation. She was successfully weaned off of O2 to room air during the daytime at rest and during ambulation. She received dexamethasone in the ICU and will be treated for four additional days with prednisone (day ___. Continued use of duonebs. CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES ====================== #Acute kidney injury: Her Creatinine is approaching baseline with good PO intake. #Non-insulin dependent type II diabetes - Home meds held while hospitalized. #Hypertension - Her HCTZ-losartan pills were removed from her pillbox. Her amlodipine was restarted and was well-tolerated. Recommend follow up with primary care provider if ___ second agent is needed for hypertension controlled. #CODE: Full (confirmed) #CONTACT: ___ (___)
summary: You were in the hospital because you were found to have a very severe allergic reaction that blocked your airway. We think that this was because of your Losartan-Hydrochlorathiazide pills. A tube was placed down your throat to help you breathe initially and treated with steroids to help the swelling go down in your airway and tongue. Once the swelling resolved, the tube was pulled and you were slowly weaned off of oxygen back to room air. You received steroids by mouth and nebulizers because we thought you were also having an asthma attack. Your amlodipine was restarted and you did not have any acute reaction to it. You should know that you are EXTREMELY allergic to LISINOPRIL AND LOSARTAN, and all other medicines in this family of medicines. The category of medicines you are allergic to are called ARBs and ACE Inhibitors.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with PMH significant for severe persistent asthma requiring multiple ED visits in last several months, Hep C cirrhosis, and HTN presenting from ED for asthma exacerbation. # Severe Persistent Asthma: Asthma has been poorly controlled over several months, as evidenced by recent h/o multiple ED visits. These recent exacerbations may be due to worsening disease in setting of not receiving his xolair in several months. He also recently ran out of his albuterol which likely contributed to symptoms. Recent flare was treated with prednisone (pt reports 60mg/day for 4 days, then 20mg/day for 4 days, but ED discharge in OMR shows 40mg/day for 4 days and 20mg/day for 4 days). Patient's symptoms seemed to worsen again once completed steroid taper. While patient seems very good in certain ways about his asthma management (e.g. testing and knowing his peak flow), in other ways, he seems to have poor follow through (does not make follow up appointments, runs out of medications), which could also be contributing to flare. He is currently having a flare and improved significantly with IV steroid treatment in ED and subsequent PO prednisone. He does not appear to have current infection, so we held off on further antibiotics. We treated him with albuterol and ipratropium nebs, Fluticasone-Salmeterol Diskus, cetirizine, guaifenesin, and prednisone starting at 60mg daily with a planned taper over 12 days. He was also restarted on tiotropium prior to dischare. He should have omalizumab monthly as an outpatient and was encouraged to follow up with allergy for this. Peak flow improved to 550mL prior to discharge, he was breathing comfortably on room air, and his ambulatory O2 sat was 94-95% on RA. While on prednisone, patient was prescribed ranitidine and an increased dose of metformin. # Pleural Effusion: Patient had small left-sided effusion on CXR. We recommended diagnostic thoracentesis to determine etiology of unilateral effusion, but patient declined. He should have follow up CXR in ___ weeks, and if it has not resolved, he should be referred to IP for thoracentesis. # HTN: Poorly controlled right now with SBPs in the 170s. Denied any symptoms of chest pain, headache, confusion. Lisinopril was recently increased in dosage. Amlodipine 5mg daily was started for better control. # Hep C Cirrhosis: Followed by ___. No evidence of ascites or stigmata of cirrhosis on physical exam. # Elevated A1c: A1c 9.3 in ___. Here A1c was 7.4. Blood sugars likely to worsen with prednisone. Finger stick as high as 341. Patient put on insulin sliding scale humulag while in house. He was discharged on increased metformin 750mg BID (from 500mg BID) while he is on the prednisone and his glycemic control should improve with decreased prednisone doses. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued statin and baby aspirin. Transitional Issues: - confirmed full code - consider starting montelukast for asthma as an outpatient and encouraged patient to resume xolair - follow up blood pressure control - pt needs repeat CXR in ___ weeks to ensure resolution of pleural effusion; diagnostic thoracentesis is recommended if effusion still present - metformin may need to be titrated with changes in prednisone doses
summary: You were admitted in the setting of an asthma exacerbation, likely triggered by allergic irritants, as well as lack of nebulizer treatments. You quickly improved on steroids and standing nebulizers. You will be discahrged home on a slow prednisone taper. While on steroids, your metformin will be increased to manage elevated sugars. Additionally, while hospitalized you were found to have a small pleural effusion (collection of fluid) in the left lung. You opted to have this monitored and potentially treated as an outpatient rather than have the fluid removed in the hospital. It will be imperative to follow-up as outpatient to monitor/manage your symptoms and repeat imaging of the lungs to monitor the pleural effusion.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PNEUMONIA, ASTHMA EXACERBATION: Ms. ___ was admitted with a left lower lobe infiltrate and was started on ceftriaxone IV and azithromycin PO for a community acquired pneumonia. The morning after admission, she was changed to cefpodoxime for an oral regimen. Given her penicillin allergy of a rash (and other antibiotic allergies of erythema nodosum), she was monitored closely for signs of adverse reaction. She had no signs of rash upon discharge after greater than 72 hours of observation. She quickly defervesced and had symptomatic improvement on those antibiotics. She completed a five day course of azithromycin, and she was given a prescription to complete a fourteen day course of cefpodoxime. On physical exam on admission, she was very wheezy. She was placed on prednisone 40 mg QDay (home dose was 10 mg) and standing albuterol nebulizers with relief of symptoms. She was easily weaned of oxygen by the morning after admission. She was evaluated by ___ who felt she was safe to return home with ___ and home ___. Her ambulatory O2 sats were in the upper 90's on room air at discharge, and she was not sent home with home oxygen. HYPERTENSION: Per Ms. ___, she was not supposed to be taking diltiazem for her blood pressure. The medication was verified on the PCP's medication list (from Dr. ___, and she was kept on diltiazem SR 180 mg QDay with well-controlled BP while in-house. ANTICOAGULATION: Ms. ___ INR became supratherapeutic after the antibiotics were started. It was initially held until it beca,e less than 3.0 (INR was 2.1 on discharge). She was told to restart coumadin at 1 mg QHS and to have her INR checked on ___. This was communicated to the pheresis unit and her PCP, ___. ___, by email.
summary: You were admitted with a pneumonia and have been put on antibiotics for it. You should continue taking the antibiotic cefpodoxime for another ten days. For the next day, you should take your albuterol every six hours to help your breathing. After that, you can take it as you regularly do, if you feel you need it. Your steroids were increased to prednisone 40 mg each day. Please take 40 mg for three days after you go home. Then take 30 mg for the three days after that, and 20 mg for the three days after that. At the end of the your taper, you should take prednisone 10 mg each day as you were doing before you came into the hospital. Your coumadin blood levels have been slightly high in the hospital because of the antibiotics. You should only take 1 mg of coumadin tonight. Please get your blood checked at the pheresis unit on ___. They will tell you whether you need to increase your dose. You have been sent home with oxygen which you may need temporarily while your pneumonia and asthma exacerbation resolve. You may find you need the oxygen more when you are moving around than when you are at rest.

The morning after admission, she was changed to cefpodoxime for an oral regimen. Given her penicillin allergy of a rash (and other antibiotic allergies of erythema nodosum), she was monitored closely for signs of adverse reaction. She had no signs of rash upon discharge after greater than 72 hours of observation. She quickly defervesced and had symptomatic improvement on those antibiotics. She completed a five day course of azithromycin, and she was given a prescription to complete a fourteen day course of cefpodoxime. On physical exam on admission, she was very wheezy. She was placed on prednisone 40 mg QDay (home dose was 10 mg) and standing albuterol nebulizers with relief of symptoms. She was easily weaned of oxygen by the morning after admission. She was evaluated by ___ who felt she was safe to return home with ___ and home ___. Her ambulatory O2 sats were in the upper 90's on room air at discharge, and she was not sent home with home oxygen. HYPERTENSION: Per Ms. ___, she was not supposed to be taking diltiazem for her blood pressure. The medication was verified on the PCP's medication list (from Dr. ___, and she was kept on diltiazem SR 180 mg QDay with well-controlled BP while in-house. ANTICOAGULATION: Ms. ___ INR became supratherapeutic after the antibiotics were started. It was initially held until it beca,e less than 3.0 (INR was 2.1 on discharge). She was told to restart coumadin at 1 mg QHS and to have her INR checked on ___. This was communicated to the pheresis unit and her PCP, ___. ___, by email.

Patient was seen by the sleep consultants who felt that his bipap should be set as follows. Baseline sat ___ on RA per OMR. --Bipap used every night and w/ daytime naps, setting ___ w/ Oxygen 2L, titrated to sats above 85-90%. --Keep oxygen saturations between 85 and 90%. --During the day patient should be on n/c oxygen ___ titrate again sats btw 85-90%. --Avoid given supplement oxygen above 4L n/c, if needed while in house please reconsult pulmonary medicine and check ABG. --cont albuterol and ipratroprium standing nebulizers q6H. ___ switch back to inhalers as desired. --cont fluticasone as prescribed. . # Asthma( see above) . # Obstructive Sleep Apnea: (See above) Patient needs follow up with Dr. ___ in sleep clinic. He was discharged on the following medications. - geodon 80mg BID - valproic acid ___ BID . # Hypercholesterolemia: -continue simvastatin 20mg daily. . # Patient needs follow up with Hematology, repeat sleep study and then appointment with Dr. ___ Dr. ___ ___

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN Mr. ___ is a ___ year old ___ gentleman with h/o AS, afib/flutter, gastric adenocarcinoma, and hypertension who presented with shortness of breath and difficulty swallowing. His shortness of breath resolved with nebulizers. For his difficulty swallowing, this was likely secondary to progression of the adenocarcinoma located at his gastroesophageal junction. He had a stent placed by the ERCP team, which improved his symptoms of dysphagia. After the stent he felt well, despite continued symptomatic orthostasis with ___ evaluation. He was discharged to home safely after evaluation and recommendations by the ___ team, and agreed to avoid ambulation at home because of this orthostasis. He was discharged home via wheelchair, with his wife on day 6 of his hospital course. # SOB: Consistent with COPD exacerbation. - Treated with albuterol & ipratropium nebs - at first, standing q6 hours. After improvement, these medications were made PRN. - Started inhaled steroid ___, patient couldn't use diskus due to difficulty producing strong enough inhalation. Wrote for Fluticasone MDI instead. No MDI on formulary but after discharge, he may be written for Symbicort MDI instead. - O2 on NC titrated to >93%. . # Dysphagia: likely ___denocarc. - GE junction partially covered metal stent - tolerated well. . # Esophageal Candidiasis: Noted on endoscopy. Treatment is fluconazole x 4 weeks. - Got baseline EKG to ensure no QTc prolongation. Please follow up with an outpatient EKG. - Started fluconazole for a 4 week course. . # Anemia: He received 1 unit PRBC over the weekend to optimize blood volume for ERCP procedure. Otherwise, this was felt to be secondary to blood loss from gastric cancer, especially as there was an appearance of recent bleed on EGD. - Increased PPI to twice a day. . # Aortic stenosis: Likely worsened from previous admission. - Decreased furosemide to 20 mg daily. Gently diuresis is because of his tenuous volume status and continued orthostatic hypotension. . # Gastric adenocarcinoma: s/p radiation + chemo, not receiving specific therapy at this time. No pain at this time. - He will follow up with outpatient Onc after discharge. - Avoided SQ heparin per team discussion (risk of GI bleed, especially as mass looked like it had recently bled on EGD). - Increased PPI to twice per day. . # Atrial flutter/fibrillation: Paroxysmal. No RVR. He was not taking his prescribed amiodarone at home. - Restarted amiodarone. - No anticoagulation given high risk of GI bleed (see above). . >> INACTIVE ISSUES: # Chronic renal failure: Stage ___ with baseline creatinine 1.6- 2.0. Creatinine remained at baseline while in house. . # Glaucoma: continue Dorzolamide 2%/Timolol 0.5%, brimonidine drops . # Gout: continued allopurinol, renally-dosed >> TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - He did initially fail ___ prior to discharge, but was discharged after agreeing to use wheelchair in home and commode in lieu of bathroom (where he can't fit his wheelchair). He is aware of diagnosis and difficulty w/ orthostatis, please follow up with this and ensure he hasn't had any recent falls at home. -- Please re-check EKG for QTc monitoring (on fluconazole) and to ensure PR is not excessively prolonged -- On fluconazole x4 weeks, monitor for side effects -- If abdominal pain, contact endoscopy (Dr. ___ at the ___ advanced endoscopy) at ___ for possible stent migration -- Decreased furosemide to 20 qd due to orthostasis. Please ensure no symptoms of pulmonary edema.
summary: You were admitted for both shortness of breath and difficulty swallowing. Your shortness of breath improved with breathing treatments. You were seen by the endoscopy team for your difficulty swallowing, which you were experiencing as a result of the cancer blocking your esophagus. You received a stent in your esophagus to help your swallowing. You were also found to have an inflammation of your esophagus, likely caused by an organism called ___, which you were treated for with an anti-fungal medication. While you were here, some changes were made to your medications. Please START taking fluconazole 200 mg once daily for a total of 4 weeks for a fungal infection of your esophagus. Please CONTINUE taking amiodarone 200 mg twice daily. Please STOP taking furosemide 40 mg daily, and START taking furosemide 20 mg daily instead. Please START taking pantoprazole 40mg twice daily, and STOP taking pantoprazole 40 mg once daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ female with a medical history of well-controlled childhood asthma who presented with symptoms consistent with asthma exacerbation, transferred for MICU for respiratory distress requiring intubation. Hospital course complicated by exertional tachycardia and pulmonary embolism. # Respiratory Distress / Wheezing / Cough: On admission, patient with significant audible wheezing with poor air movement bilaterally. Despite treatment with continuous nebulizer treatments and high-dose intravenous steroids, the patient developed tachypnea to ___ with rising lactate. The patient was transferred to the MICU, where the decision was made to intubate. Once intubated, patient's ventilatory mechanics were not felt to be consistent with high airway resistances. She was extubated after one day. Upon extubation, she was noted to have centralized monophonic wheeze, raising suspicion for vocal cord dysfunction. ENT was consulted; Bedside nasopharyngeal fiberoptic exam demonstrated good motion of her cords bilaterally, and no evidence of PVFM (Paradoxical Vocal Fold Motion). Wheezing was appreciated when vocal cords were open per ENT report. Anti-anxiolytics had no effect on her symptoms. Pulmonary recommended CT airway to further evaluate; this showed limited study due to active coughing but significant caliber change and collapse of the mid to distal trachea that was strongly suggestive of tracheobronchomalacia. It was felt that the patient should be re-evaluated by both ENT and pulmonary as outpatient to re-evaluate VC motion and re-peat CT airway. The patient was discharged on prednisone taper, low-dose Advair, and ipratropium nebulizer as needed. Working diagnosis at the time of discharge was cough-variant asthma, pending further evaluation by pulmonary and ENT. # Segmental PE: CTA on ___ demonstrated segmental pulmonary embolism with extension of thrombus into the subsegmental vessels in the right lower lobe. CTA was conducted given continued tachycardia with ambulation. She was started on lovenox with a plan to follow-up with her PCP as an outpatient. # Tachycardia: Primarily with ambulation. Though to be secondary to deconditioning and pulmonary embolism. Improved by the time of discharge. Transitional Issues: - Re-peat CT Airway to be conducted by Pulmonary team as outpatient. - Re-evaluation of VC motion by ENT. - Duration of anti-coagulation to be determined by patient's PCP.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and wheezing. While we evaluated many possibilities to explain your breathing difficulty, we do not have a clear explanation at this point. You may have cough-variant asthma, a vocal cord that is not functioning normally, or perhaps a collapsible windpipe. You will continue to investigate these possibilities further with your outpatient doctors. ___, your breathing improved over the course of your hospital stay. You were also noted to have a blood clot in your lung during your hospital stay, for which you will be treated with a blood thinner.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with PMH of atrial fibrillation on warfarin, HTN, asthma, and renal insufficiency who presents with tachycardia and dyspnea, has a UTI on bactrim, and CXR finding concerning for pneumonia. Active Issues: #Pneumonia: Pt presents with a 5 day history of dyspnea, fatigue, cough with yellow-green sputum which is concerning for a pneumonia. Pt denies fever and chills and has no elevated WBC. CXR shows a right lower thoracic density that is consistent with a possible pneumonia. Given pt significant symptoms will treat as pneumonia with levofloxacin 750mg Q48H for 7 days for CAP. Her symptoms were managed with albuterol nebs and ipratropium inhaler. Her blood and urine cultures were negative. Pt symptoms improved with this management and her dyspnea improved. #Atrial fibrillation: Pt with history of atrial fibrillation on warfarin and rate controlled on metoprolol succinate and digoxin. Pt was having runs of tachycardia into the 140's since admission. We increased her metoprolol succinate dosage to 150mg. We checked her digoxin level and found it to be sub therapeutic. We continued digoxin at 62.5 mcg daily and her digoxin level is now rising toward therapeutic level. Review of her medical records indicate that her digoxin level has been subtherapeutic aroud 0.3-0.5 for at least the last ___ years. It is unclear if this was done on purpose but we attempted to re-establish therapeutic digoxin levels. In addition, her INR was found to be supratherapeutic on admission. Her records indicate her INR was 10 on (___) prior to admission and when we admitted her the INR was 5.8. We held warfarin and her INR trended down most recently down to around 4.8. Pt was not sure if she was taking the correct dose of warfarin, metoprolol or digoxin. It appears that the patient is having a lot of difficulty trying to appropriately take her medications. Her INR was 1.9 on discharge so we restarted warfarin at 2.5mg daily. An INR should be checked again on ___ and warfarin dose adjusted as needed. #Hypertension: BP is elevated to 140's/100's however on admission she appeared hypovolemic. We held her furosemide in the setting of elevation of her creatinine and gave her some fluids. We restarted furosemide on discharge. #UTI: Pt with UTI diagnosed at her primary care office and she was perscribed bactrim for 3 day course. Review of the urine culture result shows no UTI but mixed flora indicative of a contaminated specimen. We stopped bactrim on admission. We repeated a urine culture here which was negative. #Diarrhea: Pt with diarrhea shortly after admission. Likely secondary to antibiotic use or could be infectious. C. diff was negative. O&P was also negative. Pt stools are now more solid and diarrhea has resolved. There are still some stool cultures pending but pt has improved clinically. Chronic Issues: #Iron Deficiency Anemia: Stable on iron supplementation. Hemoglobin was between ___ which is at patients baseline. #Chronic Kidney Disease: Pt with known history of kidney disease with baseline Cr of 1.5-1.8. Currently with Cr of 1.3. Transitional Issues: -Pt will need to follow up for management of INR and digoxin level as these were not in therapeutic range on admission. Likely secondary to inability of patient to appropriately take her medications. -Pt will need INR checked on ___ at rehab facility. Also digoxin levels should be checked as pt is subtherapeutic currently. -f/u stool cultures
summary: You came to the hospital because you were short of breath and were found to have a rapid heart rate. While you were here we found that you had a pneumonia which we treated with an antibiotic. We also found that your heart rate was elevated from your atrial fibrillation which was not well controlled on your current medication regimen. We measured your blood level for digoxin, a medication used to control your heart rate, and found the level to be low. We restarted digoxin and your blood level is now rising toward normal level. We also increased your metoprolol dose to better control your heart rate. We also found that your INR, which is a measure used to track your blood thinner, was dangerously high. This likely means you were taking too much warfarin at home. We temporarily stopped warfarin and your INR has come down. Your INR should be kept between ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ with a history of NSIP, HTN, and GERD presenting with 3 weeks of malaise and worsening productive cough with no leukocytosis, fever (Tm 100.3), or evidence of focal pneumonia or PE on imaging (chest X ray and CTA), most likely a viral pneumonia. Interventions/investigations: 1. Dyspnea/cough: He presented with 3 weeks of worsening cough, wheezing, and dyspnea that began with a viral-like syndrome involving rhinorhhea, congestion, and sore throat which have since resolved. He failed to improve after receiving 3 days of azithromycin from his PCP. On arrival to ___, his oxygen saturations were normal and he was not tachypneic. He developed an isolated temperature to 100.3 but otherwise temperatures were below 100. He had no leukocytosis. CTA was negative for PE. He had no evidence of focal pneumonia on chest X ray. Flu swab was negative. His symptoms were thought to be due to a viral bronchitis with post-viral bronchial inflammation contributing. Antibiotics were not initiated. He improved with standing ipratropium-albuterol nebs and was discharged with an albuterol inhaler. 2. Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia: He is followed by Dr. ___ had stable disease, with PFTs last checked ___ (FVC 71%, FEV1 74%, FEV1/FVC 10, DLCO 50%). He has historically been on prednisone but this was discontinued due to his non-progression and clinical stability. Transitional issues: - Patient discharged with albuterol inhaler for bronchitis-associated wheezing - Patient discharged with follow up with his PCP and pulmonologist, Dr. ___ - ___: full - Contact: Wife and HCP: ___ ___
summary: You were hospitalized at ___ because you were coughing and feeling short of breath for many weeks. We imaged your lungs with an X ray and a CT scan and did not see a pneumonia. Most likely your symptoms were caused by a viral infection that caused inflammation in your airway. We did not treat you with antibiotics because antibiotics do not kill viruses. We did give you medications to breath in which help open up your inflamed airways. These medications improved your breathing. We also gave you a medication to help with your cough. We will send you home with medications that you can continue to take until you feel better.

Flu swab was negative. His symptoms were thought to be due to a viral bronchitis with post-viral bronchial inflammation contributing. Antibiotics were not initiated. He improved with standing ipratropium-albuterol nebs and was discharged with an albuterol inhaler. 2. Nonspecific interstitial pneumonia: He is followed by Dr. ___ had stable disease, with PFTs last checked ___ (FVC 71%, FEV1 74%, FEV1/FVC 10, DLCO 50%). He has historically been on prednisone but this was discontinued due to his non-progression and clinical stability. Transitional issues: - Patient discharged with albuterol inhaler for bronchitis-associated wheezing - Patient discharged with follow up with his PCP and pulmonologist, Dr. ___ - ___: full - Contact: Wife and HCP: ___ ___

text: Brief Hospital Course: #Cough/SOB: Pt presented with some cough and chest tightness. Her vitals were stable on admission. A CXR did now show evidence of an infiltrate. A D-dimer was mildly elevated and thus she had a CTA which did not show evidence of a pulmonary embolism. She was given nebulizers with good improvement. Given her smoking history it was felt that her symptoms might be consistent with a COPD exacerbation vs a viral syndrome. She was ruled out for influenza. She was given guafenasin for symptom control as well as inhalers. She was discharged on spiriva and albuterol in addition to guafenasin with codeine. Her PCP was emailed in regards to possibly obtaining PFTs as an outpatient. An appointment was made for her to follow up with her PCP in the next few weeks. . #CV-Pt does have history of HTN and hyperlipidemia; an EKG on admission showed diffuse non-specific TW flattening. Pt has had 2 sets of cardiac enzymes that were negative. It does not appear that her current symptoms are cardiac in nature -She was continued on her home beta blocker, calcium channel blocker, HCTZ and aspirin. She may benefit from an outpatient echo and/or stress in the future. . #Lipids: Pt was continued on her home statin .

You were started on a mediciation called guafenasin which will help with your cough. You can take this every 6 hours as needed. In addition, you were started on some inhalers to help improve your breathing. One of them is called Spiriva which you will take once daily. The other is albuterol which you can take as needed to supplement the spiriva.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year-old woman with PMH CKD s/p creation of AV fistula (starting dialysis in ___, HTN, morbid obesity and resolved sarcoidosis, admitted with dyspnea, fever and malaise. Symptoms of malaise, fever, dyspnea and cough/congestion prior to admission are all suggestive of viral respiratory infection. Interstitial abnormality seen on CXR may suggest viral pneumonia, atypical pneumonia or viral bronchitis, vs. chronic changes from sarcoidosis (no past CXR available in OMR). Given patient's history of asthma and continued smoking, it is possible that a viral bronchitis or influenza may have triggered a COPD/asthma flare. Given severity of symptoms necessitating hospitalization, wheezing on exam, and comorbidities, we initially treated with azithromycin for COPD flare, while in conjunction treating viral respiratory infection supportively. However, the azithromycin was stopped when the patient was found to have influenza. We did not treat with oseltamivir given the duration of symptoms. We continued to treat with prednisone given history of asthma and wheezing on exam. Patient's blood sugars were difficult to control. We adjusted the patient's insulin regimen with the help of the patient's ___ physician. Ambulatory O2 sat was 92% on the day of discharge. She will taper prednisone. Patient was strongly encouraged to stop smoking. Her BP, cholesterol, and kidney disease medications were continued during this admission. Her kidney function was stable. . Transitional Issues:: # Patient was full code during this admission. # Had interstitial abnormality of uncertain chronicity on chest X-ray, which could be due to sarcoid, although atypical infection could be excluded. Enlarged hila, likely from lymphadenopathy related to sarcoid. Consider correlation with prior imaging or a nonurgent CT of the chest for further evaluation. If these findings are new compared to prior chest X-rays (we did not have priors in our system), then follow up outpatient chest X-ray or CT chest is suggested.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with fever and shortness of breath. You were found to have influenza (i.e. the flu). You were treated with IV fluids, prednisone for your asthma, and nebulizers to open your airways. You were initially treated with antibiotics, but these were stopped when your flu test came back positive, as antibiotics only work on bacterial infections, not on viruses like the flu. You are strongly encouraged to stop smoking. Smoking will worsen your lung disease and can lead to cancer and death. Quitting smoke now will improve your health in the future. Your blood sugars have been very high due to the steroids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PRESENTATION: ___ w h/o moderate intermittent asthma p/w likely asthma exacerbation and PNA. Infection likely the trigger for this asthma exacerbation. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Asthma The pt received PO prednisone 60mg QD, Albuterol nebs q6h standing and q2h PRN, ipratropium nebs q6h standing, and supplemental O2 (2L N/C). He also received levofloxacin 750mg to empirically treat for community-acquired pneumonia given new opacities on CXR. He noted improvement in his shortness of breath on this regimen. His lung exam also improved, with less expiratory wheezing by day of discharge. He was discharged to complete a 5 day prednisone burst and with prescriptions for albuterol and advair. # Pneumonia As noted above, the pt received levofloxacin 750mg to empirically treat for community-acquired pneumonia. His white blood cell count was 11.8 on admission (___) and 11.0 on day of discharge (___). At the time of discharge, blood cultures were pending. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Rhinitis The pt was without complaint and did not require treatment for rhinitis this admission. # Reflux The pt did not complain of reflux and did not require treatment for reflux this admission. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Pt will need to continue taking prednisone 60mg x 3 days - Pt will need to continue taking levofloxacin 750mg x 5 days - ___ blood final blood cultures

Please take simethicone (Gas-X) to treat this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: CHIEF COMPLAINT: SOB REASON FOR ADMISSION: ___ year old male with multiple prior hospitalizations requiring intubation for asthma exacerbation and ?additional pulmonary disease, re-presenting with acute onset dyspnea consistent with an asthma exacerbation after a recent discharge for the same. # Asthma exacerbation: Exacerbating factors for this presentation include broken nebulizer machine, life stressors, smoking, weather, and URI. Outpatient management of this patient's asthma has been extremely difficult, as he has required 6 hospitalizations this year despite back-up plans of high-dose prednisone as needed and very high doses of inhaled glucocorticoids and controller meds. During his ICU course he received standing albuterol and ipratropium q2h nebs, prednisone 60mg, azithromycin and continued his home regimen of flovent,singulair and salmeterol. On the medicine floor, he completed a 5 day azithromycin course. His PCP prophylaxis with bactrim was continued, and calcium/vitamin D given chronic intermittent high dose steroid use. Outpatient consideration for thermoplasty and consideration of therapy with zolair was discussed in emails with outpatient providers. After spacing of his nebulizers, he was discharged on a prednisone taper starting at 60mg daily x7 days, then to 40mg until followup with his PCP. He has an appointment with his pulmonologist Dr. ___ in ___, but he was encouraged to make an earlier appointment if possible. # Anxiety: Patient cited multiple life stressors, including marital discord, which are likely contributing to his frequent asthma exacerbations. Was seen by social work who recommended outpatient resources. He was given ativan 1mg prn to help with anxiety, which he will continue on discharge. # Smoking cessation: Patient reports interest in smoking cessation. He used nicotine lozenges during admission and also expressed interest in discussing Chantix with his PCP. # OSA on CPAP: Patient was on home CPAP at night, with the exception of the night spent in the ICU for more frequent nebulizer therapy. # GERD: Likely secondary to chronic steroid use, continued his home dose PPI. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Asthma exacerbation - He is on a steroid taper with close outpatient followup Life stressors - He was given a list of outpatient resources by SW Smoking cessation - He was given lozenges, and expressed interest in discussing Chantix with outpatient providers. MEDICATION CHANGES: START nicotine lozenges START calcium and vitamin D START prednisone taper at 60mg daily x7days, then to 40mg daily until following up with PCP START lorazepam for anxiety
summary: You were admitted for increasing shortness of breath. Your symptoms were consistent with an asthma exacerbation. You were treated with nebulizers, systemic and inhaled steroids, and your home medications. You will be on a prednisone taper for several weeks. Given the side effects of steroids, you were given calcium and vitamin D in the hospital, which you should continue at home. Your heart rate was very fast during hospitalization. Causes of your elevated heart rate include some of the medications that you were on, as well as anxiety. You were given low dose benzodiazepines to help with anxiety and were seen by the ___ social worker to discuss coping mechanisms and outpatient therapy resources. It is strongly suggested that you pursue out patient counseling as well as psychiatry to help address your anxiety which is likely contributing to your asthma exacerbations. You can continue to take the low dose anti-anxiolytic as an outpatient, but must not drink or operate machinery on the medication. You were counseled on smoking cessation during your stay. You were given nicotine lozenges to help with cravings during hospitalization. We strongly encourage continued smoking cessation in the outpatient setting, as smoking is contributing to your frequent asthma exacerbations. You are being sent home with lozenges and should talk to your primary care doctor about ___ prescription medicine called Chantix.
