text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ w/ h/o asthma, T2DM, and CAD, recently discharged from the ___ ED with prednisone burst for an asthma exacerbation, who presented with 1 week of dyspnea on exertion and cough due to an asthma exacerbation likely in the setting of a viral URI as a trigger, with her course complicated by ___ and worsening hyperglycemia secondary to prednisone. # Asthma exacerbation: Besides a cough, she did not have any additional symptoms concerning for an infectious process, and her CXR was clear. Peak flows on admission were 100-150. She was treated with albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers, Advair inhaler, her home dose of monteleukast, and started on a prednisone taper. Her wheezes improved throughout her stay, and she was no longer short of breath by the time of discharge. She will finish her prednisone taper after discharge and continue her regular asthma regimen, including albuterol nebulizer, albuterol inhaler, Symbicort inhaler, and monteleukast. THE PREDNISONE SHOULD BE GIVEN ___ MINUTES AFTER ADMINISTERING LANTUS AND NPH. She will be seen by pulmonary as an outpatient for further asthma management. # Hyperglycemia/T2DM: Her blood sugar was >500 when first presenting to the floor. DKA was ruled out with reassuring VBG (pH 7.41) and no ketones on UA. Her sugars were controlled between 150-400s during her hospital stay with ___ Lantus, insulin sliding scale, her home dose of glipizide, and sitagliptin. Her metformin was restarted at a decreased dose (500 mg BID) on the day prior to discharge. She will use an NPH taper while she is tapering off prednisone (16u on 20 mg prednisone ___ & ___, 12u on 10 mg prednisone ___ & ___, and 8u on 5 mg prednisone ___ & ___. She will also begin taking 20 units of Lantus daily, and if found to be safe with administration by her PCP and ___ then even after finishing the prednisone taper she will continue this. A ___ will assist her with administering the NPH and Lantus each morning ___ MINUTES PRIOR TO RECEIVING PREDNISONE, as twice a day ___ is not available and pt has not been able to draw up the appropriate amounts of insulin after being taught by floor nurse, ___, and diabetes educator. She will otherwise continue taking her glipizide 5mg BID and sitagliptin 50 mg daily. Her husband will be at home as well to supervise her and help with blood sugars. ___: Cr 1.3 on admission, which is her baseline. Creatinine increased to 1.8, likely a pre-renal etiology secondary to poor PO intake. Cr downtrended after IV fluid resuscitation and was 1.5 on discharge. # Leukocytosis: WBC elevated to 17 in the setting of prednisone use. She endorsed a cough, but no fevers, chills, sweats, or other signs of infection. This was monitored throughout her stay with no evidence of infection. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [] Please assess insulin use and diabetes management as an outpatient. Patient has close followup at ___ and will have home ___ to give insulin doses each morning. [] ___ wants to start seeing the patient as an outpatient. To schedule, patient should contact ___. Please make two appointments for the same day - first appointment with NP or MD and second appointment with a diabetes educator. [] Pt started on Lantus 20U qAM (will need to be monitored for safe administration) [] Pt started on NPH taper (taper dose with prednisone): 16U on 20 mg prednisone ___ & ___, 12U on 10 mg prednisone ___ & ___, and 8U on 5 mg prednisone ___ & ___. [] Prednisone taper 20mg x2 d, 10mg x2 d, 5mg x2 days to complete on ___. [] Metformin dose decreased from 1000mg BID to ___ BID. [] Please recheck Chem7 on ___ to assess for ___ resolution. [] Patient should follow up in ___ Pulmonary Clinic for further asthma management given exacerbations on an intensive regimen. # CODE: FULL (confirmed) # CONTACT: ___ (daughter), ___. ___ (husband), ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were having difficulty breathing along with a cough for the past week. We think that your shortness of breath is due to an asthma exacerbation which may have been brought on by a viral illness that may be causing your cough. We imaged your lungs with a chest x-ray that fortunately did not show pneumonia. During your hospitalization, we treated your asthma with nebulizers and restarted you on steroids to reduce your wheezing and help you breathe better. You should continue taking the steroid taper for 6 days after your discharge ___ = 20mg, ___ = 10mg, ___ = 5mg). You should also continue to use your standard asthma regimen, including your albuterol nebulizer, albuterol inhaler, Symbicort inhaler, and monteleukast. We were also concerned about your high blood sugars while you were in the hospital. The steroid (prednisone) is most likely causing your high blood sugars, and your sugars should return to their normal range soon after you finish the steroid course. However, we would like you to start using insulin in addition to your normal diabetes medications to help control your sugars as you finish the prednisone. We are prescribing you two types of insulin: intermediate-acting and long-acting. You should take the intermediate-acting insulin (NPH) each morning, and this dose will decrease as your steroid dose decreases. You will stop taking the NPH insulin when you stop taking the prednisone. You should take the long-acting insulin (___) each morning at the same dose every day (20 units each morning). After you finish your prednisone, you should talk with your primary care physician and ___ doctors about safely ___ each day. It is likely you will continue on this medication even after you are done with your prednisone. We are also decreasing your dose of metformin to 500 mg twice a day. You should continue taking your glipizide at the same dose of 5 mg twice a day. You should also continue taking your sitagliptin at the same dose of 50 mg daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ yo woman w/ PMHx of ___ disease who was admitted for unresolved SOB and cough despite antibiotic treatment for presumed pneumonia. # Bronchitis / ?Pneumonia. Recently diagnosed with pneumonia and completed antibiotic course of doxycycline. Per son had some intermittent cough and shortness of breath but CXR here showing no evidence of pneumonia. Currently she denies cough or SOB, has normal oxygen saturation and clear lungs on examination. Low suspicion for current bacterial process. She had occasional cough and wheezing after eating. She was evaluated by speech and swallow who thought she did overall well and did not think VSS indicated at this time. - Atrovent nebs q6h standing for 2 weeks - Fluticasone inhaler for reactive airways - Albuterol nebs PRN # Rash: developed pruritic rash after receiving Levaquin, no prior history of allergies per patient or son. ___ improved after Benadryl and currently resolved. - Added Levaquin as an allergy # ___ disease - Continue home meds. # Difficulty ambulating: Having worsening difficulty ambulating. ___ recommending rehab, patient and family only interested in ___ rehab where she has been before. ___ declined patient, so family decided to take her home with services. summary: You were admitted for occasional wheezing and cough. There was concern for pneumonia but your chest X-ray did not show pneumonia and your symptoms quickly improved with nebulizers. Antibiotics were stopped and you tolerated this just fine. Overall, your presentation is most consistent with a post infectious bronchitis or asthma like syndrome. You were evaluated by speech and language pathology who felt you would benefit from a ground diet with thin liquids to minimize your risks of accidentally inhaling some of your food when you eat (which can predispose to pneumonia and coughing fits). You were deconditioned and had difficulty moving around and physical therapy recommended going to a rehab facility. You did not qualify for acute level rehab and so you and your family elected to go home with maximal services in order to work on getting stronger at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ y/o female with a history of asthma, HTN, CKD who presented from her PCP's office with SOB and wheezing. #SOB/Wheezing: Patient presented following two weeks of productive cough (yellow sputum) and several days of SOB and wheezing. Initial exam notable for diffuse wheezes. CXR was normal. She was treated with duoneb x2 and prednisone 60 mg x1 with improvement in her breathing and wheezing. Subsequently, prednisone was discontinued (after two doses) and she was transitioned to albuterol inhaler q6h and advair BID (formulary equivalent of home symbicort). DVT US was also done given ___ edema; there was no evidence of DVT. She remained on room air throughout the admission and had a normal respiratory exam without wheezes on day of discharge. Discharged on her home medications. Also given a peak flow monitor and encouraged to use this, record her numbers, and bring them to next PCP ___. Peak flow at the time of discharge: 230. #Deconditioning: Patient was seen by physical therapy who felt she could use outpatient physical therapy to improve her balance and minimize fall risk. summary: You were admitted due to increasing shortness of breathing, wheezing, and coughing. Initially we did an xray of your lungs, which looked normal. We also gave you nebulizer (breathing) treatments and oral steroids for a day to help your breathing. This seemed to help. We then switched you back over to all of your home medicines (albuterol, Symbicort), and you did well while taking these. We believe this could have been a mild asthma exacerbation on top of an upper respiratory tract infection (i.e. a cold). Continue using your symbicort twice a day and use the albuterol inhaler as needed for shortness of breath or wheezing. Measure your peak flow (machine you blow into) once a day for the next ___ days.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ y/o female with a history of asthma, HTN, CKD who presented from her PCP's office with SOB and wheezing. #SOB/Wheezing: Patient presented following two weeks of productive cough (yellow sputum) and several days of SOB and wheezing. Initial exam notable for diffuse wheezes. CXR was normal. She was treated with duoneb x2 and prednisone 60 mg x1 with improvement in her breathing and wheezing. Subsequently, prednisone was discontinued (after two doses) and she was transitioned to albuterol inhaler q6h and advair BID (formulary equivalent of home symbicort). DVT US was also done given ___ edema; there was no evidence of DVT. She remained on room air throughout the admission and had a normal respiratory exam without wheezes on day of discharge. Discharged on her home medications. Also given a peak flow monitor and encouraged to use this, record her numbers, and bring them to next PCP ___. Peak flow at the time of discharge: 230. #Deconditioning: Patient was seen by physical therapy who felt she could use outpatient physical therapy to improve her balance and minimize fall risk. No changes were made to home medication regimen. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [ ] Peak flow at discharge: 230 [ ] Discharged on home albuterol inhaler with spacer and Symbicort. [ ] Inpatient physical therapist recommended outpatient ___ for deconditioning. #Contact: ___ ___

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, HLP, obesity, asthma, GERD, NASH, OSA, bipolar disorder, endometrial cancer presents with chest pain with normal exercise stress test except for ectopy and runs of up to 11-beat NSVT. # CHEST PAIN: Troponins negative x2, EKG without ischemia. Exercise stress with modified ___ lasted 7 min, ___ METS, fair functional capacity, stopped for fatigue. No ischemic changes but lots of ectopy with PACs, PVCs, and up to 11-beats NSVT. No recurrence of chest pain during admission. Managed on ASA 81 mg daily, Metoprolol succinate 50 daily. #Ventricular ectopy: patient with considerable ventricular ectopy in the setting of her stress test. No recurrent episodes subsequently. On the floor, patient able to walk 2 flights of stairs and elevate HR to 120s without ventricular ectopy. TTE with no abnormalities suggesting structural cause of ectopy. Started on metop succinate 50 daily; will return as outpatient to have ___ monitor for further evaluation as an outpatient. CHRONIC ISSUES =============== # Chronic venous stasis. Cont Lasix. # HTN. Residual loose bowel movements per patient which are chronic. Cont loperamide. # OSA. Cont CPAP. summary: You were admitted with chest pain and underwent a stress test to determine whether it was caused by poor blood flow to your heart. This stress test was negative; however, during the test you demonstrated an abnormal heart rhythm called a ventricular tachycardia. You underwent an echocardiogram to evaluate your heart for any structural abnormalities; none were present. You were started on a new medication, Metoprolol, which will help control your heart rate moving forward and prevent further episodes of ventricular tachycardia. You will also be using a ___ of hearts" monitor at home to track your heart rhythm and ensure there are no repeat episodes.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with pmh of poorly controlled asthma admitted with flu-like sx and asthma exacerbation with a new diagnosis of HIV. . #. Dyspnea: Asthma exacerbation likely triggered by flu. However, pt unable to tolerate nasopharyngeal swab for influenza. CXR clear so unlikely to be PNA. Because of diagnosis of HIV with dyspnea and clear CXR there was concern for PCP. Induced sputum was negative for PCP, CT scan showed no evidence of PCP, walking O2 sats stable at 95% on RA, and ABG showed PaO2=79. Pt was treated with prednisone, albuterol, and atrovent nebs. She did not respond initially so prednisone was changed to solumedrol for one day due to concern about possible poor absorption of prednisone. Advair was changed to flovent and albuterol was made PRN due to concern about beta receptor down-regulation. Upon discharge Peak flow was 240. Pt was d/ced on a prednisone taper. Pt was scheduled for Pulm follow-up . #. Fever/HA/myalgias: Likely influenza vs other viral syndrome. However, couldn't make a definitive deignosis because pt was unable to tolerate nasophayngeal aspirate. Pt was placed on droplet precaustions and treated with supportive care with fluids, cough supressant, and tylenol. . #. HIV: Pt with recent HIV test that was found to be positive during this admission. Social work saw the pt multiple times to aid with coping. ID was consulted, and pt will follow with them as an outpatient. There was concern for PCP PNA, but she ruled out. CD4 count was found to be 179 and VL was ___. Pt was started on Bactrim PPX on discharge. Multiple tests were requested by ID ith the plan to f/u results as an outpt. These include hepatitis serologies, toxo serologies, rpr, HIV genotype, LFTs (normal). Pt. was scheduled for ID follow-up. At which time she will likely begin anti-retroviral therapy . #. Trichomonas on UA: treated with single dose of flagyl. . #. Anemia: pt noted to have iron def anemia. She was treated with IV iron and given a prescription for PO iron. As an outpt pt will need to have GI follow-up to evaluate iron deficiency anemia.
summary: You were admitted to treat an asthma exacerbation. This exacerbation was likley due to infulenza. However, we were unable to confirm a diagnosis of the flu because you where unable to tolerate the nasal test. You were also noted to have a positive HIV test which was drawn on your last hospitalization. CT scan and sputum analysis were done to look for a possible infection with an oragnism call PCP. However, niether the CT scan or the sputum sample showed evidence of this infection. Dr. ___ infectious disease saw you and will follow you in clinic. Your T cell level was found to be low, so you were started on an Antibiotic called Bactrim, which you will need to take every day. You were also noted to have a low iron level causing anemia.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with hypothyroidism, osteoporosis and allergic rhinitis who presented with cough, rhinorrhea, and SOB, found to be hypoxic with mucous plugging on CT scan. # SOB/Mucous plugging: Clinical picture given prodromal symptoms and post-nasal drip was most consistent with viral process leading to post-infectious mucous plugging. There was most likely a component of reactive airway disease with inflammation based on exam and rapid improvement with nebulizers and steroids. She has no known history of lung disease. During this admission she remained afebrile and there was no evidence of pneumonia on imaging including CT chest. She was treated with a 5 day prednisone burst from ___ (60mg x 2days then 40mg x 3 days), chest physical therapy, acapella valve, guaifenesin and albuterol/ipratropium nebs with successful mobilization of congestion. She initially had an oxygen requirement, which was gradually weaned off over several days. Peak flow improved from <100 prior to admission to 195 with treatment. She was able to maintain oxygen saturations greater than 90% with ambulation prior to discharge. Pt plans on purchasing a pulse oximeter as an outpatient for home O2 monitoring. She was discharged with an albuterol rescue inhaler with spacer and was instructed on proper use before leaving. She will need to follow up with her PCP as an outpatient. # Back pain: Pt complained of ongoing low back pain that was most likely musculoskeletal in nature based on description of symptoms and exam. Pt stated that she had been improving at home prior to onset of worsening cough which exacerbated her symptoms. There were no red flag symptoms or focal neurologic changes on exam. She was continued on flexeril and Tylenol on arrival. Lidocaine patch and small dose of tramadol were added for better symptom control to encourage ambulation, which will help with respiratory status. While inpatient she was evaluated by ___ who gave her a set of home back exercises. She should keep her previously scheduled outpatient ___ appointment and follow up with her PCP after discharge for further management. # Hyponatremia: Na was found to be low at 125 on admission labs. This was most likely hypovolemic in nature due to poor PO intake, which was supported by improvement after 1L NS. Na remained improved in the ___ prior to discharge. PO intake was encouraged. # Leukocytosis: WBC count was mildly elevated on admission in the setting of recent infection. Cell counts initially downtrended before uptrending again due to steroids. # Hypertension: Pt became mildly hypertensive while holding her home labetalol, which was initially held given her respiratory status. Her home labetalol was restarted and she remained normotensive thereafter. # Hypothyroidism: Continued home levothyroxine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== - Encourage continued coughing exercises, acapella valve, guaifenesin - Prescribed albuterol inhaler to use PRN - Prescribed limited supply of tramadol to last until PCP ___ appointment on ___ ___s lidocaine patch for back pain - Outpatient ___ for back pain - Incidental RUL pulm nodule: 6 mm nodule noted on CT chest. Radiology report: if the patient is low risk for lung cancer initial ___ CT can be obtained in ___ months. If the patient is high risk initial ___ CT should be obtained in ___ months. - CODE STATUS: Full - CONTACT: ___, husband ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were short of breath. You had a CAT scan of your chest that showed mucous plugging, which was most likely a result of a recent viral infection. While you were here you were treated with oral steroids, chest physical therapy, acapella valve, nebulizers and guaifenesin to help break up the mucous and help to bring it up. Your breathing improved and you were able to maintain oxygen saturations greater than 90% even while walking. After discharge you should continue the coughing exercises that you learned in the hospital. We also gave you an albuterol inhaler to take if you begin to feel short of breath or wheezy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is the brief hospital course for a ___ year-old female with a history of dementia, osteoporosis, allergic rhinitis, and asthma who presented to us with a mildly productive cough for 2 weeks, hypoxia, and gross eosinophilia. The following interventions and management decisions were addressed during this hospitalization: . HYPOXIA AND COUGH: The patient was maintained at an oxygen saturation level of at least 89% (close to her baseline) with shovel high flow humidification, face mask, and nasal cannula as required by patient. She was eventually weaned to only nasal cannula and maintained saturations of 94% or greater. Treatment included a 4 days course of Azithromycin for anti-inflammatory effects, steroids which will slow taper over the next two weeks, and the patient's home nebulizers and inhalers. Montelukast was discontinued from the asthma home regimen as it was noted to cause symptom exacerbations in patients like Ms. ___. We monitored our progress in fixing the hypoxia by taking arterial blood gases and CXRs to assess interval change. . The work-up for the cause of the hypoxia was extensive. She was ruled out for serious events such as myocardial infarction and pulmonary embolus with EKGs, serial cardiac enzymes, and a CT angiogram of the chest. An echocardiogram with bubbles was performed to evaluate cardiac causes of the shunt seen on the patient's A-a gradient, but was inconclusive with only a small patent foramen ovale noted. The following were possible differential diagnoses for the hypoxia paired with gross eosinophilia. . INFECTION: No consolidations or collections were seen on CTA or CXR that would suggest cryptogenic organizing pneumonia or eosinophilic pneumonia. Lab tests were sent to evaluate for aspergillosis or ABPA, but no cavitary lesions were seen on imaging. Azithromycin was administered for 4 days to cover a possible PNA, but also to aid with anti-inflammation. . EOSINOPHILIA: The patient's absolute eosinophil calculation was approximately 5900 on admission. She did have a history of asthma and allergic rhinitis, although no specific history of eczema which commonly occurs with this blood count. Acute eosinophilic pneumonia seemed likely given the time course, however, she lacked imaging evidence of any consolidation for the first 3 days of her hospital stay. There was no history that would suggest fungal or parasitic exposure. No recent travel outside the country or visits from other travelers to predispose for tropical eosinophilia. Eosinophils trended down to normal with steroids. . POSSIBLE CHURG ___: The most likely etiology at this time, given the patient's rhinorrhea, eosinophilia, and sinus tenderness. Sinus x-rays were negative as well as ANCA antibody testing. . ALLERGIC REACTION: First Reclast infusion was given on ___. Recent studies have reported an association between beginning Reclast treatment and the onset of shortness of breath and decreased lung function. IgE was negative. Avoid Reclast. . ASTHMA: Commonly a second reaction on top of the patient's underlying asthma could also be exhibited as a cough and mucous production plugs leading to hypoxia and rhinitis with sinus tenderness. Outpatient asthma regimen continued. . MALIGNANCY: Malignancy was less likely given lack of evidence of mass on CT or x-ray. Patient's sister did have lung cancer ___ years ago, and the patient endorses a 35 lbs weight loss in the last year. Severe eosinophilia can be seen associated with malignancies such as lung cancer, Hodgkins lymphoma, and eosinophilic leukemia. . # ___ Body Dementia: ___ protocol enacted while in hospital. . # Osteoporosis: First ever Reclast infusion on ___. Avoid re-infusing as above. . # Hyperlipidemia: Lipids well managed on home simvastatin; continued. . Patient was seen by a dietician who recommended Fibersource supplements with goal intake = 1296 kcals and 57 gm protein.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital from ___ with severe shortness of breath. We found that you had very high levels of one particular type of cell in your blood. This cell is associated with allergies, asthma, infections, and some autoimmune diseases. We ran quite a few blood tests, imaging studies, and cultures to figure out what was causing your symptoms. In the end, the teams we had consulted about you felt that you were probably experiencing a flare of an autoimmune disease called Churg ___. The fact that you have asthma probably contributed to your shortness of breath. Also, we are recommending that you NOT have anymore Reclast injections as this could also have played a part in this event.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with PMH DM, ESRD s/p kidney transplant on immunosuppression, hypertension, history of ulcerative colitis, and asthma presenting with chest pain. . # Chest pain: The patient was admitted with severe substernal chest pain that was reproducible on palpation and was exacerbated by movement. On admission, she was ruled out for ACS by EKG and cardiac enzymes x 3. CT angiogram was negative for dissection, pulmonary embolism, and pneumonia. The patient was provided a lidocaine patch and standing tylenol for pain. NSAIDs were avoided, as the patient has a history of renal transplant. Pain improved over course of admission. . # Low grade temp: The patient had one low grade fever to 100.5 on day 2 of admission. Her WBC remained stable at 9, and she remained asymptomatic. There was no evidence of pneumonia on admission chest CT. Urine and blood cultures at the time of discharge were no growth to date. CMV viral load pending. She remained afebrile for the remainder of admission. . # Acute renal failure: Following a large contrast load for CTA, the patient's creatinine bumped from 1.0 to 1.8. She received hydration with bicarbonate shortly after the procedure, and then with normal saline when urine electrolytes continued to appear prerenal. She underwent renal ultrasound that did not show intrinsic renal disease. The patient's creatinine improved. She was discharged to home, and should follow up with her transplant nephrologist. . #Muscle cramping: On admission, the patient experienced severe muscle cramping. She was found to have derranged electrolytes, with low calcium and magnesium. Cramping may have been related to electrolyte imbalances vs. dehydration, as it improved with fluid and electrolyte repletion. . #HTN: The patient has a history of hypertension, with marked LVH on EKG suggestive that she is poorly controlled as an outpatient. She remained normotensive to mildly hypertensive throughout admission. She was continued on home lisinopril and amlodipine. The patient's lisinopril was not up-titrated in the setting of large contrast load and ___. The patient was continued on aspirin for cardiac protection. . # s/p renal transplant: The patient is maintained on prednisone, azathioprine, tacrolimus. Tacrolimus troughs remained at goal throughout admission. The patient received a large contrast load on admission, and her creatinine trended up. Her urine electrolytes appeared pre-renal, and she was bolused with 500 cc IVF. She went for a renal ultrasound that showed no evidence of intrinsic renal disease. Her creatinine began to trend down. The patient should follow up with her renal transplant physician as an outpatient. PCP prophylaxis initiation should be considered as an outpatient for chronic prednisone use. . # Chronic Anemia: Stable throughout admission. The patient was continued on home iron and folic acid. . # GERD: The patient denied symptoms of GERD on admission. Her home nexium was held on admission, as it is non-formulary, and she was started on protonix while in-house. She was also continued on home Calcium Carbonate. The patient may resume her home GERD regimen on discharge. . # DM II, well controlled: On admission, the patient was restarted on her home dose of NPH 16 units BID and humalog sliding scale. She was followed with QID fingersticks . # Ulcerative Colitis: The patient did not experience any GI symptoms throughout admission. She was continued on her home immunosuppression regimen. . # CODE: Full TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - CMV viral load pending on discharge - She needs to follow up with ___ clinic - Blood Cultures NGTD x2 on discharge should follow up final
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. You were ruled out for cardiac causes of chest pain by EKG and laboratory testing. You were also ruled out for blood clots to your lungs and pneumonia with an imaging study called a CT angiogram. Your chest pain is likely from straining your muscles in your chest. We started you on tylenol and a lidocaine patch, and your pain improved. The contrast you received with the CT angiogram caused your kidney function to temporarily worsen. We treated you with IV fluids, and your kidney function returned to baseline. You underwent a renal ultrasound that showed there was no damage to your transplanted kidney.

summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were feeling lightheaded and you had a fall and hit your head. We did some studies and found out that you had some blood in your head outside of your brain. This is called a subdural hematoma. We did another test and found out that the hematoma had not grown; our neurosurgeons evaluated you and believe that this hematoma will most likely resolve on its own. We believe that the fainting episode was not related directly to your heart or your pacemaker. We think that you can be discharged today and that you can continue your recovery at home. We have adjusted your blood pressure and heart medications to decrease the risk of you fainting again. We have added further instructions for your other treatments, including your blood thinners.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Chest pain, dyspnea: The patient was admitted to the floor hypertensive (180/82) but otherwise in stable condition without chest pressure or dyspnea (T=98.2, BP=180/82, HR=83, RR=18, O2 sat=100% on RA). EKG in the ED showed no ischemic changes, and 3 sets of cardiac enzymes were normal, making acute coronary syndrome unlikely. Urine tox was negative for cocaine use, also ruling out a syndrome X, or acute narrowing of the coronary arteries. Given the patient's lack of compliance with her HTN and GERD medications, her episode likely reflected a hypertensive crisis with possible overlying reflux. Her emphysema may have also contributed to her SOB. The patient received a prednisone taper 60 mg, ___, as well as albuterol prn, ipratropium nebs, and fluticasone-salmeterol for her emphysema and experienced improvements in her breathing. She was restarted on her home nifedipine and was half her home valsartan dose, which subsequently normalized her blood pressures. She was also started on her home aspirin 81 mg daily and ompeprazole. A lipid panel was drawn, which was within normal limits, including a LDL of 111. Giver a ___ risk score of 4%, a statin was not initiated. At the time of discharge, the patient had been free of chest pressure and SOB for 48 hours. Urinary retention with baseline ___ weakness/numbness/tingling: Despite recent initiation of opiods for pain control in the ED and on the floor, the patient's new onset of urinary retention upon reaching the floor raised concern for cord compression. L spine MR was performed showing no evidence for cord compression, spinal stenosis or foramenal narrowing. The patient's urinary retention resolved that evening and recurred the next day with constipation following her morning morphine dose. Opiods were discontinued. With a bowel regimen, the constipation and urinary retention resolved, and the patient was discharged on tylenol for pain control. Given the patient's complaints of of baseline L leg weakness and b/l ___ numbness and tingling, B12, TSH, folate levels were drawn rule out possible metabolic sources of of peripheral neuropathy. An outpatient Neurology appointment was arranged to follow up likely peripheral neuropathy underlying these symptoms. A ___ consult was called and cleared the patient for ambulation at home, though visiting home services were arranged for the patient's chronic pain and anxiety. HTN: The pt was acutely hypertensive on admission (SBP 180-190), and resolved on home nifedipine and ___ dose of valsartan with which she was discharged. RUQ pain / bruising: Likely related to patient's hepatitis, the patient's LFTs were mildly elevated and coag studies wnl. There were no acute issues during her hospital stay. PVD: No issues. A recent Cardiology appointment judged the patient's lower extremity symptoms to be unlikely due to her PVD given ABI showing minimal disease below the knees. As discussed above, her ___ weakness, numbness and tingling is ossibly due to peripheral neuropathy secondary to alcohol abuse. Outpatient neurology appointment was scheduled. Alcohol abuse: Given the patient's history of alcohol abuse, she was placed on CIWA protocol but showed no signs of withdrawal throughout her stay. Fibromyalgia: The patient was given Morphine 15 mg po q12h for pain control and then transitioned to tylenol given the patient's new onset of urinary retention and constipation. Home visiting services were arranged to help the patient cope with the patient's chronic pain and anxiety.
summary: You were admitted to the ___ on ___ with an episode of chest pressure, shortness of breath and belly pain which woke you from your sleep in the setting of not taking your acid reflux or blood pressure medications for over 1 month. This episode was possibly due to your gastric reflux as well as strain on the heart due to hypertension. Your emphysema was likely contributing to your shortness of breath. In the hospital, an electrocardiogram and lab tests showed that you did not have a heart attack. You were restarted on your home nifedipine and half dose of valsartan for your blood pressure and omeprazole for your GERD. Your home aspirin was also started to help prevent future heart attack. For your shortness of breath, you were given your a short course of oral steroids as well as ipratropium and Advair, which helped to relieve your symptoms. During your episode of chest pressure, you also sustained 3 falls on your L hip, which increased your L leg pain and weakness. An MR scan of your lumbar spine showed no evidence of spinal cord or nerve root compression that might exacerbate your symptoms. In the hospital, your pain was well-controlled on oral morphine. This medication caused you to have urinary retention and constipation. You should not take this medication in the future.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old woman presents with right-sided chest pain, shortness of breath, and tachycardia. She was treated for possible asthma exacerbation. Chest pain and shortness of breath resolved after the first night. On telemetry, the patient was found to have intermittent episodes of narrow-complex tachycardia to the 140's. Cardiology consult determined that she was atrial fibrillation. Metoprolol uptitrated for heart rate control and warfarin started. There was some concern that the patient had mild diastolic dysfunction on Echocardiogram and physical exam, so one dose of Lasix 10mg PO given. Patient was asymptomatic at the time of discharge. # Asthma exacerbation s/p 5 days azithromycin, prednisone, and nebs. # Atrial fibrillation: Metoprolol uptitrated to tartrate 100mg Q8H with HR 50-70. For better compliance, dosing changed to BID dosing with Toprol XL 100mg BID. Given that her CHADS2 score is high (HTN, Age, Hx Stroke), Warfarin 3mg started on ___. INR to be checked by ___ on ___ and f/u with PCP ___ ___. # Hypertension: On Lisinopril, Metoprolol, HCTZ # Lactic Acidosis: Lactate up to 8.6, but patient was not septic. Possibly from respiratory muscle exertion given dyspnea. ___ have been ___ Albuterol side effect. Lactate down to 1.3 on day of discharge. # Diastolic dysfunction: Patient euvolemic, does not need lasix.
summary: You were admitted with difficulty with breathing and very fast heart rate. You were given medications to help for possible asthma exacerbation. We found that you have an irregular heart rhythm called ATRIAL FIBRILLATION. This heart rhythm can cause your heart rate to get very fast. This condition also puts you at higher risk of forming blood clots in your heart that can cause stroke. This is why we are recommending that you start taking Warfarin (Coumadin), a blood thinner. For this medication, you will require regular blood checks to make sure you are on the correct dose. Your medications have been adjusted for your heart rate control, blood thinning, and blood pressure control.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. Hypoxia: Patient admitted with reported home oxygen saturation of mid-80s. Home measurement could have been accurate; however, patient's oxygen saturation at presentation to ED was at his baseline of 94%. Denies any chest pain, dyspnea, fever, chills. Patient had a cough for 4 days that was non-productive and was resolved at time of admission. This led us to believe that his presentation of hypoxia and dyspnea could have been attributed to a post-URI bronchitis. He had a leukocytosis of 11.1 upon arrival, but no bands and this appears to be his baseline white count through the past year. Patient was asymptomatic and had his baseline oxygen sat of 94% in ED and upon arrival to the floor. Levofloxacin started in ED, but not indicated in this patient. We discontinued his levofloxacin immediately after arrival to the floor. Overnight the night of admission, the patient had an oxygen saturation in the low ___. He required supplemental oxygen at 2 L/min. He then experienced some dyspnea the morning of discharge; however, this resolved with albuterol nebulizer. He was ambulated following combivent administration and was able to maintain his oxygen saturation at his baseline of 94%. For this reason, patient was discharged on Advair and combivent. He had a history of an Advair prescription for ___ years with poor compliance. #. Chronic pain from post-herpetic neuralgia: Stable at admission. We continued him on lyrica, lidocaine patch, fentanyl patch. #. Acetabular fracture: Patient reports tolerable ambulation with new walker. He was on oxycodone while hospitalized and was discharged on his prior home pain control regimen. #. Sarcoidosis: Stable with oxygen sat of 94% upon arriving to floor. Patient was continued on prednisone at 10 mg daily. He was started on calcium + vitamin D supplementation at time of discharge. #. Atrial fibrillation: Stable while hospitalized. On warfarin anticoagulation. Patient's INR on morning of discharge was 3.0. He was told to restart home warfarin regimen and follow-up as he was previously for anticoagulation.
summary: You were admitted with report of a low oxygen saturation at home. This was likely caused by your body's reaction to a recent viral infection since you reported a cough prior to coming to the hospital. You were found to have a normal oxygen saturation at admission; however, the next day your oxygen level dropped slightly. Your oxygen level came up after using a breathing treatment and Advair. Because of this, we have started you on two new medications called Combivent and Advair. Advair should be used twice daily. Combivent should be used as 1 to 2 puffs with spacer up to every 6 hours as needed for shortness of breath or wheezing. You should also use combivent if your oxygen level drops below 94%. Due to concern for osteoporosis, we have started you on calcium supplements as well as vitamin D.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, ESRD on HD, IDDM and asthma who presented with chest tightness while hypotensive at hemodialysis. #. Chest tightness, not ACS: The underlying etiology of this chest tightness was not clear. While she well have small vessel atherosclerosis leading to ischemia in the setting of hypoperfusion, her respiratory symtpoms (as below) were much more striking, raising the possibility of bronchospasm/pulmonary etiology. Alternatively, the patient's hypotension may have led to a tachycardia/tachyarrhythmia. The patient was placed on tele without event. Her troponin was elevated in the setting of ESRD although her CKs were flat and her troponin remained stable over time. A p-MIBI was completed and failed to show evidence of ongoing ischemia. She had no further episodes while in the hospital. She was started on an aspirin and advised to alert her healthcare providers should she again develop symptoms. # Hypoxia, DOE. The patient was noted to be very short of breath with desaturations to the mid-80s with minimal exertion. There was also concern for nocturnal apneic periods and further desaturations. The patient was monitored while sleeping and sleep medicine was consulted due to a high clinical suspicion for obstructive sleep apnea. A sleep study was not able to be coordinated while in-house so the patient was arranged to have an outpatient sleep study shortly after her discharge. She was set up with home oxygen to be used with ambulation as well as oxygen and autoset CPAP for use nocturnally until sleep study could occur. She also underwent V/Q scan to evaluate for PE or chronic thromboembolic disease which was low probabilty; non-contrast CT scan to evaluate for parenchymal abnormalities which showed air-trapping in the small airways; and TTE to evaluate for pulmonary hypertension or shunting which showed moderate pulmonary hypertension. PFTs were ordered to be done as an outpatient and the patient was asked to make an appointment for these to be done shortly after discharge. The patient was also set up with a PCP who was contacted in order to follow up on these studies and help to arrange/ensure that the remainder of her DOE/SOB work up be completed. #. Asthma. Alb/iprat nebs were given along with home inhaled steroid. #. Hospital acquired-PNA. The patient had one episode of fever toward the end of her hospital course. CXR was done and showed a possible consolidation in the right middle lobe. She was given vanc/cefepime initially and then transitioned to levoflox to complete a course as outlined elsewhere. # Incidental Adrenal lesion seen on CT. A 14mm lesion was noted in the patient's adrenal gland on chest CT. Given the patient's cushoingoid appearance and the presence of this lesion, there was concern for a hyperaldo state. Dex supression test was abnormal with 7:30 am value 6.7. Renin level was checked and was high. ___, plasma metanephrines and TSH levels were within normal limits. Prolactin was slightly elevated at 32, felt likely a result of ESRD. Given the complexity of interpreting these values in the setting of ESRD, endocrine was called and felt the patient would be best served by an outpatient evaluation due to fluctuating hormone levels during hospitalization. The patient was asked to arrange a follow up appointment shortly after her discharge. #. ESRD: Home meds and HD were continued. She also underwent additional ultrafiltration given some mild fluid overload. #. IDDM: Conintued QID ___ with home lantus and sliding scale coverage.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for chest discomfort. You were evaluated for signs of heart muscle damage. These tests did not show any active heart muscle damage (NO heart attack). You were noted to be very short of breath so you underwent multiple tests to look for reasons why you might be short of breath. You were found to have a moderate degree of "pulmonary hypertension" or high blood pressures in the lungs. This may be related to sleep apnea or other, as of yet, not diagnosed problems. It is very important for you to wear CPAP and oxygen at night, oxygen while walking and see the sleep doctors as ___ below. You had extra fluid removed at dialysis and should resume your regular dialysis schedule on ___ at ___. You should also speak with a social worker at ___ about ensuring that your insurance will cover an outpatient sleep study as you may want to obtain supplemental insurance to cover these costs. You are being set up with a primary ___ provider named Dr ___ will help to ensure that you are doing well and will help to facilitate some of the testing and follow up that you need.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a history of DM, asthma 2 weeks s/p recent prednisone taper, on home oxygen chronically, who presented with 1 week DOE and wheezing. Presented to ___ clinic and found to be in mild/mod distress so she was sent to ___ ED. Symptoms seemed triggered by preceding URI and allergy flare. Given continued low peak flow obtained in the ED and smoking history, there was high suspicion for COPD component to symptoms. Patient was treated empirically with duonebs, initially at q4h and spaced as tolerated. She received a prednisone burst along with Mg, with the intention of continuing a regimen for 5 days of prednisone 40 mg qd. She was also empirically given azithromycin 250 mg qd with the intention of completing a 4 day course. Her peak flows at discharge was 300 mL (from 125) and ambulatory sats were measured at time of discharge was 90% on room air. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - recommend outpatient follow-up with orthopedics for knee pain thought to be osteoarthritis - complete prednisone and azithromycin courses - patient discharged on new medication cetirizine and ketotifen eye drops for allergy symptom control # Code: FULL (confirmed w/ pt) # Emergency Contact: ___ (daughter) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were found to have significant difficulty breathing and wheezing in the setting of a recent viral illness and possible triggers by allergies. You were evaluated with bloodwork and imaging and it was discovered that you most likely did not have a pneumonia or significant infection, but rather an exacerbation of your baseline asthma. You were determined not to have an acute infection that was linked to your knee pain. You were treated with nebulizers and steroid medication, after which your breathing continued to improve. Because of the renewed wheezing symptoms you are experiencing, we will have you complete a short 5-day course of steroids following discharge from the hospital. To further better control of your baseline allergies, we have also provided an allergy medication as well as eye drops for you to take daily along with your Flonase.

She received benzonatate and codeine-guiafenesin as needed for cough. # GERD: stable, continued ranitidine 300mg daily # Constipation: stable. continued senna bid prn. # Supplements: continued ascorbic acid, multivitamin, vitamin D # Primary prevention: continued ASA 81mg daily TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # Ms. ___ was admitted for asthma exacerbation, and was treated with a total 7-day course of steroid therapy (prednisone 40mg daily ending ___, 5-day course of azithromycin (ending ___, and her nebulizers, and advair. summary: You were admitted for asthma exacerbation, and were treated with nebulizer treatments, steroids (oral prednisone), antibiotics (azithromycin), and cough medications (guaifenisin, benzonatate). Your breathing improved, and at the time of discharge you had some cough and mild wheezing but no shortness of breath. Please take your albuterol and ipratropium nebulizers three times a day, up to every 4 hours as needed for shortness of breath. Please continue to take prednisone 40mg daily until ___. Please continue to take azithromycin 250mg daily until ___. Your chest x-ray showed a small round nodule in the right upper lung.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with a history of asthma requiring intubation in the past who presented with an asthma exacerbation. ACUTE ISSUES #) Asthma Exacerbation: Though the trigger for this exacerbation is unclear, sore throat and cough on the day prior to admission suggest a possible viral trigger although no other symptoms. Leukocytosis present, but no fevers, and no localizing signs for a bacterial infection. Patient also reports allergies to dogs and cats, both of which live in the house with her. In addition, she smokes ___ cigarettes per day. Her asthma is poorly controlled at baseline, as she requires her inhaler daily and she has not been on a daily controller medication due to lack of coverage by Mass Health. In the ICU, she was started on albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers q4h standing plus q2h PRN. Her peak flows continued to improve and she was continued on prednisone 60mg daily. She was started on fluticasone here as a long term controller medication. The Pulmonary team will set up a follow-up appointment for outpatient management. Given her splenectomy and asthma history, pneumovax was given on the floor. Once transferred to the floor, she continued to improve with albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers q6h standing as well as her fluticasone inhaler and oral steroids. On the day of discharge the patient was anxious to get home to take care of her son at home. She had improved wheeze, and good oxygen saturations while on room air. She was instructed that she needs close follow up in our discharge clinic to ensure that she continues to improve as an outpatient. She was instructed in how to use her fluticasone inhaler and the importance of using it daily as a control medication and she understands. She will be given a steroid taper to complete as an outpatient. # Hypertension: Systolic blood pressures to the 170s-180s persistently while in the ICU. She is not on any anti-hypertensives at home. She was started on a low dose of captopril for better control, which was increased when she was transferred to the floor. Initially it was felt hr hypertension was due to around the clock albuterol administration as well as a high steroid load administered in the emergency department exerting mineralocorticoid activity. However, at the time of discharge she remained hypertensive and the decision was made to initiate daily HCTZ for blood pressure control. This should be followed up as an outpatient in our discharge clinic as she may need this medication discontinued should her blood pressure return to baseline when not on albuterol so frequently. # ___ abuse, marijuana abuse: The patient was counseled on the importance of smoking cessation to her overall health and to decreasing her asthma symptoms. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES The patient is to follow up in our discharge clinic for further evaluation if her HCTZ should be continued as an anti-hypertensive medication. If plan to continue - should have BMP in ___ weeks. The patient is to follow up with Pulmonology for further input into managing her asthma symptoms, but she should begin using her fluticasone inhaler daily for baseline asthma control and continue her steroid taper
summary: As you know, you were admitted for an asthma exacerbation. Given how wheezy you were in the emergency department and your history of severe exacerbations in the past requiring a breathing tube, you were initially admitted to our ICU for around the clock breathing treatments, steroids, and monitoring. You never required supplemental oxygen. You improved quickly, and were moved to a medical floor. There we continued your breathing treatments and oral steroids. During your hospital stay your blood pressure was elevated. Because of this, we will start a medication to treat high blood pressure. It is unclear if your high blood pressure is a temporary issue that may resolved with time once your acute illness has subsided, or if you will need ongoing treatment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ F w/ HTN, knee OA s/p right knee replacement ___ who presents from rehab with fatigue, malaise and found to have hypokalemia and hyponatremia. #HYPOTONIC HYPONATREMIA: Mrs. ___ presented with vague fatigue, malaise, and slight dizziness for several days in the setting of low solute intake, increased free water intake ___ pitchers/day), diuretic use, and diarrhea for the past 2 weeks. Given her euvolemic physical exam and serum osmolarity of 252 with urine osmolarity 129, the etiology was thought to be most likely dehydration. Her home HCTZ/spirinolactone was held and her Na was monitored frequently. Her Na improved from 119 on admission to within normal limits at discharge, and her symptoms resolved. Na on discharge was 133. #HYPOKALEMIA: The likely etiology of her low potassium was thought to be a combination of her HCTZ, diarrhea (on standing laxatives), poor PO intake. During this admission her K was repleted. Her diuretics and laxatives were held. Her K improved from 3.2 on admission to within normal limits at discharge. K on discharge was 5.2 #R KNEE SWELLING, WARMTH, TENDERNESS: She received a total R knee replacement on ___ and complained of right knee swelling, warmth, and tenderness, but no fluctuance or purulent discharge was noted on exam. She remained afebrile with no leukocytosis. She was evaluated by Ortho who thought that the appearance of her knee was consistent with a post-op cellulitis. She received 2 doses of IV Ancef while in house with improvement to her knee, and was discharged on PO Keflex and Doxycycline with a plan for 10 days (STOP ___. She should have repeat CRP on ___, if increasing needs a further infectious disease workup of her knee wound. She received a R knee Xray that showed hardware in the appropriate place. STABLE ISSUES: Her chronic issues of hypertension, GERD, and asthma remained stable during this admission and she was continued on her home omeprazole and inhalers PRN. Her home HCTZ/spironolactone were held due to her hyponatremia and hypokalemia. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== - We have NOT restarted her home HCTZ given her admission for hypokalemia. She should follow up with her PCP regarding optimal blood pressure management. - We have NOT restarted her home potassium 40mEq daily on discharge since we have discontinued her HCTZ. - We have NOT restarted her standing laxatives given that they may have been contributing to her hypokalemia. - We have discharged her on a 7 day course of Keflex and doxycycline. - We have discharged her on 2.5mg oxycodone PRN for pain control. - She will need blood draws twice a week for the next two weeks to monitor her electrolytes. - She should follow up with Dr. ___ in Orthopedics ___ Clinic on ___ for staple removal #CODE STATUS: Full (confirmed) #CONTACT: Son ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Acetaminophen 1000 mg PO Q8H 2. albuterol sulfate 90 mcg/actuation inhalation Q4H:PRN SOB, wheeze 3. EPINEPHrine (EpiPEN) 0.3 mg IM ONCE MR1 bee sting 4. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID 5. Fluticasone Propionate NASAL 2 SPRY NU DAILY 6. Hydrochlorothiazide 12.5 mg PO BID 7. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY 8. Spironolactone 12.5 mg PO BID 9. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 10. Senna 17.2 mg PO BID 11. Enoxaparin Sodium 40 mg SC DAILY 12. OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN Pain
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were experiencing some weakness and tiredness, and you were found to have low sodium and low potassium. During your admission we monitored your blood electrolytes and treated you for your low sodium and potassium, and your blood levels rose back to normal. We also held your diuretics and laxatives, which may have contributed to your low sodium and potassium levels. During your stay you were evaluated by our Orthopedics team (bone doctors), who felt that the swelling in your right knee was due to a skin infection (cellulitis). You were given some IV antibiotics and discharged on oral antibiotics. You should complete your course of antibiotics at rehab.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female w cardiac multiple risk factors (DM, HLD, HTN) presented from OSH with vague chest pain and shortness of breath accompanied by elevated TroponinI to 0.052, unchanged EKG, transfered here for further eval #Chest Pain. The patient was transferred from ___ with exertional chest pain, increasing in frequency, and an insignificant elevation in TropI at OSH. On arrival here, the patient was chest pain free with no EKG changes noted from prior. Her first CE was negative. The team thought her presentation was unlikely consistent with ACS, but given her risk factors was concerned about a cardiac origin of her pain. She was continued on ASA, metoprolol, diltiazem, valsartan. Her CE came back negx3. She underwent a nuclear perfusion study that demonstrated no EKG changes and two ___ perfusion defects. After Discussion with the family, it was decided that surgical interventions were not warranted. It was thought that her afib could be contributing to demand ischemia--her diltiazem dosing was increased for better rate control. She was also started on isosorbide dinitrate. . #Shortness of Breath. Patient reported subacute episodes of shortness of breath on presentation, described as non-exertional. Originally it was unclear if this was related to her episodes of chest pain. However, on HD#2, she had an episode of shortness of breath. During the episode she had poor air movement and was tachycardic. Her symptoms resolved with lasix and albuterol/ipratropium nebulizers. Her CXR did not demonstrate congestive signs c/w flash pulmonary edema. After discussion with her PCP, it was thought that poor asthma control could be responsible for these episodes perhaps complicated by fluid overload. She was started on standing inhalers, and discharged with prescriptions for a home nebulizer, scripts for albuterol and ipratropium, and advair. Her daily lasix dose was increased to 40mg daily. She was instructed to follow-up with her PCP regarding this issue. . # Chronic Afib on coumadin: INR on admision was 2.0. Given original concern that she would need an intervention, coumadin was held on admission. Coumadin was restarted at 4mg daily. . # DM: Her glyburide was held on admission and she was maintained on SSI. On discharge she was restarted on the glyburide. . #Hypothyroid - TSH on admission 0.68. She was continued Levothyroxine Sodium 112 mcg PO/NG DAILY. Patient was DNR, but OK to intubate for the duration of this admission, as confirmed with patient's granddaughter and HCP ___ ___.
summary: You were transferred here from ___ ___ for work-up of your shortness of breath and chest pain. Your cardiac enzymes and electrocardiogram were unchanged. Your stress test showed ___ decreases in blood flow to your heart, but after discussion with you and your family, decided that they should be medically managed. You were also noted to have increased heart rate in the setting of your atrial fibrillation. This may have contributed to your shortness of breath. We increased your diltiazem to help slow your heart rate down. In addition we also increased medications for asthma (advair and an albuterol/ipratropium nebulizer), as this may have been playing a role in your shortness of breath as well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with a PMHx of Crohn's disease on Remicade every 6 weeks, prednisone, and recently started on ___, and asthma presenting with c/o fever and URI symptoms, with cough, improved with steroids and antibiotic therapy. ACUTE ISSUES: ================== # Sepsis due to URI: Patient met multiple SIRS criteria on admission, unclear source at this time although given cough/congestion suspect viral versus bacterial URI, with sinus tenderness, ear fullness, and clear CXR, however lower resp infection also on ddx given immunosuppressed state. Given immunosuppressed status and recent hospitalization initially covered broadly for HCAP/CAP with vanc/cefepime/azithro, as well as tamsulosin for possible influenza, and supported with IVF. She was also started on stress dose steroids with hydrocortisone given chronic steroid use (for Crohns Disease, see below). Patient's tachycardia/hypotension has responded to IVF as well as stress steroids. By HD2, symptoms had largely resolved. Micro data ultimately growing aspergillus from sputum, negative rapid screen for influenza. ID was consulted, and felt aspergillus was a contaminant, and felt comfortable sending home to complete short 5 day course of antibiotics, so was transitioned to monotherapy with PO azithromycin (vanc/cefepime/tamsulosin stopped) and patient was discharged with one further dose to complete the 5 day course. Her steroids were successfully tapered back to her home dose of 6cc prednisolone (15mg/5cc solution) by the time of her discharge, and her pressures remained stable. # Hypotension: Initially presented with hypotension to SBP ___ the ___, likely due to an infection, though given chronic steroid use can't rule out element of adrenal insufficiency contributing. Resolved with stress dose steroids (hydrocortisone) and fluids, as well as treatment on antibiotics for presumed respiratory source. Ultimately tapered back to her home dose of prednisolone, and her blood pressures continued stable. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================== # Asthma: Patient with intermittent wheezing on exam on admission, did not appear ___ distress and not hypoxic, breathing comfortably on RA throughout her hospitalization. Following treatment of presumed respiratory infection as well as albuterol treatments, her wheezing resolved. # Crohn's: Presentation not consistent with an acute flare of her Crohns Disease. ___ discussion with her outpatient GI physician, her ___ was stopped given concern for active infection. She was given stress dose steroids with hydrocortisone as discussed above, and ultimately tapered back down to her home dose of prednisolone. She was instructed to continue her home dose of 6cc prednisolone (15mg/5cc solution) without tapering, and to continued off the ___ until her next outpatient appointment with Dr. ___. She was continued on PCP prophylaxis with ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== #Patient will take one more dose of azithromycin 500mg on ___ ___ was discontinued this admission per outpatient GI physician ___ remained off medication on discharge; will remain off ___ until follow up with Dr. ___ to determine when to restart. #Will continue 18mg prednisolone without further tapering while off ___ until follow up with Dr. ___ #Emergency Contact: ___ Relationship: Husband Phone number: ___ #Code status: FULL
summary: You came ___ with a fever and cough, with sinus pressure and congestion, ___ the setting of chronic immunosuppressive therapy for your Crohns disease. You were treated with some fluids and antibiotics, and we got an x-ray of your chest to look for deeper lung infection which was clear. Your symptoms improved with the fluids and antibiotics, and we transitioned you over to oral antibiotics. The infectious disesaes team came to see you while you were here because your sputum culture showed a fungus called aspergillus, however they agreed that this was only colonization ___ the respiratory tract, and not an active infectious process causing your symptoms.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== #Patient will take one more dose of azithromycin 500mg on ___ ___ was discontinued this admission per outpatient GI physician ___ remained off medication on discharge; will remain off ___ until follow up with Dr. ___ to determine when to restart. #Will continue 18mg prednisolone without further tapering while off ___ until follow up with Dr. ___ #Emergency Contact: ___ Relationship: Husband Phone number: ___ #Code status: FULL

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT/PLAN: ___ yo male history of hyperlipidemia, gastritis, hypertension, anxiety, alcohol abuse, presenting with acute shortness of breath with hypoxemia evidence on ABG now improved on supplemental O2 and heparin gtt. #Hypoxia: Patient presented with new hypoxia with repeat V/Q scan showing v/q mismatch in RLL c/f PE. He underwent non-con CT which is not protocoled for PE, but given the findings, radiology was highly suspicious for PE. Heme/onc was consulted as pt reported that he had genetic susceptibility testing the reports that he had abnormal metabolism of warfarin. Per heme/onc he was started on lovenox with anti-Xa levels to be monitored as an outpatient. He also had expiratory wheezing which improved with nebs c/f obstructive airway disease. He underwent PFTs which showed obstructive pattern that is likely more chronic that patient realized per Pulm. He was discharged on albuterol and advair with pulmonary follow-up as an outpatient. #Troponin Leak: Likely cardiac strain. CK-MB remains flat suggesting against NSTEMI, could be ___ to RHS in setting of PE. In addition, given chronic ___ disease, troponin elevated will persist longer. His troponins have trended down even with his CKD. His PE was managed as above. He will follow-up with pulmonary and cardiology as an outpatient. #CKD: Chronic stage III. US of kidneys not suggestive of obstruction or parenchymal disease. Recent UA bland. SPEP/UPEP were unremarkable. All meds were renally dosed and creatinine was monitored daily. #Alcohol Abuse: Reportedly drinks heavily per family members. No history of acute withdrawal or seizures, although did get defensive when discussing alcohol use. His recent epidoses of diaphoresis, tachypnea and hypertension may be related to alcohol withdrawal given that his last drink was now 48-72 hours ago. He was started on a CIWA and treated with diazepam for withdrawal symptoms. After 72 hours he was transitioned back to his home clonazepam. SW consultation was offered given the patient's need to be on systemic AC for PE as above, but patient declined. He prefers to speak with his personal acquantainces regarding alcohol cessation. #HTN: continued chlorthalidone, atenolol was changed to metoprolol given CKD. His reactive airway disease remained stable on Beta blockade while in the hospital. #GERD: continue omeprazole #Anxiety: continued home citalopram and clonazepam after cessation of CIWA scale as outlined above # Code: Full (discussed with patient) Wife ___ ___ Son ___ MD ___ TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient should f/u with heme/onc and pulm as outpt for further management of PE and Obstructive lung disease. - Patient should be sure to have age-appropriate cancer screening, as unclear etiology for his PE
summary: you were admitted to ___ with complaints of chest pain. You were found to have a blood clot in one of the arteries in your lungs and will need to be on blood thinners for at least 3 months. You were also diagnosed with obstructive lung disease (asthma) and will be discharged on two new inhalers. It is imperative that you not drink alcohol while on blood thinners as falls can increase your risk of stroke or life threatening bleeding. Additionally, you mentioned you take Klonipin/aka Clonazepam for anxiety. These medications can make you sedated and greatly increase your risk of falls and should be used with extreme caution. Please note, we changed your medication ATENOLOL to METOPROLOL as METOPROLOL is better metabolized in patients with ___ diseas rather than ATENOLOL.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman w/PMHx blindness, ___ ___, hypothyroidism, reactive airway disease presented to the ED today with productive cough p/w worsening symptoms, increased fatigue, malaise, weakness. # Reactive airway disease # Probable bronchospasm related to recent virus. Patient completed 10 day course of levaquin for bronchitis. Patient has a history of reactive airway disease. Recently saw PCP and was prescribed fluticasone and albuterol for treatment which she has not used. On exam, lungs sound clear, mild crackles at base, no wheezing and moving air well, no signs of tachypnea or respiratory distress. CXR negative for infection. She was treated symptomatically with cough suppressants and nebulizer treatments. She was given fluticasone/salmeterol BID in place of her home inhaler, which was non-formulary. # Systolic Murmur: Subtle, likely secondary to mild aortic stenosis. Echo shows mild MR. #A Fib: EKG in ER shows new A Fib, repeat and 24hrs on telemetry all NSR. Echo without evidence of significant valvular disease. Low CHADS and history of subdural as well as blindness and questions about safe, independent home living make anticoagulation risky relative to odds of stroke. Recommendation was discussed with the patient, who was in agreement. # Failure to thrive: Patient with increasing difficulties at her assisted living facility, has not taken her medications for 3 weeks, presented to urgent care without pants, is blind at baseline, started small fire in her apartment. Physical therapy recommended rehab. # Paranoid delusions: Patient with history of paranoid delusions, thinks people are breaking into her apartment, changes her locks and locks herself into rooms. Patient follows with a psychiatrist Dr. ___ of ___ and was prescribed 2 mg Haldol daily, however patient has not taken her medications in 3 weeks and her delusions have worsened per daughter without the ___. She was intermittently compliant with medication in house. Her outpatient psychiatrist was contacted and expressed similar concerns. He will work with the rehab team in continuing this assessment and working with the patient and family to determine whether it will ultimately be possible for the patient to safely transfer back to her independent living situation. # Blindness: she continues to have release symptoms from her blindness from macular degeneration, sees a white donut shape like figure in the left side, which is nearly constant. # Hypothyroidism: TSH 1.0 wnl. home dose of levothyroxine was continued # Osteoporosis/prolonged bisphosphonate therapy: previously treated with bisphosonates, no treatment for several years. # H/O SDH ___ c/b seizure activity: traumatic subdural hematoma with resultant probable focal seizures, previously on keppra, tapered off at the end of ___ given EEG was negative for seizure activity TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: #Continue discussion of risks and benefits of anticoagulation with paroxysmal a fib #Continue to assess patient's ability to safely transition home with patient and family. If patient cannot safely transition home, family may need to seek guardianship. #Monitor medication compliance, patient frequently declines meds, particularly her psychiatric prescriptions. #Consider head imaging, given history of falls and intracranial hemorrhage, if etiology of declining mental status is in question
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of fatigue following a recent bout of pneumonia. We did not find any evidence of another infection or for inadequate treatment of your pneumonia. You improved significantly and felt very well by your second day in the hospital We had our physical therapists evaluate you and they felt that you would benefit from rehabilitation to improve your strength and balance. We also found that you had atrial fibrillation when you were in the emergency room. As we discussed, this is an abnormal heart rhythm, that people can go in and out of and that increases your risk of stroke. We discussed starting a blood thinner and felt that at the present time your risk of a bleeding complication from a blood thinner outweighs your risk of stroke. You should continue this conversation with your outpatient doctors.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of asthma and anemia, who presented with one week of increasing cough, shortness of breath, and wheezing, and was admitted for management of an asthma attack. CXR negative for acute pulmonary process. She was treated with Albuterol and Ipratroprium nebulizers, with significant improvement, and will continue albuterol at home. She was given a prednisone 40 mg PO daily (d1 = ___, d7 = ___, and will been seen in follow up for further management if necessary. Symptomatically, the patient has mild persistent asthma in cooler months and moderate persistent asthma in the warmer months. Thus, she was initiated on fluticasone 110 mcg 2 puffs BID. She was also given a peak flow meter and an asthma action plan to further guide her care. # Asthma Exacerbation: ___ y.o F with hx of asthma, presenting with asthma exacerbation, likely exacerbated by URI symptoms x 1 week. CXR negative. Based on her symptoms, the patient has mild persistent asthma in the cooler months (symptoms 2 or more times per week, nighttime awakening ___ times/month, and Albuterol use greater than 2 times/week). She has moderate to severe persistent asthma in the warmer months (daily symptoms, nighttime awakenings daily, Albuterol use 3 times/day, and severe interference with daily activity). She was treated with duonebs and initiated on prednisone with a plan for a 7-day burst. She was initiated on inhaled fluticasone and given education about an asthma action plan to further guide her care. # Anemia: Admission Hb of 11.8, stable from a baseline of ~10. Normocytic. Iron studies unremarkable, although patient takes iron at home. Peripheral smear when reviewed by the medicine team significant for microspherocytes and teardrop cells, with previously Hb electrophoresis significant for Hb A2. ___ represent B thalassemia trait. Remained stable during this admission. Transitional Issues: - Patient will need to make an appointment at ___ ___ (___) for follow up for the week of ___. - Continue prednisone 40 mg daily (d1 = ___, d7 = ___, to be seen in follow up to determine if further taper necessary - Initiated on inhaled fluticasone 110mcg 2 puffs bid this admission, will need to taper down if symptoms are controlled - Peripheral smear by medicine team review showed microspherocytes, tear drop cells, target cells, and previously Hb electrophoresis was significant for Hb A2 (not quantified due to normal MCV), taken together this is concerning for B thalassemia trait/minor. Iron studies normal (pt currently on prenatal vitamin with iron) further workup deferred to PCP. Anemia very mild this admission at baseline, with normal MCV.
summary: You came in with one week of increasing cough, shortness of breath, and wheezing, and were admitted for management of an asthma attack. We treated you with nebulizers for your breathing. We also put you on an oral steroid called Prednisone. Your last dose will be on ___. We prescribed you a new inhaled steroid called Fluticasone. This will help with long-term control of your asthma. You should take 2 puffs in the morning and 2 puffs at night every day even if you do not have symptoms (see your attached asthma action plan) We also prescribed you a peak flow meter to monitor your asthma. You should use this once per day, and during attacks. You can use your Asthma Action Plan worksheet to guide you in which inhalers to use.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a pleasant ___ female with severe asthma, diabetes on oral hypoglycemics, hypertension, osteoarthritis, illiteracy, who was sent to the ___ by her PCP by phone evaluation for continued and worsened shortness of breath and wheezing. She complained also of a mildly productive cough with white sputum, had poor air movement and was treated for asthma exacerbation. #. Asthma Exacerbation: Patient has history of frequent ___ visits and hospitalizations for asthma exacerbation, particularly in the ___ and ___ time. She reports no changes in home or work environment, no smoke exposure, and no changes in detergents or soaps at home that could cause allergen induced exacerbation. This exacerbation was likely triggered by a viral upper respiratory infection, as she was having upper respiratory symptoms. She was treated with azithromycin for possible pneumonia by the ___ the weekend prior to admission, but her chest Xray in the ___ ___ showed no signs of pneumonia. She was not started on any antibiotics during this hospitalization. The patient was treated with combivent nebulizers in the ___ then started on Q4 hours standing albuterol nebulizers on the floor overnight. She was also started on 60mg of prednisone for 5 days, to be tapered down as an outpatient. She was continued on her home medications of Advair and montelukast. A sputum culture could not be sent due to poor quality of specimen. She was ruled out for flu with negative DFA from nasopharyngeal swab and low suspicion. The following description represents the course of her steroid taper: Prednisone 10 mg Tablets as directed: ___ 6 tablets until ___ (total of 60mg) 5 tablets on ___, 4 tablets on ___, 3 tablets on ___, 2 tablets on ___, 1 tablet on ___, then off. In the ___, the patient's peak flow improved from 150 to 180 after nebulizer treatment. Her peak flow on discharge was 200, and she was ambulating with O2 saturation of 95% without shortness of breath. She was encouraged to follow her peak flow at home and seek medical attention more quickly if it starts to drop. She will continue home nebulizer treatments as needed but should return if she is requiring nebulizers more often than every four to six hours. She was set up with a visiting nurse to help monitor her improvement and be compliant with treatments. Because she is illiterate, the patient may need repeated asthma education in the outpatient setting to help reinforce an Asthma Action plan specific to her needs. It may help to label her home peak flow meter with red, yellow, and green stickers to remind her when to start using her PRN medications and when to get help. . #. Diabetes Mellitus Type 2: Patient's home regimen includes glyburide and metformin; she states that her sugars typically run in the 170s at home. Her last HbA1c in ___ was 6.9. Her metformin was held initially, so in the setting of prednisone therapy for the asthma exacerbation, her blood sugars were significantly elevated into the 300s on the first day of hospitalization. Upon restarting the metformin, her blood sugars were still in the 200s. She was sent home on an insulin sliding scale to be used for blood sugars >200 just during the brief course of prednisone therapy with the help of the ___. Ms. ___ was set up with a visiting nurse to help with insulin teaching and administration and to ensure compliance with fingersticks. The patient has had to self-administer insulin in the past during courses of steroid therapy; she also regularly checks her blood sugars at home ___ times daily. . #. Hypertension: Her blood pressure was controlled on her home regimen of valsartan and HCTZ. . #. Vaginal Yeast Infection: Patient has recurrent vaginal yeast infections. She was given pads to keep her underwear dry after urination. She was continued on clotrimazole cream applied daily to vaginal area. . . Ms. ___ was kept on a diabetic diet during this hospitalization. She was given subcutaneous heparin for DVT prophylaxis.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having another asthma exacerbation. You were started on prednisone therapy to help you decrease the inflammation in your lungs. You should continue your nebulizers at home as needed to help improve your breathing. Please be sure to check your peak flow every day to make sure it is not getting worse (just prior to leaving the hospital it was 195). After starting the prednisone, your blood sugars were more elevated than usual and harder to control. Please continue to check your blood sugars at home every day ___ times per day. The home visiting nurse ___ help give you some insulin if your blood sugars are very high. Continue to use your nebulizer treatment up to 4 times a day as needed.
