text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of asthma and pulmonary embolism and with worsening shortness of breath and cough consistent with acute on chronic asthma exacerbation. On the day of discharge, the patient felt well and wanted to go home. We discussed her history, current situation, medications, and ___ plan, her nurse was present for this conversation. We worked through all of the patient's questions. She is looking forward to going home. # Acute on chronic asthma exacerbation # Acute community-acquired pneumonia # Acute Hypoxemic Respiratory Failure: Admitted with new O2 requirement. Patient with cough and CXR with basilar infiltrates possibly suggestive of pneumonia. She was treated with steroids, and broad spectrum antibiotics in the ED. On the floor, she was given prednisone, levofloxacin, standing nebs and her condition improved. She was weaned from oxygen with improvement in her subjective dyspnea, and peak flows. She ambulated without difficult or desaturation. Her peak flow was still suboptimal on discharge (150), however she overall had clinically improved, and we felt comfortable with her going home. With further discussion on discharge, we elicited that this was her third or fourth admission for asthma exacerbation this year. One at ___, and then a couple associated with viral infections. She was under the impression that inhaled steroids would lead to significant adverse effects such as weight gain and other complications. I explained how oral steroids can lead to these complications but that the amount of inhaled steroid that makes it into the body is much less, and that I would recommend she use an inhaled steroid to prevent future asthma exacerbations. After working through her concerns, I learned that she had been previously prescribed fluticasone inhaled by her primary care's office, and I reviewed the records and see that on ___ she was given fluticasone 220 mcg twice daily. However she states that she has not been using this medication, for fear of the side effects. She indicated that she would now start taking this, and did not need a prescription for it. I encouraged her to ___ closely with her PCP, which she is already planning, and suggested that a referral to a pulmonologist may be useful at some point, she agrees and will explore this with her PCP. # Pulmonary embolism: # Supratherapeutic INR: Prior PE was provoked in the setting of active malignancy and cancer treatment, currently on warfarin. Admitted at 4.3 and warfarin was held until back in normal range. It was 2.4 on discharge. Given that she had not received warfarin at all for several days (so the ___ INR is likely to be lower), and is only receiving 2 more days of steroids and antibiotics, I encouraged her to use just 10mg daily of warfarin until reassessed by her ___ clinic, which she says she will contact tomorrow, ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Warfarin management - PCP ___, consideration of referral to Pulmonary [x] The patient is safe to discharge today, and I spent [ ] <30min; [x] >30min in discharge day management services. ___, MD ___ Pager ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were short of breath. You were found to have an asthma exacerbation, possibly triggered by a pneumonia. You were treated with nebulizers, steroids, and antibiotics and your condition improved. After you leave the hospital, you will need to take several additional days of steroids and antibiotics to fully treat your condition. Additionally, after you leave the hospital you will be started on a new medication to help prevent recurrent attacks in the future. Please speak to your primary care doctor about having a pulmonary function test after you leave the hospital.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Warfarin management - PCP ___, consideration of referral to Pulmonary [x] The patient is safe to discharge today, and I spent [ ] <30min; [x] >30min in discharge day management services. ___, MD ___ Pager ___

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ w/h/o asthma(intubations as a child), anaphylaxis to contrast and endometriosis who presents to the ED with ongoing shortness of breath despite outpatient asthma treatment, pleuritic chest pain worsening with leaning forward, and viral prodrome as well as elevated CRP to 70 c/f pericarditis. # Asthma exacerbation w/ acute bronchitis # CMV infection # Costochondritis # + ___ (1:320) Patient was admitted due to dyspnea and CP after a viral prodrome and concern for asthma exacerbation. She had several courses of prednisone without improvement, so she was admitted. Steroids were stopped on admission due to initially low suspicion for asthma exacerbation but she was continued on standing nebulizer treatments and standing cough suppressants. Initially there was concern for a PE so she was briefly empirically treated with heparin, but V/Q lung scan showed very low probability of PE (she has anaphylaxis to contrast). CT chest w/o contrast showed probable bronchitis and one GGO thought likely attributable to her asthma but no other pulmonary findings. Given her young age and pleuritic pain, ___ was checked and was positive, with titer of 1:320. Complement levels were not low. EBV/CMV serologies showed POSITIVE CMV IgM and IgG and positive IgG EBV with negative IgM. Flu and respiratory viral panel were negative. Due to her complaints of pleuritic-type chest pain worse with leaning forward; CRP elevated to >70 and some (non-classical and non-specific) EKG changes, there was some suspicion for pericarditis and she was treated with toradol q6h and tramadol. She was then transitioned to ibuprofen and colchicine. However, TTE did not show a pericardial effusion or other concerning findings. Rheumatology was consulted and ultimately felt pericarditis was unlikely, that her chest wall pain was more consistent with costochondritis (which I agree with given the tenderness to light palpation of the chest wall), that the elevated CRP could be attributed to viral infection (i.e. CMV), and ___ was nonspecific in setting of no joint or skin findings to suggest a systemic rheumatologic disease as the cause of her presenting illness. Several of the more specific rheumatologic studies (dsDNA, anti-Scl, anti-Centromere) were ordered but results were still pending at the time of discharge. The ___ Rheumatology team will f/u with her on discharge in the next 2 weeks to review the results of the pending Rheum labs. Clinically she was improving over 72 hours prior to discharge and overall picture now seems to be consistent with an asthma exacerbation with SOB and severe cough, leading to chostochondritis as the etiology for her chest pain. Perhaps CMV respiratory infection (IgM+) was the triggering event. Not a lot of compelling evidence for pericarditis at this point given lack of effusion on TTE, audible rub on serial exams, or typical EKG changes for pericarditis on serial EKGs. Pleuritis also seems unlikely given the reproducible nature of the pain with light palpation of the ribs & anterior chest. With that in mind, Rheumatology advised switching from colchicine + ibuprofen to indomethacin for costochondritis, which we did on ___. On the day of discharge she was feeling much improved in terms of SOB, DOE, cough, and chest wall pain. Her ambulatory O2 sats the day prior to discharge were 89% while ambulating on RA and she was fairly symptomatic with dyspnea and lightheadedness. Today, however, her ambulatory O2 sats were normal at 96-98% and she was significantly less symptomatic during prolonged walking. Her chest wall pain also improving over past 48 hours and notably since starting indomethacin she is no longer requiring opioid pain meds (i.e. tramadol) for adequate pain control. She was discharged on duoneb taper (as she was still on QID standing nebs prior to discharge), with plans to follow up with her PCP at upcoming appointment on ___, as well as with ___ Pulmonology for PFTs and an appointment on ___.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for problems breathing and chest pain. You had several tests that showed that you do not have a blood clot, and your heart is working well. You were diagnosed with asthma exacerbation that we think was most likely triggered by a recent CMV (cytomegalovirus) infection. You gradually improved and you are being discharged on a nebulizer taper. Please plan to get yourself a new peak-flow meter. You are scheduled to follow up in ___ clinic in early ___ as noted below. In terms of your return to activity, please increase your activity level as tolerated, it is reasonable to expect that it will take a couple more days for you to be feeling back to your normal self. The pain in your chest seems most likely to have been caused by inflammation of the cartilage of the ribcage (costochondritis), which was treated with anti-inflammatory medicines. You are being discharged on a relatively short course of indomethacin (and anti-inflammatory medication) plus Tylenol (a pain medication). While you are taking indomethacin, you can take pantoprazole ("Protonix") to help protect your stomach. You were evaluated during this hospitalization by the Rheumatology doctors, and several rheumatology lab results are still pending at this time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ yo G9P1 with T2DM and asthma admitted at 23w2d for asthma exacerbation. She was transferred from the ___ to the ___ for further management given she is pregnant. On arrival, her shortness of breath had improved significantly. Her oxygen saturation was 98% on room air and peak flow was 240. She was continued on Duonebs and admitted to the antepartum service for further management. She was quickly transitioned back to her Advair and Albuterol inhalers and continued to do well. Her oxygen saturation remained 98-99% and peak flow improved to 275. She did not receive any further steroids. . In regards to her Type 2 diabetes, her fingersticks were elevated during this admission. This was due to her Metformin being held for 48 hours (since she had a CT scan with contrast) and the one-time dose of IV Solumedrol. Her 1 hour postprandial fingersticks were up to the low 200s and were covered with a Humalog sliding scale. On hospital day #3, she was restarted on the Metformin. Her fingersticks were improved significantly at the time of discharge. . Given her previable gestational age, she was monitored with fetal heartrate checks every shift. . Ms ___ was discharged home in stable condition on ___. She has an appointment scheduled with her primary Ob/Gyn at ___ in 1 week (___). She was encouraged to call before this appointment if her fingersticks were persistently elevated or if she had any other concerns.
summary: you were admitted to the ___ service due to an asthma exacerbation. You received one dose of IV steroids at ___ as well as nebulizer treatments. Your asthma symptoms improved significantly and you were switched back to your usual Albuterol inhaler. It is important that you continue taking your Zyrtec as well as the Advair and Albuterol inhalers. In regards to your diabetes, your fingersticks were elevated here because you received the steroid and you stopped the metformin (for 48hrs) since you had a CT with contrast. It important that you continue a diabetic diet and check your fingersticks at home as you have been.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient presented to the ED on ___ with tachycardia and SOB. In the ED, she was given albuterol/ipratropium nebs, prednisone 60mg, and guaifenesin to treat a suspected acute asthma exacerbation. IVF were given to treat tachycardia which was likely from dehydration + large doses of albuterol with resolution of tachycardia. Peak flows were tested and found to be 150 prior to treatment and 190 afterwards. No baseline PFTs were available for comparison. On admission to the floor, an ECG was taken to rule out cardiac causes of the patient's symptoms and a CXR was taken to evaluate the focal wheezing in the RLL heard on exam. While non-suggestive of an acute pulmonary process, the CXR did show significant herniation of the stomach into the chest cavity which is a very interesting finding especially givent he fact that the patient has adult onset asthma (in last few years) and GERD with onset a few years ago. Although it was believed that pt ___ had an exacerbation from her allergies, we strongly recommend a barium swallow and/or endoscopy to determine the full extent of the hernia as well as a outpatient evalutation by surgery since this hernia may be responsible for the heartburn as well as worsening (or even causing) airway hyperreactivity. In addition, if this testing indicates the stomach is at risk for becoming incarcerated, surgical intervention is necessary by elective repair. Pt did very well and was even on RA once arriving to the floor. She was inctructed to start taking flovent twice daily until told to stop by an MD, to take 40mg of prednisone for 3 days, then stop, as part of a steroid burst, to only take albuterol as needed as a rescue medication and not standing (as she ahd been doing for a while), and to start taking omeprazole twice daily as microaspirations of acidic fluid as happens with GERD likely is exacerbating airway hyperreactivity. We also strongly recommended that she should have pulmonary function tests done after recovering from the acute exacerbation to document asthma and her baseline.
summary: You were admitted and treated for an asthma exacerbation. You should have pulmonary function tests done after recovering from the acute exacerbation. You likely had an exacerbation from your allergies although we also discovered that you have a gastroesophageal hernia. We strongly recommend a barium swallow and/or endoscopy to determine the full extent of the hernia as well as a outpatient evalutation by surgery since this hernia may be responsible for your heartburn as well as worsening (or even causing) your asthma. In addition, if this testing indicates the stomach is at risk for becoming incarcerated, surgical intervention is necessary by elective repair.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ y/o woman 9 weeks pregnant, who is admitted with acute onset of shortness of breath accompanied by dizziness and fatigue when working in her barn. She was noted to have desaturations with ambulation in the ED as well as to mildly tachycardic. On the floor, her dyspnea rapidly improved without treatment and she had no desaturations with ambulation. Her tachycardia with ambulation also improved. It is possible that with exposure to various allergens in the barn that she had some transient bronchospasm that was not appreciated and resolved. I gave her an albuterol inhaler for use tid for 3 days and then as needed. CT negative for PE, and a cardiac defect such as a shunt (PFO) would not cause such transient hypoxia. In regards to fatigue and lightheadedness, her Ucx is positive for GNR. She was treated with macrobid and given a prescription to take home for the next seven days. I will follow up on the final culture result. It is possible that this infection, coupled with her pregnancy and work in the barn led to her fatigue and lightheadedness, both of which had resolved by her arrival to the medical floor. She is eating and drinking well and had no documented hypotension. I called the patient on ___ - she was feeling better at home, and realized that she had sprayed her barn with pesticides to combat insects, and she is wondering if that triggered her shortness of breath, ? bronchospasam. She remains on the macrobid. Patient was told she needs a non urgent left breast ultrasound given the calcifications and ? of small soft tissue mass. Her breast exam was normal.
summary: You were admitted because you developed sudden shortness of breath, dizziness and fatigue yesterday. In our ER, your oxygen level was seen to drop when you walked and your heart race increased while walking also. You had a CT scan that showed NO evidence of pneumonia or blood clot. It did show some areas of calcium on your left breast for which your outpatient doctors ___ get ___ ultrasound. Just with observation your symptoms improved considerably and your oxygen level was normal (100%) with activity, and your heart rate increases were much less. We discussed that your symptoms may have been due to some bronchospasm in your lungs when you were in the barn, and that may be the fatigue and lightheadedness were from a UTI. Please continue to use the albuterol inhaler 3 times a day for the next few days and take the antibiotic macrobid for a week. I will followup on the urine culture results. WE will give you prescriptions, but I have sent your Rx to the ___ in Target in ___ electronically.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with PMH of asthma, CAD s/p stents, HTN, multiple UTI's, and OSA (not treated) who initially presented to ___ ___ on ___ with T 104.5, left sided neck pain, and hypotension. Started on ceftriaxone/azithromycin for PNA, found to have neisseria bacteremia in 1 blood culture on ___. Increased ceftriaxone dose to 2g Q12H on ___. Transferred to ___ ___ for further management of possible meningitis and respiratory management. # Meningococcal Meningitis: Since transfer pt afebrile, hemodynamically stable, mentating well. Finished course of ceftrixone last dose 2g IV on ___ in the AM for total 7 day course from first dose. Also finished 2 day course of rifampin PO 600 mg BID for nasal decolonization. Close contacts on prophylaxis. # PNA vs atelectasis on outside CXR: Pt initially started on ctx/azithro, ctx increased for meninigitis and azithro held on transfer out of less concern for pna. Repeat CXR shows atelectasis only, lungs CTAB throughout stay. # Asthma: Pt with cough variant asthma. No wheezing on exam but pt desats on ambulation to 88 on room air. Pt states she has been told she needs oxygen in the past but has not used it except while flying, which she is very concerned about. Plan for home oxygen and for flight home. # CAD s/p stents: Without symptoms. Continued aspirin, plavix, statin, beta blocker # HTN: Normotensive. Continued spironolactone without need for potassium supplementation. # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation- Found to be in a fib at OSH, converted to sinus with mag administration. CHADS2 score 2 (although borderline diabetes hx). Has been in sinus during stay here. Deferred to outpt ___. # Hyperglycemia at OSH: no hx of DM but has elevated a1c of 6.3 and pt states she has been offered metformin in the past but did not take it as her cardiologist told her not to. Was on sliding scale while in house. Transitional issue for PCP to ___ Transitional Issues: - ___ nodular opacities in right apex seen on CXR - Discussion about ___ for paroxysmal atrial fibrillation with PCP - ___ of elevated HgbA1c 6.3% - ___ CH50 level as screening for complement deficiency
summary: You were transferred for further management of neisseria meningitis. You were continued on the correct dose of antibiotics and completed the appropriate IV antibiotic course. You also took 2 days of oral meds to clear any colonization in your upper airway. All of your close contacts should take the prescribed prophylactic antibiotics to prevent infection. During your stay you were found to have episodes of low oxygen when walking around. You have been told in the past that you might benefit from oxygen at home and it was reordered while you were here. Of note, our radiologists commented on small nodularities on your right upper lung, which is likely from an old infection. This should be followed by a 3 month chest ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ presented with acute SOB c/w a asthma exacerbation. Upon arrival to floor, Ms. ___ was treated with Albuterol Nebs q2h prn and q6h standing, with ipratropium nebs standing q6h. She also received prednisone 60 mg to begin a prenisode taper following up on the steroid that she received yesterday in the ED. Finally, she was given two more doses of Azithromycin at 250 mg in a five day course of Azithromycin. Her symptoms improved significantly and finally she was breathing comfortably at 98% on RA. We counseled Ms. ___ on the importance of using her Flovent inhaler every day to prevent asthma exacerbations in the future- we changed that to be reflected in her discharge medication orders (it had previously been given to her on a prn basis.) We also reminded her of the importance of smoking cessation to prevent asthma exacerbations as well as other lung diseases.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for an asthma exacerbation. Your asthma probably got acutely worse as a result of two factors. First, you have had a cold- likely a viral or bacterial infection- over the last week. This could worsen your breathing. Second, you were exposed to allergens- including cat hair- that worsened your breathing. In the emergency room, you received nebulizers, magnesium and steroids. We treated your asthma in the hospitalwith Albuterol and Ipratroprium nebulizers. We also continued to give you Prednisone (steroids) and gave you a second dose of Azithromycin. You have now received 2 doses of Azithromycin and will have to take 3 more doses at home. It is very important that you focus on two things to prevent asthma attacks in the future. First, you should stop smoking. Smoking will lead to asthma attacks and worse lung diseases.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a new patient evaluation for TBM in a ___ female with multifactorial respiratory failure ___ asthma/OSA) with a history of Asthma, Sjogren's, RA, pseudotumor cerebri, A-fib (on rivaroxiban,) and poorly controlled DM s/p recent RIGHT toe amputation x2, now with significant tracheobronchomalacia on dynamic CT scan and laryngoscopy without paradoxical vocal cord movement s/p rigid bronchoscopy with 2 tracheal and bronchial stents placed on ___. #Tracheobronchomalacia ___ PMH of Multifactorial respiratory failure ___ OSA, TBM) with chronic tracheostomy not vent dependent, who was recently seen in clinic and had imaging which suggested trachial and bronchial malacia had progressed so had 2 tracheal stents and b/l bronchial stents placed ___. Given that patient has ahd respiratory arrest in the past s/p tracheal decannulation it was recommended that she be observed overnight. The patient was monitored overnight. She also underwent exchange of her tracheostomy to a cuffless portex 6.0 trach. She was discharged home with red cap to be on kept on except with neb treatments. Obtained PFTs/6minute walk test before discharge. summary: You came to us for post procedural monitoring after your flexible bronchoscopy with tracheal and bronchial stenting procedure performed by interventional pulmonology on ___. You were admitted to our floor in stable condition with improved respiratory status. You spiked a low grade fever on the night after your procedure. This is normal for post-respiratory stenting patients. We monitored your fever and it resolved on its own. Your tracheostomy valve was changed to a speaking valve during this admission and then a red cap trial which went well. You also underwent pulmonary function testing this admission. We continued your home regimen of drugs except for rivaroxaban. Per interventional pulmonology, we held this medication for the procedure. We will plan on continuing to hold this medication until your next stent removal procedure with interventional pulmonology on ___. We started you on aspirin 81mg to be continued until your stent removal procedure on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient presented with chest pain. He described the pain as constant (>24hrs), sharp, left sided with radiation down the arm and into the neck. He denied trauma and did not have pain with palpation of the chest wall. He was not short of breath but had nausea and vomiting. Serial EKGs were unchanged from prior. Troponin trended from 0.13 to 0.11 12 hours later (CK and MB flat) from baseline of 0.07. SL NTG did not change the pain, but it was improved with IV pain medications and the patient's home dose of methadone. Although the patient has known CAD with positive stress test ___ (with multiple subsequent admissions for chest pain during which medical management was consistently advised by cardiology), this pain was not consistent with cardiac chest pain. His vital signs remained normal throughout admission except for hypertension on arrival which responded to 1 inch of nitro paste and 10 IV hydralazine. He was then restarted on Amlodipine 10mg po daily and continued on home dose clonidine 0.3 TID. The patient's renal function was worse than baseline (3.1 up from 2.5) on admission and improved to baseline with 1L IVF. This was likely the cause of his slightly increased troponin. The patient's lung exam was notable for expiratory wheezing and he was given nebs with good relief. He was discharged with a combivent inhaler. The patient's other chronic medical problems; chronic low back and leg pain, neuropathy, DM2, anemia, anxiety-- were all stable and managed with his home medications. Despite his history of polysubstance abuse the patient did not show any signs of withdrawl. The patient was discharged with PCP follow up.
summary: You were admitted because you had chest pain that we were concerned might be related to your heart. Your EKG was unchanged from previous studies and markers in your blood for heart enzymes indicated you were not having a heart attack. You were treated with aspirin and medications to lower your blood pressure, which was very high on admission. You were given IV fluids because your kidney function was moderately worse compared to your baseline. On the floor we examined you and found that you had diffuse wheezing in your lungs, suggesting you ___ have mild emphysema. You were given nebulizer treatments to improve your breathing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with afib on coumadin and COPD who presents with hemoptysis. # Hemoptysis: On admission he presented with hemoptysis and new SOB/wheezing. His repeat CXR and CT chest were unchanged from prior. He remianed HD stable and was satting well on room air. He was started on empiric treatment for bacterial bronchitis with ceftriaxone and azithromycin, and completed this antibiotic course while ___ the hospital. He underwent bronchoscopy, which did not reveal any cause of bleeding or evidence of active bleed. However, this did demonstrate thick secretions, consistent with bronchitis. ENT evaluation was negative for any nasal cause of hemoptysis. BAL was significant for eosinophilia, and he was also noted to have a peripheral eosinophilia. Work-up for this was concerning for ANCA-negative ___. He was started on prednisone, and treated with nebulizers and his symptoms improved significantly. He was set-up with outpatient follow-up with Pulmonology for continued work-up of this possible diagnosis. Additionally, he was not restarted on any anticoagulation or anti-platelet agents prior to discharge. His hemoptysis stopped a few days prior to discharge. # Atrial Fibrillation: Rate well controlled on this admission while on diltiazam. As before, he has a CHADS2 score of 2, and prior to last admission he was on anticoagulation with coumadin. Although his risk for CVA is high, given acute bleed we again elected to hold off on additional anticoagulation. CHRONIC ISSUES: # GERD: No active issues. Continued omeprazole. # EtOH Abuse: No evidence of withdrawal. Continued MI, thiamine, folic acid. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - ANTI-GBM Results Pending - QUANTIFERON-TB GOLD Results Pending
summary: You were admitted for evaluation when you were coughing up blood (called hemoptysis). While you were here you had a CT scan of your chest, which was similar to your prior CT scan. You were seen by our pulmonologists, who performed a bronchoscopy. This procedure did not reveal any causes for your bleeding. It is possible that you have a vasculitis called ___, which could account for your symptoms. You were treated with antibiotics, steroids, and nebulizers and your symptoms improved. You were discharged on steroids and inhalers for treatment of your lung disease going forward. Please do not take aspirin or coumadin until you see your Cardiologist and Pulmonologist and have the opportunity to discuss if either of these medications are safe for you. Also, while you were here you were evaluated by our cardiologists. They felt that you would benefit from an increased dose of diltiazam. As a result, your dose was increased, and you tolerated this change well. Please continue taking this increased dose going forward.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient was admitted to the MICU, where he was tachypneic and wheezing on presentation. He was initially treated with continuous albuterol nebs and IV steroids, with gradual improvement. ABG on ___ showed 197/42/7.35/42 while on continuous nebulizer treatments. He remained afebrile but in light of productive cough was started empirically on therapy for a community-acquired pneumonia with Ceftriaxone and azithromycin. He was noted to AG of undetermined etiology upon presentation, which had resolved by HD3. IV steroids were changed to PO prednisone. On the morning of ___, he had a peak flow of 500 and was transferred out of the ICU to the Medical ward team. Steroid course was planned for a two-week taper. Ceftriaxone was switched to oral Cefpodoxime with plan to complete a 7-day course of cephalosporin and a 5-day course of azithromycin. . Medical floor course: . ___ y/o gentleman with Asthma and Hepatitis C cirrhosis presented to the ED with asthma exacerbation, initially admitted to MICU now transferred with improved respiratory status. . # Asthma Exacerbation. Patient was much improved by ___ with PF in 550s and no subjective senation of dyspnea. Based on further interview, it appears that exacerbation was triggered by known culprit exposures (auto exhaust, cigarrette smoke, spices) and possible URI. It was felt that exacerbation was unlikely due to CAP, given no fever, clear sputum and no opacities on CXR. No sputum data was available. Patient was discontinued from cefpodoxime and completed course of Azithromycin. Pt. also had PE findings consistent with allergic rhinitis. He was continued on albuterol nebs Q2H prn, ipratropium nebs q6h, prednisone 60mg. With this treatment he continued to improve with PF of 650 on ___. Based on this response, he was discharged home with a 2wk prednisone taper, continued on Advair and singulair. Given findings consistent with allergic rhinitis, patient was started on loratadine at discharged and instructed to resume flonase after completion of PO prednisone. He was also provided with clotrimazole troches tid prn for hx of oral candidiasis with previous prednisone treatments. He was also treated with HISS for high dose steroids with ___ ranging 106 - 175. Patient was also referred to Allergy clinic to further assess for asthma triggers. . # Hep C cirrhosis: nonresponder to prior treatment as per last ___. No stigmata of chronic liver disease on exam, but last RUQ u/s consistent with cirrhosis. Not an active issue during this admission. Patient is to followup with Dr. ___ as outpatient. . # Hypercalcemia. Appears chronic at least from ___ from OMR Total Ca > 11. Alb was 4.0, Phosphate 2.4, low. PTH elevated at 153, and Mr. ___ had normal renal function. Hypercalcemia was confirmed with iCa value of 1.50. Patient did report having vague abdominal pain over the past ___ years, as well as fatigue and paresthesias on/off in ___ of the same duration. No other complaints consistent w/ symptomatic hypercalcemia. This was felt to most likely represent primary Hyperpara, given elevated PTH and nl renal function. Can also be related to chronic vitamin D defficiency, and a 1,25 Vitamin D level was sent prior to discharge. This work up was communicated with PCP via email. Patient may require 24hr calcium and bone density measurements as OP as well as parathyroid imaging. ___ need to consider Raloxefine/bisphosphonate based on above results. . # HTN. SBPs 130 - 140s. Monitored during admission. . # PPX: sc heparin, can d/c once patient ambulated tid. continue protonix given high dose steroids. . # CODE STATUS: Full Code, confirmed with patient . # Contact: Daughter ___ ___, Father ___ ___ . Patient was discharged with improved breathing, peak flows and with mild cough with appropriate follow up.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with a severe asthma exacerbation (shortness of breath, cough). It was felt that this was due to exposure to triggers known to you (e.g. car exhaust, cigarette smoke, spices) as well as a possible infection. For this you required high dose steroids, nebulizers and treated with antibiotics. You did not require intubation but did require intensive care unit stay. You will require to continue the steroids for a total of two weeks (see below). You will also requrie to take one more day of antibiotics (Azithromycin). In addition, you should also continue to take your flovent on daily basis and albuterol as needed. Finally because evidence of allergic rhinitis (allergies) were noted on your exam, you should continue to take singulair and you were started on loratadine (claritin). After you complete the prednisone tablet course, you should resume the use of your flonase on a regular, daily basis. In addition, you were found to have a high calcium blood level. Evaluation showed that this may be due to a gland in your neck.

text: Brief Hospital Course: A/P: ___ y.o. F with asthma presenting with status asthmaticus, much improved with room air/ambulatory sats in the 95%. Also with E. faecalis bacteremia. Called out of MICU on ___. #Asthma exacerbation: Patient was initially admitted to the MICU for monitoring and treatment of her asthma exacerbation. She received continuous albuterol nebs, as well as additional magnesium, Atrovent, Advair, and IV Solumedrol. Given her persistent hypoxia on ICU day 2, Azithromycin was added for atypical coverage and she completed a 5 day course while in the hospital. An arterial line was placed for monitoring. She clinically improved and oxygen was able to be weaned to room air (93%), but the patient was sypmtomatically more comfortable on a 35% humidified face tent. She was transferred to the floor on ___, and transitioned to oral prednisone. Her ambulatory saturations improved to 95%. Her peak flows were acceptable per respiratory. She was continued on a 2 week prednisone taper as follows: 60 mg x3 days (___), 50 mg x3 days ___, completed), 40 mg x3 days (___), 30 mg x3 days (___), 20 mg x3 days (___), then off.) She was tapered off of standing albuterol nebs and discharged on q6H PRN with a machine at home (SW instrumental in helping her get this at no cost). She was continued on Advair, Singulair on discharge. She was attempted on CPAP but did not tolerate the machine (had panic attack and claustrophobia). She may have a sleep study to evaluate for OSA as an outpatient. She has pertussis serology still pending at the time of discharge. #Bacteremia: Pt had pansensitive E. faecalis from ___ blood cultures. Her surveillance cultures negative. She received 10 days of IV ampicillin while in house. Afebrile and hemodynamically stable on discharge. #Fall: Pt reports a fall in ICU, and being unsteady on her feet (not at her baseline, as reports being very active.) She also had a fall overnight on ___ after receiving sleeping medication, and she hit her head and her R knee. Her neurologic exam and MS exams were stable, and her hematocrit remained stable at 38. She had a Head CT w/o contrast that showed a subcutaneous hematoma, but no evidence of intracranial bleed, midline shift, or structural malformation. She received Ibuprofen/Tylenol for soft tissue trauma/pain to her frontal head, R knee. She was kept on fall precautions and was not given sleeping medication at night thereafter. ___ was consulted, and deemed her safe to go home with physical therapy treatments. #Anxiety: She takes high doses of valium daily as outpatient, and was tolerating ativan here. Benzodiazapines were held initially during her hospitalization, as there was concern on admission for respitory compromise. While on the floor, her respirations were stable. She was continued on celexa and a lower dose of her outpatient valium (TID instead of QID.) Her outpt psychiatrist has referred her to outpt therapist to deal w/ multiple psychosocial stressors at home. SW has provided pt with resources to help w/ therapist co-pay from ___. #Hyperglycemia: in setting of acute illness and steroids. Continued on regular insulin sliding scale while in house. . #Back pain: Likely osteoarthritis, muscular pain, deconditioning. Pt was treated w/ ibuprofen (pre-treated w/ compazine for nausea) and lidocaine patches. . #Crohn's Disease: currently with diarrhea--likely antibiotic associated, given high dose steroids recently. Otherwise, disease has been in remission. . #Hypertension: Stable on HCTZ . #FEN: Regular diet, repleted electrolytes as necessary. #Ppx: Heparin SQ, PPI, bowel regimen. #Code full #Dispo: home with physical therapy.
summary: You were admitted with a diagnosis of a bad flare of your asthma known as status asthmaticus. You also were diagnosed with bacteria in your blood stream. The status asthmaticus required that you stay in the ICU for 2 days. Your vital signs and oxygen saturations were monitered closely, and you were treated with oxygen therapy, oral prednisone, and an antibiotic known as azithromycin to cover for bacteria in the lung that might have been causing an infection. You completed the azithromycin while in the hospital. Your prednisone will need to be taken over two weeks according to the taper listed below. The bacteria in the blood was treated with the antibiotic ampicillin. It proved to have cleared from your blood stream on repeat blood cultures after the ampicillin was started. You received 10 days total of this medication while in the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female G1P0 at 12w4d gestation with PMHx moderate persistent asthma without history of intubation but multiple hospitalizations presenting with asthma exacerbation. . # Asthma Exacerbation She has had frequent exacerbations of her asthma requiring hospitalization since pregnancy. She was exposed to several triggers prior to this exacarbation; no signs or symptoms of infection. She received IV steroids, magnesium, Heliox, and continuous bronchodilators, but was using significant accessory respiratory muscles and ABG with normal CO2 suggestive of respiratory fatigue. She was unable to tolerate BiPAP secondary to anxiety, and was therefore urgently intubated. While intubated she was continued on daily IV methylprednisolone and q1h bronchodilators. Her respiratory mechanics improved and was extubated on HD#3. Steroids were weanted to oral and she was transitioned to nebulized bronchodilators. Monteleukast and loratidine were started for associated allergic rhinitis as a trigger. She was transferred to the medical floor. She should follow-up with her PCP, as well as be referred back to Allergy and Pulmonary Clinics. . # Pregnancy G1P0 at 12w4d on admission. The patient was highly concerned about fetal safety. OB/MFM was consulted for medication assistance during her hosptialization. Theophylline was not used. Because of her severe asthma exacerbation, OB/MFM felt that both benzodiazepines and narcotics were not contraindicated. She did require midazolam and fentanyl for sedation while intubated. Prenatal vitamins were continued. OB/MFM was ___ agreement with the medical management of her asthma flare, as poorly controlled asthma is associated with increased adverse events ___ pregnancy. Patient also noted some vaginal spotting / bleeding, after transfer to the floor. She was re-evaluated by OB/MFM, with repeat bedside fetal US reassuring. She will have close f/u with OB ___ clinic. . # Dysuria Urine on admission was cloudy and UA concerning for infection. Although asymptomatic, she was started on nitrofurantoin for asymptomatic bacteriuria during pregnancy. Urine culture was negative, and antibiotics were discontinued, but she complained of dysuria after Foley removal and this was restarted for a 5-day course. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1. complete a Prednisone burst 2. complete a course of antibiotics for possible complicated cystitis 3. She will have close outpatient f/u with PCP and OB 4. Also recommend that she be referred back to Pulmonary Clinic and also referred to Allergy Clinic
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for difficulty breathing, consistent with an asthma flare. Your flare was severe enough to require placement of a breathing tube and use of a ventilator machine, which required admission to the ICU. You improved with systemic steroids and breathing treatments. You will complete a steroid taper. You will also start on 2 new medications called montelukast and loratadine. You also complained of pain urinating, and we are treating you with a course of antibiotics. You were also seen by the Obstetricians, including the Maternal-Fetal-Medical experts. You had some vaginal spotting, but bedside ultrasounds show that your pregnancy is stable.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female G1P0 at 12w4d gestation with PMHx moderate persistent asthma without history of intubation but multiple hospitalizations presenting with asthma exacerbation. . # Asthma Exacerbation She has had frequent exacerbations of her asthma requiring hospitalization since pregnancy. She was exposed to several triggers prior to this exacarbation; no signs or symptoms of infection. She received IV steroids, magnesium, Heliox, and continuous bronchodilators, but was using significant accessory respiratory muscles and ABG with normal CO2 suggestive of respiratory fatigue. She was unable to tolerate BiPAP secondary to anxiety, and was therefore urgently intubated. While intubated she was continued on daily IV methylprednisolone and q1h bronchodilators. Her respiratory mechanics improved and was extubated on HD#3. Steroids were weanted to oral and she was transitioned to nebulized bronchodilators. Monteleukast and loratidine were started for associated allergic rhinitis as a trigger. She was transferred to the medical floor. She should follow-up with her PCP, as well as be referred back to Allergy and Pulmonary Clinics. . # Pregnancy G1P0 at 12w4d on admission. The patient was highly concerned about fetal safety. OB/MFM was consulted for medication assistance during her hosptialization. Theophylline was not used. Because of her severe asthma exacerbation, OB/MFM felt that both benzodiazepines and narcotics were not contraindicated. She did require midazolam and fentanyl for sedation while intubated. Prenatal vitamins were continued. OB/MFM was ___ agreement with the medical management of her asthma flare, as poorly controlled asthma is associated with increased adverse events ___ pregnancy. Patient also noted some vaginal spotting / bleeding, after transfer to the floor. She was re-evaluated by OB/MFM, with repeat bedside fetal US reassuring. She will have close f/u with OB ___ clinic. . # Dysuria Urine on admission was cloudy and UA concerning for infection. Although asymptomatic, she was started on nitrofurantoin for asymptomatic bacteriuria during pregnancy. Urine culture was negative, and antibiotics were discontinued, but she complained of dysuria after Foley removal and this was restarted for a 5-day course. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1. complete a Prednisone burst 2. complete a course of antibiotics for possible complicated cystitis 3. She will have close outpatient f/u with PCP and OB 4. Also recommend that she be referred back to Pulmonary Clinic and also referred to Allergy Clinic

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY ========== ___ w/ hx of PMR (on prednisone), HTN, HLD, DII, afib (on warfarin), multivessel CAD (s/p BMS to LAD in ___ with most recent cath in ___ demonstrating moderate CAD and elevated left heart filling pressures, 3+ MR/TR, and pulmonary hypertension who presents after fall at home. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== [] Consider uptitration of bupropion or addition of another antidepressant agent (ie mirtazapine) if continued concern for depressive symptoms. [] The patient's home nifedipine was held in the setting of hypotension. Please monitor HR and BPs and consider restarting. [] The patient's lisinopril was also held in the setting of an ___. Consider restarting if hypertension. [] A TTE showed severe TR and inferior wall motion abnormalities. Consider repeat echocardiogram or further work up for progression of coronary artery disease. [] The patient had a brain MRI which demonstrated multiple chronic lacunar infarctions and evidence of involution. Consider further neurocognitive evaluation. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # Falls The patient presented after recent falls at home, one described as a fall due to imbalance and the other with less clear circumstances, concerning for syncope. He appeared dehydrated on admission, so in the light of severe TR as discovered on TTE, this could have temporarily lead to poor cerebral perfusion due to preload dependence. An MRI/MRA head and neck was done demonstrating degenerative changes as well as multiple chronic lacunar strokes in the R hemisphere, concerning for small vessel ischemic disease. This likely also contributed to his presentation and imbalance. The patient was given IVF, encouraged to increase PO intake, and he mobilized with ___. His home lisinopril and diltiazem were put on hold due to normotensive BPs and well controlled HRs. The pt was discharged to a short term rehabilitation center. # Failure to thrive # Depression On talking further with the pt, he endorsed a more subacute functional decline over the past several months. In that time frame, he had poor appetite, low mood, weight loss, fatigue, and overall failure to thrive at his senior living home. There were no obvious metabolic derangement to implicate-- TSH wnl, no protein gap, LFTs nml, and CBC near recent baseline. It was felt that depression, along with likely chronic small vessel cerebral disease were likely playing a major role in this change. The patient was continued on home bupropion. His outpatient psychiatrist was involved and provided insight into the patient's past psychiatric history. Given that he had been trialed on other anti-depressants, changes to his regimen was deferred for now. # ___ Cr was 2.4 on admission, elevated from baseline 1.2-1.3. Likely pre-renal etiology. Improved back to baseline with IVF. CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES: ====================== # Atrial fibrillation Permanent afib, w/ hx of RVR. The patient was given metoprolol instead of atenolol in the setting of ___. He was transitioned back to home atenolol on discharge. He was continued on warfarin, dosed for INR ___. # CAD # HLD/HTN The patient was continued on atorvastatin. His home lisinopril was held given ___ and normotension. # PMR He was continued on home prednisone 4mg QD. # GERD He was continued on home PPI. # Anemia Appeared to be at baseline ___. # DMII Home oral anti-hyperglycemics were held. Continued insulin sliding scale. CORE MEASURES ============= #CODE: FC, presumed #CONTACT: HCP ___, Friend (___)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you fell at home. While you were here you got IV fluids because you were dehydrated. We also did several tests to determine the cause of your falls. An echocardiogram of your heart showed that one of your valves was leaky. An MRI of your brain was done which also showed that you have had small strokes in the past. Additionally, we also had several conversations with you about your mood, because we were concerned that your depression was also contributing to your poor appetite at home. We reached out to your outpatient psychiatrist, Dr. ___, to involve him in your care too.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o woman with a significant psychiatric history including depression c/b 4 suicide attempts via overdose, intellectual disability, ___ institutionalization and ADHD who presents with worsening anxiety, insomnia and depression. #Anxiety/Depression Ms. ___ has a significant psychiatric history which includes a ___ long institutionalization and 4 previous suicide attempts. Currently, she reports persistent anxiety, manifested as restlessness, anxiety, and palpitations. Currently denies SI/HI but expresses concern that she does not feel safe on her own at home. Vital signs have been stable and with normal limits. Exam is grossly normal. Denies recent physical trauma or falls. Serum toxicology was negative. Psychiatry was consulted and advised continuation of her home regimen, with the addition of clonidine 0.1mg prn anxiety and advised holding Baclofen 10 mg TID, Lorazepam 0.25 mg q6hr PRN anxiety, Loratadine 10 mg daily. After extensive discussion with psychiatry, social work, case management, the patient the best disposition for Ms. ___ is intensive outpatient program for her anxiety/depression which was arranged. In preparation, PPD was placed 1445 ___ which will need to be read ___ after 1500, and appointment was made. # GERD: Patient complaining of intermittent "burning" chest pain that is associated with her anxiety attacks. Of note, she has a history of GERD for which she receives 20 mg Omeprazole daily. Maalox ___ mL PO/NG QID:PRN for immediate relief. #Uncomplicated UTI: Ecoli pansensitive isolated from urine. Treated with ceftriaxone ___. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [] Please ensure ___ read of PPD (48 hr =1500), fax records to intensive outpatient program [] Dr. ___: please make sure additional forms are received by ___ at ___: (___) [] Ensure follow up with psychiatrist, intensive outpatient program [] Consider home OT evaluation prn [] Restart baclofen, lorazepam, loratidine as indicated Attending Addendum: I have seen and examined the patient on the day of discharge and agree with the resident's note.
summary: Please see below for information on your time in the hospital. You were in the hospital for an exacerbation of anxiety and depression. You were also found to have a urinary tract infection We gave you anti-anxiety medications. You were seen by psychiatry, social work and together as a team we decided that an intensive outpatient program would be the best plan to treat your anxiety. We gave you antibiotics for the urinary tract infection. You improved. Follow up ___ at 3pm at your primary care office to get your PPD (tuberculosis skin test) read. This will need to be documented prior to starting your outpatient program. At this visit, make sure your Primary care has completed and faxed forms ___ attn. ___ at ___ for processing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of anxiety, depression, IBS presents with nausea and vomiting x 5 days, likely non-organic cause. # Nausea, vomiting, dizziness, paresthesias. Multiple nonspecific complaints of nausea, vomiting, abdominal discomfort, headaches, fatigue, weakness over the past three months. There have been no organic causse or abnormal objective findings on lab studies or physical exam. This is most likely related to anxiety versus depression. Of note, she recently had started amoxicillin for sinusitis seen on MRI. She also had recently started gabapentin and citalopram in the outpatient setting. These three medications were held in case they were contributing to her symptoms. Did not start an alternate antibiotic, as she reports that she had already taken 7 days of antibiotics. Inpatient workup - normal CBC, Chem-10, LFTs, albumin, lactate, EKG, CXR, UA, urine bUCG. Orthostatics negative. Physical exam normal. CK normal. HIV negative. Outpatient workup - B12, TSH, vit D, CRP, IgA, TTG, zinc, RPR, MonoSpot, Lyme serology, MRI negative for multiple sclerosis She was treated with PRN acetaminophen and ondansetron. She was tolerating a regular diet on discharge. She was discharged with PRN acetaminophen and ondasentron and has primary care follow up. # Recent sinusitis. Prescribed amoxicillin by PCP following MRI. Received amoxicillin 500 mg PO Q8H, ___, will discontinue given patient's concern for side effects and she has already received one week course. # External hemorrhoid. Known. Nonthrombosed. Hydrocortisone rectal cream PRN. # Low grade vulvar dysplasia. Followed as outpatient. Uses imiquimod 5% topical 3/WEEK, not on formulary, spoke with pharmacy, will defer substitute since patient was discharged the next day. # CODE: Full, confirmed ___ # CONTACT: ___, husband, ___ ### TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ### 1) PCP followup for further anxiety and depression management. Consider use of another SSRI. Patient apparently developed vomiting to citalopram and gabapentin. 2) Discharged with Tylenol and Zofran PRN for symptom management.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of nausea and vomiting. We reviewed your medical records including previous lab workups and office visits with multiple specialists and with your primary care physician. We checked bloodwork, electrocardiogram, urine, and chest xray, which were all normal. You had a normal physical exam. You were treated with symptomatic medications with Tylenol and Zofran for nausea. We did not find a life-threatening cause for your nausea. You improved and were able to tolerate a regular diet on discharge. The most likely cause of your symptoms is depression and/or anxiety.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with multiple sclerosis (wheelchair-bound) and new diagnosis of colon and kidney cancers (diagnosed ___, who now presents with "personality change" over the past few weeks, acutely worsening with no PO intake and now 25 lb weight loss prior to admission. # Altered personality: Altered personality is characterized by increased irritability, anger, and irrational comments or behaviors such as refusal to eat. Etiology is unclear but leading diagnosis is Major Depression with psychotic and catatonic features. Differential on presentation also included: (1) ___ inducing a multifocal inflammatory leukoencephalopathy but unlikely as this effect is usually seen after ___ weeks of ___ therapy, (2) ___ encephalopathy associated with transient hyperammonemia but unlikely as ammonium is normal today, (3) infection such as UTI, bloodstream infection, or pna, (4) meningitis. However, per Neurology, Psychiatry, and Oncology evaluations, these diagnoses were deemed unlikely. Infectious workup was negative. We held home testosterone. We started trial of lorazepam 0.5mg TID and haldol 0.5mg TID on ___. The patient had some improvement in interactions and eye contact. This regimen was uptitrated to mirtazapine 7.5mg QHS, haldol 1mg TID, and lorazepam 0.5mg TID:PRN agitation. The patient continued to show slow improvement and had more pleasant and rational interactions with staff. He increased PO intake to meet daily minimum calorie requirement of 1700 calories/day. He was then discharged with close followup with ___ on ___. # Poor PO intake: Etiology of poor PO intake likely secondary to depresion. He was hydrated with IVF and supported with PPN. He continued a regular diet which increased over the course of his stay. He received close electrolyte monitoring but did not show signs of refeeding syndrome. PPN was stopped ___ as patient was showing sufficient PO intake of calories. His daily minimum calorie intake should be 1700 calories, per Nutrition. # Urinary and stool incontinence: Likely secondary to poor verbal communication, also related to depression. UTI was ruled out with negative urine culture. C diff ruled out with negative stool C diff test. Patient was managed supportively by nursing staff with frequent diaper changes and condom catheter. # Neutropenia: New from ___ with WBC to 2.7 on admission. Per primary oncologist Drs. ___, neutropenia is late in terms of timing from FOLFOX. Neutropenia eventually recovered by ___ without intervention. Late recovery potentially ___ malnutrition. While neutropenic, the patient was on neutropenic precautions. He remained afebrile during the hospital course and did not require antibiotics or infectious prophylaxis. On discharge, WBC had been normal for several days. # New GU and Colon cancer: Primary oncologist ___ followed patient while in house. Chemo was held given more acute issues. Longterm care of kidney and colon cancers to be discussed as outpatient with ___. # h/o PE: Continued home lovenox 80mg SC QD # Multiple sclerosis: Wheelchair bound. Used plate-style adaptive call light. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # CODE: Full (confirmed with wife) # CONTACT: ___, ___ (wife) # PSYCH: Follow up with Dr. ___ ___. Continuing Haldol 1mg TID, Remeron 7.5mg QHS, and Ativan 0.5mg TID:PRN agitation on discharge pending med titration per outpatient psych. # NUTRITION: Daily calorie minimum should be 1700 calories per day.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for mood and personality changes and because you were not eating or drinking enough. This caused a 25 lb weight loss prior to admission. You were seen by psychiatry and neurology while in the hospital. They felt that your symptoms were due to major depression with psychotic symptoms. You were treated with Ativan, Haldol, and an anti-depressant called Remeron. On discharge, you should take Haldol 1mg by mouth three times per day and Remeron 7.5mg by mouth at bedtime daily. You should also take Ativan 0.5mg by mouth as needed for agitation, up to three times per day. Do not take this medication if you already feel sleepy. When you were admitted, you also needed nutrition through your IV for several days. However, you improved and by the end of your stay, you had gained approximately 5 kg and you were no longer requiring IV nutrition. Your daily calorie minimum intake should be 1700 calories.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ year old right handed woman who has remote history of colon cancer, now thought to be in remission, who presented after several staring spells as well as a witnessed generalized tonic clonic seizure. At an outside hospital, she received 2 mg IV ativan and was loaded with levetiracetam 750 mg. She was then transferred to ___. By the time she arrived to ___, she was essentially back to normal with intact mental status. Her examination was otherwise notable for bilateral IP and hamstring weakness, with diffuse hyperreflexia but downgoing toes, likely suggestive of a cervical spondylytic myelopathy. Laboratory evaluation during admission was unrevealing for any acute toxic metabolic process or infection. MRI brain showed generalized atrophy as well as chronic microvascular changes. She also reported symptoms of very poor sleep, as well as depressed mood and anhedonia, without suicidality. We feel that the etiology of her seizures is likely due to chronic microvascular changes, exacerbated by sleep deprivation. She was started on Levetiracetam 750mg BID, and tolerated this medication well. There were no further seizures during admission. She did complain of symptoms of a urinary tract infection on ___. Urinalysis was consistent with UTI. She was therefore started on Cefpodoxime and will complete a 3 day course (___). Importantly, she did not have symptoms until well after her seizures, so UTI is felt to be less likely a contributor to her seizures. Physical therapy recommended discharge to acute rehab. Driving restrictions and seizure safety awareness was reviewed. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Started on Mirtazapine due to symptoms of depression and poor sleep. Please monitor her symptoms and adjust medications as necessary. Should be referred to therapist/social worker. -Blood pressure 130-150 systolic during admission. Please monitor and adjust antihypertensive regimen as needed. -She has mild paraparesis on exam, likely suggestive of a chronic spondylytic myelopathy. We would suggest an outpatient evaluation, possibly with MRI cervical spine. She may symptomatically treat this with a soft cervical collar. -No driving for 6 months post seizure. -Complete 3 day course of Cefpodoxime ___
summary: You were in the hospital after you had a few episodes of staring and unresponsiveness. You also had an episode of shaking of all of your limbs. Based on the description, we feel these episodes were likely seizures. In the hospital, we performed several studies to determine why you had a seizure. The MRI of your brain showed that you have some findings that are typical as you age. However, these changes can cause seizures. We started you on a medication called Keppra to help prevent further seizures. We also started you on a medication called Mirtazapine which can help with sleep as well as your mood. When you leave the hospital, you should continue taking Keppra to prevent seizures. In order to prevent future seizures, it is important to get regular sleep, stay hydrated, and avoid excessive alcohol (more than one drink per night). According to state law, you cannot drive for 6 months from your last seizure. Also, you should avoid heights or ladders, as well as swimming or bathing unsupervised.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with T1DM c/b gastroparesis, retinopathy, neuropathy, and ESRD s/p living unrelated kidney transplant in ___ who presents with acute kidney injury secondary to cellular rejection secondary to medication non-compliance. ACUTE ISSUES #Acute cellular rejection #ESRD s/p renal transplant Ms. ___ suffers from depression and has a history of not taking her immunosuppression as long ago as ___. She reports now consistently taking her medication, but did present with acute rejection. A kidney biopsy this admission showed evidence of acute cellular rejection, and inflammation, with possible involvement of endothelium (___ Class Ib or IIa rejection). DSA was negative (___). She is underwent an initial 5 day course of 125mg antithymoglobulin (___), which was extended by 1.5 days for incomplete response. Her creatininte improved to 2.6, which was suboptimal, but not unexpected in the setting of acute rejection due to medicine noncompliance. Her home Tacrolimus (goal morning level of ___ was continued and Azathioprine was increased to 100 mg daily. On discharge, her prednisone was tapered to 40 mg daily for 3 days (___), then 20mg for 3 days (___), then 10mg until she sees Dr. ___. She received prophylaxis with Bactrim SS, liquid Nystatin, Valganciclovir 450 mg twice a week. #DM Type I, uncontrolled A1c found to be 11% on admission. She had challenging-to-managed blood sugars in the setting of high dose steroids. ___ followed for assistance in NPH management as well as her insulin pump. She will take NPH 10 with Pred 40, then NPH 8 with Pred 20, then NPH 6 with Pred 10 until she sees Dr. ___. #AG Metabolic Acidosis, resolved: AG acidosis developed secondary to ___ and improved with partial recovery of renal function. She was supplemented with sodium bicarbonate 1300mg BID, continued at 650mg BID at discharge. #HTN She has no documented history of HTN, and it was suspected that this was secondary to acute renal failure. Amlodipine 10 mg daily was started this hospitalization, which the patient tolerated well. CHRONIC ISSUES ================= #Depression #Anxiety This serves as a major barrier to her treatment, causing medicine non-compliance intermittently over the past year. - SW consult appreciated - Continue home Ativan and duloxetine #Gastroparesis: Tolerating PO. No acute flare at this time. #Retinopathy - Continue home Lumigan, Timolol ======================= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ======================= # Prednisone Taper: 40mg daily for 3 days (___), then 20mg for 3 days (___), then 10mg until she sees Dr. ___ # NPH Taper with prednisone: 10u daily for 3 days (___), then 8u for 3 days (___), then 6u until she sees Dr. ___. # HTN: Amlodipine 10mg daily started this hospitalization. SBPs up to the 160s. Continue to titrate as outpatient. # Continue valgancyclovir for 3 months and nystatin for 30 days # Continue to taper sodium bicarbonate as an outpatient. # Labwork on ___ and ___: Chem 10 # Follow up appointment with Dr. ___ on ___ # ___ follow up in ___ weeks for pump adjustments after Prednisone taper # Patient will follow up with her therapist, who will monitor closely, given that depression was a contributing factor to her medication non-compliance in the past. Discharge Cr: 2.6 # CODE: Full # CONTACT: Husband, ___, ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. AzaTHIOprine 75 mg PO DAILY 2. Sulfameth/Trimethoprim SS 1 TAB PO DAILY 3. DULoxetine 60 mg PO DAILY 4. Lorazepam 0.5 mg PO Q8H:PRN anxiety 5. Tacrolimus 2 mg PO Q12H 6. Timolol Maleate 0.5% 1 DROP LEFT EYE DAILY 7. Lumigan (bimatoprost) 0.01 % ophthalmic HS 8. Mirena (levonorgestrel) 20 mcg/24 hr ___ years) injection ASDIR 9. Insulin Pump SC (Self Administering Medication)Insulin Lispro (Humalog) Basal rate minimum: 0.9 units/hr Target glucose: ___ Fingersticks: QAC and HS
summary: You were admitted because of poor kidney function, as seen on routine labwork. A biopsy of your transplanted kidney was performed. You were found to have evidence of acute rejection of the transplant. Because of this, you were started on high-dose steroids. You were also given a medicine called anti-thymoglobulin to help your kidney recover from the rejection. A central IV line was placed in your neck to help deliver these medications. Your bloodwork was monitored daily to watch your kidney function and to measure the levels of immunosuppressive medicines in your blood. Your creatinine came down to 2.6. Your sugars were high because of all the steroids we gave you. Your insulin pump was adjusted, and you were given NPH insulin to help manage your sugars.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with schizoaffective disorder, HTN, BPH and ___ Disease, who was admitted for management of depression and suicidality, now transferred to Medicine service for management of rash and hyponatremia. #HYPONATREMIA: Mr. ___ had an ___ hyponatremia to 124-125 from his recent prior baseline of 131-133 about 10 days ago. Of note, he had an episode of hyponatremia in ___, also with a sodium of 125. Per patient and medical reconciliation, he is prescribed salt tabs TID. The underlying etiology of this presentation is suggestive of SIADH secondary to either medications such as fluoxetine or haldol or underlying malignancy (given reported but unconfirmed recent history of unintentional weight loss). Adrenal insufficiency was ruled out with a normal cortisol. Given his psychiatric disease, primary polydipsia would also remain on the differential, though we have been unable to obtain a reliable history of free water intake or accurate I/Os. Hypothyroidism is an unlikely cause given that he had a normal TSH earlier in this admission. Of note, Serum Osms obtained on ___ were WNL at 280, which could suggest pseudohyponatremia. With fluid restriction to 1.5L, Na improved back to baseline. Pt will likely will need fluid restriction long term. #CANDIDAL RASH & R ELBOW ULCER: Mr. ___ has an erythematous, tender rash in the fold of his pannus that has worsened over the past ___ weeks since the patient was admitted to the hospital. This rash had been presumed to be secondary to a Candidal intertrigo but has not improved on itraconazole or miconazole. Given his concomitant leukocytosis, an overlying cellulitis is also possible. Derm recs: continue ketoconazole 2% cream BID to entire panus and groin, keflex ___ q6hr for 7 days ___-, mupirocin ointment BID to ulcers on right pinky finger and right elbow, also continue bleach baths: 1 teaspoon of house hold. Bleach in pink basin of warm water, soak washcloth and wash panus and groin every other day #SOMNOLENCE: Patient with increased somnolence on ___ in the morning. Patient was easily awoken and responded appropriately subsequently. CVA seems unlikely given reassuring neurologic exam. #SCHIZOAFFECTIVE DISORDER: Continued home fluoxetine, Haldol and Risperdal per PAML. Patient may need adjustment of these medications given intermittent altered mental status but deemed stable for psychiatry transfer back to Deaconess 4. QTc 442 at discharge. TRANSITIONAL: -Patient's mental status evaluated to be baseline given AAOx2-3 and speech also understandable. However, somnolence can exacerbate symptoms and may make it difficult to assess patient. ___ need adjustment of psychiatric medications. -Patient's sodium improved with fluid restriction. Patient will need 1.5L fluid restriction long term. -Please repeat BMP ___ to assess stability of sodium level -Per Derm recs, continue ketoconazole 2% cream BID to entire panus and groin, keflex ___ q6hr for 7 days ___, mupirocin ointment BID to ulcers on right pinky finger and right elbow. Per Derm recs, continue bleach baths: 1 teaspoon of house hold bleach in pink basin of warm water, soak washcloth and wash panus and groin every other day - Please obtain EKG monthly for QTc monitoring (QTc 442 at discharge) -Pt without abnormal blood pressure while on medicine service but has previous history of hypotension. Was continued on lisinopril 10mg while hospitalized but metoprolol, a prior medication, was still held. Consider restarting this if BPs can tolerate.
summary: You were transferred from the psychiatry service to the medicine service after there was concern about your electrolytes and because there was concern about your rash. While you were with our service, we continued antibiotics for your rash. Dermatology saw you while inpatient and made recommendations which will be continued. We watched your sodium closely and it returned to your normal level before you were safely able to leave our service and go back to the psychiatry service. You should be on a 1.5L fluid restriction. Your mental status was evaluated by the medicine team to be appropriate, although at times sleepy, which may be due to psychiatric medications. These can be further adjusted as appropriate by the psychiatry service.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F presenting for evaluation of memory loss and management of hypertension and diabetes. . # MEMORY LOSS: Patient has excellent insight into her memory loss, which is more consistent with depression than true dementia. Ddx for memory loss should include, in addition to depression, multi-infarct disease, early-onset Alzheimers, hypothyroid or vitamin deficiencies. Her attention is excellent, judgment and executive function are normal on MMSE. TSH and vitamin B12 have been normal. RPR was ordered and is pending. MRI showing small vessel ischemic changes. Given her recent lifestyle changes (quitting job) and subjective feeling of sadness, depression seems most likely to be causing, or at least contributing to, her current short-term memory loss. She is being discharged on 20 mg celexa qday, a dose that was recommended to her during a recent visit to Dr. ___ in gerontology. She will have visiting nurses help her with her medications during these next several weeks as the antidepressant begins to take effect. . # HTN: Likely due to failure to remember to take medications. We continued her home BP regimen that includes clonidine, metoprolol, nifedipine, and lisinopril and her blood pressure quickly normalized. . # DMII: RISS while in house. Discharged on home dose of metformin. . # HYPERCHOLESTEROL: Continued home simvastatin in aspirin.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital when you were found to have high blood pressure in clinic. Your home blood pressure medications were given and your blood pressure improved on this. Both based on the recommendations of your home geriatrics doctor and on our evaluation in the hospital, you were started on a medication called celexa to help with your mood and energy level. You were also set up with a visiting nurse at home to help you with your medications. You were also set up with follow up with the community resources specialist and social worker at your outpatient clinic. Please work on taking all your home medications, including your blood pressure and diabetes medications, every day. You can use the memory aid techniques that you were given in the hospital. The only new medication to take is celexa 20mg daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man with PMH alcohol and cocaine abuse, p/w chest pain, abd pain, vomiting blood, and dark stools likely secondary to ___ tear. Patient absconded on ___. He was given no discharge medications or instructions prior to leaving ___. He did not inform anyone he was leaving the hospital. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Vomiting blood and dark stools: None since presentation to the ED here. The patient was guiac negative in the ED. CT Abd/pelvis showed no acute process. EGD was done and showed mild gastritis, no active bleeding. H pylori negative. Hep panel showed prior exposure to Hep B. Vomiting blood may have been ___ ___ tear from alcohol consumption with a lot of retching. He was given pantoprazole and sucralate in house. # Abd pain and chest pain: Likely musculoskeletal, perhaps from prolonged vomiting. Trops negative, multiple repeat EKGs unchanged. Pain was reproducible on exam with palpation of the sternum and the abdomen. He was given tylenol and oxycodone for pain control, which brought him minimal relief. When EGD showed very mild gastritis only, the patient was started on Naproxen in house, with some resolution of his pain. However, he became somewhat nauseous thereafter, so Naproxen was held. # Nausea/vomiting in house: The patient complained of some nausea and vomiting in house after initiation of Naproxen. Initially he was treated with antiemetics. He got a KUB which showed a large amount of stool, so was given a strong bowel regimin. The patient's nausea was mildly improved on the day he left the hospital - however it is unclear if this issue was entirely resolved at the time the patient left ___. # Skipped beats on tele: On second look at the strip, looks like Type ___ Mobitz block which occured while patient was sleeping, likely ___ increased vagal tone. patient asymptomatic. No recurrences. # Anemia: Hct 32.6 on admission. Unclear baseline given no prior records. ___ be ___ acute GI losses. Alt, could have chronic long term losses from low bleed in GI tract from gastritis (given prior h/o black stools), or other congenital cause of anemia. Had the patient stayed long enough to receive d/c instructions our team would have recommended iron studies & colonoscopy as an outpatient. # Substance abuse and depression: The patient has been depressed for the last few months with subsequent weight loss after the death of his cousin, and starting using alcohol and cocaine at that time, prior to that was "clean." Realizes this is a problem at this time, is contemplating seeking help. Told social work that he is interested in attending AA and NA meetings in ___, and that when he returns to ___ his aunt is a good influence who helps him stay sober. He was maintained on CIWA scale during the early part of his admission. Once CIWA scores were consistently less than 5 CIWA scale was stopped. TRANSITIONAL ISSES - The patient was counseled about the need for establishing a PCP in ___ to follow up with his GI, psychiatric, and substance abuse issues. The patient agrees with this plan and says he wants to return to his family doctor who he has seen on occasion. - The patient was counseled that he could benefit from an anti-depressant medication, but that he needs to establish primary care in order to be monitored while taking this drug. - The patient was offered help with coordinating inpatient drug rehabilitation, but he declined, preferring to go to NA and AA meetings when he returns to ___.
summary: You were admitted for chest pain and vomiting with blood. Your chest pain was most likely due to musculoskeletal strain in your chest and abdomen due to your recent vomiting. You received an endoscopy which showed that you have mild "gastritis" (inflammation in the stomach) and "duodenitis" (inflammation in the small intestines). This inflammation is likely caused by alcohol intake and excessive acid. We strongly recommend that you stop drinking alcohol and using cocaine permanently. You were seen by social work who recommended you pursue support services when you leave the hospital to help you quit. Cocaine and alcohol put you at significant risk of heart disease (including a heart attack), worsening inflammation in your stomach, liver disease and even death. Please talk to your primary care doctor as well in order to devise strategies to keep you sober. Please establish care with a primary care doctor as explained below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y.o woman with h.o depression, HTN, hypothyroidism who presented with fatigue and was found to have PNA and new, now resolved, afib. #community acquired pneumonia-flu neg, CXR with infiltrate. Oxygen weaned off. Ambulatory sats >92%. Pt was given ceftriaxone and azithromycin while inpatient and converted to PO cefpodoxime and azithromycin upon discharge to complete a 5 day course of azithromycin and 7 days of cefpodoxime. Oxygen was weaned off ___. She will need a repeat CXR to ensure resolution and that there is no underlying process given her history after completion of treatment for pneumonia. #atrial fibrillation-new, noticed in ED, in NSR upon arrival to the floor. Likely triggered by hypoxia. CHADs2 score of 2, CHADsvas of 4. No further events during admission. Will continue on home asa therapy. Pt instructed to continue discussion with PCP regarding risks/benefits of starting anticoagulation. #dark urine-likely due to dehydration. No urinary symptoms otherwise. Improved/resolved with hydration. LFTs WNL. UCX pending. #hypothyroidism-continued home meds #HTN-home metoprolol continued #depression-Ritalin, Effexor continued
summary: You were admitted for evaluation of cough and shortness of breath. You were found to have a pneumonia and improved with antibiotic therapy which will need to continue upon discharge to complete a 7 day total course of therapy. In addition, when you were in the ER you were noted to have an irregular heart rate called "atrial fibrillation". This resolved by the time you came to the medical floor. This can lead to increase risk of stroke. For now, you should continue to take your aspirin but discuss with Dr. ___ you need to start a blood thinner medication. You will need to have a repeat Chest Xray after treatment for your pneumonia to ensure that your pneumonia has resolved.
