text: Brief Hospital Course: # Weakness # S/p fall: Patient reports feeling weak for the past 3 days in setting of worsening insomnia, although notes that she has been weak for 7 months. Her strength exam is normal throughout; specifically no proximal or focal weakness. Her perceived weakness and fall may be related to possible medication effects; her PCP notes that the patient is confused about her medications. Patient also with significant history of depression and insomnia that may be contributing. Patient also with osteoarthritis. More acutely, she was thought to have a possible urinary tract infection though this seemed unlikely subsequently with a negative urine culture and borderline symptoms. No evidence of cardiac ischemic. Anemia chronic and stable. Electrolytes normal. Imaging without evidence of acute process to explain her weakness; no fractures. TSH wnl, CK only mildly elevated at 239 (normal: ___, CRP elevated but unclear significance. CK does not support myositis and clinical history is not quite consistent with PMR. Patient denies visual symptoms. Orthostatics checked on ___ and not orthostatic; resumed donepezil 5 mg qHS. ___ evaluated the patient and recommended rehab to re-build her strength. Outpatient neurology and neurocognitive testing already planned by PCP for outpatient follow up. # Possible urinary tract infection: Patient denied dysuria but did report incontinence over the past few weeks. Mild pyuria noted on UA but negative for nitrites and bacteria. Given possible contribution to weakness/fall, treated for UTI with ceftriaxone and completed course. # Right lower back/flank pain On the second day of admission, patient started complaining of some pain in her right side/lower back. It was worse with movement and tender to palpation over her musculature but also CVA tenderness. Did CT abdomen/pelvis to check for nephrolithiasis or other renal pathology and fortunately her imaging was unremarkable. Treated pain with lidocaine patch and Tylenol as she was hesitant to try other pain medications. CHRONIC/STABLE PROBLEMS: # Anemia: Chronic, stable. # Depression: - Continued sertraline, mirtazapine # Memory impairment: - continued donepezil # Glaucoma: - Per PCP notes, patient not using latanoprost, brimonidine but was directed to restart at her last visit; restarted
summary: You were admitted to ___ after a fall and reported weakness. We evaluated you for the fall and fortunately the imaging showed no fracture or other acute injury. We also evaluated you for weakness and there is no clear etiology at this point. It may be related to medications or poor sleep, which seems to have improved in the hospital. Fortunately it doesn't seem to be due to something wrong with your heart. We briefly treated you with antibiotics for a urinary infection but your urine culture didn't grow any bacteria, so we stopped the antibiotics. The physical therapists evaluated you and recommend rehab.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: # Weakness # S/p fall: Patient reports feeling weak for the past 3 days in setting of worsening insomnia, although notes that she has been weak for 7 months. Her strength exam is normal throughout; specifically no proximal or focal weakness. Her perceived weakness and fall may be related to possible medication effects; her PCP notes that the patient is confused about her medications. Patient also with significant history of depression and insomnia that may be contributing. Patient also with osteoarthritis. More acutely, she was thought to have a possible urinary tract infection though this seemed unlikely subsequently with a negative urine culture and borderline symptoms. No evidence of cardiac ischemic. Anemia chronic and stable. Electrolytes normal. Imaging without evidence of acute process to explain her weakness; no fractures. TSH wnl, CK only mildly elevated at 239 (normal: ___, CRP elevated but unclear significance. CK does not support myositis and clinical history is not quite consistent with PMR. Patient denies visual symptoms. Orthostatics checked on ___ and not orthostatic; resumed donepezil 5 mg qHS. ___ evaluated the patient and recommended rehab to re-build her strength. Outpatient neurology and neurocognitive testing already planned by PCP for outpatient follow up. # Possible urinary tract infection: Patient denied dysuria but did report incontinence over the past few weeks. Mild pyuria noted on UA but negative for nitrites and bacteria. Given possible contribution to weakness/fall, treated for UTI with ceftriaxone and completed course. # Right lower back/flank pain On the second day of admission, patient started complaining of some pain in her right side/lower back. It was worse with movement and tender to palpation over her musculature but also CVA tenderness. Did CT abdomen/pelvis to check for nephrolithiasis or other renal pathology and fortunately her imaging was unremarkable. Treated pain with lidocaine patch and Tylenol as she was hesitant to try other pain medications. CHRONIC/STABLE PROBLEMS: # Anemia: Chronic, stable. # Depression: - Continued sertraline, mirtazapine # Memory impairment: - continued donepezil # Glaucoma: - Per PCP notes, patient not using latanoprost, brimonidine but was directed to restart at her last visit; restarted

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o female with history of multiple hospital admissions for abdominal pain with no clear pathologic cause, presents as a transfer ___ with abdominal pain and a new speech impediment. Active issues #Abdominal pain. Patient was admitted for abdominal pain and given 3 CT scans in last month and 2 colonoscopies in the last few months, repeat imaging was not initiated. The records from her outside hospital were reviewed with the GI service and it was decided that further instrumentation or surgery of her GI tract at this point is likely to be of little to no benefit. Specifically it was felt that the sigmoid narrowing on her colonoscopy was unlikely to cause her symptoms as she has no signs of obstruction, clinically or radiographically. Her history is somewhat consistent with an IBS picture and GI considered that the pain could be ___ to spasm. They recommended continuing Linaclotide and begining Levsin prior to meals. For the pain specifically, she was given a lidocaine patch q24h, and started her on a ___ rice, applesauce and toast) diet and now that her pain is relatively controled, we instructed her to slowly advance individual foods one at a time at home. (reverse elimination diet). She received no narcotics this admission and she was instructed to avoid further narcotic use in the future. Stool studies were ordered including o+P c-diff, stool culture, pending at time of discharge. She iwll f/u with Her home gastroenterologist Dr. ___. #Stuttering: Neuro was consulted to evaluate her new onset stuttering (and left sided hemiparesis that spontaneously resolved). The differential that was considered included CVA, demyelinating disease, atypical migraine, metabolic causes and conversion disorder. CVA was less likely given MRI with nonspecific white matter changes and no vascular distribution. Neuro requested a copy of the outside scans to review with an inhouse neuroradiologist. Additionally Neuro recommended that while a CVA is unlikely to have caused the speech impediment she now manifests, optimizing her CVA risk would be warranted. Her HbA1c and lipids were drawn during her stay here and it was noted that her tryglcerides were elevated. Given her abdominal pain, we were reluctant to increase her lipid lowering agents during this acute admission but we recommend that her out patient providers consider addressing this. Speech therapy was also consulted during this admission, and the patient was given list of speech therapy clinics near her home ___. #Psych: Psychiatry was also consulted given her multiple psychiatric diagnosis and history, She has a history of multiple psychiatric disorders including mood disorder NOS, depression, and now a question of conversion disorder. She also has multiple stresses at home (Children are failing out of school) and a distant history of physical abuse ___ years ago). She has recently discontinued psychiatric follow up at ___ ___. The psychiatry team considered conversion disorder as a possibility, however, this is truly a diagnosis of exclusion especially given the severeity of her symptoms. Our psychiatry team recommended contuning her home medication regimen for anxiety including seroquel 200 at night and sertraline 150. Additionally they recommended her taking a ___ mg seroquel dose during the day (as often as TID) prn anxiety. Given her high psychosocial burden she needs close f/u for both psych med Rx and couseling. We spoke to her PCP ___ agreed to continue to RX her psych regimen now and she has an appointment for intake with a social worker at ___ on ___. Transitional issues -triglycerides elevated during this hospitalization, this warrants more aggresive outpatient therapy may consider fibric acid or increase her usual statin dose if she is more stable from an abdominal pain standpoint. -SW f/u scheduled for ___ at ___
summary: You were admitted for workup of your abdominal pain and stuttering and weakness. You were seen by the gastroenterology team who felt that your pain was most likely from colonic spasm and recommended a few medications including levsin prior that may improve this. We also recommended that you try a reverse elimination diet to ensure that the foods you are eating do not aggravate your abdominal pain. You were seen by neurology who felt reassured after examining you and reading your scans. They thought that speech therapy may be able to help with your stuttering. You were seen by speech here who provided you with the name and number to various speech doctors.

text: Brief Hospital Course: HOSPITAL COURSE BY PROBLEM ___ year old female with h/o anemia, dementia, legal blindness, depression, anxiety and dysphagia being transferred from long term care facility CareOne with asymptomatic acute on chronic anemia. # Anemia: acute on chronic and asymptomatic anemia. Found to have Hg 6.2 in ED compared to her reported baseline of ___, and was transfused 2 units PRBCs. Her stool was guaiac negative x2, labs negative for hemolysis and indicative of iron deficiency (ferritin 12%), and there was no e/o active bleeding. Troponins were negative x1, Cr at baseline, and ECG showed no ischemic changes. She was started on PO iron and miralax. After transfusion, Hg stabilized at 9. # Tachycardia: on arrival to floor, she was tachycardic to 110s-130s while agitated. ECG showed sinus tachycardia with no signs of ischemia. Most likely due to holding her metoprolol initially to avoid hypotension in case of active bleeding. Once patient became calm and metop restarted, HR remained in ___. # Dementia: patient was uncooperative with care in the ED, pulling out multiple IVs including during her transfusion, and received IM zyprexa. No fevers, leukocytosis, negative UA, to suggest infectious etiology. On arrival to floor, was again initially agitated, hostile, and pulling out IVs. However, after all lines were removed she calmed down. On discharge, she was at stated baseline and cooperative with interviews and exams. # Blood pressure control: mild/relative hypotension (SBPs high ___ 100s) on admission. Given level of anemia and concern for active bleeding, home amlodipine and metoprolol were held. On arrival to floor, once active bleed was deemed unlikely she was restarted on metoprolol. Pressures remained in 100s-140s afterward. # H/o dysphagia: given mechanical soft diet during this stay # Depression, calcium/vit D deficiency, blindness/glaucoma: continued on home citalopram, vitamin D supplements, and eye drops TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -The patient was found to have iron-deficiency anemia during this hospitalization and started on oral iron supplements. Please follow up to see that this anemia is improving. Recommend IV iron supplementation if oral supplements are insufficient. - Patient started on miralax while on iron # CODE: Full code (per facility/guardian) # CONTACT: - ___: ___ - Guardian- ___ ___
summary: You were admitted from your long-term care facility because your red blood cell levels were unusually low. You were given 2 units of blood in the emergency room to bring this level back up. You were also evaluated for possible bleeding in your gut, which was not found. You were found to have low iron levels in your body, so you were started on oral iron supplements. You were also started on miralax to prevent constipation on this medication. You also had fast heart rates in the hospital. We think this was due to not receiving one of your medications, metoprolol. You did not have signs of infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with L4-S1 laminectomy/fusion and SI fusion in ___, SSS s/p PPM ___ explanted ___ (d/t atrial thrombosis), HTN, DM2, depression, hypothyroidism who presents with recurrent syncope and chest pain, underwent broad workup for neurocardiogenic causes that was ultimately negative. ACUTE ISSUES: =============== #Syncope Was worked up for ACS, seizure, pheochromocytoma, carcinoid syndrome, UTI, intracranial abnormality including bleed, stroke, TIA. Likely vasovagal with potential functional component. All episodes have occurred with stable vital signs and telemetry within normal limits. Orthostatic vital signs positive on one occasion with various systolic BPs during admission ranging from ___ Enalapril discontinued due to lower systolic BP, and pt maintained adequate BPs following this. Will require follow up with autonomic specialist as outpatient. ___ known to benefit patients with functional neurologic d/o and seems to be helping patient. #Altered Mental Status, Unilateral Sensory and Motor Changes Likely functional d/o after extensive work up and consistent with previously recorded episodes. No postictal state, EEGs within normal limits however without capturing event, normal head CT and CTA, and carotid u/s within normal limits. Urine analysis within normal limits. #Depression with anxiety: Passive, severe SI requiring ___ with 1:1 sitter per psychiatry assessment that since improved. Upon discharge, patient is denying SI and has been removed from ___. Home meds were continued other than Lurasidone which was held due to lack of availability as non-formulary. Per psychiatry recommendations, holding this Patient later developed visual hallucinations of a man lingering in her room and was re-assessed by psychiatry. Patient has had these hallucinations before due to extensive PTSD. She was started on risperidone for this. #MSSA Bacteremia Has history of MSSA Bacteremia that was diagnosed at recent hospitalization with planned 6 week course of IV cefazolin; likely related to hardware in her back which likely needs to be removed in near future. Will follow-up with OPAT for further evolution. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #Back ___ weakness/numbness: Took Tylenol, Gabapentin, Oxycodone prn for pain #Hyperlipidemia: Continued home atorvastatin 40 mg po #Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine 150 mcg daily #Hypertension: Discontinued home enalapril 30 mg daily due to well-controlled blood pressures without it. #Diabetes: Held home metformin 1g twice daily while inpatient, re-started on discharge TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: =============== [ ] Discharged with Zio patch, to be followed-up and reviewed by PCP [ ] consider follow up with autonomic specialist regarding ongoing syncopal episodes and intermittent orthostatic hypotension [ ] follow up with psychiatrist about depression, hallucinations, and suicidality - can discuss restarting Latuda at this appointment [ ] follow up with outpatient spinal surgery about hardware in back due to likely contribution to bacteremia [ ] follow up with PCP about adjusting BP medications in light of varying BPs (Enalapril held on discharge) [ ] ensure follow up with OPAT for bacteremia [ ] consider follow up with chronic pain for ongoing low back pain - new medication(s): risperidone 0.5mg BID #CODE: full presumed #CONTACT: Deb from church: ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for chest pain and for further work-up of episodes during which you lost consciousness. We did tests multiple tests to look for a cause of your syncopal episodes, including: an ultrasound of your heart, monitoring of your heart's electrical activity, lab work to look for hormonal causes of syncope, and blood pressure monitoring in different positions. All of this work-up was normal, which is very reassuring! We suspect these episodes are caused by dysfunction of your nervous system and would like you to see a neurology doctor that specializes in this type of problem once you leave the hospital. We also think these episodes may be more frequent temporarily on a ___ because of concern about your suicidal ideation and were extensively evaluated by the psychiatry team.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY: ================ ___ is a ___ year old woman with history of Bipolar disorder, anxiety, OCD tendencies who presented with altered mental status in the setting of likely ingestion of unknown amount of klonopin, Adderall, quetiapine, and possibly other medications. ACUTE ISSUES =============== #Toxic metabolic encephalopathy #Acute Toxic Ingestion #Suicide Attempt Patient was monitored for toxic ingestion, with CIWA, EKG (QTc, QRS monitoring), benzodiazepine withdrawal monitoring, serotonin syndrome monitoring, 1:1 observation, all without significant findings. Mental status improved significantly without intervention. We have been holding all psychiatric medications. Etiology of toxic metabolic encephalopathy is most likely toxic ingestion in the setting of possible suicide attempt. Per collateral, patient has been depressed for several months and has made prior statements about suicide/taking all her medications. Patient also had a prior ingestion/ probable attempt about 2 months ago. Patient is unsure about the events preceding this hospitalization, but believes she took her entire bottle of klonopin and Adderall. Psychiatry followed the patient. She was on ___. She was cooperative and appropriate through the admission. Per recommendations of the psychiatry consult liaison team, the patient will be discharged to inpatient psychiatry for further management and medication adjustment as needed #Weakness #Elevated CK Patient presented with significant UE and abdominal weakness, as well as shoulder pain on active and passive movement. Initially was unable to lift arms more than a few inches of the bed and unable to stand/walk, but has had significant improvement over time with physical therapy. On discharge, she is able to walk and move arms without difficulty. Etiology of weakness is unclear but given elevated CK of 1218 during this hospitalization, it is possible that muscle pain is residual to to tonic-clonic seizure activity in the setting of toxic ingestion or prolonged down-time after ingestin. Possible history of incontinence and tongue biting also support seizure, although as this occurred in the setting of multiple medication ingestion, it is unlikely that the patient has an underlying seizure disorder. There was no evidence of seizure during the hospitalization. Pain was managed to little effect with Tylenol and Lidocaine patches on shoulders. #Insomnia Patient received ramelteon for sleep. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #Diagnosis of bipolar disorder All psychiatric medications were held. Psychiatry followed the patient. The team attempted to obtain collateral information from her psychiatrist, Dr ___ (___) but was not able to reach them. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== -Medications to be adjusted in inpatient psychiatry -Despite multiple attempts, the psychiatry and primary teams were unable to contact patient's outpatient psychiatrist during this admission (Dr ___ (___) -Encourage careful ambulation
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you ingested a large amount of your medications. You also had weakness in your arms and abdominal muscles. In the hospital, we monitored you for any side effects from your medication ingestions. We performed EKGs and checked labs to ensure that you were not having any dangerous side effects from your medications. You were seen by physical therapy to assist with your muscle weakness, and you had significant improvement while you were here. You were also seen by the psychiatry team in order to clarify the events that led to your hospitalization and determine where would be safest for you to be discharged after hospitalization. They determined that going to an ___ facility would be the safest choice. You will be discharged to inpatient psychiatry to continue clarifying the events leading up to your hospitalization in a safe environment, as well as to adjust your psychiatric medications as needed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F, hx HTN/HLD/DM2, OA, anx/depr, Parkinsonism/tremor, dementia, somatoform disorder, presents with acute SOB (resolved by the time of arriving the floor) and abdominal pain in context of constipation. # Dyspnea. Unclear. Resolved by the time patient arrived the floor. Her troponin was flat, making ACS unlikely. D-dimer was negative, making VTE unlikely. Lung exam and CXR were not suggestive of pneumonia or other intrapulmonary process. Telemetry did not capture any concerning arrhythmia while in the hospital. An outpatient event monitor would be helpful in this setting. It is also possible that patient had an anxiety/panic attack as mentioned by her family. Her symptoms were validated but reassured. # Abdominal pain/constipation. LFTs were within normal. UA was unremarkable. Based on her exam as well as the abdominal X-ray, she was constipated. The opioids that she was on could certainly have contributed to her constipation. Patient had a large bowel movement while in the hospital. She was given simethicone for gas discomfort. # Osteoarthritis and chronic pain. She was continued on home Tylenol, MS ___, and oxycodone. # T2DM. She was on insulin sliding scale and diabetic diet while she was in the hospital. # Hypertension. She was continued on home amlodipine and atenolol. # Hyperlipidemia. She was continued on home simvastatin. # Anxiety/Depression. She was continued on mirtazapine and lorazepam. However, it is unclear if patient was taking lorazepam at home. # GERD. She was continued on home omeprazole and famotidine. # Parkinsonism, tremor: continue pramipexole. Transitional issues: [] consider event monitor [] managing underlying anxiety [] encourage more aggressive bowel regimen
summary: You were admitted because you felt short of breath and constipated. We gave you medicine to encourage bowel movements, and you felt better after a bowel movement. Your shortness of breath went away while you were here. Your labs did not show heart attach, pnuemonia, or clots. Your breathing may have been affected by pressure from your bowels or by anxiety. You had a bowel movement while you are in the hospital. You should make sure you are taking a stool softener while you are on narcotic pain medication. Simethicone was started for gas discomfort You should make sure to talk to Dr. ___ to discuss anxiety management. You states that you are NOT taking lorazepam (Ativan) at home. Therefore, it is not included in your medication list.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with history of dementia, HTN, HLD, CKD, IDDMII presenting with abdominal pain and poor appetite, found to have ___. # Acute kidney injury on CKD: Ms. ___ has baseline ___ ~ 1.5, but presented with Cr of 2.8 in setting of poor PO intake and likely osmotic diuresis from hyperglycemia. Renal U/S was normal. She received IVF with improvement in her Cr to 2.2. Lisinopril was held and was resumed given her BP continued to be elevated. She was encouraged PO intake. # Hyponatremia: Likely hypovolemic hyponatremia as above. Admit Na was 131 and improved to ___ s/p IVF. # Protein-calorie malnutrition: Patient with poor oral intake at home. Nutrition was consulted. She was encouraged oral intake. Nutrition recommended Glucerna TID. 2 packets of beneprotein at each meal added to soups, broth, oatmeal, etc. # Abdominal pain: Patient with chronic abdominal pain, reported abdominal pain prior to presentation that has now resolved. No needed medications. # Thrombocytopenia: Unclear etiology, patient with variable platelets in past, down to 140s in ___. Was stable with no need for continued workup # Goals of care: apparently, Ms. ___ has had steady decline in overall cognitive, physical, emotional functioning over the past ___ years. The daughter attributes this to significant depression - worsened with the recent deaths of her 2 of her 6 children. She has had already lost 4 of her 6 children. The patient herself seems to have lost the will to live - and continues to have poor dysthymia despite being on Mirtazapine. The family was interested in initiating discussions about hospice with her primary care doctor. They feel that these hospitalization is not beneficial for the patient (and also tough for the family - the daughter of whom has lung CA). Certainly, dehydration and malnutrition will be ongoing issue - and hospitalization will not likely benefit her given these circumstances. The family was inquiring about additional assistance at home. To initiate assistance, ___ was provided and initiation of hospice should be considered as outpt. summary: As you know, you were admitted with dehydration and had evidence of kidney insufficiency. You had stomach pains - but workup here did not find anything wrong. Also the pain had resolved/disappeared while here in the emergency room and hospital. As you know, the dehydration is due to not drinking enough fluids. You also show signs of malnutrition (weight loss) which has been going on for years. There was no evidence of a recent disease that could account for your lack of appetite. It is likely due to depression - which is being treated with medication (Mirtazapine). We discussed with your family and will arrange for visiting nurse for you. We have also provided with Trazodone as needed for sleep.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ ___ M with sig PMHx history of untreated anxiety, depression, and bipolar disorder, with a prior suicide attempt, who was admitted after having suicidal ideations and agitation. #Suicidal ideation: The patient has a long history of anxiety and depression with ?bipolar disorder, and has been off of medications for the past ___ years. He presented to the emergency department with SI, and had plans to cut himself. His girlfriend called the police because he was "going crazy". He was placed in restraints and chemically restrained in the ED due to extreme agitation, and stated that he had a previous suicide attempt though did not elaborate. He endorsed homicidal ideation but denied any hallucinations. Given this, he was seen by psychiatry who recommended involuntary admission via Section 12a given his risk for harm and active SI. He was admitted to the general medicine service, where he was started in Depakote abd Zyprexa. He continued to experience grandiosity, stating to the nurse that "Gold told me I was the ___. He had initially refused medication but on the day of discharge took Depakote. He was discharged to ___ for ongoing management of decompensated bipolar disorder and SI. #Sterile pyuria: The pt was treated empirically with CTX and azithro in the ED given asymptomatic pyuria. He was negative for G/C. His urine culture was however positive for gram positive alpha hemolytic colonies, c/w alpha streptococcus or lactobacillus. He is currently asymptomatic. # TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== - pt will need ongoing medication titration for bipolar disorder, including outpatient psychiatric follow up after discharge.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you experience suicidal ideation. You were seen in the emergency department by our psychiatrists, who felt that you were at risk of harm to yourself and because of this required involuntary admission to the hospital for treatment. You were prescribed a medication called Depakote (divalproex) for your untreated bipolar disorder. This medication is very important to take, and will work to help control the symptoms of your bipolar disorder. While you were in the emergency department, you were treated with two antibiotics, ceftriaxone and azithromycin for a possible urinary tract infection given a urine test showing a possible infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is ___ w/PMH of depression (no prior suicide attempts) and opiod abuse ___ sobriety per pt) here with seizure in setting of intentional Ultram overdose after found out boyfriend's infidelity. . # Seizures/Ultram Overdose: Seizures were most likely medication (ultram)-induced seizures but some concern for complete serotonin syndrome. No known prior history of seizures. Toxicology was consulted and followed pt at the time of transfer to MICU and recommended to monitor lower extremity clonus and to hold home cymbalta. Serial EKGs were performed to monitor intervals have been stable. Benzo prn (versed gtt)was used for seizures and agitation; however no additional seizures occured after arrival to MICU. Pt was stablized and was able to be called out to the floor on ___ . # Airway Protection: electively intubated for airway protection in the setting of seizures in the E.D, was extubated soon after in the ICU and tolerated room air well. . #Depression: unclear history and no previous known SI/HI or hospitalizations but now with apparent attempt in setting of social stressor. Psych has seen the patient and sectioned 12 her after code purple was called for agitation and threatening behavoir. Currently denies any suicidal thoughts but remains agitated at times. Needs inpatient psych treatment once stable. Pt was able to be transferred to the floor for continued monitoring and management while awaiting psych bed placement. Pt was also restarted on cymbalta (low dose of 20mg daily) and gabapentin 300 TID initially when brought to the floor; medications were titrated per psych recs as needed in inpt psych. Gabapentin was eventually increased back to the pt's home dose of 800mg, 800mg and 1600mg (at night). . Initially plan was to transfer to ___ psych facility; however, upon reevaluation by psychiatry after transfer to the floor it was felt that pt no longer was actively suicidal and that she agreed to a concrete plan of outpt follow. She no longer met ___ criteria. It was felt that the overdose was an act of impulsivity and not consistent with a suicide attempt. Therefore, psychiatric hospitalization deemed unnecessary. As the pt was medically stable and requesting to be discharge home, she was discharged under own care with planned outpt follow-up with her PCP, psychiatrist/therapist at ___ ___, as well as the pain specialist at ___. Pt also agreed to continue with AA/NA and home group for substance abuse treatment. She also agreed to call ___ or go to the nearest ED if she was feeling worse or developing thoughts of harming herself and was in agreement w/plan to d/c tramadol.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you had a seizure as a result of overdosing on tramadol and their was concern that this was a suicide attempt or gesture of self harm. You required intubation and admission to the ICU for stabilization of your condition. You were able to be extubated after your condition improved. You were seen by psychiatry who felt that you were initially unsafe to discharge as your overdose was viewed initially as a suicide attempt or gesture of self harm. For this reason, after psychiatry's initial evaluation, you were received a ___ meaning that you were not allowed to leave the hospital setting over concerns that you may harm yourself or others. However, your condition improved and after additional evaluation and assessment it was determined that you could be discharged home with close follow-up and in the care of your family.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PRIMARY REASON FOR HOSPITALIZATION: ___ year old woman with a H/O depression s/p purposeful suicide attempt after taking multiple pills, intubated for increasing lethargy, with hypotension and seizures. ACTIVE DIAGNOSES: # Drug overdose: The patient was known to have purposefully ingested multiple medications and an unknown amount of alcohol. She was admitted to the ICU and aggressively resuscitated with interventions including intubation/mechanical ventilation, central IV access, vasopressor support, IV fluids, and nutrition support. Toxicology was consulted. She was given N-acetyl-cysteine therapy for acetaminophen toxicity. The patient stabilized, was extubated, and had her central line removed before being transferred to the general medicine floor. The patient was also monitored on a ___ protocol for alcohol withdrawal. It was determined that her polypharmacy ingestion included: Acetaminophen, Citalopram, Buproprion, Amoxicilin, Clonazepam, Methocarbamol, and EtOH. The patient can be evaluated at followup with her primary care physician to evaluate and manage any residual effects of these substances. # Seizures secondary to overdose: In the MICU the patient experienced generalized tonic-clonic seizures, likely secondary to her buproprion or citalopram ingestion. She was already intubated and required a midazolam drip to break the seizure. She was evaluated by Neurology, and an EEG was completed. The finalized results were pending at the time of discharge; however the Neurology team attributed her neurological symptoms to her exogenous substances and the patient was asymptomatic with no tremors at time of discharge. There are no specific followup requirements from a Neurology perspective. # Anemia of unclear origin: The patient was found to have Hct 33.5 which remained relatively stable during her stay, but with an unclear baseline. The patient had no evidence of GI or other bleeding during her stay and did not require blood product transfusions. Her anemia can be further investigated by her Primary Care Physician as an outpatient. # Suicide attempt, secondary to mood disorder: The patient was evaluated by psychiatry, who recommended inpatient psychiatric treatment once medically stable for discharge. The patient was placed on a ___ hold and 1:1 observation throughout her admission. Her home psychiatric medications were held. She was discharged to an inpatient psychiatric facility. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: She was transferred to an inpatient psychiatric unit for treatment of the conditions underlying the intentional suicide attempt. The patient's family has been visiting the hospital and is aware of the plan. The patient remains Full Code.
summary: You were admitted due to overdose of your medications, which caused you to become very lethargic, your breathing to slow down, and your blood pressure to drop. You were admitted to the intensive care unit with a breathing tube to assist your ventilations, and received IV fluids as well as medications to maintain your blood pressure and prevent toxic effects of the substances. You also had a seizure, which we stopped with IV medications. Once you were stable, we removed the breathing tube and moved you to a general medical floor. You were also evaluated by Psychiatry regarding the events leading to your hospital stay, and after being medically stable you were transferred to care under their recommendations.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ was admitted to the trauma team after a suicide attempt. He was taken to the operating room to repair the enterotomy. The patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the floor for close monitoring. Urology was consulted for insertion of styrofoam into penis and they recommend a cystoscopy as an outpatient, although the patient had no further urological complaints during the hospitalization. Incidentally the patient was noted to be hepatitis C positive with high viral count. Hepatology was consulted who recommended outpatient follow-up. IV ciprofloxacin and flagyl were started postoperatively as there was open bowel in the abdomen for 24 hours. Post operative day four the patient was advancing his diet and was able to tolerate PO medication. A PICC line was placed post operative day 7 for antibiotics. Post operative day 8 the wound was opened and packed. Post op day 9 the patient was febrile to 102 a fever work up was done and a CT scan showed a ___ fistula through transverse colon to midline. He was started on vanc and zosyn and then per Infectious Disease recs, changed to ceftriaxone and PO flagyl. The following day the patient became febrile again and started growing GPC's from the PICC. The PICC was removed, and the patient remained afebrile throughout the hospitalization after the PICC was removed. The patient also received a TEE to rule out endocarditis. The echo was normal. Pyschiatry was also involved in the patients care at this point to evaluate the patient. Blood cultures grew out S. viridans, and infectious disease was able to make final recommendations on antibiotics which included 1 gm IV Ceftriaxone Q24H ___ - ___ 750 mg PO levofloxacin QD ___ - ___ 500 mg PO flagyl Q8H ___ - ___. The patient self discontinued his IV access 2 days before switching from ceftriaxone to levofloxacin. The levofloxacin was started early. The patient was afebrile, tolerating a diet, voiding and pain was under control prior to discharge. The midline fistula was managed with an ostomy pouch and output had been slowing down.
summary: You were admitted to ___ after stabbing yourself with a foreign body and were taken to the operating room for an exploratory laparotomy and suturing of enterotomy and placement of drain. Your post operative course was complicated by a blood stream infection. You also have developed a fistula, which is a tract from your intestines out through your skin. You should continue to pouch with an ostomy appliance this while there is stool coming out however it may close up eventually. Incidentally, it was noted that you have an active hepatitis C infection, and the liver doctors recommend ___ treatment for this at an outpatient Liver clinic.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ woman with a past medical history significant for afib not on anticoagulation (was previously on eliquis but stopped due to expensive cost) , hearing loss, HTN, HLD, anxiety/depression, and a chronic indwelling foley catheter (with recurrent ESBL UTI) who presented with 5 days of L monocular vision loss. In the ER she was evaluated by ophthalmology who found evidence of central retinal artery occlusion with cilioretinal sparing. CRP was checked for question of giant cell arteritis in the ER. # L monocular vision loss: -Evaluated by ophthalmology in the ER and found to have a central retinal artery occlusion with cilioretinal sparing. Giant cell arteritis was also considered however her lack of headaches , no jaw claudication/temporal tenderness this felt to be less likely. Her CRP was only very minimally elevated to 6.1, ESR currently pending. -Given her known history of atrial fibrillation, the etiology of embolus was strongly felt to be due to atrial fibrillation -The patient reported being on eliquis but was taken off about a year ago due to it being expensive/ not felt to be needed at the time by her PCP. Given that she tolerated the medication well, we called her outpatient pharmacy who checked the medication cost against her insurance. It was cleared to be covered fully by her insurance and therefore eliquis would be free of cost to her. Therefore we started 5mg BID -Opthalmology recommended follow up with their retinal specialist Dr. ___ ___ weeks. We called their office to schedule her for an appointment -MRI brain was not done given that she had a ct scan of the head which did not show any acute infarcts and would have likely shown an infarct if present given that the symptoms were at least 5-days old -Echocardiogram was also deferred given that the patient has known A-fib already #Non-reccurent Falls: -Patient reported only a recent fall 2-weeks ago because she tripped over the bathtub. Before this her last fall was over ___ years ago. She ambulates with a walker to be safe but is steady. She lives alone in a one level apartment -The patient was able to ambulate independently on her own in the hospital and OT deferred consult given that the patient was able to perform all tasks appropriately without aid #HTN: -Patient had some elevated blood pressure readings while in-house. We would like her PCP to ___ the blood pressures in the outpatient setting to see if an additional agent is necessary #Hearing loss: -Patient found to be profoundly hard of hearing to the point where she is reading lips. Would recommend audiology evaluation for hearing aids if not already done (PCP to follow up) #Eliquis : As noted above, eliquis prescription ran through her local pharmacy who stated that her insurance will cover this medication fully without a co-pay. Should this change the patient should let her PCP know so that an alternative anticoagulation agent can be considered ***************** Transitional issues: 1. Patient needs f/u with Dr. ___ specialist (Message left for scheduler), call ___ if patient does not hear from them 2. Stroke follow up with Dr. ___ ___ 3. F/u with PCP ___ in ___ weeks 4. Consideration of hearing aids 5. Blood pressure check with PCP ___ on ___: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Citalopram 20 mg PO DAILY 2. Omeprazole 20 mg PO DAILY 3. methenamine hippurate 1 gram oral BID 4. Oxybutynin 10 mg PO DAILY 5. Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg PO BID 6. ALPRAZolam 0.5 mg PO TID 7. TraZODone 25 mg PO BID
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had vision disturbances particularly in your left eye for a few days. You were evaluated by ophthalmology while in the ER who found you to have a condition called: CENTRAL RETINAL ARTERY OCCLUSION. This is essentially a small stroke caused by a blood clot in the artery of the L eye. You have lost vision in this eye. We hope that with time some of your vision will return. The cause of your stroke is most likely from atrial-fibrillation. We have started you on a blood thinning medication called ELIQUIS (also called apixaban) . You must take this medication every day TWICE a day to prevent further strokes.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH significant for bilateral cerebellar/pontine ischemic stroke ___ with residual L>R dysmetria, migraines, recurrent DVT/PE on Lovenox, HTN, HLD, hypothyroidism and depression who presents to ___ with slurred speech and left sided weakness. # Slurred speech/ L sided weakness/ dysarthria/ Prior CVA: Initial concern for new ischemic stroke related to VTE but no evidence of this on examination or imaging. Reportedly with facial drooping on EMS evaluation and in ED but this resolved upon admission to the medical floor. A code STROKE was called in the ED and patient was evaluated by Neurology who determined that she did not have acute ischemia. Her symptoms were felt to most likely represent a toxic metabolic encephalopathy +/- recrudence of prior neurologic deficits. Differential also included medication side effect (patient was instructed to reduce her clonazepam at her last discharge but has not), untreated OSA, uncontrolled depression or infectious etiologies. Infectious workup was negative and she remained afebrile throughout admission. Toxicology screen and RPR negative. TSH elevated at 52, vitamin B12 decreased at 232. She was started on vitamin B12 supplementation at 2000mcg per day for 2 weeks after which she should be transitioned to 1000mcg per day. Her levothyroxine was also increased to 200mcg daily. She will need a repeat TSH level in ___ weeks. She should be referred for a sleep study as an outpatient. # Headache: Patient has a history of migraines and the unilateral nature of headache makes migraine most likely. summary: You were admitted because of increased weakness, fatigue and difficulty speaking. Our initial concern was that you were having another stroke but your imaging, lab work and physical exam showed that you did NOT have a stroke. You were followed by the Neurologists who also agreed that you were NOT having a stroke. Your symptoms were likely due to a combination of undertreated hypothyroidism, depression and as a side effect of your clonazepam. We held your clonazepam and you improved. We also increased your levothyroxine dose. You will need to recheck your thyroid hormone levels in ___ weeks to see the effect from this dosage change. We have also started you on a vitamin B12 supplement as you were found to be deficient. Vitamin B12 deficiency can also cause extremity numbness and confusion if longstanding.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male, with history of suicidal ideation, polysubstance abuse (cocaine, heroin, methadone), presenting with syncope, hypotension, admitted to ICU for hypotension. # Hypotension: Patient found to be hypotensive in the ED, seemingly improved with IVF. Most likely hypotensive in the setting of poor PO intake. Urine specific gravity elevated at 1.027 with 10 ketones, likely representing poor PO intake. Patient had improvement with IVF, supporting, and was administered anti-HTN in the ED. Other etiologies include sepsis, as patient with history of IV Drug use and could have transient bacteremia in setting of injection. Patient also with right knee effusion, however does not appear to be septic on physical exam. No cough for aspiration, and no fevers. Could also be hypotensive in the setting of heroin overdose. Unlikely to be cardiac in setting of normal cardiac enzymes, and normal TTE (no valvular disease). His lisinopril was held. His BP normalized. He also had a VQ scan which was negative. . # Polysubstance Abuse: Intention for suicide. Urine tox + for benzo (admits clonazepam), opiates (heroin), methadone, and cocaine. Currently feeeling as though not withdrawing, however received methadone in the ED. Seen by psychiatry in the ED who initially recommended inpatient once medically stable. He was then seen by three other pyschiatrists who confirmed that there was no pyschiatric contra-intradication to discharge. He was continued on methadone at 30 mg per day which was the dose that was confirmed with the ___ clinic. . # His psychiatric meds were held while he was an inpatient given his initial sedation. He then remained off then while on medicine. Given his complicated discharge, this was was not re-started on discharge. . # Hypertension: Patient with now prsenting with hypotension as above. Received in the ED. - This was held. # Chronic Pain/Neuropathy: Continue Gabapentin 800 mg TID # Osteoarhtritis: Patient with history of OA, with right knee pain. On exam, unable for full flexion. He was previously on narcotics but given the history of broken narcotics contract at ___ these were held and he was continued solely on methadone. . # HCV: Patient with history of Genotype 3a, per patient no prior treatment. No evidence of cirrhosis. Last EGD ___ without varicies. He reports past history of enceaphalopathy, likely not hepatic. # History of Seizures: Unclear if primary process, or secondary to polysubstance abuse. Patient describes history, currently on topiarmate. - Continue topiramate BID # Hematuria: Likely in the setting of traumatic foley and therapeutic lovenox received in the ED. Initial U/A without hematuria and therefore was not apparent on admission. This was not an issue during this admission. . # THREATS TO STAFF: During his ___ admission he reported to his hospitalist that he would "shoot the doctor that stopped his oxycodone". This was reported to psychiatry. He then reported to an RN that he understood the shooting at ___ and he understood why patients feel that way. Security was called and he was placed on a security watch. He recanted his threats to staff. He was not thought to be a threat to others. A team meeting was held. He was given the option of a cab voucher to a ___ ___. From there he chose to make his way to ___. Pt also advised that if he made threats to to staff he would no longer be able to receive care at ___. Pt also advised that he will not be receiving a prescription for narcotics upon discharge. . DISCHARGE MEDICATIONS: Pt informed that Dr. ___ will continue the treatment plan that he had in place while an inpatient which consisted of topamax/apap/ibuprofen and neurontin without the addition of any other medications. He demonstrated an understanding or this.
summary: You were admitted with syncope (fainting), chest pain and hypotension. You had a VQ scan that was negative for a pulmonary embolus. Your blood cultures returned positive for bacteria but it was coag negative staph which was considered to be contaminant. You then said that you understood why the patient at ___ killed a doctor but then said that you did not mean to threaten the staff in any way. You were continued on your outpatient dose of methadone 30 mg daily confirmed with ___ ___. You were seen by psychiatry because you expressed thoughts of wanting to commit suicide. You were seen by three different psychiatrists who thought that it was safe to discharge you from the hospital and they did not think that it was in your best interest to be admitted to a psychiatric facility.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Syncope: patient had a witnessed syncopal episode, without head trauma, after walking ~ 10 minutes and poor p.o intake that morning. EKG was unremarkable and troponins were not elevated,echo wnl making a cardiac etiology less likely. Stress test did not reveal CAD. Given that he had evidence of atherosclerosis on carotid US, he was started on aspirin and simvastatin. Patient was seen by neurology who were not concerned for neurologic etiology. He was orthostatic on admission, he recieved IVF and orthostatis resolved. His syncope was felt to be orthostatic in nature. He was encouraged to continue to take good oral fluids. Diabetes: patient was found to be hyperglycemic on lab evaluation, with HgA1C of 6.6. This was discussed with the patient. He will start metformin on ___ (48 hour wait after recieving contrast for stress test). He can recieve education from the ___ diabetes nurse while inpatient on ___ 4. He should continue to follow up with his PCP on discharge from psychiatric facility for continued care. Ankylosing spondylitis: he is on enbrel 50mg IM weekly(on ___ followed by outpatient rheumatologist, and has had difficulties with back pain with this. Also had recent MVA. He has also has a history of multiple narcotic prescriptions from different providers. He reported having been told he had mass at t10, however OSH imaging (___, ___) showed discitis and spinal stenosis. He had CT on ___ on admission here which showed Acute appearing superior endplate derformity of the T10 vertebral body, for which pain control is recommended. He is weight bearing and ambulatory. He should follow up with his PCP and the pain clinic associated with his ___ clinic on d/c from inpatient psychiatry. . Depression: He expressed suicidal ideation, and was thus seen by psychiatry who recommended inpatient psychiatry to which he was agreeable avoidance of z-sleep aids and avoidance of benzodiazepines. His lorazepam was discontinued as was his ambien. He is being discharged to inpatient psychiatry. He is medically cleared.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had passed out. This was felt to be because of not having had enough to eat and drink that day. You got some fluids and you improved. You had a stress test to look at how your heart was functioning, and this showed that your heart was working well. We did start you on two new medications called simvastatin and aspirin to continue protecting your heart. We identified that you also have diabetes. You were started on a new medication for diabetes called metformin on ___. You were seen by the psychiatrists who felt that due to the level of your depression, you would benefit from being admitted to the psychiatric hospital until you improve.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year old male h/o CAD s/p CABG (6 vsl in ___, MI s/p DES to ___, HTN, GERD, Anxiety, Depression issues presenting with chest pain and shortness of breath. . # Chest Pain: CP is consistant with stable angina likely complicated by anxiety/depression from his psychosocial stressors (see below). He reports baseline ___ chest pain at home, which had increased leading up to admission. Troponins were negative, and cath was clean for new coronary disease, although he was noted to have SVG to OM stenosis with 9 mm aneurysm (see further details in pertinent results). Pt continued to have ___ pain in-house after cath, so we increased his home imdur from 30mg to 60mg daily, and put him on standing tylenol. Pain was back down to baseline ___ the following morning after cath. He was continued on home plavix 75mg daily, asa 325, rosouvastatin 40mg daily, lisinopril 20mg daily. We changed his home metoprol tartrate to toprol-xl 100mg po daily. His home imdur was increased to 60mg daily as above. . # Depression/anxiety: Pt is known to suffer from anxiety and depressive disorders, which have increased recently with the death of his wife last year and subsequent death of his son 2 weeks ago. He reports depression/anxiety attacks and difficulty coping with current emotional situation which likely have exacerabated his chest pain. Pt insisted that he did not have feelings of self harm or harm toward others. Psychiatry was consulted who recommended increasing his home citalopram from 20mg to 30mg daily. He was also sent home with a small prescription for ativan 1mg q8h prn for anxiety. He was encouraged to follow up with his outpatient therapist at ___ who he will set up an appointment with. It may also be reasonable to set up him with outpatient psychiatric follow up for further medication management. From a psychiatric standpoint, we felt he was safe for dishcarge home. . # HTN : We continued home meds of lisinopril, and changed his home metoprolol tartrate to toprol-XL 100mg daily. Pressures were controlled over night after his cath. . Inactive Issues: . # HL : continued Rosuvastatin 40mg daily . # Gout : continued Allopurinol ___ mg daily and Colchicine 0.6 mg daily. . # GERD : Continued ranitidine 300 mg BID . Follow up issues: -Depression/anxiety should be readdressed with his PCP follow up and consider referral to psychiatrist. Pt should also follow up with his outpatient therapist.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. You had a cardiac catheterization which did not reveal any significant new coronary disease. We have increased one of your medications (imdur) to help with your pain. We also recommend you take tylenol every 6 hours for the next few days to help with the pain. You should not take more than 3 grams of this daily, however. We also stopped your metoprolol tartrate (lopressor) and started you on Toprol-XL (long acting metoprolol) at 100mg by mouth daily. You also saw one of our psychiatrists who recommended increasing your citalopram to 30mg daily. We strongly recommend seeing your therapist at ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ is a ___ male who was found down after being found down ___ tylenol ingestion as part of suicide attempt. # ACETAMINOPHEN INGESTION / LIVER FAILURE: The patient was transferred from an outside hospital for management and transplant evaluation and admitted on ___ after being found unresponsive in his home and intubated. He was managed in the ICU for 5 days with evaluation by toxicology, transplant surgery and hepatology. He received 6 days of NAC with initial worsening of his LFTs associated with an elevated acetaminophen level. In preparation for possible transplant, he received 2U FFP and 1mg IV vit K. However, ultimately this was followed by a steady downtrending of his LFTs, normalization of his INR and improvement in his mental status. He was extubated ___. Thorough workup for other causes of liver failure was found to be negative. He was transferred to the floor on ___ for further monitoring and continued to improve. NAC was stopped on ___ and pt was deemed to be medically stable on ___ for transfer to psychiatry. # S. VIRIDANS BACTERMIA: The patient was found to be bacteremic on initial (___) blood cultures. The patient was started on vancomycin on ___ and this was switched to ceftriaxone on ___ with a plan for a two week course (tentative end date ___. His bacteremia cleared quickly and all subsequent blood cultures were negative. ID was consulted and theorized that recent dental procedure instrumentation (which the patient endorsed) or oral trauma while unconscious/intubated were possible etiologies. The patient did deny current mouth or tooth pain and on exam did not have obvious tooth decay. He had a TTE without evidence of vegetation or valvular abnormality. Blood cultures will be repeated on an outpatient basis following completion of a two week antibiotic course in order to determine the utility of TEE, and the patient will have a Panorex to evaluate for oral infection. # PANCYTOPENIA: On initial presentation, the patient had a relatively normal CBC, but progressively developed pancytopenia through the first few days of his hospitalization. This was presumed to be secondary to inflammation from liver failure with component of marrow suppression from sepsis. An HIV was checked and was negative. The patient's CBC fluctuated through his stay but he required no blood products. His CBC should be rechecked as an outpatient. # BIPOLAR D/O: The patient was followed by psychiatry and social work during his hospitalization. His home medications were held per psychiatry recommendations. He was monitored by a sitter throughout his stay. He was discharged to an inpatient psychiatric facility following medical stability. # Transitional - PICC placed, will continue ceftriaxone to complete a two week coursece (day 1 = ___ through ___ - ID recommends repeat blood cultures ___ days after completing antibiotics; if positive, the patient will require a TEE to evaluate for endocarditis - The patient will also require a Panorex to evaluate for dental infection - Repeat CBC one week after discharge given pancytopenia during hospitalization; continue to trend appropriately until full recovery achieved. - Repeat LFTs one week after discharge to ensure continuing resolution of liver injury
summary: You were admitted to the ICU following an overdose of tylenol, and you were treated supportively and with medications to reduce the injury to your liver. You were also treated with antibiotics for a blood infection that we found when you were admitted. You were evaluated by a number of teams, including the liver transplant team. Ultimately, you improved enough to be moved to a normal floor, where we continued to monitor your clinical progress and improvement. You were ultimately deemed medically well enough to be discharged to a facility to receive further psychiatric care. You will continue antibiotics there and will be evaluated again to ensure that your blood infection has cleared and your liver has recovered.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M ___ HCV cirrhosis (c/b portal hypertension), depression, Alcohol abuse, HTN, ___ syndrome (demyelination of Corpus callosum associated with alcohol use), recent ___, who was referred to ___ with abnormal labs found to have ___ and complicated cystitis. #COMPLICATED CYSTITIS: Patient visited PCP ___ ___ and labs were checked. UCx from this visisted revealed E.coli, susceptible to ceftazidime. Given abnormal labs and positive u/a, pt was sent toe ED for evaluation. Pt reported urinary urgency. Exam was not concerning for prostatitis or pyelonephritis. Due to possibility of pyelonephritis and obstruction, renal ultrasound was obtained and was normal with no obstruction or hydronephrosis. He received Zosyn in the ED and was switched to Ceftazidime IV to complete 10 day course (last day ___ for complicated UTI. His WBC downtrended from 12.0 to 9.3. Urinary sx improved. ___: Pt presented with ___, with creatinine of 1.6 (baseline <1.0). The patient reported nausea/vomiting and poor PO intake for many days prior to admission. Etiology of his ___ was thought to be prerenal (especially given his hypovolemia and hyponatremia). ___ resolved with intravenous fluids and albumin 75gm x2. #Hyponatremia: Likely hypoosmolar hypovolemic due to pre renal/dehydration. Normalized after albumin and fluid. #Depression: Pt is debilitated by chronic depression and anhedonia. At home, he has trouble sleeping and reports he has not left his room for weeks. He was initiated on sertraline 50 mg daily. CHRONIC PROBLEMS: #EtOH/HCV Cirrhosis: History of complications with encephalopathy and non-occlusion PVT but no history of varices. MELD-Na 29 on admission. MELD-Na 19 on ___. Hep C viral load undetectable. -Continued lactulose, thiamine, magnesium -Continued Ledipasvir/Sofosbuvir for HCV #Anemia: Hgb at baseline. Previous iron studies reveal ACD. No active signs of blood loss. #Coagulopathy: Most likely related to liver disease. summary: You came to the hospital with abdominal pain and abnormal labs measured at your primary care visit. While you were in the hospital, you were found to have a urinary tract infection and decreased kidney function. Your infection was treated with intravenous antibiotics. Ancatheter was placed in your left arm so that you can finish your antibiotics when you leave the hospital. Your kidney function improved with intravenous fluids and albumin. You were also started on a new medication for your depression. When you leave the hospital, you will go to ___ where you will complete your antibiotic course for urinary tract infection (Ceftazidime 500 mg IV every 12 hours, LAST DAY ___. You will also continue taking sertraline for your depression.

He received Zosyn in the ED and was switched to Ceftazidime IV to complete 10 day course (last day ___ for complicated UTI. His WBC downtrended from 12.0 to 9.3. Urinary sx improved. ___: Pt presented with ___, with creatinine of 1.6 (baseline <1.0). The patient reported nausea/vomiting and poor PO intake for many days prior to admission. Etiology of his ___ was thought to be prerenal (especially given his hypovolemia and hyponatremia). ___ resolved with intravenous fluids and albumin 75gm x2. #Hyponatremia: Likely hypoosmolar hypovolemic due to pre renal/dehydration. Normalized after albumin and fluid. #Depression: Pt is debilitated by chronic depression and anhedonia. At home, he has trouble sleeping and reports he has not left his room for weeks. text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o M who presented to ___ ED s/p suicide attempt with lacerations to his left wrist and right neck. A trauma stat was sctivated. He was tachycardic upon arrival. The patient was given 2 units of blood and 1 L of IV fluid. The patient was able to protect his airway and was alert so no intubation was necessary. IV access was difficult to obtain so an IO was attempted in the left leg which was unsuccessful. A MAC line was placed in R femoral vein. The patient received Tdap and Ancef. CXR was ordered which showed no evidence of injury, CT head was negative, CTA neck showed no evidence of vascular injury. Left wrist x-ray revealed a left wrist laceration with a small amount of adjacent soft tissue edema/hematoma. Hand Surgery was consulted and repaired the left wrist laceration. As the patient's right neck laceration was considered a contaminated wound, the wound was left open and dry sterile gauze dressings were applied. Psychiatry was consulted and the patient was admitted with a 1:1 sitter. The patient was hemodynamically stable and admitted to the Trauma Surgery service and he was transferred to the surgical floor. The patient demonstrated symptoms of opiate withdrawal and he was started on ___ and received Clonidine 0.1 mg PO TID:PRN. The patient was seen by psychiatry who recommended transfer to inpatient psychiatry once medically stable. The patient was started on Suboxone. The patient was alert and oriented throughout hospitalization; pain was managed with acetaminophen and ibuprofen prn. He remained stable from a cardiovascular and pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. The patient tolerated a regular diet, intake and output were closely monitored. The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs of bleeding and he remained hemodynamically stable on the floor. The patient received subcutaneous heparin and ___ dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was tolerating a diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan and he was discharged to ___ Psychiatry.
summary: You were admitted to ___ (___) after a suicide attempt with lacerations sustained to your right neck and left wrist. You underwent imaging which showed both injuries to be stable without injury to the major underlying blood vessels. As your neck wound is considered contaminated, this was left open to prevent infection and a dry gauze may be applied to the area until it heals. The Hand Surgery team closed your left wrist wounds with sutures. You were admitted to the Acute Care Surgery service for monitoring and have remained stable. The Psychiatry team evaluated you and recommended that your care be transferred to ___ 4, an inpatient Psychiatry unit at ___. You are now medically ready to be discharged from the medical/surgical floor at ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year old female with past medical history of seronegative neuromyelitis optica and depression who presented with blurry vision and eye pain. Patient two weeks prior to this admission had been admitted and treated for a flare with similar complaints. Patient was treated with three days of high dose steroids. Patient, this admission, received five days of high dose steroids. Per Dr. ___ was stopped and she was started on methotrexate and will be started on rituximab as an outpatient. Patient's left eye was affected greater than her right eye and on discharge her vision ___ on the left and ___ on the right. Patient throughout admission complained of left eye pain. Patient is already treated for this pain with duloxetine, gabapentin, oxcarbamazepine, and topiramate. We increased her dose of duloxetine (in conjunction with psychiatry) and gabapentin. We tried to introduce pregabalin, but patient did not think medication was leading to improvement. Patient does get some relief with PRN toradol, but trying to limit given history of gastritis. Patient was transferred to the inpatient psychiatry unit because of depression and suicidal ideation. Patient stated these symptoms were secondary to her neuromyelitis optica and chronic eye pain. Patient prior to admission had suicide attempt with taking multiple tinazidine pills.
summary: Your presented to the hospital with eye blurriness and pain and were determined to have an neuromyelitis optica flare. We treated you with five days of steroids and your vision has shown signs of improvement. Remember it can take time for your vision to improve and it will take time for your body to repair. Per Dr. ___ have started you on methotrexate and you will start rituximab as an outpatient. Your eye pain has been difficult to control. We have increased your dose of gabapentin and will obtain a pain consultation. We tried starting you on pregabalin, but this medication did not seem of benefit. We have transferred you to the psychiatry service to address your depression and suicidal ideation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o male with a history of depression, anxiety, and PTSD who presented with increasing abdominal distension for 1.5 months found to have new onset ascites, elevated LFTs, hyperbilirubinemia, elevated INR, and hyponatremia. # Alcoholic Hepatitis: Patient with new onset ascites in the setting of significant alcohol use likely representing new onset cirrohsis. His AST/ALT ratio was consistent with alcoholic liver disease. He also initially presented with abdominal pain. Dianostic paracentesis done in the ED showed no evidence of SBP. The hepatology team was consulted and he had therapeutic paracentesis performed twice with resolution of his abdominal pain and SOB. First therapeutic para with 6.25 L taken off and 50g of albumin given. Second therapeutic para ___ guided and with 1.5L taken off. His AFP was negative. He was started on lasix and spironolactone for diuresis. HBsAb and HBcAb were positive, indicating previous but cleared HBV infection, and HAV IgG was positive as well. HCV antibody was negative. HIV negative. Thiamine, folate, and a multivitamin were started and a low sodium diet intiated. # Asymptomatic Hyponatremia: His sodium was 125 on admission and improved to 130 with fluid restriction. His hyponatremia is likely due to hypervolemic hyponatremia in the setting of liver disease. He was educated about continued fluid restriction at home. # Alcohol Abuse: He has a significant history of alcohol abuse and 'shakes' with withdrawal. No history of DT. The patient was placed on the CIWA protocol and required diazepam one time for CIWA of 10. Social work was consulted and was given a list of AA locations and discussed a plan to stay sober and attend AA meetings. # UTI: He presented with an elevated WBC count, dysuria, and urine cultures growing pansensitive E. coli. He also had blood cultures drawn, which were not finalized, but showed no growth to date. He was started on ciprofloxacin for his UTI and his last day of treatment will be ___ to complete a total 7 day course. # Nutrition: He presented initially with albumin of 2.9 and several risk factors for nutritional deficiency given his alcohol use and liver disease. Nutrition was consulted and he was started on ensure 4x daily along with thiamine, folate, and a multivitamin. He was given information about what to buy and what to avoid with a low-sodium diet. # Constipation: He had constipation on arrival present for the past 1.5 months. He was started on standing senna/colace and PRN miralax/lactulose and his bowel movements became more regular. # Hemorrhoids: He had some blood-streaked stools and was found to have non-thrombosed external hemorrhoids on exam. He was started on the bowel regimen as above and will follow-up with GI surgery as an outpatient for possible banding. # Depression: He has a history of depression and was not on medication when admitted. He was seen by social work and started on citalopram. His mood was stable over the course of his stay. Transitional issues: - Ciprofloxacin started for UTI - last day ___ - Started on a number of new medications at this visit - assess ability to adhere to complicated new treatment regimen (started: furosemide, spironolactone, folate, thiamine, multivitamin, senna/colace, citalopram) - Given information about AA groups and says he will follow-up - Found to have external hemorrhoids on exam - has follow-up with GI surgery as an outpatient - Started on citalopram due to depressed mood and history of depression - consider if it needed and/or tolerated -Blood culture with NGTD, final pending -peritoneal fluid culture from ___ pending at discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having abdominal pain and distension of your belly. We found that you have liver disease due to alcohol use and fluid in your belly causing the pain. While you were here we took fluid off of your belly and you felt much better. We also started you on medications to help keep the fluid off called lasix and spironolactone and started you on vitamins. It will be very important for you to stop drinking alcohol. We also found that you have hemorrhoids while you are here. This is causing the blood you see when you go to the bathroom. We started you on medications to help move your bowels.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr ___ is a ___ y/o M with a h/o HIV and non-hodgkins lymphoma diagnosed in ___, recurrent PE and DVT ___ on anticoagulation, and complex migraines who presented ti the ___ on ___ with ___ days of headache and visual disturbance in the setting of medication nonadherence for ___ wks secondary to financial inability to obtain medications. His hospital course is summarized by system below. . # Headache/Pain: Mr. ___ was initially complaining of head and abdominal pain. He is chronically on percocet, methadone, dilantin, and gabapentin all for migrain and regional pain syndrome. He had not taken percocet or gabapentin in 2 months. We initially treated him with tylenol and methadone. After consulting with his primary doctors, ___ and ___ as to desired medication regimen and his being approved for Mass Health on ___ all his home medications were restarted. His pain improved and then resolved. . # Neurological symptoms: He states some neurologic symptoms are new and some chronic. He states his last complex migraine was on the order of ___ years ago and was not as severe as the symptoms that brought him in today such as the right visual field deficit. Fortunately his symptoms had resolve by the time of presentation, however he was monitored frequently. Given the question of a possible pituitary mass on th CT read from the OSH. We had our neurosurgeons and neurologist assess the patient. They felt there was no need for surgical intervention. An MRI was obtained which showed no evidence of an intracranial mass, specifically no pituitary mass. . # Depression: On the evening of HD#1 the patient began expressing active suicidal ideation including a plan to kill himself by laying down a cranberry bog to freeze to death. Psychiatry was consulted and placed the patient under section 12and Paxil was restarted. A one-to-one sitter was not required. The following morning the attending psychiatrist did not feel an inpatient psychiatric stay was required. We arranged for him to follow in a partial psychiatry program near ___ as well as connected him with an HIV support program near ___. . # HIV: Pt has been on antiretroviral therapy beginning in ___ with AZT. His most recent CD4 count was 716 from ___. In the hospital his CD4 was tested to be 393. Mr ___ was started on HAART on ___. . # HTN: thoughout the hospital stay MR. ___ BP was elevated to 150s/80s-90s, on the day of discharge his BP was as high as 181/102. He received 1 dose of IV hydralizine and was started on 12.5mg hydrochlorothiazide. . # Social Issues: The patient had the good fortune of having his Mass Health application approved the night of ___ at midnight. He has close follow up with psychiatry in the outpatient. . On ___ Mr. ___ neurological symptoms had all resolved on resuming his home medications. Psychiatry felt his suicidal ideation was not active or a high enough risk to warrant hospitalization and he was discharged to home with follow up with his PCP ___ ID physician, ___.
summary: You were admitted due to an abnormal Head CT concerning for a pituitary mass seen at an outside hospital. You were closely examined by Neurosurgery who looked at your CT. We repeated an MRI and you in fact do NOT have any mass in your brain. You were complaining of blurry vision and headaches and Neurology evaluated you. These symptoms were felt to be due to complex migraines in the setting of not taking any medications. Since you now have Mass Health insurance, all of your medications were safely restarted. If you are running out of medications again, you should call Dr. ___ further guidance. It is unsafe for you to stop these medications abruptly without ___ knowing first. During this admission you reported active suicidal ideations.
