text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o F with DM, HTN, and spinal stenosis with a recent hospitalization for viral gastroenteritis and ___ who represented with malaise. # General Malaise: Differential diagnosis considered included: infectious, toxic-metabolic, endocrinopathy, deconditioning, anemia, depression. CXR without focal process, repeat UA without signs of infection. LFTs wnl, TSH wnl. AM cortisol wnl. SPEP/UPEP negative. Given negative workup thus far most likely malaise/fatigue secondary to sedating medications at home (morphine and diazepam) and depression/anxiety. The patient was counseled on the amount of morphine and diazepam that is safe to take at home and she expressed understanding. # Spinal Stenosis: surgery scheduled for ___. Pain control with low dose morphine (15mg Q 8 hrs) and diazepam (2.5mg Q 12 hrs). Pre-surgical stress test completed with results discussed below. # Stress test showed possible reversible defect in LAD territory unchanged from study in ___ at which time cath showed non-occlusive disease in DM-1 and mid-LAD that did not require intervention. For this reason spoke with cardiology who recommended starting patient on beta blocker to optimize cardiac status in the ___ time period. No cath necessary at this time given no anginal symptoms or anginal equivalent symptoms. Patient started on metoprolol at 12.5 mg BID. # ___: Creatinine elevated to 1.3 on admission and improved with IVFs. Likely pre-renal in setting of decreased PO intake. Patient with ~5% of eos on peripheral smear however no clear culprits for AIN. Home metformin and lisinopril initially held in acute setting and restarted upon resolution. Cr 0.9 on discharge. # Anemia: HCT 33.8. Previous baseline ~37-40. Iron studies obtained during last admission and wnl. #HTN: Normotensive currently and without cardiac complaint. Lisinopril initially held in setting ___ and restarted on ___. Patient started on metoprolol. #DMII: uncontrolled, last A1c 8.1; on metformin at home. Maintained on ISS in house. Metformin initially held in setting ___ and ___ restarted on ___. Transitional Issues: -CT scan recommended per radiology given findings on CXR: "Bulging contour in the aortopulmonary window, most likely due to enlargement of the main pulmonary artery or somewhat unusual course, but in order to exclude lymphadenopathy, chest CT is suggested when clinically appropriate, including with intravenous contrast administration if possible." -Pt started on metoprolol to optimize cardiac status prior to surgery. -Continue emotional support and addressing patient's anxiety. -SW set patient up with ___ help program -follow up with Dr. ___ prior to scheduled surgery -___ follow up prior to surgery
summary: You were admitted to the hospital becuase you were feeling fatigued. When you first came in it was felt that you were very sleepy likely from to much sedating medications at home. These medications were adjusted. It is recommended that you only take morphine 3 times per day at max and diazepam 2 times per day at max. Do not drink alcohol or drive while taking these medications. Lab work was sent to evaluate for other causes of your fatigue and was reassuring. While you were here you had a cardiac stress test for pre-surgical workup. The results of this test were similar to the test you had in ___. We discussed the results with the cardiology (heart) doctors who recommended we start you on a new medication called metoprolol to help improve your outcomes for your upcomming surgery.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH of depression and anxiety presenting with 3 episodes of witnessed syncope with +LOC and ___ within 30 minutes of each other, with no ___ symptoms or signs of cerebral bleed. ==================== ACTIVE ISSUES ==================== Syncope - hx is consistent with vasovagal syncope in the setting of alcohol use (not an inappropriate or unusual amount but it may have contributed) and marijuana use - do not suspect situational or orthostatic based on hx, exam and labs - neurogenic seems unlikely as well, no ___ activity - do not suspect PE - cardiogenic also unlikely, and had no events on telemetry here - he and I discussed the ideas of CT head and TTE -- I explained that given the lack of any focal signs or symptoms suggesting pathology in these areas, I felt they were unnecessary and unlikely to provide clarity, but if he would feel anxious having not gotten them, I'd be more than happy to arrange for them -- he would rather defer these tests, and if he has problems in the future, he'll discuss getting them with his PCP - ___ think this is a reasonable and safe approach given the complete lack of ___ -- I think the recurrent syncope was due to him still having an excess of parasympathetic tone (due to vasovagal process) and getting up too quickly after the first syncopal episode -- I counseled him about this, and the importance of avoiding injury with repeat falls in the future ==================== CHRONIC ISSUES ==================== # Depression. Mood good during hospital course. # Anxiety. The patient took home dose of lorazepam for insomnia.
summary: You were admitted for ___ and treatment of multiple fainting episodes (syncope), which you experienced the day before admission. The most likely cause of syncope was a combination of alcohol and cyclobenzaprine. We tested your blood pressure when you were lying, sitting and standing to see if dehydration may have caused your syncope. According to this test, dehydration is unlikely to be the primary cause of syncope, although the test was conducted a full day after your symptoms ended. We also used an electrocardiograph (EKG) and overnight telemetry monitoring to look at the electrical activity of your heart, and these tests did not find evidence of heart problems that could have caused syncope. Although you hit your head when you fell, you did not have a headache or nauseousness, signs that would suggest a bleed from the falls. Additionally, we kept you on your home doses of lorazepam for anxiety/insomnia and fluoxetine (Prozac) for depression. Please do not combine your insomnia medications with alcohol as this can be very dangerous to your health.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ yo RHM with depression and anxiety who presented with several weeks of eye fluttering, bilateral extremity shaking, speech changes and falls without injury, all intermittent. Eye fluttering could be briefly suppressible and was generally involuntary, though he later disclosed during the hospitalization that he could also voluntarily produce the same movement. In addition, he endorsed extremity twitching, speech difficulty, and generalized weakness. Of note, he has an extensive history of anxiety and depression, and he recently withdrew from college due to mental health issues. He is not currently on any psychotropic medications. He denied active PDW or SI. His neurological exam was normal. We observed several episodes of rapid eyelid fluttering that was suppressible and decreased when the patient was distracted. In addition, he was volitionally able to produce these movements. We felt that his symptoms were most consistent with a functional neurological disorder, with mixed features (motor and seizure). We did an MRI brain that was normal. EEG captured several episodes of eyelid fluttering, as well as bilateral extremity shaking that did not show an epileptiform correlate; his background was also normal. He was evaluated by physical therapy who recommended outpatient therapy. He should follow up with a psychiatrist to discuss further psychotropic management and therapist for cognitive behavioral therapy as an outpatient. He was counseled regarding the diagnosis and further reliable information sources (neurosymptoms.org). Patient and mother were agreeable to discharge, and will be returning home to ___ in the next few days. Transitional issues: If patient returns to live in ___, could consider referral to ___ as wholistic therapies have previously been helpful for him Outpatient psychiatry follow up
summary: You were admitted to ___ due to eye fluttering, tremors, twitches, and muscle weakness. In your neurological exam, you sometimes had difficulty with strength but other times had excellent strength. Your labs and MRI brain were normal. We did an EEG that captured several episodes of eyelid fluttering. We found that these events are not seizures. During your hospitalization, we also spoke with Dr. ___ updated him on your results and discharge plan. You were diagnosed with a Functional Neurological Disorder. This is a disorder where your neurological symptoms (such as the eye fluttering and limb weakness) are due to a problem with how your brain functions. It is not due to damage of the brain. We do not understand precisely why functional neurological disorders occur in certain people, but we do know that we can treat the symptoms. We are giving you a prescription for physical therapy. In addition, managing stress, anxiety, and depressive symptoms is a key part of your recovery. We would like you to resume working with a therapist for cognitive behavioral therapy and a psychiatrist to discuss medication management. Please schedule an appointment with your primary care provider ___ ___ days following discharge.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ male with PMhx of H Pylori who presents with worsening abdominal complaints over the past year. # IBS: #Functional dyspepsia: Patient has had one year of abdominal symptoms, and thus far despite relatively extensive GI work-up that to date has not elucidated a precise etiology. GI team has evaluated the patient and feel that symptoms most consistent with functional dyspepsia with visceral hypersensitivity, likely magnified in the setting of untreated anxiety/depression. A Patient will benefit from a biopsychosocial approach to his symptoms.He was discharged on trial of rifaximin for IBS and an PPI for possible GERD. #Anxiety/depression: Patient denied suicidal or homicidal thoughts, but markedly depression. Unclear if adjustment disorder secondary to ongoing GI symptoms or if GI symptoms are a somatic manifestation of his depression, perhaps both. Patient started on amitriptyline 25mg qHS to help both with his depression/anxiety but also his GI symptoms. Social work met with the patient and provided outpatient resources for counseling. Quick follow up was arranged with his PCP with recommendation for referral to a psychiatrist for ongoing treatment of his anxiety/depression. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ================= [] patient started on amitriptyline to help with depression/anxiety and functional GI symptoms. Please monitor for adverse effects and titrate as indicated. [] Recommend outpatient psychiatry and counseling care > 30 min spent on coordination of discharge
summary: It was a priviliege to care for you at the ___ ___. You were admitted for further evaluation of your abdominal pain. You were seen by our Gastroenterology team who extensively reviewed your record and feel that no further imaging or endoscopies are needed at this time. We feel that your symptoms are due to functional dyspepsia and likely irritable bowel. The cornerstone to treatment will be focused at symptom reduction. We have prescribed you a two week course of an antibiotic to take, which has been shown to improve symptoms. We also encourage that you continue taking the antacid medication. Lastly, as we discussed, treatment of underlying depression/anxiety is also an essential component to your treatment. We have started you on a medication that can help with your anxiety/depression that is also helpful for your other symptoms. It is very important that you take this medication consistently (unless you have intolerable side effects). We are working with your primary care doctor, ___, to coordinate mental health follow up. You have also been given contact information for therapist by our social worker.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #GI bleed: Patient did not have an episode of BRBPR while hospitalized. His HCT remained stable throughout the hospitalization and trended up from 33 to 36. He underwent an EGD which showed mucosal irritation consistent with gastritis. His colonscopy showed small internal hemorrhoids and diverticulosis. He was subsequently started on omeprazole for gastritis and a high fiber diet to help with internal hemorrhoids. - repeat colonoscopy was recommended in ___ years. . #Depression/Suicidal Ideation: Patient had specific thoughts of walking into oncoming traffic during the week prior to admission and continued to express suicidality during the hospitalization. He had 1:1 sitter throughout the time on the floor and was continued on his home psychiatric medication of depakote and citalopram. Psychiatry was consulted and recommended inpatient psychiatric care after medical issues have been stabilized. At the time of discharge, his medical issues were resolved and he is to be discharged to an inpatient psychiatric facility. . #Bipolar Disorder: Patient had depressive symptoms but denies suicidal ideation. He was continued on depakote and citalopram as above. He is to be discharged to an inpatient psychiatric facility as above. . #Alcohol Abuse: Patient had no history of delirium tremens in the past. His last drink was on ___. While hospitalized he was monitored on a CIWA scale and did not have signs or symptoms of withdrawal. . #Hypertension: His blood pressure was well controlled throughout the hospitalization; he was continued on his home dose of lisinopril. . #Hyperlipidemia: He was continued on home regimen of fenofibrate. . #Diet: Regular initially; his diet was changed to high-fiber after the finding on colonoscopy. . #Diet: High-Fiber Diet . #Code: Full
summary: You came to the hospital because you were not feeling well due to bleeding from your lower GI tract and excessive alcohol consumption. You also had suicidal thoughts during the week prior to hospitalization. During your hospitalization, you underwent an endoscopy which showed gastritis (stomach irritation), internal hemorrhoids and diverticulosis (outpouching of colon that can also bleed sometimes). You were started on a medicine called omeprazole (prilosec) to treat gastritis. You initially had a productive cough during the hospitalization. An X-ray of your lungs showed no infection. Your cough resolved on its own during your stay. We think the cause of your cough is smoking.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of hypothyroidism, depression with multiple psychiatric admissions in the past, presenting with depression with suicidal ideation along with syncopal event in the ED associated with diaphoresis, tinnitus, and bradycardia. Patient was given 2L of NS with improvement in clinical status with subsequent negative orthostatics and no further episodes. Patient will go to a psychiatric facility for further treatment. # Syncope Patient with syncopal episode in the ED. Developed transient hypotension with blood pressure of 60/30 and HR in the mid-___. Patient walked to the bathroom prior to this event and subsequently stared off blankly and syncopized per report. She had 2 L bore IVs placed and BP improved after administration of 2L NS. Patient with rapid breathing during event. Low concern for PE, arrhythmia, structural heart disease, carotid artery disease. No signs of infection. No signs of seizure. Most likely orthstatic hypotension vs vasovagal syncope in the setting of walking to the bathroom given post-episode diaphoresis and tachycardia. At risk given anticholinergic side effects from anti-psychotics. Patient was subsequently stable and cleared for psychiatric bed placement. # Suicidality # Depression # BPD Patient brought to hospital for concern for escalating depression and suicidal ideation with recent self-cutting. Patient has taken husbands clonazepam in the setting of worsening anxiety and depression. With active suicidal ideation in the emergency room. Patient was continued on her psychiatric medications, other than clonazepam, the dose of which was cut in half per her outpatient psychiatrist. # Geriatrics: Follows with Dr. ___. Did not require treatment for agitation during the hospital stay. # HTN: BP well controlled c/w amlodipine 2.5mg daily # Hyperlipidemia: C/w pravastatin 20mg daily # Hypothyroidism: TSH WNL. C/w levothyroxine 125mcg daily # History of pancreatitis. H/o pancreatitis, but remote. Patient no longer drinks alcohol. # Executive dysfunction and cognitive impairment: Patient with detailed neuropsych testing that revealed executive dysfunction with MoCA score of ___. Transitional Issues =================== - Further psychiatric care per Inpatient Psychiatry - Per outpatient psychiatrist, her Clonazepam dose was cut in half, as pt has taken husband's clonazepam in the past. Dose changed to 0.25mg QHS and 0.125mg BID - Other home medications were not changed - CODE: FULL CODE (Confirmed) - CONTACT: ___, sister and law and HCP (___)
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were threatening to hurt yourself. Shortly after arriving in the ED, you had an episode of lightheadedness and dizziness with slow heart rate and low blood pressure. You appeared dehydrated and were given fluids and improvement in your blood pressure. You were then transferred to the medical floor for clearance prior to placement in an inpatient psychiatric program. You had no further events of low blood pressure or dizziness. We monitored your heart rate on telemetry and you had no abnormalities. We also looked for infections in your urine and found none. You most likely have something called vasovagal syncope, which was triggered by your medications and dehydration. Several of the psychiatric medications can cause low blood pressure as a side effect, particularly when you are dehydrated and quickly move from a seating to standing position. We advised you to drink ample quantities of fluid and make sure to rise slowly from a seated to standing position. If these episodes continue, you should see your primary care doctor for referral to a cardiologist. You were evaluated by psychiatry during this admission and it was felt that you could benefit from a stay in an inpatient psychiatric program. You were transferred directly to a program.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. Delirium due to suicidal Ingestion of tranquilizers due to Suicidal Ideation: - Pt took a total of approximately 20mg of Xanax in a suicide attempt. Tox screen was positive for benzos and tricyclics. ___ was sommolent on arrival and was briefly hypotensive which responded to painful stimuli and IVF. Toxicolgy was consulted and did not reccomend further intervention. The patient was monitored in the MICU and remained stable. She was transfered to regular medical floor where VS remained stable, EKG was stable, no events on telemetry, Chem 7, LFTs, CBC normal. Currently medically stable s/p overdose; stable for transfer to psych bed. - Clonazepam for CIWA was continued given possible withdrawal per psych. - Patient continues to be actively suicidal, threatening to kill herself if allowed to leave. Per psychiatry consultation, needed inpatient psychiatry admission. - We continued a 1:1 sitter until transfer. - Further intensive treatment of depression is needed. # Schizoaffective, Histrionic Borderline Personality: - diagnoses as per inpatient pysch evaluation. - Further treatment defered in medical hospital setting. - She has a history of following with psychiatry in ___ and ___ and has been on Respiridal, Effexor, tegretol, xanax, trazadone, and prozac in the recent past. Medication regimen should be clearified at inpatient psych facility. She also appears to be getting medications from multiple sources. # Abnormal EKG: - Serial EKGs show NSR with isolated q wave in III, no TWI or ST changes. Qtc within normal limits. Poor R wave progression. - Changes are not accompanied with cardiac symptoms, VS stable, no events on telemtry. - Medically stable for pyschiatry transfer. - Follow up EKG by PCP in future is warrented. # Hypothyroidism: - Patient on levothyroxine as outpatient. TSH wnl in hosptial. #. Hyperglycemia: - Patient with no stated history of diabetes per daughter, however, has filled prescription for metformin in past. She says she has not needed metformin since her wt loss procedure in ___. HgbA1c within normal limits, fingersticks normal, no need for Insulin or metformin #. Code - Full code confirmed with daughter on admission #. ___ - daughter - ___ ___ ___ on Admission: alprazolam 0.5mg 1 tab BID - 60 tabs filled ___ fluoxetine 20mg 1 tab daily - 30 tabs filled ___ levothyroxine 137mcg 1 tab daily - 30 tabs filled ___ trazodone 50mg 1 tab daily - 30 tabs filled ___ metformin 750mg PO BID - 60 tabs filled ___ Effexor- from ___ Risperdone- from ___ Tegretol-from ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you attempted to hurt yourself by taking an large quantity of xanax. This was not safe and you were found to be sleep. You were admitted to the intensive care unit for close monitoring. You were evaluate by the medical service and felt to be medically cleared from injury. You were seen by psychiatry who felt that you were a threat to yourself and required additional treatment in an inpatient psychiatric facility. You are being transferred to the care of a psychiatry unit. You will no longer need to take Metformin. This is a medicine for diabetes and it was felt you do not have diabetes. Your Synthroid was continued for your hypothyroidism. You were given a nicotine patch to help alleviate your desire to smoke cigarettes. All of your other medications were stopped and you should discuss with your psychiatrist what medications to continue before going home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # SBP: Pt re-presented to the hospital after a + peritoneal fluid anaerobic culture with 1 colony of Gram positive rods(propinobacter acne). Pt had no leukocytosis, no fever, WBC in peritoneal fluid only 39, and no abdominal pain. Pt was treated for SBP with albumin and ceftriaxone for 5 days. Pt was switched to Bactrim on d/c (given hallucinations on levofloxacin, so cipro was not used). Peritoneal fluid on discharge showed no growth. Pt was put on home lasix/spironolactone (40/100) since ascities appeared to be reaccumulating during hospitalization. # Abdominal pain: started ___ ___ and continued throughout hospitalization potentially ___ ?SBP. Started 2 hours prior to paracentesis. Worsening pain overnight ___. KUB preformed overnight showed no s/o obstruction; showed minor ileus. CT Abd/pelvis showed no s/o hematoma/hemorrhage. Not hypotensive and without fever during hospitalization. No signs of bleeding on imaging. Pt remained afebrile, normotensive, NL WBC count, Tbili downtrending. Tramadol was given for pain and oxycodone was limited I/s/o history of hepatic encephalopathy. # HE: pt had mild asterixis ___ AM but no other confusion. No asterixis on d/c. Maintain goal of ___ BM /day on lactulose. # Cirrhosis: due to EtOH. complicated by diuretic refractory ascites, encephalopathy, varices (grade III ___ s/p banding ___. He is s/p TIPS ___. H/o of HE, recent admission(discharge ___ for HE in the setting of med non-compliance. No signs of HE on d/c. During last admission, home diuretics held in the setting of concern for bleeding, and was discharged off diuretics. Since ascities reaccumulated and the pt had good kidney function, he was restarted on Lasix/spironolactone. Continued on lactulose and rifaximin. Large volume therapeutic (and dx) pericentesis (___) showed <250 WBC. Continued on ursodiol 300mg PO BID for biliary tree protection. Continued on folic acid, MVI, thiamine, magnesium. # ?UGIB: Pt reported recurrent epistaxis and an episode of hematemesis about 10 days prior to presentation with initial c/f UGIB, now with +stool guiac on re-admission. No recurrent epistaxis/hematemesis, however H&H remained below normal limits (but above H/H ___. ENT c/s as outpatient re: recurrent epistaixs # Diabetes: BS controlled on ISS during hospitalization. Will continue insulin at home. # Chronic back pain: Pt continued on gabapentin (uptitrated to home dose) and cyclobenzaprine. Narcotics avoided I/s/o c/f HE. # Depression: also endorsed passive SI x >1mo. Seen by psych. Recommended switching home citalopram 20mg daily to escitalopram during hospitalization. TRANSITIONAL CARE: - follow up blood and peritoneal fluid cultures - Since he expressed interest in exploring palliative care, advised to follow up as an outpatient - discharged on Bactrim indefinitely for SBP prophylaxis (given hallucination with levaquin, so can't use ciprofloxacin) - Lasix/spironolactone restarted at 40/100, at recent discharge, discharged off Lasix/spironolactone for plan for outpt restart
summary: You were admitted to the hospital to be treated for a bacterial infection of the fluid collection in your abdomen. Your diagnosis is called spontaneous bacterial peritonitis. Although we were unable to isolate any bacteria from your abdominal fluid, you were treated with antibiotics (ceftriaxone) and given albumin for 5 days for this infection. After you leave the hospital, please continue to take bactrim(an antibiotic) daily to prevent recurrence of infection. You had discussed exploring palliative care during your hospitalization with your primary liver doctor, ___. You can schedule an appointment with the Palliative Care Clinic for when you leave the hospital to get further information on this process. You also spoke with the psychiatry team at ___ about your prior thoughts of suicide and history of depression. They recommended a change in your medication from citalopram to escitalopram.

Maintain goal of ___ BM /day on lactulose. # Cirrhosis: due to EtOH. complicated by diuretic refractory ascites, encephalopathy, varices (grade III ___ s/p banding ___. He is s/p TIPS ___. H/o of HE, recent admission(discharge ___ for HE in the setting of med non-compliance. No signs of HE on d/c. During last admission, home diuretics held in the setting of concern for bleeding, and was discharged off diuretics. Since ascities reaccumulated and the pt had good kidney function, he was restarted on Lasix/spironolactone. Continued on lactulose and rifaximin. Large volume therapeutic (and dx) pericentesis (___) showed <250 WBC. Continued on ursodiol 300mg PO BID for biliary tree protection. Continued on folic acid, MVI, thiamine, magnesium. # ?UGIB: Pt reported recurrent epistaxis and an episode of hematemesis about 10 days prior to presentation with initial c/f UGIB, now with +stool guiac on re-admission. No recurrent epistaxis/hematemesis, however H&H remained below normal limits (but above H/H ___. ENT c/s as outpatient re: recurrent epistaixs # Diabetes: BS controlled on ISS during hospitalization. Will continue insulin at home. # Chronic back pain: Pt continued on gabapentin (uptitrated to home dose) and cyclobenzaprine. Narcotics avoided I/s/o c/f HE. # Depression: also endorsed passive SI x >1mo. Seen by psych. Recommended switching home citalopram 20mg daily to escitalopram during hospitalization. TRANSITIONAL CARE: - follow up blood and peritoneal fluid cultures - Since he expressed interest in exploring palliative care, advised to follow up as an outpatient - discharged on Bactrim indefinitely for SBP prophylaxis (given hallucination with levaquin, so can't use ciprofloxacin) - Lasix/spironolactone restarted at 40/100, at recent discharge, discharged off Lasix/spironolactone for plan for outpt restart

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old F with HIV, depression with psychotic features, chronic pain syndrome here with pain radiating down RLE. 1. Pt with no cord compromise though has some neuroforaminal narrowing in lumbosacral spine that could account for sx. Her pain seemed to be well controlled on standing tylenol, her fentanyl patch, oxycodone 5 mg q6 hrs prn. When she did not know she was being observed, she would lie comfortably in bed, talk on the phone and watch TV. ___ RN or MD would enter her room, she would profess severe pain and request escalating doses of her narcotic regimen, or changing to dilaudid in higher dosage. Her pain seems to be a combination of anxiety, real neuropathy, and depression, with a possible component of narcotic tolerance/dependence. She was able to ambulate independently prior to discharge with some deconditioning. See ___ note in OMR. ___ recommended outpatient ___ for back pain. Of note, the MRI of her spine showed an incidental finding - see below read about abnormal marrow signal. It's not clear what the signficance of this is. 1. No evidence of spinal cord compression. 2. Abnormal marrow signal within the S1 and S2 vertebral bodies suggestive of marrow edema. Infectious process is not entirely excluded. Contrast-enhanced MRI or CT of the pelvis is recommended for further evaluation. 3. Lumbar spondylosis, greatest at the L4-L5 and L5-S1 levels, as described, with moderate left neural foraminal stenosis at L4-5 and moderate right neural foraminal stenosis at L5-S1. 2. Urinary retention - need to better characterize - may be due to her multiple anticholinergic meds. Her UCx was without growth. She failed several voiding trials. She was discharged to home with an indwelling Foley as it was felt she would likely not be able to coordinate intermittent bladder catheterization and she was instructed to f/u in Urology on ___. It is hoped that her bladder will recover sufficiently between the time of discharge and her follow up. 3. ___ - likely ___ NSAID use -improved/resolved prior to discharge -patient was instructed to avoid NSAID exposure 4. HIV - continue home meds Reyataz, truvada, norvir 5. Mood disorder - continue trazodone, lorazepam, topamax - no evidence for psychotic symptoms 6. Peripheral neuropathy - continue gabapentin 900 qhs On the day of discharge, Ms. ___ was ambulating independently, tolerating normal po intake, appeared comfortable (watching TV, talking on the phone) on current PO pain regimen. Her husband and she were instructed by the RN about management of the Foley bag and she was discharged to home with ___ given her poor executive function.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with pain and kidney injury. Your kidney function improved and may have been related to taking ibuprofen. You should avoid taking ibuprofen or any medication in its class (NSAID) like aleve, advil, naproxen. You may take Tylenol (also called acetaminophen) 650 mg by mouth every ___ hours as needed for pain. You may also take the oxycodone for the pain every ___ hours for pain. The oxycodone may make you sleepy. You should not drive a car while taking this medication or do anything that requires you to be extremely alert. You were seen by physical therapy who recommended an outpatient treatment program for your back pain when you are well enough to see them. You had trouble urinating - your bladder did not empty well. You were not able to empty it without a catheter. You should wear this catheter for ___ days until ___ when you see the Urology experts (see below) to have a trial at removing it again.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is a ___ yo female with History of depression with psychosis involving delusion of dysphagia resulting admission for in failure to thrive and also found to have a decrease in hematocrit, was transfused a total of four units. At the time of discharge, the Hct was stable and there were not any signs of GI bleeding. Over the course of the admission the patient was transfused 4u PRBCs. The hematocrit was stable for the two weeks prior to transfer to psych. Multiple courses of ECT were done. Colonoscopy was partially done, one biopsy was done, and virtual colonscopy was then done. ___ says that she was clear for transfer with assistance out of bed. # s/p ECT - Patient has had multiple courses of ECT and is much improved in affect. She received ECT after consent was obtained from her daughter in ___ after getting legal and ethics involved and a psych guardian established. Was tried on Haldol IV, QTc not prolonged but was having posturing, so it was stopped on ___, restarted zyprexa zydis, then d/c for continued posturing, now holding all antipsycotics # Dysphagia/Failure to Thrive with psychosis: Long history of delusion causing inability to swallow worse over the last several months with a history of normal swallow studies. Admitted with failure to thrive. Required starting TPN after a nutrition consult on ___. Patient continued to have difficulty consuming anything ___ including her medications. Psychiatry evaluated patient and stated that if her depression was treated she would likely improve her eating, since she has done so in the past. She was tried on oral dissolving medications and only intermittently will take them. At the time of transfer, the patient was tolerating ___, all TPN had been discontinued and the PICC line was removed. # Anemia: On admission her hematocrit was 30, was previously in ___. Had a prior history of anemia, but unclear if this occurred in setting of surgery. She had a large bloody stool shortly after admission on ___. Then constipation, but hematocrit dropped to low of 24.9, may have been even further lowered in part from rehydration. She was seen by GI, and refused a EGD or colonoscopy. Refused to take a bowel prep, attempt was made to place an NGT for the bowel prep. Patient removed tube immediately after placement. Eventually consented to abd/pelvic CT scan on ___ with no oral contrast and it showed constipation, but poor study since no ___ contrast. Given 2 units pRBCs on ___. In total, patient was tranfused four units. At the time of transfer, there were not any signs of GI bleeding and the Hct was stable. # FOLLOW UP COLONOSCOPY TO BE REPEATED IN ONE YEAR PER GI RECOMMENDATIONS # Resolved UTI - Urine cx with E. coli and corynbacterium both senstitive to ceftriaxone - completed ceftriaxone 10 day treatment, stopped ___. A repeat UA was checked a week later and was negative and there was no evidence of UTI at the time of discharge. The foley was removed since the patientw as able to self-void, however, the patient is incontient. The foley was removed to reduce the risk of another UTI. Nurses were told to check the cath Q6 and straight cath if she was not voiding on her own. # Hyperlipidemia: LDL was 151. Patient only sometimes refused Lipitor. # DNR/DNI status was established with the patient and her healthcare proxy. PENDING LABS ARE B12 TO FOLLOWED UP BY PCP. COMMUNICATION IS WITH SON-IN-LAW ___ ___ ___
summary: You were admitted for an inability to swallow that was shown to be of psychological origin related to your dementia. There was also concern of your drop in hematocrit that was thought to be a slow GI bleed, over which the course of your admission, you were transfused a total of four units of blood. Your Hct was stable and there were no signs of GI bleeding at the time of transfer. Psych was consulted and you were found to be severely depressed and demented. You were given multiple courses of ECT after legal and ethics became involved and established your daughter as your guardian. Nutrition was involved and you were started on TPN and this was stopped as you began to resume eating. Please return to the hospital if you stop eating, become severely depresed, and continue to lose weight.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with a PMHx significant for CAD s/p CABG, CVA, HTN, HLD, DM2 (last A1c 6.8, ___, and anxiety who presents 1 day s/p unwitness fall in the bathroom with loss of consciousness and found to be hyponatremic to 125, lactate 2.2, wbc 11.3. # syncope- According to the family, syncopal episode was sudden, he had a gray-ashen color, and regained consciousness quickly, he remembered the event. There was no bowel or bladder incontinence. EKG was within normal limits. Given symptoms most likely vasovagal. However, he had an episode of orthostatic hypotenstion in the hospital and had been fluid restricting himself on the day of syncope. Renal was consulted to advise on managing orthostatic hypotension given the hyponatremia. Family was hesitant to give fluids since hyponatremia was improving. Physical therapy evaluated the patient and recommended home ___, but the family declined. He was advised to increase protein and sodium intake and continue to restrict water. # SIRS (Fever, leukocytosis) w/elevated lactate- Mr. ___ presented wtih elevated temperature, elevated wbc, elevated lactate. He had a remote history of cough and was started on levofloxacin in the ED. UA clear, CXR clear, lung exam clear. No meningeal manifestations. Per family, he is at baseline. Clinically, no source of infection can be identified. Levofloxacin was stopped on the floor given low clinical suspicion of pneumonia. However, given low grade fevers throughout admission, infection cannot be ruled out. Patient was instructed to return if fevers persisted or there were any concerns. # Hyponatremia- patient has longstanding and documented history of hyponatremia with baseline sodium in high 120s to low 130s, likely due to Paxil and SIADH. He has been followed by PCP and they have decided to keep pt on Paxil 20mg daily to help mood stability. At home he fluid restricts to 16 ounces of water, 2 cups of coffee, and 1 cup of tea a day. On admission sodium was 125, which was likely due to increased water intake after episode of syncope. After water was restricted in the hospital and food intake improved, his sodium improved to 128. CHRONIC ISSUES: # DM2- on metformin at home. Last A1c 6.8. Well controlled. FSBG 228 after syncope. # anxiety/depression- on paxil 20mg daily and valium ___ daily for anxiety and depression. Even though paxil likely medication causing hyponatremia, benefit of mood stability outweights risk. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - He should have follow up appointment with primary care doctor to discuss hyponatremia and low blood pressures - please follow up blood and urine cultures - Patient and family were instructed to return should he develop any fevers or concern for ongoing infection since he had low grade fevers in the hospital (100.3)
summary: You came into the hospital after a fall at home, some increased urinary frequency, nausea and vomiting, and were found to have a Sodium level of 125. We restricted the amount of water you were drinking and your sodium improved to 129. At first there was concern for an infection because you had a fever and some elevated white blood cells, but your fever resolved and your white count went back to normal. You also had some episodes of low blood pressure when standing. You were seen by the renal team who recommended that you increase the amount of protein and salty foods that you eat and limit your fluid intake to 32 ounces a day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o M with h/o major depression, recent suicide attempt, DM on metformin, HTN, dyslipidemia, and chronic pain, admitted w severe metabolic gap acidosis with concurrent new acute renal failure and liver enzyme abnormalities concerning for toxic ingestion/suicide attempt vs. metformin induced lactic acidosis. Patient with profound metabolic acidosis on admission with lactate levels 7.9 --> 9.6 --> 6.2. Osmolar gap ~ 16 with correction of alcohol level. Other than toxic ingestion, other causes of profound lactic acidosis include metformin use in the setting of ARF, INH toxicity, Thiamine deficiency, and dehydration. EKG with QRS interval WNL. Toxicology consult said to start empiric Fomepizole treament for possible ethylene glycol ingestion. Sent urine for oxalate crystals (to eval for ethylene glycol) & for evaluation under woods lamp for flourescence even though low suspicion. Sent blood for ethylene glycol, methanol. Tox screen came back positive for alcohol. Started on empiric Thiamine treatment 100mg IV daily x 3 days. EKG q8 hour x 24 hours. Metformin d/ced. Patient was hydrated and was able to take in PO. Anion Gap closed with hydration. Unclear if current picture related to major depression and if patient ingested toxic substances due to depression. Psych consult does not believe so and said to d/c Venlafaxine to make sure this is not contributing to organ disfunction. They also d/ced 1:1 Sitter and suicide precautions as they spoke to outpatient psychiatrist and said they don't believe this to be the issue currently. Acute renal failure this admission may be related to toxins or to poor PO intake. Picture unclear but renal believe it to be ATN, Hold ACE inhibitor/metformin. Renal following and does not think dialysis is needed. Renally dose meds. For diabetes, cover w/regular insulin sliding scale and hold glipizide for now. New LFT abnormality this admission with AST/ALT >x5 ULN. No history of hypotension on admission with SBP's stable in the 100's to 110's. Unclear etiology. Hold statin prescirbed for dyslipidemia. Monitor LFTs and synthetic function, including INR (currently 1.4). Continue to monitor BP. For chronic pain, d/ced Gabapentin Q24 and no NSAIDS given bc of poor kidney function. For prophylaxis, continued on omprazole, given regular cardiac/diabetic diet, ambulation, PPI, Pneumoboots. Patient is full code. Patient was then monitored on the floor for two days after transfer from the unit. He felt well except for some mild abdominal pain. It was likely related to his elevated lipase levels. By discharge his lab values had normalized and he was discharged home feeling well. He was off of IVFs and staying even with PO intake and urine output.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after fainting. You were found to have some severe abnormalities in your electrolytes. We treated you with IVFs and monitored your heart and other organs very carefully. You were in the ICU for about two days. We continued to monitor your organs. We found that your kidneys and liver were injured. They continued to improve and we gave you IV fluids to help you as your body healed. Psychiatry also saw you and felt that your depression was well controlled. They stopped your antidepressant because it is metabolized by the liver and we did not want to add to your injury. Dr. ___ start another antidepressent as an outpatient, once your liver function has totally improved. We also treated your diabetes with insulin. We think the metformin may have been a cause of your metabolic problems. We are going to stop all your oral diabetes medicines and send you home on insulin. Please return to the hospital for any more fainting, dizziness, abdominal pain, chest pain, shortness of breath, or any other concerns.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with hx of pancreatic divisum with recurrent pancreatitis on TPN who presented with witnessed seizure. She was admitted to Medicine with Neurology consulting. # Seizure: Potential etiologies include hypoglycemia or electrolyte abnormalities related to TPN, infection, convulsive syncope, and primary neurologic process. The patient was not hypoglycemic on admission. Orthostatic vital signs were negative. Infectious workup (UA, BCx, CXR) was negative. MRI was unrevealing. EEG showed possible epileptiform focus, but neurology recommended basing the decision to initiate anti-epileptic therapy on prolonged EEG to be perfomed as an outpatient in ___. The patient was continued on clonezepam prn while hospitalized. # Recurrent pancreatitis/malnutrition: On admission, the patient was tolerating 1 meal per day PO in addition to TPN. Nutrition was consulted and the decision was made to discontinue TPN given the patient's difficulting maintaining normal blood sugar on TPN. Dobhoff feeding tube was placed and tube feeds with Fibersource HN initiated on ___. These were up-titrated to 80mL/hr for 12hr per 24hr period. The patient is being discharged on TFs. PICC line was removed on ___. . # Depression: The patient was continued on citalopram daily and clonezepam prn while hospitalized. Given the patient's hx of severe depression requiring ECT and family concerns over multiple recent hospitalizations, possible eating disorder, and possible secondary gain, psychiatry was consulted. The patient was offered voluntary psychiatric admission to re-initiate ECT treatment. The patient initially declined this offer and then decided to accept it. TSH was normal during this admission. #Anemia: The patient's Hct was stable during admission and stools were guiaic negative. Iron studies showed MCV 99. Folate replete, B12 replete, nl Fe, low/nl TIBC, increased ferritin. Likely anemia of chronic disease. . # Hypertension: The patient was continued on metoprolol while hospitalized.
summary: You were hospitalized following a witnessed seizure. This seizure may have been due to low blood sugar from insulin in your TPN. TPN was discontinued in consultation with the nutrition service and you were started on tube feeds to supplement your caloric intake by mouth. You should continue these tube feeds and consult your primary care physician regarding tube feed weaning. Your blood sugar was normal after stopping TPN, so you may no longer need insulin therapy. Please discuss blood sugar monitoring with your primary care physician. Neurological workup including EEG revealed a possible epileptiform focus in your brain.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with past medical history significant for LR Renal Tx ___ with CKD, and recent urosepsis (admitted here from ___ who presented with altered mental status secondary to delerium and depression. 1. AMS/Depression: Patient brought to hospital because of concern for depression and delirium after not getting out of bed at home, not taking medications and not answering questions appropriately. The patient had a similar waxing and waning course during her prior admission. The patient also endorsed anhedonia and depression. Psychiatry was consulted who designated this as delerium from multiple etiologies including recent hospitalization, medications, and uremia. summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you had confusion at home called delerium. Delerium is a common condition that can be caused by many factors, especially infection and prolonged hospitalizations. Delerium should get better. However, it may take a while until you are feeling back to normal. You may also have brief epsiodes of confusion at home that should pass. In order to decrease the risk of delerium, it is important that you do not take medications like ambien or xanax. Also, it is important not to take more than ___ pills of Fiorcet per day and it is even better if you do not need this medication at all. You may feel depressed over the next few weeks while you are not feeling 100%. You will regain your independence as you begin to feel better and I fully expect you to be active and healthy. You will have increased nursing care at home as you transition back into your normal routine.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o F with PMHx DM, HTN, symptomatic gallstones, with several weeks of ongoing L abdominal pain and nausea. She was initially admitted to OSH and was unhappy with lack of definitive diagnosis there and left AMA. Then presented to another ___ and was transferred here. Prior OSH course notable for imaging showing mesenteric lymphadenitis. ___ also notable for pt undergoing CCY, new diagnosis of leukopenia, EGD showing ___. # ABDOMINAL PAIN # ?MESENTERIC ADENITIS/PANNICULITIS The patient presented with ongoing L-sided abdominal pain in the setting of recent prolonged admission at OSH for same. That course was notable for patient undergoing CCY given HIDA with low gallbladder EF, with no improvement in her pain. OSH imaging has also been notable for mesenteric LAD, biopsy now showing low-grade follicular lymphoma (see below). Labs on presentation here most notable for mild transaminitis with lipase elevation concerning for pancreatitis (? gallstone pancreatitis ___ retained stone following CCY). ERCP, ACS, and GI involved. ERCP recommended MRCP which did not show any signs of biliary obstruction or pancreatic inflammation. It was unclear why patient continued to have persistent severe pain and inability to tolerate PO. While pt did have new diagnosis of lymphoma, unlikely that was explaining pt's severity of abdominal pain and very likely that there was a psycho-somatic component given pt's severe anxiety and depression over lymphoma diagnosis. Pt however, has very little insight and is very resistant to this notion. CT on admission also showed some findings c/f mesenteric panniculitis, which could have caused some of her pain, but this has self-resolved on CT obtained just prior to discharge. She was treated with several days of TPN which was eventually discontinued as it was of unclear benefit iso fully-functioning gut. Diet was advanced and she was tolerating some solids and liquids on discharge with no exacerbation of abdominal pain. She still c/o nausea but did not have any emesis and was discharged on multiple anti-emetics and bentyl and short course of dilaudid prn breakthrough pain. # LOW GRADE LYMPHOMA: Pt diagnosed with low-grade follicular lymphoma on mesenteric LN biopsy. Will f/u with Dr. ___ in Onc here. PET scan scheduled prior to pt's appt. # DEPRESSION/ANXIETY: pt appears very anxious and depressed, likely contributed to pt's perception of abdominal pain. She was very resistant to this notion and keeps reiterating that everything "thinks it's in her head." Seemed very distrustful of the medical team at times. Her mood had drastically improved on discharge after getting a final diagnosis and finding that her prognosis was actually relatively good. # ___ ESOPHAGUS: On H2B, PPI. # DM2: With some episodes of hypoglycemia during admission. Will hold metformin after discharge until PO intake impoves. # HTN: Continued home verapamil. Billing: greater than 30 minutes spent on discharge counseling and coordination of care
summary: You were admitted for evaluation of abdominal pain and nausea. Your lab work was notable for possible inflammation in your pancreas; however, an MRI of your abdomen was performed and did not show evidence of this. Your imaging was notable for enlarged lymph nodes in your abdomen as was seen in your prior CT also. We did a biopsy of these lymph nodes, the pathology showed follicular lymphoma. You will seen an Oncologist after discharge to discuss further treatment options for this. You were also having severe abdominal pain during your hospitalization. It is possible that the pain was from inflammation of fat and soft tissue surrounding the lymph nodes, a condition called mesenteric panniculitis. This can occur sometimes in the setting of lymphomas. You had a CT scan on discharge which confirmed that this inflammation had resolved. You were also briefly treated with TPN, but this was stopped and you were able to tolerate some food and liquid on discharge. Please return for further evaluation if you have severe abdominal pain that does not improve with pain medications, nausea, vomiting, or if you are unable to tolerate any food or liquids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF COURSE ___ with depression and bipolar disorder here with episodes of loss of consciousness accompanied by jerking motions of arms and legs concerning for seizure vs. pseudoseizure. . ACTIVE ISSUES . # Loss of consciousness/myoclonic jerking episodes: Patient presented with 1.5 months of episodes of loss of consciousness accompanied by whole body jerking movements. Episodes often accompanied by emotional outbursts. Start with blank stare and unresponsiveness, progress to automatic movements of head then involves rhythmic jerking of arms and legs. Patient is unresponsive during these episodes. They last for a few minutes but her oxygen saturation and vital signs remain stable, and there is no tongue biting or loss of bladder or bowel function. Episodes end with yawn and arching of back and return to awareness. She is groggy for a few minutes afterwards but is soon able to answer simple questions and follow commands. On her first day, she had ___ of these episodes. She initially received 2mg IV ativan (total of 6mg over 24 hours) for the episodes but as her vitals remained stable and the episodes were short-lived, no further medications were administered. Spot EEG showed no epileptic focus. OSH records from recent psychiatric hospitalization at ___ suggests she presented for similar episodes a few weeks ago but no neurologic workup was done at that time as her fainting spells were felt to be related to emotional distress. She was seen by neurology and psychiatry here who felt she should undergo continuous EEG monitoring to rule out organic epileptiform seizures. . # Chest pain/back pain: Endorsing point tenderness over low back and mid-sternum. Unclear if there is a history of trauma though by report, patient underwent CPR last week for one of her unresponsive episodes. Did not obtain imaging due to frequency of her lost of consciousness episodes and concern about her being off the floor unaccompanied. . # Bipolar disorder/Depression/PTSD: History of traumatic car bombing and per report, increased stressors in life recently including miscarriage which might be triggering these episodes. Not on medications currently but followed by psychiatrist/therapist as outpatient and has had multiple psych hospitalizations in the past. Patient was seen by social work and psychiatry during this stay. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # Needs psychiatry follow-up ____________________________ ___ Course on Neurology: Neuro: Ms. ___ was placed on bedside EEG to characterize her events. She did have two events in the first 24 hours of recording and these were found to not have an EEG correlate. It was discussed with Ms. ___ that there can be events that are stress induced events and she was understanding and accepting of this. She expressed hope that they would resolve with time and treatment including relaxation techniques like guided imagery, progressive muscle relaxation as well as psychotherapy and possibly pharmacotherapy. As Ms. ___ has an extensive system of mental health providers in place at ___, she is encouraged to keep up these connections. She will follow up with Dr. ___ in clinic as well as with Dr. ___ in ___ Care Associates. **Ms. ___ was noted to be B12 deficient and was started on supplementation at the time of discharge. A perscription was provided.
summary: You were admitted to ___ on ___ for evaluation of your events of loss of consciousness and shaking concerning for seizures. You were monitored on telemetry and and EKGs to look for any abnormalities in your heart rates or heart rhythms and these were normal. You also were hooked up to EEG to see if these events were seizures. These events were captured on video and on EEG which looks at your brain waves and were found not be epileptic seizures. While these events are not epileptic seizures, you do lose consciousness with these and should not drive until these events are under full control and you have been cleared by a physician.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old man with history of advanced ___ disease, urinary retention requiring chronic indwelling catheterization, who presents with suicidal ideation and decreased functioning at home. #HD Tetrabenazine was stopped on admission due to concern it was contributing to suicidal ideation. Olanzapine was stopped due to risk of developing apathy as well as ineffectiveness. We started aripiprazole 10mg daily for chorea and mood. #SI Mr. ___ was placed on 1:1 observation, and psychiatry was consulted. They recommended starting sertraline daily for mood, and it was titrated up to 75mg daily prior to discharge. He was continued on Ativan 0.5mg BID prn anxiety. Per psych consult, patient no longer expressing SI, and did not have intent or plan. 1:1 observation was discontinued, and we continued medication titration. During his stay, his mood fluctuated, and he continued to report symptoms of depressed mood. His sertraline was increased to 75mg daily ___, and he understood it may take several weeks before he feels the mood benefits of this medication. # CAUTI On admission UA positive but urine was collected through chronic indwelling foley. Repeat UA ___ through new foley was positive with >180 WBC. He was started on ciprofloxacin 500mg BID PO for 14 day course for CAUTI. Discussed with patient risk of infection if foley is kept in, and he refused a trial to void or urologic consultation because he wants to keep foley and continue to follow as an outpatient. Continue Ciprofloxacin 500mg Q12H until ___. # Swallow Deemed safe for thin liquids and pureed foods by speech and swallow. Transitional Issues: Monitor mood symptoms, can increase sertraline to 100mg if no improvement over the next few days after discharge. Continue antibiotics for UTI until ___. Follow up with Dr. ___ at ___ Unit
summary: You were hospitalized for changes in your mood, including thoughts of hurting yourself. We stopped your tetrabenazine and olanzapine because we thought these medications may have been contributing to your mood. We found that you also had a urinary tract infection, and treated you with antibiotics for that for a 14 day course. We changed your medications to help improve your mood, and increased your sertraline while you were here. We discussed how it might take a few weeks for you to start feeling mood improvement on the medications. We discussed trying to take out your foley, but you did not want us to do this. We got you ready to go to ___ Unit, where you will have specialized care for your symptoms. Start taking Sertraline 75 mg daily and Aripiprazole 10mg daily to help with your mood.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Summary: ___ y/o F with Hep C Cirrhosis, end stage c/b encephalopathy, and severe depression with SI who was admitted to the psychiatric service for worsening depression, and possible plans to drink ___ ___, transferred to ___ for hepatic encephalopathy in setting of decreased bowel movements. . # Hepatic encephalopathy: Patient has marked laxative requirement. She improved with aggressive bowel regimen. A Gardnella UTI was also treated with flagyl. . # Depression: On extensive psychiatric medications. Has had multiple previous suicide attempts/thoughts. While on ___ 10, she did not endorse SI, and her mood improved. Psychiatry felt she was safe to be discharged with outpatient follow-up . # Unsteady gait: Likely secondary to deconditioning/obesity, Ms. ___ a fall without injury in the shower this admission, but was ultimately cleared by ___ to go home. . # HCV Cirrhosis: Continued PPI. Patient had colonoscopy done this admission for transplant screening. . # Dacroadynitis: Evaluated by ophtho while on deac 4. Felt may be secondary to sarcoidosis. Needs outpatient follow-up given elevated ACE levels and CT chest done as outpatient previously. . # Chronic pain: Has narcotic contract. Likely secondary to rheumatoid arthritis. Narcotics were used judiciously to prevent worsening encephalopathy. Due to chronic abdominal pain, a colonoscopy was obtained (twice due to improper prep during the first attempt) which demonstrated a single polyp which was not removed secondary to coagulopathy. Ms. ___ was maintained on her home pain regimen. . # Insulin dependent diabetes: -Sliding scale, glargine at night -Regular finger sticks . # OSA: Continue CPAP
summary: You were admitted to the medicine service from Psychiatry for confusion. This most likely occurred due to your liver disease and not receiving medications for this; the lactulose makes you have bowel movements, which clears your mind. You also underwent the endoscopy/colonoscopy that was planned with Dr. ___. It showed you do not have to be on special medications for your esophagus and polyps in your colon were biopsied (sampled). Dr. ___ will update you on the results from these samples. You were also seen by Ophthalmology while you were here for a red, swollen right eye. You were treated with oral antibiotics and responded well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: A/P: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o female with a history of depression, alcohol abuse, CAD s/p PCI (DES in LAD) and DM2 who presented intoxicated noted to have electrolyte abnormalities including hyponatremia and hypomagnesemia. . 1) Depression: Her depression appears to be situational and notes that she becomes very depressed during the holidays. She denies any suicidal or homicidal ideation. Social worker visited her and recommended a few facilities which offered therapy. She was advised to schedule and outpatient psychiatric appointment after she visits her PCP. . 2) Alcohol Abuse: She notes that she wants to stop drinking and is willing to quit. She has never withdrawal symptoms including shakes or seizures. She did not require detox due to short period of alcohol abuse. She was advised not to continue drinking and was told about the danger of withdrawal. . 3) Hyponatremia: Her sodium was down to 123 when she presented to the ED. After 1L of NS her sodium increased close to her baseline of 130. Her hyponatremia was most likley secondary to hypovolemia and decreased oral intake. Urine Osm was 168 and FeNa was calculated at 2.6%. She is on HCTZ at home which may confound her urine lytes. She was restarted on her HCTZ prior to discharge. . 4) Hypomagnesemia: Her magnesium was noted to be 1.1. this is most likley secondary to malnutrition and alcohol use. Magnesium corrected prior to discharge. . 5) CAD: She is chest pain free and it appears that her chest pressure was due to her anxiety and depresion. However with her history of CAD and electrolyte abnormalities she would benefit from telemetry. No significant events on telemetry. . 6) DM2: She is on metformin at home however will be held during this admission and placed on an insulin SS. She was restarted on her home regimen.
summary: You were admitted because you were drinking a lot of alcohol and you were found to have electrolyte abnormalities. Your sodium and your magnesium were noted to be very low. You were given some fluid and magnesium. Prior to discharge your electrolytes normalized. Drinking a lot of alcohol can be life threatening and does cause low sodium and low magnesium. You are on a medication called hydrochlorothiazide which can result in worsening imbalance. The social worker visited you and left you information for support groups. This all appears to be due to your depression. You would benefit from psychiatry evaluation as an outpatient. Please discuss with your primary care doctor about scheduling an appointment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ hx of Bipolar disorder, who presented to ___ s/p MVC suicide attempt (car vs wall @ 50mph) with prolonged ___ transferred to ___ care for small R subarachnoid hemorrhage, open right knee fracture with complete patellar tendon tear, right pulmonary contusions and c/f pneumothorax, multiple facial lacerations, C7/T1 transverse process fractures and left inferior orbital floor fracture. Patient received CT of the head/neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/RLE as well as plain films of R femur, R tib fib, and L shoulder. Neuro: The patient presented with a small R subarachnoid hemorrhage that was stable on repeat head MRI. Neurosurgery was consulted and recommended Q4 neuro checks, no Keppra, and outpatient follow-up w/ ___ clinic. Patient remained alert and oriented and neurologically intact throughout his TSICU course. His pain was initially managed with IV pain meds and then transitioned to oral medications once tolerating a diet. Per NSGY ok to start sqh on HD2. CV: The patient received 1u pRBCs for hypotension on ___ upon arrival in the Emergency Department. He has otherwise remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint and has since then not required other transfusions; vital signs were routinely monitored. Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. He remained comfortable on room air throughout his TSICU course. GI/GU/FEN: Once the patient was medically stabilized, his diet was advanced sequentially to a Regular diet, which was well tolerated. Patient's intake and output were closely monitored. ID: The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. He has completed his course of ___ antibiotics per Orthopedics. HEME: The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs of bleeding, of which there were none. MSK: Patient presented with open right knee fracture with complete patellar tendon tear. Patient was taken for washout of R knee w/ Orthopedic surgery on ___ and then takeback for repair of R open patellar tendon injury on ___. He currently has ___ brace on the RLE and is weight-bearing as tolerated per Ortho. Tertiary survey performed by trauma team on HD2 and no additional injuries were identified. Psychiatry: Patient drove car into wall in attempted suicide. Followed by psychiatry while inpatient. Had a 1:1 sitter in TSICU and on floor, likely ___ after medically stabilized, Lamictal. Txfr to psych after OR w ortho & on floor Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and ___ dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was tolerating a diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.
summary: You were admitted to ___ after a suicide attempt crashing your car into a wall. CT imaging showed multiple injuries: a small amount of bleeding in your brain, spine fractures at C7 and T1, left orbital fracture, and open right patella fracture. Neurosurgery has been following you for the traumatic brain injury, which does not require any intervention. You can follow-up in the Cognitive Neurology clinic for post-concussive syndrome as needed. Spine examined you for the C7 and T1 fracture. They recommended non-operative treatment with a hard collar at all times. Opthalmology examined your eye due to the orbital fracture, and deemed it to be normal with no injury. Orthopedics took you to the operating room twice for repair of the tendon and open knee fracture. You should keep it in the ___ brace and can bear weight on the leg. You are now doing well and medically clear for discharge to inpatient psyche for ongoing therapy.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ hx of Bipolar disorder, who presented to ___ s/p MVC suicide attempt (car vs wall @ 50mph) with prolonged ___ transferred to ___ care for small R subarachnoid hemorrhage, open right knee fracture with complete patellar tendon tear, right pulmonary contusions and c/f pneumothorax, multiple facial lacerations, C7/T1 transverse process fractures and left inferior orbital floor fracture. Patient received CT of the head/neck/chest/abdomen/pelvis/RLE as well as plain films of R femur, R tib fib, and L shoulder. Neuro: The patient presented with a small R subarachnoid hemorrhage that was stable on repeat head MRI. Neurosurgery was consulted and recommended Q4 neuro checks, no Keppra, and outpatient follow-up w/ ___ clinic. Patient remained alert and oriented and neurologically intact throughout his TSICU course. His pain was initially managed with IV pain meds and then transitioned to oral medications once tolerating a diet. Per NSGY ok to start sqh on HD2. CV: The patient received 1u pRBCs for hypotension on ___ upon arrival in the Emergency Department. He has otherwise remained stable from a cardiovascular standpoint and has since then not required other transfusions; vital signs were routinely monitored. Pulmonary: The patient remained stable from a pulmonary standpoint; vital signs were routinely monitored. Good pulmonary toilet, early ambulation and incentive spirometry were encouraged throughout hospitalization. He remained comfortable on room air throughout his TSICU course. GI/GU/FEN: Once the patient was medically stabilized, his diet was advanced sequentially to a Regular diet, which was well tolerated. Patient's intake and output were closely monitored. ID: The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. He has completed his course of ___ antibiotics per Orthopedics. HEME: The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs of bleeding, of which there were none. MSK: Patient presented with open right knee fracture with complete patellar tendon tear. Patient was taken for washout of R knee w/ Orthopedic surgery on ___ and then takeback for repair of R open patellar tendon injury on ___. He currently has ___ brace on the RLE and is weight-bearing as tolerated per Ortho. Tertiary survey performed by trauma team on HD2 and no additional injuries were identified. Psychiatry: Patient drove car into wall in attempted suicide. Followed by psychiatry while inpatient. Had a 1:1 sitter in TSICU and on floor, likely ___ after medically stabilized, Lamictal. Txfr to psych after OR w ortho & on floor Prophylaxis: The patient received subcutaneous heparin and ___ dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was tolerating a diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient was transferred to our institution by ambulance from ___ ___ where he presented following a suicide attempt through a self-afflicted neck laceration. Upon arrival to our emergency department, patient was hemodynamically stable and complaining of minimal pain on laceration site. Patient did not complain of cough, hematemesis, or hoarseness. Given extent of the injury and scattered areas where the wound seemed to violate the platysma, a CT angiogram of the neck was obtained. The study was reassuring, confirming the absence of major vascular injuries. Patient was taken to the operating room shortly afterwards where a neck exploration, wound washout, control of small bleeders were performed (see operative note for details). No injuries to vital structures were evidenced. A tracheoscopy performed in the operating room was also negative for injuries. Wound was copiously irrigated and closed by primary intention. Patient tolerated the procedure well and was transferred to the ward after a brief stay in the PACU. ___ was filed and patient remained on a one-to-one monitoring throughout hospitalization. Once stable from his injury, the psychiatry team was consulted for evaluation and disposition recommendations. Upon evaluation, patient was deemed suitable for recovery in an inpatient psychiatric unit once medically stable. Psychiatric medications were resumed as recommended. Case management was involved for bed search. Of note, on hospitalization day #2, patient complained of mild epigastric pain. An abdominal ultrasound did not show any acute pathology, but to fatty liver and splenomegaly. Liver function tests were normal but to elevated transaminases, likely at baseline given patient's hepatitis C status. He was started on a proton-pump inhibitor for likely reflux symptoms. Upon discharge, patient's vital signs were all stable. Cardiopulmonary and abdominal exams were benign. Incision looked clean, dry and intact. He was tolerating a regular diet and ambulating with out assistance. Discharge instructions were communicated and patient shows understanding and agreement with follow-up plan.
summary: You were seen at our institution after being transferred from an outside hospital to take care of a self-inflicted wound to your neck. Fotunately, your evaluation upon arrival was reassuring and imaging tests performed confirmed that important structures in your neck had been spared from injury. You were taken to the operating room to further explore your wound and clean it, as well as to make sure that no inadvertent injuries were present. An evaluation of your trachea (windpipe) was reassuring. Given the context of your wounds as a suicidal attempt, the psychiatry team in our institution was involved in your care. They deemed appropriate for you to recover in an ___ facility upon medical clearance. We now feel comfortable clearing you from a medical standpoint. You have a few stitches in your neck that will need to be removed in our clinic in ___ days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo woman with depression, anxiety, fibromyalgia, colitis (possibly IBS or Crohn's) who presents with a protracted history of increasing disability over the past 6 months leading to more frequent falls over the last ___ months leading eventually to admission for work-up and care of severe diffuse pains but especially in legs and feet. During the hospitalization, Ms. ___ has been maintained on her home pain regimen. Her regimen has been verified many times with her PCP and various pharmacies. Her pain was controlled with her home Vicodin ___ tabs every 6 hours prn and MS contin 30mg BID. Due to continual pain, her Gabapentin was increased from 300mg TID to ___ TID. She tolerated this well. We also started Lidocaine patches to her feet bilaterally which also helped with the pain. Her pain was well controlled on this regimen. In regards to the subjective report of bilateral leg weakness, she was worked up for possible peripheral neuropathy. Her exam inconistent and very heavily effort dependent. She had difficulty with standing due to severe ansiety. She was able to squat on her toes which takes more muscle strength than standing. The sensory exam is patchy. B12, ESR/CRP, A1C, HepC, HepB, ___ lab tests was unremarkable. No evidence of spinal cord comprssion or abnormal enhancement. MRI spine with contrast showed mild spondylosis of the cervical and lumbar spine with no evidence of spinal cord compression, spinal cord signal abnormality, or abnormal enhancement. MRI brain was also unremarkable with no abnormalities that account for the symptoms. An LP was attempted both on the floor with ___ guidance but neither were successful. It was recommended that Ms. ___ have a repeat ___ guidance attempt but she refused. It was also suggested that Ms. ___ have a EMG to rule out possible peroneal nerve dysfunction but she also refused EMG while in the hospital. We will schedule an EMG outpatient and Neurology Outpatient Clinic follow up.
summary: You were admitted to ___ Neurology Inpatient Service due to not being able to walk and being in pain. During the hospitalization, we continued you on your home pain regimen. We did increase your Gabapentin to 600mg three times a day. We also started lidocaine patches on your feet because that is where the pain is. We did a spine MRI which did not show any spinal cord abnormalities. We did a brain MRI which does not show any explanation for your leg weakness and pain. We also attempted to obtain CSF from a spinal tap but was unable to do so with multiple attempts including one with Radiology. You refused to have any further attempts. We suggested a EMG, but you refused this test. We believe you most feet pain and difficulty standing is not due to an abnormality in your brain or your spine. You have pain in your feet bilateral which is causing great anxiety to stand and with deconditioning, you also have tightness in your heel cords. We recommend that you have intensive inpatient therapy. You are also scheduled for a EMG outpatient.
