text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of depression, hypertension, and hypothyroidism, admitted to the ICU for AMS, hypotension, hypothermia, following intentional polysubstance ingestion. # AMS/hypotension/hypothermia/metabolic nonanion gap and respiratory acidosis: Patient called EMS reporting toxic ingestion and was found with pills and glass strewn around her living area. At home patient is on seroquel, ativan, gabapentin, nadololm which were suspected of being ingested at supratherapeutic levels. Although serum and urine tox screens are negative, seroquel, gabepentin and ativan would not show up on routine tox screens (all of which she is prescribed). Additionally, given her relative bradycardia (60s) in the setting of hypotension, would additionally be concerned for toxic nadolol ingestion (BB overdose). She was admitted to the ICU for close monitoring. She was hypotensive to MAPs int he high ___ and so was started on neosynephrine peripherally. Given concern for beta blocker toxicity, patient was bolused with glucagon 5mg IV push with improvement in HR from ___ to 70-80s and resultant improvement in blood pressures. She was then started on a glucagon ggt at 5mg/hr and neosynephrine was weaned off. Occasional boluses of 1L LR was administered for MAPs in the low ___ and patient improved. Infection was ruled out with negative UA, CXR which showed low lung volumes with some atelectatic changes at the bases without focal PNA and blood cultures which showed no growth at time of discharge. Toxicology followed the patient in house and recommended continuation of supportive measures. Warming blanket was applied for hypothermia and Qtc was monitored out of concern for seroquel ingestion (Qtc 407 prior to trasnfer from ICU). Lactate 0.7. No signs of seratonin syndrome (hyperthermia, agitation, clonus, hyperreflexia). Flumazenil was avoided, as patient is on chronic benzodiazepines, and therefore would be at risk of precipitating seizures and benzo withdrawl. Over the night, patient's vital signs and mental status returned to baseline. Patient confirmed that she had intentionall tried to kill herself. Psychiatry was consulted and patient was trasnferred to the medicine floor for further monitoring where she remained alert and oriented x 3, continued with 1:1 sitter and medically cleared for transfer to inpatient psych. # Depression: Pt has h/o depression and reports recent worsening anxiety and depression related to financial difficulties and the death of her cat. She attempted suicide by ingesting multiple prescribed medications including ativan, ambien, gabapentin, seroquel and cutting her forearms with broken glass. She endorses anxiety related to transfer to inpatient psych, but acknowledges the need for further treatment in order to ensure her safety. # ___: Unknown recent baseline (no reported history of CKD, last Cr 1.0 in ___, Cr 1.65 at OSH prior to fluid resuscitation. Improved to 1.1 with agressive fluid resuscitation within the first day. Stablilized at 1.3. # Hypothyroidism: continued on levothyroxine 62.5mcg IV (equivalent to home 125mcg PO) in ICU. # UTI: Pt complained of dysuria, urinary frequency ___, foley recently dc'd after transfer from MICU. UA showed 44 RBCs, >180 WBCs, + protein. Started bactrim for planned 3 day course. Urine culture pending. Transitional Issues: # Communication: HCP is friend Ms. ___ ___ # Code: DNR/DNI # Depression/SI: Psych meds held this admission with exception of prn lorazepam for anxiety. She will be admitted to inpatient psych for further management and titration of her medications. # UTI: Urine culture ___ pending at time of discharge.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after taking an overdose of your home medications due to depression. You were sent to the intensive care unit for confusion and low blood pressure and heart rate where you were briefly given IV medication to support your blood pressure and you were hydrated with IV fluids. You were later transferred to the medical floor and your mental status and blood pressure where back to normal. Due to concern for your safety, a safety sitter remained present in your room. You were again evaluated by the psychiatry team who recommended that you be transferred to an inpatient psych floor for further management of your depression. Prior to transfer, you had frequent, painful urination and a urine test showed evidence of a urinary tract infection. You were started on an antibiotic called Bactrim that you should continue to take twice a day for the next 3 days and we expect your symptoms to improve.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ y.o female with h.o fibromyalgia, depression/anxiety, GERD who presents with reports of GERD, dysphagia/odynophagia, and chest cramping/spasm. . #GERD/sternal burning/dysphagia/odynophagia-Pt reports that these symptoms were present prior to last esophageal dilation and had improved, now are worsened. DDX includes gastritis/esophagitis, PUD, esophageal spasm, esophageal stricture, motility/functional disorder. Pt does have a history of GERD and has been taking motrin at home. CXR does not show free air, EGD shows erythema in the stomach, but otherwise no PUD. LFTs/lipase normal. EKG is unchanged from prior and 2 sets of cardiac enzymes were negative. GI recommended changing PPI to 20mg BID, continuing h2 blocker and starting sucralfate. GI also recommending considering motility study and/or video swallowing evaluation. However, in order to obtain an accurate motility study, pt would not be able to take narcotics and benzodiazepines which she is currently taking. Pt did not wish to pursue this study at this time for fear of "withdrawal" from these medications. As her diet was successfully advanced, this further work up (video swallow) was deferred to the outpatient setting. Stools were ordered for guaiac. . #constipation-increased bowel regimen to senna, colace, miralax . #depression/anxiety-continued home meds of fluoxetine, klonapin, doxepin, seroquel, tegretol. Denied worsening of symptoms or SI. . #chronic pain/fibromyalgia/neuropathy-continued home regimen of neurontin, vicodin, held NSAIDS for now given symptoms as above. . #hyponatremia-likely hypovolemic due to poor PO intake as resolved with IVF. But did consider SIADH from psychiatric meds . #anemia-normocytic baseline 35-37. Remained close to baseline. Denied melena/brbpr . #FEN-regular . #ppx-hep SC TID . Transitional care: -consider ordering video swallow study and/or gastric emptying study (pt would have to be off narcotics)
summary: You were admitted with reports of painful and difficulty swallowing, as well as abdominal pain. For this, you underwent an endoscopy that showed some mild irritation (red areas) in your stomach. Biopsies were taken and PENDING at the time of discharge. Given your history of reflux and endoscopy findings it was recommended that you take prilosec 20mg twice a day, pepcid twice a day, and started on a medication called sucralfate to protect your stomach. You were able to eat a regular diet after endoscopy. Your symptoms may be due to a spasm in your esophagus. You may discuss treatment options, if this is the case, with your gastroenterologist. Please discuss the need for a motility study and/or a video swallowing test at your next GI appointment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo male with crohn's disease s/p subtotal colectomy on prednisone, depression, and recent NSTEMI s/p bare metal ___ p/w rectal bleeding and prolapse x 3 days and decreased hct ___. . # rectal bleeding: amount of bleeding difficult to quantify per pt's history. However given decreased Hct, h/o pouchitis, and new-onset prolapse, pt underwent sigmoidoscopy. Biopsy ___ did not show any dysplasia, but given his extensive h/o Crohn's disease and FH and although colon was removed, must consider malignancy. Hemorrhoids also likely. Pt on plavix and aspirin for ___, per cards pt may not d/c these. He received 2 units of RBCs in ED and his Hct stabilized at 29 on discharge. CT scan showed now clear abonormalities or obstruction. Sigmoidoscopy confirmed pouchitis with erythema c/w prolapse. No interventions were necessary. He will f/u with his GI specialist. . # anemia: in acute setting of bleeding, but MCV slightly low and pt has significant crohn's disease. Iron studies confirmed iron deficiency anemia. He would benefit from supplemental iron. He may have chronic slow bleeding from constant rectal irritation. He will f/u with his PCP. . # crohn's disease: s/p subtotel colectomy ___, prednisone chronically, diagnosed at young age, was on Remicade which has been held for recurrent infections. Pt has pouchitis confirmed by sigmoidoscopy. . # chronic pain: pt has pain contract with his physician and takes methadone and oxycontin PRN. He was discharged on his regular pain regimen and will f/u with his PCP. . # cardiac: s/p recent NSTEMI 3 weeks, w/ bare-metal ___ to LAD, otherwise clear cath. No current chest pain but has experienced chest pain since cath. He will continue atorvastatin, beta blocker, statin, plavix, aspirin, glucose control. He will f/u with outpt cards and may need repeat exercise stress test once medically stable. . # psyche: significant h/o depression, +SI per pt, pt agrees to notify staff if has plan. He appears to have chronic depression. His PCP confirmed that he was at his baseline. MSW was consulted and spoke with the patient. . # DM: ___ chronic prednisone, HbgA1c 7 ___, on lantus 32 QHS and sliding scale as outpt. . # contact: sister-in-law ___ ___ . # Dispo: home
summary: You were admitted with rectal prolapse and bleeding. Your blood count was a little low and you received 2 units of blood and your blood count increased appropriately. Aspirin and plavix, which you need to take for your cardiac ___ increase bleeding. You cannot stop this medication at this time for risk of the ___ occluding. You may discuss with Dr. ___ in Cardiology at your appointment if any of these can be held. You were seen by gastroenterology who did a sigmoidoscopy which did not show any concerning bleeding but confirmed redness consistent with your prolapse. Nothing interventional needs to be done at this time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ has had several recent admissions for chronic abdominal pain here at ___. We initially considered surgical possibilities for her pain, but none were apparent upon review of her clinical status and laboratory assessment. A CT scan at an OSH showed no evidence of ileus or obstruction. Given a slowly decreasing hematocrit, GI was consulted; there was however no evidence of frank melena or hematochezia and her anemia was felt to be secondary to malabsorption/iron deficiency in the setting of her complex bowel anatomy. Her G tube output was also clear. We felt constipation may have been contributing to her pain so we started her on an aggressive bowel regimen. Her pain was otherwise managed with dilaudid 4 mg q4. She had no evidence of hepatitis given the prior history of tylenol ingestion; her APAP level was negligible and there was no need for NAC administration. Her abdominal pain improved slightly with improved bowel movements. Her G tube was kept to gravity and remained clear (no need for suctioning). We felt that a lot of her abdominal pain was exacerbated by her underlying psychiatric comorbidities including depression and significant family stressors at home. In the setting of prior tylenol intake (although she claims was non-intentional) and self-inflicted injury (per OSH report and numerous bruises throughout her body and RN witnessed self abuse - pinching, etc) we felt her depression needed to be addressed directly in the inpatient setting rather than as an outpatient. Working with social work, we discussed her depression and suggested an inpatient voluntary psychiatry admission. Psychiatry was consulted; after a multidisciplinary discussion, Ms ___ did feel that inpatient psychiatry would help her with her depression and acknowledged depression as a major barrier for improvement in her quality of life. Her celexa was increased to 40 mg from 20 mg and she was given trazadone for assistance with insomnia. She did endorse passive suicidal ideation intermittently; she was initiated on seroquel for anxiety and her lorazepam was weaned to 1 mg TID. She was then transferred to ___ for ongoing inpatient psychiatry care.
summary: You were admitted because of increasing abdominal pain and increasing need for tylenol and pain medications. You had reported blood in your stool; to investigate this possibility further, we consulted the GI team and the general surgery team. Although you did have low blood counts, we did not feel this was because of active bleeding from your gut. Your low blood counts are probably because of low absorption of iron; for this reason we continued your iron supplementation. We also gave you an aggressive bowel regimen to keep your bowels moving; constipation may have been playing a role in your increasing abdominal pain. You also had an elevated tylenol level at ___ prior to your admission here; our tests revealed your tylenol level was non-toxic so this did not require further work-up. We had psychiatry see you because of your long-standing depression and they started you on Seroquel and decreased the amount of Ativan you were needing. We agreed as a team and support your decision to pursue inpatient psychiatry as a way to help bring your depression under control so that you are more able to manage some of the other medical and social issues that are present in your life.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ has had several recent admissions for chronic abdominal pain here at ___. We initially considered surgical possibilities for her pain, but none were apparent upon review of her clinical status and laboratory assessment. A CT scan at an OSH showed no evidence of ileus or obstruction. Given a slowly decreasing hematocrit, GI was consulted; there was however no evidence of frank melena or hematochezia and her anemia was felt to be secondary to malabsorption/iron deficiency in the setting of her complex bowel anatomy. Her G tube output was also clear. We felt constipation may have been contributing to her pain so we started her on an aggressive bowel regimen. Her pain was otherwise managed with dilaudid 4 mg q4. She had no evidence of hepatitis given the prior history of tylenol ingestion; her APAP level was negligible and there was no need for NAC administration. Her abdominal pain improved slightly with improved bowel movements. Her G tube was kept to gravity and remained clear (no need for suctioning). We felt that a lot of her abdominal pain was exacerbated by her underlying psychiatric comorbidities including depression and significant family stressors at home. In the setting of prior tylenol intake (although she claims was non-intentional) and self-inflicted injury (per OSH report and numerous bruises throughout her body and RN witnessed self abuse - pinching, etc) we felt her depression needed to be addressed directly in the inpatient setting rather than as an outpatient. Working with social work, we discussed her depression and suggested an inpatient voluntary psychiatry admission. Psychiatry was consulted; after a multidisciplinary discussion, Ms ___ did feel that inpatient psychiatry would help her with her depression and acknowledged depression as a major barrier for improvement in her quality of life. Her celexa was increased to 40 mg from 20 mg and she was given trazadone for assistance with insomnia. She did endorse passive suicidal ideation intermittently; she was initiated on seroquel for anxiety and her lorazepam was weaned to 1 mg TID. She was then transferred to ___ for ongoing inpatient psychiatry care.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ======== Ms. ___ is a ___ F w/ hypertension, atrial fibrillation on warfarin with an MRI compatible pacemaker for sick sinus syndrome, hyperlipidemia, hypothyroidism, depression, anxiety, and bronchiectasis with recurrent PNA who presented with four weeks of diarrhea and was discharged with outpatient GI follow-up. ACUTE PROBLEMS: =============== #Diarrhea Patient p/w diarrhea x 1 month. Previous C diff and stool cultures negative at urgent care ___. Labs here notable for normal LFTs, lipase and WBC count. No overt medications that would cause this. TSH at 1.15 last month. Patient is on several mood medications but clinically not consistent with serotonin toxicity. Patient does have a history of IPMN but do not suspect this is contributing, as no mass was noted on a CT scan at ___ ___ recently reportedly, although we were unable to obtain these records. History concerning for possible inflammatory bowel disease including microscopic colitis versus less likely a malignant cause of secretory diarrhea. Celiac disease testing pending. She was given a referral to ___ for colonoscopy with biopsy once her INR normalizes, as it was 7.8. She had a very mild acute kidney injury, but otherwise no showed no signs of dehydration or hypotension that required hospitalization. She was tolerating good PO intake at discharge. ___ Baseline Cr 0.7 per Atrius outpatient records. Presented with Cr 1.1, likely pre-renal ___ iso GI losses. Resolved to 0.8 after 2L LR. PCP recommended to start outpatient ACEI, but held this during hospitalization. #HTN Patient with history of HTN in the past, controlled with diltiazem (on for Afib). Over the past few days, SBPs elevated to 180s and patient was recommended to start ACEI by PCP. Continued home Diltiazem Extended-Release 240 mg PO DAILY. Did not start lisinopril as planned by PCP given diarrhea and mild ___. SBPs were in the 120s-130s. #Afib s/p pacemaker Goal INR ___, CHADS2VASc=4. Patient currently takes warfarin 6.25mg M/F and 7.5mg 5d/week. Patient notes INR has been okay despite changed diet recently. INR was 7.8 on admission. She was given script for repeat labs on ___ and instructed to call her ___ clinic, as she was discharged after hours. She was instructed to hold warfarin until then. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #HLD Continued Simvastatin 10 mg PO QPM #Depression/anxiety Continued BusPIRone 15 mg PO QAM, BusPIRone 30 mg PO QHS, Sertraline 150 mg PO DAILY, and Mirtazapine 5 mg PO QHS (currently tapering down from 30mg qhs, takes ___ pill ~5mg) # Insomnia Takes 3 mg melatonin at home. Instructed her she can increase to 6 mg. #Bronchiectasis c/b recurrent PNA Dyspnea currently at baseline. Takes fluticasone furoate-vilanterol 100-25 mcg/dose inhalation daily at home (non-formulary), provided fluticasone-salmeterol while inpatient #Overactive bladder Held home mirabegron as nonformulary. #Hypothyroidism TSH nl last week. Continued Levothyroxine Sodium 112 mcg PO DAILY #CODE: Full confirmed #CONTACT/HCP: ___ (husband) Phone number: ___ ___ ISSUES: ===================== [] Ensure colonoscopy in next ___ weeks with biopsy to rule out microscopic colitis. If she can not be booked at ___, please refer to ___ in ___ or another gastroenterologist close to home [] Follow up pending TTG antibody (total IgA level was normal) [] Obtain records from ___ re: CT scan (requested multiple times and did not receive) [] Please check INR on ___ and recommend warfarin dose [] Make plan with GI clinic regarding need for bridging for procedure [] Recheck Cr and BPs before restarting lisinopril
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you are having diarrhea. You were given IV fluids. You were seen by the gastroenterology team who recommended an outpatient colonoscopy given that you showed no signs of dehydration or bleeding. We also noted that you had a high INR, so we felt it would not be safe to do colonoscopy with biopsy at this time. You were referred to gastroenterology office at ___ for outpatient work-up. If you do not hear from this office within 48 hours, please call them at the number below to ensure that your colonoscopy scheduled. Please try to stay hydrated and eat foods that do not upset your stomach. Please do not take warfarin until told to do so by your ___ clinic. Please have labs drawn on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is ___ with an extensive psychiatric history who was admitted following a self-extricated MVC and police dog bite on his medial and lateral posterior right calf. In the ER, he was found to have a right popliteal artery occlusion that reconstituted distally and tibial artery vasospasm, therefore, Vascular surgery was consulted. He was started on heparin gtt. During this admission, he continually had dopplerable signals (___) in the distal foot monitor at regular intervals. Non-invasive arterial study of the right limb later in this admission revealed no flow-limiting stenosis. Once it was determined that no surgical intervention would be needed, the heparin was transitioned to Xarelto. Early in the admission he had erythema and pain around the dog bite wounds consistent with SSI. Unasyn was started in the ED and later transitioned to Augmentin for a 14d course. The wounds continually drained serous fluid. The erythema resolved and pain improved. He was afebrile during this entire admission. A small 1.5cm fluid collection was noted on RLE U/S but given his vital sign stability, lack of leukocytosis, and clear improvement with antibiotics no intervention was warranted. Orthopedics was consulted early in this admission due to concern for ligamentous injury to the knee but no interventions were warranted. He worked with ___ that found he had difficult dorsiflexion that will require further ___. Mr. ___ most active issue that was a primary cause of his admission is his paranoid delusion. The patient revealed that he was attempting to out run the police at 120 mph and purposefully wrecked his car (rollover) in order to commit suicide because he feared the police were going to kill him. His delusions centered around persecution by his parents by bribing others to torture him for being a male. He expressed distrust for women and expressed this to many staff. He was never violent but was occasionally was agitated, nevertheless, he never required restraints (neither physical or chemical). Although he generally followed the recommended treatment plan, toward the day of discharge he begin to refuse lab draws and medications. Psychiatry was consulted and deemed him to be without capacity and he was placed under ___. His health care proxy's, his parents, met with providers to provide collateral of his significant past psychiatric history. He was started on Zyprexa 2.5mg qhs but no other medications were initiated during this visit. By discharge, his only active issues were wound care, physical therapy needs, and acute paranoid delusion. The patient has been medically cleared for discharge to in-patient psychiatry
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you were involved in a MVC. While fleeing the accident site, you received a dog bite to the right posterior lower extremity. You were started on an antibiotic. A tourniquet was applied to the leg after the injury. A CTA of the leg was done which showed a right popliteal artery occlusion and you were started on a heparin drip and Doppler pulse checks of the foot. You were seen by the Orthopedic and vascular services. You have been undergoing dressing changes to your leg. The Psychiatry service was consulted for mental status evaluation concerning paranoid psychosis. The patient has been followed by the Psychiatry and recommendations made for discharge to a in-patient psychiatric facility.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with a past medical history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, hypertension, hyperlipidemia, EtOH abuse, depression presenting with afib and RVR and suicidality. # Paroxysmal atrial Fibrillation: Presented to the ED with Afib/RVR and rates to the 140s. Likely precipitant was recent excessive alcohol use. He was successfully cardioverted to NSR on HD2 after a TEE showed no atrial thrombus. He maintained sinus rhythm for the rest of this admission. He was also started on flecainide and home atenolol was changed to metoprolol succinate 25mg daily. He was started on warfarin with a Lovenox bridge for anticoagulation given his cardioversion this admission. Will need to be anticoagulated for at least 4 weeks after cardioversion, and would consider longer anticoagulation given his PAF. He will have his INR monitored at his PCP's office ___ office) and they weren otified of this by phone. An exercise stress and echocardiogram were ordered for 1 month from discharge to assess for arrhythmia or QRS widening with exertion and increased HR while on flecainide. He was also sent home with ___ of Hearts monitor. He will follow-up with Dr. ___ in clinic. # Chest Pain: He reported episodes of substernal chest pain this admission while at rest. EKG was normal with each episode and cardiac enzymes were negative. He had a treadmill exercise stress test which showed no ischemic EKG changes, but he did again have chest pain with exertion during the test. Patient was reassured that his chest pain is unlikely to be cardiac in etiology. # Suicidality: Patient expressed suicidality while he was admitted, reports recent psych admission after he intentionally cut himself at home a few weeks prior to presentation. He had no specific plan and was able to contract for safety. Psych was consulted and felt that there were no acute safety concerns to warrant inpatient admission. He was arranged for an outpatient substance abuse and depression program. He was continued on Wellbutrin, Paxil and trazodone. # Hypertension: Normotensive on metoprolol 25mg daily. # Chronic pain: Initially reported that he takes Percocet ___ 2 tabs every 4 hours. Confirmed with home pharmacy that his prescribed dose is Percocet ___ 1 tab every 6 hours as needed. He was continued on this home regimen while in house. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued pravastatin. # CODE STATUS THIS ADMISSION: Full # EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ - ___ # Transitional issues: -Continue Lovenox until INR is 2.0-3.0 -INR will be monitored by Dr. ___ ___ ___ in ___, needs at least 4 weeks of anticoagulation -Ordered for repeat stress test and TTE in 1 month for monitoring after starting flecainide -Sent home with ___ of Hearts monitor for arrhythmia monitoring -Will be enrolled in outpatient psych patrial program at discharge
summary: You were admitted with palpitations and found to have atrial fibrillation. You underwent a cardioversion and were back in normal rhythm afterwards. It is very likely that your alcohol use precipitated the atrial fibrillation and you should stop drinking alcohol. We also started you two new medications, flecainide and metoprolol to try to keep the heart in normal rhythm. You will need a heart monitor, an echocardiogram and a stress test in 1 month to monitor this medication. Please call ___ to schedule the stress test and exercise stress test, these should be done around ___. You have also been given a heart monitor which you should use as directed. You will need blood thinners for at least 4 weeks after your cardioversion. You should continue Lovenox until your INR is therapeutic (2.0-3.0).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o male with a history of cerebral palsy, past psych history of depression and anxiety with 1 prior psychiatric hospitalization and 2 prior suicide attempts, who initially presented to the ED with one day of fever, complaints of abdominal pain and several panic attacks. For his abdominal pain, patient initially expressed that his "guts were about to explode." Physical exam was benign and CT abdomen was negative for acute process. Regarding his mood disorders, psychiatry was consulted and recommended inpatient psychiatry for further treatment. Patient remained on the medicine floor for 9 days until an inpatient psych bed became available. #Depression with PTSD and anxiety: Previous history of suicide attempt in ___ when he was being emotionally and physically abused by his brother. With input from the Psychiatry team, he has been maintained on a regimen of Escitalopram Oxalate, Mirtazapine, Olanzapine, and Propranolol. For severe agitation, Haldol, Ativan, and additional Olanzapine were recommended as needed but they were not required during his hospital stay. QTc on day of discharge was: 416. #Back pain: Chronic in nature. Per his guardian, patient has pressure ulcers that he was treated for in ___. Physical exam revealed an erythematous patch on his back, likely in the setting of stasis from immobility. Treatment with frequent shifts, Tylenol PRN, Naproxen, and Lidocaine patch BID, which the patient feels has helped. #Hypertension. SBPs intermittently to 170/190s. No known history of hypertension. Blood pressures likely elevated in the setting of his back pain. Pain was treated with the regimen above and his blood pressures stabilized (on day of discharge, SBP 123-146). #Abdominal pain: Unclear if this is chronic, new or a component of his PTSD. CT abdomen was negative and physical exam was reassuring against peritoneal signs, though occasionally with mild diffuse tenderness. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES -------------------- #QTc on discharge was 416 #PSYCHIATRIC MEDICATIONS ADDED: Olanzapine, Propranolol, Haldol #PSYCHIATRIC MEDICATION DOSING CHANGE: Mirtazapine #For severe agitation, consider: Olanzapine 5 mg BID prn, Haldol 5 mg po or IM, or Ativan 2 mg po or IM [ ]Repeat Na in 3 days. Na on day of discharge: 147 [ ]Advise PCP to set up physical therapy after discharge from inpatient psychiatry [ ]Please have patient see his PCP after discharge from inpatient psychiatry [ ]Advise keeping patient upright, and sitting in his chair for most of the day [ ]Advise continued physical therapy during inpatient psychiatry stay #Code Status: Full confirmed #Emergency Contact/HCP: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted for worsening panic attacks, anxiety and abdominal pain. For your abdominal pain, labs and images of your abdomen were reassuring against more concerning processes. For your panic attacks, anxiety, and depression, you have been stabilized on a medication regimen that we believe will help. The inpatient psychiatry team believes that you will benefit from further treatment at ___ psychiatry facility for a period of time. For your back pain, it is important to move around and out of bed as much as possible because immobility is likely aggravating your pain. We have been treating your pain with Tylenol, Aleeve, and Lidocaine patches twice daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with h/o depression, PTSD, and recent suicide attempt 2 weeks ago, presents with tylenol OD. # Tylenol OD: At the time of presentation to ___, the patient was approximately 60 hours post the start of her tylenol ingestion which was estimated to be approximately ___ in total. She was maintained on NAC protocol which had been started at OSH and continued while in transfer. The liver transplant service was made aware of the patient on admission. Liver and renal fucntion were monitored every 6 hours. Pt continued to receive NAC for approximately 72 hours. Her liver enzymes peaked on hospital day 2 and continued to decline up until the time of discharge on day 4. At discharge, her enzymes were ALT 1743, AST 286 , Bili 0.6 and INR 1.4. It was recommended that her liver function be followed-up daily post discharge to document continued improvement and to ensure resolution in the setting of starting home medications. # PTSD/Depression/Anxiety: All psychiatric medications were held on admission and psychiatry was consulted. Recommendations were made for inpatient psychiatric evaluation once medically stable. Throughout her visit, pt was off of clonidine and ativan with significant anxiety but without changes in mental status or hemodynamics. Prior to discharge, pt was restarted on home meds/doses, with the eception of clonidine which was started at 0.1mg and Ativan which was given only BID given liver insult. Pt was discharged into the care of inpatient psychiatry on HD 4. # Hypothyroidism: pt was continued on her regular dose of levothyroxine 125mcg.
summary: You were admitted for evaluation following an overdose of tylenol. You were treated with IV medication to assist in tylenol clearance and prevent liver failure. Your liver function was carfully monitored and showed gradual improvement over several days. Although improved, your liver still demonstrates evidence of damage. Because you will be restarting your home medications, you will need undergo daily monitoring to for the next few days. Due to your underlying depression and anxiety, psychiatry has recommended inpatient evaluation and treatment. You are being discharged into the care of inpatient psychiatry.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Brief Hospital Course: ___ PMH significant for bipolar disorder, substance abuse (EtOH, cocaine, heroin), multiple suicide attempt (multiple overdose episodes, ___ with SI), who was originally admitted to the ICU after an intentional ___ overdose, s/p hemodialysis, then transferred to medicine where she was medically stabilized prior to discharge to inpatient psychiatric facility #Lithium Overdose: Pt ingested 100 pills of lithium in an intentional overdose. Lithium level 4.4 on admission, normal therapeutic level 0.6-1.2. Toxicology and nephrology were consulted. While no ___, patient was dialyzed prophylactically. Lithium levels improved s/p hemodialysis. Per toxicology removed HD line on ___, and levels no longer trended since ceased rising s/p HD. Psychiatry evaluated patient as described below. Given risk for further ingestions, prescriptions patient had on her person were removed and destroyed. Given resolution of such issue, we felt that she was appropriately medically stabilized and therefore appropriate for transfer to psychiatric facility. #Suicide Attempt Patient has a history of suicide attempts, and presented s/p suicide attempt by lithium overdose, in setting of recent heroin overdose. Per psych, history is consistent with preliminary diagnoses of Bipolar Disorder and substance-use disorder. Given high risk for subsequent suicide attempt, patient was sectioned and had 1:1 sitter. While on the medical service she remained agreeable and pleasant, expressing some regret about what happened. Given family and patient's wishes, she was discharged to inpatient psychiatric facility of their choosing to continue further care for re-initiation of pharmacotherapy for her mental illness. #Bipolar Disorder: Complicated by substance abuse and multiple suicide attempts. Given overdose as discussed above, lithium was held. Given family and patient's wishes, she was discharged to inpatient psychiatric facility of their choosing to continue further care for re-initiation of pharmacotherapy for her mental illness. Would not favor restarting lithium given patient's new understanding of its potential for toxicity #Transitional Issues: 1. During workup, patient was found to be non-immune to hepatitis B and needs vaccination 2. Patient needs re-initiation of pharmacologic agents for her bipolar disorder # CONTACT: ___, father, ___ # CODE STATUS: FULL
summary: You were admitted to the intensive care unit for trying to hurt yourself by taking an overdose of your Lithium prescription. We used laxatives to clear remaining Lithium from your bowels, and you had dialysis to filter the Lithium from your blood. We also stopped your home medications temporarily to avoid any harmful interactions and we tested your heart repeatedly with an EKG to see whether the Lithium was affecting your heart. When the levels of toxin in your blood returned to normal, you were evaluated by the psychiatric team, who determined you would benefit from additional inpatient psychiatric care.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Brief Hospital Course: ___ PMH significant for bipolar disorder, substance abuse (EtOH, cocaine, heroin), multiple suicide attempt (multiple overdose episodes, ___ with SI), who was originally admitted to the ICU after an intentional ___ overdose, s/p hemodialysis, then transferred to medicine where she was medically stabilized prior to discharge to inpatient psychiatric facility #Lithium Overdose: Pt ingested 100 pills of lithium in an intentional overdose. Lithium level 4.4 on admission, normal therapeutic level 0.6-1.2. Toxicology and nephrology were consulted. While no ___, patient was dialyzed prophylactically. Lithium levels improved s/p hemodialysis. Per toxicology removed HD line on ___, and levels no longer trended since ceased rising s/p HD. Psychiatry evaluated patient as described below. Given risk for further ingestions, prescriptions patient had on her person were removed and destroyed. Given resolution of such issue, we felt that she was appropriately medically stabilized and therefore appropriate for transfer to psychiatric facility. #Suicide Attempt Patient has a history of suicide attempts, and presented s/p suicide attempt by lithium overdose, in setting of recent heroin overdose. Per psych, history is consistent with preliminary diagnoses of Bipolar Disorder and substance-use disorder. Given high risk for subsequent suicide attempt, patient was sectioned and had 1:1 sitter. While on the medical service she remained agreeable and pleasant, expressing some regret about what happened. Given family and patient's wishes, she was discharged to inpatient psychiatric facility of their choosing to continue further care for re-initiation of pharmacotherapy for her mental illness. #Bipolar Disorder: Complicated by substance abuse and multiple suicide attempts. Given overdose as discussed above, lithium was held. Given family and patient's wishes, she was discharged to inpatient psychiatric facility of their choosing to continue further care for re-initiation of pharmacotherapy for her mental illness. Would not favor restarting lithium given patient's new understanding of its potential for toxicity #Transitional Issues: 1. During workup, patient was found to be non-immune to hepatitis B and needs vaccination 2. Patient needs re-initiation of pharmacologic agents for her bipolar disorder # CONTACT: ___, father, ___ # CODE STATUS: FULL

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ fibromyalgia, PTSD, depression, opiod dependence on suboxone, and benzodiazepine abuse with recent benzodiazepine detoxification who presents with pseudo seizure. ACUTE ISSUES: ----------------- # PRESYNCOPE: Evidenced by further characterization with Dr. ___ ___ ___, ___) who stated that the episode was witnessed by a staff member and that patient did not experience loss of consciousness and that she "slid down in her chair". Alternate etiologies for her seizure-like episode include 1) cardiac dysrhythmias, which remain less likely in the absence of events over >48hours of electrophysiologic telemetry monitoring; 2) structural heart disease, which remains less likely in the absence of physical exam findings findings or ECG findings which might suggest the same; 3) ischemic heart disease, which remains less likely given stress echo without evidence of ischemic changes; 4) intrinsic seizure disorder, which is less likely given EEG which is not suggestive of primary seizure disorder; 5) benzodiazepine withdrawl seizure, which is less likely given the absence of tachycardia, agitation, or other symptoms of benzodiazepine withdrawl while hospitalized at ___ or ___. The patient did not score on the CIWA scale sufficiently to receive any benzodiazepines. The patient was prescribed NO STANDING BENZODIAZEPINES after ___ AM. She remained stable for 96 hours without ANY benzodiazepines. #DISPOSITION: The patient's sons refused to house the patient upon discharge. Social work met extensively with the patient, providing her with resources to ensure her safe transition to a homeless shelter upon completion of her medical workup. Dr. ___ at ___ confirmed that the patient was discharged from ___ upon transfer to ___. The patient continued to evidence competence at the time of her discharge. # SUICIDAL GESTURE: RESOLVED. Patient denied suicidal thoughts throughout her hospital stay at ___. # CHEST PAIN: Resolved. Due to atypical rest pain. Ischemic or cardiac etiologies remain extremely unlikely given the absence of CAD, no concerns on EKG and trop neg x 2. # HTN: controlled. cont atenolol. # OPIATE DEPENDENCE: STABLE. Confirmed suboxone dosing and schedule as SUBOXONE 8mg-2mg BID with office of Dr. ___ ___ at ___, ___ CHRONIC ISSUES: # FIBROMYALGIA: - Continue gabapentin # GERD: cont pantoparzole # MDD: Continued duloxetine. ----------- TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # Cervical LAD: Noted incidentally on CT. Please followup with your PCP to ensure resolution. # VERTIGO: Followup with ___ Neurology Urgent Care # SYNCOPE FOLLOWUP: Please followup with ___ Neurology Urgent Care for MRI and repeat EEG given the brief runs of left anterior to midtemporal sharp slowing superimposed upon a slower than average backgrounds noted on ___ EEG. #LEFT ARM PAIN: Please followup at ___ Neurology Urgent Care for an EMG to evaluate for median neuropathy and left wrist splint.
summary: You were admitted due to chest pain after you slouched in a chair at ___ ___. You underwent an extensive heart workup which did not show a reason we could find in the pumping or rhythm of your heart. We examined your brain for any abmormal electrical activity of your brain which would explain your symptoms, with some finding suggestive of benzodiazepine use.Your benzodiazepines have been discontinued and you were monitored while you were in the hospital for possible withdrawal symptoms. Your arm pain and dizziness is probably due to compression of some nerves in your cerivcal spine, and the neurologists ask that you followup in urgent care neurology clinic for more imaging that would help confirm this diagnosis. Please avoid benzodiazepines and opiate medications in the future. We have provided you with resources to assist with your ongoing abstinence from these medications. The phone number for the BEST team is below. Please get referal for counselling and cognitive behavioral therapy from your psychiatrist or primary care physician to help better manage your anxiety.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with past medical history of depression and mulitple admissions for alcohol intoxication adn pancreatitis presented after jumping in front of a bus while intoxicated with possible alcoholic pancreatitis, cirrhosis on MRI, discharged back to shelter in stable condition with outpatient follow up and alcohol detox phone numbers provided. #Depression- the patient denied that she was trying to kill herself when she jumped infront of the bus. She was evaluated by psychiatry who felt that she was not suicidal and would benefit from her outpatient psychiatry that she is already arranged into at ___ ___ however she did not want to go back there and agreed to go back to shelter with outpatient psychiatric follow up. The patient reported that her mood was worsened in the setting of being off of her cymbalta and a verbal altercation with her boyfriend over her alcohol abuse. She was stable prior discharge with no suicidal ideations. Per psychiatry recommendations, she was provided with cymbalta for another 7 days upon discharge. #Alcohol Dependence/Withdrawal- patient drinks ___ pints of vodka per day and has no history of withdrawal DTs or seizures or previous ICU admissions. this is her 3 visit to ___ for similar reasons in the past month. She was monitored on a CIWA scale and treated with diazepam for scores in the low ___, due to tremulousness. She had no signs of DTs during her stay. She was seen by social work who provided her with alcohol detox contact numbers. #Alcoholic pancreatitis- the patient has had multiple bouts of pancreaitis and the most likely etiology is alcohol given her heavy use. She was counseled to stop drinking alcohol. She also has a history of pancreatic cysts which could have been contributing to her pain. She was managed with IVF, bowel rest and pain medications. She did not follow-up previously with hepatoloy and should do so. She is provided with her PCP's contact phone number to schedule a follow up appointment and through the PCP to get scheduled for hepatology follow up at ___. # ETOH Cirrhosis: AST slightly elevated at 45. MRI in ___ suggestive of Hepatomegaly with steatosis, cirrhotic morphology (no concerning liver lesions) along with splenomegaly and mild paraesophageal varices. Patient had outpatient hepatology f/u which she did not attend. HCV Ab negative in ___. She is provided with the contact number as above to schedule follow up. We also strongly recommended her to have an EGD done to evaluate for her epigastric pain (gastritis, PUD) and also to further evaluate her varices seen on MRI in the setting of cirrhosis. Patient stated she will try. #Diabetes: The patient has a history of poorly controlled diabets. She is on a large dose of Lantus qday at home. While inpatient and NPO this was decreased. She has been seen by ___ in the past due to her high A1c. It is unclear what the barriers are to her blood glucose control, as she is homeless and if staying at a shelter. She reports having the glucometer at home. #Tooth pain- the patient recently had a molar extracted on the bottom left of her mouth. She reported continued pain in this area. On exam it did not appear to have any pus coming from the site and her oropharynx was clear with no lymphadenopathy. Transitional issues: - EGD to evaluate for gastritis/duodenitis/PUD and also to further evaluate varcies - Cirrhosis management
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for concern that you were suicidal after you steped infront of a bus and you had abdominal pain and labs consistent with a flare of pancreatitis. You were evaluated by psychiatry who felt that you were not a danger to yourself or others currently, and recommended that you follow-up with your outpatient psychiatrist. For your pancreatitis, this is likely due to your alcohol consumption recently. To treat this we gave you fluids through an IV and held you food intake while the pain improved and you were tolerating food at the time of your discharge. To prevent you from going into withdrawal we managed your alcohol withdrwal symptoms with medications and you had no episdoes of seizures or DTs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old s/p OLT ___ who presented with insomnia and resultant fatigue and confusion. #Encephalopathy/insomnia: Her acute confusion symptoms were a result of taking too many sedative medications: gabapentin, promethazine, and xanax. She was found to be medically stable, and psychiatry was consulted to further evaluate her insomnia. Psychiatry and the primary team determined her insomnia is likely a result of severe untreated depression and anxiety. She had no suicidal ideation or plans, and no signs of psychosis. She was given zolpidem 5 mg for sleep, and would take Ativan 0.5 mg ___ hours later, and would receive about ___ hours of sleep at a time. # Hepatitis C cirrhosis s/p OLT ___: Pt with history of OLT in ___ in ___. She is s/p treatment of her hepatitis C with SVR. Of note, she has evidence of advanced fibrosis of the graft on recent ultrasound and Fibroscan. Continued home tacrolimus and daily tacro level. # Hypothyroidism: Continue home levothyroxine TRANSITIONAL ISSUES #Discharged with zolpidem 5 mg and Ativan 0.5 mg for sleep to bridge until PCP ___ ___ she is instructed to take one Zolpidem at night, and if this is inadequate she is able to take one Ativan #Stopped gabapentin, promethazine and Xanax #Medication management and reconciliation #Psychiatric f/u for depression and anxiety
summary: You were admitted with confusion and altered mental status, likely as a result of taking too many medications to help you sleep. Your evaluation showed that you are medically stable. From your description of your symptoms, you are suffering from depression and anxiety, and this is what is causing your insomnia. You were seen by the psychiatric team while in the hospital, and they agreed with this assessment. It is important that after you leave here you see your primary care physician and you see a psychiatrist. The only way your sleeping will improve is if you are treated for depression and anxiety as well, and this requires close psychiatric care. We are giving you two new medications for sleep, zolpidem and lorazepam.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is an ___ year old woman with occasional urinary incontinence, gait difficulties, and cognitive decline who presented for NPH evaluation. Patient's chronic urinary incontinence and gait difficulties are likely multifactorial in etiology; however, acute on chronic decline is likely secondary to cervical spondylotic myelopathy. Additionally, patient likely has some underlying cognitive dementia, evidenced on MOCA (score= ___, as well ongoing depression which complicates her efforts. ___ lumbar puncture was performed, but patient showed no improvement in symptoms following the procedure; as such, there is no further clinical suspicion for NPH. - Patient should wear soft neck collar at night. - Patient should follow-up with her Neurologist ___ - Patient should participate in physical therapy for lumbar spine. - Patient should NOT undergo physical therapy of the neck as this may worsen her gait. # NPH evaluation: Patient was transferred from ___ ___ for a workup of Normal Pressure hydrocephalus. MOCA and a timed walking exercise were performed before and after a large volume lumbar puncture. Patient did not show any significant improvement in symptoms following LP (___ scores: Pre-LP:20; Post-LP: 22). Walk test from room 1117 to window 43 sec, to window and back 1:50 min prior to lumbar puncture. Walk post-LP 1:30 to window, 3:20 to window and back. Patient did not improve in walk time or gait post-LP. Additionally, infectious workup for lyme (serum antibody) and RPR were both negative. Patient's chronic urinary incontinence and gait difficulties are likely multifactorial in etiology; however, acute on chronic decline is likely secondary to cervical spondylotic myelopathy, demonstrated on cervical spine MR. ___, patient likely has some underlying cognitive dementia, evidenced on MOCA (score= ___, as well ongoing depression which complicates her efforts. Patient was instructed to wear a soft-collar at night to help alleviate her symptoms. Inactive issues # A-fib: Patient to continue on home dose ASA; Cardizem. # Depression: Celexa 20 mg - according to pharmacy this is her home dose - will therefore leave at this level for now and let PCP ___, discontinue ___ # Macular degeneration, glaucoma: Continue timolol, latanoprost, brimonidine, Ocuvite # GERD: Cont omeprazole
summary: You were admitted because you were having difficulty with urinary incontinence and with your walking. Because there had been some convern that you might have a condition called Normal Pressure Hydrocephalus (NPH), in which you have increased volume of cerebral spinal fluid, we performed a large volume lumbar puncture and removed cerebral spinal fluid. You did not show significant improvement in your symptoms, which shows that you do not have NPH. We performed an MRI of your cervical spine, which showed cervical stenosis with contact and molding of your spinal cord. In other words, your spinal cord (at the level of your neck) is being compressed and irritated, which has affected your walking and your urinary continence. Going forward, we recommend that you sleep in a soft neck collar. You only need to use this at night. This will straighten your neck spine and lessen the degree of compression and irritation. Additionally, our physical therapists have recommended that you participate in physical therapy for your lumbar spine. Please do NOT participate in any physical therapy for your neck; this will only serve to irritate it further. Please see below for further instructions.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and Plan: This is a ___ yo F with h/o diverticulitis, recurrent UTIs, depression p/w urinary retention leading to abdominal pain. # Urinary Retention: CT ruled out acute surgical process. GI consulted and feel this may be secondary to an over-distended bladder. Also, h/o some relief with urination, and feeling as if her bladder was not completely empty post void. Patient had foley catheter in place overnight with some relief. Removed the following day and patient able to void on own with minimal post void residuals. Patient had significant suprapubic pain through hospital stay controlled with standing tylenol and oxycodone as needed. The pain decreased on day of discharge as she did not need oxycodone. Her blood cultures were negative growth to date on day of discharge. UA and Urine culture were negative. No fever or wbc to suggest infection. Etiology unclear at this point. Patient admits to starting Adderall 2 weeks prior and she is taking Nortriptylline 50 mg qhs. Both of these medications can induce urinary retention. Her colonoscopy may have exacerbated her urinary retention, as intra/post-op analgesia can cause urinary retention. Nevertheless, the patient's urinary symptoms improved through the course of admission. She will follow up with her pcp and her psychiatrist ___. She is also scheduled to see Urology in 2 weeks if symptoms don't improve. # Depression: Patient's symptoms remained stable through course of admission. She was continued on her home regimen of fluoxetine, nortryptilline, quetiapine, levothyroxine, and mirtazapine. Patient was not continued on Addarall. She is to follow up with her psychiatrist this week for further management of these medications. A discussion of stopping or decreasing Nortriptylline and Addarall during this follow up is advised as this may be partially to blame for patient's acute urinary retention. # Hypertension: Stable throughout admission. Her home medications of amlodipine, lisinopril, and propranolol were continued.
summary: You were admitted for lower abdominal pain. You had a CT scan of your abdomen which did not show any gastrointestinal or surgical process. You were found to have a distended bladder and a foley catheter was placed. You pain improved some. The following day, your catheter was removed and you were able to urinate well, with minimal residual urine left in your bladder. Your pain was controlled with tylenol and oxycodone on an as needed basis. On day of discharge, Ibuprofen and Acetaminophen were used for pain control with good effect and you did not need oxycodone. We talked to Urology and they stated that bladder distension pain can take some time to relieve itself after your bladder is drained. It was felt that your bladder distension was a culmination of your medications, mainly Adderall and Nortriptylline. Other inciting factors may be the fact that you were mildly constipated and your colonoscopy procedure anesthesia may have exacerbated the distension.

text: Brief Hospital Course: . #Chest pain - Patient was admitted with atypical pleuritic chest pain that felt like "someone beating on his chest". Patient was ruled out for MI by cardiac enzymes. He had a negative ECG x 2. In the emergency department, the patient underwent exercise stress test, but was recommended for a persantine stress test as he was unable to exercise beyond 3 minutes. Persantine stress test showed no perfusion deficits or EKG changes. The patient was maintained on home metoprolol, ranexa, losartan, ASA, and imdur. Although symptoms were inconsistent with gastrointestinal etiology of pain, patient was placed on ranitidine and sucralfate due to history of gastric ulcer. Home omeprazole was discontinued due to interaction with plavix. Despite pleuritic nature of chest pain, no evidence on exam or history to support pneumonia, PE. Pain possibly musculoskeletal, as patient tender to palpation along rib cage. Per discussion with patient's cardiologist, patient has presented to ___ ED with similar complaints on several occasions and has undergone multiple negative stress tests in the last 6mths (reports were unable to be obtained) Pt was encouraged to follow up with his primary cardiologist after discharge from psychiatry. . #DM II - Blood sugars controlled following gastric bypass surgery. Patient was placed on finger checks with insulin sliding scale during admission. . #Bipolar disorder - On admission, patient denied depression, SI/HI. He was continued on home trazodone, citalopram, depakote, and seroquel. On day two of admission, the patient started endorsing suicidal ideation. He was seen by psychiatry who recommended inpatient psychiatric admission. He was placed with a 1:1 sitter for the remainder of his hospitalization and was transferred directly to inpatient psychiatry on ___. . #hypercholesterolemia - Patient was continued on niaspan ER 500 mg PO qHS . #hypertension - BP remained stable throughout admission. He was continued on home metoprolol and losartan.
summary: You were admitted for left sided chest pain and nausea. We performed several blood tests and electrocardiograms to make sure you were not having a heart attack. You were given an exercise stress test. Because you could not tolerate the exercise test, you underwent a chemical stress test. The chemical stress test showed no problems with blood flow to your heart. Because of your history of a gastric ulcer, we also started you on a medication to coat your stomach in case stomach irritation contributed to your symptoms. In the course of your admission, you began to feel suicidal. You were seen by psychiatry who recommended inpatient psychiatric admission for your safety. We took precautions to make sure you would remain safe on the medical floor. You were discharged to psychiatry with the assurance that you are medically stable.

Patient was placed on finger checks with insulin sliding scale during admission. . #Bipolar disorder - On admission, patient denied depression, SI/HI. He was continued on home trazodone, citalopram, depakote, and seroquel. On day two of admission, the patient started endorsing suicidal ideation. He was seen by psychiatry who recommended inpatient psychiatric admission. He was placed with a 1:1 sitter for the remainder of his hospitalization and was transferred directly to inpatient psychiatry on ___. . #hypercholesterolemia - Patient was continued on niaspan ER 500 mg PO qHS . #hypertension - BP remained stable throughout admission. He was continued on home metoprolol and losartan.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo F with GERD, DM, depression and s/p treatment for H. pylori who presents with 2 days of intense, non-radiating epigastric pain and 1 day of increased N/V from baseline N/V on H. pylori regimen in the setting of 1 month of progressively increasing epigastric pain. . #Abdominal Pain: GERD and esophagitis v. Gastroparesis v. Candidiasis v. Gastroenteritis. KUB did not demonstrate a pattern consistent with obstruction so erythromycin and reglan given due to concern for gastroparesis (given DM and narcotics for chronic pain). EGD demonstrated esophagitis and clinical picture c/w GERD (burning epigastric pain, worse with food or lying flat and associated with acid reflux into mouth). Nausea was treated with anti-emetics and pain regimen was altered to reduce use of narcotics, as they slow GI motility. Diet was advanced as tolerated to a regular, diabetic diet. - Increased PPI to 40mg BID, added sucralfate, reglan (as needed) - f/u with ___ gastroenterology - Consider urease breath test to confirm that she has cleared H. pylori, given sx - Gastric motility study as an outpt if N/V returns . #Diabetes: Complicated by neuropathy and retinopathy. Given unclear what regimen she is using at home, based on the length of time since filled prescriptions and pt description of recent depression with days without eating and having "extra" insulin at home, she was placed on SSI and was slowly transitioned to QHS Lantus. HbA1c of 8.3. - d/c off oral anti-diabetic agents and on 12L QHS - re-evaluate ___ in outpt setting and consider regimen for long term use . #Depression, Suicidality: Pt had not recently filled her seroquel prescription, so was on perphenazine while admitted. Psych was consulted and they were in touch with pt's outpt therapist. Initially recommended inpt psych placement for stabilization, but with improvement of pain, pt was no longer +SI. In addition, history of provocative statements regarding self harm were relayed by therapist. Was on 1:1 sitter throughout hospitalization, but was determined by psychiatry on the day of discharge that she was no longer +SI and was safe for discharge. - Please set up ___ psychiatrist for pt to help manage her medications - Encourage close f/u with outpt therapist . #Chronic pain in extremities over the last 6+ months: given intractable chronic pain over the last few months, obtained pain consult with new pain regimen, found in discharge meds. Has improved her chronic pain and has allowed her GI motility to improve. - Tizanidine, Tylenol, Gabapentin with oxycodone for breakthrough pain .
summary: You were admitted ___ with nausea, vomiting, diarrhea and abdominal pain. Given your pain and vomiting, having just finished a course of antibiotics to treat a bacteria in your stomach (H. pylori), you received an upper GI endoscopy which was negative except for signs of esophagitis and an abdominal x-ray which showed no obstruction or other acute process. We think this was due to multiple issues including gastric reflux of stomach acid leading to esophagitis (irritation of your esophagus by the acid from your stomach) and likely gastroparesis (slow transit of food through your GI system, likely due to your diabetes and the narcotics in your chronic pain regimen). Given your chronic pain we also got a pain service consult which made some alterations to your pain control regimen to control your pain without needing to take as many narcotics (which slow down your bowel function).

text: Brief Hospital Course: IMPRESSION: ___ y/o male w/ HCV cirrhosis, ESLD, w/ hx of varices and encephalopathy who presents from OSH, w/ tylenol ingestion, and likely psychosis. Continued homicidal ideation toward wife and son and suicidal ideation. EEG normal ___. . Patient would likely be discharged to rehab if not for his endorsement of voices telling him to kill his wife with a knife. Patient endorses these voices at different times of the day at other times he denies them. . # Homicidal ideation w/ ? Psychosis vs. Delirium: Pt appears to be responding to internal stimuli. MMSE very high, Still endorses voice that tell him to kill his wife and son w/ a knife. He denies that he would actually do this. He has no active plans of Suicide or murder. These symptoms come and go during the day. Psychiatry feels that the waxing and waning does not fit entirely with a primary psychiatric disorder. However patient is atypical for hepatic encephalopathy. Cont to monitor for improvement with long term plans of trial of inpatient psych evaluation. Patient needs to have all psychiatric comorbitidies controlled for him to be considered for liver transplant. . Also of note, wife and son were notified of these homicidal ideations on ___. Patient was continued on the following psych medications, Citalopram 10mg daily, Risperidone 3mg po bid, 0.5mg risperidone PRN, Haldol ___ IV q4PRN. Pt was calm for the 48 hours prior to transfer to deac 4. . # Encephalopathy: Patient does not appear encephalopathic, ammonia 22, no asterixis, MMSE very high today, perfect on serial sevens, and memory. Patient not currently encephalopathic, but will become so if he does not continue his current dose of rifaximin/lactulose . #Transient Hypotension: Pt was noted to have, transient sbp of 86 after receiving spironolactone and nadolol with in 30min of one another. On recheck 15min later patients blood pressure was 96/50 baseline. We suggest that patient receive 40 of lasix in the morning, spironolactone at noon and nadolol at night. . # Cirrhosis: INR is stable at patient's baseline per records, continue to trend. Patient with ascites, and unclear if compliant with diuretics. Continue aldactone and Lasix. Continued on nadolol dose reduced from 40mg to 20mg for history of esophageal varices. INR at baseline. Platelet count 22. Felt that patient did not have SBP clinically. MELD score near baseline at MELD 19. Patient follows with Dr. ___ the liver service at ___. . #Tylenol ingestion: history consistent with daily tylenol ingestion on a cirrhotic liver, though exact dose/intent/timing is unclear. Patient received NAC infusion with 5 additional hours for 16 hours at 17.5 mg//kg/hr dosing as well as NAC infusion at ___ prior to transfer . # Thrombocytopenia: Nadir of 15 this hospital stay continue to monitor, at transfer platelets were 20. Platelet transfusions are likely only accumulate in spleen. . #Anemia: HCT ___ pt is near baseline. Probably related to anemia of chronic disease given liver disease. . #Leukopenia: Stable, likely related to liver disease. . #. Chronic back pain: continue oxycontin. Reduced dose from 40mg BID to 20mg BID, during hospital stay. . #. GERD: continue protonix . #. FEN: low salt diet. Hyponatremia of 130s at baseline. . #. PPx: Avoided heparin sq, as patient all ready coagulopathic. . #. Access: PIV . #. Code: Full . # Dispo: Depending on psych issues. ___ 1.
summary: you were transferred to ___ from ___ out of concern for your mental status changes and your high tylenol levels. While you were at ___ at while you were at ___ you received mucomyst which helped protect your liver from tylenol toxicity. There was also intial concern that you might have an infection in your abdomen, but this was felt not to be the case by the team. You were still very confused when you were in the ICU. You needed medications to help calm you down. You were given medications to help control any confusion caused from your liver disease. You had a test called an RPR which rule out any syphyllis causing your confusion. You had an EEG which did not show any seizure activity. You did not have any infection in your urine. You continued to hear voices and be confused during your hospitalizations. You repeatedly stated that you heard a voice telling you to kill your wife, your son and yourself. As a result we are transitioning you to a psychiatry facitilty to determine if you have a primary psychiatric problem on top of your other liver issues, and to further assess if you are a danger to yourself or others.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ y/o man with history of ulcerative colitis, depression and distant polysubstance abuse who presented with worsening of his abdominal pain. # Abdominal pain- The patient presented with abdominal pain and associated nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. He was seen by gastroenterology who felt that his pain may have been due to mesalamine. This medication was stopped with resolution of diarrhea and nausea. The patient was able to tolerate a regular diet prior to discharge. CRP was checked and was 0.5 making UC flare very unlikely. Stool studies were also ordered to rule out infection but these were not sent as the patient's diarrhea resolved. Prednisone was continued at 40mg daily. The patient had persistent abdominal pain thought to be an exacerbation of his chronic pain. Please see below. #Depression- The patient expressed symptoms of depression (anhedoina, poor sleep, pain, feeling hopeless) and was seen by a psychiatrist while here. He recommended starting Buproprion 150mg in AM. This was started while hospitalized. He would benefit from outpatient CBT and should follow up with his therapist ___ as depression may be driving some of his recent symptoms. #Chronic pain/ Narcotic use On chronic Dilaudid 4mg PO Q4hrs. The patient was evaluated by the chronic pain service who recommended adding tizanadine 2mg at HS. This medication was added. They also agreed with CBT and recommended tapering of narcotics when possible. No changes were made to the patients narcotics while hospitalized and he was not given a script on discharge. #Leg pain The patient had complaints of bilateral leg pain that was preventing ambulation. CK was checked and was low. Pain improved spontaneously. #Back pain The patient complained of some bilateral lower back pain on the day of discharge, which he felt was related to "kidney pain." UA was checked and WNL. #Anemia Chronic and stable- likely due to chronic disease. Has had recent episode of bleeding. Should be followed as an outpatient. #Leukocytosis Due to chronic steroids. No signs or symptoms of infection. # Hepatits C- viral load >100,000. Not actively treated at this time. # Rash on chest wall- no e/o pyoderma gangrenosum or erythema nodosum (which can be associated with UC), nor does it have the appearance of a vasculitis. Asymptomatic. # GERD- PPI
summary: You were admitted with abdominal pain and diarrhea. It was thought that your diarrhea may have been a paradoxical reaction to mesalamine so this medication was stopped with improvement in your diarrhea. You were also seen by our pain physicians who recommended starting a medication called Tizanadine. This medicaiton should be taken at night because it can make you sleepy. You were also seen by psychiatry who recommended starting the antidepressant Buproprion 150mg daily. Both the pain physicians and our psychiatrists recommended that you follow up with a psychologist for possible cognitive behavioral therapy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M s/p sibling allo SCT for CLL complicated by chronic graft-versus-host disease, severe depression, passive SI who was admitted with bilateral knee pain and L knee swelling and redness. . # Left knee bursitis: admitted with left knee erythema and swelling and initially started on vancomycin and ceftriaxone for coverage of joint infection. Left lateral knee spontaneously drained and culture grew MRSA. He was continued on 2-week course of IV vanco after drainage and did not require surgical drainage given clinical resolution. Knee pain improved slightly though the bilateral pain was likely due to AVN, as below. He will be discharged to rehab and will followup with Dr. ___ need outpatient surveillance cultures. . # Bilateral knee AVN: found to have severe avascular necrosis of tib/fib, femur, and patellae bilaterally. Likely in setting of prednisone use for chronic GVHD. He was evaluated by orthopedics who felt that surgical intervention was not needed at this time as both articular surfaces were intact. He will follow up with orthopedics clinic in 2 weeks to discuss possible pin placement into joints as a prophylactic measure. He was continued on prednisone 10mg BID. His home pain control regimen was increased to MS ___ 45mg BID with breakthrough oxycodone. He may consider initiating a fentanyl patch for pain control as an outpatient if his pain persists. . # Left leg DVT: patient has history of upper extremity DVTs and PEs, recently completed 6-month course of coumadin in ___. He was declining SC heparin prophylaxis during the hospitalization despite numerous conversations regarding high risk for DVT. He developed LLE edema and found to have a DVT extending from his common femoral vein to the ankle. He was started on heparin gtt bridge to coumadin. His INR became supratherapeutic to >8 on 5mg coumadin and his coumadin was held. He was not reversed given no clinical bleeding and large clot burden. His INR trended down and he was restarted on coumadin upon discharge. Near discharge he was short of breath; we performed a V/Q scan given DVT which came back at very low probability. He will likely need lonterm therapy given repeated episodes of his DVT. Will have his INR monitored at rehab for goal ___. . # CLL with chronic GVHD: patient is s/p allo SCT in ___ with chronic GVHD, on immunosuppressive therapy with cyclosporine, MMF, and prednisone. As above, he developed b/l knee AVN likely in setting of prednisone use but given his significant GVHD he was continued on prednisone 10mg BID and home doses of other medications. He had a port placed on this admission given history of difficult access and upper extremity DVTs/PEs in setting of PICC lines previously. He was continued on prophylaxis with posaconazole, atovaquone, and valganciclovir (CMV viral load negative). His counts were otherwise stable. In terms of his capillary leak and chronic lower extremity edema, he was continued on lasix 40mg PO daily. He will follow up with Dr. ___ in 1 week. . # Transaminitis: has slightly elevated LFTs at baseline though no known liver GVH. He had transaminitis which was stable throughout the hospitalization, CMV viral load negative. ___ be in setting of medication effects or less likely GVH. This should be trended as an outpatient. . # Acute renal failure: Prerenal in nature; we held lisinopril and lasix until this resolved and restarted at half dose at time of discharge.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with knee pain and found to have an infection in a part of your knee called the bursa. You received a course of IV antibiotics for the infection and improved. An MRI of your knees showed a condition called avascular necrosis which is a decrease in blood flow to certain parts of your bone with resulting damage there. This can be caused by steroid use which you are taking for your graft versus host disease. While you were in the hospital, you also developed a clot in your left leg and were restarted on your blood thinner coumadin. Your levels were too high for a few days and coumadin was held, but you should continue to take this after discharge. You are going to a rehab facility to regain your strength.

text: Brief Hospital Course: HOSPITAL COURSE: Pt is a ___ YO with history of ___ transferred from OSH for management of TCA toxicity. She was intubated for airway protection, and toxicology was consulted. Serial ECGs were done with ABG's and frequent lytes. She was treated with sodium bicarb and aggressive lyte repletion. Her QRS was prolonged, and decreased with sodium bicarb to 106. . # TCA overdose: Patient was intubated and sedatied on admission with a QRS >100 on admission to the MICU. No seizure activity was witnessed. She was intubated for airway protection, and toxicology was consulted. Serial ECGs were done with ABG's and frequent lytes. She was treated with sodium bicarb and aggressive lyte repletion. Her QRS was prolonged, and decreased with sodium bicarb to 106. Patient was called out to the medicine floor for further management prior to transfer to psychiatry. On the floor she was monitored w serial EKGs and BID lytes. She was stable on the floor and did not require aggressive repletion of lytes. Her QRS was <100ms for >36hours prior to discharge. . # Suicide Attempt: Patient has a history of depression and previous psychiatric admission. Psychiatry consulted and recommended restarting home dose of lamictal and trilafon. Following medical clearance patient will be admitted to psychiatry. . # Normocytic Anemia: Hematocrit on admission was 32, prior baseline 37. All cell lines had decreased and anemia was thought to be likely dilutional. Probable also iron deficiency anemia given young female, however, not microcytic. Pt also with large phlebotomizing during this admission. Fe studies suggestive of Fe deficiency anemia. Currently 29.8 and stable. PO iron supplementation was started in the ICU and dc'd on the floor for constipation. . # Tooth pain: s/p chip in L molar after eating apple. Pt has not had recent dental care as she lost her insurance. She complains of mild/moderate discomfort but tolerated po intake and solid food. Tylenol was continued for pain control and benzocaine spray or anbesol could be applied if needed. Pt would benefit from a dental evaluation during her psych stay if possible as she does not have dental insurance. . # Tachycardia: noted on telemetry this AM. In conjunction w pt c/o thirst and "dryness", would attribute to dehydration. Low suspicion for PE given lack of hypoxia, pleurisy and normal EKGs. Could also be related to ICU deconditioning however she was intubated for only 1 day and is healthy otherwise. Pt reports concentrated urine which supports theory of dehydration. EKGs have been sinus rhythm. Recent TSH wnl.She was given 2L NS boluses and observed to have appropriate oral intake of fluids. Tachycardia resolved. Orthostatics were check and wnl - she was ambulated and not symptomatic. . # substance abuse: hx of EtOH abuse (stopped ___ and opioid dependence in past. Would avoid narcotics and benzos if possible. . # Depression: Outpt psych Dr. ___ at ___. Recently restarted lamictal per psych recs which pt states is helpful. Has had multiple suicidal attempts in past most recently 1mo ago, discharged from ___ 3 days prior to admission. She was continued on home lamictal and trilafon doses w plans to defer initiation of antidepressants until psych facility placement. She was monitored w 1:1 sitter and ___ in chart. . # Transaminitis: AST/ALT were mildly elevated on admission. The etiology was unclear thought possibly med effect vs. viral vs. decreased perfusion. Unclear if BP transiently low in setting of OD. Patient's LFTs resolved to normal in ICU.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a medication overdose. You required ICU level care and needed life support until the medication was weaned. You were transferred to the general medicine wards for observation and additional care prior to transfer to psychiatry. Psychiatry was consulted during your stay. It was felt that you required inpatient psychiatric treatment for your mental health issues. Per psychiatry recommendations, the inpatient psych facility will manage your medications and initiate antidepressant therapy upon transfer. Regarding your chipped tooth (L upper molar) you should be evaluated by a dentist upon discharge from the psychiatric facility or in house if possible. Since you are able to tolerate food and drink, your tooth does not require urgent evaluation.
