PO iron supplementation was started in the ICU and dc'd on the floor for constipation. . # Tooth pain: s/p chip in L molar after eating apple. Pt has not had recent dental care as she lost her insurance. She complains of mild/moderate discomfort but tolerated po intake and solid food. Tylenol was continued for pain control and benzocaine spray or anbesol could be applied if needed. Pt would benefit from a dental evaluation during her psych stay if possible as she does not have dental insurance. . # Tachycardia: noted on telemetry this AM. In conjunction w pt c/o thirst and "dryness", would attribute to dehydration. Low suspicion for PE given lack of hypoxia, pleurisy and normal EKGs. Could also be related to ICU deconditioning however she was intubated for only 1 day and is healthy otherwise. Pt reports concentrated urine which supports theory of dehydration. EKGs have been sinus rhythm. Recent TSH wnl.She was given 2L NS boluses and observed to have appropriate oral intake of fluids. Tachycardia resolved. Orthostatics were check and wnl - she was ambulated and not symptomatic. . # substance abuse: hx of EtOH abuse (stopped ___ and opioid dependence in past. Would avoid narcotics and benzos if possible. . # Depression: Outpt psych Dr. ___ at ___. Recently restarted lamictal per psych recs which pt states is helpful. Has had multiple suicidal attempts in past most recently 1mo ago, discharged from ___ 3 days prior to admission. She was continued on home lamictal and trilafon doses w plans to defer initiation of antidepressants until psych facility placement. She was monitored w 1:1 sitter and ___ in chart. . # Transaminitis: AST/ALT were mildly elevated on admission. The etiology was unclear thought possibly med effect vs. viral vs. decreased perfusion. Unclear if BP transiently low in setting of OD. Patient's LFTs resolved to normal in ICU.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old deaf man with a history of depression/SI and alcohol-associated seizures, who presents as a transfer from ___ where he presented with suicidal ideation and seizures. He has a prior history of ETOH withdrawal seizures from ___. He was recently admitted to ___ ___ for similar reasons (suicidal ideation and seizures), with ethanol level of 180 on admission. Of note on this admission there were several events witnessed by clinical staff that were suspicious for non-epileptic seizures. On that admission he underwent EEG which was negative. MRI brain was also negative. Despite these negative studies he was discharged on Keppra. For this admission he presented to ___ ___ after an argument with his brother at home which was complicated by suicidal actions/ideation. # ETOH Withdrawal: # Seizure Activity: The patient has an extensive history of alcohol use disorder with a prior history of EtOH withdrawal complicated by withdrawal seizures. He presented to the ___ acutely intoxicated with an EtOH level of 240. In the ___ at ___ he was witness to have seizure activity. He was loaded with IV phenobarbital and transferred to ___ for further neurology consultant care. He was then transferred to ___ for further evaluation. In the ___ upon arrival to ___ he was observed to have an event suspicious for pseudo-seizure. The patient was given Lorazepam 2mg, Keppra 750mg, ketorolac 15mg, nicotine patch, and morphine sulfate. Neurology was consulted and felt that his observed seizure activity in the ___ was most consistent with non-epileptiform seizures, but noted that he is at risk for ETOH seizure and should be continued on Keppra. The patient was monitored on the CIWA protocol and did not score or require any benzodiazepines per the score. The patient then had another episode of witnessed seizure-like activity overnight from ___ and then again in the afternoon on ___. During the second episode he was able to follow commands, and did not have a post-ictal state. After speaking with neurology the patient was monitored on an EEG for 24hours per neurology which showed no epileptiform activity. Per neurology recommendations, the patient was continued on ___ 750mg BID. He was continued on thiamine PO 100mg daily and folic acid 1mg PO QD daily. # Suicidal Ideation: The patient presented with active suicidal ideation with a gesture of violence towards self. Psychiatry saw the patient in the ___ and per their assessment they deem that the patient had capacity and did not meet ___ criteria. Upon admission, the patient's amitriptyline was held given unclear fill history and an allergy listed in the PAML. Psychiatry did not express any additional concerns this admission, and he did not demonstrate any additional suicidal ideation. # Muscle Strain: The patient had complaints of left shoulder pain after having seizure like activity in the ___, with plain film radiographs negative for any fracture or dislocation. Pain was controlled while inpatient with Acetaminophen 1000mg Q8 PRN, Lidocaine patches, and occasional PRN oxycodone. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [] PCP, ___ follow up [] Patient would likely benefit from further psychiatry follow up as an outpatient [] Ongoing EtOH support counseling [] Patient would benefit from further connection with social work to help with his challenging home situation
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because there was concern that you may have had a seizure. You were also admitted to monitor for withdrawl symptoms from alcohol. While you were in the hospital you were given medications to help prevent withdrawl symptoms from alcohol. You were seen by our neurologists who recommended continuing your medications. Your labs were checked and were normal. You underwent an EEG study which did not demonstrate any concerning findings. Based on the workup, it appears that you may have a seizure-like disorder which is not epilepsy. You were kept on a medication, keppra, which should help prevent recurrence.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ with hx of C3-C6 laminectomy and fusion (___), htn, breast ca s/p bilateral mastectomy, MDD presenting with sepsis likely ___ UTI. #Sepsis - GU source, UCx grew pan-sensitive E.coli, transitioned off zosyn to po Cipro prior to discharge. significantly improved since admission, with resolution of white count and fever curve and now with improvement in urinary symptoms as well. She noted loose stool with watery diarrhea x3 the night before discharge and 1 the morning of discharge but unable to collect stool sample to rule out C diff (no further episodes prior to discharge). Given normal white count and benign abdominal exam, felt to still be safe for discharge. --clear return precautions provided --discharged on po ciprofloxacin for 4 more days to complete a 7 day total for complicated cystitis with urosepsis now resolved #Transaminitis - felt to be related to Tylenol toxicity, held Tylenol, replaced with prn toradol for pain or fever. Significant improvement in Transaminitis since admission though still mildly elevated. Will need outpatient follow up for this. Advised to avoid Tylenol altogether on discharge given suspicion for Tylenol induced liver toxicity / injury. # Hypertension: - Resumed home losartan and amlodipine the day before discharge, tolerating without issue # Hx of breast cancer: - Continued home letrozole # L shoulder pain: Chronic, attributed to L rotator cuff impingement and myofascial pain. She has relatively preserved mobility with pain to palpation over supraspinatus and acromion. - Held Tylenol - Toradol 15 mg q6h prn --> transitioned to po tramadol and provided short script on discharge - continued home pregabalin # Depression: - Continued home sertraline Ms. ___ is clinically stable for discharge today. The total time spent today on discharge planning, counseling and coordination of care today was greater than 30 minutes.
summary: You were admitted for fevers and were found to have a urinary tract infection. We started you on intravenous antibiotics initially but after your tests confirmed an infection in the urine caused by a bacteria called E. coli which is susceptible that antibiotics that can be given orally, we switched you over to oral Ciprofloxacin which you will continue to take for 4 more days after you leave the hospital. Your infection has improved significantly since you first came in and the cells that fight infection or your white blood cells have come down nicely since you first came in (they are now normal). We also stopped your Tylenol because you had evidence of liver injury based on your blood tests and we think this was likely related to Tylenol toxicity. We instead treated your fevers and your pain with an anti-inflammatory medication and your liver enzymes have actually been coming down nicely since you were admitted as well. We advise you to avoid Tylenol altogether when you leave the hospital and avoid any herbal supplements or other over the counter medications that you haven't discussed with your healthcare providers. continue to hydrate well after you leave the hospital. antibiotics can cause loose stool and diarrhea, but if you find that you're having persistent watery or loose stool more than 3 times a day you should call you PCP as you may need to be tested for a gut infection called C diff which can be caused by antibiotics. For now, given that your blood tests look normal and you've been without fevers or abdominal pain we have low suspicion for this kind of infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ was admitted for concern of increased seizure frequency. Upon admission LTM EEG was performed and she had many several events, which did not show abnormal electrical activity in the brain. These events appeared to be provoked and worsened by stress and anxiety, consistent with non epileptic events. Psychiatry was consulted for depression and suicidal ideation upon admission for which she was placed on 1:1 observation briefly. They also taught her techniques that were helpful in decreasing the frequency of the nonepileptic seizures. Ativan and other benzos did not stop these events. We have also increased her Zoloft. Her prior anti-seizure medications were not changed. Upon discharge, her mood was improved and no longer suicidal. The psychiatry consult team was able to setup intensive outpatient therapy as well as a visiting nurse to come home and make sure things are going well. - Please present to ___ on ___ at ___ (___) - A referral has been made for home nursing services through Child and Family Services (___) - A referral has been made to receive outpatient mental health provider through ___ ___ Health (___) .
summary: You were admitted for concern of increased seizure frequency. Upon admission an EEG was performed during several of your events, which did not show abnormal electrical activity in the brain. These events appeared to be provoked and worsened by stress and anxiety, and we believe them to be "nonepileptic seizures" - seizures that are NOT caused by abnormal electrical activity. Psychiatry was also consulted who helped you with techniques to prevent and decrease these events. Ativan and other benzos did not stop these events and we do not suggest you use ativan for these events. We have also increased your Zoloft dose.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ woman with a history of hypertension and depression who presented with SDH/SAH after fall potentially secondary to syncope. Serial CT scans of the head were stable, and neurosurgery determined that there was no acute surgical intervention indicated. #Subdural Hemorrhage/Subarachnoid Hemorrhage #Fall #Syncope She has no memory of falling or hitting her head. CT at OSH revealed right frontal SDH and SAH. Repeat CT at ___ showed stable bleed. Neurosurgery evaluated the patient and determined that no acute surgical intervention was indicated. Orthostatic vital signs negative. Telemetry without arrhythmia. Echocardiogram showed EF of 60% with no intracardiac cause of syncope identified. Physical therapy was consulted and recommended discharge to rehab. She was given seizure prophylaxis with Keppra 500mg PO BID x 7-days. Day 1 = ___. Day 7 = ___. Patient should follow-up with the Concussion Clinic. #Dizziness/Vertigo Patient reporting worsening dizziness/vertigo since her fall. Neurology was consulted and determined that the etiology of central vs. peripheral cannot be determined at this time. MRI of brain could be obtained to determine etiology, but would not change management. She was started on meclizine BID. This was uptitrated to TID, and helped with her dizziness. This should be given PRN as standing doses can worsen symptoms. #Hyponatremia Patient with mild hyponatremia to 131. Urine lytes suggestive of SIADH. She was maintained on a 1L fluid restriction. Please continue to monitor. #Nausea #Nutrition Patient reports that she has not been able to eat food for over ___ years without vomiting. She has been living on Ensure shakes and low sodium V8 drinks. She should have outpatient GI Follow-up. #Depression #SI without plan Psych was consulted in the ED and did not feel that she needed to have a ___. TSH/B12 normal. Collateral from the patient's brothers was that the patient has been suffering from a depressed mood for many years due to chronic back pain. She does not believe in western medicine and did not want help for her pain. Throughout the hospitalization, she asked for "a pill to end it all." Psychiatry recommended holding inpatient initiation of antidepressant medication at this time. Social work was consulted. #Constipation Patient has baseline constipation that requires manual disimpaction. This may be related to her poor PO intake She had a standing bowel reg. #Hypertension SBP on presentation was as high as SBPs in 240's. She was initially on a nicardapine drip, which was discontinued and her pressures have remained better controlled. She was started on amlodipine 5mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================= - Patient should follow-up with the Concussion Clinic. - Upon discharge, will need outpatient ___ rehab. Please make sure this is arranged prior to discharge. - Outpatient GI follow-up for persistant nausea after eating - Patient should establish care with primary care doctor - Patient with signs of depression. Consider outpatient initiation of SSRI. - Discharged on amlodipine 5mg daily for hypertension. Uptitrate as needed. - Continue meclizine on PRN basis. Standing meclizine can worsen symptoms.
summary: You were hospitalized at ___. You came to the hospital after a fall. You were found to have a bleed in your brain. You had two separate CT scans of your brain, which showed that the brain bleed was stable. The neurosurgery team did not think you needed surgery since the bleed was stable. You were very dizzy after your fall. We consulted the neurology team, who determined that the brain bleed was causing the dizziness. We gave you medication for the dizziness. This should slowly improve as your body gets rid of the blood in your brain. It is very important that you establish care with a primary care doctor after you leave rehab. It may also help for you to follow-up in the concussion clinic and in the ___ rehab clinic for your dizziness. Please continue to work with physical therapy to get stronger.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and Plan: ___ year old woman w/PMH of atypical ___ dz, depression, HTN, p/w altered mental status and general disinhibition at nursing home, became less agitated throughout stay, seen by psychiatry and neurology. After medical reasons for delirium were ruled out, pt. was eventually sent to rehabilitation. . # AMS: Pt. initially with continuing belligerence, agitation, and delirium, though patient was AAO x 2, not knowing place. Pt. was actively kicking and pushing people away. Daughter mentioned that this has been going on for the past few months, very difficult to take care of pt. at home. Labs demonstrated an initially high lactate with derangements in electrolytes. Though no source of infection besides a likely contaminated UA (which upon repeat was negative), it is possible that patient was infected at some point and was now presenting with abnormalities that are causing some of her delirium. Pt. with a mild leukocytosis, no bands, no fever. Causes of reversible delirium have been recently ruled out (nl B12/Folate in ___, nl TSH). RPR was sent and negative. Pt. was given fluids. Was far less agitated the next morning, AAO x 3, however harboring SI, mentioned that she wished that she would die because her life is not a life to live. Did demonstrate insight that the reason why she's in the hospital is because she was acting "crazy." Was able to converse, intermittently perseverative about her husband, who she erroneously believed was dead. As per neurology, EEG normal, unable to obtain LP after 2 attempts due to patient non-compliance. Psychiatry consult obtained and initially suggested a sitter along with discontinuing all psychoactive medications, including duloxetine, clonazepam, and anti-dementia medications including donepezil and aricept. Pt. was started on seroquel QHS and progressively became less agitated -- was subsequently transferred to a long term care facility. # Atypical ___: Pt. diagnosed for ___ years w/atypical ___, per daughter has trouble with gait and movement, though was able to wildly kick and borderline assault any individual within striking distance fairly easily on initial presentation. Pt. was also having short-term memory difficulties, possibly ___ w/dementia or comorbid with Alzheimer Dz. Had neurology consult who recommended continuing Sinemet (anti dementia medications discontinued as per psychiatry). No active issues thereafter. # Glaucoma: Continue outpt. eye drops (xalatan, azopt) # Osteoporosis: Cont. raloxifene, calc. carbonate, monthly ergocalciferol administrations, multivitamins. # Follicular Rash: patient with new follicular rash likely heat rash. She is receiving sarna lotion for the itch.
summary: You were admitted due to agitation and having changes in your mental status that made you belligerent. Upon arrival, we did several scans, one of your head, of your chest, and of your urine all of which did not show anything significant. We gave you some medications to help calm you down. In addition, we had the neurology and psychiatry doctors ___ and ___, and they determined that you should go to a long term care facility. We had several conversations with your husband and your daughter, ___ (who is your healthcare proxy) along with the case managers who corroborated that conclusion. You should be seen by your primary care physician ___ 1 to 2 weeks after your arrival to the rehabilitation facility.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ gentleman with history of alcohol dependence, insulin-dependent diabetes, depression, anxiety, chronic pancreatitis, hypertension and hyperlipidemia presenting with chest pain, multiple electrolyte abnormalities and polymorphic VT. Mr ___ ruled out for ACS with negative troponins x3. TTE and exercise MIBI also revealed poor exercise tolerance but structurally normal heart with normal EF (65%). Chest pain ultimately resolved with Maalox and tums, as well as with improvement of electrolyte disturbances as described below. On arrival, Mr ___ was noted to be in polymorphic VT that did not require defibrillation. Electrolytes revealed hypomagnesemia to 1.2, hypokalemia, hypochloremia, hypophosphatemia in the context of recent nausea, vomiting, diarrhea. Patient also has history of alcohol abuse raising concern for malnutrition as well. Electrolytes were repleted aggressively and returned within normal limits. Despite patient reporting that he had cut down on his alcohol consumption to a can of beer every few days, he was started on CIWA scale to prevent acute withdrawal symptoms. Of note, correction of electrolytes did not resolve prolonged QT noted on EKG. It was noted that Mr ___ was taking citalopram and quetiapine for his anxiety/depression, both of which are QT prolonging medications. Both were held this hospitalization. At discharge, QTc is 442ms, but review of old EKGs in the system also suggests that patient may have prolonged QT at baseline. Mr ___ has remained completely asymptomatic. Finally, given the discontinuation of quatiapine and citalopram, Psychiatry was consulted for anxiety/depression management. Patient was offered 50 mg Trazodone twice daily as needed for anxiety and started on Mirtazapine 15 mg at night for depression. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] Medication changes: - citalopram and Seroquel (quetiapine) were discontinued - continue trazodone 50 mg BID: PRN ANXIETY. Please avoid taking at night because of sedation when added to mirtazapine - continue Mirtazapine 15 mg QHS on ___. Increase to 30 mg QHS on ___. - started amlodipine 5 mg daily for blood pressure control - started lisinopril 5 mg daily for blood pressure and nephroprotection [ ] Recommend follow-up with outpatient mental health within a week of discharge [ ] F/u arranged with PCP ___ [ ] F/u arranged with Cardiology regarding QT prolongation [ ] continue supplementation with thiamine and multivitamin daily # CODE: Full Code # CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___
summary: Because you were having chest pain and were noted to have abnormal heart rhythm on EKG We repleted your body salts (electrolytes) which were very abnormal. We checked your labs and found that your chest pain was NOT caused by a heart attack. We stopped your quetiapine and citalopram because they can cause your heart to go into an abnormal and dangerous rhythm. We did an ultrasound of your heart that showed us it is structurally normal and pumping blood well. We took pictures of your heart as you were walking on the treadmill, which showed your heart responds normally to exercise and stress. We started you on a blood pressure medicines called 'amlodipine' and 'lisinopril' because the lower number on the blood pressure reading was too high. Lisinopril will also protect your kidneys from damage from your diabetes.

At discharge, QTc is 442ms, but review of old EKGs in the system also suggests that patient may have prolonged QT at baseline. Mr ___ has remained completely asymptomatic. Finally, given the discontinuation of quatiapine and citalopram, Psychiatry was consulted for anxiety/depression management. Patient was offered 50 mg Trazodone twice daily as needed for anxiety and started on Mirtazapine 15 mg at night for depression. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] Medication changes: - citalopram and Seroquel (quetiapine) were discontinued - continue trazodone 50 mg BID: PRN ANXIETY. Please avoid taking at night because of sedation when added to mirtazapine - continue Mirtazapine 15 mg QHS on ___. Increase to 30 mg QHS on ___. - started amlodipine 5 mg daily for blood pressure control - started lisinopril 5 mg daily for blood pressure and nephroprotection [ ] Recommend follow-up with outpatient mental health within a week of discharge [ ] F/u arranged with PCP ___ [ ] F/u arranged with Cardiology regarding QT prolongation [ ] continue supplementation with thiamine and multivitamin daily # CODE: Full Code # CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ was admitted to the surgical service after jumping out of a second story window and suffered bilateral subarachnoid hemorrhages, a right occipital condyle fracture, mandibular fracture, R ___ rib fractures, small L apical pneumothorax, sacral fracture, T1/T12 burst fracture, R acetabular / femoral neck fracture. She was ultimately treated with a right total hip replacement and a CTLSO brace. She was started on a 7 day course of keppra for seizure prophylaxis and admitted to the ICU and intubated with an NGT. She initially required pressors (neo) for hemodynamic stability. Serial hemotocrits were drawn and were steadily drifting from 30 to 21, at which point she was transfused 1U on HD3. She was started on TF on HD2. She had a repeat hCT which was stable. She went to the OR with the spine service on ___ for a T10-L2 lumbar fusion. Please note, as of the writing of this discharge summary there is no operative report. She went to the OR the following day with ortho for a right total hip replacement for her right acetabular and femoral neck fracture. Post-operatively, she has restrictions regarding posterior hip precautions but otherwise she is weight bear as tolerated on the right leg. She was also placed into a CTLSO brace to be worn when HOB > 30 degrees or OOB, otherwise with logroll precautions in place. She was weaned to pressure support and then extubated on ___. Psych also evaluated in the patient in the ICU and recommended a 1:1 sitter. She was noted to have been non-compliant with her home psych medications in the period prior to her suicide attempt. She was started on effexor and ability and transitioned to pristiq when she tolerated PO. She was transferred to the floor on ___. She had a speech and swallow eval on ___ which recommended pureed solids, and as her mental status improved she was reevaluated by speech and swallow who advanced her to a regular diet with thin liquids. Her foley was pulled on ___ and she failed to void, the foley was replaced. It was again attempted to be pulled on ___, and replaced when she failed to void. She will go to rehab with the foley for urinary retention. Her central line was discontinued on ___ with peripheral access. Her 1:1 sitter was discontinued by psychiatry on ___ for resolution of suicidal intent. She will follow up with ACS in two weeks. She will also follow up with ortho in 8 weeks. She will need her staples on her right hip removed on ___. Her appointment was established for her. She will also follow up with neurosurgery in one month with an AP/lateral film of her thoracic and lumbar spine. On the day of discharge, her pain was well controlled, she was ambulating with assistance. She has a foley in place for urinary retention. She was given discharge instructions and understands the plan. She was restarted on her home medication pristiq.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after falling from a second story building. You sustained multiple injuries, including fractures to your femur, pelvis, sacrum, thoracic spine, sternum and skull, injuries to your lung, and some bleeding and contusions in your brain. Immediately after your accident, you were monitored closely in the intensive care unit (ICU). Your medical care was managed by the trauma surgery, neurosurgery, orthopedic, and psychiatry teams. On ___ you went to the operating room for surgery to fix your thoracic spine fractures and on ___, you went to the operating room for repair of your right hip fracture. Once you were stable, you were transferred out of the ICU where you continued to make strides towards recovery. You are now tolerating a regular diet and your pain is under control on oral medication. You're ready to be discharged to a rehab to continue your recovery and work on gaining strength. You required a foley catheter to be reinserted post-operatively due to urinary retention; another voiding trial may be attempted at rehab.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with dual diagnosis of depression and EtOH abuse presents for EtOH detoxification in the setting of 3 day h/o N/V with mild hematemesis now resolved. . #Hematemesis: Patient presented to ED with single episode of blood streaked emesis after 3 day h/o persistent vomiting and wretching. Most likely related to partial ___ tear at the GE junction. No additional episodes of hematemesis this admission, and given that hct and hemodynamics are stable, and guiac negative, deferred EGD to outpatient setting. Maintained active T&S, and Hct trended BID on day of admission, and remained stable. No red flags, as reported weight loss can be explained by chronic alcoholism and malnutrition. . #N/V: Several day h/o non-bilious watery N/V. Most likely represents acute alcoholic gastritis, given associated abdominal pain, and h/o EtOH use. Also thought to be potentially related to H. Pylori infection, as her serology was positive. Other acute inflammatory abdominal process like pancreatitis, cholecystitis, and alcoholic hepatitis thought to be less likely in the setting of essentially benign physical exam and laboratory data. Emesis resolved with zofran x1 and IV pantoprazole on admission. Required additional dose of IV zofran on the floor, but tolerated full liquid diet and was advanced to solid diet prior to discharge. Started on prev-pack for (+) h. pylori to be completed ___. Also started oni ranitidine 150mg BID PRN abdominal pain. Will f/u with outpt PCP for EGD and test of cure. . #EtOH abuse/?withdrawal: Last drink on day of arrival. Started on CIWA protocol in-house, but only scored >10 x2 requiring 5mg PO diazepam. Patient denied any h/o seizure or DT. Wished to undergo EtOH addiction treatment program. For this reason, she was transferred to 30 day rehab program. Started on MV, thiamine, folate. . #Chest/abdominal pain: Abdominal pain thought to be related to acute gastritis and musculoskeletal pain associated with prolonged N/V. No concern ACS as no association with activity or associated diaphoresis, dizziness, or pre-syncope, and pain was reproducible with palpation. EKG WNL. RUQ US without evidence of cirrhosis, hepatitis. Continued on ranitidine as above. Pain nearly resolved on day of dischrge .. Transitional Issues: #Should obtain outpatient EGD within next ___ months
summary: You were admitted because of persistent nauseas and vomiting. On the day prior to admission, ___ vomitted a small amount of blood. We believe this was due to a small tear in your esophagus. ___ were monitored closely, and your red blood cell count did not change, which showed that ___ were not actively bleeding. Your stomach and chest pain is likely due to inflammation of your stomach lining (gastritis) due to your alcohol use. To prevent this from reoccuring, ___ should abstain from alcohol after ___ complete detoxification. ___ have a stomach infection with a bacteria called H. Pylori. This is a common bacteria, but can cause ulcers in your stomach and worsen your stomach inflammationYou were treated with medication that should be completed after 10 days. ___ should obtain an endoscopy to look at your stomach and esophagus in ___ months to test for cure. Please discuss this with your primary care physician. ___ were monitored in the hospital and only showed mild evidence of alcohol withdrawal. ___ expressed interest in undergoing rehabilitation. For this, ___ were transferred to a facility that specializes on this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with frontotemporal dementia admitted after Tylenol overdose from suicide attempt. complains of ingestion of 70 tablets of extra strength Tylenol on ___ at 1 pm, now with AMS, worsening LFTs, concerning for fulminent liver failure. . . #Tylenol overdose/Acute liver failure: Patient admitted to ingesting 70 tablets of extra strength Tylenol. She was admitted to the ICU with evidence of hepatic necrosis and synthetic liver dysfunction with encephalopathy and coagulopathy but without hyperbilirubinemia. She was treated with NAC protocol with improvement in mental status and coagulopathy. To rule out other causes of acute liver failure a RUQ US was obtained which showed normal hepatic vasculature and an AFP was wnl. Serologic workup for viral hepatitis and autoimmune liver diseases were negative. Her antipsychotic and anxiolytic medications were held in the setting of ALF but were restarted at low doses when liver chemistries, INR, and mental status improved. Her chemistries were not normal on discharge, but had been downtrending for a number of days and PCP was contacted to recheck at follow up visit. # Coag negative staph bacteremia: This was felt to be a contaminant as it grew from only ___ bottles and patient did not have clinical evidence of infection. She was started on antibiotics during transplant evaluation but this was discontinued when liver function normalized. #Frontotemporal dementia: The patient's home medications were held and then restarted at lower dose (including Seroquel) when liver function normalized. #+UA: Patient was initially started on CTX but this was discontinued when UCx grew mixed flora and felt not to have true UTI. #Depression: Given severe depression with suicide attempt the patient was kept on suicide precautions. She was evaluated by inpatient Psychiatry who felt that patient would benefit from inpatient psychiatry admission. Given acute liver failure, home medications were held and were not restarted when liver function normalized as these would be managed by the patient's inpatient psychiatry facility to which the patient was discharged. Transitional Issues: # HTN: While hospitalized on the medical floor, the patient had SBPs 150s-170s. Before hospitalization, the patient was not on any anti-hypertensives medications. She was started on Metoprolol tartrate 25 mg BID with fair control of BPs and discharged on metoprolol succinate 50mg daily. Please follow her blood pressures and discontinue them if not necessary. It is possible some of her hypertension is related to anxiety and taking less than her home dose of Ativan. #Liver injury: The patient should have LFTs checked weekly until normal.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with tylenol toxicity. You were initially admitted to the intensive care unit and given a medication that helps treat the effects of tylenol toxicity. Your liver function tests were continually improving and will continue to be followed as an outpatient. Our transplant surgery evaluated you and felt that you did NOT need to have a liver transplant because your liver was improving on its own. Because of your improvement, you were transferred out of the intensive care unit and came to the medicine floor. While on the medicine floor, your mental status continued to improve and your liver function also started to get better. We also noted that your blood pressures were a little high while you were here and we started you on a blood pressure lowering medicine. We also started giving you some ativan at night before bed to help you sleep. You will be transferred to a psychiatric rehabilitation facility where you will continue to receive care. The psychiatry team there will assess you and restart the rest of your medications when it is safe to do so depending on your liver enzymes.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, DM2, alcoholism who presents s/p alcohol withdrawal seizure. # Alcohol withdrawal: Timing of seizure (10hrs after previous drink) seems very close, but patient also reports drinking less than usual and inconsistently taking his clonidine, so this may have been contributory. Pt had no further seizures, was no longer requiring diazepam per CIWA scale by day 3 of admission, and reported his symptoms overall improved. He was also started on thiamine, folate, and a multivitamin. He was seen by social work and psychiatry (see below) and recommended for dual-diagnosis psychiatric admission for alcohol abuse and depression. # Depression/suicidal ideation: Pt reported suicidal ideation at admission, with plan to cut his wrists, which he has done in the past. He was seen by the psychiatric consult service, determined to meet Section 12a criteria and observed by a 24-hr sitter until he was medically cleared for psychiatric admission. He was restarted on his outpatient paroxetine, which he had not been compliant with for several months, but his haloperidol (for psychotic features) was held given he denied hallucinations and was calm during this admission. # Neck pain: Likely due to trauma from seizure. Possible ligamentous injury seen on CT ___. Further evaluation with MRI ___ was negative for acute injury. He wore a ___ collar until he was radiographically and clinically cleared on ___. # Transaminitis due to Chronic Hepatitis C: Likely related to alcoholic intake - was downtrending with detox. Pt denies cirrhosis. He believes he has had LFT abnormalities during prior admissions that also resolved with detox. His hepatitis serologies during this admission showed positive hepatitis C antibody with HCV viral load pending ad discharge. Hepatitis A serology was negative and HBV serologies were negative, including HBsAb so he was given 1 dose of hepatitis B vaccine. # Dyspepsia: Patient complaining of some burning abdominal pain. Concern for possible gastritis in the setting of heavy EtOH use. He was given antacids with resolution of symptoms. RESOLVED: # Leukocytosis: WBC count was 15 at admission, but normalized quickly with fluids. Likely in setting of withdrawal/seizure and/or hemoconcentration. No left shift, patient afebrile without focal infectious findings. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Type 2 Diabetes Uncontrolled with complications: HgbA1c during this admission was 10. Poorly controlled due to his outpatient noncompliance with insulin. Complicated by peripheral neuropathy. - Titrated glargine and sliding scale humalog during admission # Benign Hypertension: Unclear what medications other than clonidine he was taking previously. Possibly lisinopril based on pharmacy records. - Continued clonidine, did not restart lisinopril given he was normotensive during this admission TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Will need outpatient followup with hepatology for HCV infection, with cirrhosis screening and completion of hepatitis B and A vaccination series. - HCV viral load and genotype pending at discharge; will need follow-up with hepatology as outpatient - Incidental finding of 2 mm lung nodule in the left lung apex on CT ___. Followup CT is recommended in 12 months.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for alcohol withdrawal after a seizure. We treated your withdrawal with diazepam and you improved. You also had some neck pain after the seizure, which was likely due to ligament injury seen on your CT scan and MRI. You wore a J collar for neck stabilization until your cervical spine was cleared by MRI and physical examination. You were also having active thoughts of suicide while you were here, so once your medical issues were stable, you were transferred to an inpatient psychiatric facility to manage your depression and alcohol abuse. You were diagnosed with hepatitis C infection during this hospitalization.

summary: You were in the hospital because you were vomiting blood and having black stools. You were given medication to help you detox. You had a scope to look at your esophagus and stomach, which showed inflammation but no active bleeding. It's extremely important that you stop drinking to prevent life-threatening bleeding in your esophagus and irreversible damage to your liver. As discussed with our social worker, please ask for a leave of absence from your work to focus on your health. Please also go to the addiction treatment clinic near your house that has walk-in intakes all day ___, and of course, depend on your family/friends for support. You were approved for insurance during your stay.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female w/ history of ESRD secondary to IDDM and HTN on HD MWF and presenting to ___ c/o neck and back pain after a mechanical fall from standing position onto the floor. ACUTE ISSUES: ============== #s/p fall: Mrs. ___ presented s/p fall at home with AMS after recent hospitalization for klonopin overdose. Pt reports hitting her head, and on exam was drowsy and had difficulty conversing. ___ and CT c-spine were both normal. Labs were notable for a negative serum tox screen (ASA, EtOH, tylenol, benzos, barbiturates, tricyclics). Notably, pt was admitted for Klonopin overdose days prior to this admission, and admitted that the overdose was a suicide attempt. For this hospitalization, pt denies excessive medication use or illicit drug use. Pt was seen by psychiatry while hospitalized, and per their recommendation neurontin, hydroxyzine, and seroquel were discontinued, and her methadone was reduced to 40mg daily, as it was believed all could contribute to AMS and delirium. Psychiatry also suggested using haldol in cases of agitation or delirium with this patient. In addition, psychiatry determined that she was not a suicide risk. Pt's mental status improved rapidly thereafter, and on discharge she was fully oriented and alert without evidence of delirium. She denied SI during this hospitalization, and was discharged after being cleared by psychiatry to follow up with her psychiatrist the following day, ___. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ #ESRD on HD: Pt presents with HD schedule of ___ and ___. While hospitalized, she received dialysis ___ and ___ while hospitalized. Patient was continued on her home nephrocaps and sevelamer. #IDDM: During ___ hospital course, she was noted to have BG in the 300's to 400's intermittently while on her home insulin regimen of Lantus 12 QHS and SSI. It was discovered that she was consuming soda, and after discontinuation and placement on diabetic diet, pt's blood glucose improved. #Hypertension: Continued home norvasc #Depression: Continued home venlafaxine TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== #Patient scheduled for outpatient follow up with her psychiatrist ___ and her PCP ___ consider continuing medication adjustments as described above
summary: You were admitted after falling at home and presenting with some confusion. A CT of your head and neck were performed, and the results were normal. During your stay, you were seen by psychiatry, and it was advised that your methadone be decreased and your neurontin, hydroxyzine, and seroquel stopped, as they may have contributed to your confusion and fall. We reduced your methadone dose to 40mg daily and held your neurontin, hydroxyzine, and seroquel while hospitalized. After this change, your mental status improved dramatically. It is strongly recommended that you discuss reducing your dose of methadone and discontinuing neurontin, hydroxyzine, and seroquel with your psychiatrist and primary care physician. In addition, it is important for you to regularly check you blood sugar and take sliding scale as necessary in addition to your nightly Lantus.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ with history of annorexia ___ chronic abdominal pain with recent PICCs for TPN admitted with anorexia for past week after discharge. . # Anorexia: patient presented with abdominal pain when eating, an inability to tolerate POs and a reported 7 lbs weight loss at home. The patient was admitted by GI after calling their clinic with these complaints. While inpatient the patient underwent Colonoscopy which didnot show any evidence of obstruction or inflammatory changes. The patient subsequently had a gastric emptying study which again was normal and a MR ___ was also performed that did not show any evidence of a motility disorder. As far as metabolic workup was concerned a lead level was sent and was normal as well as cortisol and TSH, a work up of AIP was deffered. The patient's inability to tolerate POs was felt not to have an anatomic etiology and patinet was seen by both nutrition and psychiatry to aid in her PO intake. She was discharged home to follow up with her local GI providers. #NUTRITION: patient was found to be at 83% of ideal body weight, during past admisisons the patient had been started on TPN through ___ lines complicated by superficial thrombosis. During this admission it was felt that the patinet's nutritional status did not require parentral supplementation and as there was ___ anatomic barrier to PO intake she was actively encouraged to eat. Nutrition and psychiatry provided recommendations and guidance for initiating a tolerable PO regimen for the patient to follow as an outpatient. As TPN was not indicated the patient's lovenox was discontinued and ___ additional attempts at IV access were made. #DEPRESSION: patient's depression and eating disorder were felt to be the main drivers of her presenting anorexia. Given this psychiatry was consulted and recommended that the patient start Trilafon 4mg BID for nausea along with Remeron 7.5mg po QHS and outpatient referral to the ___ at ___ for Mind/Body therapy. An effort was made to discontinue the patinet's buprorprion given the risk for seizures and its suppression of appetite, though the patient refused such a medication change and was aware of the consequences. # MIGRANES: stable, patient continued on excedrin/ferocet while in the hospital as well as her home propranolol. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -patient was discharged to follow up with home GI providers -___ were pending at the time of discharge -patient was made aware of the risks associated with buproprion use -ideal body weight was 83% at time of discharge -enoxaparin was discontinued at discharge
summary: You were admitted for evaluation of your inablity to tolerate foods. You were seen by our gastroenterologists who did an extensive work up for anatomic reasons for your inability to tolerate foods. These tests included a colonoscopy which was normal, a gastric emptying study which was normal, a MRI of the small bowel which did not show a motility disorder as well as a number of specialized lab tests to asses for alternative causes of your abdominal complaints. Fortunately these were all negative. You were also seen by our psychiatrists who helped make recommendations regarding medications to help treat your depression and nausea simultaneously. As one of the greatest barriers to you being able to eat is your depression it is important that you follow up with your local psychiatrist Dr. ___ therapist ___ upon discharge. You were also seen by our nutritionists who provided you with several strategies to make eating easier for you and to maintain a balanced diet. Fortunately it was not felt that you required parentral nutrition and you did not require additional TPN. Because of this you may stop taking enoxaparin your blood clots will resolve on their own. A full summary of your hospital course and studies will be sent to your gastroenterologist in the next few days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old male with past medical history of venous insufficiency, recent workup for bilateral lower extremity pain thought to relate to potential peripheral neuropathy of unknown etiology, admitted ___ w orthostatic hypotension, resolving with IV fluids, course complicated by episode of delirium though to have been precipitated by recent increased stress and decreased sleep, subsequently ambulating safely, able to be discharged home with PCP and neurology ___. # Orthostatic Hypotension / Neuropathy / Lower Extremity Pain - as documented in prior discharge summary and neurology notes, patient with peripheral neuropathy of unclear etiology; he presented with episode of orthostatic hypotension and reported recent syncopal episode; suspected etiology of syncope was orthostatic hypotension; he received IV fluids with subsequent normal orthostatic vital signs. He subsequently revealed increased stress at home related to marital discord and that he had only been sleeping < 2 hours per night as a result. He continued to report ongoing pain and tingling in his lower extremities, unchanged from his recent admission. He was seen by the neurology service who recommended outpatient ___ for additional workup and repeat EMG. Stress and lack of sleep were felt to be a major driver regarding his ongoing symptoms, and recent reported decreased PO intake (likely the etiology of his hypovolemia / episode of orthostatic hypotension). At time of discharge he was able to safely ambulate in the hall today without issue. Continued home gabapentin, prn Tylenol, methocarbamol. Trialed on lidocaine cream. # Delirium - On evaluation by social work and neurology patient appeared to be responding to internal stimuli and making bizarre and disorganized statements clearly awake. Given concern for a primary psychiatric process, he was seen by psychiatry who felt he had acute encephalopathy as a result of intense stress and sleep deprivation with underlying major depressive disorder and anxiety disorder. Case discussed with neurology who did not believe this was related to a primary neurologic issue. Psychiatry recommended increasing duloxetine dose to 90mg daily. # GERD - continued ranitidine Transitional Issues - Discharged home with prescription for increased dose of duloxetine and trial of lidocaine cream.
summary: You were admitted with dizziness. You were seen by neurologists and given IV fluids. Your symptoms improved and your blood pressure improved. The neurologists recommended following up with them in clinic for additional testing--they will be in touch regarding scheduling a time. During your hospital stay you were were confused. You were seen by psychiatry who felt that this was a reaction to too much stress and not enough sleep. They recommended increasing the dose of your Cymbalta and considering using melatonin to help with your sleep. It is really important that you find health ways to manage your stress--please consider seeing a therapist to discuss this. You are now ready to leave the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yof with h/o HTN, diverticulitis, prior c.diff colitis, p/w with two days of fever and diarrhea. # Diarrhea w/fever and abdominal pain: She presented with two days of bloody diarrhea and fevers. In the ED, the patient had negative UA, negative CXR and CT abd and pelvis with no acute findings. On the day of admission she was febrile to 102 on the floor and had two episodes of guaiac positive diarrhea, not grossly bloody. Initial differential diagnosis included acute cholecystitis, c diff, diverticulitis, infectious gastroenteritis, hepatitis. CBC showed leukocytosis. AST, ALT, Tbili, and alk phos were elevated. Hepatitis serologies were negative. Lipase was normal. Serum acetaminophen was 16.1, serum ASA was negative. Stool cultures, O + P and c.diff studies were sent and negative. Patient was started on empiric therapy to cover hepatobilliary flora with IV flagyl and IV cipro. The patient had an allergic reaction to the IV cipro and her meds were switched to PO without complication. A RUQ-US was performed and unremarkable. Toxicology was consulted and the patient was started on NAC for 21 hour course. Patient rapidly improved, diarrhea resolved and patient remained afebrile. Antibiotics were continued for a 5 day course. Liver enzymes gradually trended down throughout her admission and were marginally elevated at the time of discharge. Patient was instructed to stop taking tylenol x 2 weeks and to limit tylenol dosage to 2.4g per day or less for the future. Her statin was also held and not restarted at discharge in setting of elevated LFT's. . # Depression: Patient reported depressed mood since the death of her husband ___ years ago. She additionally reported that since she became acutely ill, she worries that she will never get better and that she will be a burden to her son and others. She demonstrated a tearful affect intermittently during her admission and has made repeated comments about considering that she may not want to continue living. Psychiatry was consulted and recommended starting low dose remeron for appetite improvement, mood stabilization and improved sleep. At the time of discharge, patient was set up with visiting psychiatric nurse to assist with medications, evaluate blood pressure and evaluate for signs of worsening depression. . # Anemia: HCT remained in mid 30___s throughout admission with baseline based on OMR between ___. This was felt to be secondary to frequent blood draws and fluids. Of note, her stool was guiac + but not grossly bloody. Hemolysis labs were also sent and unremarkable. Patient should receive outpatient colonoscopy in setting of guiac + stool and no recent colonoscopy. . # BP management: patient had normal to low-normal blood pressures at admission. Orthostatics were possitive and patient was gently rehydrated with several liters of fluid. Patient was evaluated by ___ and felt to be stable on feet with walker. Her blood pressure medications were held throughout her stay and were not restarted upon discharge. ___ was instructed to perform daily BP readings and contact PCP if they begin to rise for guidance in restarting her prior BP regimine. . # Adrenal insufficiency: patient reported past h/o adrenal insufficiency ___ high dose steroids for PMR. She was maintained on home dose of prednisone throughout her stay. A cortisol stimulation test was performed and unremarkable. At the time of discharge, her home prednisone dose was continued. . # Hypothyroid: patient was maintained on home synthroid dose. . # Polymyalgia Rheumatica: patient was maintained on home prednisone dose and naproxen. She was also given oxycodone at discharge PRN for pain and was continued on a substantial bowel regimine. . # Dispo: patient was instructed to follow up with her PCP for further evaluation.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for evaluation of abdominal pain and diarrhea. Your initial physical exam was quite concerning for tenderness in your abdomen over your liver. We performed some tests which showed that your liver was inflammed and we subsequently discovered that the ammount of Tylenol in your blood was too high. We gave ___ a medicine to help prevent further liver damage and your liver seems to be slowly recoveringYou were also started on antibiotics for a potential gastrointenstinal infection and your diarrhea subsequently resolved and your remained afebrile. Please continue these antibiotics for 1 more day. During your, stay we also checked to ensure that your adrenal glands were working properly. They were were normal. We are also concerned about how sad ___ seemed during your stay with us. We asked our psychiatric colleages to see ___ and they recommended starting a low dose medication (Mirtazepine) that will help elevate your mood, increase your appetite and help ___ sleep at night. They also felt that ___ would benefit from following up with a psychiatrist as an outpatient and we feel the same. During your stay we also noticed that your blood pressure was normal and did not require blood pressure medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ gentleman with a history of depression who presented after suicide attempt via tylenol ingestion. He was transferred to ___ on NAC gtt and completed NAC protocol, with normalization of LFT's and acetaminophen level prior to completion of third NAC bag. He is being discharged to an inpatient psychiatric facility for ongoing treatment of his depression. ACTIVE ISSUES ---------------- # Acute Tylenol Ingestion: Patient ingested 27.5 grams of Tylenol in an attempt to end his life around 9am on ___. He was admitted to an outside hospital, where acetaminophen level was 194 three hours after initiation of NAC (level not checked on arrival). LFT's peaked in 40's. Patient tolerated NAC well. He was transferred to ___, where he was seen by Transplant Surgery and Toxicology. He completed his third bag of NAC per protocol. Prior to completion of protocol, LFT's, INR, and acetaminophen level normalized. Toxicology was consulted, who advised patient did not need additional NAC. On morning of ___, EKG showed normalization of intervals. His LFTs trended up slowly prior to discharge and toxicology was re-consulted. The pattern and rate of elevation was not consistent with ongoing acetaminophen toxicity. Most likely due to liver repair, and they plateaued prior to discharge. Hepatitis serologies were sent which revealed HAV immunity, and that he is HBV non-immune. # Depression, Suicide Attempt: Patient reported several months of worsening depression which was exacerbated by disciplinary incident at school. He initially stated he intended to end his life; he later said he had looked up timing of acetaminophen toxicity and planned the time he would notify school RN, noting that "they'd just give me another liver" if he miscalculated and became ill. His Lexapro was recently increased from 5 to 10 mg daily. He has an outpatient therapist whom he sees weekly. Psychiatric medications were held on admission in the setting of acetaminophen overdose. He was seen by psychiatry on ___, who advised restarting home abilify and escitalopram. He was monitored on a 1:1 sitter. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES -------------------- - Patient's code status is full and contact was mother, ___: ___ - Consider further titration of psychiatric medications - Please draw repeat AST and ALT on ___ and fax results to PCP ___ (Phone: ___, Fax: ___. ICD9: 790.4. - Needs intensive psychiatric care as an outpatient after discharge from inpatient psychiatric facility - No acetaminophen until LFTs normalize, then no more than 2g/day - PCP follow up regarding immunization/re-immunization for HBV given lack of surface antibodies
summary: As you know, you were admitted after taking an overdose of Tylenol in an attempt to end your life. You were treated with a medication to protect the liver in Tylenol overdose and, fortunately, your liver continues to function well. Your liver function tests returned to normal and the infusion we given for Tylenol toxicity was stopped. Over the next 2 days, your liver function tests increased again, but in a pattern not consistent with ongoing active acetaminophen toxicity. We sent hepatitis serologies, and you labs will need to be followed as an outpatient to ensure they return to normal over time. We hope that you will not have long-term liver damage, and it will be essential to avoid toxic levels of all drugs in the future. However, we are VERY worried about your depression and safety.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ ___ ___ gastric bypass, opioid abuse, depression p/w recent increased fatigue and found to be hypoglycemic. # HYPOGLYCEMIA: Likely secondary to recently decreased intake; pt endorsed trying to reduce "junk foods" immediately prior to onset of symptoms. HbA1c <6, indicating pt has not developed DM2. Various endocrine pathologies considered included cortisol deficiency, insulin antibodies, insulinoma, or ___ hyperplasia secondary to gastric bypass surgery [nesidioblastosis], which appeared much less likely given glucose stability in the hospital. AM cortisol WNL. Did not become hypoglycemic on admission so no labs for insulin, ___, or ___ were drawn. - STARTED ___ and cyanocobalamin 100 mcg PO/NG DAILY ___ ___ bypass - Rx given for glucometer, lancets, and testing strips to monitor FSBG when symptomatic - Follow up with surgeons for further management of diet, workup of NIPS # Contaminated urinalysis: intially treated with nitrofurantoin for ?UTI, though repeat without epis did not show e/o infection. # NARCOTICS ABUSE: Lives in sober home. Has not used Percocet in over one year and has agreements on her ___ medications. No narcotics, muscle relaxants, or benzos in the house. Ibuprofen 600 mg Q8H:PRN pain. # DEPRESSION/ANXIETY: H/o. Continue home citalopram 40 mg PO QD. # MIGRAINES: H/o. Home ___ mg oral TID:PRN migraine, can get one/day here. Ibuprofen 600 mg Q8H:PRN migraine. # RIGHT HIP, LEG PAIN: H/o. Has had several imaging studies. No narcotics, muscle relaxants, or benzos in the house. Ibuprofen 600 mg Q8H:PRN pain. # CANDIDAL DERMATITIS: Physical exam shows erythematous, itching rash below pt's inferior pannus. Pt describes long history of rash, occasionally flaring. Candidal dermatitis thought most likely given high incidence among obese patients and appearance of rash. It is possible that changes in your body since your gastric bypass are causing some of your symptoms. You need to follow up with bariatric surgery along with a nutritionist and your primary care physician to create ___ lifestyle plan that will work for you. Your second urine sample returned clear without signs of infection and you do not need antibiotics. If you feel lightheaded or shaky, please eat something with sugar immediately or drink orange juice or cola. Take your blood sugar by finger prick, if less than 70 then consume juice or cola and let your regular outpatient doctor know.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Please see discharge summary from a few days prior for more details on recent NSTEMI workup and treatment ___ ___ F PMHx HTN, insulin dependent DM, recent admission for NSTEMI now s/p LAD BMS, course c/b fall with small sub-arachnoid hemorrhage, now with psychosis and catatonia (which has been going on for a year but recently exacerbated). # MDD c/b Catatonia - pt w likely depression w catatonic features, diagnosed during recent admission; felt not to be underlying general medical condition, primarily driven by a psychiatric illness likely exacerbated by medical illness; concern that patient was unable to take PO medications. On last admission she was transferred from cardiology service to psychiatry for further treatmentn of catatonia and then transferred back to medicine service because of cocnern she was not taking in PO and would need IV ativan. While on the medicine service she was taking her medications by mouth every day. She needed help to eat mainly because she is blind from DM. At times during the day she was catatonic but this usually resolved over a few hours and during end of hospital stay she was no longer demonstrating catatonia. She was given the following psych meds: standing 1mg ativan PO q6hrs, aripiprazole, citalopram. Psychiatry saw pt and felt she was well enough to go to be d/c;d with follow up with psychiatry in ___. ___ evaluated the patient and recommended the patient go to rehab given how unstead she was on her feet however patient and family preferred going home with 24 hour supervision at home. It was explained to the family in detail about the concern that the patient is at risk for going home and falling and that the risks could include possible death becasue the patietn is on plavix. An interpreter was present during ___ family meeting and the family (husband and son-in-law) and patient herself understool the risks involved. PCP was contacted and updated about hospital course and he will follow with her as an outpatient. # CAD s/p DES to LAD - ___ had DES placed to LAD for 99% stenotic lesion, had no CP during this hospitilizations. ASA, atorva, metoprolol, plavix (she will continue to take for total of 1 month from ___, lat day will be ___ were continued # HTN : pt had uncontrolled HTN on last admission and on this admission her SBPs were at goal in the 120s. - we continued lisinopril, amlodipine, clonidine patch # DM - continued standing NPH and sliding scale humalog # s/p Fall w ___ - will need f/u Dr. ___ and ___ in ~2 weeks and repeat head CT then # R-lobe thyroid nodule - f/u outpatient ultrasound TRANSIIONAL ISSUES #catatonia/psychosis- pt will follow with psychiatrist at ___ ___ # s/p Fall w ___ - will need f/u Dr. ___ and ___ in ~2 weeks and repeat head CT then # R-lobe thyroid nodule - f/u outpatient ultrasound #HTN: should be followed as outpatient
summary: You came to the hospital a couple of weeks ago intially because you had a heart attack, you went to the cath lab and a stent was placed to open up the vessel that was blocked. While you were here you had behavior where you would freeze up which is called catatonia. The psychiatrists were involved and initially you went to the psychiatry ward in the hospital. They then decided you would be best treated on the medicine floor because you needed intravenous ativan for a period of time. We continued giving you medications to help you loosen up. We would like for you to follow up with the psychiatrists. This is very important. While you were here you were evalutated by the physical therapists and there is great concern that you could go home and fall. This risk includes falling, further bleeding, expansion of your current head bleed, brain bleeding, fracture, and even death if there was a large bleed. We had a big talk with you and your family and you have all understood that risk and still prefer to take her home rather than bring her to rehab.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT & PLAN: ___ yo F w/ h/o anxiety, depression (multiple prior suicide attempts and ECT treatments), chronic low back pain, ___ disease, OSA, and h/o narcotics abuse presenting w/ falls. . # Falls- Unclear why patient has fallend 3 times in past day. She is a poor historian and is unable to explain the circumstances leading to her fall beyond saying she was dizzy. Tox screen positive for amphetamines and benzos, but she is known to be on these drugs. Not positive for other illicit materials. Infectious work up so far negative w/ normal UA and CXR and no localizing sxs. Very drowsy on arrival to floor and this could have contributed to falls at home. CT head and neck negative for fracture. Patient had normal orthostaics. Patient was ambulating with minimal right sided limp throughout day. Patient would leave floor and hospital and come back despite being told this was against hospital policy. ___ saw patient and felt she did not need ___ services as she was able to ambulate without assistance. Her B12 was normal (384). We avoided giving patient her clonazepam as it is sedating. This medication could be titrated down by her outpatient physician. We also gave acetaminophen for patient, and were wary about giving narcotics in a patient with hx of narcotics abuse. However, patient stated had pain so we gave some vicodin only as inpatient. Patient still able to ambulate during these pain episodes. We had neurology come see the patient who felt falls were likely related to polypharmacy and taking her medications on an empty stomach. Though a seizure could not be ruled out, given hx of medication noncompliance felt no need to add medication currently. She has an outpatient neuro followup. The patient was also monitored on telemetry with no arrythmias . # Drowsiness- Is alert and oriented x3 despite drowsiness and neuro exam is non focal. No asterixis. Answers questions appropriately. ___ be related to medications patient is taking vs. toxic effect of ingestions we are unaware of. Tox screen only positive for known substances. Has woken up quite a bit more since arriving on floor. We monitored the patient's neuro exam while she was on the floor. She was alert and responsive. . # Anxiety/depression- h/o suicide attempts in past. We continued home duloxetine, floxetine. Her outpatient physician could consider using one medication for the patient. We held the patietn's clonazepam given drowsiness. We also held her lyrica given her drowsiness. . # Crohns- No sxs of active flare. We continued home mesalamine. Patient also mentioned an episode of stool incontinece though on exam here had intact rectal tone and sensation with no incontience while inpatient. She states she has had episodes of incontinence in the past with her history of Crohns. Per history, unclear if this is true incontinence or diarrhea. As patient had formed stool here and this is not an active issue, patient should follow up on this at her GI appointment next week.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for complaints of falls. In the Emergency Department you had a head CT which was negative for an acute intracranial process and a neck CT which showed no fractures. You also had a chest xray which showed no signs of infection. Our neurologists came and saw you and felt that your falls could be related to your medications. You should discuss with your primary care physician about adjusting some of your medications. Our physical therapists (___) saw you and felt you did not need ___ services. You were also monitored on telemetry to check the rhythm of your heart and it was reassuring.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ PMHx ESRD ___ lithium nephropathy on HD ___, schizoaffective disorder, CAD, HTN, admitted w/ creatinine 12 (improved back to baseline after restarting HD ___, and psychosis ___ schizoaffective d/o in setting of medication non-adherence and delirium), w/ course c/b hypotension ___ HD vs restarting clozapine, improved), who was discharged to inpatient psychiatry. #AMS/Schizoaffective disorder: On admission, pt expressed thoughts of self-harm and perceptual disturbances and was noted to be grossly disorganized and confused. Psychiatry was consulted. Unclear if AMS is ___ toxic metabolic encephalopathy/delirium in setting of ESRD versus related to psychosis ___ medication non-compliance and underlying schizoaffective disorder. Improved after re-initiating HD and restarting psych medications. Pt was restarted on home clonazepam (decreased to 0.25mg BID given deliriogenic effect), Divalproex ___ po daily, and venlafaxine 300mg PO QAM. Clozapine was restarted at a lower dose given risk of hypotension and slowly increased. Of note, pt frequently refused his medications despite extensive conversations regarding the utility, risks and benefits of the medications. Pt was maintained on 1:1 observation for safety. Pt was medically cleared, placed on ___, and discharged to inpatient psychiatry for further management. # Suicidal ideation: The patient was placed on a 1:1 observation for safety and was restarted on his home antipsychotic medications. The patient was seen by Psychiatry during admission (as above). The patient denied SI on discharge. # ESRD on HD: Pt w/ a h/o ESRD ___ lithium nephropathy, recently initiated on HD MWF. On admission, patient was found to have creatinine of 12 (up from a baseline of ~7). The patient was re-initiated on HD on ___. Dialysis was complicated by hypotension, which responded to IVF. Pt was started on sevelamer. # Hypotension Pt developed hypotension requiring fluid resuscitation. Hypotension likely ___ reinitiating HD as well as restarting clozaril (which was subsequently decreased). Pt was restarted on Clozapine at a lower dose due to risk of hypotension. Would recommend continuing to increase Clozapine as recommended by Psychiatry, and as tolerated by blood pressure. 2. Would recommend monitoring ANC weekly while titrating up Clozapine. 3. Pt will need close follow-up with outpatient psychiatrist, Dr. ___ can be reached at ___ 4. Pt will need to continue hemodialysis on ___, and ___ #CODE: presumed full #CONTACT: HCP: Sister ___ home: ___ cell: ___ social worker: ___ office: ___
summary: You were admitted because of missing several sessions of dialysis and not taking your home medication including medication which helped treat your schizoaffective disorder. Because you missed your medications, you developed symptoms of hallucinations and delusions and thoughts of hurting yourself. During your admission we restarted your medications which improved your symptoms significantly. Also during admission you were evaluated by the psychiatry service who recommended admission to inpatient psychiatry for further management. In addition, you underwent dialysis on your second day of admission. During the dialysis session your blood pressure became low and you were given IV fluids. You continued to have low blood pressure after dialysis which was corrected with more IV fluids. Your blood pressure improved prior to discharge.

Pt noted to have lumbar compression fracture of unclear duration, head CT negative. . # FALLS AND LUMBAR FRACTURE AND NEUROPATHIC PAIN: Pt with concerning presentation, unable to support own weight. Neuro exam does not demonstrate motor or sensory findings. Pt does have osteopenia, lumbar fracture (unclear if acute) and also decreased range of motion of neck. Cardiac causes for falls unlikely. Pt does have e/o ischemic changes on head CT, but no e/o active bleed. No history of syncope or pre-syncope, mostly appears ___ weakness. Pt has chronic urinary incontinence but no loss of bowel control. No e/o lytic lesions on x-ray of lumbar spine. Pt does not appear to have any clear altered mental status. B12 and folate recently normal. Hip/femur/knee/thoracic/cervical x-rays were negative for fracture. L1 compression fracture noted on x-ray and CT scan demonstarted severe canal stenosis. She was started on calcium and vitamin D. She was started on pain control with diclofenac, tylenol, lidocaine patch and tramadol/oxycodone(prn). She c/o neuropathic pain in bilateral thighs. She will be started on neurontin 300mg at bedtime and this dose can be uptitrated. Physical therapy recommended rehab placement. . # HYPONATREMIA: new since checked ___. Likely ___ poor PO intake, pt appeared hypovolemic. With h/o cancer and gammaglobulinopathy, SIADH could be considered. Her sodium improved well with normal saline. She had no evidence of altered mental status. . # FAILURE TO THRIVE and NUTRITION: concerning for recurrent neoplasm. Source could be colonic, s/p adenoCA with resection. Pt did not have surveillance colonoscopy in ___ as planned. Pt also with symptoms of constipation (chronic) but no melena or BRBPR. She c/o "fullness" sensation with eating and recent dry heaves. No EGD in past. She has a history of monoclonal gammopathy of unclear significance and has declined hematology consultation in past. Negative mammography one year ago. Pt has h/o GERD but not on medications. She also has significant history of depression and her medications were recently changed. Nutrition was consulted and recommended ensure supplements. She also received a CT scan of the abdomen and pelvis to screen for new masses and this showed persistent post-surgical seroma, but no other concerning findings. Her albumin was 3.6. She will need a colonoscopy as outpatient. Depression appears to be the most significant cause of her presentation. . # DEPRESSION/ANXIETY: appears significant. Recently changed antidepressant. Also with panic attacks. Continued ativan QHS, prescribed for restless leg. ___ increase PRN but with caution. Pt also has h/o sundowning while hospitalized. Her citalopram dose was increased. She will need outpatient psychotherapy, psyche evaluation. . # H/O COLON CA and CONSTIPATION: due for surveillance colonoscopy. Failure to thrive and weight loss concerning for recurrence. Obtained ___ CT that showed no clear new masses or nodes. She needs to be on an aggressive bowel regimen. Outpatient colonoscopy recommended. . # PERNICIOUS ANEMIA: on monthly B12. Hct ___ high ___ to ___. . # HYPERTENSION: continue lisinopril. . # MONOCLONAL GAMMOPATHY: unclear significance. Has been stable. Pt has declined work-up in past. . # CONTACT: son ___ ___ daughter-in-law ___ (active in pt's care) ___ (h); ___ (w)
summary: You were admitted with multiple falls. You had a compression fracture of your lumbar spine. You have narrowing of your spine in the lower back region that may be accounting for your pain. You were started on pain control medications. You were seen by physical therapy who recommended rehabilitation. Other x-rays of your bones did not show any fractures. You also have lost a lot of weight. You have a history of cancer. Therefore an abdominal cat scan was done to see if there are any new changes. It showed some post-operative changes and narrowing of your spinal canal. You will need a colonoscopy as an outpatient. Your depression also appears to strongly influence your nutritional state.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The following problems were addressed during this admission: 1. Atypical chest pain. The patient's lack of cardiac risk factors, negative cardiac enzymes and negative EKG makes cardiac etiology unlikely. Given reproducible chest pain on physical exam, clear CXR and pleuritic nature of pain, costochondritis is likely, possibly secondary to post-viral syndrome. Anxiety may also play a role in this CP; SOB experienced by patient on ___ ___ may be related to anxiety given that it occurred at rest and resolved with deep inspiration. During this admission, the patient's chest pain has been well-controlled on Tylenol and NSAIDs. Will recommend OTC analgesics until pain resolves. 2. Depression/anxiety. The patient's report of worsening depression since beginning Lexapro and Xanax on ___ is concerning for the risk of self-harm. Currently patient reports thoughts of death but no active suicidal ideation or plan, and states that she would seek help if such thoughts occurred. She also reports symptoms during this admission consistent with panic attacks, such as rapid breathing, awareness of heart beat, nausea, lightheadedness and sense of "fear." She acknowledges that these symptoms are likely related to anxiety and seems open to the idea of treatment for possible panic disorder. Patient was seen by social work and psychiatry during this admission, who noted that she may benefit from a regular mental health provider. She was provided with contact information and the names of psychiatrists and social workers whom she can call to arrange follow up. In addition, the psychiatry team felt that she would benefit from more effective treatment of her insomnia, as (per her report) this lays at the heart of some of her issues with anxiety and general stress. Therefore, she was discharged with a prescription for Ambien (14 days).
summary: You were admitted to ___ with a complaint of chest pain. An EKG and blood tests showed that you did not have a heart attack. A chest x-ray showed clear lungs with no signs of infection. Your physical exam demonstrated clear point tenderness over the place where three of your ribs meet your sternum. This finding indicates a condition called costochondritis is likely causing your chest pain. This condition will resolve on its own with time; in the meantime, we recommend pain control with over-the-counter medications such as Tylenol (acetominophen) and ibuprofen. Please read the labels of these medications and use only the appropriate doses as liver or kidney damage can occur with over-use. Additionally, when you were here we recognized symptoms of anxiety. This is the likely cause of your nausea, rapid breathing, strong heart beat and sense of fear.
