The patient's lack of cardiac risk factors, negative cardiac enzymes and negative EKG makes cardiac etiology unlikely. Given reproducible chest pain on physical exam, clear CXR and pleuritic nature of pain, costochondritis is likely, possibly secondary to post-viral syndrome. Anxiety may also play a role in this CP; SOB experienced by patient on ___ ___ may be related to anxiety given that it occurred at rest and resolved with deep inspiration. During this admission, the patient's chest pain has been well-controlled on Tylenol and NSAIDs. Will recommend OTC analgesics until pain resolves. 2. Depression/anxiety. The patient's report of worsening depression since beginning Lexapro and Xanax on ___ is concerning for the risk of self-harm. Currently patient reports thoughts of death but no active suicidal ideation or plan, and states that she would seek help if such thoughts occurred. She also reports symptoms during this admission consistent with panic attacks, such as rapid breathing, awareness of heart beat, nausea, lightheadedness and sense of "fear." She acknowledges that these symptoms are likely related to anxiety and seems open to the idea of treatment for possible panic disorder. Patient was seen by social work and psychiatry during this admission, who noted that she may benefit from a regular mental health provider. She was provided with contact information and the names of psychiatrists and social workers whom she can call to arrange follow up. In addition, the psychiatry team felt that she would benefit from more effective treatment of her insomnia, as (per her report) this lays at the heart of some of her issues with anxiety and general stress. Therefore, she was discharged with a prescription for Ambien (14 days).

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with depression and anxiety, history of suicide attempts, presents with suicidal ideation and dizziness. # Suidical ideation - Due to depression and PTSD. Psychiatry concluded that patient would benefit greatly from transfer to inpatient psych when instability is better managed. Pt was placed on ___ and with 1:1. We continued home sertraline 150 daily and alprazolam bid prn anxiety. We held prazosin as it may be contributing to her unsteadiness. Resuming prazosin will be in the discrection of our psychiatry colleagues or pt's outpatient psychiatrist. # headache - Patient has history of migraine but has been using analgesics (oxycodone, compazine, toradol, reglan, fioricet (daily use). Thus, this is chronic daily headache d/t medication overuse with underlying migraine vs post-LP low CSF pressure headaches. We discontinued NSAIDS, narcotics and fioricet. We managed headaches with tylenol and anti-emetics. We started amitriptyline 10mg QHS for prophylaxis. We did a trial of propranolol given risk of overdose with TCA's in a suicical pt but pt's HR was in the ___ and we discontinued it. Neurology was consulted and recommended MRI to r/o low CSF pressure/leak but MRI was unremarkable. However, given orthostatic nature of headaches, neurology recommended pain anesthesia consult for blood patch. Pain anesthesia concluded that blood patch is not urgently indicated and addressing underlying psychiatric issues has priority as her symptoms may very likely be functional/somatic. Thus, pt was transferred to psychiatric unit for further management. We recommend that neurology continues to follow up to assess and make further evaluations for headaches/unsteadiness. Headaches d/t low CSF pressure and medication overuse self resolve with time. We managed pt conservatively with aggressive hydration and caffeine intake. # Instability - The patient complains of lightheadedness, "rocking boat", on standing with gait instability. No nystagmus on exam. Onset of these symptoms appear to coincide with her recent hospitalization for viral illness and may be a consequence of post-LP headache (see discussion above). Presentation and exam indicate functional etiology vs. peripheral neurititis given history of viral illness about a month ago vs. LP complication. Pt was evaluated by ___ who cleared pt and recommended using a walker for ambulation. All possible etiologies mentioned above self resolve with time. We discontinued prazosin as it may be contributing to her orthostatic symptoms. Orthostatics are negative for orthostatic hypotension; however, pt is symptomatically orthostatic with ambulation. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [] prazosin was held throughout hospital course on medical floor as it may be contributing/causing her unsteadiness. Resuming it will be in the discretion of our psychiatric colleagues for nightmares. [] neurology follow up while admitted on inpatient psych for headache/dizziness [] pt on amitriptyline 10mg QHS for headache prophylaxis. Given overdose potential in patients with SI, we recommend re-evaluation about dosing and administration
summary: You presented to us with suicidal ideation as well as headache and unsteadiness. We consulted our psychiatry colleagues who recommended inpatient admission to monitor you closely and ensure your safety. We also consulted our neurology colleagues for your headaches/unsteadiness. We believe your headaches are most likely due to pain medication overuse and/or low cerebral spinal fluid pressure. We performed an MRI and it was negative for any evidence of low CSF pressure. We stopped your analgesics and started you on a medication called amitriptyline for prophylaxis. With medication overuse headaches and low CSF pressure headaches, it will self resolve with time. We consulted physical therapy to evaluate your unsteadiness. We consulted pain anesthesia regarding repeating blood patch. They concluded that blood patch is not urgently indicated as MRI was negative and such headaches self resolve with time. You were further admitted to inpatient psychiatric floor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year-old female with long-standing diabetes, HTN, HL, depression, diverticulitis, who presented with ___ in the setting of poor PO intake related to ongoing dyspepsia and recent LLQ pain. . #. Abdominal pain: Her intermittent epigastric discomfort with bloating and occasional reflux were consistent with dyspepsia. In the setting of chronic poor PO intake for the last year, would also consider diabetic gastroparesis as a contributing factor, though patient is without profuse emesis. Finally, depression in older patients can present atypically; her decreased PO intake is likely related to this. As for her recent LLQ pain that brought her to the ED, the abdominal CT ruled out diverticulitis, SBO, nephrolithiasis, and hydronephroureter - she likely had a viral gastroenteritis. She was initially kept NPO with IVF, but then her diet was advanced as tolerated. By the time of discharge, she had no abdominal complaints but still had quite low PO intake. She was started on a PPI to treat symptoms of dyspepsia, and will follow up with PCP within ___ few weeks. . #. ARF: Creatinine was elevated to 1.4 in the setting of poor PO intake, likely due to prerenal ___. She was given IVF, and then was encouraged to eat by mouth. At the time of discharge, Cr was 1.2. She was instructed to drink plenty of fluids at home and she will follow up in a few days at ___ to recheck her Cr. . #. Diabetes type 2: unclear how her baseline glycemic control is. This is particularly important because if she has poor glycemic control, this could be contributing to neuropathy/gastroparesis. Also, it is significant considering her current renal impairment. On the other hand, recently she has had decreased PO intake at home, which puts her at risk for hypoglycemia. While she was in the hospital, her home antihyperglycemics were held, and she was continued on Lantus with sliding scale coverage. HbA1c was ordered and will be followed up by her PCP. . #. Hypertension: Stable on admission, she was continued on home HCTZ. Given her elevated creatinine, her Lisinopril was held. . #. Hyperlipidemia: She was continued on home Simvastatin. . #. Depression: The patient admits that her underlying depression might be contributing to her poor PO intake for the last year. She is currently not interested in seeing a therapist/___ social worker, but she will consider it. She is being continued on her home dose of Celexa. . #. 9mm pulmonary nodule: asymptomatic, incidental finding on CT. Will be followed up by her PCP.
summary: You came to the hospital with sudden belly pain. You have been eating less than normal in the last 2 months and have lost 5 pounds. We gave you some fluids in your veins and you felt better. We are not sure why you had this pain but it could be due to a condition called gastroparesis, which may be due to nerve damage from your diabetes. It may also be constipation because you haven't moved your bowels. Or it could be a consequence of the antibiotic you took a month ago for a urinary tract infection (you might have gotten another infection). When you left the hospital, however, the pain had gone away and you were able to eat and drink without pain and nausea/vomiting. We want you to take a medicine called a proton-pump inhibitor (Pantoprazole or Protonix) that may help with your belly pain. Also, during your stay, we drew your blood and one of the markers of how your kidneys are functioning, creatinine, was elevated to 1.2 which was above your baseline of 1.0. We want you to go to ___, ___ Building ___ floor (___) on ___ in the morning to get a slip to draw blood and re-check your creatinine. While you were in the emergency room, we took a picture of your belly. On this image, we found a small nodule of 9mm at the bottom of your right lung. This is probably an incidental finding but we recommend a that you get a CT scan of your chest in 3 months.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH of kidney and pancreas transplant ___, DM1, CAD s/p ___ ___, recurrent UTI's in setting of neurogenic bladder requiring self-caths, presents with abdominal pain, nausea and vomiting. # Abdominal pain/N/V: ___ be secondary to UTI given positive UA and improvement with antibiotics. Patient has had multiple UTIs in the past involving klebsiella and resistant enterococcus in the setting of her neurogenic bladder. Constellation of symptoms ___ also be suggestive of viral gastroenteritis although patient has no leukocytosis. Other possibilities include gastritis, PUD, infectious colitis, c diff. Cholecystitis, pancreatitis, hepatitis are less likely given normal LFTs and lipase. Ischemic colitis less likely with normal lactate. Patient improved with IVF, Zofran and UTI treatment with ceftriaxone. Her Nexium was replaced with ranitidine as PPIs can interact with her immunosuppression drugs. She was transitioned to cefpodoxime and will complete a 14 day course of antibiotics. # Bacterial UTI: UA grossly positive.Has history of kleb and resistant enterococcus. Patient was started on empiric treatment with ceftriaxone. On discharge she was switched to oral cefpodoxime based on culture data obtained from urine. Urine culture grew klebsiella oxycota. She will complete a 14 day course. # Metabolic acidosis: Improved after 1 dose of IV bicarb. Likely due to bicarbonate wasting given her bladder-drained pancreas exacerbated by increasing stooling. Patient has also not been tolerating bicarb pills for past few days. Patient was continued on her home oral bicarb pills. # ? Hematemesis: Patient reports 1 episode of reddish vomiting. ___ tear in setting of frequent vomiting. No further episodes. Crit remained stable. Chronic Issues: # Neurogenic bladder: Patient will need to continue straight cath BID to prevent urinary retention. # Renal and pancreas transplant: Performed in ___. Renal function was at baseline during hospitalization. Transplant nephrology was consulted in house and patient was set up with outpatient follow up. She was continued on her home tacro, MMF, prednisone. # Coronary artery disease: Patient s/p NSTEMI with DES to RCA ___. EKG at baseline, trop neg. Continued on home aspirin and Prasugrel. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued atorvastatin # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine # Peripheral neuropathy: Continued gabapentin # Obstructive sleep apnea: Patient uses special machine at home that delivers PEEP 10 and pressure support that flexes between ___. Used BiPAP while in hospital in the past but was unable to tolerate mask on this admission so was on nasal cannula while sleeping. Transitional Issues: - pt instructed to urinate every 3hrs and straight cath twice daily - pt instructed to call transplant to make appointment for follow up - was discharged with minimal amounts of clonazepam due to worsening anxiety and depression since discontinuation of her Celexa. This should be addressed as an outpatient and either Celexa should be re-started or a new medication should be started.
summary: You were admitted for abdominal pain, nausea, and vomiting which ___ have been related to a urinary tract infection. You were treated with fluids, nausea medications and antibiotics and your symptoms improved. Your bicarbonate level was also very low so this was supplemented with IV bicarbonate. Please continue to straight cath yourself two times a day. You should urinate every ___ hours when you are awake. This is because when you retain urine in your bladder from not emptying it completely, you are predisposed to getting a urinary tract infection. You were given a small amount of anxiety medication to help you during your stay in the hospital. You should talk with your primary care doctor and kidney doctor about re-starting a medication in the long term for your mood.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with past medical history of dermatitis but with otherwise no past psychiatric or neurological history presenting following an episode of sudden behavioral arrest. Patient initially was brought to the emergency department at ___ by EMS following an episode of unresponsiveness to voice or touch, but without rhythmic movements of the eyes, face, or limbs. While in the emergency department, she remained in this state but would resist examination, and was unable to cooperate with a CT scan. She was given a total of 4 mg IM lorazepam and 10 mg IM haloperidol in the emergency room and was minimally reactive and unable to provide history or participate in physical exam upon initial consultation. Patient was subsequently hospitalized for further evaluation. A non-contrast head CT showed slightly enlarged ventricles but was otherwise unremarkable. CXR was unremarkable. Laboratory studies did not demonstrate any toxic, metabolic, or infectious cause. The next morning, the patient was alert and oriented to person, place, month and year, and situation. Her sister and the patient both reported that she was back to her neurological baseline. Patient's physical exam was non-focal, and her laboratory studies continued to be unremarkable. cvEEG showed a normal background and no epileptiform activity. MRI w/wo contrast did not show acute intracranial abnoramility or other structural or vascular cause of her presenting symptoms. Patient did acknowledge recent depressive symptoms due to considerable recent stress financially and physically at work, for which a social work consult was obtained. She denies desire to self harm, suicidal ideation, homicidal ideation, and audiovisual hallucinations. Upon further discussion with social work, it was discovered she has experienced significant past trauma and non-consensual sexual encounters. Social work is assisting on setting patient up with further resources including trauma resources, therapy at the ___ Violence Prevention, Therapy for Dissociation, Support Groups and possibly psychiatry Differential diagnosis for the patient's symptoms initially included non-convulsive status or complex partial seizures with postictal state, limbic encephalitis, psychogenic non-epileptic seizure. However, given the patient's normal cvEEG without evidence of epileptiform discharges and her unremarkable toxic, metabolic, infectious, and structural findings on our thorough work-up, it appears that a provoked or unprovoked seizure is unlikely in this patient. Moreover, given the patient's rapid improvement the next day, limbic encephalitis is unlikely. It is likely that the patient's recent social and physical stressors are underlying her current presentation. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: =============================================== - Follow up with PCP ___ 1 week after discharge - Will need monitoring of mood and safety - Social work following and looking to establish further resources including trauma resources, therapy at the ___ Violence Prevention, Therapy for Dissociation, Support Groups and possibly psychiatry
summary: You presented to the hospital with an episode of unresponsiveness, for which you were admitted for further evaluation. While in the hospital, you had laboratory studies, a CT scan of your head, a MRI of your head, and an EEG (which monitors the electrical activity of your brain). The CT and MRI of your brain was without acute abnormalities that would explain your symptoms. Your EEG showed normal electrical activity of your brain. Your lab work did not show a toxic metabolic or infectious source of your symptoms. Upon discharge, you expressed you felt back to your baseline. During your admission, social work was involved in finding resources to ensure that you have support and safety upon discharge. They are working to set up different resources for you including Trauma Resources and therapy at the ___ Violence Prevention. The number for the ___ Violence Prevention is ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with a history of depression with past hx of suicidal attempts (latest ___ yr ago w/ lisinopril OD) presents with multi-drug overdose, required stabilization in the surgical ICU, intubation for airway protection and transferred to medicine after acute hepatic issues had resolved. SICU COURSE: HD1 (___) patient arrived from ___ intubated. She was started on a NAC drip for hepatic protection through her time in the SICU. Per OSH report, no derangement in lytes or CBC. On arrival to ___, She had SBPs down to the 80's nd received a 2L NS bolus over 2 hours between 3 and 5 AM. Her blood pressure issues resolved, no pressors were required. She was extubated in the SICU around 9AM, she was awake, alert, quickly transitioned from face tent to nasal cannula oxygenation with good sats. Her LFTs were trended and she had no significant bump within 24 hours. On HD2 (___). The patient was stable, normal LFTs and lytes. She was seen by hepatobiliary service the prior day and felt that patient was stable for the floor by this point. MEDICINE FLOOR COURSE: Active issues: # Multi-drug overdose: Patient OD on multiple medications. Mental status improved, VSS. On initial transfer to floor she was delirious, likely secondary to continued medication effect. Her mental status soon cleared. Her LFTs never bumped and her EKG remained normal. She was maintained on a 1:1 sitter throughout her admission and seen by psychiatry. She was not allowed to leave AMA as ___ (pt did not attempt to leave). TSH, B12 normal. Neg RPR. Patient was restarted on low dose klonopin with taper managed in conjunction with psychiatry to prevent withdrawal. She was restarted on a lower dose olanzapine qhs (10mg from 20mg home dose) with PRN olanzapine for agitation/anxiety. We held her gabapentin and sertraline in the setting of her OD. Patient was medically cleared/stable for ECT prior to transfer to psych. # UTI: Patient grew pan-sensitive pseudomonas on urine culture. Was already on cefepime for VAP (see below) so was appropriately treated with ___iarrhea: Patient complained of diarrhea with 6 BMs in one day. C.diff was negative. Her diarrhea resolved without intervention after 2 days. # VAP vs Aspiration PNA: CXR was concerning for aspiration vs ventilator-associated pneumonia. On ___, she had tachypnea and labored breathing. She was afebrile throughout admission. She was started on Vanc/Cefepime/Flagyl for ___nding on ___. PICC line was placed for antibiotic administration. # HTN: While BPs were initially low in the setting of her overdose, BPs slowly became more elevated. On ___, we restarted home lisinopril and atenolol. # Arm pain: Patient is recently s/p left humerus fracture. Husband was concerned about possible re-injury in the setting of OD. X-ray during this admission didn't show new distal fracture although no prior studies available for comparison. Patient was seen by both ___ and OT. Chronic issues: # Hypothyroidism: TSH normal. Continued home levothyroxine. # GERD: Started pantoprazole in place of home lansoprazole # HLD: Held home simvastatin. # Med rec: Patient was listed as being on leucovorin at home for unclear reason. Called pt's pharmacy as PCP's office unclear of Rx. Medication was prescribed by her psychiatrist and was last prescribed in ___, therefore is actually not an active med. Transitional issues: -Medically cleared and stable for transfer to psychiatric facility -CODE: Full -EMERGENCY CONTACT: Husband ___ (cell: ___, home: ___, work: ___ -Will need significant psychiatric care as patient has previously overdosed multiple times in addition to this episode. -Patient needs continue ___ therapy for Left humerus fracture - without continued therapy she is at risk for frozen shoulder. -Patient is medically clear for ECT.
summary: You were admitted after consuming a large quantity of your medications. You required intubation in the ICU and we were very concerned that you had permanently damaged your liver. Luckily, your liver function remained normal. You were soon able to be extubated. You initially were very confused due to the medications you had taken. Your confusion gradually improved. You were seen by psychiatry throughout your admission and we adjusted your medications based on psychiatric input. You will need admission to an inpatient psychiatric facility. Additionally, you developed a pneumonia. We believe you may have aspirated while you were unconscious. We treated you with antibiotics and you improved.

Patient was medically cleared/stable for ECT prior to transfer to psych. # UTI: Patient grew pan-sensitive pseudomonas on urine culture. Was already on cefepime for VAP (see below) so was appropriately treated with ___iarrhea: Patient complained of diarrhea with 6 BMs in one day. C.diff was negative. Her diarrhea resolved without intervention after 2 days. # VAP vs Aspiration PNA: CXR was concerning for aspiration vs ventilator-associated pneumonia. On ___, she had tachypnea and labored breathing. She was afebrile throughout admission. She was started on Vanc/Cefepime/Flagyl for ___nding on ___. PICC line was placed for antibiotic administration. # HTN: While BPs were initially low in the setting of her overdose, BPs slowly became more elevated. On ___, we restarted home lisinopril and atenolol. # Arm pain: Patient is recently s/p left humerus fracture. Husband was concerned about possible re-injury in the setting of OD. X-ray during this admission didn't show new distal fracture although no prior studies available for comparison. Patient was seen by both ___ and OT. Chronic issues: # Hypothyroidism: TSH normal. Continued home levothyroxine. # GERD: Started pantoprazole in place of home lansoprazole # HLD: Held home simvastatin. # Med rec: Patient was listed as being on leucovorin at home for unclear reason. Called pt's pharmacy as PCP's office unclear of Rx. Medication was prescribed by her psychiatrist and was last prescribed in ___, therefore is actually not an active med. Transitional issues: -Medically cleared and stable for transfer to psychiatric facility -CODE: Full -EMERGENCY CONTACT: Husband ___ (cell: ___, home: ___, work: ___ -Will need significant psychiatric care as patient has previously overdosed multiple times in addition to this episode. -Patient needs continue ___ therapy for Left humerus fracture - without continued therapy she is at risk for frozen shoulder. -Patient is medically clear for ECT.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with medical history of major depressive disorder, anxiety, anorexia nervosa, restrictive/exercise type, transferred from ___ for 24 hours of zero PO intake, bradycardia and hypotension. #Hypotension On admission to the ___ ED patient was started on IV NS and her blood pressures responded nicely. NS was continued throughout day 2 of her admission and remained stable. #Bradycardia: Following IV rehydration patient had no further episodes of bradycardia into the ___ or ___. She maintained HRs in the ___ and ___ and following replenishment w/PO thiamine, K+ and multivitamins she remained clinically stable. #Anorexia Nervosa During her hospital course Ms. ___ continued to lack insight into the severity of her condition. On multiple occasions she requested suppository laxatives to relieve constipation, but refused PO stool softeners. With 1:1 sitting and observed eating consistent with ___ eating disorder protocol she became medically stable. At the time of admission and discharge all electrolytes were found to be wnl. At the time of her admission she weighed 37.9 kg. Upon discharge following our eating disorder protocol her weight was 41.5 kg. *****TRANSITION ISSUES***** 1. Continued management for eating disorder 2. Continued psychiatric evaluation and treatment
summary: You were admitted to ___ from your outpatient care facility, ___, following a 24 hour period where you were not eating and drinking which resulted in dangerously low heart rates and dehydration leading to low blood pressure. While at ___ you were treated with intravenous fluid rehydration and placed on our standardized nutrition protocol for individuals identified at being high risk for malnourishment and/or low body weights. While admitted we performed various tests on your blood to ensure you did not have any electrolyte abnormalities which can affect your heart and other organs to function properly. You were given multivitamins and were encouraged to eat a diet compliant with your request for gluten free meals. You required little additional medical management. Following a short duration of, your heart rates and blood pressures returned to safe ranges. At this point we considered you medically stable to discharge back to your care facility, ___. We gave them instructions that you should continue a gluten free diet as it exacerbates your IBS.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o M w/ h/o DM, HTN, Hyperlipidemia, and depression presents with 65lb weight loss, and pain with eating. . #. Weight Loss/Anorexia: Most likely patient is suffering from depression leading to poor appetitie. Patient's review of systems is consistent with depression. He endorses depressed mood, lack of energy and interest in activities, decreased appetite, insomnia, guilt, psychomotor agitation. He denies suicidal ideation. The patient reports a history of severe depression leading to weight loss in the past. It is uncertain how recent this weight loss is. Since ___, in OMR we have a documented weight loss of 45lbs. The PCP's office records show a 6 lb weight loss in the past month. The patient reports symptoms of fullness and early satiety. He denied abdominal pain associated with eating or at any time. The patinet did not have any significant lab abnormalities. In any elderly patient with weight loss, early satiety and decreased appetite must consider malignancy. The patient denied B-symptoms. The patient and his wife both reported that the patient had undergone a colonoscopy in the last ___ years and that they were told it was normal. I recommend that if the patient has not had a colonoscopy, that he have one done. The patient's prostate exam was slightly enlarged consistent with BPH with a mildly elevated PSA. We recommend that the patient compare this PSA with prior prostate cancer screening and follow up as appropriate. A CT scan of the torso was negative for obvious masses or malignancy. The patient had a gastric emptying study to evaluate for gastroparesis which was negative. The patient was not malnourished by exam or by laboratory analysis. The patient was tolerating POs and was discharged with close PCP ___. The PCP had referred the patient to a mental health provider and we encouraged the patient to follow up with that provider. . #. Depression: We continued the patient on his home dose of sertraline and trazadone. He may benefit from the addition of remeron to help promote sleep and improve appetite. . #. Constipation: It was unclear what role this plays in patients poor PO intake. The patient was given a bowel regimen with fiber, colace and senna with resolution of his constipation. . #. Renal Failure, acute likely: The patient had a previously normal Cr several years ago. His Creatinine improved with IVF. Unimpressive Ua would argue for either pre-renal or post-renal etiology. The patient's U/A did show Proteinuria; we recommend that he continue ACE inhibitor for diabetic proteinuria . # Hypertension: On admission the patient had a hypertensive urgency requiring IV anti-hypertensive to control his BP. BP medications were confirmed by reviewing his medication bottles which his wife brought in. However, once his home medications/doppler. The patient was only mildly symptomatica and the were restarted the patient had a drop in his blood pressure to 70/doppler. This resolved with holding the patient's BP medications and IV fluids. The patient did not have any evidence of infection or other cause for his hypotension. The patient's terazosin dose was decreased, his lisinopril dose was decreased and his diltiazem was discontinued. The patient will require close blood pressure monitoring . #. Atrial Fibrillation: The patient was in sinus rhythm on admission. The patient states that he is s/p cardioversion and has not had to take coumadin since his cardioversion. We recommended that he discuss the utility of further anticoagulation with his PCP. . #. Diabetes: Diet controlled. His fingersticks ranged between 100-___ontrolled. . #. Hyperlipidemia: Continued patient's statin. . #. Gout: chronic. renally dose allopurinol & indomethacin PRN . #. BPH: continue home medications. . EMERGENCY CONTACT: Wife: ___: ___
summary: You were admitted for concern over a 50lb weight loss over the past couple of months. You were evaluated with lab tests, a gastric emptying study and a CT scans of your torso. The final report of the gastric emptying study is still pending but the preliminary report is negative for poor stomach motility (gastroparesis). We did not find any medical cause for your weight loss. You were not malnourished. You were tolerating food and fluid prior to discharge. We feel that your loss of appetite was most likely related to depression. We recommend that you see a psychiatrist as an outpatient. During this admission, you had an episode of low blood pressure so we held you blood pressure medications. We have restarted these medications at a lower dose.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ man with past medical history or hypertension, depression, chronic back pain, and chronic opiate use, who has felt unwell for a few months. He complains of pressured behind left ear for which he was treated with antibiotics for possible ear infection. He reports a prior Negative cardiac workup and prior MRI/A brain and MR neck negative by report in ___ except C4-6 spondylosis. On day of admission he developed paranoia and agitation and then had 2 "GTC seizures" involving "whole body shaking" (began with stuttering after being asked about medication complete). He was loaded with Keppra at the outside hospital, changed to valproic acid, and discontinued on day of admission. Patient also reported to have residual right-sided weakness and new bifacial tingling since ___, as well as hand and feet tingling for several weeks (B12 439 this admission). On suboxone; formerly chronically on MSO4 for pain. Had witnessed episode here of variable, asynchronous bilateral movements while still speaking and following commands, consistent with a non-epileptic event. Several episodes of involuntary leg convulsions, facial and extremity paresthesiae, and cognitive disturbance were captured on EEG without correlate. Traumatic LP showed 3 wbcs (1poly, 47 lymphs, 7 monos, 45 macroph), 1 rbc, protein 71, glucose 64. MRI +/- gad shows no acute pathology. Exam today remains stable without involuntary movements. Likely functional non-epileptic episodes. He reports considerable stress. Discussed this with patient and (by phone) with his wife. Referred to ___ website, and recommended counseling/psychiatry follow up. No AEDs. Still requiring walker to ambulate, which will require adiditional physical therapy. Tranitional Issues: [] Please call ___ to schedule a follow up neurology appointment at ___. [] Please follow up with your primary care provider, and have him ___ with your thrombocytopenia, psychiatry/psychology ___, and neurology ___. Please have your primary care provider ___ with your CSF results but are still pending including your autoimmune encephalitis panel as well as your HSV PCR. Please have your primary care provider continue to refer you to physical therapy.
summary: You were hospitalized for episodes of convulsions at home and en route to the hospital. Your work-up included a lumbar puncture which did not show any CSF abnormalities (suggestive of brain infection or inflammation); MRI imaging of your brain which showed no new abnormalities. Lastly you completed 24 hours of EEG which captured several episodes of convulsions, thinking difficulty, and tingling which were not seizures. Instead, it is likely that these symptoms reflect a functional neurological disorder (FND), which may be tied to your recent stressors. We are not making any changes to your medications at this time. Please ___ with your primary care provider, to set up local neurology and psychiatry ___ closer to where you live. For further education, please visit neurosymptoms.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old M with hypertension, diverticulosis with recent bleed, and recent CDiff colitis admitted hyponatremia. History of labs suggestive of combined SIADH and hypolvolumia # Hyponatremia: Based on history and labs there is components of both SIADH and hypovolumia. On admission definitely dry and Na rapidly improved with IVFs. However Na remained low, though improved to 129 from 120 on admission, despite additional IVFs and pRBCs. While he did have some mild pleural effusions on CXR and trace ___ on exam he did not appear volume overloaded and improvement in NA with IVFs without worsening clinical status suggests against hypervolumia. Last TTE relatively normal ___. No masses on CXR or recent CT A/P to suggest malignancy related SIADH. However, on admission Urine lytes with high UNa and UOsm elevated suggests SIADH a component as well. Potentially this is related to Prozac discharged on a reduced dose of 10mg with plan to potentially continue weaning and discontinue altogether in the outpatient setting. TSH and Cortisol checked during admission and were both normal. # C. difficile colitis: He received ___ days of treatment for a planned 15 day treatment course with resultant resolution in diarrhea and normal stools during admission so Flagyl discontinued. No diarrheal symptoms during admission. # Anemia: He had anemia which was attributed to prior hospitalization with GI bleed. However, his retic count was suppressed and he had low iron and ferritin all consistent with iron deficiency anemia. He was started on ferrous sulfate supplementation and a bowel regimen. Transfused with 1 unit pRBCs ___ with appropriate bump in Hct. He will need ongoing close outpatient follow up to ensure improvement. He had no evidence of continued GI bleed during this admission. # Thrombocytosis: Likely secondary to iron deficiency anemia. No evidence of thrombosis. # Depression: Long standing history of severe depression requiring multiple inpatient admissions to ___ for depressive symptoms. Continued Fluoxetine as above but reduced dose to 10mg given likely SIADH. Potential the patient may not tolerate a lower dose and so needs continued close follow up. Would consider discontinuing altogether eventually though if patient can be managed with fluid restriction alone he may benefit from remaining on the medication. # HTN: He was continued on his home lisinopril. Transitional issues: - Likely SIADH related to Fluoxetine. Discharged on half the home dose, recommend continuing to taper and eventually discontinue. - 1.5L fluid restriction - As previously the patient has an ascending colon polyp seen during recent admission, recommend repeat colonoscopy, the patient has appointments already scheduled for this - Recommend repeat Lytes and CBC at next outpatient visit
summary: It was a pleasue treating you during this hospitalization. You were admitted with dehydration and low sodium levels. This was initially thought to be due to poor food intake over the last week. You were treated with IV fluids and diet modifications with improvement in your sodium levels. However, you were also found to have a condition called "SIADH" which results in excessive water causing a low Na. It is important that you not drink more than 1.5L of fluid per day. This is the equivalent of 3 bottles of water per day MAXIMUM. You should also reduce the dose of Fluoxetine to 10 mg because this is likely causing SIADH.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female history of Type I DM (no longer on insulin) s/p kidney/pancreas transplant with altered mental status. . # Altered mental status- suspect ___ benzodiazepine overdose. Urine tox (+) for benzos. Patient has clorazepate, a long-acting benzo, on a recent med list. Head CT (-) for ICH. No recent symptoms concerning for meningitis. No evidence of cardiac ischemia. No improvement in field with D50 or Narcan. Unclear if this was an intent to harm self; patient does have h/o depression per life partner (not husband). In the ICU, patient's airway was monitored. She never required endotracheal intubation. No hypercarbia. After 48 hours, mental status began to improve. Culture data negative to date. FSBGs were also closely monitored. Prior to improvement in somnolence, health care proxy was declining lumbar puncture. Patient was transferred to floor on ___. Then became much more interactive, back to baseline. . # Type I DM s/p kidney/pancreas transplant- creatinine 2.8, slightly above baseline of ~2.5. Patient had adequate urine output, and creatinine improved to 1.8 upon transfer. Her creatinine fluctuated between 1.8 and 2.4 based largely on what her fluid intake was. Her decrease in renal function appeared to be dehydration. Creatinine has been stable prior to discharge. Will need monthly cyclosporine levels (inhouse, appropriate levels). Next level to be checked on ___ and sent to Dr. ___. . # Depression, likely Suicidal Ideation - High suspicion for overdose/intent to harm, with well thought out plan. As mental status improved, patient had 1:1 sitter. On the floor, Dr. ___ from the Psychiatry service had built a great report with the patient and offered her anti-depressant therapy, which she refused. Once she reached the floor, she remained extremely depressed, though more interactive. Her HCP and her were initially very resistant to psychiatry, and became more accepting of it as her admission went on. She was felt to not be acutely suicidal, and psychiatry felt she could safely be discharged to an intensive psych day program. . # Elevated CK - likely ___ benzo overdose. trop (-). will give D5LR and D5NS overnight at 125 cc/hr. statin overdose less likely. CK peaked around 750. Her CK trended down to normal upon discharge. . # HTN - Anti-hypertensives have been restarted. Orthostatic hypotension is likely due to chronic dysautonomia, known to the patient. . # HL - Statin was initially held, then restarted with resolved CK. . # Deconditioning: Patient felt by our physical therapists to be deconditioned, but she was still able to walk to and from wheelchair/bathroom. Patient and husband refusing ___. She was given an outpatient ___ script upon discharge, and this should be discussed again with PCP. . #Communication: Ed, partner and HCP, ___ ___ on Admission: Codeine unknown dose, not sure if active med Hydrocodone unknown dose, not sure if active med Doxazosin 1 mg tab PRN clorazepate unknown dose Oxycodone unknown dose, not sure if active med ranitidine 150 mg BID warfarin 5 mg daily cyclosporine 75 mg BID Cosopt 0.5% to 2% BID OD prednisolone 1% drops 1 gtt ___ prednisone 5 mg daily pravastatin 10 mg QHS estradiol 0.01% cream QHS
summary: You were admitted to ___ after taking too many of your prescription medications. You were monitored in the ICU and had imaging tests done to make sure there wasn't any trauma to your brain, and it was normal. You were given medications to help with symptoms from the medication that you took. You were given fluid to help your kidney function which improved during admission. Once you were stable you were transferred to the general medical floor. You were monitored for multiple days and your lab values were stable, and your kidney function was better than baseline. You had some dizziness and unsteadiness on your feet, but this is a chronic problem for you. On discharge, you were medically stable.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Hospital course by system: NEURO: The patient was admitted and initially started on phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis. EEG revealed very frequent seizures, suggestive of juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, and the patient was therefore switched to valproic acid for seizure prophylaxis. As the level was increased, she was also given standing ativan to decrease seizure occurrence. However, she developed hyperammonemia with encephalopathy and was therefore switched to Keppra and received IVF, with gradually improved mental status and seizure control. Creatine was sent and is pending at the time of discharge. Ativan was able to be tapered off prior to discharge. PSYCH: The patient endorsed chronic suicidal ideation throughout her hospitalization, though she did not report having a plan in the hospital. A sitter was present in the room at all times, and she was followed by psychiatry. The clozaril dose was weaned off per psychiatry recommendation, since this can decrease seizure threshold. Haldol was held, since this can also decrease seizure threshold; benztropine was therefore discontinued as well. Lithium was also discontinued due to persistent myoclonic seizures; the seizures improved after this. The only standing psychiatric medication that the patient continued to receive on the neurology service was venlafaxine, with Clonazepam PRN behavioral difficulty and Trazodone PRN sleep. Psychiatry recommended also using Seroquel PRN if needed. Psychiatry recommended restarting other psychiatric medications if necessary once the patient had been transferred back to a psychiatric facility. Of note, Keppra can cause behavioral changes; please initiate discussion with the epilepsy department (Dr. ___ at ___ if this medication needs to be changed. ENDO: The patient was initially found to have a slightly elevated TSH with normal T3 and T4, likely subclinical hypothyroidism due to lithium. The levothyroxine dose was increased from 137mcg to 150mcg daily, and the TFTs normalized within several days. However, TFTs are likely to change again now that lithium has been discontinued. Please consider rechecking thyroid function in ___ weeks. GI: After her hyperammonemia on the Depakote, the patient developed a mild transaminitis which was improving prior to discharge. CARDIOVASCULAR: The patient was taking atenolol 50mg PO daily on admission; however, this was discontinued during the hospitalization due to low heart rate (50s to ___ after receiving beta-blockers) and normal blood pressures. This could be restarted as an outpatient if necessary. Once the patient was stabilized from a neurologic point of view, the BEST team was contacted to arrange placement back at a psychiatric facility for further management of her psychiatric disease and suicidal ideation.
summary: You were admitted with seizures, which are due to a condition called Juvenile Myoclonic Epilepsy. This tendency to seizures is made worse by certain medications. You were initially started on the anti-seizure medication Dilantin and monitored by continuous EEG, which revealed frequent myoclonic seizures; you were therefore switched to the anti-seizure medication Depakote, which is better for this type of seizure. However, the level of ammonia in your blood got too high on Depakote, so you were switched to Keppra which is working well. You also received Ativan to help prevent seizures,but this was weaned off before you were discharged. Psychiatry was consulted because the clozaril and lithium had to be discontinued in order to control your seizures; they recommended restarting a psychiatric medication at the psychiatric facility. Your thyroid function tests revealed subclinical hypothyroidism, which can occur in patients taking lithium; your levothyroxine dose was increased to manage this.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Hospital course by system: NEURO: The patient was admitted and initially started on phenytoin for seizure prophylaxis. EEG revealed very frequent seizures, suggestive of juvenile myoclonic epilepsy, and the patient was therefore switched to valproic acid for seizure prophylaxis. As the level was increased, she was also given standing ativan to decrease seizure occurrence. However, she developed hyperammonemia with encephalopathy and was therefore switched to Keppra and received IVF, with gradually improved mental status and seizure control. Creatine was sent and is pending at the time of discharge. Ativan was able to be tapered off prior to discharge. PSYCH: The patient endorsed chronic suicidal ideation throughout her hospitalization, though she did not report having a plan in the hospital. A sitter was present in the room at all times, and she was followed by psychiatry. The clozaril dose was weaned off per psychiatry recommendation, since this can decrease seizure threshold. Haldol was held, since this can also decrease seizure threshold; benztropine was therefore discontinued as well. Lithium was also discontinued due to persistent myoclonic seizures; the seizures improved after this. The only standing psychiatric medication that the patient continued to receive on the neurology service was venlafaxine, with Clonazepam PRN behavioral difficulty and Trazodone PRN sleep. Psychiatry recommended also using Seroquel PRN if needed. Psychiatry recommended restarting other psychiatric medications if necessary once the patient had been transferred back to a psychiatric facility. Of note, Keppra can cause behavioral changes; please initiate discussion with the epilepsy department (Dr. ___ at ___ if this medication needs to be changed. ENDO: The patient was initially found to have a slightly elevated TSH with normal T3 and T4, likely subclinical hypothyroidism due to lithium. The levothyroxine dose was increased from 137mcg to 150mcg daily, and the TFTs normalized within several days. However, TFTs are likely to change again now that lithium has been discontinued. Please consider rechecking thyroid function in ___ weeks. GI: After her hyperammonemia on the Depakote, the patient developed a mild transaminitis which was improving prior to discharge. CARDIOVASCULAR: The patient was taking atenolol 50mg PO daily on admission; however, this was discontinued during the hospitalization due to low heart rate (50s to ___ after receiving beta-blockers) and normal blood pressures. This could be restarted as an outpatient if necessary. Once the patient was stabilized from a neurologic point of view, the BEST team was contacted to arrange placement back at a psychiatric facility for further management of her psychiatric disease and suicidal ideation.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with a history of sickle cell anemia and a prior right parietal IPH with resultant epilepsy, who presented with episodes of confusion and bizarre behavior, right sided face/arm twitching, and staring spells in the setting of recent medication noncompliance. He also endorsed severe depression. # Confusion: He was monitored on EEG which did not show seizure activity. On exam is inattentive in the setting of severe depression which may account for his episodes of confusion, but his regular antiseizure medications were also restarted on admission. He was seen by psychiatry who felt he would benefit from elective inpatient psychiatric admission for stabilization of mood, particularly given his lack of resources outpatient (homeless, jobless). He had not been taking sertraline because of GU side effects; one dose was given while inpatient that resulted in nighttime incontinence and it was discontinued. He was started on low dose citalopram which was titrated up to 30mg daily prior to discharge. He continued his home vimpat 200mg BID. His zonisamide was changed to 100mg BID to ___ qhs for ease of dosing. # Sickle Cell Anemia: Has chronic hemolysis with mildly elevated retic and LDH and low haptoglobin. However his hematocrit is stable and at baseline. He complains of no pain crisis symptoms. Continued his home sickle cell pain control regimen with morphine with oxy prn. # C.Diff carrier: He has a history of C. diff infection, treated multiple months ago. He had no diarrhea at admission, no fever, and no leukocytosis. He had some loose stools after his constipation was treated with 4 stool softeners/laxitives. After holding the stool softeners, he had no further diarrhea. He was not treated with antibiotics as he was felt to be an asymptomatic carrier. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -HCP: Girlfriend ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. FoLIC Acid 5 mg PO DAILY 2. Gabapentin 900 mg PO BID 3. Hydroxyurea 500 mg PO BID 4. LACOSamide 200 mg PO BID 5. Morphine SR (MS ___ 15 mg PO Q12H:PRN sickle cell pain crisis 6. OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) 10 mg PO Q6H:PRN sickle cell pain crisis 7. Zonisamide 100 mg PO BID
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with episodes of confusion. We monitored you on EEG and did not see any seizures. We also started you on your regular antiseizure medications which you were not taking consistently at home. You discussed with us that you were very depressed and we think your episodes of confusion may be related to your depression. We started you on a new medication for your depression called Citalopram. You will be referred to an intensive outpatient therapy program for treatment of your depression and will follow up with Dr. ___ ___ psychiatry. You had a repeat C. diff test which indicated that you are still a carrier of C.diff. You do not have signs of infection so do not require treatment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ gentleman with EtOH abuse s/p recent detox at ___ (last drink 5 days ago) who presented s/p fall with subgaleal hematoma as well as one episode of BRBPR. His Hct was 10 points below baseline so he was admitted and observed; he had no more episodes of bloody stools so he was discharged to an alcohol treatment program. ACTIVE ISSUES #. BRBPR: likely represents LGIB, now resolved. One episode of bloody stools, though Hct is 10 points less than baseline ~1 month ago. Though he is an EtOH user, it is unclear if he has cirrhosis/varices - but it is reassuring that he has no elevated BUN/Cr or thrombocytosis or instability or melena to suggest upper GI source. ___ is ___ (depending on whether he has liver disease from EtOH/HCV). Likely LGIB (hemorrhoids vs AVM vs diverticulosis). He remained hemodynamically stable throughout his stay. He was initially put on clear liquid diet and was observed overnight. He had no episodes of bleeding in the hospital and his Hct remained stable. He will benefit from an outpatient colonoscopy, especially in the setting of iron-defciency anemia (see below). he will follow-up with his PCP to arrange colonoscopy. #. Headache: s/p fall with subgaleal hematoma. He was kept on Tylenol and Tramadol as needed for pain. Did receive a few doses of Morphine but was able to be discharged off opiate pain meds. #. Iron deficiency anemia: low Hct, low ferritin. He was started on iron supplements as well as a stool softener to prevent constipation. He will follow-up with his PCP in order to arrange colonoscopy. #. EtOH abuse: w/recent detox, no tremulousness. He was started on Diazepam PRN CIWA>10 but he did not require any doses so CIWA was discontinued. At the time of discharge, he felt unsafe with regards to his alcohol abuse. He is a high risk of relapse. It was felt that he was unsafe for discharge to a shelter so he was evaluated by a Psychiatry team and BEST team and was placed at an alcohol treatment program. #. PTSD/Bipolar disorder: very depressed. Per paperwork from ___, he has been on Carbamazepine and Gabapentin. He was continued on these meds. He was very depressed and tearful, with passive suicidal thoughts but no suicide plan and no homicidal ideation. He was evaluated by Psychiatry. He will benefit from continued medication titration and mental health follow-up after discharge. #. Unsteady gait: alcohol neuropathy. He was noted to be slightly unsteady in his gait (complained of "room tilting") so he was evaluated by Physical Therapy. It is felt that his unsteadiness is likely related to alcohol neuropathy and is unlikely to resolve. At the time of discharge, he was ambulating steadily with a cane. He was discharged with a cane, and can follow-up with his PCP if gait instability continues to be an issue, as he may benefit from outpatient ___. INACTIVE ISSUES #. HCV: untreated. He will have outpatient f/u with his PCP. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES Code: Full (discussed with patient) Communication: Patient Emergency Contact: ___ (brother) ___ Labs pending at discharge: None
summary: You were admitted because you had a headache from your recent fall, and also you had a brown bowel movement with blood. During your stay, we made sure you did not have a head bleed. You did have a bruise on the scalp (subgaleal hematoma). You should continue Tylenol and Tramadol as needed for head/scalp pain. With regards to the blood in the stool, you were found to be anemic (blood count was low). Because of the anemia with low iron, you should take iron supplements. While the low blood level may be related to your recent fall/injuries, it could also possibly be due to bleeding from the colon, so it is very important to undergo colonoscopy. This was not done as an inpatient, as your blood count was not falling further. This can be done as an outpatient (Dr. ___ arrange this). You are being discharged from the hospital and you have plans to go back to ___ for coontinued help with alcohol abstinence. You were evaluated by Physical Therapy, and it was felt that you would benefit from a cane. Your unsteadiness seems to be due to alcohol-related neuropathy. You walked safely with a cane.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female who presented to the ED after an intentional ingestion of ativan and vicodin, and an attempt to suffocate herself. . MICU course: The patient was initially evaluated by toxicology in the ED who continued to follow her in the MICU. Her acetaminophen level was followed and trended down, as did her LFTs. She was treated supportively with IVF. Her respiratory status was followed and remained stable. She was given a 1:1 sitter, and psychiatry was consulted. They recommended standing lorazepam for benzo withdrawal and PRN lorazepam per CIWA scale. She will require inpatient psychiatric treatment once medically cleared. . General Medicine Floor Course: The patient was transferred to the general medicine floor on ___. She denied any further suicidal ideation. Her respiratory status was stable, and she did not have any evidence of airway compromise. Her mental status continued to improve. Her transaminases remained within normal limits, although her INR was slightly elevated and should be monitored as an outpatient. She was also noted to have an iron deficiency anemia, and was started on iron supplementation. Per patient's report, anemia is chronic issue, and colonoscopy done ___ years ago was negative for polyps or other pathology. HCT was stable during admission, and patient remained hemodynamically stable. HCT should be monitored as an outpatient, and patient will follow-up with new PCP at ___ ___ in ME. . She did have complaints of urinary retention, however patient was able to urinate and did not have significant post-void residuals on bladder scan. She did not have dysuria and UA checked the day prior was unremarkable and not concerning for UTI. Patient's symptoms could be secondary to uterine prolapse, and issue should be addressed in outpatient setting if symptoms persist. . The patient was medically stable for transfer to inpatient psych on ___.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after an overdose of ativan and percocet pills. You were seen by the toxicology team, who felt that you should be closely monitored in the ICU. You were brought to the ICU, where you continued to improve with supportive care and IV fluids. Your liver enzymes remained normal, and your kidney function tests were also normal. You did not require any breathing support. You were also seen by the psychiatry team, who felt you would need inpatient psychiatric treatment to ensure your safety once you were medically stable. You were transferred to the general medicine floor for ongoing treatment on ___. We continued you on lorazepam to prevent you from going into withdrawal. We started you on a lidocaine patch to your back for additional pain relief. We found you have an iron deficiency anemia, and started you on an iron supplement. You should have your blood levels checked as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and Plan: This is a ___ year old female with a history of anxiety, depression, who presents with an unwitnessed fall two days ago. . # Fall: Unclear etiology especially since fall was unwitnessed however we believe the fall is most likely ___ orthostatic hypotension or vasovagal syncope. She has not been orthostatic while in the hospital, but we provided fluids and encouraged hydration. We were reassured that there were no events on tele, the EKG was within normal limits, and the echocardiogram showed no abnormalities. Given the fact she had head trauma during her fall, we performed a head CT which was within normal limits. . # Rib fractures: Given her significant left sided pain, a CT was performed which showed fracture of the left 6, 7 and 8 rib. Patient was able to breath comfortably but had significant pain with movement. She was given oxycodone 5mg every six hours and had moderate relief of her symptoms. She started a bowel regiment to help prevent constipation. We did feel the patient needed any acute intervention for the rib fractures. We will discharge the patient on acetaminophen. . # Tension headache: Patient's headache was most consistent with tension headache. Given the lack of neurological signs and normal head CT, and prior medical histories, migraine headache was less likely. There was also a likely component of headache from medication overuse. Of note, the patient had been taking ibuprofen on a daily basis, which probably contributed to her headache. We continued to monitor the headache and did not see evidence of new neurological symptoms that may suggest an alternative diagnosis. We were reassured by a normal head CT. . # Anxiety: Patient has significant stress/anxiety in her life, especially since loosing her job. We continued her home medications. . # Depression: The patient has prior diagnosis of major depressive disorder. Her appetite, energy, and psychomotor have been depressed. On questioning, the patient denied active suicidal or homicidal ideation. We held mirtazepine since the patient did not like the side effects.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after your fall. We evaluated you with a CT and found you had a fracture of three of your left ribs. These fractures will heal on their own, and we have provided you with pain medication to help you feel more comfortable during the healing proscess. We feel that your fall is most likely due to dehydration and we recommend drinking lots of fluid. We are reassured by normal cardiac studies including a normal EKG and a normal echocardiogram. We monitored your heart for more than 24 hours and saw no evidence of abnormal heart beats. We also performed a CT of your head since when you fell you reported hitting your head. Your CT of the head was normal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of esophageal rings/dysphagia, medication-induced tremor, severe depression with psychotic features, who presented from OSH for sensation of food in throat. He reported having pills, followed by bread and pear with a sensation of food unable to go down. Per report from his family and PCP, he has a history of severe dysphagia with multiple video swallows showing aspiration of all consistencies of food. For the week prior to admission he had had increased regurgitation of food contents. At the ___ he had a finding on telemetry concerning for a wide complex tachycardia, for which he was transferred to ___. At ___ ER he was seen by Cardiology and this was felt to represent artifact from his tremor. Due to his symptoms he underwent an EGD which showed multiple esophageal rings without a food bolus. The rings were successfully dilated. However, after the procedure Mr. ___ demonstrated severely worsened dysphagia, with gross aspiration of all consistencies of food and liquid. It was felt that he was not safe to swallow crushed pills, and the patient and his family noted this was also highly uncomfortable for him. The possibility of a short-term NG tube was discussed but the patient expressed a strong preference to avoid all feeding tubes. Goals of care were discussed, and it was felt that the patient would most want to go home with hospice services, with possibility of eating for comfort. His DNR/DNI status was reaffirmed. He was briefly treated with IV Unasyn for possible aspiration pneumonia but antibiotics were stopped on discharge in light of patient's goals of care. He also had urinary retention requiring straight catheterization and ultimately foley catheter placement prior to discharge for comfort in line with goals of care. Transitional issues; #DNR/DNI [] Management of symptoms per Hospice team [] Consider resuming oral medications if patient's swallowing improves at home and if those medications are felt to be within his goals of care (i.e. psychiatric medications if needed). >30 minutes spent on discharge planning
summary: You were admitted because ___ had a sensation of food being stuck in your throat, and we were concerned that this would worsen. We did an endoscopy and found an esophageal ring but no food stuck there. Unfortunately, after the procedure ___ had significant aspiration of food and liquids. We spoke with ___ and your family about what this means for nutrition, and we all decided that using a feeding tube was not something ___ would want. We also decided that ___ would prefer to be as comfortable as possible and return home with hospice services, which we arranged. We wish ___ the best with your return home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is a ___ woman with a past medical history of JME, anxiety and depression who presents for characterization of new events of ? tremor, ? myoclonus and ? vertigo. # LTM EEG for event Capture She was admitted to the neurology service. Admission labwork and CXR were benign. She was started on cvEEG which revealed high amplitude bursts of ___ Hz spike and wave and occasionally polyspike and wave discharges. During this admission, multiple episodes of myoclonus were captured and did not have a clear or consistent EEG correlate. Her other event of "mini tremor" appeared to be primarily a mild postural tremor. Her vertigo/lightheadedness was unable to reproduced this admission despite standing, head turning and positional maneuvers. There was some concern for possible posterior circular insufficiency to explain her symptoms and she underwent MRI/MRA which was normal. No medication changes or titrations were done during this admission. She subsequently was felt safe for discharge home. # Depression - Patient was continued on her home fluoxetine. She was seen by our Epilepsy-Psychiatry consultation service. Notably, she endorsed several times that she felt the her pharmacist may have been filling her Fluoxetine medications with placebo. She was started on Mirtazepine 7.5 QHS for further antidepressant action. This may be increased to 15mg PO QHS on an outpatient basis if well tolerated.
summary: ___ were hosptialized at ___ as part of a planned admission to better understand your new events of "mini tremor", intermittent vertigo/dizziness and twitching concerning for seizure. While hospitalized ___ underwent Lab evaluation, EEG and MRI/MRA imaging. ___ did not have any seizures on EEG. We also did not capture any of your events of dizziness. The MRI of your brain and blood vessels looked normal on our review. The radiologists will look at it as well. You were seen by Dr. ___ psychiatry who recommended starting mirtazapine 7.5mg at night to treat your mood. If ___ would like to see a psychiatrist near ___, please ask your primary care doctor for ___ referral.

text: Brief Hospital Course: She was admitted to the Acute Care Surgery team. Psychiatry was consulted for concerns over possible suicidality given the nature of her trauma. She was placed on 1:1 sitters with close follow up from Psychiatry. She did have significant pain control issues and nausea felt associated with some of the narcotics that were used initially. Several adjustments to her medications were made. Her pain is currently well controlled with standing Tramadol, Tylenol and prn Morphine Sulfate ___. Her nausea has also improved with Reglan. It was later determined by Psychiatry that she was not a suicide risk rather that she would benefit from an inpatient drug rehabilitation program on a voluntary basis. Social work was then consulted to assist with this process. A meeting with patient and her sister was arranged but patient's sister did not make the meeting and was unable to be contacted. Patient was provided with information on drug rehabilitation programs and on homeless shelters.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after a motor vehicle crash where you sustained rib fractures. Due to the nature of your trauma you were seen and evaluated by trauma surgeons, psychiatrists and social workers. Your rib fractures did not require any operations. The evaluation done by the psychiatrist and social worker determine that it is highly recommneded that you voluntarily go into a drug rehabilitation program in order to adress your substance use issues. Information on these types of programs were provided to you by the Social Worker. Rib fractures which can cause severe pain and subsequently cause you to take shallow breaths because of the pain.

She was placed on 1:1 sitters with close follow up from Psychiatry. She did have significant pain control issues and nausea felt associated with some of the narcotics that were used initially. Several adjustments to her medications were made. Her pain is currently well controlled with standing Tramadol, Tylenol and prn Morphine Sulfate ___. Her nausea has also improved with Reglan. It was later determined by Psychiatry that she was not a suicide risk rather that she would benefit from an inpatient drug rehabilitation program on a voluntary basis. Patient was provided with information on drug rehabilitation programs and on homeless shelters.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F w/ Hepatitis C Cirrhosis and CVA with UTI on bactrim and worsening symptoms of depression with suicidal ideation and substance abuse (cocaine, benzos) contributing to worsening weakness and confusion. ## Weakeness/Depression - Patient presented with weakness in context of substance abuse (last used cocaine on ___, poor self-care and poor PO intake due to depression, following her husband's death by suicide a few months ago. UTOX +Benzos +Cocaine, not scoring on CIWA scale. Patient may also be experiencing fatigue given diagnosis of urinary tract infection; however this is unlikely a large factor as patient is on antibiotics without urinary symptoms, recent fevers, elevated white blood count, or unstable vital signs. Medical work up has otherwise been unrevealing (CXR negative for pneumonia, blood cultures are thus far negative, patient without signs of hepatic encephalopathy- no asterixis, A&Ox3 - and according to children has been taking lactulose regularly). Given concern for suicidal ideation and major depressive disorder, psychiatry was consulted. Psychiatry felt patient was in a major depressive disorder, with substance abuse, and a danger to herself. ___ was put in place given SI and BEST has been consulted given patient is medically cleared. Social work was consulted and is planning to file a 51A, in contact with sister ___ ___ who is currently caring for her ___ old twins. Physical therapy was consulted and did not feel patient required physical therapy in any capacity (inpatient or outpatient). ## Uncomplicated Urinary Tract Infection - Patient was found to have an asymptomatic urinary tract infection (urine culture grew pan-sensitive e.coli, resistant only to ampicillin). She was started on Bactrim DS PO BID for a total of a 5 day course (___) and should continue full treatment course in outpatient setting. ## Measured low blood pressures - Patient has significant peripheral vascular disease, and given the known bilateral subclavian stenosis seen on CTA in ___, this is likely the cause of her low blood pressure readings. Blood pressures in her legs are normal (120-130s/70s) and patient has been afebile, and not tachycardic. Additionally, patient is completely asymptomatic (not dizzy, able to work with physical therapy). She is s/p 1L fluid bolus with no change in arm blood pressures, but improved lower extremity blood pressures, further supporting stenosis as an explanation for falsely low blood pressure. - Patient with hx of Left Subclavian Stenosis (complete), Right Subclavian 50% stenosis -> CHECK BPs ON RIGHT CALF for improved accuracy. . ## VARICES: Patient has a previous history of known upper gastrointestinal bleed in ___, however no active issues at this time. Last EGD on ___ showed no varices of the esophagus. EGD also negative for portal hypertensive gastropathy. Patient should have routine EGD, not due for many months. . ## PRODUCTIVE COUGH: ___ weeks of cough without fever or elevated WBC. CXR shows on pneumonia. Patient was monitored. Has not had productive cough while in the hospital. There was concern for aspiration given drug abuse, but speech and swallow evaluated the patient and felt she was safe for a regular diet with thin liquids. . ## Hepatitis C with CIRRHOSIS: Patient is s/p HCV treatment which was discontinued secondary to upper gastrointestinal bleed in ___. Cirrhosis labs are at baseline. We continued her home lactulose to prevent hepatic encephalopathy and monitored her bowel movements with a goal for ___ bowel movements per day. . ## Cerebral vascular accident/AMAROSIS FUGAX: Continued statin and aspirin. . Transitional Issues: # Disposition: Patient was ___ by psychiatry. She is medically cleared for inpatient psychiatry unit. Social Work will be filing a 51A. # Communication: ___ (HCP, sister) ___ # Code status: Full Code

The psychiatrists saw you and felt you were a dnager to yourself and have insisted that you be admitted to an inpatient psychiatry facility for treatment. Please take Bactrim 1 tablet twice daily for 4 days to treat your urinary tract infection. Last dose on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with ___ year history of atrial fibrillation on anticoagulation, hypertension, hypothyroid and depression presents with palpitations and presyncope, found to be in alternating atrial flutter and sinus bradycardia, with prolonged conversion pauses. # Tachy-brady arrhythmia: Pt with long history of atrial fibrillation that has since converted to atrial flutter, with episodes of atrial flutter followed by long (>4 sec) conversion pauses and sinus bradycardia. Pt's symptoms corresponded exactly to episodes of flutter (paliptation) and conversion pauses (presyncope). Pt preferred to defer ablation and try medical therapy at this time. Held nodal blockers (diltiazem, metoprolol) and flecainide to initiate 3-day dofetilide protocol while monitoring pt on telemetry. Pt less symptomatic, with fewer episodes of atrial flutter and decreased duration of conversion pauses while on dofetilide. Electrolytes remained within normal limits. - Dofetilide 500 mcg PO Q12H - rivaroxaban 20mg po daily # Chronic issues: - Hypertension: cont ___ 81 - Depression: cont alprazolam - Hypothyroid: cont levothyroxine
summary: You came in because of palpitations and feeling faint. We monitored your heart rhythm and found that you have atrial flutter, during which you feel palpitations, followed by long pauses of your heart beat, during which you would feel faint. We stopped all the anti-arrhythmic medications you were taking - the diltiazem, metoprolol and flecainide. We started you on dofetilide and monitored your heart rhythm for 3 days. The frequency of your atrial flutter and the length of your pauses decreased after we started dofetilide. You felt less symptoms. We will send you home on dofetilide. You must not take any Metoprolol Succinate or Diltiazem. As this could cause a dangerously low heart rate. Please take 3 recordings per day with your heart monitor and also record if you have symptoms. Please transmit these recordings as you were previously doing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, mild HL, possible prior DVT, osteopenia, anxiety, constipation, recent diagnosis of depression, and recent difficulty with nausea/vomiting and hyponatremia who presents with same. She also had recent dark stools with positive stool guaiac. # Gastritis, duodenitis: Pt presented with "sour stomach" that had been going on for several months. She was started on a PPI empirically, with some improvement in her symptoms. EGD revealed gastritis and duodenitis, so she was given a script for high dose PPI. On discharge she was able to tolerate a regular diet and felt much better. # Hyponatremia: This was likely related to po oral intake in the setting of gastritis, as her sodium normalized with IV fluids, and was stable for the rest of the hospitalization. There was some discussion of whether the pt's recently started SSRI had caused the hyponatremia, but this seems less likely. This was stopped, however, in favor of low-dose mirtazepine. # Nausea: Likely from gastritis. Tsh, cortisol were reassuring. # Pos stool guaiac: No anemia or bleeding was present during the hospitalization. Colonoscopy was unrevealing except for a polyp. # History of constipation: Notes comment on longstanding difficulty with constipation. She denied constipation currently, though Zofran will put her at increased risk. She was continued on a bowel regimen. # HTN: Continued home Lisinopril # Depression: She and her dtr ___ were very concerned about this. They agreed to trial Mirtazepine 7.5mg qhs. This had a good effect, with the pt reporting "excellent sleep" in the hospital. Her home At___ was continued, with the plan that as Mirtazepine starts to work hopefully she can taper off of this, since it is not an ideal geriatric medication. Adv Care Planning: HCP is dtr ___. Full code. Transitional- RECOMMENDATION(S): For incidental pulmonary GGO- Chest CT in 3 months.
summary: You were admitted with nausea and low blood sodium level. You were treated with fluids, nausea medication and you were started on a low dose depression medication (Mirtazepine) at the recommendation of your primary care doctor. You had several tests and their findings are outlined below. CAT scan of the abdomen and pelvis- incidental findings of likely left adrenal adenoma and left kidney cyst, too small to fully characterize on CT CAT scan of the chest- incidental "sub cm ground-glass lesion, most likely inflammatory, requires follow-up chest CT 3 months with the expectation that the lesion will have resolved, excluding the need for further follow-up.
