text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of depression with previous suicide attempts, rheumatoid arthritis, IBS and recent C. difficile colitis who presented to the emergency department on ___ with suicidal ideation and requesting help with her eating disorder, found to have fever to 100.6 and reported recent diarrhea, so admitted for concern for C. diff. #FEVER/#Diarrhea: Patient presented w/out localizing symptoms or leukocytosis, but new low grade fever to 100.7 in ED and reports of recurrent diarrhea, initially concerned for c. diff so started on PO vanc, but subsequently found to be c. diff neg. No episodes since admission. Patient had completed course of PO Vancomycin on ___ for recent c. diff. CXR, UA, and blood cultures negative for infection, HIV negative. Resolved prior to discharge. Fever was likely from rheumatoid arthritis and the patients fevers resolved. She was restarted on her hydroxychloroquine and her symptoms improved. #SUICIDAL IDEATION #DEPRESSION #ANXIETY - Pt reports problems with depression and eating disorder and presented seeking residential treatment but needs medical clearance. Has a long history of anxiety and depression with multiple hospitalizations for suicidal ideation/attempts. Says that she has been struggling with increasing anxiety and depression recently in the context of stress at work and recent hospitalizations for infection. She was initially safe from a psychiatric perspective. However when she was refused from the initial facility she was acutely suicidal and was placed on a ___. She code purpled (called security and psychiatry) for agitation and required chemical restraints. Psychiatry reevaluated the patient on ___ and Dr. ___ removed the ___. Spoke with patients outpatient therapist. The patient felt safe to go home with her partner and would not harm herself. #EATING DISORDER: patient reports longstanding history of eating disorder for which she has received inpatient care in the past. Describes mainly restrictive symptoms, currently eating "less than one meal per day". She estimates ___ weight loss in recent months, although her weight is stable from admission ___. BMI 20 on admission. Reports remote history of purging, but not recently. Her work up was negative. She had no hemodynamic instability, electrolyte issues or signs of dehydration. Social work was consulted and attempted to place her at ___ however she was declined. She became acutely suicidal. This resolved prior to her discharge. Her restricted intake resulted in hypoglycemia to 48 treated with glucose. Soon after she resumed eating. She was cleared by psychiatry as documented above. She was eating and drinking prior to her discharge. She requested her information be sent to ___ ___ to set up intake phone session on ___. She also had referrals made to ___ but she declined. The patient and partner felt like she had a safe disposition. She will need residential placement. Started on thiamine, MVI and folate. #RHEUMATOID ARTHRITIS: Previously in remission prior to recent flare during hospital admission. She presented s/p prednisone taper since her last flare. She had a low fever on admission, but no signs of active flare on joint exam on admission. She has been off immunosuppressive medications for several months due to her recent infections. Was seen by her rheumatologist on ___ who recommended restarting hydroxychloroquine, but she has not yet taken it due to GI issues (has caused her significant diarrhea in past). Hydroxychloroquine restarted during hospital stay. Etanercept held and should be restarted as per rheumatology discretions. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - follow up with therapist - follow up with PCP - follow up with rheumatology for RA. Hydroxycholorquine started during admission and tolerated well. Etanercept on hold for now will need to discuss with her provider - patient ___ need treatment for her eating disorder and will do intake appointments with several facilities
summary: You came to the hospital because you had fevers and diarrhea. Your fever was likely from your rheumatoid arthritis. Your diarrhea resolved. We tested you for C.diff and it was negative. You were looking to obtain medical clearance for placement at ___ for treatment of your eating disorder. You are medically cleared to go to an eating disorder facility however you were unable to get a bed at ___. You were offered alternatives but declined. You were seen by our psychiatry team that felt you were safe from a psych perspective for discharge. From a medical perspective you improved. You have requested evaluation at a residential facility for your eating disorder. Your information has been sent to those facilities.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ Graves' (s/p radioiodine ablation, now hypothyroid) and depression who was admitted with epigastic pain and vomiting. #FUNCTIONAL ABDOMINAL PAIN #FUNCTIONAL DYSPEPSIA CT was unremarkable, LFTs and lipase were normal. By history, her symptoms initially appeared most consistent with gastritis, but a she underwent EGD during her admission, which was normal. By process of elimination, she was diagnosed with functional pain. When this presumed diagnosis was discussed with the patient, she became quite emotionally distraught and told us about numerous sources of familial and professional stress in her life. We planned to treat the patient with BID pantoprazole and initiation of amitriptyline, with numerous PRNs for immediate relief while slowly advancing her diet. Unfortunately she grew frustrated with her situation and left AMA; she accepted a prescription for a PPI but declined to initiate a low-dose TCA. The patient has close follow up scheduled with her PCP. #HYPOVOLEMIA #HYPOKALEMIA #HYPOPHOSHATEMIA #ACIDOSIS The patient had numerous mild metabolic abnormalities related to poor PO intake and vomiting. She received IV fluids, and electrolyte repletion during her hospital stay. ******************* TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ******************* 1) Please follow-up on pathology results from biopsies taken during the endoscopy to rule out H pylori. 2) Please continue to discuss initiation of amitriptyline. 3) Patient may benefit from counseling or stress management modalities. 4) Please recheck BP and phos. 5) Adrenal nodule: ___ CT A/P revealed incidental 14 mm right adrenal gland nodule of indeterminate etiology, most likely an adenoma in the absence of primary malignancy. Per radiology report, a dedicated adrenal protocol CT or MR may be considered for further characterization.
summary: You were admitted after several days of burning abdominal and throat pain, nausea, vomiting, and occasional diarrhea. In the ED, you were found to be dehydrated and were not able to keep down any food or liquids. You were started on antacid medications to help decrease the acid production in your stomach and alleviate some of the burning sensation in your abdomen and chest. You refused these medications in discharge. You were seen by our Gastroenterologists and underwent an endoscopy, which did not find any structural abnormalities in your stomach. During this procedure, they took biopsies and will be in touch with those results soon. You requested to leave the hospital against medical advice, so you were discharged. We discussed the risks of the decision including dehydration and abdominal pain, and you were able to voice back this understanding.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old M w/ PMH of ___, anxiety, and HTN who was found unresponsive after intentional ativan overdose. He was initially unresponsive and somnolent but his mental status and medical condition improved significantly throughout the course of his hospitalization. #Acute toxic metabolic encephalopathy: This was secondary to intentional Ativan overdose of approximately 12 mg, ingested at unclear time on the night of ___. While initially somnolent and unresponsive, his mental status improved significantly during his hospitalization. During his early hospitalization, patient had episodes of agitation overnight and multiple therapeutic modalities were tried to manage this. Notably, the patient experienced paradoxical agitation to Haldol. He was then started on seroquel 25 mg qHS that was then increased to 50 mg qPM. Patient's nighttime agitation was also managed by decreased vital signs checks and scheduling ___ medications several hours before bedtime. Ativan and other benzodiazepines were held during this hospitalization. #Depression/PTSD s/p Intentional Overdose: Patient endorsed intentional overdose of Ativan. Trigger was distressing night terrors that acutely worsened 3 weeks prior to overdose. Discussion with patient's psychiatrist, Dr. ___, revealed that patient has never endorsed SI in the past. She sees the patient outpatient 1x per month. Patient has tried multiple antidepressants that have been unhelpful. Patient had a 1:1 sitter throughout hospital stay. Psychiatry was consulted for assistance during this hospitalization. ECT was started on the medical floor on ___ while patient awaited bed transfer to ___ for inpatient psychiatry admission. He received 5 total ECT sessions (___) with improvement in his night terors. He was offered prazosin for night terrors but patient did not want to try this. Patient and family refused voluntarily inpatient psychiatric admission and refused referral to partial day program. On day of discharge, the patient did not meet criteria for involuntary admission and he was discharged to home with family in consultation with his outpatient psychiatrist, Dr ___. Family verbalized understanding that the patient cannot be left unsupervised in light of recent suicide attempt. Patient will continue to follow with Dr. ___ outpatient psychiatry and patient will continue ECT sessions (duration/course determined by Dr. ___. #LLL Pneumonia: The atient presented with radiographic evidence of LLL infiltrate with bibasilar crackles on exam. Given history of benzodiazepine overdose, there were concerns of aspiration. He completed an 8 day PO course of cefpodoxime (___). Resolution in cough and on RA prior to discharge. #New onset AFib: Patient had an episode of AFib with RVR on ___ AM and received IV diltiazem x 1 for management. Patient was in normal sinus rhythm verified via telemetry for several days post isolated episode, thus tele was discontinued on ___. He remained in NSR for rest of admission. ___ Disease: Patient continued on regimen Carbidopa-Levodopa #HTN: Amlodipine and labetalol were continued. #Traumatic Foley Removal: Pt self removed foley during hospitalization. Urology was consulted, a foley was replaced for ~3 days to tamponade bleeding. Urology felt his urethral bleeding and hematuria was secondary to trauma to prostate. Oxybutinin was added to treat bladder spasms as needed. Patient successfully completed a void trial on ___. He was voiding well clear yellow urine prior to discharge home. ####TRANSITIONAL ISSUES#### - Medications added: QUEtiapine Fumarate 50 mg PO/NG QPM - Medications stopped: Ativan - Medications changed: carbidopa-levodopa 2 tablets TID (earlier administration of nighttime dose) - Continue titration of ECT and Seroquel to minimize PTSD symptoms - Avoid BZDs and Haldol # CODE: DNR/DNI # CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ ___ (son) ___ Daughter in law ___ ___
summary: D@ ___, You were admitted because ___ were very sleepy and unresponsive. We treated ___ for a pneumonia by giving you antibiotics and oxygen. Your pneumonia was fully treated. We worked with the neurology and psychiatry team closely. The neurology optimized the dosing of your ___ medicines. The psychiatry team were very concerned about your intentional overdose with Ativan. ___ had a "sitter" in your room who watched ___ closely and made sure ___ were safe. Psychiatry started ECT sessions during your hospitalization. Your received 5 total ECT sessions prior to discharge. Your night terrors resolved shortly after ___ started ECT and we think the ECT is helping ___. ___ and your family wanted to take ___ home today. We strongly recommend ___ remain in the hospital for voluntary inpatient psychiatry treatment. Since ___ did not want to remain in the hospital for psychiatric treatment, we recommended ___ enroll in a partial psychiatry program to help ___ as an outpatient basis. However, ___ and your family did not want this at this time. During this especially vulnerable time, we do not want ___ to be left alone. We want ___ to stay safe. It is really important that ___ follow up with your psychiatry Dr. ___ as an outpatient.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old M w/ PMH of ___, anxiety, and HTN who was found unresponsive after intentional ativan overdose. He was initially unresponsive and somnolent but his mental status and medical condition improved significantly throughout the course of his hospitalization. #Acute toxic metabolic encephalopathy: This was secondary to intentional Ativan overdose of approximately 12 mg, ingested at unclear time on the night of ___. While initially somnolent and unresponsive, his mental status improved significantly during his hospitalization. During his early hospitalization, patient had episodes of agitation overnight and multiple therapeutic modalities were tried to manage this. Notably, the patient experienced paradoxical agitation to Haldol. He was then started on seroquel 25 mg qHS that was then increased to 50 mg qPM. Patient's nighttime agitation was also managed by decreased vital signs checks and scheduling ___ medications several hours before bedtime. Ativan and other benzodiazepines were held during this hospitalization. #Depression/PTSD s/p Intentional Overdose: Patient endorsed intentional overdose of Ativan. Trigger was distressing night terrors that acutely worsened 3 weeks prior to overdose. Discussion with patient's psychiatrist, Dr. ___, revealed that patient has never endorsed SI in the past. She sees the patient outpatient 1x per month. Patient has tried multiple antidepressants that have been unhelpful. Patient had a 1:1 sitter throughout hospital stay. Psychiatry was consulted for assistance during this hospitalization. ECT was started on the medical floor on ___ while patient awaited bed transfer to ___ for inpatient psychiatry admission. He received 5 total ECT sessions (___) with improvement in his night terors. He was offered prazosin for night terrors but patient did not want to try this. Patient and family refused voluntarily inpatient psychiatric admission and refused referral to partial day program. On day of discharge, the patient did not meet criteria for involuntary admission and he was discharged to home with family in consultation with his outpatient psychiatrist, Dr ___. Family verbalized understanding that the patient cannot be left unsupervised in light of recent suicide attempt. Patient will continue to follow with Dr. ___ outpatient psychiatry and patient will continue ECT sessions (duration/course determined by Dr. ___. #LLL Pneumonia: The atient presented with radiographic evidence of LLL infiltrate with bibasilar crackles on exam. Given history of benzodiazepine overdose, there were concerns of aspiration. He completed an 8 day PO course of cefpodoxime (___). Resolution in cough and on RA prior to discharge. #New onset AFib: Patient had an episode of AFib with RVR on ___ AM and received IV diltiazem x 1 for management. Patient was in normal sinus rhythm verified via telemetry for several days post isolated episode, thus tele was discontinued on ___. He remained in NSR for rest of admission. ___ Disease: Patient continued on regimen Carbidopa-Levodopa #HTN: Amlodipine and labetalol were continued. #Traumatic Foley Removal: Pt self removed foley during hospitalization. Urology was consulted, a foley was replaced for ~3 days to tamponade bleeding. Urology felt his urethral bleeding and hematuria was secondary to trauma to prostate. Oxybutinin was added to treat bladder spasms as needed. Patient successfully completed a void trial on ___. He was voiding well clear yellow urine prior to discharge home. ####TRANSITIONAL ISSUES#### - Medications added: QUEtiapine Fumarate 50 mg PO/NG QPM - Medications stopped: Ativan - Medications changed: carbidopa-levodopa 2 tablets TID (earlier administration of nighttime dose) - Continue titration of ECT and Seroquel to minimize PTSD symptoms - Avoid BZDs and Haldol # CODE: DNR/DNI # CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ ___ (son) ___ Daughter in law ___ ___

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o male with a past medical history of dementia, ADHD, depression and anxiety who presented from his ALF with confusion, failure to thrive, and s/p fall. # Depression with SI: The patient has a long standing history of depression, requiring inpatient hospitalization and ECT. On presentation, the patient reported sadness and desire to go to sleep and not wake up. The patient was found to have flat affect with psychomotor slowing. The patient was evaluated by psychiatry who recommended 1:1 sitter and placed patient under ___. It was thought that the patient's depression may be contributing to his worsening confusion. The patient's psychiatric medication regimen was adjusted as below. The patient was discharged to an inpatient psychiatric facility and should follow up with these psychiatric providers for further titration of medication regimen and further management. # Confusion: The patient reported progressively worsening confusion, which was corroborated by his sister whom he speaks to on the phone nearly daily. The patient was evaluated with a CT head which showed no acute changes. Similarly, electrolytes, UA, Utox and serum tox were found to be within normal limits. TSH, B12 and urine culture remained pending at the time of discharge. The patient's confusion was thought to be due to his worsening neurocognitive condition (Alzheimer's disease versus vascular dementia versus mixed) vs. worsening depression vs. polypharmacy. The patient was evaluated by psychiatry who recommended discontinuation of buspar, and mirtazapine as well as reduction in duloxetine dosing. They recommended discharge to inpatient psychiatric facility at ___. The patient should f/u with psychiatric providers for further evaluation and management. # s/p fall: The patient reportedly had a fall prior to admission, in which he fell onto his lower back. Though the patient did not recall the exact circumstances of his fall, it was suspected to be mechanical in origin given his history of unsteady gait and possible peripheral neuropathy. The patient's ECG showed sinus arrhythmia and the patient reported no history of chest pain, lightheadedness or dizziness. The patient was evaluated as above and his medications were adjusted as above. CT Head, CT C-Spine and CXR did not show any acute changes or injury. The patient was evaluated by physical therapy who felt that intermittent gait disturbance was likely secondary to his underlying medical and psychiatric conditions. # DM: the patient was restarted on his home metformin and glipizide at discharge (he was managed on ISS while in the hospital) # HLD: continued home statin # CAD: continued aspirin, metoprolol # h/o EtOH use: The patient reported his last drink was years prior. He was continued on thiamine, folate, MVI Transitional Issues: - f/u with psychiatry for further management of confusion and management of medications - consider evaluation of Alzheimers as well as ___ Disease as an outpatient. - Please consider MRI as outpatient if confusion/falls persist. - Consider down titration of lorazepam as tolerated as this medication may not be ideal in the geriatric population - Contact: ___ HCP ___ ___ (sister) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of your fall and because of confusion. We evaluated you with a CT scan of your head and xrays of your chest and spine which showed no acute changes. We also evaluated your electrolytes and your blood counts which were normal. We also checked your B12 level and your thyroid function. The results of these tests are still pending. We also evaluated you with urine culture. These results are still pending as well. We believe your fall and your confusion may have happened because of the medications you are taking. We stopped your mirtazapine and your busiprone and we decreased your duloxetine.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ___ with Celiac disease, GERD, depression and restrictive eating disorder who presented with hematemesis and abdominal pain. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Hematemesis and abd pain: There was concern for ___ tear in the setting of vomiting, however EGD did not show evidence of esophageal disease. She was found to have streaky gastritis and atrophy / congestion in the duodenum. Her hematocrit remained stable during this admission, and she was treated with protonix 40mg IV, and was discharged on pantoprazole 40mg daily and ranitidine 300mg QHS. Her hematocrit remained stable during this hospitalization. # Restrictive eating disorder: She presented with poor PO intake for many days, and was found to have streaky gastritis. A NJ tube was placed ___ during EGD with the consent of her HCP. She was started on tube feeds (regimen listed below in trasitioal issues), and she was allowed to order food (with ensure supplements), however was not placed on the eating disorder protocol. She removed her NJ tube on ___ and it was replaced with fluoroscopic guidace on ___. Her admission weight was 43 kgs (94.80 lbs), and her discharge weight was 45.0kg. Importantly, she is weighed each day and she is not told her weight for therapeutic purposes. # Abdominal pain: She has frequent, persistent somatic complaints related to abdominal pain. Although she was found to have gastritis, much of these complaints are attributed to her eating disorder. She received tylenol, tramadol, and ativan. No narcotics were given. If she has further abdominal pain at her LTAC, please direct these complaints to her primary PCP ___. ___ should not be transfered to OSH for these vague persistend abdominal complaints. # Hypotension: Occasionally she complained of lightheadedness with BPs ___ requiring IVF resuscitation. Her BP was fluid responsive. Secondary to poor po intake and the high dose trazodone she received for insomnia. After discontinuation of trazodone, her BPs have improved. There was no evidence of sepsis/infection. At the time of discharge her blood pressure was stable at SBP ___. #Right arm cellulitis: she developed cellulitis / thrombophlebitis in the left arm from a peripheral IV. This IV was discontinued, and she was treated with a 7-day course of bactrim. Her cellulitis improved without complication. #Oral Herpes Reactivation: She developed an oral lesion similar to prior outbreaks. She was treated with Acyclovir (Day 1 is ___ of 5 day course). CHRONIC ISSUES: # Insomnia: A chronic issue for her. She was initially on Ativan and Trazodone, however she was switched to ambien 10mg from high dose trazodone to limit medicaiton induced hyportension. # Celiac disease: Continued gluten free diet. # Depression: Continue Venlafaxine # Leukopenia: Stable, likely in the setting of malnutrition. # Migraines: Continued sumatriptan and Fioricet as needed TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Had change of guardianship from to ___ on ___ ___ guardian ___ Cell: ___ -Patient is eager to go to eating disorder treatment center in ___ however there were no beds recently. The facility is ___ of ___ in ___. You can reach ___ in admissions at ___. - If she has further abdominal pain at her LTAC, please direct these complaints to her primary PCP ___. She should not be transfered to OSH for these vague persistent abdominal complaints. Tubefeeding Regimen: Two Cal HN Full strength with 1 packet/day Benecalorie Starting rate: 75 ml/hr with cycling (start at ___, end at 1100). Can advance rate to 90cc/hr if she tolerates. Flush w/ 150 ml water q4h -Her tube feeding amount cannot be decreased per patient request. -Would not give trazodone for insomnia given her resulting hypotension -She cannot have narcotics -She cannot be told her daily weight (therapeutic reasons)
summary: You were admitted for bloodly vomit. You underwent an upper endoscopy, which showed a normal esophagus and irritation of the stomach from acid buildup. You were treated with medications to reduce acid in the stomach (pantoprazole, ranitidine, maalox / diphenhydramine / Lidocaine). You also received a ___ tube for tube feeds, and were also allowed to eat. You were not on the eating disorder protocol during this admission. Your abdominal pain was treated with tylenol and tramadol. You received ativan for anxiety as well as sleep. Your blood pressure was occasionally low during this admission, and you received IV fluids when necessary. You also had an outbreak of oral herpes which we are treating you with acyclovir. You will continue this medication through ___. Please review the list below as we have made some changes to your medication regimen.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of panic disorder and mood disorder NOS who was referred to the ED on a ___ by her psychiatrist after confessing to a serious suicide attempt via Tylenol overdose several days prior. She reports a lifelong history of mildly depressed mood, but the recent stressors that led up to her suicide attempt are numerous: several deaths of close family members, caregiver burden, and reluctance to be emotionally vulnerable around friends or family. She has developed some psychotic symptoms as well in the past few months, with belief that her food is being poisoned at work and some odd beliefs about communicating through the television. # PSYCHIATRIC: ___ was admitted to the ___ inpatient psychiatric unit for monitoring and observation as well as treatment of her severe depression and suicidal ideation. She signed a CV on admission and was monitored with safety checks q15 minutes. On admission, she was tearful and endorsed a depressed mood which she attributed to the multiple stressors described above, but denied ongoing active suicidal ideation. Later in her course however, she described her unsuccessful suicide attempt as "another failure" and stated that she was fearful that it seemed to occur without warning - i.e., the first day she experienced suicidal ideation, she immediately decided to act on it without informing friends/family or reaching out for help first. She did have insight into her triggers, namely feeling highly overwhelmed by multiple deaths and illnesses in the family, excessive demands at work, and feeling like she needed to be "the rock" for all of her family members - they could come to her for help, but she would never feel comfortable asking them for the same. A family meeting with her husband ___ was held to discuss this (and the suicide attempt itself): he demonstrated clear sympathy and desire to help, but did not have great insight into her need for more of a "partnership" when it came to their relationship. Also of some concern on the unit was that ___ seemed fairly self-isolating and reluctant to discuss her depression and suicide attempt with the team. She did not attend much group therapy, stating that it was not helpful to her and that she would have preferred group therapy dedicated to grief counseling. This seems to reflect a lifelong history of depression and difficulty with self-expression that will need to be explored more closely in the outpatient setting. She had previously seen a therapist via ___ but felt that they did not connect well, so our social worker arranged for a new appointment with a therapist and partial program. She will also follow up closely with Dr. ___ d/c who was also kept updated on progress during her hospital stay. ___ has been referred to ___ Women's Partial Hospitalization Program on discharge, which is scheduled to begin on ___. We would also recommend considering referral to a bereavement/grief support group if she is open to this as an outpatient. Regarding pharmacotherapy, ___ outpatient dose of Lexapro 5mg daily was uptitrated to 20mg daily in the hospital without side effects. Since she also endorsed paranoid delusions (fear of food being poisoned at work, suspicion that the television might be able to monitor its viewers), her Seroquel was uptitrated to 3500mg HS. She complained of some morning fatigue on the higher dose, which improved upon moving the dose to earlier in the evening (8:00pm). Her home Lorazepam 1mg qAM for anxiety was continued. # MEDICAL: -- Anemia: received monthly B12 1000mcg SC injection on ___. Continued home iron supplementation. -- GERD: continued home omeprazole. -- Fibromyalgia: supportive care.
summary: You were referred to the ___ inpatient psychiatric unit by your outpatient psychiatrist Dr. ___ was very concerned by your worsening depression and recent serious suicide attempt via pill overdose. In the hospital you described many serious psychosocial stressors that triggered your worsened depression: recent deaths and serious illnesses in the family, feeling pulled in too many directions by family and career demands with no time for self-care, and ongoing grief exacerbated by living in the home of your mother who had recently passed away. You also had some paranoid thoughts about food at work being poisoned -- which, as we discussed, is a common symptom of depression when it becomes severe. We increased your antidepressant medications in the hospital; with this and with therapy, you had some improvement in your mood. We have arranged for you to attend the ___ Women's Partial Hospitalization Program (see below for scheduling info) upon discharge where you can continue being treated for your grief and depression.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ h/o AAA (declined intervention), h/o kidney stones, BPH, and recurrent UTIs, who was admitted with weakness for > ___ year. # Weakness: Unclear etiology, likely multifactorial including deconditioning, depression. He has declined outpatient colonoscopy, so cannot exclude malignancy. Exam/prior notes also concering for neurologic disease such as ___, given his flat affect and resting tremor. Extensive prior work-up by PCP with no lab abnormalities to explain his weakness. Low concern for infecion, given lack of infectious sysmptoms; although pt is concerned about tooth infection, there is no tenderness or evidence of infection on exam. He received IVF on admission, and was orthostatic but asymptomatic. Nutrition was consulted and he was started on Ensure + supplements with meals. He was also started on buproprion 100 mg BID for depressed moods, to be uptitrated by PCP as needed. Consider outpatient referral to Psychiatry and Neurology. # Anemia/Thrombocytopenia: Anemia has been chronic. Platelet count at baseline is low-normal. No evidence of ongoing blood loss or bleeding. Would repeat CBC at PCP ___. If abnormalities remain persistent, would ask patient to reconsider colonoscopy (which he has previously declined), multiple myeloma work-up with SPEP/UPEP and consider referral to Hematology. # BPH: although unusual, pt notes that finasteride makes him feel very tired; it was therefore held during admission and on discharge, to be followed up by PCP and outpatient urologist. Finasteride was continued. No diffuculty urinating during admission. Has ___ in place with urologist. Could consider whether he has low testosterone levels to explain his symptoms, which could be exacerbated by finasteride. If he does have consistently low testosterone levels, could consider referral to Endocrine for trial of testosterone therapy. # Visual hallucinations: pt reports seeing men in buckets in his room overnight, as well as bricks on the walls. He is unconcerned about these hallucinations. Low concern for infection, given lack of signs/symptoms and clear UA on ___. Most likely etiology is underyling dementia vs depression. Work-up for weakness so far without metabolic abnormalities to explain hallucinations. # GERD: continued on home ranitidine. Asymptomatic. # hx UTI: UA on admission without evidence of infection, asymptomatic throughout stay. Urine culture with less than 10K organisms, so UTI unlikely. # CKD: medications were renally dosed, nephrotoxins were avoided. Creatinine remained stable. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== # Finasteride held at discharge due to patient preference that he feels "much much better" without this medication. Voiding without difficulty, and UA on ___ without evidence of infection. Could consider checking testosterone levels off of finasteride to see if low testosterone could be explaining his symptoms. # Consider further outpatient work-up for fatigue, including further discussion about depression. # Started on buproprion 100mg BID for depression on ___. Would increase to TID dosing if he's doing well on current dose. # Consider referral to Neurology for ? dementia # Consider referral to Psychiatry for ? depression # Would repeat CBC at PCP ___ to assess for resolution/improvement in his anemia and thrombocytopenia. If these abnormalities remain, would encourage colonoscopy (which patient has previously declined), w/u for multiple myeloma with SPEP/UPEP and consider referral to Hematology.
summary: You came to the hospital because you were feeling very weak. We made sure this wasn't due to an infection or to any problems with your heart, which it was not. We held your finasteride because you said it makes you feel tired, and you felt much better; your outpatient doctors ___ provide advice on whether or not you will need a medication like this in the future. We also had you work with Physical Therapy, who recommended more physical therapy sessions once you get home. Finally, we talked with you about your moods recently, and we all agreed that it would worthwhile trying a medication called buproprion to help improve your energy and mood.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ woman with failure to thrive and years of feeling fatigued/malaise, as well as generalized weakness who developed L leg weakness about ___ year ago. She now presents with worsening LLE weakness and L arm weakness that began a few days ago. The patient is incredibly apathetic and fatigued during the encounter, and she also has a history of refusing work up in the past which has made diagnosis of certain illnesses near impossible. In the past year, the patient has become completely dependent on her daughter and son-in-law for all of her ADLs which has been incredibly difficult on them. text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ alcohol abuse, IV heroin use, HCV, who presents on the day of his arrest with atypical chest pain. # BLE SCABBING LESIONS: With mild surrounding erythema, ultrasound showing no evidence of fluid collection. No fevers. Started empiric clindamycin for suspected MRSA, wound culture grew group A strep and MSSA. Given potential for group A strep resistance to clindamycin, he was treated with clindamycin and Keflex. First day of abx was ___, plan for 10 day course, with final day ___. Given Rx for Keflex and Clinda upon discharge. Should f/u with PCP or jail/prison physician for ___ of leg wounds in ___ weeks. # CHEST PAIN: Negative troponin, ECG consistent with priors. TTE unremarkable (performed after he reported history of "valvular disease" as a child). Pain is atypical in description. Furthermore, given some relief after ketorolac, there is thought that there is possibly a MSK component contributing. Also, given onset with arrest, factitious sx also possible. Still c/o mild CP at the time of discharge, but pain was improving and was not associated with any vital, lab or other clinical abnormalities. # ALCOHOL/OPIATE WITHDRAWAL: Was originally placed on valium CIWA, which was d/c'ed after he had not required benzo's in over 24 hours. He was also started on methadone, which was tapered per ___ protocol. He completed taper on ___. His ___ scores were not significantly elevated at the time of discharge. # HCV: advised patient that he can seek out treatment for HCV once sober. Should f/u with PCP ___: this issue going forward. # SUICIDAL IDEATION: SW and psych evaluated the patient. The psychiatry team recommended: -If released from custody, patient might benefit from voluntary inpatient dual dx treatment if that were available, otherwise partial hospital or residential treatment. (BEST could assist with dispo) -If placed in jail the patient should be assessed by court clinician and should be considered at elevated risk for self harm in that setting. -Treatment for depression and/or ADHD might be appropriate but would need to reassess for these conditions in the setting of sobriety.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you developed chest pain after being arrested. Our investigations show that your pain is not from your heart. You also received treatment for alcohol and opiate withdrawal during your time here. Our testing also shows that you have hepatitis C. There are now treatments to cure hepatitis C but you must stop using alcohol and heroin in order to receive this treatment. You were also found to have small areas of infection/abscess on the skin of your legs. Culture from the wound is growing bacteria for which you are being treated with antibiotics. You will need to take an additional 7 days of antibiotics for this infection and be seen by a primary care physician for ___ after you complete the antibiotics.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with known severe active depression. She presents after being brought in by ambulance due to reported poly-substance overdose in the setting of several alcoholic drinks. The patient was initially admitted to the MICU for SI with overdose of seroquel, trazodone, clonazepam and mildly long Qtc, which has since improved. Toxicology and Pyschiatry were consulted and assisted in the management of the patient. Psych initially recommended to restart lamictal, but not pt's other psych meds other than cymabalta once Qtc improved. On ___ pt had repeat EKG and cymabalta was restarted. Ultimately, the abilify was also restarted, although her Deplin was not restarted prior to discharge and can be restarted at ___ as desired. After admission, the patient reportedly showed mild signs of benzodiazepine withdrawl. After discussed with psych, it was recommended for the patient to be restarted on Klonipin near her home dose. She remained on a 1:1 sitter throughout her admission. Acute issues: #Poly-substance overdose: Initially had several medications held, which were gradually restarted as her QTc improved. We continued to hold her seroquel and trazodone throughout her admission in concert with the psychiatry recommendations. She remained on a 1:1 sitter throughout her admission due to concerns her overdose was in the setting of active suicidality and severe depression. #Possible early benzodiazepine withdrawal versus significant anxiety: The patient initially had her benzodiazepine doses held in the setting of the overdose. This medication was ultimately restarted at her home dose, and may require further adjustments during her psychiatric admission. The patient initially reported taking 4mg daily, but this was later clarified to be a significantly lower dose. #Active major depression with active SI/SA Psychiatry consult liaison team continued to follow the patient throughout her admission. She will transition to ___ at discharge for ongoing psychiatric care, potentially including ongoing ECT therapy and medication adjustments. A copy of her ECG on ___ was copied and sent with the patient. #Isolated actelectasis noted on CXR ___, without evidence of pneumonia or rhonchi: We did not suspect active pneumonia or evolving infection due to no hypoxemia, no rhonchi on exam and only mild non-productive cough which was improving at the time of discharge. Chronic issues: #GERD: The patient reportedly takes PPI at home, which was continued during her admission. #We restarted vitamin D supplements as pt takes at home, as well. #The patient should continue to follow with her PCP following discharge from the planned psychiatry admission, for ongoing chronic issues.
summary: you were admitted for being sleepy and feeling poorly after taking an overdose of your medications in the setting of drinking alcohol. You required a stay in the medical ICU, due to the impact of the medications on your heart eletrical activity adnormalities due to the overdose. This has since improved with treatment during your hospital stay, and your heart electrical activity now stable. We are holding your seroquel and trazodone as a result of these abnormalities. You were evaluated by the toxicology and psychiatry doctors during this ___. The psychiatry doctors recommended that ___ be transferred to ___ for ongoing therapy due to your severe depression and suicide attempt before this admission. We understand this is where you have been receiving recent treatments. Your Abilify was restarted on the day of discharge. You can restart the Deplin after transfer to ___, if the doctors ___ to continue this for you.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man with a history of conversion disorder (can't use or feel his R leg), wheelchair bound, opiate dependance presenting with increasing nausea over the last week. # Nausea/Vomiting: Unclear etiology, differential includes PUD, gastritis, viral infection, and NSTEMI. He has had several ED visits given psychiatric hx, also concern for conversion disorder. Recent NSAID use and symptoms worsened with food lends more to PUD. The patient was treated with pantoprazole 40mg IV daily, given IV fluids and his diet was advanced to a BRAT diet as tolerated. He was also put on standing zofran and compazine as well as thiamine, folate and multivitamins. # S/p fall: Patient sustained an unwitnessed fall in the bathroom where he struck his head and neck. He denied loss of consciousness and exam was benign without any spinal tenderness. CT head was negative for intracranial process and CT C-spine was negative for acute fracture. # ECG changes: He has new ST depressions in lateral leads and TWI in inferior leads which are new from previous admission. He denies any chest pain or shortness of breath. His troponins were trended and were negative, repeat EKG was within normal limits. # Substance Abuse: Pt has a hx of opioid and tobacco dependence. He has been on suboxone for several years without relapsing. He denies any recent drug, alcohol, or tobacco use. He was continued on his home suboxone and nicotine polacrilex. # Hep C:Diagnosed in ___. Has not been treated due to depression. LFTs at baseline. # Psychiatric Disorder: Pt has a hx of depression with hx of suicide attempt several years ago. No current SI but patient does feel depressed regarding current homeless situation. He also has been dx with conversion disorder and cant use or feel his right leg. His bupropion, citalopram and clonazepam were continued. Clonazepam was tapered prior to discharge. # L shoulder pain: Most likely injury to rotator cuff. Was evaluated on previous admission and was supposed to follow up with ortho. However, it does not appear he ever followed up with them. He had been taking NSAIDS prior to stomach pain for pain control. NSAIDs were held due to concern for PUD and he was given tylenol as needed for pain. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Paperwork will need to be sent from ___ to ___ for enrollment in suboxone program - If issues after discharge, call Dr. ___ office and speak to social worker ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Acetaminophen-Caff-Butalbital ___ TAB PO Q8H:PRN headache 2. Atenolol 50 mg PO DAILY 3. Buprenorphine-Naloxone (8mg-2mg) 1 TAB SL TID 4. BuPROPion (Sustained Release) 200 mg PO QAM 5. Citalopram 40 mg PO DAILY 6. Clonazepam 0.5 mg PO BID 7. Gabapentin 800 mg PO TID 8. Ibuprofen 400 mg PO Q8H:PRN pain 9. Nicotine Polacrilex 2 mg PO Q1H:PRN craving 10. Sumatriptan Succinate 50 mg PO ONCE Duration: 1 Doses 11. Clonazepam 1 mg PO DAILY insomnia
summary: You were admitted for nausea, vomiting and abdominal pain. Your abdominal exam was not concerning for anything that would require surgical intervention. Your exam was monitored and you were treated symptomatically with zofran, phenergen and IV fluids. Your nausea improved as did your abdominal pain. You fell in the restroom but did not sustain any injuries and your head and neck CT scans were negative for any fractures or bleeding. We are working with Dr. ___ to help you get enrolled in a suboxone program at ___. Paperwork was sent to ___ and they must fill this out and send it to ___ prior to your enrollment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ gentleman with PMH HIV, CKD, CAD c/b STEMI s/p stenting, Hodgkin lymphoma s/p autologous SCT, SCC of the tongue, and depression c/b multiple suicide attempts and requiring ECT, who is admitted following suicide attempt by lacerating his AV fistula. # AV Fistula Repair: # Cellulitis: Patient initially admitted to transplant surgery service and underwent ligation with partial excision of left upper arm AV fistula on ___ after suicide attempt. He developed marked redness surrounding AV fistula incision site. Treated with cephalexin x7d. # Depression: Patient has major depression complicated by several suicide attempts. Currently on ___ with 1:1 sitter awaiting placement at ___ after most recent suicide attempt by cutting his AVF. Continued on Zyprexa 5mg qhs, Zyprexa 2.5mg prn, and Fluoxetine 40mg daily # Orthostatic hypotension: Patient has chronic orthostasis for which he has been taking midodrine. Overnight ___, he was getting up to the bathroom when he felt orthostatic and fell after losing consciousness. A ___ head CT was normal. Patient had symptomatic orthostatic hypotension with drop from SBPs 120s to ___. Possible etiologies include volume depletion following his suicide attempt. Orthostasis is also a frequent (>10%) effect of olanzapine. Unlikely cardiac cause given EF 55% and mild aortic regurgitation unchanged on TTE. Symptoms improved w/ fluids and initiating fludrocortisone. Continued midodrine. # CKD: Patient has baseline CKD stage IV/V secondary to medications (lithium, tenofovir) and prior chemotherapy exposure, and is followed by Dr. ___ in Nephrology. He had a left brachiocephalic AV fistula placed by transplant surgery in ___, but as of yet has not had any indication for dialysis. During his current hospitalization, his Cr has elevated from 3.6 to 4.7. Likely prerenal in the setting of acute blood loss without adequate volume resuscitation. Improved to baseline w/ fluids. # Acute on chronic anemia: Patient has acute drop in Hgb to 8s at presentation from baseline ~11 in ___. Likely secondary to acute blood loss in the setting of suicide attempt. Patient received 2U pRBC at OSH, did not required additional transfusions. Continued iron supplement. Chronic anemia may be secondary to baseline renal dysfunction. # Anion gap metabolic acidosis: Patient had notable metabolic acidosis at presentation, now with widening anion gap to 20 as of ___. BUN stably elevated at 40. Improved after fluid administration. Likely multifactorial iso CKD. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================== #HIV: CD4 count ___ was 512. Continued ___, Maravaroc, Dolutegravir. #HLD Continued Rosuvastatin. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [] Last day of cephalexin for cellulitis is ___. He should receive his last dose in the evening on ___. [] Please ask transplant surgery to remove sutures from L AVF site on ___. Please page ___. [] Patient has a years long history of orthostasis for which he is on midodrine and fludrocortisone. Please encourage adaptive strategies (sitting up slowly, standing slowly, assistance to the bathroom, etc) to manage this as it is a chronic issue.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after a suicide attempt. You had cut your dialysis access fistula in this suicide attempt. You had surgery on your fistula to repair your injury. You were evaluated by Psychiatry who recommended transfer to an inpatient psychiatric facility for further therapy. You were placed on 1:1 supervision and ___ for your safety. You were treated with antibiotics for a skin infection around your fistula. You received fluids for orthostasis (blood pressure dropping with standing). You were started on a medication (fludrocortisone) for your orthostasis. You had a CT scan of your head after a fall. There was no evidence of a fracture or bleed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with anorexia nervosa and recent admission who presents after inpatient psychiatric admission for suicide attempt (via hanging). In brief summary, patient was placed on an eating disorder protocol with tube feeds via Dobhoff, eventually transitioning to po intake. She was also treated for mutism, psychosis, and depression with lorazepam standing regimen and Zyprexa qHS, and she became more interactive throughout admission. Her weight on admission was 33.6 kg and was 43.2 at discharge Acute Issues ============ # Anorexia nervosa with severe malnutrition: Recent discharge from ___ on ___ and failed admission to inpatient program in ___ on ___. She returned after suicide attempt. BMI 13 on admission to ___. Here, she was enrolled in eating disorder protocol and received tube feeds via Dobhoff tube. Psychiatry, nutrition and social work were consulted and were following her case. She was started on olanzapine for weight gain as well as paranoid/delusional/disorganized thoughts. In addition, guardianship was obtained from her mother (___). Daily weights were checked for weight gain as well, with the goal of 70% of ideal body weight, or 38.5 kg. Patient taking solid food with ensure supplement for remaining half of calories required for each meal. Weight responded to the regimen, and pt was 44.8kg upon discharge. # Mutism, withdrawn behavior: Shortly after admission, patient became mute and refused to open eyes or follow commands. This was suspicious for psychosis vs severe malnutrition (various metabolic derangements including hypoglycemia), however, based on physical exam, this appeared to be largely voluntary. Anoxic brain injury following attempting hanging also possible, however, mental status initially was relatively normal, making delayed anoxic injury less likely. She had an EEG on ___ that was normal, as was MR brain normal. In consultation with psychiatry, she was continued on the olanzapine as above, with addition of lorazepam, with good response. She began intermittently refusing olanzapine, and had to be transitioned to IM dosing. Her regimen on discharge was 5mg olanzapine at 1200 and 2200, offered PO initially and given IM if refused, and 1mg lorazepam IV at 0800 and 1600. # Suicide attempt: Initially admitted by ___ inpatient psychiatry. Patient was not actively suicidal on admission. She had 1:1 sitter throughout admission. MRI brain and EEG did not reveal evidence of anoxic brain injury. # Tachycardia: Thought to be secondary to malnutrition and dehydration vs anxiety/agitation. She remained on telemetry, which showed sinus tachycardia that was not persistent throughout admission. Tachycardia was responsive to fluid boluses of 500cc's of NS. Electrolytes were repleted as needed. Tachycardia was no longer being recorded on telemetry by the time of discharge. # Bacterial UTI: Pt was found to have a catheter-associated UTI, which grew E. coli. Subsequently, her catheter was removed, and she was started on po Bactrim suspension for 10 days. She remained afebrile without a significant leukocytosis. Chronic Issues ============== # History of Anxiety: She was given lorazepam PRN. # Celiac disease: Anti-tissue transglutaminase was greater than assay on last admission. She will require GI endoscopy in the future for biopsy for definitive diagnosis of celiac disease, which will be managed as an outpatient. She was given a gluten-free diet. # Transaminitis: Pt underwent extensive work-up previously, including hepatic biopsy, which was negative, during last hospitalization. Transaminitis though to be secondary to anorexia nervosa with possible component of celiac's disease contributing. ============= Transitional issues: - ongoing anorexia nervosa - suicidal ideation with attempts - celiac disease without confirmed endoscopic biopsy, can consider biopsy in outpatient follow-up - transaminitis of indefinite origin.
summary: You were admitted to our hospital as a transfer from ___ after a suicide attempt. During your treatment here, you were seen by a psychiatrist, and you were under direct supervision by a 1:1 sitter due to the concerns for a repeated suicidal attempt. In addition, we managed your eating disorder with a "refeeding protocol" in which we administered nutrition via a temporary feeding tube. For this, a nutrition team oversaw your nutritional needs. In addition, you were found to have a urinary tract infection which we treated with antibiotics. After a medicine team managed your medical concerns, including a fast heart rate, malnutrition, and urinary tract infections, you were transferred to a psychiatry team who managed your anxiety, eating disorder, and suicidal ideation.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with anorexia nervosa and recent admission who presents after inpatient psychiatric admission for suicide attempt (via hanging). In brief summary, patient was placed on an eating disorder protocol with tube feeds via Dobhoff, eventually transitioning to po intake. She was also treated for mutism, psychosis, and depression with lorazepam standing regimen and Zyprexa qHS, and she became more interactive throughout admission. Her weight on admission was 33.6 kg and was 43.2 at discharge Acute Issues ============ # Anorexia nervosa with severe malnutrition: Recent discharge from ___ on ___ and failed admission to inpatient program in ___ on ___. She returned after suicide attempt. BMI 13 on admission to ___. Here, she was enrolled in eating disorder protocol and received tube feeds via Dobhoff tube. Psychiatry, nutrition and social work were consulted and were following her case. She was started on olanzapine for weight gain as well as paranoid/delusional/disorganized thoughts. In addition, guardianship was obtained from her mother (___). Daily weights were checked for weight gain as well, with the goal of 70% of ideal body weight, or 38.5 kg. Patient taking solid food with ensure supplement for remaining half of calories required for each meal. Weight responded to the regimen, and pt was 44.8kg upon discharge. # Mutism, withdrawn behavior: Shortly after admission, patient became mute and refused to open eyes or follow commands. This was suspicious for psychosis vs severe malnutrition (various metabolic derangements including hypoglycemia), however, based on physical exam, this appeared to be largely voluntary. Anoxic brain injury following attempting hanging also possible, however, mental status initially was relatively normal, making delayed anoxic injury less likely. She had an EEG on ___ that was normal, as was MR brain normal. In consultation with psychiatry, she was continued on the olanzapine as above, with addition of lorazepam, with good response. She began intermittently refusing olanzapine, and had to be transitioned to IM dosing. Her regimen on discharge was 5mg olanzapine at 1200 and 2200, offered PO initially and given IM if refused, and 1mg lorazepam IV at 0800 and 1600. # Suicide attempt: Initially admitted by ___ inpatient psychiatry. Patient was not actively suicidal on admission. She had 1:1 sitter throughout admission. MRI brain and EEG did not reveal evidence of anoxic brain injury. # Tachycardia: Thought to be secondary to malnutrition and dehydration vs anxiety/agitation. She remained on telemetry, which showed sinus tachycardia that was not persistent throughout admission. Tachycardia was responsive to fluid boluses of 500cc's of NS. Electrolytes were repleted as needed. Tachycardia was no longer being recorded on telemetry by the time of discharge. # Bacterial UTI: Pt was found to have a catheter-associated UTI, which grew E. coli. Subsequently, her catheter was removed, and she was started on po Bactrim suspension for 10 days. She remained afebrile without a significant leukocytosis. Chronic Issues ============== # History of Anxiety: She was given lorazepam PRN. # Celiac disease: Anti-tissue transglutaminase was greater than assay on last admission. She will require GI endoscopy in the future for biopsy for definitive diagnosis of celiac disease, which will be managed as an outpatient. She was given a gluten-free diet. # Transaminitis: Pt underwent extensive work-up previously, including hepatic biopsy, which was negative, during last hospitalization. Transaminitis though to be secondary to anorexia nervosa with possible component of celiac's disease contributing. ============= Transitional issues: - ongoing anorexia nervosa - suicidal ideation with attempts - celiac disease without confirmed endoscopic biopsy, can consider biopsy in outpatient follow-up - transaminitis of indefinite origin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Legal status: The patient arrived on a section 12a for involuntary hospitalization, signed a conditional voluntary section ___, and was admitted to the inpatient psychiatric unit. 2. Psychiatric: Adjustment disorder with mixed anxiety and depressed mood: The patient initially endorsed a "depressed" mood in the context of multiple psychosocial stressors, including not being accepted to any of the 22 doctoral programs that she had applied to and a recent break-up with a boyfriend. According to the report from the patient's school counselor, the patient had expressed suicidal ideation with a plan to overdose with pills. On further evaluation, the patient was able to elaborate with more detail about the statements she expressed to her outpatient therapist and clearly denied suicidal ideation, plan or intent. She was clearly future oriented, looking forward to going on a trip to ___, looking forward to being baptized, and completing her ___ program. She benefited from the support and structure the unit provided, and at time of discharge she was bright, euthymic in her mood, felt like she had a clear path of what to do in life in the next few months, and denied all symptoms of depression. She does note having been hard on herself through time and has the tendency to "make things more complicated than they are." She slept well, ate well, maintainted her hygiene. Had a discussion with her therapist on the phone in the hospital and follow up for further therapy was arranged. Collateral from her friends at church was obtained and they will remain in support of her after discharge and did not have safety concerns. Antidepressant medication was discussed during the course of the admission. There was no clear indication for medication at this time, Through out her admission the patient was pleasant, cooperative, attended groups and was appropriately engaged in the milieu. 3. Medical: During her hospitalization she was seen by the gynecology service to discuss the High grade squamous intraepithelial lesion (Vulvar intraepithelial neoplasia II, focal III) found on biopsy. The patient understood the importance of ongoing outpatient follow up.
summary: Stress Relief: A Positive Lifestyle Learning to manage stress doesn't happen overnight. It's a process. The more you keep at it, the more you'll feel in control of daily events. Set Limits Trying to fit too much into a day is a major cause of stress. Setting limits will help you feel more in control. This sometimes means saying no-to people and even to things you might want to do. This can be hard at times. But knowing your priorities can help you make choices. Know Your Priorities Using your time and energy wisely is a good way to control stress. Save time for the things that matter most in your life. Ask yourself: Do I really need to do this? Do I want to do this? If you answer "yes," go ahead. But keep in mind you can also answer "no." Learn to Accept Support Everybody needs support now and then. So don't feel embarrassed to ask for help when you need it. Most people are glad to lend a hand. And asking for help can open up new lines of communication and friendships. Be Part of Your Community Taking part in community or faith-based events can offer a sense of belonging. It also helps put you in touch with active and caring people nearby. So whether it's a cleanup day at a local park or taking meals to the elderly, try to reach out to friends and neighbors. Just remember: Taking time for yourself once in a while makes it easier to help others later on.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo man with hx PUD, HTN, HLD, depression presented to OSH with RUQ pain, found to have choledocholithiasis, transferred to ___ for ERCP. ERCP on ___ showed no dilated ducts, no evidence for CBD stones. A sphincterotomy was performed and sludge was extracted, but no pus. Surgery was consulted given his positive HIDA scan and symptoms. He was continued on empiric abx coverage given his fevers and concerns re: cholangitis/cholecystitis. Surgery recommended laparoscopic cholecystectomy, which was performed on ___ and his care was transferred to the surgery team postoperatively. His antibiotics were stopped after his surgery, and he was given a regular diet and monitored with serial abdominal exams. He tolerated his diet well, and his abdominal exam was remarkable only for very mild RUQ pain with no rebound or guarding. His LFT's continued to trend down, with his Tbili being 1.3 on the day of discharge (was 4.7 on admission). He was noted to have a lipase of 347, up from 182 the day prior, but in the context of minimal abdominal tenderness in the ___ area only and no pain with oral intake he was felt to be stable for discharge home, and I explained to him with a ___ interpreter that if he began to experience increasing abdominal pain, pain with eating, pain that radiates to the back, fever, nausea, vomiting, or any other concerning symptoms that he should return to the emergency department for evaluation. He was given a follow up appointment to be seen in the Acute Care Surgery Clinic on ___ at 230pm, and was discharged home on a regular diet with oral oxycodone for pain.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with an inflammed gallbladder and concern over gallstones blocking your bile ducts. You were transferred to our ___ from ___ for a procedure called an ERCP. This was done because your common bile duct was noted to be dilated. The ERCP showed no gallstones in the duct. The specialists were able to open up the valve (Sphincter) where the common bile duct enters the small intestine. They opened the valve by making an incision in the muscle (called a sphincterotomy). For this reason, you MUST avoid taking any medications that can thin your blood for 5 days. Examples of medications you MUST AVOID include: aspirin, NSAIDs (ibuprofen, aleve, naprosyn, advil) or other medications that can thin your blood (warfarin, coumadin, heparin) for five days.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o female with H/O seizure disorder, obesity, and significant tobacco abuse presenting with inconsistently localizable chest pain reproducible by palpation with extension to "whole body." # Chest Pain: No acute cardiac cause identified. Pain lasted over 24 hours at a reported ___ intensity without EKG changes or biomarker elevation for myonecrosis. Also, pain was reproducible on exam, although her history and exam were quite variable from examiner to examiner. Patient was able to fall asleep despite complaints of ongoing pain. She was unable to perform nuclear stress testing due to inability to lay still on her back, which according to the patient is a chronic problem for her. She was also unable to cooperate with a non-imaging exercise stress test. Since ischemia was unlikely given ongoing chest pain without evidence of infarction, other pharmacological stress testing or imaging modalities were not pursued. An echocardiogram was obtained which was unrevealing for any acute wall motion abnormalities but did note grade I diastolic dysfunction. A GI cocktail and PPI did not improve the pain. She was treated with standing Tylenol and ibuprofen for presumed costochondritis. A subclinical seizure leading to fall was a possibility, but CK's were not elevated and pt could not remember any such episode. Patient also had no evidence for pulmonary embolus or aortic dissection by ___ angiogram. The radiology department there felt the 3.9cm ectasia of the ascending aorta seemed chronic and stable and was very unlikely to be causing any of her symptoms. The plan was to have repeat outpatient CT scan of chest in 6 ___ year to assess stability and size. She eventually became pain free prior to discharge. # Seizure disorder- continued divalproex per outpatient schedule # Depression/anxiety - continued quetiapine, clonazepam, escitalopram, and trazodone. Social work was consulted to ensure adequate community resources were available prior to discharge. # Atypical cells on Differential - After discussion with heme/path, the initial report of 12 atypical lymphoctes was changed to 2 reactive lymphcytes, and an automated differentual the next day was reported as negative for atypicals. Plan was to have repeat surveillance WBC differential performed as an outpatient. # Smoking- Patient was given a nicotine patch and encouraged at smoking cessation. Social work was consulted.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. We made sure you did not have a heart attack by checking EKGs and several blood tests. We also made sure you did not have a blood clot in your lungs by performing a special type of CT scan. We tried to have you perform different types of stress tests but you could not lie flat because of chronic back discomfort. You did have an echocardiogram which did not reveal any acute cardiac pathology. Although we are not certain what the cause of your pain is, it is most likely musculoskeletal given that your whole body hurts, and we could reproduce the pain by examining you. The pain is is not related to your heart, lungs or major blood vessels in your chest. We incidentally found a small widening of your aorta which is not causing your pain, but it should be monitored with another CT scan in 6 months to make sure it is not getting bigger. Dr. ___ schedule this study for you. We also found very few atypical blood cells on your bloodwork. These are also not the cause of your pain, but a repeat blood test can be performed as an outpatient to make sure they resolve. We will give you prescriptions for pain control.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with ___ chronic back pain, depression, presents to the ED with acute back pain and fecal/urinary incontinence. # Back pain: Patient's back pain thought to be acute on chronic back pain secondary to injury possibly causing lumbar disk degeneration and bulging. The exacerbation is likely due to lack of access to pain medication (patient's workman's compensation had stopped). Social work was consulted to help patient attain access to medications. Because of her incontinence, spine and neuro were consulted to r/o cord injury, they cleared her for any risk of acute spinal injury. They did note that the moderate cervical cord compression was stable and probably not contributing to her symptoms. The patient's pain was very persistent and difficult to manage. On Dilaudid ___ PO Q3H, her pain was still an ___ however she was starting to become somewhat sedated and she said she had bad reactions to toradol, motrin and neurontin. Pain management was consulted and they have been helping to modify her pain regimen to improve her symptoms. We believe much of her low tolerance to pain is related to her severely depressed mood and her pain management would significantly improve upon starting an SSRI and inpatient psychiatric observation. Pain management consult will continue to follow the patient. # Depression: Patient with MDD and is currently very depressed. Patient has had a multitude of complaints that would come and go including headache, chest pain, nausea, SOB, leg pain, arm numbness and tingling that were worked up with extensive imaging, EKGs all deemed negative. The patient admitted that many of her complaints including her chest pain could be attributed to her anxiety. These complaints have been thought to be psychosomatic. Psychiatry was consulted to help manage patient's significant depression and anxiety. Patient remained tearful, depressed, and anxious throughout the admission. The psychiatry team had recommended inpatient transfer to psychiatric unit where they would start patient on an SSRI and observe her course. The patient is being transferred to ___ for further evaluation. # UTI: Patient with a UTI likely secondary to her fecal incontinence. Culture grew pansensitive pseudomonas. Patient without white count, fever, or any signs of systemic toxicity. CT urogram negative for evidence of stones or pyelonephritis. This UTI was thought to be an uncomplicated UTI. Patient on 2 day course of levofloxacin, patient still with ?dysuria (hard to gauge as patient has many complaints that are likely psychosomatic) therefore the regimen was changed to start 3 day course of doxycycline to treat the UTI. # Ambulation: Patient was admitted not able to ambulate likely due to pain, which is what probably led to the incontinence. As the patient's pain control improved, patient was able to ambulate with physical therapy for 30 feet on ___. This morning patient was able to get out of bed and sit in the chair. Physical therapy, nursing, and the medicine team believe that much of her inability to walk is more related to feelings of hopelessness and lack of motivation rather than purely pain.
summary: You have been diagnosed with acute on chronic back pain. Your pain probably originated from your injury in ___. Your pain probably increased because of lack of access to medications. You have met with the social workers to help you gain access to medications through your medicare and Mass Health insurance. Because of your incontinence, there was concern that you might have injury to your spine. After extensive MRI of your entire spine, and evaluation by our neurology and spine experts, you were cleared of any acute injury to your spinal cord. You also experienced severe pain during your stay here. We attempted to treat your pain to high dose dilauidid, unfortunately this was unable to completely control your pain. We consulted the pain specialists who prescibred specific medications to help with back spasm and back pain. They recommend seeing you as an outpatient to consider surgical options to treat your chronic back pain. You also developed a urinary tract infection during your admission. This is being treated with a 3 day course of doxycycline antibiotic. You are being transferred to ___ for further psychiatric evaluation. There they will treat your depression and monitor your improvement.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo F past medical history of mild aortic stenosis with last echo in ___, HTN, hyperlipidemia, osteopenia, who was admitted after losing balance and falling, found to have very depressed mood on exam and history. . # s/p Fall in pt with malnutrition (severe): Patient fell after straining to have BM which could suggest a vagal episode, however, no pre-fall diaphoresis, clamminess, N/V, lightheadedness. All trauma films without acute pathology. Does have mild AS and an echo every ___ years is reasonable to assess valve area although unclear what interventions would be appropriate in a ___ year old. Pt has had poor PO intake over last few weeks, however, orthostats negative (after fluid administration). Pt is extremely frail, has lost a lot of weight, clearly malnourished, and in context of mood (see below), component of depression/ adjustment-related malnutrition. Mirtazepine was continued, psychiatry was consulted, and pt seen by ___ while hospitalized. . # Depressed mood: Has not been eating well since moving to ___ since does not like food and feels depressed becuase lost independence. More recently, male friend moved away. Pt not actively suicidal, feels helpless and hopeless. Mitazepine was continued, and psychiatry was consulted. No new medication recommendations, but did suggest close outpatient follow-up with geripsychiatrist. SW also saw pt while hospitalized. . # Aspiration: concern for aspiration episodes since hospitalized, and given dry mouth, may not be forming good food bolus in mouth. No reported PNA on admission CXR, but pt with cough, though no leukocytosis or fever. Seen by speech/swallow who were concerned about chronic aspiration, so pt had video swallow on ___ that revealed pt is indeed aspirating. Recommended thickened fluids and ground solids. HOWEVER, given clinical context of this admission and pt's already decreased PO intake, some concern that may be conterproductive to place diet restrictions in this pt who already has minimal PO intake. Provided artificial saliva to increase likelihood of food bolus formation, and discussed with pt at ___ re: aspiration precautions and behavioral modifications when eating. Swallow therapy to continue at ___. . # Acute on Chronic renal insufficiency: Recent baseline 1.2-1.3 in recent months, but admission Cre 1.5, likely due to poor PO intake and prerenal etiology. ARF resolved with rehydration. . # Anemia: HCT at baseline . # Hypertension: Continued on home ___. . # Hypercholesterolemia: Continued on home lipitor. Fasting LDL<70. . # Osteopenia with h/o L1, L4, and T12 compression fractures: continued on home calcium, vitamin D. . # Code Status: FULL
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of a fall, but luckily, you didn't have a fractures or injuries greater than a few bruises and scraped knees. While you were here, however, we were very concerned about your mood, as you seemed very unhappy. We think part of this might be because you've recently moved to the ___ and haven't yet adjusted to your new environment. In addition, we noticed that you were having some difficulty swallowing your food without choking. We had the speech/swallow specialist come see you, who recommended you have a video study to see how well you swallow. The video showed that some of your food is going into your airways rather than your digestive system, and you can decrease how much food does this by taking smaller bites, and performing maneuvers that the swallow therapist taught you, and also drinking thickened liquids. It's likely that the artificial saliva that we started will also help.
