text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year-old right-handed woman with a history including headache, depression, heroin use, and hypertension who presented to the ___ following the sudden onset of weakness and sensory changes in the right arm - and possible right facial droop - in the setting of headache. At the time of her arrival, a code stroke was called, and a CT/A of the head and neck was done. The imaging showed no evidence of hemorrhage, masses, or obvious signs of ischemia. There did not appear to be stenosis, dissection, or aneurysm on angiography. She was admitted to the stroke service overnight (___) for further evaluation and care. . Although the concomitant occurrence of headache is atypical for an isolated ischemic infarct, an MRI of the brain was performed given the patient's vascular risk factors, incomplete history, and persistent sensory changes. The study revealed no acute infarcts or other clearly contributory lesions. There was evidence of scattered FLAIR hyperintensities in the subcortical and periventricular white matter suggestive of chronic ischemic microvascular disease. To evaluate modifiable risk factors for stroke, lipids and glycosylated hemoglobin were measured. The LDL was found to be 74. The HBA1C was 5.4 %. An echo was not repeated in the absence of acute infarct and an unrevealing stress echo performed in ___. Since the team ultimately learned that she has not been taking antihypertensives, and her blood pressure was in normal range throughout the hospitalization, they were not restarted at the time of discharge. . By the time of evaluation the following day, examination showed no concerning persistent abnormalities. She reported persistent head discomfort. The headache was thought to reflect a muscle tension headache. Since Ms. ___ reported the tramadol failed to offer relief, we recommended stopping it. It was further suggested she talk with ___ and ___ about discontinuing the topamax in favor of an agent such as nortriptyline (starting at 10 mg at night, for example) or a serotinin-specific reuptake inhibitor given significant psychosocial stressors and history of depression. In the interim, massage and heat in the shoulder and neck regions were advocated. . A social work consultation was obtained prior to discharge. Together with the social worker, Ms. ___ participated in relaxation exercises and guided meditation with some apparent relief. She discussed a willingness to continue relaxation exercises - and perhaps try acupuncture - as modes of pain control and coping. She was given the contact information for health centers where she could pursue these options. It was recommended that Ms. ___ also work with Dr. ___ to identify a counselor with whom to work longitudinally. .
summary: You presented to the ___ with sudden onset weakness and sensory changes in the right arm in the setting of headache. Upon your arrival imaging was done to evaluate for brain and vessel abnormalities. The studies showed no signs of stroke or torn blood vessels (which can be associated with headache and stroke-like symptoms). The current examination shows no concerning persistent abnormalities. The headache likely refelects a muscle tension headache. Since the tramadol does not offer relief, we recommend stopping it. Please talk with ___ and ___ about stopping the topamax in favor of an agent such as nortriptyline (starting at 10 mg at night, for example) or a serotinin-specific reuptake inhibitor. In the interim, massage and heat in the shoulder and neck regions could be beneficial. It will be important to meet with ___ and ___ in follow-up as well. Please decide with Dr. ___ it would be helpful to meet with a counselor or therapist.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year-old right-handed woman with a history including headache, depression, heroin use, and hypertension who presented to the ___ following the sudden onset of weakness and sensory changes in the right arm - and possible right facial droop - in the setting of headache. At the time of her arrival, a code stroke was called, and a CT/A of the head and neck was done. The imaging showed no evidence of hemorrhage, masses, or obvious signs of ischemia. There did not appear to be stenosis, dissection, or aneurysm on angiography. She was admitted to the stroke service overnight (___) for further evaluation and care. . Although the concomitant occurrence of headache is atypical for an isolated ischemic infarct, an MRI of the brain was performed given the patient's vascular risk factors, incomplete history, and persistent sensory changes. The study revealed no acute infarcts or other clearly contributory lesions. There was evidence of scattered FLAIR hyperintensities in the subcortical and periventricular white matter suggestive of chronic ischemic microvascular disease. To evaluate modifiable risk factors for stroke, lipids and glycosylated hemoglobin were measured. The LDL was found to be 74. The HBA1C was 5.4 %. An echo was not repeated in the absence of acute infarct and an unrevealing stress echo performed in ___. Since the team ultimately learned that she has not been taking antihypertensives, and her blood pressure was in normal range throughout the hospitalization, they were not restarted at the time of discharge. . By the time of evaluation the following day, examination showed no concerning persistent abnormalities. She reported persistent head discomfort. The headache was thought to reflect a muscle tension headache. Since Ms. ___ reported the tramadol failed to offer relief, we recommended stopping it. It was further suggested she talk with ___ and ___ about discontinuing the topamax in favor of an agent such as nortriptyline (starting at 10 mg at night, for example) or a serotinin-specific reuptake inhibitor given significant psychosocial stressors and history of depression. In the interim, massage and heat in the shoulder and neck regions were advocated. . A social work consultation was obtained prior to discharge. Together with the social worker, Ms. ___ participated in relaxation exercises and guided meditation with some apparent relief. She discussed a willingness to continue relaxation exercises - and perhaps try acupuncture - as modes of pain control and coping. She was given the contact information for health centers where she could pursue these options. It was recommended that Ms. ___ also work with Dr. ___ to identify a counselor with whom to work longitudinally. .

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman, who emigrated from ___ recently, with several days of inappropriate responses as per boyfriend (examples include saying they should get married and saying the ipad was evil), nighttime "shaking" episodes without preceeding illness/fever/toxic exposure, and lethargy found to have MRI with non-specific white matter hyperintensities on T2/FLAIR. # Shaking Episodes: EEG is negative for SZ. # White Matter changes on MRI: Initially treated for possible ADEM with 3 days of steroids, however repeat imaging was more consistent with chronic process (maybe PVL as she was premie at birth). Very long chain fatty acids (to rule out adrenoleukodystrophy) and anti-NMDA antibodies (to work up possible autoimmune encephalitis) were pending at the time of discharge although suspicion is low. # Thyroid Nodule: Seen by endocrine to assess mildly elevated cortisol level who felt this was a stress response and not consistent with ___ syndrome. She was found to have a thyroid nodule and will have a biopsy outpatient. She had some mild abnormalities in her thyroid hormones which are consistent with steroid use and should resolve. # Personality / Mood Changes: She was seen by psychiatry who thinks she may have some elements of psychosis. At presentation she briefly admitted suicidality although this resolved prior to discharge. Her behavior improved on olanzapine. She will follow up with them outpatient. Upon further questioning, we discovered she has multiple stressors including being fired from two jobs, recent move to the ___ 1 month ago, and she was worried she may be pregnant. Ruled out pregnancy with urine HCG. We think she has baseline cognitive dysfunction from a ___ brain injury and may have difficulty coping with stressors and a tendency to act out. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - establish care with PCP to discuss contraception and coordinate her care - thyroid nodule biopsy - follow up with psychiatry, who will manage her olanzapine - follow up VLCFA and anti-NMDA labs
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with behavioral changes and shaking movements. Part of your workup included a brain MRI which showed some abnormalities in your brain. We repeated the MRI to confirm that most likely these abnormalities are old. We think they have been there your whole like and were caused by an injury because you were born early. You had a study called an EEG which showed that your shaking movements are likely not seizures. You were seen by psychiatry and endocrine. The endocrine team found a thyroid nodule that will need a biopsy; this has been scheduled outpatient in ___. You will continue to see psychiatry and neurology outpatient as well. We made an appointment with a primary care doctor as well to help coordinate your care, discuss contraceptive options, and follow up on a couple pending labs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with history notable for osteosarcoma s/p cisplatin/adriamycin and surgical resection c/b allograft displacement and lumbosacral plexus compression s/p removal as well as hypothyroidism presenting for evaluation of two days of intermittent jerking movements of the right foot, followed closely by movements of the right arm and left arm. By the second day of admission, asynchronous and arrhythmic jerking of bilateral lower extremities followed by involvement of the upper extremities and trunk were noted. The episodes were followed by rapid return to baseline with preservation of awareness; moreover, refocusing exercises, such as asking to deliberately dorsiflex or plantarflex the affected foot, resulted in cessation of the movements. Preliminary review of EEG was notable for frequent bursts of generalized slowing, but otherwise did not reveal evidence of seizures. Potential contributors to Ms. ___ symptoms, including marked recent life stressors as well as increasing anxiety and dysthymia, were discussed at length, and we agreed with Ms. ___ plans to establish care with psychiatric providers for management of these concerns. At Ms. ___ request, we resumed sertraline (which she had previously tolerated well) at a low starting dose pending her transfer of care to a psychiatric provider. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES 1. Would avoid excess use of benzodiazepines for management of shaking episodes, favoring biofeedback, refocusing techniques, or CBT as available, supplementing ongoing physical therapy. 2. Outpatient titration of sertraline or alternate agent as indicated. 3. Consider Pain Management referral for chronic right hip pain. This patient was prescribed, or continued on, an opioid pain medication at the time of discharge (please see the attached medication list for details). As part of our safe opioid prescribing process, all patients are provided with an opioid risks and treatment resource education sheet and encouraged to discuss this therapy with their outpatient providers to determine if opioid pain medication is still indicated.
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of episodes of foot and arm jerking movements. EEG monitoring showed that these movements were not due to seizures, and we found that refocusing exercises were helpful in slowing down and stopping these movements. It is likely that these movements are related to your recent symptoms of stress and anxiety, so we agree that you would benefit from seeing a therapist and psychiatrist to help manage these symptoms. At your request, we restarted one of your prior medications (sertraline) that was previously helpful with your mood symptoms, though you will need to establish care with a psychiatric provider to help further adjust this medication or consider alternatives. You may also benefit from biofeedback or mindfulness exercises to help manage your foot and arm movements.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o F w/ a hx of depression, anxiety, self-reported bipolar, and borderline personality disorder, who was transferred from OSH to ___ s/p klonopin overdose and self-inflicted stab wounds to the chest x4. At the OSH, there was reported pericardial effusion. On FAST exam at ___, there was no definite pericardial effusion noted. CXR and CT Chest showed no evidence of visceral injury in the chest. There was no pneumothorax or pneumomediastinum. The patient was initially transferred to the TICU for pulmonary assessment and hemodynamic monitoring. She did not require intubation. After remaining hemodynamically stable, the patient was transferred to the surgical floor. On HD2, the patient had an ECHO which revealed normal left ventricular cavity size with low normal global systolic function. Mild mitral regurgitation. No pericardial effusion. Psychiatry was consulted and the patient was placed under ___. The patient received prn lorazepam to help manage the patient's anxiety. The patient's occlusive wound dressings were removed and dry sterile gauze dressings were placed. The patient's foley catheter was removed and she voided. The patient tolerated a regular diet. The patient's fever curves were closely watched for signs of infection, of which there were none. The patient's blood counts were closely watched for signs of bleeding, of which there were none. The patient received subcutaneous heparin and ___ dyne boots were used during this stay and was encouraged to get up and ambulate as early as possible. At the time of discharge, the patient was doing well, afebrile and hemodynamically stable. The patient was tolerating a diet, ambulating, voiding without assistance, and pain was well controlled. The patient received discharge teaching and follow-up instructions with understanding verbalized and agreement with the discharge plan.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital following a drug overdose and four self-inflicted stab wounds to your chest. You were admitted to the Trauma Surgery service for monitoring. You had an ultrasound of your chest done in the Emergency Department that demonstrated a small amount of fluid around your heart that was not causing any abnormalities. You then had a formal echocardiogram of your heart that demonstrated no abnormal fluid around your heart or other abnormalities in heart function. You were evaluated by Psychiatry and it was determined that you would benefit from further care in an inpatient Psychiatry unit.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF HOSPITAL SUMMARY: ___ year old ___ woman w ___ prior ischemic stroke x 2 in ___ on ASA 325mg dialy at home, depression, hypothyroidism, presented ___ with difficulty walking as a transfer from ___ ED after she was found to have a left basal ganglia IPH on head CT. On admission exam she had an expressive aphasia with decreased verbal fluency and anomia for low frequency objects, which is new per husbands report and is likely ___ IPH. She also has a slight R facial droop, R arm drift, and a R hemianopia which are likely chronic given distribution of prior strokes. She also has an uncertain history of spinal stenosis which was noted on an OSH DC summary, which could be contributing to her walking difficulty. On arrival to ___ her exam remained stable and there were no underlying toxic-metabolic abnormalities. On the morning of ___ she had a repeat NCHCT with CTA that was stable and showed no vascular anomalies. She also had an MRI at 1800 that evening that continued to show stable hemohorrage without evidence of mass or abnormal GRE signal to suggest amyloid angiopathy. Overnight she had a temperature of 101.4 and was pan-cultured by clinically remained unchanged. She was discharged to rehab with a plan to resume aspirin 2 weeks after the bleed (___) should she remain without evidence of deterioration. . ACTIVE ISSUES # Intraparenchymal hemorhage: The exact etiology of IPH remains uncertain though the differential would include transient HTN, cannot exclude malignancy (given history of breast ca in family members, or colon polyps seen on prior imaging), unlikely to be amyloid or AVM based on imaging. Amlodipine was started for hypertension. Aspirin continues to be held in the acute setting, to be restarted in 14 days. Routine EEG was without seizure. Repeat CT ___ evening) was without significant change. . #RENAL: The patient was treated for hypovolemic hyponatremia while in hospital. This improved with IV fluids and she maintanied a normal sodium after repletion without need for ongoing supplementation with NS. . #ID: spiked to 101.4 on ___ overnight. No antibiotics were started and no source identified. This did not recur. This may have been mild, transient aspiration. . #PSYCH: Depression anxiety as baseline for which ativan is prescribed. Decrease to 0.5 mg TID for now to not cloud exam, if mental status stable would increase back to baseline, may increase benzo back to home dose of 1 mg TID to prevent withdrawl. continued Zoloft. . #ANEMIA: New, mild. Nl MCV, RDW. Mildly low retic INDEX (1.5). Possibly due to phlebotomy - iron studies relatively normal. Please repeat CBC in 1 week, further investigate if anemia remains. . INACTIVE ISSUES # GI h/u colitis: unclear if this is IBD or irritable bowel or something else. She was previously on chloroquine and entocort for unknown etiology . # ENDO: Continued home levothyroxine 100mcg daily for hypothyroid. A1c was normal. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # INTRAPARENCHYMAL HEMORRHAGE: - MRI to be done prior to appointment with Dr. ___ - ___ up with ___ as below - Hold aspirin until ___ (2 weeks post-bleed) - titrate amlodipine to SBP < 135 - please follow up final blood cultures . # Anemia: - Follow hematocrit. . # PSYCH: - Resume home dose lorazepam (1mg TID) if mental status returns to baseline . # LEFT FOOT: Near time of discharge, foot was tender - no deformation, no history of trauma or gout. Please watch for gout, evaluate if changes.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for weakness on your right side which was found to be due to a bleed in the left side of your brain. ___ should not take your aspirin until ___ follow up with Dr. ___ it will be decided at that time when ___ can resume aspirin (which inhibits platelet function, predisposing ___ to bleeding). We will arrange for an MRI of your brain prior to your appointment. We also found that ___ have a mild anemia; this should be followed up by your rehab facility and iron studies are pending which may be able to shed more light on your low blood counts. ___ have follow up scheduled with Dr. ___ as listed below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Intentional Drug Overdose: Patient was brought in with pill bottles of multiple different drugs and intentional drug overdose, triggered by the death of a family member. ___ was most consistent with sedative type overdose, with bradycardia, and increased somnolence requiring intubation. While in the ICU she was seen by Toxicology, and was treated with IV Bicarb with serial EKG monitoring. Serial EKGs demonstrated no signs concerning for cardiac conduction abnormality. Within 48 hours of admission her mental status improved significantly. She was extubated and called out to the general floors on ___. She exhibited no significant medical sequelae from her overdose, and by ___ after further monitoring, she was medically cleared for discharge from the hospital. She will be transferred to the inpatient psychiatry service for ongoing psychiatric care. # Benzodiazepine use: prior to admission pt had been on Alprazolam TID scheduled, but admitted quitting cold ___ shortly prior to her overdose. Given this, and signs of mild benzodiazepine withdrawal (diaphoresis, tremors, mild tachycardia), she was loaded with and started on a phenobarb taper in the ICU, which was continued on arrival to the floors. This will be continued on discharge to Deac 4. On ___ she should take 8mg in the morning and evening, on ___ she should take 4mg in the morning and evening, and then stop. # Depression / PTSD: Patient with long history, unclear given not followed here. Given sedation initially, held her psych meds. Psychiatry consulted as above.
summary: As you know, you were admitted to the hospital after an intentional overdose. While it was not clear exactly what was ingested, you were very somnolent on arrival, requiring placement on a mechanical ventilator to help you breathe. Within days of admission you gradually began to wake up. The breathing tube was removed, and you were transferred to the general medicine floors on ___. You were monitored for the next few days, and showed no signs of any serious medical complications from your overdose. You were seen by the inpatient psychiatry team, who recommended transition to the inpatient psychiatry ward for ongoing care.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year old woman with long history and anxiety/depression and possible seizure disorder transferred from ___ after possible seizure activity. She reports that she had a "seizure" last week and presented to ___ where she was found to have toxic level of Dilantin. She was then discharged off of Dilantin and she went into ___ ___ with suicidal ideation two days before the transfer to the ___. She reports that she had a "seizure" while at ___ hence she was transferred here. She reports that in addition of recent stopping of Dilantin, she was abruptly discontinued from her Ativan when she presented to ___. Given the history of seizures, she got admitted to the neurology service and she was started on bedside/video EEG monitoring to assess for her seizures. She was tapered off of Ativan and Neurontin was also slowly tapered in goals of capturing seizures. She had several episodes that were similar to confusion and shaking that she described as her seizures but these had no ictal EEG correlate. She had a normal background as well. Psychiatry was consulted on hospital day #1 and it was determined that she was actually transferred from ___ with ___ for suicidal ideation. They determined that she is not allowed to leave the hospital against medical advice and recommended discharge back to inpatient psych facility once medically cleared. Several medical facilities were contacted with her permission to obtain medical records and we were not able to corroborate any of her medical history including seizure history. Dr. ___ has not seen her since ___ and has never prescribed any seizure medications for her. He believes that she was on Neurontin when he last saw her but it was prescribed by someone else for possible mood stabilization. Patient was found to have several odd behaviors including self-inducing vomiting. Psychiatry continued to follow her during this admission and continued to recommend that she needs inpatient psych treatment and asked neurology to call BEST service once medically cleared. After extensive monitoring, it appears that she does not have epilepsy. She has episodes of shaking and head bobbing with unresponsiviness which are consistent with nonepileptic psychogenic events. She had no epileptic seizures and no interictal epileptiform discharges. We therefore recommended that Dilantin should not be restarted but she will be restarted on Neurontin 600mg every 6 hrs as before given that it may be effective for her as a mood-stabilizer. There were some medication adjustments made during this admission per psych input including discontinuation of Amitriptyline and decrease of Luvox. Patient is completely cleared a from medical/neurologic perspective. BEST service was contacted for placement given that ___ no longer has a bed available for her. However, patient refused referrals per BEST service hence discharged to shelter after extensive discussion. She was seen by psychiatry who did not feel that she still required ___ or inpatient psychiatric care. Patient has been given 24-hour hotline number for referral. Patient requested prescriptions for her meds but she was only given Vistaril prescription since that was the only new medication. She was also given a letter stating that she received Methadone 135mg daily during this admission which was the dose corroborated per ___ but not with ___.
summary: You were transferred from ___ after having a possible seizure activity. You were monitored on bedside/video EEG monitoring for 3 days which captures several events that were similar to your "seizures" and there were no EEG evidence of seizures. Psychiatry was consulted given that you were transferred from inpatient psych facility for possible suicidal ideation. Your Elavil was discontinued and your Luvox was decreased per their recommendation. Given that you did not have any evidence of epilepsy, you are not recommended to restart Dilantin. Your Neurontin was not changed since it may have been prescribed to you for mood stabilization. You were seen per BEST service during this admission who recommended several places but you refused referral.

She was also given a letter stating that she received Methadone 135mg daily during this admission which was the dose corroborated per ___ but not with ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ yo man with h/o depression, multiple suicide attempts, and EtOH abuse with withdrawal seizures, who presents s/p suicide attempt with BB, CCB, and EtOH overdose. . #. Overdose: The patient reportedly ingested 25 tablets of Atenolol and 25 tablets of Norvasc at approximately 5 ___ on ___, after drinking 16 beers the hour prior. He was admitted to the MICU and was bradycardic (56) with first degree AV block, but his BP remained stable with SBP in the low 100-110s. Peak action of BB and CCB should be seen within ___ hours of ingestion and he did not experience any concerning symptoms throughout his MICU stay. His finger sticks remianed in low 100s. Once transferred to the floor on ___, he continued to have stable vital signs, HR between 56-60 with BP 126-174/68-95. Atenolol was restarted and amlodipine was added to maximize BP control on the floor. #. Suicidal Attempt: The patient continues to endorse suicidal ideation, and states that recent overdose was a suicide attempt. Psychiatry has seen him and will manage his depression and SI now that the patient is medically clear. Pt. was on 1:1 observation for his entire stay in the MICU and on CC7. # Hypertension: The patient has a history of hypertension, for which his Atenolol dose was recently increased to 50 mg BID. His Atenolol was restarted ___ and Amlodipinbe was added on ___ for further control. #. Polysubstance Abuse: Pt. has a history of alcoholism and cocaine abuse in the past. He consumed a reported 16 beers along with his meds. His ETOH was 189 on presentation. Pt. was put on a CIWA scale and did not score >10 or require valium. #. Chronic LBP: The patient has a history of LBP, for which he takes Neurontin and Ultram. Both of these were held initially, now on Neurontin with good pain control. Ultram was not restarted. # Gout: Pt. has a history of gout previously treated with indomethacin. Pt. complained of his typical gout pain in his right great toe and indomethacin was given with good effect. Has not needed any further treatment of this, but indocin ordered prn. # FEN: Patient was NPO initially and consumed regular diet on the floor without incident. # Prophylaxis: Subutaneous heparin # Access: none - no longer needed IV access (medically cleared, no IV meds)\ # Code: Full (discussed with patient) ___ on Admission: Trilafon 4 mg po qhs prn insomnia Atenolol 50 mg po BID Neurontin 300 mg BID, and 600 mg at night Prilosec 40 mg po daily Ibuprofen Ultram for pain Multivitamin
summary: You were admitted to the to hospital after an attempted overdose on Atenolol, Norvasc, and alcohol. You were admitted to the ICU for observation and then kept on the medical floor for one day to make sure that you were not suffering from any side effects of the medication you took or any withdrawal symptoms. You were then transferred to psychiatry for further management of your suicidal ideation and depression. You were started back on your blood pressure medication, Atenolol, and another medication (Amlodipine) was also started in order to treat your high blood pressure once you were no longer at risk from the effects of your overdose and your blood pressure came up. You were given indomethacin for your gout symptoms with improvement.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ speaking woman with no known PMH brought to the ED by her cousin after stabbing herself in the L anticubital fossa with a knife. EBL 750ml. Unclear whether the injury was intentional or accidental. In the ED initial VS 79, 114/63, 16, with 96% on RA. Vascular surgery evaluated and contained bleeding, vein ligated. Hct 32.3 on admission, dropped to 23.9 ten hours later. Upon further questioning, c/o SOB w/ exertion and HA/CP x 1 week. ED felt EKG showed ST depression in V2-V4. Patient was given Morphine 2mg IV x1, Nitro SL 0.4 x1. She was admitted to the medicine service for workup of CP and management of anemia. Psychiatry evaluated the pt and felt she had depression w/ psychotic features. Likely unsafe at home, so made ___ with 1:1 observation. . # Anemia - Secondary to blood loss. Hct 32.3 on admission, dropped to 23.9 ten hours later and found to be 24.5 the following morning. Pt was orthostatic so we wanted to transfuse 1 unit PRBCs, however the patient declined this. She was given 2L NS instead. Her Hct eventually increased to 25 and has been stable. She is no longer orthostatic and vitals have been stable. - No need for further H/H trending . # Chest pain - The patient describes the pain in the middle of her chest and epigastric region, intermittent, lasts several hrs at a time for ___ year. We were suspicious for GERD, however the pain does not appear to be related to food intake. LFTs/amylase/lipase were normal, making hepatic or pancreatic etiologies unlikely. Although the ED EKG showed ST depression in V2-V4, a repeat EKG on the floor was normal and cardiac enzymes were negative x3, therefore cardiac ischemia was unlikely. Chest pain is likely in the setting of anxiety. . # Depression w/ psychotic features - The pt is ___ and has had a 1:1 throughout this admission. No reported SI/HI. - Medically stable for transfer to psychiatric unit for further evaluation . # Orthostatic Hypotension: Patient was orthostatic by vitals on the morning after admission. This resolved with fluid bolus, but was again orthostatic on the third day of hospitalization without any symptoms. We checked AM cortisol which was normal so adrenal insufficiency ruled out, fasting glucose was normal x3 so not diabetes, RPR, B12 and Folate also all normal. Given the patient is asymptomatic it is likely neurogenic and can be worked up as an outpatient. . # Cardiac History: There was a question of whether or not the patient had a history of aortic arch aneurysm (per report from ED, unsure where this info came from). The patient denied this history and the patient's cousin denied this history so we felt this was unlikely. There is some chance that the patient has previously been seen at ___ and may have medical records there but we were unable to obtain copies. . # Social Situation: The patient is originally from ___ and does not speak ___. The patient was seen by social work and case management in addition to psychiatric team. She refused to fill out paperwork for ___ though she has no insurance. We attempted to have her cousin help with this situation but this was unsuccessful. -Patient will need assistance filling out patient's application for health care. -Once health insurance is applied for, the patient needs a PCP appointment at ___ ___ for ongoing medical care.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with bleeding from your left arm. You were evaluated with lab tests and were found to have anemia due to the blood loss. We have been monitoring this and giving you fluids, and your blood counts are stable. You also told us that you have had chest pain off and on over the past year. An EKG and blood tests were normal, so the pain is not due to your heart. It is possible that the pain is due to anxiety. You were evaluated by our psychiatrists who felt you were at risk to hurt yourself and would benefit from inpatient psychiatric stay. You were not previously on any medications and we did not start any medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is a ___ with ETOH abuse and prior GI bleeds who was brought in by EMS for intoxication and hematemesis. Active Issues ============= #Alcohol Withdrawal: Pt has hx of alcohol abuse and presented with blood alcohol levels of 435. Her agitation in the ___ was managed with haldol 5mg. She did not have signs of alcoholic hepatitis (AST96, ALT88, AlkPhos 164) and no abd pain. Pt was given IVF, thiamine, VitB12, and folate. On HD1, pt repeatedly scored 0 on CIWA but had a seizure in the ___. She was treated for her seizure as below. She has no hx of EtOH withdrawal or seizures, and pt had no tremors over the course of her stay. She was placed on low-dose standing valium with taper over 2 days. By the time of discharge, pt was not scoring on CIWA and did not exhibit signs of tremulousness and denied any hallucinations or formication. #Seizure: Pt had a seizure on HD1 after scoring 0 on CIWA throughout the course of the day with last read at 18:30. She did not receive any ativan. Pt was found to be seizing by nursing at 19:30 (RUE tremor and cyanosis) for an unknown duration. Her seizure was controlled with IV ativan 5mg. Her BP was 135/82, HR107, SO2100%2LNC, glucose 121. Pt's had an episdoe of desat to 89%2LNC which resolved. Pt was transfered to ICU where she reamined stable without oxygen requirement. She underwent EEG that did not reveal focal seizure activity. CT head was negative for any acute intracranial processes. She was transfered back to the floor after stablization and did not any additional seizure activities prior to discharge. #Hematemesis: Per family report, pt had a bout of hematemesis prior to admission which was not confirmed by pt. Pt presented with Hct of 43.7, and post IVF, pt had a Hct of 36.9 likely secondary to dilution. Pt was given IVF and pantroprazole and GI consult was not concerned for an active bleed. Recent EGD (___) showed mild gastritis with no esophageal varices, and pt was H.pylori negative. Pt's vital signs remained stable, and she has had no further episodes of emesis and no hx of melena. Her hct was stable at 35.6 at the time of discharge. # Anion Gap: Resolving--Pt presented with anion gap of 25 that improved to 22 with IVF and vitamin B12/folate/thiamine supplementation. Her anion gap was likely secondary to alcohol induced ketoacidosis, as pt did not have respiratory sxs, or signs of renal damage. Chronic Issues ============== #Alcohol Abuse: Pt has hx of alcohol abuse. Social worker met with the pt and pt is clear that she does not need or want help/support at this time. She denies interest in alcohol treatment resources and shares multiple times that she is "strong" and "will get through this." #Depression: Pt has been unable to cope with her husband leaving her ___ years ago, and has recently lost her job. She has a hx of alcohol abuse leading to multiple hospitalizations. Pt states that she is depressed and had decreased apetite with weight loss, decreased energy, concentration, interest, and poor sleep. She denies SI/HI. Pt is amenable to therapy. Transitional Issues =================== #Alcohol withdrawal: Pt came in with alcohol level of 435. She has gone through hospital-assisted withdrawal in the past needing only ___ medical therapy. She has no hx of withdrawal sxs. Given that pt requested to leave the hospital, she was educated on the risks of alcohol withdrawal and asked to seek medical assistance should she decide to stop drinking alochol and notice any of the withdrawal sxs. #Full code
summary: You were admitted after being brought in by your family for being intoxicated and vomiting blood. Your blood count was reassuring and you did not have further vomiting in the hospital. During the second day of your admission, you were found to be unresponsive with rhythmic arm movements that made us concerned you were having a seizure. Your seizure was treated and you were briefly transferred to the ICU for monitoring. This seizure likely occurred due to withdrawal from alcohol. Once you were stable, you returned to the general medicine floor, where we treated your withdrawal symptoms with low-dose benzodiazepines and monitored your symptoms to make sure you were not at risk for further adverse events due to alcohol withdrawal. If you wish to stop drinking, PLEASE SEEK MEDICAL ASSISTANCE to prevent life-threatening withdrawal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o ___ homeless F with PMHx of TBI, HIV+, HCV, polysubstance abuse (alcohol, crack cocaine, and heroin), unspecified mood disorder (MDD with psychotic features vs. substance-induced mood disorder), and chronic AH who presented from ___ station via EMS with complaint of multiple head strikes found to have EtOH intoxication and SI admitted to MICU for EtOH withdrawal and CIWA monitoring, now stabilized and transferred to the floor, now medically stable for discharge to psychiatric facility ACTIVE ISSUES: ======================== # EtOH intoxication/withdrawal: on phenobarbital withdrawal protocol. Last drink ___. Patient was loaded with phenobarbital, which may continue if the accepting psychiatric facility is okay with administering, however there is no contraindication to discontinuing. -Phenobarb protocol while inpatient, stopped at discharge -Continued MVI, thiamine, folate -Hydroxyzine PRN for additional agitation -seen by psychiatry and social work - appreciated. # Suicidal ideation: patient repeatedly stating "I have suicidal thoughts" and "I'm depressed," though is unable to fully elaborate. Psychiatry saw the patient and had the following recommendations: -Patient meets ___ criteria for involuntary admission, may not leave AMA, should continue 1:1 observation. -Would hold on any psychiatric medications given acute alcohol withdrawal. -If chemical restraint necessary, please call psychiatry for specific recs. Please be aware that patient has cited "jaw locking" with Haldol administration in the past, thus would consider alternative antipsychotic. - seen by psychiatry - recommended inpatient psychiatric placement, and transferred to deac 4. # FEVER: Patient febrile to 101 in the ED. No localizing signs of symptoms of infection. Suspect that this was likely related to acute ingestion, however given her murmur which has not been documented previously, obtained TTE which showed no evidence of vegetation or endocarditis. # Psychosis: suspect this is part of underlying psych disorder and not necessarily alcoholic hallucinosis. Will defer to psychiatry. -Appreciate Psychiatry recs CHRONIC STABLE ISSUES: ======================== # HIV: not on HAART, started Bactrim for PCP prophylaxis given last CD4 count was <200, will send repeat CD4 count on ___ if still inpatient. Patient was previously not taking her HAART medications, will defer to outpatient. # HCV: not on active treatment - Monitor LFTs, as above Transitioanl issues: Should see PCP re HIV and HCV once psychiatrically stabilized.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for evaluation of numerous falls, alcohol and substance abuse. While you were here you were briefly in the intensive care unit where you were started on phenobarbital to help you withdrawal from alcohol and prevent delirium tremens. You also disclosed that you were not taking any of your HIV medications. You were started on Bactrim as prophylaxis for opportunistic infections, and should follow up with your outpatient providers regarding restarting your HIV medications and for evaluation of your hepatitis C. You were deemed medically stable for discharge to a psychiatric facility.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo RH woman with a hx of a coagulopathy, b/l cerebellar/pontine ischemic stroke, and significant depression, on enoxaparin, who presented with vague symptoms of feeling off balance, nausea, and right face numbness. Noteably she stopped using a cane to help with her balance about 2 weeks ago. She also reports increased stress at home and "not feeling like herself" for the past few weeks. Her neurological exam shows the patient to have a depressed affect, monotonic prosody, maintaining eyes closed throughout the interview despite being able to open them easily when asked, slowed processing, but accurate on tests of attention such as MOYB. Mental status, cranial nerves (with exception of sensation) intact. Full strength. Dysmetria on left FNF and toe-to finger/HKS. Intact on right. Sensation exam is difficult and has significant functional overlay as she splits the midline at sternum and forehead to vibration. She reports decreased cold, pin, vibration, and proprioception on right hemibody including face. Gait shows good initiation, able to ambulate independently with a widened stance, slightly unsteady. Unable to tandem. Sways on Romberg. Labwork so far is normal, although UA/UCx pending. ___ show no blood, unchanged from prior. CXR pending. Overall the patient's symptoms are vague and she has had multiple similar visits in the past. Her exam is not concerning. Etiology of symptoms were consistent with etiology of metabolic/infectious disturbances vs mood related. During this admission, SW consult was obtained and pt was seen by psychiatry. Based on their recommendations, increased bupropion SR 150 mg daily -> BID. Continued sertraline. Pt was already known to psychiatry, and they also scheduled follow up appointments with your therapist and with your psychiatrist. During this admission, pt was evaluated by physical therapy, who thought that her level of functioning was close to her baseline, and her walking probably more limited by anxiety. She was given a prescription for outpatient ___.
summary: You were admitted because you felt off balance and because you complained of numbness on the right side of your body. On neurological exam, we found that your findings were compatible with your previous examinations. Mostly, your neurological problems stem from your previous stroke. Also, we found that you are depressed, and we believe that some of the stress in your mind shows up as physical symptoms in your body. We asked the psychiatry doctors to help with your assessment, and their findings were similar to ours. They suggested increasing your bupropion (Wellbutrin SR) from 150 mg daily to twice daily. You should keep your sertraline and all your other medications the same.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with a PMHx significant for hepatitis C cirrhosis s/p liver transplant in ___, recurrent cirrhosis and portal hypertension s/p TIPS in ___, and s/p Hep C treatment and sustained viralogic response who presented to the ER ___ with left sided chest pressure and pain that radiated to the arm as well as diffuse abdominal pain. These symptoms had been present without remission for a few days. She also described chills, diffuse fatigue, depression, anhedonia, and low energy. She denied trauma to the chest wall. In the ER she had CT abdomen-pelvis that showed no cause of her symptoms. A CXR was normal. EKG and troponins x2 were not indicative of acute coronary syndrome. She was admitted for inpatient stress testing. Given the nature of her exam, which included tenderness over the entire chest wall and entire abdomen, and her historical description of chest pain that had been present for days without change, we felt that this was highly unlikely to be anginal. She requested oxycodone repeatedly and we thought that she did not have a clear indication. Her pain may have been due to musculoskeletal strain of the intercostal muscles, but this would not explain her diffuse abdominal pain without imaging correlate. She has a history of recent discontinuation of her buprenorphine opiate abstinence therapy due to high insurance copay. Overall, we felt that angina was unlikely and that she could have an outpatient stress test safely. We also recommended to her that she seek psychiatric therapy for depression treatment, which appeared to be a major background complaint. She would also benefit from alternative opiate abstinence therapy if there are more cost effective options, such as naltrexone. PROBLEM LIST: #Atypical Chest pain #Cirrhosis due to hepatitis C #History of liver transplant #History of opiate addiction #Abdominal pain #Hypertension #Depression #Chronic opiate addiction, in remission MEDICATION CHANGES: none TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: []behavioral health follow up for titration of antidepressants []reinitiation of opiate abstinence therapy []follow up with PCP and liver clinic in ___ weeks []consideration of outpatient stress testing
summary: you were admitted to the ___ because of chest pain. You had an EKG and blood work to look for heart attack and these were negative. You had no arrhythmias on the cardiac monitor. A CT scan of your abdomen showed nothing to explain symptoms of abdominal pain. A chest Xray showed no broken bones or pneumonia. The ER admitted you to be evaluated for a stress test to look for coronary artery disease. After further questioning, the chest pain that you described was clearly reproducible with pushing on the chest and did not sound like chest pain associated with heart disease. Because of your age and history of high blood pressure, it may be reasonable to get a stress test to look for heart disease, but this can be done by your primary care doctor in clinic. You also described symptoms of depression in the hospital. We would strongly recommend that you see a psychiatrist in the future to augment your depression treatment as citalopram (Celexa) does not appear to be adequate.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with a PMHx significant for hepatitis C cirrhosis s/p liver transplant in ___, recurrent cirrhosis and portal hypertension s/p TIPS in ___, and s/p Hep C treatment and sustained viralogic response who presented to the ER ___ with left sided chest pressure and pain that radiated to the arm as well as diffuse abdominal pain. These symptoms had been present without remission for a few days. She also described chills, diffuse fatigue, depression, anhedonia, and low energy. She denied trauma to the chest wall. In the ER she had CT abdomen-pelvis that showed no cause of her symptoms. A CXR was normal. EKG and troponins x2 were not indicative of acute coronary syndrome. She was admitted for inpatient stress testing. Given the nature of her exam, which included tenderness over the entire chest wall and entire abdomen, and her historical description of chest pain that had been present for days without change, we felt that this was highly unlikely to be anginal. She requested oxycodone repeatedly and we thought that she did not have a clear indication. Her pain may have been due to musculoskeletal strain of the intercostal muscles, but this would not explain her diffuse abdominal pain without imaging correlate. She has a history of recent discontinuation of her buprenorphine opiate abstinence therapy due to high insurance copay. Overall, we felt that angina was unlikely and that she could have an outpatient stress test safely. We also recommended to her that she seek psychiatric therapy for depression treatment, which appeared to be a major background complaint. She would also benefit from alternative opiate abstinence therapy if there are more cost effective options, such as naltrexone. PROBLEM LIST: #Atypical Chest pain #Cirrhosis due to hepatitis C #History of liver transplant #History of opiate addiction #Abdominal pain #Hypertension #Depression #Chronic opiate addiction, in remission MEDICATION CHANGES: none TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: []behavioral health follow up for titration of antidepressants []reinitiation of opiate abstinence therapy []follow up with PCP and liver clinic in ___ weeks []consideration of outpatient stress testing

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old right handed woman with a history of left temporal lobe epilepsy secondary to a TBI, depression, anxiety and PTSD who presented on ___ with increased anxiety and had 2 atypical events while in the emergency room and reported generally increased seizure frequency. She has been hospitalized as recently as ___ for seizures and was increased on Vimpat which she has since decreased in dosing due to apparent toxicity. She reported being under increased stress and potential sleep deprivation over the weekend prior to admission which may have contributed to above. She was seen by psychiatry in the ED and a ___ was placed but on review on ___ was not deemed actively suicidal and this was rescinded. Psychiatry felt that she was anxious and having considerable difficulty with anxiety and depression and had no safety concerns. She was felt to have and some worsening mood symptoms but did not have a plan or intent for self harm although did feel anxious about being alone given the frequency of her seizures. On their recommendations, she was started on a trial of mirtazapine started at 7.5mg HS. They also felt that given her social isolation that consideration of referral to drop in program ___ should be made. She had no further atypical events and a few olfactory seizures and patient reported improvement in anxiety. It was felt that she did not require LTM and was discharged home on ___ with close psychiatry and neurology follow-up. She was continued on her home AED doses and no changes were made to her regimen save addition of mirtazapine to better manage the anxiety and insomnia.

You were reviewed the following day and felt that you were safe and rescinded the section. They also recommended mirtazapine which we started for sleep and anxiety. You were deemed appropriate for discharge and were discharged with follow-up with Dr ___ Dr ___ on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY ======= ___ with PMHx of anxiety/depression who presented with intoxication, altered mental status in the setting of multiple toxic and drug injections requiring brief intubation period in ICU with NAC treatment. Psych and SW consulted with plan for enrollement in partial inpatient program at home for outpatient psychiatric support of anxiety. ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # Toxic metabolic encephalopathy # Medication overdose Patient presented altered/agitated, intubated for airway protection in the ED. Patient endorsed ingestion of Etizolam, roommate's Vyvanse, marijuana and half a bottle of mucinex. Toxicology was consulted in the ED. APAP level was 32 and he was treated empirically with 3 doses of IV NAC, although LFTs and INR were within normal limits. APAP levels returned to ___. He was sedated initially which was quickly weaned and patient self-extubated overnight on ___. Mental status back to baseline. # Rhabdomyolysis: found to have CK with peak 9500 in the setting of restraints placed given agitation prior to arrival as well as fall out of bed during period of intoxication. Treated with IV fluids with downtrend to 5000 prior to discharge and no evidence of ___. Encouraged increased PO intake prior to discharge. # Anxiety/Depression. Patient with significant anxiety since starting college over the past 6 months. Patient with reported anxiety, had been self medicating with ___ agents such as Kavinance and Etizolam he purchased online. Has previously used Sertraline and Paxil and recently started on Escitalopram. Continued Escitalopram at 10mg inpatient. Psychiatry consulted who recommended partial hospitalization program outpatient with coordination with school program as well. Has intake planned for program at home in ___ and provided with resources for back up plans and future directions after intensive program has been completed. Transitional Issues: ========================= - Continued on Escitalopram 10mg daily - Pt should go to the following location for Intake Assessment ___, ___ Ph: ___ Fx: ___ - PHP average length of treatment is ___ weeks in the program, with a minimum being 2 weeks. # Below are other options in case there are difficulties initial program: 1. Secondary PHP Program if ___ has a waitlist at ___: ___ ph: ___ 2. Additional IOP Program option at ___ outpatient behavioral health: ___ ph: ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were feeling more agitated and confused after taking different medications at home. You were found to have elevated levels of acetaminophen levels and received a medication to reduce toxic effects of that. You required a brief stay in the ICU with intubation given confusion. You improved significantly once the medications were cleared from your system. You were seen by our social worker and psychiatrist to set up a partial hospitalization program back at home to continue getting support. Please make sure that you continue drinking fluids after leaving the hospital as your muscle enzyme levels were high and will be cleared with fluids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ woman with a history of ___ disease, HTN, DM, CKD, hypothyroidism, presenting from rehab with confusion. #Suspected metabolic encephalopathy No evidence of infection: urinalysis negative, normal WBC, CXR clear, no abscess or collection or inflammation evident on CT abdomen and pelvis, neg urine and blood cultures. Head CT was negative. In addition, a toxicology panel was negative for ethanol, aspirin, acetaminophen, benzodiazepines, barbituates and tricyclics. 7 months ago, she had normal levels of B12 and TSH, currently has slightly elevated TSH of 4.4 (although this is normal for patients in this age group) with normal free T4 7.7. She has a history of hypothyroidism and not likely contributing to current mental status. She was seen by geriatrics consult service who recommended discontinuation of standing Quetiapine. If agitated can give Quetipine 12.5mg PO or if persisting symptoms can give Ativan 0.25mg PO. We discontinued her Alprazolam. Patient repeatedly spoke about not being able to pay for medical services but this is not a new idea for her as her family said she said this many time over the past ___. # L3 compression fracture was found on CT abdomen/pelvis. Appears to be an old fracture. She was evaluated by the Orthopedic/spine surgeons who found no indication for intervention at this time. Her pain was managed with Tylenol and lidocaine patch. On day of discharge, she was ambulating well with a walker. # Vulvovaginal candidiasis: On exam she was found to have general erythema in groin, vulva and vagina areas. +odor, no discharge appreciated. She was treated with Miconazole powder and Fluconazole 150mg PO x1. CHRONIC STABLE ISSUES: # Chronic kidney disease Stage III: Patient had baseline Cr 1.3-1.6. # Diabetes Mellitus type II: Continued lantus at bedtime. Her blood sugars were controlled with an insulin sliding scale. # Hypertension: During admission she was normotensive. Continued lisinopril and Atenolol. # Hyperlipidemia: Held simvastatin while on fluconazole (5 days). # Hypothyroidism: TSH 4.4 which is within normal limits for this age group. Continued on levothroxine. # ___: Continued Carbidopa-Levodopa and Entacapone at home doses. # Depression: Continued Citalopram. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Consider outpatient ultrasound of ovarian cyst (2.2cm) and renal cyst (2.5cm)- seen on CT during this admission. Radiology notes that provider ___ "consider" obtaining ultrasound if clinically indicated in the outpatient setting non-urgently. - restart her simvastatin on ___ - recommend geriatric psychiatry to follow her # CODE STATUS: Full code. CONTACT: Daughter ___ ___. ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Atenolol 50 mg PO QAM Hold for SBP < 100, HR < 60 2. Atenolol 25 mg PO QPM Hold for SBP < 100, HR < 60 3. Calcium Carbonate 600 mg PO BID 4. Citalopram 20 mg PO DAILY Start: In am 5. entacapone *NF* 200 mg Oral DAILY 6. Tamsulosin 0.4 mg PO HS 7. Januvia *NF* (sitaGLIPtin) 50 mg Oral DAILY 8. Glargine 28 Units Bedtime 9. Levothyroxine Sodium 100 mcg PO DAILY Start: In am 10. Lidocaine 5% Patch 1 PTCH TD DAILY To back 11. Lisinopril 20 mg PO DAILY 12. Nitroglycerin SL 0.4 mg SL PRN chest pain 13. Senna 2 TAB PO HS 14. Quetiapine Fumarate 75 mg PO QHS 15. Simvastatin 40 mg PO DAILY Start: In am 16. Carbidopa-Levodopa (___) 1.5 TAB PO QID 17. Acetaminophen 975 mg PO Q8H:PRN pain 18. ALPRAZolam 0.5 mg PO BID:PRN anxiety Hold for oversedation, RR < 10
summary: You were admitted from your rehabilitation facility for agitation and confusion. During your admission, you were calm and sometimes confused, eating well, able to walk and urinate well. Sometimes, you became confused but we think it was caused by being in an unfamiliar environment with alot of noises and distractions. We stopped one of your medications, Seroquel because you were taking a high dose which can make you sleepy and confused. You had no signs of a urine or blood or lung infection. While you were in the hospital, we found that you had a yeast infection and you were treated with fluconazole 150mg 1 tablet and Miconazole powder to help with the itching. Since we gave you medication for this infection, we have stopped your cholesterol medication, simvastatin for 5 days. Please start taking the cholesterol medication on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ male with the past medical history and findings noted in H+P who p/w acute loose bowel movement and abdominal pain but now with constipation. Etiology most likely flare-up of IBS in setting of social stressors. Less likely c diff colitis given lack of fever, leukocytosis and no BM x24hrs. His hospital course was complicated by ___ with hyperkalemia, non-compliance, abusive behavior to staff, and eventually leaving AMA. # Acute on chronic abd pain syndrome # Constipation # IBS # h/o C. difficile Symptoms most consistent with IBS from above conditions now with constipation. He had been taking his cholestipol which may have been constipating. CT abd reviewed. He had no other symptoms of infection and CT was otherwise benign. We initially trialed bowel regimen with no improvement in symptoms. GI consulted and recommended increasing his nortriptyline, adding Metamucil, and continuing his Librax. Over the course of hospitalization he seemed to have some improvement. He said that only additional narcotics, and especially intravenous narcotics were the only medicines that helped him. Despite counseling about the risks of these and that narcotics were not indicated, he was very upset, yelled, was abusive to staff, and ultimately left AMA. # Depression/Anxiety: # Abusive behavior to staff # Splitting with concern for features consistent with borderline personality disorder Recent home loss. Poor coping skills and supports. D/w primary psych Dr. ___. Recommended inpatient psych who were consulted and agreed with increasing TCA. HE voluntarily stopped about 20 different prescriptions as an outpatient but could not make the connection that this decision was harmful as it caused him to have worsening anxiety which contributed to worsening pain. He did not acknowledge this, but was able to say that adding back some of the medicines was a good idea. SW also consulted for coping and resources support, but no alternative housing was accepting of him. When he was not receiving additional narcotics, he would yell at staff, only eat some foods and what was described by nursing as behavior that berated the kitchen staff trying to help him. He was very pleased with increased attention when he was hypoglycemic, but said that any doctors that saw ___ in the past 4 days never came to see him and were not his doctors. ___ evidence to the contrary, he held these beliefs. I had multiple discussion on the phone with psychiatry. We discussed the possibility of adding back Buspar, but he left AMA prior to this happening and had the capacity to leave AMA. ___ and hyperkalemia - caused by decreased PO intake and concurrent NSAID use. His Cr peaked to 2.6, but improved to 1.4 on the day of leaving AMA. His K was high as 7.1, but improved with IV fluids, insulin, dextrose, Calcium. It was 5.3 on discharge. He was counseled about low K diet and stopping Lisinopril. # Glaucoma # Dry eye Seen by ophtho ___, started on Brimonidine, artificial tears, continued here. # Ear hearing Loss: He knows he needs to follow up with ENT about this.
summary: You were hospitalized for evaluation of increased abdominal pain with diarrhea mixed with constipation which was due to your IBS flare. You were evaluated by the GI team. You were also seen by our psychiatry team and our social worker to help you with anxiety and to help find you a living placement. Because of your low intake, you developed kidney injury and high potassium. These did not resolve prior to leaving, but you chose to accept this risks of organ failure, heart arrhythmia, heart attack and death and leave regardless. This is known as "AMA" or against medical advice. Please avoid eating bananas, potatoes, tomatoes, and foods containing potassium. Please drink plenty of water.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is an ___ year old male with atrial fibrillation, subtherapeutic on Coumadin, seizure isorder on Keppra, hyperlipidemia, and prior strokes who presents with multiple transient episodes of aphasia with a negative MRI and normal flow on MRA. He also has an unclear history of seizure for which he was previously on dilantin and is now on levetiracetam. He is transferred from ___ for continuous video EEG monitoring. # Neuro: Mr. ___ presented with episodes of aphasia, history of lacunar strokes and depression. He was transferred from ___ with a negative stroke work up and concern for seizures. His Keppra was increased from 500mg BID to ___ mg at ___. Mr. ___ was maintained on Keppra 750mg BID during his hosptailization. He had 24 hour continuous EEG monitoring which showed no epileptiform activity. It is recommended that he continue Keppra 750 mg BID at home. During this hospitalization, he continued Aspirin, Atrovastatin, Citalopram and Coumadin 3mg qday. His INR was 2.0 on ___. We spoke to his PCP, ___ who will follow up his INR outpatient. The etiology of his episode could be a transient ischemic attack or seizure. # CV: Mr. ___ has atrial fibrillation on Coumadin, although his Coumadin was subtherapeutic. His Coumadin was increased to Coumadin 3mg qday with an INR goal of ___. His INR was 2.0 on ___. His heart rate was stable throughout hospitalization, however when he was about to be discharged on ___, his has episodes of tachycardia to 140s. Due to concern for RVR, cardiology was consulted. Cardiology recommened Metoprolol 25mg qam.
summary: You were hospitalized at ___ for EEG monitoring. Your episode of change in speech and change in ability to read is either due to a seizure or transient ischemic attack. Your work up for stroke at ___ did not show that you had a new stroke. Therefore, it was thought that you might have had a transient ischemic attack or seizure. On EEG, it did not show that you had any seizures or epileptiform activity during the time you were monitored. We have increased your seizure medication, Keppra from 500mg to 750mg twice a day. We have also increased your Coumadin to 3mg every day since your INR was low and that can predispose you to having a stroke or transient ischemic attack.

You underwent an aspirin desensitization so that you could get aspirin for the procedure. This went well with no signs on an allergic response. You are now being transferred back to ___ for continued psychiatric care. No baths or pools for one week, no lifting more than 10 pounds for one week.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female w/ pmh significant for SLE, CNS involvement, seizure disorder, GERD, HTN, migraines, PTSD, dissociative identity disorder p/w paranoia who is brought in by EMS after she was found to be non-verbal. . # AMS: Per her outpt social worker, pt is normally an intelligent female who articulates herself well. Her initial presentation was a significant change from baseline. Unclear initially if her presentation was a toxidrome, neurological or psychiatric. LP WNL, head CT negative, EEG WNL, so likely a primary psych process. Psychiatry evaluated her on the day of dispo and felt that she was safe to go home. . # Psych: Pt has extensive psych hx and is overall minimally verbal- non-verbal for the 3 days PTA as per her son. Possibly due to severe depression leading to catatonia. Pt's Urine tox and serum tox were negative, making it unlikely that pt may have overdosed on meds. Psychiatry put patient on a ___ initially, but after patient's mental status improved and she was responding to questions psychiatry felt that pt did not meet criteria for an inpatient psychiatric hospitalization and removed the ___. Therefore we sent her home with next day psychiatric follow-up and strongly encouraged her to attend the f/u appt. . # Hypokalemia: pt has hx of hypokalemia on Kcl at home, so likely had not taken her KCl in the past few days PTA which caused her admission K of 2.5. EKG was unchanged. She was given 60 mEq of KCL PO prior to coming to the floor. Given another 40 mEq IV at 1000NS at 150cc/hr on the floor overnight and again another 40mEQ in 500cc NS, and another 40mEQ in 500NS x2 and on dispo was 3.3 at last check (but given another 60 mEQ prior to leaving). . # Low grade temp: resolved. She is on Mycophenalate which is immune supressive therapy for the SLE, so this may put pt at higher risk for infection. We checked UCx and BCx which are pending. Her initial UA was a dirty catch and the resulting UCx was positive for bacteria, but her second UA (clean catch) was negative, so we did not treat this. Her LP was negative for infectious process also. Her fevers resolved as her MS improved. . # Tachycardia: pt tachycardic on admission, somewhat responsive to IV fluids and Ativan. Pt was also on propanalol at home and on verapamil at home. Uncertain how long pt had not taken this meds, possibly leading to tachycardia if stopped abruptly. We restarted her home meds including propanolol and verapamil once patient was taking PO's. . # HTN: continued on diovan, verapamil and propanolol when she started taking POs. . # SLE: on mycophenalate. . # SZ: uncertain when pt's last seizure was, she was on Keppra and topamax, which we continued while she was an inpatient. . # GERD: continued her PPI . # Contact: ___ ___ (father of children) PENDING LAB RESULTS: BCx ___: pending
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for unresponsiveness, a fast heart rate and an elevated blood pressure. We did a head CT which was negative for any acute process, we did an EEG which was negative for seizure activity and a chest X-ray which was negative for any acute process. You became well enough that you were able to be sent home. Psychiatry saw you and felt that you were not a danger to yourself and others and approved you going home also. We want you to follow up with your social worker ___ tomorrow (___) and try to get involved in a longer-term psychiatric therapy group. We started you on a medication regimen for H. Pylori, which you have been previously diagnosed with, but not treated for. This infection may explain your recent weight loss. You will take these antibiotics for a total of 14 days to finish on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with a history of hypertension, dyslipidemia, RA and Sjogren's with sudden onset vertigo. Her hospital course is as follows: . # Vertigo: Most likely secondary to BPPV given positive ___ maneuver. No arrhythmias on telemetry or history of heart disease/palpitations. Neuro exam, vertigo, and gait instability improved during the course of her admission. Her neck pain raised the concern for dissection. CTA of the head/neck was performed. The read demonstrated normal vertebral arteries but was suboptimal for the head. Therefore, an MRI was performed. This showed normal parenchyma, and normal vasculature with fetal type PCA with good flow and no evidence of cerebrovascular disease. She was taught the Epley maneuver and urged to follow up with her PCP as soon as possible. . # EKG changes: ___ raised the concern for ACS given her EKG (see above). However, patient was without symptoms of chest pain, shortness of breath, and her only complaints seem more related to vertigo throughout her hospital course. 2 sets of cardiac enzymes negative over 2 days during her stay here, 1 set negative or borderline from ___. The patient does have cardiac risk factors with HTN, dyslipdemia, and smoking. Telemetry throughout her hospital course continued to show stable repolarization abnormalities with no evidence for acute changes or arrhythmias. Her BP was controlled with home amlodipine and HCTZ, and lisinopril was started. She would benefit from an outpatient cardiac stress test. . # Hypertension: The patient presented with SBP in 190-200s, which was reduced to the 150-160s by discharge. Her home amlodipine dose (5mg daily) was increased to 10mg; she was continued on HCTZ and lisinopril 5mg daily was started. She was discharged with instructions to follow up with her PCP in the next week and repeat her electrolytes and kidney function in the next ___ weeks . # Hyperlipidemia: LDL 178, HDL 39, Trig 275. She was started on pravastatin 20mg daily. LFTs only with ALT 44, otherwise unremarkable. She will follow up her cholesterol and LFTs in ___ weeks. . # Depression: Patient's home prozac and imipramine were continued. She continued to be in good mood and affect throughout her hospital stay. . # Restless leg syndrome: Patient's imipramine . # Rheumatoid Arthritis: We continued her home regimen of nabumetone, leflunomide chloroquine, and ultram. . #? UTI: She was asymptomatic though her UA did show trace blood. Her urine culture was contaminated. She should have a repeat urinalysis as an outpatient
summary: You were diagnosed with a condition called BPPV, or benign paroxysmal positional vertigo. This is caused by small particles in your middle ear which are out of place. They normally serve to tell your brain the position of your body and head but when they move out of place, this results in the vertigo that you experienced. You have been instructed on how to perform the Epley maneuver to reposition these particles. If you continue to feel vertiginous, you should perform this maneuver 3 times daily. This should result in an improvement in the vertigo/dizziness. We have increased the dosage of one of your medications due to your high blood pressure. You should now take 10mg of amlodipine (Norvasc) daily instead of 5mg daily. We have also started a new medication called lisinopril which you should take 5mg daily. Both of these changes are to better control your blood pressure. You have also been started on pravastatin for your cholesterol.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old ___ female with a history of AVNRT s/p ablation, HTN, HLD, depression who presented with palpiations and chest heaviness secondary to a pulmonary embolus. ACTIVE PROBLEMS: 1. PULMONARY EMBOLUS: Her palpitations and subxiphoid heaviness were caused by a right lower lobe segmental pulmonary embolus that was likely precipitated by excessive bed rest over the past several weeks. She lacked other PE/DVT risk factors such as trauma, surgery, HRT, smoking, or personal/family history of clotting. She is up to date with age-appropriate cancer screening. There was no sign of right heart strain on EKG. She remained hemodynamically stable. She was started on a heparin drip in the ED and was then placed on subcutaneous lovenox the following morning on the medicine floor. She began coumadin that day and was later discharged with a lovenox bridge, and will continue anticoagulation for ___ months. She received lovenox teaching with the aid of her son who interpreted, and communicated understanding. The need for regular activity was stressed to avoid stasis and further clotting. 2. PALPITATIONS/TACHYCARDIA: the patient has a long-standing history of palpitations which have recently become more frequent. Several episodes of brief tachycardia lasting minutes were captured on telemetry with rates achieving 150s at rest. While an EKG was not obtained at the time, telemetry suggested a possible atrial tachycardia which may explain her symptoms. Her toprol XL was uptitrated to 50mg daily. Electrophysiology follow-up was recommended. 3. ANXIETY/DEPRESSION: She has become increasingly distressed by her palpitations and had essentially restricted herself to bedrest to avoid further episodes. She was continued on venlafaxine and reassured at length that normal activity is safe and essential. After confirming with her PCP, she was given a trial course of ativan to help control worsening anxiety. INACTIVE PROBLEMS: 4. HYPERTENSION: She was modestly hypertensive in house, and was continued on her home antihypertensives. PENDING TESTS AT DISCHARGE: NONE TRANSITIONAL CARE ISSUES: - will need EP follow-up of possible aberrant atrial tachycardia and palpitations - will need monitoring of INR for ___ months of anticoagulation
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to palpitations. You were found to have a blood clot in your right lung (pulmonary embolus). You were started on blood thinners to treat this clot. You are being started on a pill called Coumadin that you will need to take for at least ___ months. You will need to have bloodwork checked frequently in order to make sure that your Coumadin dose is appropriate. Since the Coumadin takes several days to work, you also need to take Lovenox (another blood thinner given as a subcutaneous shot) for the next several days. Please continue this medication until your primary care doctor tells you to stop. In addition, you were found to have an intermittent fast heart rate and your metoprolol dose was increased.

text: Brief Hospital Course: =================== PATIENT SUMMARY =================== ___ h/o GERD, ___ esophagus, IBS, anxiety/depression, ___ disease, hyponatremia, who presents as a PCP referral for suicidal ideation, found to be hyponatremic. Her hyponatremia was thought to be predominately SIADH, with contribution from low solute intake. She was treated with fluid restriction and salt tablets. She developed a simple cystitis and was treated with ceftriaxone for this. Psychiatry placed this patient on ___, and she was admitted to inpatient psychiatry after medical stabilization. =================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [] Hyponatremia: Please continue to monitor the patient's serum Na regularly. Patient should have chem 7 checked in 7 days ___ discharge Na 136. [] The patient has iron-deficiency anemia. She received 2 doses of 125mg ferric gluconate while admitted. She may benefit from additional IV iron repletion given her chronic GI symptoms. [] Please ensure that the patient has age-appropriate GI cancer screening given iron deficiency anemia. [] Weight loss: The patient endorsed weight loss this admission. Please continue to monitor her weight and determine whether she would benefit from age-appropriate cancer screening if her weight does not improve. [] GI: The patient is scheduled to see an outpatient gastroenterologist for chronic dyspepsia/GERD symptoms. She may benefit from 24h esophageal pH monitoring or other testing. Please ensure that the patient is able to see her gastroenterologist. =================== ACTIVE ISSUES =================== #Euvolemic, hypotonic hyponatremia: Labs were most c/w SIADH, though a component of low solute intake may be at play. Recently upped her dose of sertraline as outpatient, which correlated with the rise in Na. AM ___ low, but review of ___ records from ___ reviewed a normal ___ stim test. Renal was consulted. She was placed on fluid restriction at 1.5L and NaCl tablets at 2g TID. #Depression #Suicidal ideation Psychiatry saw patient and placed her on ___. They felt her presentation c/w MDD. Per ___ chart also some c/f anorexia and laxative abuse in the past. Zoloft was stopped given hyponatremia. She was given home dose of Ativan, 0.25-0.5mg BID PRN for anxiety. Psychiatry reevaluated patient and felt that she would benefit from inpatient psychiatry admission. #Simple cystitis UA positive with symptoms. No systemic s/sx or leukocytosis. Urine culture grew out Citrobacter Freunii complex. She was treated with 3d ceftriaxone 1g daily. No systemic s/sx. As Citrobacter Freunii may have inducible beta-lactamase, repeat UA and culture were obtained, which showed that her simple cystitis had cleared. #Iron-deficiency anemia Patient's IV iron store deficiency is 880mg from hgb 8.7 to 12 accoutning for 500mg iron store repletion. Patient received 2x 125mg ferric gluconate while admitted. She may benefit from ongoing IV iron repletion given her chronic GI symptoms. #GERD #Dyspepsia Patients with Parkinsons have a high prevalence of GERD. Already has outpatient follow up with a gastroenterologist where she may benefit from 24h esophageal pH monitoring and H pylori testing since BID PPI doesn't seem to be effective. A component of this may be associated with IBS as well. Her symptoms have been a point of distress for the patient. She was put on 40mg BID pantoprazole and standing BID ranitidine. Additionally given Maalox and simethicone PRN. =================== CHRONIC ISSUES =================== #Weight loss Her weight loss is likely in the setting of depression and possibly decreased appetite with severe GERD symptoms. If weight does not improve, she would benefit from age-appropriate cancer screening. #Thrombocytopenia Per review of OSH records, plt fluctuated between ___ to 200s. Etiology unclear. Stable in-house. ___ disease Not on meds at home. #CODE: presumed full #CONTACT: ___ Phone number: ___
summary: You were having suicidal ideation and abdominal/GI discomfort at home. You were found to have a low sodium level. This is likely due to the Zoloft (sertraline) you were taking. We stopped this medication and you should not take it in the future. To treat your low sodium level, we also put you on a fluid restriction and gave you tablets of salt, per discussion with our Renal doctors. Our psychiatrists saw you and determined that you should be admitted to an inpatient psychiatric facility once your sodium level was corrected. You developed a urinary tract infection, and we gave you antibiotics for this. Please continue to work with the psychiatry colleagues to improve your mood.
