text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN and depression who presents with R ankle fracture after first seizure s/p ORIF. . ACTIVE ISSUES: #. Seizure: This was his first seizure. Per the primary team and neurology, it was thought to be due to clonazepam withdrawal and/or excesive tramadol use in the setting of chronic sertraline use. Electrolytes and Blood Glucose were normal, ruling out an abnormality of these as a cause. Patient without infections type symptoms to suggest this a cause. CT head ruled out any major CNS pathology as cause and MRI further confirmed this. His EEG was unremarkable. The patient refilled his clonazepam on ___ per pharmacy which is a 30 day supply, but reportedly took more than prescribed and ran out for a week prior to presentation. He did not seize during his hospital stay. We re-started his clonazepam at home dose which was reconciled with pharmacy and we held tramadol. We recommended he refrain from driving x 6 months and he did not need follow up in epilepsy clinic amd AEDs were not recommended per neuro. . #. R ankle fracture/dislocation: Was reduced by ortho in the ED and patient was cleared for surgery by medicine. His operation was on ___ (see operation report above). He was neurovascularly intact and had issues with pain control. We placed him on his home morphine dose of 60mg BID but we held his tramadol. He was on a dilaudid PCA the first day and changed to PO oxycodone and toradol with adequate pain control. He is discharged to rehabilitation in a stable condition. Per Ortho, post operatively he needs a splint for ___ days, physical therapy, touchdown weight bearing, 3 doses of ancef, and 4 weeks of lovenox 40qd. . #. Urinary incontinence: thought to be due to opiates. He was straight cathed during his hospitaliztion for a bladder scan of > 500cc. He was urinating well on discharge. . INACTIVE ISSUES: . #. HTN: we continued his lisinopril and monitored his electrolytes which were within normal limits. . #. Depression: we continued his seroquel, sertraline and nortriptyline . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Please follow up with - orthopedics follow up after - pain medication and neuropsychiatric medication titration given interaction between tramadol and sertraline, and withdrawal of clonazepam - the patient deals with depression after having lost his job. Please assess and follow up.
summary: You were admitted with a right ankle fracture and a new onset seizure. For your fracture in the emergency room the orthopedic doctors reduced your ___. Once you were cleared for surgery you were emergently sent to the operating room for operative repair (see below for the details of the exact names of the procedures). You had problems with pain control given your acute on chronic pain. Given that you were taking more tramadol in the events leading up to your seizure, we avoided this medication and advise against using it with your sertraline as the combination decreases your seizure threshold. Your pain was well controlled on oxycodone/toradol and your home morphine dose. Physical therapy evaluated your function for discharge. For your seizure, we did an extensive workup. Initially we thought that your recent change in medication usage caused your seizure(not taking clonazepam for a week, and increasing your tramadol frequency while you were on sertraline). We looked at an Electroencephalogram to look at your brain electrical activity and did not find anything concerning. We also did an MRI of your brain to look for any structural causes for a seizure. Given that there were no other obvious reasons for the seizure, the change in medications is the most likely cause. You should NOT be driving for the next six months.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ yo anxious woman who presented with 1 day of increasing weakness in the setting of chronic nausea/weight. She was seen in the ER by Neurology and not found to have any acute neurological process contributing to her symptoms. She was admitted from the ED for management of her nausea and weakness. Weakness: Patient was found to have full strength in her extremities and a normal neuro exam. This is unlikely an acute neuro process. She was seen by neurology in ER who agreed and recommended outpatient MRI if her outpatient Neurologist felt it was necessary. Weakness likely d/t anxiety/psychiatric illness vs. malnutrition. Serial neuro exams were performed while she was in house and were stable. Additionally, she had her outpatient EEG done as an inpatient and the results of this will be followed by Dr. ___ as an outpatient as planned. Psychiatry placed her on ___ and she is awaiting an inpatient psychiatry bed. Nutrition: Patient's electrolytes were followed and she was given 2 L IVF to help with patient's subjective dehydration. Her orthostatic vital signs were negative and her laboratory work did not indicate that she was signficantly dehydrated (creatinine was within normal limits). Nutrition was consulted in house and recommended nutritional supplements. On the day of transfer, the pt reported that she was able to tolerate these supplements. You will be transferred to the psychiatric service from the medicine service. This will be for ongoing management of your depression.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Anorexia/Depression: Ms. ___ was admitted for a rapid decline of her weight in the last 2 weeks in the setting of multiple social stressors in her life, including the anniversary of her mother's death, the recent inpatient psychiatric hospitalization of her youngest daughter, and her husband's continued alcoholism. She endorsed suicidal ideations and a plan for death via starvation. She was aggressively hydrated with IVF and started on the eating disorder protocol. Due to her suicidality, she was maintained on 1:1 observation. Nutrition was involved and MVI, thiamine, and folate supplementation was started. She was very compliant with the protocol, eating 100% of her meals and not requiring liquid nutrition or NGT. Her weight did not make a significant change. Her admission weight was 118 lbs. Her discharge weight was 118.5 lbs. She was offered low doses of either olanzapine or quetiapine at meal times to assist with meal related anxiety. She continued to be followed by psychiatry daily. In a multi-disciplinary meeting with psychiatry, nutrition, social work, nursing, and the medical team it was decided that transfer to an inpatient psychiatric facility for further treatment of her depression/suicidality was the best treatment for her. She was continued on her home fluoxetine and lorazepam at night. She was medically stable for transfer at discharge with normal electrolytes, and stable vital signs. Orthostasis: The patient remained persistently orthostatic despite aggressive IVF resuscitation. Clinically, she was volume replete. After further investigation, she was noted to have been previously diagnosed postural orthostatic tachycardia syndrome and autonomic dysfunction. Thus her continued orthostasis was not due to volume depletion. This is not a sign of medical instability. She was started on midodrine and titrated up with improved effect. She should also continue to wear TEDS stockings at all times. Thyroid: Mildly elevated TSH with mildly depressed free T4 without major signs of hypothyroidism. Given the risk of weight loss with thyroid supplementation, we held off on treatment. Further decisions regarding thyroid supplemenation deferred to PCP. Chest pain: Ms. ___ had one episode of chest pain in the ED that resolved with IVF. Her EKG was without signs of ischemia. Two sets of cardiac enzymes were negative. Osteoporosis: We continued calcium and vitamin D. Anxiety: Continued lorazepam in the evening. She was given the choice of olanzapine or quietiapine with meals. Precautions: She had a 1:1 sitter.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of weight loss and worsening depression with suicidal thoughts. While you were in the hospital, we monitored your vital signs and nutrition. You are being discharged from the hospital to an inpatient psychiatric unit. You physicians and counselors will help you with your depression. You have orthostatic changes in your blood pressure. This means that your blood pressure will decrease when you are standing. This had been diagnosed by a neurologist several years ago. Given your continued orthostasis, a medication called midodrine was added to support your blood pressure. You should continue to wear the TEDS stockings.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ yo woman with a pmh of depression/anxiety/alcoholism/Hep C (s/p intermittent treatment) who presents for an ___ overdose. She arrived to the MICU intubated and sedated. Given her subsequent medical improvement and lack of further symptoms, she was transferred to the general medicine service on ___. Hospital course by problem summarized below. ___ overdose c/b by toxic metabolic encephalopathy and failure to protect airway: The greatest danger from TCA overdose is in the first 6 hour period after ingestion. The patient ingested 17 tablets at 1am on ___. She was kept on a bicarb drip during this period and hourly electrolytes, ABG's, and EKG's were monitored. Her pH was targeted to between 7.45-7.55 as alkalosis helps prevent TCAs from entering the tissues. Her QRS remained less than 100 msec. She did not require intralipid. Her vent settings were titrated per above and she began to follow commands by ___. She was extubated in the am on ___ but remained extremely somnolent and had pupils that were essentially fixed despite being able to follow commands as a result of her intoxication. Her mental status improved over the next several days, and by the morning of ___ she alert and oriented to person, place and location. # EtOH Withdrawal. Patient initiated on phenobarbital pathway given progressive tachycardia and agitation. Received loading doses on ___ (240 mg x 1, 180 mg x 2 q3h) with taper. Despite the phenobarbital protocol, however, she became extremely agitated and required 60 mg of haldol over a 24-hour period on ___ in addition to a maximum-strength precedex drip and restraints. She continued to require haldol and restraints on ___ but her mental status/agitation improved by ___. On ___, she was able to give a history but was still not fully oriented and frequently tried to wander out of the unit. She was transferred to the general medicine service on the evening of ___. Her mental status cleared and she was back to baseline on discharge. #Tachycardia: Likely due to TCA overdose as isolated in nature and trending down with time. Also likely contributed to by alcohol withdrawal. She experienced tachycardia to the 160's after her acute TCA intoxication had resolved, which was likely from alcohol withdrawal and agitation. She experienced intermittent episodes of SVT on ___ stimulated by physical activity and relieved with valsalva. On ___, she was started on metoprolol given her frequent spikes in heart rate. On the morning of ___ her metoprolol was discontinued. She had no further episodes of tachycardia during the day on ___ or ___ following discontinuation of metoprolol. #Depression/anxiety: Patient has reportedly taken psychiatric medications (prazosin, tramadol, welbutrin) in the past but is poorly compliant. She will need outpatient psych ___. #Delirium: Psychiatry was consulted for suicide attempt on ___ and the patient was given a sitter. She could not be assessed for capacity, however, given her agitation and delirium. Her delirium subsequently improved over the next few days with resolution of her medical symptoms. #Heptatitis C: Patient has been treated intermittently as she follows up poorly with healthcare. She will need PCP ___ after discharge.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for an overdose of amitryptylline. You had a very rapid heart rate and altered mental status. At ___ you received activated charcoal and gastric lavage which yielded broken pill fragments. You were started on bicarbonate and fluids to treat your overdose, and you were transferred to us at ___ for further management. While in the intensive care unit, you continued to be treated for your overdose. You were intubated and sedated to help you breathe. You were also treated for alcohol withdrawal with a phenobarbital taper. You had a rapid heart rate which was initially controlled using metoprolol, but the medication was subsequently discontinued and your heart rate remained normal. Given your improvement you were transferred to the general medicine floor, and referred to psychiatry for further evaluation and treatment. It is important for you to see your psychiatry and primary care providers regularly. Please see further instructions below.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, AF on Coumadin, prior CVA, morbid obesity, OSA, chronic back/hip pain on narcotics, severe anxiety on benzodiazepines, tobacco use, and ongoing active alcohol abuse found to be hypotensive at ___ along with labs showing ___ and hyponatremia. Hypotension, ___, hyponatremia all resolved with IVF and medication adjustment. Major issue became ? of alcohol withdrawal vs severe anxiety. # Alcohol abuse # Mild alcohol withdrawal complicated by # Reportedly severe baseline anxiety: In long discussions with SW, it sounds like he has had several months of worsening anxiety, and he says this drove him to drink as the Ativan prescribed by his PCP was not bringing enough relief. His presentation on arrival to floor was c/w mild alcohol withdrawal versus severe anxiety and he was treated with a diazepam CIWA. This was quickly tapered over course of 48 hours, and hydroxyzine was added PRN for anxiety. He was seen by psychiatry for ? of his severe anxiety and further treatment, and duloxetine was recommended. This was initiated in house and continued at discharge. PCP was comfortable with continuing this medication outpatient. - Continue Vistaril PRN anxiety for now - Continue duloxetine 20 BID for now, can uptitrate in the future as needed # Hypotension # ___: Creatinine improved from 1.8 on admit to 1.3 on arrival to floor after receiving 1 L NS suggesting ___ etiology. Down to 0.9 the following day, so resolved. His baseline creatinine appears to be as high as 1.1. - Continue to encourage PO intake - Held diuretics at discharge # Hyponatremia: Received IVF with delayed improvement. Likely from diuretic overuse and hypovolemia. Resolved. # Chronic pain due to # Hip OA and # Low back pain: Stable, no signs of respiratory depression or MS changes. He was very compliant with narcotics contract while here. - Continue gabapentin, methadone, oxycodone - Discussed with PCP - ___ of these meds were prescribed by me at discharge # Hypertension: Stable. - Continue BB as below - Resumed Lisinopril - Discontinued atenolol/HCTZ/triamterene given ___ presentation # pAF with prior CVA: Patient refused heparin bridge per Dr ___. Coumadin was continued here and he had a therapeutic INR at discharge - Continue Coumadin, titrate to INR ___ - DCd atenolol permanently given this episode, started Toprol instead -- continue 25 BID for now # OSA: Stable. - Continue CPAP Billing: >30 minutes spent coordinating discharge to home
summary: You were admitted with hypotension related to alcohol abuse and ___ of your antihypertensive medications. Your medications were adjusted and you were given fluids with resolution of your low blood pressures. You were treated for severe anxiety and alcohol abuse while here. You were treated empirically for an alcohol withdrawal syndrome with diazepam, which is a benzodiazepine. You were seen by the social workers and the psychiatrists. You were started on duloxetine, which is a ___ forming medication that can treat both anxiety and chronic pain. You need to follow up with a therapist and ideally get a psychiatrist to treat your anxiety issues. In the meantime, we are discharging you with close PCP follow up and a visiting nurse service that will ideally provide social work support in the home.

Will follow up with psychiatry at ___, does not need neurology f/u. H/o hepatitis with transaminitis while inpatient - so depakote not resumed. Will discharge on low dose lamotrigine for mood. Will f/u with ___ GI/hepatology.

F with history notable for T2DM, IBS-C, depression who presented with nausea, vomiting, abd pain x3 days, found to have hyperglycemia (FSBG 421, Hba1c on admission 16%), pH 7.39, anion gap 22, ketonuria, HCO3 19. text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ___ female with locally advanced pancreatic cancer initiated on FOLFIRINOX ___, GERD, depression (no cardiac hx; echo in ___ showed EF 55%) presented from ___ clinic for urgent evaluation of chest pressure during first infusion of ___, with dynamic ST elevations on EKG. On cath, found to have myocardial bridging but no significant disease (had mid LAD-myocardial bridging), and no evidence of vasospasm. #CORONARIES: without significant disease #PUMP: LVEF>55% (___) trivial mitral regurg #RHYTHM: sinus ================================= ACTIVE ISSUES: ================================= #Chest pain I/s/o ___ #Myocardial bridging #ST Elevations in I and aVL She experienced chest pain I/s/o first ___ administration and was found to have ST elevations in 1 and aVL. Coronary catheterization revealed significant myocardial bridging (with significant reduction in caliber during systole) in LAD. This was in the setting of active chest pain during cardiac catheterization. There was no observed vasospasm on cath, despite ___ being known to cause vasospasm. It is possible that she had underlying myocardial bridging that was made severe by vasospasm caused by ___. She had no other lesions on cardiac catheterization. She was started on diltiazem, discharged with dose of 120mg XL daily, for the myocardial bridging. Her echocardiogram revealed asymmetric LVH with septal wall hypertrophy and inducible LVOT. This is possibly consistent HCOM, and one could consider cardiac MRI in future (pending status of pancreatic cancer). She will follow up with cardiology-oncology shortly after discharge to discuss further workup, such as cardiac MRI. She will also require re-evaluation regarding appropriateness of ___ and whether other agents could be used instead. #Pancreatic cancer #Risk of neutropenia Followed by Dr. ___ as an outpatient. Continued symptomatic management, diazepam, zofran, reglan, immodium prn. CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES: #Duodenitis/GERD -continued omeprazole, ranitidine #Depression/Grief - continue sertraline ========================= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ========================= [ ] She will require re-evaluation regarding appropriateness of ___ and whether other agents could be used instead. [ ] TTE ___ showed asymmetric LVH with septal wall hypertrophy and inducible LVOT. Possibly c/w HCOM, could consider cardiac MRI in future. #NEW MEDS -diltiazem 120 XR PO daily -loperimide prn for diarrhea (chemotherapy side effect) #CHANGED MEDS: none #DICONTINUED MEDS: none #CODE STATUS: full, presumed #CONTACT: Husband, ___ ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Ondansetron 8 mg PO Q8H:PRN nausea 2. Prochlorperazine 10 mg PO Q12H:PRN nausea 3. LORazepam 0.5-1 mg PO QHS:PRN anxiety, insomnia 4. OxyCODONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q6H:PRN Pain - Severe 5. Omeprazole 40 mg PO BID 6. Sertraline 75 mg PO DAILY
summary: You came to the hospital because of chest pain. In the hospital, your EKG showed that parts of your heart were getting insufficient bloodflow. You had a procedure ("cardiac catheterization") that looked at the vessels that supply your heart. It showed that some of the vessels were squeezing down because they were buried in the heart muscle("myocardial bridging"). We have put you on a new medication called diltiazem to help prevent this condition from causing you any problems down the road. You also probably had some squeezing of the blood vessels ("vasospasm") that can occur with the chemotherapy that you received starting on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ was admitted for events concerning for seizure. He had multiple events while monitored on EEG, and none showed any evidence of epileptiform activity. The semiology of his events was also consistent with non-epileptic events. Please see the included EEG report from ___. His brain MRI was largely normal - other than two small punctate FLAIR hyperintensities consistenent with prior trauma, and of no clinical significance (i.e. these will not cause left-sided weakness or seizures). A number of psychosocial stressors were identified, he was seen by psychiatry, and ultimately cleared for discharge in his family's care. # PSYCHOGENIC NON-EPILEPTIC SPELLS (PNES) ___ had numerous events during his admission, some lasting up to 15 minutes with dramatic whole-body shaking. These *were not* generalized epileptic seizures on EEG. He remained mostly alert throughout these, remained hemodynamically stable, and oxygen saturation remained normal. He was often able to follow commands (to squeeze fingers) through these events. None of these features are consistent with true generalized seizures. **They do not require treatment with abortive medication such as Ativan**. **He does not require evaluation in an emergency department for these events**. **He should be monitored during them only to ensure his physical safety.** # PSYCHIATRIC PROBLEM Per psychiatry evaluation: ___ is a ___ year old with history of TBI and alcohol use with DWI 4 months ago, who presented to ___ following two seizure-like events, now with ongoing non-epileptiform seizure-like activity. On interview, Mr. ___ endorses increased anxiety, depression, and daily alcohol use since the end of a relationship 4 months ago. He states that recent seizure-like events have not occurred prior to this past week, and he is comfortable discussing the fact that they are not epileptiform seizures. On mental status exam, he is somewhat blunted appearing at first but becomes more euthymic over interview, and describes overall depressed mood, but is linear and organized, as well as cognitively intact. Diagnostically, the most pressing psychiatry problem is alcohol use disorder. Although the patient minimizes the problem, there are indications that is has been present for a few years and has accelerated in recent months. It is challenging at this time to disentangle the effects from the patient's TBI and alcohol use disorder on his current mood, anxiety and episodes of seizure-like activity. The larger developmental context of all of his current psychiatric symptoms should be noted as well. Mr. ___ went through an extended period of severe distress with what appeared to be an extended life-threatening illness in his triplet sister. He did relatively well subsequently until he lost the opportunity to go to a ___ and play varsity sports because of his various injuries. This appears to have "derailed" him and both he and his parents say he has never fully recovered from this loss. EKG-telemetry findings: During this stay, the patient had asymptomatic episodes of around ___ beats without P waves on his telemetry confirmed with EKG. Cardiology was curbsided with this EKG - they felt that this was likely a junctional rhythm that overtook the normal sinus rhythm. Because the junctional rhythm rate was around the same rate as his normal rate - in the ___, there was no further workup or intervention needed. This was confirmed to be a benign finding. PLAN: -Patient is amenable to alcohol rehab following hospital discharge; this will be done as an outpatient -Will defer initiation of psychiatric medications at this time; as it is difficult to decipher mood/anxiety from alcohol use at this time He was seen subsequently by psychiatry and cleared for discharge home with family on ___.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for symptoms concerning for epileptic seizure. We monitored you on EEG and were able to record many of these episodes and saw no epileptic electrical activity with them. We identified a number of stressors and symptoms of depression that are frequently seen with your presentation of psychogenic non-epileptic spells (PNES). There are no medications needed to treat this condition and it is not inherently dangerous to your brain or body. The treatment involves psychology/psychiatric therapy to address the underlying causes, treatment of depression, and addressing alcohol use. For this, your family has arranged for alcohol rehabilitation program.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ male with a history of depression, anxiety and remote history of EtOH abuse who presented with tremors, mental status changes and hypertension, and was found to have electrical (but not clinical) seizures on EEG (without clinical correlate) at initial presentation. He was admitted to Medicine, then transferred to General Neurology. He was started on valproic acid and lacosamide, had a non-contrast head CT which showed no acute process, and further EEGs showed only moderately severe encephalopathy with no seizure activity. His brain MRI showed "no evidence of acute hemorrhage, infarction, or enhancing mass lesion, with minimal nonspecific T2/FLAIR signal hyperintensity in the periventricular and subcortical white matter which in a patient of this age may be reflective of chronic small vessel ischemic disease." Despite treatment of his seizures, ___ interactions continued to be inconsistent, with severe anhedonia, low speech output, flat affect, and low appetite. He consistently endorsed profound depression and hopelessness, but denied desire or plan to hurt himself. Infectious, metabolic, vascular, and paraneoplastic etiologies were pursued, with no evidence on labs or imaging to suggest a specific etiology for his cognitive decline. Psychiatry was involved and recommended restarting some pharmacotherapy for his depression. He was started on citalopram and seroquel, achieving a dose of 30 mg citalopram and 100 mg seroquel by the time of transfer. By transfer, ___ was speaking more fluently and with more output, and completing accurately all of the tasks in the mental status exam. He still had some deficits in memory occasionally. Ultimately, in conjunction with Mr. ___ and his family, determined that an inpatient psychiatry admission could be helpful for his severe depression and anhedonia. ___ also had some mild hyponatremia during the admission, which corrected after his food and drink intake increased. He also had some scrotal swelling, and an ultrasound showed a likely epididymal cyst vs loculated hydrocele. The swelling subsequently improved. Of note, ___ had a chest CT to eval for malignancy (given concern for paraneoplastic process) which revealed no tumors, but did show a small 6 mm right upper lobe nodule which will require follow-up.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of increasing confusion and memory problems over the past few months. Here, we found that you were having seizures. You were started on medications to stop the seizures and had no further seizures here. Your depression and hopelessness continued, however. We sent off many labs and did many tests to evaluate for other causes of depression and cognitive changes, but everything was negative. Because depression can cause severe memory problems and confusion, our colleagues in psychiatry evaluated you and together with you and your wife, determined that you could benefit from an inpatient psychiatric admission to better manage your depression symptoms and optimize treatment. We are hopeful that you will continue to improve with further care.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with history of idiopathic ___ disease recently admitted to the neurology service for worsening Parkinsons who presents from ___ ___ due to an "exacerbation" of her ___ as well as suicidal comments. Medicine admisison: # Suicidality: psychiatry felt she was a danger to herself while in the ED and put on ___ with 1:1 sitter. Psych initially felt that she was delirious, however, so it was felt that she could not be completely evaluated. On ___ after her mental status cleared, she confessed profound sadness over her physical limitations and ___ diagnoses, but she was no longer endorsing SI and was very forward thinking and hopeful. She was continued on her ativan and sertraline # Delirium: initially on evaluation in the ED was felt to be delirious. She did endorse mental fogginess as well as dry eyes, blurry vision, and dry mouth/throat, so it was was felt her mental and physical symptoms were due to anticholinergic side effects of tolterodine. This was stopped and her mental status returned quickly to baseline. She also was found to have a UTI which was treated with macrobid. She was admitted to the medicine service. #URINARY TRACT INFECTION: UA on admission concerning for infection, she was given a 3 day course of macrobid, culture grew ___ E. coli. Two days later, patient still with burning on urination, so UA/UC were collected once again on ___ which was very positive and she was started on a 7 day course of ciprofloxacin on discharge on ___. Neurology admission: # ___ disease: Patient had subacute worsening in symptoms in context of UTI however she then developed acute worsening of her gait such that she was unable to stand, she was transferred to the neurology service from medicine on ___. Her mobility issues seems to stem from severe postural instability with significant retropulsion on assessment. She had an LP on ___ which revealed WBC 1 RBC 9 Pr 38 Glc 55. This was sent for MS profile given a family history of MS which revealed no abnormalities and no OCBs. ___ antibody was also tested although her presentation is unlikely stiff person syndrome and is pending. Given her worsening Parkinsonism, sinemet was increased from 1 tab 100/25 tid to 2 tabs 100/25 tid on ___. Amantadine was added on ___. She seemed to have a mild improvement in her rigidity and bradykinesia on this regimen but still severe problems with mobility and standing. She was deemed appropriate for transfer to ___ where they have a PD unit on ___ and she has outpatient ___ with Dr ___ on ___. # Urinary incontinence. Due to worsening urinary incontinence for which she was using pads, urology were contacted and she has an outpatient appointment for urology ___ for this on ___. UPDATES TO THE PROVIDER - ___ dose doubled to 50/200 TID from ___ TID - Amantadine 100mg BID added - Discharged on ___iprofloxacin for UTI ___ - ___ for UTI - culture pending - Has movement disorder (___) and ___ clinic follow up appointments OUTSTANDING ISSUES [ ] F/U repeat urine culture [ ] F/U serum ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ for confusion and suicidal ideation. We stopped your medication called Tolteradine, which we think was contributing. Because you were unable to walk, we doubled your dose of Sinemet, and started you on another medication for ___, called amantadine. We did a lumbar puncture (spinal tap which was entirey normal and MS panel was also normal. After this, we got you a bed at a rehabilitation facility which works with ___ patients. We also talked to the urologists and set you up with them as an outpatient, for your urinary incontinence (with Dr. ___. Lastly, while you were here, we noted that you had a urinary tract infection, which did not respond to 3 days of macrobid, so we sent you out on a 7 day course of a stronged antibiotic called ciprofloxacin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ yo M with PMH of CAD s/p CABG/PCI, ICM 27% s/p ICD, VT s/p ablation, PVD s/p numerous PCI, CVA, LV thrombus, recent admission for chest pain who presented with dizziness and chest pain. . # Dizziness/orthostasis: Pt presented with complaint of dizziness and was found to have symptomatic orthostatic hypotension in the ED. Most likely from volume depletion in the setting of higher furosemide dose and recent addition of spironolactone and isosorbide. Admission weight was below that of dry weight with an increase in his creatinine. Diuretics were held on admission and pt hydrated with IVF and symptoms improved. Lasix was restarted at a lower dose (20mg) and patient was educated about avoiding abrupt positional changes to avoid dizziness. Spironolactone was held at discharge . # Chest Pain/CAD: Patient reported chest pain during episode of dizziness prior to admission. Troponins remained stable at 0.03, no ischemic changes on ECG. Had no chest pain during admission. Continued home pravastatin, ASA, metoprolol, imdur. . ___: Cr 1.5 on admission from baseline 1.1. Most likely prerenal from over-diuresis after increase in Lasix dose and initiation of spironolactone. Improved with IVF and adjustment in diuretics. Cr 1.2 on day of discharge. . # H/o V-tach: Status post ablation ___. Patient had several short runs of NSVT on telemetry during admission. EP followed and recommended continuing amiodarone. . # chronic systolic CHF: Pt appeared hypovolemic on initial exam, weight remained below most recent dry weight of 61.6kg. Held spironolactone and decreased Lasix dosing as above. . # History of LV thrombus and CVA: On warfarin which was briefly held and restarted at lower dose for increase in INR from 2.3 to 3.2 within 24 hours. Will need close outpatient INR monitoring now that he is on amiodarone.
summary: You came to the hospital because you were having dizziness and chest pain. You were found to be dehydrated when you came in, so you were given fluids and your diuretics were temporarily held. We also found that you has some injury to your kidneys which resolved after getting IV fluids. Your lasix was restarted at a lower dose to prevent you from getting dehydrated again in the future. You may continue to feel some dizziness when you stand or sit up quickly. This is to expected from the medications you are on, but these medications are important for the health of your heart. To reduce any dizziness symptoms you have, you should stand or sit up slowly to allow your body time to get used to the change in position. Your dizziness should resolve within a few minutes.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with h/o HOCM identified on cardiac MRI previously and hypertension who presented with bilateral lower extremity edema, reduced exercise tolerance, and profound anemia. and was found to have decompensated diastolic heart failure. ACTIVE ISSUES ========= # Acutely decompensated congestive heart failure with preserved ejection fraction Overall, his worsened lower extremity edema was felt to be due to acutely decompensated diastolic heart failure. TTE revealed E/E' of 19 and E/A of 1.44, indicative of diastolic dysfunction. There also was a component of high output heart failure in the setting of profound anemia, discussed below. Despite previous diagnosis of HOCM on cardiac MR, HOCM physiology was not prominent on TTE upon admission. Perhaps also playing a role in his lower extremity edema was hypoalbuminemia/possible liver dysfunction with an albumin of 2.9 and a history of low grade transaminitis. He was diuresed with IV furosemide then transitioned to a maintenance dose of 40 mg daily furosemide upon discharge. Overall with diuresis he was well over 7L net negative. Discharge weight was 72.2 kg. His home atenolol was switched to metoprolol succinate. He will have close cardiology ___ scheduled. # Normocytic anemia Profound anemia noted upon admission likely also contributed to his decreased exercise tolerance. Rectal exam revealed brown stool that was guaiac positive, suggestive of subacute bleeding. He received 2U pRBC and his hematocrit bumped appropriately. As he maintained his hematocrit over the course of several days, GI endoscopy was deferred to the outpatient setting. Anemia labs were notable for iron deficiency, and he was started on 325 mg TID ferrous sulfate. He should have a hematocrit check on ___ during his PCP ___, and have EGD/colonoscopy in the very near future. Discharge hematocrit was 29. # Hyponatremia His sodium was in the 128-131 range while inpatient despite diuresis. His hyponatremia was presumed to be hypervolemic given persistent volume overload. There were no previous sodium values available to understand if this is chronic or not. Given overall stability, further monitoring and work-up was deferred to the outpatient setting. # Urinary retention/asymptomatic bacteriuria Mr. ___ has had an indwelling foley catheter for some time. It remained in place while inpatient. Urinalysis revealed findings consistent with colonized foley, and urine culture taken in emergency department revealed pan-sensitive MSSA. As he had no fever, leukocytosis or dysuria, no antibiotics were administered. He apparently underwent recent urodynamics study and is to ___ with urology as an outpatient. CHRONIC ISSUES ========== # Hypertension A stable issue while inpatient, and he was continued on his home diltiazem with his beta blockade switched from atenolol to metoprolol succinate as above. # Hyperlipidemia A stable issue, he was continued on his home pravastatin. # Cataracts A stable issue, he was continued on his home artificial tears. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ============== #Anemia: - Needs very close hematocrit monitoring at ___ ___ - discharge hematocrit 29 - Needs very close GI ___ (requested) for ___ for evaluation of subacute hct drop and +guiac. #HOCM/CHFpEF: -Patient discharged on 40mg lasix daily. Atenolol transitioned to metoprolol succinate. Please monitor BP, HR, and renal effect of both meds and titrate accordingly - discharge weight is 72.2 kg #LFT abnormalities -LFTs elevated as outpatient and slightly as inpatient. #Pancreatic duct dilatation -Cardiac MR in ___ read as 6mm pancreatic duct dilatation and recommended dedicated MRCP.
summary: You were admitted because of leg swelling and shortness of breath. We believe this is due to a condition known as heart failure. You were a given medication called furosemide to remove fluid from your legs and lungs, and your breathing improved. We discharged you on this medication. We also stopped your atenolol and started metoprolol because this leads to better control of your heart rate. You also had a very low blood cell count. We believe that you may be bleeding from your gastrointestinal tract. It is of utmost importance that you follow up with gastroenterology to undergo imaging of your upper and lower GI tracts. An appointment has been requested, and you should receive a call within ___ days after discahrge. If you do not, please call the number listed below. We started you on iron this visit. This will help keep your blood cell count high.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with h/o HOCM identified on cardiac MRI previously and hypertension who presented with bilateral lower extremity edema, reduced exercise tolerance, and profound anemia. and was found to have decompensated diastolic heart failure. ACTIVE ISSUES ========= # Acutely decompensated congestive heart failure with preserved ejection fraction Overall, his worsened lower extremity edema was felt to be due to acutely decompensated diastolic heart failure. TTE revealed E/E' of 19 and E/A of 1.44, indicative of diastolic dysfunction. There also was a component of high output heart failure in the setting of profound anemia, discussed below. Despite previous diagnosis of HOCM on cardiac MR, HOCM physiology was not prominent on TTE upon admission. Perhaps also playing a role in his lower extremity edema was hypoalbuminemia/possible liver dysfunction with an albumin of 2.9 and a history of low grade transaminitis. He was diuresed with IV furosemide then transitioned to a maintenance dose of 40 mg daily furosemide upon discharge. Overall with diuresis he was well over 7L net negative. Discharge weight was 72.2 kg. His home atenolol was switched to metoprolol succinate. He will have close cardiology ___ scheduled. # Normocytic anemia Profound anemia noted upon admission likely also contributed to his decreased exercise tolerance. Rectal exam revealed brown stool that was guaiac positive, suggestive of subacute bleeding. He received 2U pRBC and his hematocrit bumped appropriately. As he maintained his hematocrit over the course of several days, GI endoscopy was deferred to the outpatient setting. Anemia labs were notable for iron deficiency, and he was started on 325 mg TID ferrous sulfate. He should have a hematocrit check on ___ during his PCP ___, and have EGD/colonoscopy in the very near future. Discharge hematocrit was 29. # Hyponatremia His sodium was in the 128-131 range while inpatient despite diuresis. His hyponatremia was presumed to be hypervolemic given persistent volume overload. There were no previous sodium values available to understand if this is chronic or not. Given overall stability, further monitoring and work-up was deferred to the outpatient setting. # Urinary retention/asymptomatic bacteriuria Mr. ___ has had an indwelling foley catheter for some time. It remained in place while inpatient. Urinalysis revealed findings consistent with colonized foley, and urine culture taken in emergency department revealed pan-sensitive MSSA. As he had no fever, leukocytosis or dysuria, no antibiotics were administered. He apparently underwent recent urodynamics study and is to ___ with urology as an outpatient. CHRONIC ISSUES ========== # Hypertension A stable issue while inpatient, and he was continued on his home diltiazem with his beta blockade switched from atenolol to metoprolol succinate as above. # Hyperlipidemia A stable issue, he was continued on his home pravastatin. # Cataracts A stable issue, he was continued on his home artificial tears. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ============== #Anemia: - Needs very close hematocrit monitoring at ___ ___ - discharge hematocrit 29 - Needs very close GI ___ (requested) for ___ for evaluation of subacute hct drop and +guiac. #HOCM/CHFpEF: -Patient discharged on 40mg lasix daily. Atenolol transitioned to metoprolol succinate. Please monitor BP, HR, and renal effect of both meds and titrate accordingly - discharge weight is 72.2 kg #LFT abnormalities -LFTs elevated as outpatient and slightly as inpatient. #Pancreatic duct dilatation -Cardiac MR in ___ read as 6mm pancreatic duct dilatation and recommended dedicated MRCP.

text: Brief Hospital Course: REASON FOR HOSPITAL ADMISSION; ___ DVT, cholangiocarcinoma (recently diagnosed, presumed metastatic) stopped coumadin/lovenox 1 wk prior for supraclavicular LN FNA, p/w progressive SOB, with acute dyspnea today, with tachycardia, found to have multiple PEs on CTA, and LLL pulmonary infiltrate. # Pulmonary Embolism/DVT: Patient has a history of asymmetric leg swelling (L>R) in early ___, and was found to have a DVT, and was started on coumadin with a lovenox bridge. Recently stopped these medications about a a week prior to admission and was taking Vitamin K for reversal over the last 3 days in preparation for a supraclavicular LN FNA bx. CTA on arrival with multiple PE's, R>L. Does have some evidence of R heart strain on CT, with elevated troponin and elevated BNP, though EKG without signs of right heart strain. Bedside ECHO with R ventricular dilation and hypokinesis, which along with the elevated BNP and troponin, as well as the eccentric nature of the clots on CT, possibly suggesting some element of chronicity regarding some of his PEs. LENIs only revealed superficial clots in both legs. He was put on 140mg lovenox BID weight-based dosing. Moving forward he should be maintained on lovenox rather than warfarin given his concurrent malignancy diagnosis. Initially in the ICU he required a nonrebreather to maintain O2 sats but by the time of discharge was using 4L NC comfortably on the floor. He was discharged to rehab for physical therapy. # Leukocytosis: Initially 23 on admission down to 18 by discharge. CT findings suggestive of potential PNA v. atelectasis in left lung base but no cough and pt had no fevers throughout the admission. He was initially given levoflox to cover CAP but this was immeidately stopped and CXR findings felt to be atelectasis, with elevated WBC in the setting of DVT/PE. # Acute Kidney Injury - may have been in the setting of some decreased PO intake at home due to shortness of breath but rapidly corrected initially with IV fluids. # Hemorrhoid - pt had one episode of bleeding per rectum and had a hemorrhoid on exam. He stated that he has had hemorrhoids in the past. He was on anticoagulation for 2 days subsequent to that episode without further bleeding and with Hct stable (in fact, trending upwards slightly). He was started on standing bowel regimen to prevent straining. # Intrahepatic cholangiocarcinoma, presumed metastatic: Diagnosed in ___, during workup for his DVT/coagulopathy. Was planned for supraclavicular LN FNA bx per above for definitive diagnosis of metastatic disease and staging, but now that cessation of anticoagulation has resulted in thrombotic complications, per his oncologist this procedure will be deferred, perhaps indefinitely. He is currently being offered a palliative regimen of gemcitabine plus cisplatin. He will follow up as an outpatient with his oncologist. # Hyponatremia - Sodium was stable around 130 throughout his hospital course, on the day of discharge it was 129. Pt reported severe dehydration prior to admission which was consistent with creatinine bump and he had begun to hydrate effectively PO by the time of discharge, so this may have been hypovolemic hyponatremia however it was stable throughout the hospitalization. # Pancreatitis - pt is known to have pancreatitis secondary to his cholangiocarcinoma with an area of necrotic pancreas seen on prior abdominal CT scans. Abdominal pain was well controlled with occasional PO dilaudid. # Hypothyroidism: h/o Graves' disease, s/p radioiodine therapy, on levothyroxine, which was continued during his hospital stay. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: Please ensure pt follows with his outpatient oncologist Pt will need lifelong lovenox therapy
summary: You were admitted with shortness of breath and found to have a large blood clot in the lungs. We re-started your blood thinners and this should be adequate treatment. Your breathing improved but you were still requiring oxygen at the time of discharge. Our physical therapists thought you needed rehab and so you were sent to rehab for more physical therapy. It is really important you take colase and senna every day to prevent constipation which would cause hemorrhoidal bleeding. You really don't want to aggravate that bleeding while you are on blood thinners or it could pose a very dangerous problem! It is important that you schedule a follow-up apppointment with your oncologist Dr. ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with ESRD on HD, HFrEF (EF 25%), HIV not on medications, mechanical valve, medications non-compliance, who presented with one day of chest pain and shortness of breath. He was found to have hypoxemic respiratory failure due to volume overload and was admitted to ICU for Bipap. He received HD and his hypoxemia improved. His last HD session was on ___. Patient left against medical advice (AMA) on ___ with a subtherapeutic INR. # Acute on Chronic Systolic CHF: # Acute pulmonary edema # Acute hypoxemix respiratory failure: Last admission in ___ his EF was ___ from 35-40% prior at ___. ProBNP >70000 this admission. He was discharged on metoprolol, though patient was not aware of this medication. Hypoxia was likely secondary to increased fluid/solute intake, volume overalod in the setting of missing HD sessions, as well as a pre-existing right pleural effusion that was unable to be fully addressed with HD. There was a possibility of hypertension-induced pleural effusion, however has right pleural effusion at baseline and appears to live chronically at these pressures. He initially required BiPAP, then was transitioned to NC and eventually to RA. Patient had TTE this admission which showed LVEF=30%. He was placed on nitro gtt and underwent HD with improvement of his O2 saturation. #Mechanical MVR: Had MVR in ___ after MSSA endocarditis requiring anticoagulation. He was discharged on warfarin last admission, however was subtherapeutic on admission at INR 1.2. He was treated with warfarin with a heparin drip bridge. He wanted to leave on ___ for a funeral. We strongly recommended that he stay in the hospital for the heparin drip until INR is therapeutic but he refused. He understood and acknowledged the risks of having a stroke if he leaves the hospital and stops the heparin. He decided to leave against medical advice. #Medication Non-Adherence: Patient was discharged on warfarin last admission as well as metoprolol. He came in without knowledge of which meds he was taking. He had belonging with him on arrival, but not his medications and he reported only taking his medications when he wanted to. Per ED notes, he stated his medications are managed at ___. SW was consulted to aid in pt maintaining med compliance. #Hypertension: Patient presented with SBP's of 180's without symptoms of exertional chest pain, changes in vision, or original headaches. He was hypertensive on prior admission. With history of HTN and medication non-compliance, there was high suspicion he lives in these pressures at baseline. Patient was initially treated with nitro gtt and then was transitioned to home losartan 100 mg daily and labetalol 200 BID. ================ CHRONIC ISSUES ================ #ESRD: HD MWF, received HD ___, and ___ without issue. #HIV: Not currently on HAART. Followed by Dr. ___ at Healthcare for the Homeless. CD4 count 649 at last admission ___. #HCV: Not on treatment. HBV serologies previously checked, immune. ***TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:*** - Please discuss importance of compliance with dialysis as scheduled - Please discuss importance of compliance with warfarin - Review all medications with patient as he does not know his med list - Check INR on ___ and adjust dose of warfarin as needed - Patient needs to follow-up with a nephrologist
summary: You came to the hospital because of shortness of breath and chest discomfort. You were found to have a lot of fluids in the lungs. You were monitored in the intensive care unit. You underwent dialysis with removal of fluids and your breathing improved. You were also being treated with a blood thinner to protect you from strokes in the setting of having a heart valve. Your blood is not thinned enough and you should stay in the hospital for a blood thinner through the vein (heparin) until the effect of Coumadin kicks in. You understood and acknowledged the risks of having a stroke if you leave the hospital and stop the heparin. You decided to leave against medical advice.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ male with a history of coronary artery disease, s/p CABG in ___, s/p ___ dual chamber pacemaker implant in ___ for 2:1 AV block, right sided nephrectomy (non malignant etiology), who presented to the ___ ED with acute on chronic heart failure. . #SOB: Pt gives h/o gradually increasing SOB over the past few weeks culminating in an episode of acute shortness of breath while lying in bed the night before admission. CXR did not show any acute process. Iniitial thoughts included a CHF exacerbation or worsening pulmonary hypertension. On admission, both his lasix and HCTZ were held because of a rising creatinine (3.3 from baseline of 2.5-3.0). Echo showing normal LV systolic function, and no major changes from his prior echo in ___ making worsening pulmonary hypertension less likely. We felt that this was likely a CHF exacerbation, especially given an elevated BNP on admission. Renal was consulted in house who agreed, and recommended restarting his diuretic regimen despite his rising creatinine. Furthermore, the decision was made with renal to start dialysis as an outpatient given his worsening renal function. He was restarted on his lasix 20mg/40mg QID regimen and HCTZ the day of discharge. He will follow up with renal as an outpatient to establish hemodialysis. Of note, he endorsed no further episodes of SOB in the hospital after his initial symptoms at home. . # CORONARIES: previous cardiac cath performed in ___ and found 3 vessel CABG was patent. Has not had a cath since then, and no h/o any CP, palpitations, syncope, presyncope, fatigue, leg pain/cramps. Of note, troponin was elevated at 0.07 on admission but remained stable on recheck and can likely be attributed to his renal failure given his normal CKMB. ___, and imdur were contiued. . # RHYTHM: A sensed A paced, rate at 75/min, PR interval prolonged signifying first degree heart block, wide QRS intervals seen, LBBB like pattern, RVH. Pt without symptoms of lightheadedness, dizzyness, CP, or shortness of breath throughout admission . # CKD : Pt with baseline CKD secondary to his nephrectomy, however on admission creatinine was 3.3 up from his baseline of 2.5-3.0 likely secondary to recent increase in lasix dose for increasing SOB as an outpatient. Cr trended 3.3-->3.2-->3.4-->3.3 over course of admission. He was seen by nephrology in-house who discussed the ultimate need for dialysis with him, which will be set up as an out-patient. In the meantime, renal recommended restarting his diuresis for CHF which was initially held on admission. Pt was sent out on a regimen of lasix 20mg/40mg alternating everyother day. He was also continued on his home HCTZ 12.5 mg daily. . # HTN : Pt initially continued on his home regimen of Carvedilol 6.25 BID, isosorbide dintrite 20mg BID, and hydralizine 50mg TID. However, BPs were noted to regularly fall below 100 systolic throughout admission, so hydralazine was d/c'd and pt was not sent home on this. . # ANEMIA : Hct remained at his baseline throughout admission. We continued Ferrous sulfate, vit B6, B12 . # GOUT: continued allopurinol . # WOUND CARE: pt with very fragile skin which would easily tear, especially with the EKG stickers. Upon discharge, he was set up with home ___ wound care for this
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of shortness of breath, as well as worsening kidney function. We did blood tests and don't believe you had a heart attack. We also did an echocardiogram (heart ultrasound), which looked to be relatively stable from your prior echo in ___. We feel that you likely had an exacerbation of your heart failure which subsequently improved. We initially held your lasix and hydrochlorothiazide because of your kidney function, but after being seen by our kidney doctors, we restarted you on them because of your heart failure. Because of your kidney function, however, you will likely need dialysis soon, and this will be set up through Dr. ___. Your breathing improved and we feel you are safe for discharge. It is very important that you weigh yourself daily at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ female with ESRD secondary to congenitally undeveloped kidneys and with deafness (Alports disease) s/p renal transplant in ___ and then again in ___ complicated by advanced chronic allograft dysfunction, recently initiated on HD, CMP 40%, DM2, HTN, presenting from HD with new afib. ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # Decompensated Systolic Heart Failure (EF: 40-45%): BNP 47000, significantly elevated. Evidence of hypervolemia on exam, with accumulation in the lungs primarily. Recently underdialyzed (has been doing half sessions), likely resulting in accumulation of fluid. TTE in ED showed small pericardial effusion, with no evidence of tamponade. Dry weight from last d/c 50kg, 52kg on admission. In the ICU, the patient underwent HD for fluid removal, with removal of 3.5L fluid on ___. Home diuretic regimen restarted on ___. Patient subsequently remained euvolemic, and was discharged on her home diuretic regimen. # Hypotension: Patient hypertensive on admission, possibly in the setting of 3.5L fluid removal with dialysis the day prior vs medication effect from rate control meds, given she was bolused with IV metoprolol due to afib w/RVR. Bolused with 1L IVF. Did not require pressors, but remained normotensive with only b-blockade. Hypotension resolved prior to discharge from the ICU. # CONS Bacteremia: 1 bottle grew CONS; started on vanco/cefepime (day ___ prior to speciation in setting of hypotension with dialysis. Vanco/cefepime stopped ___ once speciated as CONS, presumed contaminant. Patient became febrile to 100.9 on ___ X 1 with no further fevers or presumed infectious etiolgy. # Afib w/RVR: CHADS2 3. New-onset, likely triggered by volume overload in setting of HFrEF with underlying enlarged LA without evidence of ischemia, thyroid disease, or concern for VTE. Given high risk of CVA, started on coumadin and maximized oral beta blockade with NSR x 24 hours. Should continue to optimize volume status and enteral rate control as an outpatient. Coumadin started as an inpatient, but was initially supratherapeutic so held for several days. INR and coumadin dosing and will be managed by the ___ clinic moving forward. # Troponinemia: Trop peaked to 0.20 on admission, CK-MB otherwise flat. Likely in setting of Afib w/RVR. Patient otherwise without chest pain or EKG findings suggestive of ischemia. # ESRD: Secondary to ___'s s/p transplant x2 now on HD. The patient was continued on HD. Her home Tacrolimus and Predisone were continued. # ___ pain: sigificant pain: Has been limiting dialysis sessions. The patient was continued on her home Gabapentin, Amitryptiline, and Oxycodone. CHRONIC ======= # Hypertension: continued home Carvedilol. Held home lisinopril. # Hyperlipidemia: Home aspirin and atorvastatin continued. # Diabetes: last HgbA1c 9% on this admission. Continued home 70/30 and ISS. # GERD: Continued home omperazole # Anemia: Continued home darbapoetin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== - Please ensure close follow up with INR monitoring and coumadin dosing, as patient has not yet been stabilized on a set home dose - Patient has been having pain during dialysis, and should be treated with her home pain medication regimen - Patient will receive home ___ on discharge #CODE: Full (confirmed) #EMERGENCY CONTACT HCP: ___ Relationship: Sister Phone number: ___ Cell phone: ___
summary: You were admitted for chest pain and shortness of breath, and found to have a heart arrythmia called atrial fibrillation. You were transferred to ___ for further management. We adjusted your home beta blocker to help control your heart rate, and you were started on a new medication, warfarin, to help prevent blood clots from forming. You will have a visiting nurse service help you out at home for the next several days. They will measure your blood coagulability and help you to properly dose your warfarin moving forward, in consultation with the ___ clinic. You will continue to receive hemodialysis on ___, and ___.

Coumadin started as an inpatient, but was initially supratherapeutic so held for several days. INR and coumadin dosing and will be managed by the ___ clinic moving forward. # Troponinemia: Trop peaked to 0.20 on admission, CK-MB otherwise flat. Likely in setting of Afib w/RVR. Patient otherwise without chest pain or EKG findings suggestive of ischemia. # ESRD: Secondary to ___'s s/p transplant x2 now on HD. The patient was continued on HD. Her home Tacrolimus and Predisone were continued. # ___ pain: sigificant pain: Has been limiting dialysis sessions. The patient was continued on her home Gabapentin, Amitryptiline, and Oxycodone. CHRONIC ======= # Hypertension: continued home Carvedilol. Held home lisinopril. # Hyperlipidemia: Home aspirin and atorvastatin continued. # Diabetes: last HgbA1c 9% on this admission. Continued home 70/30 and ISS. # GERD: Continued home omperazole # Anemia: Continued home darbapoetin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== - Please ensure close follow up with INR monitoring and coumadin dosing, as patient has not yet been stabilized on a set home dose - Patient has been having pain during dialysis, and should be treated with her home pain medication regimen - Patient will receive home ___ on discharge #CODE: Full (confirmed) #EMERGENCY CONTACT HCP: ___ Relationship: Sister Phone number: ___ Cell phone: ___

text: Brief Hospital Course: HOSPITAL COURSE Ms. ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of diastolic CHF, chronic stable coronary heart disease, atrial fibrillation on coumadin with recent cardioversion ___, hypertension, who presented from nursing home with acute onset shortness of breath x 1 day and an acute viral syndrome concerning for influenza. . ACTIVE ISSUES 1. DYSPNEA: Patient's shortness of breath is of unclear etiology but likely due to combination of CHF exacerbation plus influenza. Influenza DFA returned positive. ___ also be exacerbated by conversion into atrial fibrillation with RVR (triggered by viral infection/ fever/dehydration). No cough or focal consolidation on CXR concerning for pneumonia. She was continued on tamiflu for 5 days. She was diuresed with IV lasix which improved her shortness of breath. Patient did not complain of SOB for the remainder of her hospital stay. . 2. ATRIAL FIBRILLATION with RVR: Status post cardioversion on ___. TTE showed normal LVEF but left atrium enlargement. She was noted to be in atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response in 140s on admission to the ED with no effect on blood pressure. She was started on amiodarone drip in the MICU as as lopressor and diltiazem had little effect on her rate control. Her heart rate improved to high ___ once amiodarone drip was started. TSH was noted to be within normal limits. Her cardiac enzymes were negative x 2 with unchanged EKG. -patient will continue on Amiodarone as outlined. Repeat cardioversion can be attempted in about a month, once amiodarone has been loaded. -patient will continue on Metoprolol increased dose for better rate control. . 3. ELEVTED LACTATE: Level of 4.3 concerning for abdominal process such as mesenteric ischemia, however has come down to 1.6 overnight. CT abdomen at admission showed no acute process (although limited by lack of IV contrast). Likely secondary to CHF. . 4. ELEVATED LIPASE: Perplexing marker in patient without epigastric pain, very minimal epigastric TTP, and normal CT findings of pancreas. Possible mild, asymptomatic pancreatitis. Ddx is the following: both renal disease and furosemide and also bowel ischemia can cause elevations in lipase. This is likely subclinical, since patient denies any abdominal symptoms and there are no physical exam findings consistent with these etiologies. . 5. ELEVATED INR: Probably secondary to antibiotics given in ED. Coumadin was held on admission. Coumadin restarted on HD 3 when INR at goal ___ at 2.5mg per day. This will be monitored at rehab with goal INR ___. . 6. HYPONATREMIA: Most likely secondary to acute CHF and volume overload state. Resolved on discharge. . 7. CHRONIC KIDNEY INSUFFICIENCY: Baseline Cr 1.9-2.2, consistent with values this admission. . 8. HYPERGLYCEMIA: No known history of diabetes, but may have undiagnosed case in the setting of multiple elevated glucose levels vs starting of prednisone by primary care three days ago. Treated with sliding scale insulin. . 9. HYPOTHYROID: She was continued on home dose of levothyroxine. TSH was normal on admission. . 10. Knee Pain: Patient complained of Right knee pain, that was relieved with a hot pack. There was no swelling or warmth at the site, with full range of motion, with mild tenderness on the medial aspect. A X-ray of the knee suggestive of osteoartritis. . 11. Leukocytosis: patient initially presented with elevated white blood cell count, which normalised during her stay. It became elevated again prior to D/C but patient was afebrile and asymptomatic. There was no apparent gut pathology on the CT of ___. This should be followed up on as outpatient. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # Medical Management: Started amiodarone # Elevated WBCs, and Lipase: Patient could have had subclinical bowel ischemia. Her CT scan here as of ___ showed no acute CT findings to explain elevated lactate (resolved on discharge). This likely needs follow up as outpatient. # Code Status: DNR/I
summary: You were admitted for evaluation and management of the flu and shortness of breath. You were found to have atrial fibrillation, an irregular fast heart beat that required admission to the intensive care unit. You were started on a new medication to help control this fast heart rate, amiodarone, and increased dose of metoprolol. You were not cardioverted, a procedure that gives a small shock to your heart in order to restore the normal rhythm, since it was decided that you will do better with medications. You were noted to have an exacerbation of your heart failure. You given medications to help remove some of the fluid that had collected in your lungs and legs. You were also treated for Influenza A and completed a 5 day course of therapy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with history of CAD s/p recent MI and stenting, prior history of stenting, obesity, HTN, hyperlipidemia, smoking and polysubtance abuse who presents with dizziness and lightheadedness for one week. . # Dizziness: Was felt to be due to polypharmacy/aggressive anti-hypertensive regimen in setting of mild dehydration. Patient was unclear about what medications he had been taking, and it is possible he was taking two doses of Imdur in addition to high doses of lisinopril and metoprolol succinate. He may also have been taking furosemide, though patient did not have medication with him. Suspected volume depletion also contributed to dizziness, and patient's symptoms improved following IVF administration and holding of his antihypertensive medications. An arrythmia causing his symptoms seemed less likely, and there were no events on telemetry. Cerebellar function was intact on exam, and ___ maneuver did not elicit symptoms. There was no evidence of an infectious process. Patient's dizziness had fully resolved prior to discharge, and he was not orthostatic. He was restarted on lisinopril at a lower dose of 5mg daily, and restarted on metoprolol succinate at lower dose of 25mg daily. Imdur was not restarted, and patient was instructed to continue holding amlodipine. He will need a follow-up blood pressure check in his ___ clinic, and may need ongoing adjustment of his anti-hypertensive regimen. . # CAD: h/o CAD, and recent STEMI with placement of DES to LAD at ___ on ___. Exercise stress test demonstrated average exercise capacity, and atypical symptoms in the absence of ischemic changes. Nuclear report showed no evidence of reversible ischemia, though did show a new fixed severe perfusion defect involving the distal anterior and the apical wall, as well as a previously seen mild fixed lateral wall perfusion defect. Patient was chest pain free, with cardiac enzymes negative x3. As it was not felt he would benefit from additional intervention at this time, he was continued on medical management. He was continued on aspirin, restarted on plavix, and also continued on a statin. His ACE inhibitor and beta blocker were continued, but at lower doses given hypotension on admission. He will follow-up with his PCP and cardiology. . # Chronic sCHF: EF in ___ was 40-45%, though patient found to have EF of only 25% during recent admission to OSH in setting of anterior STEMI. Repeat echo this admission showed recovery of EF to 45-50%. Patient appeared euvolemic on exam, though did have orthopnea and PND prior to admission. CXR did not show significant pulmonary edema, and BNP was only mildly elevated. He was continued on an ACE inhibitor and beta blocker, though at lower doses than on admission as above. He was not restarted on diuretics, given he appeared clinically euvolemic and concern for hypotension on admission. He will follow-up with his PCP and cardiologist. # Hyponatremia: Was felt to be secondary to hypovolemic hyponatremia in setting of dehydration. Na improved to within normal limits with IVF administration and increased PO intake. . # Dysplipidemia: Continued statin. . # Recent PNA: Resolving. Patient was afebrile without leukocytosis, lungs CTAB. Will need f/u CXR ___ weeks from ___ to document complete resolution. . # Anxiety: Continued clonazepam, citalopram. . # Finger pain: s/p traumatic amputation. Continued outpatient regimen oxycodone. Encouraged patient to enroll in ___ ___ at ___, as he has been urged to do by his PCP. LABS/STUDIES PENDING AT TIME OF DISCHARGE: none TRANSITIONAL CARE ISSUES: -Patient's code status was full code during this admission -Patient will need follow-up CXR ___ weeks from time of ___ to document resolution of multifocal PNA -Patient will need blood pressure check in PC follow-up and may need titration of antihypertensive regimen
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with dizziness, which was due to low blood pressure. The low blood pressure was likely caused by taking your blood pressure medications while being slightly dehydrated. We decreased the doses of your blood pressure medications, gave you IV fluids, and your dizziness improved. You had a stress test, which shows your heart has had some damage from your recent heart attack, but that the heart's pumping function has improved since you were discharged from ___. We restarted you on a medication called plavix (clopidogrel), which you will need to take for at least the next ___ months. It is very important you take this medication every day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY: ___ year old man with non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, LBBB, initially admitted to ___ ___ for acute bacterial meningitis, transferred to ___ after VFib arrest, s/p ICD placement on ___. . # Ventricular fibrillation: The patient was transferred to ___ following an OSH Vfib arrest while being treated for GBS meningitis. He was initially loaded on amiodarone, and this medication was subsequently discontinued. It was felt that the most likely etiology of his arrythmia was LBB re-entrant tachycardia, though no arrythmia was induced while in the electrophysiology lab on ___. Overnight on ___, the patient developed hemodynamically stable supraventricular tachycardia in the setting of acute urinary retention. The patient underwent a cardiac MRI and ICD placement on ___. . # Bacteremia: Blood culture from OSH was positive for Streptococcus aglacticae which was sensitive to Ampicillin and ceftriaxone. Suspected source was bacterial meningitis given results of a lumbar puncture. ID was consulted and felt that there was no contraindication to ICD placement given that blood cultures had been negative for several days. An MRI of the spine revealed the possibiliy of discitis, and ID recommended a 6 week course of ceftriaxone, to complete on ___ (2g q12 until ___, then 1g q24 until ___. . # Nonischemic Cardiomyopathy/Chronic systolic congestive heart failure. The etiology of his cardiomyopathy remains unclear. The patient's CHF was felt to be stable during his hospital course. He did require some gentle diuresis but was otherwise euvolemic. He was continued on his ACE-I and beta-blocker. Given his depressed LVEF, Spironolactone should be considered as an otpatient. . # HTN: Continued on carvedilol and lisinopril. The patient did have some orthostatic hypotension, that was felt secondary to deconditioning. . # HLD: The patient's simvastatin was initially held given transaminitis. This medication was restarted, and his LFTs continued to normalize. . # Anemia: Unclear etiology at this time. HIV negative, TSH was within normal limits. His hematocrit remained stable and there was no suggestion of acute blood loss. Will need continued work-up/follow-up as an outpatient. And age-appropriate malignancy screening should be considered. . # Transaminitis: Most likely secondary to hypotension (shock liver) during cardiac arrest. Resolved over time without intervention. Simvastatin was initially held, and LFTs continued to normalize after this medication was restarted. Other potential etioliges included drug induced transaminitis from amiodarone. ___ and HBV, HCV serologies were negative. . # Hyperglycemia: The patient did have some hyperglycemia early in the hospital course. Hb A1c was 5.6% suggesting this was most likely secondary to his acute illness . # Lower-back pain: Ongoing since pre-hospitalization, possibly exacerbated by discitis, well-controlled with prn Oxycodone and Acetaminophen . # Buttock ulcerations: At home, the patient was treated with hot packs, and developed several ulcerations on his buttock. Subsequent to transfer, the patient developed ___ additional small ulcerations. Wound care was consulted, and recommended routine wound care. These were not felt to be pressure ulcers, and the exact etiology is unclear. These need to be followed as an outpatient, and if he continues to develop new lesions, further work-up may be indicated. . ================================== Transitional issues- -Consider Spironolactone initiation given depressed LVEF. -LFTs, CBC with diff, Chem-7 and ESR and fax to ___ clinic noted on page 1. -Pull PICC line after completion of antibiotic course on ___ -Continue work-up / follow-up of stable anemia, consider age-appropriate malignancy screening. -Renal cyst noted on cardiac MRI, further work-up may be indicated -Monitor buttock ulcerations for improvement, consider work-up if more lesions develop
summary: You originally went to ___ on ___ with a high fever and diagnosed with bacterial meningitis. You were slowly getting better when you experienced an episode of cardiac arrest on ___ due to an irregular heart rhythm called ventricular fibrilation. You were stablized and then transferred to ___ on ___ for further management of this irregular heart rhythm. Several different studies were done on your heart here at the hospital, and it was decided that a pacemaker with a defibrilator should be placed to help keep your heart beating at a normal rate and rhythm. This device was placed on ___ and has been functioning appropriately. Your heart is a bit weaker than before your admission, but the medications will help. You are now doing much better and you are ready to transfer to rehab.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ M with history of HTN and OSA, who was admitted with an inferior STEMI. . ACUTE # Inferior STEMI - Pt taken to cath and shown to have a subtotal ostium occlusion in the posterolateral branch of the RCA. There was successful PTCA and stenting of the distal RCA into the posterolateral branch with a 3.0 x 18 mm Promus drug-eluting stent. He was continued on aspirin and started on plavix, ACEI, statin, and switched to metoprolol from his home atenolol. . # CHF: No history of CHF but has not had an echo since stress echo in ___. ECHO showed EF 45-50% with mild to moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with severe hypokinesis of the basal inferior and inferolateral walls, Mild-moderate aortic and mitral regurgitation, and dilated thoracic aorta. . # RHYTHM: Evidence of first degree AV block on EKG back in ___ ___s now. He was monitored on telemetry and found to be in paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. He was anticoagulated with heparin drip and bridged to Pradaxa for long-term anticoagulation. . CHRONIC # Hypertension: His home amlodipine was held in favor of starting ACEI, and he was switched from his home atenolol to metoprolol for better cardioprotection. He was slowly titrated up to 50 mg metoprolol BID, which was equivalent to his home atenolol dose. He was also put on lisinopril 5mg. . # Hyperlipidemia: Per notes, he has history of hyperlipidemia,but patient reports good cholesterol levels. Lipid panel showed HDL 43, CHOL/HDL 4.0, LDL 104. He was started on atorvastatin 80mg daily for cardioprotection. . # Obstructive Sleep Apnea: He was continued on CPAP. . # BPH: He was continued on home tamulosin and finasteride. . # Low testosterone: Wife reports pt takes 200mg every 2 weeks. He was put on testosterone patch while in-house. . TRANSITIONAL # Pt will have follow-up with Dr. ___ on ___. . # Pt will have follow-up with Dr. ___ on ___.
summary: You were admitted because you had a heart attack. Images of your heart showed that critical vessels that supply blood to your heart were blocked, and so a stent was placed in order to keep the blood vessel open. it is extremely important that you take aspirin and plavix every day for one year to prevent the stent from clotting off and causing another heart attack. Do not stop taking aspirin and plavix or miss any doses unless Dr. ___ it is OK. You developed a very common irregular heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation. You have tolerated this well but you now have an increased risk of stroke. Therefore, we have started ___ blood thinner called Dabigitran or Pradaxa to prevent a stroke. This medicine can cause easy bleeding so you may bruise easily or your gums could bleed with vigorous brushing. However, you also could have bleeding in your bowel that will give you dark or bloody stools, lightheadedness and dizziness.

text:
Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with a history of dCHF, severe CAD s/p CABG who presents with worsening dyspnea on exertion, associated with orthopnea, and lower extremity edema. Patient treated for acute exacerbation of CHF, primarily through diuresis. . # Acute on Chronic Diastolic congestive Heart Failure
Echo from ___ showed normal systolic function with diastolic dysfunction. Pt was started on lasix gtt with poor response, then required bumex gtt to diurese. Started on bumex PO again after ARF resolving. On RA with good sats. Not orthostatic over weekend, Bumex restarted and taking once daily. Pt needs to be followed daily with weights and fluid assessment. Will need to increase Bumex frequency and dose as pt recovers. No ___ ___ hyperkalemia. will cont with Carvedilol BID. . # RHYTHM: Currently in NSR with no significant VEA. . # CORONARIES: Patient has known severe CAD s/p CABG and multiple PCIs. Denies CP, or usual anginal symptoms. EKG unchanged from baseline. Continued on ASA, Clopidogrel, Carvedilol, Atorvastatin. . # Acute on Chronic renal failure: Current Cr 1.7. Baseline 1.3. Likely ___ poor forward flow in the setting of acute exacerbation of diastolic heart failure. Pts creat also increased to high of 3.1 after diuresed with Bumex IV. You were given medications to remove the fluid with significant improvement of your breathing, and with resolution of your chest pain. Blood tests and EKGs did not show you had a heart attack.
