text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ year old woman with hypertension, who presented with a fall and chest pain. Patient then found to have an NSTEMI, now s/p cath ___ with plain old balloon angioplasty (POBA) of LCx and ramus. Hospital course has been complicated by UTI and urinary retention. #CAD/NSTEMI: patient admitted with chest pain and rising troponins without EKG changes. She had a cath on a ___ with balloon angioplasty to left circumflex and Ramus. TTE showing EF 35%. Following cath patient remained chest pain free and with no acute cardiac events. Discharged on aspirin, Plavix, atorvastatin. No beta blocker due to allergy to atenolol (lip swelling). #Acute systolic heart failure: Patient with EF 35% on echo after MI. Patient with pulmonary edema on exam and CXR. Was diuresed with IV Lasix and transitioned to torsemide 20mg for discharge. Holding losartan and spironolactone in setting of hyponatremia and ___. Patient not on beta blocker as above for allergy. #Urinary Retention: Thought to be secondary to UTI. New problem this admission. Has a foley in. Will need to follow up with urology. #Hyponatremia: Initially thought to be secondary to SIADH vs inadequate solute intake. Renal was consulted. Little improvement with fluid restriction or increase in solute. Did improve with diuresis and worsen when diuresis held, so this was thought to be likely secondary to CHF. #Acute kidney injury: Patient with baseline creatinine 0.9 up to 1.2 with ___ to 1.4. Likely combination of pre-renal and Bactrim increasing creatinine on admission. Creatinine improved on discharge to 1.1. #Treated for Pneumonia: Patient had increased cough and sputum on day 4 of admission with a new small infiltrate on CXR. No fever or increased white count at that time. Received vancomycin and aztreonam given multiple allergies for three days. Developed increased pulmonary edema after that and in this context the symptoms were felt to be more likely HF related. Antibiotics were discontinued and the patient did not show any signs of active infection. #Fall: Patient describes a mechanical fall in which she was getting out of bed, and tripped over her slippers. CT head at OSH negative for bleed. #Hypertension: well controlled. Holding losartan and spironolactone as above. Discontinued home diltiazem in setting of systolic heart failure #Peripheral neuropathy: Continue home gabapentin, but renal dose to 300 BID. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -holding spironolactone and losartan for ___. Can consider restarting when Cr stabilizes -torsemide started at 20mg on day of discharge. will likely need to be titrated as an outpatient -will get f/u labs on ___ to be sent to cardiologist to follow up -f/u with urology for retention / foley -discharge weight 140 lbs
summary: You were admitted because you had chest pain after falling, and were found to have had a heart attack. You underwent a procedure called a cardiac catheterization to look at the blood flow in your heart vessels. You were found to have a blockage in two of the vessels, and these vessels were opened. However, no stent was placed because the vessels were too small. You were continued on aspirin and a medication called clopidogrel was added. It is very important that you take both of these every single day. You were also treated for a urinary tract infection with antibiotics. You had difficulty urinating because of the infection, so you have a urinary catheter.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Pt is ___ yo M with hx of DM2, constrictive pericarditis since ___ (likely viral, last EF 55%) who presents with progressive R heart failure likely in setting of worsening pericarditis. . # Constrictive pericarditis - viral etiology, initially diagnosed in ___ and treated with NSAIDs; subacute clinical progression with worsening dyspnea on exertion, leg edema, and abdominal swelling over past several years and specifically since ___. He saw Dr. ___ in clinic in ___. MRI was done last week showed evidence of pericardial constriction with focal thickening, calcification and hyperenhancement of the pericardium (described above), ventricular interdependence and tethering of the myocardium. Last TTE EF 55% with no clear criteria for constrictive pericarditis met. Pt underwent catheterization on this admission which showed nearly equal diastolic filling pressures in chambers with elevated pulmonary pressures. Pericarditis has likely been progressing for a few years and resulting in CHF and atrial fibrillation (newly diagnosed in ___, see below). Pt was volume overloaded on admission, and aggressively diuresed on lasix drip with ~2L output/day. He was started on standing PO K 20mg BID. CT surgery was consulted for possible pericardial window or pericardial stripping, and pt will return for surgery on ___. . Wound care and podiatry were consulted regarding ulcers on feet ___ chronic edema; foot x-rays were negative for osteomyelities and wet-dry dressing changes were recommended. . # Atrial flutter/fibrillation - pt diagnosed with a fib in ___, failed 3 cycles of DC CV and started on coumadin and sotalol. Coumadin stopped on ___ in preparation for cath and possible intervention. ECG on admission showed NSR with diffuse TWI. Monitored on tele and in sinus rhythm throughout hospitalization. We continued sotalol and held coumadin given upcoming surgery. . # DM2 - on high doses of lantus and humalog at home. A1C>12% and very poorly controlled blood sugars at home. ___ was consulted and determined that pt does not have a good understanding of using his humalog and much of the medication is not injected when they observed him using the syringe. To sipmlify his regimen, he was discharged on U-500 insulin, 20U TID with meals. It is very important for him to have strict control of blood glucose pre- and ___. He will follow up with ___ as an outpatient. . # OSA - on CPAP at home. Likely contributing to pulmonary hypertension and R heart failure. Continued CPAP in house.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for evaluation of your pericarditis. We did an MRI, a cardiac catheterization and an echo which showed constrictive pericarditis, meaning that the sac around your heart is stiff and causing you to retain fluid. We gave you medication to remove fluid from your body and your breathing and edema improved. Your heart has been in normal rhythm throughout your hospitalization. You will come back next week (___) to have a surgery on the sac around your heart. In the meantime, you should continue your home lasix dose. We are giving you potassium to take twice a day (see below) to keep your electrolytes at good levels. Your sugars have been very elevated throughout the hospitalization and your A1C level is 12.5%, which means that your sugars have been high for the past few months. We have made changes to your diabetes regimen; please stop taking lantus and humalog. Instead, you should inject 20 units of U500 insulin (a stronger form of insulin) with meals.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of persistent afib on warfarin, HTN, endometrial CA s/p hysterectomy, ___ presenting with dyspnea, found to have new systolic CHF exacerbation. # Acute systolic CHF exacerbation: Patient presented with HFrEF. Dyspnea with elevated BNP, congestion on CXR in the setting of Lasix dosing changes and missed dose, with recent cellulitis infection, now improved with diuresis, consistent with CHF exacerbation. TTE with EF35% new from prior 50%, moderate-severe TR, left ventricular dyssynchrony, dilated hypokinetic RV. TTE initially with question of ASD, but repeat TTE w/ agitated saline negative for ASD/PFO. On ___, patient appeared near her dry weight of 165 lbs, euvolemic on exam with residual bilateral lower extremity edema, lungs clear. We restarted her home lasix dose at 40 mg daily. Her newly depressed EF and LBBB with QRS 160 make outpatient stress testing and consideration of CRT indicated. Her atorvastatin was increased from 10 to 40 mg daily for presumed CAD. We switched her atenolol 100mg to metoprolol XL 200mg for evidence-based mortality benefit in systolic CHF. She continues on candesartan. She improved back to baseline with diuresis. Discharge dry weight: 76kg. # Persistent afib: On warfarin. Stable. Supratherapeutic INR to 3.8 on admission, so we held her warfarin during the admission. On the day of discharge, INR had trended down to 2.9, so she was restarted on 2mg of warfarin daily (14mg per week, previous dose was 17mg/week). We switched her from atenolol to metoprolol as above. # Anemia: Stable, now resolved. Recent H/H drop despite diuresis. Unclear etiology, possibly dilutional. We continued to monitor her CBC throughout the admission and maintained an active type and screen. # RLE cellulitis: Significantly improved per patient. Started on outpatient penicillin in ___ for 14 days and then apparently was started on suppressive course with penicillin twice a week ___, Th). Currently no evidence of any cellulitis. Stopped prophylactic penicillin that patient was taking twice per week (T/Th) to prevent resistence. If PCP feels strongly about restarting, should be dosed daily. ================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ================== Transitional Issues: -Needs outpatient consideration of CRT given EF 35% and QRS 160. -Consider outpatient stress test to workup etiology of newly depressed EF. -Increased atorva from 10 to 40mg for presumed CAD. -Changed atenolol 100 to Toprol XL 200 (equivalent dosing) -Stopped prophylactic penicillin that patient was taking twice per week (T/Th) to prevent resistence. If PCP feels strongly about restarting, should be dosed daily. - To establish care with Dr. ___ as her outpatient cardiologist
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath due to missing a few doses of your lasix and precipitating a mild congestive heart failure exacerbation. We changed your atenolol to metoprolol. We also increased your dose of atorvastatin. You should continue to take your lasix and maintain a low salt diet. Your INR was high at 3.8 so we held your warfarin while you were in the hospital. You should restart your warfarin at 2mg daily. We also discontinued your penicillin to reduce your risk of developing resistant infections. If your primary care doctor feels that you should continue prophylactic antibiotics, they should be dosed daily, not twice a week.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with paroxismal atrial fibrillation s/p 2 PVIs, several ___ transferred from ___ CCU after a syncopal episode related to ongoing, refracrory superventricular tachycardia. # Supraventricular Tachycardia : EKG with a long RP and morpholology more consistent with atrial tachycardia vs. atrial fibrillation. She has been difficult to control medically and at time of admission she was now status post failed cardioversion on ___ in ED at outside hospital. Recent syncope likely related to blood pressure drops in setting of rapid arrhythmias and some possible orthostatic contribution as well given positional component on history. She was amiodarone loaded at OSh and then placed on a drip at 0.5mg/kg/min x 24 hours and transitioned over to 400mg PO BID alongside metoprolol tartrate 25mg PO BID which was initially increased to 37.5mg BID but felt to have little effect other than some contribution to low blood pressure so it was discontinued to make further room for improvement in occasional mild hypotension. Initially continued on anticoagulation with coumadin but this was soon discontinued as an ablation procedure was set up given her persistent HRs in the 130-170s range despite multiple medications and OSH cardioversion. On ___ she underwent left atrial ablation, and tolerated procedure well. She remained in sinus rhythm subsequently and is discharged on metoprolol. Her amiodarone was discontinued. Because the left atrium was acceses the patient was started on anticoagulation with heparin and discharged on lovenox to warfarin bridge. # Femoral bruit: Post procedure she was noted to have a femoral bruit but no other vascular compromise and its significance is not clear. Perhaps it may reflec underlying PVD. # Non occlussive, thrombus: Post ablation the patient underwent venous ultrasound of the lower extremity and noted to have a non occlusive thrombus. She was already on anticoagulation post left atrium ablation and no further changes were made. #Coronaries: No prior MIs, no cardiac catheterizations in past and no hyperlipidemia per patient. No evidence of CAD, CEs were normal. No complaints of chest pain during hospital course. #Pump Function: On admission the patient had a newer mild oxygen requirement with oxygen saturations in low ___ on ___ NC. Concern for possible tachyarrhythmia induced CMY and CHF development. Fluid overloaded on exam with JVP at 12-13cm and crackles at bases bilaterally. BNP slightly elevated and CXR with pulmonary edema as well. Thus, she was aggressively diuresed with IV lasix and >4 liters removed in about 2 days. Diuresis limited in setting of some droppping blood pressures with systolic ranges in high ___, but she remained asymptomatica t all times with no signs of dizziness or complaints. Shortness of breath did improve with diuresis. Lasix discontinued on ___. An ECHO was done as indicated in the previous page. #Osteoporosis/Osteoarthritis: She will resume her usual q-weekly 70mg Alendronate once discharged. Tylenol PRN for joint pains during her hospital course.
summary: You were admitted with a very rapid heart rate after experiencing lightheadedness and a recent fainting episode. You were found to have a rapid heart rate called atrial tachycardia. Multiple oral and intravenous medications were given to you to try to slow down the heart rate and a cardioversion was done but was unsuccessful as your heart returned to an abnormal rhythm again soon thereafter. The cardiology team decided to do an ablation procedure in order to stop this rapid rate. You should take blood thining medications for one month post procedure. You should take coumadin, and lovenox until the coumadin level is greater than 2. A visiting nurse ___ come to help you measure the level of coumadin. You also had some fluid overload in your lungs causing mild shortness of breath. This was due to the less effective pumping of blood from your heart. This is called congestive heart failure . Your symptoms improved after you were given oral and IV medications to help you to urinate more and decrease this fluid overload.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ yo woman with dCHF, CAD, HLD admitted for worsening DOE, weight gain, and pedal edema in the setting of inadequate home diuresis most consistent with decompensated dCHF. Also found to have ___ and ___ that is consistent with prior exacerbations without CP or EKG changes. # Acute decompensated ___: Patient has had difficult to manage CHF given borderline blood pressures and history of orthostatic hypotension. On her last admission her carvedilol and torsemide were held for this reason. Her dry weight is likely around 110 lbs and she was 126.8 lbs on admission, now ___ lbs. Had episode of acute tachypnea/hypoxemia overnight that lasted <30 minutes. Unclear if this was flash pulmonary edema (no ischemic change on EKG), as no enzymes or CXR. She was eventually diuresed to around 104 lbs (46.8kg), which may be below her dry weight. She had an increase in BUN, Cr with this. Her diuretics were held for a day and her kidney function improved. She was discharged home with Rx for 10mg torsemide daily and lab check in a few days. Spironolactone, atorvastatin, and metoprolol were started. BPs tolerated this with sBP in 100-110s. # Hypoxemic respiratory distress: Subacute to chronic. Has home O2 now on 2L. PFTs ___ showed mild obstructive pattern, likely from 30 pack years. CXR on admission shows mild congestion and edema w/ R pleural effusion. Had episode of acute tachypnea/hypoxemia a couple of days into admission that lasted <30 minutes. Unclear if this was flash pulmonary edema (no ischemic change on EKG), as no enzymes or CXR. Was tolerating room air on discharge without hypoxemia, however has some desats with exertion. Home O2 (2L) was continued. # ___: NSTEMI not likely from plaque rupture but from demand ischemia ___ high wall stress in setting of hypervolemia. Did not start heparin on admission and patient remains CP free with flat enzymes. Has had low level troponin elevations in the setting of CHF exacerbations but has declined cath in past and is CP free. Trop 0.17-0.19 x3, CKMB 3 x3. She was started on metoprolol on discharge. Her aspirin/clopidogrel was continued. # Acute on chronic renal insufficiency: Likely secondary to dCHF and renal venous congestion. Improved with diuresis, to 1.1, then worsened as she became slightly hypovolemic to 1.6. Improved again with holding diuretics x1 day prior to discharge. Discharge Cr 1.5. # POAG: Continued glaucoma meds while in house, though some home meds not formulary and were substituted. # Hypothyroidism: On home levothyroxine. TSH normal. # HLD: Started on fish oil ___ because history of statin myalgias. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -needs assessment of BP on metoprolol, spironolactone -needs outpatient chemistry f/u to make sure she has improved renal function and does not become hyperkalemic on spironolactone
summary: you were admitted with worsening shortness of breath and edema in your legs. This was due to an exacerbation of your heart failure. While you were here there was some death of some of your heart muscle, which is probably associated with the fluid overload of your heart failure. You were treated with diuretics to remove fluid. You lost weight, felt better, and required less oxygen. At discharge your weight was 102 lbs which is slightly lower than your normal dry weight of 107 lbs. Your kidney numbers were slightly elevated from diuresis but are improving at the time of discharge. You should have these checked later in the week to make sure it continues to improve.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ yo woman with dCHF, CAD, HLD admitted for worsening DOE, weight gain, and pedal edema in the setting of inadequate home diuresis most consistent with decompensated dCHF. Also found to have ___ and ___ that is consistent with prior exacerbations without CP or EKG changes. # Acute decompensated ___: Patient has had difficult to manage CHF given borderline blood pressures and history of orthostatic hypotension. On her last admission her carvedilol and torsemide were held for this reason. Her dry weight is likely around 110 lbs and she was 126.8 lbs on admission, now ___ lbs. Had episode of acute tachypnea/hypoxemia overnight that lasted <30 minutes. Unclear if this was flash pulmonary edema (no ischemic change on EKG), as no enzymes or CXR. She was eventually diuresed to around 104 lbs (46.8kg), which may be below her dry weight. She had an increase in BUN, Cr with this. Her diuretics were held for a day and her kidney function improved. She was discharged home with Rx for 10mg torsemide daily and lab check in a few days. Spironolactone, atorvastatin, and metoprolol were started. BPs tolerated this with sBP in 100-110s. # Hypoxemic respiratory distress: Subacute to chronic. Has home O2 now on 2L. PFTs ___ showed mild obstructive pattern, likely from 30 pack years. CXR on admission shows mild congestion and edema w/ R pleural effusion. Had episode of acute tachypnea/hypoxemia a couple of days into admission that lasted <30 minutes. Unclear if this was flash pulmonary edema (no ischemic change on EKG), as no enzymes or CXR. Was tolerating room air on discharge without hypoxemia, however has some desats with exertion. Home O2 (2L) was continued. # ___: NSTEMI not likely from plaque rupture but from demand ischemia ___ high wall stress in setting of hypervolemia. Did not start heparin on admission and patient remains CP free with flat enzymes. Has had low level troponin elevations in the setting of CHF exacerbations but has declined cath in past and is CP free. Trop 0.17-0.19 x3, CKMB 3 x3. She was started on metoprolol on discharge. Her aspirin/clopidogrel was continued. # Acute on chronic renal insufficiency: Likely secondary to dCHF and renal venous congestion. Improved with diuresis, to 1.1, then worsened as she became slightly hypovolemic to 1.6. Improved again with holding diuretics x1 day prior to discharge. Discharge Cr 1.5. # POAG: Continued glaucoma meds while in house, though some home meds not formulary and were substituted. # Hypothyroidism: On home levothyroxine. TSH normal. # HLD: Started on fish oil ___ because history of statin myalgias. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -needs assessment of BP on metoprolol, spironolactone -needs outpatient chemistry f/u to make sure she has improved renal function and does not become hyperkalemic on spironolactone

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ yo woman with a history of CAD s/p PCI with CHF, Afib (on xarelto), CKD, psoriasis w/ psoriatic arthritis, and recently diagnosed UTI (___) who presents with several days of nausea, vomiting, and hypotension found to be hypotensive with ___. # nausea/vomiting: Chronic in nature over last ___ months. Patient did endorse symptoms of early satiety and "food getting stuck", thus a gastric emptying studying was performed with evidence of delayed emptying. Throughout her hospitalization, she did not any further episodes of N/V. Patient will follow-up with PCP to determine utility of initiating pro-motility agents #Acute on Chronic Kidney Injury: Cr of 3.8 on admission from baseline ~2 attributed to analgesic-induced nephropathy. Thought to be pre-renal in setting of recent history of vomiting, poor PO intake, and hypotension. SCr continues to improve back to baseline while holding her home lisinopril. #pustular psoriatic Arthritis: She is followed by rheumatology at ___ and dermatology at ___. Given leukocytosis as below, patient was evaluated by dermatology and thought to have worsening pustular psoriasis vs AGEP from ceftriaxone for which a punch skin biopsy was obtained. She was continued on her home clobetasol cream with follow-up with Dr. ___ ___ dermatologist. #leukocytosis: Patient with chronic leukocytosis, however was above baseline of ___. Infectious w/u negative and thought to be from psoriasis. Derm evaluated rash and believe rash is consistent with pustular psoriasis which could explain the elevated WBC with no evidence of overlying cellulitis/infection. #complicated UTI: UA on ___ consistent with cystitis. Given hx of chronic immunosuppresion for psoriasis, she met ___ guidelines for complicated UTI and was treated with CTX however developed rash as described above. Ceftriaxone was then listed as an allergy in setting of AGEP. #Afib: CHADS = 3. Currently in Afib and rate controlled. Anti-coagulation with rivoroxaban which was initially held in setting ___ that was restarted prior to admission. #chronic compensated sCHF: Most recent echo ___ with EF 45% and WMA c/w IMI. Lungs clear. No edema. #Type 2 Diabetes: last A1c 7.4 in ___. - hold home glipizide - HISS Transitional Issues: -given evidence of gastroparesis, would consider initiating a pro-motility agent (Regalan) in the outpatient setting -will need f/u with dermatology for pustular psoriasis and f/u derm biopsy; dermatology team considering starting retinoin therapy per outpatient dermatologist based on skin biopsy -given reaction to CTX, would avoid administering this medication in the future -continue to monitor renal function -home lisinopril held in setting of ___ can consider restarting as outpatient -home HCTZ held in setting of hypotension; can consider restarting as outpatient if BP remain stable or elevated -code status: full -contact: ___ (___) ___
summary: You came to the hospital with nausea/vomiting. Your lab tests showed that your kidneys were injured, most likely from dehydration. You underwent a test called a gastric emptying study to see if food passed through your stomach appropriately and it showed that there was evidence of slowing of food passing through. This could potentially explain your nausea and vomiting, which improved throughout your hospitalization. You were also evaluated by the dermatologists who recommended continuing your steroid creams with follow-up with your outpatient dermatologist, Dr. ___. Given your reaction to the antibiotic ceftriaxone, please avoid taking this in the future.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with a history type II DM, recent admission for septic left hip and ARF, here with persistently elevated ESR. . #.Decompensated acute systolic heart failure: With new systolic dysfunction, global hypokinesis LVEF ___ on this admission. The differential for this non-ischemic included ETOH induced CM (though self-discontinued ETOH several months ago), tachyarrythmia induced CM (HR low 100s on admission, though suspected that this was a compensatory response to volume overload), versus CM associated with viral illness. On admission, he was grossly volume overloaded, with over 35 lbs up from baseline weight. He was diuresed with lasix and started on BB, ACEI, and ASA. He had a CMR that showed no e/o CAD. He was near euvolemic on discharge. He was discharged with the above cardiac regimen, including lasix 40 mg PO. He no longer required supplemental O2 at discharge. . #. Septic hip: He is s/p irrigation and debridement left hip joint, capsulotomy and irrigation and debridement left hip muscle on ___ with wound cultures growing out ___ swabs MSSA and ___ swabs coag neg staph resistant to nafcillin. He was also found to be bacteremic with MSSA. Since his blood, joint and other hip swab all grew MSSA, attributed the coagulase negative staph to likely contaminant and is being continued on nafcillin rather than switch to vanc since naf is a much better agent for the MSSA. He is currently in his ___ week of nafcillin. His TEE on prior admit, could not rule out a vegetation and thus is being treated empirically for endocarditis. His source for infection is thought to be secondary to his recent right second toe infection which could have seeded his left hip. His ESR continues to remain elevated, ESR here 132 (ESR 92 on prior admission) and he continues to also have thrombocytosis suggesting that his infection may not be clearing adequately. He does remain afebrile and without leukocytosis. MRI could not rule out superinfection of seroma. On ___, he had a debridement of collection left tensor fascia along thigh and arthrotomy of hip with washout by the orthopedic surgery team. He tolerated the procedure well and was discharged with orthopedic surgery f/u. Cultures from wound negative, and all blood cultures during this hospitalization were negative as well. He was continued on nafcillin, course to be determined at his outpatient infectious disease follow up. . #. Acute renal failure: Secondary to contrast nephropathy with SCr of 1.7 at discharge (SCr 0.6 at baseline) on discharge from last hospitalization. He had a mildly elevated eos with negative urine eos on ___. On this admission, his renal function improved with diuresis and afterload reduction as noted above. SCr at discharge was 1.4. .
summary: You were admitted with a new oxygen requirement and persistently elevated inflammatory markers that were suggestive of persistent infection of your hip. You were evaluated by our orthopedic surgeons, and had a wash out of your hip. You were also evaluated by our cardiologists and were found to have a congestive heart failure or poor pump function of your heart. You were treated with several medications to improve the pump function of your heart and were also started on medications to remove the fluid in your lungs and your legs. Please note that your neurontin dose has been decreased. In addition you have been started on lasix to continue to keep fluid from accumulating in your lungs. You have also been started on carvedilol and lisinopril to improve your heart function.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with longstanding hypertension and pre-diabetes who presented with hypertensive emergency complicated by acute heart failure along with troponin leak secondary to demand and acute on chronic renal failure. #Hypertensive urgency: Initially hypertensive to SBP 190s-200s that improved with Labetolol IV. Troponin leak secondary to hypertensive emergency, downtrended by discharge. Initially on nitro drip upon arrival at ___ ___ but discontinued soon on the medical floor as patient was stabilized and asymptomatic. Patient has hx of medication non-compliance, only anti-hypertensive as outpatient was labetolol TID that he took "when I feel like it" approximately ___ doses weekly. Anti-hypertensive regimen was changed and his BP were under better control with Carvedilol 25mg BID, Hydralazine 25mg TID, and Isosorbide mononitrate 30mg daily. Regimen chosen due to patient's reduced cardiac function. No ___ due to renal function. #CHF: Patient had dyspnea, orthopnea, and PND; presented with evidence of pulmonary congestion that resolved with Lasix 20mg IV. He did not require further diuresis while in the hospital. Echocardiogram showed severe systolic congestive heart failure with an EF of ___, with evidence of biventricular dilation and failure. Patient preferred follow up by cardiologist near his home. #Acute on chronic renal failure: Creatinine on admission was 2.5, up from 1.8 in ___. Creatinine uptrended and stabilized at 3.0. Renal was consulted regarding worsening creatinine, suspect underlying CKD from uncontrolled hypertension. Renal US unrevealing. Patient preferred to defer further work up to outpatient providers. #Pneumonia: Patient was started on doxycycline at OSH for question of pneumonia based on CXR. Patient was afebrile, asymptomatic, and without leukocytosis on admission. Completed 3-day regimen for CAP with doxycycline. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES FOR FOLLOW UP: -Echocardiogram revealed LVEF= ___. Please coordinate referral for cardiologist (Dr. ___ for monitoring of heart failure. -Acute on chronic renal failure: Creatinine plateued at 3.0. Please coordinate referral for nephrologist for monitoring of chronic renal failure. -Patient seen by nephrology, recommended MRA of the kidney but patient would like to have imaging done as outpatient. Please coordinate scheduling of this study.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of severely high blood pressure that caused you to become short of breath. Hight blood pressure caused damage to your heart and kidneys. Your symptoms resolved once we got your blood pressure under better control. We strongly advise that you see a cardiologist to monitor your heart failure and to see a nephrologist to monitor your kidney function. We have set up an appointment to see your Primary Care doctor (___) who can help you get connected to a cardiologist (Dr. ___ nephrologist near your home. You were seen by ___ nephrologist who recommended getting an MRA of your kidneys, please speak to your nephrologist to set up this study. You will be discharged with new medications for blood pressure. Please adhere to the prescribed regimen to prevent high blood pressure and worsening of your heart and kidney function. Patient with high blood pressure are at increased risk for heart attack, stroke, and kidney failure. Please also adhere to a low-salt diet to help control blood pressure. You will also be discharged with antibiotic to complete treatment for pneumonia.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Precis Mr ___ is a ___ man with previous significant coronary disease with fixed perfusion defect/hypokinesis/Q-waves in a mid to apical anteroseptal/anterior distribution. He suffers from likely unstable angina and presented with two episode on the morning of presentation. Sublingual nitrates relieved this pain. He was admitted to Cardiology for further work-up and rule out of infarction. Three sets of cardiac enzymes were negative, EKG unremarkable (by comparison to previous) and there have been no further symptoms. Heparin was stopped, Imdur tolerated (although systolic down to 95 mmHg overnight) - this was started to give nitrate cover during the night. It remained unclear whether symptoms were esophageal - perhaps spasm - relieved by nitrates, or cardiac pain. In view of normal cardiac work-up, further studies were deferred. He will be discharged on Imdur and omeprazole with close follow-up by Drs. ___. Snoring may be worth working-up with a sleep study given that this may worsen cardiovascular function over time and may, although less likely, be responsible for symptoms. Hospital Course by Problem Coronary Artery Disease Significant coronary disease with fixed perfusion defect/hypokinesis/Q-waves in a mid to apical anteroseptal/anterior distribution corresponding to LAD occlusion. Previous catheterization also revealed circumflex disease. No obvious reperfusable tissue previously (area seen on viability here is thin on echo), and now without new EKG findings or present ischemic event. Less likely responsible for symptoms. No further work-up or review for CABG at this time. ASA 325 and intravenous heparin were given during the admission, along with regular medications and the addition of Imdur. Cardiomyopathy Hypertensive and ischemic changes, with hypertrophy and regional hypo/akinesis and depressed left ventricular ejection fraction ~ 40%. No clinically manifest heart failure, but elevated BNP without previous value. Gave 20 mg IV Lasix upon reaching floor, with appropriate diuresis. About one liter down on discharge. Valsartan and atenolol were continued. Hypertension Long-stand and likely underlies hypertrophy, contributing to cardiomyopathy (which is predominantly post-infarctive). Presently well-controlled. Valsartan and atenolol were continued. Imdur will likely further reduce blood pressure, so valsartan may subsequently be decreased. Dyslipidemia Continued rosuvastatin. Epilepsy Followed by Dr. ___ (___). Continued on phenytoin 150 mg QD PO QPM. Possible GERD ___ be responsible for noctural symptoms. For example, esophageal spasm given history and relief by nitrates. Started PPI - omeprazole 40 mg daily. It may be that Mr. ___ would benefit from seeing a gastroenterologist for further evaluation of these symptoms, should they continue.
summary: You came to the hospital with chest pressure. You did not have a heart attack. Heart attack was excluded by EKG and laboratory tests. Given that nitrates alleviated this pain, you were started on a low dose of long-acting nitrate (Imdur). This can reduce your blood pressure slightly, so we recommend caution when getting up during the night over the coming days. Given that this discomfort was associated with burping and lying down, it is possible that these sensations are esophageal, so recommend taking an medication that reduces stomach acid secretion (omeprazole). You also report significant snoring, we will pass this on to Dr. ___ may request a sleep study.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH osteoarthritis, HTN, and HLD presents with dyspnea and nonspecific chest/abdominal symptoms, found to be fluid overloaded with 4+ MR. ___ and underwent ___ cardiac catheterization, where she suffered brief VFib arrest after contract injection to the RCA, ROSC achieved with external defibrillation, subsequent bradycardia treated with atropine x1 w/o further complication. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # s/p VFib Arrest: Likely in setting of dye injection into the RCA. ROSC after inital shock, bradycardic initially, given atropine, hemodynamically stable thereafter. No subsequent events or complications. # Severe MR: ECHO showed preserved EF, but severe 4+ MR and asymmetric LVH. No prior echocardiograms for comparison. Symptomatically decompensated on presentation, but based on ECHO and cardiac catheterization findings (elongated L atrium, only mild elevation of PCWP), likely not acute. Lack of papillary muscle compromise and negative troponins made ischemic cause unlikely. Indicated for mitral valve replacement Current indication for MVR are active symptoms and elevated PA pressure (PASP 52mmhg on ECHO). Patient was seen by Dental pre-operatively, and had TEE performed. She will be called by Cardiac Surgery to arrange an operative date. # CAD: Cardiac catheterization showed single vessel LAD 70% stenosis in the proximal portion. Plan is to preform CABG with LIMA during MVR surgery. Continued home aspirin, started on 40mg atorvastatin. # dCHF: Acute diastolic CHF with LVEF of >55%, no history of chronic CHF, previously well. Acute CHF likely related to severe 4+ MR related to flail posterior leaflet. After several days of diuresis, asymptomatic and without signs of volume overload on exam, remained euvolemic. Continued home lisinopril and restarted home dose lasix 10mg PO (this is for hypercalcemia). CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Hyperparathyroidism: Chronic, stable. Held home PO lasix for hypercalcemia while diuresing with IV lasix, restarted ___ once euvolemic. # Hypothyroidism: Chronic, stable. Continued home levothyroxine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - To be contacted by Cardiac Surgery for date of operation, and with further ___ instructions.
summary: You were admitted because of fatigue and shortness of breath. We found on heart ultrasound that your mitral valve is more leaky than before. We initially treated you with some IV medication to take off fluids. You underwent cardiac catheterization to look at your heart vessels and pressures which showed 70% blockage of only one of your coronary arteries. Cardiac Catheterization was complicated by arrhythmias (ventricular tachycardia) that resolved shortly with shock and medications. You further underwent a transesophageal echocardiogram as part of your preop workup. We consulted dentistry to see you while inpatient but they were unable to.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ with PMHx of AS, CAD, GI bleed, was admitted to the inpatient medicine service at ___ for management of his shortness of breath, leg edema, and anemia. His SOB and edema were thought to be most likely ___ new congestive heart failure, and he was given 20mg IV Lasix x2 with good response in UOP and improved symptoms. His anemia was found to be an iron deficiency anemia ___ possible GI bleed (trace guaiac positive in ED, no stool in vault on floor), and he was transfused with 1U pRBC with initial poor response (Hgb 7.0 to 7.4) and then another 1U pRBC with better response (Hgb 7.4 to 8.0). An echocardiogram was scheduled but not performed in-house. The patient did not report any ongoing chest pain/tightness on the floor. He had no evidence of bleeding during hospital course. Symptoms improved prior to discharge. #new diastolic and systolic congestive heart failure: Patient's presentation thought to be due to new congestive heart failure (along with new worsened anemia as discussed below), given his symptoms, ___ edema, elevated JVP, and CXR showing mild pulmonary edema and bilateral pleural effusions. He was given 20mg IV Lasix on both HD1 and HD2 with good response in UOP and improved symptoms; his lower extremity edema improved and he reported decreased dyspnea on exertion and orthopnea. The cause of his new CHF was unclear -- he had no appreciable murmur to suggest worsening AS, troponins negative x2 and no ongoing chest pain on the floor to suggest ischemia. TTE prior to discharge showed mildly depressed EF 50%, mod MR, and symmetric LVH with elevated E/E' suggestive ___ systolic and diastolic CHF. His discharge weight was 81.9 kg and discharge creatinine was 1.0. At time of discharge, the patient was breathing well on room air with improvement in dyspnea. He was able to ambulate down the hallway with his walker. #Anemia: His anemia (admission Hgb 7.0) was found to be an iron deficiency anemia (iron 14, ferritin 19, TIBC 443, MCV 70) ___ possible GI bleed (trace guaiac positive in ED, no stool in vault on floor), and he was transfused with 1U pRBC on ___ with initial poor response (Hgb 7.0 to 7.4) and then another 1U pRBC on ___ with better response (Hgb 7.4 to 8.0). The cause of his anemia is unclear, as the patient does not report any clear history of melena or hematochezia and did not have any evidence of ongoing bleeding on the floor. He will need an outpatient EGD/colonoscopy to assess for possible sources of GI bleeding. #Atypical CP: The patient has a history of CAD, HTN, HLD and presented with chest pain that is atypical for angina (present at rest, not associated with exertion). He had reassuring findings on EKG (no new ST segment changes) and negative troponin x2. PE was considered unlikely given lack of tachycardia, new O2 requirement, or pleuritic component to chest pain. The patient did not report any chest pain or tightness on the floor and was not experiencing any pain at time of discharge. #HTN/HLD: The patient was continued on his home lisinopril and atorvastatin. #BPH: The patient was continued on his home finasteride and tamsulosin. #GERD: The patient was continued on his home pantoprazole. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] patient presented with symptoms of likely new CHF. Will need TTE as outpatient for further workup. Consider initiation of beta blocker as outpatient [ ] patient discharged on PO Lasix 20mg daily. Please check electrolytes at PCP follow up ___ [ ] patient will need GI follow up and EGD/colonoscopy for workup of iron deficiency anemia [ ] discharge weight: 81.9kg. Discharge Cr 1.0 Code: full Contact: ___ (wife) ___, ___ (daughter) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for evaluation and treatment of your shortness of breath, leg swelling, chest tightness, and anemia (low red blood cell count). It was thought that your shortness of breath and leg swelling were most likely due to congestive heart failure, since you have a history of some issues with your heart. In congestive heart failure, the heart does not pump blood forward as well as normally, leading fluid to build up in your legs and your lungs, which can cause shortness of breath. We gave you a medication called Lasix which causes you to urinate and helps with getting rid of some of this fluid. You should speak with your primary care physician and cardiologist to schedule an echocardiogram, which is an ultrasound of your heart, in order to check for heart failure. Regarding your anemia, you had some lab tests done that demonstrated low iron levels, which could be due to your diet or bleeding somewhere in your stomach or intestines. You did have a test in the emergency room that showed a very small amount of blood in your stool, although you do not have any signs of ongoing bleeding in the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with history of bicuspid aortic valve and moderate aortic stenosis who presents with chest pain concerning for unstable angina, progression of severe aortic stenosis, new cardiomyopathy with EF of 20%, and dilated ascending aorta to 4 cm planned for further work up though left against medical advice prior to work up being completed. # Unstable Angina Patient presented with chest pain likely secondary to unstable angina. Trops X 2 negative. Cardiac catheterization was obtained which demonstrated RCA with an anomalous origin directly from the ___. Angiographically, has no stenotic disease. Other coronaries without disease. Aspirin 81mg was continued. Coronary vessels were to be further evaluated by coronary MRI though patient left against medical advice prior to this being obtained. Patient understood the risk of death and worsening heart failure and despite this left the hospital. # Moderate to Severe aortic stenosis Newly diagnosed as severe in the setting of bicuspid aortic valve. Most recent TTE showed peak gradient 44, mean gradient 26, ___ 1.1. Diagnostic coronary cath performed and CT chest performed. On cardiac catheterization however, gradient felt to be closer to Moderate severity aortic stenosis with mean transvalvular gradient 25 mm Hg, and calculated ___ 1.3 cm2. Plan for patient to follow up as outpatient with cardiac surgery though left against medical advice prior to appointments being set up. # Cardiomyopathy/ Systolic heart failure EF 25%: Unknown etiology. ___ be secondary to progressive AS. Other studies including iron studies, TSH normal. ___, HIV negative. Patient started on aspirin 81 mg daily, Metoprolol 12.5 mg PO BID, lisinopril 2.5 mg daily, and Lasix 20 mg daily. Dextroamphetamine-amphetamin recommended to be stopped in this setting. # Progressive MR ___ MR likely related to progressive AS. # Ascending Aorta Dilation TTE ___ showed 4.1 - 4.4 cm. Possible increased in size noted on most recent TTE 4.6 cm. Patient to be followed by outpatient cardiac surgery though left the hospital against medical advice prior to this being set up. #Rib Fracture: R. sided broken ribs post-injury a few months ago. The patient's brace was continued and he was written to continue his home oxycodone ___ prn pain. ***TRANSITIONAL ISSUES*** ========================= The following medications were started: aspirin 81 mg daily, Metoprolol 12.5 mg PO BID, lisinopril 2.5 mg daily, and Lasix 20 mg daily. - dextroamphetamine-amphetamine 20 mg oral BID was stopped due to cardiomyopathy - Recommend MRI to evaluate if his anomalous origin of his RCA could be contributing to his angina - Weight: 79.2kg - Needs to follow up with CSurg as an outpatient to evaluate aortic valve and ascending aorta dilation. - Code: Full
summary: You came to the hospital with chest pain found to have worsening aortic stenosis and valvular disease. You also had worsening of the pump function of your heart. We evaluated this further with a procedure called a cardiac catheterization. This demonstrated that you did not have disease of your coronaries, although did have some different anatomy, which could be the cause of your chest pain. This was going to be further worked-up with a CT scan if your coronary blood vessels, but you did not want to stay in the hospital for this to be completed. You understood the risks of not staying in the hospital to further work-up your chest pain including worsening of your heart disease. Despite these risks you left the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with MDS and recent worsening dementia with a componant of delerium. Admitted for a cough and delerium, echocardiogram reveals new systolic heart failure, MRI heart consistent with iron deposition. Family would like to take him home with hospice today. # end of life care: hospice arranged, appreciate their support. for now, continue with weekly xfusions at home prn. transfused prior to discharge. # ___ system failure: noted to have new ___, transaminitis (including clotting factor synthesis dysfunction), hyperuricemia, hyperphosphatemia, leukocytosis on admission. Likely heart failure as primary cause, although individual systems were likely weakened by age, chronic disease status, hemachromatosis, etc. # systolic heart failure: - iron deposits on cardiac MRI - continue Bb/ACEI, promotes comfort by improving cardiac function - not currently receiving diuretics - continues to appear euvolemic - no diuretic for now, can be added prn by hospice if it appears that fluid overload is causing discomfort # liver failure: likely shock liver vs congestion from heart failure vs hemachromatosis vs drug effect vs viral vs multifactoral combination of above - hepatitis panel neg - as no further treatment will be aimed at this, will stop assessing liver function # Metabolic ___. Hypernatremeia/hyperchloremia/hypokalemia: likely secondary to overdiuresis, improved with gentle hydration will monitor lytes. # aspiration: - S/S bedside eval limited by dementia/agitation - video eval with family to accompany and minimize agitation - continue IV abx empirically (d/c'd vanco today given ___, change cefepime to meropenem for increased anaerobic coverage) - d/w family, they would like to continue to feed him/allow him to drink for comfort, aware of risk of choking, pneumonia - plan to continue ___ for ___ course, initiated ___, afebrile, d/c ___ ___ # hyperglycemia: hx of type 2 DM, overall well controlled on po regimen. - d/w family today and have decreased lantus for hypoglycemia per ___ recs. - recommend checking blood sugar every AM. If ___ < 120, do not give lantus. O/w give 10units lantus. Family can stop this at any time they are comfortable. # MDS: supportive care - s/p 6 cycles decitabine, last ___ - support with blood products prn # anemia - ___ MDS - see above # thrombocytopenia - ___ MDS - xfuse plt if < 10 # dementia with delerium - hold sleepers - d/w family, will trial low dose ultram for discomfort. # DVT prophylaxis: none, plt too low for systemic, pneumoboots will cause agitation # Access: POC # FEN: regular diet with thin liquids, pureed solids, supplements, nutrition status worsening # Bowel regimen: home bowel regimen ordered # Contact: ___ # Disposition: home hospice today # Code status: DNR/DNI. Family gradually accepting of prognosis and the realities of end of life care. Date of discharge, greater than 30minutes spent in counseling and coordination of care.
summary: You were admitted for confusion, with a concern for infection in the setting of your myelodysplastic syndrome. It turns out that you were fluid overloaded, and this is likely because you have too much iron in your organs. The extra iron is making it more difficult for your heart to pump. We removed the fluid until you felt comfortable again. You will take the Toprol XL and lisinopril to help maximize your heart function and keep you comfortable. If you and your family decide that you no longer wish to take these pills, please stop. You will inject Lantus to help keep your blood sugars under control. You can check your blood sugars every morning. If the reading is 120 or less, do not give lantus. You may stop the Lantus if at any time this causes agitation or discomfort. You will be going home with hospice to help keep you comfortable.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ speaking ___ y/o female with a h/o A-fib, right sided CHF, and severe pulmonary hypertension who presented with hematemesis and BRBPR, who was admitted to the MICU after massive transfusion protocol intiated in ED. During her acute episode of hematemesis and BRBPR, she had a syncopal event with loss of consciousness and head strike but negative trauma workup. # GI Bleed: Initial H&H of 6.8 and 21.2 and with BUN elevated to 60 with normal Cr consistent with bleeding. She had a ___ score of 19 and massive transfusion protocol initiated in ED (she received 5U PRBC + 2U FFP, 1 megapack platelets). EGD done on arrival to MICU and revealed a Dieulafoy's lesion. The patient also has a history of large colonic adenoma in the past and therefore could have a concurrent lower GI lesion. She was intubated for airway protection and successfully extubated on ___. Gastroenterology was consulted and she was placed on a PPI drip (and then transitioned to BID PPI and eventually to daily PPI). HCT was 21 on admission and was ~29 after EGD with epinephrine injection and endoclip placement to the Dieulafoy. She had a repeat EGD on ___ for ongoing melena, which showed stability of clipped lesion and non-bleeding gastritis and duodenitis. She may continue to have melena for a few days until residual blood is cleared from GI tract. # UTI: on ___, patient had a fever to 100.8. Patient had U/A c/w UTI and was started on ciprofloxacin for complicated (hospital-acquired) UTI. She was put on a 7-day course of antibiotics on ___ to end ___. Culture was pending at time of discharge. # Hypernatremia: On ___, pt had a sodium of 149. She appeared volume down and had not gotten any fluid besides blood on ___. Had 1.5L free water deficit, which corrected with D5W. # Mild Hypoxia: Patient had an O2 desaturation to the low ___ the day prior to discharge. She had crackles on exam. She improved with 2L O2. O2 was weaned, and CXR showed cardiomegaly, small bilateral pulmonary effusions, but no edema or consolidation. She may have had a minor aspiration event. She was sat-ing well on RA prior to discharge. Home Lasix was restarted prior to discharge. # Right Side CHF: Preserved EF on ECHO in ___, hepatic congestion and splenomegaly seen on CT in ___. Her home lasix and beta-blocker were held in the setting of GI bleed and restarted prior to discharge. # Atrial Fibrillation: Pre-admission, she was on metoprolol for rate control, but no anticoagulation. Home metoprolol was held in the setting of GI bleed, but restarted once hemodynamically stable. # Chronic Constipation: Held home miralax and colace in setting of GI bleed, but restarted prior to discharge. # Nutritional Supplements: Held home folic acid, iron, glucosamine, and potassium while NPO, but restarted prior to discharge. Transitional Issues: - full code - urine culture pending at discharge - pt put on a 7-day course of antibiotics for UTI to end ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with a gastrointestinal bleed. You were taken to the ICU, where you were intubated (breathing tube placed) and given several units of blood through an IV. You were seen by the gastroenterologists, who did an upper endoscopy (an "EGD"), which showed bleeding from a Dieulafoy's lesion (an abnormal blood vessel in your stomach). It was injected with medication, and clips were placed on the vessel, which stopped the bleeding. Your blood counts were stable following the procedure, and you were successfully extubated (breathing tube was removed) and brought to the regular medicine floor. There you continued to have some blood in your stool, so a second EGD was done, which showed inflammation of your stomach, but no bleeding. Your blood counts were again stable. You were put on a medication called pantoprazole to decrease inflammation and risk of bleeding from your stomach. Finally, you were found to have a urinary tract infection, which can be treated with oral antibiotics.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF HOSPITAL COURSE ___ yo F with severe AS (0.4 valve area), HTN, HLD, DM presented for elective cardiac catheterization, found to have 2 VD (RCA 90% @ bifurcation of PDA and LAD 70%) who developed new onset AF with RVR in the setting of right heart catheterization. Treated with amiodarone with return to sinus rhythm. Will be sent out on amiodarone taper 200mg TID x1 wk (through ___, 200mg BID x1 wk (through ___ and 200mg daily afterwards. No coumadin needed as outpatient due to short duration of Afib and clear inciting factor (endocardial irritation from cath). Will follow up with Dr. ___ as outpatient for further workup of critical aortic valve stenosis. Cardiac surgery office will contact patient with appointment for further workup. As part of cardiac surgery workup, UA done showing large leuk est and >182 WBC. Pt asymptomatic but given upcoming surgery sent out on 7 day course of Bactrim DS BID. AT time of discharge a final read on non-contrast CT of chest is pending. ACTIVE ISSUES # AF with RVR: Developed in setting of RHC, possibly secondary to endocardial irritation from cath. Per EP due to poor substrate this is unlikely the first episode of AF, since patient was asymptomatic. Patient amio loaded and sent out on weekly taper of amiodarone with 200mg TID x1 wk (through ___, 200mg BID x1 wk (through ___ and 200mg daily afterwards. Patient started on heparin gtt in-house. Although EP felt patient should be anticoagulated since she likely goes in and out of AF without symptoms, given short duration, age, onset during RHC and possible upcoming surgery, patient discharged without coumadin. # Critical aortic stenosis, valve area 0.4 cm^2 noted on cath with mean gradient of 56 mmHg and peak gradient of 84 mmHg, peak velocity of 4.7 m/sec. Manifests by dyspnea climbing 5 steps and orthopnea now on 2 pillows. Patient's home metoprolol was held due to preload dependence. Dr. ___ patient and recommended outpatient followup for possible TAVI. His office will contact patient with appointment time. Cardiac surgery consulted and workup for possible intervention initiated. UA showed Large leuk est and >182 WBCs without symptoms, but given possible upcoming intervention pt sent home with 7 day course of Bactrim BID. Non-contrast CT of chest done on day of discharge with final read pending. Cardiac surgery will similarly contact patient with appointment time. # CAD: Patient found to have two vessel disease on LHC without intervention. Post cath check revealed no developing hematomas. Patient started on ASA 325. Home Crestor was contininued. Home nitro patch discontinued due to preload dependence. Home metoprolol also held as above. INACTIVE ISSUES # Chronic Diastolic CHF. EF > 55% on echo ___. Patient euvolemic on this admission. # HLD: continue rosuvastatin # T2DM: On this admission. Held home glyburide in house. Restarted on discharge. # Glaucoma: Stable. Cont cartelol 1% eye drop right eye HS # Hypothyroidism: stable and continued levothyroxine home dose TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # f/u Dr. ___ further ___ for TAVI vs valvuloplasty # f/u cardiac surgery. Will need MSSA swab of axilla, breast, nares which wasn't able to be done this admission due to patient needing to leave # Downtitration of amiodarone # Needs to finish 7 day treatment of postive UA, will need repeat UA # f/u non-contrast CT of chest
summary: You were admitted for a left and right heart catheterization as part of your workup for possible replacement of your aortic valve. You were found to have some blockages in two of your coronary vessels which will need to be addressed at a later time. In addition, during the procedure you developed an abnormal, very fast and irregular heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation. We started you on the medicine amiodarone to slow your rate down and eventually you returned to a normal rhythm. You'll need to keep taking this medicine to keep you from going back into a rapid rate. You amiodarone needs to be adjusted over time. Please continue to take amiodarone 200mg three times a day until ___. At that time, please take amiodarone 200mg twice a day until ___. At that, continue to take amiodarone 200mg daily. While you were here you were seen by cardiac surgeons to see if you would be a candidate for surgical valve replacement. They recommended a CT scan of your chest which was done on your day of admission.

As part of cardiac surgery workup, UA done showing large leuk est and >182 WBC. Pt asymptomatic but given upcoming surgery sent out on 7 day course of Bactrim DS BID. AT time of discharge a final read on non-contrast CT of chest is pending. ACTIVE ISSUES # AF with RVR: Developed in setting of RHC, possibly secondary to endocardial irritation from cath. Per EP due to poor substrate this is unlikely the first episode of AF, since patient was asymptomatic. Patient amio loaded and sent out on weekly taper of amiodarone with 200mg TID x1 wk (through ___, 200mg BID x1 wk (through ___ and 200mg daily afterwards. Patient started on heparin gtt in-house. Although EP felt patient should be anticoagulated since she likely goes in and out of AF without symptoms, given short duration, age, onset during RHC and possible upcoming surgery, patient discharged without coumadin. # Critical aortic stenosis, valve area 0.4 cm^2 noted on cath with mean gradient of 56 mmHg and peak gradient of 84 mmHg, peak velocity of 4.7 m/sec. Manifests by dyspnea climbing 5 steps and orthopnea now on 2 pillows. Patient's home metoprolol was held due to preload dependence. Dr. ___ patient and recommended outpatient followup for possible TAVI. His office will contact patient with appointment time. Cardiac surgery consulted and workup for possible intervention initiated. UA showed Large leuk est and >182 WBCs without symptoms, but given possible upcoming intervention pt sent home with 7 day course of Bactrim BID. Non-contrast CT of chest done on day of discharge with final read pending. Cardiac surgery will similarly contact patient with appointment time. # CAD: Patient found to have two vessel disease on LHC without intervention. Post cath check revealed no developing hematomas. Patient started on ASA 325. Home Crestor was contininued. Home nitro patch discontinued due to preload dependence. Home metoprolol also held as above. INACTIVE ISSUES # Chronic Diastolic CHF. EF > 55% on echo ___. Patient euvolemic on this admission. # HLD: continue rosuvastatin # T2DM: On this admission. Held home glyburide in house. Restarted on discharge. # Glaucoma: Stable. Cont cartelol 1% eye drop right eye HS # Hypothyroidism: stable and continued levothyroxine home dose TRANSITIONAL ISSUES # f/u Dr. ___ further ___ for TAVI vs valvuloplasty # f/u cardiac surgery. Will need MSSA swab of axilla, breast, nares which wasn't able to be done this admission due to patient needing to leave # Downtitration of amiodarone # Needs to finish 7 day treatment of postive UA, will need repeat UA # f/u non-contrast CT of chest

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old woman with h/o Hodkins, DCIS breast, and most recently metastatic endometrial cancer presently on pembrolizumab, atrial fibrillation with SSS s/p PPM, HFpEF, severe CAD, past h/o GIB, who presented to OSH with dyspnea on exertion and presyncope, found to have hemoglobin of 4.5, positive guaiac, and with EGD showing esophagitis but without clear source of bleeding. Unfortunately patient was not able to complete colonoscopy prep, and requested this to be done as an outpatient. Her bleed appears to be slow given hemodynamic stability on admission and multiple past CBCs with significantly low Hgbs in 6s. There also appears to be component of marrow suppression and iron deficiency. # Anemia # Melena Presented with profound normocytic anemia, dyspnea. Initially held rivaroxiban, continued aspirin given significant cardiac history. required 4upRBC total (1 at ___, 3 at ___, which appropriately increased her Hb to >7. Also had evidence of significant iron deficiency so was given 1x 125 IV ferric gluconate, and then resumed on PO regimen. No evidence of hemolysis. She was started on IV PPI BID for concern for UGIB given her history of duodenal angioectasias. She also developed melena on day 2 of admission. On admission, GI was consulted. Patient unable to fully prep for ___, but obtained EGD which showed esophagitis and recommended BID PPI for 4 weeks. Also recommended colonoscopy and if negative, capsule study, but patient did not want to stay for repeat prep for colonoscopy. Restarted rivaroxaban on ___, and discharge Hgb 7.4 from high of 8.1. # Metastatic endometrial cancer: (ER+, MSI-high) s/p 2 cycles palliative carboplatin and paclitaxel [stopped ___ significant neuropathy ___. Continues on q3 week pembrolizumab (last dose ___, due ___ for next cycle, deferred as inpatient). # Chronic cancer associated pain - Cont home oxycodone #ATRIAL FIBRILLATION - Held home rivaroxaban until ___. fractionated home metop to 12.5mg q6 hours until day of discharge. # HFpEF: Euvolemic on admission. Last echo ___ ___ with preserved EF, mild pHTN. Con't home torsemide and fractionated metoprolol. No evidence of worsening overload with transfusions. #Hx of DVT/PE - Restarted home rivaroxaban on ___. Has IVC filter in place #CAD s/p MI w/PCI -Con't home atorvastatin, ASA 81mg, and metoprolol # Hx of Pericardial Effusion - OSH echo ___ at ___ without effusion #History of abdominal infections - Con't home doxycycline suppressive abx # Moderate malnutrition - Pyridoxine #T2DM - Substitute glargine for levemir; dose reduce to 6units bid - HISS #GERD: - Holding home PPI; IV pantoprazole for now #ANXIETY - Con't home alprazolam TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== []please check CBC and iron ___ require additional IV iron []patient to take PO BID PPI for 4 weeks (Prilosec 40) after which can go back to regular home dose of 20mg daily []Please schedule for colonoscopy ASAP for further work up of significant anemia CODE: Full (confirmed) COMMUNICATION: Patient EMERGENCY CONTACT HCP: Health care proxy chosen: Yes Name of health care proxy: ___ Relationship: sister Phone number: ___ Cell phone: ___ Proxy form in chart: Other Other location: attorney Comments: Also named daughter ___ ___ as HCP Verified on date: ___
summary: You were noted to have a dangerously low blood count at another hospital, after you presented there feeling lightheaded and short of breath. You were transferred to ___ in ___. You were given blood transfusions that helped to improve your blood levels. You were found to have stool that was positive for blood. You were given iron. You were seen by the ___ doctors who wanted to do an upper endoscopy and colonoscopy. Unfortunately, the colonoscopy prep did not adequately clear your stool, and you did not want to stay for another attempt at a colonoscopy as an inpatient. You had an upper endoscopy which showed some irritation of the esophagus but no clear sign of bleeding.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo RH woman with a PMH of CAD, AF (on Coumadin), a prior small right ___ stroke as well as bilateral cerebellar strokes, HTN who was found to be lying on floor and non-verbal. She was apparently last seen normal > 4 hours before being found and on arrival to the ED was outside of the time treatment for thrombolysis. Her CTA revealed no occlusive disease and although CT head did not reveal obvious acute stroke and revealed extensive WMD, R ___ lesion as well as bilaterall cerebellar lesions, clinically her examination was notable for no meaningful speech sounds, left gaze deviation; RIGHT facial droop and RIGHT sided ___ weakness pattern on the right side consistent with an acute CVA. Etiology was felt to be due to subtherapeutic INR. She was started on ASA bridge to coumadin with goal INR of ___. At time of admission, she was also found to be in atrial fibrillation with RVR as well as acute CHF. She required IV diltiazem gtt, CHF managment with IV lasix. Due to decreased alertness, she was unable to participate in speech and swallowing evaluation initally, however upon reassessment on ___ was found to have aspiration with all liquid consistencies and was deemed only with ground solids as safe for PO intake. Given the number of co-morbidities and patient's prior wishes of no invasive interventions, including permanent feeding tubes a goals of care discussion was held and it was agreed based on patient's prior wishes that she would not have desired artificial nutritional support and would not desire a feeding tube. A decision was made to focus on comfort care and hospice. It was agreed that patient would take meals for comfort and take medications needed for comfort via this direction. She was a risk for aspiration with all consistencies of fluids and was not noted to aspirate with pured solids. Given the fact that she exhibited respiratory distress with atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response, it was agreed that PO regimen of Verapamil would be used for rate control to prevent dyspnea and palpitations sensation. ACEI was also continued to prevent flash pulmonary edema in setting of severe diastolic HF. In addition, patient was found to have a UTI with urinary incontinence. She was treated with 1 day of IV CFTX and then changed to Bactrim to prevent urinary incontinence, pain on urination and urgency. This should be completed by ___. Per daughter's wishes, she is to be continued on coumadin. Her last INR was 3.2 on ___ and patient had not received coumadin since. HCP understood that this may require further INR checking to prevent ICH/GIB and another stroke if she were to continue with Coumadin (INR should be checked on arrival to Hospice and coumadin resumed if subtherapeutic). It was also discussed that if patient were to develop PNA, another stroke, or another disabling illness, she woudl be made CMO by her HCP at that time. Patient will not have an IV access upon departing ___. It was agreed that patient would not return to the hospital should an acute illness arise again.
summary: You were admitted to ___ after being found at home with inability to move or speak. You were felt to have an acute stroke. Head imaging revealed prior older strokes in bilateral distribution of multiple vessels. Your hospital course was complicated by recurrent fast heart rate and heart failure episodes. You were also found to have persistent episodes of aspiration with all consistencies of liquids. Because your wishes had been made clear prior to this event regarding no artificial feeding and based on discussion with your family, it was agreed that the course of care you would have wanted would be consistent with Hospice care. Your medication regimen was adjusted to reflect this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ ischemic cardiomyopathy LVEF 10% s/p ICD/LVAD, CAD/multiple MIs (since ___, AF on warfarin, history of pulmonary embolism, and VT, who presents w/ subtherapeutic INR, admitted for heparin gtt to therapeutic warfarin level. #Subtherapeutic INR: INR was 1.5on admission. Warfarin dose was started at 3mg but INR rapidly rose, so dose was decreased to 2.5mg to good effect. Had heparin bridge initially which was eventually stopped. INR on day of discharge was 3.6, but is expected to fall in light of lowered coumadin dose. Next INR to be checked on ___ at home by ___ w/ results sent to cardiology office for further adjustment. #Ischemic CM, systolic CHF on LVAD: Patient noted to have elevated MAPs during hospitalization so lisinopril dosing was uptitrated. Patient was noted to have some slight discharge around LVAD drive line site so had swab sent for gram stain and culture with results pending by time of discharge, but have since shown no PMN's on gram stain, culture pending at time of discharge. Patient never had leukocytosis or fever. #Chronic back pain: Patient described persistence of chronic back pain ___ old traumatic injury in air force) and was given oxycodone during his stay. On time of discharge was instructed to return to tramadol use or discuss the issue with his primary care doctor. Chronic Issues: #Hx/PE: Subtherapeutic INR as above. Heparin bridge with warfarin. As above, was therapeutic by time of discharge. #HLD: will continue crestor #Anxiety: will continue Klonopin Transitional Issues: ============================================= 1. Patient was noted to have some slight discharge around LVAD drive line site so had swab sent for gram stain and culture which needs to be followed up at next outpatient appointment. 2. Patient had lisinopril uptitrated so MAP needs to be reassessed as outpatient 3. INR on day of discharge was 3.6, but is expected to fall in light of lowered coumadin dose.Next CBC/INR to be checked on ___ at home by ___ w/ results sent to Dr. ___ (___) for further coumadin adjustment. 4. Patient should discuss his back pain with his primary care doctor as he notes that the tramadol prescribed is insufficient. He was not given a script for narcotics upon discharge. 5. Slight edema in ankles at time of discharge, so was instructed to take 20mg of torsemide once at home. # CODE: FULL # CONTACT: HCP is ___ (son) ___
summary: As you know, you were admitted because your INR was below the correct level which could cause a clot to form in your LVAD. Fortunately, we were able to correct your INR with an adjustment of your warfarin dose. You were discharged on 2.5mg daily which you should continue until your next INR and blood count check on ___. Your cardiologist will then call you with instructions on how to change your dosing if needed. If you do not hear from them, please give them a call. Also, we changed your lisinopril dosing to improve your blood pressure. Please see the medication sheet for details. Lastly, we swabbed the area around your drive line to ensure that you dont have any infection. If the results of the swab are positive we will let you know immediately.

text: Brief Hospital Course: TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [ ] Needs INR checked on ___ [ ] Once INR reaches >2.0, can stop Lovenox [ ] Follow up BMP at next PCP appointment to evaluate Cr and ensure down trending [ ] Follow up BPs and HRs-- recently started on Metoprolol [ ] ___ benefit from addition of ACE ___ in future [ ] Follow up weights and determine diuretic regimen-- currently on 10mg PO Lasix 2x/week. [ ] Consider stopping daily aspirin for primary prevention given ACC/AHA ___ recommendation again routine use of ASA for primary prevention SUMMARY ======= This is a ___ with a history of HFpEF, CKD III, hypertension, and dyslipidemia who presented to the ED with shortness of breath and was found to have atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular response and a newly reduced EF with acute heart failure. =============== ACTIVE ISSUES: =============== # AFib w/ RVR The patient presented with shortness of breath and was found to have Atrial Fibrillation with rapid ventricular response in the ED. All of his prior EKGs had sinus rhythm and he had no known history of atrial fibrillation. His EKG showed no ischemic changes on EKG. His infectious work up was negative. He was noted to be volume overloaded and this was thought to be contributing to his afib w/RVR. His CHADS2VASC was calculated to be ~4. He was initially managed with metoprolol 50 mg PO Q6H and started on a Heparin gtt for anticoagulation to bridge to warfarin. He was aggressively diuresed (see below) with a Lasix gtt until euvolemic. On ___ he underwent a successful TEE-cardioversion with improvement in his rates. His metoprolol was converted to succinate 25mg PO QD. He was discharged on Coumadin 5mg PO QD with Lovenox 80mg SC QD until his INR was >2.0. On the day of discharge (___) he was started on Amiodarone 400mg PO BID for 14 days to be followed by amiodarone 200mg PO BID x14 days, then amiodarone 200mg PO QD. # Acute decompensated heart failure with borderline EF (45%) The patient has a known history of heart failure with borderline EF (45%. Given his mild pulm edema on CXR, rales on ECG, 1+ pitting edema, new hypoxia requiring 2L O2 and elevated BNP he was diagnosed with acute decompensated CHF, which was thought ___ be contributing to his afib. A repeat echocardiogram was done showing a LVEF to 45% on TTE ___ (from 60% in ___. He is currently on furosemide 10mg twice a week at home. While inpatient, he received furosemide boluses and was subsequently started on a Lasix gtt with good effect. The patient reached a euvolemic state and the Lasix gtt was stopped and further diuresis was held given a mild ___. His home metoprolol was fractioned as noted above and he was discharged on Metoprolol Succunate 25mg PO QD. His home amlodipine was discontinued given soft blood pressure. Further afterload reduction was held in the setting of aofter pressures and ___. # ___ on CKD III The patient has a recent baseline Cr 1.6 - 1.8. He presented with a Cr is 1.9 suggesting mild ___. While inpatient his Cr bumped to 2.5 after diuresis and was down trending at the time of discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== # Hypertension - Discontinued amlodipine as above # Dyslipidemia - Continued atorvastatin 80 mg daily # Donepezil - Continued home donepezil 10 mg daily #BPH - Continued home tamsulosin
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath and a very rapid heart rate. In the emergency room, you were found to have an irregular heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation with a very rapid heart beat. You had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have fluid on your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out. You were also started on a medication called metoprolol to treat the rapid heart rate and a blood thinner to prevent blood clots from forming in the heart. We performed an electrical cardioversion which sent a shock to your heart, to bring your heart rate back to a normal rate and rhythm. You improved considerably and were ready to leave the hospital. You were started on new medications to help your heart and make you feel better.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/hx of ___ who presents s/p fall with dyspnea. # Hypoxia: It is unclear why the patient was hypoxemic and differential includes anxiety from falling. However, more likely is that, due to his tremor from his ___, we were not able to get an accurate pleth and oxygen saturation. His BNP was elevated, and in the setting of his anxiety and hypertension, it is possible that there was an element of pulmonary edema. In that setting, while in the MICU, he was given IV Lasix and then was extubated the morning after admission to the unit. His oxygen saturations remained stable afterwards. # Fall: It is unclear why the patient was found down on the floor, but differential includes includes a syncopal event from orthostatic hypotension vs. symptomatic bradycardia vs. ? of seizure activity. Patient has documented variation in his BP and was previously advised to increase salt intake and wear compressions stockings and is unclear if he has been compliant with that. He also has documented asymptomatic bradycardia. EKG on admission ___ Aflutter with ventricular rate of 80BPM, unchanged from prior, but certainly cardiogenic with high degree heart-block should be considered. Pt not known to have seizure activity, but this should also be considered. However, pt relates a history of falling asleep in his chair prior to his fall so it is likely that the pt fell asleep and fell out of his chair, hitting his head on the way down. # subconjunctival hemorrhage: Unfortunately, the day prior to discharge, the pt was hit in his right eye with the bed lift resulting in a subconjunctival hemorrhage. Ophthamology evaluated the pt and recommended bacitracin ointment TID x 3 days with follow up as an outpatient at the ___. The pts family was aware of this as they were visiting at that time. The primary team apologized for this unfortunate event. # head laceration - Sutures placed ___. To be removed in 7 days. Wound was without signs of infection. # ___ disease: The patient was continued on his home carbidopa-levodopa. # Atrial Flutter: The patient was monitored on telemetry and continued on his home ASA. # Psoriasis: continued home skin care # living situation: This has been an issue for the patient in the past, as he has been living alone and there is concern for his ability to take care of himself. For the pts safety, physical therapy recommended home with ___ care. This was arranged with the family and the pt was additionally sent home with home ___ services and home physical therapy. Transitional issues: * f/u w/ ophthomologist at the ___ in ___ days * needs ___ supervision to ensure home safety * help with medication administration * help with ADLs * bacitracin ointment applied to right eye three times daily for three days (last day ___. * suture removal ___
summary: You came into the hospital because you had passed out. When you were in the hospital, you had a breathing tube for a short period of time because the doctors were concerned that you were not getting enough oxygen to your body. You were able to come off of this breathing tube quickly. We looked for reasons why you passed out but could not find any. When you fell, you hit your head. You have stitches that were placed on ___. You should have these removed ___. Please make an appointment with your primary care doctor for removal. To help your eye, please use bacitracin for a total of 3 days, 3 times daily.
