His BNP was elevated, and in the setting of his anxiety and hypertension, it is possible that there was an element of pulmonary edema. In that setting, while in the MICU, he was given IV Lasix and then was extubated the morning after admission to the unit. His oxygen saturations remained stable afterwards. # Fall: It is unclear why the patient was found down on the floor, but differential includes includes a syncopal event from orthostatic hypotension vs. symptomatic bradycardia vs. ? of seizure activity. Patient has documented variation in his BP and was previously advised to increase salt intake and wear compressions stockings and is unclear if he has been compliant with that. He also has documented asymptomatic bradycardia. EKG on admission ___ Aflutter with ventricular rate of 80BPM, unchanged from prior, but certainly cardiogenic with high degree heart-block should be considered. Pt not known to have seizure activity, but this should also be considered. However, pt relates a history of falling asleep in his chair prior to his fall so it is likely that the pt fell asleep and fell out of his chair, hitting his head on the way down. # subconjunctival hemorrhage: Unfortunately, the day prior to discharge, the pt was hit in his right eye with the bed lift resulting in a subconjunctival hemorrhage. Ophthamology evaluated the pt and recommended bacitracin ointment TID x 3 days with follow up as an outpatient at the ___. The pts family was aware of this as they were visiting at that time. text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ===================== ___ yo F with CAD s/p DES to OM and RCA (___) and LAD (___), active ___, HLD, strong early family history of CAD and MI who presented to CHA with STEMI, transferred to ___ for urgent cath, now s/p cor angio with DES placed to mid-RCA. Mildly dilated right ventricle with normal free wall systolic function. Mildly dilated thoracic aorta. ACUTE ISSUES ============== #STEMI #CAD Patient presented to OSH with inferior STEMI, s/p LHC with DES to mid-RCA on ___. On DAPT with ASA and ticagrelor, switched from clopidogrel. summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of a major heart attack caused by a blocked blood vessel. This is also called a "STEMI". You underwent a procedure to open the blocked vessel in your heart and place a stent. You were also treated with medications for your heart attack. You improved and were well enough to go home. We changed your clopidogrel to ticagrelor. Ticagrelor can be more effective in ensuring the stent does not block. Please be sure to take this medication every day, twice daily. It is important you do not miss ___ dose as this can cause a fatal heart attack. Please be sure to take aspirin every day as well, this will also help prevent future heart attacks. Congratulations on quitting smoking! Smoking increases your risk of future heart attacks and stroke. Given you already suffered from several major heart attacks, quitting smoking will help prevent future ones.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ASSESSMENT AND PLAN: ___ yo female with PMH HTN and MR who presents with chest pain, EKG changes, and normal coronary arteries on cath consistent with stress induced cardiomyopathy. . # Stress Induced Cardiomyopathy: Pt presented to ___ with chest pain after significant life stressors and findings of deep TWI in V2-V3 with elevation in her troponins. She was emergently transferred for cardiac cath which showed no angiographic evidence of coronary artery disease, wall motion abnormalities of the mid anterior and lateral walls sparing the apex and base on LV gram, consistent with stress-induced cardiomyopathy. Patient also had an ECHO cardiogram which showed preserved EF of 45-50% and moderate to severe mitral regurgitation, with hypokenesis of the mid anterior and lateral walls in a non-vascular distribution. Unable to beta block given patients low resting heart rate, lisinopril uptitrated to 5 mg daily. No evidence of heart failure on exam or by imaging, EF relatively preserved at 45-50% with sparing of the apex therefore no anticoagulation started. Patient discharged to follow up with cardiologist. . # MITRAL REGURGITATION: Moderate to severe MR noted on echo consistent with patient's known history. treatment as above for stress myopathy. . # ANXIETY: was seen by social work who provided empathetic listening. . # HTN: lisinopril up titrated to 5 mg for goal SBP<100 due to stress myopathy. . # ATHEROMA: patient was incidentally noted to have an aortic atheroma seen on ECHO cardiogram. Patient should have fasting lipids checked as an outpatient with appropriate intervention made. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -repeat ECHO in 1 month -continued evaluation of MR ___ lipid check -Patient is a full code
summary: You were admitted for evaluation of your chest pain and elevated cardiac biomarkers. You were taken to the cath lab out of concern for a heart attack. Fortunately your coronary arteries did not show any atherosclerotic plaques or blockages. Instead you were diagnosed with stress cardiomyopathy or "broken heart syndrome" where acute stress causes your heart to pump improperly. This should correct in the next few weeks, but in that time you should try to avoid stressful situations, avoid heavy exercise and focus on relaxation. We would recommend attending yoga or meditation classes if you feel these would be beneficial. You also had your dose of lisinopril increased to 5 mg daily from 2.5 mg daily, we would like to try to keep your blood pressure as low as possible without causing you any symptoms of dizziness or light headedness.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F w multiple medical problems including sarcoidosis, dCHF, CAD who presents with worsening SOB, DOE since being discharged on ___. PLAN: # SOB: Likely multifactorial in the setting of mild CHF exacerbation, possible reactive airways disease (although does not have obstructive physiology on PFTs from ___. PE unlikely given therapeutic INR. Her flu swab returned positive and she was treated with a 5 day course of tamiflu and azithromycin. She also received diuresis with IV Lasix which was stopped shortly after transfer from the ICU as she appeared euvolemic. She also received a prednisone burst which was extended to a prednisone taper for ongoing RAD symptoms with which she showed symptomatic improvement. Her slow improvement of symptoms was attributed to her kyphosis, significant deconditioning, and underlying lung disease (history of sarcoid). She was evaluated by ___ who felt that she could return home with home ___. # ACUTE ON CHRONIC DIASTOLIC CHF: CXR without pulmonary edema, BNP at baseline. That said, pt has not been taking her lasix (it appears that she was not discharged on this medication). Moreover, it appears that she did have some pulmonary vascular congestion. Patient is unclear about which medications she takes at home. TTE demonstrated diastolic heart failure with a preserved ejection fraction, severe left atrial dilation and elevated pulmonary artery pressures. She was given lasix IV and continued on her home dose of atenolol. The patient was not continued on her home Lasix as she developed a contraction alkalosis and had poor po intake. # ATRIAL FIBRILLATION: Pt s/p ablation with PPM placement. Continued on atenolol and warfarin. Her home warfarin dose was decreased to 2.5 mg qday. # HTN: Stable. Continued atenolol. # HYPOTHYROIDISM: Continued levothyroxine. # ASYMPTOMATIC BACTERIURIA: Pt's urine culture grew E.coli resistant to Cipro (has been on this for chronic UTI). She denied any dysuria symptoms so her Cipro was d/c'd. She was not transitioned to another abx for her asymptomatic bacteriuria. # DM2: Currently diet-controlled. Maintained on diabetic diet with ___ QACHS. Transitional Issues: ============================ # Monitor volume status and consider restarting lasix as outpatient. # Pt will need repeat INR check after decreasing Coumadin dose to 2.5 mg qday. # Pt wil have f/u with Dr. ___ in ___ ___ # HCP: ___ (daughter, HCP) ___ # CODE STATUS: Full code (confirmed)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had worsening shortness of breath. We found that you had fluid in your lungs as well as the flu. We treated you with medication to help get rid of the extra fluid. You also received medication to treat the flu. You also developed significant wheezing as a result of your infection so you were started on oral steroids which you will continue once you return home. You were evaluated by physical therapy who found that you were strong enough to return home, but you will continue to require physical therapy at home. You were also very constipated in the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with dementia, and past CVA who had an unwitnessed mechanical fall with imaging suggesting acute on chronic subdural hematoma and transferred to medicine for management of atrial fibrillation and CHF exacerbation . # GOALS OF CARE: The patient's daughter and HCP, ___, notes that her father wished to stay out of the hospital. She wishes that we focus on making him comfortable. She agreed that hospice fits the goals of his care given his poor prognosis based on his multiple medical conditions. Per palliative care, he was started on low dose Zyprexa and oxycodone PRN for symptoms management. He was transfered to hospice facility near daughter's house. . # Systolic CHF: On admission he was SOB, orthopnea, rales, ___ edema, elevated JVP with CXR findings concerning for CHF. TTE on ___ showed LVEF ___ with symmetric LVH and global ventricular hypokinesis. BNP 7955, up from 186 in ___ at ___. He was initially diuresed with IV lasix. His lasix was discontinued given euvolemia and the fact that he was not keeping up with his po intake. . # Afib with RVR. New atrial fibrillation. CHADS2 score is 4 (age, HTN, past CVA, CHF). TSH and T4 wnl. Infectious labs were negative. His metoprolol succinate was increased to 200 mg daily with better control of his HR. . # Dementia c/b delirium: Delirium possibly related to urinary retention prior to Foley placement or discomfort due to the presence of the catheter itself. He was started on trazodone and olanzapine 2.5 mg PRN. He received his home dose of donepezil. He was intermittently confused and agitated. At baseline he has dysarthria which makes it difficult to understand patient. Patient also has significant hearing impairment . # Acute on chronic SDH. Small old R subdural hematoma with minor acute evolution on CT head; per neurosurgery, no need for intervention. ASA was held. . # BPH: The patient had urinary retention and had a Foley placed, which he pulled on, causing traumatic hematuria leading to clotting. His hematuria subsequently improved. He was continued on tamsulosin and started on finasteride to help with BPH symptoms. He intermittently has urinary retention and required intermittent straight caths. . # CKD vs. ___: Per ___, baseline Cr is 1.1. Cr in the ED on ___ was 1.4. Cr on the day of discharge was 1.2. . # HTN: Normotensive on ___ pm. We stopped the patient's home nifedipine and increased his metoprolol as above. . # CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI; No MICU Transfer; No invasive procedure: Focusing on symptom management and transition to hospice # CONTACT: ___, daughter ___ or ___ . Transitions of care: - Please monitor Is/Os; and bladder scan Q8H and straight cath if >500.
summary: you were admitted to the hospital after you fell. Imaging of your head showed a small subdural hematoma (bleed under your skull but outside of your brain) that was stable thereby not requiring any surgical intervention. When you came to the hospital, your heart was in atrial fibrillation, which means that your heart was beating very quickly with an irregular rhythm therefore you were started on a medication to slow down your heart. You were also having trouble breathing. An ultrasound of your heart (echocardiogram) showed that your heart was beating very weakly and not pumping blood effectively which had resulted in fluid build-up in your lungs which we treated with lasix. During this hospital stay, we had discussion about your goals of care with your daughter ___ who felt that you will be better served in ___ facility with focus on alleviating any symptoms you may develop.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY STATEMENT: ================== ___ is a ___ year-old woman with a history of HIV (on Biktarvy), craniopharyngioma s/p resection complicated by IPH, vision loss, adrenal insufficiency and diabetes insipidus, HTN, and chronic DM, presenting with 1 month of progressive DOE and orthopnea, found to have worsening EF and HFrEF exacerbation of unclear etiology, which improved with diuresis, as well as mild coronary artery disease on cardiac catheterization. # CORONARIES: Mild CAD (Cath (___): LAD: multiple mild irregularities, Cx: 40% stenosis in proximal segment, RCA: 30% stenosis in mid-segment). # PUMP: EF 26% # RHYTHM: Sinus rhythm ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #Progressive cardiomyopathy with HFrEF Progressive cardiomyopathy initially noted in ___, and since declined (45%->35%->26%), with new onset decompensated congestive heart failure due to reduced ejection fraction explaining orthopnea and dyspnea; improved with diuresis. Abnormal ECG was related to new cardiomyopathy, although it was unclear if reported intermittent chest pain for years was related. The differential diagnosis included ischemic etiology given risk factors [age, gender, hypertension, diabetes, family history of coronary artery disease with mother having a myocardial infarction in her ___, HIV, hypertension/diabetes related cardiomyopathy, or idiopathic cardiomyopathy. Cardiomyopathy not associated with Biktarvy. There was no significant thickening of left ventricular walls to suggest an infiltrative cardiomyopathy. Iron studies and free T4 were normal. Mitral regurgitation was likely secondary to cardiomyopathy with moderate pulmonary hypertension, in part related to decompensated heart failure. She did have an episode of syncope in ___ with some characteristics that suggested a vasovagal etiology, but required arrhythmia monitoring given her cardiomyopathy. Right heart catheterization revealed elevated PC wedge pressure to 24 and elevated RV systolic pressure to 59. Left heart catheterization found mild coronary artery disease. The patient was diuresed with IV Lasix with improvement of dyspnea and placed on a maintenance dose of Lasix 20 mg PO prescribed at discharge. She was continued on her home lisinopril and started on aspirin, metoprolol, spironolactone, and pravastatin. #Diarrhea Etiology was unclear, but most likely a side effect of Biktarvy considering timing of symptom onset and the patient was free from other signs of infection. Considered C. diff (given prior diagnosis and recent visit to a hospital); C. diff was negative and diarrhea improved prior to discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #DM2 - Insulin while in-house, held home oral diabetic medications during hospitalization. #Hypopituitarism - Continued home medications including desmopressin, levothyroxine, steroids. #HIV - Continued home Biktarvy. #GERD - Continued PPI. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] Dry weight at discharge: 152.12 lbs (69 kg) on ___ [ ] Creatinine at discharge: 1.1 [ ] Hemoglobin at discharge: 10.3 [ ] Blood pressures at discharge: 90s-120s/60s-90s [ ] Heart rates at discharge: 70s-80s [ ] Please check CBC, chem-10 within 1-week (by ___ to monitor potassium and creatinine on furosemide and spironolactone. [ ] Consider repeat TTE in ___ months to evaluate for any recovery in EF [ ] New medications at discharge: furosemide 40 mg daily, spironolactone 12.5 mg daily, metoprolol succinate 25 mg daily, pravastatin 20 mg daily and aspirin 81 mg daily. [A review of the current literature revealed no significant interactions or contraindications for ASA and pravastatin while on Biktarvy]. [ ] ___ consider initiation of Entresto if insurance covers in the setting of HFrEF [ ] Consider need for treatment of obstructive sleep apnea. The patient reported that she had completed a sleep study, but was unsure of the results or if she requires CPAP or BiPAP at night. CODE: Full Code CONTACT: HCP: ___, (Mother), ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had shortness of breath and tests found that your heart was not pumping as effectively as it should normally. In the hospital, you were given diuretic medications to decrease the amount of fluid in your body to make it easier for your heart to pump and to improve your shortness of breath. You underwent a coronary catheterization to take pictures of the arteries in your heart which found mild heart disease. You also underwent a right heart catheterization which found elevated pressures in your heart caused by excess fluid, which was treated with a diuretic medication ("water pill"), with improvement of your shortness of breath.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Previously healthy ___ M with no prior medical history presenting with dyspnea due to afib with RVR from his PCP's office, and mildly fluided overloaded on exam, but hemodynamically stable. # New onset atrial fibrillation: He was rate controlled with dilitazem 480 mg daily, which he tolerated, and started on anticoagulation. Echocardiogram indicates that his left atrium is mildly dilated, and he likely has mild diastolic dysfunction. -Discharged on 240 mg -Lovenox 80 mg BID -Warfarin 5 mg daily, connected with ___ clinic, ___ -___ convert in a few weeks per Dr. ___ # New onset heart failure in the setting of atrial fibrillation with signs of fluid overload in legs, lungs, and JVD as well as history of orthopnea. Echo with preserved ejection fraction. -Allowed to auto-diurese and improved on exam, with only mild SOB on discharge
summary: You were admitted because you had shortness of breath and a new diagnosis of an abnormal heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation. This means that the top chambers of your heart are not pumping efficiently. This also caused your heart rate to be very fast, so we gave you a drug called diltiazem to slow your heart rate down. Because atrial fibrillation can cause blood clots and strokes, we started you on lovenox 80 mg twice a day, and warfarin. After a few days you can stop the lovenox and take only the warfarin. You will need your levels checked frequently until you get to a steady dose. In a few weeks, you can try to come back and do a cardioversion to shock your heart back into a normal rhythm. Your ___ will have your blood work drawn on ___. Dr. ___ will follow up on this level and let you know if your warfarin dose needs to be changed. We gave you many 1mg warfarin tablets so that you can easily change your dose based on your Dr. ___.

Patient was monitored here o/n; hgb was stable. Patient was started on PO iron supplementation given microcytic anemia. Iron studies and hemolysis labs were pending at d/c. Creatinine 1.7 at discharge. summary: You were admitted to the ___ for anemia (low blood level), which you have had in prior. You were observed overnight and had repeat labs which showed stable blood counts. It is likely that your blood levels are low because of chronic disease, low iron, in addition to very slow oozing from your gastrointestinal tract. We noted that you had an upper endoscopy during your prior hospitalization; this did not show any evidence of active bleed. Therefore, we think it is possible that you might be having ongoing, slow losses from your colon (which is not uncommon if you have polyps). We recommend further discussions with your primary care doctor to assess the risks and benefits of getting a colonoscopy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with history of recurrent large right pleural effusion likely secondary to CHF, repeated admissions with worsening SOB and recurrent Right pleural effusions, lymphocytic and exudative in nature that required thoracentesis with negative cytologies and unknown etiology. She's s/p elective uneventful right pleurodesis with talc and pleural biopsy on ___. She was admitted to medical floor for pain control from the talc and for fine tuning of CHF which was successfully managed. . # Recurrent Right pleural effusion s/p pleurodesis: Unknown etiology, has been exudative in the past, lymphocytic with negative cytology. On ___ she had elective pleural biopsies and talc pleurodesis with pleurex placement by Interventional Pulmonology service during which 600 ml clear fluid was removed. IP service followed closely during her stay. Her pain was controlled with IV dilaudid PCA, which was afterwards controlled successfully by tylenol and tramadol as needed. She used incentive spirometry. She was maintained at negative fluid balance by following closely her Input/Output and administering IV lasix as indicated. NSAIDs were not used. She needed another talc injection on ___ which was successful in dramatically reducing the amount of fluid being drained. The chest tube was removed on ___ but pleurX catheter remains in place at time of discharge. During this admission it was drained daily by interventional pulmonology; their plan was to keep the catheter in place, with once-daily drainage, until re-evaluation at a follow-up pulmonology appointment withint 2 weeks. . # Hypertension: Well controlled while in hospital. Her ACEi and ___ were held during a prior admission due to acute rise of Cr from 1.1 to 1.7. She was not on these medications when she was admitted this time. These were not administered during her stay. Home medications Nifedipine CR 90 mg PO daily & Labetalol 100 mg PO twice daily were continued while in the hospital. Restarted her ___ at time of discharge. Did not re-start her ACEi out of concern for cough possibility interfering with pleurodesis attempt. . # Diastolic Heart Failure: Stable, diastolic, Echo showed LVEF > 55% with normal LV chamber size and thickness, but the left ventricular inflow pattern suggested impaired relaxation. ACEi and ___ were held on a prior admission due to Cr rise from 1.1 to 1.7. On this admission, these medications were not administered, but ___ was restarted at time of discharge. Her Input/Output was strictly followed with diuresis as needed. Her beta blocker and calcium channel blocker were continued. . # Mild leukocytosis and fever: After her prior talc instillation, she developed temporary low grade fever with mild leukocytosis for ___ days. This is most likely secondary to pleural inflammation secondary to talc administration on ___ and ___. We followed carefully in-house and she did not develop any signs of focal infection . # Hypothyroidism: continued home Levothyroxine Sodium 75 mcg daily. . # Hyperlipidemia: continued home Simvastatin 40 mg PO once daily at bed time. . # Anemia: Patient's Hct dropped to 23.7 baseline ~30 with borderline macrocytosis. The acute drop is most likely due to bloody drain from the chest tubes and in-hospital phlebotomy. Stable in the past 48 hours. Baseline chronic anemia could be due to her hypothyroidism, her TSH was 4.2 (upper normal is 4.2). Folate level was normal but B12 was low. No h/o EtOH consumption. Methyl-malonic acid ordered by outpatient NP was still pending. . . ________________________ Transitional Issues: 1. Please follow CBC - will need Hct re-checked in ___ days. 2. Please drain through pleurex catheter daily. 3. follow-up with interventional pulmonology as below
summary: You were admitted to ___ to manage the fluid around your right lung. The lung doctors placed ___ tube and another thin tube called "PleurX" to help control the re-accumulation of the fluid. A substance called "Talc" was injected twice in your right chest to help reduce the fluid accumulation. When the amount of fluid coming out of the tubes was minimal, the lung doctors ___ to remove the chest tube and leave the thin tube in place to keep draining any fluid that would re-accumulate. During your stay you received IV diuretic that further helped to remove fluid from your body as a result of your heart failure. We re-started your Diovan: START TAKING DIOVAN (VALSARTAN) AGAIN at 320 mg by mouth per day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of severe AS ___ 0.8 cm^2 on ___ TTE worsened from 1 cm^2 in ___, LVEF 25% ( down from 55% in ___, HTN, and dilated LA admitted here on ___ with new onset atrial fibrillation in setting of recent n/v for 2 days prior to admission. His hospital course is summarized by problems below: # PUMP/RHYTHM/new onset atrial fibrillation: Presented with pulmonary edema and new onset atrial fibrillation with known dilated RA in setting of moderate AS. Not taking oral diuretics at home. Improved with gentle diuresis (avoiding significant afterload or preload reduction given AS). TTE showed newly depressed LVEF as above (previously normal) and new wall motion abnormalities.Rate control with metoprolol initiated on HD#1. Demonstrated CHF here, thus CHADS2 is 3. Patient was initially diuresed with lasix gtt but this was held when patient developed ___ with Cr of 1.4. ___ resolved after diuretic was held. Patient was believed to be euvolemic at goal dry weight of 67.1 and thus diuretics were not restarted. Patient will followup with cardiologist as an outpatient at which point he will be weighed and reevaluated for diuretic need. Discussed with PCP ___ ___ regarding starting anticoagulation- no history of GIB, frequent falls not an issue, thus initiated anticoagulation with bridge to therapy on heparin gtt. Patient was started on coumadin. Patient was in afibb throughout this entire admission and an attempt on ___ was made to cardioinvert the patient to NSR. However a TEE prior to ___ on ___ showed a thrombus in the ___ and ___ attempt was aborted. Patient was continued on coumadin and will be sent home with a lovenox bridge. Proper nursing instruction was provided to patient for lovenox injections. # CORONARIES: No known CAD. Presented with mildly elevated troponin in setting of RVR, suspicious for demand ischemia. Insignificant biomarker elevation on 24 hr trend. Cath on ___ showed CAD with stenosis of ___ % LAD and Cx, 40 % of RCA. Patient was started on metoprolol 100 ___. He also received lisinopril 2.5 for HTN. # Nausea, vomiting, transaminitis, cholecystic wall thickening: Presents with signs/symptoms suggestive of hepatobiliary congestion in setting of acute CHF. Thickened gallbladder wall on ___ RUQ US. Low suspicion for acalculous cholecystitis given more plausible congestive etiology, negative ___ sign, non-toxic appearance, and lack of abdominal pain. Hepatitis serologies are normal and iron studies have been normal thus ruling out hemochromatosis. LFTs have been improving w diuresis. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Full code throughout hospitalization. - Anticoagulation: Patient was discharged home with lovenox in attempt to bridge over to coumadin. His PCP ___ was contacted to arrange for coumadin monitoring. Patient also sent home with ___ for assistance in this matter. -Elevated LFT's- as mentioned above, LFT's have been trending down with diuresis. He has negative hepatitis serology and normal iron studies. Will need labs monitored as outpatient - Cardiology follow-up was arranged with Dr. ___. Will discuss patient's appropriateness for obtaining core valve.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of a very rapid heart rhythm, called "atrial fibrillation". This unfortunately caused heart failure. You required fluid to be removed from your body using a drug called furosemide, also known as Lasix. You are starting a new medication, called metoprolol (or Toprol ___, which will control your heart rate. You are also started on Lisinopril to control your blood pressure. You were found to have a blood clot in your area of your heart called the left atrium. You were started on a medication called warfarin (or Coumadin) and lovenox to treat this clot. You will need to have your blood levels (INR) checked routinely to make sure you are taking the adequate dose of Coumadin. You will need to take lovenox until you are therapeutic on coumadin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs ___ is a ___ y/o female with h/o paroxysmal symptomatic atrial fib/flutter likely ___ ASD repair (___), s/p a-flutter ablation and pulmonary vein isolation (___) and a-tach ablation (___), diastolic heart failure, s/p cardioversion ___ diabetes, presents after cardiac arrest. She was transferred from an OSH and admitted to the CCU. When she arrived she was already extubated and stable. Post Cardiac Arrest: She had cardiac arrest in field with ROSC after compressions and epinephrine. -There was no need for cooling protocol as she was neurologically intact. She was continued on her home dose of Amiodarone 200mg daily. An Echo was performed and showed an EF of 35% with moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction, c/w CAD and another superimposed global process. Mild mitral regurgitation. She should follow up with Dr ___ in 1 month Possible ACS: Patient had a bump in CE's, with questionable EKG changes. She was treated with ASA 325mg daily, metoprolol 25mg BID, Atorvastatin 80mg daily, lisinopril 5mg daily. Respiratory Distress: She originally presented in respiratory distress. Was briefly intubated at OSH. Chest Xray shows predominant abnormalities in the lower lungs are pleural effusions, small on the left, small to moderate on the right, with no pulmonary edema and no pneumothorax. She was rate controlled with metoprolol. She was restarted on Coumadin before discharge. She should Follow-Up to monitor her anticoagulation. ___- Mrs ___ was 1.3 from her nl baseline. summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had an difficulty breathing following your recent cardioversion. When you called the paramedics you were in respiratory distress which led to a pulseless arrythmia. You underwent CPR and received medicine to restore your heartbeat. You also required a breathing machine assist you in breathing. Your echocardiogram or ultrasound of the heart showed a decrease in the pumping function of your heart from 55% to 30%. This is called systolic heart failure. This is when the heart cannot meet the demands of the body and, although not often, can happen following a cardioversion. You quickly recovered with diuresis (medicine) to assist the heart in getting rid of the extra fluid the body had accumulated and we were able to remove the breathing tube.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with CAD s/p MIs, CABG, and stenting, critical AS s/p recent valvuloplasty, CHF with EF improved to 50%, paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, and HTN who presents with lightheadedness for 1 week. . # Lightheadedness: The patient states that he experiences some mild lightheadedness on standing after prolonged periods of sitting, but denies any associated symptoms (tunnel vision, n/v, diaphoresis). The patient recently saw his PCP ___ ___ with the same complaint. At that time, his orthostatic symptoms were attributed to hyper-beta-blockade and his metoprolol was decreased from 25 mg 2XD to 12.5 mg 2XD. The patient was also admitted with evidence of dehydration, although he does not endorse poor PO intake at home. Given the patient's aortic stenosis, it is difficult for him to augment his cardiac output during an orthostatic response due to a fixed obstruction. Furthermore, his beta-blockade limits his ability to elevate his heart rate. These factors all conspire against the patient's ability to maintain an adequate cardiac output and contribute to problems with orthostasis, post-prandial hypotension. As such, his metoprolol and lisinopril were discontinued, and the patient was discharged on a smaller dose of lasix (from 80 mg to 20 mg once daily). . # Aortic Stenosis: The patient is s/p recent aortic valvuloplasty ___, with some improvement of his aortic stenosis. However, aortic stenosis is likely contributing to his orthostatic symptoms. The patient is preload dependent, making his fluid status, and overall outpatient medication management challenging. The patient's preload-reducers and diuretics were held, and he was discharged on a decreased dose of lasix as above. . #CAD: The patient had a recent bare metal stent placed in his SVG->D1 on ___. He was continued on his aspirin, statin, and plavix. The patient was also ruled out for an acute MI while he was in the hospital. EKG was unchanged from prior and cardiac enzymes were negative x 3. . # Ischemic Cardiomyopathy: The patient's last EF was 50-55% (TEE) on ___. His metoprolol and ace-inhibitor were held and he was discharged on a lower dose of lasix, as above. . # Atrial Fibrillation: The patient has a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. On a recent hospitalization earlier this month, the patient was started on amiodarone. He was continued on this medication during this hospitalization, albeit at a lower dose. The patient was discharged on 400 mg amiodarone once daily (previously 400 mg twice a day). The patient might need to be monitored on a Holter monitor on an outpatient basis to determine how frequently he is in atrial fibrillation (and whether or not he needs to be continued on amiodarone).
summary: You came to the hospital because you had been experiencing some lightheadedness when you stood up after prolonged periods of sitting, which has been intermittent since you were discharged from the hospital on ___. Given your heart troubles in the past, you were admitted to ___ to make sure you did not have a heart attack. After collecting some bloodwork and performing EKGs, we were able to say that you did not have a heart attack. Your lightheadedness is due to a combination of factors - most importantly dehydration and the use of diuretics and medications that slow your heart rate. Since you have aortic stenosis (the aortic valve is stiff), it is difficult for your heart to increase the strength of its contraction during exercise. When you stand after a long period of sitting, your heart takes a while to adjust to the increased activity level. Additionally, your heart rate is a little bit low, and one medication in particular (metoprolol) can worsen this. While metoprolol can be beneficial in many patients with heart problems, we recommend that you stop taking this medication.

Furthermore, his beta-blockade limits his ability to elevate his heart rate. These factors all conspire against the patient's ability to maintain an adequate cardiac output and contribute to problems with orthostasis, post-prandial hypotension. As such, his metoprolol and lisinopril were discontinued, and the patient was discharged on a smaller dose of lasix (from 80 mg to 20 mg once daily). . # Aortic Stenosis: The patient is s/p recent aortic valvuloplasty ___, with some improvement of his aortic stenosis. However, aortic stenosis is likely contributing to his orthostatic symptoms. The patient is preload dependent, making his fluid status, and overall outpatient medication management challenging. The patient's preload-reducers and diuretics were held, and he was discharged on a decreased dose of lasix as above. . #CAD: The patient had a recent bare metal stent placed in his SVG->D1 on ___. He was continued on his aspirin, statin, and plavix. The patient was also ruled out for an acute MI while he was in the hospital. EKG was unchanged from prior and cardiac enzymes were negative x 3. . # Ischemic Cardiomyopathy: The patient's last EF was 50-55% (TEE) on ___. His metoprolol and ace-inhibitor were held and he was discharged on a lower dose of lasix, as above. . # Atrial Fibrillation: The patient has a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation. On a recent hospitalization earlier this month, the patient was started on amiodarone. He was continued on this medication during this hospitalization, albeit at a lower dose. The patient was discharged on 400 mg amiodarone once daily (previously 400 mg twice a day). The patient might need to be monitored on a Holter monitor on an outpatient basis to determine how frequently he is in atrial fibrillation (and whether or not he needs to be continued on amiodarone).

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ M with h/o dCHF (EF >55% this admission), HTN, CAD, Afib (on Apixaban), hx of lung CA p/w several weeks of worsening nonproductive cough and DOE. #ACUTE ON CHRONIC DIASTOLIC CHF: Mr. ___ presented with a 3 week history of worsening DOE, which was thought to be an exacerbation of his known diastolic CHF due to his increased BNP (2592 from baseline ___, increased weight (up 5 lbs from baseline), and crackles on exam. He was diuresed with Lasix 100mg x1 and an increase in his home torsemide dose (to 60mg daily instead of 40mg daily) and put out ___ over two days, following which his SOB improved and he was able to be weaned from 2L NC to RA. Discharge torsemide is 40mg. Discharge weight is 77.3kg. #CAP: Mr. ___ also endorses a nonproductive cough for the past 3 weeks. He received a CXR in the ED that showed a LUL and LLL consolidation concerning for infection, and he was treated with an empiric course of levaquin for CAP. He remained afebrile with no white count throughout his admission. He was discharged on levaquin 750mg PO Q48H x3 doses (last dose ___ #Hypotension: His blood pressures have been soft this admission (90s-110s) most likely iso active diuresis for CHF exacerbation. His home lisinopril was held and he was continued on his home amlodipine and doxazosin with improvement in his BPs. His BP was 101/32 on discharge. STABLE ISSUES: His atrial fibrillation, hyperlipidemia, gout, and peripheral neuropathy remained stable this admission, and he was continued on his home medications of apixaban, metoprolol, pravastatin, and allopurinol. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - He was discharged on levaquin 750mg PO Q48H x3 doses (last dose ___ - He was maintained on a higher dose of his home torsemide (60mg daily instead of 40mg daily) for diuresis during this admisison; we have restarted his home dose at discharge -Discharge torsemide is 40mg. -Discharge weight is 77.3kg. # CODE STATUS: Full (confirmed) # COMMUNICATION: HCP ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were experiencing shortness of breath on exertion and a cough for several weeks. You were evaluated by the Medicine team who felt that your shortness of breath and cough were most consistent with an exacerbation of your existing heart failure, although you may also have an overlying lung infection that is contributing to your symptoms as well. You were treated with Lasix (a diuretic) to help you lose the extra fluid in your lungs and an oral antibiotic to treat you for suspected pneumonia. During your stay your shortness of breath improved and you no longer required oxygen. Your weights and total body fluid balance were monitored and both improved.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female with h/o PE on coumadin, had syncopal episode a few days prior to admission w/out prodrome, ECHO showed sig LVOT obstruction, concerning for HOCM. # Syncope: Upon admission, the patient's bedside echo showed a resting LVOT obstruction consistent with hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy and findings consistent with impaired relaxation / dCHF (hyperdynamic LV and E/A ratio < 0.7). Aortic stenosis was not present. EKG showed NSR, with new RAD. A CTA was performed that did not show any PE. Her last echo ___ showed no significant valvular disease, with LVH and dCHF. Throughout hospitaization, no arrhythmias were noted on telemetry. In the setting of HOCD, syncope could have been precipitated by events that acutely increase outflow obstruction such decreased preload due to dehydration or diuretic use. The patient did become hypotensive in the ED (87/38) but responded to a 1 L NS bolus (121/65). Upon arrival the the floor, the patient's home furosemide (20 mg every other day, alternating with 40 mg every other day) was held, as well as the patient's home methazolamide, captopril, and amlodipine. Metoprolol tartrate 25 mg TID was started. The patient remained hemodynamically stable on the floor with good PO intake. Due to stable blood pressures, furosemide was added back on as 20 mg every other day by day of discharge. She was also started on lisinopril 10 mg daily. She was discharged on lisinopril 10 mg daily, metoprolol 25 mg TID, and furosemide 20 mg QOD. Amlodipine and captopril were discontinued. Methazolmide 25 mg BID was restarted upon discharge. # History of PE: Patient had previously been admitted for syncope secondary to PEs and has since been on coumadin. CTA in the ED was negative, INR on admission was therapeutic at 2.7. Patient received 3 mg of warfarin in the ED and on the floor received 4 mg of warfarin daily. Her INR was 3.6 on day of discharge, so she was instructed to skip her warfarin dose that day and then was restarted on 3 mg warfarin daily (her home dose) with an INR check with her PCP 3 days after discharge. # Hypertension: At home, patient was taking amlodipine 5 mg daily, captopril 50 mg daily, methazolamide 25 mg BID, and furosemide 20 mg every other day, alternating with 40 mg. When the patient received hypertension meds in the ED, became hypotensive to 87/38 but since receiving IVF her blood pressures improved to ___. On the floor, amlodipine, furosemide, methazolamide, and captopril were initially held, and she was started on metoprolol tartrate 25 mg TID. She remained hemodynamically stable throughout hospital stay, with systolic BPs ranging in the 120s, to the 130s with activity. On the day of discharge her SBP ranged from 94-163, with orthostatic BP measurements showing an SPB of 165 with standing. Her medications were altered on day of discharge as noted above. # Anemia: Patient had a history of a lower GIB secondary to lovenox in the past. Her hemotocrit was monitored closely during her hospitalization, with her Hct at 31.8 at day of discharge. Throughout her hospital stay, there were no signs of bleeding. # Hyperlipidemia: The patient continued on her home dose of simvastatin 20 mg daily.
summary: You were admitted because you had a fainting episode. We imaged your heart and found it to be a little thick, which can make it harder for the heart to pump blood to the body. It is possible that your blood pressure was low, which may have caused you to faint. Because of this, we altered some of your medications while you were in the hospital. Your Captopril was replaced with Lisinopril. In addition you were started on metoprolol. Amlodipine was discontinued. We also encourage you to wear compression stockings on your legs which can help keep your blood pressure adequately elevated and help prevent lower extremity swelling. Please do not take warfarin today and resume on ___ at 3 mg daily. Please have your INR checked on ___ at Dr. ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ yo male with PVD, LLE ulcer/cellulitis being treated with augmentin, s/p R BKA, DM2, HTN, hyperlipidemia, h/o osteomyelitis on chronic rifampin supression presenting with chest pain and worsening SOB x7 days. . # Chest Pain - Possible etiologies included PE given known DVT w/ subtherapeutic INR, ischemic cardiac disease (multiple risk factors including PVD) though story not typical, pericarditis given small pericardial effusion, and an upper GI etiology as the pain started in his throat. Troponin in ED mildly elevated at 0.08 from baseline of 0.04. His BNP was elevated, no baseline in system. These finding could result from right heart strain from new PE. His CXR and exam were consistent with overload and atelectasis. His troponins were flat after the initial elevation and his CK-MB was never elevated. EKG was unchanged and his chest pain resolved by the time he was on the floor. The pain and mildly elevated troponin were likely due to demand, either from a pulmonary embolism or his worsening heart failure. . # Shortness of breath - The patient complained of steadily increasing shortness of breath. Main differential was worsening heart failure with increasing pleural effusions with or without a new PE (INR 1.6 on admission). A CTA chest was considered dangerous with his CKD, and a V/Q would almost definitely be abnormal with his large pleural effusions, so no testing was done to look for a pulmonary embolism. He will need 6 months of anticoagulation either way, and an echo did not show signs of right heart strain. He was bridged with heparin until his INR was once again therapeutic. He was diuresed with BID IV lasix, up to 80mg each dose with symptomatic improvement. Metolazone was added one day, but resulted in an increase in his creatinine. He was discharged on 40mg of PO lasix BID, up from 20mg on admission. His discharge weight was 187lbs with his shoes and RLE prosthesis. . # DVT w/ subtherapeutic INR - He was initially continued on coumadin at 3mg daily and bridged w/ a heparin drip. His coumadin was increased to 5mg, then 4mg daily. His INR was up to 3.8 the day of discharge. His coumadin should be held for two nights, then he should get 3mg/4mg daily on alternating days. . # Pericardial effusion - the patient was noted to have a new pericardial rub the morning of admission. An echocardiogram was done revealing a small, echodense pericardial effusion. A repeat echocardiogram was done four days later, showing no change, so no further work-up was done for the source. . # CKD - his creatinine was at baseline. He was persistently mildly hyperkalemic throughout his stay despite being on high doses of lasix. He received one dose of kayexalate, but otherwise the K+ was simply trended. Highest at 5.4. K+ was 4.9 on discharge. . # Hypertension - was hypertensive on admission in the setting of pain. When his pain improved he remained hypertensive, so his amlodipine was changed to 10mg daily with some improvement. . #LLE Ulcer - Appeared stable, so he was simply continued on augmentin, and will follow-up with Dr. ___ to determine the timeline of treatment. . #H/O MRSA osteomylitis: Patient is on chronic rifampin supression, which was continued through this admission. . #Back Pain - patient has severe, chronic low-back pain on several medications, previously on oxycontin SR BID, vicodin prn. These medications were continued, and he was kept on an aggressive bowel regimen.
summary: You were admitted with chest pain and shortness of breath. You were found to have a small amount of fluid around your heart and had a lot of extra fluid in your lungs and legs. We treated you with Lasix (furosemide) to help you urinate more and clear this extra fluid. A repeat ultrasound of your help showed no change in the fluid around your heart and your trouble breathing improved. You are now ready to go back to rehab and work on getting strong. You have heart failure and will accumulate fluid if you are not very carefully. Your weight when you left here was 187lbs with your shoes and prosthesis. You should be weighed every day, and call Dr. ___ if your weight changes by more than 3lbs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Primary Reason for Hospitalization: ___ year-old female w/PMHx of HLD, hypothyroidism, sarcoidosis & anemia who p/w left-chest pain, SOB, N/V and elevated troponin. . # Chest pain: The patient developed sudden onset left sided chest pain that occured during a moment of severe emotional stress while she was at a wake. Initial troponin-T at OSH was 0.047. Repeat troponin from ___ ED was 0.47. However cardiac cath was clean. There was no evidence of PE or dissection on CT. TTE showed depressed EF (45%) and mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction which are new compared to echo in ___. Although she did not have the classic appearance on echo these constellation of findings may represent stress induced cardiomyopathy. Another possibility would be a focal myocarditis however that seems less likely given the acuity of presentation. Troponin was trending downwards prior to discharge and pain was well controlled with tylenol and ibuprofen. Final Cardiac MRI report was pending prior to discharge. However prelim report confirmed a focal area of hypokinesis not consistent with any vascular distribution. There was no evidence of infarct. There was mild pericardial effusion but there was no focal inflammation to explain the focal wall motion abnormality. There was a small epicardial area of inflammation that could be suggestive of Sarcoidosis but did not explain the totality of her findings. She will follow-up with Dr. ___ in cardiology on ___. A repeat TTE was ordered prior to that visit that the patient will call to schedule. If this is stress induced cardiomyopathy we would expect near total resolution at the time of that echo. She was started on lisinopril 2.5mg daily to aid in cardiac remodeling. Metoprolol could be added if BPs tolerate. . # PUMP: LVEF 45%. mild regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with hypokinesis of the mid-inferolateral/lateral segments. Prior to discharge she was clinically euvolemic without evidence of heart failure on exam. She was continued on home dose of lasix 40mg daily. She was also started on lisinopril 2.5mg as described above. . # Splenomegaly: This was seen incidentally on CT chest. Uncertain if this is a new finding. Etiology is uncertain but could be from her Sarcoidosis. She also has mild leukopenia. These findings are unlikely to be related to current hospitalization, but may warrant further workup as outpatient. No significant abormalities on LFTs. CBC with diff did not show any suspicious findings. She will f/u with PCP ___ further workup if indicated. . # Hyperlipidemia: She will continue home dose of Simvastatin. . # Hypothyroidism: She will continue home dose of levothyroxine 88mcg daily. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: . # Studies Pending at the time of Discharge: final cath report, final CT report, Cardiac MRI report. . # She will need chem-7 checked prior to appointment with Dr. ___ she was started on lisinopril prior to discharge.
summary: You were originally brought to ___ after you suddenly developed chest pain. At ___ one of your blood tests suggested you could be having a heart attack. Therefore you were transferred to ___. Here at ___ a cardiac catheterization was performed which did not show any blockages in your coronary arteries to explain why your heart had taken some damage. An echocardiogram was performed which showed that a part of your heart is beating weaker than the rest. These findings can be seen in a disorder called Stress Induced Cardiomyopathy. In this condition the heart muscle will usually heal itself. We started you on one new medication (lisinopril) which can help the heart function better. We also performed a cardiac MRI, which helped confirm that you did not have a heart attack and most likely had stress induced cardiac muscle damage as mentioned above.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with T2DM and h/o cardiomyopathy of unknown etiology, with EF of 40% ___ years ago), now 20% with global hypokinesis on TTE with cardiac catheterization demonstrating clean coronaries now s/p ICD placement for primary prevention. # Cardiomyopathy with reduced EF: The patient initially presented to OSH with chest burning found to have newly depressed LVEF from 40% (___) to 17%. She was transferred to ___ for evaluation of CRT versus ICD given mildly prolonged QTc. Upon transfer, the patient underwent cardiac catheterization which demonstrated clean coronaries. TTE consistent with OSH with LVEF 20%, severely dilated LV, and global hypokinesis. TSH normal and ___ negative. Etiology of cardiomyopathy remains unknown. The patient had an episode of 30 beats of ventricular tachycardia on ___. She was asymptomatic and HD stable throughout the event. Following the episode, the patient underwent ICD placement with EP for primary prevention (CRT not done as QTc only midly prolonged). She was discharged on Carvedilol 3.125mg BID (unable to tolerate metop ___ diarrhea), lasix 40mg daily, spironolactone 25mg daily, losartan 50mg daily, Atorvastatin 80mg daily, and ASA 81 daily. # Troponin elevation: Had elevated troponin at OSH without acute EKG changes or other clinical evidence of ACS. Cath negative for CAD and patient denied any chest pain throughout her stay at ___. Likely troponin elevation ___ demand ischemia in the setting of CHF exacerbation. # ___: The patient Cr rose from 1.0 to 1.5 at OSH in the setting of diuresis for acute exacerbation of CHF. Diuresis was held at ___ and her Cr improved. Following cardiac catheterization, the Cr up-trended again likely CIN given contrast load during the procedure. Following discharge, her Cr again down-trended and the patient was restarted on her home furosemide and spironolactone. Cr=1.4 upon discharge. # Hyperkalemia: K+ mildly elevated at 5.6 in the setting of elevated creatinine on ___. Likely decreased clearance given ___. Asymptomatic and EKG unchanged. Downtrended with improvement of renal function. Restarted on home lasix upon discharge. Plan to check labs within 1 week. # Type 2 Diabetes: Restarted on home lantus and glipizide upon discharge. On ISS while in the hospital. # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine # Anxiety: Continued home bupropion with ativan 1mg prn. Plans to follow-up with PCP regarding psychiatry referral for worsening anxiety. Transitional Issues: ===================== - s/p ICD placement by EP on ___ - stopped metoprolol due to GI side effects and started carvedilol 3.125mg BID - continued on home Lasix 40mg daily, aldactone 25mg daily - patient has significant anxiety and has plans to follow-up with psychiatry as an out-patient. Consider starting SSRI as out-pt. - Re-check Cr within 1 week of discharge as was elevated in setting of diuresis and recent cath (discharge Cr=1.4) - Discharge weight: 101.0kg - Contact: ___ (___) - Code: Full
summary: You came in because of your heart failure. Your echocardiogram showed that your heart was not pumping as well as before. You had cardiac catheterization, which did not show any blockages in your heart. You were also seen by the electrophysiology doctors. ___ you went into an irregular rhythm called ventricular tachycardia, it was decided that the safest measure would be to place an defibrillator. The device was placed on ___ and you tolerated the procedure well. This device will help ensure that if you enter the irregular rhythm again and become unstable, you will receive shock therapy to help you return to a normal rhythm. We started you on a new medication called carvedilol. You should take 3.125mg twice a day.

The patient had an episode of 30 beats of ventricular tachycardia on ___. She was asymptomatic and HD stable throughout the event. Following the episode, the patient underwent ICD placement with EP for primary prevention (CRT not done as QTc only midly prolonged). She was discharged on Carvedilol 3.125mg BID (unable to tolerate metop ___ diarrhea), lasix 40mg daily, spironolactone 25mg daily, losartan 50mg daily, Atorvastatin 80mg daily, and ASA 81 daily. # Troponin elevation: Had elevated troponin at OSH without acute EKG changes or other clinical evidence of ACS. Cath negative for CAD and patient denied any chest pain throughout her stay at ___. Likely troponin elevation ___ demand ischemia in the setting of CHF exacerbation. # ___: The patient Cr rose from 1.0 to 1.5 at OSH in the setting of diuresis for acute exacerbation of CHF. Diuresis was held at ___ and her Cr improved. Following cardiac catheterization, the Cr up-trended again likely CIN given contrast load during the procedure. Following discharge, her Cr again down-trended and the patient was restarted on her home furosemide and spironolactone. Cr=1.4 upon discharge. # Hyperkalemia: K+ mildly elevated at 5.6 in the setting of elevated creatinine on ___. Likely decreased clearance given ___. Asymptomatic and EKG unchanged. Downtrended with improvement of renal function. Restarted on home lasix upon discharge. Plan to check labs within 1 week. # Type 2 Diabetes: Restarted on home lantus and glipizide upon discharge. On ISS while in the hospital. # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine # Anxiety: Continued home bupropion with ativan 1mg prn. Plans to follow-up with PCP regarding psychiatry referral for worsening anxiety. Transitional Issues: ===================== - s/p ICD placement by EP on ___ - stopped metoprolol due to GI side effects and started carvedilol 3.125mg BID - continued on home Lasix 40mg daily, aldactone 25mg daily - patient has significant anxiety and has plans to follow-up with psychiatry as an out-patient. Consider starting SSRI as out-pt. - Re-check Cr within 1 week of discharge as was elevated in setting of diuresis and recent cath (discharge Cr=1.4) - Discharge weight: 101.0kg - Contact: ___ (___) - Code: Full

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man with prior inferior MI ___ and PCI ___ for exertional angina (DES x2 to LAD) presents as transfer s/p cardiac arrest with single shock returning patient to sinus rhythm. Intubated and sedated for cooling protocol given inability to follow commands on arrival at ___. #Cardiac Arrest: Likely hemodynamically unstable VT by history of prodrome of lightheadedness and diaphoresis with subsequent syncope. He was defibrillated x1 by police with return of spontaneous circulation. He initially was sedated and intubated at ___ for depressed mental status and transferred to ___ for post-arrest care. Coronary angiography showed known chronic RCA occlusion without new intervenable lesion. He was therapeutically cooled to 34 degrees x 24 hours with sedation and neuromuscular blockade. He briefly required vasopressors. EEG showed no seizure activity. Once warmed, sedation was weaned and he was extubated with apparently normal neurologic status other than mild short term memory impairments of undetermined long-term significance. Echocardiography showed severely depressed left ventricular ejection fraction without apical akinesis to warrant warfarin. Electrophysiology was consulted and recommended cardiac MRI and implantable cardioverter defibrillator. CMR demonstrated severe left ventricular systolic dysfunction with extensive transmural scar in the inferior and inferolateral walls. ICD was placed without complication on ___ after an EP study induced VT. #Systolic heart failure, compensated: Newly depressed EF of 20% from 50% earlier this month on myocardial perfusion stress test. Likely related to arrest and shock as coronary angiography showed no new lesion and he did not have large infarction by cardiac enzymes. He was treated with aspirin 81mg daily, lovastatin (home medication, history of statin intolerance), and was started on lisinopril and metoprolol succinate. He requires repeat echocardiogram in the future to evaluate for transience of LV dysfunction. Lisinopril was held just prior to discharge because of orthostatic hypotension. Metoprolol was continued. ACEI and spirinolactone should be started in the outpatient setting for systolic heart failure. #Coronary artery disease: Stent to LAD ___ for angina; medically managed inferior MI in ___. Had a nuclear stress test on ___ which showed inferior infarction with marginal ischemia with exercise (estimated LVEF ~50%). He was initially treated with heparin, which was stopped after ___ coronary angiography showed known old RCA occlusion, and no new lesion. Treated with beta-blocker and aspirin as above. He should have a trial of a high potency statin. #Acute kidney injury: The patient has baseline creatinine of 1.1. It peaked at 2.4 mid-way through admission, thought to be related to decreased PO intake and newly started ACE inhibitor. Hypovolemia was confirmed by orthostatic hypotension. ACE inhibitor was held and he was given gentle IV fluids, which improved his creatinine. Cr on discharge was 1.5 #Hyperlipidemia: Continued home lovastatin. He should be evaluated for new statin given CAD (rosuvastatin potentially). #Gout: prior to discharge the patient complained of gout in ___ joints. He had evidence of right great toe podagra. He was treated with prednisone given inability to take NSAIDs/colchicine based on serum creatinine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [ ] Complete steroid taper for gout [ ] F/U ___ after d/c for consideration of VT ablation if continued episodes of VT [ ] device clinic 1 week [ ] cognitive neuro f/u [ ] needs spironolatone and ___ started based on blood pressure [ ] consider trial of high potency statin [ ] Cont to monitor Cr to ensure resolution back to baseline
summary: You were admitted following a cardiac arrest. Your body temperature was cooled to protect your brain following your arrest. You required a breathing tube transiently to support your breathing. A cardiac MRI revealed evidence of a scar which was likely the cause of the arrhythmia which resulted in your cardiac arrest. You underwent placement of an internal defibrillator device to shock you out of any lethal arrythmia. You also underwent a cardiac catheterization which showed that you have previous blockages in your heart arteries that did not need to be fixed at this time. Your heart is very weak after the arrest and you will need to weigh yourself every day in the morning. Call Dr. ___ your weight increases more than 3 pounds in 1 day or 5 pounds in 3 days. Your weight at discharge is noted on your discharge sheet.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ h/o CAD with BMS to RCA, NSTEMI and CVA x 2 who presents to the ED complaining of HA and backpain which are consistent with prior episodes of coronary events. # NSTEMI: Pt presented with mid-scapular back pain which was concerning for her anginal equivalent. She was initially started on a nitro drip titrated to eliminate back pain. She was therapeutically anticoagulated, so she was not started on a heparin drip. She was also given aspirin, statin, BB, and ___. Once her INR drifted down to a safe level, she underwent cardiac cath. This study showed angiographically significant disease in the LAD. She underwent successful ___ to the mid-LAD. # DM II: Very poorly controlled as an outpatient with last HgbA1C of 11.1 in ___. Per discussion with her PCP, this is likely secondary to non-compliance. Here she was maintained on basal and sliding scale insulin, which was uptitrated based on FSBG levels. # CHF: Pt complained of some "panting" which may be consistent with shortness of breath, she was not able to fully characterize these symptoms. On admission, he BNP was elevated, but similar to her prior readings in ___ and ___. CXR showed stable cardiomegaly and some increased vascular congestion. Her last echo in ___ revealed an Ef of 45%. She was treated with lasix bolus dosing as needed. On discharge she was sent with lasix 40 mg PO daily for diuresis. She was also maintained on her home regimen of spironolactone, aspirin, BB, and ___. # Hypertension: On admission and during the first hospital day she remained very hypertensive, despite treatment with her home antihypertensive medication regimen. We added nifedipine CR, which was uptitrated quickly. We acheived good blood pressure control with the addition of this medication. She was discharged with this new medication, as well as her other home meds. CHRONIC ISSUES: # H/O CVA: She has had two documented CVAs in past. Most recent occuring in ___ at ___ which affected the Midbrain. She has been on systemic anticoagulation for stroke prevention. Her INR on admission was therapeutic, and was allowed to trend down to allow for safe cath. Prior to discharge, she was restarted on coumadin for ongoing anticoagulation. Additionally, she complained of persistent vertigo, and she was scheduled for outpatient evaluation by neurology for this issue. # Hypothroidism: Continued levothyroxine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - may require further uptitration of her insulin regimen as an outpatient.
summary: You were admitted for evaluation of your back pain, which was concerning for cardiac angina. While you were here you underwent a cardiac catheterization, which showed some narrowing of your coronary arteries. You had a stent placed in one of your arteries. Because of this stent, you need to be on plavix going forward, unless instructed to stop by a CARDIOLOGIST. Also, while you were here, we noticed that your blood pressure and blood sugars were both very high. We added a new medication to help better control your blood pressure. This medication is called nifedipine. Please continue taking this medication as an outpatient. Additionally, we increased your dose of basal lantus insulin and mealtime insulin as well. Please continue using these increased doses at home. Also, you were noted to have some evidence of heart failure on admission. You were treated with lasix, and you should continue to take this medication as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #NSTEMI: The patient was transferred for management of NSTEMI. His peak cardiac markers were CK 890, MB 127, trop 2.11 on ___ at OSH. Cardiac cath was deferred secondary to bacteremia but the patient will benefit from elective cath following a complete course of antibiotic therapy. He was treated with heparin gtt for 48 hours. He was given aspirin, plavix, toprol, lisinopril, and statin, all of which will be continued upon discharge. Telemetry did not reveal any arrhythmia. The patient remained euvolemic, without signs and symptoms of decompensated CHF. TTE showed mild inferolateral hypokinesis, mod-severe AS, LVEF 50%. The patient was given lifestyle and medication counseling. He will be seen in cardiology follow up 3 days after discharge. . #E. Coli Bacteremia: The patient was afebrile upon transfer to ___, and was not clinically septic. He was treated with IV cipro/flagyl empirically and then changed to PO cipro when culture and sensitivity data became available. His white count quickly trended down. Urinalysis, RUQ U/S, and CT of chest and abdomen did not reveal a source of the bacteremia. Other than a mildly elevated AST, LFT's were WNL. However, the patient has never had a colonoscopy, and it was theorized that there may have been translocation of gut bacteria from an occult GI malignancy. Colonoscopy was deferred to the outpatient in light of recent MI. Blood cultures after his arrival here have not grown to date. He was seen in consultation by ID, who recommended completion of a 14 day course of cipro. . #C. diff colitis - The patient had a positive toxin assay on ___ and was begun on flagyl to complete a 14 day course. The patient was counseled about not drinking alcohol while taking flagyl. . # RLL nodules - Incidental finding on chest CT, but were felt not to be related to the E. Coli bacteremia. The patient will need a follow-up chest CT in ___ months. . # DM: Patient's glyburide was held upon admission and he was covered with an insulin sliding scale. Glyburide was restarted and well-tolerated prior to discharge. . # HTN: Amlodopine was discontinued due to concerns for negative inotropy. Lisinopril was continued at the usual dose. Atenolol was replaced with lopressor. He will be discharged on a dose of Toprol XL 50 mg daily, a dose which was well-tolerated during the admission. . # Hyperlipidemia - Statin was continued. . #F/E/N - Given a cardiac, diabetic diet. . #PPx - SC heparin was used for DVT prophylaxis once heparin drip stopped. FOR OUTPATIENT PROVIDERS: 1) Colonoscopy as soon as possible 2) Follow-up CT chest in 3 months (___)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with a heart attack and a blood stream infection. You were also diagnosed with an infection in your intestines. Your infections were partially treated with antibiotics. Please continue taking ciprofloxacin through ___. Please continue taking flagyl as prescribed through ___ ___. Do not drink alcohol while taking Flagyl as the combination may produce serious side effects. You were started on plavix 75 mg daily and your aspirin dose was increased to 325 mg daily. Your simvastatin dose was increased to 80 mg daily. Taking these medications EVERY day will decrease your risk for another heart attack. Your atenolol was changed to a similar medication called toprol XL 50 mg daily. Please refrain from taking norvasc any longer as it may decrease the heart's ability to pump.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old lady with history of Mitral valve prolapse ___ bioprosthetic MVR, atrial flutter (on Coumadin) morbid obesity, sleep apnea who presented with 2 days of worsening dyspnea, orthopnea, peripheral edema, weight gain consistent with acute on chronic CHF exacerbation. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Acute on chronic systolic CHF/MV regurgitation: Evidenced by symptoms of worsening DOE, orthopnea, pulmonary rales on exam, and pulmonary edema on CXR. Patient's baseline weight is 180lb and was noted to have 194lb at PCP's office. On medicine floor, patient was diuresed with 40mg IV lasix x3 with 2L urine output. She had TTE which showed markedly dilated RV with abnormal systolic septal motion consistent with RV pressure overload. Her estimated PASP was 70 mmHg. She also had severe mitral stenosis. There was also noted abnormal MVR motion c/w partial dehiscence. 2+ MR. ___ was evaluated by heart failure team who recommended that patient be transferred to cardiology team for further diuresis and for urgent evaluation of mitral valve replacement. Upon transfer to Cardiology service patient was started on a lasix drip and was diuresed from admission weight of 85.5kg to 76kg by day of admission. Cardiothoracic surgery was consulted who recommended that she undergo MVR during this hospitalization and pre-op evaluation was completed. However, patient requested to be discharged for the weekend to set up proper home care for her mother for whom she is the primary care provider ___. She was discharged on torsemide 20mg PO daily and schedule to present on ___ to the cath lab for a RHC prior to scheduling an OR date for MVR as she ___ required further diuresis prior to surgery. . # A-flutter: Currently sinus rhythm without episodes of tachyarrhythmia which is likely secondary to known atrial enlargement. She was continued on home amiodarone, metoprolol and warfarin and her INR was monitored daily. She was instructed to stop taking warfarin 2 days prior to coming back to the hospital for RHC. # Code: Full (confirmed) # Communication: Patient, HCP ___ ___ TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient was discharged to secure care for her mother, she will present on ___ to the cath lab for RHC with plans to go either for MVR or to CCU for further diuresis.
summary: you were admitted to the ___ after gaining weight despite your water pills at home. You were given strong medications through an IV to get water out of your body. It was also noted that there was a leak around your mitral valve and unfortunately, this valve needs to be replaced. The plan is for you to go home for several days to take care of your mother and then come back to the Hospital on ___ to have the special catheter placed to measure the pressures in your heart prior to going to surgery for a valve replacement. In preparation for your procedure, your last dose of coumadin (warfarin) should be on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old woman with h/o morbid obesity, diastolic CHF (EF 60%), DMII, PAF on coumadin, h/o prior CVA, PVD who presented from her nursing home with anemia without evidence of active bleeding or blood loss, and dyspnea secondary to acute on chronic decompensated diastolic heart failure off her daily diuretics after her last hospital admission. She was also found to have persistent klebsiella UTI for which a PICC was placed and the patient was started on a course of IV meropenem. . # Anemia Patient's Hct had been trending downwards at the nursing home, Hct this AM is 27.5. However, this is within her baseline of ___ with known iron deficiency anemia. Her guiac was negative and she has no signs of active bleeding. Last iron studies on ___ WNL. Her hematocrit was stable during this admission and she had no clear signs of bleeding. We conservatively tried to keep her INR between 2 and 2.5 given her history of GI bleeding in the past. Of note, during her last admission with GI bleed, she had a colonoscopy on ___ which was normal but a poor prep. She also had upper endoscopy which was only notable for one lymphangiectasia in her duodenum which per GI, was not the cause of her bleed. Currently the patient does not need further workup of this. If she has another bleed in the future, repeat colonoscopy with a better prep, then capsule endoscopy may be the next step. . # Dyspnea Etiology of her dyspnea was thought most likely secondary to decompensated congestive heart failure while she was off of her lasix. Most likely patient was taken off of her lasix when she was hospitalized for the GI bleed and it was not restarted. We restarted her lasix and her breathing improved. She was stable on room air. We also continue to encourage smoking cessation. . # History of paroxysmal afib Patient was maintained with appropriate heart rate control and also with anticoagulation with coumadin. Given her previous GI bleed, she was maintained with a conservative INR of ___. She will need close monitoring in rehab. . # Acute on chronic kidney disease Patient with Cr on admission of 1.9 which is slightly elevated from her baseline of 1.4-1.7, currently 1.9. She was thought to be pre-renal secondary to cardiogenic causes. She was restarted on her furosemide with improvement both in her shortness of breath and improvement of her creatinine from 1.9 to 1.7. Her lisinopril was held during this admission and should be restarted at rehab. . #. Recent UTI Patient recently finished course of bactrim for Ecoli/Klebsiella UTI. Patient denies any dysuria; however her recent Klebsiella UTI was an ESBL Klebsiella and sensitive to meropenem and zosyn only. She was started on a course of IV meropenem through her PICC line and should complete a 7 day course. . # Ischemia Patient had no evidence of active ischemia or chest pain during this admission and had two sets of cardiac enzymes which were both negative. She was continued on her aspirin, statin, and Toprol XL. . # Pump Patient had an ECHO in ___ which demonstrated a normal EF of 60% but had an elevated LVEDP and diastolic dysfunction. BNP was elevated to ~1300 but decreased when compared to prior admissions. CXR clear and exam without signs of volume overload. She was restarted on her home dose lasix. Her ACEI was held in the setting of her slightly elevated creatinine. Her lisinopril should be restarted at rehab. She was otherwise continued on her beta blocker. . # h/o CVA on ___ - Patient without new symptoms. . # DM Patient was maintained on her home diabetes regimen of lantus 10 units qhs with humalog 2 units tid with an additional HISS. .
summary: You were admitted to rule out a gastrointestinal bleed due to decreasing blood levels at your rehab facility, and some symptoms of shortness of breath and continued pain on urination. You were found to have not indications of active bleeding and your anemia was stable. You were given additional lasix water pills for fluid in your lungs and your shortness of breath was improved with this. We also repeated your urine culture and you required intravenous antibiotics for continued urinary tract infection. An intravenous line was placed and you were started on intravenous antibiotics for your urinary tract infection.
