She will need close monitoring in rehab. . # Acute on chronic kidney disease Patient with Cr on admission of 1.9 which is slightly elevated from her baseline of 1.4-1.7, currently 1.9. She was thought to be pre-renal secondary to cardiogenic causes. She was restarted on her furosemide with improvement both in her shortness of breath and improvement of her creatinine from 1.9 to 1.7. Her lisinopril was held during this admission and should be restarted at rehab. . #. Recent UTI Patient recently finished course of bactrim for Ecoli/Klebsiella UTI. Patient denies any dysuria; however her recent Klebsiella UTI was an ESBL Klebsiella and sensitive to meropenem and zosyn only. She was started on a course of IV meropenem through her PICC line and should complete a 7 day course. . # Ischemia Patient had no evidence of active ischemia or chest pain during this admission and had two sets of cardiac enzymes which were both negative. She was continued on her aspirin, statin, and Toprol XL. . # Pump Patient had an ECHO in ___ which demonstrated a normal EF of 60% but had an elevated LVEDP and diastolic dysfunction. BNP was elevated to ~1300 but decreased when compared to prior admissions. CXR clear and exam without signs of volume overload. She was restarted on her home dose lasix. Her ACEI was held in the setting of her slightly elevated creatinine. Her lisinopril should be restarted at rehab. She was otherwise continued on her beta blocker. . # h/o CVA on ___ - Patient without new symptoms. . # DM Patient was maintained on her home diabetes regimen of lantus 10 units qhs with humalog 2 units tid with an additional HISS. .

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with vertigo likely secondary to BPPV, with nonfocal neuro exam and no headache or associated symptoms concerning for intracranial pathology. She was initially unsteady, and with her coumadin, at risk for a bleed if she fell. . # Vertigo Per neurology consult, symptoms were consistent with BPPV, rather than stroke or other central cause. Her head CT was negative and neurology did not recommend MR. ___ received multiple doses of meclizine with symptomatic improvement. She was initially on fall precautions. By the evening of the first day of admission, she could walk with no assistance. She was discharged on meclizine 12.5 mg ___ tablets q6h prn. . # UTI Ms. ___ was put on a 3-day course of ciprofloxacin, with culture sent. Preliminary culture shows gram-negative rods, and sensitivities are still pending. . # Hypokalemia Her initial K was 2.8, resolved with PO K repletion. . # Diabetes mellitus She was continued on her home glargine dose, as well as a sliding scale, with admission glucose of 131, AM glucose on the morning of discharge 141. . # CHF The patient has a history of CHF, with primarily diastolic dysfunction documented. EKG showed atrial fibrillation with a slow ventricular response at 58bpm, with RBBB and prolonged QT - no signs of ischemia. Her home medications, including torsemide, metolazone, carvedilol, candesartan, amiodarone, isosorbide mononitrate, aspirin, and atorvastatin were continued in house. . # A-fib She was continued on 2mg warfarin daily in house, which was thought to be her home dose. The dosing was somewhat unclear, with differing reports from her family, PCP, and prior d/c paperwork regarding her warfarin dosing (2mg vs 5mg daily) and insulin dosing and whether she was still on amiodarone. INR remained in the target ___ range. . # ESRD All medications were renally dosed. Her creatinine did rise from 3 to 3.6 during hospitalization, with a likely prerenal etiology due to poor PO intake. . # Hypertension Her home diuretics, carvedilol, ___, and nitrate were continued as above. . # Gout She was maintained on allopurinol. . # OSA She received CPAP in house. . # Diet: Regular cardiac/low Na, renal, diabetic/constant carb
summary: You were admitted with dizziness. You were evaluated by a CT scan of the head and by the neurology team. Your dizziness is most likely from benign paroxysmal positional vertigo. This is a disorder of equilibrium, as we discussed. It should get better over the next ___ hours, but this disorder does sometimes recur. We gave you a new medicine to try, Meclizine, that could help the symptoms. Also, you can try the Epley maneuver with your family. You were also found to have a urinary tract infection, please finish the course of antibiotics. You only need to take one more pill of Ciprofloxacin. You should have an up-to-date list of medications with you at all times.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ gentleman with a history of CAD s/p CABG x3, who presented with epigastric/chest pain, and underwent stress testing which revealed systolic dysfunction but no definite perfusion defect. He was discharged home with GI and Cardiology follow-up, and he may benefit from an elective cardiac catheterization in the future. . # CORONARIES: He does have many concerning risk factors for CAD, namely PVD, HTN, HLD, smoking history. He has had CABG x3 ___ years ago, and there is always concern for graft occlusion. However, his story is not typical for angina (not necessarily exertional, occasionally lasts 10+ minutes or more, is more epigastric than substernal.) He ruled out for acute MI. He underwent P MIBI which did show poor systolic function (20%) but no discrete perfusion defect. He did drop his SBP with exercise (not symptomatic) but he is A-V paced and had a blunted HR response as well. It is possible that his systolic dysfunction is due to ischemia that has affected the heart globally, and he would likely benefit from an elective cardiac catheterization. This could be done after his GI workup (see below). He will continue ASA (81mg given possible GI bleed), Plavix, and Metoprolol. He will follow-up with his local Cardiologist as well as Dr. ___. . # PUMP: Patient does not carry diagnosis of CHF; his EF was 50% in ___, but it was 20% during this admission. He denies any symptoms of PND/orthopnea/leg swelling but he does take Lasix every other day. He is currently euvolemic on exam. He was continued on Lasix every other day. . # RHYTHM: Currently A-V paced, not in atrial fibrillation. He has a pacemaker for h/o tachy-brady syndrome, and he is on Amiodarone for h/o atrial fibrillation. He was continued on Amiodarone for rhythm control and Metoprolol for rate control. . # Hypertension: currently SBP range is 135-150; he was continued on his home regimen: Metoprolol, Lisinopril, Imdur, Lasix but he may benefit from increased dose of Lisinopril in the future. . # Hyperlipidemia: he takes Pravastatin 40mg at home, and hiven that his lipid profile was: Total Chol: 142, Triglyc: 66, HDL: 55, LDLcalc: 74, he was continued on this med. . # Anemia: Hct was 30.7 (was 41.3 in ___. Based on iron studies, is due to iron deficiency. He has never had a colonoscopy (had a sigmoidoscopy many years ago), and guaiac positive stool in the ER is concerning for malignancy in a gentleman of ___ years of age, especially considering his malaise/fatigue. This complicates the management of his cardiac issues (for example, Heparin gtt was considered at first but was held because it could potentially exacerbate any GI bleeding.) He was scheduled for an outpatient colonoscopy for 4 days after discharge, and he will follow up with his PCP/Cardiologist.
summary: You came to the hospital because of chest/abdomen pain. We made sure you were not having a heart attack. In order to further work up your discomfort, you underwent a stress test which showed that the heart is not pumping as well as it should be, but there were no issues seen that indicated the need for emergent cardiac catheterization. During the admission, you were noted to be anemic from iron deficiency, which could indicate chronic blood loss. This is concerning because your stool was positive for blood. You have been scheduled for a colonoscopy in 4 days after discharge to further work up your anemia. Please read the enclosed colonoscopy info packet.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Sra. ___ is a ___ ___ lady with AFib, MV stenosis s/p mechanical MV replacement ___ on Warfarin (INR goal 2.5-3.5), severe tricuspid regurgitation, chronic right-sided heart failure (EF 50%), with congestive hepatopathy and ascites, severe pulmonary hypertension, and CAD s/p CABG ___ who had ongoing tooth pain as an outpatient. She was directly admitted to Medicine for Heparin bridge while her INR was allowed to drift downwards in order to undergo tooth extraction. She underwent uncomplicated single extraction of tooth #28 on ___ and then remained in house on a Heparin drip until her INR was therapeutic again, and then she was discharged home. ## DENTAL INFECTION & PAIN: resolved s/p extraction. No signs or symptoms of severe infection. OMFS performed uncomplicated single extraction of tooth #28 on ___, with pre-operative Unasyn. No bleeding complications. The procedure was done in the OR but only under local anesthesia. Sutures will dissolve. She will follow up with her dentist or OMFS, as she prefers. ## MECHANICAL MV REPLACEMENT: on Warfarin, goal INR 2.5-3.5. As soon as she was directly admitted to the Medicine floor, she was placed on Heparin gtt and her Warfarin was held. She was therapeutic for the duration of her admission, except for stopping the drip 6 hrs pre-procedure. It was able to be re-started immediately post-operatively, and she was restarted on her Warfarin post-operatively as well. Heparin gtt was stopped and she was discharged when her INR was >2.5. Home Warfarin dose was not changed; she will f/u tomorrow at ___ ___ clinic for INR check. - 5mg given ___ mg given ___ (INR in am = 1.9) - 7.5 mg give ___ (INR in am = 2.3) - Discharged ___ (INR in am = 3.0), please take 5 mg at home ## CHF: stable issue, euvolemic. Her tricuspid regurgitation is severe and moderate pulmonary hypertension; she was euvolemic on exam. She was continued on her home meds (torsemide, metoprolol, hctz, spironolactone). ## HYPONa: asymptomatic, stable. Na 133 on admission. Euvolemic on exam. Baseline Na appears to be 133-140. She remains on HCTZ, Torsemide, Spironolactone which could be contributing. Na was stable at 132 on the day of discharge. ## DM 2: stable. She was continued on her home Lantus 60mg QHS. She initially declined the proposed sliding scale (she felt that she would drop too low since she was NPO for the procedure) and was subsequently hyperglycemic in the 200's-300's. When her family brought in her home sliding scale, it was entered and she complied, with much better blood glucose control. She did not have any hypoglycemia during this admission. ## GOUT/OA: stable. She was continued on Febuxostat and low-dose Prednisone. ## TRANSITIONAL ISSUES Emergency Contact: ______ Studies/labs pending at discharge: --Panorex was viewed/interpreted by ___ so no Radiology read pending --Extracted tooth pathology
summary: You were admitted for extraction of your right premolar tooth and management of your Warfarin/INR during this procedure. Your Home INR(International Normalized Ratio) goal is 2.5 - 3.5 given your mechanical mitral valve. Your Warfarin was stopped the day before the procedure, and Heparin was started to maintain anticoagulation. The oral and maxillofacial surgeons (___) performed the extraction in an operating room while Heparin was off. The operation was successful and there was minimal bleeding. Sutures were left in the location of the former tooth, and these sutures will dissolve on their own. The ___ team suggests you follow up with them, or your home Dentist. The ___ phone number is ___ if you wish to follow up with them. After the procedure your Heparin was continued and Warfarin was restarted to reach your INR goal of 2.5. On the day of discharge your INR = 3.0.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y.o M with PMHx of ESRD s/p renal transplant, DM, CAD with inferior MI and CABG, cardiomyopathy with EF 45% ___ presenting with shortness of breath now on HD. # Acute on chronic sCHF with preserved EF (46%)- BNP elevated to ___ on admission (though down from previous admission for CHF). Likely in setting of diuretic non-compliance as well as renal failure. Repeat TTE poor quality, but largely unchanged. The patient was started on nitro gtt and dobutamine to maximize medical management. However, urine output remained poor suggesting renal failure. Unfortunately, unable to perform RHC in setting of inability to lie flat to confrim, however patient initated on HD with resolution of volume overload. Patient continued on 100mg Torsemide on non-dialysis days. Other medication changes this admission with regards to patients heart failure: reduced carvedilol to 3.125mg qD and added isosorbide dinitrate. Did not start ACE ___ CKD and EF relatively preserved at 45%. Finally, R chest tube was placed and drained ~3L pleural effusion. While exudative by criteria, felt to be more likely pseudoexudate in setting of significant diuretic usage this admission. Cytologies negative for malignant cells, ___ negative and no evidence of infection this admission. # CKD - s/p transplant in ___ complicated by chronic allograft nephropathy. As above, the patient remained volume overloaded despite aggressive medical therapy and was reinitated on HD this admission. Tacrolimus reduced to 1mg qD, Cellcept reduced to 250mg qD and continued on Bactrim this admission. Follwed by renal transplant service this admission. The patient was arranged to continue dialysis on ___ schedule. # Hypoxia - unclear etiology, likely initially related to volume overload and pleural effusions though patient had small O2 requirement ___ down from 5L earlier this admission) despite effective volume management later this admission. Repeat CXR with peristent L effusion but no evidence of PNA on Xray and patient without cough. Felt likely persistent hypoxia ___ remaining effusion as well as deconditioning. Patient was discharged on home O2 to maintain sats >90%. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Coronary Artery Disease: s/p stents many years ago. CABG in ___. EKG on admission consistent with prior, without symptoms of chest pain. Continued ASA 81mg, Atorvastatin 20mg qHS and Carvedilol 3.125mg BID. # GERD: continued Omeprazole 40 mg # BPH: Continued home Finasteride and Tamsulosin. # HTN: Continued on home carvedilol 3.125mg BID, hydralazine 25mg TID, isosorbide dinitrate 20mg TID # HLD: continued Atorvastatin 20 mg q ___ # T2DM: Increase glargine 25U qHS. Continue gabapentin 300 mg q AM and 600 mg q ___ for neuropathy. Duloxetine Tapered 20mg qD (Start ___ with plan for off ___. # Hypothyroidism: continued home levothyroxine 150mcg qAM # Anemia: Hgb 9.4 on admission and remained stable this admission. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - outpatient dialysis ___ schedule - 100mg Torsemide on NON-dialysis days - Titrate duloxetine to off --> discharged on 20mg qD. Plan discontinue on ___ as contraindicated in HD - Patient refused ___ services - discharged on home oxygen
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath. We feel that this was due to significant volume overload (too much fluid) in your body. We attempted to give you medications to make you pee, however your kidney did not respond well to the diuresis. Therefore, you were started on dialysis. You also had a chest tube placed for a significant amount of fluid around your lung. Your symptoms improved significantly with the fluid removal. You will need to continue dialysis after discharge. You will be set up with a dialysis schedule for ___ and ___. You should also continue to take your diuretic (torsemide 100mg) on days when you do not have dialysis. You were also seen by the physical therapists who recommended discharge to a rehab for improvement in your strength however you decided against medical advice to go home. Furthermore, you had a persistent oxygen requirement. We were interested in monitoring you further this hospitalization, however you requested to leave against our advice so you were sent home on oxygen.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ h/o CHF, DM, CAD, CKD p/w SOB x 2 days and confusion. . # Acute on chronic systolic heart failure: Two weeks prior to admission, the patient noticed some increased shortness of breath. He complained of nasal congestion at that time, but denied other cold symptoms such as cough, fever, malaise. On the day of admission, per the ED report, his family noticed some confusion. He was placed on oxygen and both his shortness of breath and confusion improved when on 3L NC. He was transported to the ___ ED, where he was tachypnic to 28 breaths per minute while satting 97% on 2L NC. He was given 20mg IV lasix with 300 UOP in response. Chest x-ray was consistent with increased pulmonary edema, likely due to heart failure. He was then transferred to the floor for further management. Given his age and presentation, he was started on a ___ which returned negative x 3. The second set of cardiac markers did show a slight increase in troponin-T which may have been due to systolic strain. On hospital days 1 and 2, he was maintained on 2L NC and given 40 mg IV lasix daily for diuresis with roughly 500-1000 cc of urine output per dose of lasix. On hospital day 3, he was transitioned to 60 mg PO lasix, taken off oxygen, and underwent an echocardiogram to assess his systolic function. Echocardiogram showed no change from prior echocardiogram with an ejection fraction of 45%. His breathing improved considerably throughout the hospital course. On discharge, he was satting 97% on room air and was discharged on 60 mg PO lasix daily. . # Altered mental status: On the day of admission, the patient's family had reported some confusion at his assisted living facility that improved with supplemental oxygen. On admission to the floor, he was alert and oriented to person, place, and time while on 2L NC. He was maintained on supplemental oxygen on hospital days 1 and 2. On hospital day 3, given his improved respiratory status, supplemental oxygen was discontinued and he was found to be alert and oriented to person, place, and time while on room air. On discharge, he denied confusion or loss of consciousness. . # Normocytic anemia: On admission, the patient's hematocrit was 26.6, which was down from his baseline of 30's. He was asymptomatic throughout his hospital course. Stool guaiac in the ED was negative. CBC revealed an MCV of 89, indicating a normocytic anemia. Iron levels were drawn and returned normal. His hematocrit was followed daily and reached a nadir of 25.2 on hospital day 3, but rose to 26.1 on the day of discharge. His normocytic anemia was likely due to fluid overload from his heart failure. . # Chronic renal failure: On admission, the patient's creatinine was elevated at 2.5. On closer review of his hospital records, this was found to be close to his baseline. Throughout the hospital course, his creatinine remained stable between 2.5 and 2.6 and he had good urine output. On discharge, his creatinine was 2.5. . # Diabetes mellitus: Stable. He was continued on glipizide and covered with a regular insulin sliding scale.
summary: You have been admitted for shortness of breath and confusion. Workup for infection has been negative. You have been afebrile with a normal WBC throughout your hospital course. Chest x-ray showed some pulmonary edema. Echocardiogram showed no changes since your last echocardiogram in ___. You were treated with IV lasix 40 mg daily over four days with good urine response. We believe your shortness of breath was due to an exacerbation of your heart failure. You were also admitted for confusion. Your confusion resolved at your assisted living facility with oxygen. You have not had confusion during your hospital stay. We believe your confusion is most likely related to your heart failure. We have increased your dosage of lasix to 60 mg a day by mouth. We are also looking into getting oxygen at home, but this may take several days to determine whether you meet criteria for home oxygen. Please keep taking all other medications at their home doses.

Echocardiogram showed no change from prior echocardiogram with an ejection fraction of 45%. His breathing improved considerably throughout the hospital course. On discharge, he was satting 97% on room air and was discharged on 60 mg PO lasix daily. . # Altered mental status: On the day of admission, the patient's family had reported some confusion at his assisted living facility that improved with supplemental oxygen. On admission to the floor, he was alert and oriented to person, place, and time while on 2L NC. He was maintained on supplemental oxygen on hospital days 1 and 2. On hospital day 3, given his improved respiratory status, supplemental oxygen was discontinued and he was found to be alert and oriented to person, place, and time while on room air. On discharge, he denied confusion or loss of consciousness. . # Normocytic anemia: On admission, the patient's hematocrit was 26.6, which was down from his baseline of 30's. He was asymptomatic throughout his hospital course. Stool guaiac in the ED was negative. CBC revealed an MCV of 89, indicating a normocytic anemia. Iron levels were drawn and returned normal. His hematocrit was followed daily and reached a nadir of 25.2 on hospital day 3, but rose to 26.1 on the day of discharge. His normocytic anemia was likely due to fluid overload from his heart failure. . # Chronic renal failure: On admission, the patient's creatinine was elevated at 2.5. On closer review of his hospital records, this was found to be close to his baseline. Throughout the hospital course, his creatinine remained stable between 2.5 and 2.6 and he had good urine output. On discharge, his creatinine was 2.5. . # Diabetes mellitus: Stable. He was continued on glipizide and covered with a regular insulin sliding scale.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Active Issues: # Acute on chronic heart failure, right sided : She presented significantly volume overloaded with leg edema and weight gain. Attempts at outpatient diuresis had been limited by her kidney function. She was treated with a furosemide drip primarily at a rate of 10 cc/hr with significant improvement in weight and exam. Diuresis was discontinue due to rising BUN and significant improvement on exam. She was transitioned to oral torsemide and was stable on this regimen for several days while hospitalized. Additional intravenous furosemide was given in the context of hypoxia discussed below. She was diuresed for a total weight loss of 14 kg with a discharge weight of approximately 80 kg. # Pulmonary hypertension : Her ECHO on admission again showed severe pulmonary hypertension. She does have a home requirement for oxygen. In consultation with her outpatient pulmonologist, she was trialed on sildenafil, she did tolerate 10 mg three times a day of this medication. However, on the day she was dose increased to 20 mg, she developed hypoxia consistent with pulmonary edema, a known potential side effect of this medication. Her sildenafil was discontinued and she was treated with intravenous furosemide. She was stable on her home oxygen requirements. # Myeloproliferative disease : She was initially continued on her home dose of twice a day ruxolitinib, followed by a dose reduction to 5 mg daily at the request of her oncologist. She was persistently anemic despite transfusions of a total of 4 units. Her oncologist felt that this could represent increased splenic sequestration and an overall worsening of her myeloproliferative disease. A spleen ultrasound was performed to help plan for this procedure. Chronic Issues: # Chronic kidney disease - Her kidney disease has been a limiting factor to diuresis. Her creatinine remained relatively stable at 2.0-2.3, but her BUN did rise from the ___ to the 100s. Transition Issues: # Splenic radiation - Her outpatient oncologist will be speaking with her colleagues about potential for spleen radiation as a palliative treatment for anemia. # Goals of care - Her oncologist, pulmonologist, and cardiologist met with the patient and expressed concerns about her overall progress and limited future options. At this time, Ms. ___ was not interested in pursuing hospice care or changing her goals of care.
summary: We treated this with a furosemide (lasix) drip and ___ had significant weight loss with this medication. We are discharging ___ on torsemide twice a day. We also did a trial of the medication sildenafil for your pulmonary hypertension in consultation with your pulmonologist Dr. ___. This was unfortunately complicated by worsening of your edema and this medication was discontinued. ___ had anemia as well as low platelets during this hospitalization. This is likely caused by your MDS. ___ received 4 units of red blood cells during this hospitalization, but did not improve your counts significantly. We did reduce the dose of your Jakafi. Dr. ___ is discussing with her colleagues the possibility of setting ___ up for splenic radiation. We did a spleen ultrasound to help facilitated this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with the past medical history of chronic systolic (EF 20%) and diastolic heart failure, atrial fibrillation on Coumadin (INR 3.7 on arrival), coronary artery disease, prior CVA with residual R sided deficits, CKD ___, T2DM, rheumatoid arthritis, referred from her nursing facility for evaluation of acute on chronic anemia in absence of overt hemorrhage. She is s/p recent ___ admission ___ for hematochezia/blood loss anemia without source identified despite EGD/colonoscopy. The etiology of her anemia is likely multifactorial including chronic anemia related to chronic kidney disease and likely chronic inflammation in setting of RA. Low suspicion for acute, active bleeding given neg guaiac, stable H/H, no evidence of hematuria or bruising or any evidence of HD instability. ACUTE/ACTIVE PROBLEMS: # Acute on chronic anemia # Chronic anemia related to CKD and likely chronic inflammation - Serial Hgb stable - Required 1 unit PRBC for Hgb<7 - Stool guaiac neg - Coumadin held during admission for elevated INR - Advised to f/u w/ GI as an outpatient, number provided on d/c # Atrial fibrillation with history of prior CVA, on Coumadin # ___ CAD # Chronic systolic/diastolic heart failure, mild volume overload without respiratory or hemodynamic compromise # HTN # CKD stage ___, approximately at recent baseline - INR 2.9 on discharge day. Recommend holding Coumadin on ___ and checking INR on ___. Would adjust Coumadin pending INR draw on ___. Consider decreasing dose given supratherapeutic INR on admission - Hold ASA, advised pt to discuss with PCP prior starting - Continue home torsemide 20 mg daily - Metoprolol XL, hydralazine per outpatient dose - Atorvastatin # Rheumatoid arthritis # Inflamed R thumb IP joint - Continue prednisone, hydroxychloroquine, methotrexate per outpatient dosing - Advised to f/u w/ outpatient rheumatologist, hand surgeon # T2DM - Resume linagliptin on discharge - Insulin SS # Hypothyroidism (s/p thyroidectomy) - Cont levothyroxine 175 mcg daily Greater than 30 minutes spent on discharge planning and coordination
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for acute on chronic anemia. You were given 1 unit of red blood cells. After the red blood cell administration, your hemoglobin was above 7. Your stool was checked multiple times for evidence of bleeding however it was negative. Serial hemoglobin monitoring showed that you had stable hemoglobin suggesting that you did not have any evidence of acute bleeding. You had no other signs of acute blood loss such as bruising, history of trauma, blood in the stool, dark stool or blood in the urine. You were found to be borderline iron deficient but otherwise did not show any evidence of nutritional deficiencies including B12 or folate deficiency. You are otherwise afebrile and hemodynamically stable. Given this findings and stable hemoglobin on repeat laboratory testing, you are being discharged with the plan to ___ with PCP and gastroenterology. Of note, you also complained of right thumb pain during her hospitalization.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #Chest Pain: In setting of risk factors for CAD and positive enzymes, it was thought her pain may be due to myocardial ischemia. Her pain was resolved with SL nitroglycerin. She was continued on aspirin, labetolol, and atorvastatin. Her ekg was difficult to assess for ischemia given her LBBB, and so she was sent for a pharmacologic nuclear stress. This study was indeterminate because of soft tissue attenuation, and she did not develop anginal symptoms during the study. She also had an echo which showed depressed global Lv systolic function, with an LVEF of 35%. Her old echo ___ years ago showed a LVEF of >50%. this new echo finding was thought to be be possibly due to ischemia. Cardiology was consulted, and after speaking with her primary cardiologist Dr. ___ was determined that the most likely cause of her global systolic dysfunction was uncontrolled hypertension. She will be scheduled to undergo a stress test as an outpatient, and to follow up with Dr. ___. She was also discharged on labetolol instead of metoprolol for her hypertension. #: dyspnea: Her shortness of breath was thought secondary to an acute systolic heart failure exacerbation. CXR showed vascular engorgement, and her BNP was elevated. She was hypertensive on presentation, and it was thought that uncontrolled hypertension may have caused her CHF exacerbation. The patient was diuresed with lasix and spironolactone, and her blood pressure was controlled with her outpatient regimen of metoprolol, clonidine, amlodipine, and Imdur. . #. paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: The patient was supposed to be taking coumadin 5mg daily for her atrial fibrillatioon, however her INR on presentation was 1.1. She was restarted on her coumadin 5mg daily. She was instructed to have her INR after discharge. . # acute renal failure: Patient presented with increased creatinine, thought secondary prerenal etiology from heart failure. Her spironolactone, lasix, and valsartan were initially held, then restarted when the creatinine returned to baseline. . # DM2: lantus was continued. . # HTN: the patient was continued on clonidine, metoprolol, amlodipine, imdur, valsartan. She was discharged home on labetolol instead of metoprolol by recommendation of cardiology. . # OSA: CPAP at night per home regimen.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and chest pain. Your symptoms were thought secondary to a CHF exacerbation from uncontrolled hypertension. Our evaluation revealed that your heart was not pumping as effectively as it was ___ years ago. You have been scheduled for an outpatient stress test. Please call ___ to schedule your Exercise Stress Test. You are to have this test done prior to your next visit with Dr. ___. You have been started on a new medication for treatment of your blood pressure and heart failure. Please take Labetalol 200mg three times per day. You have also been given a prescription for labwork. You are to obtain an INR (coumadin) level on ___. You are to have these results faxed to your primary care doctor, ___ ___ at ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with multiple medical problems including Afib, HTN, diastolic HF, CAD s/p stent placement of LAD, and chronic renal insufficiency who presents with worsening fatigue and found to be anemic, course compicated by acute diastolic heart failure. . #Anemia: The patient's hematocrit on admission was 19.4. Her iron studies showed Fe of 12, ferritin of 11, and a TIBC of 481, consistent with an iron deficiency anemia. She was guaiac negative and denied bloody or black stools in the past. Of note, the patient was started on coumadin ___ year ago. This was likely from an slow occult bleed. Her coumadin and aspirin were held initially and she was transfused slowly with two units of RBCs with 20 mg IV lasix after each unit. Her hematocrit rose and remained stable over the next two days at about ___. Her coumadin was restarted before discharge, and Aspirin was discontinued. The patient was started on a PPI. #Chest pain and dyspnea: In the setting of receiving the two units of RBCs, the patient experienced chest pain and dyspnea with upsloping 1mm ST depressions, likely due to strain from hypertension, tachycardia and volume overload leading to CHF exacerbation and pulmonary edema. She was diuresed with IV lasix and her chest pain and hypertension improved. Troponin levels rose from <0.01 to 0.03 and then returned down to 0.02. The ST depressions resolved by the day of discharge. She was given one dose of aspirin, but was not discharged on it in setting of possible occult GI bleed. #Hypertension, uncontrolled: Her hypertension was well controlled previously, but became elevated to 190s systolic in setting of blood transfusiions, likely from volume overload. She was given IV lasix, morphine, and hydralazine, and her blood pressure improved. She continued to be diuresed with IV lasix over her ensuing hospital course. She responded well with good urine output, resulting in stable BP control. #Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: In setting of blood transfusions, the patient had a CHF exacerbation, likely from volume overload, leading to pulmonary edema. This was despite IV lasix after each unit. She continued to be diuresed with IV lasix after her transfusion, to which she responded well with increased urine output and improved pulmonary edema. A chest xray obtained on the day after her transfusion showed no evidence pulmonary edema. She was euvolemic on discharge. #H/o atrial fibrillation: The patient remained in sinus rhythm throughout her hospital course. She was continued on her amiodarone, but her coumadin was held on admission in setting of possible occult GI bleed. It was restarted at her home dosing schedule before discharge. An INR obtained the morning of discharge was 2.7. Coumadin will be re-evaluate as an outpatient. #CAD: The patient has a history of CAD s/p LAD stent in the past. Her aspirin was held on admission in setting of possible occult GI bleed. The patient then had chest pain in the setting of blood transfusion with 1mm ST segment depressions and slight bump in troponins (<0.01->0.02->0.03->0.02). She was diuresed, and her chest pain and ST segment depressions resolved. She was given asa x 1 on the day after transfusions, but was not discharged on asa, which will be reevaluated as an outpatient. the patient has PCP ___ scheduled for 4 days after discharge. Written by ___, ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for low blood count (anemia). You were given two units of red blood cells to improve your anemia. We did not find a source of your loss of blood, but believe you may be loosing small amounts of blood through your gastrointestinal tract that over time have added up to making you anemic. In the course of receiving the two units of blood, you developed some chest pain because your heart was having difficulty with the increased fluid that came along with the blood cells. We gave you diuretics to remove the excess fluid from your body, and you started to feel better.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with CHF (EF 20%), CAD s/p MI, p/w hypotension, malaise, and dry cough, treated for CAP with 10 day course of levofloxacin. Shock of Unknown Etiology - Patient presented to the ED for 1 week of malaise & non-productive cough which is worse at night. In the ED, patient became hypotensive with systolic BP in the ___ that was refractory to 2L fluids, so patient was transferred to the MICU. Put on levophed drip in ED, but did not require the drip in the MICU. BP on arrival to MICU was 102/48 and his MAP has ranged between 50 and 65. In searching for the cause of his hypotension, the patient has significant systolic heart failure confirmed by TTE revealing an ejection fraction of 20%. AMI unlikely due to no changes in EKG from baseline (Sinus rhythm; q-wave in III, V2, V3, ST ele in Vi, V2, V3, flattening of T waves). Patient was on multiple home anti-hypertensives which were held in the MICU (metoprolol 25 QD, amiodarone 200mg QD and furosemide 20mg QD). Food/liquid intake has been appropriate in the weeks leading up to admission as per patient report. Patient had an initial WBC of 12.7 (trended down over admission), unimpressive U/A, nonproductive cough x 1 week in the setting of generalized fatigue/weakness. A rise in creatinine to 2.2 (baseline 1.7) which resolved after 2 liters of fluid pointed toward a prerenal/dehydration status. O2 sat 97% 2L NC, desaturing to 91% without O2, upon admission and was O2 sat 94% 4L NC on transfer from the MICU. Urine output remained sufficient at about 40-60 cc/hr, but was given small dose of 10mg furosemide with goal of improving congestion and O2 sats, with little effect. Patient was stable for transfer to the medicine ward on ___. Patient continued to have a dry cough during admission. CXRs showed multiple patchy infiltrates and patient was treated for CAP with 10 days of levofloxacin. It was thought that pulmonary process could be a result of amiodarone toxicity. CT scan was gotten and confirmed isolated patchy infiltrates and ground glass opacity surrounding affected areas. Radiology felt strongly that this was not amio toxicity, which is clinically correlated as patient would not have recovered so quickly if it was due to amio-related fibrotic changes. He was stable on the floor with SBPs in the 100-120s. His home metoprolol was restarted, which he tolerated well. Atrius cardiology recommended holding his amio and lasix. WBC was WNL and O2 sats between 94-96% on RA, even when working with ___, Patient felt well. Upon discharge, hypotension was of unknown etiology but thought to be due to sepsis ___ PNA. . # hand numbness- The patient continued to complain of right arm tingling and numbness throughout admission. NCHCT was negative. No other neurological deficits were detected and we monitored this condition closely throughout admission. Symptoms should be followed-up as an outpatient if they do not further resolve. . #. systolic CHF (EF 20%)- BPs meds were held during hypotension. Metoprolol was restarted on the floor and was tolerated by patient well. Continued to hold lasix and amiodarone, per atrius cards recs. Cards also recommended started low dose losartan as an outpatient (do not recommend ACE-I as patient has dry cough). . # CAD - ECG was stable compared to previous ECGs. ASA 81mg was continued. . # Hyperlipidemia - continued home simvastatin. . # Hypothyroidism - not active, home levothyroxine continued. . # Dispo: home with cane # Code Status: Full Code
summary: You came into the hospital because you were not feeling well and your blood pressure was very low. You went to the intensive care unit because you were not doing well. We got a chest xray and a CT scan of your chest that showed that you had a pneumonia. We were concerned that the amiodarone that you are for your heart was affecting your lungs and causing these problems so we asked the cardiology team to see you and they felt that the problems with your lungs were due to infection, and not due to this medication. You are being treated for your pneumonia with an antibiotic (Levofloxacin) which you will need to take for another 5 days. You are taking it every other day and your next dose should be on ___. We made changes to your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M w/HTN, HLD, OSA, T2DM, Addison's disease and recent admission for ___ edema, DOE managed for cardiogenic shock and CHF, currently being worked up for cardiac transplant, admitted for ___ activation and fatigue. #Life Vest Activation: Alarm was discussed with ___ vendor, and no dysrhythmia was found. #sCHF: The ___ recent EF is (___) on ___. The ___ was complaining of dizziness and found to have orthostatic hypotension. Given the extent of his HF, he is being worked up for a cardiac transplant. During this admission he had a cardiac MRI and liver biopsy for question of cirrhosis (biopsy did not show cirrhosis, only nonspecific inflammation; results of the MRI are reported separately in OMR; MRCP was recommended in ___ year for small pancreatic cysts, and ultrasound for R kidney upper pole focus thought simple cyst). Pt was found to have evidence of CAD in Cx and will undergo high risk PCI in the future to revascularize. summary: You were brought to ___ because you had an alarm activation of your life vest which was found to not be concerning. While in the hospital we began some of the work up for your evaluation for heart transplant. You underwent a liver biopsy and some imaging of your abdomen and chest. We also decreased the dossages of some of your heart medications and you got better.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and Plan: This is a ___ year-old ___ speaking woman with obesity, DM2, CKD, dCHF, OSA on CPAP, and PAF with recent discharge for management of acute on chronic lower back pain, c/b hypercapnic respiratory failure, CHF exacerbation with intermittent afib with RVR, all markedly improved, p/w episode of Chest pain not found to be ACS, but with acute on chronic dCHF exacerbation. By Problem: # Chest Pain: Resolved on own during admission. This was not felt to be cardiac as enzymes were flat, ekg had no concerning changes, and patient had a normal p-MIBI ___. Based on last admission, it appears her chest pain was a combination of volume overload, afib with RVR, coughing from PNA, and back pain. Resolution of these problems (with diuresis, better rate control, levoflox tx, and ultram), allowed her to be CP free. Her left heart cath did not show any significant CAD. # Acute on Chronic Diastolic Congestive Heart Failure: Initially noted to have improved pulmonary edema on chest x-ray in ED. Initially diuresed with PO lasix 80 mg BID and extra doses of 80 mg IV lasix to a goal of negative one to one and a half liters per day. Ambulatory sats during diuresis showed a worsening picture as she would range 80-85% O2 on room air during ambulation. At home, she required no oxygen during the day, so this was a markedly worsening picture. A repeat chest x-ray showed worsening pulmonary edema despite diuresis. She was placed on a lasix drip to further increase her diuresis. Given the continued concern of the specific etiology behind this exacerbation and the most appropriate management, Cardiology was consulted. It was felt that her diuretics should be changed to torsemide 40mg BID and that aldactone 25mg daily should also be added. Additionally, she was sent for left and right heart caths for better delineation of her coronary disease, and heart pressures. Her cath showed clean coronary arteries, with elevated filling pressures. # Atrial fibrillation: Rate controlled on diltiazem and metoprolol succinate. Continued to have adequate rates throughout hospitalization. Of note, also has had intermittent asymptomatic bradycardia to the ___. Continued on home dosage of warfarin with INR in therapeutic range of ___ throughout admission, until the time it was held in preparation for cath. She was bridged prior to cath with a heparin gtt and post cath sent home on lovenox as a bridge to coumadin. Her metoprolol was increased to 150mg daily for better rate control and she has close follow up with her cardiologist, Dr. ___, ___ discharge. # Acute on chronic kidney injury: baseline creatinine appears to be at ~1.4. Cr peaked to 1.5, remained stable with diuresis, and was back to her baseline of 1.4 at the time of discharge. # Lower Back Pain: Continued but tolerable. Likely related to compression fractures. Pain controlled with lidocaine patch, ___ gay and ultram prn. Did not give anything stronger given her severe drug allergy to fentanly causing cardiac arrest. # HLD: continued lipitor and fenofibrate # HTN: Stable. Continued metoprolol and diltiazem. # Hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine. # DM: Stable. Continued Humulog sliding scale and transitioned back to glipizide upon discarge.
summary: You were admitted because you had chest pain. You did not have a heart attack. During this admission, your oxygen levels became very low when you walked. You were found to have too much fluid in your lungs. This is because your heart was not pumping well and caused back flow of fluid into your lungs. Cardiology was consulted and they felt that you should be changed to a new medication called torsemide instead of lasix and added another medication called spironolactone. They also suggested a cardiac catheterization to look at the arteries around your heart and the pressures inside your heart. The catheterization showed that the pressures were elevated in your heart, which is likely due to your sleep apnea and heart failure.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with past medical history of left ventricular outflow tract obstruction, diastolic heart failure and HTN who presents with syncope, likely non-cardiac and in setting of orthostasis. . # Syncope: occurred in context of raking leaves and poor intake all day, had preceding R side neck pain and blurry vision. Cardiac etiology was considered given history of LV outflow obstruction, ECG was unchanged and cardiac enzymes not suggestive of MI. CXR did not show PNA or CHF. Pt was monitored on tele without significant events. She had positive orthostatics on admission and given IVF. Lisinopril was continued to be held (stopped by PCP 1 week prior to admission due to positive orthostatics at outpatient visit). TTE was done which did not show significant change from previous study, stress test did not show any perfusion abnormalities. Pt continued to have positive orthostatics by BP drop, though not symptomatic. She was given compression stockings and PO intake encouraged. She was advised to have tilt table testing as outpatient, will follow up with her PCP and cardiologist. ___ did not recommend services because pt was ambulating independently. Pt was counseled on lifestyle modifications (adequate hydration and PO intake, time between changing positions, etc) to minimize symptomatic orthostasis. Her atenolol was continued on discharge given SBP up to 160 prior to discharge, she should consider restarted low dose lisinopril at next PCP ___. . # ___: slightly elevated above baseline on admission, improved to normal level with fluids. . # Anemia: Lower than baseline though pt reports blood in stool recently, ASA was stopped and she had EGD done 1 week ago. HCT was stable throughout hospital stay, she will follow up with PCP about results of her endoscopy and when she can restart ASA. No active bleeding during admission. . # Diastolic CHF: As above, TTE showed 75-80% EF with hyperdynamic circulation, decreased LV size and symmetric LVH, mild LV outflow obstruction. No valvular abnormalities to account for syncope. LV outflow obstruction in setting of orthostasis and possible dehydration may have contributed to syncope. Pt will f/u with cardiologist as outpt for management of CHF, discharged on home atenolol, lisinopril continued to be held as above.
summary: You were admitted to ___ after losing consciousness. We were concerned that this may have been due to a problem with your heart. It is also possible that this was due to orthostasis, a drop in your blood pressure when you change positions, especially since you have had recent changes in your medications and you did not eat that day. We measured your orthostatics in the hospital and they showed that your blood pressure drops when you change positions quickly. We did an echo of your heart, which again showed mild ventricular outflow obstruction and decreased filling of one of the chambers of your heart. You had a stress test, which showed normal blood flow to your heart. Your syncope was most likely due to orthostasis. We spoke to you about the importance of having regular fluid and food intake and changing positions slowly. You should also wear compression stockings to help mobilize blood flow to your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is a ___ with a PMHx of HFpEF, old LBBB, HTN, carotid artery disease, DM, OSA on CPAP, HLD who presented with dyspnea and orthopnea, found to have newly depressed EF (34%) and wall motion abnormalities (severe hypokinesis of the anterior septum and anterior walls and apex). Akinesis on TTE was concerning for missed MI vs stress-induced CM. She was without CP, with neg trop at OSH and in ___ ED and EKG did not meet sgarbossa criteria. On admission she appeared volume overloaded with elevated JVP and dyspnea with movement. She was diuresed to euvolemia and was transitioned to oral diuretics. She was started on a isosorbide dinitrate, valsartan, spironolactone and carvedilol as she had poorly controlled hypertension (SBPs 180s-200s). Coronary angiography did not demonstrate any culprit flow-limiting coronary lesions, though the patient does have CAD (Intermediate LM and RCA disease). She was stabilized on a maintenance diuretic dose of torsemide 10mg qday. Takotsubo's cardiomyopathy is a possibility and the patient should have a repeat TTE performed within about 3 months to look for recovery of LVEF. Right heart catheterization notable for mild pulmonary hypertension with transpulmonary gradient of 14 (PCWP mean 13, mean PA 27, RA mean 5), for which she will see pulmonologist as an outpatient. She will continue using her home CPAP for her OSA. While attempting to tightly control her blood pressure in the ___ SBP for her new systolic heart failure the patient developed dizziness with standing. Carotid ultrasound revealed retrograde flow in the right vertebral artery c/w subclavian steal syndrome, and R subclavian duplex study was consistent with this. Indeed, she was found to have asymmetric blood pressure readings ___ higher in the left arm). Vascular surgery was consulted and a CTA was performed of the Circle of ___, carotids, and subclavian arteries. Vascular surgery could not identify a culprit stenosis that would be amenable to surgical correction and therefore recommended tolerating a higher blood pressure target for her despite her systolic heart dysfunction. A goal of SBP 120-140 was decided on since she becomes symptomatic at ~110 SBP. === TRANSITIONAL ISSUES === - New systolic CHF: Needs repeat TTE in 3 months to look for change/recovery in LVEF. Started on carvedilol, spironolactone, valsartan, isosorbide and torsemide. - Needs Chem-10 checked on ___. Results will be faxed to Dr. ___ and Dr. ___ office). - Right subclavian steal syndrome: not amenable to surgical correction. Will tolerate higher SBP goal of 120s-140s to avoid dizziness / cerebral malperfusion. She is at slightly higher risk of falling given intermittent mild dizziness even on this BP regimen, however patient and medical team both agreed that this risk could be appropriately managed by always using walker for stabilization and rising slowly from seated position. - Anemia: Hgb in 7's-8's. Stable during admission.Normal iron studies, B12, folate. Reportedly normal colonoscopy within last ___ years. Please continue workup as outpatient. - Dry weight on discharge: 72.8 kg. - Diuretic regimen on discharge: torsemide 10mg PO qday - Pulmonary follow up for OSA (on CPAP already) in setting of newly depressed EF. - ___ services for heart failure monitoring, vitals, medication teaching. Should also be set up with a LifeLine bracelet or similar way for patient to alert EMS if she falls. - Please consider referral to ___ Pain ___ in attempt to better manage her chronic back and leg pain. She is dramatically affected by this. - MRI (___) showed multiseptated lobular cystic mass without enhancing nodules in the head of the pancreas and uncinate process. Differential includes cystadenoma or cystadenocarcinoma. Due for 6 month f/u (OVERDUE FOR F/U IMAGING). This should be arranged for by her PCP.
summary: You were hospitalized at ___ after noticing some difficulty breathing and having decreased heart function, or "heart failure". You were given medications to help remove the excess fluid, called diuretics, during your stay. You had a cardiac catheterization which showed no evidence of blockages in the arteries of your heart that would cause the decreased heart function. You will need to continue the medications below, and will have a repeat echocardiogram in 3 months to evaluate your heart function. We incidentally found that you had an abnormality of one of your blood vessels. We believe this is causing the dizziness you experience intermittently when your blood pressure is even at appropriate levels. You were seen by vascular surgery for consideration of whether a surgery was possible to correct this, but no such procedure was recommended or deemed possible. Instead, we have recommended a more liberal (higher) blood pressure goal for you to help minimize your dizziness.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is an ___ year old man with history of moderate-to-severe aortic stenosis, persistent atrial fibrillation, hypertension, systolic and diastolic congestive heart failure (LVEF >55%), hyperlipidemia, and coronary artery disease who presents with lower extremity edema, chest tightness and dyspnea on exertion X2 days, likely CHF exacerbation. . #. Pump: Patient's LVEF was preserved on last ECHO in ___ and during this admission. Thus, patient likely has chronic diastolic heart failure. ___ have some component of systolic heart failure as well. Is followed in Heart Failure Clinic. Given acute on chronic renal failure last admission, patient has been drinking extra amounts of fluid ___ water bottles more than baseline) and not restricting his sodium intake. These are likely triggers for current CHF exacerbation. Patient has mild lower extremity edema (non-pitting), shortness of breath. No JVD and elevated BNP currently. Patient does have severe aortic stenosis likely contributing to his current symptoms. The patient was actively diuresed with Lasix 80mg IV up to twice daily and the patient diuresed 4.5 pounds. He was continued on metoprolol and lisinopril. The patient was not restarted on Spironolactone given history of potassium issues with it. The patient is to start telemonitoring from home with the Heart Failure Clinic and will follow-up with Dr. ___ regarding ___ heart failure management and starting discussions of aortic stenosis management options (medical vs. valvuloplasty vs. aortic valve replacement - percutaneous or open heart). . #. CAD: Patient endorsed some chest tightness/heaviness, nonradiating and not associated with other symptoms (nausea, vomiting, syncope, numbness/tingling). First troponin 0.03 but in setting of Creatinine 2.4. Second troponin also stable. Given elevated BNP and no EKG changes, coronary etiology for current symptoms low on the differential. The patient was continued on home aspirin, statin, metoprolol and lisinopril. Of note, his Toprol XL was switched to short acting metoprolol tartrate while in-house. This was switched back to metoprolol succinate upon d/c. . #. Rhythm: Sinus rhythm with episodes of atrial fibrillation. Patient is not on coumadin given history of GI bleed. He was monitored on telemetry without events. . #. Hyponatremia: Most likely in setting of hypervolemia with heart failure. He was fluid restricted to 1.5 liters daily, his serum sodium trended daily with ultimate resolution of hyponatremia. His mental status remained stable throughout. . # CKD: Creatinine has been gradually increasing over the course of the last ___ years (1.5-1.7). It bumped acutely in ___, during which the patient has remained asymptomatic. Patient's most recent admission was for acute on chronic renal disease and felt that the patient most likely has cardiorenal syndrome, poor forward flow. His creatinine function has also continued to be exacerbated by lasix and lisinopril. Renal ultrasound performed last admission revealed cortical atrophy of the right kidney but no obstruction. Patients renal function was stable with creatinine of 2.3-2.5. Has renal f/uw ith Dr. ___ ___ later this month who is aware of admission. . # Anemia: Has history of lower GI bleed in ___ secondary to peptic ulcer disease (EHD visualized ulcer), exacerbated by coumadin. Also likely anemia of chronic disease (chronic renal failure). Current hematocrit ___ points lower than baseline of ___, possibly due to volume overload vs. repeat GI bleed. Patient was guaiac negative in the ED. Active type and screen was maintained in house. Because of Hgb of 7.9 and Hct of 24.3, patient was transfused one unit packed red blood cells before discharge. Likely will start epo as out pt by renal. . # Foot pain: Has some mild pain and tenderness of left foot around the ___ tarsal-metatarsal joint. Able to ambulate without increased difficulty and pain responds to tylenol.
summary: You were admitted with shortness of breath and increased leg swelling. You were found to have an exacerbation of your congestive heart failure, likely because you were drinking more fluids and eating saltier foods than normal. You received intravenous water pills with good effect, and lost 4.5 pounds in fluid. You also have aortic stenosis, a tightening of one of your heart valves, which can cause shortness of breath. You should discuss ___ management of this tight valve with your primary cardiologist, Dr. ___. Your blood count was low while you were here, likely from your chronic kidney disease. You wre given a blood transfusion to increase your blood count as it may be contributing to your shortness of breath.

These are likely triggers for current CHF exacerbation. Patient has mild lower extremity edema (non-pitting), shortness of breath. No JVD and elevated BNP currently. Patient does have severe aortic stenosis likely contributing to his current symptoms. The patient was actively diuresed with Lasix 80mg IV up to twice daily and the patient diuresed 4.5 pounds. He was continued on metoprolol and lisinopril. The patient was not restarted on Spironolactone given history of potassium issues with it. The patient is to start telemonitoring from home with the Heart Failure Clinic and will follow-up with Dr. ___ regarding ___ heart failure management and starting discussions of aortic stenosis management options (medical vs. valvuloplasty vs. aortic valve replacement - percutaneous or open heart). . #. CAD: Patient endorsed some chest tightness/heaviness, nonradiating and not associated with other symptoms (nausea, vomiting, syncope, numbness/tingling). First troponin 0.03 but in setting of Creatinine 2.4. Second troponin also stable. Given elevated BNP and no EKG changes, coronary etiology for current symptoms low on the differential. The patient was continued on home aspirin, statin, metoprolol and lisinopril. Of note, his Toprol XL was switched to short acting metoprolol tartrate while in-house. This was switched back to metoprolol succinate upon d/c. . #. Rhythm: Sinus rhythm with episodes of atrial fibrillation. Patient is not on coumadin given history of GI bleed. He was monitored on telemetry without events. . #. Hyponatremia: Most likely in setting of hypervolemia with heart failure. He was fluid restricted to 1.5 liters daily, his serum sodium trended daily with ultimate resolution of hyponatremia. His mental status remained stable throughout. . # CKD: Creatinine has been gradually increasing over the course of the last ___ years (1.5-1.7). It bumped acutely in ___, during which the patient has remained asymptomatic. Patient's most recent admission was for acute on chronic renal disease and felt that the patient most likely has cardiorenal syndrome, poor forward flow. His creatinine function has also continued to be exacerbated by lasix and lisinopril. Renal ultrasound performed last admission revealed cortical atrophy of the right kidney but no obstruction. Patients renal function was stable with creatinine of 2.3-2.5. Has renal f/uw ith Dr. ___ ___ later this month who is aware of admission. . # Anemia: Has history of lower GI bleed in ___ secondary to peptic ulcer disease (EHD visualized ulcer), exacerbated by coumadin. Also likely anemia of chronic disease (chronic renal failure). Current hematocrit ___ points lower than baseline of ___, possibly due to volume overload vs. repeat GI bleed. Patient was guaiac negative in the ED. Active type and screen was maintained in house. Because of Hgb of 7.9 and Hct of 24.3, patient was transfused one unit packed red blood cells before discharge. Likely will start epo as out pt by renal. . # Foot pain: Has some mild pain and tenderness of left foot around the ___ tarsal-metatarsal joint. Able to ambulate without increased difficulty and pain responds to tylenol.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is a ___ with history of CAD s/p CABG in ___ who presented with nausea and coffee-ground emesis who presented with troponin elevation to a peak of 5, and was subsequently found to have an inferior STEMI. An echocardiogram on admission demonstrated a newly depressed ejection fraction to 35% with inferior wall hypokinesis, consistent with PDA distribution of hypoperfusion. She went for stent placement on ___ with successful revascularization with 3x DES to a totally-occluded proximal LCx and LMCA. This procedure was c/b hypotension and unresponsiveness, managed with placement of an IABP and levophed. Pt initially admitted to the CCU for post-procedural monitoring. She was found to have a mild acute anemia, for which she received a total of 2uPRBC. She had a mild ___ to a peak Cr of 1.7, thought to be volume depletion (I/s/o nausea and vomiting) vs. cardiorenal; this improved with treatment of her STEMI and fluid administration on admission of 3L. # Inferior STEMI: With troponins to a peak of 5. Received ASA 325, Plavix 600mg load, and heparin bolus in cath lab. S/p multiple DES to totally occluded proximal LCx and LMCA. LIMA-LAD patent. Inferior wall hypokinesis, EF~35%, and mild-moderate MR based on bedside echo. Procedure initially c/b hypotension and AMS requiring IABP placement; this was removed on ___. Pt will continue on ASA 81mg daily, clopidogrel 75mg daily (for at least ___ year), atorvastatin 80mg daily, and metoprolol succinate XL 25mg daily. #HFrEF: Newly-depressed EF at 35%, likely ischemic cardiomyopathy. Her home diuretics were initially held, but she was restarted on her home furosemide (20mg every other day) for preload management. She was started on metoprolol succinate XL as above, and should consider starting an ACE-I as an outpatient. # Acute Anemia: Source unclear. No evidence of hematoma at R femoral access site or RP hematoma. Upon arrival to CCU her Hgb was 6.3 down from 9.7. Received 2uPRBCs with appropriate response. Will need close follow-up and anemia workup as an outpatient. # Leukocytosis: To a peak of 22.8, without obvious infectious source and negative UCx. Likely a stress response in setting of STEMI. A viral etiology giving rise to her nausea/vomiting is also possible. Flu negative, and without CXR consolidation. This downtrended to 10.4 prior to discharge. -continue to trend CHRONIC/RESOLVED ISSUES # ___: RESOLVED. Cr 1.7 from a presumed baseline ~1.0-1.2. There may have been a volume depletion component I/s/o nausea and vomiting, however newly depressed EF and STEMI and hypotension raises possibility of cardiogenic shock. S/p 3L IVF since admission. Cr improved to baseline # Anxiety: Continued clonazepam BID # Hypothyroidism: Continue home levothyroxine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: # CODE: DNI, OK to resuscitate in ___ period only (i.e. DNI/DNR reversed temporarily for procedure) -- confirmed with daughter ___ # CONTACT/HCP: Daughter ___ (___) [ ] RADIOLOGY FINDINGS REQUIRING F/U: - 1.2cm exophytic lesion in upper pole of left kidney for which U/S was recommended. - Total compression with T11, and some interval degenerative changes with lumbar stenosis. [ ] ACUTE ANEMIA: - Pt with acute Hb drop of unclear etiology. Started on pantoprazole 40mg daily while in-house. Consider GIB w/u as outpatient if still anemic at follow-up visit. [ ] MEDICATION CHANGES: - Added: Clopidogrel 75mg daily, atorvastatin 80mg daily, pantoprazole 40mg daily - Changed: Metoprolol succinate (50mg daily -> 25mg daily) - Discontinued: Famotidine, pravastatin - Held: Losartan (though can consider restarting home losartan as an outpatient, or at Cards f/u with Dr ___. [ ] FOLLOW-UP LABS: - To be drawn on ___. - Please collect CBC and chem-10 to ensure no worsening anemia or acute kidney injury.
summary: You were seen at our hospital because you were having nausea and vomiting. Based on your lab work, we found that you were having a heart attack due to several blocked blood vessels. We looked at your heart using a special camera ("echocardiogram") and found that your heart was not squeezing as well as it should, which we believe was due to your heart attack. Our cardiologists helped open up your blockages with special stents, improving blood flow to your heart. During the procedure, your blood pressure became very low; for this reason, we temporarily had to place a special device in your heart ("intra-aortic balloon pump," or "IABP") to keep your pressures up. This was removed within 24 hours. Your pressures returned to normal, and we started you on some new medications to keep your heart healthy after these procedures. Because your heart is not squeezing as well as it should, please eat a low-salt diet.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with right hip fracture s/p ORIF in ___, chronic diastolic CHF, pulmonary HTN on home O2, CKD, DM2 who presented with RLE weakness and pain for two days. Right lower extremity weakness, likely due to spinal stenosis: She presented from home and was recently discharged from a prolonged rehabilitation stay for a right hip fracture s/p ORIF in ___. During this hospitalization, she was evaluated by orthopedic trauma in the ED who felt that her presentation was consistent with a slow to heal fracture and did not require acute surgical intervention. All imaging, including CT head, MRI spine, plain films of hips demonstrated chronic degenerative changes without new fracture or acute process. She was evaluated by neurology. The most likely cause for her RLE pain/weakness was thought to be multifactorial, secondary to her slow healing R hip fracture, spinal stenosis and chronic compression fractures, and deconditioning. She was evaluated by ___ and was able to ambulate successfully with a walker and was cleared to go home with ongoing outpatient physical therapy. Pain was managed with Tylenol and oxycodone as needed. Urinary tract infection: She was also found to have a symtomatic uncomplicated UTI based on UA and culture and was treated with a 5 day course of oral ciprofloxacin. Acute diastolic CHF with exacerbation: She also reported increased shortness of breath from baseline. She chronically retains CO2, sating 90-92% on 2.5L O2 at home. Her volume status was slightly elevated given crackles on pulmonary exam and CXR with mild pulmonary edema. She was diuresed with IV lasix followed by increasing her home PO lasix from 20 to 40mg daily. At the time of discharge, pulmonary status was at baseline and she was scheduled to see her outpatient cardiologist for follow up. ___ will assist with daily weight monitoring. All of the patients other chronic health issues (hypertension, hyperlipidemia, GERD, DM, hyperkalemia, psych) were managed with home medications as prescribed. Patient was presumed FULL CODE throughout admission. Transitional issues: - Outpatient lab work: Na, K, CL, HCO3, BUN, Cr and glucose on ___ to be followed up by her PCP/HCA discharge clinic - Recommend conisideration of treatment for osteoporosis given compression fractures
summary: You were admitted to the hospital on ___ because of right leg pain and weekness for two-three days. You were unable to bear weight, keep in your balance, or walk as a result. At the hospital, your head, body, and leg were scanned with x-rays and CT scans, which showed your chronic spine problems but no new changes. Orthopedic surgery and neurology evaluated you and felt that you did not need any interventions. You were also found to have an urinary tract infection which we are treating with ciprofloxacin for 5 days. You also had some trouble breathing and your lungs had some fluid. We gave you oxygen and an extra dose of lasix.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old with a history of hypothyroidism, sarcoidosis, hypertension and constrictive pericarditis, who presented with progressive abdominal pain and dyspnea on exertion. ACTIVE ISSUES # CONSTRICTIVE PERICARDITIS: diagnosed by by CMR, TTE, and history with equivocal constrictive changes on LHC/RHC. He reports the progressive development of symptoms (dyspnea, abdominal pain) over the preceding ___ months, with acute worsening since ___. He was last able to walk without dyspnea in ___. The patient has a history of hypothyroidism, which could be contributing, however, his TSH is normal (though was mildly elevated to 5.7 in ___. Furthermore, he also has a remote history of isolated pulmonary sarcoidosis (diagnosed, and treated with steroids in ___. It is possible that the constrictive process in the pericardium is related to sarcoidosis, however imaging is not suggestive of this. Lastly, idiopathic and viral etiologies should be considered. On admission, the patient appeared volume overloaded and he was diuresed successfully. Admission weigth 103 kg. Weight on discharge 96.7 kg. Discharge diuretic regimen included torsemide 80 mg daily. Patient was evaluated by cardiac surgery for pericardial stripping, which is planned as an interval procedure, on ___, with Dr. ___. # ABDOMINAL PAIN: likely related to pericardial process. Improved with diuresis. Will plan for surgical management of constrictive pericarditis, as above. # TRANSAMINITIS: normal LFTs on admission with evidence of right heart failure. After diuresis, AST/ALT noted to rise to ___. Maintained BPs throughout hospitalization, so ischemic hepatopathy unlikely and congestive hepatopathy unlikely, as volume status improved. Will follow up as outpatient. # PLEURAL EFFUSIONS: likely cardiac in nature. Though has had one effusion with evidence of exudate, may have been in setting of diuresis. He is s/p L sided pleurodesis (___). CXR demonstrated small bilateral pleural effusions with atelectasis. Based on imaging, the effusions appeared stable/unchanged. Symptoms of effusions improved with diuresis. As likely secondary to pericarditis, interval surgical management planned. Diuresis regimen as above. CHRONIC ISSUES # HYPERTENSION: normotensive currently. Managed as an outpatient with atenolol, though likely getting largest beneft from loop diuretics. Atenolol held, as to avoid negative inotropy with constrictive pericarditis. Diuresed, as above and discharged on above regimen. # HYPOTHYROIDISM: most recent TSH (___) 3.4 on 50 mcg levothyroxine. Continued home levothyroxine. # SARCOIDOSIS: patient with remote history of isolated pulmonary sarcoidosis, confirmed with biopsy and treated with steroids. He denies any recurrence of his disease since that time (___). Sarcoidosis may be invovled in presence of constrictive pericarditis. Should follow up pathology post-pericardial stripping. ** TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ** - Patient had PPD placed on ___ at 2:00pm on the right forearm. Instructed to return to ___ on ___ or Wedensday ___ to have this read. - Patient needs to have Chem6 and LFTs repeated when he visits his PCP's office either on ___ or ___ to monitor creatinine and electrolytes on torsemide as well as LFTs. - Patient had trasnaminitis to the ___. The etiology was not clear. His volume overload improved so this was not felt to be congestive hepatopathy. Please recheckeck LFTs as noted above. - Seen by cardiac surgery while inpatient with tentative plans for pericardial stripping as an oupatient on ___ with Dr. ___. However, they will follow-up with him for finalization of plans. - Atenolol was discontinued because he does not benefit from prolongation of diastolic filling time it the setting of constrictive pericarditis.
summary: You came to the hospital because of adominal pain and poor appetite. Your symptoms were from fluid building up in your body as a result of constrictive pericaridits. Your Lasix was changed to another diuretic called torsemide and were were able to remove a lot of fluid from your body with improvement in yoru symptoms. You were evaluated by the cardiac surgery team who felt that you would be a good candidate for a procedure called pericardial striping, which helps to improve the symptoms from constrictive pericarditis. In preparation for this you had a catheterization of your heart. This showed some coronary artery disease, but nothing needed to be stented. This also showed elevated pressures in the right side of your heaert which is consistent with constrictive pericarditis. As planned, you'll return for your pericardial stripping surgery on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT SUMMARY: ================ ___ with history of prostate cancer (in remission) PEA arrest in the setting of traumatic fall ___ and heart failure with recovered ejection fraction who presents with worsening dyspnea on exertion admitted for CHF exacerbation with again reduced LVEF (25%); without evidence of significant CAD on cath. #Acute decompensation of chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (recovered) LVEF now 33% down from (45% in ___, 25% in ___, diagnosed in setting of PEA arrest after traumatic fall. Etiology likely ischemia in setting of PEA arrest, no significant coronary artery disease. Patient had signs of volume overload (BNP, crackles, elevated JVP), likely in the setting of stopping diuretics in ___. Current exacerbation is unlikely ischemic given the absence of elevated cardiac biomarkers and the absence of chest pain). However TTE with worsening EF and wall motion abnormalities in distribution of LAD, but with no CAD on cath. Patient was diuresed with furosemide IV, then transitioned to 40 PO daily. Afterload/NHBK were managed with Losartan Potassium 25 mg PO/NG DAILY, Carvedilol 12.5 mg PO/NG BID, Spironolactone 25 mg PO/NG DAILY, Patient may benefit from primary prevention ICD if no improvement over upcoming months #Non sustained ventricular tachycardia Patient with multiple episodes of asymptomatic runs of NSVT, approximately ___ per day/night runs of ___ seconds. Patient was started on amiodarone 200 BID for ongoing episodic NSVT in setting of reduced EF. ================ CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ #Hx Cardiac arrest: Appears to have been PEA arrest in setting of trauma, no cath in ___ records from that event. #pAfib: Patient was mostly in sinus rhythm with the exception of runs of NSVT (see above). He was continued on his home Carvedilol 12.5mg BID and home Rivaroxaban 20mg daily. #Prostate cancer: Stable. Currently disease free. S/p radical prostatectomy in ___, XRT up to ___, hormonal therapy up to ___. See outpatient note from Dr ___ on ___ for whole course. #Osteopenia: Osteopenia proximal femur and osteoporosis distal radius. Currently gets Prolia every six months, last infusion ___. Patient was continued on supplemental calcium and vitamin D #Low CD4 count: Borderline low 129-347 per outside records. Patient had been prescribed acyclovir BID for prophylaxis for shingles by oncologist, but had not yet picked up from pharmacy. Patient declined to start acyclovir ppx in house. #Gout: has not had flare in years. Continued on home allopurinol #Hyperlipidemia: Continued on home Simvastatin 20mg QPM since no evidence of CAD. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== -Discharge weight: 80.1kg / 176.59lbs -Discharge creatinine: 1.0 [ ] Will need repeat echo approximately 1 month post discharge to evaluate for consideration of primary prevention ICD. [ ] Started amiodarone for asymptomatic runs of NSVT in setting of reduced LVEF. Initiated loading with 200 mg BID on ___ - will have completed 10g loading dose on ___. Please transition to 200 mg daily at that time. CONTACT: Wife, ___ ___
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath You were admitted to the hospital because you were short of breath. This was because you had extra fluid around your lungs due to your heart failure. You were given medications through the IV to help you urinate out the extra fluid. You had testing done of your heart to look at how well it pumps (echocardiogram, "TTE") and the arteries that feed your heart (coronary angiography "cardiac cath"). They showed that your heart isn't pumping quite as well as it was last year, and that you don't have any significant blockages in the arteries that feed your heart. Your weight at discharge is 176.6 pounds. Please weigh yourself today at home and use this as your new baseline. Please weigh yourself every day in the morning.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH of severe aortic stenosis s/p TAVR in ___, CAD s/p multiple (5) PTCAs, and atrial fibrillation on anticoagulation 325mg ASA with recent admission (___) for dizziness/light headedness who presented to the ED with episodic chest pain. #Chest Pain. During admission, patient had multiple episodes of day and night time chest pain. All studies were negative for STEMI. He did stay in Afib throughout admission. One episode of chest pain was associated with monocular visual changes (loss of color and "shadow." Neurology and Opthamology were consulted. All of your lab values and studies were reassuring that you were not having a heart attack. During one of these episodes you had a concerning change in your vision. Our neurology and opthamology teams were consulted and confirmed that you did not suffer any damage to your eye or suffer a stroke. Subsequent images revealed that you had a some cholesterol build up in the main arteries in your neck on both sides. You were started on a medication, Crestor, to help lower cholesterol to prevent any further damage. Additionally, our cardiology team evaluated you. As we already knew you have an irregular rhythm, "atrial fibrillation." Additionally, an Echocardiogram of your heart did not show any significant change since it was last evaluated.
