text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMHX HTN, HLD p/w indolent onset of ___ shortness of breath, dyspnea on exertion found to have atrial tachycardia/flutter and systolic CHF with an EF of 30% in the context of uncontrolled BP and afib. He responded well to a lasix drip and his HR and BP was well controlled with metoprolol. He was anticoagulated with heparin and started on warfarin. The pt can be considered for cardioversion in ___. # Dyspnea on exertion/Narrow complex Tachycardia: pt has had symptoms of progressive DOE for few weeks PTA, BP was uncontrolled he has been off antihypertensives x 7+ months (since last ___) including prescribed atenolol and lisinopril. EKG initially showed ST changes but trops were flat suggesting they may be rate-related changes. MI was ruled out with negative troponins. Pt was rate controlled with escalating doses of metoprolol and will be discharged on metoprolol tartrate 150mg QAM and 100mg QPM. Given EF of 30% pt will likely benefit from fluid restriction and should continue with torsemide 20mg PO as an outpt. # systolic CHF - BNP in the 800s on admission and ___ on ___, but no specific trigger for CHF aside from afib found so far; ferritin WNL, TSH WNL, total protein 6.7. Pt drinks ___ EtOH drinks per week. While this would be a low amout of EtOH to cause EtOH-related dilated cardiomyopathy, pt was counseled to stop. Trops flat suggesting no recent cardiac event. EF of 30% on echo ___. Pt was initially unresponsive to IV lasix but successfully underwent aggressive diuresis with lasix drip up to 10mg/hr with excellent urine output, losing ~10kg over course of hospital stay and will be discharged on torsemide 20mg QD. Currently planning for for rate control with HR goal <100-110 with TEE/cardioversion or ablation to be planned in ___ as an outpt. Pt will be continued on ___ 81mg daily as an outpt. # HTN: uncontrolled initially 160s/120s but have been improved around 100s-120s systolic with HR around 90 on the metoprolol as above. The lisinopril 10 mg daily that he was prescribed was held in setting ___ and NOT continued on discharge given elevated creatinine. # Hyperlipidemia: Pt was restarted on Simvastatin 40 mg ___. # HX of CKD stage 3: last Creat was done in ___ and was elevated at 1.41, eGFR of 50. Cr 1.3 on admission and rose to 2.3 with initial diuresis and came down to 2.2 on discharge. # Obesity: As pt gained 36 lbs in the last ___ years, it was recommended that he be seen at the weight management clinic. He should discuss this with his PCP. In ___ his A1c was 5.8%, was 5.5% on admission. # OSA: Pt controls it with positional sleeping. CPAP was not indicated. # Transitional Issues - recommend repeat ECHO as an outpatient now that patient is adequately diuresed - Set up with ___ clinic. Started on warfarin with goal INR ___ for afib/flutter. INR 1.9 on discharge. INR due on ___, warfarin may need titration. Discharged with many tabs of warfarin 1mg and 2mg to allow for titration. - CHEM10 due on ___. Will need to monitor Cr and electrolytes at upcoming appointments this week. - should be started on ACEI as an outpatient when kidney function improves to baseline. Lipids should also be rechecked and patient should be restarted on a statin as an outpatient. - started on metoprolol tartrate 150mg Qam and 100mg ___ 81, torsemide 20mg daily, potassium 10meq daily - Pt is a good candidate for cardioversion ___ after being sufficiently anticoagulated, per cardiologist's discretion
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath and were found to have atrial fibrillation and heart failure with your heart pumping about half as well as a normal person (we describe this as an Ejection Fraction - yours is 30% and a normal one is between 55-70%). In heart failure, your heart cannot pump out as much blood as is coming back to your heart through your veins. This causes fluid to leak out into your lungs, abdomen and legs. We gave you a lot of medication to help remove this fluid by making you urinate. You urinated significant amounts of fluid and your shortness of breath and swelling improved. With our treatments you lost over 20 pounds of extra fluid that was weighing your body down. The medicine we gave you can affect your kidneys so we balanced improving your symptoms with maintaining good kidney function as much as possible. For the rapid heart rate you were started on medications to slow your heart rate down and blood thinning medications to prevent the development of blood clots which can cause stroke.

text: Brief Hospital Course: (1) Congestive Heart Failure - EF noted to be 10% on prior echoes, with recent weight gain of 6 lbs over the past week prior to admission and increasing lower extremity edema. Chest-xray on admission revealed clear evidence of pulmonary overload. Lasix and torsemide contradindicated secondary to recent rash felt to be secondary to these medications; ethacrynic acid used with effect for diuresis. During hospitalization, she was net -500 - 1L negative per day. Digitalis level was within normal limits. Her coreg was continued. She was discharged on ethacrynic acid with follow up with allergist at discretion of outpatient cardiologist for possible desensitization as an outpatient against furosemide and torsemide. The allergist's contact information is: ___. . (2) Rash - Felt to be secondary to hypersensitivity reaction from prior torsemide administration given timing. Had dermatology evaluation as an outpatient consistent with the above. Hydrocortisone cream was continued. See appointment with allergist as above.
summary: You were admitted for volume overload. Because you were allergic to Lasix, we started you on a new medication called ethacrynic acid, which will also help you urinate off the excess fluid. You have responded to this medication well and are tolerating it. The rash, however, will take a few weeks to completely resolve. You can follow up with the Allergy Clinic. Please weigh yourself every morning and call Dr. ___ ___ if you gain more than 3 lbs. If you gain more than 3lbs in one day increase your ethacrynic dose to 100mg by mouth twice a day. Your INR is low. Please take Coumadin 2mg by mouth on ___ and ___. You can resume Coumadin 1mg by mouth once a day starting ___. Please have your labs checked ___ and faxed to your primary care physician.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with PMH of severe MR and moderate mitral stenosis who presented ___ for planned TMVR. Course c/b ___, pneumothorax with chest tube insertion s/p thoracentesis, hemolytic anemia, nausea with poor appetite, and hypotension with GIB requiring brief CCU transfer. #Severe MR: s/p TMVR ___ (26 ___ 3); Post TMVR echo shows EF 65%, mitral valve mean gradient 4 mmHg, small left to right atrial shunt. - ASA 81 #GIB/Normocytic Anemia: s/p CCU transfer for hypotension likely related to GIB as evidenced by +melena/guaiac. GI Consulted and signed off as she does not wish to have EGD. Last PRBC transfusion ___. Hemolysis likely contributing in setting of shearing on MVR as evidenced by low haptoglobin, elevated LDH, and schistocytes on smear. She expresses her wish to not have lab draws or blood transfusions at this time. - Pantoprazole PO ___: Suspected to be related to hemodynamic changes ___ and diuresis. Urine sediment suggestive of ATN, consistent Urine Na<20 suggesting prerenal etiology. Negative renal US. - PO intake encouraged #Frailty/Malnutrition: Albumin 3.4, Continues with poor appetite/intake, c/o ongoing nausea. Attempted dobhoff placement at bedside ___, very uncomfortable, patient refused advancement of tube. - Unclear etiology of nausea, patient reports it prior to ___. - ___ consulted, appreciate recs. - Marinol was attempted with no improvement of symptoms - Appreciated palliative care's recs of Carafate, scheduling Zofran, low dose zyprexa at night. - Per palliative, 4 mg Dexamethasone daily for nausea. #Palliative Care: Patient has frequently expressed the wish to be comfortable and to minimize unnecessary interventions. Palliative care consulted and following. - Continue Carafate, scheduled Zofran, low dose Zyprexa, dexamethasone as above. # Chronic diastolic heart failure, EF>55%: ___ d/c wgt = 42.9kg. - PRELOAD: holding Torsemide at this time d/t ___ - INOTROPY: Carvedilol - AFTERLOAD: holding ACE d/t ___ RESOLVED ISSUES: # Hyponatremia # Hypotension # Left pneumothorax s/p L chest tube: bilateral effusions s/p thoracentesis, with left PTX. She does not want dialysis, phlebotomy, blood transfusions, invasive testing/interventions, or heroic measures. Palliative care team involved. DISPO: Hospice Discussion with patient on day of discharge, ___ - she confirms her wishes to transition to hospice. The consent forms were reviewed with her prior to signing. ___ MD, Dr. ___ has reviewed all information, has seen the patient, and agrees with her plan for transition to hospice.
summary: You were admitted after your trans-catheter mitral valve replacement procedure. The procedure was successful in repairing the function of your mitral valve. However, you developed some complications after your procedure. These complications include: hematuria, pleural effusion with thoracentesis that was complicated by a pneumothorax requiring the insertion of a chest tube, acute kidney injury, low blood pressure requiring a brief transfer to the ICU, gastrointestinal bleeding, hemolysis of your red blood cells requiring blood transfusions, and nausea with extremely poor appetite. To manage some of these complications, some different specialist teams were involved in your care. Through the evolution of your post-procedure hospitalization you have made clear to these team members that you would no longer like to receive this type of care. On ___ you signed a medical order for life-sustaining treatment through which you expressed your wishes to not be resuscitated or intubated going forward. After a discussion with your family and ___ medical team you have decided to pursue Hospice care; the goals of which will be to manage your symptoms and comfort while focusing on your priorities towards the end of your life.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man with Hx of pafib, HTN/HL, CAD, s/p CABG in ___ (LIMA-LAD only), ___ s/p Cx stenting (LCX), Aortic stenosis (last OMR echo ___ -> 1.0cm2 Ao), Renal artery stenosis, s/p left renal artery stenting ___, Hypertension, Hyperlipidemia, Hypothyroidism, and Large cell Lymphoma, s/p R-CHOP who presents to ED from home in ___ w/ worsening SOB/DOE and chest pressure episodes over the past 3 wks. . # SOB: DOE in last 2 months with findings suggestive of PND. Likely attributable to afib vs AS. Last ECHO ___ (unavailable data), omr report ___. TTE performed ___ showed worsened AS (severe grade). He was continued on home aggrenox, additional asa was discontinued. He was continued no betablocker and pravachol therapy. . #. Afib, slow: Apparently new per family, but unclear as pt. on coumadin. Most likely ___ Xanax and small degree of peripheral neuropathy (proprioception impaired), but has some evidence of impaired ___ coordination. No focal neuro findings. Renal u/s on ___ showed no evidence of renal artery stenosis. RAS is unlikely etiology of pt's DOE and heart failure given normal findings on renal u/s. . # Lymphoma, NH s/p tx now in remission. summary: You were admitted to the hospital with complaints of shortness of breath with exertion and spells of breathlessness at nighttime. You were having some anxiety alleviated by xanax at home however it was felt that your symptoms of difficulty breathing are due to your heart valve disease and atrial fibrillation (heart arrhythmia). You were also evaluated for any change in your renal artery stenosis using a renal ultrasound which showed improvement in your kidney size and did not indicate that this was the cause of your heart failure. A ultrasound of your heart, an echocardiogram, was performed which showed worsened aortic stenosis, valve disease in your heart. You were sent for valvuloplasty on ___ which showed an improvement in the valve function. The arteries on your heart were assessed and it was determined that you do not require any stenting or additional intervention at this time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ hx of HTN, HLD, DM2 and CAD s/p PCI and esophageal spasms admitted for ACS rule out. # Chest pain: S/p ASA 325 mg x 1. Chest pain atypical in presentation-- did not start with exertion, not relieved by SLN. EKG without new ischemic changes and troponin negative x 2. Of note, per Partner's ___ review, pt was seen in ED and also ruled out for ACS at ___. Aditionally, pt had repeat episode of chest pain overnight. EKG once again unchanged and troponin/CK-MB negative. However, given that pt endorses new DOE, concern for progression of CAD and therefore underwent stress/MIBI. Stress test with atypical symptoms in the absence of ischemic EKG changes. Poor functional capacity limited by dyspnea. Imaging notable for normal myocardial perfusion with no reversible defects at the level of exercise achieved, though slightly increased left ventricular cavity size and decreased left ventricular ejection fraction of 50%, previously 59%. No evidence of heart failure on exam and therefore encouraged to follow-up with outpatient cardiologist. #Esophageal spasms: Likely a contributor to chest pain. CCB considered for hx of esophageal spasm, however pt has tried diltiazem without relief in past, and is already on amlodipine. Home nitrate was uptitrated: imdur 120 mg QD increased to imdur 180 mg QD, and he was encouraged to follow up with outpatient GI. ===transitional issues==== -IMDUR 150 increased to IMDUR 180 QD to improve esophageal spasms. -LFTs mildly elevated: AST 45, ALT 66. Nml alk phos and t.bili. Likely ___ fatty liver. Pls continue to monitor as outpt
summary: You came to the hospital because you were having chest pain. The largest thing we worry about is a heart attack, particularly in you since you have had prior stents placed in your coronary arteries before. Thankfully, your EKGs did not show any evidence of a heart attack, nor did the markers for cardiac dysfunction in your blood, even after a repeated episode of chest pain you had overnight. Therefore, your symptoms are not from a heart attack. Unfortunately, we are unable to tell you what is causing them exactly; likely it is a component of your esophageal spasms, and therefore we have uptitrated your home nitrate. We have increased the dose of your isosorbide mononitrate from 120 mg to 180 mg daily.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ hx of HTN, HLD, DM2 and CAD s/p PCI and esophageal spasms admitted for ACS rule out. # Chest pain: S/p ASA 325 mg x 1. Chest pain atypical in presentation-- did not start with exertion, not relieved by SLN. EKG without new ischemic changes and troponin negative x 2. Of note, per Partner's ___ review, pt was seen in ED and also ruled out for ACS at ___. Aditionally, pt had repeat episode of chest pain overnight. EKG once again unchanged and troponin/CK-MB negative. However, given that pt endorses new DOE, concern for progression of CAD and therefore underwent stress/MIBI. Stress test with atypical symptoms in the absence of ischemic EKG changes. Poor functional capacity limited by dyspnea. Imaging notable for normal myocardial perfusion with no reversible defects at the level of exercise achieved, though slightly increased left ventricular cavity size and decreased left ventricular ejection fraction of 50%, previously 59%. No evidence of heart failure on exam and therefore encouraged to follow-up with outpatient cardiologist. #Esophageal spasms: Likely a contributor to chest pain. CCB considered for hx of esophageal spasm, however pt has tried diltiazem without relief in past, and is already on amlodipine. Home nitrate was uptitrated: imdur 120 mg QD increased to imdur 180 mg QD, and he was encouraged to follow up with outpatient GI. ===transitional issues==== -IMDUR 150 increased to IMDUR 180 QD to improve esophageal spasms. -LFTs mildly elevated: AST 45, ALT 66. Nml alk phos and t.bili. Likely ___ fatty liver. Pls continue to monitor as outpt

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with CAD s/p CABG and PCI, HTN, dyslipidemia, and angina, who presented with new onset heart failure and pulmonary edema. . # Diastolic heart failure/ hypoxia: Patient was hypoxic on admission in setting of acute on chronic heart failure and pulmonary edema. She had JVD to her jaw and 2+ ___ edema. An ABG was obtained and was notable for a pO2 of 53. She also had an ECHO, which was consistent with diastolic heart failure. She received IV lasix and PO metolazone and was negative about ___ liters over her hospital stay. Her diuresis was complicated by renal failure and hyponatremia (see below). She was requiring ___ liters of O2 by NC for the first few days, but was breathing RA and satting in the low ___ on discharge; she had no ___ edema and no JVD on discharge. Throughout her hospital stay, she was kept on a low salt diet, and her blood pressure was closely monitored to keep her afterload low (SBP<130). She was discharged on a PO lasix dose of 20 mg qd to maintain her diuresed state. # Acute Renal Failure: As mentioned above, diuresis with IV lasix was complicated by acute renal failure, which set in quite rapidly following onset of diuresis, well before the patient had fully diuresed or improved clinically from a CHF standpoint (renal failure was most likely prerenal--she had a FeUrea of 6.72%). When her creatinine went from 1.0 to 2.0 over the first three days, her diuresis was slowed down, which improved her kidney function to a creatinine of 1.2 over the next few days. However, she continued to remain quite hypoxic with worsening dyspnea and worsening exam. Thus, her diuresis was increased again, with added PO metolazone in addition to IV lasix, which helped her hypoxia and dyspnea, but worsened her kidney function, with a peak creatinine of 3.4. She was discharged with an improved creatinine of 3.1 and with plans for close follow up within the next few days involving lab draws and a visit with her PCP. . #Anemia: She was admitted with a chronic normocytic anemia, with hematocrit of 32.9. With IV lasix and fluid mobilization, her Hct dropped to ~27, but then increased to 33 again and was 29 on discharge. B12, Folate, Methylmalonic acid, and hemolysis labs were obtained and were normal. Iron studies were consistent with anemia of chronic disease: Her serum iron was low (25), but her ferritin was normal (191). Although ferritin can be elevated in inflammatory states, a level of 191 would be inconsistent with low underlying ferritin, even in presence of inflammatory state (would expect ferritin of 60 or less). Thus her total body iron stores appeared to be normal. Her TIBC and transferrin were not elevated, consistent with normal iron stores. . # Hyponatremia: The patient's Na nadired at 127 in the setting of diuresis, probably ___ low effective circulating volume. With continued fluid mobilization, her sodium stabilized, and remained in the 128-138 range for the rest of her hospitalization. Her daily neurological exam remained stable. . #HTN: The patient's blood pressure was well controlled during her hospitalization. We kept her SBP < 130 for afterload reduction in setting of her heart failure. We continued her BB, amlodipine, isosorbide, and ___ and ___ her HCTZ and her clonidine. We discharged her on her home meds, but decreased her ___ to 160 mg in setting of renal failure, and dc'd her HCTZ and clonidine, as her BP had been well controlled without these. . #CAD: The patient had a significant history of CAD and had presented with some chest discomfort. She was ruled out for MI, had no EKG changes, and was kept on her BB, ASA, and statin. . #Angina: The patient had known exertional angina and was continued on her ranolizine. She requested SL nitro on one occasion, but had no associated EKG changes, and CEs were negative. Written by ___, MSIV
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with shortness of breath and increased lower extremity edema. This was from fluid backing up from your heart into your lungs and legs. This was caused in part by the amount of salt in your diet and many years of hypertension. You had an ECHO (a type of ultrasound) of your heart which showed that you have diastolic heart failure, which means that your heart has reduced ability to pump blood through your body. You recieved lasix through your IV line, a medication which makes you urinate and removes excess water from your body. This helped to remove water from your lungs, which allowed you to breath better and get more oxygen into your blood. Please maintain a low salt diet. This will help to prevent re-accumulation of fluid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: PATIENT LEFT AMA ON ___. # NSTEMI: Patient had peak troponin of 1.5 at OSH, with no ST elevations. Upon admission at ___, Cardiac enzymes were trending down (see above), and ECG was stable w/ LBBB. Patient chest pain free since ___ through hospitalization at ___. She had cardiac catheterization on ___, which showed 1. Three vessel native coronary artery disease. 2. Widely patent LIMA-LAD, SVG-Ramus and SVG-AM grafts. 3. Elevated right sided filling pressures. 4. Left subclavian stenosis with a 20mm Hg gradient. 5. Placement of a drug-eluting stent in the ___-LCX. She was maintained on aspirin, clopidogrel, metoprolol, isosorbide mononitrate, atorvastatin. She received heparin IV at OSH for 48 hours, and therefore did not require it on this admission. Her medication regimen on discharge was aspirin 325mg daily, plavix 75mg daily, metoprolol 25mg daily, Imdur 45mg daily, and atorvastatin 40mg daily. Upon discharge, she was chest pain free. # Acute on Chronic systolic CHF: Patient with DOE intermittently during her hospitalization; also with crackles and mildly elevated JVP on exam. She was diuresed with IV lasix PRN, with careful monitoring of renal function. On discharge she was euvolemic. She was discharged on metoprolol. ACEi was not started in the setting of acute renal failure. She should continue lasix 20mg BID as an outpatient. # Diabetes: Patient maintained on home Lantus 4units qam and conservative HISS, as she reported sensitivity to humalog. Her sugars were often slightly low ___ was consulted to re-evaluate the patient's sliding scale. Her lantus was dropped from 3 to 2 units in the morning and her sliding scale was also reduced. Her sugars were under improved control after these changes were made. ___ also asked us to perform a cosyntropin stim test on the patient but she refused the test. # Acute on chronic kidney disease: Baseline Cr 1.5, down from 2.0 on admission. Patient was administered gentle prehydration and mucomyst prior to catheterization. Creatinine on discharge was 2.1. Patient left AMA despite her rising creatinine. # Hyperkalemia- Patient noted to have bump in creatinine and potassium on AM labs on ___. Chem7 was rechecked in ___ and found to have creatinine of 2.1 and K+ of 7.2. Patient adament about leaving the hospital despite repeatedly being informed of the danger of leaving with these lab values. She signed out AMA on ___ with plans to get her labs drawn the following day at her PCP's office. # UTI: Questionable positive UA with several epithelial cells at ___. She was started on cipro there, but UA here was negative and she remained asymptomatic so no antibiotics were given. # Normocytic Anemia: Patient with Hct of 26 (around her baseline, but down from Hct 29 on admission). Likely in the setting of her CKD; no evidence of acute bleeding. Patient very reluctant to receive blood transfusion, although she did consent if absolutely needed. Also in setting of her acute sx of CHF, did not want to transfuse to Hct 30, and no clear evidence of benefit to transfusing to this level. # Sick sinus syndrome s/p pacer: Patient with irregular pacing noted on tele on ___. EP was consulted and readjusted sensitivity of pacer.
summary: PATIENT LEFT AMA. You were admitted to ___ on ___, because you had a small heart attack. You had a cardiac catheterization on ___, which is a test that looks for blockages in the blood vessels supplying your heart. We found a blockage in one of your arteries that we placed a stent in to open it up. Since then, you have not had any signs of heart pain. You were able to ambulate well without any chest discomfort at all. In addition, the ___ team slightly modified your insulin regimen so you only get 2 units of lantus in the morning. Upon discharge, you were chest-pain free and comfortable. You were also having shortness of breath, so we took some fluid off of your lungs with lasix through your IV. We also did an echocardiogram to be sure that there were no new problems with the pumping function of your heart, which showed your heart function is slightly worse.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ year old male with PMH of dilated cardiomyopathy with ___, dilated ascending aortic aneurysm (6.3cm), moderate AI, moderate MR, moderate TR, and AFib on ASA only with no coumadin ___ falls, and vascular dementia who was transferred here from ___ for further evaluation of an abdominal mass highly suggestive of malignancy and found to have + UA. General surgery was consulted, and did not feel that the mass could be resected with pt return to baseline, nor did they favor drainage of abdominal wall fluid collection. As such, family meeting was held, at which time Health Care Proxies jointly decided to pursue comfort measures only. Pain relief initially with morphine IV, but after lost IV access, pain relief with SL morphine. Pt rested comfortably until discharged to hospice care. Did not complete antibiotic course for antibiotics given CMO status, and did not continue many home medications. However, did continue carvedilol after CMO given that pt has A-fib and did not want to cause erratic rate control and potential discomfort if this medication was discontinued.
summary: You were hospitalized at ___ for evaluation of a large mass found in your abdomen. The surgeons met with you and your family, but unfortunately, they felt that the mass could not safely or successfully be surgically resected. As such, we had several meetings with your family to determine the next best course of action given your previously expressed wishes for health care in situations such as these. Together, we planned to maximize your comfort and not to pursue further work-up or aggressive diagnotic testing. You are being discharged to hospice care, and goals of care will be on maximal comfort and symptom relief.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ ___ yo M with history of severe aortic stenosis who presents with progressive DOE, orthopnea, and ___ edema concerning for CHF exacerbation, likely related to worsening of aortic stenosis. #Acute on Chronic diastolic congestive heart failure: likely the cause of his symptoms of SOB and likely related to severe aortic stenosis. Recently started Lasix 20 po qd but continues to develop symptoms. Repeat echo performed normal global systolic function and a preserved ejection fraction (65%). Aortic valve area 0.8 cm. He was diuresed with boluses of 20mg IV Lasix and had symptomatic improvement in breathing. Creatinine remained stable. Established on a Lasix 20 mg po daily on discharge. #SOB: likely due to aortic stenosis and CHF exacerbation, but there was concern for PE given subacute onset. D-dimer was elevated. ___ negative for DVT, and V/Q scan obtained showed a very low likelihood for PE, ruling out PE. SOB improved with Lasix. #Aortic stenosis: repeat echo as above. Underwent left heart catheterization while hospitalized which showed 80% stenosis of diagonal branch of LAD, medical therapy recommended. TAVR team to call Mr. ___ to schedule an appointment for further workup. #H/o AFib with RVR: sinus rhythm on presentation. Labetalol continued. #MDS: s/p BMT 71 days ago. Infection prophylaxis continued and CBC WNL while hospitalized. Scheduled for heme/onc follow up. CHRONIC ISSUES ___ lymphoma: in remission per last PET scan ___ #GVHD: on cyclosporine, no evidence of GVHD at this time #Insomnia: Ativan and CPAP #GERD: omeprazole TRANSITIONAL ISSUES [] Patient's diuretic regimen is Lasix 20 mg po daily [] Follow-up with TAVR team, will contact patient for appointment [] Heme/onc appointment on ___, please check creatinine on current diuretic regimen **Discharge weight 70.4 kg**
summary: You were hospitalized for shortness of breath. We felt that this was related to the severity of your aortic stenosis, so we used medication to help get extra fluid off of your lungs. We discharged you home on a dose of Lasix 20 mg daily. We also ruled out any clots in your lungs with imaging. You had a heart catheterization which showed disease in one vessel that did not require any intervention. You should hear from the TAVR team on ___. At discharge, you weighed 155 lbs (70.4 kg). It is very important that you weigh yourself every morning before getting dressed and after going to the bathroom.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ h/o CAD s/p multiple DES, HFrEF 45%, DMI, ESRD on PD p/w generalized weakness and symptomatic hypotension in the setting of midodrine non-compliance. ACTIVE ISSUES: #HYPOTENSION: pt normotensive while supine, but quickly hypotensive in context of standing upright consistent with orthostatic hypotension. Had previous admission where thought to be 2'/2 autonomic dysfunction and started on low dose midodrine. Pt did not fill prescription as out patient, and subsequently hypotension persisted. No evidence to suggest sepsis, new cardiac event, or adrenal insufficiency. Pt was below dry weight, so given IVF. Midodrine up-titrated to 10mg TID but the pt became htn while supine. He was seen by the autonomic neurologists who thought that superimposed on his autonomic dysfxn was hypovolemia 2'/2 diarrhea and dialysis. He was given 1L NS boluses and NaCl tablets to increase preload. His midodrine was gradually down titrated to 5 qAM, 5 @ 1400, and 0 qHS with stable BP in the 140s/90s supine and 95/55 standing and a subsequent decrease in symptomatic orthostatic hypotension. #ESRD on PD: Pt admitted with worsening metabolic disorder including hypocalcemia, hyperphosphatemia, and metabolic acidosis c/w worsening ESRD. Pt given increased calcium acetate, sodium bicarb, and aluminum hydroxide (for 3 d). His phosphate decreased, calcium increased and bicarb increased as well. He was discharged on additional HCO3, calcium acetate, and sevelemer. #C DIFF: pt was treated for severe c diff with vanco PO during last admission and discharged with prescription to continue treatment as out patient. Pt never filled prescription, but his diarrhea had resolved. Given ESRD, DMI, and sCHF, opted to restart treatment as in patient but this however caused an increase in his diarrhea up to 8 BM/d. Repeat C diff negative, so vanco PO stopped and BM returned to normal (3BM daily). CHRONIC ISSUES: #CAD: pt continued on home regimen #sCHF: pt continued on home regimen #DM1: pt continued on home regimen #LATENT TB: pt continued on home regimen #BPH: tamsulosin was held for hypotension. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: #HYPOTENSION: ___ symptoms and BP at PCP ___. Determine if still needs midodrine #ELECTROLYTES: ___ HCO3, Ca, Phos at PCP ___ #BPH: held tamsulosin for hypotension, restart when stable
summary: You were admitted to ___ for symptomatic low blood pressure (hypotension). You had no signs of infection, your cortisol was normal, and your EKG did not reveal any changes in your Heart. Your low blood pressure is likely related to nerve dysfunction from long standing diabetes. We increased your medicine called Midodrine to help you maintain a high blood pressure while standing. You should take 5 mg Midodrine each morning at 8am as well as 5 mg each afternoon at 2pm to help keep your blood pressure up. We also adjusted some of the medications to take to maintain normal electrolytes with your kidney disease. You should take 3 pills of calcium acetate daily as well as sevelamer three times per day W/MEALS and sodium bicarbonate three times per day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ gentleman with no prior cardiac history who presented with recent fatigue, dyspnea, lightheadedness and was found to have A. fib with slow ventricular response. Patient was transferred to ___ for EP consultation and management. ACUTE ISSUES ============================== # ATRIAL FIBRILLATION WITH SLOW VENTRICULAR RESPONSE: Presented with new atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response with HR in ___. TSH was normal and TTE did not show evidence of structural defects. EP was consulted for further work-up. He was started on apixaban 5 mg BID on ___. He had TEE/cardioversion on ___ and converted to normal sinus rhythm. After cardioversion, he had atrial ectopy with rates in the ___ indicating increased risk of A. Fib recurrence. For this, he was started on flecainide 75 mg PO BID and metoprolol succinate XL 12.5 mg PO daily. On the night of ___, his telemetry was concerning for conduction delay and Wenckebach. Telemetry was evaluated by EP and flecainide and metoprolol were discontinued to avoid exacerbation of conduction disease. He was monitored on telemetry after discontinuing flecainide. He was discharged with close EP follow-up for evaluation of PPM. He was discharged with ___ of Hearts monitor as well. # ACUTE DIASTOLIC HEART FAILURE EXACERBATION Patient had no history of heart failure and TTE showed preserved EF. He likely had an acute decompensation in the setting of his bradycardia. He was diuresed with Lasix 20 mg IV once on ___ at ___. His volume status was monitored throughout his admission and he did not require further diuresis. # HYPERTENSION Patient did not have history of HTN and previous SBP were in 120-130s as outpatient. He was started on captopril 6.25 mg TID which was transitioned to lisinopril 5 mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =============================== Discharge weight: 78.1 kg [] Lung nodules visualized on chest X-ray at ___ ___. Recommend CT chest as outpatient for further evaluation. [] Discharged with ___ event monitor. Patient had ___ on telemetry and will follow up with EP within one week for PPM evaluation. [] Please repeat Cr at next follow-up visit. Patient discharged on Apixaban 5 mg PO BID. If he has elevation of Cr > 1.5, would consider decreasing Apixaban to 2.5 mg PO BID.
summary: You were feeling fatigued and short of breath and went to ___. Your heart rhythm was found to be in atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response. You were transferred to ___ for further management of your heart arrhythmia. You were started on a blood thinner called Apixaban to decrease your risk of having a stroke. You should continue this medication indefinitely. You had a procedure called cardioversion which gives your heart an electrical impulse to reset the rhythm. Your heart rhythm changed from atrial fibrillation back to normal sinus rhythm. You had abnormal electrical beats after your cardioversion. This increases your risk of your rhythm going back to atrial fibrillation. You were given ___ of Hearts monitor to evaluate your heart rhythm outside of the hospital. Your telemetry showed evidence of electrical conduction problems. There was concern that the flecainide and metoprolol were worsening this problem so these medications were stopped. Your heart rate was monitored on telemetry until you were discharged from the hospital.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ gentleman with no prior cardiac history who presented with recent fatigue, dyspnea, lightheadedness and was found to have A. fib with slow ventricular response. Patient was transferred to ___ for EP consultation and management. ACUTE ISSUES ============================== # ATRIAL FIBRILLATION WITH SLOW VENTRICULAR RESPONSE: Presented with new atrial fibrillation with slow ventricular response with HR in ___. TSH was normal and TTE did not show evidence of structural defects. EP was consulted for further work-up. He was started on apixaban 5 mg BID on ___. He had TEE/cardioversion on ___ and converted to normal sinus rhythm. After cardioversion, he had atrial ectopy with rates in the ___ indicating increased risk of A. Fib recurrence. For this, he was started on flecainide 75 mg PO BID and metoprolol succinate XL 12.5 mg PO daily. On the night of ___, his telemetry was concerning for conduction delay and Wenckebach. Telemetry was evaluated by EP and flecainide and metoprolol were discontinued to avoid exacerbation of conduction disease. He was monitored on telemetry after discontinuing flecainide. He was discharged with close EP follow-up for evaluation of PPM. He was discharged with ___ of Hearts monitor as well. # ACUTE DIASTOLIC HEART FAILURE EXACERBATION Patient had no history of heart failure and TTE showed preserved EF. He likely had an acute decompensation in the setting of his bradycardia. He was diuresed with Lasix 20 mg IV once on ___ at ___. His volume status was monitored throughout his admission and he did not require further diuresis. # HYPERTENSION Patient did not have history of HTN and previous SBP were in 120-130s as outpatient. He was started on captopril 6.25 mg TID which was transitioned to lisinopril 5 mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =============================== Discharge weight: 78.1 kg [] Lung nodules visualized on chest X-ray at ___ ___. Recommend CT chest as outpatient for further evaluation. [] Discharged with ___ event monitor. Patient had ___ on telemetry and will follow up with EP within one week for PPM evaluation. [] Please repeat Cr at next follow-up visit. Patient discharged on Apixaban 5 mg PO BID. If he has elevation of Cr > 1.5, would consider decreasing Apixaban to 2.5 mg PO BID.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ yo male with a h/o CAD s/p multiple stents who presented with ___ sub-sternal chest pain radiating to his L jaw shortly after tracheal stent placement. #. Chest Pain: Patient has a history of CAD and presented to the ED on ___ with ___ substernal chest pain, radiating to his jaw. Troponins were <0.01 x7, his ECG did not show any signs of ischemia, he did not respond clinically to NTG, and his Stress-MIBI showed a large, fixed defect at the distal anterior and apical regions. Patient continued to have ___ chest pain during his hospital stay, so he was treated with 600 mg Ibuprofen TID for suspected pericarditis. Patient's reported chest pain with infusion of dipyradimole during his stress test, so he underwent cardiac catheterization on ___, which showed a 95% occlusion in the distal LAD. Patient had a Xience stent placed and subsequently reported that his chest pain resolved. Patient was continued on his home doses of Metoprolol, Plavix, Atorvastatin, and NTG, and the treatment for suspected pericarditis was discontinued after his pain resolved s/p cardiac catheterization. Patient's aspirin was increased to 325 mg daily and his Imdur was increased to 60 mg daily during this hospital stay. # Jaw Pain: Throughout this hospital stay, patient reported that he had ___ jaw pain in the proximal portion of his trachea. He described this as a dull pain which radiated to his ears bilaterally. Patient was experiencing an associated cough, which was productive for occasional blood clots. Patient had a rigid bronchoscopy performed on ___ and a CXR which demonstrated no acute process and minimal secretions. The jaw pain and productive cough were thought to be secondary to his recent tracheal stent placement, and he was followed closely by Interventional Pulmonology during this hospital stay. He was started on a Z-Pack for this productive cough, per Interventional Pulmonology recommendations, at discharge, and he has a follow-up appointment with IP in four weeks. # Congestive Heart Failure: Patient has a h/o systolic CHF secondary to ischemic disease. Patient had a TTE on ___, which showed LV enlargement with regional systolic dysfunction. The space anterior to the apex showed a mass, which was most likely a fat pad, but could also represent a loculated anterior pericardial effusion. Patient was started on his home dose of Lasix during this admission, but his Cr increased from 1.0 to 1.3 with this addition. He did not appear to be fluid-overloaded on physical exam, and thus his home Lasix was discontinued. He was continued on his home doses of Digoxin, and Metoprolol. The patient's Lasix was held at discharge, and he was instructed to call his PCP, ___ he experienced leg swelling, shortness of breath, orthopnea, or coughing at night. # Fever: Patient developed low-grade fevers on ___ and ___. Blood cultures and a urine culture were performed on ___, which did not show any signs of infection. The patient had a productive cough, though a CXR performed on ___ did not show any signs of infection. Patient was placed on a Z-pack on the day of discharge, per IP recommendations. The patient was afebrile 48 hours prior to discharge. #. Transaminitis: On admission, patient had an INR of 1.3. LFTs were checked during this admission, which showed ALT 54, AST 61, LDH 285. Patient had no focal findings on PE. Hepatitis serologies were sent, which were all negative. Patient was believed to have hepatic congestion for CHF. #. DM: Patient has a h/o diabetes and currently takes Januvia and Actos at home. Patient was transitioned to an insulin sliding scale while in the hospital. Blood glucose levels were consistently in the low 200s, so Lantus was increased on ___ to 24 Units daily. Patient did not have any acute events in regards to his diabetes during this hospital stay. #. Code: Full
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you experienced extreme chest pain, radiating to your jaw, after Interventional Pulmonology placed a stent in your trachea last ___. We monitored the function of your heart, and it did not appear that you had a heart attack. However, you continued to have chest pain over the weekend, and we decided to examine the blood vessels around your heart on ___. We found that you had a blockage in one of your previous stents. We fixed this blockage, and your chest pain resolved. While you were here, you also had jaw pain, which radiated to your ears. We looked at the stent in your trachea, and it appears that the stent is in the correct location. The Interventional Pulmonologists would like to keep the stent in for now and reevaluate you in four weeks.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ diastolic HF, mild BiV hypertrophy, HTN, mild AR/MR, RLE DVT presenting with worsening lower extremity edema(R>L). #LOWER EXTREMITY EDEMA Mr. ___ lower extremity edema is multifactorial including diastolic heart failure, deep venous thrombosis and untreated obstructive sleep apnea. The lower extremity edema was previously responsive to PO furosemide but during the 3 weeks prior to admission was refractory to outpatient management. He was admitted for further workup of lower extremity edema, including a right heart catheterization. Liver function tests returned within normal limits. Albumin measured at 4.5. No evidence of pathologic proteinuria. Right heart catheterization revealed mild pulmonary hypertension and elevated R/L filling pressures. He was diuresed with furosemide 40mg IV x 2 and started on torsemide 40mg PO daily. He was advised to seek treatment for his obstructive sleep apnea. Anticoagulatin with Pradaxa was continued. Amlodipine was discontinued as it may contribute to lower extremity edema. # DIASTOLIC HEART FAILURE: Mr. ___ echocardiogram demonstrated biventricular hypertrophy, diastolic heart failure, mild pulmonary hypertension and mild MR/TR. On admission the patient complained of worsening lower extremity edema but denied SOB worse than baseline, PND and orthopnea. Right heart cardiac catheterization revealed elevated R/L filling pressures. A right heart biopsy was not performed. He was given furosemide 40mg IV x 2 and started on torsemide 40mg PO daily. Blood pressure control was changed from ___ to losartan/carvedilol. Iron studies not concerning for hemochromatosis. SPEP pending at discharge. # HYPERTENSION: Mr. ___ regimen was changed from ___ (amlodipine-olmesartan) ___ mg qday and atenolol 50 mg PO BID to Carvedilol 12.5 mg PO BID and losartan 100 mg PO DAILY. Amlodipine was eliminated as it may contribute to lower extremity edema. Blood pressure at discharge 140s/80s. # RHYTHM: Mr. ___ was in normal sinus rhythm throughout his hospitalization. He has no known history of an arrhythmia. # CORONARIES: Mr. ___ has no known history of coronary artery disease. # OBSTRUCTIVE SLEEP APNEA: The patient has known obstructive sleep apnea which is not currently treated as the patient is unable to tolerate CPAP devices. OSA is likely contributing to his lower extremity edema, pulmonary hypertension and systemic hypertension. At night the patient was witnessed to have desatruations to the mid ___ a/w bradycardia. The patient would surely benefit from CPAP at night. # RESTLESS LEG SYNDROME: Stable. Mirapax and carbidopa/levadopa continued. # CHRONIC BACK PAIN: -Fentanyl patch continued. -Pregabalin continued. # MICROSCOPIC HEMATURIA: Incidentally discoverage on UA. Pt asymptomatic. Consider cytology or urology referal as outpatient. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ******************* -follow up SPEP -UA with microscopic hematuria, please consider urine cytology as outpatient
summary: You were admitted for further investigation of your lower extremity edema. As part of the workup a cardiac catheterization was performed on ___. You were diagnosed with diastolic heart failure, which causes fluid to back up in your body and causes leg swelling. This was treated with additional intravenous lasix to remove some of the fluid. Your kidney and liver functions were both normal. At night you were observed to have significant sleep apnea which under ideal circumstance could be treated with CPAP. Please discuss with your sleep specialist again if you have any additional options. You have a small amount of blood in your urine.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year old male with PMH significant AAA s/p recent enodvascular repair, CAD s/p CABG, who presents with increasing shortness of breath and new onset lower extremity edema, found to be in new acute likely systolic heart failure . #) Volume Overload/Acute Systolic Heart Failure: CXR on admission consistent with mild CHF, patient with no prior history of CHF, no echo in our system. The cause of his new onset of CHF may be related to excess fluid in the post-operative setting, underlying infection with possible PNA, or he could have flashed in the setting of being hypertensive, however the symptoms seem to be more chronic over the past two weeks. He was successfully diuresed with IV lasix, his echo showed a depressed EF of 40-45% which was global in nature, suggesting a non-ischemic etiology. His atenolol was discontinued and carvedilol was started at 6.25mg BID, and his home lisinopril dose was down titrated to 20mg daily. He was also started on lasix 10mg daily. . #) Endovascular Abdominal Aortic Aneurysm (AAA): Patient was recently discharged after his endovascular repair, his pedal pulses remained equal, he was continued on his home ASA and statin. Atenolol changed to carvedilol as above. . #) CAD: patient was ruled out for an MI with negative cardiac enzymes, given the new onset of CHF, there was initial concern that it could be related to ischemia. However, given the global nature of LV dysfunction, it was felt to be more likely non-ischemic in nature. He was continued on his home statin and ASA, started on carvedilol as above. . #) Bibasilar opacities: CXR with bibasilar opacities L>R. Ddx included atelectasis vs infection, however given his symptoms of productive cough, it was decided to treat him for community acquired pneumonia with 5 days of 750mg of levofloxacin. . #) Fall: patient initially presented from home after falling out of bed in the setting of taking 20mg of ambien. He denied loss of consciousness, by history appeared to be a mechanical fall. A CT of his head was negative at the time of admission and plain films of his left hip were negative for fracture, since he had a large ecchymosis over the area. The fall was likely related to ambien taken at home . #) DM: held home metformin and started ISS while in the hospital, restarted metformin at the time of discharge. . #) H/O TIA in ___: continued home ASA and statin . #) HTN: changed atenolol to carvedilol given the cardiomyopathy and decreased home lisinopril to 20mg daily . #) Hyperlipidemia: continued home statin . #) Chronic Low Back Pain: held home percocet in the setting of a recent fall related to ambien use, used tylenol as needed for pain control.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with weakness and shortness of breath, we found that you had too much fluid in your lungs and in your legs. The extra fluid made it difficult your heart to pump as effectively as it had previously. We helped remove some of the fluid with a diuretic medicine called lasix. After removing some of the extra fluid your breathing improved and you felt better. Your blood pressure was also not as well controlled, so we made some changes to your medications. Then we had physical therapy work with you and they felt that you were okay to go home, but had recommended you go home with physical therapy. However, you declined home physical therapy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with history of DVT/PE, CHF, AF and bradycardia s/p St. ___, now admitted for CHF exacerbation. #CHF exacerbation: She presents with clinical CHF exacerbation. Per conversation with Dr. ___ has had recent ___ lb weight gain (though baseline weight is unclear). Apparently she is intermittently compliant with medications. BNP 3208 on admission. Her admission exam was concerning for severe TR secondary to pacemaker placement given characteristic murmur, elevated JVP and pulsatile liver. She had echocardiogram which confirmed severe tricuspid regurgitation, moderate mitral regurgitation, mild aortic stenosis and regurgitation. Preserved LVEF >55%. ___ cardiology records were requested. She was diuresed with IV furosemide with good effect, ultimately transitioned to torsemide and discharged on torsemide 40mg BID. #History of DVT/PE: Per patient, diagnosed ___ years ago. Uncertain if this was provoked. INR 3.7 on admission. She had supratherapeutic INR on admission which subsequently became subtherapeutic. Of note patient endorsed different diet during early admission including more salads. Apixaban was discussed with patient but declined due to concern of irreversible bleeding risk and high cost. She was briefly bridged with heparin gtt until INR became therapeutic again, then discharged on warfarin. #Acute on chronic kidney disease: Etiology of CKD unknown, though possibly from hypertension. Now with ___ likely in setting of CHF. Subsequently improving after diuresis. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - She will need continued INR monitoring and titration of warfarin dose after discharge. She initially requested home INR monitoring, but ultimately opted to follow up with her PCP's office for INR monitoring. She has recently established primary care with Dr. ___ (___). The inpatient team spoke with Dr. ___ office and confirmed that INR monitoring could be performed through the PCP ___ (contact person: ___, RN). The patient will travel to the PCP's office for INR draws, the first of which is scheduled for the day after admission (___) - Electrolyte panel (chem 10) and INR should be checked in one week. These have been ordered through ___ so that they can be performed at the time of her PCP visit on ___ and will be available by the time she has cardiology follow-up with ___ ___ NP on ___. - Discharge weight = 53.9kg - ___ services were recommended at discharge, declined by patient
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to heart failure exacerbation (too much fluid in the body). This was treated with medications to remove excess fluid. You had an echocardiogram which showed dysfunction of multiple heart valves. You will continue to take a water pill (called torsemide) twice a day in order to prevent the fluid in the body from re-accumulating. The dose of torsemide which you will take on leaving the hospital is 40mg twice a day. You will also continue to take coumadin (also called warfarin) as a blood thinner. Since the coumadin level in the blood can vary quite a bit, you will need frequent blood draws to make sure you are taking the right dose of coumadin. The first blood draw will be tomorrow.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, HPL, CABG x4 in ___ p/w NSTEMI s/p cath with DES to LAD c/b agitation, delerium now calm and cooperative. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # NSTEMI: Patient initially presented to OSH with chest pain which started on ___ while the patient was watching TV at 5:45am. The pain was ___ and relieved with nitro. At OSH ED, ___ was treated with a heparin drip and plavix x3 doses, no load and 325mg ASA. EKG showed evidence of non-specific ST/T changes and troponins peaked at 3.23. Cath on ___ showed 3 vessel disease but occluded LAD now s/p a DES to the LAD. Patient's medication regimen was optimized so ___ was continued on ASA 81, Plavix 75 mg daily, rosuvastatin 40 mg daily, and started on metoprolol succinate 150 mg XL, and Lisinopril 40 mg daily. A TTE was done which showed "moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction, c/w CAD. Mild pulmonary hypertension. LVEF approxiately 35%." Post-cath groin check was stable with no hematoma, no bruit and good pulses. ___ did not experience any chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, jaw pain, arm numbness/tingling during the rest of his hospitalization. # Atrial fibrillation with RVR: Mr. ___ developed atrial fibrillation with RVR as noted on telemetry during his hospitalization. ___ has not had any previous history of atrial fibrillation. Initially ___ developed atrial fibrillation (on ___ with RVR into the 150s that resolved spontaneously without any intervention. This was followed by atrial fibrillation with RVR on the following day. During these episodes ___ was asymptomatic and hemodynamically stable. Given the paroxysmal atrial fibrillation noted throughout hospitalization, Mr. ___ was rate controlled with metoprolol. Additionally, to control his rhythm for this new onset atrial fibrillation, ___ was started on amiodarone 400 mg TID. After one dose of the amiodarone, ___ converted to sinus rhythm. At the time of discharge, ___ was continued on the metoprolol and amiodarone and was in sinus rhythm. Amiodarone regimen will include 400 mg PO TID (to end ___ followed by 200 mg PO once per day. Given his atrial fibrillation and decreased left ventricular ejection fraction, anticoagulation was necessary. ___ was started on rivaroxaban 15 mg PO daily. # Agitation/delerium: Patient reportedly was agitated and delerious after cath on ___. This was likely in the setting of receiving multiple medications during the cath including 5mg diazepam, 2 versed, and 100 fentanyl. ___ was reportedly uncooperative, aggressive, and did not recognize his wife but knew the date. During that time, ___ remained hemodynamically stable. On arrival to unit ___ was calm and cooperative. ___ was alert and oriented to person and time. ___ was placed on delerium precautions and left the unit alert and oriented x 3. ___ did not experience any delirium episodes during the rest of the hospitalization. # Nausea: Patient complained of nausea when arriving to CCU. ___ was written for Zofran but patient did not take it given that his symptoms subsided. The nausea was likely ___ medication side effects post-cath. ___ did not require any Zofran during the rest of the hospitalization. CHRONIC ISSUES: # HTN: Patient on Metoprolol 50 daily and Lisinopril 20 BID at home. ___ was hypertensive in the setting of missing anti-hypertensive doses during the cath. ___ was continued on Metoprolol (increased to 150 mg XL) and Lisinopril 40 mg PO daily. # HLD: Patient on Rosuvastatin 40 mg at home. ___ was discharged on Rosuvastatin 40 mg daily. # GERD: Patient on Omeprazole 20 mg at home. No hearburn complaints during hospitalization. Omeprazole was held. ___ was discharged on his Omperazole 20 mg dailyo. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== # Amiodarone Monitoring: Given the addition of amiodarone to his medication regimen, LFTs and TSH will need to be followed as an outpatient.
summary: You were admitted with a heart attack and needed a drug eluting stent in a coronary artery to open the vessel. It is extremely important that ___ take aspirin and clopidogrel (plavix) every day without fail to prevent the stent from clotting off and causing another heart attack. Do not stop taking aspirin and clopidogrel for any reason for the next year unless Dr. ___ it is OK to do so. Your heart is weaker after the heart attack and ___ may develop fluid build up or heart failure. We expect that your heart will get stronger over the next ___ months. Please weigh yourself daily in the am before breakfast. Call Dr. ___ weight increases more than 3 pounds in 1 day or 5 pounds in 3 days. This may indicate ___ have fluid retention. ___ also need to avoid salt in your diet as this leads to fluid retention. The weak heart and the new rhythm, atrial fibrillation, makes it more likely that ___ will develop blood clots in your heart. ___ have been started on a new medicine, rivaroxaban (xarelto) to prevent these blood clots.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with HTN, HPL, CABG x4 in ___ p/w NSTEMI s/p cath with DES to LAD c/b agitation, delerium now calm and cooperative. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # NSTEMI: Patient initially presented to OSH with chest pain which started on ___ while the patient was watching TV at 5:45am. The pain was ___ and relieved with nitro. At OSH ED, ___ was treated with a heparin drip and plavix x3 doses, no load and 325mg ASA. EKG showed evidence of non-specific ST/T changes and troponins peaked at 3.23. Cath on ___ showed 3 vessel disease but occluded LAD now s/p a DES to the LAD. Patient's medication regimen was optimized so ___ was continued on ASA 81, Plavix 75 mg daily, rosuvastatin 40 mg daily, and started on metoprolol succinate 150 mg XL, and Lisinopril 40 mg daily. A TTE was done which showed "moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction, c/w CAD. Mild pulmonary hypertension. LVEF approxiately 35%." Post-cath groin check was stable with no hematoma, no bruit and good pulses. ___ did not experience any chest pain, shortness of breath, nausea, jaw pain, arm numbness/tingling during the rest of his hospitalization. # Atrial fibrillation with RVR: Mr. ___ developed atrial fibrillation with RVR as noted on telemetry during his hospitalization. ___ has not had any previous history of atrial fibrillation. Initially ___ developed atrial fibrillation (on ___ with RVR into the 150s that resolved spontaneously without any intervention. This was followed by atrial fibrillation with RVR on the following day. During these episodes ___ was asymptomatic and hemodynamically stable. Given the paroxysmal atrial fibrillation noted throughout hospitalization, Mr. ___ was rate controlled with metoprolol. Additionally, to control his rhythm for this new onset atrial fibrillation, ___ was started on amiodarone 400 mg TID. After one dose of the amiodarone, ___ converted to sinus rhythm. At the time of discharge, ___ was continued on the metoprolol and amiodarone and was in sinus rhythm. Amiodarone regimen will include 400 mg PO TID (to end ___ followed by 200 mg PO once per day. Given his atrial fibrillation and decreased left ventricular ejection fraction, anticoagulation was necessary. ___ was started on rivaroxaban 15 mg PO daily. # Agitation/delerium: Patient reportedly was agitated and delerious after cath on ___. This was likely in the setting of receiving multiple medications during the cath including 5mg diazepam, 2 versed, and 100 fentanyl. ___ was reportedly uncooperative, aggressive, and did not recognize his wife but knew the date. During that time, ___ remained hemodynamically stable. On arrival to unit ___ was calm and cooperative. ___ was alert and oriented to person and time. ___ was placed on delerium precautions and left the unit alert and oriented x 3. ___ did not experience any delirium episodes during the rest of the hospitalization. # Nausea: Patient complained of nausea when arriving to CCU. ___ was written for Zofran but patient did not take it given that his symptoms subsided. The nausea was likely ___ medication side effects post-cath. ___ did not require any Zofran during the rest of the hospitalization. CHRONIC ISSUES: # HTN: Patient on Metoprolol 50 daily and Lisinopril 20 BID at home. ___ was hypertensive in the setting of missing anti-hypertensive doses during the cath. ___ was continued on Metoprolol (increased to 150 mg XL) and Lisinopril 40 mg PO daily. # HLD: Patient on Rosuvastatin 40 mg at home. ___ was discharged on Rosuvastatin 40 mg daily. # GERD: Patient on Omeprazole 20 mg at home. No hearburn complaints during hospitalization. Omeprazole was held. ___ was discharged on his Omperazole 20 mg dailyo. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== # Amiodarone Monitoring: Given the addition of amiodarone to his medication regimen, LFTs and TSH will need to be followed as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with a history of eating disorder NOS, ESRD on dialysis and sCHF who presents with worsening orthopnea and dyspnea on exertion. ACTIVE ISSUES; # Dyspnea/orthopnea: Clinically, patient was euvolemic throughout her admission. Her CXR showed small b/l pleural effusions and no evidence of pulmonary edema. She had a normal ambulatory sat (97% on ___ while climbing and descneding stairs. Her chronic pleural effusion without pulmonary edema are likely secondary to CHF. We started lisinopril and continued beta-blockade to optimize her chronic CHF treatment. She had ~1L of fluid removed during HD, with some symptomatic improvement. With regards to her pleural effusions, she was evaluated by interventional pulmonology, who stated that there was no indication for urgent thoracentesis and she was scheduled for an outpatient appointment with interventional pulm for possible therapeutic thorascentesis. # Chronic systolic CHF: Patient has chronic systolic congestive heart failure, thought to be primarily caused by carnitine deficiency in the setting of her eating disorder. She also has severe mitral reurgitation. Repeat ECHO showed stable, severe mitral regurgitation, with no acute changes. Throughout her admission, patient did not demonstrate any clinical features of an acute exacerbation of her heart failure. She was evaluated by cardiology, who agreed that her CHF was at baseline, and that her MR, while severe, could be evaluated in the outpatient setting. She was started on lisinopril 5mg daily. Her metoprolol was continued, and her ASA was switched from 325 to 81mg daily. # Hyponatremia: Most likely secondary to poor po intake. CHF unlikely as pt has no evidence of exacerbation and there is no evidence of effective circulating volume depletion/decreased end-organ perfusion. Previously hypoNa noted and corresponding TSH was wnl. As patient appears euvolemic, the DDx includes primary polydypsia vs. poor solute intake vs. siADH. SiADH is unlikely given her relatively dilute urine. Free water restriction and normal saline administration resulted in resolution of patient's hyponatremia, suggesting primary polydypsia and poor solute intake as primary etiologies behind patient's chronic hyponatremia. Her sodium went from 123 to 132 in the course of approximately 16 hours. Patient was instructed to limit her free water intake to less than 1.5L per day to prevent hyponatremia at home.- CHRONIC ISSUES: # ESRD on hemodialysis: Continued on MWF schedule. Currently dialyzed through a tunnelled catheter. Has left arm fistula, but requires surgical intervention in ___ for dilation. Continued nephrocaps, sevelamer, Vit. D. # Chronic "stomach" problems: Currently without abdominal pain, nausea or vomiting. Appetite intact. In the past, this has been labeled as either an eating disorder NOS or somatoform disorder. Currently, patient's appetite appears intact; however, she should have close followup with her PCP with regards to this issue. # GERD: Continued pantoprazole =============================================================== #Hyponatremia: Patient should have her electrolytes monitored in outpatient setting as she likely has primary polydypsia as well as poor solute intake, which could lead to dangerously low sodium levels. #Hematuria: needs follow up to make sure this resolves
summary: You were admitted for worsening shortness of breath. This is likely due to the fluid collections around your lungs, as well as heart failure. You had fluid removed during dialysis and your symptoms improved. You were evaluated by interventional pulmonology, who determined that the water around your lungs did not need to be drained now, it may need follow-up as an outpatient in about 1 week. You were also evaluated by cardiology who believe that your heart failure is not acutely worse and that you should have furthur evaluation by an outpatient cardiologist with regards to your mitral valve dysfunction. You were also found to have low sodium levels. This is likely due to poor dietary intake and too much water intake. You should limit your water intake to less than 1.5 liters per day and to increase your salt intake.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Outpatient Providers: ___ female with a past medical history of T2DM, hyperlipidemia, hypertension, HFpEF, moderate mitral stenosis, and dementia who presented for evaluation of dyspnea. =============== ACTIVE ISSUES: =============== #Dyspnea: #Acute on chronic HFpEF: Patient presented with dyspnea, CXR with volume overload, and elevated BNP higher than prior. Trigger could be undertreated hypertension or inadequate home diuretic. Responded well to diuresis for total fluid balance -5945 cc, though had minor ___ (below). TTE with improved MS though functional MR and severe pulmonary HTN. Nuclear stress was obtained to evaluate for ischemia, which showed partially reversible deficit in LAD distribution. Cardiac catheterization showed 40% LAD stenosis; no intervention performed. Was symptomatically improved and tolerating exercise well on discharge. Hydralazine was added for escalation of anti-hypertensive regimen, and torsemide was increased to 30 mg in the morning and 20 mg in the evening. ___ on CKD: Cr uptrended to 1.9 when torsemide 30 mg BID trialed. Patient received 1L fluids after cath and Cr improved to 1.8. #Mitral stenosis: Moderate on last TTE in ___ mild-moderate on TTE during admission. HRs were appropriately controlled on home labetalol. ================ CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ #DM2: -Continued glargine, ISS. #HTN: -Continued home labetalol, amlodipine, losartan. Added hydralazine 50 mg TID. #Normocytic anemia: likely multifactorial ___ CKD, DM Transitional Issues [] Please obtain BUN, Cr, K at outpatient follow-up on ___. [] Discharge weight = 81.9 kg (180.56 lb), discharge Cr = 1.8. [] Severe pulmonary hypertension noted TTE ___, worsened from prior. Seemed out of proportion to left heart failure. Consider outpatient sleep study if not already obtained, and treatment for OSA. [] Consider workup of secondary hypertension if not already done, given requirement of 4 anti-hypertensive agents.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having shortness of breath with activity. You were found to have too much fluid in your body and were admitted for monitoring while we removed the extra fluid. You were given medications to make you pee more to remove fluid from your body. We increased your daily torsemide dose to 30 mg in the morning and 20 mg in the evening. We started a new medication for your blood pressure. You had a stress test of your heart which showed that a part of your heart was not receiving enough blood flow when you exercise. Because of this, you had a procedure called a cardiac catheterization, which did not find any severe blockages in your heart.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with esophageal cancer s/p gastroesophagectomy chemo and XRT, recently identified asymptomatic AF/AFL (___) and newly depressed LVEF 35-40% (___), NYHA fc I, on Xarelto. He is admitted for dofetilide load for rhythm management of tachycardia-mediated cardiomyopathy. # Atrial fibrillation: s/p DCCV this AM for recurrent AF and has not maintained SR. Currently, he is in AF with brief periods of SR. - discontinue dofetilide 500mcg bid - continue uninterrupted anticoagulation with Xarelto - initiate amiodarone 200 mg BID on ___ then reduce to 200 mg daily -LFTs (normal) and TSH (pending) checked today, PFTs to be done as outpatient - ___ in 1 month at ___ - close outpt f/u with Dr. ___ in ___ to discuss further medical therapy/PVI # Esophageal cancer s/p minimally invasive gastroesophagectomy, chemo, and XRT: followed by Dr. ___ at ___, last seen on ___. # GERD: - continue home omeprazole #Dispo: - to home with wife ___ on ___: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Diltiazem Extended-Release 240 mg PO DAILY 2. Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg PO DAILY 3. Losartan Potassium 100 mg PO DAILY 4. Multivitamins 1 TAB PO DAILY 5. Omeprazole 40 mg PO DAILY 6. Vitamin B Complex 1 CAP PO DAILY 7. Fish Oil (Omega 3) 1000 mg PO DAILY 8. Rivaroxaban 20 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to be started on a medication called dofetilide to help regulate your heart rhythm. Serial EKGs were performed to ensure that your heart rhythm was not affected. Your electrolytes were monitored and optimized as needed. Unfortunately the dofetilide did not keep you in a normal rhythm and you underwent cardioversion which also unfortunately did not keep you in a normal rhythm. Dofetilide has now been stopped and Dr. ___ like to start you on amiodarone *ON ___ (allows time for dofetilite to wash out of your system). This will be 200 mg twice a day for 1 month and then decrease down to 200 mg once a day. Dr. ___ will make arrangements with Dr. ___ cardioversion in ___ in 1 month. Please continue taking your Diltiazem, losartan, and Xarelto. Amiodarone was escripted to your Pharmacy and may be picked up on the way home from the hospital. Start *ON ___. You were given an up to date list of medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with past medical history significant for chronic diastolic heart failure, atrial fibrillation on warfarin, pulmonary hypertension, chronic kidney disease & sick sinus syndrome/CHB recently admitted to ___ ___ to ___ for congestive heart failure and generalized arthralgias which was attributed to methimazole is readmitted ___ for Urinary tract infection and found to have worsening congestive heart failure. . ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # Acute on Chronic congestive heart failure with systolic and diastolic dysfunction: On admission, he complained of worsening abdominal distention and weight gain from 131 to 142lb. TTE in this admission shows worsened EF of 35% from 55% in ___ ___s septal dyssynchrony and worsening MR/TR. He as taken for right heart catheterization which showed moderate pulmonary hypertension and normal RV filling pressure, elevated RV systolic pressure. Right heart failure is attributed to left heart failure. Abdominal distention is related to right heart failure with ascites. He was treated with a furosemide drip augmented by metolazone with goal negative ___. At discharge, furosemide was discontinued and Torsemide 100mg Daily started. At discharge, his weight was 58.7kg, down from 63.5kg. He was discharged with a plan for pacemaker upgrade int ___ weeks and will continue warfarin until then. . # Atrial fibrillation: warfarin was held for right heart cath and resumed at the At the time of discharge, warfarin was held in anticipation of BiVentricular pacer upgrade and restarted on the day of discharge. He will have ___ check his INR. . # Urinary tract infection: Patient complained of dysuria and had recently been hospitalized with a foley catheter in place. Urine culture grew pan sensitive e. coli and he was treated with cefpodoxime x 10 days. . # Hematuria: related to foley and warfarin, resolved by HD 2 with no significant blood loss. . INACTIVE ISSUES ============== # CORONARIES: Cath in ___ shows two vessel disease. He did not complain of chest pain, EKG did not show ischemic change. Continued medical management. - Continue Aspirin 81 mg PO/NG DAILY - Continue Metoprolol Tartrate 25 mg PO/NG BID - Continue Simvastatin 20 mg PO/NG DAILY . # RHYTHM: Ventricular pacing, with underlying atrial fibrillation therapeutic on warfarin, rate controled with metoprolol. . # Right foot abrasion: patient with two abrasions on right foot, no sign of infection. lateral lesion closed by the time of discharge, medial abrasion remained dressed but open. - Wound care consult . # Left calf muscle cramp: patient complained intermittantly of left calf ___ horse", exam did not show asymetric swelling, an area of induration over lateral left calf consistent with contracted muscle was paplated. Low suspicion for DVT as patient was therapeutic on warfarin on admission. . # Myalgias: patient with ___ shoulder pain and arm pain. previously attributed to reaction to methimazole. - Continue TraMADOL (Ultram) 25 mg PO Q6H:PRN - Continue Acetaminophen 650 mg PO/NG TID . # Anemia: Microcytic, last colonoscopy ___ shows normal colon to cecum. Previously attributed to CKD, previously on darvopoietin. - Continue Ferrous Sulfate 325 mg PO/NG DAILY - Continue Cyanocobalamin 1000 mcg PO/NG DAILY - Continue FoLIC Acid 1 mg PO/NG DAILY . # Hyperlipidemia Continue simvastatin as above . TRANSLATION OF CARE =================== Reasons for readmission - Poor monitoring of weight and salt intake leading to CHF exacerbation, he has ___ and daily weights but has accumlated >10lbs without intervention in the past. . # FULL CODE . # Contact: Daughter ___
summary: As you know, you were admitted because you were having blood in your urine which happened because you are on coumadin and had a foley catheter in your last hospital stay. You did not loose a significant amount of blood and bleeding resolved. We noted that you have a urinary tract infection which we are treating with antibiotics. You will need to continue taking antibiotics for 2 more days after leaving the hospital. We noted that you had a large amount of fluid in your stomach and treated you with medication (furosemide and metolazone) to decrease this fluid. We examined your heart using an ultrasound and noted that your heart does not squeeze as hard as it did a few months ago which is related to heart failure. We performed a cardiac catheterization to evaluate your heart failure and concluded that you would best benefit from changing your pacemaker.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old woman with a past medical history of diastolic congestive heart failure and hypertension admitted with dark tarry stools, urinary frequency and cough. . # GI BLEED: The patient presented with complaints of dark tarry stools and was found to be guiac positive. She underwent EGD that demonstrated gastritis. She was started on pantoprazole 40mg IV Q12 which was changed to PO at time of discharge. We suggested taking 40mg PO BID x 14 days and then reducing frequency to 40mg daily thereafter. Hct stable during hospitalization. Gastroenterology consult team suggested outpatient capsule study. Patient scheduled to follow up with her ___ GI physician ___. Biopsies from EGD pending at time of DC. Asked that patient STOP ASA and refrain from taking any NSAIDs. # URINARY TRACK INFECTION: The patient was found to have an uncomplicated E. Coli UTI. She had no fever, leukocytosis or additional systemic complaints. She was started levofloxacin which was changed to cefpodoxime (given better sensitivities) for ___. # HTN: The patient was continued on her home regimen of atenolol and lisinopril
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after presenting with dark stool and discomfort with urinating. You were found to have a urinary tract infection and have been started on an antibiotic called cefpodoxime for a 7 day course (___). You were evaluated by the Gastroenterologist for your history of dark stools. You underwent an upper endoscopy to evaluate for source of bleeding which showed inflammation in your stomach. Biopsies were taken during this procedure and results are pending at this time. It is also recommended that your gastroenterologist schedule a capsule study that will be able to evaluate for potential bleeding sources in your small bowel. This study can take place as an outpatient. Please avoid taking aspirin and non-steroidal anti-inflammatories (i.e. advil or aleve) for the time being while we work-up this gastrointestinal bleeding. Speak with your primary care physician before restarting this medication.
