text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Acute on Chronic Diastolic CHF, Hypoxemia - Markedly high BNP makes CHF exacerbation a virtual certainty, however the etiology is a broader list. A pulmonary embolism would certainly be a trigger for tachycardia and hypoxemia leading to acute diastolic failure. Given this cancer is highly thrombogenic, despite renal functino we pursued rule out of pulmonary embolism, via ___ which was negative and CTA which did not note a large PE, but did note a large pleural effusion. - Rate control as tolerated with metoprolol - Oxygen to keep SAO2 > 93% - diuresis with IV lasix - Would guess that ST-Depressions are likely rate related demand ischemia 2. Acute Renal Failure, Hyperkalemia - This is most likely due to CHF causing poor renal perfusion - Renal dose medication - Avoid nephrotoxins - Hold ___ - Send urine for sediment, renal lytes - Keep euvolemic 3. Lung Cancer - This makes her high risk for DVT/PE - ___ oncologist was consulted, and will work with family about goals of care as an outpatient. FULL CODE
summary: You were admitted for a sudden worsening of your shortness of breath that you've had for the past two months. We think you are having trouble breathing due to both lung disease and heart disease. Your underlying lung disease (COPD and squamous cell lung cancer) make your breathing difficult at baseline, and your congestive heart failure causes fluid to build up in your lungs, making your breathing even more difficult than baseline. You were tested for lower extremity blood clots by ultrasound and found not to have any clots. You were tested for blood clots in your lungs by CTA and found not to have any lung blood clots either. You were given an echocardiogram to look at your heart by ultrasound, which found that your heart pumps out a low volume of blood. The CT of your lungs show that your lung cancer has grown since the previous scan.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Acute on Chronic Diastolic CHF, Hypoxemia - Markedly high BNP makes CHF exacerbation a virtual certainty, however the etiology is a broader list. A pulmonary embolism would certainly be a trigger for tachycardia and hypoxemia leading to acute diastolic failure. Given this cancer is highly thrombogenic, despite renal functino we pursued rule out of pulmonary embolism, via ___ which was negative and CTA which did not note a large PE, but did note a large pleural effusion. - Rate control as tolerated with metoprolol - Oxygen to keep SAO2 > 93% - diuresis with IV lasix - Would guess that ST-Depressions are likely rate related demand ischemia 2. Acute Renal Failure, Hyperkalemia - This is most likely due to CHF causing poor renal perfusion - Renal dose medication - Avoid nephrotoxins - Hold ___ - Send urine for sediment, renal lytes - Keep euvolemic 3. Lung Cancer - This makes her high risk for DVT/PE - ___ oncologist was consulted, and will work with family about goals of care as an outpatient. FULL CODE

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ gentleman with a PMH of ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF ___ s/p ICD, who presented for elective RV ICD lead replacement and generator change, extraction complicated by tricuspid valve dysfunction. # ICD ___ for ischemic CM s/p lead extraction and revision ___ and ___ As previously documented, during the extraction, the patient developed new severe TR caused by mechanical avulsion of the anterior &/or septal valve leaflets during the procedure. The lead was again adjusted to good effect on ___ and he tolerated the procedure well. He was given IV antibiotics during and after both cases and his creatinine remained stable, in fact improving to 2.2 with many of his home medications being held. He was discharged on PO Keflex ___ mg TID for 7 days for antibiotic prophylaxis. # Tricuspid Regurgitation During the lead extraction, the patient developed new severe TR caused by mechanical avulsion of anterior and/or septal valve leaflets. Acutely during the procedure, he became more hypoxic, thought to be caused by right to left shunting via patent foramen ovale from acute RA volume overload. He also required phenylephrine to support his blood pressure while in the lab. He was transferred to the CCU after the procedure. His blood pressure improved quickly and phenylephrine was discontinued. He received furosemide 40 mg IV and was extubated shortly after. He remained in the CCU overnight and was stable. His device was interrogated the following morning and noted to have high thresholds. He returned to the EP Lab and the lead modified and was successful. The device was interrogated ___ and was within normal limits. CXR revealed good placement. He did well after the procedures and was deemed appropriate for discharge to home on ___. He will follow up in Heart Failure Clinic with Dr. ___ and this appointment was scheduled prior to discharge. # CKD Stage IV (GFR 24) While hospitalized, the patient was found to have an elevated creatinine at 2.7, not changed significantly from one year ago. His creatinine clearance was also noted to be love at 29.1 ml/min. His Lisinopril, Metformin, Glyburide, Spirinolactone, and HCTZ were appropriately held. His creatinine improved to 2.2 on the day of discharge, however, he was discharged off of his Metformin and Glyburide and his HCTZ was also discontinued. His PCP's office was contacted to recommend (and was scheduled) for early follow up to discuss and start alternative therapy. His case was reviewed with Dr. ___ EP attending, who recommended workup as an outpatient. He was started on Lasix (vs. HCTZ) for his chronic systolic and diastolic heart failure management. Recommendation for a nephrologist was discussed with Dr. ___. # DM Type 2 The patient was maintained on a SSI while hospitalized. Given his kidney function, his oral medications were held. A ___ consult was deferred and instead the patient was referred for early follow up with his PCP. The patient should continue to follow a carb consistent diet. # Acute on Chronic Systolic and Diastolic Heart Failure The patient develop acute heart failure during his procedure due to his PFO and TR avulsion as noted above. He was given IV Lasix and felt to be near euvolemic following his second procedure on ___. His HCTZ continued to be held (ultimately discontinued at discharge as well) and he was discharged on PO Lasix. He will have close follow up with the Heart Failure team at ___. # AFIB/AFLUTTER s/p ablation ___ The patient remained clinically stable in sinus rhythm on telemetry. His Metoprolol was continued the evening of ___ and noted to be near euvolemic on discharge. He was noted to have atelectasis following his two procedures as seen on chest X-RAY and this improved significantly at the time of discharge. His INR was 1.2 on ___ and he was advised to take 7.5 mg on ___ with an INR check on ___. His ___ is managed by his PCP. He is on chronic dosing as noted on his discharge medication list.
summary: You underwent a planned lead and generator change on ___. During the procedure your old lead was removed and a new lead inserted. During extraction of the old lead, there appeared to be an evulsion of the septal or tricuspid leaflets with severe tricuspid regurgitation. You were intubated for hypoxia (decreased oxygen) and extubated shortly thereafter without difficulty. The following day interrogation of your pacemaker showed evidence of dislodgement and you returned to the EP Lab for adjustment of the lead. There were no complications noted and you returned to the general floor for further management. You had mild fluid overload following the initial procedure and were given IV Lasix. Some of your home medications were held due to your underlying kidney function (Lisinopril, hydrochlorothiazide, Sprinolactone as well as your Metformin and Glyburide). Your fluid status appeared normal on exam following your second procedure and no further Lasix was given. Because of your kidney function, you were placed on a sliding scale insulin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M w/ PMHx of CAD s/p 4 vessel CABG who p/w worsening shortness of breath found to have fixed perfusion defect on stress test and reduced ejection fraction consistent with progressive worsening of CAD, now s/p catheterization w/placement 1xDES. # Coronary artery disease/Dyspnea on Exertion - The patient presented with dyspnea on exertion that was progressive over the prior months, with associated chest pressure at times of exertion, with a change in his baseline, consistent with unstable angina. At presentation he did not have indications of significant volume overload ___ CHF, indicating that this was more likely due to a CAD based etiology. There were no indications of an acute coronary syndrome given patient remained chest pain free during hospitalization, and had negative troponins throughout his hospitalization. He did have an abnormal stress test showing partially reversible perfusion defect which was indicative of a lesion amenable to cardiac catheterization. On ___ he had cardiac catheterization w/ ONE drug eluting stent placed in his CABG vein leading to the obtuse marginal artery. His catheterization site was checked and showed no indication of hematoma/bruit. On ___ prior to discharge he was walked with MD, and noted that unlike a week prior to admission, he was not short of breath after a walk around the cardiology unit. He felt that his symptoms were resolved. S/P stenting, he will now need ASA 325mg (home dose) for ONE month and then transitioned to 81mg ASA daily for life, and 75mg Plavix for AT LEAST one year until ___. # Dyspnea on exertion - Likely related to underlying heart failure with reduced ejection fraction. EF 30%. Clinically presented with DOE, has evidence of pulmonary vascular congestion on CXR and he was improved with lasix. However, BNP only mildly elevated. Thus, his DOE could be his anginal equivalent. See above for " # Heart failure with reduced ejection fraction - EF 30%. Likely from ischemic cardiomyopathy given hx of CAD. However, may have damage from EtOH given he is an everyday drinker. Counseled patient during this admission on importance of abstinence from alcohol and any smoking. # CKD Elevated to 1.4 above baseline of 1.1 creatinine, received post-cath hydration with no indication of CHF. As an outpatient, will need to obtain lab work to check renal function, and follow up within 2 weeks of discharge with PCP and cardiologist given the dye load during cath. During the hospitalization in addition to providing hydration, we renally dosed medications and avoided nephrotoxic agents. As noted below, we lowered the lisinopril dose to 10mg, and this can be modified by outpatient PCP/cardiologist if desired. # Diabetes Placed on sliding scale during hospitalization with diabetic diet. On discharge can continue his oral hypoglycemics. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ============== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Needs ASA for life, and Plavix 75mg PO qdaily for AT LEAST one year until ___. - Needs followup with PCP and ___ within two weeks of discharge. - Lisinopril dose lowered to 10mg from 40mg in context of increased creatinine above baseline (1.4 vs 1.1) and dye load. As an outpatient can consider raising dose back to 40mg a day. - ASA dose of 325mg daily for one month (home dose) and recommend switching to 81mg daily after one month. - Should abstain from alcohol use - DISCHARGE WEIGHT IS 96KG or 211.6 lbs
summary: You came to the hospital with increasing shortness of breath which we suspected might be due to worsening of your coronary artery disease (blockage of the blood vessels that feed your heart). You received imaging of your heart which showed that your heart was not beating as well as it could. You received a cardiac catheterization on ___, which showed that you had multiple blockages including one that could be repaired by a "stent" which is a small tube that is placed in your blood vessel to improve the blood flow. You received ONE "drug eluting stent" to one of your coronary bypass blood vessels, so as to provide blood to your "obtuse marginal" coronary artery.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old gentleman with past medical history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, history of paroxysmal SVT presented with worsening dyspnea and CP on exertion, found to have Afib and systolic heart failure, most likely secondary to tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. # acute systolic Heart failure: Patient presented with progressively worsening dyspnea on exertion over the last ___ months and found to be in Afib with RVR and clinical findings of heart failure with pro-BNP 1330, mild troponin leak and EF of 30%. No prior history of Afib per patient but per records since ___ he has been on diltiazem for paroxysmal SVT without recurrence. At that time per records he had ETT and carotid non-invasives performed and they were normal. During his hospitaliztion, he was diuresed with IV lasix 20 mg daily with good response. Afib was managed as below. Metoprolol was initiated during hospitalization with gradual down-titration/discontinuation of diltiazem given new diagnosis of heart failure. Captopril was initiated and uptitrated as tolerated, and was eventually replaced with lisinopril 5 mg upon discharge. Diuretic regimen on discharge is 40 mg daily. Discharge weight was 81.2 kg. He had coronary catheterization which showed only 30% lesion in his LAD. The most likely cause of his heart failure is tachycardia-induced cardiomyopathy. # Afib with RVR: CHADS-2 score= 3 (Age,HF,HTN). Currently rates are better controlled 90-100's. No murmur on exam to suggest valvular disease. PMH of hypertension might be contributing. No current alcohol intake. TSH 1.3. No valvular significant valvular disease on TTE. Diltiazem was gradually downtitrated and eventually discontinued with initiation and uptitration of metoprolol. Heparin drip was initiated since admission with initiation of coumadin as inpatient. On ___ he had TEE and cardioversion which successfully restored sinus rhythm. There was concern for left atrial stasis, and therefore he was maintained on a heparin drip while coumadin was initiated. Initially enoxaparin bridging was deferred over patient concerns for self-administration of the injection. However, after it became clear that attainment of therapeutic INR would take several days, Mr. ___ changed his mind and was successfully able to self administer. He was discharge on 7.5 mg coumadin daily (which he received two doses of, and will receive two more doses of prior to INR check) as well as 80 mg lovenox BID. His INR will be followed by ___ ___, and he will have an INR check on ___. INR on discharge was 1.2. # Hyperlipidemia Mr. ___ was initially on pravastatin, which was switched to atorvastatin given his initial presentation. This was switched back to pravastatin after his cardiac catheterization, and he was discharged on 20 mg PO pravastatin daily. Transitional issues: - therapeutic INR and ongoing anticoagulation management with coumadin - INR check on ___ with subsequent coumadin dose adjustment
summary: You were admitted because of progressively worsening shortness of breath on exertion. We found that you have a fast irregular rhythm called "atrial fibrillation" with low pumping activity of your heart that we think is due to fast heart rate over the last few months. You were given IV lasix to take fluids away in addition to medications to control your heart rate to an acceptable range. You had catheterization of your heart vessels which didn't show blockages. You had an echo through your esophagus which didn't show a clot and you had a successful cardioversion which brought your rhythm back to normal "sinus". You are started on new medications including a blood thinner called coumadin.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ y/o woman with HTN, CHF, DM, and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation who presents from an outside hospital with LOC and R sided weakness. #Neuro: upon admission to ___, her neurological exam was non-focal. NCHCT and CT/CTA head/neck did not show acute intracranial abnormality. MRI did not show a stroke but did show diffuse white matter disease likely ___ chronic microangiopathy. She was continued on rivaraoxaban and aspirin per outpatient cardiologist. Differential of her presentation includes seizure given the large burden of white matter disease vs vasovagal syncope (dehydration and immediately drinking a glass of cold water leading to a vagal response) vs toxic-metabolic (?UTI). An extended routine EEG was negative. #CV: Ms. ___ had an acute exacerbation her CHF with worsening dyspnea the night of ___ likely ___ going into afib with RVR that was treated with metoprolol. A chest radiograph showed pulmonary vascular congestion. She was diuresed 5L and electrolytes were repleted prn.
summary: You were admitted for loss of consciousness and perhaps left sided weakness that was initially concerning for a transient ischemic attack. Your MRI of your head did not show a stroke but did show extensive white matter disease. Because of this, a seizure workup was also performed. The EEG read was negative for seizures. You were also found to have some mild volume overload on your chest x-ray likely from your congestive heart failure. During your second night in the hospital, you had an episode of atrial fibrillation with rapid ventricular rate where you heart rate went into the 150s. This was treated with metoprolol, and you did well. Unfortunately, this likely precipitated a heart failure exacerbation, which caused you to have some trouble breathing thus requiring oxygen. You were diuresed with IV Lasix and did well.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with afib on warfarin, DM2 on insulin, h/o CVA, bradyarrhythmia s/p pacemaker, presented with dyspnea and productive cough, found to have acute CHF with possible community-acquired pneumonia. # Diastolic heart failure, acute. Likely trigger is infection vs hypertensive urgency. TTE showed preserved ejection fraction but increased left ventricular filling pressure, mild aortic and mitral regurgitation, moderate to severe tricuspid regurgitation, and moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. She improved with IV diuresis. She initially needed 2L oxygen by nasal cannula, but at discharge was satting >96% on room air. Lasix dose was increased from 20 to 40mg daily. Discharge weight was 70.4 kg. # Community-acquired pneumonia: The patient's dyspnea on exertion was most likely due to pulmonary edema; however, chest xray could not exclude pneumonia. In the setting of productive cough and leukocytosis, and as she is asplenic, we covered for CAP with levofloxacin 750mg Q48H for 5 days. # Hypertensive urgency: Initial SBP in the 190s, and continued in the 160s-170s on all of her home anti-hypertensives. We increased her dose of Imdur temporarily but returned back to her home doses on discharge. # Atrial fibrillation: She presented subtherapeutic with INR 1.7, but regained therapeutic level in the hospital. Likely affected by antibiotics and nutrition. INR was 3.0 on both day of discharge and day prior to discharge, so we reduced her dose to 2.5mg daily. Recheck INR as outpatient. ## CHRONIC ISSUES ## # DM2: we continued home insulin # GERD: we continued home omeprazole # Hyperlipidemia: we continued home pravastatin # History of CVA: we continued home ASA, statin *TRANSITIONAL ISSUES* -Lasix increased from 20mg to 40mg daily -Warfarin reduced to 2.5mg daily -Recheck Chem-7 and INR at end of week on ___ -Follow up with PCP ___ ___
summary: You came to the hospital for shortness of breath, dizziness, and a cough. We found that you had pneumonia and treated you with antibiotics. You also had extra fluid in your lungs, which got better with increased Lasix (water pill). This fluid was due to mild heart failure. You should continue to take Lasix at home to avoid future episodes such as this one. We increased your dose of Lasix from 20mg to 40mg every day. Your INR was 3.0 on the day of discharge. Sometimes, INR is affected by antibiotics and being sick in the hospital. Please take a reduced dose of warfarin 2.5mg daily until you have your INR rechecked on ___ and see your primary care doctor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Primary Reason for Hospitalization: ___ with hx of CAD s/p CABG and multiple PCIs, dCHF (EF55-60% ___, bioprosthetic AVR, and recent stress test showing reversible anterior wall ischemia who presented with chest pain. . ACTIVE ISSUES: # CAD s/p PCI of proximal LCx ISRS with BMS: Currently chest pain free. She will continue ASA 325mg daily as she was previously and will likely need to be on aspirin indefinitely. Plavix had been stopped 2 weeks prior to admission because of vaginal bleeding but was re-started on this admission. She will need to take it for at least one month. Duration to be determined by patient's cardiologist Dr. ___. The patient reports that she takes ___ 5mg only once per week. This was confirmed by her pharmacy. The reasoning for this atypical dosing schedule was not known. It is unclear if patient had a sensitivity to statins such as myalgia that was a problem at higher doses. This should be clarified by the patient's PCP and cardiologist to ensure that she is maximized from a medical standpoint. Prior to admission the patient was taking atenolol 100mg daily. Because this medication is renally excreted there was concern for hypotension in the setting of ___. Therefore the patient was started on metoprolol succinate 25mg daily instead of atenolol. She will continue lisinopril but at a lower dose as discussed below. . # Acute Kidney Injury: Patient had Cr 1.3 on admission, (Recent Baseline ~0.9). The ___ was likely pre-renal. Cr was 1.2 prior to discharge after a heavy contrast load. Because of the ___ several medications were adjusted. Digoxin was supratherapeutic on presentation and therefore was held initially and then resumed at half dose. Dig levels should be checked at f/u with Dr. ___. Prior to admission the patient was taking atenolol 100mg daily. Because this medication is renally excreted there was concern for hypotension in the setting of ___. Therefore the patient was switched to metoprolol succinate 25mg daily instead of atenolol. . #HTN: Systolic BPs were consistently in the ___ to low 100s on metoprolol only, therefore there was concern for causing hypotension by resuming all home meds at prior doses. For this reason Amlodipine was stopped, lisinopril was decreased from 40mg daily to 2.5mg daily and Lasix 40mg was switched from daily to QOD. . Chronic issues: # Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure: Last TTE demonstrated in ___ of this year showed EF 55-60% but had been as low as 20% in the past. Lasix 40mg was switched from daily to QOD as patient looked a little dry on admission. KCL was also switched to QOD. Lisinopril was decreased as discussed above. . # Vaginal Bleeding: Patient had no episodes during this admission. She should follow-up with gynecology as planned. Likely the procedure will need to wait at least a month because of plavix therapy. Coumadin was not re-started as discussed below. If the patient does not end up having the procedure then can consider re-starting coumadin. . # Hx of Afib and sick sinus syndrome: Patient has dual chamber ICD/pacer. Currently a-paced. No afib during this admission. She will continue prior dose of amiodarone. Her CHADS score is 3. Coumadin was stopped 2 weeks prior to admission because of vaginal bleeding. After discussion with the patient's outpatient cardiologist, Dr. ___ will not resume coumadin until after the planned GYN procedure. If the patient does not end up having the procedure then can consider re-starting coumadin. . Dig levels and electrolytes should be checked at f/u with Dr. ___ - ___ dose schedule (weekly versus daily) - CODE STATUS: confirmed DNR/DNI durign this admission - EMERGENCY CONTACT: ___ (son) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of chest pain. A catheterization of your heart was performed which demonstrated a narrowing in one of the arteries in your heart. A stent was placed to open up this blockage. After having a stent placed it is VERY important that you keep taking Aspirin and Plavix until told that you can stop by your cardiologist. You will need to take Plavix for at least one month however Dr. ___ decide on the exact duration. You will need to take Aspirin indefinitely. During this hospitalization your blood pressures were on the lower side. For this reason we changed some of your medications.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yoM w/ history of CAP s/p MI with stenting and late instent thrombosis, DM, HTN, Hyperlipidemia presents with chest pain and pressure consistent with unstable angina, increased numbness in ankle, and face and word finding difficulties. No new infarct on MRI, cath with small VD, no intervention, cervical stenosis on MRI. Discharged in stable condition with neurology follow up. # Left sided Numbness/Tingling: On admission complained of increased ankle tingling Improving in face and UE, same in ankle. Has at baseline. Exam today unchanged from yesterday. stroke team following. MRI done with no acute infarct. Per neuro's read, there is possible R MCA stenosis and patient needs CTA head for further evaluation. Has out patient CTA scheduled and neurology follow up scheduled. If has significant R MCA stenosis, would need systemic anticoagulation though patient is on aspirin and Plavix currently. MRI also showed cervical stenosis which could cause facial numbness/tingling and neck pain. # Chest Pain/CAD: Cath with diffuse small vessel disease, no intervention done. Had known CAD s/p MI with stenting and late instent thrombosis ___ and diminished EF of 30%. EKG with previously noted STE, no change from prior. Cardiac biomarkers with negative troponis CK in 400's. Toprol increased to 100qd and imdur 30mg qd added. Continued on lipitor 80mg daily, ASA 325mg daily, plavix 75mg daily. Cardiology f/u as out-patient # Chronic Systolic Heart Failure: Euvolemic on exam. Echo with EF 30%, LV hypokinesis of anterior septum and ant free wall. Severe hypokinesis of apex with focal akinesis - no thrombus seen. Continued on lisinopril and toprol as above. # Diabetes: A1C 6.9%. Continued on home regimen of insulin 75:25 54 units in a.m. and 50 units in p.m., dose confirmed with patient. # Neuropathy: Secondary to diabetes. Continued on home gabapentin. # PROPHYLAXIS: received heparin.
summary: You were admitted with chest pain, word finding difficulties and increased numbness on your left side. There was concern that you were having a stroke but MRI of your brain showed nothing new. However, it did show a possible narrowing of one of the arteries in your brain. You are scheduled for CT scan of your head to further evaluate that narrowing. That CT is on ___ ___ at 4:40pm. Be sure not to eat anything for 4 hours prior to this. The MRI did show some narrowing of your spinal canal in your neck which may explain your facial tingling and shoulder pain. Your neurologist will help further evaluate that. Additionally, because of your chest pain there was concern for your heart and you had a cardiac cathertization. Numerous blockages were found but nothing that could be intervened on.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year-old female with a history of right heart failure, microscopic colitis, and Afib presenting with several days of diarrhea, urinary incontinence, instability, mechanical fall x2, and hyponatremia. #Hyponatremia: Her low sodium was most likely secondary to hypovolemia with ongoing diarrhea and poor PO intake. The low sodium was also likely responsible for her neurologic symptoms of gait instability, urinary incontinence, and 2 falls without LOC, as reported by her daughter. Her initial low sodium improved with normal saline and increased PO fluids: 118->120->122->130->134. Normal pressure hydrocephalus was also considered, but the patient's high cognitive function and improvement of gait on discharge make this etiology much less likely. #Diarrhea: The onset of the patient's diarrhea coincided with a one-week taper off a six-week course of budesonide, making a flare of microscopic colitis the most likely etiology. As an inpatient, GI was consulted. Infectious diarrhea was ruled out with negative stool cultures, negative ova and parasites, and negative C. diff. She was trialed on a lactose-free, low fiber diet as well and an IgA level with anti-TTG is still pending for celiac disease, though suspicion of these etiologies remains low. She was, therefore, restarted on budesonide 9mg. Her stools became more formed and were less frequent at the time of her discharge. Contact was established with outpatient GI for follow-up. #Hypertension: With increased PO and IV fluid intake, the patient's BP was noted to increase, while off home medications as an inpatient. Because of this, half of her valsartan dose initiated (160mg) as an inpatient with good response and escalated back to her home dose of 320mg on discharge. Her furosemide, spironolactone, and amlodipine continue to held given fluctuating volume status. She will have ___, Chem7, LFTs on ___. #Fever: The patient had one documented fever in the ED. An infectious workup was negative. Aside from urinary incontinence, she had no other urinary symptoms and there were no findings on her UA indicating infection. She also had no respiratory symptoms, and CXR was unremarkable. No antibiotics were initiated. Blood cultures are still pending. Without recurrence, the source may be related to inflammatory diarrhea, given recent prolonged steroid course that was just completed. #HLD: Continue home rosuvastatin #Afib: As an inpatient, her metoprolol was held given her propensity for hypotension and lightheadedness with GI losses. Warfarin was also held, as her INR was supratherapeutic 2.1->3.3->4.2. The increasing INR without warfarin may be a result of a mild hepatic synthetic dysfunction vs malnutrition. Her INR should be repeated to adequately resume warfarin. Transitional Issues: -Blood cultures pending -Please check a ___, Chem7, LFTs on ___. Please fax to: Dr. ___ ___ -Medication changes: Started budesonide 9mg daily; warfarin, spironolactone, furosemide, and amlodipine held at discharge pending labs above. -Findings on imaging that will need follow-up: 1. Large right thyroid nodule measures up to 2.2-cm. A non-urgent thyroid ultrasound is recommended for further evaluation. 2. Partially imaged ground-glass opacity in the right lower lobe should be followed up by chest CT non-urgently. 3. Indeterminate 1.2 cm left renal hypodensity should be followed up by renal ultrasound non-urgently. # Code: DNR/DNI # Communication: Patient # Emergency ___ ___ (dtr)
summary: You were admitted for diarrhea. We think it is related to your microscopic colitis. We checked you for an infection and it was negative, preliminarily. We started you on Budesonide with the help of the Gastroenterologists. They will see you as an outpt and decide on the taper plan. Your sodium was also low, we attributed it to the diarrhea. It normalized today. Please continue to hydrate at home. In terms of your cardiac meds, we restarted your valsartan and metoprolol. You should continue to hold the lasix/furosemide and aldactone/spirinolactone. Please check your weight daily. If there is an increase in your weight of ___ pounds, please call Dr. ___ for instructions on how to restart lasix.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo male with CAD s/p CABG and multiple PCI, s/p ICD, CHF (EF 30%), presenting with recurrent chest pain. Pain was slightly different than his usual angina in that it was diffuse throughout his chest rather than substernal. He was worried about having a blood clot. Pain was relieved in the ED with pain medicine. Patient has had numerous admissions in the last several months for similar complaints. Patient endorses many social stressors at home such as his mom who was recently diagnosed with breast cancer. See below for discussion of each problem. . # Coronaries: With known CAD s/p CABG and PCI, with recent stress MIBI in ___ without new reversible perfusion defects. Today cardiac biomarkers neg x2 to complete 12 hour rule out. Was reassured that his d-dimer and negative and that he wasn't having a heart attack. Felt comfortable with going home wihtout further testing. We reinforced that he really needed to follow up as an outpatient for medicine refills from his physician who started the meds and knows him best. Could possibly have a stress test as an outpatient. We continued home regimen ASA, plavix, metoprolol, atorvastatin, isosorbide mononitrate, and lisinopril. We discharged him on norvasc as well. He had been prescribed it in the past for increased angina relief, but has not filled the prescription before. . # PUMP: Known ischemic CM with EF 30%. Euvolemic on exam. Continued home regimen of lasix. . # RHYTHM: NSR, continued home regimen of sotalol and mexiletine. . # Hypertension: was slightly elevated so hoped to add the norvasc to his current regimen. Not sure he will be compliant but had a discussion about how it might make him feel better, so he might fill it this time. . # Patient was discharged home in stable condition and was chest pain free. Was relieved that his pain was nothing dangerous. He did ask for a refill of oxycodone for pain, so he was given approximately a week's worth of pills to ensure he follows up as an outpatient.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. We found that you weren't having a heart attack and that you did not have a blood clot in your lungs. We think your pain may be from your coronary artery disease, but it is not getting worse based on recent tests you have had done. The pain could also be from many other things, too. It could be from heart burn, anxiety or your chest muscles. It is very important that you follow up wiht Dr. ___ in the next several weeks. He will help determine if further testing is needed at that time. He knows you best and can help manage your medicines to optimize your regimen. Please return to the hospital with any chest pain, shortness of breath, abdominal pain, nausea, vomitting, fainting or any other concerns.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Chest pain: Likely demand ischemia in the setting of anemia from insidious GI bleed (see below). ST depressions in V5-V6. He had no further episodes of chest pain after he received blood transfusions. He has had prior episodes of similar pain that also resolved with resuscitation. His most recent cath showed 3 vessel disease and it was felt that it would not be beneficial to attempt further revascularization. He had prior surgical evaluation that felt he was too high risk for CABG. He was continued on asa, atorva, clopidogrel and his carvedilol. He may benefit from a repeat surgical evaluation. # GI bleed: He presented with Hgb of 7.5 from 10 on discharge on ___. He reports having black stools ever since starting plavix and had a melanotic bowel movement while admitted. He received 2 units of pRBC and H/H rose appopriately. During the prior admission, he underwent EGD that showed 2 small oozing AVMs and a colonoscopy that showed lots of diverticula, but no obvious source of bleeding. This admission he underwent EGD that showed one AVM that was clipped but again unlikely to explain all of the bleeding. He then underwent capsule endoscopy (results pending). He will be discharged on pantoprazole 40mg BID and close GI follow-up. # Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: Patient initially appeared volume overloaded and received IV lasix in the ED and additionally when receiving blood. He remained relatively euvolemic by discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Chronic Kidney Disease: He remained close to baseline (1.6-1.8) with discharge Cr of 1.9. # Cirrhosis: Stable. Secondary to EtOH. He had no history of decompensated cirrhosis and had no varices on EGD. # Hypertension: Continued amlodipine and carvedilol. # Diabetes Mellitus: Diet controlled. HISS while in house. # Hyperlipidemia: Continued atorvastatin. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Discharge H/H: 8.___.6 - ___ benefit from cardiac surgery re-evaluation - Being discharged on pantoprazole 40mg BID (rather than daily) which could be decreased per outpatient GI - Results of capsule endoscopy are pending - Full code
summary: You were admitted on ___ after having chest pain. Your blood counts were found to be very low, and when blood counts are low, there is not enough blood reaching the arteries that feed your heart and this is likely why you had chest pain. You received 2 blood transfusions and felt better. You have been having black bowel movements since your last admission. Black bowel movements suggest that you are bleeding in your GI tract, as digested blood can turn you stool black. You had a procedure called an endoscopy, where a camera is put through your mouth into your stomach. A clip was placed in your stomach to prevent bleeding from a vascular site but there were no signs of active bleeding to explain your low blood counts.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms ___ is an ___ woman with a past medical history of HTN, HLD, HFpEF, CAD, PVD w/ prior critical limb ischemia, and DM2 who presented with progressive shortness of breath concerning for HFpEF exacerbation. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure Patient presented with dyspnea, elevated BNP, pulmonary edema on Chest X ray concerning for acute exacerbation of heart failure. Likely triggered by UTI (see below). History was difficult to obtain given patient's dementia, so it is unknown if dietary or medication noncompliance contributed to her decompensation. ECG, troponin, symptoms not concerning for ischemia as trigger. Patient was diuresed with IV furosemide and was transitioned to PO. For afterload she was continued on losartan. Home hydralazine was discontinued given diastolic dysfunction, and she was started on amlodipine for BP control. She was also started on spironolactone. Home carvedilol was continued. #UTI: Patient vague historian, intermittently reporting pain with urination in addition to chronic urinary incontinence. Given this and new pyuria as well as confusion, treated as a UTI. Micro showed klebsiella pneumonia sensitive to Bactrim and ceftriaxone. She was given ceftriaxone then Bactrim for a ___: Cr 1.3 from baseline 1.0. Given concern for HFpEF above, likely from heart failure exacerbation. Creatinine improved with diuresis. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #Hypertension Continued home losartan and carvedilol, and as above discontinued home hydralazine and started amlodipine and spironolactone. #Atrial fibrillation, paroxyxmal Continued home apixaban and carvedilol # Coronary artery disease status post inferior wall MI ___, proximal left circumflex occlusion status post stent implantation in ___ (details unknown) Continued home ASA, statin, carvedilol #HLD Continued home statin #Peripheral vascular disease status post left below-the-knee to popliteal bypass ___ (___) and left tibioperoneal trunk angioplasty ___ Continued home ASA, statin #Carotid disease, ___, R < 40%, left 40-59% Continue home ASA, statin #Type 2 diabetes Held home metformin and managed with sliding scale insulin #Mild/moderate AR, moderate MR, TTE 4.17 # Dementia Continued home Donepezil # Hypothyroidism Continued home Levothyroxine Sodium # GERD Continued home omeprazole TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] discharge weight: 156.97lbs (71.2kg), discharge creatinine 1.0, discharge diuretic torsemide 80mg PO daily [ ] please check sodium within 3 days to ensure hypernatremia resolving [ ] please assess volume and adjust torsemide dose accordingly [ ] please monitor creatinine, potassium, or magnesium [ ] potassium supplementation discontinued so please monitor potassium within 3 days and provide supplementation as needed [ ] ___ ___ RN, ___ for collateral. Did not receive return phone call on ___.
summary: You were admitted because of shortness of breath You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and you were found to have fluid on your lungs. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out. You were treated with antibiotics for a urinary tract infection Your weight at discharge is 75.7 kg (166.89 lb). Please weigh yourself today at home and use this as your new baseline. Please weigh yourself every day in the morning.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with PMH significant for HFpEF (EF 60-65%), mild AS, CKD (b/l Cr 1.7), HTN, and HLD who presents with acute shortness of breath, syncope, and bradycardia who was found to have new high degree AV block. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # High Degree AV Block Patient presenting with syncope, shortness of breath, and bradycardia with new high degree AV block on EKG. He was initially treated with dopamine and ultimately temporary pacing wires were placed on ___ at outside hospital. Patient was transferred to ___, and underwent SJM dual chamber pacemaker placement on ___. Differential for etiology of new AV block most likely ___ degenerative conduction disease, ischemia. However, TTE at ___ showed EF preserved EF without evidence of new wall motion abnormalities, cardiomyopathy, or valve disease. Ischemia less likely given lack of chest pain on admission and troponin < 0.02 (peak 0.23). Consider stress testing as an outpatient for underlying CAD. Infectious workup, including blood cultures and Lyme titers, was negative at ___. He will be discharged to complete 1 day of cephalexin therapy. #Afib/Aflutter Patient noted to have new onset AFib/Aflutter on admission. CHADSVASC: 4. Started Apixaban 2.5mg BID post-procedure, which patient should continue indefinitely. # Leukocytosis - Resolved Likely stress response, now down-trending. Otherwise, no localizing symptoms of infection or fevers. Infectious workup at ___, was negative. WBC down-trended without antibiotics. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #HFpEF (EF 60-65%) Patient does not appear volume overloaded on exam. Continue home regimen with Lasix 20mg daily and lisinopril 40mg daily. #Primary prevention: Continue home ASA, statin #HLD: Continue home statin, fenofibrate #HTN: Continue home lisinopril #BPH: Continue home tamsulosin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =========================== - Consider stress testing as an outpatient for underlying CAD. - Consider cardioversion if patient remains symptomatic from atrial flutter. - Patient started on apixiban at reduced dose of 2.5mg BID for afib/aflutter
summary: You were admitted because you had an abnormal heart rhythm. This was causing your heart to beat too slow, and making you feel lightheaded and dizzy. This is likely what caused you to faint at home. You had a procedure to place a permanent pace-maker. This pace-maker will monitor your heart rhythm and help pace your heart if it is too slow. You were also started on a blood thinner called Apixaban. Abnormal heart rhythms like yours can cause stroke, so you will need to continue to take this medication to decrease your risk of stroke in the future. You will continue to take Apixaban twice a day at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo female history of longstanding IDDM, HTN, CKD, chronic diastolic CHF and recently diagnosed systolic CHF admitted for pre-cath hydration prior to planned cardiac cath which revealed 3VD. Cards recommended CABG but CT surg feels she is too high risk and only the LAD is amenable to bypass. She will proceed to cath next ___ for PCI with LAD stent to separate the two dye loads. . #CORONARIES: Patient has multiple risk factors for CAD and had fixed defects on stress MIBI in ___. Cardiac cath revealed 3VD, but she is too high risk for CABG. LAD stent via PCI will be placed next ___. She was given NAC before and after cath. She was continued on an full strength aspirin and beta blocker. She was restarted on atorvastatin. . #PUMP: Patient has history of ___ since ___ and new ___ since ___ with EF 40-45%. Currently euvolemic on exam, but pulmonary artery pressurs in the ______ on cath. She was continued on hydral and Imdur for afterload reduction. Her furosemide was increased to 40mg daily to optomize her fluid status prior to her next catheterization. Spironolactone was being held secondary to hyperkalemia, and is currently not needed. She is not on an ___ secondary to adverse reaction of cough. ___ for a second trial should be considered as an outpatient after her second cardiac catheterization. . #RHYTHM: Normal sinus rhythm during admission. She was monitored on telemetry. . #CKD: Secondary to diabetic nephropathy. Creatinine peaked at 3.3 in ___ after last contrast load. Creatinine at nadir of 1.6 on the day of catheterization, peaked at 1.9 that evening and was 1.7 on the day of discharge. . #Type 1 Diabetes: ___ years duration complicated by nephropathy, retinopathy, and neuropathy. An A1C was noted to be 7.5% during admission. She was continued on her home Lantus and Humalog sliding scale. . #Hypothyroidism: Stable, without symptoms. She was maintained on her home Synthroid. . #Hypertension: She reports excellent control when she was previously on labetalol and diltiazem. However, she was switched to hydral, isosorbide mononitrate, and metoprolol given her new diagnosis CHF and has been persistently hypertensive. Her Lasix was increased during admission and upon discharge. An ___ should be considered after her second catheterization as mentioned above. . #Hyperlipidemia: She was previously well controlled on Lipitor, but was switched to simvastatin. Simvastatin was discontinued after she experienced right lower extremity myalgias. Her lipids were poorly controlled on Niacin at the time of admission. Her Lipitor was restarted during admission without issue. Niacin was discontinued to decrease risk of complications from statin therapy. She should have LFT's checked in six weeks time.
summary: You were admitted for pre-cath hydration. Cardiac catheterization revealed diffuse three vessel disease in your coronary arteries and high pulmonary pressures indicating pulmonary hypertension. The cardiothoracic surgeons were consulted but determined that you are too high risk for coronary artery bypass grafting. Your next cardiac catheterization and LAD stenting will be on ___. Please do not take your Lasix on ___ night. Take the first dose of N-acetylcysteine on ___, and the second dose on ___ morning. Come in to the cath lab on ___ morning for pre-cath hydration. Do not have anything to eat or drink after midnight on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY/ASSESSMENT: ___ w CHF, PPM, on AC for CVA but no clear Afib, found to have obstructing gallstone with 10mm CBD, transferred here for ERCP. Notably without pain so may have passed stone. No evidence clinically or radiographically of cholangitis. Now s/p ERCP with removal of sludge; found to have narrowing of CBD now s/p brushings and stent. ACUTE/ACTIVE PROBLEMS: # Abdominal Pain # Biliary Obstruction # Cholelithiasis S/p successful ERCP (___) with sphincterotomy and extraction of a small amount of sludge. Distal CBD possible filling defect noted s/p brushings and stent. Brushings were negative for malignant cells. Plan for EUS and ERCP in 4 weeks for stent pull and further eval (for CBD narrowing). His home apixaban was resumed on ___. #?CHF His home carvedilol and Lasix were resumed on discharge. # Thrombocytopenia - resolved (likely plt count of 61 was erroneous; HIT panel cancelled) # Metabolic acidosis - resolved -? ___ to IVF hydration # Daily alcohol use No history of withdrawal. He did not exhibit signs of withdrawal during this hospital stay. He was counseled on his alcohol use and advised against drinking alcohol at all for at least 1 week after ERCP until his LFTs normalize. >30 minutes spent on complex discharge
summary: You were admitted to ___ because of concern for a gallstone that was obstructing your common bile duct. You had an ERCP procedure to clear the common bile duct. They cleared out bile sludge. They found that after they removed the sludge, there was still narrowing of the common bile duct. They took samples of cells in the bile duct and put in a stent to keep the duct open. In 4 weeks, you will need to return for a procedure to remove the stent and to look at the surrounding area with an ultrasound. The pathology from the cells taken from the common bile duct should result in about 1 week. You should receive a phone call from the gastroenterologists with those results.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ PMHx T1DM, CAD s/p CABG ___ PCIs, PAD s/p bilateral BKA, sCHF, and CKD admitted to the ___ service after a fall resulting in a right hip fracture transferred to Medicine for pre-op optimization. She underwent successful TNF ___. Hospital course was complicated by ___ ___ intravasc depletion and hypoxia ___ atelectasis. # Right hip fracture s/p trochanteric nail fixation: patient presented after a mechanical fall complicated by comminuted right hip fracture. She was originally admitted to the ___ team; original plan for surgery on ___, however in setting of uptrending creatinine and complicated cardiac history the decision was made to transfer the patient for Medicine for pre-surgical optimization. She was evaluated by Cardiology, who determined she was high risk for procedure. Her Cr was monitored and addressed as below. Following improvement in creatinine and optimization of cardiac regimen, she underwent successful trochanteric nail fixation on ___ by Ortho. She was continued on Tylenol/tramadol for pain control. She will continue on Lovenox for x28 days post-op. # ___: on admission to ___, patient's Cr uptrending from baseline 1.0 to 1.7. Concern for volume overload given ___ IVF, elevated BNP, (+) hepatojugular reflux vs contrast-induced nephropathy. Further IVF were held prior to procedure and Cr downtrended. Due to hypoxia and concern for vol overload she received IV diuresis; her Cr uptrended to 1.5. Further IV diuresis was held and she was encouraged to take po. Her Cr was monitored and returned to baseline 1.1. Her home dose Lasix and lisinopril were restarted at discharge. # Hypoxia: following admission pt developed new 2L O2 requirement. CXR c/f vasc congestion. Thought to be ___ pulmonary edema in the setting of Lasix being held, receiving ___ IVF vs atelectasis from being in bed vs splinting ___ pain. No current infectious symptoms. Has been anticogulated, moving, with decreasing O2 dependence less concerning for PE. She received IV diuresis, was continued on incentive spirometry. Her O2 was weaned to 1L prior to discharge. # CAD: patient with cardiovascular history notable for CABG (___), multiple PCIs, and recent admission to ___ (___) ___ unstable angina. Prior to surgery, she was evaluated by Cardiology and was deemed high risk from a CV standpoint. She was continued on her asa/Plavix, imdur, metoprolol, zetia, and lisinopril. #Type I DM: long-standing, followed by Dr. ___. She was followed by ___ in the hospital and her insulin sliding scale was adjusted as needed. # Chronic systolic congestive heart failure: TTE in ___ notable for EF 40%. Patient appeared overloaded on admission, with Pro-BNP elevated to 4000. She received IV diuresis with improvement in hypoxia. She was transitioned to home regimen of imdur, metoprolol, lisinopril, Lasix on discharge. # UTI: during hospitalization patient had foley catheter placed; urinalysis (+) for WBCs. She received fosfomycin x1. # Dilated biliary ducts: incidental finding on CT during trauma evaluation. Patient was without abdominal pain and LFTs within normal limits. Recommend non-emergent MRCP for further evaluation. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [] Continue Lovenox 40mg subcut daily x28 days (last day ___ [] Discharge weight: XX, dry weight likely XX [] Please adjust Lasix as needed to maintain euvolemic status [] Discharge BUN/Cr: 32/1.1 [] Continue pain control with Tylenol/tramadol PRN [] O2 requirement likely ___ post-operative atelectasis; please continue to wean as patient's ambulatory status improves [] Insulin regimen uptitrated compared to home regimen; please continue to monitor FSBG [] High dose statin not started given patient's previous intolerance [] Continue dry sterile dressings PRN to right hip wound # CONTACT: ___ (daughter) ___ # CODE: Full
summary: You were admitted to ___ after a fall. You fractured your right hip and had Orthopedic Surgery to repair your hip. To prevent development of a clot, you were started on Lovenox. You will need to give yourself one injection per day for the next month. Please see their care instructions below. Your hospital course was complicated by injury to your kidneys. You received some intravenous fluids and this resolved. You also developed some difficulty breathing after surgery and needed oxygen to help; please continue to work with Physical Therapy and use your incentive spirometer to keep your lungs open.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with PMH of afib on warfarin, HFpEF, and recent admission (___) for failure to thrive who presents from home with sepsis ___ ___ cellulitis +/- UTI. # Sepsis # ___ cellulitis Presented with fever, leukocytosis, and elevated lactate ___ rapidly progressing ___ erythema and edema encompassing the entire lower extremity. ___ negative for DVT. Received vancomycin IV and 500 mL bolus in ED. Blood culture from ___ grew CoNS - likely a contaminant. All other blood cultures negative. She improved daily on vancomycin. She does have a history of MRSA from a urine culture in ___, so she was continued on MRSA-coverage when she was switched to PO antibiotics on ___ (Bactrim + cqphalexin). Her cellulitis continued to improve on oral antibiotic therapy. Day 1 of antibiotics was ___. Planning for a 10-day course of antibiotics, the last day will be ___. # ? Urinary tract infection UA c/w infection. She takes prophylactic fosfomycin at home once weekly. No urinary symptoms, however, pt is not a reliable historian. She was initially started on ciprofloxacin for empiric therapy due to beta-lactam allergy. The urine culture was consistent with fecal contamination. By the time the urine culture had resulted, she had received a full course of cipro for UTI (3 days). Her home fosftomycin was held during this admission and resumed on discharge. # Altered mental status/somnolence - resolved As of ___ the patient appeared to be back at her baseline. She keeps her eyes closed and is blind at baseline, but she wakes to voice and responds to questions appropriately. Likely her altered mental status was due to sepsis and hypovolemia. There may have been some contribution from the haloperidol she received the day prior as well. She improved with gentle IVF. # Hypoxia - resolved # HFpEF (EF 55%) with mild exacerbation She had a new oxygen requirement (2L O2) as of ___. Her CXR was remarkable for worsening bilateral pleural effusions and pulmonary edema likely due to CHF in the setting of sepsis and volume resuscitation. Her home furosemide (20 mg PO MWF) was initially held due to sepsis, but was resumed on ___. After this, she was weaned off supplemental O2 on ___. She appeared euvolemic on discharge and was oxygenating well on room air. # New normocytic anemia No evidence of bleeding. INR had been supratherapeutic on admission, but with holding warfarin it decreased to goal range. Iron studies consistent with iron deficiency anemia and mild hypoproliferation (retic index 1). Held off on iron replacement for now given infection; can be considered as an outpatient # Failure to thrive Recent admission (___) for failure to thrive. Geriatrics consulted during that admission and extensive discussions were had with the son/HCP regarding goals of care. Son stated it was within the patient's GOC to return home with services and also requested she remain a full code based on previous discussions he had had with his mother. This was discussed with her son, ___, again on ___. He reiterated that he does not want his mother to go to rehab. He is accepting of home health services. Based on his prior experiences with rehab facilities, he feels like it would be unethical to send her to a rehab. # Atrial fibrillation # Supratherapeutic INR INR on admission was 5.4. In this setting, warfarin was held. She had no evidence of active bleeding. Her home dose of warfarin was resumed on ___. Likely due to the Bactrim she is on, her INR increased dramatically from 2.7 to 3.5. This was discussed with pharmacy. They recommended holding the dose on ___ and resuming warfarin at a reduced dose (1 mg qhs) on ___. Her son was instructed that she should keep taking warfarin 1 mg until she completed the Bactrim on ___ and followed up with Dr. ___ PCP) on ___. Ms. ___ was clinically stable for discharge on ___. The total time spent today on discharge planning, counseling and coordination of care was greater than 30 minutes.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with sepsis due to cellulitis. You were initially treated with IV antibiotics (vancomycin), then switched to oral antibiotics (Bactrim and cephalexin). You were also initially treated with ciprofloxacin because it looked like you might have had a urinary tract infection, however you did not have significant growth in your urine culture. Your warfarin was initially held due to a high INR, and resumed once your INR returned to a therapeutic range. Because the antibiotics will cause the INR to be higher than usual, the warfarin dose will be adjusted as follows: HOLD the dose on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ h/o PAD, mesenteric ischemic s/p SMA/celiac stent, CAD with stable angina, chronic diastolic heart failure, poorly controlled HTN, and CKD IV who presents with a few days of worsening cough and SOB with clinical presentation concerning for acute on chronic diastolic heart failure. Hospital course complicated by fevers, rash, acute on chronic renal failure. # Dyspnea and cough Initially though to be ___ acute on chronic diastolic heart failure and began IV diuresis. TTE showed EF 66% and mild valvular disease with an elevated CI. No clear evidence of high output failure. Diuresis was limited by episode of severe hypotension (SBP ___ with syncope and renal failure. Dyspnea and cough improved after diruesis. CXR did not show any evidence of PNA. Ultimately symptoms may have been more infectious than CHF related given that patient received fluids and symptoms did not return. Diuretic (torsemide) held upon discharge, see below. # Fevers, Rigors, Sweats # Elevated CRP Patient presented from home with rigors and had a documented fever while hospitalized to ___. She also had a couple of episodes of NS while admitted. These symptoms quickly dissipated after admission. Blood cultures were negative (though were never taken at time of sweats or fever). Urine culture was also negative. Lyme serologies were negative. TTE showed mild valvular abnormalities. ID was consulted for yield of ___ in the diagnosis of culture negative endocarditis. Given that pre-test probability was low, TEE was deferred. CRP was checked as part of this work-up and was remarkably elevated to ~250. Without intervention, CRP improved to 144 at discharge. She will need repeat labs drawn to ensure that Cr returns back to baseline as it was 1.9 at discharge. Torsemide and valsartan were held at discharge. # Labile hypertension # Episode of hypotension and syncope Patient experienced wild swings in BP with an associated episode of hypotension with syncope to SBP of 70. Medications were adjusted and she was maintained on carvedilol and imdur for 72+ hours with SBPs ranging from 130-160. This was discussed with her PCP who agreed in keeping her BP in this range. Valsartan was held at discharge. She was advised to take BP twice daily before medications and to hold BP meds if SBP < 120. # Dysphagia: Patient complained of esophageal dysphagia symptoms. Tertiary contractions seen on barium swallow which may be cause of dysphagia and commonly seen in presbyesophagus. Advised that patient need slowly. No webs/strictures seen. Further work-up with endoscopy was deferred. # Rash: Patient had faint erythemaous rash on her arm, lower abdomen and left breast which improved without intervention. ?Viral exanthem. Will need outpatient follow up if rash persists. Chronic Medical Problems . GERD: continued on omeprazole. Transitional Issues: 1. Monitor Cr in coming week 2. Trend CRP 3. Further BP medication titration
summary: You were admitted with shortness of breath, and cough which was thought to be an exacerbation of heart failure. You received diuretics through the IV. With that you became dehydrated and your kidney function worsened. After stopping the diuretic and increasing your hydration, your kidney function improved and is close to normal. It is very important that you remain very hydrated. We suggest that you drink at 1.5L of fluids per day (which is about 3 of the pink pitchers in the hospital). You will be seeing Dr. ___ (___) and will likely have your labs redrawn. On the first night of your admission, you had a fever to 101. You also had shaking chills while you were home and sweats while you were admitted. You had an extensive work-up that included blood cultures, several blood tests and a chest xray that did not reveal a cause. You also had an ultrasound of your heart that did not reveal an infection your heart valves. You were seen by the infectious disease doctors who did not feel that you needed for further work-up because you did not have further fevers. Ultimately, we were unable to uncover the true cause of these symptoms however you have been stable for 3 or days and your cultures are negative which is reassuring.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ man with HTN, HLD, BPH, CKD stage III, well controlled DM2, recently discovered severe aortic stenosis and CAD on stress, who presented with flash pulmonary edema and mild overload. Found to have new severe TR on TTE, being discharged with plan for expedited outpatient TAVR work up. #Flash pulmonary edema #Severe AS #New Severe TR The patient was found to have BP 200/100 and moderate pulmonary edema on CXR at presentation, both improved with Nitropaste and IV Lasix 20mg. This was considered likely to be flash pulmonary edema in the setting of severe aortic stenosis and hypertension. There was concern for possibly contributing ischemia given known CAD. Previous Echo and stress test in ___ were concerning for severe AS and CAD, although he had never previously had a cath. EF was 50-55% at that time. He had never been diagnosed with heart failure clinically but with orthopnea/PND, elevated JVP and BNP on admission, which resolved after IV diuresis with 20mg Lasix boluses. TTE ___ redemonstrated severe AS in addition to new 4+ TR and 3+ MR, with EF of 65% at that time. Coronary angiogram ___ without significant obstructive disease. Labs for new heart failure including TSH, tox screen, iron studies were unrevealing. We continued home verapamil given documented allergy to metop for nausea/vomiting. We restarted diuresis before discharge with plan for Lasix 20mg 2x/wk (___). Given new severe TR, we consulted C-surg about open AVR and tricuspid repair, however after reevaluation it was determined that the severity of TR/MR was likely less than documented on the original read. Given this, it was felt he was a better candidate for TAVR. He was seen by the structural heart team, with plan for expedited TAVR work up as outpatient. #CAD Recent stress as outpatient with reversible defect consistent with TTE showing wall motion abnormality in diagonal distribution. He has been medically managed, with no history of cath given CKD and concern for contrast load. On admission, ECG with TWI in lateral leads, which has been present on prior visits and trops negative x2. Continued home Atorvastatin 40 mg PO/NG QPM, home ASA 81mg qd. #HTN BP 200s on admission in the setting of flash pulmonary edema, unclear which came first. Blood pressures improved after patient received nitropaste and IV Lasix. Continued home Verapamil 240 mg PO Q12H. For afterload, we initiated hydralazine 75mg TID, while holding home irbesartan and HCTZ in the setting of cath and ___. ___ on CKD III: Cr near baseline on admission 1.8-2.1 with slight uptrend to 2.4 after receiving contrast. Held ___ and thiazide as above. Temporarily held diuresis which was restarted before discharge #Type 2 Diabetes: Diet-controlled, recently A1c 6.6 in ___. Fingersticks held given that the patient did not require home meds for diabetes #BPH: - continued home terazosin #Glaucoma - continued home brimonidine and latanoprost TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== Discharge weight 80.1 kg (176.59 lb) MEDICATION CHANGES: - started furosemide 20mg 2x/wk (___) - started hydralazine 75mg TID - held home irbesartan 300 mg qd, HCTZ 25mg qd [] Scheduled for outpatient labs ___ [] Should follow up with structural cardiology team for expedited TAVR work up [] Would restart home irbesartan and HCTZ after renal function stable and no longer getting contrast for TAVR work up. Can decrease hydralazine if BPs too low to tolerate irbesartan [] Repeat TTE after TAVR given severe TR/MR which may improve after valve replacement # CODE: Full presumed # CONTACT: HCP: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had shortness of breath You were found have fluid in your lungs as well as very high blood pressures. You were given medication to remove to the extra fluid from your lungs. We performed an ultrasound of your heart called an echocardiogram which showed you had worsened function in some of your heart valves (called the tricuspid and mitral valves). We performed an angiogram to look at the blood vessels in your heart and found that you were not at high risk for a heart attack, as you did not have serious narrowing in your vessels. You improved and were ready to leave the hospital.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY ======= ___ man with hx of non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy (___ Class II-III, LVEF 13% as of TTE ___ and T1DM (dx ___ years ago as latent T1) who presented to ___ on ___ with nausea and vomiting. Found to have mild transaminase elevation and elevated JVP concerning for acute on chronic heart failure. Treated with IV diuresis and underwent right heart catheterization and ICD implantation for primary prevention of fatal arrhythmia. ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # Acute on chronic heart failure (EF <20%): Non-ischemic, ___ class II-III, LVEF 13% in ___. Thought to be viral/idiopathic. He presented with a BNP of ___, elevated from previous in the 800s. Weight on admission was 107 kg. His TTE on ___ showed new mild-moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension in addition to RV dilation and hypokinesis. He was treated with IV Lasix boluses with good output and symptomatic improvement. Later transitioned to torsemide 30 mg daily which he was discharged with to replace his previous home Lasix. Also increased Imdur to 60 mg daily. Given biventricular dysfunction, he was evaluated for ICD which was implanted on ___. Procedure was well tolerated. CXR was concerning for possible migration of device leads, however repeat device interrogations were reassuring. He will follow up with Device Clinic in 1 week. Also planning to follow up with regular cardiologist. #Pulmonary hypertension, Class II: TTE on ___ showed new mild-moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension in addition to RV dilation and hypokinesis. He underwent right heart catheterization on ___ which demonstrated elevated biventricular pressures, elevated pulmonary pressures, and borderline cardiac output and index, demonstrating that he has class II pulmonary arterial hypertension due to his left-sided heart failure. Optimized home heart failure medication regimen as discussed above: increased Imdur to 60 mg daily, transitioned to torsemide 30 mg daily. # Nausea/Vomiting: Nausea resolved with 1 mg of lorazepam. Ddx thought to be abdominal congestion due to right heart failure vs. gastroparesis due to diabetes. His volume overload was managed with diuresis as described above and symptoms did not recur. # Elevated transaminases, down-trending. Patient presented with mild transaminitis in a hepatocellular pattern as well as elevated INR consistent with mildly decreased synthetic function. Unclear whether this is acute or chronic process given last LFTs checked ___. Most likely due to congestive hepatopathy in setting of cardiomyopathy. Patient denied excessive ETOH use. Hepatitis serologies were negative for hepatitis A and C and demonstrated non-immune hepatitis B status. Labs were largely resolved with only mildly elevated ALT of 55 at time of discharge. CHRONIC PROBLEMS ======================= # IDDM: Managed with sliding scale as inpatient. # HLD: Stable. Continued home atorvastatin. # Anxiety: Received lorazepam PRN and continued on home venlafaxine. # Allergies: Home fluticasone and cetirizine. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ======================== [ ] Discharge weight: 100.2 kg (220.46 lbs) [ ] Discharge diuretic regimen: torsemide 30 mg PO daily [ ] ICD placed on ___. Activity and lifting restrictions provided to patient. [ ] Will obtain repeat Chem10 on ___, with results faxed to ___ Heart Failure Clinic and PCP's office. If potassium remains borderline elevated, can consider reducing spironolactone dose. [ ] Consider need for genetic testing in the setting of non-ischemic dilated cardiomyopathy. Can consider referral to Dr. ___ at ___. [ ] If nausea/vomiting returns, can consider discontinuing dulaglutide as an outpatient given that it causes gastric slowing [ ] Persistently anemic (Hgb ___. Consider repeat CBC, additional workup as outpatient [ ] HBsAb and HAV Ab negative - consider Hepatitis vaccination series CONTACT: ___ (Mother) ___ CODE STATUS: FULL CODE
summary: You were hospitalized for nausea and vomiting. An ultrasound of your abdomen showed no acute problems that could be causing your symptoms. The nausea and vomiting resolved but could have been due to delayed gastric slowing from the diabetes (which can be worsened by the trulicity) or could be caused by a virus that has since resolved. You had a mild elevation in the laboratory markers of your liver function, which we think is most likely because your liver was experiencing increased pressure on account of your heart not pumping as well. An ultrasound of your heart showed decreased pump function which resulted in increased fluid in your arms, abdomen, and legs. We treated this with medications to help you urinate off the fluid and your weight decreased. You were also started on new medications to benefit your heart. Given the decreased pump function of the heart, the heart rhythm doctors recommended ___ (implantable cardioverter-defibrillator) placement which was performed on ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with postpartum cardiomyopathy (EF 28%), pre-eclampsia ___, prior gastric bypass in ___ with revision in ___ c/b intestinal perforation, and hypothyroidism not on synthroid, who presented with shortness of breath, fatigue and chest pain. # Chest Pain/SOB: Pt p/w worsening chest pain and shortness of breath iso h/o post-partum cardiomyopathy with most recent EF 28% per patient report. Pt was ruled out for ACS (negative troponin, no ischemic signs on EKG). Repeat TTE ___ showed improved EF of 55% with no focal WMA. Pt was ruled out for PE with CTA. There was concern for gastrointestinal etiology to chest pain given h/o gastric bypass and Fe deficiency anemia discussed below however EGD ___ was without abnormalities to explain chest pain. The gastroenterology team recommended discontinuing patient's PPI and sucralfate 1g QID. Shortness of breath is possibly explained by Fe deficiency anemia discussed below and improved prior to discharge after IV Fe treatments in addition to levothyroxine initiation. # Fatigue: Likely multifactorial iso subclinical hypothyroidism discussed below, poor nutrition and anemia. Management below. # Anemia: Microcytic anemia upon admission, MCV 79 w/ hemoglobin 11.4. Fe studies notable for Fe 21, Ferritin 8.1, TIBC 454, TRF 349 (___) c/w Fe deficiency anemia. Pt received IV Fe during admission and was discharged on oral iron supplementation, in addition to stool softener for iron-derived constipation. # Malnutrition: Pt found to have Vitamin B12 and Vitamin D deficiencies. She was discharged on Vitamin B12 and Vitamin D, following in-house supplementation. # Hypothyroidism: Thyroid labs ___ notable for elevated TSH of 7.2, normal free T4 of 1.2 consistent with subclinical hypothyroidism. Patient was started on levothyroxine 50 mcg QD. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== [ ] If weight changes by 5 lbs from (242 lbs discharge weight) it is very important to see your doctor as soon as possible [ ] Continue levothyroxine 50 mcg QD. Take in the AM on an empty stomach, 60 minutes prior to meals or 2 hours after a meal. 4 hours from calcium/iron/vitamin supplements [ ] Repeat TSH in one month from starting levothyroxine (TSH, free T4) [ ] Continue Ergocalciferol 50,000 units every week for 8 weeks (first week ___ [ ] Continue Cholecalciferol (Vitamin D3) 2,000 units after completing 8 weeks of ergocalciferol [ ] Repeat Vitamin D level ___ months after discharge [ ] Continue Vitamin B12 1000mcg PO daily, SL or chewable preferred [ ] Avoid NSAIDs in outpatient setting [ ] Continue on PO iron supplementation as outpatient (150 to 300 mg 2 to 3 times a day) # CODE: Full # CONTACT: ___ (husband) ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having chest pain and difficulty breathing, which was concerning given your history of heart failure. During your admission we determined that you were not having a heart attack or clots in your lungs causing pain. You underwent an echocardiogram which actually showed that your heart's ability to pump had improved to normal (ejection fraction 55%). You underwent an endoscopy which was also reassuringly normal. You were found to be anemic with several nutritional deficiencies which we began to correct. You should continue taking the prescribed supplements at home. You continued to have chest pain, the cause of which is not entirely clear however as discussed above dangerous causes of this pain were excluded on work-up.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old male a PMH of CAD s/p CABG, lower extremity cellulitis, anemia, diabetes, recent CHF admission and aortic stenosis. He has noted a decline in functioning, and ambulates only a short distance before experiencing SOB, fatigue and dizziness. He frequently uses a wheelchair. He was admitted for a TAVR. His procedure was without complications. A post-procedure Echo showed a well seated valve. Post-procedure, He continued to have dizziness and an unsteady gait. He was seen by neurology, who did not feel that there was any acute neurological process causing his symptoms. The pt also developed prolonged pauses and bradycardia with AF w RVR after restarting his Digoxin and Metoprolol . He was transferred to the CCU for close monitoring CCU Course: Patient transferred s/p TAVR c/b tachy-brady syndrome. He was previously on digoxin and metoprolol and was having infrequent symptomatic pauses. He was transferred to CCU for monitoring for 1 day and a PPM was subsequently placed and patient was transferred to floor Return to the floor: Pt remained stable on return to the floor. His Metoprolol was restarted, and he had no further RVR. He paced appropriately. He continued to have dizziness with movement. He received IVF and his Torsemide was decreased. He was seen and evaluated by ___, but vestibular testing was not completed r/t orthostasis. A small left groin hematoma and femoral bruit were noted. An US was negative for fistula or psuedo- aneurysm. He was medically cleared for discharge, but will need ongoing ___. He has follow up in place at the Device Clinic and with The Structural Heart Team. If his dizziness does not improve, he will need further/ongoing medical evaluation with his PCP.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a procedure to treat your aortic valve. There were no complications during your procedure. After your procedure, however, your heart rate was very low at times and had pauses. You were transferred to the CCU for further monitoring. You underwent placement of a pacemaker to treat your low heart rate. You also continued to have dizziness and low blood pressure with movement. You were seen by Neurology, who did not feel that there was an acute process occurring. A very small stroke was seen on imaging, but this was NOT felt to be the cause of your dizziness, no were your enlarged ventricles. You were also seen by Physical Therapy. Changes were made to your medications to help the dizziness. It is hoped that with your new valve, pacemaker and ongoing physical therapy, that the dizziness with decrease over time. Please weigh yourself every morning before you eat and after you urinate, and continue to follow a low salt diet. Inform your Cardiologist if your weight increases by ___ lbs over several days. Your weight on discharge is 69.6 kgs or 153 lbs.
