His Metoprolol was restarted, and he had no further RVR. He paced appropriately. He continued to have dizziness with movement. He received IVF and his Torsemide was decreased. He was seen and evaluated by ___, but vestibular testing was not completed r/t orthostasis. A small left groin hematoma and femoral bruit were noted. An US was negative for fistula or psuedo- aneurysm. He was medically cleared for discharge, but will need ongoing ___. He has follow up in place at the Device Clinic and with The Structural Heart Team. If his dizziness does not improve, he will need further/ongoing medical evaluation with his PCP.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Summary ====================================== ___ PMH of severe AS s/p TAVR ___ (c/b PNA, septic shock), CAD (s/p DES to RCA), TIA (s/p CEA), and CLL, discharged on ___ after hospitalization for ___ exacerbation, who presented with weakness. Thought to be ___ overdiuresis at home, so lasix was held and gentle IV hydration was given. EP interrogated pacemaker but did not find a likely precipitating event for weakness. However, they did note newly diagnosed Atrial Fibrillation. During stay, patient had rapid ventricular response requiring B-Blocking medication, and was eventually rate controlled on ___ Metoprolol Succinate daily. Afib with RVR ====================================== On day following admission patient was noted to be in Afib on telemetry and was experiencing palpitations. Overnight he rapidly stood from bed to urinate and fell, hitting his head. On assessment he was neurologically intact with normal head CT and C-spine CT. He was started on metoprolol and rapid ventricular reponse did not recurr. Anticoagulation was not initiated in light of fall. Patient will need to follow up with his cardiologist regarding this issue. During stay, patient had rapid ventricular response requiring B-Blocking medication, and was eventually rate controlled on ___ Metoprolol Succinate daily. Patient was cleared by physical therapy once atrial fibrillation was controlled but discharged w/ home ___. CHF with preserved EF ====================================== Home lasix was initally held in setting of likely hypovolemia due to taking his lasix BID instead of once daily. Prior to discharge, patient started to re-accumulate peripheral edema so he was restarted on his home dose lasix. Since he was comfortable w/ stable vitals he was discharged on home dose lasix 20mg daily with outpatient cardiology follow up appointment. He was also discharged with ___ assistance for medication management and telemonitoring for better asessment of weights. He was instructed to weigh homeself daily and call his cardiologist for acute changes >3 pounds for adjustment of lasix dose. Transitional Issues ================================ 1. Patient will follow up with cardiology regarding his rate control of atrial fibrillation, diuretic dosing for diastolic CHF and risks/benefits of starting anticoagulation for Atrial Fibrillation. 2. During admission found elevated TSH of 7.5. Should be retested as and outpatient adjustment of levothyroxine dosing as necessary. 3. Patient was restarted on home dose lasix 20mg po daily. This dose may need adjustment. Patient was instructed to weigh himself daily and call his cardiologist for acute changes >3 pounds to adjust lasix dose. 4. Patient was started on metoprolol to help control heart rate and episodes of Afib. 5. Patient was discharged with ___ for assistance with medication management, CHF education, and telemonitoring for better assessment of weights. 6. Hgb remained 9 and platelets ~110 during hospitalization, likely ___ CLL, but would benefit from outpatient CBC re-draw at next outpatient primary care appointment. # CODE: FULL # EMERGENCY CONTACT: Name of health care proxy: ___ ___: wife Phone number: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Furosemide 20 mg PO DAILY 2. Aspirin 81 mg PO DAILY 3. Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY 4. Levothyroxine Sodium 25 mcg PO DAILY 5. Pantoprazole 20 mg PO Q24H 6. Simvastatin 20 mg PO QPM 7. Potassium Chloride 20 mEq PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted for weakness which was liekly due to dehydration. The dehydration was likely due to taking your lasix twice a day instead of once. We gave you some intravenous fluids to rehydrate you and held your lasix for a few days. You also had a few episodes of a rapid heart rate while you were here. We started you on a medication called metoprolol to control this. You should continue to take this medication as an outpatient. Ideally, we would have started you on a blood thinner to prevent a stroke in the future, but in light of your fall we think it would be too dangerous to start at this time. However, you should discuss this issue with your cardiologist on discharge. While you were here you also had a fall. This was likely due to your weakness and the rapid heart rate. There was no serious injury from this fall, but at home it will be very important to take care when walking around. Please use your walker or cane. Finally, it is important to take your weight every day. Quick changes of 3 pounds or more are caused by fluid loss or retention.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with history of CAD, T2DM, CHF (EF=30%), afib on Coumadin, with recent admission for cellulitis, transferred from ___ for vascular evaluation after MRI showed osteomyelitis. Vascular surgery and podiatry recommended ABI/PVR and based on these studies in addition to other co-morbidities, they recommended no surgical intervention and treatment with IV antibiotics. ACTIVE ISSUES ========= # Right leg cellulitis/R ___ toe osteomyelitis. As an outpatient since his last discharge, he had been on antibiotics Cefazolin, Cephalexin, TMP/SMX and ciprofloxacin (alternating due to various sensitivities). He did not tolerate Cephalexin or TMP/SMX and clinically worsened despite being on Ciprofloxacin. On admission, he improved dramatically with IV Vancomycin. Podiatry and Vascular surgery were consulted. He underwent ABI testing and based on these results, his multiple other co-morbidities and his clinical improvement on the IV Vancomycin, surgery was not recommended. ID was consulted, a PICC line was placed and he was set up with OPAT for a planned 2 week course of IV Vancomycin with continuation to PO regimen to be decided in outpatient ID follow up. His cellulitis dramatically improved and his toe was non-erythematous and non-tender by the time of discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES ========== #T2DM: Continued on ISS while inpatient. # CAD: Continued Atorvastatin 80 mg daily and Aspirin 81 mg daily # Chronic systolic CHF: Echo (___): (EF 30%, 1+AR,3+TR, 3+MR, mild pHTN). Continued home torsemide 10 mg daily. # Atrial Fibrillation: Pt was not on BB due to prior bradycardia. Continued home warfarin 3mg. INR was 2.3 on the day of discharge with next INR to be checked on ___. #Hypothyroidism: Continued home levothyroxine. # Insomnia: Continued home Temezepam Transitional Issues ============ # Osteomyelitis/Cellulitis - Enrolled in OPAT - Antibiotic: Vancomycin 1g q24 hrs - Start Date: ___ - End Date: ___ (may be extended per ID) - Please obtain weekly labs: CBC with differential, BUN, Cr, Vancomycin trough, ESR, CRP - Fax labs to attn: ___ CLINIC - FAX: ___ - All questions regarding outpatient parenteral antibiotics after discharge should be directed to the ___ R.N.s at ___ or to the on-call ID fellow when the clinic is closed. # Anticoagulation - Continued on home dose of warfarin 3mg. Recommend next INR to be drawn on ___, with results faxed to PCP ___. ___ @ ___ # Vitamin D Dosing: - Pt on both calcitriol and Vitamin D per home medications--consider whether both are necessary. # CODE: Full # CONTACT: ___ (Wife) ___
summary: You were transferred from ___ for further evaluation of your right toe osteomyelitis and cellulitis by the vascular surgeons and podiatrists. We continued you on Vancomycin while you were for treatment of your infection. We tested the blood flow to your foot which showed adequate blood flow. Based on the results of this test, the vascular surgeons and the podiatrists did not think that surgery was indicated and wanted you to continue with IV antibiotics. Your were seen by the infectious disease experts. You should continue IV antibiotics (Vancomycin) for at least 2 weeks (through ___ and then discuss with your infectious disease doctors about the need for continuing antibiotics further.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with history of CAD s/p STEMI with DES to OM, dyspnea of unknown etiology with normal PFTs, Alzheimer's and microvascular dementia, and known hematochezia ___ hemorrhoids, presents with BRBPR and continued dyspnea with resolving right-sided, musculoskeletal chest pain. . ACTIVE ISSUES . # BRBPR: Known internal hemorrhoids s/p hemorrhoidectomy and multiple bandings. His hematocrit was stable on admission, so this was not considered to be a hemodynamically significant bleed. He does have a history of diverticulosis, but the most likely source of this bleed is the hemorrhoids. His hematocrit was monitored and fell slightly, in the setting on continued bowel movements with bright red blood. He was watched closely and discharged with a hematocrit at a safe level. His Plavix was held on discharge and he should follow-up with surgery to discuss further management of his internal hemorrhoids. . # Chest pain (hx of CAD s/p MI): DES to OM and balloon angioplasty to diag, on ASA/Plavix with recent cardiac cath showing mild to moderate CAD without any intervention. His presentation ___ most consistent with musculoskeletal chest pain. Initial troponin <0.01 and EKG without any acute changes compared to prior. Cardiac enzymes were trended and within normal limits. His aspirin was continued, but his Plavix was held in the setting of GI bleeding. He was continued on his statin and B-blocker. . # Acute on chronic dyspnea and dCHF: Patient has had this complaint for some time now, with outpatient management consisting of management of diastolic heart failure (though EF 43%). Stress MIBI in ___ showed fixed inferior and inferolateral wall defect, unchanged from prior. Cardiac cath showed normal PCWP and mild pulmonary hypertension, with mild to moderate CAD. PFTs were done and unremarkable. He did appear volume overloaded on exam and with resolved pain, he is not splinting. He did not have an oxygen requirement and a relatively stable hematocrit ruled out anemia as a cause for SOB. BNP was slightly elevated, with baseline in the 600s, admitted with a level of 1203. However, previous BNP as an outpatient have also been in this range. He was continued on his home diuretic dosing, without any additional diuresis. His heart failure regimen was continued with metoprolol (at recently increased dose from 12.5 to 25mg daily), ASA, and a statin. . # Atrial fibrillation with CTA finding of chronic PEs: He is currently rate controlled on metoprolol, but not on any anticoagulation. Based on a chart review, this is due to his fall risk/peripheral neuropathy as well as spinal stenosis. CHADS2 of 3. We did not start any anticoagulation for now in the setting of his GI bleeding. Mild pulmonary hypertension on recent cath demonstrates the chronic PEs are not significant at this point. He will follow-up with Dr. ___ as an outpatient for further management of the chronic PEs and possibility of starting anticoagulation. . # Benign paroxysmal positional vertigo: Patient demonstrated classical signs of BPPV as an outpatient and continued to have them while hospitalized. He was discharged with home physical therapy for the purposes of vestibular training. . INACTIVE ISSUES . # Hypertension: He was continued on his home metoprolol, as above. . # Dyslipidemia: He was continued on his home dose statin. . # Alzheimer's/microvascular dementia: He was continued on donepezil/namenda. . # Peripheral neuropathy: He was continued on gabapentin. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES . # Follow-up: He will be following-up with Pulmonary to discuss management of his chronic PEs. This referral will be made by his PCP, ___. He will also be seeing Surgery about management of his hemorrhoids. He was sent home with physical therapy for vestibular training for his BPPV.
summary: You were admitted with increasing chest pain, mild shortness of breath, and bleeding from your hemorrhoids. We performed a scan of your lungs to be sure that you did not have a more serious cause for your shortness of breath. Our blood tests showed that you did not have any damage to your heart either. Your bleeding was well controlled and your blood counts were stable. The chest pain you described was worse with movement, so we believed that this was more of a muscle pain. You were discharged once your pain was under control and your bleeding had slowed down. The CT scan done in the Emergency Room showed some blockages in your pulmonary vessels. This will be followed as an outpatient by Dr. ___. We have not made any changes to your medications. Please continue to take them as prescribed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is an ___ year old ___-speaking male with past medical history of chronic diastolic CHF, mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, hypertension, GERD, recent admission ___ for acute diastolic CHF, admitted ___ with chest pain, found to have chest CT w 10mm suspected foreign body in RLL bronchus, s/p bronchoscopy for removal of foreign body (e.g. appearing to be a food bone), comfortable, recommended for modified diet (Soft solids and thin liquids, with aspiration precautions), able to be discharged home to complete course of antibiotics # Chest Pain / Dyspnea / RLL endobronchial foreign body: Admitted with chest pain and dyspnea; was ruled out for ACS and empirically diuresed for concern for heart failure; subsequent chest imaging showed RLL lung collapse and CT showed a 10 mm suspected foreign body in RLL bronchus, calcified density in RLL leading to consolidation in its subsegements, prompting transfer to medicine service. On ___, patient underwent rigid bronch/flex bronch + balloon and basket retrieval of what appeared to be an aspirated fragment of rib bone from food lodged in the RLL surrounded by granulation tissue. Specimen sent for analysis. Following the procedure, patient was breathing comfortably on RA. Seen by swallow therapy who recommended dysphagia diet with nectar thickened liquids. He was also started on a 14-day course of Amoxicillin-Clavulanic Acid ___ mg PO/NG Q12H (day 1: ___ to complete as an outpatient on ___. # Chronic Diastolic CHF: As above, patient was empirically treated for heart failure exacerbation, but likely did not have acute failure. Repeat TTE revealed ___ MR and TR, but did not show any progression of his EF or severity of his MR/TR. His home losartan was increased from 40mg daily to 80mg daily for afterload reduction. Discharge weight: 63.7 kg. # BPH: continued flomax # GERD: Continued on ranitidine. ============= TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ============= - Complete augmentin course: last day ___
summary: You were admitted to the ___ for chest pain. During your admission all of your laboratory and imaging studies were reassuring that you did not have a heart attack. However, we did note that your heart failure was not controlled well. We performed a procedure to examine your lungs more closely and found that your chest pain was due to an inhaled foreign body in your right lung. We medically managed your heart failure and our interventional pulmonary team removed the foreign body that you had accidentally inhaled. We also had our speech and swallow team evaluate you, and they recommended that your diet be modified to soft solids with nectar thickened liquids.

text: Brief Hospital Course: . ASSESSMENT: An ___ year-old woman with a history of aortic stenosis and hypertension presents with new atrial fibrillation. . #) Atrial fibrillation: Likely secondary to hypertension. CHADS score was 2 (age, hypertension). She was mildly symptomatic with increased SOB. Duration was unclear; no clear onset. Evaluation of possible etiologies included normal TSH and negative tox screen. She received diltiazem IV and PO in the ED with only brief improvement in HR. Her BP was initially >150. Rate control was changed to metoprolol, and she was given multiple IV and PO doses. Lisinopril and furosemide were stopped, and she was given IV fluids. She was guaiac negative and had no other contraindication to anti-coagulation, so she was started on a heparin drip. . After increasing doses of IV and PO metoprolol during her first 24 hours of hospitalization, blood pressure fell from 150s to ~120 systolic and heart rate remained in the 130s. Digoxin was loaded, and PO metoprolol continued. However, the patient subsequently began refusing all medications and telemetry (as below), so further treatment of her atrial fibrillation could not be undertaken. . #) Pump: She was apparently euvolemic. EF >55% on echo ___ at which time she was diagnosed with diastolic CHF. . #) Hypertension: She was initially hypertensive with BP 150s/100s. Lisinopril and furosemide were stopped to allow more room for rate control. Blood pressure fell into the 120s systolic with metoprolol uptitration. . #) History of EtOH abuse: Patient stated on admission she has recently only been drinking a maximum 1 glass wine daily, and that she was able to go for multiple days without drinking without adverse effect. However, she became agitated and left against medical advice. Her family suspected that this was because she wanted to drink alcohol. . #) GERD: PPI was continued. . #) Discharge against medical advice: The patient became agitated and began refusing vital signs and telemetry as well as all medications. Her sons were contacted and confirmed that she was at her baseline mental status. She was also seen by psychiatry, who deemed her competent to leave against medical advice. The risks of refusing medical care given her inadequately controlled atrial fibrillation were explained to her in detail (please see separate note in OMR). She understood this and decided to leave anyway. An appointment was made for her with her primary care physician, who was also made aware of the situation.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of an irregular and fast heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation. You received medications to make your heart beat more slowly. We had not yet controlled your rapid heart rate or high blood pressure, but you have decided to leave against medical advice. YOU HAVE CHOSEN TO LEAVE THE HOSPITAL AGAINST MEDICAL ADVICE. YOU WERE EVALUATED BY PSYCHIATRY WHO DETERMINED THAT YOU DO HAVE CAPACITY TO CHOOSE TO LEAVE AGAINST MEDICAL ADVICE. BY LEAVING, YOU ACKNOWLEDGE THAT YOU UNDERSTAND THE RISK OF STROKE, HEART ATTACK, AND DEATH THAT YOU COULD SUFFER AS A RESULT OF YOUR DECISION TO LEAVE THE HOSPITAL BEFORE OBTAINING APPROPRIATE THERAPY.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Acute on chronic CHF exacerbation: Pt presented with dyspnea, weight gain (approximately 10 lb since discharge on ___, elevated JVP, and ___ edema. His BNP was 6619. His chest xray showed evidence of pulmonary edema. Likely triggers included a UTI and excess fluid intake. Pt got 140 mg furosemide in the ED. His O2 saturation is 99% NC. When he arrived on the floor, he received another 140 mg IV furosemide bolus. He was then started on a furosemide drip at 10 mg/hr. He diuresed very well and on ___ his furosemide drip was decreased to 5 mg/hr. He was also started on dobutamine 2.5 mcg/kg/min drip given an elevated lactate and LFTs and concern for poor perfusion. Patient's isosorbide mononitrate was fractionated to isosorbide dinitrate (uptitrated during hospitalization) and hydralazine was also uptitrated. He was maintained on metoprolol. He was also started on losartan 12.5 mg per day. Patient became hypotensive and tachycardic on ___, at which time dobutamine was discontinued with normalization of vital signs. Cr increased on ___ and diuretics were held, PO intake was encouraged, and Cr improved. Patient was transitioned to PO Torsemide 140mg daily on ___. Discharge weight 133.6kg. # UTI: Pt with urinary symptoms and mildly positive UTI and UCx growing 10,000-100,000 organisms/ml of coagulase negative staph. He was treated with a five day ceftriaxone course (1 g IV Q24H, day ___, day ___. Patient had no further symptoms # Supertherapeutic INR: INR 4.3 on warfarin 7.5 mg daily on presentation. His warfarin was held for one day and was then restarted at his home 7.5 mg per day dose. INR was supratherapeutic prior to discharge. Warfarin dose was adjusted. Patient will have his INR re-drawn at the time of PCP ___ (___) with further dose adjustments as indicated. # Coronary Artery Disease: History of two-vessel coronary artery disease, LCx totally occluded and RCA with a distal 60% lesion, s/p PCI of LCx with drug eluting stents. Pt reported chronic upper epigastric pain in the ED which he said felt like GERD and not cardiac pain. He had no acute changes on EKG on arrival. Troponin mildly elevated to 0.03, but this appeared to be his baseline in the setting of CKD. He continued ASA, atorvastatin. Transitional Issues: # CHF: Patient with known systolic CHF with LVEF= ___, with severe hypokinesis to akinesis of the basal-mid inferior and inferolateral walls. Patient was started on losartan 12.5mg daily, and was intermittently on Lasix gtt and bolus (gtt d/c'd due to rising lactate, which downtrended after gtt discontinued). Isosorbide and hydralazine were uptitrated as tolerated. - Cardiac Medications: Amiodarone 200mg daily, Aspirin 81mg daily, Atorvastatin 80mg QHS, Digoxin 0.0625mg daily, Hydralazine 75mg TID, Isosorbide mononitrate ER 180mg daily, Losartan 12.5mg daily, Metoprolol XL 50mg daily, Torsemide 140mg daily, Warfarin 7.5mg daily. - Discharge weight 133.6kg. # Atrial Fibrillation: Patient takes Warfarin 7.5mg daily. Patient maintained on warfarin 7.5mg daily (decreased to 6mg x1 dose on ___ for INR 3.1). Discharged on warfarin 7.5mg daily. Patient follows with ___ recommend re-checking INR at the time of primary care ___ (___). - Recommend redraw ___ on ___ at PCP ___ # Medications Changes: Initiated Losartan 12.5mg daily, increased isosorbide mononitrate ER to 180mg daily, Hydralazine increased to 75mg daily. Metoprolol XL decreased to 50mg daily # Labs: - INR should be drawn on ___. Warfarin managed by ___ ___ clinic. - Please check chem7 on ___. Labs should be sent to ATTN: Dr. ___ at ___ ___. # Code: Full (confirmed) # Contact: patient, HCP ___ AND ___ (sisters) ___ AND ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for a heart failure exacerbation. You were given medications through your IV to help you increase the amount you urinate get rid your body of excess fluid. You were found to have a urinary tract infection which was treated with IV antibiotics (ceftriaxone). You increased your urine output as a result of the diuretics, and were able to rid your body of a large amount of excess fluid. Once your weight decreased, you were transitioned to pill diuretics which you will continue after discharge. You should also avoid fluid intake as much as possible and try to drink less than 2 liters of fluids per day.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and plan: ___ with history of anemia (sickle cell trait, chronic GI blood loss), 3+MR, ___ presents with acute blood loss anemia, melena and DOE concerning for acute GI bleeding superimposed on a mild CHF exacerbation. # Acute blood loss anemia, GI bleeding: On prior ___ from ___ pt had an AVM in ___ part of duodenum and evidence of acute heme on ___. She reports taking her ppi omeprazole 20 mg QAM at home. Has not had melena for years, but started over weekend. Was guaiac positive in the ED on rectal exam, but no further stools since admission. Patient and family discussed extensively the risks vs. benefits of EGD with GI team. Together, they decided that the likelihood of finding lesions that would require endoscopic intervention was low and that most likely the recommendation from EGD would be to increase her PPI dose. As she remained hemodynamically stable, on telemetry without event other than bradycardia with HR in the ___, held steady hemoglobin without further melena, it was decided to manage conservatively without EGD. Patient and family were in agreement. She received total of 3 units of PRBC with appropriate increase in Hb/ HCt from ___ to 7.8/25. On day of discharge was feeling well, denied lightheadedness, headache, shortness of breath. She was able to walk ___ nursing in the hall without issue and without increased dyspnea. O2 saturation was mid-high ___ on room air. She felt ready to return home. - home nursing to draw CBC and BMP and send results to Dr. ___ (colleague of pt's PCP ___ on ___ - f/u d/c appt w/ Dr. ___ on ___ - Has IV infusion of iron rescheduled for ___ # Acute on chronic diastolic CHF: If pt is having an exacerbation it is quite mild based on physical exam. No true JVD after having received 20 IV Lasix now. Pt states that she notes good UOP to her home Lasix dosing. Per pt she always has PND and this is unchanged. Reported breathing felt better than on admission. - restart home furosemide today # consolidation on CXR: per radiology, could be asymmetric edema but could not r/o pneumonia. Did not endorse pna symptoms so did not give abx. No leukocytosis, no fevers. # tinnitus, occasional dizziness: pt endorses constant sound, can be high pitched. Denies pulsation, whooshing, or correlation with her heart beat. Has been occuring for several weeks and she was seen at one time for ear pain by her PCP. may be Meniere's, may be improved with diuresis. - consider ENT referral and head imaging as outpatient if persistent # HLD: Cont home statin. # Htn: held home nifedipine given anemia, GI bleeding on admission but restarted at discharge as she was hemodynamically stable. # Insomnia: Cont home amitriptyline # DNR DNI # PPX: teds # dispo: home w/ ___
summary: You were admitted for melena, which is dark stool with blood from a source of bleeding in the intestines or stomach. Your hemoglobin and hematocrit had dropped well below your normal. You were given 3 units of blood and your blood levels improved to hemoglobin of 7.8 and hematocrit of 25.2. Your normal levels are hemoglobin about 6.5 and hematocrit 20. You were seen by the GI doctors who explained that it is possible that even with an endoscopic exam, we might not find anything that needed any other type of treatment than an increased dose of your omeprazole. Together you and your family and the GI team agreed that the risks of the procedure were not worth doing the procedure because of the likelihood of not finding a bleeding lesion that needed endoscopic treatment.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ G1P1 POD# ___ s/p primary LTCS at 36w4d for PPROM/chorio who p/w chest tightness and SOB . 1. Respiratory distress: Given the evidence of pulmonary edema seen on CXR, this is likely CHF, possibly ___ post-partum cardiomyopathy. However, there was concern as well for PE given her post-op state as a risk factor. Significant effort was undertaken to get an IV and draw labs (see below) but the patient left AMA before this could be done. She received Lasix 20mg po qd with good urine output. She left AMA before a followup CXR could be done. She was advised to get a TTE as an outpatient. Given her anxiety, there may be a psychiatric component to this as well. 2. Chest pain: This was likely ___ CHF as above. ECG showed non-specific ST/T changes. Attempts were made to draw labs to rule out MI (see below) but she left AMA before this could be done. She was monitored on telemetry and had one five-beat run of NSVT but otherwise had no events. 3. s/p C-section: OB team followed her while in-house. They recommended labs including LFTs, CBC, uric acid, Cr, but these could not be drawn before she left AMA. Incisional pain control was achieved with Dilaudid. She received ativan and xanax on ___ in an attempt to place an IV (see below), so she was advised not to breast feed for 24 hours. # anxiety: The patient has significant needle phobia and refused multiple attempts at blood draws and IV placement. A significant amount of time was spent trying to convince her to allow IV placement. She received a total of 0.5mg Xanax and 3mg po Ativan ___ throughout the day, and by the end of the day she allowed 2 attempts at IV placement and lab draws, which both failed. She agreed to stay overnight to be monitored on telemetry, and the following morning after Dilaudid for pain control, she agreed to another IV placement attempt. However, she changed her mind again and demanded to leave AMA. She voiced understanding of the risks of leaving, including death, without ruling out conditions such as PE or MI. She was strongly advised to follow up with her PCP as soon as possible and to get an echocardiogram.
summary: You were admitted with symptoms of shortness of breath and chest pressure. This may be due to fluid overload, and you were given two doses of Lasix. Your anxiety with regards to needles required a significant amount of time and medications including benzodiazepines to enable you to allow us to attempt IV placement and blood draws. Despite this, two attempts were unsuccessful. Because of your refusal to allow further attempts at IV placement or phlebotomy, we have been unable to rule out life-threatening conditions such as a pulmonary embolus or heart attack. You have decided to leave against medical advice. You should not breast feed for 24 hours due to the medications (benzodiazepines) that you received yesterday.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with history of anxiety and ?cardiac conduction abnormalities of unknown significance presents s/p fall and LOC after an episode of profound, non-exertional chest pain and tightness. On echo, he was found to have a depressed EF of 45% and global ventricular dilatation. . #. Fall with loss of consciousness: From the history, it is still unclear as to how long he was unconscious for. He received a full trauma work-up upon admission, with full body CT scan. From the negative trauma work-up, it is probably safe to say the cause of his LOC was biological rather than mechanical. Patient has been taking citalopram for a number of months, which can both lower the seizure threshold and prolong the QT interval leading to torsades, but neither of these etiologies seemed applicable in this case. Neurology and electrophysiology were both involved in his case. EEG over 24 hours did not show any epileptiform changes. . #. Chest pain: His pain did recur once during admission, but did not result in loss of consciousness. It was not associated with any shortness of breath or diaphoresis. He did explain a previous history of "regurgitation" when eating, with an esophageal study in the past, only showing a hiatal hernia and no apparent dysmotility. He is somewhat anxious at baseline and a Psychiatry consult was called to help assess any pscyhiatric causes of his chest pain (i.e. panic attack), but they did not indicate any. An echocardiogram was recommended by the Cardiology team and it showed an ejection fraction of 45% and global dilatation of the ventricles. He will be followed closely by the electrophysiology team as an outpatient with cardiac MRI and follow-up appointments to further explore this structural abnormality and work-up this apparent dilated cardiomyopathy. We also recommended following up with Gastroenterology to investigate his regurgitation symptoms and have set up an appointment for him at ___. . #. Follow-up: ___ be establishing care here at ___ after his hospitalization with a number of new physicians. He understands that we are still not sure as to why he lost consciousness and knows that it will be important to continue seeing the cardiologists to manage and work up his suspected cardiomyopathy and visit Gastroenterology to address his regurgitation symptoms. He was instructed to continue on citalopram and omeprazole. His PCP and GI appointments are in ___, and cardiac MRI and EP appointments are in ___.
summary: You were admitted after you felt chest pain, fainted, and fell down a flight of stairs. You received CT scans of your head, chest, and abdomen to make sure you were not bleeding anywhere from your fall. There was none. The Emergency Room physicians placed ___ breathing tube and you were admitted to the intensive care unit. You were evaluated by the Neurology service. An MRI of your brain was done and it was normal without evidence of stroke or focus of seizure. An EEG was performed and there was no evidence of seizures. You were evaluted by the Cardiology (electrophysiology) service. We performed an echocardiogram, which showed an abnormal reduction in the heart's pumping ability, which will require further evaluation with the cardiologists.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o M ischemic cardiomyopathy EF 15%, now s/p VT/VF arrest transferred with cardiogenic shock. # Shock/Arrest: The patient appeared to have VF with evolution to VT throughout his ACLS treatment at the OSH. The patient had relatively flat biomarkers and no evidence of new ischemia. The likely etiology is scar mediated VT/VF. The patient arrived with pressor support of dopamine and norepinephrine. The patient quickly had his pressors weaned to off and he remained hemodynamicaly stable. He has been intubated for airway protection at the outside hospital. He required minimal respiratory support and was extubated on ___ without complication. He was started on amiodarone while he was inpatient and was discharged with instructions to continue taking this. The patient also underwent ICD placement prior to discharge. # CAD: The patient has 2 vessel CAD with a DES in his RCA. He was continued on aspirin, clopidogrel and statin. He originally was started on heparin gtt and nitroglycerin. The nitroglycerine drip was weaned off, considering an ischemic process was less likely. While the patient did have a positive troponin, his CK's were negative. # Cardiomyopathy: The patient has a history of ischemic cardiomyopathy with an EF of 15%. Echo was performed and showed severely dilated LV with apical aneurysm and severe global hypokinesis and new dilation with free wall hypokinesis in the RV (see above for full report). He underwent ICD placement prior to discharge. # LV Thrombus: The patient has a history of LV thrombus, for which he is on coumadin. However, there was no thrombus seen on repeat echo's. On INR was initially supratherapeutic on presenation, and his coumadin was help. He was on a heparin gtt when his INR then became subtherapeutic. He was discharged on coumadin. # Altered Mental Status: Once extubated and weaned off of sedation, the patient was noted to have some depressed mental status. CT head did not show any acute process. MRI head showed findings consistent with microangiopathic small vessel disease and alchoholism (see above for complete report). By the time of discharge, the patient's mental status was improving. He was seen by neurology, who felt that his symptoms were common in the post-arrest period. However, they recommended outpatient evaluation by cognitive neurology if he did not continue to improve. They also recommended continued anticoagulation for stroke prophylaxis in setting of low ejection fraction. # Diabetes: Finger sticks have improved to normal range. Does not require insulin at home but poorly controlled on oral agents. The patient was placed on an insulin sliding scale while in house. He was discharged on his diabetic regimen. # Elevated LFT's: Likely shock liver in the setting of his cardiac arrest. LFTs were improving at the time of d/c. # Acute Renal Failure: Likely ATN in the setting of his cardiac arrest. Creatinine was improving at the time of d/c.
summary: You had a cardiac arrest that was due to an abnormal rhythm. You were brought to ___ for treatment and was started on amiodarone to prevent abnormal rhythms. You also received an internal defibrillator that will shock your heart into a normal rhythm if this happens again. No showers for 3 days after you get home, you can then take off the bandage and shower, leave the steri strips in place. Avoid soaps or sprays directly on the steri strips. No lifting your left arm over your head or lift more than 5 pounds for 6 weeks. You cannot drive for 6 months per ___ law. Please discuss driving with Dr. ___ Dr. ___ 6 months.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with pmhx of likely metastatic lung cancer, CAD, sCHF Ef 35%, pathologic C2 Fx s/p fixation, ESRD on HD, p/w acute SOB. . # Acute-on-chronic Systolic CHF - much improved after HD x 2 during this admission. 4.5 liters (~10 lbs) of UF removed. Continued medical CHF regimen with ACE-I, BB. Strict I/O's and daily weights. Low Na diet emphasized . # ___ - CK, CK MB flat. Trop T at baseline (ESRD). Continued medical therapy with ___, statin, BB, ACE, nitrate. monitored on telemetry. . # ESRD on HD - underwent HD in house. . # Parotiditis - appears improved. C$ontinue outpt vancomycin IV qHD, levofloxacin q48h. ENT consulted, scheduled for outpt f/u . # C Diff - continued flagyl PO, changed from tid to bid given renal dysfunction. Pt is to take for 2 weeks following d/c of vanc and levo. maintained contact precautions. . # C2 Fracture- s/p fusion. continued hard collar. xrays stable. continued outpt pain control with oxyCONTIN q8h and oxycodone prn . # Met NSCLC - given pain control as above, deferred chemo plans to outpt onc team . # HTN - continued OP meds . # prophylaxis: sc heparin, bowel regimen, PPI
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with an exacerbation of your heart failure. You were treated with IV lasix followed by your regularly scheduled hemodialysis, where 4.5 liters (almost 9 pounds!) of fluid was removed. This quickly corrected your fluid overload and your shortness of breath disappeared. You had dialysis a second time with almost 4 liters of fluid removed. At discharge, you were able to walk hundreds of feet in the hallways without becoming short of breath. At your next dialysis session on ___, you should have your blood counts checked, as you may need at transfusion if your blood counts are low. The ENT doctors saw your for the parotiditis (cheek swelling/ infection) and thought that you looked much better. They would like you to see them as an outpatient.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with pmhx of likely metastatic lung cancer, CAD, sCHF Ef 35%, pathologic C2 Fx s/p fixation, ESRD on HD, p/w acute SOB. . # Acute-on-chronic Systolic CHF - much improved after HD x 2 during this admission. 4.5 liters (~10 lbs) of UF removed. Continued medical CHF regimen with ACE-I, BB. Strict I/O's and daily weights. Low Na diet emphasized . # ___ - CK, CK MB flat. Trop T at baseline (ESRD). Continued medical therapy with ___, statin, BB, ACE, nitrate. monitored on telemetry. . # ESRD on HD - underwent HD in house. . # Parotiditis - appears improved. C$ontinue outpt vancomycin IV qHD, levofloxacin q48h. ENT consulted, scheduled for outpt f/u . # C Diff - continued flagyl PO, changed from tid to bid given renal dysfunction. Pt is to take for 2 weeks following d/c of vanc and levo. maintained contact precautions. . # C2 Fracture- s/p fusion. continued hard collar. xrays stable. continued outpt pain control with oxyCONTIN q8h and oxycodone prn . # Met NSCLC - given pain control as above, deferred chemo plans to outpt onc team . # HTN - continued OP meds . # prophylaxis: sc heparin, bowel regimen, PPI

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ PMH of Rheumatic heart disease (s/p MVR - bioprosthetic ___, AFib (s/p MAZE, PPM, on A/C), HTN, HLD, CAD (s/p CABG and PCI), CKD (Baseline Cr 2.1) who presents from clinic for hypokalemia/hyponatremia with volume overload. #Hyponatremia: Pt presented w/ hyponatremia in setting of poor PO intake and recent wt loss of 7lbs in less than 1 week after augmenting diuretic regimen to Torsemide BID + metolazone. Urine Lytes w/ Na of 24 and Osm of >200 despite diuretics taken today at home. Given hypoosmolar, wt loss, and urine lytes, it was suspected that this was due to overdiuresis. Fortunately, his hyponatremia was asymptomatic. Slow correction was begun, first with 1L NS bolus. Then, he was slowly corrected with diuresis with resolution. #Hypokalemia: Resolved. As above, suspected to be due to overdiuresis. He had no hx of GI losses. Transcellular shift possible given elevated HCO3. Given his known CKD, repletion was performed cautiously with q6h BMPs and repletion with PO potassium. Upon resolution, lytes were monitored and repleted on a BID basis during active diuresis. #Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: Pt has hx of dCHF from severe/wide open TR, w/ evidence of volume overload on exam at initial presentation (2+ edema in lower extremities and cXR w/ some pulmonary edema). Diuresis was begun after initial correction of potassium. He was initially started on lasix bolus at 80mg IV + infusion at 5mg/hr. Initial weight was 72.9kg. He had excellent diuresis on 5mg/hr lasix infusion without bolus therapy. He was then transitioned to PO torsemide at 40mg QAM and 20mg QPM. He was continued on toprolXL 50mg converted to metoprolol tartrate dosage of 12.5mg q6h. Weight upon discharge was 62kg. #CAD s/p CABG and PCI w/ stenting in ___ (LCA, ___ OM): He was continued on metoprolol and atorvastatin with sl NTG prn. He was chest pain free during his hospitalization. #Positive UA: Pt has chronic foley ___ urinary retention with foley changed every 5 weeks. UA on admission w/ >150 WBC but negative nitrite which may be ___ chronic inflammation from indwelling foley, or evidence of infection. Culture demonstrated ecoli + enterococcus. Pt is afebrile with borderline WBC. Given clinical stability, foley was replaced, as he was due for replacement, and repeat cultures were obtained. Of noted, during catheter replacement (on ___, he received a one time dose of ciprofloxacin due to instrumentation. Repeat cultures were drawn after foley changed and are also growing mixed e.coli and enterococcus (full speciation). As patient was afebrile without symptoms, this was felt likley colonization. #AFib: S/P maze w/ dual chamber PPM most recently. Initially warfarin was held due to supratherapeutic INR (initially at 3.7). Coags were trended. Once trending into the therapeutic range, he was restarted on home wafarin at 0.5mg. #Hypertension: stable during his hospitalization. He was continued on his metoprolol as above. #Hyperlipidemia: Continued on atorvastatin #Emphysema: He was breathing unlabored on room air. Sybicort was held given non-formulary but continued on duonebs. #Urinary Retention: Pt has hx of BPH and chronic foley since ___, w/ foley changes q5weeks. Foley was changed during this hospitalization on ___. He was continued on flomax/proscar. He will need GU follow up on discharge. #RUE swelling: Given asymmetrical swelling w/ edema it is c/f DVT, however, recent U/S was negative for DVT. As diuresis was continued, swelling decreased substantially. #Constipation: No bowel movement for a week. He was given fleet enema with good effect. Transition Issues: []Please titrate diuretics (discharged on torsemide 40mg BID) []Please check electrolytes on ___ and ___ and then q2-3 days. He is being started on spironolactone so please follow K closely. []Follow up with urology regarding foley change in 6 weeks (changed ___ []Discharge weight: 62kg []f/u urine culture from ___ Contact: Daughter ___ CODE: FULL Confirmed
summary: You were recently hospitalized at the ___ ___ for low sodium and low potassium levels. These are electrolytes in your blood that help your body function. In addition, you were also treated for a congestive heart failure exacerbation or a condition where fluid builds up in your body making it more difficult to breathe. For your low potassium, you were given additional potassium. For your low sodium, we treated you by removing additional fluid from your body with a continuously running medication known as lasix. We were able to switch this back to your home torsemide after removing lots of fluid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yo M with h/o AS, dual chamber PPM for sinus and AV nodal dysfunction (___), CHF (dx in ___, hypertension, and dyslipidemia who presented with worsening dyspnea both at rest and on exertion and orthopnea after failure of PO Lasix. He was admitted for evaluation of AS, CHF exacerbation, IV diuresis, and work-up for AVR. #Acute on chronic diastolic and systolic HF: Pt volume overloaded on admission refractory to 80mg PO Lasix. He responded well to Lasix 40mg IV and torsemide 40mg PO. Diastolic dysfunction most likely due to LVH ___ to long term HTN and AS. Worsening AS may have precipitated this most recent decompensation. Aortic valve diameter 0.9 on ___ cath, which also showed moderate single vessel disease, mod-severe AS and MR. ___ of ~50- 55 % on TTE. His renal function was stable during admission. Surgical AVR vs TAVR workup: CT chest showed extensive atherosclerotic disease and bilat pleural effusions. Carotid US ___ showed significant plaque with bilateral 60-69 percent carotid stenosis. Pt deemed appropriate for surgical AVR and scheduled for ___ with Dr. ___ at 0700. #HLD: Continued home aspirin and atorvastatin. #Symptomatic sinus/AV nodal dysfunction, PPM in place. NSR on this admission and asymptomatic. Transitional Issues: # CODE: Full # CONTACT: ___ (daughter, HCP) ___. # Candidate for surgical AVR, scheduled for ___ with Dr. ___ at 7:00 AM. # started on torsemide 40 mg daily # f/u with PCP to monitor renal function on torsemide # ___ and OT consulted and cleared patient to go home with services and a very supportive family.
summary: You were admitted to the ___ for concerns over your worsening shortness of breath and leg swelling. It was determined that your congested hearth failure (CHR) was causing these symptoms as well as the narrowing of your aortic valve (aortic stenosis). Our team gave you your Lasix through a vein and tried similar medication called torsemide, which took some fluid off your lungs and allowed you to breath better. Our cardiothoracic surgery team met with you and did some tests and procedures, including the heart catheterization, to determine if you would be a candidate for a surgical aortic valve replacement or a TAVR (transcatheter aortic valve replacement). You will need to meet with a second surgical team after you leave the hospital to complete this work-up.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with DM, CAD, cirrhosis, h/o pancreatitis, and Crohns admitted for abdominal pain. . ABDOMINAL PAIN: Patient with left flank and left upper quadrant pain that radiated around back. On exam, she had muscle spasm. CT shows cecal inflammation but no clear explanation for the pain. No hematuria to suggest a renal stone. Not obstructed. No evidence of mesenteric ischemia, as pain was not worse with eating and lactate normal. No evidence of pancreatitis flair. C.diff negative. Pain appeared to be multifactorial, including muscle spasm, constipation, and chronic abdominal pain with possible mild Crohn's flair. She was continued on home narcotics regimen and given flexeril for muscle spasm and an aggressive bowel regimen. She received a short course of antibiotics. Her pain was somewhat improved on discharge. - Would consider follow up MRI as patient lost IV access and was not able to complete the study as an impatient. . ANEMIA: Patient remained at baseline Hct of ___. She has a h/o GIB of unclear source (may have been hemorrhoidal) and chronically G+ stool with known diverticula, prior PUD but no varices. She had no evidence active bleeding. . DIABETES: She was continued on insulin. . CORONARY ARTERY DISEASE: s/p BMS to RCA in ___. No evidence active ischemia. Continues ASA, statin, beta-blocker, and ___. . Chronic diastolic HEART FAILURE: Mildly dry-to-euvolemic on presentation. Continued ___, beta-blocker. . CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE: Creatinine trended up slightly during admission ___ poop PO intake. FeNa of 5.5% argued against pre-renal failure as exclusive cause. Her baseline Cr 1.1-1.7. Her Cr trended down with some gentle hydration. . CHRONIC PAIN: Continued narcotic contract and gabapentin, duloxetine. She was discharged with Rx for percocet as there is a national shortage of oxycodone. This change was approved by her PCP. . RIGHT KNEE WOUND: She was seen by wound care consult who found knee to be healing well. She will follow up as an outpatient. . Code full Communication sister ___ ___ ___ on Admission: Amylase-Lipase-Protease 33,200-10,000- 37,500 unit Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: Two (2) Cap PO TID W/MEALS (3 TIMES A DAY WITH MEALS). Duloxetine 30 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: Two (2) Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO DAILY (Daily). Folic Acid 1 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). Gabapentin 300 mg Capsule Sig: Two (2) Capsule PO three times a day. Mesalamine 400 mg Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) Sig: Four (4) Tablet, Delayed Release (E.C.) PO TID (3 times a day). Omeprazole 20 mg Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) Sig: One (1) Capsule, Delayed Release(E.C.) PO DAILY (Daily). Hydroxyzine HCl 25 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a day as needed for itching. Insulin Glargine 100 unit/mL Solution Sig: Forty (40) Units Subcutaneous qHS. Simvastatin 10 mg Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). Aspirin 325 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). Oxycodone 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO Q6H (every 6 hours) as needed for pain. Oxycodone 20 mg Tablet Sustained Release 12 hr Sig: One (1) Tablet Sustained Release 12 hr PO Q12H (every 12 hours). Zolpidem 5 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO at bedtime as needed for insomnia. Multivitamin Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO DAILY (Daily). FerrouSul 325 mg (65 mg Iron) Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a day. Vitamin D 400 unit Tablet Sig: Two (2) Tablet PO once a day. Valsartan 40 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO once a day. Metoprolol Tartrate 50 mg Tablet Sig: One (1) Tablet PO twice a day
summary: You were admitted for abdominal and back pain. This pain was possbily related to your chronic pancreatitis or chrons disease. Your back pain was likely related to muscle spasm. You were started on a muscle relaxant to treat this. You should continue pain medications as per your pain contract, but can substitute percocet for oxycodone. Percocet contains Tylenol and you should be careful not to have more than ___ mg of Tylenol daily, as this can be toxic for your liver. Outpatient physical therapy may also help you with your back pain. If you have further problems with constipation, you can try colace and senna.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old male with atrial fibrillation, HTN, diastolic heart failure and prostate CA s/p distant XRT, who presents with hemturia and anemia. HEMATURIA ANEMIA, ACUTE BLOOD LOSS Pt was seen by Urology and initially started on CBI, however his urine did not clear despite CBI. He was then taken to the OR for cystoscopy which revealed mucosal changes c/w radiation cystitis. Fulguration was done, as well as clot evacuation and bladder stone removal. Post-cystoscopy, CBI was continued, followed by a clamp and void trial, which the patient unfortunately failed. A Coude catheter had to be placed, with drainage of clear urine, without evidence of hemtauria or clot. As such, Urology recommended that Foley be left in place till f/u with outpt Urology. Of note, pt's anemia remained stable during the hospitalization, so PRBC's were NOT transfused. HCT on discharge= 26.4 HEART FAILURE, DIASTOLIC No signs or symptoms of heart failure currently and given hematuria with intermittent obstruction, Lasix was held. The patient developed hypontatremia, likey due to hypervolemia. He was restarted on his home lasix dose with improvement in his sodium from 126->130 on discharge ACUTE RENAL FAILURE Due to intermittent bladder outlet obstruction leading to obstructive nephropathy, as proved with initiation of CBI, his ___ resolved quickly. ACEi was initially held, but has been restarted once creatinine returned to baseline and creatinine continues to be stable. . ATRIAL FIBRILLATION Currently on Digoxin. Level was checked and within limits. ASA was initially continued for anticoagulation, but then discontinued due to persistent hematuria. Urology recommends holding ASA x 1 week after discharge. HYPERTENSION, BENIGN Lasix and lisinopril initially held. However, once ___ resolved, lisinopril has been restarted and BP remains in good range. LEUKOCYTOSIS The patient developed leukocytosis on ___ a urine culture was sent and was negative. The patient had no signs or symptoms of infection. He specifically denied cough, shortness of breath, dysuria, rash, fever or chills and he was not noted to be febrile. He was therefore not started on antibiotics, and was advised to follow up for a repeat CBC and for infectious symptoms. Code status: The patient was DNR/DNI during this hospitalization. Transitional issues: - please follow sodium, white blood cell count and hemoglobin on follow up - bladder stone pathology pending - will need to follow up with Urology for voiding trial
summary: You came to the hospital with persistent bloody urine (hematuria) with large clots in your Foley catheter. Due to the obstruction of your bladder from the clots, you had some mild acute injury of your kidney (___), although this has now resolved. You were initially treated with saline irrigation of your bladder, but you failed to clear the clots, so you were taken to the Operating Room by the Urologists to undergo cystoscopy, which revealed an irritable bladder wall, most likely due to your previous radiation therapy for your prostate cancer. This condition is called radiation cystitis. You were also found to have a bladder stone, which could have may the bleeding worse, and this stone was removed. After cystoscopy, you had persistent urinary retention, so a Foley catheter was placed, which drained clear urine and did not show evidence of active bleeding. Urology recommends that you leave the catheter in place until outpatient follow-up. You also developed a low sodium level. This is likely due to your furosemide being held. This was resumed prior to discharge. Your primary care physician should follow your sodium level. Your white blood cell count was also elevated. You had no signs of infection so you were not started on antibiotics.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of TBI, seizures, HTN, and pituitary adenoma s/p resection in ___ complicated by blindness, adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism who presented with ongoing ___ edema and dyspnea after recent admission for the same 1 month earlier. ___ Edema: Patient was recently admitted 1 month earlier with this same complaint ___ edema and dyspnea and was treated as new diastolic heart failure (TTE with EF > 60%) and started on Lasix which was ultimately uptitrated to 100 mg BID. She was also on 50 mg daily spironolactone. On presentation, she reported that her legs were increasingly heavy and she still had dyspnea/orthopnea. Her BNP was 23 and a chest X ray was negative for pulmonary edema. She did not have notable pitting edema although clinical exam was limited by morbid obesity. Her TSH was normal and per her outpatient endocrinologist, her dosing of steroids was unlikely to be contributing to edema. Her "heavy legs" may have to do with muscular weakness in the setting of deconditioning. She was discharged to rehab to continue building strength. It may be worth considering if her hydrocortisone dosing is too low, contributing to her weakness. She was continued on her home diuretics and set up with cardiology follow up. #Dyspnea/orthopnea: Patient with recent admission for new diagnosis of diastolic CHF as above but no signs of volume overload on chest X ray. Her dyspnea was thought to be due to OSA causing nocturnal symptoms prompting her complaint of orthopnea. She refuses to use CPAP so was recommended to use nocturnal O2. She had no evidence of ischemia on ECG and had negative troponin/CK-MB. Transitional issues: [ ] Discharge weight 102.6 kg [ ] Please follow up hyperdensity noticed in pancreatic tail on CT during previous admission (CT A/P ___ [ ] recommended use of TEDs [ ] continued patient's home Lasix 100MG PO BID [ ] monitor renal function / creatinine, K every other day while on Lasix [ ] avoid amlodipine as may worsen edema [ ] please follow up C diff as patient was having loose stools; please hold all stool softeners [ ] can consider neurology work up for chronic ___ weakness/heaviness if not improving with ___ and no other etiology found [ ] recommend home O2 use for symptomatic relief [ ] ___ verified by attending
summary: You were recently admitted to the hospital ___ for swelling in your legs and shortness of breathing, particularly when you lie flat. You have previously had these symptoms but the swelling had not improved on diuretics. During this admission, we continued to monitor your edema while on your diuretics and used compression stockings to reduce the swelling. To understand the cause of your symptoms, your thyroid function was assessed and found to be normal. Steroid use is a necessary part of your care but may be one reason for your weight gain. Deconditioning related to immobility may also contribute to this weight gain. Please continue your ___ as recommended on discharge. We also suggest that you continue use of O2 through nasal cannula for symptom relief.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is a ___ year old woman with a history of TBI, seizures, HTN, and pituitary adenoma s/p resection in ___ complicated by blindness, adrenal insufficiency and hypothyroidism who presented with ongoing ___ edema and dyspnea after recent admission for the same 1 month earlier. ___ Edema: Patient was recently admitted 1 month earlier with this same complaint ___ edema and dyspnea and was treated as new diastolic heart failure (TTE with EF > 60%) and started on Lasix which was ultimately uptitrated to 100 mg BID. She was also on 50 mg daily spironolactone. On presentation, she reported that her legs were increasingly heavy and she still had dyspnea/orthopnea. Her BNP was 23 and a chest X ray was negative for pulmonary edema. She did not have notable pitting edema although clinical exam was limited by morbid obesity. Her TSH was normal and per her outpatient endocrinologist, her dosing of steroids was unlikely to be contributing to edema. Her "heavy legs" may have to do with muscular weakness in the setting of deconditioning. She was discharged to rehab to continue building strength. It may be worth considering if her hydrocortisone dosing is too low, contributing to her weakness. She was continued on her home diuretics and set up with cardiology follow up. #Dyspnea/orthopnea: Patient with recent admission for new diagnosis of diastolic CHF as above but no signs of volume overload on chest X ray. Her dyspnea was thought to be due to OSA causing nocturnal symptoms prompting her complaint of orthopnea. She refuses to use CPAP so was recommended to use nocturnal O2. She had no evidence of ischemia on ECG and had negative troponin/CK-MB. Transitional issues: [ ] Discharge weight 102.6 kg [ ] Please follow up hyperdensity noticed in pancreatic tail on CT during previous admission (CT A/P ___ [ ] recommended use of TEDs [ ] continued patient's home Lasix 100MG PO BID [ ] monitor renal function / creatinine, K every other day while on Lasix [ ] avoid amlodipine as may worsen edema [ ] please follow up C diff as patient was having loose stools; please hold all stool softeners [ ] can consider neurology work up for chronic ___ weakness/heaviness if not improving with ___ and no other etiology found [ ] recommend home O2 use for symptomatic relief [ ] ___ verified by attending

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ is a ___ yo widowed woman (___ Witness member who would accept transfusion of her own blood for cardiopulmonary bypass surgery, but would not accept blood products from others) with known severe aortic stenosis (AVA0.7-0.8 cm2 and grad 71/46 mmHg in ___ status post TAVR with ___ without complication. # Severe symptomatic aortic stenosis ___ 0.6cm2, mean gradient 55mmHg) s/p TAVR with ___ Patient received ___ on ___ without complication. Post procedure patient was continued on prophylactic Vanc 1000 mg IV Q 24 hours and cefazolin Q8 hours for 24 hours. Her serum glucose was closely monitored and kept below 180 24 hours post-procedure. Initially following the procedure nitro gtt 0.05 mcg/kg/min titrated to SBP <160 and phenylephrine 0.5 mcg/kg/ min titrated to MAP > 60 were used. The patient was given aspirin 325 mg and 300 mg plavix load prior to procedure and discharged with 81 mg aspirin indefinately and 75 mg plavix daily for 3 months completing ___. # CAD - s/p BMS-LAD (___) Patient continued on 81 mg daily. Atorvostatin 40 mg started and continued on discharge. # Acute on chronic diastolic/systolic CHF (LVEF > 55%). Following stabilization of blood pressure 24 hours post-procedure the patients valsartan restarted at 50 mg daily and amlodipine 5 mg daily. lasix 40 mg daily restarted prior to discharge. Metoprolol 12.5 mg BID also started prior to discharge. # Acute kidney injury post-cardiac catheterization up to 1.6 now downtrended to 1.0 - monitor I/O's closely - trend Cr # Anemia The patient received Procrit 2 months prior to procedure given she is ___. Ferrous sulfate restarted prior to discharge. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ========================= -plavix 75 mg should be taken daily for 3 months post TAVR procedure -aspirin 81 mg should be taken indefinately -metoprolol 12.5 BID was started during this hospitalization -atorvostatin 40 mg daily was started during this hospitalization -amlodipine was decreased to 5 mg daily, from 10 mg daily -valsartan was decreased to 50 mg daily, from 100 mg daily -lasix was changed to 40 mg daily, from 20 mg TID -follow up renal function and electrolytes -follow up blood pressure control (consider uptitrating valsartan and amlodipine if needed for better blood pressure control)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for severe aortic stenosis. You had been evaluated by cardiac surgeons and deemed to be of Extreme risk for conventional surgical aortic valve replacement. You were screened for transcatheter aortic valve replacement and found to be a candidate. The procedure was completed without difficulty. It is very important that you take 81 mg aspirin every day. It is also important that you take plavix 75 mg everyday for the next 3 months. You did not receive any blood products during your stay. Please note that it takes your heart muscle ___ weeks to adjust to the new valve.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yoF with diastolic CHF on 2L home O2, DM, HTN, pAfib on coumadin s/p pacemaker, DM and OSA who presented with chest pain and neck pain. # Chest pain: She presented with acute onset of right sided chest pain and upper back pain. There was initial concern for aortic dissection and CT chest was done (without contrast due to renal function) which showed no displacement of her aortic calcifications and therefore dissection was unlikely. She did have a pleuritic component to her chest pain and PE was considered, but her INR was therapeutic on admission and she had reproducibility of her neck pain with palpation so this was felt less likely. Her pain resolved shortly after admission and did not recur. She was ruled out for MI and monitored on telemetry. # Mediastinal lymphadenopathy: Was found to have extensive lymphadenopathy on chest CT and an enlarged neck LN on thyroid ultrasound. Abdominal CT showed no signs of malignancy other than likely IPMN in her pancreas and showed no abdominal lymphadenopathy. She had a workup for causes of enlarged lymph node. CBC showed that she is slightly more anemia than previous, but WBC/diff were unremarkable. LDH was not elevated. HIV was negative. LFTs and RPR were normal. EBV serologies and ___ are pending. She should have an outpatient PPD vs quantiferon gold testing to rule out TB as a cause. If her lymphadenopathy does not improve, she will need a lymph node biopsy to rule out occult malignancy. This was discussed verbally with her PCP and the patient. # Acute on chronic diastolic CHF: Was found to have acute pulmonary edema on CT chest (although CXR looked improved) and she endorsed mild worsening of her chronic SOB. She is on home 2L O2 which was continued. She was kept on her home torsemide and diuresed with IV lasix as needed for SOB. Also continued on home BB, ___, CCB. # Afib: Paroxysmal but none recently. CHADS score 3, on coumadin. INR mildly supratherapeutic at 3.2 on admission but returned to therapeutic range on home dosing. # Anemia: Hct ___ which is worse than recent baseline of ___. MCV also now 75 which is new in last several months. Iron studies showed likely mild iron deficiency and she should have a colonoscopy for evaluation if she agrees. CBC should be followed as an outpatient. # Acute kidney injury: Creatinine up to 1.9 in the ED, from recent baseline of 1.0-1.5. Returned to 1.5 with gentle diuresis. # Sick sinus syndrome s/p pacer: Pacer placed in ___, recently interrogated in device clinic and working properly. Monitored on telemetry. # T2DM: Controlled with oral medication at home. Given HISS in house. # HTN: Stable, continued home regimen TRANSITIONAL ISSUES - Needs follow-up of new lymphadenopathy with possible lymph node biopsy - Pancreatic body cystic lesion seen on CT chest and abdomen which appears stable and most likely represents IPMNs per report. Should have 6 month f/u MRI - Pending labs: ___, EBV - Should have outpatient PPD vs quantiferon gold testing to rule out TB as cause of lymphadenopathy - Heterogeneous and nodular thyroid gland with a small left isthmus nodule can be followed with continued sonographic surveillance - Needs repeat CBC as outpatient and consideration of colonoscopy given anemia
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. You were not found to have any concerning cause of your chest pain and it resolved. You also had shortness of breath which was felt to be due to fluid in your lungs due to your heart failure and you were given diuretics with good effect. You should weigh yourself every day and call your MD if your weight changes. You were also found to have enlarged lymph nodes in your chest or neck. The cause of these lymph nodes are not clear and you have multiple studies pending to evaluate them. If they remain enlarged, you will need a biopsy of a lymph node to make sure that you do not have cancer.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of ESRD on HD, CHF, DM s/p recent diabetic foot amputation who presented from rehab with sepsis from osteomyelitis of the right foot and developed ischemic colitis. # Sepsis: Patient presented from rehab with fevers/chills and was intially hypotensive but responded well to 2L IVF and 1 U pRBCs. Also with leukocytosis of 19 at presentation. Urine culture grew yeast and was thought unlikely to be the cause of his sepsis. Infectious disease was consulted and determined likely source to be osteomyelitis as he underwent R ___ digit amputation on ___ which grew acinetobacter and VISA. He was treated with Unasyn and linezolid and WBC count normalized. He was discharged with a PICC in place to continue linezolid and Unasyn an an outpatient for a 6 week course. # Ischemic Colitis: Patient with BRBPR x2 during admission. Seen by GI who performed colonoscopy revealing dusky colored bowel in ascending colon. CT abd revealed possible ischemic colitis vs infection with possible microperforation. Patient had a soft, mildly tender abdomen and his H/H remained stable. He was seen by colorectal surgery who recommended a PO challenge (he did well with this) and follow up colonoscopy in ___ weeks. His warfarin was held until ___, then re-started without bridging. # Anemia: Patient presented with a Hgb 6.7 and was given 1 U pRBC. Hgb remained below 6 so he was given 2nd unit pRBC with improvement to 7.6. No acute source of bleed was identified, although later in his course he developed BRBPR associated with likely ischemic colitis as described above. His H/H remained stable for the remainder of this hospitalization. Hemolysis labs were negative. # Hyperkalemia: Hyperkalemia up to 6.3, without symptoms or EKG changes. Given calcium gluconate/insulin/glucose and kayexelate Likely due to ESRD. Potassium returned to normal with medical management and ongoing dialysis. # CKD on HD: Patient was continued on ___ HD. # CAD/PVD: Patient was continued on his home Plavix # DVT: H/o of thrombosis in ___ graft on warfarin. This was held due to GIB but was restarted prior to discharge without bridging. Transitional Issues: - Patient needs repeat colonoscopy in ___ weeks for melena/ischemic colitis - Gabapentin decreased significantly from 1600 mg qHS and 800 mg qAM to 100 mg TID due to his ESRD (current dosing is recommended HD dosing). - warfarin was held during admission due to GIB, but was restarted on discharge. INR on discharge was 1.1, he was given a dose of 10 mg on ___ and 5 mg ___ and then was discharged on his home dose of 5 mg daily. - Continue Linezolid and Unasyn for 6 week course ___ with PICC in place. - Started on amlodipine for hypertension. Should follow-up with PCP regarding monitoring. - CODE: FULL - CONTACT: daughter- ___: ___, wife- ___: ___ ___ on Admission: The Preadmission Medication list is accurate and complete. 1. Clopidogrel 75 mg PO DAILY 2. Docusate Sodium 100 mg PO BID 3. Gabapentin 800 mg PO DAILY 4. Lactulose 15 mL PO DAILY 5. OxycoDONE (Immediate Release) ___ mg PO Q4H:PRN pain 6. Rosuvastatin Calcium 20 mg PO QPM 7. Senna 17.2 mg PO QHS 8. Warfarin 5 mg PO DAILY16 9. Glargine 15 Units Breakfast Glargine 30 Units Bedtime Insulin SC Sliding Scale using HUM Insulin 10. Furosemide 80 mg PO DAILY 11. Metolazone 2.5 mg PO DAILY
summary: You were admitted to ___ for loss of consciousness at home. It was found that you had a blood infection. You also were found to have an infection in the bone that was amputated the week before your admission. It is likely that the blood infection came from this infected toe. You had a PICC line placed and are being treated with antibiotics. While in the hospital, you also had 2 episodes of bloody diarrhea. You had a colonoscopy by GI which showed that part of your colon had loss of blood flow. It is likely that this is what caused your bloody stools. You were evaluated by colorectal surgery who did not think you needed surgery at this time.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female presented from OSH w/ fall and R trochanteric fracture and concomitant NSTEMI Active Diagnoses #NSTEMI: patient was transferred and started on a heparin gtt while trops were trending up. She was otherwise medically optimized with high-dose atorvastatin and beta-blockers. She was taken off heparin for surgical repair of her hip. Post-operatively, her trops were trending down and she was maintained on prophylactic lovenox. Her troponins continued to improve and her chest pain resolved. #Acute-on-Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure: patient appeared to be fluid overloaded on admission. She was diuresed successfully with IV lasix prior to her operation. Cardiac anesthesia was consulted for her operation and gave minimal fluids intraoperatively. She continued to do well from a fluid standpoint and was restarted on her home dose PO lasix prior to discharge. Urine output was monitored closely. #Right intertrochanteric fracture: patient was evaluated by orthopedic surgery and taken to the OR. Repaired successfully and ortho continued to follow, signing off for discharge to rehab. #Anemia: patient presented with baseline anemia. In the presence of her NSTEMI and post-operative drop in Hct to 27%, she was transfused 1 unit pRBCs with appropriate increase in hct. She continued to remain stable and asymptomatic following this point. #UTI: patient's urine was analyzed and had 182 WBCs in the sample. A urine culture grew >100K CFUs of e. coli. Patient was treated with 3 days of ceftriaxone and completed the course while in the hospital. #Hyponatremia: patient was found to be hyponatremic upon admission w/ Na 130. Her sodium was monitored and was stable from 127-134. She denied any symptoms at this time. Urine lytes and osmoles indicated a mixed picture showing both mild SIADH and diuretic-induced hyponatremia. Patient was placed on a fluid restriction of 1500mL daily and tolerated this well. Chronic Diagnoses: #Hypertension: lisinopril was held during this admission due to hypotension but resumed prior to discharge. Blood pressures were stable. #Diabetes Mellitus, Type 2: home oral glycemic control were held on admission and blood sugars was controlled adequately on Humalog Insulin Sliding Scale. #Glaucoma: patient denied any symptoms of glaucoma. Home eye drops were continued while inpatient. #Hyperlipidemia: home simvastatin was converted to high-potency atorvastatin on admission, and will continue on this medication at home. Transitional Issues: -------------- - Please monitor hyponatremia closely. Please fluid resrict to ___ until improved to baseline ~130 with adequate pain management. - please monitor patient for myalgias and rhabdomyolysis, previous episode on lower doses of statin therapy - Pain management - Please monitor fluid status and adjust lasix dose as needed. - Please monitor BP and HR closely and adjust medication as needed. - Right ___ is weight bearing. - Monitor H&H. - Please monitor for bowel movement while on narcotics. - Need to continue DVT prophylaxis with lovenox until orthopedic follow up on ___.
summary: You were admitted following your fall, in which you broke your right hip. At the same time, it appears that you had a heart attack. Prior to your surgery, you were diuresed because it appeared you were having an exacerbation of your heart failure. Your fracture was repaired successfully by orthopedics surgeons and they recommended physical therapy, who recommended rehab to build up your strength and abilities. You received a blood transfusion for low blood counts after your surgery. For your heart attack, you were medically optimized with anticoagulants prior to surgery and your symptoms improved. You continued to do well while being monitored and awaiting rehab placement. Upon admission, your urine was evaluated and you were found to have a urinary tract infection, which was treated with IV antibiotics and you completed your course here. You were also found to have low serum sodium, which was stable. To improve your sodium counts, you were placed on a free fluid restriction of 1500mL daily. You have been started on new medications. We have started calcium and vitamin D to prevent bone loss. You should take acetaminophen for pain and oxycodone for severe pain associated with your surgery. Please also begin taking a baby aspirin rather than a full strength aspirin.
