text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of CML on Gleevec, CLL s/p Campath/Ritux ___, AF not on anticoag, HFpEF, CKD baseline Cr 1.3 p/w poor PO intake/fatigue, ___ transferred from ___ for suspicion GIB. # GI bleed: She is s/p recent EGD w/o significant findings of bleeding. She presented from OSH with melena and hematocrit drop. She receive 2units of pRBC. EGD was performed which showed friable areas with erythema and an area with blood clot. A colonoscopy was attempted but could not be completed due to stricture. She underwent a virtual CT colonoscopy which did not demonstrate evidence of tumor. The likely etiology of bleeding was due to oozing from her coagulopathy from her progressive CLL. During her admission, she did receive 1 unit platelets and 1 unit cryo (prior to colonoscopy). She was given BID PPI. Her diarrhea and melana resolved. Her hct was stable without blood products. Her aspirin was held given high risk of bleeding. # Hypotension: She initially was fluid resuscitated in the ICU. She was also started on empiric antibiotics for a dirty UA. Her hypotension resolved. At the time of discharge systolic blood pressures ranged from upper ___ to 110s. # Anemia acute blood loss: # Anemia, chronic: She has longstanding macrocytic anemia. She has CLL/CML which may be contributing. She is on Gleevec. Her retic count was low. Her outpatient heme/onc physician was notified and she will follow up in their clinic for further evaluation and management. Her acute blood loss anemia resolved and her hct was stable at the time of discharge. She should get her blood drawn within a week to ensure stability. # Thrombocytopenia: Likely from consumptive process (bleed) and CLL/CML. Her plt were stable at the time of discharge. She will follow up with heme/onc. # Acute on chronic Diastolic Heart Failure: ECHO at OSH reportedly with preserved LVEF and mild diastolic heart failure. She has significant pitting edema (hypoalb likely contributing) and elevated JVD. In addition, she required fluid resuscitation due to her hypotension. She ultimately received diuresis with return of weight to below recent baseline level (125 at discharge, baseline 128 per report). She will need daily weights with consideration of starting low dose torsemide if she is retaining fluid. # Chronic CKD: # ARF: She baseline is near 1.2. She initially was much higher. Her creatinine improved to near apparent baseline prior to discharge. Likely secondary to volume status. # Hypoalbuminemia: Likely secondary to poor nutrition and chronic disease. Likely contributing to anasarca. No evidence of significant proteinuria. She should be encouraged to have good nutrition. # CML: Chronic issue. w/o signs of blasts on automated smear. Follow up with hematology oncology. # CLL: The pt's lymphadenopathy and cytopenias have worsened. The pt will follow up with Dr. ___ as an ___ and may need a biopsy. Dr. ___ of admission and will arrange follow up appointment next week. # Transaminitis: Stable to improved during admission. Ultrasound with evidence of NAFLD. Can be monitored as an outpatient. # Imaging findings: No evidence of acute abdominal process to correlate for fluid collections. Likely sterile given signs and symptoms. Chronic Issues: # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: She was in sinus rhythm during her stay. She was continued on amiodarone but metoprolol (due to pressures and rate control) and aspirin (due to GI bleed) were discontinued. # HLD: Restarted simvastatin on discharge Transitional: Heme/onc follow up She needs daily weights. If gains more than ___ pounds, or has evidence of fluid retention, please start diuretics. Follow up labs - CBC, LFTs, chemistry to ensure stability or improvement
summary: You were originally admitted to the ICU for low blood presure. This corrected after being given some blood products to correct your anemia from GI bleed. You had an upper endoscopy which showed gastritis which may be causing you to have slow oozing. We stopped your aspirin and gave you acid suppression medication (Pantoprazole) with improvement. Dr. ___ tried to do a colonoscopy but the stricture could not be passed. Thus, you had a CT virtual colonoscopy that showed no evidence of tumor or mass. You will need labs in a few days to ensure stability. You also had swelling from heart failure and low protein levels in your body. We treated with you with diuresis. You will need to be weighed daily. If you are gaining weight you may need diuretics as an outpatient.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with history of CML on Gleevec, CLL s/p Campath/Ritux ___, AF not on anticoag, HFpEF, CKD baseline Cr 1.3 p/w poor PO intake/fatigue, ___ transferred from ___ for suspicion GIB. # GI bleed: She is s/p recent EGD w/o significant findings of bleeding. She presented from OSH with melena and hematocrit drop. She receive 2units of pRBC. EGD was performed which showed friable areas with erythema and an area with blood clot. A colonoscopy was attempted but could not be completed due to stricture. She underwent a virtual CT colonoscopy which did not demonstrate evidence of tumor. The likely etiology of bleeding was due to oozing from her coagulopathy from her progressive CLL. During her admission, she did receive 1 unit platelets and 1 unit cryo (prior to colonoscopy). She was given BID PPI. Her diarrhea and melana resolved. Her hct was stable without blood products. Her aspirin was held given high risk of bleeding. # Hypotension: She initially was fluid resuscitated in the ICU. She was also started on empiric antibiotics for a dirty UA. Her hypotension resolved. At the time of discharge systolic blood pressures ranged from upper ___ to 110s. # Anemia acute blood loss: # Anemia, chronic: She has longstanding macrocytic anemia. She has CLL/CML which may be contributing. She is on Gleevec. Her retic count was low. Her outpatient heme/onc physician was notified and she will follow up in their clinic for further evaluation and management. Her acute blood loss anemia resolved and her hct was stable at the time of discharge. She should get her blood drawn within a week to ensure stability. # Thrombocytopenia: Likely from consumptive process (bleed) and CLL/CML. Her plt were stable at the time of discharge. She will follow up with heme/onc. # Acute on chronic Diastolic Heart Failure: ECHO at OSH reportedly with preserved LVEF and mild diastolic heart failure. She has significant pitting edema (hypoalb likely contributing) and elevated JVD. In addition, she required fluid resuscitation due to her hypotension. She ultimately received diuresis with return of weight to below recent baseline level (125 at discharge, baseline 128 per report). She will need daily weights with consideration of starting low dose torsemide if she is retaining fluid. # Chronic CKD: # ARF: She baseline is near 1.2. She initially was much higher. Her creatinine improved to near apparent baseline prior to discharge. Likely secondary to volume status. # Hypoalbuminemia: Likely secondary to poor nutrition and chronic disease. Likely contributing to anasarca. No evidence of significant proteinuria. She should be encouraged to have good nutrition. # CML: Chronic issue. w/o signs of blasts on automated smear. Follow up with hematology oncology. # CLL: The pt's lymphadenopathy and cytopenias have worsened. The pt will follow up with Dr. ___ as an ___ and may need a biopsy. Dr. ___ of admission and will arrange follow up appointment next week. # Transaminitis: Stable to improved during admission. Ultrasound with evidence of NAFLD. Can be monitored as an outpatient. # Imaging findings: No evidence of acute abdominal process to correlate for fluid collections. Likely sterile given signs and symptoms. Chronic Issues: # Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation: She was in sinus rhythm during her stay. She was continued on amiodarone but metoprolol (due to pressures and rate control) and aspirin (due to GI bleed) were discontinued. # HLD: Restarted simvastatin on discharge Transitional: Heme/onc follow up She needs daily weights. If gains more than ___ pounds, or has evidence of fluid retention, please start diuretics. Follow up labs - CBC, LFTs, chemistry to ensure stability or improvement

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ man with ulcerative colitis, hypertension presenting with dyspnea, found to have atrial fibrillation. Active Issues: -------------- # Atrial fibrillation: CHADS2 of 1, CHADS2-VASc of 2, giving him low to more likely intermediate stroke risk, as discussed with patient. Symptoms have been going on for several days.Monitored on telemetry, he ramained in atrial fibrillation with intermittent asymptomatic RVR. TSH was normal. He was planned for TEE cardioversion; TEE was within normal limits and without evidence of thrombus, however, cardioversion failed, as patient temporarily converted into sinus rhythm and but promptly reverted back to atrial fibrilaltion. In this setting, he was started on Sotalol 80mg PO BID and put on ___ of Hearts monitor. He was also transitioned to Diltiazem 120mg ER for rate control. He was transitioned from a heparin gtt to Xarelto near discharge. His home lisinopril was restarted at discharge. He was discharged with plan to return to echo lab on ___, for another cardioversion. Resolved Issues: ---------------- # Dyspnea on exertion: resolved. most likely related to atrial fibrillation, CXR does show mild edema. Got 1L IVF in ED. Relatively euvolemic on exam with slightly elevated JVP. Feels mildly SOB with exertion still. Anginal equivalent could be a possible etiology but would be atypical presentation, though he does have known reversible defects on past stress testing. Primary pulmonary process such as infection or emphysema much less likely. Well's score, low PE very unlikely. 20mg IV lasix x1 on ___, troponins flat, BNP 2369. CHRONIC ISSUES: --------------- # Ulcerative colitis vs Crohns: well-controlled; continued home balsalazide and home budesonide as needed. # Hypertension: held lisinopril in setting of occasional soft BPs in setting of atrial fibrillation; this was restarted at discharge. # Possible CAD: reversible defect ___, no history of MI. Continued aspirin 81. # seasonal allergies: continued home loratidine PRN. ***TRANSITIONAL ISSUES*** - being discharged with ___ of Hearts Monitor which will be read by Dr. ___. - started sotalol on day of discharge in addition to ER diltiazem. - plan for repeat electrical cardioversion next ___. - discharged on Xarelto for anticoagulation.
summary: You came in because you were having shortness of breath at home and were found to have an irregular rhythm and rapid heart rate in the hospital. This is not uncommon as people age. We controlled your rate while you were here with a medication called diltiazem. We attempted to cardiovert you back into normal rhythm with an electric shock, but this did not work. We then started a medication for rhythm control while you were here. This medication is called Sotalol. You were also started on a blood thinner called Xarelto to prevent clots and stroke. Please continue to take these medications after discharge. You will go home with a special heart monitor to have continuous monitoring of your rhythm. We hope that by starting you on the Sotaolol that we can correct your rapid heart rhythm next week via a second cardioversion. We are working on getting you an outpatient appointment for this second cardioversion.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ with PMH significant for ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF ___ s/p BiV ICD, CAD, Afib/flutter s/p cardioversion and ablation, HTN, HLD, and IDDM who was transferred to ___ from ___ for management of his decompensated systolic CHF and for rate control. Patient was successfully cardioverted ___. ACUTE ISSUES: # decompensated systolic CHF: Patient was admitted at ___ for decompensated CHF, he was symptomatic for dyspnea, PND, and edema, for which he was diuresed on bumex. Patient on transfer to ___ appeared to be mildly volume overloaded. Dry weight per patient is ___ lbs (88.5 kg), weight on admission 89.6 kg. Increased home metop tartrate from 25 mg to 37.5 mg PO Q6H, given home lasix 20 mg PO daily, and home valsartan. # A fib/ Atrial flutter s/pablation and cardioversion in ___. CHADS score 4 CHADS2-VASC 6. On floor, patient was rate controlled. Recent ECHO ___ shows no intracardiac thrombus seen, depressed left ventricular systolic function, mild mitral regurgitation, mild aortic regurgitation. ICD interrogation showed episodes of A fib starting on ___. Successful cardioversion ___. Increased warfarin from 5 mg to 6 mg daily (INR at 1.8), stopped metop tartrate 25 mg PO Q6H, started Metoprolol Succinate XL 100 mg PO daily, started amiodarone 400 mg PO daily (started 400 mg ___, last day ___ for total 2 weeks; decrease to 200 mg daily starting ___. # Hypertension: Patient with bp 154/76 on admission, and could benefit from better hypertensive control. Given valsartan 160 mg, started amlodipine 5 mg daily. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Diabetes, Type II: Continued home insulin ___ follow up to adjust Lantus dose at night, held home metformin (Cr elevated. # CKD: Patient's baseline Cr 2.2., with Cr 2.4 on admission. Cr monitored daily, held metformin (as above). # CAD: Patient has 75% occlusion of LCx. Continued home ASA 81 mg PO daily, atorvastain 80 mg PO daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - on amiodarone 400 mg daily through ___, then 200 mg daily starting ___ - Patient discharged on amlodipine 5 mg daily prior to discharge for persistent sbp's in 180s, asymptomatic (reported taking this medication at home but unsure of dose). Further titration as outpatient - Lantus 25 units QPM: patient reports not always taking this at night and titrating as needed. During admission, he was hypoglycemic to ___. Advised patient to continue to take only if eating sufficiently and deferred further insulin management to outpatient pcp. Home januvia held.
summary: You presented to ___ from an outside hospital because you had atrial fibrillation and atrial flutter, and you were admitted to control the rate and for cardioversion (returning the heart to a regular rhythm). During this admission, you were also diuresed in order to remove excess water, which helped treat your heart failure. You will continue to take your home dose lasix 20 mg daily in order to help treat and control your edema (swelling) and water retention. You will continue to take warfarin (coumadin) 5 mg daily in order to prevent blood clots in your heart. You also started a new medication, amiodarone 400 mg daily, and on ___ it will decrease to 200 mg daily. We also started you on metoprolol succinate XL 100 mg daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with h/o obesity, HTN, DMII, h/o EtOH abuse, and b/l ___ edema admitted for chest pain, SOB and ___ edema, now ruled out for MI. # Decompensated CHF: Pt presenting with ___ edema felt to be ___ decompensated ___. Per report up 25 pounds from dry weight. Treated with IV lasix and metalazone with significant symptomatic improvement. A transthoracic echo was done which was poor technical quality due to patient's body habitus. It demonstrated probable mild inferior and inferolateral hypokinesis. The right ventricle was not well seen but appeared dilated and hypokinetic. No pathologic valvular abnormalities were seen. Moderate pulmonary artery systolic hypertension was appreciated. Dilated thoracic aorta. No EF provided given limited exam. Simvastatin 20mg PO qD was started for cardioprotection on day of discharge. # ___ Edema: chronic appearing, per pcp ongoing for years, R > L, does not appear consistent with cellulitis given no warmth and bilateral nature of discolorations. Improved with diuresed with IV lasix. Legs elevated. Defered restarting patient's spironalactone, which he was not taking, to outpatient. # Chest Pain w/SOB +/- palpitations: Likely anxiety/panic. Ruled out for MI. No arrythmias on tele. PE unlikely given stable normal vital signs. CKs initially elevated now resolving but with negative BM/trop. EKG similar to prior, with only incomplete RBBB, stable. Continued on aspirin, given ativan for anxiety. # Pain: pt describes ongoing pain across his entire body and reports going to a pain clinic that moved recently and says he is on oxycontin but when asked says he never got the script filled (not on his list from his pharmacy). Treated with tylenol and prn oxycodone. NSAIDs restarted after patient ruled out for MI. # Concern for withdrawal: pt reports distant history of etoh abuse but none in years. No evidence of intoxication, pcp does not know of any recent etoh use. Serum and urine tox negative. Given pt's inconsistent history patient was covered with a CIWA prn ativan scale which he did not require for CIWA scale. # HTN: Continued on home regimen with good control. Lasix increased to 80 PO BID for additional diuresis given ___ edema. # DMII: unclear baseline control, A1c 7.3, hypoglycemics held on admission, restarted on discharge (glyburide). # OSA: continued on home CPAP regimen.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain and swelling in your legs. Our tests showed you were not having a heart attack. The cause of your pain might be anxiety, and we suggest that you return to the care of a psychiatrist as you were before. You may require medications on a regular basis to control your anxiety. You also were concerned that your legs were infected. We do not believe that your legs have any active infection at this time. We gave you some diuretics to help remove some of the fluid from your legs. We did an echocardiogram of your heart and did not find any significant heart failure to explain the fluid in your legs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mrs. ___ was a ___ year-old woman with DM2, dCHF, CKD, Obesity and HTN admitted for hypoxia and felt to have pneumonia. . # Pneumonia: Initially felt to be due to atypical CAP that was multifocal. Cultures were negative. Patient underwent treatment of 3 days of CFTX/Azithro without improvement. Her hypoxemia actually worsened on HD2. Although initially she appeared hypovolemic and her lasix was held x 24 hours, she was subsequently felt to be mildly volume overloaded, and was diuresed (~ 1.5L). ECHO showed diastolic dysfunction, RV dilatation and PAH. Her hypoxemia worsened and was out of proportion to her CXR. V/Q scan showed no PE but LUL abnormality that was further investigated via CT. She was broadened to Vanco/Cefepime/Azithro on ___ given concern for possible viral PNA with superinfection. She completed an 8 day course for HAP. CT showed multifocal PNA with peribronchial opacities, but the differential diagnosis also included cryptogenic organizing pneumonia as well as interstitial lung disease. She was transferred to the ICU for further monitoring. She was never intubated, nor did she receive bronchoscopy. HIV and ANCA were negative. She slowly improved after completing her Abx course for HAP and atypical pneumonia. She was slowly weaned from her oxygen; however, did desaturate to the low ___ and qualified for ___ supplemental oxygen. She is being discharged with supplemental oxygen, was felt to be slowly improving with regard to her exam and hypoxia, and will be discharged on ___ NC. She has close PCP and pulmonary ___, and it is expected that she should continue to be able to wean from her supplemental oxygen. . # Atrial fibrilaltion and atrial flutter: Converted with metoprolol PO. She has DM/HTN/CHF, thus high risk for stroke (8.5% annual risk of stroke). Her current afib/flutter was felt to be likely due to infection. She was started on heparin gtt and was started on coumadin. On discharge, her coumadin dose is 5 mg and INR is at 1.5. She will be set up with ___ clinic on discharge. She will receive metoprolol succinate 150 mg daily as a new medication for rate control. . # DOE/CHF: Multifactorial, see above. Also with evidence of CHF w/RV failure and PAH on TTE. She was felt to be euvolemic on discharge at lasix 40 mg daily. . # Anemia: acute on chronic, normocytic. HCT stable throughout hospitalization. Fe low, otherwise Fe studies nl. She was started on iron sulfate. She may need conoloscopy given guaiac positive stools. . # Diabetes: Held glipizide. Maintained on glargine and HISS. On discharge will resume her home medications. . # Hypertension: she will stop her losartan given hyperkalemia. This can be re-addressed by her PCP. She has a new medication, metoprolol succinate, 150 mg daily, for rate control. Her lasix dose will be 40 mg daily to maintain euvolemia. . # F/U: with PCP and pulmonary. Being discharged to home with services, physical therapy, and supplemental oxygen. Is being set up with ___ clinic.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for fever, shortness of breath and cough. You were evaluated and treated by the medicine service. You were found to have pneumonia and were started on appropriate antibiotics. Your pneumonia made it difficult for you to breathe and you required supplemental oxygen and treatment in the intensive care unit. You breathing improved with the appropriate therapy and you were transfered back to the general medicine floor where you continued to improve. You will be provided with supplemental oxygen ___ liters as needed) for home use while your breathing completely recovers and should continue with home physical therapy.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment and Plan: ___ female with ESRD, DM2, chronic CHF with preserved EF, afib who was admitted bacteremia and back pain for 1 week 1. Bacteremia: ___ staph bacteremia from HD line. Currently on vancomycin on HD protocol. No evidence of endocarditis on TTE. TEE not pursued after extensive conversation with ID, Cardiology, and Geriatrics. Patient is not cooperative, agitated and delirious, would require TEE under general anesthesia. In addition, if pacer lead were infected it would be risky procedure to remove since it has been calcified into myocardium. Recommended to continue on empiric 6 week treatment of vancomycin. Bone scan and gallium scan showed no source of infection. Blood cultures have been negative since. - Continue vancomycin by level at HD (started on ___, to be continued until ___ - ___ with PCP - ___ with ID - weekly outpatient labs 2. Acute lower back pain: Abscess ruled out by ___ CT. CT found mild degenerative changes. Bone scan and gallium scan showed no sources of infection. Pain likely due to degenerative disease and herniation. Pain was managed with ativan, dilaudid, and oxycontin. Patient started exhibiting some signs of delirium which was most likely due to the pain medication. Pt would ask people to set the table for dinner, and act very agitated towards hospital staff. The pain medications were scaled back towards the end of the admission. On discharge pt was on 0.5 mg ativan BID PRN pain, 10 mg oxycontin PO BID and being tapered off pain medications. The night before discharge patient had huge multiple bowel movements with immediate relieve of back pain and discomfort. * ___ consider daily lactulose in future if problem arises again. 3. Anxiety: pt exhibited a fair amount of anxiety for which ativan was given 4. ESRD: Patient's tunnelled HD line was removed due to line sepsis. A temporary catheter for HD was placed and subsequently replaced with a new tunnelled HD catheter at right IJ 5. DM2: During admission, pt received outpt insulin regimen. Fingersticks were followed and insulin sliding scale was implemented with good glucose control. 6. Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation - not on coumadin, is s/p dual chamber pacemaker placement in ___ for sick sinus syndrome 7. Chronic Diastolic/systolic CHF - patient was continued on outpatient regimen of beta ___. Volume management with HD. 8. Constipation - patient received senna, colace, and bisacodyl for constipation. After having large bowel movement the night before discharge, improved patient's agitation and back pain. 9. Pruritis - pt developed an itchy rash on her left side likely due to pain mediction which was treated with sarna lotion.
summary: You were admittes with fevers and weakness, and back pain. You were found to have an infection in your blood. Your hemodialysis line was changed and you were treated with antibiotics. A bone scan and gallium scan were also performed looking for sites of infection with no significant findings. Because you were unable to tolerate a transesophageal echocardiogram, you will be continued on a 6 week course of vancomycin for empiric treatment of endocarditis in light of your positive blood cultures. Your back pain was likely related to muscle spasm and degenerative changes in your spine as well as being severely constipated. You were treated with medications for pain and spasm and were seen by physical therapy. You should not drive while taking sedating pain medications.

The night before discharge patient had huge multiple bowel movements with immediate relieve of back pain and discomfort. * ___ consider daily lactulose in future if problem arises again. 3. Anxiety: pt exhibited a fair amount of anxiety for which ativan was given 4. ESRD: Patient's tunnelled HD line was removed due to line sepsis. A temporary catheter for HD was placed and subsequently replaced with a new tunnelled HD catheter at right IJ 5. DM2: During admission, pt received outpt insulin regimen. Fingersticks were followed and insulin sliding scale was implemented with good glucose control. 6. Paroxysmal atrial fibrillation - not on coumadin, is s/p dual chamber pacemaker placement in ___ for sick sinus syndrome 7. Chronic Diastolic/systolic CHF - patient was continued on outpatient regimen of beta ___. Volume management with HD. 8. Constipation - patient received senna, colace, and bisacodyl for constipation. After having large bowel movement the night before discharge, improved patient's agitation and back pain. 9. Pruritis - pt developed an itchy rash on her left side likely due to pain mediction which was treated with sarna lotion.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF HOSPITAL SUMMARY ====================== Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with history of diastolic heart failure, severe mitral regurgitation after MitraClip (___), atrial fibrillation, chronic kidney disease, hypertension, and coronary artery disease who presented as transfer from ___ with encephalopathy, type-2 NSTEMI, and decompensated heart failure. He was ultimately found to have new cirrhosis in the setting of congestive heart failure thought secondary to cardiac amyloidosis. Hepatology was consulted for input regarding liver dysfunction. His hospitalization was complicated by hemorrhagic shock following paracentesis to drain his ascites requiring 3 pressors and pRBC/FFP transfusion and ultimately required embolization with ___. Pt was admitted to the CCU where he was intubated for airway protection and anticipated respiratory distress given large volume resuscitation. Throughout his hospital course, pt was weaned off majority of pressors, but had continued dopamine gtt requirement to main blood pressures and heart rate. As patient was unable to be weaned from dopamine, goals of care were discussed. He elected to pursue comfort measures and will be discharged with hospice care. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ========================== [] transfer to hospice with comfort measures only [] Provide bowel regimen and pain medication as needed [] Finasteride 5 mg/ daily for comfort [] Bumex 2mg daily for respiratory comfort ACTIVE ISSUES ============= # HFpEF exacerbation # Severe MR ___ MitraClip with residual MR # Type 2 NSTEMI # CAD Mr. ___ presented to ___ with altered mental status and weakness. On exam, he was grossly volume overloaded. Labs notable for pro-BNP of 10,647 and troponin to 0.31 (at OSH). TTE demonstrated eccentric moderate-severe mitral and tricuspid regurgitation, and moderate LV hypertrophy with small cavity size. He was diuresed with IV diuretics with clinical improvement. Ultimately, the elevated troponin was attributed to supply-demand mismatch in setting of heart failure exacerbation. In the CCU, Mr. ___ required pressors to maintain adequate perfusion. He was ultimately weaned to dopamine alone but was unable to be weaned off. He will be discharged on a maintenance diuretic for comfort. # Hemorrhagic Shock # Right inferior epigastric artery bleeding On ___, Mr. ___ was found to be hypotensive to systolic in the ___ after undergoing a diagnostic paracentesis. He was not responsive to fluids and required ICU trasnsfer for pressor support. He was found to have hgb drop from 9 to 5.8 and underwent 4xRBC 1xFFP transfusions. Upon transfer to CCU (___), he was intubated for airway protection and given up to three pressors for hemodynamic support. CT torso revealed right inferior epigastric artery bleeding and he underwent successful emergent embolization with ___ with resultant stabilization of his H/H. # Decompensated cirrhosis # Ascites At outside hospital, CT abdomen demonstrated liver cirrhosis with moderate to large volume ascites. Labs were notable for t-bili of 0.8, albumin 3.0, and Plt 175. Diagnostic paracentesis (___) consistent with hepatic ascites. Further workup, including Hep B, Hep C, ___, and AMA was unremarkable. Ultimately, his cirrhosis was attribute to congestion from his heart failure. # Urinary tract infection At outside hospital, urinalysis was positive with 50-100 WBCs. He was never symptomatic. He received a course of ceftriaxone. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #Afib Mr. ___ was switch from home warfarin to heparin gtt during his hospital. Given decision for CMO, anticoagulation was discontinued prior to discharge. #Chronic Kidney Disease, Stage II Mr. ___ medications were ___ during his hospital stay.
summary: You were found to have excess fluid in your lungs and abdomen. You were also found to have liver disease called cirrhosis which is likely due to your poor heart function. You were treated with water pills to remove the excess fluid. You underwent a procedure to remove the fluid from your abdomen. You had bleeding from one of the arteries in your abdomen and underwent a procedure with interventional radiology to stop the bleeding. You were transferred to the intensive care unit for further support. You had a breathing tube attached to a mechanical ventilator to help your breathing. You were treated with medications to maintain your blood pressure and heart rate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with CAD s/p CABG, severe, PVD, DMt2, CHF (EF 35-40%) presents to his PCP office with progressive DOE x 2 weeks. Pt was found to be in atrial flutter with a positive troponin. Pt transferred to ___ for further management and cardiac cath. . #. Pump: Known history of CHF (EF 35-40% on echo in ___. Shortness of breath is likely manifestation of worsened CHF. Recent decline is likely due to possible ischemic event vs poor rate control with atrial flutter. CXR did not have changes from before and there was mild fluid overload. Patient was diuresed with lasix drip and fluid restricted up to 1.5 L per day. With these measures his breathing improved significantly. Repeat echocardiogram showed depressed bi-ventricular function with an EF of 30%. . #. CAD: Pt has known history of CAD with CABG performed in ___. Pt has several risk factors for ACS including HTN, HL, PVD, DM and history of CAD. Presentation was concerning for ACS given EKG changes and positive cardiac enzymes. He was started on heparin gtt, integrillin gtt, aspirin, plavix and high-dose statin. His HgbA1C was 6.7% and his lipids cholesterol 94, triglycerides 83, HDL 33 and LDL 44 with a ratio of 2.8. He was taken to the cath lab where he was found to have 100% stenosis of mid-RCA, 90% of left main, 100% of mid-LAD, 60% of D1 and 100% of proximal circumflex. LIMA was patent, occluded SVG-PDA-OM stump and graft was not seen in aortography. It was decided that patient was not a candidate for intervention or redo-CABG. The decision was made to pursue medical management. He was discharged on aspirin, plavix, atorvastatin, metoprolol and lasix. Patient will need to be started on ___ once kidney function recovers. Patient was educated in healthy lifestyle including not-smoking, exercise and diet as well as in the importance of taking his medications. . #. Rhythm: Pt has known history of A-flutter first diagnosed in ___. Prior to presentation to PCP office today he was believed to be in nsr. Recent onset of atrial flutter is likely the result of increased atrial stretch in the setting of worsened CHF. Pt is on home coumadin for anticoagulation and is rate controlled on daily beta blocker. He had no vegetations on TEE and as a result underwent successful atrial flutter ablation on ___. He was in sinus rhythm for the rest of the hospitalization on telemetry. He was discharged on his coumadin home-dose and will need INR follow up with goal ___. Patient was educated in the food he cannot eat and was advised to call his PCP before starting any new medication. . #. CRI: Cr 1.8 on presentation; uncertain baseline. Creatinine of 2.1 at discharge trending down after zenith of 3.2 thought to be contrast-induced. Patient will need creatinine follow up as outpatient. . #. Respiratory Alkalosis: pt's ABG consistent with respiratory alkalosis due to tachypnea in the setting of pulmonary edema. His respiratory status improved after diuresis. . #. DMt2: Pt is on glipizide at home. Oral hypoglycemics were held while in house and he was controlled with insulin sliding scale. He was discharged in his home regimen. . #. FEN: Follow and replete electrolytes. Cardiac diet. No IVF at present. . #. Access: PIV . #. PPx: PO diet. . #. Code: FULL CODE; son ___ ___ . #. ___: Home.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after an episode of shortness of breath. You were found to be in "heart failure" which was likely triggered by a small heart attack. The heart failure caused fluid to back up into your lungs and made it difficult to breathe. You were given medications that helped remove the extra fluid and your shortness of breath improved. To prevent recurrence of these symptoms it is important that you limit your fluid intake to 1.5 liters per day and maintain a low salt diet. It is also important that you weigh yourself every day. If your weight increases by more than three pounds you should notify your physician. On admission you were found to have an irregular rhythm called atrial flutter. You underwent a procedure called an ablation to fix the irregular rhythm. The procedure was successful and was without complications. During this admission you were started on blood thinning medication to prevent blood clots and strokes.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/oF with PMH of Turner's Syndrome on chronic HRT transferred to the CCU following STEMI s/p DES to LAD # CAD: Presented with STEMI to LAD and went to cath lab where a DES was placed with no significant atherosclerosis in remainder of coronary arteries. Does have significant CAD as well as risk factors including longterm hormone replacement therapy and Turners Syndrome. She was started on Aspirin, plavix, metoprolol, and atorvastatin. Her premarin was discontinued as it puts her at risk for CAD. ECHO showed left ventricular systolic function is mildly depressed (LVEF= 35-40 %) with septal, anterior and apical hypokinesis to akinesis. Hemoglobin A1c was 5.6 and lipid panel showed Cholest 193 Triglyc 93 HDL 62 CHOL/HD 3.1, LDLcalc 112. The patient was monitored on the Cardiac ICU for over 24 hours and then she was transferred to the cardiac floor. On the floor she continued to be tachycardica dn her heart rate would increase to the 130s with minimal exertion, her metoprolol XL was doubled to 100mg PO Daily. She continued to be tachycardic. While this was to be expected to an extent given her myocardial injury a limited ECHO of the LV was performed which showed mild to moderate regional LV systolic dysfuntion consistent with mid-LAD infarction. The other segments are hyperdynamic. No LVOT gradient seen. She was discharged home with follow up in the outpatient setting. . # CHF: Appears mildly volume overloaded with crackles on pulmonary auscultation and evidence of elevated filling pressures on RH catheterization. Pt was started on spironolactone because EF was less than 40%. Lisinopril 10mg PO daily was also started. She was satting well on room air and was transferred to ___ 3 for further care. She will need a repeat ECHO in the coming weeks to re-evaluate the extent of disease. . # Turners Syndrome: Given her CAD, we held her hormone replacement therapy. Calcium/ vitamin D was started given risk of osteoporosis. She was not restarted on her premarin as it increases her risk of CAD. . # leukocytosis: WBC count elevated to 15, likely stress response to recent STEMI. No evidence of infectious etiology such as fever, cough, dysuria. She was not started on Abx and her WBC normalized on hospital day 2. . CODE: Full Code
summary: You had a heart attack and required a cardiac catheterization where a drug eluting stent was placed in your left coronary artery to clear the blockage and keep the artery open. You will need to take aspirin and Plavix (clopidogrel) every day for at least one year to keep the stent from clotting off and causing another heart attack. Do not miss any doses or stop taking Plavix unless Dr. ___ that it is OK. Your heart is weak after the heart attack and you had some fluid buidlup in your lungs because of the weak heart. WE started you on some medicines to help the heart pump better and we expect that in ___ months, your heart will be stronger.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Summary ========================= ___ year-old woman with history of hypertension, Syndrome X (prior exertional chest pain with no CAD at angiography), and paroxysmal atrial fibrillation on coumadin who presents with dyspnea on exertion and found to have anemia (Hb down to 9.2 from ___ baseline) in setting of upper GI bleed. Acute issues: Dyspnea ========================== It was felt both anemia and CHF were contributing to patients symptoms. Accordingly, she was treated with 1 unit pRBC transfusion and conservative IV lasix. EGD/colonoscopy was held due to not being consistent with goals of care. Patients symptoms improved with this treatment. She was seen by ___ and able to ambulate without dyspnea and O2 sat 90-94% prior to discharge. She was set up with home ___ and discharged in good condition. Chronic issues: Atrial fibrillation ========================== In sinus rhythm on admission, with known LBBB. No TWI or STD on EKG. CHADS2 risk score is 4 (HTN, age, prior TIA), CHADS2Vasc 6. Warfarin was initially held and then restarted prior to discharge. INR at discharge was 2.0. Hypertension ========================== Normotensive on arrival, diltiazem decreased to 120 long acting daily on discharge. Transitional Issues =========================== 1. Patient was set up with PCP appointment on ___ with gerentology. 2. ENT appointment for chronic swallow difficulty was made for ___. Cardiology appointment was pending at time of discharge. 3. Diltiazem was decreased to 120mg daily as HR and BPs were well controlled on this regimen while inpatient. HR and BP should be rechecked at upcoming PCP ___. 4. Pt was started on 10mg po lasix daily. She was instructed to weigh herself daily and call the doctor for acute >3 pound changes. 5. Pt was started on pantoprazole for recent GI bleed, with calcium and vitamin D supplementation. 6. Home ___ was established to assist with home telemonitoring of weights, CBC and INR checks, monitoring of patient's hemodynamics (HR and BP) after starting lasix and home ___. 7. Due to 2 episodes of GI bleeds in the past year, CBC should be routinely checked with INR to determine if patient is chronically anemic from subacute GI bleeds. 8. PCP could consider outpatient sleep study for further w/u of pulmonary hypertension found on TTE. # Emergency Contact: ___ (son) ___ # Code: DNR, okay to intubate
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath. This was partly related to some recent bleeding which caused the dark red stool you had 2 weeks ago. We treated this a blood transfusion and are starting a new medication called pantoprazole to help prevent bleeding in the future. Was are also asking you take calcium and vitamin d while on this medication. We also decreased your diltiazem to 120mg as your heart rate and blood pressures were doing well during admission. Your shortness of breath was also due to some congestive heart failure. This means that some of the fluid your heart is pumping, is actually going into your lungs. We gave you a medication called lasix to help with this. You will take this medication daily on discharge.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F w/ PMHx CAD s/p CABG X4 (___), diastolic CHF (LVEF>55), renal artery stenosis s/p stent X2, hypertension, DM, s/p pacer and ICD placement admitted with chest discomfort and shortness of breath . # Shortness of breath/Chest Pain: The patient has anginal symptoms in the setting of hypertension. She had no symptoms of chest discomfort with a blood pressure les than 140 systolic. THe patient has difficult to control hypertension secondary to suspected renal artery stenosis and problems with medication adherence secondary to medication side effects/intolerances. The patient has declined further evaluation or intervention with cardiac catheterization. During her hospital stay her episodes of chest pain were relieved with ___ sublingual nitroglycerin. Her EKGs during this admission are relatively c/w prior with mildly more pronounced ST dep in inferior leads which may reflect strain versus demand ischemia in setting of tachycardia and hypertension. She is being discharged on a medication regimen she feels she can tolerate with as few intolerances as possible. She will be discharged on clonidine 0.3mg BID, Imdur 120mg QHS, nifedipine SR 60mg, hydralazine 75mg Q6 losartan 50mg and nitroglycerin 0.3mg/hr patch. . # UTI: Completed a 7 day course of cefpodoxime ending on ___. Previously on levofloxacin for UTI suppression but most recent UTI resistent to ciprofloxacin. Given few oral options remaining to cover UTI, further suppressive antibiotics were deferred. UA on discharge with no evidence of infection. The patient was started on estrogen cream to assist with dysuria secondary to atrophy. . # Constipation: The patient was given an aggressive bowel regimen while in house. She should continue on this regimen as needed upon discharge. . # Diabetes, CKD, and GERD: Stable; home medications were continued. . # The patient was DNR/DNI throughout this hospitalization.
summary: You presented to the hospital with chest pain and very high blood pressure. During your hospital stay, your heart was monitored with telemetry and EKGs. Your blood pressure control was improved when with taking your previous blood pressure medications. You currently are not taking your diuretic bumex as you are concerned about it harming your kidneys. You should continue to monitor your weight daily and ___ your primary care physician if your weight increases by more than 3 pounds. Your urinary tract infection was treated with cefpodoxime. At the time of discharge your had no evidence of a urinary tract infection. You have been started on a vaginal estrogen to help treat your dysuria.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of aortic stenosis ___ bicuspid AV, vulvar squamous cell carcinoma, chronic hypercarbia with home trilogy, erosive severe RA on chronic steroids, chronic pain on opiates, and anxiety who p/w acute on chronic hypoxia likely ___ acute on chronic diastolic heart failure. # Acute on chronic mixed hypercarbic & hypoxemic respiratory failure" # Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure: # Obesity hypoventilation syndrome: Pt presented with significant volume overload (weight 226lbs), BLE pitting edema and high O2 requirements. She was aggressively diuresis with IV Lasix and returned to dry weight of 207lbs without any evidence of kidney injury. After pt had reached relative dry weight and was doing much better but was still experiencing DOE, requiring between ___ of NC O2. ___ was consulted and pt underwent a CHest CT that showed enlarged peripheral based nodules, follow up PET was recommended. We reached out to her Rad Onc, Gyn Onc, Rheum and Pulm teams to ensure everyone was aware of the findings. It remains unclear if these are rheumatoid nodules or if they are due to metastatic spread of vulvar malignancy. Dr ___ has ordered a PET scan that pt will have completed in the next ___ weeks. ___ has recommended additional work up including PFTs, repeat TTE with bubble and sleep study to better understand her ongoing O2 requirements as well as optimize her trilogy. Her ___ was discontinued as it was felt to be unnecessary and ___ had suggested ongoing diuresis but pt was really eager to return home. She will continue Lasix 80mg BID with plan to take Metolazone for ___ of weight gain. She is already taking potassium repletion at home that corresponds with her Lasix regimen and agrees to notify her PCP if she is taking metolazone as she will need close follow up of her labs. Pt was discharged with plan for close PCP and pulm follow up for additional work up and will be getting PET scheduled in the next ___ weeks for follow up of these nodules. # Rheumatoid arthritis: Severe erosive seropositive RA, followed by Dr. ___. You underwent a Chest CT that was notable for multiple pleural based nodules that appears more prominent than the last chest CT. As we discussed, it is important for you to get the follow up PET scan to help guide next steps. We have not made any changes to your Lasix regimen but have provided a prescription for Metolazone 5mg to be taken with Lasix if your weight increases by ___. Please notify Dr. ___ you are taking Metolazone because it can really deplete your potassium stores.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with history of HFpEF (>55% in ___, morbid obesity, OSA on BIPAP at night, paroxysmal Afib on warfarin, and ovarian cysts presenting with recurrent tuboovarian abscess. # Recurrent ___ Patient initially treated with CTX/flagyl (___). Vanc added ___ when BCx grew GPCs, but vanc stopped when speciated to CONS (thought to be contaminant), also added cipro ___ empirically given history of citrobacter with inducible cephalosporin resistance). ID consulted who agreed with this abx course, and recommended ertapenem on discharge for 4 weeks total (until ___ w ID follow up. ___ was consulted for drainage of abscesses, requested pelvic ultrasound. On review of ultrasound images felt that there was not an appreciable collection to drain and the abscess had decreased significantly with medical/antibiotic management. OB/GYN requested follow-up as an outpatient. ___ Patient with creatinine bumped from baseline of 1.0-1.7 on ___. Potentially due to decreased p.o. intake in the setting of diuresis. ___ also be ATN due to contrast load of imaging. Given a 500 cc bolus of fluids, initially held home torsemide and lisinopril. Creatinine improved to 1.1. Restarted home torsemide and lisinopril prior to discharge with normalization of Cr. # HFpEF # Chronic hypoxemic respiratory failure No evidence of acute exacerbation. Denies any symptoms of dyspnea. No ___ edema, no PND, no orthopnea. Reports that her sensation of breathing is at her baseline and weight is near dry weight. Wears oxygen from time to time for comfort. Some lower extremity edema noted though patient did not feel significant increase in edema. No significant increase in weight during hospitalization, diuretic was restarted shortly after admission once creatinine began to downtrend. Home metoprolol was continued during hospitalization. Lisinopril restarted prior to discharge. # HTN Initially held home lisinopril, was restarted day prior to discharge. Continued home amlodipine during hospitalization. # Paroxysmal AF CHADS2VASC 3 (gender, CHF, HTN). Deemed not a candidate for DOAC therapy in setting of elevated BMI. Presently in sinus. Continue to home metoprolol XL 25mg BID. Initially held warfarin for 2 days in setting of possible ___ procedure, home warfarin dose was restarted prior to discharge with INR monitoring. # Pre-diabetes New diagnosis. A1c was 6.3% on ___ and she was started on metformin. Metformin held on admission, however patient reportedly not taking metformin at home. # Chronic Pain - Home tramadol changed to oxycodone in the inpatient setting. Written for a few tablet script as pain was improving on discharge. # OSA # Pulm HTN - CPAP ordered, pt not using >30 minutes spent on patient care on day of discharge. Transitional issues: ==================== [] F/U with OB/GYN; appointment scheduled [] f/u with ID re: Ertapenem, weekly labs ordered with ___, OPAT to schedule and will contact patient [] F/u with PCP ___: hospitalization f/u and Metformin. Pt prescribed but reported not taking. Consider further discussion re: need. [] Wean oxycodone as able [] Dc weight 508.16 lb/230.5 kg though somewhat unreliable scale
summary: You came to the hospital because you were having abdominal pain, feeling like you had a fever/chills, and increased shortness of breath. In the hospital we did imaging of your abdomen with xray and ultrasound and found you had an infection on your ovary (tubo-ovarian abscess). You have had this problem in the past. We treated you with antibiotics which helped fight the infection and treated your pain. You received a special IV (___ line) to enable you to receive antibiotics outside of the hospital. You will be discharged on intravenous antibiotics which will be administered through your IV (PICC line). A home ___ will come each day to help you with this. The ___ will schedule follow up regarding monitoring for your antibiotics.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ man with history of hypertension, hyperlipidemia, PVD, ESRD on HD, aortic stenosis s/p porcine AVR ___, who presents with symptoms of chest pain and shortness of breath. . #. Fluid overload (pump) - The patient presented with symptoms concerning for a possible new cardiomyopathy. Given that he has ESRD on HD, fluid was removed via ultra-filtration while at HD. TTE showed no evidence of a cardiomyopathy, and the patient was noted to have a preserved EF of 60%. Cardiac catheterization confirmed the patient's preserved EF at about 50%. The Renal HD team was following while the patient was admitted, and he was dialyzed on his normal home dialysis schedule, with additional ultra-filtration for fluid removal. . #. CAD (ischemia) - The patient presented with symptoms concerning for unstable angina. His ECG showed evidence of a prior septal and inferior MI, although it had no acute ischemic changes and was unchanged since ___. His cardiac enzymes were also negative. It was believed that he may have had a missed event ___ days prior to his presentation, at the onset of his symptoms of chest pain and shortness of breath. He had a TTE that was not suspiscious of ischemic disease, and a cardiac catheterization that revealed normal coronary arteries with mild disease. He had been treated with aspirin, plavix, metoprolol, high-dose atorvastatin, and Imdur while inpatient, but he was resumed on his home outpatient medications on discharge. . #. AS s/p porcine AVR - The patient has a history of AS s/p porcine AVR in ___. He now presented with chest pain and shortness of breath. Evaluation by TTE showed a new elevated trans-aortic gradient and velocity with decreased valve area. Per the TEE team, ___ would be unlikely to provide more information regarding the aortic valve, as TTE allows for better views of the aortic valve. Cardiac catheterization was done to evaluate the stenosis, which revealed only a mildly elevated trans-aortic gradient and otherwise normal bio-prosthetic valve. . #. Rhythm - The patient was in NSR during his hospitalization, with one episode of idio-ventricular NSVT noted. . #. ESRD on HD - The patient's ESRD is likely due to his hypertension, and he is on hemodialysis every ___, and ___. The Renal HD team was following while the patient was admitted, and he was dialyzed on his normal home dialysis schedule, with additional ultra-filtration for fluid removal. . #. Leukocytosis - The patient had no obvious evidence of infection, and no left shift but a significant eosinophilia on his differential. He noted recently starting a new medication, Erythromycin, approximately 2 weeks prior to his admission, for his skin graft surgery on his left leg. The eosinophilia was believed to be a drug reaction, although no clear allergy symptoms were elicited. He was changed from Erythromycin to Keflex during this hospitalization, and continued on Keflex throughout. He completed his planned course of prophylactic antibiotics while inpatient. . #. Peripheral Vascular Disease - Cardiac catheterization and aortography revealed a dilated and tortuous aorta with a small aneurysm in the abdominal aorta and the left iliac artery, and a patent stent in the right iliac artery. A CT-Angiogram was recommended for further evaluation of the abdominal aorta, iliac arteries, and extent of peripheral vascular occlusive disease. The patient was advised to discuss this with his primary care physician / cardiologist as an outpatient, and to follow-up after the CT-Angiogram with either his primary vascular surgeon or with Dr. ___ at ___. Dr. ___ office is available at ___. The CT-Angiogram will need to be scheduled with respect to the patient's home hemodialysis schedule. .
summary: You were admitted to ___ on ___ for congestive heart failure that was noted at ___ ___. You underwent dialysis at ___ prior to your transfer to take off some of the fluid on your lungs, and you were admitted to the CCU here. You resumed your normal dialysis schedule and continued to do well. You had an Echo of your heart which had some findings concerning for tightening of your aortic valve prosthesis but was otherwise normal. You then had a cardiac catheterization which showed that your aortic valve prosthesis was actually normal as well, and that you still had extra fluid on your lungs. You were stable for discharge on ___. Your symptoms of shortness of breath and chest pain were likely due to you having too much fluid in your system, which put a strain on your heart and caused congestive heart failure, giving you shortness of breath.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #BRADYCARDIA: Patient presented after her daughter noted that her pulse was slow at home. In the ED, the patient was noted to have heart rates in the ___. She was symptomatic of her bradycardia and felt very weak. Her TSH was found to be slightly elevated, but T4 was normal. Her symptoms were felt to be most consistent with sick sinus syndrome. She was evaluated by the electrophysiology service and a dual-chamber pacemaker was placed on ___. She tolerated the procedure well. The device was interrogated on POD 1 and was functioning well. She has a follow up appointment scheduled in the device clinic on ___. #ACUTE ON CHRONIC HFpEF: She has a known diagnosis of HFpEF. TTE obtained in the ED demonstrated EF of 70%. She had exam findings on presentation consistent with mild fluid overload so was diuresed with IV Lasix. This was transitioned back to her home dose of PO Lasix 30mg the day of discharge. She was continued on amlodipine. Her irbesartan-HCTZ was replaced with valsartan due to concomitant use of loop diuretic. She should have a repeat BMP drawn on ___ to check electrolytes and kidney function. #PAROXYSMAL AFIB: On warfarin with therapeutic INR on admission. Now in V-paced rhythm. She was continued on her warfarin at the same dosing. #HTN: The patient was noted to have systolic blood pressures in the 180s on arrival to the ED. She was transitioned to valsartan 160mg daily and continued on her previous dose of amlodipine. Her BPs were well-controlled on this regimen. Her nitroglycerin patch was stopped and not restarted. Systolic BPs in 180s on admission, now much improved #UTI: Patient reported dysuria while hospitalized. UA was suggestive of infection, culture positive for Klebsiella. She was given 3 days of ceftriaxone while hospitalized and transitioned to cefpodoxime at discharge for a total of 5 days of antibiotic therapy. She has a documented history of allergy to Keflex, but had no symptoms of allergic reaction while on ceftriaxone. Her allergy history should be revisited at outpatient follow up and clarified in case of future need for antibiotics.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had a slow heart rate You were evaluated for placement of a pacemaker, which was put in on ___. You tolerated the procedure well and your heart rates were improved after. You were noted to have findings on physical exam suggestive of fluid in your lungs, so you were given IV medications to help pull some of the fluid out. You were put back on your home Lasix at discharge. You reported some burning with urination. We checked your urine and found that you have a urinary tract infection. You were given IV antibiotics in the hospital and then transitioned to an oral antibiotic at discharge. Please take the entire course of antibiotics.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ with history significant for Aflutter, CVA ___ (R ___, DVT/PE on warfarin, dementia, HTN, HLD, DM2, mild MR, and diastolic CHF who presented from home with possible change in mental status. Per the children and care-takers she spends most of the day in bed, alert but not very verbally interactive. She was noted to be "nodding off" during the day and was found to have a BP of 89mmHg on check. PCP recommended ___ referral and she was admitted for workup. While in the hospital she had no fevers, leukocytosis, observed seizures, significant arrhythmia, severe electrolyte abnormality. C diff testing was negative. TSH and cardiac enzymes were normal. Iron studies showed deficiency with microcytic anemia. Chest Xray revealed bilateral effusions without pneumonia. She was observed and children noted her to be back to baseline shortly after admission. Therefore she was discharged home with her high-level of home care without a clear cause for her falling asleep or nodding off on the day PTA. We started iron supplementation and PCP may consider low dose diuretic for her pleural effusions and likely dCHF. # Lethargy with episode of transient hypotension: Patient presented with one day history of lethargy. Guaiac negative in ___. Chest Xray revealed bilateral effusions without pneumonia. Source may have been slight volume overload leading to fatigue. Electrolyte disturbance with hyponatremia may have contributed. Patient also on gabapentin 400 mg QHS which could lead to slight fatigue. Received 20 mg furosemide in ___. Gabapentin held and reversible causes of delirium investigated with negative RPR/TSH/B12. Infectious workup with U/A, C.diff negative. Cardiac workup with negative Trop and no ischemic changes on EKG. with Energy level at baseline per family at bedside. CHRONIC ISSUES # Hyponatremia: Na 129 on admission, likely hypervolemic given evidence of volume overload in ___ and improvement with furosemide in ___. Na 136 on discharge. # Type II DM: not on oral hypoglycemic at home. Followed with QID glucose checks. No changes made. # Atrial Flutter: not on any rate controlling agents. INR therapeutic throughout admission but was at 2.9 on day of discharge so we recommend holding ___ dose and restarting on ___ with 2mg warfarin. # Hyperlipidemia: continued atorvastatin 80mg. Consider downtitrating dose given age and side effects of long term use. # Anemia, Microcytic: started iron supplementation with ascorbic acid for better absorption. # GERD: continued pantoprazole 20mg. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [ ] assess need for ongoing gabapentin [ ] continue iron, discuss with family if anemia workup warranted [ ] follow up RPR
summary: You were admitted to the ___ after your family was worried about a change in your mental status. We found that you had some extra fluid in your lungs and generally from a mild increase in you chronic heart failure. You were given medication to help you take off this extra fluid and did well. You were evaluated for a number of causes of changes in mental status, including vitamin deficiency, infection, and heart problems. We did not find any evidence of abnormalities to explain a change in alertness. We did notice that you were iron deficient, so we started iron and vitamin C to help you absorb iron. We will also ask Dr. ___ to consider a low dose diuretic to help remove extra fluid from around your lungs, but because you were not short of breath we did not start this here.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of IDDM, CAD s/p CABGx5 in ___, CHF (EF 40-45%), HLD, and orthostatic hypotension with increasingly frequent unresponsive episodes ___ to difficult to manage orthostatic hypotension due to diabetic autonomic neuropathy and deconditioning. # Orthostatic hypotension: Patient presented with episodes of unresponsiveness/staring spells after standing where he would slowly start to fall over. He had no shaking, incontinence, or tongue biting. In the ED, he received CT of head and c-spine for recent headstrike during an episode, which did not show any acute injuries or processes. Continuous ECG monitoring for 48hr did not find any clinically significant arrhythmias. EEG was negative for seizure activity. Carotid ultrasound found his L carotid was patent s/p prior endarterectomy, and R carotid was 60-69% occluded. He was found to be significantly orthostatic, with SBP falling from 150s to ___ on standing. Midodrine was uptitrated with worsening of orthostatics, which was thought to be due to increased afterload worsening outflow from aortic stenosis. Therefore, midodrine was discontinued. Cardiology, was concerned for low flow state underestimating AS, so he got dobutamine stress which showed that AS was not severe. Autonomics consult ___ felt his orthostatic hypotension was due to autonomic dysfunction but that a major contributor is "deconditioning and lack of activity" and he recommended extensive ___. The ability to increase fluids was limited in him due to his CHF. However, his weight did downtrend during this hospitalization on his home torsemide. Therefore this was decreased from 20 mg to 10 mg daily and then held for 2 days and he was given some gentle IV fluid to bring him closer to his dry weight of 122 lbs. On discharge his torsemide was restarted at 10 mg every other day. # Diabetes: initially placed on insulin sliding scale, switched back to home 70/30 42U before breakfast and dinner on ___ with sliding scale for correctional doses. Gabapentin was held during this stay to avoid potential contribution to intermittent unresponsiveness # CHF: continued home ASA, torsemide dose reduced as above TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Patient discharged on 10 mg torsemide every other day as his weight was decreasing on 10 mg daily contributing to worsening orthostasis; please adjust as needed -Discharge weight: 115.2 kg -Patient required some low dose sliding scale insulin to maintain normal blood sugars while inpatient; please follow up diabetes control as an outpatient -Patient's topiramate was held due to concern that it would worsen peripheral neuropathy and increase chance of falls. He was on this for weight loss. -Patient's gabapentin held due to concern for contributing to dizziness, may restart if symptoms are stable as an outpatient -Patient should wear daily compression stockings -Patient found to have 60-69% stenosis of the R carotid artery. Consider increasing statin dosing if tolerated. # CODE STATUS: Full # CONTACT: ___ (wife, HCP) ___ ___
summary: As you know, you were admitted to the hospital because you were having increasingly frequent "staring spells" at home, during which you were not responsive and which you did not remember. You were given CT scans of your head and neck since you recently fell and hit your head at home; the scans did not find any abnormalities. You were placed on 24-hour monitoring for your heart, which did not find any abnormalities that would cause your symptoms. Monitoring of your brain for possible seizures also found nothing abnormal. An ultrasound of your neck found that your right carotid artery is narrowed but does not require further treatment. Your blood pressure was found to fall significantly when you sat or stood up. To treat this, we changed the dosing of the midodrine you took at home. This did not improve your symptoms, so we stopped the midodrine and the cardiology team evaluated you and recommended a stress test of your heart with an ultrasound. This procedure found that your symptoms are not being caused by your heart. You episodes are most likely cause by an inability of your blood pressure to increase normally when you stand or sit up. This was probably caused by your diabetes. The best treatment is to keep your weight right at your ideal weight of 269 lbs. If your weight is higher you will have trouble breathing due to fluid on your lungs from your heart failure. If you weight is much lower, you will be dehydrated and will have more lightheadedness. Eating and drinking normally and taking your Torsemide as prescribed should help. Your torsemide has been decreased to 10 mg every other day. You will need to check you weights every day to make sure this is the right dose for you.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of IDDM, CAD s/p CABGx5 in ___, CHF (EF 40-45%), HLD, and orthostatic hypotension with increasingly frequent unresponsive episodes ___ to difficult to manage orthostatic hypotension due to diabetic autonomic neuropathy and deconditioning. # Orthostatic hypotension: Patient presented with episodes of unresponsiveness/staring spells after standing where he would slowly start to fall over. He had no shaking, incontinence, or tongue biting. In the ED, he received CT of head and c-spine for recent headstrike during an episode, which did not show any acute injuries or processes. Continuous ECG monitoring for 48hr did not find any clinically significant arrhythmias. EEG was negative for seizure activity. Carotid ultrasound found his L carotid was patent s/p prior endarterectomy, and R carotid was 60-69% occluded. He was found to be significantly orthostatic, with SBP falling from 150s to ___ on standing. Midodrine was uptitrated with worsening of orthostatics, which was thought to be due to increased afterload worsening outflow from aortic stenosis. Therefore, midodrine was discontinued. Cardiology, was concerned for low flow state underestimating AS, so he got dobutamine stress which showed that AS was not severe. Autonomics consult ___ felt his orthostatic hypotension was due to autonomic dysfunction but that a major contributor is "deconditioning and lack of activity" and he recommended extensive ___. The ability to increase fluids was limited in him due to his CHF. However, his weight did downtrend during this hospitalization on his home torsemide. Therefore this was decreased from 20 mg to 10 mg daily and then held for 2 days and he was given some gentle IV fluid to bring him closer to his dry weight of 122 lbs. On discharge his torsemide was restarted at 10 mg every other day. # Diabetes: initially placed on insulin sliding scale, switched back to home 70/30 42U before breakfast and dinner on ___ with sliding scale for correctional doses. Gabapentin was held during this stay to avoid potential contribution to intermittent unresponsiveness # CHF: continued home ASA, torsemide dose reduced as above TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Patient discharged on 10 mg torsemide every other day as his weight was decreasing on 10 mg daily contributing to worsening orthostasis; please adjust as needed -Discharge weight: 115.2 kg -Patient required some low dose sliding scale insulin to maintain normal blood sugars while inpatient; please follow up diabetes control as an outpatient -Patient's topiramate was held due to concern that it would worsen peripheral neuropathy and increase chance of falls. text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old man with cirrhosis, type 2 diabetes, coronary artery disease, hypertension, congestive heart failure with EF 40%, and seizure disorder presenting with cough, fevers, and consolidations on chest x-ray consistent with pneumonia. . 1. Pneumonia: Chest x-ray from admission showed right middle and lower lobe consolidations. His oxygen saturations remained above 92% on room air. He was monitored in the intensive care unit overnight and transferred to a general medical floor the morning after admission. He was started on vancomycin and ampicillin-sulbactam to complete a two-week course for hospital-acquired versus aspiration pneumonia. A PICC line was placed ___ for intravenous access to complete the course of antibiotics, ending ___. . 2. Hypernatremia: Asymptomatic and due to free water depletion. His free water flushes were increased to 400 cc q4h with improvement in sodium. His sodium should be monitored intermittently and his free water flushes should be adjusted accordingly for hypernatremia. . 3. Question urinary tract infection from nursing home: The patient was on nitrofurantoin on admission, and it is unclear whether this was for treatment or prophylaxis of urinary tract infection. This was discontinued when the above antibiotics were started for pneumonia. He can restart nitrofurantoin if this was being given for prophylaxis when the course of vancomycin and unasyn is complete. . 4. Mental status: It was believed that the patient was delirious on admission, however, after discussion with the patient's wife and the nursing home his mental status was thought to be at baseline. He was treated for pneumonia as above. He was frequently redirected. . 5. History of nonacloholic steatohepatitis/cirrhosis: The patient is status post TIPS. He is not transplant candidate currently due to his multiple comorbiditis. His MELD score was 5 on admission. He was continued on rifamixin and lactulose. . 6. Chronic systolic congestive heart failure: EF is 40%. His metoprolol was continued during admission. The patient was hypovolemic on admission and lasix was held. Lasix was restarted prior to discharge. . 7. Seizure disorder: No active issues. The patient was continued on keppra, topomax and zonisamide. There was initial confusion regarding his dose of keppra, and the patient was initially given 2250 mg on admission, however, this was subsequently changed to his home dose of 500 mg twice daily. . 8. Type 2 diabetes: No active issues. The patient was continued on glargine 100 units twice daily as per his outpatient regimen. He received humalog sliding scale insulin as needed. . 9. Coronary artery disease: No active issues. The patient was continued on metoprolol. He is not on aspirin or statin at baseline, likely due to his liver disease, and this can be readdressed as an outpatient. 10. Hypothyroidism: The patient was continued on his outpatietn dose of levothyroxine 400 mcg daily. During admission, his T4 was elevated to 1.9 with normal TSH. His laboratories should be checked after resolution of this acute illness and his dose of levothyroxine adjusted accordingly. . 11. Sacral decubitus ulcer: The patient was followed by the wound care nurse. . 12. Pancytopenia: His blood counts were at baseline during admission. His pancytopenia is believed secondary to liver disease. This should be monitored intermittently.
summary: You were transferred to the hospital with fevers and a cough. You were found to have a pneumonia and PICC line was placed in your arm so that you can complete a two week course of antibiotics (12 more days). You were also noted to have high levels of sodium in your blood, and this is probably because you were not getting enough water in your diet. You are being given more water with your tube feeds. We did not change any of your medications (except adding those two antibiotics for two weeks). Your thyroid levels were high, and they should be re-checked and the dose of your thyroid medicine may need to be adjusted.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ year old man with CAD s/p CABG in ___ c/b an inferior MI, s/p left circumflex stenting in ___ and ___ ___ diagonal in ___, paroxysmal a fib not on AC, CKD, and HTN who presented with a months long history with progressive dyspnea on exertion as well as 1 day of atypical chest pain. # CORONARIES: 3 vessel disease, s/p CABG in ___ and PCI of LCx in ___ # PUMP: normal LV function, from ___ MIBI # RHYTHM: paroxysmal atrial fibrillation #Atypical Chest Pain: As above, Mr. ___ has an extensive ischemic cardiovascular history. He distinguished his chest pain on presentation from his prior ischemic-type pain. It was felt that his pain was consistent with musculoskeletal etiology, as it began within 12 hours of his lifting a heavy box. In further support of this, the pain was protracted without EKG changes or troponin elevation, reproducible with palpation, sharp/stabbing pain, non-positional, non-exertional, non-pleuritic, and not relieved with nitroglycerin. A D-dimer was normal for age adjustment. Otherwise, his cardiovascular medications were continued as below. #DYSPNEA ON EXERTION ___ Mr. ___ endorsed symptoms of progressively worsening dyspnea on exertion and orthopnea over months, as well as appeared volume overloaded on exam with significant peripheral edema and elevated JVP. proBNP was not elevated, but this may be invalid in the setting of his obesity. Per review of ___ records, there is a mention of history of CHF but no formal diagnosis is documented in ___ records. It was felt that Mr. ___ likely has ___ given prior pMIBIs show normal EF ___ with EF65%; ___ with EF55%). He was already on Lasix 40mg PO TID at home "for leg swelling." His possible dry weight on review of At___ records is roughly 230lbs. He received diuresis with Lasix 80mg IV x2 days, then was transitioned to 80mg PO BID. In terms of afterload reduction, he was continued on his home anti-hypertensive, lisinopril. He received neurohormonal blockade with his home metoprolol. #PAROXYSMAL A FIB, CHADSVASc of 5: While in the hospital, Mr. ___ was noted to be in a fib without RVR. It was felt this could be contributing to his dyspnea, but he was otherwise asymptomatic. He was noted to not be on anticoagulation. Anticoagulation was discussed with both the patient and his outpatient cardiologist and decision was made to start xarelto 20mg daily. He was continued on beta blockade, though his metoprolol succinate dosing was decreased from 50mg to 25mg due to his borderline bradycardia. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== #CAD s/p CABG and PCI: As above, there was low concern for ischemic origins of Mr. ___ chest pain. He was continued on his beta blockade as above. It was unclear why he was on full dose aspirin, nor why he was a moderate intensity statin vs. high intensity. Given initiation of Xarelto, full dose aspirin was changed to 81mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ================== DISCHARGE Wt: 226.63lbs / 102.8kg NEW/CHANGED MEDICATIONS New [ ] Xarelto 20mg with evening meal Changed [ ] Lasix 40mg TID --> Lasix 80mg BID [ ] Metoprolol succinate 50mg daily --> Metoprolol succinate 25mg daily [ ] ASA 325mg --> 81mg - Please obtain TTE to evaluate EF as outpatient. This was ordered but not completed while inpatient. - Patient was changed from full dose ASA to 81mg given initiation of anticoagulation with Xarelto - Consider switch of simvastatin 20mg to high intensity statin # CODE: Full Code (note: initially DNR/DNI, changed after conversation with daughter and pt) # CONTACT: HCP: ___, daughter, ___
summary: You had chest pain, which was concerning given your history of heart disease. We ran tests to make sure you were not having a heart attack. We felt your chest pain was probably due to a pulled muscle. However, we were concerned that your difficulty breathing and leg swelling were signs of a weak heart ("congestive heart failure"). We gave you medications to get the extra fluid out of your body, which can happen when you have a weak heart. We spoke with your outpatient heart doctor about your irregular heart rhythm ("atrial fibrillation" or "afib") and we decided to put you on a blood thinner called Xarelto.
