text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old male with hypertrophic cardiomyopathy and chronic substernal chest pain who presented with substernal chest pain similar in nature to his usual symptom following consumption of 3 beers the day prior to admission. The patient's course was complicated by right-sided weakness just prior to discharge. The patient had a head CT that was negative for stroke. The patient was also seen by neurology who did not feel that this was the result of a vascular of ischemic event and felt that there was a very large psychogenic overlay. Right-sided weakness- Pt developed acute R sided weakness right before plan to be discharged home. A code stroke called and he was immediately evaluated by the stroke service. He underwent emergenct CTA which was completely normal. His neurologic exam was notable for giveway weakness and non-physiologic visual loss. Given his neurologic exam and the timing of his event there was suspicion for a functional deficit. No interventions were taken given that there was no evidence of a vascular occlusion. Neurology did not feelt hat the event was vascular or ischemic in nature but rather that there was a very large psychogenic overlay to the patient's symptoms. He was continued on his home aspirin. Despite these conclusions the patient continued to complain of lack of coordination on the right side. he was evaluated by physical therapy who felt that he would benefit from acute rehab. Chest pain: The patient presented with chest pain which was similar in quality to that which he experiences chronically from his hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. The pain occurred in the setting of ethanol use and likely dehydration. It was felt that this acute episode of pain was likely related to supply and demand mismatch in the setting of this dehydration. He had no evidence of ishemia on EKG. He had two sets of negative cardiac enzymes making acute coronary ischemia unlikely. His metoprolol was continued at 100 mg three times a day. He was also continued on verapamil to optimize cardiac function in the setting of his hypertrophic cardiomyopathy. His goal heart rate should be less than 100 and his goal systolic blood pressure should be less than 130. Given his right sided weakness his narcotic medications were discontinued. He was counseled on the relationship between consumption of beer and caffeinated beverages and the worsening of his symptoms. Coronary Artery Disease: The patient had no significant coronary disease on most recent catheterization in ___. He was continued on aspirin and metoprolol. Chronic Diastolic Congestive Heart Failure: The patient had a preserved ejection fraction on his most recent echocardiogram in ___. He has a history of hypertrophic cardiomyopathy with severe LVOT gradient. He is s/p ethanol ablation in ___ and ___ with persistent gradient. His regimen is to optimize diastolic filling time with metoprolol and verapamil. His metoprolol was increased during this hospitalization given evidence of tachycardia with exertion. Non-Sustained Ventricular Tachycardia: The patient is s/p AICD placement for primary prevention. He was monitored on telemetry during his stay without significant arrhythmia. He was continued on metoprolol. Alcohol Use: The patient denies a history of alcohol withdrawal. He was counseled regarding the use of alcohol. He was continued on folate and was placed on thiamine and a multivitamin for nutritional support. He was seen by our psychiatry service and he declined assistance for his alcohol use. Leukopenia: The patient has a chronic leukopenia ranging from 3.0 to 4.5 since prior to ___. The etiology is unclear but may be related to his ethanol consumption. This may be readdressed by his primary care physician. GERD: No active in patient issues. He was continued on his home proton pump inhibitor.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital with chest pain. Lab tests were drawn which showed you did NOT have a heart attack. This appears to be the same chest pain you have had in the past due to your hypertrophic obstructive cardiomyopathy. The chest pain was likely exacerbated by dehydration. You had an operative evaluation at a prior admission which unfortunately concluded that you are not a candidate for surgical repair of your disease. Therefore, we strongly encourage you to avoid alcohol use as well as caffeine ingestion, and to stay well-hydrated. This should reduce your episodes of chest pain. Prior to you discharge you experience right-sided weakness and fell. You had a head CT that did not show evidence of a stroke. You were also evaluated by neurology who felt that your symptoms were not caused by a stroke.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ==================== PATIENT SUMMARY ==================== Mr. ___ is a pleasant ___ gentleman with PMH significant for mitral regurgitation and HLD who presented with 2-month history of worsening BLE edema and 5 day history of progressive dyspnea with exertion - found to have acute diastolic heart failure. ==================== ACUTE ISSUES ==================== #acute exacerbation of chronic diastolic heart failure. #HFpEF: Presented with increasing BLE edema and dyspnea on exertion. On admission exam, was found to have elevated JVP, bibasilar crackles, and 2+ BLE pitting edema; as well as elevated BNP in the setting of ___ TTE whcih showed worsened MR and new MVP. We obtained a TTE, which showed moderate 2+ MR with an eccentric jet and moderate-severe MVP. In terms of underlying etiologies, no chest pain and Trops were <0.01. The trigger was thought to be worsened MR and new MVP in the setting of high NaCl intake. Mr. ___ was started on Lasix gtt with good urine output, and was eventually transitioned to PO Torsemide 80mg daily at discharge. His discharge weight was 98.7 kg (217.5 lbs) and he was net negative 6 L since admission. Additionally, he was started on Lisinopril 2.5mg daily. ==================== CHRONIC ISSUES ==================== #PVCs: Present throughout hospitalization and were asymptomatic. Appeared to be bigeminy, and his prior EKGs were with PVCs as well (however with increased frequency on admission). Thought to be due to underlying structural disease (MR, ___) and CHF exacerbation, as his electrolytes were normal (and were repleted with diuresis). We monitored throughout his hospitalization with telemetry. #Alcohol Use: Reportedly drinks ~21 drinks/week and has chronic macrocytosis, however no h/o withdrawal. He was initially placed on CIWA (did not score or require Ativan), and completed a 3-day course of IV Thiamine. #HLD: Continued home Pravastatin #Seasonal Allergies: Continued home Fluticasone and Loratadine. ==================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== [ ] DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 98.7 kg (217.5 lbs) [ ] DISCHARGE DIURETIC: Torsemide 80mg daily [ ] DISCHARGE ANTICOAGULATION: None [ ] FOLLOW UP LABORATORY TESTING: Repeat BMP on ___ (to be followed by the heart failure clinic). [ ] MEDICATION CHANGES: [ ] NEW: Torsemide 80mg daily, Lisinopril 2.5mg daily [ ] STOPPED: Furosemide 20mg BID [ ] CHANGED: None [ ] consider checking B12 level to evaluate macrocytosis
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had been feeling short of breath and had swelling in your legs. You were found to have fluid on your lungs, in your legs, and in your abdomen. This was felt to be due to a condition called heart failure, where your heart does not pump hard enough and fluid backs up into your lungs and body. You had an ultrasound ("Echo") of your heart, which showed that you have a leaky mitral valve in your heart. You were given a diuretic medication through the IV to help get the fluid out, and were eventually transitioned to a diuretic medication that you can take at home. You improved considerably and were ready to leave the hospital.

[ ] MEDICATION CHANGES: [ ] NEW: Torsemide 80mg daily, Lisinopril 2.5mg daily [ ] STOPPED: Furosemide 20mg BID [ ] CHANGED: None [ ] consider checking B12 level to evaluate macrocytosis

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ w/ Hx of CAD s/p CABG and CHF s/p ICD placement who presented with a fever, hypoxemia, and septic shock found to be flu positive. His course was complicated by hypoxic respiratory failure, ARDS, NSTEMI (demand ischemia) and ___. # Septic shock: Initially required pressure support ___ the ICU. Found to be flu positive and suspected to have sepsis from pulmonary source. Treated with 5 days of oseltamivir. See below for anti-bacterial abx course. # Hypoxemic respiratory failure: Pt was intubated at outside hospital for respiratory distress and hypoxemia. CXR w/ bilateral infiltrates. P to F ratio consistent with severe ARDS. Pt flu positive. Pt ventilated according to ARDSnet protocol and sedated with propofol and fentanyl and then fentanyl and precedex. Pt paralyzed ___ order to improve ventilation and esophageal balloon placed to monitor trans-pleural pressures. Pt was several liters positive over the course of his stay and was eventually aggressively diuresed. Pt was successfully extubated on ___. #Pneumonia: Pt's CXR difficult to assess for infiltrate due to diffuse alveolar process. Pt initially treated with vancomycin, cefepime, and levofloxacin. Antibiotics discontinued for ___ecame febrile. Antibiotics restarted and Flagyl added on ___. Vancomycin, cefepime, and levofloxacin continued for ___hronic systolic CHF: Pt w/ hx on CHF on home Lasix 40mg daily. CXR w/ b/l diffuse alveolar process. Home Lasix initially held due to hypotension and clinical euvolemia/hypovolemia. EF down to 15% from 25% on previous echo. Likely due to decreased cardiac function ___ setting of sepsis. Once pressor requirement resolved, pt was initialy diuresed agressively with BID Lasix dosing. This was held on the floor due to hypovolemic hypernatremia (see below). #Type II NSTEMI: Pt has hx of CAD with known occluded vein grafts and patent LIMA to LAD. Had a significant trop leak ___ the setting of tachycardia at OSH, with tropT peaking at 1.2. No clear correlate on EKG. Felt that this was unliklely plaque rupture and more likely ___ supply/demand mismatch. Felt that an occlusion fo his LIMA graft would have a more fulminant cardiac presentation. ASA and plavix were continued, and he was heparinized for 48 hrs. No coronary angiography. B-blocker was held while septic but ultimately restarted. Losartan was restarted at a lower dose on discharge. #ARF: Cr at 1.8 and initially uptrended to 2.4, but then down to 1.1 prior to discharge. Likely due to ATN ___ setting of renal hypoperfusion. #Hypernatremia: Na as high as 151, due to hypovolemia. Treated with D5W and slowly corrected to 139 prior to discharge. #Hematuria: Urine ___ Foley was initially frankly bloody. Likely due to traumatic Foley placement. Pt eventually had decreased urine output and bladder was irrigated producing several clots. Urology was consulted, symptoms were improving and no intervention indicated. ___ need follow up to ensure no blood ___ urine after resolution of foley trauma. #Anxiety: Pt very anxious while intubated. Home SSRI and benzo started, but benzodiazepines were stopped as he was experiencing disorientation/delusions. Pt also started on trazodone. At next PCP ___, this should be assessed as to continue or d/c. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES [] please repeat CBC within one week and chem panel within 5 days. [] Please closely monitor fluid status and restart home lasix (40mg daily) when appropriate [] please uptitrate losartan as blood pressures tolerate (home dose 50mg QD, being discharged on 25mg QD) [] needs cardiology follow up within approx 2 weeks for follow up of CHF and decrease ___ LVEF and demand ischemia [] if voice hoarseness does not improve, will need ENT evaluation of vocal chords [] Trazadone was a new medication, consider discontinuation when no longer needed for sleep [] follow up UA ___ 1wk to ensure resolution of hematuria
summary: You were transferred for influenza that caused respiratory failure that required intubation. You were treated for influenza and supported through the illness, and able to be extubated and weaned off oxygen to room air. Your course was complicated by electrolyte abnormalities and acute kidney injury, which had improved prior to discharge, but has not completely normalized. You had a history of congestive heart failure ___ the past, but it was found that your heart squeeze was weaker than seen on previous ECHO's. Your fluid balance was monitored carefully and you were given diuretics (lasix) as needed, and will need to be continue to be watched closely at rehab. As you were dry at discharge, your lasix was being held.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with sCHF, CAD, afib on coumadin who had a sudden onset of SOB, hypoxemia, hypotension who had spontaneously recovered. . # Acute on chronic systolic CHF exacerbation (flash pulmonary edema): In setting of tachycardia. Patient largely already treated at her rehab prior to arrival here. We continued diuresis, ruled her out for MI. Renal doppler done to eval for possible renal artery stenosis Telemetry revealed AFib/Flutter with inital PVCs and few runs of non-sustained VT. Cardiology consulted and recommended medical management. Lasix changed to 40mg dialy, Lisinopril titrated to 7.5mg daily. Other regimen unchanged. She is likely someone with little buffer room regarding her volume status. Her "dry weight" appear sot be 114 lb., here and I discussed with ___. ___ her rehab that this would appear to be a good goal to keep her at, with daily weights in the a.m. . # Coronary artery disease: Prior stenting in ___. EKG from ___ shows WCT at ___ bpm. EKG here shows afib at rate of 85 bpm, incomplete LBBB, inferior q waves. She was ruled out by enzymes and did not show any further evidence of ischemia. We continued her ASA, BB, ACE-I, statin. . # Acute renal failure: Likely ATN from hypotension v. prerenal from CHF. it improved with diuresis and normotension. Ultrasound confirmed no evidence of renal aretery stenois. Discharge Cr = 1.3 . # Supratherapeutic INR: Found on admission. Mildly elevated LFT on admission - all felt to be most likely related to hypotension with mild shock liver. Coumadin held throughout hospitalization. INR on day of dishcarge = 2.8 . LFTs and INR should be repeated at rehab. Recommend resuming Warfarin 1.5mg at rehab ___. . # Leukocytosis: LIkely stress reaction. No evidence of infection. . # Atrial fibrillation: Coumadin held for supratherapeutic INR. INR on day of discharge is Amiodarone was continued at her prior dose. . # Hyperlipidemia - statin continued . # Hypertension - Home antihypertensives continued . # Hypothyroidism - Levothyroxine continued . Code: DNR
summary: You were admitted with acute on chronic congestive heart failure. Your medications were adjusted and you remained clinically stable. You were seen by cardiology who did not feel that any treatment for arrhythmia was indicated other than your medical treatment at this time. Your warfarin INR level was high and will need to be monitored carefully at your rehab. You will restart taking the warfarin tonight. You had some kidney disfunction which resolved. You were noted to have a R foot drop which is consistent with a peroneal nerve neuropathy. This is usually due to compression of the nerve from sitting with legs crossed. Please avoid this. I am recommending that you receive an Ankle-Foot orthotic at your rehab facility.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ year-old right-handed woman with PMH of afib (previously on warfarin), CAD, CHF, PD, Graves disease and recent hospitalization for right IPH who presented with worsening symptoms of right facial numbness, drooling and concern of facial droop. # NEURO: She presented with right facial numbness and concern of facial droop, however this finding was present on prior admission and minimal deficits found on exam. However, given her description of the episode and CHADS=5, there was high probability of thromboembolic event. Neuroimaging showed stable hemorrage and MRI showed now acute lesion to account for her symptoms. Repeat TTE was unchanged. She was increased to Aspirin 325mg daily for stroke prevention. Dr. ___ spoke with her cardiologist Dr. ___ Dr. ___ outpatient neurologist. They decided not to restart her coumadin given concern for recurrent bleeding risk if she resumes coumadin. There is also concern for possible underlying neoplasm that could be a high risk for recurrent hemorrhage. Her seizure prophylaxis was stopped as there was no sign of seizure since the intraparenchymal hemorrhage several weeks earlier. She will be followed up by Dr. ___ in clinic. Likely will need repeat imaging as suggested by radiology on MR report. # ___ DISEASE: her symptoms were stable and she continued her home carbidopa-levodopa. # CARDIOVASCULAR: She has a history of hypertension, CAD, and CHF. CHADS2 = 5 but will not restart coumadin as per attending-to-attending discussion. TTE was repeated which was unchanged. Initially, her blood pressure was allowed to autoregulate given concern for infarct, however her home antihypertensives and diuretics were restarted on discharge. # IDDM: Recently diagnosed, she continued her home regimen of 70/30 12 units BID. Initially she was poorly controlled because a sliding scale was not included. She was discharged on this regimen and will likely need further insulin titration by her outpatient provider. (Please note although OMR lists glargine 14 units as her home dose, the patient says this was change to the ___ regimen listed above.) # Grave's disease: stable, she continued home methimazole. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: 1) full code 2) will need further insulin uptitration 3) will need repeat MRI brain imaging in ___ weeks as suggested by radiology on MRI/MRA read 4) do not restart coumadin (see cardio section above) 5) MRI pelvis from ___ showed two possible side branch intraductal papillary mucinous neoplasms of the pancreas. Repeat MRI of the pelvis with contrast was recommended in six months. Referral to pancreas clinic was also recommended.
summary: You were admitted to ___ on ___ for a right facial droop and worsened right facial numbness in the setting of a recent bleed in your brain. Your symptoms had improved by the time you arrived here, and an MRI showed no evidence of stroke. The bleeding in your brain has also decreased in size. We believe your symptoms may be related to a transient ischemic attack, possibly due to being off your coumadin. We discussed restarting your coumadin with your cardiologist and decided not to restart this medication. We did increase the dose of your Aspirin from 81mg to 325mg. You should continue this increased dose of Aspirin. Also, we discontinued your Keppra (levetiracetam) as this is no longer needed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This ia a ___ year old male with a history of CAD s/p 4V CABG in ___, DES to RPL in ___, HTN, HL, ICD ___ for EF 20% p/w worsening chest discomfort and SOB at rest. #. NSTEMI- The patient's chest pain was likely related to a NSTEMI. The patient's troponin rose from 0.14 on admission to 0.17->0.19 (CK 61 MB 2.2) and then decline on the day prior to discharge to Trop of 0.18/CK of 54 and MB of 2.0). He was given a loading dose of plavix 600mg, ASA, and started on a heparin gtt on arrive to the hospital. His statin was increased from atorvastatin 40mg to 80mg daily. He was taken for cardiac cath and found to have a moderate LCx lesion. This lesion was not stented because the fractional flow reserve was 0.82, consistent with a non-hemodynamically significant stenosis . He was started on Imdur 30mg daily. His systolic blood pressure drop to the high 70's after starting Imdur. His lisinopril dose was dropped from 40 to 20mg. His blood pressure remained in the high 90's to 120's on Imdur 30mg, lisinopril 20mg, and metoprolol 50mg for 1 day prior to discharge. He was discharge on those three medications as well as ASA 325mg, atorvastatin 80mg, and clopidogrel 75mg. The patient had taken plavix for ___ year after a DES was place in ___ and was recently stopped in ___. Plavix was restarting after this patient's with a plan for nine months of treatment for NSTEMI (CURE trial) . # Chronic systolic heart failure: He remains euvolemic during hospitalization. Filling pressures were only mildly elevated on right heart catheterization. He may benefit from resynchronization therapy as he has a wide QRS on ECG and classs III symptoms. He will have an appointment scheduled with arranged with Dr. ___ consideration. . # Arthritis: continued Tylenol PRN . # Health maintenance: continue MVI
summary: You were hospitalized because you had a heart attack - the symptoms of your heart attack were chest pain and shortness of breath. Heart attacks are caused by blockages in the coronary arteries that supply the heart muscle, whose pumping function depends on a constant supply of oxygen delivered by the blood supplied by these arteries. Severe blockages in the coronary arteries can cause the symptoms that you experienced because they deprive your heart of oxygen. During this hospitalization, the blockage in your coronary artery that were causing your symptoms was visualized during a procedure called a cardiac catheterization. The lesion was not hemodynamically significant and no stent was placed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Assessment: ___ year old female with a history of non-infarct related cardiomyopathy (LVEF 15%) and persistent atrial fibrillation/atrial tachycardia s/p PVI ___ and re-do PVI and AT/flutter ablation ___. She continues to have multiple atrial arrhythmias with possible flecainide toxicity post redo ablation. Of note she had bi-atrial tachycardia and left sided atrial tachycardias that were not terminated with ablation. She appears volume overloaded on admission. Additionally she has dysphagia in setting of recent left sided ablation concerning for esophageal irritation. # Afib: continues with episodes of AT s/p PVI ___. TSH WNL. Flecainide stopped. Plan to return for ETOH ablation of ligament ___ as outpt. - increased omeprazole to 40mg bid for esophageal irritation s/p PVI with good effect - hold aspirin - INR supratherapeutic today at 3.5 home dose decreased from 5mg to 3mg. Given Rx for Cipro but pt did not want to take d/t interactions with meds, side effects etc. Given IV abx ? Unasyn at OSH. Dysuria improved. Urine culture neg here after ABX givin. - Macrobid ___ BID x 5 days last day ___ pm
summary: You presented to ___ with palpitations, shortness of breath and pain with swallowing. You were transferred here for treatment. You had a fast heart rhythm and a reaction to the flecainide. The flecainide was stopped. Your Metoprolol was continued for heart rate control. The EP team will call you at home to schedule a procedure called alcohol ablation to treat the fast heart rhythm. You were found to be volume overloaded and treated with IV diuretic (Lasix), your home dose of Lasix was doubled to 40 mg twice a day. You were found to have a urinary tract infection and treated with antibiotics. You will continue on Macrobid for 5 days. Last day will be ___. Your throat irritation improved with increasing your Omeprazole to 40mg twice daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with hx of DM2, HTN, Aortic Stenosis s/p AVR and now has bioprosthetic critical AS and pacemaker presenting on admission from wards floor with acute respiratory distress and flash pulmonary edema. . # Pulmonary Edema/diastolic CHF: Patient has a history of multiple CHF exacerbation admissions in the past. On this admission, patient reportedly missed ___ medications (lasix) and received some IVF when she was being admitted for episode of hypoglycemia. She was found by nursing after transfer to the wards floor to be in respiratory distress, and CXR showed ___ flash pulmonary edema, hypertensive to sBP200s, and ABG 7.30/55/63/28. She was placed on BiPAP, morphine, Hydralizine IVx1, Lasix IV40mg, and transferred to the CCU. Troponins were 0.01->0.02->0.05. She was diuresed with IV lasix, then transitioned to home Lasix, and her respiratory status improved to baseline. On discharge, the patient was oxygenating well on room air. . # Hypertension: On transfer to the CCU, patient was found to have sBP in 200s. When remeasured, had L arm sBP 207 and R arm sBP 106, however, history of ___ subclavian stenosis (R-80%; L-40%) therfore opted not to get CT to check for aortic dissection; CXR did not show evidence of dissection/widened mediastinum. She had received hydralizine 20mg IV prior to transfer. Her home medications were reconciled with her pharmacy, and she was placed on Valsartan 120mg BID and Carvedilol 25mg BID. Her home nifidipine and hydralizine were held since her BP was well controlled with a range of 106-135/40-53, and concern for afterload reduction in critical AS. . # Ao Stenosis: patient has hx of critical bioprosthetic AS s/p AVR ___. According to TTE of ___ and ___: patient has high transvalvular gradient and valve area of 0.7. No further ECHOS have been done, per Dr. ___, as patient ___ not proceed with any intervention anyway. Discussed with patient and family, and confirmed that she would not like to pursue surgical correction. Therefore, no ECHO or pre-operative evaluation was performed on this admission. . # Diabetes: patient originally presented with low blood sugars to the ___ and LOC. Typically on Lantus 35U at home, and Lispro. In the hospital, she was started on Lantus 20U and insulin sliding scale to estimate her insulin requirements. Her final Lantus dose at discharge was titrated at 23U in addition to ISS. She will be discharged on this regimen, in addition to Lispro for extra mealtime coverage. # CAD s/p CABG: Patient has known long-standing disease CAD. CABG x 1 with SVG to PDA in ___, PCI to LAD ___. Recent flash pulmonary less concerning for possible ischemic event, CE normal range and no associate CP. She was continued on ASA 81mg, Carvedilol 25mg BID, Simvastatin 40mg PO daily, and Zetia 10mg during hospitalization, and at discharge. # History of left atrial appendage thrombus: Patient was started on Coumadin 5mg on admission, due to unknown home amount. Coumadin was held for INR 3.1, and medication reconcilation started on home dose of 4mg the following day. On discharge, patient is on Coumadin 4mg with INR 1.9 (related to held dose). # Mild Dementia: per patient's son, she has mild dementia at baseline. She was continued on Aricept 10mg daily. During hospitalization, she appeared oriented and appropriate. #FEN: she was continued on a heart healthy diabetic diet. #Prophylaxis: -DVT ppx with coumadin -Pain management with tylenol -Bowel regimen with colace, miralax (unknown allergy to senna)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were found to be unresponse in your day home facility and had a very low blood sugar level. You were taken to the hospital and given juice and fluids to improve your blood sugar level. Because of these symptoms you also missed your evening dose of lasix. Overnight while in the hospital you had worsening symptoms of shortness of breath and high blood pressure. This was believed to be an acute exacerbation of you chronic known congestive heart failure. You were admitted to the ICU to treat these symptoms. You were given medications (IV lasix) to help remove excess fluid from your lungs and also treated with a specially oxygen mask. Your symptoms improved quickly with these treatments and you were able to be discharged home.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with hx of DM2, HTN, Aortic Stenosis s/p AVR and now has bioprosthetic critical AS and pacemaker presenting on admission from wards floor with acute respiratory distress and flash pulmonary edema. . # Pulmonary Edema/diastolic CHF: Patient has a history of multiple CHF exacerbation admissions in the past. She was found by nursing after transfer to the wards floor to be in respiratory distress, and CXR showed ___ flash pulmonary edema, hypertensive to sBP200s, and ABG 7.30/55/63/28. She was placed on BiPAP, morphine, Hydralizine IVx1, Lasix IV40mg, and transferred to the CCU. Troponins were 0.01->0.02->0.05. She was diuresed with IV lasix, then transitioned to home Lasix, and her respiratory status improved to baseline. When remeasured, had L arm sBP 207 and R arm sBP 106, however, history of ___ subclavian stenosis (R-80%; L-40%) therfore opted not to get CT to check for aortic dissection; CXR did not show evidence of dissection/widened mediastinum. She had received hydralizine 20mg IV prior to transfer. Her home medications were reconciled with her pharmacy, and she was placed on Valsartan 120mg BID and Carvedilol 25mg BID. text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ year old M with a PMHx of CAD and severe mitral regurgitation s/p recent CABG/MVR (LIMA-LAD, SVG-AM and dRCA) in ___. He also has a history of dilated cardiomyopathy with CHF- EF ___ post-CABG/MVR, severe pulmonary hypertension on sildenafil and CKD (recent baseline Cr 1.6-1.9). Recently admitted to ___ CCU on ___ in cardiogenic shock in the setting of atrial tachycardia. Admitted to ___ from home for planned elective EP study and ablation of A.Tach. # CHF: Chronic, Systolic CHF with LVEF 30%. Has a history of dilated cardiomyopathy of non-ischemic cause and had a mitral ring in ___ with CABG. Worsening LVEF in CCU likely tachycardia incuded cardiomyopathy. Acute exacerbation this admission with increased ___ likely ___ recurrence of atrial tachycardia and poor systolic function ___ tachycardia. Clinic Note: Weight: 85.45kg in ___, 81.8kg ___ discharged from ___ (___) at 87.6kg. 82.1kg on admission. He was treated for heart failure with Aspirin 81mg Daily, Metoprolol succinate 12.5mg BID, Lisinopril 5mg Daily, Simvastatin 20mg daily, Toresemide 80mg PO Daily and Spironolactone 25mg was started for Systolic CHF and to augment diuresis. Metoprolol was uptritated to 50mg QAM and 25mg QPM for rate control as below. In addition we gave him IV lasix daily, which produced prompt, vigorous diuresis. # Atrial Tachcardia: Occasionally has runs of RVR with exertion though spontaneously returns to rates in ___ with rest. Plan was for ablation of A.Tach but this was not able to be completed because of thrombus in ___ found on TEE morning of procedure. Instead he was rate controlled with goal to keep HR<100 with ambulation. Metoprolol was uptitrated to 50mg QAM and 25mg QPM. Initially presented with INR>7 in setting of Amiodarone initiation as an outpatient. Coumadin was held, no vitamin k given and INR returned to <3 when Coumadin restarted per home regimen. # Chronic Kidney Disease: Chronic with baseline 1.6-1.9, likely related to Diabetes and Hypertension. Stable without acute exacerbation while hospitalized # Diabetes Mellitus: Chronic, Stable, Insulin Dependent as an outpatient. Last A1c 6.0%. Continued Insulin Glargine 10units QHS and HISS # Hyperlipidema: Chronic, Stable as an outpatient. Last Cholesterol panel at goal (T.Chol 012, LDL 58 ___ continue Simvastatin 20mg Daily # Hypertension - Chronic, Stable, last BP 102/74 at clinic (___). Metoprolol as above titration and continued Lisinopril 5 mg Daily # Pulmonary Hypertension: Chronic, Severe, with associated PFO. Right heart catherization in CCU showed improvement in pulmonary hypertension with NO. Continued Sildenafil 20mg TID TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient will require 1 month of anticoagulation before TEE and A.Tach ablation can be attempted again - Coumadin restarted on discharge, INR should be monitored closely - Would consider a Bi-V ICD in this patient with chronic systolic CHF, symptomatic and with LVEF<30%
summary: You were admitted to ___ for heart failure management and for ablation of your atrial tachycardia. You received IV diuretics with improvement in lower extremity edema and crackles in your lungs. Your INR was monitored because it was very high on admission and your coumadin was held until your INR was less than 3.0, this was restarted day before discharge. Your ablation could not be completed because you were found to have a clot in a part of your left atrium found on transesophageal echocardiogram. You will require 1 month of anticoagulation before this procedure can be completed. Your heart rate was controlled by increasing the dose of your metoprolol.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with cardiac sarcoid c/b sCHF (___), pulmonary sarcoid, HLD, gout, with worsening shortness of breath and exercise tolerance, admitted for right heart cath and possible milrinone. She was found to have fever and likely UTI, which may have worsened her symptoms and hemodynamics. She also developed a gout flare. RHC showed CI 2, and pt's exercise tolerance improved slightly after treatment of UTI. Milrinone was not initiated. ============= ACUTE ISSUES ============= # Acute on Chronic Congestive Heart failure: The patient was admitted with an EF of ___ and concern for low-output cardiac failure despite medication optimization. She had ___ class III symptoms with recent worsening of her SOB and decreased exercise tolerance but was euvolemic on exam without signs of volume overload. The patient underwent right heart cath to assess cardiac output and was then transferred to the CCU. The results of the cath were as follows: CO 3.8, CI 2, Pressure RA 6, PCWP 21, MEAN PA 25, Venous SaO2 54% and Central Venous SaO2 51%. Given these findings, the patient was restarted on Lasix 20mg PO. The decision to start an inotrope such as milrinone was deferred at this time due to acute illness (infection v. gout flare) causing poor vascular tone. Following cath removal, the patient remained stable and was transferred back to the heart failure service on ___. Patient felt symptomatically improved following treatment of UTI (see below) so milrinone was ultimately not initiated. #UTI with Fever: Following admission, a mild leukocytosis was noted on CBC and the patient spiked a fever to ___. A UA showed leukocytes and bacteria but the patient denied any specific urinary symptoms. Blood cultures X 2 were drawn and Ceftriaxone 1g q24 was started on ___. In the CCU, the patient continued to spike fevers overnight on ___ as high as 102.4F. As such, blood culture X 1 was drawn along with a CXR that was negative. Her UCx initially grew mixed flora and a subsequent UCx did not grow. She received 3 days of treatment with Ceftriaxone. #Gout: The patient has a history of gout including a flair for which was hospitalized about one month prior to this admission. While in the CCU, the patient developed pain in her feet bilaterally, as well as erythema and swelling of the R ___ MTP joint, concerning for a gout flair. She was treated with colchicine with improvement in symptoms. # Pulmonary Sarcoid ___ Cough: The patient sees Dr. ___ as an ___. Previous PFTs showed severe obstruction (___). Pulm (Dr. ___ was consulted and recommended repeat PFTs. PFTs showed obstructive pattern with FVC and FEV1 unchanged from ___, no response to bronchodilators and decreased DLCO. =============== CHRONIC ISSUES =============== # Cardiac sarcoid: (biopsy proven at ___ ___ s/p ICD: stable. The patient has known cardiac sarcoid with decining functional status. # Hyperlipidemia: The patient is not on statin at home due to pancreatitis # GERD: During this admission, lansoprazole was continued. ===================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== -GOUT: Responded well to colchicine for treatment of gout flare during admission. Consider gout prophylaxis as outpatient -PFT final report not yet uploaded to ___ medical record at time of discharge
summary: As you know, you were recently hospitalized at ___ ___ because of worsened symptoms from your heart failure. You had a right heart catheterization to see if you might benefit from a drug called milrinone. You were in the intensive care unite briefly with the catheter in place to measure the pressures in your heart before being transferred back to the regular cardiology floor. Ultimately, we decided that you did not yet need milrinone. You also had a fever while you were here, and you were treated for a urinary tract infection. You had the beginnings of a gout flare, so you received colchicine for that.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Dyspnea: Likely multifactorial, given advanced pulmonary hypertension and right-sided heart failure. Last echocardiogram without systolic dysfunction. The chest X-ray suggestive of pulmonary congestion and edema thought due to acute on chronic diastolic CHF. The patient was continued on her home iloprost and sildanefil therapies. Her nifedipine was stopped due to the need to diurese without compromising blood pressure. The patient was initially given an uptitration of Lasix, but that did not produce desired diuresis. She was then placed on torsemide, with a one-time dose of metolazone. By discharge, the patient was more than 2 liters negative and had lost five pounds. Her weight on discharge was 161 pounds, and she was at her baseline of 3L O2 and able to ambulate the halls without desaturation. Pt was discharged with plan for ongoing ___ support to help with volume management. . # Pulmonary hypertension: The patient was continued on her home doses of iloprost and sildanefil. Her nifedipine was stopped due to need to diurese. . # Diastolic CHF: Known diastolic CHF. On admission, her proBNP was at the highest levels in our records. The patient underwent diuresis as above. She was discharged on lower doses of lisinopril and metoprolol because she was normotensive without them. . # Hypertension: The patient was normotensive through her hospital stay. Some of her home anti-hypertensive therapies (lisinopril and nifedipine) were held because of the need to diurese. Metoprolol dose was lowered. By discharge, she was reinstated on lisinopril and metoprolol. . # DMT2: Controlled via insulin sliding scale on admission. . # CKD: The patient was discharged slightly above baseline, at a creatinine of 1.7, which may have been secondary to diuresis. . # Anemia: Remained at baseline during hospitalization.
summary: You were admitted for weight gain and increased shortness of breath. These symptoms were thought to be secondary to your pulmonary hypertension and heart failure. We gave you medications, called diuretics, to remove some extra fluid. Once that fluid was removed, you began to feel better. Your breathing and oxygen requirements returned to your baseline. One of your diuretics was changed: we replaced your furosemide with torsemide. We also reduced the doses of your blood pressure medications. STOP taking furosemide. START taking TORSEMIDE 40 mg daily. START taking LISINOPRIL at 5 mg daily. START taking METOPROLOL XR at 50 mg daily. STOP taking nifedipine. Weigh yourself every morning. Your baseline weight is now ___ pounds.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M w hx s/f CHF (EF 35%), Afib on Coumadin, critical AS (0.9 cm2), MI s/p CABG & stents, PVD, DM2, CRI (baseline ~2.5-2.7), MRSA bacteremia s/p 6 wk vanc presents from rehab with worsening SOB, 18lb wt gain, and increased ___ edema admitted for aggressive diuresis. ACTIVE ISSUES: #. CHF EXACERBATION: The patient was admitted with pulmonary edema, ___ edema, and 18lb wt gain. The patient was diuresed with IV lasix and metolazone, with a net output of ___. On discharge, the patient was in near euvolemic condition; discharge weight is 76.8kg (admit weight around 82kg). Repeat TTE showed EF 35% with worsened tricuspid regurgitation, left and right ventricular function and pulmonary hypertension. The patient was discharged on a heart failure regimen of Torsemide 40 PO daily, Coreg 6.25mg PO daily, ASA 81mg PO daily, fluid restriction of 1L, and a low Na diet. He will need to have daily weights for monitoring of his tenuous fluid status. He may need uptitration of torsemide or addition of metolazone if patient becomes fluid overloaded. Spironolactone was held on discharge due to concern over worsening renal function. . #. MRSA Bacteremia: He had started vancomycin for 6 weeks due to positive MRSA blood culture, and was treated for suspected endocarditis which could not adequately be ruled out without TEE, which he refused. He completed this course in house on ___. . #. Urinary retention: No urinary sx and cultures neg; patient will require foley. . # Afib: INR 3 today on discharge ___ yesterday on ___, decreased coumadin from 2mg to 0.5mg on ___. will need reassessment of INR every 48 hours and adjust dose of coumadin to reach therapeutic levels of INR ___. Cont coreg, rates were adequately controlled with beta blockade. . #. CKD: Patient has stage IV CKD with baseline Cr in the 2.7 range, increased to 2.9 on d/c with aggressive diuresis. Worsening uremia to 130s as well. No current plans for HD. Stopped his spironolactone due to concerns for potassium clearance and worsening renal failure. Continued calcitriol, vitamin D. He was on epoeitin as an outpatient. #. CAD: He has no chest pain and his cardiac enzymes were near his baseline, likely elevated due to CHF and renal disease. Not on ace-i (severe AS). Cont coreg, lipitor, aspirin # Skin impediments: seen by wound care for sacral decubitus ulcer, please see notes in discharge orders for wound care. INACTIVE ISSUES . #. DM2: Was placed on an insulin sliding scale, A1C to 8.5. #. HLD: Cont lipitor 80mg PO daily . #. HTN: Cont coreg, normotensive . # CODE: FC; discussed with patient; family meeting ___ with wife and son ___ regarding patient's clinical status and future steps. patient and family prefer to cont' care at rehabilitation care centers at this point but is aware of hospice services for future care. PENDING TESTS AT DISCHARGE: - none TRANSITIONAL CARE ISSUES: - needs INR monitoring due to decreased coumadin dose - needs daily weights and volume assessments to titrate diuretics - ongoing hospice hospice discussion if pt/family amenable
summary: You were hospitalized because you had an acute worsening of your pre-existing heart failure - your symptoms were increased lower extremity swelling and and shortness of breath. You have congestive heart failure, which is a condition in which the pumping function of your heart is impaired. As a result of your heart's impaired pumping function, you are prone to accumulating excess fluid in parts of the body where fluid should not normally be; fluid that accumulates in the lower extremities causes swelling, and fluid that accumulates in the lungs causes shortness of breath. During this hospitalization, you were given medicines that removed the exess fluid from your body and your heart medicines were optimized; however, what we have done for you in the hospital is not the end of your treatment. It is very important that you adhere to the fluid restrictions prescribed and that you refrain from consuming excess salt; failure to do so may result in you accumulating excess fluid and requiring re-hospitalization.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o ___ man with a history of CAD s/p stent to LAD, a-fib s/p TIA/CVA in ___, recent DVT on lovenox, and severe mitral regurgitation currently undergoing MVR evaluation who presented with chest pain and shortness of breath, found to have afib with RVR as well as acute on chronic systolic heart failure. ACS was ruled out given negative trops, EKG without clear signs of ischemic changes, recent cath ___ with patent stent, and chest pain that resolved with heart rate control. For his afib with RVR, ___ was given diltiazem 10mg IV and 30mg po in the ED to maintain HR <120. On the floor ___ was loaded with digoxin IV for one day and started on PO digoxin. ___ was also started on metoprolol tartrate. Diltiazem was discontinued given patient has low EF. His lovenox was stopped and ___ was switched to apixiban for anticoagulation as there was no indication for Lovenox. By time of discharge ___ was maintaining HR in the ___, up to ___ with ambulation. Patient's dyspnea was thought to be partially secondary to acute on chronic systolic heart failure given elevated BNP. ___ was diuresed with Lasix 40IV with improvement in his symptoms. ___ did not appear to be grossly overloaded on exam and was switched to Lasix 40 mg PO with good fluid management. Patient was briefly on levofloxacin given concern for CAP based on CXR, but this was quickly discontinued as ___ did not have clinical signs of pneumonia. ___ was maintaining adequate saturations of 95-99% with ambulation on discharge. Patient had TEE to evaluate mitral valve, which showed only moderate MR with normal valve leaflet morphology. It was determined that ___ no longer needed to complete evaluation for surgical MVR. summary: You were admitted to ___ because you were experiencing chest pain and shortness of breath. Your symptoms were thought to be due to rapid heart rate as well as fluid buildup. You were treated with medications to slow down your heart rate and get rid of the extra fluid with improvement in your symptoms. You also received a procedure called a transesophageal echocardiogram to evaluate your mitral valve. This showed that your valve was not as diseased as we previously thought and at this time, you do not require any surgery. We have started you on a fluid pill (furosemide) to remove fluid from building up in your lungs and your legs. At discharge, you weighed 72.7 kg. It is very important that you weigh yourself every morning before getting dressed and after going to the bathroom.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1) Pneumonia: Although patient noted dyspnea, she denied cough, sputum, or fevers. However, her husband reported patient coughing, and imaging was highly suggestive of pneumonia. She was admitted on ceftriaxone and azithromycin due to a skin reaction to levofloxacin. The former was switched to cefpodoxime prior to discharge for a 5 day course. She remained afebrile and had no leukocytosis. 2) Chronic diastolic heart failure: Patient had noted dyspnea and functional decline since her diagnosis of Afib in ___. She also noted chronic orthopnea and PND, and BNP was elevated, suggestive of heart failure, which likely explained her pleural effusion on CT. As she had stable O2 sats and no signs of volume overload on physical exam, she was not given additional diuresis beyond her home furosemide. 3) Atrial fibrillation: Her recently started flecainide was stopped as this was felt to be contributing to her nausea and weakness. She noted symptomatic improvement with discontinuation. She remained rate controlled with metoprolol. She is on warfarin anticoagulation, and her INR was supratherapeutic at 4.4-5.5. Warfarin was held and no vitamin K was given as she has a cardiology appointment later on the day of discharge for possible cardioversion. Warfarin will be resumed when her INR allows. 4) Fall: On hospital day two, patient was returning into bed when she fell and hit her head. She denied any headache or confusion, and remained alert, oriented, and neurologically intact afterward. Stat head CT due to her elevated INR showed no acute findings. ___ evaluated her and noted some instability, which completely resolved the next day. She had received zolpidem the evening prior to the fall, which may have contributed to her instability. She was advised to not take this medication in the future, and return to the hospital if any neurologic changes develop.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of feeling nauseated and weak. Some of your symptoms were thought to be related to flecainide, a medicine you recently started. We stopped this medication. You are scheduled to see Dr. ___ electrophysiologist on ___ for further management of your atrial fibrillation. While you were here, you had imaging which showed an area of pneumonia in your right lung. You were given a dose of an antibiotic called levofloxacin. You developed itching and redness when you were given this medication. You should let any health care providers know that you have an allergy to this medication and should not take this again. Your antibiotics were switched to azithromycin and cefpedoxime.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ yo with advanced and end-stage ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF ___ on TTE ___, chronic AF NOT on coumadin due to fall risk, BiV/ICD, UTIs, hematuria, and severe RA who was admitted with CHF acute exacerbation who is being discharged on hospice. . # Acute on chronic systolic heart failure: Patient with stage 4 heart failure with ___ weeks of increasing ___ edema and worsening dyspnea. There was concern for ischemia as the precipitating cause. However, due to his end-stage prognosis, stress imaging/cardiac catheterization were not pursued in discussion with his outpatient attending as the end result would likely be similar with or without intervention. His home beta blocker/ACE were held due to low BPs and he was diuresed with a lasix drip with good result in terms of I/O and weight, however he remained with JVD to above the ear, 3+ lower extremity pitting edema and rales on lung exam. on ___ (5 days into diuresis) he started to become symptomatically hypotensive to the ___ previously, he was tolerating SBP in the low ___, responsive to fluids. It is possible that his UTI was partially responsible for his symptomatic hypotension, but more likely he was overdiuresed in spite of his physical exam. His symptomatic hypotension occurred only when he was sitting upright for prolonged periods of time. There were discussions with the patient about this problem and he chose not to pursue any medications to help with his blood pressure. . # UTI: on ___ after he started to become hypotensive, an infectious workup was pursued as he had mild progressive leukopenia and a low temperature. His urine was floridly positive for infection. Initially he was started on vancomycin/ceftriaxone as his previous UTI grew enterococcus sensitive only to vanco (not cephalosporins). This was narrowed to ciprofloxacin. He will finish a total of 7 day course of this on discharge. . #Goals of care: patient expressed understanding that his condition is terminal and that he would not like to pursue any further aggressive treatments. He was seen by the palliative care team and he decided to go home on hospice today. Because of this he asked to have his the defibillator portion of his pacer to be turned off. This was done on ___. Transitional Issues: Pending labs: None Medications started: 1. Ciprofloxacin 500mg pill by mouth twice a day (antibiotic) Medications changed: None Medications stopped: 1. Metoprolol succinate (because of low blood pressure) 2. Lisinopril (because of low blood pressure) 3. Pravastatin (because no short term benefit) 5. Torsemide (because of low blood pressure) Follow-up needed: -We did not give you your scheduled dose of remicaide because you have a Urinary tract infection. Please discuss with your PCP when you should have this medication next. -You will need to follow-up with Dr. ___ you have an appointment on ___.
summary: You were admitted for increasing lower extremity edema and shortness of breath with exertion. You were found to be in an acute heart failure exacerbation. It is unclear what this was due to. Likely it was due to a further weakning of your heart, as an echocardiogram showed worsening cardiac function. You were put on a lasix drip with good urine output, however eventually your blood pressure dropped too much to continue taking fluid off and we stopped taking fluid off. Your blood pressure remains on the low side, so we recommend that you sit up and stand up slowly so as to not become lightheaded. We also found that you had a urinary tract infection, which we are treating with antibiotic pills. You have decide to go home on hospice and to not pursue agressive treatment for your heart failure. We have set you up with hospice services who can help to make you more comfortable at home. Transitional Issues: Pending labs: None Medications started:

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ female with a past medical history significant for CAD s/p CABG and ICD placement, vascular dementia, and HTN issues who presents after an unwitnessed fall at her nursing home. Per the patient's report, this was a mechanical fall and she reports no LOC, but is a poor historian due to dementia. She was found to have a urinary tract infection and new systolic CHF. . ACTIVE ISSUES: # FALL: She had negative cardiac biomarkers and no changes on EKG making cardiac ischemia unlikely. There were no events on telemetry, making arrhythmia an unlikely cause; likewise, interogation of her pacer revealed no events. She was not orthostatic. She was found to have a UTI which may have triggered the fall, this was treated as below. . # SPINE INJURY: On admission she had mild tenderness to palpation of the cervical spine and significant tenderness to palpation of the thoracic and lumbar spine. CT scan performed in the ED revealed old compression fractures in the thoracic and lumbar spine. Flexion-extension films of the c-spine were performed on ___ and were determined to be negative for displacement or acute injury requiring bracing. Ortho-spine was consulted in the ED and determined that the fractures were likely not acute and did not require bracing or operative management. The patient was placed in a soft collar at night for comfort. She complained of significant pain in her lower back on admission and was treated with tylenol and tramadol ___ mg Q6 PRN, with control of the pain. . # URINARY TRACT INFECTION: Urine culture grew e coli resistant to cipro and ampicillin. She was started on Bactrim double strength BID on ___ for a 5 day course to end on ___. She was febrile on ___ and afebrile thereafter. # Congestive Heart Failure: Patient was found to have mild hypoxia with pulmonary edema on exam and chest x ray. Negative cardiac enzymes. TTE showed mild CHF consistent with known CAD (regional AK, ___) and EF of 45-50% implying acute systolic and diastolic heart failure. She was continued on carvedilol and lisinopril. Lasix was added. She will follow up with cardiology as an outpatient. She will need a labs check later this week to ensure electrolyte stability on lasix. . Transitional issues: 1. Complete Bactrim course for UTI 2. Has new diagnosis of heart failure. Will require follow up with a cardiologist. Labs have been requested for ___ to ensure electrolytes stable on lasix; lasix titration PRN.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital following a fall ar your nursing home in which you hit your head. There was some question regarding whether or not you lost consciouness before the fall, so you were admitted for evaluatino to be be sure you were not at risk for episodes of losing consciousness. During your time as an inpatient, a full work up for syncopal (passing out) episodes was performed and it was determined that there was no obvious risk for you to have these episodes. Therefore, the fall was most likely not the result of a loss of consciousness. In addition to the above, you were treated for your pain from the fall and from past spine disease.

summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were feeling poorly at home and were found to have a heart attack. One of the blood vessels that supply the heart was clogged. We put a stent in to keep it open. You were started on medications to keep this stent open as below. Please continue taking these medications. You had trouble with breathing and we initially gave you antibiotics but then gave you a medication called lasix that helps get rid of extra water in the body. You responded well to this and your breathing improved. You developed a fast abnormal heart rhythm called atrial fibrillation. We started on a blood thinner for this.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with history of etoh induced non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with diastolic and systolic CHF (LVEF ___, s/p ICD implant in ___, and s/p ablation for VT in ___, presented with episodes of non-sustained VTach and SOB. # Rhythm: Patient presented with frequent PVCs and episodes of vtach for the past 5 days. He is s/p ablation for vtach in ___. On EP evaluation in ED, pacer did not shock patient, but had been in antitachycardia pacing during episodes of vtach. Given recent travel, with possible dietary changes, running out of his lasix with possible different lasix potency/dosing variation in ___, and mild pulmonary edema on CXR with SOB, likely cause of his PVC and ventricular tachycardia was from volume overload causing increased catechols. Although ventricular irritation was on the differential, his leads were in place on CXR, functioning well by EP eval. Digoxin toxicity was also a consideration although less likely without EKG changes and Toxicology was consulted. After being started on amio (150mg x 2 in ED then gtt), and diuresis with lasix, his symptoms improved and he had decreased frequency of ventricular tachycardia. His digoxin was held, and levels were followed and found to decrease to 3.0 on day of discharge with no evidence of dig toxicity. He was written for Dig level on ___. # Acute on chronic diastolic and systolic heart failure: LVEF ___ in ___. Although new ischemic event as cause of worsening CHF was on the differential, thought less likely given previous cath showed non-obstructive CAD. Trop elev was thought demand ischemia in the setting of CHF exacerbation and volume overload. Acute exacerbation of CHF due to above factors during travel, including diet, medication changes. He was diuresed with lasix and continued on his home carvedilol, lisinopril and spironolactone. # Coronaries: He has history of cath in ___ which showed non-obstructive CAD on cath in ___. ***It was unclear why patient was on ASA 325mg daily*** He was continued on simvastatin 20mg daily in addition to medications for heart failure as above. # HTN: Continued carvedilol, lisinopril, spironolactone, amlodipine per above. # T2DM: Held glyburide and metformin. Managed on ISS with home lantus 20units QHS. # Gout: continued allopurinol. # GERD: continued omeprazole TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -His diabetes medications on admission included glyburide and "PRN" lantus, which should be clarified by PCP.
summary: It has been a pelasure taking care of you this admission. You were admitted for your complaints of shortness of breath, palpitations and the feeling of being shocked by your ICD. Testing revealed that you were having a heart failure exacerbation meaning that the heart was not able to meet the demands of the body causing you to become short of breath and gain weight. This was also becuase you had stopped taking yoru Lasix. Additionally the shocks you were feeling were related to an arrythmia you were experiencing likely because of the heart failure and the fact that your digoxin levels were high. We stopped your digoxin and started you on a new drug called amiodarone to help prevent this arrythmia from occuring in the future. We also started you on a more powerful medication to assist the heart in getting rid of extra fluid in your body called Torsemide.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with history of etoh induced non-ischemic cardiomyopathy with diastolic and systolic CHF (LVEF ___, s/p ICD implant in ___, and s/p ablation for VT in ___, presented with episodes of non-sustained VTach and SOB. # Rhythm: Patient presented with frequent PVCs and episodes of vtach for the past 5 days. He is s/p ablation for vtach in ___. On EP evaluation in ED, pacer did not shock patient, but had been in antitachycardia pacing during episodes of vtach. Given recent travel, with possible dietary changes, running out of his lasix with possible different lasix potency/dosing variation in ___, and mild pulmonary edema on CXR with SOB, likely cause of his PVC and ventricular tachycardia was from volume overload causing increased catechols. Although ventricular irritation was on the differential, his leads were in place on CXR, functioning well by EP eval. Digoxin toxicity was also a consideration although less likely without EKG changes and Toxicology was consulted. After being started on amio (150mg x 2 in ED then gtt), and diuresis with lasix, his symptoms improved and he had decreased frequency of ventricular tachycardia. His digoxin was held, and levels were followed and found to decrease to 3.0 on day of discharge with no evidence of dig toxicity. He was written for Dig level on ___. # Acute on chronic diastolic and systolic heart failure: LVEF ___ in ___. Although new ischemic event as cause of worsening CHF was on the differential, thought less likely given previous cath showed non-obstructive CAD. Trop elev was thought demand ischemia in the setting of CHF exacerbation and volume overload. Acute exacerbation of CHF due to above factors during travel, including diet, medication changes. He was diuresed with lasix and continued on his home carvedilol, lisinopril and spironolactone. # Coronaries: He has history of cath in ___ which showed non-obstructive CAD on cath in ___. ***It was unclear why patient was on ASA 325mg daily*** He was continued on simvastatin 20mg daily in addition to medications for heart failure as above. # HTN: Continued carvedilol, lisinopril, spironolactone, amlodipine per above. # T2DM: Held glyburide and metformin. Managed on ISS with home lantus 20units QHS. # Gout: continued allopurinol. # GERD: continued omeprazole TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -His diabetes medications on admission included glyburide and "PRN" lantus, which should be clarified by PCP.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with PMH CAD s/p PCI, sCHF + dCHF, s/p BiV ICD, afib on coumadin, CKD (baseline 1.6), and prostate cancer s/p surgery sent in by cardiologist for decompensated heart failure. # Acute on chronic systolic congestive heart failure: -Patient reports ?20lb weight gain over several months with 6 lb weight gain in the week prior to admission. He noted increased abdominal distension with minimal ___ edema. BNP was elevated to 6000 and increasing dyspnea/orthopnea was c/w CHF exacerbation. Patient admitted to dietary nonadherence with consumption of salt, fats, and oils. This was the likely instigating factor as there was no evidence of infection or ischemia. ICD interrogated and found to be working appropriately with no evidence of dysrhythmia leading to exacerbation. Patient was treated with 160mg IV lasix BID and switched to torsemide 100mg prior to discharge. He was started on captopril. 2g Na diet and 2L fluid restriction maintained and home carvedilol were continued. Daily weights were monitored though I/O could not be followed due to incontinence. TTE was obtained showing LVEF<25% with global hypokinesis, similar to prior. Patient lost about 8 lbs in the first day of diuresis with subsequent stabilization. As Cr started to rise, it appeared patient was nearing dry weight and was discharged on higher dose of daily torsemide with reduced abdominal distension and absent respiratory sx. # CAD, status post PCI: Anatomy unclear, attempted unsuccessfully to obtain OSH records. Stable on home carvedilol, ASA, statin. # CKD: Creatinine 1.6 at baseline. Cr rose to 2.0 in reponse to diuresis but was downtrending to 1.8 by time of discharge. # Afib: Stable on warfarin with INR at goal. # Gout: Stable on home allopurinol # HTN: Stable on home carvedilol # HLD: Stable on home simvastatin. Home fenofibrate held during admission and restarted on discharge. # DM: Stable on ISS while inpatient. Home glimepiride held during admission and restarted on discharge. # Dementia: Stable on home donepezil, memantine, Seroquel, venlafaxine. Transitional Issues: -Home torsemide dose increased from 80mg to 100mg daily. -Patient started on captopril 3.125 mg TID -Patient will require guidance to maintain dietary adherence as he readily admits he continues to eat things he should not eat.
summary: You were admitted to ___ for weight gain and difficulty breathing. You were treated for worsening of your congestive heart failure. You given intravenous furosemide, a medicine to help you eliminate fluid through the urine. You lost about 8 pounds of fluid weight with this treatment. You have been switched to 100mg daily of oral torsemide, which is a slightly higher dose of the medication you were on at home. Your pacemaker was evaluated and found to be working correctly. You should make sure to avoid eating salty foods and drinking a lot of fluids to help prevent further worsening of your heart failure.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old female with atrial fibrillation s/p ablation/PPM, ILD ___ amiodarone, BOOP, diastolic dysfunction admitted ___ with epistaxis in setting of supratherapeutic INR; also with hypoxia and dyspnea on exertion. Brief hospital course was as follows. (1) Epistaxis: Likely related to dry nasal mucosa from oxygen via nasal canula and supratherapeutic INR. Requiring intranasal cocaine and packing in the emergency department, with plan to remove packing in 48 hours. At discharge, her daughter planned to remove packing following day (48 hours post-placement) and treat with Afrin for ___ days. She was also given a prescription for humidified oxygen to prevent recurrence. (2) Decompensated heart failure: Progressive dyspnea on exertion x1 week, 10lb weight gain, perhilar fullness on chest radiograph consistent with decompensated heart failure. Patient felt trigger was likely dietary noncompliance; at present and over past 1 week she had not had symptoms concerning for ACS. BNP was elevated but well below her peak value. TTE demonstrated new focal severe hypokinesis of the distal inferior wall and apex compared to prior TTE in ___. Cardiac markers were within normal limits, indicating insult was not recent. She was diuresed with Lasix 100mg IV x1 (dose required several years ago for adequate diuresis). She was continued on carvedilol 6.25 mg PO/NG BID per home regimen. She was requested to stay for further diuresis but wished to be home with her family for the holidays; close follow-up was arranged with her primary care physician. Aspirin and ACE inhibitor were not initiated due to reported allergies to both. On discharge, she reported her symptoms were at her baseline. She was requested to limit salt intake, weigh herself daily, and return taking Bumex 3mg PO daily per home regimen. (3) New wall motion abnormalities: Likely related to interim infarction over the past ___ year. EKG v-paced. The result was discussed with the patient, and conveyed to her primary care physician and cardiologist. Given her high pretest probability for CAD, we discussed with her she would likely require cardiac catheterization for definitive evaluation; she reported that she was not interested in having a catheterization. Management of her CAD was limited to beta-blocker; she reported allergies to aspirin and ACE inhibitor, and has not tolerated a statin in the past. (4) Small centrilobular nodules/ILD: CT chest was ordered initially as exact etiology of her dyspnea was not clear. The imaging was similar to prior except for small increase in size of centrilobular nodules. This result was conveyed to patient and her pulmonologist with plan for close follow-up. In the emergency department she was initially treated for pneumonia; antibiotic was given low probability of pneumonia after CT chest. For her underlying lung disease, she was continued on DuoNebs, budesonide 0.5 mg TID, prednisone 10mg PO daily, supplemental oxygen per home regimen. (5) Atrial fibrillation s/p ablation/PPM: CHADS2=3 (CHF, age, HTN). Supratherapeutic INR which came down with holding coumadin (vitamin K, FFP not required). On EKG she was v-paced. On discharge coumadin dose was decreased with plan for close follow-up for INR recheck. (6) Chronic left hip and thigh pain: She was continued on ___ 20mg PO BID per home regimen. (7) Hypothyroidism: She was continued on levothyroxine per home regimen. (8) Chronic suppression of septic prosthesis: She was continued on dicloxacillin per home regimen. (9) CKD: Serum creatinine remained at baseline despite aggressive diuresis. FULL CODE, confirmed with patient
summary: You were admitted to ___ on ___ for a nosebleed and shortness of breath. Your nose was packed with gauze and the bleeding stopped. To evaluate your shortness of breath and cough, you underwent a chest CT which was similar to a prior study in ___ except for a small increase in the size of some nodules; this should be followed up with Dr. ___ in the pulmonary clinic. You also underwent an echocardiogram which showed a change in your heart function; this should be followed closely with your cardiologist. Your shortness of breath was likely related to fluid backing up behind your heart. You were given Lasix which helped to remove fluid. Please continue to weigh yourself every morning.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. CHF, systolic, acute on chronic: The patient's presenting symptoms of dyspnea and peripheral edema were thought to be due to worsening of her CHF. The precipitation for this exacerbation may have been development of atrial fibrillation. There was no evidence for an acute myocardial infarction, though she continued to have a troponin leak. She was seen by Dr. ___ cardiology who recommended cardiac catheterization. This was performed on ___ and demonstrated three vessel CAD; medical managment was recommended. Her dyspnea improved with diuresis, and she continued her ACEI and metoprolol. She will follow up with cardiology for further management of her heart failure. 2. Atrial fibrillation The patient denied any history of atrial fibrillation, and there was no documentation of prior atrial fibrillation in the records available to us. She was anticoagulated with heparin bridging to warfarin was begun. ***On the day of discharge, the pt's INR was 1.2. She was continued on IV heparin until the time of dischage; she will be due to her first dose of subq Lovenox around 8PM this evening.*** Her rate was adequately controlled on oral metoprolol, and she remained hemodynamically stable throughout her hospitalization. Her PCP ___ adjust her warfarin dosage as required. 3. Coronary artery disease She was ruled out for acute MI. Prior stress testing had showed inferolateral WMAs consistent with single vessel CAD. She underwent cardiac catheterization on ___ that showed three vessel disease. The cath procedure was complicated by kinking of the Guide catheter. With assistance from vascular surgery, the catheter was unkinked and removed; a BMS was placed in the left external iliac artery to repair damage to the intima. She will continue her aspirin, beta blocker, statin, and ACEi (doses of these titrated up) and follow up with cardiology for further management. Plavix was addded to her regimen. 4. DM: The patient continued her home glargine and humalog; the doses of these were titrated while in the hospital. 5. HTN: The patient's hydralazine and amlodipine were discontinued. She will continue metoprolol and lisinopril as an outpatient. 6. RLE ulcer: Diabetic ulcer versus pyoderma gandrenosum per records. From discussion with outpatient dermatologist ___ ___ ___ and rheumatologist ___ ___ and PCP there was no plan to continue her on long term sterids, therefore her PO prednisone was tapered. She will followup with the vascular service (and dermatology as needed) for further management of the leg wounds.
summary: You came into the hospital because of increased shortness of breath. Your symptoms were likely due to worsening of your heart failure. You were also found to have an irregular heart rate (atrial fibrillation). You were treated with medicines to get rid of excess fluid and keep your heart rate under control, and your breathing improved. In the hospital, you were evaluated by the cardiology team who recommended that you undergo cardiac catheterization to assess the blood supply to your heart. This study showed XXX Because of the irregular heart rate, you will need to take a blood thinner (warfarin) to reduce your risk of stroke. While taking warfarin, you will need regular blood tests (INR measurements) to make sure the dose of warfarin is correct.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old ___ "___" speaking female with a history of adenocarcinoma of the lung (on crizotinib, c/b metastatic disease in R cerebellum), atrial fibrillation (not anti-coagulated due to oncologic prognosis and fall risk), HTN and left eye blindness who presented to the ED with an unwitnessed fall and hypothermia and was found to be in non convulsive status epilepticus: #Non-convulsive Status Epilepticus: Her initial exam was grossly non-focal but notable for minimal speech output and incompletely following commands. She ultimately was connected to EEG which showed non-convulsive status. She was subsequently loaded with keppra (now on 750 mg BID) and then required lacosamide (initially on 200 mg BID but subsequently decrease to 150 mg BID). In terms of her work-up for NCSE, her NCHCT was without large hemorrhage or acute infarct but did show known, stable metastasis. MRI showed stable right cerebellar met and punctate area of restricted diffusion left parietal region which may represent seizure focus vs. small infarct. LP with 2 wbc, 1425 rbcs, normal protein and glucose. Cytology was negative for malignant cells; rare lymphocytes and monocytes were seen. Immunotyping, and paraneoplastic panel were pending at time of discharge. She will follow-up with an epileptologist as an outpatient. #HFpEF: She had 2+ pitting edema on admission. She required intermittent diuresis for volume overload. Her weights are the best way to trend her volume status exam according to her oncologist. She was ultimately resumed on her home dose torsemide 40 mg BID. Her anti-HTN were held initially due to sbp 90-100s but ultimately her home Valsartan 25 mg daily was resumed. Her home metoprolol 25 mg XL was held due to HRs ___. Discharge weight 106.1. Sodium on day of discharge was 146. #Afib: Not on anticoagulation given oncologic prognosis per cardiology. Was found to be in sinus rhythm on admission with HRs in 50-60s. Given possible infarct seen on MRI, felt she at least needed to be on aspiring 81 mg daily. Metoprolol 25 mg XL held due to HRs in ___. EKG checked given initiation of lacosamide and PR interval 188 on ___. # Lung CA: stage 4 with brain mets; stable on recent f/u. She was continued on crizotinib 250 mg BID per oncology. FYI'd Dr. ___ re patient's hospitalization; Dr. ___ (___). TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - ongoing goals of care discussion with oncology
summary: You came to the hospital because you fell. While you were in the hospital we performed a number of tests to better understand what might have contributed to you falling at home, why you became confused, and why you were found to have low body temperatures. Your blood tests did not initially show any abnormalities that could explain your confusion. We performed a CT scan of your head that showed you did not have any bleeding in your brain. You had brain wave testing which showed you were having seizures. We started you on medicine to stop the seizures and you began to get better. You also had an MRI of your brain which showed your brain tumor has not gotten larger. You also had a spinal tap which did not show any evidence of infection. Now that you are leaving the hospital you will go to a rehab facility where you will continue to get strong.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ with history of CAD s/p DES to LAD ___, hypertension, hyperlipidemia and prior colon cancer s/p partial colectomy presenting with confusion, shortness of breath for the last 2 weeks found to have elevated troponins though with flat mb. # Troponin leak: Concerning for demand ischemia in the setting of nonspecific symptoms and mild ECG changes with elevated troponin but flat MB though without clear inciting factor. Elevated troponin were however at baseline compared to prior troponins. Anemia and mild CHF exacerbation could have contributed in the setting of worsening DOE and mild elevation in BNP (896). - Troponins: 0.25 > 0.31 > 0.33 > 0.32 - TTE showed normal EF but possible diastolic dysfunction. Poor quality. - Would consider nuclear perfusion stress test or also consider cath given body habitus and limitation of various tests. # Volume overload: Possibly this is from undiagnosed dCHF (BNP 896). Unclear dry weight but patient 290 lbs here (285lbs last year ___. - 20mg IV lasix x 1 on ___ and discharged on Lasix PO 10mg daily - DISCHARGED ON Lasix 10mg PO daily - DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 126.6kg/278 Ibs # Coronary artery disease status post DES to the LAD in ___. RCA known to be totally occluded, minimal disease in the circ. - Aspirin, metoprolol, lisinopril, atorvastatin - see above for management of troponin leak - Continued to hold Plavix given recent GIB and anemia. # Anemia: Patient with hx of gastric ulcers while on plavix and aspirin. Patient does note that he has been off of plavix and denies melena/hematochezia. He remains hemodynamically stable and is noted to have 2 unit drop over the last 5 months. - patient refused rectal - H/H was stable - Continued home PPI BID - Consider EGD as outpatient if patient continues have anemia # RUE swelling: initially concerning for clot given hx port placement but u/s with no evidence of thrombosis. Also consider if patient needs cath given body habitus may affect results of a stress test [ ] Patient will need outpatient titration of his furosemide dose; He's discharged on lasix 10mg PO daily [ ] further outpatient workup of worsening anemia, consider EGD [ ] patient should have H/H checked on ___ to ensure that his anemia is stable [ ] Patient should have chem 10 checked by ___ and monitor for electrolytes given new diuretis began.
summary: You were admitted to ___ with worsening shortness of breath. We were concerned that your symptoms may have been from strain on your heart. We gave you medicine (called Lasix or furosemide) to make you urinate in order to remove extra fluid from your body and help relieve the strain on your heart. We also did an echocardiogram (an ultrasound of your heart) to see how it was pumping. The image quality was poor but it looks like your heart pumps blood efficiently but does not relax well in between pumping (called diastolic heart failure). We are starting you on Lasix that you can take by mouth in order to keep fluid from building up and to help your heart pump and relax. You will be discharged on Lasix (also known as furosemide 10mg daily). All of your medications are detailed in your discharge medication list. It is important that you weigh yourself every day. If your weight increases, it may be a sign that fluid is building up in your body.
