He remains hemodynamically stable and is noted to have 2 unit drop over the last 5 months. - patient refused rectal - H/H was stable - Continued home PPI BID - Consider EGD as outpatient if patient continues have anemia # RUE swelling: initially concerning for clot given hx port placement but u/s with no evidence of thrombosis. No evidence of infection to have caused signs/symptoms. #HTN: Continued home lisinopril #HLD - Continued home atrovastatin #PMR - Continued prednisone 2 mg daily #Chronic Pain - Continued home oxycodone - Continued home gabapentin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [ ] patient will need further outpatient cardiology workup to determine if he has diastolic heart failure, consider a nuclear stress test as well given his troponinemia. Also consider if patient needs cath given body habitus may affect results of a stress test [ ] Patient will need outpatient titration of his furosemide dose; He's discharged on lasix 10mg PO daily [ ] further outpatient workup of worsening anemia, consider EGD [ ] patient should have H/H checked on ___ to ensure that his anemia is stable [ ] Patient should have chem 10 checked by ___ and monitor for electrolytes given new diuretis began.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o F with hx of CAD, afib, TR and ___ disease presents with intermittent shortness of breath and true hypoxia per EMS. Unclear etiology, currently feeling well. Had one episode while an inpatient without hypoxia. See below for discussion of each problem. . # SOB episodes - unclear etiology. Diff dx includes aspiration events, but her CXR showed no PNA or pneumonitis; afib episodes, but has been in NSR since here; PE but ___ negative; or worsening valvular disease in setting of some fluid overload, but that is also unlikely. While here, she had a CT chest to evaluate the old nodules that were seen previously, which showed that they were resolving. It was probably an infectious process that is now better. She did have one episode while here, seen by H.O., in which she felt extremely short of breath, was tremoring and had a stomach ache. Her O2 sat was 100% on RA. She felt better with 2L NC, although her sats did not change. It appeared to be an anxiety attack. She was given clonazepam and her symptoms resolved, but she felt "drugged" and refused the pill subsequent times. The teams believed it was anxiety and she was discharged home with the instructions to take clonazepam if tolerated, and to be sure not to take mirtazapine or ambien if she takes the clonazepam. . # Afib - in NSR with HR in the high ___ to ___ with no events on telemetry overnight. Do not think it is neccessarily the cause of her SOB. Her toprol was held while here for low HRs. She was instructed to only take half of her 50 mg dose if her AM heart rate was less than 60. That is what she was doing at home. Continued her amiodarone while here. . # CAD - had cath last admission with no coronary disease. We continued aspirin for prophylaxis. She ruled out for MI. . # CHF - patient with some depressed LV function, has severe 4+ TR, which could be cause of her symptoms if valvular disease is changing and worsening. Could have few episodes of flash edema in her lungs, although CXR clear. Seems euvolemic on exam. We continued all her home diuretics. She had one low BP with SBP around 90, she received a 250 cc bolus and improved. We also held her lisinopril once. . # ARF - had mild elevation of Cr on admission to 1.4; resolved the second day of admission to her baseline of 1.1; was likely prerenal. . # Chronic Abd pain - has already had extensive workup, patient seems more or less comfortable for now. We continued maalox and lidocain mix PRN. She was having small BMs daily while here. . # ___ disease - per patient seems to be worsening and having increasing tremors; less mobile that before with generalized weakness. We continued carbadopa/levodopa and neurontin. She has neurology follow up at ___ in the next few weeks for a second opinion. . # She was discharged to home with PCP and ___ follow up if she is willing to talk to one; she has refused in the past.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for being short of breath. An EKG was unchanged and your chest ___ was normal. We think you are having intermittent episodes of shortness of breath because of anxiety. We think you should take the clonazepam as neurology recommends. Remember, though, if you try the clonazepam, do not take the Remeron or the Ambien that night. You can consider following up with the geriatric psychiatrists. A CT scan of you chest was also done and showed that you had improvement in the prior nodules in your lung. It likely was an infection that is now almost completely resolved. Your heart failure was in good control and you were not fluid overloaded. Your heart rhythm was normal without any episodes of atrial fibrillation. Please continue to take the Amiodarone as prescribed. Please stop taking the Lisinopril until Dr. ___ it is ok to restart it. Please have your pulse checked in the morning, if it is greater than 60 please take 25mg of the Metoprolol Succinate.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY ==================== ___ yo man with CAD s/p CABG (LIMA-LAD, SVG-LDPA, SVG-OM1, SVG-OM2, SVG-D1, SVG-D2, SVG-ramus ___, with known occlusion of all vein grafts) and PCI (proximal SVG-OM2, distal SVG-OM2), AFib/Aflutter s/p ablation on apixaban who presents for acute onset dyspnea and nausea/vomiting c/f NSTEMI. Latest echo EF 27%. Now s/p Successful PCI with drug-eluting stent of the circumflex coronary artery. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= # NSTEMI S/P PCI and Cx DES # CAD s/p CABG Presentation with acute onset nausea/vomiting/diaphoresis and impending doom at rest with troponin 0.14 at OSH, consistent with NSTEMI. Transferred here and was started on heparin gtt. Troponin trended down, pt has not had symptoms while in house. PCI with Cx ___ on ___, held diuretics one day before and on the day of PCI. Pt got 325mg of aspirin on day of PCI but was not discharged on aspirin. He was discharged on apixaban and clopidogrel. We educated him several times re importance of clopidogrel and apixaban adherence in order to maintain stent patency. Restarted and uptitrated his carvedilol to 12.5mg but his BP has been soft, decision was made to discharge on his home dose 6.25mg. Isosorbid mononitrate was stopped. # Acute on chronic systolic heart failure # Ischemic cardiomyopathy Baseline weight 202-206 lbs. Presented with elevated BNP 1660 in setting of not taking torsemide for 6 weeks due to urinary incontinence. Had heart logic alert prior to admission for index ___, increased impedence likely due to volume overload. repeat TTE EF 27%. uptitrated carvedilol per above. not on spironolactone as outpatient due to hyperkalemia. previously trialed sacubitril-valsartan as outpatient but stopped due to intolerable headache and dry mouth. He was diuresed close to euvolemia however requested to leave despite lower extremity edema. We advised him of the increased risk of rehospitalization if he is discharged before euvolemic. # Hx paroxysmal AFib/Aflutter s/p ablation ___ # Hx Atrial tachycardia s/p ablation ___ Currently in sinus rhythm. Was having afib episodes on the night of admission but HD stable. Started on heparin gtt then transitioned to apixaban after procedure. Uptitrated carvedilol per above. HR around 60bpm. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== # Hypertension: We decreased his lisinopril dose to 10mg daily given his relative low BP iso diuresis. # Hyperlipidemia: not on statin outpatient due to intolerance (myalgias) #CODE: Full code (confirmed ___ #CONTACT: ___ Relationship: wife Phone number: ___ TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ==================== Dry Weight: 202-206, discharge weight 205.25lbs. Discharge Cr: 1.2 [ ] pt s/p DES to circumflex. Should be continued on apixaban and clopidogrel to prevent stent thrombus. [] Pt not on statin due to allergy (myalgias with rosuvastatin and simvastatin), consider alternative lipid-lowering agent. [] Please recheck bmp at next pcp visit to assess for electrolyte and renal function stability on new diuretic dose. [] Pt not taking home isosorbide mononitrate, BP has been well controlled while in house, did not restart on discharge. [] Pt not taking home carvedilol, restarted and tolerating well on discharge. [] Pt discharged on apixaban and clopidogrel, no aspirin. Pt was not taking aspirin at home. [] Pt not taking home torsemide for 6 weeks before admission, required IV diuretics while in house, discharged on the same dose of torsemide he was prescribed. [] Pt was taking lisinopril 20mg daily prior to admission but BP has been soft in the setting of diuresis, decreased to 10mg daily. [] Pt needs to follow up with cardiologist to discuss above treatment. [] Pt was originally treated for pneumonia with antibiotics, reported brown sputum more consistent with heart failure, and repeat CXR here did not show any focal consolidation. Stopped antibiotics. Please follow up and symptoms and WBC if needed.
summary: You were transferred here because you had an NSTEMI(a type of heart attack). We gave you heparin (a type of blood thinner) and stented one of your heart vessels treat your heart attack. We did an ECHO (an ultrasound of the heart) to evaluate your heart function which showed worsening of heart failure among other findings. We gave you diuretics (water pills) to treat your heart failure. You were advised to stay for one or more days given ideally we would continue giving you diuretics until you are back to your dry weight and no longer overloaded with fluid. But you preferred going home understanding that your heart failure may worsen given you are still overloaded with fluid now.

text: Brief Hospital Course: #. CAD: Patient with no prior history of CAD. Initially her history was challenging given her confusion (see below). On HD2 a more clear history was obtained and stated she had been having CP for several days. Her admission EKG revealed ___epressions V4-V6, but her cardiac biomarkers were (-) x3 ruling her out for an MI. Upon further questioning it was found that her CP was reproducible upon applying pressure to her chest wall, making the diagnosis of ACS less likely and musculoskeletal pain more likely. Her pain was subsequently treated succesfully with tylenol. . #. Pump: Patient with history of sCHF although EF 55% on last TTE. She does have a history of valvular disease with MVR due to rheumatic heart disease in ___. Presented with signs/symptoms of CHF exacerbation. Unclear cause but dietary indiscretion or atrial fibrillation with heart rate in the 100's could have precipitated it. She was treated with IV lasix and once her symptoms had resolved she was restarted on her home dose of 80 mg PO q AM of lasix with an addition 20mg PO in the evening. . #. Rhythm: Patient with history of atrial fibrillation. This could have precipitated CHF exacerbation (as above). She is on coumadin for afib and mechanical mitral valve. On admission her INR was subtherapeutic at 1.9. She was started on heparin gtt and her coumadin dose increased to 7.5 mg daily. Once her INR was with in range (2.5-3.5) the heparin gtt was stopped. Her INR remained therapeutic thereafter but on ___ it was 4.2, her coumadin was held and subsequently changed back to 5 mg daily. . #. Mechanical Mitral valve: Patient s/p MVR due to rheumatic heart disease. On admission her INR was subtherapeutic at 1.9. She was started on heparin gtt and her coumadin dose increased to 7.5 mg daily. Once her INR was with in range (2.5-3.5) the heparin gtt was stopped. Her INR remained therapeutic thereafter but on ___ it was 4.2, her coumadin was held upon discharge with plans to restart at 5mg qday when her INR is back at goal of 2.5 to 3.5. . #. Fall: Patient suffered from a fall and hit her head on ___. She underwent CT head and c-spine given her risk for bleed due to anticoagulation. These ruled out an acute ICH. Her neurological exam remained at baseline. Repeat CT head on ___ showed no change. . #. Confusion: Patient found to be confused on transfer to the floor (from the ED) and unable to answer questions. This was thought to be due to delirium caused by large amount of narcotics received at both OSH and ___ ED. Her neurological exam revealed no deficits. Her mental status returned to baseline on HD2 and she had no more issues with delirium. . #. CKD: Patient with history of CKD with reported baseline of ~2.0. Her renal function was within baseline and it actually improved from baseline during her hospital stay. Cr was 1.6 on the day of discharge. . #. Anemia: Patient has a history of anemia. Her last baseline was around ___. On admission her Hct was 30 but it slowly decreased to 24, throughout her hospitalization, where it stabilized. This was thought to be due to a combination of anemia of chronic disease and CKD as she was found to be guaiac (-). Hemolysis throught the mechanical valve was also in consideration but this diagnosis was not pursued as no treatment would be available. She transfused 1 unit PRBC's on the day prior to discharge with improvement of Hct to 29. . #. Deconditioning: Patient was seen by physical therapy who recommended ___ rehab as the patient was noted to be weak.
summary: You were transfered to ___ for management of your chest pain. Your EKG had some changes but your blood test did not show you had a heart attack. Your chest pain was most likely musculoskeletal as it was worse with pressing on your chest and responsive to tylenol. You were also found to have a mild worsening of your congestive heart failure and were treated with diuresis to which you responded well and this resolved. Your INR was found to be subtherapeutic and due to your mechanical heart valve we started you on an IV blood thinner called heparin. We increased your coumadin dose to reach a goal INR of 2.5-3.5, and once therapeutic re-started you normal home dose. Do not take coumadin until your INR is less than 3.5 as it was 4.2 on discharge. You were seen by the physical therapists who reccomended you go to a rehabilitation facility or recieve physical therpy at home. You also had right hip pain but your X-ray was normal. You suffered from a fall and hit your head while in the hospital. Your head imaging and neurological exams were normal.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o M with h/o systolic dysfunction, HTN, CAD s/p MI who presented with acute on chronic CHF exacerbation. . # Acute on chronic systolic CHF: Patient presented with a JVD of 10-12cm, along with +1 ___ edema, and bibasilar rales. CXR and BNP c/w pulmonary edema/volume overload, Intially, Mr. ___ was maintained on 5L 02 and then decreased to 2L. We continued to diurese the patient and it wasn't until Hopital day #3 that he was able to tolerate RA and keep his saturations up. Pt continued to diurese ___ L/day and has had significant in house weight loss. EF 35-40% on most recent echo with mixed systolic and diastolic dysfunction and is followed by Dr. ___. We will discharge the patient on Lasix 60mg daily and contine his home dose of carvedilol. He is not on an ACE given his allergy. Continue low salt diet at home. . # CAD: Given Mr. ___ presentation w/ SOA, we obtained 3 sets of Cardiac Enzymes and ruled him out for an MI. Additionally, on hosp day #3, he had some CP that was agained ruled out and not indicative of ACS. His episode was limited and atypical. He does have mild troponin elevation in setting of baseline CKD, CK's flat. We will continue medical management with aspirin, statin, carvedilol. . # L ___ Pain: When patient presented, patient complained of LLE pain that had been present for about two days. His limbs did not appear asymmetrical, nor more erythematous than the other. Given his physical limitations and reason to be more sedentary, Bilat ___ dopplers were performed and r/o presence of DVT. . # CHRONIC KIDNEY DISEASE: Pt's baseline Cr is 3.0. His Cr was maintained w/ diuresis and we renally dose meds if necessary. . # POST-HERPETIC NEURALGIA: Pt's LLE pain may have been as a result of his neuralgia. Mr. ___ described his pain as being different from his usual neuralgia, and given his SOA, we obtained ___ dopplers. Will continue gabapentin. . # DM2: We held his home glipizide in house and started sliding scale insulin. BS were maintained between 130-200. We will send patient home on Glipizide 5mg and recommend close monitoring of sugars. . # HTN: BP was maintained between SBP 130-150s. Prior to admission, Mr. ___ had recently been uptitrated to hydralazine 100 tid. Will continue his home meds and doses hydralazine 100 tid, amlodipine 5mg, and carvedilol 6.25mg.
summary: You were hospitalized because you had an acute worsening of your heart failure. During your stay, we diuresed you and removed some of the excess fluid that was likely causing your shortness of breath. You were initially on oxygen because of your shortness of breath, but as we removed some of the fluid on your lungs, your shortness of breath improved and you were able to maintain oxygen saturations above 90% on room air. We made some changes to your medication regimen. We decreased your dose of lasix to 60mg daily and have started you on Calcium Acetate, a medication we would like you to take three times daily to protect your kidneys.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ was admitted to ___ ___ with acute exacerbation of chronic diastolic heart failure. He was given 100mg lasix IV with successful diuresis > 1L overnight. His creatinine was further elevated from prior at 6.6, and he complained of right flank pain overnight. A renal ultrasound the next day was unremarkable. On ___ he was given his home dose of Torsemide 40mg which he likely had not started taking at home yet (on review it doesn't appear to have been filled). He reported feeling improved since his admission in terms of his dyspnea. He was later transferred to an inpatient bed for further management. On ___ he was evaluated by the ___ diabetes team, they made adjustments to his insulin regimen while here in hospital. He was also evaluated by nephrology and recommendations were to prepare patient for hemodialysis and AV fistula. Ultrasounds were completed on left arm. Discussions were held between MD/NP staff and patient/family regarding next steps including need for hemodialysis and cardiac catheterization. Risks were discussed with contrast and existing acute on chronic renal dysfunction and patient and family requested cardiac catheterization. Plan with nephrology discussed with pre/post hydration plans. On ___, cardiac catheterization completed with pre/post hydration and 80-90% stenosis to OM1 LPD and no intervention was completed given high LVEDP in the ___. Plan made for additional diuresis and return for PCI in a few days or discharge to return for this procedure. It was ultimately determined that it would be best to set him up for access for dialysis and to stabilize with continued diuresis prior to stenting. Torsemide added back to regimen and goal was diuresis of at least -1L daily. He was maintained on a 2 gram low sodium carb consistent diet with a 2 liter fluid restriction, daily weights and I&O's. He reports his baseline weight is 238 lbs. or 108 kg. His weight did spike up to 109.8 kg on ___ from 109.3 on ___. Discharge day weight is 109.6 kg. He continued with the 80 mg Daily Torsemide and had an improved creatinine of 6.7 on day of discharge, down from 7.0 the prior day. His Keflex was discontinued during this admission, as was his Losartan and isosorbide dinitrate which was replaced with Isosorbide mononitrate. Substantial teaching was performed by the Diabetic Educator and he will have early follow up with ___ following discharge. He has early follow up with all of his providers which include PCP, ___, Infectious Disease, ___ and Nephrology and Podiatry, all detailed on his discharge worksheet. At his upcoming Nephrology appointments, access will be planned as will addition to the transplant team. It is recommended that he establish care with the Heart failure service as well.
summary: You came to our cardiac direct access unit with symptoms of cough, shortness of breath and chest pain. You were also noted to have worsening renal failure. We removed fluid with diuretics and your breathing improved. You also had a cardiac catheterization done to evaluate your heart function and it was found that you have a blockage in one of the branches of your heart that we were not able to repair with stent placement at this time due to your kidney function and risk of contrast exposure so soon after your initial catheterization this admission. It is recommended that you continue your workup with the renal doctors for ___ and future dialysis before we continue with this procedure for stent placement. You are followed by a nephrologist who will help manage dialysis and planning.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male w/ CAD s/p NSTEMI in ___, previous PCI (OM2, OM3) with transferred from OSH with nonsustained Vtach. . # RHYTHM: On admission patient in normal sinus rhythm with monomorphic bursts of ventricular tachycardia. Electrolyte notable for magnesium of 1.1. Electrolytes monitored closely and aggressively repleted. Patient started on metoprolol and uptitrated to 50mg BID. Ectopy, though lessened, persisted post-repletion and beta-blocker initiation. Etiology: prior NSTEMI scar with contribution of new ischemic nidus. Cardiac catheterization performed. DES placed in Lcx and OMI1. Post stenting ectopy largely resolved and patient asymptomatic. Patient remained hemodynamically stable throughout stay. . # PUMP: Patient's presenting OSH complaints: dyspnea, ___ edema. CXR with mild pulmonary vasculature congestion. Patient not on standing diuretic regimen. BNP: ~5000. Admission exam consistent with volume overload with elevated JVP, bibasilar crackles. TTE demonstrated an EF~45%, BNP ~5000. (No TTE or BNP in our system for comparison) Patient intermittently diuresised. Diuresised ~ 7lbs while hospitalized. Diuresis held in days prior to discharge as creatinine elevated in the post-catherization setting. - Patient would likely benefit from standing diuretics when creatinine normalized. . # CORONARIES: TIMI score: 4 (age, risk factors, prior stenosis >50%, use of ___ Pt with h/o CAD s/p NSTEMI in ___ recent cardiac cath in ___ with two vessel disease, no interventions performed. EKG without ischemic changes. Pt is not on an optimal cardiac regimen as cites h/o mylagias with statin use and sexual dysfxn on beta-blocker. Decision made to repeat catheterization which demonstrated selective coronary angiography in this right dominant system; two vessel coronary artery disease. Separate ostia were present. The LAD has moderate noncritical disease, unchanged from prior. The LCx has a 70% stenosis in the OM1 proximal to the prior stent. The RCA is 100% occluded as noted in prior catheterization. DES to LCx and OM1. Medication regimen optimized post-procedure: ___ 325mg, Crestor 5mg (with plans to uptitrate), Metoprolol 100mg XL. Valsartan held in setting of elevated creatinine. . # Hypomagesium. Patient found to be quite deficit on admission to OSH. Repleted on admission without episode of replete hypomagnesium. Urine magnesium elevated at 12. No clear etiology - if renal wasting would have expected magnesium to continue to downtrend in house. Hypomag has been seen in patients with chronic omeprazole use however this is typically in the setting of hypocalcemia as well. ?Secondary to EtOH use vs GI loss vs renal loss vs PPI use. Patient without complaints of GI losses ie chronic diarrhea. - monitor serum lytes and replete aggressively . # Dyslipidemia. Lipid panel (___) in house Cholest 177 Triglyc 120 HDL 43 LDLcalc 110. Patient started on low dose statin while hospitalized with plan to gradually uptitrate as outpatient. . # Diabetes. On monotherapy with metformin as outpatient. HgA1c: 7.7. In house sugars monitored regularly and maintained on an insulin sliding scale. Patient transitioned to glipizide at time of discharge due to chronic kidney disease. Sugars stable. . # Alcohol use. Per report ___ drinks/day, ___ drinks on weekend. No history of withdrawl. LFTs wnl. Patient counseled on the importance of drinking cessation. . # Chronic Kidney Disease. Patients baseline creatinine 2.1-2.3. Admission creatinine 2.2. Uptrended post-cardiac catheterization, peak 2.8. UA negative. I/Os closely monitored. Creatinine at time of discharge: 2.4 UOP stable throughout stay. . # Gerd. Patient continued omeprazole. .
summary: You were admitted to ___ due to progressive shortness of breath. On admission your heart was found to be in an irregular rhythm known as ventricular tachycardia. Your electrolytes were monitored closely and repleted as needed. You were also taken to cardiac catheterization to evaluate the perfusion of your heart. Additional drug eluding stents were deployed to ensure adequate blood flow to the flow. After the correction of electrolytes abnormalities and successful deployement of stents your heart rhythm normalized. It will be of the utmost importance for you to regularly see your cardiologist to ensure this does not happen again. While hospitalized your kidney function was closely monitored and was found to be deteriorating during your stay. On discharge, it was stable. Medications, including Metformin and Valsartan, were stopped to avoid any further damage to the kidneys.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Patient is a ___ y.o. male with h/o CAD s/p CABG, and h/o paroxysmal Atrial fibrillation who presents with Atrial fibrillation with RVR . #. Atrial fibrillation with Rapid Ventricular Rate - In ED patient was found to have afib with RVR. He was transitioned to diltiazem 45mg QID overnight after discontinuing diltiazem gtt started in ED. He converted to NSR. On the morning after admission diltiazem was discontinued and he was restarted on his home medication Toprol XL at an increased dose of 100mg daily. He had no evidence of arrhythmia throughout the remainder of his hospitalization. His coumadin was briefly held while awaiting possible removal of his cholecystostomy tube but was restarted prior to discharge. . #. History of Cholecystitis and percutaneous biliary drain placement - The patient was recently admitted with acute cholecystitis and had a percutaneous drain placed. His antibiotics ceftriaxone and flagyl have been discontinued per suregery recommendations. He underwent tube cholecystogram to evaluate for evidence of cholethiasis. He was found to have persistent cystic duct blockage and cholelithiasis so his perc. drain was maintained. He will follow up as scheduled in general surgery clinic. . #. CAD - Continued on aspirin, lisinopril, toprol xl and atorvastatin. . #. Chronic Diastolic Heart Failure - the patient's home dose of bumex was decreased to 1mg daily. He maintained adequate diuresis with this regimen. . #. Diabetes mellitus Type 2 - The patient was evaluated by ___ for poorly controlled glucose levels. His dose of lantus was increased to 28U daily. He is scheduled to follow up with ___. . #. HTN - the patient's home dose of Toprol XL was increased to 100mg daily. He was trialed on lisinopril 2.5mg daily however his BP would not tolerate the addition of this medication. . #. GERD - Continued on pantoprazole
summary: You were admitted with palpitations and were found to have Atrial fibrillation with a rapid ventricular rate. You were treated with IV medications to control your rate. Your medication Toprol XL has been increased to 100mg daily. You have been restarted on your medication coumadin for stroke prevention as you have atrial fibrillation. Please continue to take your coumadin as directed. You should have your INR checked on ___ for further coumadin dosing. Your antibiotics ceftriaxone and flagyl have been discontinued by surgery. You had a cholecystogram performed which demonstrated gall stones and a persistent blockage of your duct. You should continue your current biliary drain care.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old woman with hx of HLD CHF and recently diagnosed atrial tachycardia managed with amiodarone an ASA presents as OSH transfer with c/f L MCA syndrome. Not candidate for intervention (not in window for tPA). Subsequently transferred to ___ where ___ was 18 ( detailed above). CT head showed worsening ischemic changes (ASPECT <7). CTA showed diffuse atherosclerotic changes and possible M3 occlusion. She was not deemed an endovascular candidate given location and ASPECT score. She was intubated for airway protection and admitted to neuro-ICU for further management. Etiology of her stroke likely cardio-embolic with atrial fibrillation documented on telemetry as new diagnosis for her. She was extubated on ___. ICU course complicated by acute-on-chronic congestive heart failure, likely tachycardia-induced and multifocal aspiration pneumonia. Her heart rate and tachypnea improved with digoxin load and with diuresis through furosemide/albumin. Amiodarone was discontinued. She was maintained on an increased dose of metoprolol and on standing digoxin. Neurologic dysfunction characterized by global aphasia with more pronounced expressive aphasia. Her goal volume status on discharge from ICU is euvolemia. She was started on apixaban for her afib. She was mute with global aphasia, and NGT-dependent for nutrition initially. Her swallowing function was monitored as PEG not thought to be within goals of care for her when discussing with family. Patient's advance directive states that she would not want to prolong things and would not want artificial feeding if prognosis was poor. Consulted palliative to further discuss swallow plan. As she did not improve enough for swallowing on her own it was decided after a family meeting with her son, palliative, and primary team on ___ that patient would be made comfort measures only, as ___ would not be in her goals of care as she had previously made known. She was transferred to hospice, all extraneous interventions were discontinued and medications for comfort were administered. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES stroke: Follow up comfort.
summary: You were hospitalized due to symptoms of right sided weakness resulting from an ACUTE ISCHEMIC STROKE, a condition where a blood vessel providing oxygen and nutrients to the ___ is blocked by a clot. The ___ is the part of your body that controls and directs all the other parts of your body, so damage to the ___ from being deprived of its blood supply can result in a variety of symptoms. This affected your swallowing and language. As your swallowing had not improved greatly, and ___ had indicated in your advanced directive that ___ would not artificial nutrition or to prolong things, ___ care was changed to comfort care, after a period of time had passed and it became apparent that the swallowing was not improving to the point where a PEG tube would not be necessary.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ## Orthostasis: Likely autonomic neuropathy in the setting of type 1 Diabetes. Patient has not been taking midodrine at home as prescribed apparently because his pharmacy did not have it available. Does not report nausea, vomitting, diarrhea or urinary frequency and looks euvolemic on exam. Cr slightly elevated at 1.2, but baseline appears to be 1.1 to 1.3 so doubt pre-renal azotemia. Hct at baseline, so unlikely orthostasis from bleeding. Patient states that severe orthostatic changes in blood pressure are his baseline, however he is usually asymptomatic. Initially, patient's standing SBP was undetectable and he was mildly lightheaded. Patient received 2 L IVF, received midodrine and SBP was ___ on standing, with patient asymptomatic. He ambulated with ___, who felt cane for stability was adequate. He will see an autonomics specialist as an outpatient. . ## CAD s/p CABG: Symptoms unlikey due to ACS give documented orthostasis and EKG at baseline. Continued beta blocker, statin, aspirin. . ## CHF: Euvolemic on exam, but CXR shows slightly increased pleural effusion. No SOB or hypoxia. No indication for diuretics at this point. Continued beta blocker, ace-inhibitor and aspirin. . ## DM1: Somewhat well controlled given A1c of 7.7. Continued home dose lantus 10 U daily and ISS. . ## Pleural effusion: Unclear etiology but likely due to recent MI and CABG. No signs of overt total body overload. Patient without leukocytosis or fevers concerning for infection. Monitor for improvement after post-op period.
summary: You were admitted for low blood pressure on standing, which is called orthostatic hypotension. This is a chronic condition called by autonomic dysregulation. This means that the nervous systems that regulates "flight or fight" or activity versus rest are not balanced normally, such that when you stand your nervous system is unable to appropriately increase your heart rate and blood pressure. Gravity causes blood to pool in your legs and your brain does not receive enough blood flow. This is why you may feel dizzy or weak. There are many causes of this type of nerve damage, but for you the cause is diabetes. You can help prevent symptoms by sitting very gradually, and taking 2 minutes to sit before standing up. You should also make sure you stay well hydrated.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF SUMMARY STATEMENT: Mr. ___ is an ___ M w/ CAD, hx diastolic CHF, DMII, and prior CVA who presented to ___ w/ chest pain then found to have NSTEMI w/ severe 2 vessel CAD (90% pLAD, 90% pCx, 40% RCA). Pt. transferred to ___ for CABG eval. CABG eval was done and CT surgery. He was offered a CABG surgery. However, despite pt. understanding that CABG would be the best option for him in terms of improving life expectancy, he elected for the PCI at this time. PCI was done and BMS (given recent GI bleed) were placed in the LAD x1 and LCx x1. Pt. tolerated the procedure well and was discharged on aspirin and plavix. ACTIVE ISSUES ================ # NSTEMI / CAD: Pt. presented to OSH with chest pain. He ruled in with cardiac enzymes and was taken for coronary angiography. OSH Cath revealed 90% pLAD, 90% pCx, 40% RCA. Given evidence of 3 vessel disease, pt. was transferred to ___ for CABG evaluation. CT surgery was consulted, performed pre-op evaluation, and offered the pt. CABG. Upon further discussion, the pt. stated that he is not interested in CABG at this time. We explained to the pt. that the stent approach is not our first choice given the additive mortality benefit of CABG in patient's similar to him. Pt. understood that this was the second best option and elected to proceed with coronary catheterization. As such, catheterization was performed with deployment of BMS x1 to LAD and x2 to LCx (BMS used given pt's recent large volume diverticular bleed). Pt. was continued on his home dose of metoprolol, lisinopril, aspirin, and plavix. He was switched to atorvastatin. CHRONIC ISSUES ================= # Diabetes mellitus type II: Pt's home regimen held on admission. Managed with ISS. Restarted on oral agents on discharge. # CVA, embolic: Pt. is s/p ___ partial R eye visual loss thought to be due to subclavian artery issue. Pt. was continued on ASA 81 and atorvastatin 80mg. # CKD: Pt's baseline per PCP's office ranges between 1.4 - 2.0. He remained within this baseline during hospitalization. # Hypertension: Stable. Continued on lisinopril. His HCTZ was discontinued. # Hyperlipidemia: Switched from pravastatin to atorvastatin. # Anemia: Remained stable on hospitalization, likely ___ recent GI bleed. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================== # Pt. should continue on lifelong aspirin. He should take plavix for at least one month. Discontinuation of plavix should be discussed with cardiologist prior to any medication changes. Attention should be given to whether plavix is also needed for prior stent placed for treatment of subclavian steal. # Med Changes: Pt's pravastatin was replaced by atorvastatin. His HCTZ was stopped and lisinopril was initiated. # CONTACT: ___ (HCP/Daughter) ___ # CODE: Full - confirmed
summary: You were admitted with chest pain from an outside hospital. At the outside hospital you were found to have had a heart attack. The ___ sent you to ___ for a possible bypass surgery. The surgeons saw you and felt that you were a good candidate for surgery. We explained to you the benefits, but because of your upcoming plans, you elected to proceed with a stenting procedure. Two stents were placed in 2 of your major heart arteries. You had no recurrence of chest pain while in our hospital. Please continue to take plavix and aspirin everyday. Do not stop either of these medications unless you speak with your cardiologist.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # CORONARIES: Patient with known CAD and recent admission concerning for crescendo angina, who now presented with recurrent SSCP and troponin leak. During admission earlier in the week, patient had stated that she would not be amenable to PCI and opted for medical management with increased anti-anginal therapy however she was unable to afford the renaxa prescribed at that visit. On this admission her primary cardiologist, Dr. ___ spoke with her and her family regarding the possibility of cath, however this was deemed a higher risk than continuing medical management given that hte patient is ___ years old and likely has 3 vessel disease that will not be amenable to PCI. Moreover, she would not be a surgical candidate. She was started on verapamil ER in the hopes of controlling her anginal symptoms because she has been unable to tolerate other anti-anginal medications (imdur) in the past and has not been able to afford the renaxa. # PUMP: Patient with known systolic CHF. On admission appeared slightly fluid overloaded on exam and BNP was slightly elevated from earlier in the week. This was positive with urine culture showing gardenerella vaginalis. She was given one dose of metronidazole gel vaginally for treatment of this. summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath and chest pressure. We feel this is likely related to your heart, but in speaking with Dr. ___ both agreed that a heart procedure would not be the best option at this point. Instead, we added a medication called Verapamil to help with your discomfort, and Dr. ___ has sent a prescription to your pharmacy for this. You should stop taking ranolazine. You also had a vaginal infection. We prescribed you a medication for this that you will get vaginally for five days. The medication name is flagyl. You got your first dose here in the hospital and will get the remaining four days at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with a history of DM2, HTN, HLD, MR, knee OA, recent L3 compression fracture who presents shortly after return home from rehab with nausea/vomiting with course c/b delirium . #Acute toxic metabolic encephalopathy #Hyperactive delirium: #Garbled speech: Delirium likely multifactorial due to dehydration, infection, and hospitalization. Pt responded to small doses of Zyprexa. Her family said her speech was more garbled (first noticed changes in speech on ___, so she underwent head imaging - NCHCT negative, MRI that demonstrated no evidence of stroke or other pathology. Infectious workup was negative. Mental status much improved by discharge, although per family not quite back to baseline. #Nausea/vomiting #Gastroenteritits with evidence of mild proctocolitis Most likely gastroenteritis and mild proctocolitis given h/o nausea/vomiting/diarrhea. Received 1 dose of cipro/Flagyl in the ED, then symptoms resolved without further treatment. Norovirus negative, C. diff negative, stool studies negative including fecal culture for vibrio, Yersinia, E. coli 0157:H7, cryptosporidium/giardia DFA, and ova and parasites. HIDA scan negative. Pt tolerating PO without nausea or diarrhea at time of discharge. #Arrhythmia Irregular rhythm on tele and ECG, unclear whether AFib or other sinus arrhythmia as p waves seem to be present. No prior history of afib in chart. CHADS2VASC 5. Not further characterized on telemetry, discussed with family that there's no clear indication for anticoagulation. ___: Cr on presentation 1.1 from recent baseline closer to 0.8, most likely ___ dehydration in setting of gastroenteritis as above. Improved to 0.7 at time of discharge. #MITRAL REGURGITATION: Home lasix was held in setting of recent ___ and ___ PO intake; restarted on discharge. Of note, TTE ___ with mild global left ventricular hypokinesis, mildly dilated right ventricle with normal systolic function. No evidence of MR. ___ ISSUES =============== #DM: Continued on home metformin and glimepiride upon discharge (ISS while in house) #Hypothyroid TSH 1.0 on admission. Continued home Synthroid #HLD: Continued home atorvastatin #Recent L3 compression fracture Outpatient Spine f/u w/ Dr. ___ in ___ weeks. Continued home gabapentin. #?CHRONIC UTI Reported in med history and on daily cefpodoxime, possibly for UTI suppression. Held during this hospitalization but resumed upon discharge. Transitional issues: [] Patient had TTE which showed EF 42% and mild global hypokinesis. Discuss with patient the need for further heart failure treatment; as inpatient no evidence of volume overload. [] Her delirium was felt to result from hypovolemia and hospitalization. She responded to PRN olanzapine, particularly at night. Not back to baseline on discharge per family, please continue to monitor mental status with input from family. Time spent: 45 minutes PCP notified of hospital discharge.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having nausea, vomiting, and diarrhea. You had lab tests and imaging including an ultrasound of your liver and CT scan of your abdomen. These showed that you have mild proctocolitis (inflammation of the colon and rectum) and diverticulosis (outpouchings in the colon). Your symptoms were most likely due to viral gastroenteritis, and they resolved with supportive treatment. You became confused in the hospital, likely caused by delirium due to your infection and being in a new location. Changes in your speech were noticed and you had imaging of your brain including CT and MRI scans. They didn't show signs of a stroke.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o M with PMhx of Diastolic HF, severe MR, severe pulm HTN and pAFib/flutter on apixaban who was admitted after right TKR to the ICU, received IVF for low BPs and UOP, now being transferred to the floor after restarting PRN home diuretics with improved BP and renal function. # Acute on chronic Diastolic CHF # Severe MR # Severe Pulm HTN # Post-op hypotension, ___, hyponatremia requiring ICU admission ___ Patient received fluid in the OR and after the OR for hypotension and also received epoprostenol in the OR. Admitted to the ICU post-op with hypotension, hyponatremia, ___, felt to be due to intravascular volume depletion related to fluid shifts, improved with fluids. Subsequently restarted PRN torsemide without further issue (last received ___. Losartan held due to low BPs. At baseline he takes torsemide PRN. Given ongoing low BPs, orthostasis, and stable labs, torsemide was held during the days prior to discharge. Will need daily weights and volume status assessment and likely will need additional dose(s) in upcoming days #Orthostatic hypotension Patient noted to have orthostatic hypotension on the day of discharge when working with ___. However this was only noted after he had successfully ambulated in the hall, and he denied any dizziness. He did endorse fatigue after this activity, although also stated that it was the most activity he has one since the surgery. Suspect his orthostatic hypotension is likely related to ongoing equilibration of fluid shifts and volume status. Continuing to hold torsemide and losartan today, and will need ongoing monitoring. Considered blood transfusion as hemoglobin remains low at 7.1, but will instead continue to monitor at this time, as patient is overall feeling improved from recent days. He will be able to get a blood transfusion at his facility if needed. #RLE edema The patient had edema distal to the surgical site, which was most likely the result of his surgery and likely worsened from fluid retention compounded by compromised venous return in setting of recent surgery. Suspect some redistribution given increasing ambulation. Will require ongoing monitoring. He was on apixaban from post-op day 1 and not having muscle compartment pain, so DVT unlikely. Preliminary read of duplex study from day of discharge negative for DVT. #Anemia Patient with ___ point hgb drop post-operatively, likely related to blood loss and poor production. Hemoglobin 7.1 on day of discharge. Decided not to transfuse given his overall symptom improvement, but recommended close monitoring and transfusion if hgb<7 at the acute care facility he is being discharged to. # Right TKR: Underwent TKR ___. Pt is now ambulating with walker. Has Tylenol for pain, not requiring significant narcotics prior to discharge, but would consider PRN oxycodone if needs while working with ___. Plan is continued ___ #Hx of Submassive PE: Continued home Apixaban 5mg BID (restarted post-op day 1) #Paroxysmal Afib/flutter: S/p cardioversion on ___. Continued on home amiodarone 200 mg QD, and apixiban 5 mg BID. #OSA, not on CPAP: Continued home regimen of 2L NC O2 overnight #HTN Held home losartan 50 mg QD given orthostasis and low-normal BPs #Hyperlipidemia Continued home atorvastatin 40 mg QD ===================================== TRANSITIONAL/FOLLOW-UP ISSUES - continue to monitor orthostatics, daily weights, edema - torsemide to be used PRN for worsening edema or weight gain - please check CBC on ___ and if hemoglobin <7.0 then transfuse RBCs; if hemoglobin >7.0 then would continue to trend CBC ___ - check BMP ~2x weekly (or more if appropriate based on his clinical course) - consider restarting losartan if becomes hypertensive and no longer having orthostatic hypotension ===================================== >30 minutes in patient care and coordination of discharge
summary: You were admitted to the hospital following a knee replacement, and initially required admission to the ICU due to low blood pressures. Your condition gradually improved and you were ultimately discharged to rehab. The following are instructions from the orthopedic surgeons: You may not drive a car until cleared to do so by your surgeon. SWELLING: Ice the operative joint 20 minutes at a time, especially after activity or physical therapy. Do not place ice directly on the skin. You may wrap the knee with an ace bandage for added compression. Please DO NOT take any non-steroidal anti-inflammatory medications (NSAIDs such as Celebrex, ibuprofen, Advil, Aleve, Motrin, naproxen etc) until cleared by your physician.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is the brief hospital course for a ___ year-old black male here for evaluation of a persistent dry cough, right lower extremity swelling, and increasing dyspnea on exertion for the past week. In summation, Mr. ___ was aggressively diuresed during this hospital stay and his dyspnea markedly improved. His right lower extremity swelling, although present, was at its baseline in this patient. Finally, the non-productive cough was not altogether corrected during his stay, but the patient was given a cough suppressant for sleep and the cough improved. The patient acquired a red, weepy, and irritated right eye on the last 24 hour period of his stay. He did not know how he developed it, but it appeared to be a viral or bacterial conjunctivitis or a corneal abrasion. He was given antibiotic ointment to apply to the eye for 5 days. . #Acute on chronic diastolic CHF with Dyspnea: -IV LASIX diuresis done for fluid removal -followed electrolytes and Creatinine carefully during diuresis -continued CPAP as at home -recommended new PFTs as outpatient . #Right lower extremity swelling: continued coumadin, but at higher dose to improve INR to therapeutic level, no deep-vein thrombi noted on doppler ultrasound -warfarin 5 mg daily @ 4PM with frequent INR checks to be continued as outpatient -continue wound care at ___ for venous stasis ulcers and dermatitis . #Hypertension, benign: normotensive for admission. -continue Terazosin to 4 mg QHS at home -diuresed as above -continued home Imdur -continued home Hydralazine . #Diabetes ___, type 2 uncontrolled with complications: controlled in house on humalog sliding scale only, patient informed and encouraged to change to this regimen which was given to him at discharge . #ARF: Creatinine stayed at baseline for admission. -monitored Creatinine closely during stay . #ANEMIA of CKD: chronic and stable with no change on this admission, renal consult pending for outpatient with possible option of starting procrit following iron repletion -iron studies consistence with iron deficiency -informed patient that colonoscopy is recommended as outpatient for anemia work-up . Patient given follow-up visit with PCP and advised to get referrals from him regarding renal and pulmonary issues. He needs possible Procrit from renal and new PFTs from pulmonary. Patient also warrants a colonoscopy with his anemia. . Home nursing and wound care follow-up to continue. Home nursing will begin drawing and reporting INRs for Coumadin titrations.
summary: You were admitted with a persistent dry cough and increasing shortness of breath with activity. While you were here, we took some fluid out of your body using pills and IV medicine called diuretics. They made you urinate more frequently. Because these medications can sometimes make your kidneys work harder, we monitored your kidney function very closely while you were here. To improve your shortness of breath, we kept your same home medications here, but also gave you breathing treatments when you felt the most short of breath. During the first day you were also on oxygen through your nose as well. Your legs, although mildly swollen, were better than we have seen them in the past.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo M with PMH HTN and HLD presenting with acute STEMI s/p cardiac catheterization with BMS to LAD. ACUTE ISSUES: # Acute Coronary Syndrome: EKG at ___ was concerning for an anterior STEMI so he was given ASA 325 mg, heparin drip, and transferred to ___. On arrival to the ED here, his EKG change had resolved, however on arrival to the CCU his EKG showed re-appearance of anterior ST-segment elevations so he was taken for emergent cardiac catheterization. 3-vessel disease with mid-thrombus LAD was found, so he received a bare-metal stent to the mid-LAD thrombus. He was then optimized medically with ASA 325mg initially then 81mg daily, clopidogrel 75mg daily, atorvastatin 80mg daily, and metoprolol 25mg po daily. ACE-inhibitor and spironolactone should be started in the near-future though his BP would not tolerate it in-house. # Acute systolic heart failure: On arrival exam and CT consistent with acute systolic heart failure secondary to STEMI. Echo post cath with extensive regional systolic dysfunction (LVEF= ___ consistent with CAD ___ LAD territory). He was gently diuresed initially with 20mg IV lasix daily, then 20mg po lasix daily, and was subsequently clinically euvolemic so did not require further diuresis. He was managed with metoprolol 25mg po daily as above. He will need an Echo in 6 weeks to re-evaluate his ejection fraction to determine whether primary prophylaxis with ICD placement is indicated. # Aortic dissection: Unknown chronology. CT scan revealed focal dissection in the infrarenal aorta measuring approximately 2.5 cm in length, which on repeat imaging in-house was stable after his cardiac catheterization. His BP was controlled on the medications above. Vascular surgery followed in-house without need for intervention. He will need outpatient vascular follow-up. # Decision-making capacity: After his cardiac catheterization, he adamantly insisted on leaving the hospital to go home. Due to his STEMI and degree of acute systolic heart failure, this decision posed a very serious and realistic risk of death from a fatal arrythmia. He was urgently evaluated by Psychiatry and determined to lack decision-making capacity, so his grand-daughter who was previously chosen by him as his HCP took over. After this decision, he remained very cooperative throughout the rest of his hospital course without. Prior to discharge, he did request that his son be made his HCP though given plans for discharge this paperwork was not completed in-house. # ETOH withdrawal: He had symptoms of agitation and mild tremors early in his hospital course, however due to his acute cardiac dysfunction the typical CIWA scale parameters particularly HTN and tachycardia could not be assessed. He was therefore treated empirically with 5mg po diazepam BID initially, tapered to 5mg po daily, and then subsequently stopped. He remained stable without any severe withdrawal symptoms. # Alzheimers: Stable. Continued on home donepezil. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Echo in 6 weeks to re-evaluate his ejection fraction to determine whether primary prophylaxis with ICD placement is indicated. - ASA 81mg daily, clopidogrel 75mg daily, atorvastatin 80mg daily, and metoprolol 25mg po daily. - ACE-inhibitor and spironolactone should be started as outpatient if BP tolerates - monitor volume status to determine if po furosemide needed - outpatient follow-up with vascular surgery for aortic dissection - patient voiced preference for his son to become HCP. can complete this HCP paperwork as outpatient - social situation complicated, would benefit from social work assistance
summary: You were admitted this hospitalization for a heart attack. You underwent a procedure called a cardiac catheterization to explore the blockages and open a blockage in the left anterior descending artery that supplies the front portion of the heart muscle. We placed a bare metal stent where the blockage is during this procedure. We also found that the pumping function of your heart is depressed. This is called heart failure. It is of the utmost importance that you watch your sodium intake, do NOT drink or smoke and weigh yourself daily. Taking in too much sodium and alcohol makes the heart have to work harder and harder and can lead to hospitalization and death. Weighing yourself daily will allow you to see if you are retaining too much fluid thus making your heart work harder than it needs to.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old man with a PMHx of alcohol abuse and CAD who presents with atypical chest pain x 2 days and alcohol withdrawal. ACUTE ISSUES: 1. Chest pain: Muskuloskeletal. Patient was ruled out for MI with EKG and cardiac enzymes. Given initial CXR showing increased size of cardiac silhouette, there was concern for pericarditis. Patient did not have EKG changes suggestive of pericarditis and ECHO was not markedly different than baseline. MSK was determined to be the most likely etiology given that patient reported falling on to chest recently in setting of alcohol abuse, pain was very reproducible on exam, and cardiac enzymes and EKG's were not suggestive of ACS. Patient received pain control with Tylenol and oxycodone acutely and then with Tylenol. 2. Alcohol Withdrawal: Patient reports drinking 1 pint to 1.5 L vodka/day, and was actively withdrawing upon arrival. He received diazepam via CIWA scale and was supplemented with thiamin, folic acid, and a multivitamin. His withdrawal resolved. 3. Electrolytes- Patient's electrolytes were monitored and he was repleted to K>4 and Mg >2 as needed given cardiac history. 4. Right Foot pain: The right foot pain patient reported upon presentation was determine to be due to splinter. Splinter was removed and pain resolved without complication. Foot x-ray negative for fracture. Patient received TDaP vaccine. 5. Vertigo: Patient reported multiple falls due to dizziness in setting of alcohol abuse. A CT head (-) ruled out acute intracranial bleed, and patient was monitored on fall and seizure precautions. He was maintained on tele, with no pauses. Patient was found to have BPPV and ___ was consulted to perform an Epply maneuver. By day of discharge, dizziness was markedly improved. 6. Nausea and Vomiting: After patient finished withdrawing from alcohol, he had persistent post-prandial nausea and vomiting. Pancreatitis was considered in the setting of chronic alcohol abuse but negative lipase made it less likely. Patient's known BPPV was considered as an etiology but also felt to be less likely given the strict association between eating and n/v. Patient was maintained on Zofran as needed for nausea and received gentle IV fluids for hydration when he vomited persistently. A barium swallow was normal. GI was consulted who performed an EGD that showed gastritis. Patient's PPI omeprazole was increased to 40 mg BID. CHRONIC ISSUES: 1. CHF: EF ___ secondary to ischemic, tachycardia induced or alcoholic cardiomyopathy. Patient's home furosemide and losartan were continued. His ECHO was not markedly different than prior study. 2. HTN: Patient was continued on losartan. 3. Hx PE: Patient had a documented PE in ___ and reported taking both Lovenox for a. fib and Dabigitran for PE as outpatient. Patient reported that, one month prior at ___ ___ in ___, he was hospitalized and fluid was removed from his lungs. He said this was due to clot. Contacted ___, who had no record of the patient ever receiving care, and patient denied using alternate names at that institution. Could not confirm more recent hx of PE at other providers' either. Therefore, patient was considered to have completed a 6 month course of dabigitran and the medication was discontinued. He was continued on Lovenox for a.fib. 4. Atrial fibrillation: Continued on Lovenox. No a.fib during this admission. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient is homeless and has significant barriers to accessing ___ care - Continue to counsel on abstinence from alcohol - Monitor GI symptoms - Monitor cardiac status
summary: You were admitted to ___ with chest pain and withdrawal for alcohol use. During your stay your EKG was reassuring and your blood work showed that you did not have a heart attack. We provided you with medications to treat your withdrawal. During the hospitalization you were busy because of vertigo. Physical exam findings were consistent with benign paroxysmal positional vertigo. Your dizziness improved. You also had significant nausea and vomiting associated with meals. An EGD showed an irritation of lining of your stomach and concern for an abnormality at the stomach and esophageal junction. There were no ulcers or masses. Biopsies were taken and a report will be sent to your primary care physician.

MSK was determined to be the most likely etiology given that patient reported falling on to chest recently in setting of alcohol abuse, pain was very reproducible on exam, and cardiac enzymes and EKG's were not suggestive of ACS. He received diazepam via CIWA scale and was supplemented with thiamin, folic acid, and a multivitamin. His withdrawal resolved. 3. Right Foot pain: The right foot pain patient reported upon presentation was determine to be due to splinter. Splinter was removed and pain resolved without complication. Foot x-ray negative for fracture. Patient received TDaP vaccine. 5. Vertigo: Patient reported multiple falls due to dizziness in setting of alcohol abuse. A CT head (-) ruled out acute intracranial bleed, and patient was monitored on fall and seizure precautions. He was maintained on tele, with no pauses. Patient was found to have BPPV and ___ was consulted to perform an Epply maneuver. By day of discharge, dizziness was markedly improved. 6. Therefore, patient was considered to have completed a 6 month course of dabigitran and the medication was discontinued. He was continued on Lovenox for a.fib. 4. Atrial fibrillation: Continued on Lovenox. No a.fib during this admission. text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year-old man with hypertension, heart failure, coronary artery disease, atrial fibrillation and dementia, probably of the Alzheimer type presenting after a fall, and admitted on suspicion of spinal stenosis. Fall/Incontinence: Cervical MRI on the day of admission revealed no obvious cord pathology nor edema, therefore, sx less likely from spinal stenosis. Per ___, his incontinence and difficulty with gait were not new issues. Fallen three times in the last month. Most recently, this was when trying to pick something up from the floor. Neurological examination revealed decreased vibration sense at the left metatarsophalangeal joint, but position sense was preserved. ___ have some degree of neuropathy contributing to unstable gait. His head CT showed only an external hematoma/soft tissue injury on the posterior head, no internal bleeding. Pt was able to ambulate with a walker at discharge. If has change in mental status may need a repeat head CT to evaluate for subdural hematoma development. Pleural effusion/Hepatomegaly: Pt had decreased breath sound and dull percussion over the right base, posteriorly. There was also an enlarged liver span and blunt edge. This right upper quadrant was mildly tender. Chest x-ray also reveals a raised right hemidiaphragm, effusion and atelectasis of the right base. This finding is largely unchanged from ___ this year. Together with his ejection fraction of 20%, the differential diagnosis for these findings includes effusion and hepatosplenomegaly secondary to heart failure. Malignancy cannot be excluded. These are chronic issues and not active during admission. Pt will need further out patient workup by PCP. At this time the pt refused invasive procedures. Dementia: Had evidence of dementia and some delirium during his hospital course. Had episodes of 'sun-Downing' that was easily redirectionable and did not require medication. UTI: Was treated with a course of Cipro completed on ___. Hypertension: Control was poor on arrival, but improved during his stay after home meds were restarted. Elevated INR: had a mildly elevated INR, likely due to nutritional deficiency sine it improved with a course of Vitamin K. ___ have resulted from inadequate vitamin K production by gut flora, or intake. Venous insufficiency sores on lower extremities: Had no evidence of acute infection. Sores were superficial, were cleaned and partially healed during the admission. Continued use of antibiotic ointment and clean dry dressing recommended. Has a follow up appointment with dermatology. Pt was discharged back to ___.
summary: You were admitted to the hosptial after a fall. Inital scan (CT) of your head did not show any injury to the brain or bleeding within the skull, but blood and swelling of the soft-tissue at the back of your head. You were suspected of having narrowing of the spinal canal with compression of the spinal cord, as a cause of your falls and were admitted to the hospital. Your spine was imaged (MRI of the cervical spine). No damage to the spinal cord was seen. You were also suspected of having an infection of the urinary tract, which was treated with antibiotics. While in the hospital it was noted that your liver was enlarged and you had some fluid around your right lung. These were examined further with ultrasound. We recommend that this is investigated further after discharge.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ male with advanced bladder cancer on home hospice, HTN, HLD, aflutter on coumadin, and complete heart block s/p pacemaker, and CHF ___ amyloid cardiomyopathy, who presented to the ED due to painful urination, frequency, and incontinence. The discomfort is felt to be due at least in part to the infiltration of his tumor into the penis and urethra. #DYSURIA The case was given extensive consideration by the urology and ___ services. Foley is not feasible given his penile tumor burden, and suprapubic catheter felt to be too risky because of extensive bladder tumor that would lead to bleeding and then likely clotting of the catheter. ___ feels that given the unimpressive evidence for obstruction, a nephrostomy tube would not divert enough urine to truly relieve his symptoms, and that the risks would therefore likely outweigh any potential benefit. Therefore the focus was shifted to medical management of the patient's symptoms. He was started on standing pyridium and Tylenol TID and given PRN low-dose morphine pills. Long-term potential toxicities of phenazopyridine were discussed with the HCP and she is aware this long-term use is an off-label compassionate use given her father's ___ goals of care. He was also started on Flomax for obstructive voiding symptoms, although it is unclear whether this will help or not. #CHRONIC DIASTOLIC/SYSTOLIC HEART FAILURE ___ The patient has cardiac amyloidosis; LVEF is ~45%. He appeared clinically dry and had ___ on arrival; ___ improved with holding torsemide for two doses. The patient was formerly on torsemide 20 mg daily, but his daughter tells me his PO intake has dropped off markedly. The torsemide dose was halved to 10 mg daily. His lisinopril was also held for ___ and ___ BP. He needs a volume exam and a Creatinine check within a week. #A-FLUTTER Of note the patient initially received vitamin K in anticipation of a procedure, so his INR will be expected to take several days to become therapeutic again on his home coumadin dose. His lisinopril was also held since his systolic blood pressures were mostly close to 100. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Follow CBC if pyridium is to be continued. - Follow up volume exam and Creatinine on his reduced torsemide dose. - restart lisinopril if tolerated (held for ___ and soft BP)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of pain with urination. We have prescribed a medication to help numb the bladder and urethra (PHENAZOPYRIDINE). This medication is usually only used for a few days (it can cause serious but reversible blood disorders with long-term use), but since the main goal of your care right now if make you as comfortable as possible, I suggest you just keep taking it regularly. This medicine will stain your urine an unusual bright orange color; this is a NORMAL side effect of this medicine. Please take one phenazopyridine (sold over the counter as AZO) and two acetaminophen tabs (extra strength Tylenol) three times daily. If you have pain despite these meds, take half a tab of MORPHINE. We also gave you TAMSULOSIN (Flomax), which may make it easier to urinate without any straining. You are likely to have worsening difficulty urinating as time goes on and may completely obstruct at some point.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with cardiac and pulmonary sarcoidosis with predominant RV dysfunction, AV block s/p pacemaker ___ and subsequent upgrade to biventricular ICD ___ in setting of prolonged VT presenting with a few weeks of worsening fatigue and rising Cr on outpatient labs. On admission, the patient was felt to be volume overloaded and was diuresed with IV Lasix. His symptoms improved with this management and he was transitioned to oral Torsemide at the time of discharge. The patient had an exercise stress test which was notable for low VO2peak (Weber Class D) with low ventilatory threshold as a percentage of predicted VO2peak. The patient's Coumadin was held and he was started on Lovenox in anticipation for outpatient right cardiac catheterization. #HFpEF and significant RV dysfunction: Most recent EF 55%. Currently listed for OHT UNOS status II. Pt was s/p BIv ICD placement in ___ due to prolonged VT. Pt with several month history of fatigue though has not complained of sudden worsening. He did have recent onset of palpitations concerning for atrial tachycardia though was in sinus rhythm when he presented for cardioversion. He did not report symptoms or hypervolemia and is not hypervolemic per exam other than elevated JVP. He was diuresed with IV lasix and transitioned to PO torsemide. He appeared euvolemic and was clinically stable at the time of his discharge. His home warfarin was held upon discharge, and he was sent out on lovenox in anticipation for his outpatient RHC. #A-fib on Coumadin - Continued Amiodarone 200 mg PO/NG DAILY - Held warfarin pending RHC. Discharged on lovenox #CKD Stage 3B: Baseline Cr appears to be around 2.0. Cr 1.9 today ___, likely hypovolemia in the setting of recent diuresis. Patient does not appear markedly volume overloaded. He will have Cr checked as an outpatient. - Held lisinopril #Hypothyroidism - Continued Levothyroxine Sodium 50 mcg PO/NG DAILY CHRONIC ISSUES: #Ulcerative Colitis - ContinueD Mesalamine (Rectal) 1000 mg PR QOPM #Gout - ContinueD Allopurinol ___ mg PO/NG DAILY - ContinueD Colchicine 0.6 mg PO/NG ONCE:PRN Gout flare #Insomnia - ContinueD LORazepam 1 mg PO/NG QHS:PRN TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Discharge weight: 99.3 kg -Discharge diuretic regimen: Torsemide 20 mg PO QOD (starting ___ alternating with Torsemide 30 mg PO QOD (starting ___ -Patient's home Coumadin held at discharge in anticipation of right cardiac catheterization next week -Patient discharged on Lovenox ___ mg SC QDaily until planned right cardiac catheterization on ___ -Plan for repeat labs (Chem 7) on ___, results faxed to Dr. ___ for elective right cardiac catheterization on ___ ___
summary: As you know, you were admitted with worsening fatigue we feel was related to volume overload. We managed you with intravenous diuretics and your symptoms improved. We discharged you on Torsemide (please see medication list for specific instructions). You are scheduled next week to have a procedure called a right heart catheterization to evaluate the pressures in your heart. We stopped your Coumadin in anticipation of this procedure. You will need to take an injected medication called Lovenox while your Coumadin is stopped. You are also discharged with a prescription to have your labs checked on ___ and have the results sent to Dr. ___.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with hx of afib (not anticoagulated), dCHF, critical AS, ESRD on HD, indwelling ureteral stents and chronic catheter, compression fractures p/w dyspnea and hypotension and found to have UTI, new compression fractures of T3, T10, L5 since ___ and moderate loculated right pleural effusion and atelectasis. Hypotension and dyspnea likely secondary CHF and critical AS, possibly with some component of bacteremia from UTI. # Hypoxia: AS, MR and fluid overload likely cause given hypoxia has markedly improved with HD and fluid removal. Moderate loculated chronic R pleural effusion and atelectasis (likely chronic) also compromise respiratory capacity. Initially required 2L O2, now off O2. Initially treated with doxycycline ___ concern for PNA based on CXR, which was discontinued after CT was more consistent with chronic effusion # Nausea: Likely due to antibiotic and constipation (no BM for 5 days.) Improved after doxycycline d/c'd on ___ and 3 BM after aggressive bowel regimen with senna/colace/bisacodyl BID. Treated with odansetron 4 mg PO PRN. # Compression fractures: Appears to have both chronic T12 and L3 fx and subacute fractures at T3, T10, L5 that occured likely 2+ months ago per level of edema. Pain treated with Tylenol ___ mg q8h, with vicodin for breakthrough pain, which patient requested once in evening every day during her stay. A TLSO brace was fit per neurosurgery recommendations and should be worn whenever she is sitting up or head is elevated > 30degrees. ___ is okay with brace. BRACE MUST BE WORN FOR SUPPORT unless patient refuses. # UTI: Klebsiella predominant bacteria, though non-speciated gram negative and yeast also growing in lesser quantity per ___ urine culture. Patient was treated for klebsiella. Foley was exchanged on HOD1 at ___, which likely treated yeast component. Patient was started on ceftazidime (day 1: ___, discontinued ___ on cipro 500 mg PO Q24H on ___ for total 7 day course with last day of treatment ___. # Hypotension: Improved after 4 L of fluid removed via HD over HOD1 and 2. Patient's baseline BPs in ___. Continue HD regimen. # Aortic stenosis: Critical per repeat TTE on ___. Has explored options for valve replacement at ___, deemed not possible given concommitant TR and MR. ___ to be volume sensitive. Treat volume status via HD. # Atrial fibrillation: Not on anticoagulation, per PCP due to history of duodenal AVM. Currently in sinus rhythm. Was continued on amiodarone. # DVT PPX: Heparin allergy per ___ with borderline heparin antibodies. Was not treated with heparin, and fondaparinaux not possible in ESRD. DVT prophylaxis was given with pneumoboots. # ESRD on HD: HD ___ with 100-200 cc UOP daily, bilateral hydronephrosis s/p stents changed every 2 months and chronic indwelling catheter. Phos has been intermittenly elevated. Per renal, does not need phos binder unless Phos >10 and phos binder not shown to change clinical outcomes. Home dialysis schedule was continued with additional session on ___. # Goals of care: DNR/DNI, confirmed with patient ___. # Anemia: Presumably secondary to ESRD. # Coagulopathy: INR 1.4, likely nutrition-related. ===================================================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES ===================================================== - f/u in 4 wks with Dr. ___. - had mild transaminitis with ALT to 106 and AST to 51 on ___, thought to be related to her ABx regimen. Repeat LFTs in ___ days after discharge and abdominal ultrasound if RUQ pain - Complicated UTI: needs to finish 7 day course of antibiotics with Cipro 500 mg Q24H, last day of treatment ___. - Needs to wear TLSO brace at all times unless lying in bed.
summary: You were admitted for low oxygen levels and low blood pressure, which improved after hemodialysis and removal of extra fluid from your blood. During your admission, you were found to have several new compression fractures of your back. Our colleagues in neurosurgery fit you for a brace that goes around your chest and belly. IT IS IMPORTANT TO WEAR YOUR BRACE WHENEVER YOU ARE OUT OF BED AND WITH ANY ACTIVITY TO PREVENT FURTHER INJURY TO YOUR BACK. It is also important to follow up in one month with Dr. ___ surgeon. During your admission, you were also found to have a urinary tract infection. It is important to continue to take antibiotics (ciprofloxacin) to get rid of the infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is an ___ year-old man with H/O CAD s/p PCI to RCA (___), BPH s/p TURP, initially admitted to ___ for episodic lightheadedness abd flushing since surgery, subsequently found to have complete heart block and multiple episodes of VT, transferred to ___ for further management now s/p dual chamber ICD placement ___. ACTIVE ISSUES: # Complete Heart Block, Ventricular Tachycardia: Patient reported to be in complete heart block on telemetry at ___ ___, then had multiple episodes of VT, the last of which occurred early AM on ___. Complete heart block documented with 12-lead EKG. This was felt to be likely scar mediated given prior history of CAD. The patient received an IV amiodarone load at ___ and was then placed on an amiodarone gtt at 0.5 mg/hr prior to transfer to ___. There were no further episodes of ventricular tachycardia after starting amiodarone gtt and no further symptoms of flushing or dizziness. The patient then underwent placement of Inogen EL Dual Chamber ICD on ___. There were no procedural complications, and the patient tolerated the procedure well. Device interrogation revealed ICD functioning normally with acceptable lead measurements and battery status. The patient was given two doses of vancomycin 750 mg IV before being transitioned to cephalexin 500 mg PO/NG Q6H. He will continue cephalexin for 2 days after discharge for total 72 hours of ___ prophylaxis. The patient was also discharged on amiodarone 200 mg PO daily. Patent's initial TFTs, LFTs, and chest X-ray WNL and without evidence of amiodarone toxicity. # HFrEF: Patient had worsening dyspnea on exertion prior to admission, found to have NT-Pro-BNP 8922 at ___, treated as CHF exacerbation prior to transfer. At time of admission, no signs of volume overload on exam, saturating well on room air. Further diuresis held given clinically euvolemic for the duration of his hospitalization at ___. He was continued on home metoprolol succinate 25 mg and losartan potassium 100 mg PO daily. # CAD: Patient previously on simvastatin 40 mg PO daily, transitioned to rosuvastatin 5 mg QPM as patient has adverse reaction to atorvastatin. Needs high-intensity statin. Continued on ASA 81 mg PO. CHRONIC ISSUES: # BPH s/p TURP: Continued home tamsulosin. summary: You were admitted because you had had been having episodes of dizziness, lightheadedness, and flushing. You initially when to ___ and were found to have an abnormal heart rhythm which likely was causing these symptoms. You were then sent to ___ in ___ for further evaluation. You had a device called an Implantable Cardioverter Defibrillator (ICD) placed. This went well and you experienced no complications. You were started on a new medication, called amiodarone, to lower your risk of having another arrhythmia. Your statin medication (which lowers your cholesterol level) was switched to Rosuvastatin. You were given antibiotics to lower the likelihood of an infection after your ICD was placed. Keep the area where the ICD was placed COMPLETELY DRY for the next 3 days.
