text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ with history of htn, DM2, CABG in ___, and PCI in ___ of the LAD, who presents with three hours of substernal chest pressure and shortness of breath. . Mr. ___ presented with substernal chest pressure and shortness of breath which was concerning for MI or PE. He ruled out for MI with two sets of negative cardiac enzymes and an EKG on admission unchanged from baseline. Due to the elevated D-dimer, he had V/Q scan which ruled out a PE. He had a stress test, cardiac perfusion test that showed no defects on which to intervene, and an echo with EF of >55%, mostly unchanged from ___. Through his hospitalization he did not have any other recurrences of the chest pressure, though he described midsternal muscle spasm that responded to tylenol. Because he reported shortness of breath, orthopnea and gradually worsening lower extremity edema, we diuresed him with two days of IV lasix in addition to his home lasix. We also began mechanical methods to mobilize the fluid in his legs which improved his lower extremity edema. However his Cr trended up with urine electrolytes c/w pre-renal injury. Due to the increasing Cr, we held his home lasix but did not give IVF as he was mobilizing fluid from his legs into systemic circulation. On discharge his Cr was trending downward. He likely had a component of CHF exacerbation causing his SOB and exacerbating ___ edema, on top of an ongoing venous insufficiency secondary to obesity and CABG venous harvesting. His worsening kidney function appears to be ___ to the patient increasing his lasix dosage at home over the last few weeks in addition to recieving extra lasix as an inpatient. TO DO: Check Cr for continuing downward trend (2.2 on discharge) Restart home lasix 80mg daily (holding for increased Cr)
summary: You came to the hospital with chest pain and were admitted to make sure you weren't having a heart attack and didn't have a blood clot in your lung. Your EKG and tests of your heart function showed you didn't have a heart attack and your lung imaging showed you didn't have a clot in your lung. While you were here, you seemed to have more fluid in your body, so we gave you some extra furosemide, elevated your legs and placed stockings on your legs to push the fluid out. Your kidney function seemed worse than normal, which we think was due to having extra lasix both before your hospitalization and during hospitalization. Please continue all your home meds EXCEPT for your furosemide and olmesartan (benicar). Please wait till your kidney function is checked and you see Dr. ___ on ___ before restarting your lasix and olmesartan. Please purchase compression stockings (large or extra-large, medium tensile, open toe) to wear at home to prevent reaccumulation of fluid.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ history of ESRD secondary to glomerulonephritis s/p cadaveric renal transplant in ___ (baseline Cr 2.5-3), CAD s/p PCI with stents x 5 in ___ presenting with acute dyspnea/pulmonary edema with hypoxemia likely in setting of NSTEMI and acute systolic heart failure. He was admitted to the CCU for management. # NSTEMI. Patient presented with acute shortness of breath and lower extremity edema with likely acute systolic heart failure (TTE with LVEF ___ %, prior reportedly normal) in setting of NSTEMI given positive biomarkers. He has known CAD with last cardiac cath in ___ and is s/p prior stents at ___. He was placed on nitro gtt and lasix gtt for pulmonary edema and was weaned off BiPAP to facemask. Repeat TTE on ___ demonstrated improved EF of 40-45%, mild to moderate regional left ventricular systolic dysfunction with basal inferior akinesis, and mid to distal septal, distal inferior, distal lateral and apical, ___, 1+MR and small-moderate pericardial effusion without signs of tamponade. Troponin was elevated and climbed to 4.40 with CK-MB elevated but stable by the day after admission. He was placed on ASA 325, plavix 75mg, metoprolol, atorvastatin 80mg, and heparin gtt in anticipation of cardiac catheterization on ___. Cardiac catheterization demonstrated 80% stenosis of LAD, and he received ___ 2 mid LAD ostial lesion, minimal jailing of large ramus branch, with a 70 cc dye load. Pt's creatinine returned to baseline of ___. Pt was diuresed and weaned off supplemental O2. He was sent home with a prescription for 40 torsemide BID, with discharge dry weight documented at 175 lbs. # RLL opacification: On admission CXR, RLL opacification noted that could be atalectasis, pneumonia, or mass - uncertain given moderate pulmonary edema. Pt had dry cough. He was empirically started on levofloxacin for CAP, and blood cultures were sent, which showed no growth. He remained afebrile, with no leukocytosis. He was sent home to follow up with his PCP, who should get a repeat CXR in ___ weeks to ensure full resolution. # Normocytic, normochromic anemia: Pt had Hct down to 23 at OSH, with 1 unit PRBC transfusion per OSH records. He had no s/s bleeding, but was guaic positive. Repeat Hgb/Hct here were stable. Transfusion was initially held given pt on high amount of oxygen and concerning for worsening fluid overload, but hct continued to be stable at ~8 and decision was made not to transfuse. He will follow up with his PCP for further evaluation of his anemia. # Acute on chronic kidney disease: Likely from CHF with poor forward flow but will need to exclude other causes as post-transplant patient. Renal transplant team followed the pt and tacro levels were monitored and adjusted with goal of ___. He was continued on cellcept, and prednisone as well. His Cr was 4.6 at discharge; he will follow up with renal transplant to ensure resolution of his ___. # Hypertension: Normotensive on admission. He was continued on amlodipine, started on metoprolol, and lisinopril held given acute renal failure. # DM2 (last A1c unknown): Continued HISS and lantus TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: -Needs 6-week CXR to ensure resolution of lung opacity -Needs PCP ___ to evaluate anemia -Needs labs drawn this week and renal ___ to ensure further resolution of kidney injury
summary: You had rouble breathing and was transferred to ___ for a cardiac catheterization. The catheterization showed a blockage in your left anterior descending artery and a drug eluting stent was placed in the artery to open the blockage. It is extremely important that you take aspirin and clopidogrel (Plavix) every day without fail for at least a year to keep the stent from clotting off and causing another heart attack. Do not stop taking aspirin and plavix or miss any doses unless Dr. ___ ___ that it is ok. Your heart is weaker after the heart attack and you are now on a new medicine, torsemide, to help keep fluid from building up in your lungs.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o F w/ PMHx AFib, DM, HTN, HLD, GERD, suspected diastolic CHF, CAD, and cholecystectomy presenting with two ___ episodes. ACTIVE ISSUES: ============== # ___ No actual syncopal event. Has symptomatically returned to baseline on presentation to the hospital. General causes considered included orthostatic hypotension as from sitting to rising position with positive orthostatics on admission. Given 1L IVF overnight with improvement and negative orthostatics in the AM. Cardiac causes also considered given known CAD, but EKG stable with no e/o active afib with trops negative x1 and no cardiac sx. Does have prolonged QTc though no significant changes. Also has CHF but does not appear to be volume up from her baseline. Could be ___ given situation occuring while getting up to go to the bathroom per history, but does not have typical presentation with prodrome and not actively micturating to lead to vagal response. Neurologic such as stroke with no focal deficits or BPPV given history that would not be consistent for it. No other clear triggers such as change in medications or infections based on labs. On morning of dispo, pt remained free from symptoms and was ambulating without issues. # Erythematous patch under R breast Appears to be candidal infection, though lacked typical satellite lesions, but defined circular appearance that lacked scale though was from maceration underneath the breast. Differential included intertrigo given location in high moisture bearing areas with high friction. Asymptomatic for patient and recently noticed it presentation. Given clotrimazole cream for application. Recommended f/u with PCP to evaluate for resolution. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ # Atrial Fibrillation Rhythm controlled on amiodarone. On admission, in NSR. Continue amiodarone, dilt, carvedilol and dabigatran. # ___ w/ CAD Does not appear to be volume overloaded based on history or pt report. Appears to be at dry weight ___. No e/o ACS. Continue dilt, carvedilol, Atorvastatin, hydrochlorothiazide, and ASA. # HTN Elevated in setting of not taking cardiac meds, continue home HTN meds with improvement. # Diabetes Stable. Held metformin on HISS but restarted on dispo. # GERD Stable, continued omeprazole # Anemia Stable, continued cyanocobalamin # HL Continued atorvastatin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ====================== - Continue f/u with PCP - ___ for resolution of rash under right breast
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because ___ felt dizzy/lightheaded. The most likely cause were that ___ were a little dehydrated and should make sure to drink plenty of fluids. Also, especially for people with high blood pressure, as they age, ___ may have drops in your blood pressure as ___ stand up too quickly. Please take time as ___ change from a laying down or sitting position to standing. ___ had a rash underneath the right breast that may be a fungal infection. ___ can treat it with the clotrimazole cream. Please ___ with your primary care physicians for it. ___ will likely need physical therapy either at home or as an outpatient to develop better gait and strength. Take care.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ woman with ESRD ___ MGUS and thrombotic microangiopathy s/p DDRT ___, gout, HLD, HTN, arthritis, recurrent left pleural effusion s/p talc pleurodesis, dCHF, post-op a-fib s/p cardioversion, aortic stenosis who presented with 3 weeks of diarrhea, fevers, and malaise and was found to have CMV viremia with colonic biopsy negative for CMV and C difficile. ================== ACTIVE ISSUES: ================== # CMV Viremia: The patient was previously on valganciclovir but it was discontinued due to leukopenia and evidence of negative viral load on ___. However, CMV viral load found to be 26,400 on ___. Ophthalmology confirmed no evidence of CMV retinitis during this admission. She was treated with Ganciclovir 130 mg IV Q12H. She underwent flex sigmoidoscopy, which showed normal colonic mucosa and biopsies that were negative for CMV. She was transitioned to oral Valcyte 450 mg BID, which should be continued until further changes to dose by Infectious Disease. # C. difficile: The patient presented with loose stools. A C. difficile PCR was positive. She was started on vancomycin PO on ___ for a total 14-day course to end with last dose ___. Her diarrhea had resolved by discharge. ================== CHRONIC ISSUES: ================== # ESRD: s/p DDRT ~6 months prior to admission. Continued home prednisone, lamivudine, Bactrim. Decreased tacrolimus from 3 mg BID to 2.5 mg BID. AM tacro level goal trough ___. Decreased MMF from 500 mg BID to ___ mg BID. # CAD: Continued carvedilol, ASA, simvastatin. # Gout: Continued allopurinol. # Hypertension: Continued amlodipine, carvedilol, hydralazine. # Osteoporosis: Bone density scan in ___ at ___, which revealed a T-score straining from -1.6 to -3.1 consistent with osteoporosis. Held alendronate while hospitalized and restarted on discharge. Continued Vitamin D. # Anemia: Stable. # Chronic diastolic congestive heart failure: Held home Lasix in setting of diarrhea. Restarted upon discharge. ===================== TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== # Oral vancomycin should be continued for 14-day total course for C.diff (last dose ___. # Oral Valcyte should be continued for CMV viremia at 450 mg BID unless otherwise instructed by infectious disease at follow-up appointment. # Patient to get CBC, Bun/Cr, CMV viral load for monitoring weekly. She will receive these labs at outpatient renal appt on ___. Subsequently, she will have labs drawn at ___ Vanguard on ___, followed by ID appt on ___. # Decreased tacrolimus to 2.5 mg Q12H based on levels drawn in-house. Decreased Cellcept dosing from 500 mg BID to ___ mg BID per renal team. # Patient had mildly elevated LFTs while in-house. Hepatitis serologies drawn with no change from prior but please follow-up pending hepatitis B viral load given patient was on decreased dose of lamivudine for a few days while in-house. # Discuss timing of flu vaccine at next nephrology appointment. # Code: Full
summary: You were admitted to the hospital due to diarrhea and CMV infection found in the blood. You were found also to have C. difficile infection. You were treated for both with antibiotics. You received a sigmoidoscopy to evaluate for your colon and biopsies were performed. These results revealed that you did not have CMV infection in your GI tract. You have been discharged on oral vancomycin to complete a 14-day total course. Your last dose will be on ___. You will also need to continue on oral Valcyte until you see the infectious disease doctors. ___ have scheduled for you to see the renal doctors ___ ___, where you should get lab tests (CBC, Bun/Cr, CMV viral load). The following week, you should have repeat labs done on ___ at ___ as we discussed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with afib/flutter s/p ablation in ___ and DDD dual chamber pacemaker ___, on coumadin, presents after 2 short-lived episodes of presyncope associated with diaphoresis, likely vasovagal. # presyncope: lasted minutes and were associated with diaphoresis but no chest pain, palpitations or dypsnea. Pt was observed to be atrially paced while on telemetry, and experienced no further episodes of presyncope. Pacemaker was interrogated and revealed no malfunction. No medication changes since pacemaker placement. ACS ruled out with trop neg x 2 and no ischemic changes on EKG. Presyncope was thought to be vasovagal. Pt was instructed to follow up with electrophysiologist Dr. ___ placed her pacemaker. # Afib / Sick sinus syndrome s/p pacemaker on coumadin: INR supratherapeutic at 3.8. Warfarin was held during admission. Pt was instructed to check INR daily at home and call ___ clinic when INR <3.0 for warfarin dosing instructions. Pt was maintained on metoprolol succinate XL 100 mg PO BID, and was discharged on scheduled reduction in amiodarone dose to 200mg daily. # Non-displaced rib fracture: Pt found with subtle nondisplaced fracture of posterior left 11th rib, after direct impact trauma to the area during a mechanical fall on ___. Pt discharged on acetaminophen 1000mg q6h x 7 days for pain control. # CHF (EF 35%): Pt appeared euvolemic during admission and was maintained on furosemide 40 mg daily and irbesartan 150 mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUE - Electrophysiologist to consider possible changes in pacemaker settings by reducing PR interval via RV pacing to optimize ventricular synchronization, vs. maintaining atrial pacing with current prolonged PR interval.
summary: You came in with 2 episodes of lightheadedness. Your description of what happened is very consistent with what we call "vasovagal syncope" - the same thing that happens to people when they faint. We monitored your heart rhythm and saw that the pacemaker was functioning properly. We had the electrophysiologists tweak the pacemaker so that it does the best job to help the way your heart pumps, given your heart failure. You should decrease your amiodarone to 1 pill daily. Your INR was too high. The goal is 2.0-3.0 Don't take your warfarin until your INR is less than 3.0. When your INR is less than 3.

Pt was maintained on metoprolol succinate XL 100 mg PO BID, and was discharged on scheduled reduction in amiodarone dose to 200mg daily. # Non-displaced rib fracture: Pt found with subtle nondisplaced fracture of posterior left 11th rib, after direct impact trauma to the area during a mechanical fall on ___. Pt discharged on acetaminophen 1000mg q6h x 7 days for pain control. # CHF (EF 35%): Pt appeared euvolemic during admission and was maintained on furosemide 40 mg daily and irbesartan 150 mg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUE - Electrophysiologist to consider possible changes in pacemaker settings by reducing PR interval via RV pacing to optimize ventricular synchronization, vs. maintaining atrial pacing with current prolonged PR interval.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # 1. Shortness of breath - (a) Atrial fibrillation: Ms ___ was found to be in atrial fibrillation on presentation. We were unable to find a clear precipitant. Her TSH was normal. The most likely cause for her atrial fibrillation was secondary to worsening hypertension and pulmonary edema over the last several weeks. This is likely because her antihypertensives were recently discontinued following an ___ admission for hypotension. She was previously taking HCTZ, diltiazem and lisinopril, all of which were discontinued. The discontinuation of a thiazide likely led to increasing fluid retention. The discontinuation of diltiazem and lisinopril in combination with HCTZ probably led to increased hypertension along with volume overload, probably precipitating this atrial fibrillation. While in the hospital, she was in atrial fibrillation with RVR with rates of 140-150 ___fter restarting her diltiazem at 90 mg qid, her rates improved to a rate of 60 - 80 bpm. Her CHADS2 score was calculated at 2. Her aspirin dose was increased from 81 mg daily to 325 mg. Given her score > 1, she should be on coumadin however given her history of anemia, this was deferred until after she receives her colonoscopy (see below). She has an appointment with her cardiologist set up next week. . (b) Volume overload: Her JVP was elevated to near her ear which we felt was secondary to her 3+ tricuspid regurgitation rather than florid heart failure. She did have 1+ lower extremity edema. Given her history of orthopnea on presentation, we diuresed her with IV lasix 10 mg X 2 with improvement in her dyspneic sensations and her lower extremity edema. At the time of discharge, we did not feel that she need further diuresis so we did not send her home with lasix. We did also consider the possibility of pulmonary embolus, but we felt this was unlikely given that she was saturating normally on room air. . 2. Hypertension: Her admitting blood pressure was near 150 systolic. While on the increased dose of diltiazem, her blood pressure improved to 120-130 systolic. We dd not restart her lisinopril or her HCTZ at the time of discharge. Her outpatient cardiologist or primary care can restart these as required however this decision should be carefully evaluated given her prior admission for syncope. . 3. Anemia: Consistent w/ iron deficiency per prior labs. Given feelings of malaise and decreased appetite, colonic source is possible. In the past she has refused colonoscopy. We continued her home ferous gluconate and urged her to get a colonoscopy within the next month. Her primary care doctor ___ help set this up. We did not start coumadin but continued her on high dose aspirin given this concern. Her HCTs were stable during hospitalization. . 4. Hypothyroidism - Continued her home levothyroxine. We were concerned that overtreatment could have precipitated her atrial fibrillation but her TSH was within normal ranges at 2.9. . She was discharged home, saturating normally on room air, ambulating without distress, cleared by physical therapy.
summary: You were admitted because you had some difficulty breathing at home and your heart rate was a little fast. When we brought you into the hospital, we found that your heart was beating in an irregular rhythm that we call "atrial fibrillation." Sometimes, this can make it harder for you to breathe because it can cause fluid to build up in your lungs. To treat this, we restarted you on one of your home medications called diltiazem. After restarting this medicine, your heart rate slowed back to normal although it continued to be in atrial fibrillation. Sometimes, this heart rhythm can increase your risk of stroke. For this reason, we started you on a higher dose of aspirin, which you should continue to take daily at home.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with a history of ESRD on HD, hypertensive cardiomyopathy, and DM type 2 admitted to ___ after experiencing acute CP/SOB after a scheduled AV graft angioplasty. . # Chest Pain/Shortness of Breath: Upon admission to the medicine floor, the patient's CP/SOB had resolved. He had received one dose of levofloxacin in the ED for possible PNA, but given his chest xray, clear lung exam, and lack of leukocytosis, fever, or respiratory symptoms this was not continued. Unclear what the etiology of his chest pain was, but it resolved with NTG and ASA. Telemetry monitoring revealed no events, an EKG done on admission showed no acute changes, and his troponins were negative x 3. He received 160 mg of IV Lasix and hemodialysis early on day #1 of hospitalization. After hemodialysis, he no longer had an oxygen requirement and was saturating at 97% on room air, and 93% when ambulating. During this admission, we continued his metoprolol, aspririn, and atorvastatin. He was informed that adhering to a renal, low salt diet is extremely important to prevent further episodes of shortness of breath. He was offered a nutrition consult but stated that he had received education about the proper diet during other hospitalizations. His daughters stated that he is non-compliant with his diet at home. The patient agreed that he often has dietary indiscretions. He was strongly encouraged to avoid high salt food and to limit his fluid intake to ___ ounce glasses of water per day. . # Hyperkalemia: He was noted to have an elevated K of 6.7 on admission. This was found to be lower at 5.7 following hemodialysis. Repeat EKG was without interval change. Renal was contacted and they recommended giving Mr. ___ 30 g prior to discharge. They also said that they would inform Mr. ___ dialysis ___ to use a low potassium bath for his next dialysis session. The patient was also asked to follow a low potassium diet and was given a list of foods to avoid. . # End Stage Renal Disease: Per transplant, the new graft was ok to use for the patient's morning dialysis. He was treated with hemodialysis according to his usual ___ schedule on ___. His calcium acetate and sensipar were continued. . # Dibetes Mellitus Type 2: He was continued on his home regimen of insulin, Glargine 30 units QHS and also placed on an insulin sliding scale. Given the resolution of his shortness of breath and chest pain, rule out for myocardial infarction, resolution of his hyperkalemia, and improved oxygen saturation, he was deemed medically stable and fit for discharge to home on ___. An appointment within one week of discharge was made with his primary care provider, Dr. ___ close ___.
summary: you were admitted to the hospital because of shortness of breath. This was due to fluid in your lungs that resolved with hemodialysis. It is important that at home you follow a low salt diet. This includes foods like canned soups, frozen dinners, and potato chips that contain high salt; your daily sodium consumption should not exceed 2 grams. Further, your potassium was found to be high during this admission. Please avoid foods that are high in potassium such as milk, potatoes, bananas, spinach, prunes, avocado, kale, kiwi, orange juice and grapefruit juice. In addition, you should limit your intake of fluids to no more than ___ ounce glasses of water daily.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is a ___ female with a history of CAD s/p STEMI in ___ with stent to LAD, ___, recently admitted in ___ for gallstone pancreatitis s/p ERCP with stent in CBD, transferred from ___ for this admission for abdominal pain and nausea w/ meals and subjective fevers. OSH imaging demonstrated cholelithiasis and a UTI which was treated with Macrobid. At ___, the patient was admitted to medicine for further workup and cardiology consult for pre-op clearance. CT demonstrated gallbladder distension and wall thickening but no pericholecystic inflammation, possible cholecystitis vs biliary colic. Cholecystectomy was planned following medical optimization and cardiac consultation for surgical risk assessment given patient's recent NSTEMI, reported poor systolic function, and recent DAP therapy for drug eluding stent place approximately one year ago. Patient has history of STEMI in ___, with NSTEMI in ___ during episode of gallstone pancreatitis, for which patient had been intubated and underwent diuresis with lasix gtt. Given intermediate to high risk candidate, patient was risk stratified with cardiology consult. Patient underwent TTE which showed known chronic LV thrombus. Patient was started on heparin gtt prior to surgery, and brilinta was held. The patient underwent an uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy on ___. Her diet was advanced and tolerated afterwards. She also then had her CBD stent removed on ___, no filling defects were seen. Her diet was advanced and tolerated and her anticoagulation was continued with the approval of the ___ team. Per inpatient cardiology and the patient's outpatient cardiologist and PCP, the patient should be on life-long aspirin and warfarin therapy if tolerated, or until repeat TTE with resolution of LV thrombus. Patient has cortical renal disease on imaging, Cr 1.1-1.2 baseline. Creatinine trended pre and post operatively and remained stable. Patient was continued on 3mg prednisone throughout her hospital stay for PMR. The patient is now ready for discharge home with aspirin and warfarin therapy, and ___ services for INR checks. # Choledocholithiasis: Patient was initially admitted with abdominal pain, and mild ___. CT with gall baldder distension and wall thickening but no pericholecystic inflammation, and therefore in clinical picture was not consistent with acute cholesystitis. Patient was evaluated by the ___ ___ and discussed with ___ fellow. Cholecystectomy was planned following medical optimization and cardiac consultation for surgical risk assessment given patient's recent NSTEMI, reported poor systolic function, and recent DAP therapy for drug eluding stent place approximately one year ago. ###Cardiology consult Patient was medically optimized and assess for and plan once medically optimized for lap CCY. # Acute on Chronic Systolic Heart failure, with reduced EF: # LV Thrombus: Patient has history of STEMI in ___, with NSTEMI in ___ during episode of cholecystits, for which patient was intubated, underwent diuresis with lasix gtt. Given intermediate to high risk candidate, patient was risk stratified with cardiology consult. Patient underwent TTE which showed persistent LV thrombus, which was known for the past year since STEMI. Patient was initially placed on heparin gtt prior to surgery, and brillanta was on hold. Patient was then planned to have restart of warfarin without bridge after procedure, with then life-long (if tolerable), aspirin and warfarin, until TTE without significant evidence of LV thrombus. . # Cortical Renal Scarring: patient has cortical renal disease on imaging, Cr 1.1-1.2 baseline. Creatinine trended pre and post operatively. . # PMR: Patient was continued on 3mg prednisone. . # GERD: Patient was continued on home pantopraazole
summary: You were admitted with abdominal pain and nausea with meals. Given your history of coronary artery disease and gallstone pancreatitis, you were initially admitted to the Medicine ___ for workup of abdominal pain and cardiology clearance prior to surgery. Imaging demonstrated likely gallstone disease, and you had an echocardiogram performed that demonstrated a known chronic thrombus in your left heart. Cardiology recommended continuing anticoagulation for this thrombus and you were cleared for surgery. You underwent an uncomplicated laparoscopic cholecystectomy, after which you then had the stent in your common bile duct removed by the Gastroenterologists.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year-old female with a history of a childhood Wilms' tumor s/p nephrectomy, systolic heart failure, diabetes mellitus, and hepatocellular carcinoma s/p multiple TACE most recently ___ who presented from her oncology office with new hyponatremia, hyperkalemia and hyperglycemia, found to have acute kidney injury and transaminitis. She had renal US which was showed no evidence of hydronephrosis or obstruction. Patient's ___ and hyponatremia were thought to be due to overdiuresis from her home torsemide, as she rapidly improved with fluids and cessation of diuretic. Hyperkalemia was thought multifactorial from ___, hyperglycemia, and cessation of diuretic. Patient's home torsemide and Lisinopril were held during admission given ___ and hypovolemia. Lisinopril was restarted on discharge. Patient was noted to be hyperglycemic up to the 600s. This was thought to be due to prednisone administration, which she received a couple days prior to admission for her liver MRI. ___ was consulted for management of her insulin regimen with good control of blood sugars <200. Patient was also found to have a transaminitis in the setting of bilomas seen on liver MRI, which may sometimes be seen after TACE. Interventional radiology felt that no intervention was needed unless patient experienced abdominal pain, fever, or rising leukocytosis. RUQ ultrasound showed expected post procedural changes in the hepatic parenchyma, no intrahepatic biliary ductal dilatation, patent portal vein, and cholelithiasis. Patient remained stable without fever or abdominal pain, and LFTs and leukocytosis downtrended. CHRONIC ISSUES: # Hypertension: continued home carvedilol # Chronic Systolic Heart failure - Lisinopril and torsemide were held. # CAD/PAD - Continue home aspirin and carvedilol # Hypothyroidism: Continued home levothyroxine TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - torsemide and lisinopril were held during admission in setting ___ and hypovolemia. Lisinopril restarted on discharge. Torsemide will need to be restarted as an outpatient - recheck labs at next week appointment (Chem 7 to evaluate electrolytes and Cr) - Patient had transaminitis and liver MRI with bilomas from possible biliary injury. Please continue to trend LFTs and monitor for signs of abdominal pain, fever or leukocytosis. - Please continue to monitor blood sugar. ___ diabetes recommended continuing her home insulin regimen with close endocrinology follow-up. Patient has poorly controlled IDDM with A1c 12.1 this admission. - Patient has systolic heart failure with EF ___ consider ICD placement for primary prevention if EF does not improve with optimal medical management. - Please weigh daily. Call heart failure clinic if weight goes up by more than 3 lbs in 1 day or more than 5 lbs in 3 days. - Discharge weight: 119 kg - Code status: Full - Name of health care proxy: ___ Phone number: ___ Cell phone: ___
summary: You were admitted because you were found to have electrolyte abnormalities, thought to be due from too much fluid output from your torsemide. You were also found to have high blood sugar, possibly from prednisone. We gave you fluids and insulin and you improved. We did not restart you on a diuretic. You were also found to have high liver enzymes. This may sometimes be seen after the liver procedure you had in ___. We touched base with the interventional radiologists who did your procedure, who felt that this was OK as long as you did not experience fever or abdominal pain. At discharge, you weighed 119 lbs. It is very important that you weigh yourself every morning before getting dressed and after going to the bathroom.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ with PMH including castrate-resistant prostate cancer metastatic to bone, AF on Coumadin, CAD CABGx4, chronic back pain s/p laminectomy, and recurrent umbilical hernia, who presented to ___ for first dose of XRT to spine with BRBPR and INR > ___ s/p reversal with vitamin K, currently without rectal bleeding and HDS, transferred to the floor from MICU, without bleeding since transfer, with persistent tachycardia despite increasing doses of metoprolol and diuresis c/f atrial flutter. #Lower GI bleeding: INR >10 on admission, reversed with IV vitamin K. Bleeding stopped relatively quickly. HDS. No transfusions required. Stepped down from the ICU to the floor after 1 day. No bleeding after first ___ days inpatient. #Atrial tachycardia: Anticoagulation: H/o chronic afib, though mostly in atrial tachycardia with variable conduction this admission. Thought to be driver of heart failure exacerbation. TEE/cardioversion performed ___ and was in NSR afterwards. No thrombus seen with TEE. He was restarted on warfarin with Lovenox bridge as he had a high CHADS2VASC score and his risk for thromboembolism was especially high after cardioversion. He was therapeutic for 2 days prior to discharge, and was sent home with instructions to use Lovenox to bridge if his outpatient INR checks were subtherapeutic. His outpatient PCP was contacted and was in agreement with this plan. NOACs were discussed as an option for him, however his PCP said that he had trialed this option previously but costs were prohibitive. Amiodarone was discussed as an option for him but inpatient EP team felt the costs of interaction with warfarin with the patient's labile INRs outweighed the benefits. #Acute on chronic heart failure exacerbation: Fluid overloaded on admission. Initially given 40mg IV Lasix. Urine output was not adequate so Lasix drip started, and given IV 60mg with good output. After good diuresis, patient was euvolemic. He was then started on 60mg PO Lasix daily (home dose was 40mg daily). #Possible TIA: Aphasic episode at home. Inpatient work-up was largely unremarkable. CTA head showed minimal, non-obstructive carotid stenosis. MRA head showed microangiopathic disease but no acute process. See "Atrial tachycardia" section for anticoagulation management. Was continued on aspirin and atorvastatin. #Zoster: Buttocks rash c/f zoster. Was given 7 day course of treatment dose Valtrex. TRANSITION ISSUES ================= **INR/WARFARIN** INR Warfarin dose ___: 1.3 4mg ___: 1.5 6mg ___: 1.6 6mg ___: 1.8 6mg ___: 2.2 6mg ___: 3.0 3mg ___ 3mg ***WILL HAVE INR CHECKED ON ___ AT ___, AND INR FAXED TO ___ ___ [ ] Sent home with lovenox, with instructions to bridge with 80mg q12h if INR less than 2.0. Please ensure that the patient should maintain therapeutic anticoagulation for 1 month after cardioversion to mitigate high risk of stroke ___. [ ] Discharged on 200 mg metoprolol succinate - consider adjusting as patient is now in normal sinus rhythm s/p cardioversion. Required high doses for rate control prior to cardioversion, up to metoprolol tartrate 75 mg Q6H. [ ] Discharged on Lasix PO 60 mg as patient appears to have been going into subacute heart failure on 40 mg PO at home. Please titrate as needed based on volume assessment. [ ] Atorvastatin started (was on pravastatin), thought to be contributing to diarrhea in-house, but should be on high dose statin as had recent TIA. # Code: Presumed full (spoke with patient ___ ___ # CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ Greater than 30 minutes were spent on this patient's discharge day management.
summary: You came in with very thin blood (your INR was high), so we gave you medications to reverse the effect of your warfarin, to make your blood less thin. The bleeding stopped quickly. You had too much fluid on your body, likely caused by an irregular heart rhythm. We gave you IV Lasix, a water pill, to get this fluid off through the urine. After a couple of days your fluid amount was normal. The irregular heart rhythm you were in was likely the cause of the fluid overload in your body. We thought it was the best idea to correct this rhythm to prevent this fluid problem in the future. The cardiologists performed a cardioversion that made your heart rhythm normal again. We adjusted your warfarin dose based on the INR number.

Initially given 40mg IV Lasix. Urine output was not adequate so Lasix drip started, and given IV 60mg with good output. After good diuresis, patient was euvolemic. He was then started on 60mg PO Lasix daily (home dose was 40mg daily). #Possible TIA: Aphasic episode at home. Inpatient work-up was largely unremarkable. CTA head showed minimal, non-obstructive carotid stenosis. MRA head showed microangiopathic disease but no acute process. See "Atrial tachycardia" section for anticoagulation management. Was continued on aspirin and atorvastatin. #Zoster: Buttocks rash c/f zoster. Was given 7 day course of treatment dose Valtrex. TRANSITION ISSUES ================= **INR/WARFARIN** INR Warfarin dose ___: 1.3 4mg ___: 1.5 6mg ___: 1.6 6mg ___: 1.8 6mg ___: 2.2 6mg ___: 3.0 3mg ___ 3mg ***WILL HAVE INR CHECKED ON ___ AT ___, AND INR FAXED TO ___ ___ [ ] Sent home with lovenox, with instructions to bridge with 80mg q12h if INR less than 2.0. Please ensure that the patient should maintain therapeutic anticoagulation for 1 month after cardioversion to mitigate high risk of stroke ___. [ ] Discharged on 200 mg metoprolol succinate - consider adjusting as patient is now in normal sinus rhythm s/p cardioversion. Required high doses for rate control prior to cardioversion, up to metoprolol tartrate 75 mg Q6H. [ ] Discharged on Lasix PO 60 mg as patient appears to have been going into subacute heart failure on 40 mg PO at home. Please titrate as needed based on volume assessment. [ ] Atorvastatin started (was on pravastatin), thought to be contributing to diarrhea in-house, but should be on high dose statin as had recent TIA. # Code: Presumed full (spoke with patient ___ ___ # CONTACT: ___ (wife) ___ Greater than 30 minutes were spent on this patient's discharge day management.

text: Brief Hospital Course: BRIEF SUMMARY ============= Ms. ___ is a ___ year old female with a PMH of hypertension and venous insufficiency who presented to ___ as a transfer after sustaining an NSTEMI while undergoing an exercise stress test at ___. A coronary angiogram was performed, which revealed a 90% proximal stenosis of the LAD, which was corrected with a DES. An ehcocardiogram showed an EF of 25% and severe anterior and septal wall motion abnormalities (WMA) and extensive apical akinesis, necessitating anticoagulation for at least 6 months. For her stent she was placed on aspirin and Plavix, and for her WMA she was placed on warfarin with Lovenox bridging. She was discharged to follow up with a cardiologist and her primary care physician ___ also developed nosebleed day of discharge, with packing done by ENT. ACUTE ISSUES ============ # NSTEMI ___ presented to ___ with several weeks progressive exertional symptoms and intermittent chest pains, with stress testing demonstrating an EF of 25% ischemic changes on exercise. Her troponin Ts were subsequently elevated, and she was transferred to ___ for coronary angiogram. She was started on a heparin drip and taken for a cath on ___, which demonstrated a proximal LAD lesion of 90%. A stent was placed with reduction in obstruction to 0%, and she was started on clopidogrel 75 mg daily, aspirin 81 mg daily,and atorvastatin 80 mg daily. # systolic CHF, unknown chronicity: # Anterior wall akinesis: ___ noted as above to have an EF 25% on stress testing prior to transfer to ___. ___ presented with an elevated BNP of 5000, and had crackles, JVD, and trace peripheral edema. Her systolic CHF is likely to be ischemic secondary to the 90% LAD lesion noted above. A repeat TTE demonstrated significant anteroseptal wall hypokinesis and extensive apical akinesis, necessitating anticoagulation for at least 6 months. She was started on warfarin with a Lovenox bridge, and will have her anticoagulation managed at ___. Discharge INR 1.5. Given her severely reduced EF, she was discharged with a LifeVest for arrhythmia protection. She was intermittently diuresed with IV Lasix during her course, and achieved euvolemia. Discharge medications include lisinopril 20 mg daily and carvedilol 6.25 mg BID. She will need a repeat TTE ___ months after discharge to assess for cardiac recovery. #Epistaxis: On the day of discharge, the ___ developed epistaxis requiring packing by ENT. She was given instructions to follow up with ENT within 5 days of discharge. # Hyponatremia: Likely hypervolemic hyponatremia in the setting of her CHF. It remained stable during her course. CHRONIC ISSUES ============== # Hypertension: The ___ was discharged on lisinopril 20 mg daily and carvedilol 6.25 mg BID. We discontinued her HCTZ # Insomnia - Continued trazodone 150 mg daily # Eczema Dermatitis: - lotions PRN. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== -The ___ will require lifelong therapy with aspirin 81 mg daily, and will require Plavix 75 mg daily for at least 12 months -The ___ was noted to have significant anteroseptal wall hypokinesis and extensive apical akinesis, and will require anticoagulation for at least 3 months. She was started on warfarin with a Lovenox bridge. She needs to have a repeat echocardiogram in 1 month. -The ___ anticoagulation will be ultimately managed at ___ with the exception of INR on ___, which PCP ___ manage. -Discharge INR 1.5. She will need her INR drawn on ___. Results will be faxed to PCP. -The ___ lisinopril dose was increased to 20 mg daily -The ___ was started on atorvastatin 80 mg daily and carvedilol 6.25 mg BID -The ___ should not be restarted on HCTZ. -The ___ will need a repeat TTE in ___ months after discharge -___ should have ENT follow-up within 5 days of discharge. She is to remain on Keflex prophylaxis until packing removed - ___ will be discharged with LifeVest Discharge weight: 77.4kg
summary: You were admitted to the hospital after you experienced a heart attack during an exercise stress test. You were put on blood thinners and taken for a heart catheterization, where they found a 90% occlusion of one of your main heart arteries. A stent was placed to treat this blockage, and you were put on two important medications called aspirin and Plavix (clopidogrel). These medications must be taken every single day without fail, or there is a chance that the stent will develop a blockage that could result in a large heart attack. An ultrasound of your heart (echocardiogram) showed a significant weakening of your heart. Because part of your heart was not contracting the way it should, you will require additional blood thinners to prevent clots from forming in your heart which could lead to stroke. You were started on a drug called Coumadin (warfarin), and will need to get your Coumadin level (INR) checked frequently at a ___. Because it takes a while before the Coumadin level is high enough, we discharged you on an injectable blood thinner called Lovenox (enoxaparin) to thin your blood until your Coumadin is effective.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ myelofibrosis (dx ___, grade ___ fibrosis, JAK2+) s/p ruxolitinib (stopped ___, h/o stage I NSCLC, nephrotic syndrome, and HFpEF who initially presented with fever. Now stable for discharge and afebrile. Patient was persistently hypertensive throughout hospitalization, but this improved with medication changes. ACUTE ISSUES: ============= #Leukocytosis: Likely ___ underlying end stage myelofibrosis, given elevated monocytes and worsening hepatosplenomegaly. Patient underwent full infectious workup including BCx, UCx, stool studies, and sputum studies, which all returned negative. The patient received one day of Zosyn and remained afebrile for the remainder of her hospitalization. #Myelofibrosis with anemia/thrombocytopenia: Patient is clinically worsening with increased hepatosplenomegaly ___ extramedullary hematopoiesis. U/S of spleen revealed progressive splenic enlargement to 24 cm (from 21 cm). Patient was symptomatically stable, however she likely continues to have a worsening disease process. No evidence of transformation to acute leukemia. #Hypertension: Patient had persistently elevated BPs since admission, more so in the AM, with associated holocranial headaches/neck pain. Her BP ranged between 130/80 to 180/100. Although her Isosorbide mononitrate was uptitrated, her BP remained high and she was started on hydralazine (titrated up to 20mg q6h) and amlodipine (5mg daily). Her home torsemide was increased as well (to 140mg daily), and she was continued on her previous home losartan (50mg daily) and carvedilol (25mg BID). This led to improved blood pressure control and patient remained in the 140s-150s/80s by discharge. #Goals of Care: Family and patient, along with inpatient treatment team had a meeting to discuss future dispo and goals of care planning. She is interested in bridge to hospice/palliative care and decided to be DNR/DNI. ___ evaluated the pt and stated she is safe to go home and recommend home ___. #Neck pain: Likely MSK, though could not rule out occult infection or other process. X-ray c-spine revealed some degenerative changes but no bony cancerous lesions. Patient received lidocaine patch for pain and will likely need an MRI of her neck outpatient to complete her evaluation. CHRONIC ISSUES: =============== #CKD Stage IV with nephrotic syndrome: Pt's Cr has increased from baseline of 4.0 to 5.2 on discharge. She was being diuresed with torsemide 140mg daily. Patient does not want dialysis. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== CODE STATUS: DNR/DNI CONTACT INFO: Proxy name: ___ ___: daughter Phone: ___ Date on form: ___ Filed on date: ___ Proxy form in chart?: ___ Comments: Husband ___ is secondary HCP # ___ DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 81.97 kg (180.71 lb) [] Consider ordering an MRI of her neck outpatient if neck pain continues [] Patient had family meeting and was amenable to MOLST form; this should be filled out by PCP at her next appointment [] Will need regular BP monitoring for continued titration of BP meds [] Continue to monitor serum Cr, daily weights, I/Os as pt was initially volume overload [] Check in with how patient is doing emotionally, and ensure that ___ is coming regularly and that she is comfortable at home
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were having fevers. We tested your blood, urine, saliva, stool, and you got a chest x-ray and we could not find a source of infection. You also had a mild increase in your white blood cells. All of this could be due to your myelofibrosis. You received 1 day of antibiotics and your fever went away. You started to feel better throughout your hospital stay, and you may have had a mild viral illness. Your blood pressure was also high during your stay and you had neck pain. We added a some new medications to help and your blood pressure improved. You also had more fluid in your body and we gave you a water pill to remove it. Finally, we discussed your goals for your care. We had a meeting with your family and determined the best way to go forward, by changing the focus of your health to improving the quality of your life.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with HTN, DM2 x ___ years, ESRD, diastolic CHF, cataracts, anemia presents symptoms consistent with uremia for initiation of dialysis. # Uremia/ESRD. Symptoms of fatigue, N/V, metallic taste, hiccups are all consistent with uremia. Patient's PD catheter was placed on ___ and was mature for use. Symptoms improved with PD. Given high calcium levels, calcium carbonate, calcium acetate, and vitamin D d/c'ed. Started on Sevelamer. Started Nephrocaps. Patient's PD output initially low due to poor bowel movements. Started on aggressive bowel regimen with Colase, Senna, Miralax, Lactulose TID, and Bisacodyl # Supine hypertension and orthostatic hypotension. Patient describes new orthostatic hypotension symptoms since admission. Though this is likely secondary to long standing diabetes with neuropathy. Other etiologies including HIV, hypothyroidism, and multiple myeloma which were ruled out. Orthostasis has been very difficult to manage and patient has narrow range of blood pressure she tolerates. She tends to get symptomatic if SBP < 130 or > 170. All home antihypertensives discontinued. Restarted Valsartan 80mg QHS. Compression stocking when sitting and standing and asking patient to sleep at a 45 degree angle had minimal effect. Neurology consulted and recommended Midodrine 5mg TID (to be given only between 6AM and 7PM) which resolved orthostatic hypotension. # chronic Diastolyic CHF. Given orthostatic hypotension and initiation of PD, beta-blocker, furosemide, and nitrate were discontinued. Valsartan restarted. # Type 2 DM Uncontrollred with complications: Continued on ISS. Decreased Lantus from 25 units to 20units after PD started. # Anemia of CKD: likely ___ ESRD. Continued Ferrous Sulfate 325mg PO daily TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Discharge to rehab - Continue PD per renal recs - Continue Midodrine 5mg TID during the day. Hold between hours of 7PM and 6AM and sit for at least 4 hours after dose. - Continue to monitor orthostatic hypotension - f/u with renal and neuro/autonomic
summary: You were admitted to start peritoneal dialysis given your symptoms of fatigue, nausea, and hiccups. We began the dialysis through your peritoneal dialysis catheter after consulting with your transplant nephrology team, and you tolerated it well. Your symptoms improved with dialysis. During this hospital stay you experienced dizziness when you sat up or stood. This is called orthostatic hypotension and is likely secondary to your underlying diabetes. You responded well to the Midodrine we started you on. Please make an appointment with the ___, and nephrology. Please also make an appointment neurology/autonomics.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ hx HL and NSVT on stress test p/w DOE to CDAC, found with new systolic heart failure (EF 26%), s/p cor angio with 1v disease, now admitted with new non-ischemic cardiomyopathy, possibly d/t tachycaria myopathy. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] Discharged with 20 po Lasix prn for weight gain. Monitor volume status as outpatient [ ] Should get CMR as outpatient to rule out infiltrative cardiomyopathy. If MRI suggestive, consider cardiac PET and referral to CHF (Dr. ___ [ ] 14 Day Ziopatch placed on discharge to evaluate SVT/PVC burden. If MRI to be performed earlier, ZioPatch will have to be removed. [ ] Patient found to have borderline A1c to 6.4%. Counseled on weight loss/dietary strategies. Recheck in 3 months and consider initiating Metformin as outpatient. [ ] Uptitrate Valsartan as tolerated as outpatient. Consider transitioning to Entresto. [ ] Consider checking SPEP/UPEP [ ] Ensure age-appropriate cancer-screening [ ] Check Hepatitis B panel as outpatient. Ordered as inpatient but did no get sent. [ ] Repeat TTE in ___ months DISCHARGE WEIGHT: 75.66 kg, 166.8 lbs DISCHARGE CREATININE: 1.2 DISCHARGE DIURETIC: Lasix 20mg prn # CORONARIES: 70% stenosis in proximal LAD, FFR negative. # PUMP: 26% # RHYTHM: Sinus with intermittent SVT and PVCs # Non-ischemic cardiomyopathy: # Likely tachycardiomyopathy # Acute on chronic HFrEF exacerbation (EF 26%): Presented with shortness of breath, found with volume overload (elevated BNP, JVP, ___ edema) and reduced ejection fraction 26% with global hypokinesis. New diagnosis of nonischemic cardiomyopathy with reduced ejection fraction given relatively unremarkable coronary angiogram showing single-vessel disease with LAD 70% but FFR negative and no RWMA. No history of autoimmune, thyroid (TSH normal), substance use, connective tissue disease. Also no recent infections. Does note 15 pound unintentional weight loss. Hep C neg. Hep B ordered, but did not get sent. Iron studies wnl. Serum tox neg. Given large negative workup thus far, most likely explanation is CM iso runs of SVT. Should have CMR to evaluate for infiltrative diseases and possibly cardiac PET if indicated. He received 20 IV Lasix x2 in CDAC and diuresis subsequently held given euvolemia. Discharged on Lasix 20mg prn. Home atenolol switched to Metoprolol. Started on Valsartan in preparation for potential transition to Entresto in future. Monitored on telemetery and discontinued with Zio Patch to quantify arrhythmia burden. # CAD: 70% stenotic lesion in LAD w negative functional tests. Started on Aspirin 81, Metoprolol succinate as above. Transitioned to high-intensity statin as below. #Hyperlipidemia: Changed pravastatin 40 to high intensity rosuvastatin 40 mg as per above. # SVT, nonsustained # Hx ventricular ectopy: Patient had multiple episodes of both SVT (nonsustained and asymptomatic), as well as ventricular ectopy with frequent PVCs, which was also asymptomatic. It is likely that this contributed to newly reduced EF as per above. Discharged with a ZIO Patch as per above. Metoprolol started as per above. # ___: (resolved) Presented with Cr 1.3 (from ___ 1.1-1.2), improved with diuresis to baseline on discharge. # Mild thrombocytopenia: No evidence of concomitant anemia. Has history of mild thrombocytopenia, previously as low as 100. No evidence of hemolytic anemia. # Weight loss: Notes unintentional 50 pound weight loss in the past month. This is unusual in the setting of heart failure, which we would see increased weight gain. Last colonoscopy ___ was normal. With admission 176 pounds, now down 8 pounds after diuresis at 168 pounds. He states his dry weight prior to admission was around 167 pounds. -Consider malignancy work-up if other localizing symptoms #Prostate cancer: Would follow-up with outpatient urologist, especially in setting of recent weight loss. He has outpatient urology follow-up.
summary: You presented with shortness of breath over the last two weeks. A stress test was done, which showed concern for a heart blockage. You were admitted for evaluation. You were found to be fluid volume overloaded and given IV diuretics (Lasix) to help you urinate off fluid. An echocardiogram (ultrasound of the heart) showed a decrease in your hearts pump function. A heart catheterization was performed, which showed a partial blockage of one of the vessels, however it was not significant enough to explain the decrease in heart pump function. You were started on new medications to optimize your heart pump function, including valsartan and metoprolol.

text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old male with a history of CAD s/p MI in ___, CHF with EF 35%, ICD placed in ___ prophylactically but with known NSVT in ___, who presents with syncope. Problem 1) Syncope The patient's first troponin and CK-MB were negative. He had no acute ST/T changes on his EKG. His orthostatics were negative. His ICD was interrogated and no tachyarrhythmias were uncovered. His pacer had paced less than 0.01% of the time, indicating that a bradyarrhythmia was very unlikely. The patient likely had an episode of neurocardiogenic syncope secondary to the heat and standing for a long period. This was a clinical diagnosis based on the history and a diagnosis of exclusion. Problem 2) CAD/Chronic CHF The patient was continued on his home medications, including rosuvastatin, aspirin, carvedilol, lisinopril, and a lipid panel was ordered (pending). Problem 3) Anemia: iron studies, B12 and folic acid levels were sent (pending). Problem 4) Prostate Cancer, diagnosed ___. ___ 7. The patient was continued on his home tamsulosin, and will followup as an outpatient. The patient was given subcutaneous heparin for DVT prophylaxis.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had an episode of syncope (you fainted) while at your ___ barbeque. Because of your history of heart disease, this was quite concerning and you were evaluated in the hospital for cardiac causes of your fainting. Your defibrillator was interrogated which found no evidence of an acute cardiac event. On further questioning you told us that you had an identical event like this when you had your blood drawn previously. Given your story, this event was most likely caused by what we call vaso-vagal syncope, which is simply fainting and is not life-threatening.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: The patient is a ___ year old male with a history of CAD s/p MI in ___, CHF with EF 35%, ICD placed in ___ prophylactically but with known NSVT in ___, who presents with syncope. Problem 1) Syncope The patient's first troponin and CK-MB were negative. He had no acute ST/T changes on his EKG. His orthostatics were negative. His ICD was interrogated and no tachyarrhythmias were uncovered. His pacer had paced less than 0.01% of the time, indicating that a bradyarrhythmia was very unlikely. The patient likely had an episode of neurocardiogenic syncope secondary to the heat and standing for a long period. This was a clinical diagnosis based on the history and a diagnosis of exclusion. Problem 2) CAD/Chronic CHF The patient was continued on his home medications, including rosuvastatin, aspirin, carvedilol, lisinopril, and a lipid panel was ordered (pending). Problem 3) Anemia: iron studies, B12 and folic acid levels were sent (pending). Problem 4) Prostate Cancer, diagnosed ___. ___ 7. The patient was continued on his home tamsulosin, and will followup as an outpatient. The patient was given subcutaneous heparin for DVT prophylaxis.

text: Brief Hospital Course: # Acute on chronic systolic CHF exacerbation: Pt with weight gain, SOB, ___ edema. He was given a 120 mg bolus of IV furosemide on admission and was started on a furosemide gtt (10 mg/hr). He is approximately 2 L negative over night. His lactate was mildly elevated on admission and will be trended this morning. Dry weight 115 kg. He was treated with a furosemide gtt 10 mg/hr and metolazone 2.5 mg daily initially, but this was stopped on ___ in the setting of a rising lactate. He also continued spironolactone and ramipril. His BBlocker was initially held in the setting of an elevated lactate. Patient was diuresed with bolus doses of IV Lasix (ultimately with sufficient urine output to 160mg IV Lasix), with electrolytes monitored during active diuresis. Afterload was reduced by further uptitration of home ramipril dose from 1.25mg daily to 1.25mg BID. He was restarted on his home torsemide dose of 60mg BID, and was monitored for fluid status. The patient remained net even to slightly negative on Torsemide 60mg BID, and as a result he was not restarted on his home metolazone while in-house, and was not instructed to restart this medication after discharge. # Paroxysmal Atrial Fibrillation: INR on presentation supratherapeutic at 3.9. Rates controlled. Warfarin initially held and INR trended daily. The INR goal is ___. When patient's INR became therapeutic at 2.6, and at that time, his home dose of warfarin was initiated at 2.5mg daily. The patient's INR became subtherapeutic on ___ at 1.7, for which he was initiated on a Lovenox bridge, and his warfarin dose was increased to 3mg daily. He was discharged on Lovenox ___ and warfarin 3mg daily, and will continue to use Lovenox until his INR returns to therapeutic range. # The patient was observed to have several prolonged runs of VTach (approx. 30 seconds x1 episode, and ___ seconds on 2 other occasions). The patient's home metoprolol (fractionated to tartrate) was resumed at that time as the patient was no longer in decompensated heart failure and his lactate had normalized. Metoprolol was increased to XL 37.5mg at the time of discharge for a brief run of VT on ___ while on metoprolol XL 25mg. # Acute Kidney Injury: Baseline Cr 1.1. Cr on admission 1.3. Likely cardiorenal, given overall picture of acute on chronic CHF. He was diuresed as above, his Cr was trended, and his medications were renally dosed. Cr gradually downtrended with further diuresis, and improvement in lactate. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== Discharge Weight: 118.1kg Discharge Creatinine: 1.3 - Please check INR and chem7 at PCP follow up. # Acute on Chronic Heart Failure (Systolic and Diastolic): Mixed etiology, including tachycardia vs EtOH vs viral. LVEF = 40%, with mild symmetric LVH, and regional LV systolic dysfunction (inferior and inferolateral hypokinesis). Patient intermittently maintained on IV Lasix boluses and gtt's and was diuresed to euvolemia. - Cardiac Medications: Aspirin 81mg daily, Torsemide 60mg BID, Pravastatin 40mg QHS, Ramipril 1.25mg BID, Spironolactone 25mg daily, Warfarin 3.5mg daily - Patient will require repeat BMP at the time of PCP ___ to ensure stable renal function and electrolytes # Supratherapeutic INR: Patient admitted with supratherapeutic INR to 3.9 on admission; warfarin was held. INR downtrended to subtherapeutic range on ___ for which he was bridged with Lovenox and given increased dose of warfarin. Patient was restarted on 3mg daily. - Warfarin and INR followed by Dr. ___ - ___ re-checking INR at the time of ___ with PCP (___) # Diabetes: Patient discharged on home regimen of NPH 40 units with breakfast and Metformin 1000mg daily. # Medication Changes: Ramipril increased to 1.25mg BID, Colchicine 0.6mg every other day discontinued (patient without symptoms and did not require while in-house). Metoprolol XL was increased to 37.5mg due to NSVT. # Code: Full (Confirmed) # Contact: ___ (___)
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because of shortness of breath, and were found to have excess fluid in your lungs which made it hard to breathe. You were started on medications to help rid your body of excess fluid by causing you to urinate more. Your INR was not therapeutic upon discharge. Because you are at a high risk of stroke, we will be discharging you with lovenox, which will protect you while your INR is low. You should continue this until your INR is therapeutic. In the meantime, we have adjusted your warfarin dose for now so that you do not become supratherapeutic again. Please speak with your physician prior to making any changes.

text: Brief Hospital Course: SUMMARY FOR ADMISSION: ====================== Mr. ___ is an ___ w/ heart failure (recovered LVEF 30->60%, atrial fibrillation, HTN, & newly diagnosed liver dysfunction, who was referred to ___ for evaluation of increasing ascites and body edema. ISSUES: ======== # Acute on chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction: # Cardiac amyloidosis: Patient presented from clinic with new large volume ascites and lower extremity edema. A diagnostic paracentesis demonstrated a SAAG of 1.8 & total protein of 2.5 suggesting portal hypertension of cardiac origin. Patient was also found to have an elevated proBNP of >9500. Patient underwent ~4L paracentesis ___ and was diuresed daily with furosemide 80 mg IV BID. He had a diuresis of >26L & 40-pounds. A TTE on admission demonstrated concern for infiltrative disease. Fat pad biopsy was negative for amyloid deposition. Pyrophosphate nuclear medicine scan however was consistent with wild-type amyloidosis confirming a diagnosis of cardiac amyloidosis. He will be managed with diuresis and will be discharged on torsemide 60mg (on day prior to discharge, on torsemide 40 mg had UOP ~1L after receiving dose; average daily intake ~1 to 1.2 L). He has follow-up with ___ HF and has a Cardiologist in ___ who can also follow with. # Cirrhosis: Patient was noted to have a MELD-Na 20 on admission with a recent diagnosis of cirrhosis of unclear etiology on outside CT. Patient was noted to have large volume ascites and portal hypertension with largely intact synthetic function. Extensive work-up was negative and leading differential is cardiac cirrhosis, NASH, or ETOH-related. Quite possibly a combination of all is the etiology. He had no liver-related combinations on admission and has scheduled Hepatology follow-up at ___. # Chronic Kidney Disease: Unclear etiology, possibly cardiorenal. Creatinine 1.9 on admission and this was thought to be perhaps close to his baseline. He was monitored w/ diuresis with improvement in creatinine to 1.4 on discharge. # Atrial fibrillation: In NSR entire hospitalization. His metoprolol was held once a diagnosis of cardiac amyloidosis was made. He was admitted on rivaroxaban which was switched to apixaban given renal function and age. # Anemia: Patient was noted to be anemic on presentation but his baseline hemoglobin levels were unknown. There are no overt signs of bleeding during his hospital stay. Anemia workup revealed a hypoproliferative state with low reticulocyte count. Patient was also noted to be iron deficient. Patient's CBC were trended daily and he was transfused with IV iron to decrease his iron deficit. # Hypothyroidism: His home levothyroxine was continued. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES =================== [] Discharge weight: 78.16 kg (172.31 lb) [] Discharge creatinine: 1.4 [] Discharge diuretic: torsemide 60mg [] Please recheck chemistries at ___ follow up [] Spironolactone decreased from home dose of 25mg to 12.5mg due to low blood pressure (systolic 100s) and can be uptitrated as tolerated. [] Home rivaroxaban switched to apixaban 2.5 mg BID for renal function and age [] The patient has a mildly dilated ascending aorta. Based on ___ ACCF/AHA Thoracic Aortic Guidelines, if not previously known or a change, a follow-up echocardiogram is suggested in ___ year; if previously known and stable, a follow-up echocardiogram is suggested in ___ years. # CODE: FULL, confirmed # CONTACT: ___ ___
summary: You came to the hospital because you had swelling all over caused by build up of fluid. You had fluid removed from your abdomen. You were given medications to help you urinate all of that fluid off your body. You had a biopsy from your abdomen which showed that you have a disease called "amyloidosis." This is a disease in which protein accumulates around your body. This was causing your heart to not pump as good as it could which was causing fluid to buildup. You were evaluated by the liver doctors because your liver does not work as good as it should. This is probably because of the fluid back up that your heart was causing.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ is an ___ with a history of CKD, severe MR, and CAD s/p PCI ___ (DES to LCx) who presents with chest pressure, palpitations, and lightheadedness which has now resolved, developed new SOB and evidence of volume overload on CXR which has now improved. # Chest pressure # Palpitations Patient presented with mild chest pressure/tightness and irregular heart rate. She was started on a heparin drip the night of her admission. Troponin was 0.06, which is actually below recent baseline, ECG with mild ST depressions in V3/V4 that resolved on repeat. Trop downtrended to 0.05 so her heparin drip was discontinued. Telemetry showed multiple PACs so patient's home metoprolol was increased from 12.5mg to 50mg. After this change, telemetry showed resolution of her PACs. She was continued on her home ASA/Plavix and imdur. # SOB # Flash pulmonary edema Patient with new-onset SOB worse with laying flat. CXR ___ with new pleural effusions and worsening pulmonary vascular congestion. Was given 500cc IVF ___ for dizziness, likely had flash pulmonary edema from IVF in the setting of severe MR. ___ with IV lasix with improvement in her symptoms and exam. # Urinary discomfort Patient reported discomfort upon urinary stream initiation. UA with few bacteria, 3 WBCs, trace leuks. Started on Bactrim on ___ but urine cx now shows mixed flora consistent with contamination so Bactrim was discontinued on ___. # Hypernatremia Na 148 on presentation. Resolved with IVF. CHRONIC/STABLE ISSUES: ====================== # Severe mitral regurgitation Per patient, OP cardiology is planning for repeat echocardiogram 1 month after PCI to re-evaluate for improvement in MR, and potential need for evaluation for valve replacement. She was treated with IV lasix per above during admission. # CKD Baseline Cr 1.7-1.9. Remained at her baseline during admission. # CAD s/p PCI ___ (DES to LCx) Continued home clopidogrel and imdur. Increased metoprolol to 50mg daily, per above. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== [ ] Changed medications: Metoprolol Succinate XL 12.5mg PO daily changed to 50mg PO daily [ ] Patient should have complete metabolic panel drawn within 1 week. She was treated with IV lasix while in the hospital and should follow up to ensure her electrolytes are within normal limits. [ ] Patient should follow up with her primary care doctor to discuss whether imdur is still needed Transitional issues from recent hospitalization in ___: [ ] Repeat ECHO 1 month after PCI [ ] Started on DAPT - length of DAPT treatment should be discussed with the patient in the outpatient setting. [ ] Consider ACE inhibitor - patient should be on an ACE inhibitor give her CHF and CAD s/p stenting. At the time of discharge, however, patient's BP were soft and her Cr had just stabilized from ___ on CKD. Initiation of ACE inhibitor should be considered in the outpatient setting. Discharge time 35 min
summary: You were admitted because you were having chest pressure and felt like your heart was beating irregularly. You had an ECG done which showed that your heart was beating too fast. We increased the dose of your home medication, metoprolol, to help slow your heart rate down. This caused you to stop feeling like your heart was beating irregularly. While you were here, you started feeling short of breath. We did a chest X-ray which showed that your lungs had a lot of fluid in and around them. We treated you with a medication called Lasix that helped remove some of the fluid in and around your lungs, and your breathing subsequently improved.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with history of CKD, sCHF (EF35%), CAD, HTN, HL, hypothyroidism presents s/p mechanical fall found to have fracture of left distal radial fracture # S/p Fall: Mechanical in nature, though could have been caused by weakness from UTI. No arrythmias on tele. #. Fracture of left distal radius - patient is s/p fall. She was evaluated by orthopedic surgery who performed reduction of the radius and splinted the arm. Patient will need to follow up in orthopedic trauma clinic in one week for re-evaluation. Patient was evaluated by ___ and OT during this hospitalization who recommended a short stay at a rehabilitation facility. Pain was well controlled using tylenol ___ mg TID. Patient started on calcium and vitamin D. #. UTI - patient was found to have UTI on urinalysis. She had been largely asymptomatic. She was treated with 3 days of ciprofloxacin on this admission. #. CKD - patient with known chronic renal insufficiency with baseline creatinine 1.8-2.0. Urine lytes were sent which show FENA of 0.9%, suggesting prerenal etiology. Patient was encouraged to take PO fluids. Her home lasix was continued as patient's daughter reports that she easily tips into CHF exacerbation without it. Lisinopril was held on account of the elevated creatinine. Patient will need to have chem 7 rechecked at rehab and creatinine followed. #. HTN - lisinopril held because of elevated creatinine. Patient was continued on metoprolol. SBP ranged 130-150s on this admission. Reinitiation of lisinopril should be considered if creatinine returns to her previous baseline #. Hypothyroidism - patient was continued home dose of levothyroxine #. Systolic CHF - has an EF 35%. She was continued on metoprolol and furosemide per home regimen. Lisinopril was held for elevated creatinine, but should be restarted when creatinine returns to baseline. #. GERD - continued on ranitidine #. CAD - continued on plavix and aspirin ----- Transitional Issues: # CKD - please encourage PO fluid intake - please check chem 7 on ___ for reevaluation of creatinine. If back to baseline and if BP can tolerate it, please restart lisinopril # Fracture - please call and make a follow up appointment for her in ___ trauma clinic). His office number is ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospitalized because you broke your wrist after you fell. The orthopedic surgeons were called to see you and they fixed your wrist and put a cast on it. In the emergency department, you were also found to have urinary tract infection. We treated this with three days of antibiotics while you were in the hospital. Your blood work showed that your kidneys were not functioning quite as well as it had been in the past. This is most likely due to the urinary tract infection, but it also suggests that you have been more dehydrated. Please try and keep yourself adequated hydrated. We will have the rehab facility continue to watch your kidney functions to make sure they they improve.
