text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with h/o CHF, restricted pulmonary disease, Pott's disease with severe kyphosis admitted for L mid-back and flank pain. # L mid-back and flank pain: no fevers/chills or leukocytosis and affected area with no erythema/warmth/swelling, making an infectious process like osteomyelitis/cellulitis unlikely. Pain disappears when laying down flat on bed and worsens with movement, which makes a musculoskeletal cause more likely especially given his distorted anatomy from Pott's disase. The pain also has nearly resolved on the day of discharge without any interventions. Spinal and rib x-rays show no acute changes from x-rays in ___. Recent renal ultrasound on ___ demonstrated no hydronephrosis or (only a simple cyst on R kidney). CT abdomen without contrast with no evidence of nephrolithiasis or large masses like an aneurysm. CXR with no definite focal consolidation. Troponin was negative. LDH was elevated at 268, but lab noted that the blood specimen was slightly hemolyzed and that can falsely elevate LDH. # Back neck pain: reports that pain is chronic and started one year ago, but usually does not take any medications for it. On x-ray studies, there are moderetely severe degenerative changes involving the cervical spine at C5/6 that has been present since ___. # ___: increased creatinine of 1.9 from baseline of 1.1 during admission and lisinopril and metformin were held. On day of discharge, creatinine resolved and was back at baseline. # mild anemia: patient with a drop of hematocrit from 40 at baseline to 34-35. Patient has a history of hemorrhoids and gastritis, but denies recent bloody stools, BRBPR, melena, hematemesis, hemoptysis. Iron studies were fairly normal and vitb12 and folate were normal. There is no sign of hemolysis - tbili normal, hapto is elevated, retic count normal. Last colonoscopy on ___ was a virtual colonoscopy that showed no evidence for significant polyps or masses greater than 5 mm. # CHRONIC ISSUES: -CHF: appears euvolemic on exam. CXR with probable mild interstitial pulmonary edema, but on lung exam crackles sound mostly dried. Last echo was in ___ showed ___ with EF > 55%. He was continued on his home diuretic. -restrictive lung disease: ___ to pott's disease, obesity, OSA. Continued on 4L O2 nasal cannula -DM: continued on glipizide. Patient was kept on FSS and sliding scale while hospitalized -gout: stable, no flare for last ___ years, continued on home allopurinol -HTN: continued on home atenolol -OSA: continued on biPAP -hyperlipidemia: continued on home simvastatin # TRANSITIONAL ISSUES -Patient desaturated to 84% on ambulation and stated that this was his baseline. Please recheck O2 sats when ambulating and increase oxygen to ___ as needed to have saturations >90% -please recheck LDH and hematocrit
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for back pain. Images of the bones in your neck, back, and ribs did not show any new fractures. Your pain resolved, making us think it was likely related to muscle tension. The pain in the back of your neck is due to degenerative changes in the bones at the back of your neck that has been present for some years. Your kidney function was decreased on admission as well but this improved on its own, suggesting it might have been related to the ibuprofen you were taking for pain. Please avoid ibuprofen and instead try tylenol and warm packs if your back or neck pain returns.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M with h/o CHF, restricted pulmonary disease, Pott's disease with severe kyphosis admitted for L mid-back and flank pain. # L mid-back and flank pain: no fevers/chills or leukocytosis and affected area with no erythema/warmth/swelling, making an infectious process like osteomyelitis/cellulitis unlikely. Pain disappears when laying down flat on bed and worsens with movement, which makes a musculoskeletal cause more likely especially given his distorted anatomy from Pott's disase. The pain also has nearly resolved on the day of discharge without any interventions. Spinal and rib x-rays show no acute changes from x-rays in ___. Recent renal ultrasound on ___ demonstrated no hydronephrosis or (only a simple cyst on R kidney). CT abdomen without contrast with no evidence of nephrolithiasis or large masses like an aneurysm. CXR with no definite focal consolidation. Troponin was negative. LDH was elevated at 268, but lab noted that the blood specimen was slightly hemolyzed and that can falsely elevate LDH. # Back neck pain: reports that pain is chronic and started one year ago, but usually does not take any medications for it. On x-ray studies, there are moderetely severe degenerative changes involving the cervical spine at C5/6 that has been present since ___. # ___: increased creatinine of 1.9 from baseline of 1.1 during admission and lisinopril and metformin were held. On day of discharge, creatinine resolved and was back at baseline. # mild anemia: patient with a drop of hematocrit from 40 at baseline to 34-35. Patient has a history of hemorrhoids and gastritis, but denies recent bloody stools, BRBPR, melena, hematemesis, hemoptysis. Iron studies were fairly normal and vitb12 and folate were normal. There is no sign of hemolysis - tbili normal, hapto is elevated, retic count normal. Last colonoscopy on ___ was a virtual colonoscopy that showed no evidence for significant polyps or masses greater than 5 mm. # CHRONIC ISSUES: -CHF: appears euvolemic on exam. CXR with probable mild interstitial pulmonary edema, but on lung exam crackles sound mostly dried. Last echo was in ___ showed ___ with EF > 55%. He was continued on his home diuretic. -restrictive lung disease: ___ to pott's disease, obesity, OSA. Continued on 4L O2 nasal cannula -DM: continued on glipizide. Patient was kept on FSS and sliding scale while hospitalized -gout: stable, no flare for last ___ years, continued on home allopurinol -HTN: continued on home atenolol -OSA: continued on biPAP -hyperlipidemia: continued on home simvastatin # TRANSITIONAL ISSUES -Patient desaturated to 84% on ambulation and stated that this was his baseline. Please recheck O2 sats when ambulating and increase oxygen to ___ as needed to have saturations >90% -please recheck LDH and hematocrit

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with CAD s/p CABG in ___, DM, HTN, HLD, stage ___ CKD, PVD s/p stent in the RLE, who presents with SOB and DOE concerning for acute systolic CHF exacerbation. ACUTE ISSUES: ============== # Hypoxia: Patient significantly hypoxic (to mid-80s) on admission, requiring NIPPV and high-flow nasal cannula for most of his ICU stay. In addition to pulmonary edema, concern for pneumonia or pulmonary embolus. Patient developed productive cough and fever during hospitalization so was started on HCAP antibiotic coverage. Also empirically started on heparin IV drip for presumed PE; eventually ruled-out by CTA once bedside HD was initiated. Respiratory status improved with ongoing diuresis by dialysis, with patient sat'ing well on room air on discharge. # Acute on Chronic Systolic Heart Failure Exacerbation: TTE showed EF 38% w/LV hypokinesis (unchanged from previous echo in ___, BNP elevated, CXR with bibasilar fluid collection, 7kg up from last documented weight (82kg) and 8lbs up from self-reported dry weight, and presented with symptoms of dyspnea on exertion and orthopnea consistent with acute exacerbation. Thought most likely due to volume overload from worsening kidney function and increased resistance to diuretics. Other contributing factors include his highly resistant hypertension (per ___ PCP visit note and patient report, systolic BPs have been in the 170s recently, and he is on 3 medications for attempted control) and dietary non-compliance. When patient did not respond adequately to high dose furosemide, he was initiated on dialysis with resolution of his symptoms. Discharge weight was 78.9Kg. # End Stage Renal Disease, now on Hemodialysis: Gradually worsening since ___, but Creatinine 7.5 on admission was acute jump from baseline of 5.5-5.6. Not hyperkalemic. He has marked increase in his BUN since last admission (92 from 64), and a mildly worsening metabolic acidosis (delta/delta = 1). Initially suspected to be secondary to poor forward flow from ___ exacrebation, but creatinine and uremia actually worsened in setting of diuresis. A tunneled line was placed and dialysis was initiated, which the patient tolerated well. He was started on Sevelamer and nephrocaps. Sensipar was continued. A left-sided AVF was placed prior to discharge. # Healthcare-Associated Pneumonia: Patient was febrile with productive cough. He was treated with vancomycin and cefepime for HCAP coverage x8 days. # Anemia: Admission H/H 8.5/___.1 (baseline range 8.3-9.1/___-30 in ___. MCV 88, low-normal iron studies ___. Likely represents progression of renal disease, with contribution from anemia of inflammation / anemia of iron deficiency. On oral iron supplementation per Nephrology. # Hypertension: Poorly controlled on home regimen with SBPs in the 170s, which may represent worsening of his renal disease. Carvedilol recently added to his regime. Nifedipine, Carvedilol and hydralazine were discontinued after initiation of dialysis. Patient was started on metoprolol XL 100mg and losartan 25mg daily. # Type 2 Diabetes c/b Nephropathy: Well-controlled on insulin # Dyslipidemia: Continuing home rosuvastatin. # Hypogonadism: Patient has not been on testosterone cream at home. To follow up outpatient with endocrinology for further management. # Gout: Per last D/C Summary, was supposed to have started 100mg allopurinol. Was started on this medication here. # Papillary Thyroid Ca s/p Thyroidectomy: Per last D/C Summary, on levothyroxine 175 6x/week and 262.5 1x/wk. Last TSH ___ 0.95. TSH on recheck here 0.41. Discharged on levothyroxine 175mcg daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ===================== - Patient to follow-up with Endocrinology hypothyroidism and hypogonadism. - Patient to follow-up with Transplant Surgery for L-sided AVF. - Patient will follow-up with ___ for his diabetes. - Patient is scheduled for ___ outpatient dialysis.
summary: You were admitted with symptoms of shortness of breath and lethargy and diagnosed with a heart failure exacerbation likely because of both your diet and your renal disease. A heart failure exacerbation occurs when the heart cannot effectively supply the body with as much oxygenated blood as it needs. This can be made worse by eating a diet high in salty foods which increases blood volume making the heart work harder. This can also be made worse with renal failure and uncontrolled high blood pressure when the kidneys are not able to get rid of excess fluid efficiently. Since your admission we have titrated your medications and started you on dialysis to help your kidneys get rid of the extra fluid and toxins that it is not able to do on its own. You have both an indwelling line for hyemodyalisis that was placed as well as a surgical fistula that will need to mature before it can be used. You are now doing well enough to be discharged home with the plan for outpatient hemodyalysis. WE have made quite a few changes to your medications that are listed in your paperwork.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y.o. with CKD on dialysis, CHF, T2D, HTN with recurrent falls here one day s/p fall from bed with trauma to left orbit and left shoulder pain found to have non-operative L maxillary and orbital floor fractures. # Syncope: Most likely orthostasis leading to syncope, likely related to dialysis/volume shifts and from her hypertensive regimen. CT Head was without ICH, ECHO showed no change (good systolic function and no AS). No arrythmia on EKG or tele. Her four antihypertensives were held given that her BP's had been controlled to 120s systolic which can increase risk for falling. While admitted, her BP was at goal for age. # L eye pain: Optho evaluation in-house noted no globe damage, recommended artifical tears and optho follow up in ___ weeks. # Incidental Renal Masses: Several bilateral renal lesions, some potentially suspicious for solid masses as opposed to just complex cysts. Radiology recommended multiphasic CT or MR to exclude RCC. Discussion was had with the patient about whether she would want a diagnosis given her age and she wanted to discuss further with out outpatient providers before undergoing further imaging. This will have to be readdressed in the outpatient setting. # Anemia: Acute on chronic anemia given drop in Hg from 10.3 at baseline on ___ to 8.5 on ___ admission. Guaiac negative, no melena, no blood noted in stool. Likely due to hematomas secondary to fall - hematomas on left face and left hip. She was transfused 1 unit pRBC with dialysis on ___. She will need to have follow up hematocrit to ensure stability as an outpatient. # ESRD: MWF dialysis; last dialysis was on ___. # Chronic diastolic CHF: at dry weight (estimated as of ___ as 41.5kg), euvolemic during this admission. # Thrombocytopenia: Likely due to consumptive process secondary to trauma. Also has history of thromobycopenia raising possibility of more chronic issue. This cna be addressed as outpatient. Transitional issues: [] BP medication management: BPs had somewhat wide, will allow higher goal given increased risk for falls with low blood pressure in the elderly. [] follow up renal incidental CT findings (cysts vs masses, MRI recommended to rule out RCC) if clinically appropriate. This was discussed and she wants to discuss this with her renal doctor as an outpatient. [] soft diet for 2 weeks from fall (___) [] Optho: Artificial tears ___ drops BID and ___ week follow up [] Repeat CBC in 5 - 7 days after discharge for Hg/HCT and thrombocytopenia [] Facial fractures: non-operative management, sinus precautions for 1 week from fall - no straw use/no blowing nose, etc. Can follow up with plastics if desired on ___ # CODE: DNR/DNI
summary: You were evaluated after fainting and falling to the ground. CT scans of your head and spine did not show any blleding in your brain or new abnormalities in your spine. CT scan of your face showed small fractures which were evaluated by plastics, and do not need surgery. You also had a bruise around your eye and fractures near the eye, so the eye doctors saw ___ and recommend eye drops and follow up as an outpatient. Because you had a fracture in your sinuses, you should keep a soft diet for 2 weeks (blended, soft) and sinus precautions for 1 week. Sinus precautions include no using straws, sneeze with mouth open, no sniffing, no smoking, keep head of bed elevated to 45 degrees.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo F with history of CHF s/p pacemaker, MI and TIA on ___, and double mastectomy presents with dysphagia. . ACUTE # dysphagia: Pt describes sensation of food getting stuck as well as symptoms with solids and liquids. There was initially concern for an esophageal motility disorder vs malignancy vs CVA. There was no weight loss asociated, and the new onset was concerning for a central process especially in the setting of her recent TIA. She was evaluated by speech and swallow and initially made NPO. However, video swallow analysis demonstrated no swallow pathology. Her diet was advanced and she was able to tolerate PO on discharge. The etiology of her intermittent dysphagia remains unclear. It may in fact have been caused by her cough which also resolved while in the hospital. . # pleural effusion: Differential included CHF vs PNA vs malignancy. Likely ___ CHF. supported by cough productive of clear foam, trace edema on exam, lack of WBC count or fever. Other etiology could be malignancy, especially in the setting of her dysphagia complaint. However, as discussed above, there was no dysphagia while in house. While in house, pt was not short of breath and oxygenated well. She was given a dose of lasix once tolerating PO. No additional work-up was initiated during this admission. . # cough: As discussed above, there was really no evidence of infection, and a malignancy workup was not warranted in this ___ year old patient. Her dysphagia was not present while in house. She was diuresed with a single dose of lasix, and her cough resolved while in the hospital, further supporting the likelihood that this was related to CHF. . # end of life discussion: Palliative care was consulted to initiate discussions as the patient has expressed concern and anxiety regarding increasing frequency of hospitalizations. The palliative care team will plan to meet with the patient and her daughters at her next appointment with Dr. ___. . CHRONIC # CKD: Stable. Meds were renal dosed. # CHF: see above for management # s/p stroke and inferior MI ___ - Continued asa, simvastatin # HTN: Continued diovan, metoprolol. # hypothyroidism: Continued levothyroxine. . TRANSITION # f/u electrolytes as pt recently started on lasix with discharge # f/u anion gap metabolic acidosis - no w/u initiated in hospital # continue discussion with palliative care
summary: You were hospitalized because you difficulty swallowing as well as a cough and fluid on your lungs. Our speech team saw you, and evaluated your swallow by video and found that your swallowing was not impaired. You were restarted on a regular diet and tolerating it well. Regarding your cough, we suspect that it was likely due to some fluid on your lungs. We gave you a water pill to hopefully help you get rid of the fluid. This morning, you did not complain of this symptom. You were also seen by the palliative care specialists who discuss options to improve your quality of life and help prevent you from having to undergo the stress of being readmitted to the hospital frequently. We will send you home with one additional medication, lasix (water pill). All of your other medications should be continued as before.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ M pmh Hodgkin's lymphoma on palliative brentuximab (last ___, afib on coumadin, and TAVR c/b L femoral artery pseudoaneurysm s/p embolization (___), further c/b extensive LLE hematoma s/p surgical evacuation (___) and abscess ___ who presents with LLE hematoma confirmed on CT, with stable H/H and no neurovascular compromise. ACTIVE ISSUES: #LLE Hematoma, Hemarthrosis Vascular surgery was consulted and determined that there was no indication for surgical intervention. ___ was consulted, and recommended dedicated U/S imaging. This showed that the hematoma was unable to be drained. Ortho was consulted given the hemarthrosis of the knee. Per ortho, pt should undergo ___ and be weight bearing as tolerated. Arthrocentesis was felt to be high risk for seeding an infection, especially given his history of infected hematoma. TAVR team was included in discussions of anticoagulation as hematoma felt to be related to Coumadin use. His main indication for anticoagulation is Afib with CHADS2Vasc 3. In conjunction with TAVR team (Dr. ___, patient should continue on Coumadin for a lower INR goal of 1.8-2. This was discussed with his outpatient ___ clinic providers who are in agreement. Patient's INR was initially reversed with vitamin K 5 mg PO in the ED but was discharged on Coumadin at 2 mg, no bridge, for a new goal INR 1.8-2. ASA 81 daily was held during admission but restarted upon discharge. CHRONIC ISSUES: #Heart Failure with Reduced EF: Felt to be euvolemic and compensated during admission. # CKD: Cr at baseline during admission # AS s/p TAVR: anticoagulation as above # AFib: remained in NSR. continued home amiodarone. AC as above; INR goal upon discharge 1.8-2.0. Discharged on warfarin 2 mg daily. # Hodgkin's Lymphoma: Stage IV based on bone marrow involvement, currently on palliative brentuximab (last ___. Not active this admission. # Hypothyroidism: s/p thyroidectomy, clinically stable on levothyroxine. continued home levothyroxine 150 mcg daily #HLD: continued home pravastatin TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient discharged for new INR goal of ___ as described above - Patient should undergo home ___ as scheduled - Per ___, if hematoma does not resolve over weeks and if pain worsens, please consider re-imaing with u/s as hematoma may be more amenable to drainage in the future. As it is, blood component appears thickened and not able to be drained at this time. -CT read of LLE indicates underlying intermuscular lesion cannot be definitively excluded due to extensive thigh hematoma. This was discussed with radiology and whether MRI should be done at this time. Preference is to wait until further resolution of hematoma and if concern for underlying mass exists at that time to consider MRI. Therefore, please consider MRI of the thigh after further resolution of thigh hematoma. ***Medication Changes*** Added: none Changed: Warfarin 4 mg PO 4X/WEEK (___), 3.5 mg PO 3X/WEEK (MO,WE,SA) to warfarin 2 mg daily, with plan for outpt monitoring and goal 1.8-2.0 Stopped: none
summary: You were seen at ___ due to pain in your leg. This was felt to be due to a large bruise (or a hematoma) under the skin. This extends through your thigh and your knee. It is most likely related to your blood thinner as well as the complicated history you have had with this leg (including surgeries and infections). We consulted a vascular team and an interventional radiology team who did not think surgery would be helpful for this. Instead, we reversed your blood clotting medicine and we will discharge you on Coumadin for a lower INR goal. The hope is that this lower INR goal will help prevent worsening of the bleed.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o M with complex cardiac history including anterior MI (LAD), ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF=20%), HTN/HL, pAfib on AC, and recurrent VT s/p ablation (___) and pacer. He was sent from ___ clinic to the ED due to symptomatic hypotension. . #. Hypotension: On admission to floor, patient back to baseline BPs 100s-110s systolic. Review of history as well as pacer interrogation suggested that patient likely hypotensive from hypovolemia ___ poor PO intake around time of root canal. Also appears patient has gone back into atrial fibrillation, potentially also around time of root canal, leading to loss of atrial kick. Reassuringly, device interrogation showed no VT/VF to explain his symptoms. Patient has appropriately compensated on the floor and was discharged normotensive. Also considered on differential potentially transient bacteremia with distributive physiology in context of root canal; however, little degree of odontogenic manipulation during this initial phase of the root canal seems less likely to be the driver. It was felt that antibiotics prior to the next phase of root canal would be less useful. We did advise (see transitional issues) that patient hold antihypertensives prior to second phase of his root canal. There was no evidence of other driver, notably pulmonary embolus or ACS, which was reassuring. Patient will follow up with ___ clinic as well as cardiology as an outpatient. . #. Afib - Presently v-paced with likely underlying afib. Continued coumadin. Patient therapeutic. . #. HL: Continued statin. . #. CAD: Continued ASA, statin, initially held ACEi, beta blocker due to reported hypotension. Re-initiated on discharge. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient admitted with symptomatic hypotension. Felt to be secondary to hypovolemia as well as paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and loss of atrial kick. - Patient should hold lisinopril and furosemide on the morning of his root canal (part 2)
summary: We monitored your blood pressures in the hospital and they were improved to your baseline. We performed several studies to rule out serious causes of hypotension, including chest imaging, pacemaker interrogation, and laboratory studies. These have been reassuringly negative to date. We feel that your hypotension is likely due to hypovolemia (less fluid in the blood vessels) as well as contribution of atrial fibrillation and decreased pumping power of the heart. Please review your medication list closely. We are continuing all of your home medications. ON ___ MORNING, PRIOR TO THE SECOND PART OF YOUR ROOT CANAL, PLEASE DO NOT TAKE FUROSEMIDE (LASIX) OR LISINOPRIL to limit the likelihood of further drops in blood pressure.
original_text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ y/o M with complex cardiac history including anterior MI (LAD), ischemic cardiomyopathy (EF=20%), HTN/HL, pAfib on AC, and recurrent VT s/p ablation (___) and pacer. He was sent from ___ clinic to the ED due to symptomatic hypotension. . #. Hypotension: On admission to floor, patient back to baseline BPs 100s-110s systolic. Review of history as well as pacer interrogation suggested that patient likely hypotensive from hypovolemia ___ poor PO intake around time of root canal. Also appears patient has gone back into atrial fibrillation, potentially also around time of root canal, leading to loss of atrial kick. Reassuringly, device interrogation showed no VT/VF to explain his symptoms. Patient has appropriately compensated on the floor and was discharged normotensive. Also considered on differential potentially transient bacteremia with distributive physiology in context of root canal; however, little degree of odontogenic manipulation during this initial phase of the root canal seems less likely to be the driver. It was felt that antibiotics prior to the next phase of root canal would be less useful. We did advise (see transitional issues) that patient hold antihypertensives prior to second phase of his root canal. There was no evidence of other driver, notably pulmonary embolus or ACS, which was reassuring. Patient will follow up with ___ clinic as well as cardiology as an outpatient. . #. Afib - Presently v-paced with likely underlying afib. Continued coumadin. Patient therapeutic. . #. HL: Continued statin. . #. CAD: Continued ASA, statin, initially held ACEi, beta blocker due to reported hypotension. Re-initiated on discharge. . TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: - Patient admitted with symptomatic hypotension. Felt to be secondary to hypovolemia as well as paroxysmal atrial fibrillation and loss of atrial kick. - Patient should hold lisinopril and furosemide on the morning of his root canal (part 2)

text: Brief Hospital Course: IMPRESSION AND RECOMMENDATIONS: ___ yo man who presented to CDAC on ___ with CHF exacerbation, elevated Troponin thought to be demand ischemia with HF, recent PND/DOE/edema/chest discomfort, stopped taking his meds a while back ___ insurance issues and only recently resumed outpt. He was admitted for NSTEMI, HF management and management of dry gangrenous left great toe. # Acute decompensated heart failure: EF 45-50% on TTE on ___. Dry weight is unknown as patient does not have a scale at home, office visit 3 months ago with the same weight as admission possibly reflecting furosemide 40 mg daily was insufficient. Diuresed well on Lasix gtt which was stopped on evening of ___ after 6 Liter cumulative negative output. 5 pound weight loss from admit weight. S/P cath ___ with elevated left heart filling pressure. -Continue spironolactone 25mg -Start torsemide 40 mg daily -Daily weight, 2 gram sodium diet -F/U with Dr. ___ # NSTEMI: Troponin obtained in the setting of history of CAD and nonspecific chest discomfort positive at 0.13, down trended to 0.12 and negative MB. First part of nuclear perfusion test done ___, delayed by positive troponin, volume overload. Continued to diuresis and revisited second day ___. Completed pharm nuclear ___ which showed severe perfusion defect involving RCA territory. S/P coronary angiogram ___ SVG-RCA graft occluded. No interventions done. -Continue increased carvedilol 12.5 mg BID -Continue ASA 81, atorvastatin 80 -Continue increased dose of losartan 50 mg daily for BP control # Dry gangrene left toe: Appreciate Podiatry/Vascular surgery consults. Now s/p left great toe debridement and nail removal by podiatry ___. -non-invasive studies ___ (ABI, PVR, Arterial duplex), vein mapping completed on ___ per vascular request, results above -outpatient follow-up next week for ___ angio Vascular Surgery Office: ___. -plan per vascular is to do diagnostic angiogram of Left foot as outpatient, does not need to be while inpt. ___ angio scheduled for ___ -Daily betadine dressing by ___ -Per podiatry, will see patient after vascular procedure as outpt -ID consulted re input on oral antibiotic transition at discharge; appreciate recs: discontinued iv antibiotics, no need to continue iv or po antibiotics per ID -IV Ceftriaxone discontinued ___ per ID recs -IV Vancomycin discontinued on ___ per ID recs -Home with ___ to help with dressings, wound checks and heart failure monitoring # HTN: SBP 155-188 today. BP up to ___ in the afternoon. Pt asymptomatic. His bp has been 150-170 range since admission. Was briefly on po hydral 10 mg tid at which time his bp remained 120-150's. Hydral was discontinued on ___ to give room for up titration of BB and losartan. - Continue increased carvedilol 12.5 mg bid - Continue increased dose of losartan 50 mg daily - Start hydralazine 10 mg tid # Type 2 diabetes with nephropathy, hyperglycemia, neuropathy: Had episode of hypoglycemia BG ___ while NPO. Had received full dose of NPH evening prior. Treated with glucose gel and D50 25gm for BG 115. - ___ c/s as outpt for DM management - Continue home metformin and NPH - Potentially start SGL T-2 inhibitor as outpt through ___ # Mixed dyslipidemia: - Continue Atorvastatin, started on admission on ___ # Transitional: home with ___ for dressing changes, wound checks and heart failure monitoring arranged # Dispo: Discharge today with ___
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you had a heart failure exacerbation and you were volume overloaded (extra fluid in your body). You received diuretics through your IV to help get the extra fluid off. Your blood work showed elevated cardiac enzymes therefore you underwent a cardiac catheterization to look at the arteries in your heart. This showed one of your grafts from your prior heart surgery was blocked, but it did not require any intervention, will be managed with medications. The vascular and podiatry doctors saw ___ because of your gangrenous left toe. This was debrided. The recommendation is to have it amputated. It has been arranged for you to return on ___ for an angiogram to look at the blood flow in your leg.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This is a ___ y/o F with history of systolic/diastolic CHF (EF 45-50%), HTN, NSTEMI, who presents with sudden onset SOB and CP with clinical and radiographic signs of acute CHF and type 2 NSTEMI, s/p PCI w/ POBA to mid LAD and DES to LCX. # Acute on chronic congestive heart failure with diastolic/systolic dysfunction: LVEF 45-50% in ___ with regional LV systolic dysfunction suggestive of multivessel CAD. Was volume overloaded on admission. She was diuresed with boluses of IV Bumex (pt refused furosemide) with good effect and return to euvolemia. She also was given a 2g Na restriction. She was restarted on her home torsemide regimen, as well as home lisinopril and isosorbide mononitrate. She underwent catheterization as below. She should have a TTE repeated as an outpatient, given NSTEMI. Physical therapy evaluated her and felt she was appropriate for discharge home with services. # NSTEMI/severe 3VD: Trop peaked at 0.66 and was trending down at discharge. During hospitalization, she continued to have chest pain that was refractory to nitroglycerin SL. She was placed on a nitro drip and a heparin drip, as well as high dose statin, and her home lisinopril was continued. She was given metoprolol tartrate while an inpatient and transition to her home metoprolol XL at discharge. Given concern for ongoing ischemia, she was taken for cardiac catheterization and found to have severe three vessel CAD (see cath details above) with POBA to mid LAD and DES to LCX, via left radial access. After the procedure, which was complicated by a short linear distal LAD dissection with normal flow, she was chest pain free off the nitro drip (she complained of a "warm feeling" in her chest/neck but this was felt to not be cardiac in origin given stable EKGs and downtrending trops). She was continued on home aspirin and will require clopidogrel 75 mg daily for one year. Her home isosorbide mononitrate was restarted. Her renal function was stable at discharge. # Complicated UTI: She was found to have a complicated UTI w/ Proteus sensitive to Ceftriaxone, which she was started on IV (day ___ and transitioned to PO cefpodoxime at discharge for a 10 day course. # Gout: Her home allopurinol ___ daily was continued. # Hypothyroidism: Her home levothyroxine 112mcg daily was continued. # Spinal stenosis: Her home gabapentin 400mg BID was continued. Transition issues: - Will require Plavix 75 mg QD for one year - Should have TTE repeated after discharge as an outpatient - Discharged on Cefpodoxime to complete a 10 day course (last day ___.
summary: You were admitted this hospitalization for complaints of shortenss of breath and chest pain. You were found to have a small leak of an enzyme that is released when the heart is under stress. This is called a non-ST elevation myocardial infarction. You were also found to be in heart failure, this is when the heart cannot meet the demands of the body. Because of your shortness of breath and chest pain you underwent a cardiac catheterization to assess the coronary arteries of the heart, these are the arteries that provide blood flow to the heart muscle. We found two blockages that we were able to open with stents and you had a great result. You were also found to have a urinary tract infection which we are treated with an antibiotic called cefpidoxime. You will need to continue your antibitoics until the course is totally completed otherwise you will be at risk for another urinary tract infection.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ y/o woman with PMHx CAD s/p NSTEMI at OSH ___, a-fib on dabigatran HTN, HLD, PVD s/p L BKA ___, SCC of pyriform sinus s/p resection (___), lung ca s/p cyberknife and lobectomy (___) p/w intermittent DOE and anemia (Hct 24). ACTIVE ISSUES: ============== # Acute on Chronic sCHF Findings of DOE with weight gain, elevated BNP, and ___ edema c/w prior episodes of heart failure. Unclear trigger. Last echo performed in ___ w/ EF 30%. Pt was started on IV lasix w/ significant improvement in her sx with no complaints of SOB, satting at 99% on RA, and decreased ___ edema. Pt was transitioned to PO lasix with increase to 20 mg to better control fluid status as an outpatient. Planned close follow-up with outpt cardiology for continued adjustment of medications as needed. Weight on discharge was 50.4 kg. # Anemia For her anemia that may have also been contributing to her sx, the patient was transfused 1U pRBC with appropriate improvement in her Hct. No e/o active bleeding was noted or hemolysis. Appeared to be from depressed bone marrow function with additional significantly low ferritin. Resumed her iron supplementation with possible future consideration for IV iron as needed if not responsive to oral supplementation. Further work-up for anemia such as occult bleed as warranted on an outpatient basis, though while here her Hct otherwise stable to improved with no evidence of active bleed. Follow-up with PCP ___. # CAD H/o MIs in the past. No e/o active CP or ACS at this time w/ negative trop and stable EKG. # Afib In afib, appeared to be rate controlled. Spoke to primary cardiologist who wanted pt to continue dabigatran 150mg for now (since studies not done at 75mg) and will re-address A/C as an outpatient. Continued on metoprolol and otherwise well rate controlled. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - Follow-up Hct at next visit. Consider IV iron for low ferritin if still non-responsive to oral supplementation - F/u with cardiology, adjust diuretics as needed to maintain euvolemia - F/u w/ cardiology regarding anticoagulation for afib given mild renal impairment. Still on full dose dagibtran. - ___ benefit from nutrition eval
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath. The cause was that you were in heart failure where you had too much fluid in your lungs and body. This improved after we gave you IV lasix, a medication that makes you urinate. You will continue this medication in pill form when you leave the hospital. You also had low blood counts that might have caused your shortness of breath and could be related to your low iron levels. You were given a blood transfusion with improvement. You should continue taking iron pills. You may need to get IV iron treatments if the oral iron does not help. Your primary care doctor ___ continue to follow you for this problem.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ female with past medical history of hypertension, CAD, aortic valvular disease (severe aortic stenosis) presented with progressive dyspnea on exertion and fatigue over the past 1 month, found to be in acute decompensated heart failure. Active issues ============= #Acute on chronic heart failure #Heart failure with preserved ejection fraction The patient was found to have elevated BNP and to be volume overloaded on exam, most likely representing an acute heart failure exacerbation in the setting of a valvular normalities including severe aortic stenosis below. EF on TTE during hospitalization was greater than 55%. The trigger for current exacerbation remained unclear, possibly in the setting of a urinary tract infection as below. The patient responded well to IV diuresis and was transitioned back to her home diuretic regimen prior to discharge. Imdur was discontinued in the setting of severe aortic stenosis below, metoprolol was increased to 50 mg XL daily and lisinopril to 15mg daily. - Discharge weight: 80.9 kg (178.35 lbs) - Discharge regimen: Lasix 20 mg PO daily, Lisinopril 15mg daily, Metoprolol XL 50 mg daily #Severe aortic stenosis TTE during hospital stay demonstrated severe aortic stenosis (valve area 0.6 cm², index ___ 0.3 cm²/square meters), likely contributing to her heart failure exacerbation as above. The structural heart team was contacted for evaluation and will see her as an outpatient. #Urinary tract infection The patient's daughter, patient complained of dysuria as well as hematuria prior to admission. Urinalysis demonstrated small leukocytes and trace blood. Urine culture grew pan-sensitive proteus. In the setting of her worsening weakness, the patient was treated with a 3 day course of ceftriaxone (completed ___. #History of pulmonary embolus #Supratherapeutic INR Patient's INR was 3.9 admission, possibly in the setting of poor p.o. intake and progressive weakness as above. Warfarin was held during hospital stay. INR remained supratherapeutic in the setting of antibiotic treatment as above. INR on discharge 2.4, home dose warfarin restarted. Chronic issues ============== #CAD #History of silent MI Per medical records, patient had silent MI in the past. No chest pain no evidence of ACS during hospital stay. Baby aspirin and rosuvastatin are continued. Metoprolol was adjusted as above. Imdur was discontinued as above. #Hypertension Blood pressures range between 120-160/60-90. Imdur was discontinued given severe aortic stenosis above. Metoprolol was increased to 50 mg XL daily. Blood pressure on discharge 155/90 and therefore lisinopril was increased to 15mg daily on discharge. Follow-up and further up titration is recommended. #Hyperlipidemia Home rosuvastatin was continued. #Gerd Stable, home ranitidine was continued. #History of insomnia Stable, home paroxetine was continued Transitional issues =================== [] Discharge weight: 80.9 kg (178.35 lbs) [] Imdur discontinued in the setting of severe aortic stenosis. Metoprolol increased to 50 mg XL daily [] Lisinopril increased to 15mg daily on discharge. Please obtain CMP within one week to ensure stability of potassium (4.1 on discharge) and renal function (creatinine 0.8 on discharge) [] Blood pressure on discharge 155/90. Please follow-up and uptitrate metoprolol accordingly [] Warfarin held during hospitalization for supratherapeutic INR. Discharge INR 2.4, restarted on home regimen on ___. [] Completed 3 day course of ceftriaxone on ___ [] contacted structural heart team for evaluation regarding severe aortic stenosis. they will reach out to the patient to follow up as an outpatient. #CODE: Full (confirmed) #CONTACT: ___, daughter (___)
summary: you had too much fluid in your body which made it difficult for your heart to pump. This caused you to be weaker and more tired. you were given medication to help remove the excess fluid. a heart ultrasound showed that one of your heart valves (the aortic valve) is significantly narrowed. Therefore, your Imdur was discontinued and your lisinopril and your metoprolol were increased. We contacted our colleagues from cardiology to evaluate you for possible additional intervention. They will contact you and see you as an outpatient. you were also found to have an infection in your urine and were given antibiotics to treat the infection please take ___ the medications as detailed below. Specifically, do not take your Imdur anymore but take the higher doses of metoprolol and lisinopril. please contact an MD in case of weight gain or new confusion. please continue measuring your INR at home (your Warfarin was held for three days in the hospital because of a high INR).

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ year old female with history of known CAD s/p multiple catheterizations, most recently in ___ at ___ with PCI to ramus and multi vessel disease with 100% occlusion of mid LAD with collateralized from ramus, 100% occlusion of distal Cx with collateralization from ramus, and ramus ostial disease who was transferred from OSH for cardiac catheterization after chest pain concerning for NSTEMI. ACUTE ISSUES: ============== #NSTEMI/CAD Patient presented with chest pain consistent with unstable angina and troponin elevation consistent with NSTEMI. Underwent cardiac catheterization ___ with placement of DES in distal LM into ramus. During cardiac catheterization, patient noted to have an elevated EDP to 22. Troponins downtrending post procedure. TTE with normal (LVEF >55%) global and regional biventricular systolic function. Mild mitral regurgitation. Patient treated with amlodipine 10mg tablet, isosorbide mononitrate 120mg daily ER, losartan potassium 100mg tablet daily, and metoprolol tartrate 75mg QID. Patient's simvastatin 40mg was uptitrated to atorvastatin 80mg. Patient's triamterene/hctz was discontinued and changed to furosemide 20mg daily. #CHF: Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure exacerbation. Evidence of volume overload on cardiac catheterization with EDP elevated to 22. Post catheterization received 20mg IV Lasix as well as NS at 75cc/hr for 500cc for to prevent contrast induced nephropathy. TTE with normal (LVEF >55%) global and regional biventricular systolic function. Mild mitral regurgitation. Diuresed during hospital stay (held on day of discharge due to slight hypotension in AM to 83/42 that increased to 100/45 without intervention). Patient discharged on 20mg po lasix daily (discontinued pre admission medication of triamterene/hctz). Discharge weight 92.9kg. Continued on metoprolol and losartan per above. #Hyponatremia: Felt to be secondary to cardiorenal syndrome. Diuresed with lasix and discharged on 20mg po furosemide daily with plan for outpatient lab follow up on ___. Results faxed to PCP ___. #Acute kidney injury: Creatinine 1.1 on admission and increased to 1.4 on discharge. Peaked at 1.5 on ___. Etiology felt to be due to cardiorenal syndrome given elevated EDP on catheterization in addition to contrast induced nephropathy from catheterization performed ___. Diuresed during hospital stay and discharged on furosemide 20mg daily. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ #Gastrointestinal reflux disease: continued ranitidine 150mg daily #Diabetes Mellitus: blood sugars managed with insulin sliding scale while in hospital. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: =================== - will need plavix and aspirin for at least one year. Please optimize guideline directed medical therapy as tolerated and modify risk factors as clinically warranted. - please monitor for signs of radiation induced skin or hair damage. If noted, please contact the Interventional Cardiology Section at ___. - home triamterene-HCTZ stopped - started on furosemide 20mg daily. Please follow up and titrate as clinically warranted. - QID metoprolol changed to 250mg XL - please check creatinine and electrolytes on ___. Creatinine at time of discharge 1.4. Na 125. - simvastatin changed to atorvastatin 80mg
summary: You were transferred here after you had a minor heart attack. You had a cardiac catherization, and had a stent placed to open up a blockage in the artery going to your heart. It is very important for you to take your aspirin and clopidogrel (Plavix) every day. If you miss ___ dose of these medications, your stent could become blocked and you could have another heart attack. We also thought that you had some extra fluid from heart failure. We gave you IV medication to remove the fluid. Please stop taking your triamterene-hydrochlorothiazide. We started you on a new medication to remove fluid. This medication is called furosemide (Lasix). You should take it every day. Your weight on discharge is 92.9kg (204 lbs). You should weigh yourself every day.

ACUTE ISSUES: ============== #NSTEMI/CAD Patient presented with chest pain consistent with unstable angina and troponin elevation consistent with NSTEMI. Underwent cardiac catheterization ___ with placement of DES in distal LM into ramus. During cardiac catheterization, patient noted to have an elevated EDP to 22. Troponins downtrending post procedure. TTE with normal (LVEF >55%) global and regional biventricular systolic function. Mild mitral regurgitation. Patient treated with amlodipine 10mg tablet, isosorbide mononitrate 120mg daily ER, losartan potassium 100mg tablet daily, and metoprolol tartrate 75mg QID. Patient's simvastatin 40mg was uptitrated to atorvastatin 80mg. Patient's triamterene/hctz was discontinued and changed to furosemide 20mg daily. #CHF: Acute on chronic diastolic heart failure exacerbation. Evidence of volume overload on cardiac catheterization with EDP elevated to 22. Post catheterization received 20mg IV Lasix as well as NS at 75cc/hr for 500cc for to prevent contrast induced nephropathy. TTE with normal (LVEF >55%) global and regional biventricular systolic function. Mild mitral regurgitation. Diuresed during hospital stay (held on day of discharge due to slight hypotension in AM to 83/42 that increased to 100/45 without intervention). Patient discharged on 20mg po lasix daily (discontinued pre admission medication of triamterene/hctz). Discharge weight 92.9kg. Continued on metoprolol and losartan per above. #Hyponatremia: Felt to be secondary to cardiorenal syndrome. Diuresed with lasix and discharged on 20mg po furosemide daily with plan for outpatient lab follow up on ___. Results faxed to PCP ___. #Acute kidney injury: Creatinine 1.1 on admission and increased to 1.4 on discharge. Peaked at 1.5 on ___. Etiology felt to be due to cardiorenal syndrome given elevated EDP on catheterization in addition to contrast induced nephropathy from catheterization performed ___. Diuresed during hospital stay and discharged on furosemide 20mg daily. CHRONIC ISSUES: ================ #Gastrointestinal reflux disease: continued ranitidine 150mg daily #Diabetes Mellitus: blood sugars managed with insulin sliding scale while in hospital. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: =================== - will need plavix and aspirin for at least one year. Please optimize guideline directed medical therapy as tolerated and modify risk factors as clinically warranted. - please monitor for signs of radiation induced skin or hair damage. If noted, please contact the Interventional Cardiology Section at ___. - home triamterene-HCTZ stopped - started on furosemide 20mg daily. Please follow up and titrate as clinically warranted. - QID metoprolol changed to 250mg XL - please check creatinine and electrolytes on ___. Creatinine at time of discharge 1.4. Na 125. - simvastatin changed to atorvastatin 80mg

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with multiple co-morbidities, recurrent UTIs and recent admission for E. coli bacteremia presenting with transient abdominal pain c/w choledocholithiasis c/b demand ischemia. #NSTEMI: Patient with elevated troponin with unchanged EKG. Most likely Type II NSTEMI, potentially from anemia/demand with Hb 7.3. Patient was given 1U pRBCs with improvement of symptoms. However, patient continued to have intermittent episodes of chest pain with mild elevations in troponins that improved with SL nitro. Patient was not given heparin due to GI bleeding risk from prior admissions. Cardiology was consulted who recommended initiating beta-blockade with metoprolol for anti-anginal effect. Patient's BP remained stable in the 110-120s with HRs in the 70-80s. Patient's chest pain resolved upon discharge. Her spironolactone was discontinued in favor of uptitration of metoprolol. #SOB/Congestion: Unclear etiology. CXR not impressive for volume overload. ___ be manifestation of symptomatic anemia that resolved with transfusion. Patient was given guifenecin and PRN nebs with resolution of dyspnea. # Chronic Systolic and diastolic CHF: EF 35-40% from TTE in ___. Dry weight 160 pounds. Weight at increased 5 kg while inpatient with evidence of elevated JVP. Patient was given IV lasix with good diuresis and improvement of volume status. She was transitioned to daily torsemide for further diuresis. Spironolactone was discontinued as above. #choledocholithiasis: Patient presented with mild epigastric, RUQ abdominal pain found to have transaminitis and elevated lipase. Pain subsided and thought to be from choledocholithiasis leading to transaminitis and gallstone pancreatitis. Patient's abdominal pain improved spontaneously and LFTs downtrended. RUQ showed no evidence of cholelcystitis. #microcytic anemia: Fe/TIBC = 4% consistent with iron deficiency anemia. Patient was initiated on ferrous sulfate with aggressive bowel regimen. H/H remained stable after transfusion and initiation of iron supplementation. CHRONIC ISSUES # h/o critical AS: s/p bovine AVR (___) # Hypertension: as above # Diabetes mellitus, type II: continued home regimen of 33U ___ with HSSI # Obstructive sleep apnea: oxygen-dependent since lung resection; utilizes 2L nasal cannula O2 only at nighttime # Seizure disorder: stable and not on anti-epileptics. Transitional Issues: -consider outpatient surgical evaluation for cholecystectomy to discuss risk/benefits of procedure -f/u LFTs as outpatient -can increase metoprolol as BP and HR tolerates written for 25 mg daily of succinate -discharge weight: 79.8 kg -code: full -contact: ___ (daughter) ___
summary: You came to the hospital because of abdominal pain. Your labs showed that your most likely had a gallstone that became stuck, causing you to have abdominal pain. The stone most likely passed and your abdominal pain improved as did your liver function labs. You also experienced episodes of shortness of breath/chest pain. You were found to have anemia which most likely contributed to your symptoms for which you were given blood and iron. Your chest pain improved and your EKG did not show any changes concerning for a heart attack. You still had intermittant episodes of chest pain for which you were seen by the cardiology team who suggested further changes in your medication.

text: Brief Hospital Course: A+P: ___ woman with hx of CHF, HTN and breast cancer who presents with cough, dyspnea and bilateral pleural effusions. . # Acute on Chronic Systolic and Diastolic Heart Failure: Pt found to have bilateral pleural effusions on CXR. Initial differential included ACS, PE, malignant pleural effusions but elevated BNP (4183), elevated JVD (to earlobe on admit), and clinical improvement with diuresis (O2 sats on ambulation 92-94% on discharge) most consistent with acute on chronic CHF. Pt ruled out for ACS with plan for outpatient stress MIBI on ___ (already scheduled). ___ nl. Echo showed EF now 50-55% but worsened diastolic heart failure with a question of infiltrative process (e.g. amyloid). Could consider further work-up as an outpatient. Pt discharged on lasix 80mg daily and home ___ for CHF monitoring and education; weight on discharge 152.4 lbs. Continued on beta blocker; ACE-I changed to ___ as below. Pt to follow up with PCP ___ ___ should have Chemistries, BP, and fluid status checked at that time. Pt will also follow up with her outpatient cardiologist, Dr. ___. . # Cough: Likely due to acute on chronic CHF, may also have component from ACE-I. Lisinopril changed to Valsartan (80mg daily). . # Acute renal failure: admitted with Cre of 1.2 which normalized to 0.8 with diuresis. . # HTN: increase in SBP to 140-150s once lisinopril discontinued. Switched to valsartan, would increase HTN medications as tolerated. . # Code status: Full.
summary: You were admitted for cough and low oxygen levels and found to have fluid overload in your lungs (acute on chronic congestive heart failure) which may be due to stiffening of your heart, which was seen on your echocardiogram. Your kidney function was also slightly worse in this setting. Both your fluid overload and your kidney function improved with removal of fluid (diuresis) via intravenous lasix (furosemide). You will be discharged with an increase dose of your oral lasix and your lisinopril was discontinued and valsartan was chosen to replace it, as lisinopril may have contributed to your cough. It will be important to maintain good blood pressure control (with your medications and a low salt diet) to prevent worsening of your heart function.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is an ___ woman with DM2, CAD s/p MI ___, AFib on coumadin with pacer, CKD stage III diastolic heart failure (EF >75%), and recent admission for pyelonephritis, admitted for acute on chronic ___ exacerbation ACUTE ISSUES # Acute on Chronic Diastolic Heart failure (EF >75%): Pt presenting with worsening of leg swelling. Pt was dc-ed on PO bumax. Weight increased from 220lbs upon discharge on ___ to 234 lbs on admission. The pt initially received lasix 120mg IV BID, but diuresis was not adequate. The pt was transitioned to lasix drip and wt was down to 97.1kg while maintaining a daily net negative of -2 to 3 liters. Her creatinine remained stable during diuresis. She did have a significant contraction alkalosis from the diuresis and bicarb reached 39. Of note the family refused starting metolazone on the pt due to concern for AMS when pt previously received this medication. Once off the drip the pt started torsemide 100mg daily, which she will continue as an outpatient. # Previous Pulmonary embolism ___ fibrillation: Subtherapteutic INR on admission. The pt was started on a heparin drip to bridge until INR was therapeutic on coumadin. At the time of discharge she was off the drip w/ a therapeutic INR. She received 4mg daily while in-house, but this was changed to 3mg daily as her INR was climbing upon discharge. # L knee pain: pt c/o left knee pain during admission. there was concern for gout as this was noted in her PMH and the pt would not move her L leg and it was exquisitely TTP. per previous admission notes and the pt this is an ongoing issue. the pt did receive a couple dose of colchicine (0.6mg x2 given renal function). ortho was consulted and performed an arthocentesis which ended up being a dry tap. plain films of the L knee were c/w osteoarthritis. pain continued to improve as did ROM w/ ongoing diuresis and subsequent decrease in ___ swelling. has improved w/ diuresis and reduction in swelling . # Anemia: pt with downtrending anemia over past few months. studies in ___ showed nl b12 and folate. iron studies c/w anemia of chronic disease. will repeat w/u - send iron studies -> c/w anemia of chronic disease - send b12 and folate -> normal The pt's volume status is an ongoing issue and has been difficult to manage. Although the pt is off a lasix drip currently, she likely has more fluid which can be drawn off. It would be beneficial to continue to gently diurese w/ IV lasix as needed to obtain a net negative of -0.5L per day. Once the pt reaches her dry weight, she can continue on torsemide 100mg daily.
summary: You were admitted for shortness of breath. You were found to be volume overloaded with excessive fluid. Medications were given to draw off fluid from your lungs and body. As you approached your dry weight, your breathing improved. You also had some left knee pain. Orthopedics was asked to evaluate your knee. After tapping your knee, it did not appear as though you had increased inflammation or infection in your joint. This pain was likely related to the swelling in your leg. Over the next 5 days, your goal net negative in volume loss should be around 0.5L. This will be managed by physicians at your rehab. On ___ you should have labs drawn including ___, electrolytes and a CBC.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ F with PMH of dCHF, recent takatsubo's CMP, afib, restrictive lung disease, presents to ED with substernal chest pressure after taking potassium supplements associated once with shortness of breath. # Esophagitis: Clinically noted heartburn right after taking her potassium supplements before bed. Then, her "heartburn" transitioned to chest tightness. She called her PCP and was started on ranitidine without improvement in her symptoms. Then, she was uptitrated to prilosec and avoided acids. During this time as well, she noted difficulty swallowing. Soft foods with thin consistency are tolerated better than solids. She also has epigastric pain and intermittent nausea. She underwent EGD which revealed a gastric ulcer and esophagitis. She was instructed to take omeprazole twice a day for at least 4 weeks and sucralafate. # Afib - continued with metoprolol. Pradaxa held prior to EGD and re-started at discharge. # Restrictive lung disease with baseline 2L O2 requirement - ___ morbid obesity and history of radiation to the chest for breast cancer. Continued on home Bipap while in house with continuous O2 monitoring # CHF - Recently had been down-titrating her lasix dose. Weight at discharge 112.4kg. Discharged on 60mg PO lasix, twice daily. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ------------------- -Follow-up biopsy results - pending at discharge -Check Hpylori serologies, treat if positive -Sucralfate slurry 1gram QID x14 days -BID oral PPI until ___ in ___ weeks -Repeat EGD in ___ weeks -Holding alendronate given evidence of pill esophagitis -Consider decreasing potassium if levels stable given pill esophagitis -Follow-up volume status and assess lasix dosing. Discharge weight 112.4kg
summary: You were admitted to the inpatient cardiology service for shortness of breath and chest tightness. You did not have a heart attack. Your diuretic (Lasix) was switched to 60mg two times a day. While you were here, you reported increased difficulty swallowing with the sensation of food sticking in your throat. We had the gastroenterologists see you while you were in the hospital. They recommended undergoing a procedure called an EGD. This helped us see what was causing you discomfort with swallowing. You were found to have an ulcer in your stomach and irritation of your esophagus. We think this is most likely related to the potassium pills you are taking and possibly your alendronate. In order to help heal the ulcer and the irritation in your esophagus, you will take a medicine called omeprazole, 40mg, two times a day. Also, a medicine called sucralafate will help heal. You should stop taking your alendronate until you discuss with your primary care doctor.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ yo woman with PMHx sig. for dilated cardiomyopathy and mechanical mitral valve on coumadin who presents with epigastric burning, regurgitation, and inability to tolerate both solid and liquid po intake. # Dysphagia: Patient complains of regurgitation. She was evaluted by GI and was started on pantoprazole IV BID. She was bridged with heparin IV drip which was discontinued for no more than 8 hours during which time she had EGD ___. This showed gastritis but no evidence of active bleeding. Multiple biospies were taken and are pending at time of discharge. There was no melena, hemetemasis. Mild decrease in her HCT was observed and was stable. INR was therapeutic at 2.5 at discharge. # Chronic constipation: Admission AXR did not show a bowel obstruction. This was controlled on an aggressive bowel regimen. . # Dilated cardiomyopathy: Her last ECHO showed an LVEF 20%. Given her initially difficult with pills, her carvedilol, enalpril, spironolactone, and digoxin were initially held. Then her pills were crushed with liquids. She is back on her usual regimen. . # Mechanical mitral valve: She was bridged with heparin IV drip. This was held for less than 8 hours without incident. No active bleeding was found, so Warfarin was restarted at 5mg on ___ ___. It was 2.5 on ___. Goal INR is 2.5-3.5. She is instructed to take 4mg tonight, 3mg tomorrow night and then resume normal outpatient regimen of 4mg q ___, 3mg other days. ___ clinic was emailed. ___ will draw INR ___ and call/fax to ___ clinic. . # Atrial fibrillation: Carvedilol was initially held, replaced with IV metoprolol. Her digoxin was initially held as well, then was crushed with liquids. Usual rate controlling meds were reinsituted at their normal levels. . # Anxiety state: She was managed with SL ativan. Social work also helped. Patient indicates she will follow up with her PCP for further discussion regarding getting further help (therapist).
summary: You were admitted for complaints of regurgitation of your food. You had a procedure called endoscopy to look down your esophagus and stomach. This showed inflammation of the lining of the stomach without evidence of bleeding. Biopsies were done and are pending at time of dishcarge. You were bridged with IV heparin to protect your prosthetic heart valve from clotting. Coumadin was re-initiated on ___ and therapeutic (2.5) on ___. You are referred to ___ services for medication check and INR check. Take 4mg of Warfarin tonight, 3mg of warfarin ___ night, then resume the normal 4mg every ___ and 3mg every ___ schedule.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ Y F w/ HTN, hyperlipidemia, SBO, IBS presented ___ with fever 102.3, chills, diarrhea, hypotension and CT demonstrating colitis --> acute diastolic congestive heart failure after agressive fluid repletion in the face of hypotension and diarrhea, resolved with diuresis with nl echo. . The following medical problems were adressed as outlined below: . # Acute diastolic heart failure ___ large IVF repletions leading to pulmonary overload, NOT superimposed onchronic congestive heart failure. - occurred in the setting of agressive fluid repletion in the face of hypotension and diarrhea - was subsequently diuresed ___ per day over the next 3 days before coming off supplemental oxygen - repleted lytes agressively with diuresis - Although we had a good explantation already for acute HF an echo was to r/o WMA's, or valvular dysfunction was done and was completely normal with no WMA's, EF>65%, nl systolic and diastolic function - pt ambulated up stairs prior to d/c without supplemental O2 and CXR clear of fluid . # Infectious colitis - seen on CT scan and had fever to 102.3, abdominal distention, chills, hypotension to sbp80's on admission - thought likely viral given hx and cDiff neg for x 3 but cont flagyl given risk a possible false neg c. diff - diarrhea initially picked up to ___ bm's per day then resolved on day #3 - Blood cxs neg - repleted K agressively - awaiting toxin B on d/c; pls f/u as an outpt . # Hypercholesterolemia - cont statin # Osteoporosis - cont raloxifene # Diet controlled DM - cont diabetic diet # GERD - Cont PPI (switch from prevacid to protonix while inpt) # FEN - ___ diet # Proph - sc heparin, PPI # contact - ___ (HCP) ___
summary: You were admitted and treated for colitis of ascending colon, likely caused by a viral gastroenteritis. You were given antibiotics for a possibel bacterial cause of the gastroenteritis as untreated a bacterial cause could be very severe. Please take flagyl as directed for another 3 days to complete the antibiotic course. You were also given large amounts of IV fluids to prevent hypotension and dehydration at the time you had diarrhea and because of this you had fluid build up in your lung. This was treated with diuretics and you no longer have any fluids in your lungs. Please stop taking Telmisartan since your blood pressure is well controlled without this medication. Please talk to your primary care provider about the utility of taking lipitor and about colon cancer screening prevention.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Ms. ___ is ___ year old woman with a history of paroxysmal atrial fibrillation, cardiomyopathy (thought due to both ischemic and non-ischemic disease), and systolic CHF with an EF of 25% who presented with dyspnea on exertion. Telemetry was significant for wide complex tachycardia. # Wide complex tachycardia: Likely atrial fibrillation with abberrancy. The patient was on amiodarone (200mg daily) as an outpatient - this was increased to 200mg BID upon arrival but returned back to daily prior to discharge. She was continued on metoprolol, but this was decreased to 25mg daily due to lower blood pressure (SBP90-100s). The patient underwent TEE and cardioversion on ___, which converted her back to sinus rhythm. Unfortunately, on ___, patient converted back to atrial fibrillation, which triggered episodes of slow ventricular tachycardia. Because of this, the decision was made to perform atrioventricular junction ablation on ___. As for anticoagulation, pradaxa was discontinued due to her low CrCl (<15). summary: As you recall, you were admitted because you were having difficulty breathing. This was because your heart beat was irregular and fast, which caused your heart to not pump as efficiently. You underwent a procedure called cardioversion to attempt to put your heart back in a regular rhythm. Unfortunately, your heart continued to be irregular. Because of this, you underwent a second procedure, called atrioventricular junction ablation. Your heart rate will now be paced. We also treated you with medications to get the fluid out of your lungs. Because of your irregular heart beat, we started you on coumadin to prevent clot formation and stroke. You will need to get your blood level (INR) checked periodically to make sure the coumadin dose is correct.

text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ was admitted for stroke rule out after presenting with episodic vertigo in the setting of multiple vascular risk factors. Neurologic exam was limited by patient cooperation and pain limitation. MRI was obtained, which showed no acute stroke. His vertigo was therefore felt to be peripheral in etiology, either BPPV or vestibular neuritis, both of which are self-limited and treatable with vestibular ___. Patient was noted to have increased edema consistent with flare of underlying congestive heart failure and was transferred to the medical service for further management. He underwent multiple days of diuresis. During this time, he briefly had toxic metabolic encephalopathy likely a result of hospital delirium (infectious work-up and other reversible causes for delirium unrevealing). His mental status improved and his edema improved. He was discharged to rehabilitation facility for further recuperation. #Vertigo Presented with dizziness with extensive work-up as described by neurology. Diagnosed with vertigo v. vestibular neuritis, per above. #HFrEF: Acute on chronic systolic CHF exacerbation with LVEF 20%. Evidence of moderate decompensation, given edema and weight increase (210lbs on ___ to 225 on ___, though difficult to ascertain true weight given bed measurements). BNP 13.8k. TTE w/ severe global left ventricular hypokinesis (LVEF = ___ %) and moderate mitral regurgitation. Patient diuresed with Lasix 40 mg IV one to two times daily. We continued home carvedilol. Held home lisinopril i/s/o of ___, which was restarted at discharge. Maintained 2L fluid restriction. #Osteomyelitis, Chronic: On Vanc/ertapenem as outpatient. After discussion with AST, he was transitioned to vanc/ceftazidime/flagyl while inpatient, with plan to resume vanc/ertapenem on discharge. Podiatry consulted to reassess weight-bearing status and for wound management. #Acute Renal Failure: He was noted to have an ARF with Cr 1.2 from baseline 0.6 on admission. It did not improve after 500cc fluid bolus. Cr rose on ___ to 1.5. Medicine was consulted for workup and management. Likely due to cardiorenal syndrome, with contribution from vanc toxicity and contrast insult. Renal u/s without evidence of obstruction. Vanc trough supratheraputic and adjusted. Lisinopril held then restarted on discharge. #Afib: Pt on dofetilide as outpatient. Given ___, after discussion with EP, neurology team held medication on admission. Will need re-initation as appropriate as outpatient. #T2D: -held home glimepiride with insulin sliding scale while inpatient #Ear lesion: Concerning for basal cell carcinoma. -outpatient follow up CHRONIC: ======== #CAD: -continue home pravastatin and clopidogrel #HTN: -holding home lisinopril #GERD: -continue home omeprazole =========================================== Transitional Issues: -Vestibular ___ if still symptomatic on discharge. -ID follow-up for R calcaneal osteomyelitis, on ertapenem/vanc six-week course (started on ___, projected to end on ___ -Podiatry f/u for osteomyelitis and ulcers in one week -On warfarin for afib, target INR ___ -Vancomycin dose adjusted while inpatient, currently 500mg IV Q24h needs trough before 4th dose on ___ -Cardiology f/u as an outpatient to consider restarting dofetilide for pAfib as need (normocardic as inpatient) -Dermatology f/u for R ear lesion concerning for basal cell carcinoma - Renal cyst seen on imaging, requires repeat renal US in 6 months, letter sent to patients house as a reminder -HCP: Daughter ___ ___ -Full code ***LAST INPATIENT BED WEIGHT: ___ 98.43 kg 217 lb ***
summary: You were hospitalized due to symptoms of dizziness. We were initially worried you may have had a stroke, but you DID NOT have a stroke. Your dizziness is coming from a problem with the inner ear, which will get better with time and physical therapy. While you were in the hospital you had worsening of your heart function, which caused water to accumulate in your body. You were treated with an medication through your IV to help your body eliminate this extra water. The podiatry service examined your foot ulcers to ensure they were healing properly. You were kept on strong antibiotics through your IV in order to treat the infection in your R heel bone. You were noted to have a lesion in your R ear that is concerning for skin cancer and will need to see a dermatologist as an outpatient.

text: Brief Hospital Course: 1. Dyspnea- Patient presented with recent diagnosis of pneumonia while hospitalized from ___. Started on antibiotics prior to admission (levoquin) and then got vanc/ceftaz/azithromycin initially on admission before switching back to levoquin. Last dose of levoquin was on ___- patient was stable. Possible etiologies of shortness of breath are pneumonia, fluid overload in the setting of diastolic HF or CO2 retention. Chest x-ray showed basilar atelectasis and pleural effusions. Patient started on levoquin and vancomycin. BNP- 3013. Placed on 4L O2 NC and was satting well. Had episode of desaturation to 70% on 4L and was triggered. Was briefly placed on NRB to get sats back up. Then placed on 4L O2 NC and 35% facemask. Stabilzed and satted well throughout the night. ABG drawn after trigger event- pO2 93, pCO2, pH- 7.26. Showed CO2 retention. She was also given Lasix 20mg IV x 1 for gentle diuresis with the goal of -500ml-1000ml fluid balance. Patient's symptoms did not improve despite our interventions. She remained comfortable on the 4L O2/35% face mask. 2. Chest pain- Patient has history of diastolic CHF, afib (on aspirin for anticoagulation- was on coumadin but patient is high risk for falls), and RVR (rate controlled on metoprolol). EKG and cardiac enzymes done. All three sets of enzymes are negative. TTE performed in ___ showed "Normal biventricular cavity sizes with preserved global and regional biventricular systolic function. Mild pulmonary artery systolic hypertension. Dilated ascending aorta. Compared with the prior study (images reviewed) of ___, the findings are similar." Patient was monitored on telemetry overnight. Telemetry was discontinued the following morning following discussion with patient's family regarding goals of care. 3. Altered mental status- Patient had extensive history of AMS and mild dementia. She was not currently on any sedating medications. Considered toxic/metabolic and infectious causes. Cultures have been drawn. Patient not febrile. No elevated WBC count but patient did have a left-shift (N- 83.7). Could related to pneumonia or other infection. Also, may be ___ CO2 narcosis/retention. We kept the patient on IV medications and NPO. Giving ABG results showing high level of CO2 retention, it is thought that the patient's mental status is due to that primarily. 4. Chronic diastolic HF - Last ECHO ___, preserved EF. CXR with effusions consistent with fluid overload. BNP of 3013 on this admission. We gently diuresed patient with 20mg lasix IV one time per day before comfort care measures were implemented. There was minimal symptomatic improvement after diuresis. 5. Afib- Was rate-controlled with metoprolol. Was monitored on telemetry overnight the first night. Anticoagulated with aspirin (warfarin discontinued a few months ago due to fall risk). 6. Goals of care: ___ admissions in last 3 months. Patient is DNR/DNI. Goals of care were discussed with attending physician and family. Daughter ___ is patient's proxy. After a extensive discussion, her daughter decided to pursue comfort measures only with the goal of sending the patient home with hospice care. This was a decision the family was comfortable with. All medications, vital sign monitoring and lab draws were discontinued. Patient was started on IV morphine PRN pain and IV ativan PRN anxiety. Palliative care was consulted and case managers set up hospice services for the patient.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for shortness of breath and altered mental status. While in the hospital your oxygen sats were low and you were temporarily placed on a 100% non-rebreather to raise your oxygen saturations. After you were stabilized, we placed you back on the 4L nasal cannula and added a 35% face mask for additional oxygenation. You also required some gentle diuresis to remove some of the fluid from your lungs. Despite our interventions, your condition did not improve to the degree we would have liked. We discussed goals of care with your daughter ___. After a lengthy discussion, it was decided that your comfort was the most important priority.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with Diabetes, CKD (stage IV), SSS s/p AV pacemaker, CHF, HTN admitted for altered mental status with FSBG 20 in the field in the setting of insulin administration without checking FSBG with good response to D50. . #Hypoglycemia: Presented with altered mental status, found by EMS to have finger stick glucose of 20. Became alert after receiving D50 with FSBG rising to 178. Patient's hypoglycemia was most likely secondary to incorrect administrations of sliding scale insulin, failure to monitor blood sugars, poor social situation (see below), and exacerbated by poor renal clearance in the setting of her stage IV CKD. Patient admitted to taking unknown amounts of insulin novalog three times a day and 25 units of lantus at night; however she has not measured her finger stick glucose for the past 9 months. Without clear infectious exacerbant with no fever or leukocytosis. Morning cortisol of 17.2. Her fingersticks were monitored off of insulin, and were always within a range from 80-180s, while eating a diabetic diet in house. Her long acting insulin was therefore discontinued due to unfavorable risk/benefit analysis. Patient was transferred to ___ with only sliding scale insulin. She will follow up with PCP for diabetes care going forward. . #CKD: Patient has history of CKD most likely secondary to DM and/or HTN. Creatinine of 3.1 on admission. Patient's electrolytes were within normal limits, her urine lytes were remarkable. Per patient, her old nephrologist moved to a different practice and she has not seen a nephrologist in few months. Patient's PCP ___ would like to introduce patient to a new nephrologist in the ___ practice during her upcoming PCP ___. . #Unsafe Living Conditions: EMS reported finding patient in poor living situation with too much clutter to even take the strecher into her apartment. Physical therapy, social work/ and case management were consulted to evaluate patient's safety to return to her home. Per ___ evaluation, patient had unstable gait and recommended rehab vs nursing home. Social work spoke with patient daughter and discovered that patient has been hoarding plastic cups and paper for the past ___ years which makes her living conditions unsafe. Patient initially refused to go to rehab but after thinking over it agreed to go to rehab. Patient is being discharged to ___ House rehab. While she is rehab, social services at patient's apartment will arrange to clean her house before she moves in again. . #HTN: Hypertensive to 180/100 on admission in the setting of running out of her home hypertensive medications for the last week. She was continued on her 10mg amlodopine. Lisinopril was initially held but restarted at the time of discharge with good BP control. Atenolol was discontinued. . #CHF: CXR demonstrated mild interstitial pulmonary edema, most likely secondary to diastolic dysfunction from her chronic hypertension. Largely appeared euvolemic on exam. EKG on admission demonstrated normal sinus rhythm. Lasix restarted on a 3x/week schedule. . #CAD: SSS s/p av pacemaker. EKG without ischemic changes. She was continued on medical management. . Transitional Issues: -Patient will require finger stick checks with each meal to determine need for sliding scale insulin. Patient will also benefit from education about finger sticks and proper way to adminster sliding scale insulin. -___ rehab patient should get her electolytes checked on ___ couple of days after reinitiation of her Lasix. -After discharge from rehab, patient will need outpatient appointment with her PCP who will then determine the approriate therapy for patient diabetes. -PCP ___ then make arrangement for outpatient nephrology referal for patient during their appointment.
summary: You were admitted to the hospital because you were found by your daughter at your apartment not responding and confused. EMS who came to the scene measured your finger stick glucose to be 20 which is a dangerously low value. After receiving D50 (high glucose solution), your blood sugar came back up to normal and ___ became more alert and awake. In the hospital your blood sugars were in the 90-180 range, and ___ did not require any insulin. Therefore your low blood sugar was most likely caused by giving yourself too much insulin without measuring your finger stick glucose regularly. ___ also had additional tests, which ruled out some of the other causes for low blood sugar, such as adrenal insufficiency. Because of future risk of low blood sugar you were discharged without any long acting insulin. Please continue sliding scale at rehab.
