text: Brief Hospital Course: Mr. ___ is a ___ year old male with PMH of CAD with 3VD s/p 4 vessel CABG in ___, s/p PCI in ___ with successful stenting to SVG-LAD and SVG-RCA, s/p PCI in ___ with unsuccessful attempt to recanalize occluded OM1, and s/p left CEA in ___ who presented with new onset orthopnea and DOE with imaging and clinical exam suggestive of acute CHF exacerbation. . #. Acute CHF exacerbation: The patient presented with orthopnea and dyspnea on exertion with CXR evidence of mild pulmonary vascular congestion. His physical exam was consistent with volume overload with bibasilar crackles and elevated JVP. He required 3 liters of oxygen via nasal cannula on admission to maintain his saturation on admission. The patient is not on Lasix at home and per his report had never been SOB requiring Lasix. The etiology of his new onset CHF is most likely secondary to chronically progressive ischemic cardiomyopathy. His EKG had some non-specific ST changes and troponin=0.02 on admission which was not concerning for acute ischemia. The patient was diuresed a total of 2.5 liters during his length of stay with dramatic symptomatic improvement and resolution of his oxygen requirement. He will be started on standing Lasix 20mg PO daily at home. An echocardiogram should be performed as an outpatient to re-evaluate his ejection fraction. . #. Chest Pain: The patient has stable angina at baseline. He did have one of his typical episodes of chest pain as an inpatient after some exertion. The chest pain responded to nitroglycerin and was associated with subtle EKG changes and a troponin bump to 0.03. The patient is not a candidate for further intervention based on his last cath report. ECG showed ST depressions laterally and LCx is known to have 90% stenosis with prior cath unsuccessful at stenting of OM1. An acute coronary event was ruled out with serial cardiac enzymes. He will continue on aspirin 325mg and was monitored on telemetry with no events throughout his stay. His home Atenolol and Captopril doses were continued. A lipid panel was checked and there was no need to adjust his statin dosing. . #. GERD: The patient's home Prilosec was continued. . # CODE STATUS: The patient's code status was confirmed as full code for this admission. . # CONTACT: ___ ___
summary: You were admitted to ___ for evaluation of increased shortness of breath. You were found to have a condition called congestive heart failure or CHF. This means that your body has retained too much fluid and some of it has backed up into your lungs causing you to become short of breath. You were given a medication called furosemide which helps you urinate all of the extra fluid out of your body. You will take this same medication at home on a daily basis to prevent this from happening again in the future. You should try to adhere to a low sodium diet because a diet high in salt makes your retain more fluid. You should also weigh yourself on a daily basis to make sure that you are not gaining extra water weight.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ with ESRD on home HD, HFpEF, CAD, AS and MS ___ CABG + AVR with mechanical valve, on coumadin, who presents with ___ days of dyspnea on exertion, hypoxia, and dark tarry stools, found to have guaiac positive DRE and hct of 24 in the ED, c/w upper GIB. Received a total of 3 units PRBC before H/H stabilized. # Acute blood loss anemia secondary to GIB: EGD and c-scope in MICU without obvious source of bleeding. Given AS and ESRD, higher risk for AVMs. We did not reverse INR d/t mechanical aortic valve. Her warfarin was held and a heparin gtt was started until her H/H stabilized. We then restarted warfarin. GI followed. A capsule study was completed and did not show any signs of an actively bleeding lesion. Continued pantoprazole 40 mg PO daily. # ESRD on dialysis: Recently transitioned to 5x/week dialysis. Renal has been following with TTS HD while in house. Continued prednisone 5 PO daily. Per renal, calcium acetate with meals. To have dialysis on ___ prior to resuming home HD. # ___ AVR with mechanical valve: Goal INR is 2.5-3.5, not reversible even in setting of GIB. Warfarin initially held with heparin gtt due to GIB. Restarted warfarin with heparin gtt once stable. INR trended up to 2.2 prior to discharge (slightly below goal) but patient requested discharge with the understanding that she is at increased for thromboembolic event. Patient to have close follow up of INR. # HTN: per renal physician and patient, her BPs are very labile and run high. They decreased significantly with 5x/wk HD per patient and her home BP meds were stopped. Started Labetalol 200 mg TID and increased to 300 mg TID. # Chronic diastolic heart failure with EF 60% in ___: no evidence of volume overload on exam, says she is 3kg up from her dry weight. Dialysis as above. # PVD: ___ Right BKA and L toe amuptations. - Holding ASA while GIB. Restarted on discharge. - Continued statin, gabapentin, held oxycontin - Seen by vascular in house for guidance on prosthetic use # HLD: Continued gemfibrozil, statin. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: [ ] Check INR on ___ at HD, send results to Dr. ___ at fax ___. Goal 2.5-3.5 in setting of AVR with prior hx. of stroke [ ] home BP medication titration per outpatient nephrologist [ ] repeat colonoscopy in ___ years with Dr. ___ # Communication: Patient and husband (___) # Code: Full
summary: You were admitted to the hospital for blood in your bowel movements. ___ required blood transfusions and our gastroenterologists did an EGD (a scope of your stomach and upper intestine), a colonoscopy, and a capsule study which took pictures of your small intestine. None of these studies identified a site of bleeding. It is most likely that ___ had an blood vessel in your intestine that started bleeding and stopped by itself. While ___ were bleeding we stopped your warfarin and gave ___ IV heparin to keep your blood thinned. We transitioned ___ back onto warfarin but your INR is still less than the goal range of 2.5-3.5. Despite this and knowing that ___ are at a slightly increased stroke risk, ___ requested to be discharged. ___ should take 7 mg of warfarin tomorrow and have your INR checked at dialysis on ___ and send the results to Dr. ___. We continued your dialysis while ___ were in the hospital. Your next dialysis day is on ___ and after that they will arrange to start your home dialysis. Your blood pressure was high while you were here and we restarted your blood pressure medications. ___ should continue to take this until ___ can have your blood pressure checked with home dialysis.

text: Brief Hospital Course: This ___ with h/o afib on warfarin, CAD, AS, and GIV presented with 2 dark BM worked up for GI bleed with complicated hospital course by urosepsis. . # GI bleed: Admitted with 2 dark guiac positive stools on coumadin. Had been worked up prior for massive GI bleeds with no etiology found. Hct trended down slightly, HD stable, GI was consulted with plan to scope him. Cdiff negative stools. Started IV PPI BID, held coumadin. Initially q6 hcts, 2 large bore IVs, maintained active T+S. Given 2 units of PRBCs and ___ FFP prior to scope to reverese INR. First scope attempt was ___ but ___ developed acute back pain. Likely ___ renal stones, UTI, early presentation of sepsis. Transferred to MICU for sepsis treatment as below. When called out of MICU, underwent colonoscopy which showed two polyps that were removed and an EGD which showed dulefoy lesion likely accounting for guaic positive stools, subsequently clipped. Hct stable post procedure. Changed PO PPI 40mg to daily. . #Bacteremia/hypotension: Most likely secondary to E coli urosepsis(Blood and urine cultures positive) ___ transferred to MICU where CVL was placed and Ceftriaxone was started. Hypotension resolved with IVF resuscitation. There was concern over pneumonia on CXR but the L lung base opacity was stable and more likely to represent atelectasis vs. small pleural effusion. He does have any upper resp symptoms or fever with improving leukocytosis so more likely to be atelectasis, possibly volume overload more likely than pneumonia. Ceftrixone would cover most PNA bacteria. Survellience Blood cultures negative to date. Switched to PO levaquin to complete 2 week course. . # CHF: Echo from ___ LVEF 55%, slightly volume overloaded by exam, BNP in 6000s which is at baseline. Repeat echo "the ventricle is less vigorous, but without definite regional dysfunction. The severity of aortic stenosis, aortic regurgitation, and mitral regurgitation are similar" EF 50-55%, held lasix. Monitored I/Os, monitored daily weights. Remained mostly euvolemic except some trace pretibial edema which improved with elevation. . # Blue fingers: likely vasoconstriction, concern for cold blue but blanching fingers for ischemia, but no signs of embolic lesions, consider cryoglobulinemia which was pending upon discharge. HEPC VL not detected. Gloves for comfort. . #CKD: ___ has Cr baseline 1.5, continued gentle hydration, renally dosed meds, followed urine output. Held lasix and can be restarted as outpatient. . #Afib:rate controlled without nodal agents, previously on coumadin, tele picking up ~2 sec pauses. EP consulted for concern over pacemaker but will hold off for now as he is asymptomatic and pauses <3sec. Will follow with cards as outpatient. Coumadin was discontinued indefinitely as he now has GI bled multiple times. . #CAD: no chest pain, no acute EKG change, cardiac enzymes stable, monitored on tele. . #BPH: continued home medication regimen . #hypothyroid: TSH elevated, followed as outpatient, continued current synthroid at current dose. . #General Care: IVF for gentle hydration, replete lytes prn, clear cardiac diet advanced to regular after GI bleed stabilized, PPX: PPI, pneumoboots, ACCESS: PIV, CVL into R IJ, CODE: full, confirmed, CONTACT: dtr ___ ___, discharged home with ___.
summary: You were admitted after your had a large dark bowel movement which was concerning for bleeding from your GI tract. We monitored you and had the gastroenterologists follow you. We checked your stool and made sure you did not have C.Difficle in your stool. After another dark bowel movement, the GI doctors decided to ___ in side your stomach to see if there was any evidence of bleed. They found a small area in your upper GI tract that may have explained your symptoms. You also had 2 polyps removed on your colonoscopy. We then monitored your blood counts and found you to be stable. We also had the cardiologist come assess you because of your slow rhythm.

text: Brief Hospital Course: ___ w/ disastolic CHF, valvular disease (severe TR ___ pacemaker wire) and resultant RV dilation, persistent Afib (c/b SSS; s/p ablation, PPM; on no A/C ___ ICH), cerebral amyloid angiopathy (s/p ICH ___ seizure precipitated by same), moderate dementia presenting with several days of malaise and episodic dyspnea consistent with acute-on-chronic diastolic heart failure exacerbation. #Acute-on-chronic dCHF exacerbation, complicated by severe TR ___ ___ wire) with resultant RV dilation. On initial exam mildly volume up (bilateral rales without peripheral edema). TTE showed EF 55-60%, severe TR with worsened RV dilation from prior. She was diuresed lasix 100mg IV x 2 and home torsemide dose was increased from 60mg to 40mg daily. Home lisinopril 2.5mg was stopped as her BPs were marginal and it was felt that she would gain greater benefit from the pulmonary vasodilatory activity of amlodipine in the setting of severe TR and a dilated RV. She was continued on metoprolol 50mg. Weight at discharge 66.6kg #Persistent AFib: Patient has history of Afib. Anticoagulation was discontinued ___ d/t risk of ICH in setting of cerebral amyloid angiopathy. ___ interrogation ___ showed normal function and leads, no events. As above, continued home metop 50 qHS #Dementia/cerebral amyloid angiopathy: continued home memantine, keppra. #DM II: Continued on home glipizide. TRANSITIONAL ISSUES: ==================== - continue torsemide 60mg daily (increased dose) - home lisinopril was discontinued - dry weight/weight at discharge: 66.6kg # Contacts: ___ ___ (daughter/HCP); ___ ___ ___ (daughter/HCP) # Code: no intubation, OK to shock, confirmed w/ ___ on ___
summary: Your mother was hospitalized with shortness of breath, which we determined was due to an exacerbation of her diastolic heart failure from severe tricuspid valve disease. We treated her with IV diuretics and increased her daily dose of torsemide from 40mg to 60mg. We hope that an increased diuretic dose will prevent her from accumulating fluid in the future. The best way to determine that she is on the right dose of medication is to weigh her frequently. Her weight on leaving the hospital is 146.8 pounds, which we consider to be her "dry weight." If she gains more than 3lbs, please get in touch with her cardiologists who will adjust her medications accordingly.
